posts.summary: Jeff Atwood presents his curated reading list for software developers, emphasizing that the best programming books focus on understanding people rather than technology because computers change rapidly but human nature remains constant. The list spans software construction (Code Complete), project management (Mythical Man-Month, Rapid Development), team dynamics (Peopleware), usability and design (Don't Make Me Think, About Face, Design of Everyday Things), and technical craftsmanship (Programming Pearls, Pragmatic Programmer). Atwood notes that the average programmer reads less than one technical book per year, so simply reading these books sets developers apart. The underlying theme is that software development failures stem more from human factors—poor communication, classic mistakes, and misunderstanding users—than from technical limitations.
key_points: The best programming books focus on understanding people, not technology, because computers change but humans don't
Most software projects fail due to repeating classic mistakes rather than encountering new technical challenges
Developers are 'Homo Logicus'—freakish extreme users who mistakenly believe they can make usability decisions for regular users
Reading even one technical book per year puts you ahead of 90% of developers
Usability problems are best solved through quick, dirty testing with real data rather than endless debates
money_quotes: Computers may change, but people don't.
The average programmer reads less than one technical book per year. The very act of reading this book already sets you apart from probably ninety percent of your fellow developers.

The vast majority of software development projects will fail: they will overrun their schedules, produce substandard results, or sometimes not even finish at all. This isn't an argument; it's a statistical fact.
Half of success is not repeating the same mistakes you, or other people, have made.
It doesn't matter how many 'coding superstars' you've got when none of them can talk to each other, or agree on anything.
Developers are freakish, extreme users at best—'Homo Logicus' versus 'Homo Sapiens.'
Designing software is difficult, to be sure, but designing a door is difficult too.
Remember, absolute power corrupts absolutely. But it also rocks absolutely.
posts.key_insight: To build better software, invest in understanding human behavior and avoiding classic mistakes rather than chasing the latest technical trends.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains his decision to use Movable Type for his new blog rather than building custom software, despite being a .NET developer. He chose the Perl-based solution because it was the easiest to get running, valuing pragmatism over technological purity. The post provides a technical walkthrough for installing Movable Type on Windows Server 2003, including setting up ActiveState Perl, MySQL, and resolving a missing DBI module issue. This inaugural Coding Horror post establishes Atwood's practical, results-oriented approach to software.
key_points: Chose existing blog software over building his own because 'life's too short' and 'never write what you can steal'
Selected Perl-based Movable Type over .NET solutions purely because it was easier to set up
Documents the friction of running Perl/CGI on Windows Server 2003's locked-down security model
Encountered and solved a missing DBI Perl module dependency not mentioned in documentation
Demonstrates pragmatic tool selection based on results rather than technology allegiance
money_quotes: Writing code all day sort of saps my will to come home and... write more code.
Life's just too short, and besides, never write what you can steal – right?
I suspect the .NET solutions may have been more advanced, but this is the one that was easiest to get running. Easy is good.
Also, I'm really lazy.
Note that Server 2003 has enhanced 'lockdown' security out of the box – meaning, it won't run jack squat without manual intervention.
posts.key_insight: The best tool is often the one that works, not the one that matches your technical identity or offers the most features.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood addresses the surprising lack of a good generic .NET About Box component and shares his own solution. He identifies two distinct audiences for About Boxes: users who need basic application identification, and developers who need detailed diagnostic information for troubleshooting. His solution features a tiered dialog with simple and complex modes to serve both needs. The post emphasizes that while About Boxes aren't used daily, they become critical when troubleshooting deployed code.
key_points: About Boxes serve two distinct audiences: users need basic identification (application name, version, creator), while developers need detailed diagnostic information for troubleshooting
A tiered approach with simple and complex modes elegantly addresses both user and developer needs without overwhelming either
Application identity information can be derived from AssemblyInfo files, which mirrors what users see in file properties
Decorative About Boxes that lack diagnostic information are not helpful—form should follow function
If you're checking the About Box frequently, that indicates deeper problems in your development or deployment process
money_quotes: Dogtags for your application, if you will.
If you're whipping out dogtags on a regular basis, that's symptomatic of a deeper problem.
The About Box is not meant to be used every day, but when you need it, it can be a lifesaver.
Decorative About Boxes are not helpful.
It's OK to be used infrequently, but it also needs to provide decent diagnostic info.
posts.key_insight: Good developer tools should serve multiple audiences with appropriate levels of detail, hiding complexity from casual users while making diagnostic information accessible when needed.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines CPU benchmark data for Visual Studio .NET compilation performance, comparing various processors of the era. After being spoiled by the near-instant compile times of VB6 and classic ASP, he investigates which CPUs handle .NET compilation best. The analysis reveals the AMD Athlon 64 dramatically outperforming Intel processors, likely due to its on-die memory controller and L2 cache advantages rather than raw clock speed. He concludes that the performance difference between CPUs is significant enough that upgrading hardware could be a worthwhile investment for developers who compile frequently.
key_points: The AMD Athlon 64 significantly outperforms Intel Pentium 4 processors in compilation benchmarks despite running at lower clock speeds
L2 cache size and on-die memory controller appear more important than clock speed for compilation performance
The fastest CPU compiles debug releases almost twice as fast as a 'slow' Pentium 4 3.0GHz
Developer time has monetary value, making CPU upgrades potentially cost-effective for heavy compilation workloads
CPU benchmarks rarely include development-related tasks, making this data particularly valuable
money_quotes: After working with VB6 and 'classic' ASP for so long, I got spoiled with effectively nonexistent compile times.
The Athlon 64 is absolutely mopping the floor with everything else.
It certainly can't be clock speed, since the Athlon 64 runs at ~ 2ghz.
The fastest CPU compiles debug releases almost twice as fast as the 'slowest' CPU.
Depending on how much you compile, and how much your time is worth, an Athlon 64 upgrade could pay for itself in no time.
posts.key_insight: For compilation-heavy development work, memory architecture matters more than clock speed, and investing in the right hardware can meaningfully improve developer productivity.

posts.summary: This brief post shares an article analyzing the comments found in leaked Windows NT/2000 source code. Atwood highlights the insight that self-deprecating comments like 'UGLY TERRIBLE HACK' are actually indicators of good code and conscientious developers, since poor codebases treat hacks as normal and unremarkable. The post suggests that all programmers under deadline pressure write comments they might regret, making it hypocritical to judge others for the same behavior.
key_points: Comments warning about 'ugly terrible hacks' indicate good code, not bad—because bad code treats hacks as unremarkable
All programmers under deadline pressure write comments they might later regret
Conscientious coders leave prominent warnings about tricky parts of code
Searching leaked code for embarrassing comments is hypocritical but entertaining
money_quotes: Comments like 'UGLY TERRIBLE HACK' tend to indicate good code rather than bad: in bad code ugly terrible hacks are considered par for the course.
In the struggle to meet deadlines, I think pretty much all programmers have put in comments they might later regret, including swearwords and acerbic comments about other code or requirements.
It would therefore be both hypocritical and meaningless to go through the comments looking for embarrassments. But also fun, so let's go.
posts.key_insight: Self-critical comments in code are a sign of conscientious developers who recognize technical debt, not evidence of poor engineering.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses the TiVo remote as a case study in exceptional industrial design, sharing a New York Times article that details how TiVo's design team created their iconic peanut-shaped remote in just 14 weeks. The piece reveals the iterative design process involving hundreds of sketches, foam prototypes, and extensive user testing that led to features like the famous thumbs-up/thumbs-down buttons and the oversized yellow pause button. Atwood frames this as a cautionary lesson for software developers: if manufacturers still struggle with usability on something as simple as a TV remote after fifty years, complex software faces an even greater challenge. The article highlights how TiVo's success came from treating the remote as a first-class design problem rather than an engineering afterthought.
key_points: TiVo represents one of the best out-of-box consumer product experiences due to its combination of intuitive interface and thoughtfully designed remote control
The TiVo remote was designed in 14 weeks using rapid prototyping ('early, ugly and often'), with designers encouraged to 'go off and scare' the lead designer
Traditional remotes fail because they were designed by engineers for young people with good eyesight in bright light, overloaded with rarely-used features
The design team fought 'bloody battles' over buttons, managing to hold the count to 30 despite the device's many functions—a discipline most manufacturers lack
Even excellent designs have room for improvement, as the remote's low-contrast labels and tendency to get lost in sofas demonstrate
money_quotes: If we haven't nailed usability for something as simple as a television remote by now – clearly we've got our work cut out for us to make complex software usable.
Buttons proliferate on remotes like rabbits.
There tends to be this conservatism in the design process. I encourage young designers to go off and scare me.
They were designed by – and I hate to say it because I am one of them – engineers.
These are the devilish details that often get overlooked.
I don't think that you reach that level of simple elegance by accident. It's designed the way remotes should be designed.
They work well if you're sitting in bright light and you have good eyesight and you're 20 or 30 years old.
posts.key_insight: If fifty years of iteration haven't produced consistently usable TV remotes, achieving usability in complex software requires deliberate, user-centered design work—not engineering instinct alone.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his heavy daily use of the basic Windows Notepad and his discovery of Notepad2 as a superior replacement. Despite Notepad's severely limited functionality, it had remained a core part of his developer toolkit for years. Notepad2 maintains the original's philosophy of being small and fast while adding substantial features like syntax highlighting for numerous languages, regular expressions, and Unicode support. The post celebrates finding a tool that enhances functionality without sacrificing the simplicity that made the original valuable.
key_points: Notepad.exe, despite its limitations, is used dozens of times daily as a core developer tool
Notepad2 adds substantial features while only increasing size from ~100kb to 550kb
The best tools follow the principle of being small, fast, and without unnecessary frills
Key additions include syntax highlighting for 20+ languages, regex search, and Unicode support
money_quotes: Given the severely limited functionality of Notepad, it's incredible that it has taken me this long to find a suitable replacement for what is, evidently, a core part of my developer toolkit.
The original Notepad shipped with Windows is probably the handiest program of all times, small, fast, without frills!
Look at everything you get for that additional 450kb.
posts.key_insight: The best developer tools maintain simplicity and speed while thoughtfully adding functionality—proving that lightweight doesn't have to mean featureless.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces an update to his About Box sample code, which he submitted to Code Project. He explains why he uses this About Box component in every application he writes: it provides developers with crucial debugging information about loaded assemblies and their locations. This is especially valuable in smart client scenarios where assemblies come from multiple sources like HTTP downloads and the GAC.
key_points: The About Box sample has been updated with a simplified tab interface
Knowing which assemblies are loaded and their source locations is valuable debugging information
Smart client applications complicate assembly management with multiple sources (HTTP, GAC)
Developers often fail to keep shared controls up to date, making assembly tracking important
money_quotes: As a developer, it's always helpful to know exactly what assemblies you're loading, and where they are coming from.
posts.key_insight: A well-designed About Box serves as more than vanity—it's a diagnostic tool that helps developers understand their application's runtime assembly composition.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines how Moore's Law has created an unintended consequence: as hardware costs plummet, software's relative cost has skyrocketed from 5% to 20% of a computer's total price. He explores Microsoft's response through tiered geographic pricing, offering drastically reduced prices in markets like Thailand, and draws parallels to the pharmaceutical industry's controversial pricing strategies. Atwood argues that this approach, while economically rational, is deeply unpopular and increasingly difficult to enforce in a connected global market. He predicts that software will face the same 'grey market' pressures that have plagued drug companies, as consumers resist paying ten times more for identical products based solely on geography.
key_points: Moore's Law has inverted the hardware/software cost ratio—software that was 5% of system cost in 1997 became 20% by 2004
Microsoft's geographic pricing (offering Windows XP + Office XP for $38 in Thailand vs. hundreds in the US) mirrors pharmaceutical industry tactics
Tiered pricing strategies are profoundly unpopular and increasingly difficult to enforce in the internet age
The same grey market pressures affecting pharmaceutical companies will inevitably affect software
Linux emerges as one natural response to the question of what happens when software can never be free
money_quotes: As hardware becomes cheaper, software becomes more expensive.
What if software was never free? One answer, obviously: Linux.
Ignorance is bliss, at least, when it comes to someone in Thailand paying less than one-tenth what I am for Office XP and Windows XP.
In a global market, it's inevitable.
It was OK for a retail copy of Windows to cost $99 when computers regularly cost $2,000 to build, circa 1997. That's only five percent of the total cost.
I don't see any reason why, given a strongly tiered per-country pricing structure, the same problems (and pressures) won't come up in software, too.
posts.key_insight: Geographic price discrimination for software, while economically logical, is unsustainable in a globally connected market and will inevitably face the same grey market pressures as the pharmaceutical industry.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood makes a strong case for multi-monitor setups as essential productivity tools for developers, drawing on survey data and personal experience. He notes that developers universally prefer two monitors over one larger screen, particularly for programming and debugging tasks. The post provides practical guidance for moving from dual to triple monitor configurations, including hardware compatibility considerations. Atwood observes diminishing returns beyond three monitors and highlights UltraMon's per-monitor taskbar feature as transformative for managing multiple windows.
key_points: Survey results show developers unanimously prefer dual monitors over single larger displays, especially for programming and debugging workflows
The barrier to multi-monitor adoption has dropped significantly due to affordable dual-head video cards, reasonable LCD prices, and mature Windows XP support
Moving from two to three monitors provides incremental benefit but with diminishing returns - four monitors may require too much head movement
Hardware compatibility can be problematic when mixing video cards from different vendors on different buses
Per-monitor taskbars (via UltraMon) dramatically improve window management by showing only relevant windows on each display
money_quotes: This is really a no-brainer for any developer who values his or her time.
Nobody answered that they preferred 1 monitor over 2 even a little bit.
All the 'extra stuff' I couldn't fit on the primary or secondary panels finally found a home on the third one.
I think the rule of diminishing returns will definitely kick in for more than three.
I'm also wondering whether I could physically see four monitors without moving my head around a bunch more than I normally do.
I had no idea how significant this feature was until I tried it. It's huge!
posts.key_insight: Multiple monitors are an obvious productivity investment for developers, with dual displays being the sweet spot and diminishing returns setting in beyond three screens.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the ElseIf construct is overused and harmful, comparing it unfavorably to GOTO. He demonstrates how experienced programmers repeatedly choose chains of ElseIf statements when a Select Case (switch) statement would be cleaner and more readable. The post highlights that Select Case naturally encourages handling edge cases through its Case Else clause, while ElseIf chains often leave unexpected conditions unhandled. Atwood considers ElseIf destructive outside of very specialized niches.
key_points: ElseIf chains are frequently used where Select Case would be clearer and more appropriate
This anti-pattern appears consistently across many experienced programmers, not just beginners
Select Case offers Case Else which encourages proper handling of unknown conditions
ElseIf chains often silently ignore edge cases like unexpected return values
The author considers ElseIf comparable to GOTO in its potential for misuse
money_quotes: I think the ELSEIF keyword is destructive and, like GOTO, has no good use outside of a very specialized niche.
ELSEIF, in my experience, is abused far more than it is used properly.
I have a very hard time coming up with any valid justification for the use of ELSEIF.
98 times out of 100, SELECT CASE is easier to read and offers the CASE ELSE condition, which encourages developers to handle unknown conditions properly.
In the above example, what happens when the dialog returns DialogResult.Cancel?
posts.key_insight: Switch/case statements are almost always preferable to ElseIf chains because they're more readable and naturally prompt developers to handle unexpected conditions.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood introduces a set of classes for handling exceptions with a user-friendly interface, inspired by Alan Cooper's 'About Face' book on UI design. The solution automatically captures screenshots, diagnostic information, and notifies developers via email when unhandled exceptions occur. Atwood emphasizes the value of creating a tight feedback loop between developers and exceptions to enable quick fixes. He also shares a tip about using an old GIF tool for creating animated screenshots for documentation.
key_points: Exception handling should present a consistent, user-friendly interface rather than cryptic error messages
Automatic capture of screenshots and diagnostic info at exception time aids debugging
Emailing exceptions to the entire team creates both accountability and quick response times
A tight feedback loop between developers and exceptions facilitates responsive bug fixes
money_quotes: There's nothing like an extremely tight loop between developers and exceptions to facilitate quick, responsive fixes to broken code.
And since we have it set up to email every team member for every Unhandled Exception – it's a therapeutic form of punishment, too!
I have used the hell out of this code and it works like gangbusters.
posts.key_insight: Treating exception handling as a user experience problem and creating tight developer feedback loops leads to better software quality.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents the process of debugging a mysterious production deadlock in ASP.NET, where the ASPNET_WP process would randomly become unresponsive multiple times daily. After exhausting standard troubleshooting, he turned to Microsoft's advanced debugging tools (WinDbg and SOS.dll) to capture and analyze memory dumps. By examining thread states and call stacks, he discovered that all blocked threads were waiting for Oracle database connections. The root cause turned out to be a known bug in Microsoft's Oracle client that fails to properly dispose pooled connections after exceptions, causing connection pool exhaustion.
key_points: Production debugging requires specialized tools like WinDbg and SOS.dll when standard troubleshooting fails
Memory dumps can reveal exactly what each thread is doing at the moment of a hang or crash
The !clrstack command in SOS.dll shows .NET-specific call stacks that pinpoint the blocking operations
Connection pool exhaustion from improperly disposed connections can cause random, hard-to-reproduce deadlocks
Microsoft's own Oracle client had a known bug where it would reuse connections it knew to be bad
money_quotes: This is painful. It means the server becomes unavailable for over three minutes, and any pending requests return errors after ASPNET_WP is cycled.
The best part is, this happens completely randomly. We can't force it to happen or duplicate it, we just have to wait for it to happen.
We went through all the normal troubleshooting procedures and exhausted them all, which left... the tough stuff.

Armed with this information, rather than 'gee, ASPNET_WP is deadlocking a lot,' we were able to determine that the real problem is a pooled connection is not disposed by Microsoft .NET Managed Provider for Oracle when an exception occurs.

The Microsoft System.Data.OracleClient is blindly re-using connections that it knows to be bad, which of generates a NullObjectException, and sooner or later – basically at random – causes ASPNET_WP to fall over and cycle.
Good times... good times...
posts.key_insight: When facing mysterious production issues that can't be reproduced, advanced debugging tools that capture memory dumps and thread states can transform vague symptoms into precise root cause identification.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that misleading error messages are far worse than obviously bad ones. He recounts a frustrating debugging experience where a well-formatted exception stack trace pointed to a server-side Oracle DLL issue, when the actual problem was a missing dependency on the client side. Despite the error message being detailed and professional-looking, it sent the team on a wild goose chase for an entire day. The experience illustrates how confident, informative-looking error messages can be more dangerous than cryptic ones because developers trust them implicitly.
key_points: Well-formatted, detailed error messages that point to the wrong cause are worse than obviously cryptic or broken ones
The trust developers place in professional-looking stack traces can lead them astray for hours or days
Remoting and distributed systems can make error origins particularly deceptive, as exceptions may appear to come from the server when the issue is client-side
Even when logic suggests an alternative explanation, developers often refuse to believe it because the error message seems authoritative
Debugging requires questioning even the most confident-looking diagnostic information
money_quotes: But as bad as those are, they pale in comparison to what is, hands down, the worst kind of error message: a beautiful, well-formatted, informative, incorrect error message.
Just what you want to do in a production system, make sweeping changes in the back end soon after deployment.
Intellectually, I knew there was no way I could be getting different results from a server call than any other client.
But I still refused to believe this.
I've probably spent more time chasing down erroneous error messages than the time I've spent on all other error messages combined.
Evidently computers, like people, are big fat stinkin' liars!
posts.key_insight: A polished, detailed error message that points you in the wrong direction will waste more of your time than an obviously broken one, because you'll trust it.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to Eric Raymond's frustrations with UNIX printing usability by arguing that the UNIX community is culturally incapable of prioritizing usability. He contends that despite Raymond's well-intentioned call for better UI design, the open source community's foundation in kernel hacking, C code, and command-line tools makes usability a foreign concern. Atwood asserts that usability is harder than writing an operating system kernel because it requires understanding actual user behavior. He concludes that meaningful usability improvements will come from Microsoft and Apple, companies that have cultivated cultures respecting usability, rather than from the open source world.
key_points: UNIX's cultural foundation in technical excellence and command-line tools makes usability adoption fundamentally incompatible with its community values
Usability is an order of magnitude harder than writing technically competent code, even harder than writing an OS kernel
The open source community has had thirty years to produce a usable GUI and history suggests the next five years won't be different
Good UI design requires understanding what users actually do versus what they say they do, which conflicts with open source developers' preference for 'the fun stuff'
Companies like Microsoft and Apple will lead usability improvements because they've cultivated cultures that respect it
money_quotes: UNIX will never be usable.
Nobody gives a damn how technically competent your code is when they can't figure out how to use it. Without usability, you have nothing.
Usability is easily an order of magnitude harder than writing technically competent code, even harder than writing your own operating system kernel.
The fact that this mess is an example of our best in action, rather than our worst, just highlights how appallingly low our standards have been.
It is comically unrealistic to ask a community predicated on C code, kernel hacking, and the utility of command line tools, to suddenly wake up and get the usability religion.
Open-source developers don't have time for things that are a pain in the ass – like users, their conflicting needs, and their general disdain for computers and technology.
Good UI design is not a result of black magic, it just requires paying attention.
posts.key_insight: Cultural values, not technical capability, determine whether a software community can prioritize usability—and UNIX's hacker culture fundamentally conflicts with user-centered design.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood enthusiastically announces the release of Code Complete 2nd Edition, a book he considers essential reading for all developers. He shares his personal practice of owning multiple copies and urges developers to read the top books on his recommended list. The post highlights Steve McConnell's observations about how developers use buzzwords like 'agile' or 'object-oriented' as excuses to skip fundamental practices like requirements and design. Atwood also mentions McConnell's upcoming book on software estimation, humorously dubbed the 'I'm 99% done except for some cleanup!' book.
key_points: Code Complete 2 is Atwood's top recommended book for developers, important enough that he owns multiple copies
Developers frequently use trendy methodologies (OOP, Agile) as excuses to skip fundamental practices like requirements gathering and design
The gap between talking about good practices and actually using them has been a constant in software development for decades
Good programmers invest effort to learn current practices properly; poor programmers just learn the buzzwords
Software estimation remains a 'black art' worthy of its own dedicated book
money_quotes: If I was rich enough to buy a copy of those books for every developer on earth, I would.
There are still far more people who talk about good practices than who actually use good practices.
I see far too many people using current buzzwords as a cloak for sloppy practices.
Most of those people weren't really doing object-oriented programming – they were hacking, and the results were predictable, and poor.
Right now, people are saying 'I don't have to do requirements or design because I'm doing agile development.' Again, the results are easy to predict, and poor.
Good programmers invest the effort to learn how to use current practices. Not-so-good programmers just learn the buzzwords, and that's been a software industry constant for a half century.
posts.key_insight: Buzzwords and methodologies change over time, but the pattern of developers using them as excuses to skip fundamental engineering practices remains constant.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that modal dialog boxes are overused in GUI programming and disrupt user workflow by forcing acknowledgment of often trivial information. He advocates for passive notification approaches like highlighting fields with colors or using inline alerts instead of stopping user interaction. The post highlights Windows XP SP2's introduction of non-modal notification bars in IE6 as an excellent alternative to dialog boxes. Atwood draws inspiration from the game Dungeon Keeper 2, which queued notifications without interrupting gameplay, suggesting games solved this UX problem years before mainstream applications caught on.
key_points: Modal dialog boxes break user flow by requiring acknowledgment before any other interaction can continue
Alan Cooper's principle of avoiding 'stopping the proceedings with idiocy' should guide notification design
Passive indicators like colored backgrounds or asterisks are preferable to intrusive dialogs for validation errors
IE6's notification bar in Windows XP SP2 represents a better pattern: visible, informative, and non-blocking
Games like Dungeon Keeper 2 had already solved this UX problem with queued notification systems
money_quotes: These dialogs are frequently abused for displaying all kinds of trivial information to the user, a mistake that Alan Cooper calls stopping the proceedings with idiocy.
This completely breaks any flow of interaction the user had with our app.
Whatever you do, avoid stopping the proceedings with idiocy at all costs.
There's just no question that this is a far better solution than popping a modal dialog with the same information.

It's also better than the 'put an icon in the status bar' solution, because it's more visible, it's at the top of the window where the work starts (nobody sees the status bar), and it contains more information.

The game was constantly sending you notifications of various things going on throughout your dungeon; the notifications would visually flow into a queue with a summary icon to indicate the type and severity of the notification.
posts.key_insight: Good UI design communicates with users without interrupting them—notifications should inform, not obstruct.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the contentious debate around Hungarian Notation and variable naming conventions, sharing his personal journey from traditional VB naming to .NET development. He explains how the transition to fully object-oriented languages forced him to reconsider whether type prefixes ever made sense. After presenting his own pragmatic conventions focused on simplicity and readability, he argues that internal consistency within a team matters far more than finding a perfect universal standard. He advocates for leaving room for personal interpretation rather than over-standardizing in areas without definitive right answers.
key_points: Hungarian Notation is a religious war topic with extreme polarity in developer opinions
In fully OO languages where everything is an object, type prefixes like 'obj' become meaningless
Good naming conventions prioritize simplicity, readability, and distinguishing scope over comprehensive type prefixes
Internal team consistency matters more than adhering to any particular 'perfect' naming standard
Over-standardization in areas without clear right answers is soul-crushing and counterproductive
money_quotes: What naming scheme do you use for a fully OO language where... everything is an object? objEverything isn't very satisfying.
I do not believe every single object needs a unique prefix. This is insane, and as the VB6 document illustrates, this way leads madness...
Pointing out how stupid someone's variable names are is like telling them how stupid they are for naming their first born child 'Melvin.'
It's more important to be internally consistent with a naming standard than it is to spend a lot of time sussing out some kind of perfect, interplanetary naming standard.
There's nothing quite as soul crushing as over-standardizing in a religious area where there really isn't a 'right' answer.
Naming conventions cannot guarantee good code, however; only the skill of the programmer can.
posts.key_insight: Naming conventions should prioritize team consistency and readability over rigid adherence to any universal standard, since there is no definitively correct answer in this religious debate.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood discusses his discovery of better visual diff tools while building a .NET library for creating MHT (single-file web archive) files. He needed to compare his library's output against IE6's native MHT generation and found Visual SourceSafe's built-in comparison tool inadequate. After researching alternatives, he settled on Araxis Merge as his preferred visual comparison tool, despite its high price. He laments that VSS doesn't support external diff tools for Visual Studio integration.
key_points: MHT files follow the standard Multipart MIME Message RFC rather than a proprietary Microsoft format
Visual SourceSafe's built-in diff tool is inadequate for serious comparison work
Every developer should have a quality diff tool in their toolkit
Araxis Merge is recommended as a professional visual comparison tool despite its cost
Tool limitations in VSS prevent integration with better external comparison tools
money_quotes: Every developer should have a good diff tool in their toolkit.
Surprisingly, instead of some crazy proprietary Microsoft format like you'd expect, the file follows the simple Multipart MIME Message RFC standard.
Building an .MHT file is sort of like sending an email to yourself – go figure.
It's workable, but it's kind of... ghetto.
If like me, the only diff tool you ever used was the one in VSS – you may not know how much you're missing.
posts.key_insight: Developers often settle for inadequate default tools without realizing how much better alternatives exist, and investing in quality developer tools pays dividends in productivity.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares Robert X. Cringely's influential framework from 'Accidental Empires' that categorizes workers into three types: Commandos (fast-moving innovators who build prototypes), Infantry (the masses who refine, test, and ship products), and Police (those who maintain and scale established systems). The model explains why startups succeed or fail based on having the right people at the right phase. Atwood notes this applies equally to software projects, where having commandos during maintenance or police during innovation can be destructive. The key insight is that transitions between these phases represent the greatest peril for companies.
key_points: Commandos work fast and cheap, thrive on creativity and surprise, but get bored easily and produce rough prototypes rather than finished products
Infantry are essential for turning prototypes into profitable products - they test, refine, document, and market, requiring structure that commandos hate
Police hate change and focus on scaling through economies of scale rather than new innovations - most large successful companies are police organizations
The most dangerous moments for startups are transitions between these phases, especially when the wrong type leads at the wrong time
The best leaders are experienced second-wave types who can inspire commandos while also working within organizational structures
money_quotes: A start-up's biggest advantage is speed, and speed is what commandos live for.
They work hard, fast, and cheap, though often with a low level of professionalism, which is okay, too, because professionalism is expensive.
They make creativity a destructive act.
While the commandos make success possible, it's the infantry that makes success happen.

There is an enormous difference between starting a company and running one. Thinking up great ideas, which requires mainly intelligence and knowledge, is much easier than building an organization, which also requires measures of tenacity, discipline, and understanding.
Having the wrong group (commandos) at the wrong time (maintenance) can hurt you a lot more than it helps.
These third-wave troops hate change. They aren't troops at all but police.
posts.key_insight: Software projects and companies require fundamentally different types of people at different stages, and mismatching personalities to phases is more dangerous than having no talent at all.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues passionately that Edit and Continue is the killer feature of VS.NET 2005, lamenting its absence in earlier .NET versions. He refutes developers who claim the feature is harmful, calling such views 'wrongheaded' and born from ignorance. The post details the tedious nine-step debugging cycle required without the feature versus the streamlined workflow it enables. Atwood supports his argument by citing Fred Brooks' 1975 research showing interactive debugging doubles programmer productivity. He acknowledges Microsoft's reasonable decision to cut the feature initially to prioritize platform migration, but expresses eager anticipation for its return.
key_points: Edit and Continue was the most missed VB6 feature in early .NET, and its return in VS.NET 2005 is compelling enough to upgrade on release day
Critics who view Edit and Continue as harmful are speaking from ignorance—they've never experienced the productivity gains it provides
The feature cuts bug-fixing workflow roughly in half by eliminating the restart-navigate-remember-compile-run cycle
Fred Brooks' 1975 research demonstrated that interactive debugging doubles productivity compared to batch systems
Microsoft's decision to cut the feature initially was reasonable given the priority of getting VB onto the .NET platform
money_quotes: All Edit and Continue does is tighten the loop between the time a bug is detected, and the time you can fix it. How can this possibly be a bad thing?
Edit and Continue would cut the work I have to do to fix a bug in half. IN HALF!

That is one of the most wrongheaded things I've ever read, and I have to assume it's spoken from ignorance, e.g., developers who have never had this capability in their toolset and therefore don't know what they are missing.
There is a widespread recognition that debugging is the hard and slow part of system programming, and slow turnaround is the bane of debugging.
Harr's data suggest that an interactive facility at least doubles productivity in system programming.

At the time of the exception, you can diagnose the problem – in perfect context of all the live code, which is the easiest way to determine what the fix should be – and make your fix. Then just keep on truckin'.
posts.key_insight: The immediacy of debugging—being able to fix code in the live context where you discovered the bug—is one of the most significant productivity multipliers in software development.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood makes the case for regular expressions as an essential tool in every programmer's toolkit, comparing them to a power drill - not for every job, but indispensable for common string processing tasks. He reviews several regex tools, ultimately recommending RegexBuddy for its real-time match highlighting feature that eliminates the traditional trial-and-error workflow. The post provides practical resources including cheat sheets and pre-built regex repositories. Atwood concludes with an important caveat: despite their power, regular expressions have limitations (no recursion, no reverse matching) and should remain in their proper role as string processors, not be stretched into a full programming paradigm.
key_points: Regular expressions are the 'power drill' of programming - essential for common string processing in an era of HTML, XML, and other text formats
RegexBuddy's killer feature is real-time highlighting of matches as you type, eliminating the traditional run-and-check workflow
Named captures in .NET provide clean, logical syntax for accessing matched groups
Pre-built regex libraries let you reuse proven patterns rather than reinventing the wheel
Regular expressions have hard limitations (no recursion, no reverse matching) that keep them appropriately scoped as helper tools rather than full programming languages
money_quotes: I generally don't subscribe to the UNIX religion, but there is one area where I am an unabashed convert: regular expressions.
The RegEx is the power drill of the programmer's toolkit: not appropriate for every job, but the go-to tool for a lot of common jobs.
That has always been my complaint about RegEx composition: it's difficult to tell beforehand what the effect of your RegEx will be until you 'run' it and browse all the matches.
Why build what you can steal?
Drunk with the power and possibility of regular expressions, you might start thinking regular expressions can do... well, just about anything.
Despite their undeniable power, RegExps aren't a procedural programming language. In limited string processing roles, they're perfect.
But can you imagine writing an entire application with that kind of crazy, nigh-indecipherable syntax Perl?
posts.key_insight: Regular expressions are indispensable for string processing, but their power should be wielded within their natural limitations rather than stretched beyond their designed purpose.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood makes the case that AMD's Athlon 64 processor is the optimal choice for .NET developers due to its significantly faster compilation times compared to Intel's Pentium 4. He presents benchmark data showing a 33% speed advantage at equivalent clock speeds, arguing that even the cheapest Athlon 64 outperforms the fastest Pentium 4 for compilation. Beyond development work, he notes additional benefits including gaming performance, upcoming 64-bit Windows support, and hardware security features. The post concludes with practical purchasing advice and a critique of Dell's Intel exclusivity.
key_points: Athlon 64 compiles .NET code 33% faster than equivalently-rated Pentium 4 processors
The cheapest Athlon 64 outperforms the most expensive Pentium 4 for compilation tasks
Compile time represents accumulated productivity loss since it's too short to context-switch but long enough to notice
AMD offers additional advantages: gaming performance, 64-bit support, and no-execute bit security features
Dell's exclusive Intel partnership prevents them from offering what Atwood considers superior technology
money_quotes: For compiling .NET code, the Athlon 64 is 33% faster than a Pentium 4 of the same speed.
Time spent compiling is time wasted staring at the build output window.

Compiling doesn't typically take long enough to force me to ALT+TAB away and start doing something else, so there's no real incentive to multitask... and those wasted seconds add up throughout the day.
You could buy the cheapest Athlon 64 available and still get better .NET compiler performance than the fastest Pentium 4 available.
That's cash in your pocket twice: once for the purchase of the less expensive CPU, and again as you spend less time writing the same code.
Dell has some kind of satanic pact with Intel, and even though the Athlon 64 is a superior technology – in pretty much everything except for media encoding – they will never offer it.
posts.key_insight: Developer hardware choices should be driven by compilation performance benchmarks, as the accumulated seconds of waiting for builds represent a hidden but significant productivity cost.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that computer science is poorly named because software development lacks the empirical foundation of true science. He contrasts modern scientific method—building knowledge from observations upward—with the god-like control developers have inside computers, where there are no external physical constraints to ground their work. Unlike engineers who must contend with physics, software developers operate in universes of their own making with constantly changing rules. Atwood suggests that usability research offers a path to genuine science in computing by measuring human behavior outside the machine.
key_points: Software development lacks the empirical grounding that defines true science—we work top-down from our own assumptions rather than bottom-up from observations
The term 'computer science' is borderline oxymoronic; even 'software engineering' is uncomfortable given how differently we work compared to bridge or airplane engineers
Physical engineers have an advantage: they're constrained by physics, while software developers constantly have 'the rug pulled out from under us' with no fixed rules
Usability research is one of the few areas that brings genuine scientific method to computing by measuring things outside the computer—actual human behavior
Inside a computer, developers are gods with no external reality to check their assumptions against
money_quotes: The term 'computer science' is a borderline oxymoron.
Inside a computer, we're back in the dark ages: everyone is God, and the earth is the center of the universe.
Even the term 'software engineering' makes me uneasy; if bridges and airplanes were engineered the same way I 'engineer' software, I'd be afraid to leave the house.
They don't have to build a bridge on Jupiter, or construct an airplane out of brand new, previously unknown materials.
We constantly get the rug pulled out from under us, because there are no rules in our universe – at least, none other than the ones we make up.
Ironically, the only way to achieve anything resembling objective science with computers is to measure things outside the box.
posts.key_insight: True science requires external reality to test hypotheses against, but software developers operate as gods in self-created universes—only by measuring human behavior outside the machine can we bring genuine empiricism to computing.

posts.summary: Atwood criticizes developers who compulsively add manual Dispose() calls in .NET code, arguing this reflects a misguided belief that they know better than the garbage collector. He points out that such code is at best unnecessary clutter and at worst signals a dangerous distrust of the runtime. While acknowledging that explicit disposal is appropriate for resources like files and database connections, he argues these are exceptions. The post advocates trusting default behaviors and letting variables fall out of scope naturally rather than littering code with C++-style memory management habits.
key_points: Manual Dispose() calls on objects without real-world resources (files, DB connections, handles) are unnecessary in .NET
Developers who don't trust the garbage collector are 'dangerous' because they deviate from defaults without good reason
Default behaviors exist to make our lives easier and should be leveraged whenever possible
Extra disposal code creates maintenance burden for anyone who has to read and debug it
The exception proves the rule: only dispose objects with associated real-world resources
money_quotes: At worst, coding like this screams 'I'm smarter than the Runtime!'
These kinds of developers are, in my experience, dangerous.
Default behaviors are there to make our lives easier, and to protect us – they should be leveraged whenever possible.
Why create extra work for yourself (and the people who will maintain your code) by littering your code with these unnecessary, C++ style Dispose() calls?
At best, this is extra, meaningless code that someone has to debug and read.
posts.key_insight: Trust your runtime's garbage collector instead of cluttering code with unnecessary manual disposal calls that signal distrust of the platform you're building on.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reviews Bob Reselman's 'Coding Slave,' a semi-autobiographical book about the trauma of working on large ERP implementations in Big Six consulting. While finding it entertaining and unique, Atwood struggles to relate to its portrayal of enterprise software development, questioning whether ERP implementations represent mainstream programming. He's particularly critical of the book's proposed solution—a programmer's guild building a monolithic ERP system—arguing that open source collaboration already provides a better real-world alternative. The review acknowledges the book covers many weighty topics but suggests Code Complete offers more practical guidance for programmer development.
key_points: The book portrays large corporation ERP implementations as uniquely traumatic compared to small company software work, where conflicts are resolved directly rather than through litigation
Atwood questions whether ERP development represents mainstream software development, noting none of his dozens of programmer acquaintances have worked on such systems
The concept of building a massive monolithic ERP system—even for an idealistic programmer's guild—reflects the flawed thinking it supposedly critiques
The blogosphere and open source movement already serve as a real-world programmer's guild, making the book's proposed solution unnecessary
Small companies offer direct confrontation and lifelong friendships, while large corporations create environments ripe for litigation and alienation
money_quotes: I read Coding Slave as the story of a guy from a small business background who was severely traumatized by the world of Large Corporations, Big Six Consulting, and $20 million ERP systems.
One thing I did learn from the book is that the entire concept of an ERP software package is itself kind of, well, insane.

Freely choosing to design a ginormous, monolithic software package like this strikes me as a profoundly bad idea – an idea already tainted by the bizarre mindset of Big Six IT and $20 million ERP packages.
No sane organization, even the completely fictional programmer's guild Bob proposes, would build software this way.

A system 'of the people, and by the people' wouldn't be written at all – it would be absorbed from the various open source projects and patched together communally, feeding back into the larger open source zeitgeist.
Things that are food for litigation in large corporations simply don't exist in small companies.
I'd also argue that the current blogosphere, combined with the open source movement, is a pretty good real world analog for the proposed Ye Olde Programmer's Guild. And it exists today!
posts.key_insight: The open source movement and developer blogging communities have already organically created the collaborative programmer solidarity that top-down enterprise solutions like ERP systems and proposed guilds fail to achieve.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that object-oriented programming often fails to deliver its promised benefits when applied to typical business problems. He cites industry voices who have lost faith in objects for business logic, noting that most business operations are simple CRUD that don't benefit from behavioral polymorphism. The post reflects on real project experience where teams abandoned naive object implementations in favor of simpler DataSets. Atwood proposes that IDEs should provide automatic, transparent database schema access with intellisense, eliminating the tedious mapping code that objects require.
key_points: Object-oriented inheritance and encapsulation often get refactored away for pragmatic reasons in real business applications
Business problems typically need polymorphic data handling, but OOP is optimized for polymorphic behavior—a fundamental mismatch
The clever techniques required to bridge OOP and relational data often compound complexity rather than reduce it
Simple data-centric approaches like DataSets can be more practical than elaborate object mappings
IDEs should automatically provide typed, intellisense-supported database access without requiring developer code
money_quotes: I just lost faith that objects are any good on the 'business logic' abstraction level.
Often those fits of cleverness turn into fugues of frustration because the programming techniques designed to reduce complexity have actually compounded it.
At first you're like 'whee! objects!' and then you realize – hey, this is a lot of tedious, error-prone mapping code I didn't have to write before...
Objects lost their place in that picture for me.
The Customer.Create() operation is really no different behaviorally than Product.Create().
This is only useful if it is completely automatic in the IDE, with intellisense support – that is, zero code required from the developer!
posts.key_insight: Object-oriented programming's emphasis on behavioral polymorphism is fundamentally mismatched with typical business problems that need polymorphic data handling, leading to complexity that defeats the purpose of abstraction.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his late adoption of Microsoft Virtual PC 2004, admitting he previously dismissed virtualization technology because he had enough spare hardware for testing. He explores three main use cases from Microsoft's FAQ: OS migration safety nets, rapid reconfiguration for support staff, and accelerated software testing. The post notes that same-system x86 emulation only costs about 10% performance overhead, making it practical for development work. Atwood briefly compares Virtual PC to VMWare, noting that MSDN subscribers get VPC free, and closes by mentioning server virtualization as an alternative to Google's commodity hardware approach.
key_points: Virtual machines provide a 'test lab' of multiple OS configurations on a single physical machine with rollback capabilities
Same-system x86 emulation costs approximately 10% of raw performance, making it practical for everyday development use
Graphics subsystem emulation remains the weakest area for both Virtual PC and VMWare
Virtual PC's use of standard hardware emulation (S3 Trio, SoundBlaster 16) provides better compatibility than VMWare's proprietary drivers
Server virtualization offers an alternative philosophy to Google's approach of using many cheap commodity machines
money_quotes: I'm probably the last developer on the planet to get wise to the benefits of virtual machine technology.
The additional layer of software translation – emulating an x86 PC on an x86 PC – seemed unnecessary to me, like the VGA port on a flat panel monitor.
It's like having an entire test lab full of machines, loaded with every kind of crazy OS and configuration you can imagine.
A good rule of thumb is that same-system emulation costs about ten percent of raw performance, which is reasonable.
Why not have a powerful server run 5 instances of Windows Server 2000, each blissfully unaware of the other instances?
posts.key_insight: Virtual machine technology eliminates the need for physical test hardware while adding only modest performance overhead, making it an essential tool for developers who need to test across multiple configurations.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents a frustrating bug in Visual Studio .NET 2003 where VB code outlining features are completely broken, despite working correctly in VS.NET 2002. The standard menu options for toggling outlining do nothing, and even disabling outlining mode in settings has no effect. Rather than waiting for Microsoft to patch the issue, Atwood shares a community-sourced workaround: a VB macro that manually expands and collapses #Region blocks by searching through the document. The post serves as both a bug report and a practical solution for developers affected by this issue.
key_points: VS.NET 2003 broke VB code outlining that worked perfectly in the 2002 version, with both menu commands and settings toggles failing to work
The bug makes working with #Region blocks essentially impossible for teams that rely heavily on code organization
A community member created a macro-based workaround that searches for #region tags and toggles their expansion state
The solution requires manual installation through the Macros IDE and custom keyboard shortcut assignment
Microsoft had not released a patch for this significant regression despite it being a known issue
money_quotes: Evidently the VB code outlining support is completely broken in VS.NET 2003. Why hasn't this gotten more publicity?
I can't believe this hasn't been fixed yet in a patch for VS.NET 2003, because it makes working with #Region – which we use a lot – pretty much impossible.
While it's not a fix, per se, it does add support for #Region outlining at the very least.
posts.key_insight: When major vendors ship broken features, the developer community often has to create their own workarounds rather than wait for official fixes.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the endless debate between VB.NET and C# developers is pointless and counterproductive. He emphasizes that .NET's strength lies in offering syntax choices, and developers should focus on the framework itself rather than obsessing over which language wraps it. Citing Daniel Appleman's prescient e-book, Atwood notes that while the languages are functionally equivalent, C# developers paradoxically command higher salaries due to perception rather than substance. The post concludes pragmatically by sharing tools for converting between the two languages, acknowledging that interoperability is more useful than tribalism.
key_points: The VB vs C# debate misses the point - the real value is in the .NET framework, not the syntax used to access it
Believing one syntax is 'morally superior' to another is foolish - both accomplish the same thing
C# developers are paid more than VB.NET developers despite identical capabilities, proving language choice is emotional rather than logical
If someone insists on paying more for C# work, take their money - it's capitalism working as intended
.NET's multi-language support is a feature to embrace, not a battleground for tribal warfare
money_quotes: If I wanted a single language to bind them all, I'd be writing in Java.
Any of you who feel that the syntax if() { } is somehow morally superior to If... Then End If are fools.
The real choice you make is to use the framework; getting obsessive about the bits of duct tape we use to run the framework through its paces is completely and utterly missing the point.
Language choice is as much an emotional as a logical decision.
In a case where a manager or client insists on paying more for C# development, you have a moral obligation to take their money.
If there's ever a Nobel prize for .NET coding, Lutz is the obvious frontrunner.
posts.key_insight: Programming language tribalism is a waste of energy - the framework matters, not the syntax, and salary differences between equivalent languages reveal how irrational the industry can be.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood highlights an interview with Miguel de Icaza about the Mono Project, an open-source port of .NET to Linux. The post emphasizes how Mono enables cross-platform development, allowing companies like Novell to target Windows, Linux, and Mac with a single codebase. Atwood notes Miguel's pragmatic criticism of both Unix development tools and J2EE's academic complexity, citing studies showing ASP.NET is 25-30% more efficient for real-world development. The post acknowledges Mono's focus on server-side ASP.NET rather than GUI/WinForms, and celebrates the platform flexibility Mono brings to .NET development.
key_points: Mono Project allows .NET code to run on Windows, Linux, and Mac, providing deployment flexibility that strengthens .NET's credibility
Miguel de Icaza candidly describes Unix as 'a world of pain for developers' compared to modern IDEs on other platforms
Studies found ASP.NET development to be 25-30% more efficient than J2EE, largely because J2EE became overly academic and complex
Mono strategically focused on server-side ASP.NET rather than attempting to port the GUI/WinForms components
Practical shipping software matters more than theoretically perfect architecture when facing real deadlines
money_quotes: Oh, Unix is a world of pain for developers.

The problem with J2EE really is that it became very, very academic and the complexity of all these perfectly designed systems in schools does not necessarily map when you have deadlines and all kinds of other things.
So, it's just because the technology is not as pretty as it could be or as nice as it could be, but it gets the job done.
It certainly helps .NET's credibility a lot in comparison with 'write once, debug everywhere' Java.
It helps developers focus more on what they are doing instead of with the nitty-gritty details of the specific platform.
They could write in C++, but the schedule would just go out the window.
posts.key_insight: Cross-platform development tools that prioritize practical shipping over architectural purity deliver real productivity gains, as demonstrated by Mono enabling .NET's flexibility and ASP.NET's efficiency advantage over J2EE's academic complexity.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains the nuances of properly rethrowing exceptions in .NET, emphasizing the importance of preserving the call stack. He demonstrates how to include original exceptions as InnerExceptions when adding context, and how to use a bare 'Throw' statement to rethrow without losing stack trace information. The post then transitions to broader guidance on when exceptions should be caught at all, advocating restraint and specificity. The core message is that most exceptions should not be caught unless you can genuinely handle them or add meaningful context.
key_points: Use InnerException to preserve original exception details when throwing a new exception with more context
A bare 'Throw' statement (without parameters) preserves the original call stack, unlike 'Throw ex'
The golden rule: don't catch exceptions unless you have a very good reason—let them propagate
Only catch exceptions if you can fix the problem, add meaningful information, or handle a specific error type
Always prefer catching specific exception types over the generic System.Exception
money_quotes: Unless you have a very good reason to catch an exception, DON'T.
Exceptions are supposed to be exceptional, just like the dictionary meaning: uncommon, unusual.
The hardest kinds of exceptions to troubleshoot are the ones that don't even exist, because a developer upstream of you decided to consume it.
When in doubt, let the calling routine, or the global exception handler, deal with it.
Less code is almost always better, IMO.
Always try to catch specific exceptions.
posts.key_insight: The hardest bugs to diagnose come from exceptions that are silently swallowed, so only catch exceptions when you can genuinely fix the problem or add meaningful context—otherwise, let them propagate.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the rapid adoption of .NET in 2004, citing Forrester Research data showing 56% of developers chose .NET as their primary platform versus 44% for J2EE. He attributes this success to three factors: .NET's heritage from Delphi through Anders Hejlsberg, Visual Studio's superior productivity, and the massive existing Visual Basic developer base. Atwood argues that Microsoft's timing was critical, as Java was threatening to dominate enterprise development. He concludes that Microsoft earned this mindshare legitimately by producing a quality product.
key_points: .NET adoption surged past J2EE in 2004, with developers citing ease of use and faster time to market as primary reasons
.NET is essentially 'Delphi Reloaded' thanks to Microsoft hiring Anders Hejlsberg from Borland
Visual Studio .NET set the gold standard for IDE productivity, which directly drove adoption
Microsoft's timing was crucial—a few years later and Java might have permanently captured enterprise developers
Developer mindshare is the key strategic asset, and Microsoft earned it through product quality rather than just marketing
money_quotes: .NET is basically Delphi Reloaded (that's what you get when you hire Anders Hejlsberg away from Borland). It's even better!
The Visual Studio .NET IDE is the gold standard in developer productivity. I'm not aware of any other IDE that even comes close.
Yes, the most usable product is the one people end up using. Go figure.
Where Microsoft goes, so go the Visual Basic developers – the largest group of developers in the world.
Own that mindshare, and you own the world.
The great thing about .NET is that, whatever your opinion of company, Microsoft is gaining mindshare the old fashioned way: by producing a quality product.
posts.key_insight: Developer tools win market share through genuine usability and productivity gains, not marketing—the most usable product is simply the one people end up using.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the tension between implementing Finalize methods in .NET and their actual utility. He cites Brent Rector's argument that Finalize methods rarely provide real benefit since in worst-case scenarios (abnormal process termination) they won't run anyway, while imposing significant performance penalties. However, he notes that Microsoft's best practice recommends implementing both IDisposable and a Finalizer when wrapping unmanaged resources. Atwood finds this guidance contradictory and leaves the question somewhat unresolved.
key_points: Finalize methods have a large performance penalty in .NET
In the worst-case scenario of abnormal termination, Finalize never runs anyway
Microsoft recommends implementing both IDisposable and Finalize as a safety net
The pattern uses GC.SuppressFinalize to skip finalization when Dispose is called properly
The official guidance and practical advice are contradictory
money_quotes: In a worst-case scenario, your object's Finalize method won't run until your process terminates gracefully.
Actually, the real worst case is that it never runs at all because the process terminates abnormally.
The Finalize method served no real purpose other than to keep the application from running as fast as it otherwise could.
Just say No to Finalize methods!
In a true worst case scenario – unhandled exception time – you won't get any benefit from Finalize.
It's all rather contradictory.
posts.key_insight: The supposed safety net of Finalize methods may not be worth their performance cost, since they fail precisely when you need them most—during abnormal process termination.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the practical challenges of mixing .NET 1.0 and 1.1 assemblies in a single application after his team upgraded to VS.NET 2003. He discovers that the CLR's 'unification policy' automatically redirects references from older framework assemblies to newer versions, preventing dangerous mix-and-match scenarios. The post explains this as a pragmatic solution to what would otherwise be an explosive matrix of testing combinations. Atwood also criticizes Microsoft for treating the 1.0 runtime as a second-class citizen, making it nearly impossible to get bug fixes backported from 1.1.
key_points: Referenced assemblies are forced to run under the same runtime as the main application, regardless of which version they were compiled against
Unification policy is a hard-coded binding redirect that automatically routes v1.0 framework references to v1.1 equivalents
Microsoft explicitly avoided testing mixed v1.0/v1.1 configurations due to the exponential growth in test cases
The .NET 1.0 runtime receives poor support from Microsoft, with hotfixes only being developed for 1.1
money_quotes: The only thing that makes sense is that these referenced assemblies are forced to run under the same runtime as the main application, even though they were compiled under 1.0.
Side-by-side testing naturally results in an explosive matrix of test cases, and because of this, there was no explicit testing done for mixing and matching v1.0 and v1.1 frameworks assemblies.
It's disgraceful the way the 1.0 runtime is treated like a third class citizen at Microsoft.
Just try getting a hotfix for a 1.1 bug back-ported into the 1.0 runtime.
posts.key_insight: Framework version unification is a necessary compromise that trades theoretical flexibility for practical stability, but it comes with the hidden cost of older versions being abandoned by their creators.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that new .NET developers overuse inheritance, mistakenly applying language-level design patterns to ordinary business code. He contends that while inheritance is foundational to the .NET framework itself, most developers aren't writing languages or operating systems and don't need such complexity. Citing Dan Appleman's experience of rarely finding inheritance to be the right choice despite decades of C++ programming, Atwood emphasizes that inheritance is just one of many tools for code reuse. He concludes that inheritance adds hidden complexity that should be avoided unless there's a compelling, specific reason to use it.
key_points: New developers overuse inheritance because they see it throughout the .NET framework, but framework design isn't appropriate for business logic
Inheritance introduces 'hidden' behavior and complexity that makes code harder to maintain and understand
Simple objects without inheritance are sufficient for achieving code reuse in most applications
Writing languages or operating systems justifies inheritance; typical business code does not
Added complexity should be 'guilty until proven innocent' - avoid it unless specifically warranted
money_quotes: We're writing crappy business logic code, not a language.
Inheritance is a specialized tool, and should only be used for situations that truly warrant a parent-child relationship, and all the 'hidden' behavior that entails.
In all of those years, I can think of maybe a half a dozen times, at most, where inheritance was the right choice.
If you really understand inheritance, you may find yourself living the rest of your career without ever creating a single inheritable class or component.
Inheritance is only one way of many to achieve code reuse.
How many of you are writing programming languages? How many of you guys are writing operating system kernels?
To me, the added baggage of inheritance – like all added complexity – is always guilty until proven innocent.
posts.key_insight: Inheritance is a specialized tool for language and framework designers, not a default pattern for everyday business code where simplicity and maintainability should take precedence.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood addresses the growing problem of comment spam on his blog, which includes typical spam topics like viagra and gambling. He values open discussion through comments but recognizes spam as a serious threat to that openness. He recommends MT-blacklist, an anti-spam plugin for Movable Type, which he found easy to install and effective. The post ends with a defiant message to spammers.
key_points: Comment spam (viagra, gambling, etc.) was becoming a major problem for blogs in 2004
Open commenting is valuable for two-way discussion but requires spam protection
MT-blacklist provided an effective, easy-to-install solution for Movable Type users
money_quotes: I like the way anyone can leave a comment regarding my posts – the only good discussion is a two-way discussion – but the comment spam is getting way out of hand
SUCK IT SPAMMERS!
posts.key_insight: Maintaining open discussion on blogs requires active measures to combat spam, but good tools can make that fight manageable.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the paradox of internet popularity: unlike physical goods, digital content becomes more expensive for creators as it gains popularity, due to bandwidth costs. He coins this 'the popularity tax' and uses the Slashdot effect as a prime example. Drawing on Microsoft Research data, he shows that bandwidth is disproportionately expensive compared to other computing resources. He considers micropayments as a potential solution but acknowledges their limitations. The post concludes with a warning about how asymmetric upload/download speeds could become a backdoor method for corporations to enforce copyright restrictions by limiting consumers' ability to share content.
key_points: The internet inverts the normal economics of popularity—success costs the content creator money rather than generating it
Bandwidth costs dwarf other computing expenses: $1 buys only 1GB of WAN transfer versus 8 hours of CPU time or 1GB of storage
Micropayments could theoretically solve the popularity tax but face significant barriers from the banking industry
Asymmetric broadband speeds (high download, low upload) may be an indirect corporate strategy to cripple peer-to-peer sharing
The current system creates a 'digital ghetto' where 99% of users can only consume, not distribute content
money_quotes: The more popular the content is, the more it costs the content owner.
In the world of atoms, mere popularity doesn't cost the content owner a dime.
One puts computing as close to the data as possible in order to avoid expensive network traffic.
Bandwidth is hellaciously expensive relative to almost anything else, other than perhaps labor costs.
We have this weird situation on the internet where popularity is a curse, instead of the blessing it should be.
Unless you are leveraging your popularity to sell something, you are generating a substantial net loss through bandwidth costs.
Highly asymmetric upload/download ratios can be an indirect way for big corporations to enforce copyright restrictions on consumers.
This creates a digital ghetto: 99% of users are locked into the role of ravenous consumers, with massive download capacities but a mere trickle of upload.
posts.key_insight: The internet's fundamental economics punish popular content creators with escalating costs, creating perverse incentives that discourage building things people actually want to use.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares a curated collection of Steve McConnell's IEEE Software magazine editorials from 1998-2002, recommending them as valuable reading for software developers. He particularly highlights 'Cargo Cult Software Engineering' as a standout piece. The post serves as a resource guide to McConnell's lesser-known writing that was buried on his website. Atwood adds a touch of humor by noting he omitted McConnell's Y2K editorial, using it as a reminder that even respected experts can be wrong sometimes.
key_points: Steve McConnell wrote 20 editorials during his tenure as IEEE Software editor (1998-2002)
These articles cover fundamental software engineering topics from community building to project planning
'Cargo Cult Software Engineering' is singled out as particularly recommended reading
Even highly respected software engineering experts like McConnell can make incorrect predictions (Y2K)
money_quotes: I particularly recommend 'Cargo Cult Engineering.'
I spared you guys the embarrassing chicken little-esque Y2K editorial.
I guess we can all take heart, even a guy as smart as Steve gets it wrong sometimes...
posts.key_insight: Valuable software engineering wisdom often gets buried on personal websites, and even the most respected experts in the field occasionally get things wrong.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his company's aggressive offshoring of sophisticated .NET applications he helped build, moving through stages of disbelief, anger, and eventual acceptance. He calculates the troubling cost-per-user of internal software development and acknowledges that offshoring makes financial sense even for complex projects. Drawing on Ed Yourdon's prescient 1993 predictions, he argues that global competition for knowledge work is inevitable given modern communications infrastructure. His conclusion: developers must respond by genuinely improving their skills and treating programming as a serious profession rather than a 'license to print money.'
key_points: Offshoring isn't limited to boring maintenance work—even sophisticated, actively-developed applications are being transferred to overseas teams
The economics of internal corporate software development often don't make financial sense when you calculate cost per user
Global competition for knowledge work is inevitable; the internet makes physical location irrelevant for any job that doesn't require touching something
Programmers aren't special—all knowledge workers (accountants, engineers, etc.) face the same competitive pressures
The solution is treating professional development like an investment portfolio: diversify skills, have a plan, be active, and look for value
money_quotes: What's the point? It's absolutely inevitable that given a sophisticated enough worldwide communications infrastructure, we will begin competing with a people all of the world for the same jobs.
I hate to think of my livelihood reduced to something so disposable that it can be done for a fraction of the cost by cut-rate vendors halfway across the world.
All it takes is ten years of the Internet to bring the Indians to us.
It's OK to make twice as much as an Indian programmer if you're twice as productive.
We dug this hole for ourselves through complacency; now it's time to start digging ourselves out by treating our jobs like an actual engineering profession instead of a license to print money.
