Any job that can potentially be done without physically touching something is at risk. And isn't that the very definition of 'knowledge worker?'
posts.key_insight: Global competition for programming jobs is inevitable, and the only viable response is to genuinely become more productive and treat software development as a serious profession rather than an easy path to high salaries.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores Richard Gabriel's influential 'Worse is Better' philosophy, arguing that simpler, pragmatic solutions consistently outcompete theoretically superior but complex alternatives. He illustrates this through the contrast between MIT's pursuit of 'the right thing' versus Unix's New Jersey approach favoring implementation simplicity. Using examples like x86 architecture outlasting Itanium, .config files versus the Windows registry, and the overcomplexity of COM and J2EE, Atwood demonstrates how simplicity wins in the real world. He concludes that developers should always err on the side of simplicity because simple solutions survive, prosper, and can actually be understood by people.
key_points: Implementation simplicity often matters more than interface simplicity - the Unix philosophy of returning error codes rather than implementing complex state management won out
Theoretically inferior but simpler technologies consistently outlast their 'better' competitors (x86 vs Itanium, .INI files vs registry)
Complexity is a survival disadvantage - the registry's theoretical superiority is undermined by its fragility and opacity
Programmers suffer from dangerous optimism about others' ability to handle complex solutions
The Strunk and White approach applies to code: keep removing complexity until you cannot remove any more
money_quotes: Worse-is-better, even in its strawman form, has better survival characteristics than the-right-thing.
x86 is widely regarded as total piece of crap. And it is. But it's a piece of crap honed to an incredibly sharp edge.
Anything with 'Enterprise' in the title, just substitute 'Complicated.'
Whenever possible, always err on the side of simplicity.
Why even write code at all when you can buy or steal open-source it?
Simple solutions survive and prosper because they work, and people can actually understand them.
Don't presume that everyone's smart enough to handle the fancy complex solution; optimism is a dangerous occupational hazard for programmers.
Like words on a page, when you cannot remove any more – you're done.
posts.key_insight: Simple solutions consistently outcompete theoretically superior alternatives because they can actually be understood, maintained, and evolved by real humans in the real world.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents his frustrating experience getting remote debugging to work after installing Windows XP SP2, which broke his existing setup despite having the firewall disabled. After three hours of troubleshooting and trying every documented solution, he discovered the fix was granting 'Remote Access' to ANONYMOUS LOGON in dcomcnfg.exe - a solution not mentioned in Microsoft's documentation. He also shares a cautionary tale about accidentally removing all COM permissions while testing, which required two in-place OS reinstalls to fix. The post serves as both a technical solution and a warning about the dangers of DCOM configuration.
key_points: XP SP2 broke previously working remote debugging setups even with the firewall disabled
The fix requires granting Remote Access to ANONYMOUS LOGON in dcomcnfg.exe's Edit Limits, which contradicts Microsoft's documentation
Microsoft's documentation incorrectly suggests adding your own account only if you're not an administrator
Removing permissions in dcomcnfg can completely break your OS, requiring multiple reinstalls to recover
The solution was discovered through desperate trial-and-error after exhausting all documented approaches
money_quotes: After three hours of intense frustration, I started to feel like that Doom3 zombie – the one mindlessly pounding his head against a bloody wall over and over.
Eventually you get to that desperate point where you'll try anything, no matter how crazy.
I finally got remote debugging to work with XP SP2 by granting 'Remote Access' to the ANONYMOUS LOGON account in dcomcnfg.exe on the client computer.
That doesn't even make sense; out of the box EVERYONE is granted both local and remote permissions in Edit Limits.
Friends, do not do this.
Two in-place reinstalls later, I had a functioning OS again. Revenge of COM...
posts.key_insight: When official documentation fails and you've exhausted all reasonable options, sometimes desperate experimentation is the only path forward - but document your steps carefully because you might break things worse before you fix them.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood analyzes Microsoft's whitepaper comparing .NET Remoting and ASP.NET Web Services performance, breaking down the tradeoffs between TCP vs HTTP, Windows Service vs IIS hosting, and binary vs SOAP serialization. While TCP remoting in a Windows service is technically fastest, Atwood argues the practical advantages of HTTP, IIS, and SOAP (ubiquity, tooling, transparency) outweigh minor performance gains. He uses a real-world example of a developer sending 85,000 rows to a client to illustrate that architectural decisions matter far more than protocol choices. His conclusion favors Web Services or IIS-hosted remoting with SOAP for maintainability.
key_points: TCP remoting is fastest but HTTP's ubiquity, proxy support, and compression capabilities make it worth the small performance cost
IIS hosting provides free scalability features like web farms that justify its minor overhead compared to vanilla Windows services
The DataSet object's broken binary serialization (always outputs XML regardless of settings) is a significant gotcha that affects all approaches
Real-world performance problems are rarely caused by protocol choice—they're caused by poor API design like sending 85,000 rows to a client
Web Services provide a truly generic, future-proof HTTP interface that outlasts proprietary technologies like COM or CORBA
money_quotes: A well designed API will win every time, no matter what protocol or interface it is using.
A lot of hand wringing over minor performance differences – tied to what are really implementation details – doesn't buy you much when your bottleneck is elsewhere.
This isn't a COM or CORBA flash in the pan.
It's trivial to switch to binary protocol later; just flick a switch in your .config files.
You should pick the interface that is easiest to troubleshoot and maintain.
posts.key_insight: Obsessing over protocol-level performance differences is wasted effort when poor API design—like transferring massive datasets to clients—is almost always the real bottleneck.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recommends Task Manager Extension, a Windows utility discovered through Scott Hanselman's developer tools list. Rather than replacing the built-in Task Manager he uses daily, this extension enhances it with features like process icons, file/module search, and the ability to close locked files. Atwood appreciates that he doesn't need to retrain himself on a new tool since the extension augments familiar functionality. He suggests running it at startup for seamless integration.
key_points: Extending familiar tools is often better than replacing them entirely
Task Manager is an essential daily tool for developers
The extension adds practical features like finding files in use and unlocking locked files
Good utilities should integrate seamlessly with existing workflows
money_quotes: Like Notepad, Task Manager is something I use on a daily basis: it's an essential part of my toolkit.
I took a look at some replacements, but I'm so used to Taskman at this point, I can't retrain myself.
And with Task Manager Extension, I don't have to; it adds all the 'missing' features you could possibly want.
posts.key_insight: The best tool improvements enhance what you already know rather than forcing you to learn something completely new.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that humility is the most essential trait for becoming a great programmer, drawing heavily from Steve McConnell's Code Complete 2.0. The post emphasizes that programming is inherently unsupervisable and requires intense personal discipline, meaning self-improvement is entirely the programmer's responsibility. Citing Dijkstra's famous 'Humble Programmer' lecture, Atwood explains that the best programmers recognize the limitations of their own minds and use programming practices as 'mental crutches' to compensate. He notes that candidates who can't admit 'I don't know' are dangerous hires, and concludes that the goal isn't to be better than others, but to be better than you were a year ago.
key_points: Programming is essentially unsupervisable - no one really knows what you're working on, so personal character determines quality
The best programmers recognize how small their brains are; ego prevents growth and leads to more errors
Good programming practices exist to reduce cognitive load, not as signs of weakness
Candidates who can't say 'I don't know' are red flags - 'can-do' attitudes are actually poison in software development
The goal is continuous self-improvement, not comparison to others
money_quotes: Nobody is really smart enough to program computers.
The people who are the worst at programming are the people who refuse to accept the fact that their brains aren't equal to the task.
Your employer can't force you to be a good programmer; a lot of times your employer isn't even in a position to judge whether you're good.
The more you learn to compensate for your small brain, the better a programmer you'll be.
Programming work is essentially unsupervisable because no one ever really knows what you're working on.
Candidates who can't or won't say 'I don't know' get red flagged; those types of programmers are dangerous.
It's not our job to be better than anyone else; we just need to be better than we were a year ago.
posts.key_insight: The best programmers embrace humility about their cognitive limitations and use good practices to compensate, while those whose egos prevent this self-awareness become the worst programmers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recommends The Pragmatic Programmer by Andrew Hunt and Dave Thomas, highlighting its practical wisdom for software developers. He shares his experience having Andy Hunt speak at his company offsite about offshoring trends. The post centers on the 'Broken Window Theory' as applied to software development: small issues left unaddressed lead to rapid deterioration of code quality. Atwood argues that neglect accelerates software rot faster than any other factor, and developers must fix problems immediately rather than letting entropy win.
key_points: The Broken Window Theory applies directly to software: one piece of bad code left unrepaired signals abandonment and invites further deterioration
Neglect accelerates software rot faster than any other factor - small infractions lead to serious structural damage
Even if there's no time for a proper fix, taking some action (commenting out code, placeholder messages) shows you're on top of the situation
The pragmatic approach to programming emphasizes practical wisdom over theoretical perfection
Developers who ignore code quality issues should 'plan on getting a dumpster, or moving to another neighborhood'
money_quotes: One broken window, left unrepaired for any substantial length of time, instills in the inhabitants of the building a sense of abandonment – a sense that the powers that be don't care about the building.
Don't leave 'broken windows' (bad designs, wrong decisions, or poor code) unrepaired. Fix each one as soon as it is discovered.
We've seen clean, functional systems deteriorate pretty quickly once windows start breaking.
Neglect accelerates the rot faster than any other factor.
If you continue to think like that, then you'd better plan on getting a dumpster, or moving to another neighborhood. Don't let entropy win.
It's just so damn... pragmatic.
posts.key_insight: Small code quality issues left unaddressed create a sense of abandonment that accelerates deterioration, making immediate attention to 'broken windows' essential for maintaining healthy software systems.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while developers obsess over coding design patterns, GUI design patterns deserve far more attention. Using the iPod as his key example, he demonstrates that Apple's success comes not from hardware innovation—which competitors easily replicate—but from superior interface design. The post suggests that the 'soft' skills of GUI design, not the 'mechanical' coding patterns, are what ultimately determine product success.
key_points: GUI design patterns are underappreciated compared to coding design patterns
The iPod's success stems from usability, not hardware innovation—competitors make identical products
Coding patterns are 'mechanical' and relatively easy; GUI design is the difficult, differentiating work
What developers find sexy (code patterns) is invisible to users; what users experience (GUI) drives success
money_quotes: The mechanical things (coding patterns) are actually quite easy. It's the soft GUI stuff that is difficult, and that's ultimately what makes your product sexy, desirable – and a success.
We may think coding patterns are sexy, but they're invisible to users.
Apple is the only company making a fortune at it, and inspiring fanatical product loyalty. Why? It has the most usable interface.
posts.key_insight: Coding patterns may feel important to developers, but GUI design is what users actually experience and what ultimately determines whether a product succeeds or fails.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the challenge of implementing global exception handling for .NET web services, finding that unlike WinForms or ASP.NET apps, there's no elegant solution. After reviewing the unsatisfying options (try-catch on every method, Facade pattern, or custom SOAP extensions), he discovers that .NET's default SOAP exceptions are unhelpfully minimal. He presents a solution that re-throws exceptions with additional data to populate the SOAP detail element, resulting in much more informative error responses that include exception type, message, and stack trace.
key_points: Application_Error never fires for .NET web services, leaving no good generic exception handling option
The available workarounds (try-catch everywhere, Facade pattern, custom HttpModule) are all problematic
Default .NET SOAP exceptions provide minimal information with an empty detail element
Re-throwing exceptions as SoapException with custom XML detail nodes provides much richer error information
The improved exception includes exception type, message, and stack trace in the SOAP fault response
money_quotes: According to my research, there really is no good way to generically handle unhandled exception in .NET web services.
Put a try...catch around every WebService method. These methods tend to be wrappers around other classes, so this isn't quite as bad as it sounds, but it's still not good.
Write a custom SOAP Extension or HttpModule. This sounds reasonable but... hard. If it's such a cool, important extension or HttpModule, wouldn't someone have written it already?
By default, .NET doesn't throw very good SOAP Exceptions!
You need to re-throw exceptions with some additional data to get the 'optional,' but quite helpful, SOAP <detail> error element populated.
posts.key_insight: Framework defaults often provide minimal error information, and investing effort to enrich exception details pays dividends in debuggability.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood addresses the challenges of running .NET 1.0 and 1.1 side-by-side on the same development machine, a practice Microsoft was already trying to phase out in 2004. He recommends specific utilities for binding IIS websites to different .NET versions and converting Visual Studio solutions between 2002 and 2003 formats. The post also highlights some problematic .NET APIs that developers should avoid, sourced from another developer's blog. Atwood warns that .NET assemblies loaded via HTML object tags will always bind to the latest runtime version, making side-by-side deployment impossible for that scenario.
key_points: Microsoft was actively trying to make developers forget .NET 1.0 existed, even though side-by-side compatibility was still needed
Consumer Windows versions ran a 'crippleware' IIS limited to one website and 10 connections, while Server editions had no such restrictions
VS.NET 2002 and 2003 projects were largely interchangeable due to minimal language changes between .NET 1.0 and 1.1
Several .NET APIs were problematic enough to be considered 'blacklisted' by experienced developers, including Application.DoEvents()
.NET assemblies loaded via HTML object tags cannot run side-by-side and always bind to the latest runtime
money_quotes: Microsoft is doing their level best to pretend that .NET 1.0 doesn't exist any more
As a developer you're very likely to be running the crippleware version of IIS
Application.DoEvents() - The call of the devil
I had no idea high school seniors were this smart
There is no concept of side by side for assemblies loaded this way. Shocking, but true
I would strongly advise against building object tag 'deployed' .NET apps for anything non-trivial
posts.key_insight: Platform vendors often abandon backward compatibility faster than enterprise developers can migrate, forcing developers to maintain workarounds and tribal knowledge about version-specific quirks.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood draws parallels between the TV show Monster House and software project management, observing that both involve strict deadlines, custom work, random team compositions, and minimal management intervention. After watching many episodes, he concludes that project success is almost entirely determined by how well team members work together, not by individual skill. He reinforces this with quotes from Peopleware arguing that most project failures are sociological rather than technological. The post also highlights the 'bad apple syndrome' where a single problem team member can poison an entire project if not removed quickly.
key_points: Project success depends primarily on team collaboration and communication, not individual technical skill
Most project failures are sociological in nature, not technological - yet managers focus on technology anyway
The 'bad apple syndrome' can destroy a project; the longer a problem team member stays, the more likely failure becomes
Calling people problems 'political' makes them seem intractable, but recognizing them as 'sociological' makes them solvable
Teams that argue or fragment into individuals fail; teams that communicate with minimal friction succeed
money_quotes: The success of the project is almost entirely determined by how well these five random builders work together. It has nothing do with skill.
You never know what kind of teammates you're going to get, and you never really know exactly what you're building – until you build it. But by God, it better be done on time!
The major problems of our work are not so much technological as sociological in nature.
By noting the true nature of a problem as sociological rather than political, you make it more tractable.
Failure to take action to deal with a problem employee is the most common complaint that team members have about their leaders.
Teams that communicated well and coordinated their work – with a minimum of friction – invariably succeeded. Teams that argued a lot, and those that broke apart into five individuals... didn't.
Most managers are willing to concede the idea that they've got more people worries than technical worries. But they seldom manage that way.
posts.key_insight: Technical skill matters far less than team dynamics - software projects succeed or fail based on how well people work together, not how talented they are individually.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood endorses Ned Batchelder's argument that commented-out code should be deleted rather than left in a disabled state. The core problem is that dead code creates noise and uncertainty for future developers, forcing them to wonder about the code's history and whether it might be needed again. Atwood admits to being 'angrily accused' of deleting others' commented code but stands firm: without a compelling reason to keep it, dead code is fair game for deletion. The post argues that important context should be captured in comments, not preserved as zombie code.
key_points: Commented-out code creates uncertainty by forcing developers to ask questions about why it existed and whether it's needed
If historical context matters, write an explicit comment explaining it rather than leaving dead code as implicit documentation
Code left in a 'disabled state' tends to remain forever, accumulating as technical debt
Reducing noise in a codebase helps developers work more effectively with the code in the future
money_quotes: Some of the worst enemies a developer has are noise or uncertainty in his code, because they prevent him from working with it effectively in the future.
A chunk of code in a disabled state just causes uncertainty.
If the answer to one of these questions is important for people to know, then write a comment spelling it out. Don't leave your co-workers guessing.
I have been angrily accused of deleting someone's commented code on more than one occasion.
Give me a reason not to delete it, and I won't. Otherwise, it's fair game.
In my experience this kind of 'oh, I'll get back to it' code just sits in the codebase forever, junking up the works for every future developer.
posts.key_insight: Commented-out code is not documentation—it's noise that breeds uncertainty, and the solution is either to delete it entirely or replace it with explicit comments explaining the context.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces a new CodeProject article about ASP.NET exception handling, explaining that he created it due to repeated requests after mentioning the code existed in a previous article. He reflects on the value of publishing code on CodeProject rather than his blog, noting that the platform's audience motivates better documentation and provides valuable community feedback. The post is a brief meta-commentary on where developers should share their code for maximum impact.
key_points: User demand drove the creation of this article - vague mentions of existing code led to repeated requests
ASP.NET exception handling requires different approaches than WinForms/console apps
Publishing on CodeProject yields better documentation due to audience expectations
Community feedback on CodeProject provides valuable improvements to shared code
money_quotes: I kept getting requests for this code.
I think in the future I'll avoid using my blog to host code of any significance.
formatting the article for that general 'everyprogrammer' audience motivates me to write much more comprehensive documentation than I otherwise would.
I've also gotten some truly excellent feedback/suggestions from developers in the comments.
posts.key_insight: Publishing code on community platforms like CodeProject produces better documentation and feedback than hosting it on a personal blog, because writing for a general developer audience raises the quality bar.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that HTTP compression is an essential optimization that every website should implement, noting that it can quadruple throughput by reducing plaintext size by 75 percent. While IIS 5.0's built-in compression was broken and required a third-party ISAPI filter workaround, IIS 6.0 promised a working solution. However, getting dynamic content compression working in IIS 6.0 proved frustratingly difficult due to undocumented configuration requirements and misleading documentation. After extensive troubleshooting, Atwood shares his working metabase configuration and reports dramatic performance improvements once compression was properly enabled.
key_points: HTTP compression reduces plaintext by 75%, effectively quadrupling throughput with minimal CPU cost
IIS 5.0's built-in HTTP compression was broken and should be avoided in favor of the free FlatCompression ISAPI filter
IIS 6.0 compression configuration is poorly documented, with critical details like cr/lf delimiters being incorrectly stated as space-delimited
Dynamic content compression (for .aspx, .cgi, etc.) is significantly harder to configure than static file compression
The performance improvement from HTTP compression is immediately noticeable even on fast local networks
money_quotes: HTTP compression is the ultimate no-brainer.
The network is really slow, and CPU time is effectively free and getting faster and, uh, "free-er" every day.
Every website should be serving up HTTP compressed pages to clients that can accept it.
Despite what the MS documentation says, the metabase filename extension lists are not space delimited! They are cr/lf delimited.
It's truly amazing how much faster pages appear to load, even over a 100baseT local network, with HTTP compression enabled.
posts.key_insight: HTTP compression offers massive performance gains at virtually no cost, but getting it properly configured often requires fighting through broken implementations and misleading documentation.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits the 1994 book 'Showstopper!' about the creation of Windows NT and its legendary architect David Cutler. He reflects on NT's lasting significance, noting that the world has consolidated into just Unix and NT camps, with no new operating systems being developed from scratch. The post examines key lessons from NT's development: dogfooding, long-term R&D planning, prioritizing people over process, and the importance of architectural vision. Atwood concludes by questioning whether we'll ever see another ground-up operating system in our lifetimes, given that both Unix and NT have proven remarkably durable.
key_points: The computing world has devolved into just Unix and NT camps - nobody is writing new operating systems anymore, and even Apple abandoned their Copland project for Unix-based OS X
Microsoft's success with NT came from hiring smart people and letting them figure things out, with shockingly little formal process for such a complex project

Cutler's insistence on portability over x86 optimization, despite intense pressure, proved his architectural foresight - the NT kernel survived and evolved even after all non-x86 versions were discontinued
Large corporations need to plan ten years out in R&D - NT took from 1989 to Windows 2000 to become truly viable on the desktop
If you can't explain your technical vision in practical, meaningful terms, you're probably full of crap - as proven by Cairo's failure
money_quotes: Nobody is writing new operating systems any more. The world has devolved into UNIX and NT camps exclusively.
Unix is like Cutler's lifelong foe. It's like his Moriarty. He thinks Unix is a junk operating system designed by a committee of Ph.D.s.
Microsoft didn't really manage much at all: they just chose to build the company with the smartest people they could find and let them figure it out.
If you can't explain what you plan to do in practical, meaningful terms – you're probably full of crap.
Ten years from now we'll be designing another system, and everyone will be sitting around bemoaning that it will have to be compatible with NT. That will happen.
I think it's possible we may not see another 'from the ground up' OS developed in our lifetimes.
posts.key_insight: The era of building new operating systems from scratch may be permanently over, as both Unix and NT have proven durable enough to evolve rather than be replaced.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the counterintuitive nature of code optimization through a case study where logical performance improvements didn't yield expected results. He emphasizes that developers must always measure performance rather than relying on assumptions, and that even benchmarking methodology can be flawed. The post references Jackson's famous optimization rules ('Don't do it' and 'Don't do it yet') while acknowledging that performance genuinely matters for user experience. Atwood concludes that developers must balance optimization efforts against system stability and functionality.
key_points: Performance assumptions are frequently wrong - what seems logically faster often isn't, making measurement essential
Benchmarking methodology itself can be flawed, potentially hiding real optimizations or creating false positives
The best optimization is often avoiding unnecessary work entirely rather than making necessary work faster
User experience research shows clear response time thresholds: 0.1s feels instant, 1s maintains flow, 10s loses attention
Performance optimization must be balanced against system stability and functionality
money_quotes: Always measure performance. Never assume anything will be faster or slower until you've actually measured it to be so – you'll be surprised how often your assumptions are wrong.
Sometimes the way you measure performance can even be flawed.
Rules of Optimization: Rule 1: Don't do it. Rule 2 (for experts only): Don't do it yet.
Don't optimize work that doesn't have to be done.
0.1 second is about the limit for having the user feel that the system is reacting instantaneously.
10 seconds is about the limit for keeping the user's attention focused on the dialogue.
posts.key_insight: Optimization intuition is unreliable; always measure actual performance with valid test cases before assuming any change is an improvement.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood proposes an unconventional solution to the problem of link rot: instead of using traditional absolute URLs that break over time, build links using Google search terms with the 'I'm Feeling Lucky' parameter. By extracting 3-4 unique words from a target page and constructing a Google query, you create links that remain valid even when content moves to new locations. He acknowledges the obvious downside—making Google the 'link DNS' for the entire internet—but argues it's a practical workaround for preserving access to referenced content.
key_points: Dead links are inevitable as web pages age, making absolute URLs inherently fragile
Google's 'I'm Feeling Lucky' search can serve as a permanent redirect to content, even when URLs change
The approach requires extracting 3-4 unique identifying words from the target page
Widespread adoption would make Google dangerously powerful as the internet's 'link DNS'
The technique is practical for individual use even if not suitable for universal adoption
money_quotes: Why not build all of our links using relative Google search terms?
Now you have a permanent, unbreakable link to that content.
Google would become unbelievably powerful. They would be the 'link DNS' for the entire internet.

Whenever I link to articles in my code, I try to do so through very specific google search terms, which are likely to produce valid links many years from now – even if the content moves to a different place on the internet.
posts.key_insight: Search engines can function as a content-addressable layer for the web, making links resilient to URL changes by identifying content through its substance rather than its location.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains how network sniffers have become essential to his development workflow due to the rise of REST, XML, SOAP, and .NET Remoting technologies. After evaluating several options including the open-source Ethereal, he recommends Etherdetect for its balance of functionality and ease of use. He notes that sniffing network traffic has helped solve difficult performance and debugging problems in .NET applications. The post also provides practical tips about localhost sniffing limitations and WinPcap library requirements.
key_points: Network sniffers have evolved from occasional tools to essential development utilities due to web services proliferation
Etherdetect offers the best balance of functionality and ease of use despite not being free or perfect
Sniffing network traffic can solve tough performance and debugging problems in distributed applications
Localhost traffic sniffing requires special workarounds due to loopback TCP/IP stack differences
Hyperthreading CPUs require the latest WinPcap libraries for proper operation
money_quotes: With the rise of REST, XML, SOAP and .NET Remoting in the last year, sniffing has become an essential part of my development toolkit.
Etherdetect isn't free, and it isn't perfect, but it offers the best blend of functionality and ease of use that I've found.
Peeking behind the scenes at network traffic has solved some tough performance and debugging problems in our .NET apps.
You typically can't sniff traffic going to localhost, at least not without some special workarounds; the loopback TCP/IP stack behaves very differently than the normal network paths.
posts.key_insight: As distributed computing technologies like REST and SOAP become prevalent, network sniffing transforms from an occasional diagnostic tool into an essential part of the modern developer's debugging toolkit.

posts.summary: This brief technical post documents a workaround for the .NET WebClient class's lack of native HTTP compression support. Atwood shows how to add gzip/deflate headers to requests and then decompress the response using the third-party SharpZipLib library. The post serves as a quick how-to for developers needing to reduce bandwidth usage in their .NET applications. It's a straightforward code snippet post typical of early technical blogging.
key_points: Net.WebClient doesn't natively support HTTP compression despite being able to request it
Google specifically requires a User-Agent header before it will gzip responses
SharpZipLib provides an easy workaround for decompressing gzipped responses
Network sniffers are useful for verifying compression is actually working
money_quotes: The Net.WebClient class doesn't support HTTP compression
And voila, the bandwidth, you have saved eet!
posts.key_insight: When framework classes lack features you need, third-party libraries can fill the gap with minimal additional code.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood investigates claims that .NET regex performance is '20 times slower than Java' by running his own benchmarks. After downloading the Java JDK and converting the test code to VB.NET, he finds the opposite result: .NET actually performs about 40% faster than Java. He notes methodological issues with the original benchmark, including timer resolution limitations and a potential bug in the timing loop. Atwood concludes that hardware improvements over the 6-month period may be the real story, as his Java code ran three times faster than the original benchmark on newer hardware.
key_points: The claim that .NET regex is 20x slower than Java was too extreme to be credible and warranted independent verification
.NET actually benchmarked 40% faster than Java in Atwood's controlled test
The original Java benchmark had methodological issues including 10ms timer resolution limitations and a timing loop bug
Hardware improvements over just 6 months produced a 3x speedup, dwarfing any language differences
Accurate benchmarking requires attention to details like debugger attachment, timer precision, and consistent methodology
money_quotes: First of all, them's fightin' words, and second, those results are just too crazy to really make sense.
Why would C# be over an order of magnitude slower than Java at a classic computer science problem like a regular expression parser? I don't believe it.
So... yeah. I don't know what kind of crack the guys at manageability.org are smoking, but I can't seem to find a local vendor.

Even though .NET appears to perform almost 40 percent faster than Java in this test, it's still interesting that in only 6 months since that Java benchmark was run (813ms), I can produce Java code that runs over three times as fast (266ms)!
So before we put our language war hats on, consider that perhaps the real winner here is the hardware.
posts.key_insight: Extraordinary performance claims demand independent verification, and often the real story lies in methodology flaws or external factors like hardware improvements rather than language differences.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood introduces the LinkTron 5000, a tool he built to automatically determine which words on a web page would generate unique Google search results. This follows his earlier concept of 'unbreakable links' - expressing URLs as search queries rather than absolute addresses to prevent link rot. The tool involves significant Google querying but uses caching for performance. He credits Philipp Lenssen's Google Blogoscoped as a key resource, particularly for word frequency data.
key_points: The LinkTron 5000 analyzes web pages to find words that would produce unique Google search results
The tool implements the 'unbreakable links' concept from a previous post - using search queries instead of absolute URLs
Heavy Google querying is mitigated through pre-caching, though unusual words may slow processing
Word frequency analysis is central to determining which terms are distinctive enough for unique searches
money_quotes: Great concept, but how do you determine which words on a web page are most likely to generate a unique search result?
As you might imagine, this involves quite a bit of Google abuse – all of which is pre-cached for performance.
posts.key_insight: By analyzing word frequency and uniqueness, you can automatically identify which terms on a page would serve as reliable search-based alternatives to fragile direct URLs.

posts.summary: This brief post presents the full lyrics to the Boomtown Rats' 'I Don't Like Mondays' alongside the real story that inspired it: the 1979 Cleveland Elementary school shooting by 16-year-old Brenda Spencer. Atwood provides Spencer's chilling quote explaining her actions simply as not liking Mondays and wanting to 'cheer the day up.' The post serves as a stark reminder of the tragedy behind a well-known pop song, noting that Spencer remains incarcerated with no parole and survivors still remember the event 25 years later.
key_points: The Boomtown Rats song was directly inspired by an actual school shooting in 1979
Brenda Spencer's explanation for killing people was disturbingly casual: she 'just did it for the fun of it'
The post draws a sobering contrast between the catchy pop song and its horrific real-world origin
Spencer has never been paroled and survivors continue to live with the trauma decades later
money_quotes: I just started shooting, that's it. I just did it for the fun of it.
I just don't like Mondays....I just did it because it's a way to cheer the day up.
Nobody likes Mondays.
The lesson today is how to die.
She wants to play with her toys a while.
posts.key_insight: Behind the catchy pop song about hating Mondays lies a real tragedy that reminds us how casually senseless violence can be rationalized.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits his earlier concept of 'Unbreakable Links' after discovering it has been independently conceived by others under names like Memomark, Google URL, and Robust Hyperlinks. He examines a research paper proposing sophisticated techniques for creating durable web links, including unique identifiers, tree walks, and context-based fallbacks. The paper suggests appending lexical signatures to URLs as query parameters, providing both absolute links and search-based fallbacks. Atwood predicts that all future links will eventually be constructed this way, as absolute URLs break down over time and keyword search has proven to be the most effective web navigation method.
key_points: The concept of using search engines to build unique, durable URLs has been independently discovered multiple times under different names
Robust Hyperlinks proposes combining three techniques: unique identifiers, tree walks describing document structure, and contextual information from surrounding content
Lexical signatures can be appended to URLs as query parameters, providing a fallback when the absolute link breaks
Hierarchical directory navigation (like Yahoo) was a workaround for lacking competent search engines, not a preferred design choice
Absolute link systems inevitably break down over time, making search-based navigation essential
money_quotes: All of these terms really refer to the same thing: using a search engine to build an unique URL.
I do think, at some point in the future, all links will be constructed this way.
The existing absolute link system breaks down over time, and I think it's fairly obvious by now that absolute keyword search is the most effective navigation metaphor for the web.
My apologies to Yet Another Hierarchically Organized Oracle, but that style of tree-based directory navigation was always driven by the lack of a competent search engine, not actual choice.
posts.key_insight: The web's absolute URL system is fundamentally fragile, and the future of hyperlinks lies in augmenting them with search-engine-friendly metadata that allows broken links to be recovered through keyword matching.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that dynamic typing in languages like Python and Ruby offers significant productivity advantages over statically-typed languages like Java and C++. He cites respected authors Bruce Eckel and Robert Martin who share frustrations with the overhead of strong typing, particularly the constant need for type casting. The post contrasts Java's verbose interface-based polymorphism with Python's duck typing approach, where objects only need to implement the expected methods without inheritance hierarchies. Atwood frames this as a choice between theoretical correctness and practical productivity.
key_points: Strong typing imposes a 'productivity tax' through constant casting and verbose inheritance structures
Duck typing allows calling methods on any object that supports them, without requiring specific type hierarchies
Respected figures in the Java community (Eckel, Martin) have expressed preference for dynamically-typed languages
The overhead of defining interfaces and inheritance in Java is unnecessary when Python achieves the same polymorphism implicitly
In a competitive global market, developer productivity matters more than type system purity
money_quotes: How much I hate constantly casting objects via CType() and DirectCast(). It's a waste of my time – a productivity tax.
Life in a dynamically typed world is fundamentally simpler.
You can send any message to any object, and the language only cares that the object can accept the message – it doesn't require that the object be a particular type.
You call methods on objects without the unnecessary complexity of inheritance, and most of all without the mind-numbing cast-a-thon that strong typing requires.
Do you want to be correct and pure, or do you want to be productive?
posts.key_insight: Dynamic typing eliminates the bureaucratic overhead of interfaces and type casting, letting developers focus on what objects can do rather than what they formally are.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software development is inherently a 'wicked problem' - one that can only be clearly defined by first attempting to solve it. He rejects waterfall-style upfront planning as foolish and counterproductive, advocating instead for continuous coding throughout a project's lifecycle. The post outlines how development should evolve through phases: prototyping and research early on, embracing refactoring in the middle, and having discipline to ship and gather user feedback at the end. Atwood connects this philosophy to the common thread underlying Agile, XP, and Scrum methodologies.
key_points: Software development is a 'wicked problem' that requires solving the problem once to understand it, then solving it again to create a working solution
Extensive upfront planning is counterproductive because teams cannot foresee all contingencies and edge cases
If you're not writing code for more than two days at a time, you're putting your project at risk
Development phases require different approaches: prototype early, embrace breaking and rebuilding in the middle, have courage to say 'no' and ship at the end
Each version is a stepping stone for the next - ship something and let users provide feedback
money_quotes: I can never get very far without writing code to better understand the problem I am trying to solve.

It's incredibly foolish to believe any team of developers, however talented, can plan out an entire project from start to end, foreseeing all the contingencies, emergent problems, and weird-ass edge conditions they're bound to run into.
Too much up-front planning is counterproductive and potentially disastrous.
If you're not writing code for more than two days at a time, you are putting your project at risk.
Don't fall into the trap of treating existing code as sacrosanct. You should be first in line to obsolete your own code.
You have to finish, because you're never really finished – each version is a stepping stone for the next version.
Microsoft does not make a successful product until version 3.
posts.key_insight: You cannot truly understand a software problem without attempting to solve it first, which means continuous coding and iteration are not optional luxuries but essential requirements for successful development.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the well-documented phenomenon that programming skill varies by orders of magnitude between developers, unlike most other professions. He cites multiple studies showing 10:1 to 25:1 productivity ratios between best and worst programmers, with no correlation between years of experience and performance. This leads to an uncomfortable reality: working with any given developer is essentially a coin flip between genius and incompetence. Atwood concludes that in an era of global offshoring, only those genuinely passionate about programming will survive, and continuous self-improvement through personal study is essential for career longevity.
key_points: Studies consistently show 10:1 to 25:1 productivity differences between best and worst programmers, far exceeding variation in other professions
Experience does not correlate with programming skill - 10-year veterans don't reliably outperform 2-year developers
The profession uniquely lacks the experience-to-performance relationship found in other skilled trades
Global offshoring means mediocre programmers can no longer rely on 'being good with computers' for job security
Self-directed professional development and genuine passion for programming are now survival requirements
money_quotes: I can count the number of truly great programmers I've worked with on one hand.
The good developers are really good, and the bad developers are atrociously bad.
You've got a fifty/fifty chance of working with either a genius or a jackass.
What the hell kind of profession generates so much data supporting the hypothesis that there is no correlation between experience, performance, and skill?
Unless you truly enjoy programming you should seek another profession.
The world simply can't support any more highly paid mediocre coders.
The only way to keep our jobs is to actively keep improving.
posts.key_insight: Programming is uniquely unforgiving: experience doesn't guarantee competence, skill varies by orders of magnitude, and only genuine passion combined with continuous learning can sustain a career.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood briefly recommends Chris Sells as a developer worth following, highlighting his rare combination of technical skill and communication ability. The post serves as a simple endorsement directing readers to Sells' archived blog posts and current content. Atwood expresses his wish that more developers possessed this dual talent for coding and writing. The post is essentially a link recommendation with minimal original content.
key_points: Chris Sells possesses the rare combination of strong coding skills and strong communication skills
Atwood considers everything Sells writes to be worth reading
The post recommends both archived and current content from Sells' blog
Atwood wishes more developers could both code and communicate well
money_quotes: Chris Sells is one of those rare developers who is so talented at both coding and communicating that everything he writes is worth reading.
How I wish this was more common!
posts.key_insight: The ability to both code well and communicate well is a rare and valuable combination in software development.

posts.summary: This brief post highlights the underutilized Command Window feature in Visual Studio .NET 2003, noting that many developers are unaware of its command alias shortcuts and the distinction between immediate and command modes. Atwood shares his use of the '?' command for dumping structure contents while acknowledging it as a basic approach. He looks forward to custom data visualizers coming in Visual Studio Whidbey (2005), which will provide better visual representations of data structures. For those who can't wait, he points to existing third-party visualizer tools available for VS.NET 2003.
key_points: The Command Window in VS.NET 2003 has many underused features including command alias shortcuts
Switching between immediate mode and command mode is done with '>cmd' and 'immed' commands
Visual Studio Whidbey will introduce custom data visualizers for better debugging
Third-party visualizers like DataSet visualizer and VSTweak hacks are available now for VS.NET 2003
money_quotes: One of the most underappreciated features of Visual Studio .NET 2003 is the Command Window.
This works, but it's kinda ghetto.
And naturally you can extend the visualizers with custom viewers for whatever crazy data structure you're creating, too.
posts.key_insight: Effective debugging often means discovering and mastering the underutilized features already built into your development tools.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood criticizes the persistent 'Do you want to save?' dialog that applications still present to users, referencing Alan Cooper's 1995 book 'About Face' which argued against this pattern a decade earlier. He argues that with modern hardware capabilities like large hard drives, applications should automatically save everything and allow users to undo later if needed. The post highlights the frustrating lack of progress in UI design and contrasts traditional applications with database applications that save automatically, which users find surprisingly intuitive.
key_points: The 'save changes' dialog is a decades-old UI anti-pattern that persists despite being criticized since 1995
Modern hardware capabilities (large storage) eliminate the technical excuses for not auto-saving
Applications should save automatically and let users undo, rather than asking permission upfront
Database applications already implement auto-save, proving the pattern works and users prefer it
Difficulty of implementation is not a valid excuse for poor user experience
money_quotes: This rationale is like saying that a beaten dog expects to be beaten again, so we should beat it to make it happy.
The time to make our programs better is now.
In this era of 300gb hard drives, I should never be explicitly prompted for much of anything.
Save it all, and recall the stuff I need. Don't ask me for permission, just do it, and let me take it back later if I need to.
Of course this is harder to program, but so what?
When presented with a database application for the first time, many users ask: 'But how do I save?' Imagine that. An application that saves your work automatically. Revolutionary!
posts.key_insight: Applications should embrace automatic saving and undo rather than burdening users with save dialogs—the difficulty of implementation is not an excuse for poor UX.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood addresses the frustrating Windows server administration issue where deleting website folders doesn't automatically remove their corresponding IIS website entries, leaving behind 'zombie websites.' He offers a practical solution using the often-forgotten FrontPage Server Extensions (later absorbed into SharePoint) which includes a Server Health check feature that can automatically detect and repair orphaned websites. The post is a quick technical tip aimed at lazy administrators who accumulate these leftover entries.
key_points: Deleting a folder from wwwroot doesn't automatically clean up the corresponding IIS website entry
FrontPage Server Extensions were absorbed into SharePoint around 2002, though the relationship remains confusing
The 'Check Server Health' tool in FrontPage Site Administration can auto-repair and remove orphaned websites in bulk
The extensions appear differently depending on whether you're running a full or limited version of IIS
money_quotes: Isn't it annoying how deleting a folder from your wwwroot$ doesn't automatically remove the corresponding website in IIS?
If you're lazy like me, you probably have about a dozen left-over zombie websites waiting to be deleted (or eat some brains).
Remember those hoary old FrontPage extensions? The Microsoft proprietary version of WebDAV?
I still don't fully understand the nature of this relationship, but you may see it referred to by either name.
posts.key_insight: Legacy Microsoft tools like FrontPage Extensions, despite being largely forgotten, can still provide useful administrative shortcuts for cleaning up server cruft.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques Microsoft's Calculator Plus for defaulting to an ugly custom skin instead of the standard Windows UI. While acknowledging that skinning is somewhat subjective, he argues that non-standard interfaces need strong justification and professional design. He contrasts this poorly-executed skin with Windows Media Center, which has a legitimate reason (couch usage with remote) for its custom interface. The post advocates for respecting user expectations around standard OS look and feel.
key_points: Custom UI skins should not be the default, especially when poorly designed
Deviating from standard OS look and feel requires strong justification
If you must skin an interface, hire a professional graphic designer
Windows Media Center exemplifies justified UI deviation due to its unique usage context (remote control from couch)
Usability trumps visual flair - 'boring' standard UI is often the better choice
money_quotes: My eyes! The goggles, they do nothing!
Perhaps boring, but far more usable.
At least have a professional graphic designer put together a skin if you're going to traumatize the user with a non-standard UI.
You better have a damn good reason to deviate from the standard OS look and feel that users expect.
posts.key_insight: Non-standard UI skins need both strong justification and professional execution - without both, you're just traumatizing users for no good reason.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood highlights the utility of hex editors for developers, specifically recommending Hex Workshop, a tool he's used since 1997. He notes his satisfaction with the tool's continued active development and new features. The post showcases the structure viewer feature, which allows viewing and editing binary file structures like zip files. His immediate use case involves examining MP3 ID3 tag structures in binary files.
key_points: Hex editors are specialized but indispensable tools when you need them
Hex Workshop has been actively developed since at least 1997, showing longevity in developer tools
Structure viewers allow developers to understand and edit binary file formats in-place
Binary file comparison with resynchronization is valuable for diff operations on non-text files
money_quotes: When you need it, a hex editor is indispensable.
I've used Hex Workshop since, geez, 1997!
You can actually do in-place editing on the structures, and of course, define your own.
Loverboy? Hey, I never said I had good taste in music.
posts.key_insight: Specialized developer tools like hex editors may be rarely needed, but their value becomes clear when working directly with binary file structures.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood criticizes Microsoft's decision to ship Visual Studio 2005 (Whidbey) with an updated version of Visual SourceSafe rather than a lighter version of the new Team System. While acknowledging VSS will get some improvements like remote access and better performance, he argues the decade-old application is fundamentally broken and needs replacement, not incremental updates. He sees this as fragmenting the developer community into VSS and Team System users, creating a caste system where modern source control becomes an expensive enterprise-only feature. The decision treats source control—a fundamental development need—as an afterthought.
key_points: Visual SourceSafe is a ten-year-old application that needs replacement, not incremental improvements—basic features like branching are unusable
Shipping both VSS and Team System fragments developers into incompatible ecosystems, with VSS likely seeing wider adoption simply because it's included by default
Team System appears destined for the expensive enterprise market, leaving everyday developers with inferior tools
Source control is a fundamental development function that Microsoft is treating as a stopgap concern
The VS 2005 Express products don't support source control at all, compounding the problem
money_quotes: Visual SourceSafe sucks. I'm sorry, but it has to be said.
This isn't an app that needs some incremental improvements, it's an app that needs to be taken out behind the woodshed and put out of its misery.
It is acceptable for rudimentary 'better than nothing' source control, but not much more.
We can't even use branching on our projects at work because VSS has such terrible support for it!
It creates a development caste system.
How about a nice, modern version of source control for the common folk, without all that enterprise marketing crap (and the corresponding sticker shock).
Source control is a bread and butter function of development.
posts.key_insight: Essential developer tools like source control should be modernized and democratized, not stratified into expensive enterprise products while legacy software gets life support.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood highlights Dan Appleman's argument that technical skills alone won't sustain a programming career, especially during tight job markets. The post emphasizes that personal and interpersonal skills—communication, leadership, confidence, and likability—often determine career success more than coding ability. Atwood recommends developers actively pursue activities that build these soft skills, such as writing classes, public speaking, drama, and volunteer work. He acknowledges the challenge for introverts but stresses the importance of consciously choosing situations that exercise interpersonal abilities.
key_points: Technical brilliance alone isn't enough to survive career fluctuations and tight job markets
Personal skills—communication, leadership, confidence, likability—are essential for most developers
College students should take writing classes, do public speaking, join clubs, and volunteer to build these skills
Improving personal skills is harder than improving technical skills, requiring conscious effort
Introverted developers must actively force themselves into situations that exercise interpersonal abilities
money_quotes: Sometimes, it's the non-technical things that make you a better programmer than someone in India.
It's your personal skills that will count.
Yes, there are those who are so technologically brilliant they can get away with caring just about technology, but for most of us these other skills are essential.
Don't let your technical classes get in the way of getting a good education.
It's much easier to improve technical skills than personal skills.
Like most developers, I'm an introvert, so I have to actively force myself into those situations that I would ordinarily avoid.
posts.key_insight: Career longevity in software development depends less on technical prowess and more on deliberately cultivating interpersonal skills that most developers naturally avoid.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces a CodeProject article about converting URLs to MHTML archives using .NET. The utility can save web pages in four formats (complete HTML with files, single MHTML archive, HTML only, or text), mimicking Internet Explorer's 'Save As' functionality. He explains the practical use case of needing single-file web archives for emailing reports or storing in document management systems. Atwood expresses surprise that IE's web archive format is actually based on an internet standard (RFC 2557).
key_points: The .NET class can convert any URL to four different formats in a single line of code
MHTML archives are useful for emailing reports or storing in document management systems because they contain everything in one file
IE's 'Web Archive' format is based on RFC 2557, an actual internet standard for multipart MIME messages
The functionality also works in Firefox through an extension
money_quotes: It's a lot easier to email someone a report – or store it in a document management system – when you have a single MHTML file!
I was as surprised as anyone else to find that IE's 'Web Archive' save option is based on an actual internet standard
posts.key_insight: Single-file web archives based on the MHTML standard solve the practical problem of sharing complete web pages without losing embedded resources.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood highlights Eric Lippert's argument that obsessing over micro-optimizations is counterproductive and harmful to code quality. The post critiques developers who chase trivial performance gains through 'tips and tricks' like choosing And over Mod operators, arguing such practices lead to unmaintainable and ultimately broken code. Lippert advocates instead for setting user-centered performance goals, taking rigorous measurements, and prioritizing correctness and clarity over raw speed. Atwood connects this to his observation that great developers recognize they are often their own worst enemies when it comes to optimization.
key_points: Micro-optimizations based on 'tips and tricks' produce unmaintainable code that ends up broken, which is the ultimate performance failure
Writing fast code requires analysis of user scenarios and rigorous measurement, not cheap operator tricks
Correctness and clarity ('RIGHT') should always be prioritized over speed ('FAST')
Unused or confusing code has real performance costs by hindering developers doing actual performance analysis
Great developers recognize they are their own worst enemies when it comes to premature optimization
money_quotes: 'Broken' is the ultimate in bad performance, no matter how fast the incorrect program is.
People who base their choice of operator on utterly nonsensical rationales are not going to write code that is maintainable or robust.

Writing fast code does not require a collection of cheap tricks, it requires analysis of user scenarios to set goals, followed by a rigorous program of careful measurements and small changes until the goals are reached.
RIGHT is better than FAST. Write the code to be extremely straightforward.
Code that makes sense is code which can be analyzed and maintained, and that makes it performant.
The real performance cost – it makes it harder for the devs doing the perf analysis to do their jobs well!
Great developers realize that they are their own worst enemies.
posts.key_insight: Chasing micro-optimizations produces unmaintainable code that inevitably breaks, and broken code has the worst performance of all—making correctness and clarity the true path to high-performing software.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares a quick syntax highlighting tool called Squishy Syntax Highlighter and demonstrates it with a VB.NET CRC32 implementation he had previously found. He notes that Visual Studio automatically preserves syntax highlighting when pasting into RtfTextBox controls, which sparks an idea about converting RTF to HTML instead of using hardcoded highlighter settings. The post is primarily a code snippet share with a brief musing about workflow optimization. It represents the early, more utilitarian nature of Coding Horror before the blog became known for longer-form essays.
key_points: Squishy Syntax Highlighter provides quick HTML syntax highlighting for code snippets
Visual Studio preserves IDE syntax highlighting when pasting into RTF controls
Converting RTF to HTML could preserve personal IDE highlighting preferences instead of using a tool's hardcoded colors
The CRC32 implementation uses a standard lookup table approach for calculating 32-bit checksums
Early blog posts often served as personal code snippet repositories
money_quotes: I wonder if it might be easier to just convert the RTF to HTML – at least that way, I get my IDE highlighting settings instead of the hard coded Squishy settings.
posts.key_insight: Developer tools that preserve your personal workflow preferences (like IDE color schemes) are worth building, even if alternatives exist.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reviews Robert Glass's 'Facts and Fallacies of Software Engineering,' finding value in its lesser-known insights despite criticizing its writing style and repetition of well-known concepts. He highlights the 'Rule of Three' for code reuse: building reusable components is three times harder than single-use ones, and code should be tested across three different applications before being considered truly reusable. Atwood argues that this rule actually underestimates the difficulty, claiming truly reusable code is an order of magnitude harder to write. He warns developers against prematurely building 'general purpose' solutions for hypothetical future needs, calling this the 'delusion of reuse.'
key_points: Building reusable components is at least three times harder than single-use components, and Atwood believes it's actually an order of magnitude harder
Code should be tested in three different applications with different audiences before being considered truly reusable
Developers often fall into the trap of building 'general purpose' solutions for unknown future projects that may never materialize
Sometimes resisting the urge to write reusable code is the right choice for getting current projects done
You cannot validate reusability without actually attempting reuse across different developers and problem domains
money_quotes: I believe writing a truly reusable class is an order of magnitude harder than writing a single use class.
Sometimes the right thing to do is resist the urge to write 'general purpose' solutions.
How confident are you that the so-called 'general purpose' solution you built will actually work for these… unknown future projects? Have you tried it?
You can't know if you have a strong case for reuse unless you've tried – and possibly failed – to use that same bit of code on at least three different projects, with three different audiences.
Until you've invested the additional effort to implement that 'reusable' code with different developers and different problem domains, all you have is the delusion of reuse.
I've seen too many developers fall into this trap. Myself included.
posts.key_insight: True code reusability can only be validated through actual reuse across multiple projects—anything else is premature abstraction and self-deception.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the UI design philosophy behind the game 'You Don't Know Jack,' which Jellyvision calls the Interactive Conversation Interface (iCi). The iCi approach falls between passive television (no user control) and unrestricted web navigation (complete user control), offering limited choices at any given moment. This creates a conversational dynamic where control is shared between designers and users, similar to human conversation where neither party has complete control. Atwood connects this to the familiar wizard UI pattern and Microsoft's Inductive User Interface, suggesting this guided-but-interactive approach would be significant in future OS design.
key_points: The Interactive Conversation Interface sits between passive media (TV) and fully interactive media (web), offering constrained choices at each moment
Shared control between program and user models real human conversation, where neither party can unilaterally dictate the discussion
The user influences but cannot completely determine their experience, while designers arrange all possible paths
This philosophy connects to the wizard UI metaphor and Microsoft's Inductive User Interface approach
money_quotes: You are not without influence over what you will experience, although you cannot completely decide what you will experience.
In a conversation, you can't unilaterally decide what gets discussed. The other person is not a machine.
The design team arranges for all the possible experiences. The individual's actions determine which experience actually transpires.
A television program gives you no options at all. The Web and multimedia programs usually allow you to go anywhere at any time you want. An iCi program falls between these extremes.
posts.key_insight: The most engaging interactive experiences model human conversation by sharing control between designer and user, rather than offering either passive consumption or overwhelming freedom.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts his successful adoption of POPFile's Bayesian spam filtering, achieving nearly 99% accuracy after two months. He credits Paul Graham's 2002 essay for popularizing the statistical approach to spam fighting, explaining why it works better than rule-based systems. The post examines how Bayesian filters handle evolving spam tactics like image-only emails and spoofed text. Atwood also evaluates whitelist-based alternatives like SpamArrest, which achieve near-100% effectiveness by requiring human verification, but concludes he's not a fan of burdening senders with verification steps.
key_points: Bayesian filtering assigns actual probabilities rather than arbitrary scores, making the system's decisions transparent and mathematically grounded
The technique considers both spam indicators and innocent words, so legitimate emails containing suspicious terms aren't automatically flagged
Early researchers dismissed Bayesian filtering because they failed to parse headers and HTML, missing crucial spam signals
Whitelist systems with CAPTCHA verification can achieve near-100% spam blocking but impose friction on legitimate senders
Spammers' attempts to defeat content filters (image-only messages, random word insertion) are still detectable through header and HTML analysis
money_quotes: It discovered, of course, that terms like 'virtumundo' and 'teens' were good indicators of spam. But it also discovered that 'per' and 'FL' and 'ff0000' are good indicators of spam.
The problem with a 'score' is that no one knows what it means. The user doesn't know what it means, but worse still, neither does the developer of the filter.
Nonhackers don't often realize this, but most hackers are very competitive.
This type of whitelist delivers damn near 100% effective spam blocking, with no training period required. There's simply no way a machine can pass this test.
Putting the burden of verification on the sender seems kind of onerous to me.
Bayes will always let some spam slip through, so it is arguably the only way to get an ironclad '100 percent' effective spam blocking.
posts.key_insight: Bayesian spam filtering succeeds because it measures actual probabilities rather than arbitrary scores, making the system's reasoning transparent and allowing it to weigh all evidence—both for and against—when classifying messages.

posts.summary: Atwood revisits his earlier criticism of pure object-oriented programming, citing Paul Graham's argument that OOP generates busywork disguised as productivity. Graham compares OOP enthusiasts to programmers who padded fanfold pages with elaborate comments, noting that simple operations become entire files of classes and methods. Atwood advocates for moderation, arguing that a little object orientation goes a long way and that pushing everything into objects creates maintenance nightmares. His guiding principle is simplicity, measured by producing the least volume of source code.
key_points: Pure object-oriented programming is oversold and can generate false productivity
OOP allows programmers to incorporate scaffolding directly into source code, inflating its apparent complexity
Simple operations that could be one-liners become entire files of classes and methods in OOP-heavy codebases
Moderation in OOP usage prevents 'giant masses of repetitive code'
The best approach typically produces the least volume of source code
money_quotes: Object-oriented programming generates a lot of what looks like work.
Object-oriented programming is like crack for these people: it lets you incorporate all this scaffolding right into your source code.
Something that a Lisp hacker might handle by pushing a symbol onto a list becomes a whole file of classes and methods.
It is a good tool if you want to convince yourself, or someone else, that you are doing a lot of work.
A little object orientation goes a long way.
I like to err on the side of simplicity, and that typically means the approach that produces the least volume of source code.
posts.key_insight: Object-oriented programming can become a tool for generating the appearance of productivity rather than actual simplicity, and moderation in its use leads to more maintainable code.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood discusses the inherent vulnerability of Bayesian spam filters when spammers can obtain feedback about which emails get through. He references John Graham-Cumming's presentation showing that POPFile, a Bayesian filter, can be defeated by spammers using the same technology against it. The post also explains why challenge/response email systems are problematic and praises Windows XP SP2's decision to block HTML image rendering by default in Outlook Express, which prevents spammers from using web beacons to verify email addresses and gather the feedback they need to defeat filters.
key_points: Challenge/response email systems are dismissive because they implicitly say 'my time is more important than yours'
Bayesian spam filters can be defeated by spammers using the same Bayesian techniques—but only if they get feedback about what gets through
Web beacons (tiny images in HTML emails) allow spammers to verify active email addresses and gather filter-defeating feedback
Blocking external images in email clients is an effective defense against spammer reconnaissance
The arms race between spam filters and spammers fundamentally favors whoever has better feedback loops
money_quotes: Challenge/Response is the ultimate email diss. By using it you are saying, 'my time is more important than yours.'
When pitting 'evil' POPFile against good POPFile, the good guys lose.
Spammers can use Bayesian filters to defeat Bayesian filters – if they get feedback about what mails are getting through!
Putting images in HTML seems innocent enough, but retrieving any image results in a direct request from your computer to the spammer's webserver.
With this tiny bit of feedback, they could conceivably defeat any anti-spam technology.
posts.key_insight: The effectiveness of spam filtering depends critically on denying spammers feedback about what gets through—making seemingly innocent features like HTML image loading a serious security vulnerability.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques the argument that J2EE's complexity is a feature rather than a bug. Responding to a study showing VS.NET was twice as productive as J2EE at one-tenth the cost, he addresses pushback from Java developers who claimed that difficult tools 'weed out weak developers' and produce better code. Atwood argues this reasoning is backwards—bad programmers write bad code regardless of tools, while good programmers benefit from productivity gains without sacrificing quality. He dismisses the notion that tool difficulty correlates with code quality, calling it an attempt to reframe a weakness as a strength.
key_points: A commissioned study found VS.NET twice as productive as J2EE at roughly one-tenth the cost
Dismissing a study solely because it was sponsored (rather than engaging with its substance) is intellectually lazy
The argument that 'difficult tools produce better code' inverts reality—tool complexity doesn't improve developer quality
RAD tools make good programmers more productive without causing them to write worse code
Bad programmers write bad code regardless of tooling; good programmers maintain quality while benefiting from easier tools
money_quotes: VS.NET is about twice as productive as either of the two IBM J2EE environments, at approximately one-tenth the cost.
Disregarding the report sight unseen because Microsoft sponsored it is like disregarding someone's opinion based on their ethnicity.
J2EE weeds out the weak developers! It rampages through the land, leaving a wake of crushed and broken developers weeping in its path!
At any point in any of those projects, can you ever remember saying to yourself: Developing applications is far too easy!
Bad programmers write bad code. Good programmers write good code. RAD lets bad programmers write bad code faster. RAD does NOT cause good programmers to suddenly start writing bad code.
What I find amusing is that someone would actually try to invert this argument, proposing that bad tools are good because they force you to produce better code.
posts.key_insight: Tool difficulty doesn't filter for developer quality—it just makes everyone less productive, while bad programmers remain bad regardless of their toolchain.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software developers make terrible user experience designers because they are fundamentally different from typical users. Drawing on Alan Cooper's concept of 'Homo Logicus,' he explains that programmers are outliers who thrive on complexity and control, while normal users prioritize simplicity and success. The post uses the Windows 95 'Find File' function as an example of how features that seem valuable to developers actually burden regular users. Atwood concludes that building simple, usable software requires developers to consciously abandon their natural instincts and think like ordinary humans.
key_points: Developers are 'freakishly outside the norm' and represent edge conditions, not typical users
Homo Logicus trades simplicity for control and accepts failure in exchange for understanding how things work
Normal users see additional features and parameters as more opportunities to make mistakes, not as valuable flexibility
The engineer joke perfectly illustrates the programmer mindset: prioritizing understanding the problem over surviving it
Building simple applications requires more skill than building complex ones nobody can use
money_quotes: Of all the professional hubris I've observed in software developers, perhaps the greatest sin of all is that we consider ourselves typical users.
We're not even remotely average – we are the edge conditions.
If you are letting me design your software, your project is in trouble.
The price of control is always more effort and increased complexity.
Homo logicus are driven by an irresistible desire to understand how things work. By contrast, Homo sapiens have a strong desire for success.
