While programmers also want to succeed, they will frequently accept failure as the price to pay for understanding.
Anybody can build a complex application that nobody can figure out how to use. That's easy. Building an application that's simple to use... well, now that takes actual skill.
posts.key_insight: Developers must recognize that their love of complexity and control makes them the worst possible judges of what normal users need, and great software design requires consciously overriding their natural instincts.

posts.summary: Atwood highlights an article warning against dogmatic adherence to trendy frameworks at the expense of good design. The post emphasizes that blindly following heavyweight frameworks like EJB forces developers to write code with no business value. It advocates for thoughtful consideration of lightweight alternatives like Hibernate, Spring, and PicoContainer when they can accomplish the required job. The piece concludes with Atwood's characteristic humor, joking that users are the real problem with software development.
key_points: Dogmatic conformity to frameworks can undermine good design and implementation practices
Simple problems deserve simple solutions—public fields can be better than boilerplate getters/setters
Heavyweight frameworks often require writing significant code with no business value
Choose frameworks deliberately rather than following bandwagons or rebelling against them
Consider whether a lighter framework can accomplish the required job before adopting complexity
money_quotes: Your code sucks if it dogmatically conforms to a trendy framework at the cost of following good design and implementation practices.
If a field is transparently readable and writable why not simply make the field public?
In general it's a good thing whenever you can throw out, or avoid writing, some code.
Using a heavy framework generally requires that you must write a significant amount of code that has no business value.
Never blindly adopt a heavy framework just because it's the current bandwagon.
Likewise, don't blindly adopt a lightweight framework in defiance. Always give due consideration to your choices.
The real problem with software development is the users. It's unbelievable. They've caused problems with every program I've ever written.
posts.key_insight: Framework choices should be driven by pragmatic assessment of actual needs, not by dogmatic adherence to trends or reflexive rejection of them.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques the widespread practice of using cryptic, machine-generated URLs instead of human-readable ones. He's particularly horrified by the use of GUIDs in blog permalinks, calling out DasBlog and major sites like Best Buy, Amazon, and eBay for their incomprehensible URLs. He contrasts these with cleaner examples from CNN and other sites, arguing that readable URLs are achievable with existing tools like 404 handlers and URL rewriting. The post advocates for prioritizing human understanding over machine convenience when designing URLs.
key_points: GUIDs in URLs represent the nadir of URL design, turning addresses into meaningless strings
Major websites routinely 'devalue the address bar' with cryptic, machine-oriented URLs
Clean, human-readable URLs are achievable using 404 handlers and URL rewriting techniques
Building crappy URLs is laziness, not technical necessity, given available tools in IIS and ASP.NET
money_quotes: Has it really come to this?
This is my GUID. There are many like it but this one is mine. My GUID is my best friend. It is my life.
It's bad enough that so many websites devalue the address bar with nonsensical geek-speak URLs
Building crappy URLs is just plain laziness.

We should be leveraging 404 handlers and URL Rewriting to build URLs that are simple for people to understand, instead of taking the low road and building URLs that are easy for machines to understand.
posts.key_insight: URLs are for humans, not machines, and developers should invest the modest effort required to make them readable rather than defaulting to cryptic auto-generated strings.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares an ASP.NET CAPTCHA server control he created by converting and repackaging existing code into a drag-and-drop component. The implementation requires minimal setup with only three properties to configure, though it does need an HttpHandler entry in web.config. He tested the CAPTCHA against professional OCR software and found it effective due to its low contrast and warped characters. The post serves as a brief technical announcement pointing readers to the full CodeProject article.
key_points: Converted an existing CAPTCHA code sample to VB.NET and packaged it as a reusable ASP.NET server control
The control is designed for simplicity with only three properties to configure
Requires an HttpHandler configuration in web.config to serve dynamically generated images
Tested against OmniPage Pro 14 OCR software and proved resistant to simple OCR attacks
Low contrast and character warping are the key features that make the CAPTCHA effective
money_quotes: It's as simple as I could make it: a total drag and drop, set the (three) properties and forget it implementation.
I tested this CAPTCHA against OCR software, specifically OmniPage Pro 14.
It does surprisingly well for a couple key reasons – low contrast, and all the characters are warped.
It's possible to defeat it, but it's definitely not a trivial 'drag to OCR window' situation.
posts.key_insight: Effective CAPTCHAs don't need to be complex—simple techniques like low contrast and character warping can provide meaningful protection against automated OCR attacks.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces improvements to his ASP.NET CAPTCHA server control, detailing a list of enhancements including better font sizing, improved warping algorithms, and removal of confusing characters like 1, 0, I, and O. He emphasizes the tradeoff between OCR-resistance and human readability, suggesting that most applications can use shorter, less warped CAPTCHAs since any CAPTCHA is usually sufficient to block casual bot attacks. The control is available for download from CodeProject.
key_points: The improved control now respects standard ASP.NET server control properties and adds configurable length and warping options
Confusing characters (1, 0, I, O) were removed to improve human readability
For most applications, having any CAPTCHA is sufficient to block casual bot attacks, so extreme distortion isn't necessary
The warping algorithm was improved to prevent drawing outside the bounding box
money_quotes: If you are willing to sacrifice less OCR-ability for more human readability, you can adjust the CaptchaLength and CaptchaFontWarping properties to taste.
For most applications, simply having a CAPTCHA of any sort is probably enough to block casual bot attacks, and shorter less warped phrases are definitely a lot easier to read.
posts.key_insight: Most applications don't need extremely distorted CAPTCHAs—any CAPTCHA is usually enough to deter casual bots, so optimizing for human readability is a reasonable tradeoff.

posts.summary: Atwood recounts debugging a strange duplicate-record bug that turned out to be caused by users double-clicking an OK button. This leads him to a broader critique of double-click as a UI paradigm, arguing it was a workaround forced by Apple's single-button mouse in 1984 that has persisted despite being unintuitive and difficult to execute. He notes that many users never learn the distinction between single and double-click, so they defensively double-click everything, causing problems across applications. The web's single-click model is presented as the superior approach, and Atwood calls for the death of double-click as a standard interaction pattern.
key_points: A production bug was caused by users double-clicking a button that developers assumed would only be single-clicked, illustrating the gap between developer and user mental models
Double-click originated as a workaround for Apple's single-button mouse limitation, not as an inherently good design choice
The mechanics of double-clicking are demanding—requiring precise timing and motor control—making it an unnecessarily difficult gesture for such a common action
Many users never fully learn when to single vs double-click, so they double-click everything defensively, poisoning their behavior across all applications
The web's single-click model avoids these problems and should be the standard going forward
money_quotes: No developer would ever double-click on a button, because developers have too much GUI knowledge.
Many users never fully learn the distinction between single-click and double-click. So they give up and simply... double-click everything.
Double-click has always been a bad idea.
How would anyone know, without being told, that double-click is a possible action? It's a completely hidden behavior.
Can't we just put another damn button on the mouse? Is two buttons really that hard to figure out?
Double-click is such a profoundly bad idea that it actually manages to spill over into other applications and poison them with bad learned user behavior.
Thanks, Apple... for nothing!
posts.key_insight: Double-click is a legacy workaround from 1984's single-button mouse limitation that persists despite being unintuitive, mechanically demanding, and responsible for poisoning user behavior across all applications.

posts.summary: Atwood revisits the double-click usability problem by highlighting a little-known Windows feature that maps all actions to single clicks. Introduced in Windows 98, this option transforms the desktop experience to follow web conventions, where items are underlined like hyperlinks and hovering selects them. He urges readers not to dismiss Microsoft's attempt to solve this fundamental UI inconsistency, suggesting the solution deserves more consideration than it received.
key_points: Windows 98 introduced a single-click mode that eliminates the need for double-clicking
The feature uses web metaphors (underlining, hover-to-select) to create consistency
Microsoft made a genuine attempt to solve a real usability problem that went largely unnoticed
money_quotes: Don't be too quick to dismiss Microsoft's effort to solve the double-click problem.
It's an attempt to map everything to the single mouse click, using the web metaphor.
Note the subtle selection behavior to accommodate this change; hovering your mouse over an item now selects it.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft actually tried to fix the double-click usability problem in Windows 98, but the solution was buried in settings and largely forgotten.

posts.summary: Atwood argues that most software users are 'perpetual intermediates' who quickly graduate from beginner status but never become experts, and that this intermediate majority should be the primary design target. He cites Alan Cooper's observation that beginners rapidly move to intermediate status or abandon the product entirely, while experts are rare and transient. Rather than designing for the extremes, Atwood advocates for the Inductive User Interface (IUI) approach, which combines web conventions like back buttons and activity-centric pages with traditional GUI richness. He points to Microsoft Money 2000 as the exemplar of this approach and urges developers to adopt these patterns immediately.
key_points: Most users are perpetual intermediates at nearly everything they do, including software - expertise is rare and narrow
Beginners quickly become intermediates or quit entirely; nobody willingly stays incompetent
Software development is traditionally pulled between developer advocates (experts) and marketing (beginners), leaving intermediates underserved
The Inductive User Interface combines web UI metaphors (back button, hyperlinks, single-page activities) with rich desktop capabilities
Stealing successful UI patterns from the web is 'such an utter no-brainer' that the only question is why adoption is so slow
money_quotes: I am a perpetual intermediate at a vast array of tasks, and expert at only a very, very tiny number of tasks.
When people achieve an adequate level of experience and ability, they generally stay there forever.
I think intermediate users are the only users that matter.
Beginners should either become intermediates or, in a manner of speaking, die trying.
The difficulty of maintaining a high level of expertise means that experts come and go rapidly.
Never write what you can steal.
Stealing the wildly successful web UI metaphors is such an utter no-brainer.
posts.key_insight: Design software for the permanent majority of intermediate users, not for the transient extremes of beginners and experts.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares Dave Winer's provocative 1995 slogan 'We Make Shitty Software... With Bugs!' to make a point about the nature of software development. The post argues that all software is inherently imperfect and that acknowledging this reality is healthier than pretending otherwise. Winer's philosophy emphasizes that software is a continuous process of improvement rather than a finished product. The post advocates for validating customer frustrations while committing to incremental improvements. Atwood humorously admits his own software is shitty, noting that only a handful of programmers can produce perfect code.
key_points: Software is never finished—it's an evolving process that will always have flaws
Acknowledging imperfection to unhappy customers builds trust and moves conversations toward solutions
The measure of good software development is the direction of improvement, not current perfection
Perfect software exists only in imagination; real software crashes, loses data, and frustrates users
Continuous improvement is the realistic path forward for all but the most exceptional programmers
money_quotes: We make shitty software. And so do you!
Software is a process, it's never finished, it's always evolving. That's its nature.
The only software that's perfect is one you're dreaming about.
Real software crashes, loses data, is hard to learn and hard to use. But it's a process. We'll make it less shitty. Just watch!
When you get an upgrade, you look for the process, see if they responded to your needs. Which way are they moving?
All the rest of us can do is keep making our software less shitty over time – a process of continuous improvement.
posts.key_insight: Accepting that all software is inherently flawed liberates developers to focus on what actually matters: continuous improvement and honest communication with users.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood celebrates the announcement that Windows XP Media Center Edition 2005 will finally be available as a retail product, ending Microsoft's restriction of only selling it pre-installed on complete PCs. He argues this was a significant mistake that pushed enthusiasts toward alternatives like MythTV and Linux. Atwood shares his credentials as an HTPC enthusiast and highlights MCE's technical significance as one of Microsoft's first commercial products built entirely in .NET. He also discusses the unique challenges of designing '10 foot interfaces' for TV-based applications, noting that despite MCE's quality, TiVo still has superior UI design.
key_points: Microsoft's previous restriction of MCE to OEM-only distribution was a 'great wrong' that artificially limited an excellent product and drove users to competitors
MCE was one of Microsoft's first commercial products developed entirely in .NET, serving as an important proof of concept for the technology
Designing usable '10 foot interfaces' for home theater use is radically different from traditional GUI design and requires DirectX for acceptable performance
The HTPC enthusiast community developed strong alternatives (SageTV, MythTV, Freevo) specifically because Microsoft made MCE inaccessible
Despite being one of the best HTPC applications available, MCE's UI is still inferior to TiVo's
money_quotes: This rectifies a great wrong; the prior two editions of MCE were excellent mainstream products stymied into a tiny niche because nobody could get a copy!
Did you know that MCE was one of the first commercial products from MS developed entirely in .NET?
Putting together a usable '10 foot interface' is radically different from all my other GUI design experience.
You really have to bite the bullet and develop against DirectX if you want something that looks like MCE and performs at a decent speed.
In short, I'm a giant dork – but you knew that already.
And, really, what other logical use is there for 300gb+ hard drives, other than to fill them with video?
posts.key_insight: Artificially restricting access to good software through distribution limitations only drives users to build or adopt alternatives, ultimately hurting the original product's market position.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that daily builds are no longer ambitious enough given modern hardware capabilities, and developers should instead practice continuous integration with multiple builds per day. He points to the Windows NT project, which managed daily builds despite 5.6 million lines of code and 19-hour build times, as proof that smaller projects have no excuse. The post emphasizes that code should almost always be in a compilable state and that holding files checked out overnight is a red flag. Atwood cites Martin Fowler's arguments that continuous integration catches integration bugs the same day they're introduced, dramatically reducing debugging time.
key_points: The technological argument against frequent builds (time constraints) is obsolete with modern fast processors and distributed build tools
Developers should never hold onto code changes for more than a day, and ideally should integrate every few hours
Code should almost always be in a compilable state - if you can't check in for more than a day, you may have bad programming habits
Continuous integration catches integration bugs the same day they're introduced, making bug hunting far easier
Software should be grown incrementally from a small functional base with aggressive daily check-ins as checkpoints
money_quotes: I believe, for all but the largest projects, you should be building multiple times per day.
I get aggravated when other developers leave files checked out overnight.
If you're writing code that is so broken you can't check any of it in for more than a day – you might have some bad programming habits.
I believe it's far healthier to grow or accrete your software from a small, functional base and use aggressive daily check-ins to checkpoint those growth stages.
Continuous Integration can slash the amount of time spent in integration hell, in fact it can turn hell into a non-event.
Those of us who work on projects of less staggering proportions will have a hard time explaining why we aren't also reaping the benefits of this practice.
posts.key_insight: Modern hardware has eliminated every excuse for not practicing continuous integration, and developers who can't keep their code in a compilable state for more than a day likely have deeper problems with their programming habits.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines Microsoft's Windows Error Reporting data revealing that 70% of Windows crashes come from device drivers, and more importantly, that fixing just 20% of top-reported bugs could solve 80% of customer issues. He argues that most applications fail to collect crash data from users, leaving developers blind to the real problems their software causes. The post advocates for implementing global exception handling to automatically gather error reports, emphasizing that silent exceptions represent willfully discarded opportunities to improve user experience. Atwood provides practical resources for .NET exception handling and suggests the approach is so valuable he plans to build reusable components for it.
key_points: 70% of Windows crashes are caused by device drivers, which run in privileged kernel mode where exceptions are irrecoverable
Microsoft's error reporting data shows fixing 20% of top bugs solves 80% of customer issues, and addressing just 1% would fix 50%
Most applications have 'silent exceptions' that never report back to developers, wasting valuable diagnostic data
Kernel-mode code offers performance benefits but at the cost of stability - crashes bring down the entire OS rather than just one application
Global exception handling frameworks exist in .NET that can capture and report errors without requiring application changes
money_quotes: Seventy percent of Windows crashes involve one particular kind of software: device drivers.
Fixing 20 percent of the top-reported bugs can solve 80 percent of customer issues.
Addressing 1 percent of the bugs would address 50 percent of the customer issues.
If your exceptions are 'silent,' you are willfully throwing away data that could improve your application for everyday users by eighty percent.
Just because I trust Microsoft to write stable kernel-mode code, that doesn't mean I trust J. Random Taiwanese Device Driver Coder – or myself – to.
Do your applications tell you when users encounter an error?
These are real problems encountered in the wild by actual users, arguably the most important kinds of fixes.
posts.key_insight: Automatic error reporting from production applications provides irreplaceable data about real user problems, and ignoring this feedback loop means willfully accepting a dramatically worse user experience.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares the complete quick reference guide from 'The Pragmatic Programmer,' a book he added to his recommended reading list for developers. The post presents all 70 tips from the book covering everything from mindset ('Care About Your Craft') to practical techniques (DRY, source control, testing) to team organization. Atwood notes that while the book contains valuable wisdom, readers should approach even well-considered checklists with a willingness to question them. The reference card serves both as an introduction for those who haven't read the book and a useful refresher for those who have. The tips span technical practices, professional development, debugging strategies, and software design principles that remain remarkably relevant despite the post's 2004 vintage.
key_points: The 70 tips form a comprehensive philosophy of pragmatic software development, from caring about craft to signing your work
Technical practices emphasized include DRY principle, source control, testing automation, and designing for change
Professional growth requires continuous learning, critical thinking about information sources, and effective communication
Debugging wisdom centers on proving assumptions, not panicking, and recognizing that bugs are rarely in the OS or compiler
Software design should embrace orthogonality, minimize coupling, and acknowledge that no decision is truly final
money_quotes: Why spend your life developing software unless you care about doing it well?
Turn off the autopilot and take control. Constantly critique and appraise your work.
Don't say it can't be done; explain what can be done.
No decision is cast in stone. Instead, consider each as being written in the sand at the beach, and plan for change.
It is rare to find a bug in the OS or the compiler, or even a third-party product or library. The bug is most likely in the application.
A dead program normally does a lot less damage than a crippled one.
Requirements rarely lie on the surface. They're buried deep beneath layers of assumptions, misconceptions, and politics.
Craftsmen of an earlier age were proud to sign their work. You should be, too.
posts.key_insight: Pragmatic programming is a holistic discipline combining technical excellence, continuous learning, clear communication, and professional pride in craftsmanship.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software developers should adopt the portfolio practice common among graphic designers, bringing samples of their work to job interviews rather than relying solely on resume keywords. He observes that developers rarely show their actual work during interviews, despite the obvious benefits of 'show, don't tell.' The post emphasizes that what matters most isn't just having a portfolio, but demonstrating genuine excitement about past projects. Atwood concludes that candidates who can both show their work and articulate what made it fun and challenging stand out dramatically from the competition.
key_points: Developers should borrow the portfolio concept from graphic designers and bring work samples to interviews
Resumes full of buzzwords like 'enterprise' and 'architect' are far less compelling than actual demonstrated work
A portfolio doesn't require web or UI work—well-designed console applications or clever code snippets count
Showing genuine enthusiasm for past projects is more important than the portfolio itself
Being able to communicate what made projects fun and challenging is the ultimate differentiator
money_quotes: Anyone can put together boilerplate resume text, full of assertive verbs and fancy keywords. Blah blah enterprise blah blah strategic blah blah architect blah blah.
No, you don't have to be working on a web application or website to have something worthy of putting in a portfolio.
As a fellow developer, I can appreciate the beauty of a well-designed console application, or a clever applet with hardly any interface at all.
If you've written code you're particularly proud of, show me that.
The portfolio is important, but what's more important is that you are excited about what you worked on.
If you took the time to highlight the cool stuff and bring it with you, that already puts you far ahead of every other candidate I've ever interviewed.
posts.key_insight: Showing your actual work and genuine enthusiasm for it will differentiate you far more than any polished resume full of industry buzzwords.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the debate between VB.NET and C# through a lengthy developer testimonial describing the regrets of choosing VB.NET for a large enterprise project. The post highlights numerous technical shortcomings of VB.NET including poor static analysis, aggressive background compilation issues, lack of syntactic conveniences like multi-line comments, and missing XML documentation support. Atwood partially agrees that VB.NET feels like a 'ghetto language' at Microsoft due to obvious oversights not fixed across releases. However, he pushes back on criticism of background compilation, arguing that leveraging idle CPU cycles for developer assistance is exactly what modern IDEs should do. He notes that VS.NET 2005 promises to address most issues, and closes with a playful jab at case sensitivity in C#.
key_points: VB.NET's compiler lacks thorough static analysis that C# provides, missing obvious bugs like uninitialized variables and non-returning functions
Large VB.NET solutions suffer from aggressive, uncontrollable background compilation that can't keep pace with developer typing speed
VB.NET lacks syntactic conveniences present in C#: using directives, multi-line comments, multi-line strings, and XML documentation generation
The 'automatic conversion' promise from VB6 to VB.NET was a mirage that didn't save time as management expected
Background compilation itself isn't bad—the problem is poor implementation that can't utilize available CPU power effectively
money_quotes: You have to respect a man who isn't about to let mere ignorance keep him from having an opinion.
The pointy-haired contingent won out and not a day goes by that we don't curse them for forcing VB.NET on us.
This is what a compiler is *for*. I wish VB.NET had an 'Option ReallyStrict On' so it would check things as thoroughly as the C# compiler does.
When you choose VB.NET you may get started faster but you'll be paying a tax forever, because the tools just aren't as good as the C# ones.
You definitely get the sense that VB.NET is a ghetto language at Microsoft.
Computers get faster every day. My brain doesn't. Our IDEs should be doing more for us in the background, not less.
Case sensitivity is wrong in every imaginable way. And possibly in a few additional ways I haven't yet imagined.
posts.key_insight: Language and tooling choices that seem minor at project inception compound into significant productivity taxes over time, making it worth enduring a steeper initial learning curve for better long-term developer experience.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood expresses frustration that Microsoft hasn't updated Windows Script Host to support .NET languages, leaving developers stuck with aging VBScript for quick scripting tasks. While third-party solutions exist, none carry official Microsoft support and each has compatibility issues. He references a 2001 Microsoft post promising WSH.NET plans, noting that three years later nothing has materialized. The post highlights the gap between Microsoft's .NET-centric direction and the practical needs of developers who rely on lightweight scripting.
key_points: WSH with VBScript remains useful for quick utilities that don't warrant full .NET console applications
VBScript's similarity to VB.NET creates confusion as developers accidentally try newer syntax
Third-party WSH.NET alternatives exist but lack official blessing, compatibility, and require separate installation
Microsoft promised WSH.NET plans in 2001 but delivered nothing in the following three years
The disconnect between .NET's dominance and WSH's stagnation creates friction for practical scripting workflows
money_quotes: I still use WSH to write quick and dirty command line utilities that don't justify a full blown .NET console executable.
Like UNIX shell scripts, there's a lot of power there for relatively little effort.
VBScript – the shared script language of classic ASP and the Windows Scripting Host – is rapidly being left behind in an increasingly .NET-centric world.
The worst thing is, it's just similar enough to fool you into trying some VB.NET syntax, even though you know better.
This isn't the same as a Microsoft blessing.
Whatever those 'plans' were... 3 years later, we're still waiting.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's failure to modernize Windows Script Host with .NET support left a frustrating gap between their strategic platform direction and the practical scripting needs of everyday developers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood laments the lack of a native HTML rendering control in WinForms, forcing developers to choose between the antiquated Rich Text Format or heavyweight IE interop. He argues that HTML is clearly superior to RTF for text formatting and report generation, but Microsoft only provides RTF controls natively. The available workarounds involve wrapping Internet Explorer through COM interop, which feels fragile and bloated. He wishes for a lightweight, fully managed HTML rendering control that could handle basic HTML without the overhead of embedding an entire browser.
key_points: WinForms lacks a native HTML text control, offering only RTF which is outdated and difficult to work with
RTF is described as 'HTML gone stupid' - attempting to convert between them reveals how irrational RTF's design is
IE interop solutions exist but feel like 'building a giant house of cards' with a 7.8MB dependency
Even the promised WebBrowser control in VS.NET 2005 is just the same IE interop with better packaging
A lightweight managed HTML renderer handling a basic subset of HTML would solve most real-world needs
money_quotes: RTF is HTML gone stupid.

Just try writing a RTF to HTML converter. Oh sure, it seems easy enough... but I don't think anyone can appreciate how profoundly irrational RTF is until they actually sit down and work with it in detail.
RTF seems to have evolved as the de facto document storage format for early versions of Microsoft Word, apparently based on the whims of whatever development team was working on Word that week.
Like all heavy duty .NET COM interop, you can't escape the feeling that you're building a giant house of cards, prone to catastrophic failure at the first gentle breeze.
What I'd really like to see is a completely managed, lightweight HTML rendering control written entirely in .NET.
While I'm not against interop per se, it seems like overkill to harness the entire bulk of IE to render a little HTML.
posts.key_insight: The gap between what developers need (simple HTML rendering) and what platforms provide (legacy formats or heavyweight interop) reveals how tooling often fails to evolve with actual use cases.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood discusses implementing global exception handling for .NET web services, moving from Application_Error in global.asax to HttpModule for better abstraction and easier deployment. He explains how HttpModule allows adding exception handling to any ASP.NET website without recompiling—just by dropping a DLL and modifying web.config. He then tackles the harder problem of web service exception handling via SoapExtension, which modifies SOAP messages in-flight to communicate detailed error information back to clients. The solution works well for SOAP clients but not for browser-based testing.
key_points: HttpModule provides superior exception handling compared to Application_Error because it can be added to any ASP.NET site without recompilation
The .Source property of exceptions usually contains the assembly name that generated the error, which helps identify the source of problems
SoapExtension allows modifying SOAP messages in-flight to inject detailed exception information into the <detail> node
Web services require different exception handling than websites because there's no visible UI to display error pages
The SoapExtension approach only works for real SOAP clients, not browser-based testing
money_quotes: Catching unhandled exceptions via HttpModule is a far better solution than using Application_Error in global.asax.
You can implement global unhandled exception handling on any ASP.NET website without recompiling.
This is critical for a web service, because unlike a website, there's no visible UI to present a helpful error page – and thus no way to diagnose the error without rooting through server files.
Be warned; you'd expect it to work the same in the browser, but it doesn't.
posts.key_insight: Better error handling architecture often comes from choosing the right hook point in the framework pipeline, where HttpModule and SoapExtension provide more flexible and less invasive integration than application-level events.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that successful software development requires the discipline to say no to feature requests, using a Steve Jobs anecdote about iTunes as illustration. He observes that many projects fail by trying to please everyone and piling on features indiscriminately. While acknowledging that both extremes are dangerous, Atwood believes the 'Yes to Everything' approach has greater potential to doom a project. He frames the 'Just Say No' philosophy not as negativity but as a necessary counterbalance to the natural optimism that pervades programming culture.
key_points: Innovation requires saying no to all but the most crucial features, as Jobs demonstrated with iTunes
Projects often 'kill themselves with kindness' by adding too many features to please all users
Experienced developers learn to internalize the 'Just Say No' philosophy over time
Optimism is an occupational hazard of programming that needs to be counterbalanced
The goal isn't literal rejection of everything, but careful weighing of what actually matters
money_quotes: Innovation is not about saying yes to everything. It's about saying NO to all but the most crucial features.
I've worked on dozens of projects that have essentially killed themselves with kindness: piling on feature after feature trying to be all things to all users.
If you're going to err on either side, try to err on the side of simplicity.
Keep a laser-like focus on doing a few things, and doing them exceptionally well.
It takes a lot more courage to say 'no' than it does to nod along in the hopes of pleasing everyone.
Optimism is an occupational hazard of programming.
posts.key_insight: Successful software development requires the courage to refuse features, because trying to please everyone with endless additions is more likely to doom a project than being too selective.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood compares Windows Media Center Edition 2005 with TiVo Series 2, finding MCE technically capable but frustratingly inferior in user interface design. While MCE 2005 represents Microsoft's mythical 'third version' with solid incremental improvements like DVD burning and multiple tuner support, its 10-foot interface falls short of TiVo's elegance. Atwood criticizes two specific UI failures: the painful telephone keypad text entry versus TiVo's intuitive letter grid, and the unnecessary Back button versus TiVo's simple left-right navigation paradigm. Despite calling MCE a 'fantastic product,' he questions why Microsoft reinvented proven UI patterns poorly.
key_points: Hardware MPEG-2 encoding cards are essential even for fast CPUs when recording video, with Hauppauge being the recommended brand despite higher cost
MCE 2005 as Microsoft's 'third version' delivers solid incremental improvements including built-in burning, better movie browsing, and multi-tuner support
TiVo's text entry interface using a visual letter grid is vastly superior to MCE's telephone keypad approach
TiVo's navigation paradigm of left-for-back and right-for-forward creates intuitive spatial associations that MCE ignores
Good UI patterns already exist and should be copied rather than reinvented poorly
money_quotes: Any time you're cribbing UI from a telephone, you are in deep, deep trouble.
Why reinvent a square wheel?
Cursoring to the letter you want is incredibly intuitive. Obvious even. Everything that numeric keypad entry is not.
Even the fastest 3.4ghz+ CPUs struggle with real time video encoding – at least, if you want to have any hope of multitasking without dropping massive numbers of frames in your recordings.
It's just hard to stomach these obvious lost opportunities for copying UI elements that are so stunningly effective on the Tivo.
I've used the cheaper brands (I'm looking at you, Avermedia) and it isn't worth it.
posts.key_insight: When designing user interfaces, companies should study and adopt proven patterns that work rather than reinventing inferior solutions out of pride or ignorance.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood endorses Microsoft's Rico Mariani's advice about avoiding unnecessary complexity in object-oriented programming. The post argues that advanced OOP features like delegates, inheritance, and virtual functions should only be used when they provide clear, demonstrable benefits. Atwood observes that most programmers overuse sophisticated language features simply because they're available, leading to overly elaborate solutions. He advocates for the KISS and YAGNI principles: keep solutions simple, build only what you need, and let good software evolve organically through aggressive refactoring rather than upfront architectural planning.
key_points: Advanced OOP features start at a 'net negative' and must earn their place through concrete benefits like clarity, maintainability, or performance
Most programmers have never met an object they didn't like - they overuse sophisticated features just because they can
Developers are far too optimistic about the generality of their solutions, building elaborate frameworks for problems that don't justify the complexity
OOP techniques should be 'guilty until proven innocent' in the court of simplicity
Good software evolves into what it needs to become through aggressive refactoring, not upfront planning for unknown future scenarios
money_quotes: Don't use fancy OOP features just because you can. Use fancy OOP features because they have specific, demonstrable benefit to the problem you're trying to solve.
Most programmers have never met an object they didn't like.
I think it should be the other way around: these techniques are guilty until proven innocent in the court of KISS.
As developers, I think we also tend to be far too optimistic in assessing the generality of our own solutions.
Build what you need as you need it, aggressively refactoring as you go along; don't spend a lot of time planning for grandiose, unknown future scenarios.
Good software can evolve into what it will ultimately become.
Each more sophisticated feature starts at a net negative and must somehow earn its way to positiveness.
posts.key_insight: Sophisticated programming features carry inherent complexity costs and should only be used when they demonstrably earn their way through concrete benefits, not simply because they're available.

posts.summary: Atwood critiques the tendency of developers, especially beginners, to over-engineer simple applications with elaborate abstract frameworks. He cites Anders Hejlsberg's observation that programmers often build polymorphic, extensible architectures for problems that only require simple solutions. This premature abstraction inevitably fails because the imagined future requirements never materialize as expected, leaving developers with unusable infrastructure. The post advocates for minimalism: solve the actual problem at hand rather than building frameworks for hypothetical future needs.
key_points: Developers frequently over-engineer by building abstract frameworks before understanding the concrete problem
Premature abstraction leads to designs that don't fit actual requirements when implementation time comes
Most business applications are 'crappy little business apps' that don't need space shuttle engineering
Over-engineering makes applications harder to maintain and often requires complete rewrites
Minimalism in design leads to better, more supportable software
money_quotes: One sure way to make your crappy little business app even crappier is to build it like you're building the space shuttle.
I'm a strong believer in being minimalistic.
Unless you actually are going to solve the general problem, don't try and put in place a framework for solving a specific one, because you don't know what that framework should look like.
I've run into so many software developers who delude themselves into believing they're building the space shuttle – instead of the crappy little business apps they're actually building.
They put all this infrastructure into place in the control, and then spend two days writing a crappy calendar application on top of it.
posts.key_insight: Building elaborate frameworks for simple problems guarantees failure because you cannot design abstractions correctly without first understanding the concrete implementations they're meant to support.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the proper use of exception classes in .NET, lamenting the lack of a comprehensive reference for built-in exception types. He discovers that Microsoft's own ApplicationException class was a design mistake that adds unnecessary hierarchy depth. The post pivots to discuss the significant performance cost of throwing exceptions, revealing they can be nearly three orders of magnitude slower than normal code paths. Despite this cost, Atwood concludes that exceptions remain the right choice when used properly—sparingly and not for control flow.
key_points: Microsoft admits ApplicationException was a design mistake and recommends deriving new exceptions from Exception instead
Throwing exceptions is extremely expensive—nearly 1000x slower than normal code paths, even slower than database calls
The cost is only incurred when exceptions are thrown, not from try/catch blocks themselves
Using VS.NET's 'Break on all exceptions' can reveal hidden performance problems like controls throwing exceptions on every paint
Exceptions are still preferable to error codes because they're more powerful, expressive, and less prone to abuse
money_quotes: Well-designed exception hierarchies are wide, not very deep, and contain only those exceptions for which there is a programmatic scenario for catching.
You should not define new exception classes derived from ApplicationException; use Exception instead.
Bear in mind that this has nothing to do with try/catch blocks: you only incur the cost when the actual exception is thrown.
Throwing an exception is literally slower than making a database call.
The code path through the exception throwing route took almost 3 orders of magnitude longer than the raw code.
If you are using exceptions properly, they should rarely be occurring and therefore any performance cost is moot.
They are more powerful, more expressive, and less prone to abuse than error codes.
posts.key_insight: Exceptions carry severe performance costs when thrown but remain the right architectural choice over error codes—the key is ensuring they stay exceptional rather than becoming control flow.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that users don't read dialog boxes not because they're stupid, but because they've been trained by years of experience that dialogs are productivity-blocking obstacles with meaningless technical jargon. He cites Joel Spolsky and Eric Lippert to explain how users rationally learn to dismiss dialogs as quickly as possible since most provide no useful information. The post concludes that any time software asks users to make choices they don't care about, the software has failed. Well-designed applications should aim for zero dialog boxes by anticipating needs, remembering preferences, and automatically protecting users from mistakes.
key_points: Users are trained by experience to ignore dialog boxes because they're modal interruptions filled with incomprehensible technical jargon
From a user's perspective, dialog boxes are productivity impediments that provide no useful information - just obstacles to dismiss
Any time you ask users to make a choice they don't care about, you've failed them as a designer
Well-designed software anticipates needs, remembers preferences, and automatically protects users rather than asking questions
The goal should be writing entire applications free of dialog boxes
money_quotes: There are quite a few users who simply do not read words that you put on the screen. If you pop up an error box of any sort, they simply will not read it.
It's not that users are morons or that they 'forget' to think. It's that users are trained to not think.
Users do not think of them as 'modal,' they think of them as 'preventing me from getting any work done until I get rid of them.'
Dialog boxes usually say 'If you want to tech the tech, you need to tech the tech with the teching tech tech. Tech the tech? Yes / No.'
From a user perspective, dialog boxes are impediments to productivity which provide no information.
Any time you're asking the user to make a choice they don't care about, you have failed the user.
The goal should be to write an entire application free of dialog boxes.
posts.key_insight: Users rationally ignore dialog boxes because software has trained them that dialogs are meaningless obstacles, so good software should eliminate them entirely by anticipating needs and automating protection.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood follows up on his previous post about .NET exception classes by sharing a solution to enumerate all of them. After a reader pointed him to the wincv.exe utility in the .NET SDK, he found it limiting and wrote his own console application using reflection to search for and list all exception types. The app scans major framework assemblies and uses regex matching to find classes ending in 'Exception'. He discovers 141 built-in exception classes, arguing this comprehensive list eliminates any excuse for throwing generic System.Exception when a more specific type likely exists.
key_points: The .NET SDK includes wincv.exe for browsing types, but it lacks sorting and copy functionality
Using reflection and regex, you can programmatically enumerate all exception types across framework assemblies
.NET 1.1 contains 141 distinct exception classes across various namespaces
Developers should use specific exception types rather than generic System.Exception
When no built-in exception fits, creating a custom exception is preferable to using the generic base class
money_quotes: Now there's really no reason to throw generic System.Exception, because one of these likely to be a better match – or, failing that, a custom Exception.
After reflecting on this a bit, I generated the following console app
p.s. I really am lazy.
posts.key_insight: The .NET framework provides 141 specific exception types, so developers should never resort to throwing generic System.Exception when a more semantically appropriate option exists or can be created.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques ASP.NET's heavy reliance on server-side postbacks, which causes page flicker, scroll position loss, and performance issues due to repeated viewstate submissions. He notes that Microsoft's SmartNavigation feature, meant to address scroll position issues, is broken. Atwood recommends Brad McCabe's workaround as an elegant solution and looks forward to ASP.NET 2.0's promise of XMLHTTP background postbacks, though he remains skeptical given the browser's limitations as a development platform.
key_points: ASP.NET's architecture forces almost everything through server postbacks, creating an extreme opposite to client-side JavaScript approaches
SmartNavigation was Microsoft's broken attempt to solve the postback scroll position problem
Heavy viewstate submission on every postback creates serious performance issues
ASP.NET 2.0's XMLHTTP background postbacks could abstract away the pain if browser compatibility is maintained
Third-party controls offer client-side behaviors that default ASP.NET controls lack
money_quotes: One of the issues I have with ASP.NET is that it is postback crazy.
While this is nice from an abstraction standpoint, it's kind of a pain for the client.
I am definitely not a fan of doing a lot of stuff in JavaScript and DOM on the browser – been there, done that – but we almost have the opposite extreme in ASP.NET.
Unfortunately, it's horribly broken.
It works, but it's still painful, mostly because the browser is a hideous development platform.
posts.key_insight: ASP.NET's server-centric abstraction trades client-side complexity for postback pain, and Microsoft's attempts to fix the symptoms have been broken rather than addressing the architectural tradeoff.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the conventional wisdom that stored procedures should be the default approach for database access. He argues that while stored procedures are often mandated for theoretical benefits like performance, security, and maintainability, these advantages rarely materialize in practice. Instead, developers suffer through archaic languages, poor debugging experiences, cryptic error messages, and hidden business logic. He concludes that stored procedures should be treated as 'database assembly language'—reserved only for the most performance-critical situations—while parameterized SQL offers a more practical and maintainable alternative.
key_points: Stored procedures force developers to work in archaic languages (PL/SQL, T-SQL) that are painful compared to modern alternatives
Debugging stored procedures requires context-switching between completely different IDEs and development environments
The commonly cited benefits of stored procedures (performance, security, maintainability, abstraction) are largely theoretical and don't hold up in real-world practice
Business logic hidden in stored procedures becomes invisible and unverifiable from the application layer
Modern database query plan caching has largely eliminated the performance advantages stored procedures once offered
money_quotes: Have you ever worked on a system where someone decreed that all database calls must be Stored Procedures, and SQL is strictly verboten?
These so-called languages are archaic, and full of the crazy, incoherent design choices that always result from the torturous evolution of ten years of backwards compatibility.
For context, JavaScript is a giant step up from PL/SQL or T-SQL.
Inline SQL may not be pretty, but at least I can see it in context, alongside the other business logic.
However, there's one small problem: none of these things are true in practice.
The benefits are marginal, but the pain is substantial.
Stored Procedures should be considered database assembly language: for use in only the most performance critical situations.
posts.key_insight: The theoretical benefits of stored procedures rarely justify the practical pain they inflict on development workflows, making parameterized SQL a better default choice for most applications.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood highlights two examples of poor UI design that violate established conventions. The first example shows an installer displaying elapsed time to the tenth of a second instead of the standard estimated remaining time, rendering it useless for users trying to plan their waiting time. The second example criticizes a MySQL error page that mimics Windows app conventions instead of following web standards, while also exposing technical jargon like 'threads' and 'OS-dependent bugs' that mean nothing to average users. The post argues that conventions exist for good reasons and breaking them without purpose only harms user experience.
key_points: Installer progress indicators should show estimated time remaining, not elapsed time—the latter tells users nothing useful
Showing elapsed time to the tenth of a second compounds the uselessness with absurd precision
Web apps should follow web conventions, not try to mimic Windows applications
Error messages should be meaningful to the actual users who will see them, not expose internal technical details
money_quotes: Occasionally I run into UI elements so boneheaded, I have to wonder what the programmers were thinking.
Not only elapsed time, but elapsed time down to the tenth of a second.
Way to take a useful installer convention, reverse it, and utterly ruin it. Bravo!
Web apps should not pretend to be windows apps; they should follow standard web conventions.
I doubt 'threads' or 'OS-dependent bugs' mean anything to most users browsing xbitlabs articles.
posts.key_insight: UI conventions exist because they serve users, and violating them without understanding why they exist creates experiences that are worse than useless.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while Microsoft has made progress on system-level security vulnerabilities with 'off by default' policies, the biggest security threat remains users themselves. He highlights how spyware exploits user consent buried in licensing agreements, and how Windows' legacy as a single-user system has ingrained running as Administrator into user behavior. Even if users were forced to run with limited privileges, they would simply switch to Administrator accounts to install desired software. Atwood concludes that security cannot be solved through technology alone and requires user education efforts.
key_points: Microsoft's 'off by default' policy in Windows 2003 dramatically improved security compared to Windows 2000's vulnerable defaults
The fastest growing virus vector is complicit users who consent to malware through buried licensing agreements
Windows' history as a single-user system created a culture of running as Administrator that Unix avoided
Even running as a limited user provides false security since users will switch to Administrator accounts to install software they want
Security and convenience are fundamentally at odds, and technology alone cannot solve the human element of security
money_quotes: Even if we eliminate all the system vulnerabilities, what about the biggest vulnerability of all – the user?
How do you protect users from themselves?
If users want software bad enough they will jump through any arbitrary hoop to get it, including switching to an Administrator account.
Never underestimate the power of a free copy of the latest Linkin Park album.
Security is complexity, and users don't like complexity: they just want to do their thing.
To some degree, security and convenience are mutually exclusive.
It's unrealistic to think we can 'solve' security through technology alone.
posts.key_insight: The fundamental security vulnerability in computing systems is not technical but human—users will bypass any security measure to get what they want, making education as important as technology in addressing security threats.

posts.summary: Atwood observes that SourceForge's top downloads are dominated by P2P file sharing clients and media ripping tools, illustrating that users are primarily motivated by getting free content rather than free (as in freedom) software. He notes the irony that users will accept significant security risks—including malware-laden executables—to access pirated content. The post is a brief but pointed commentary on user behavior and priorities.
key_points: Seven of ten top SourceForge downloads are P2P file sharing clients, with the remaining three being media ripping tools
Users prioritize 'free as in beer' (no cost) over 'free as in speech' (freedom/open source ideals)
Users willingly expose themselves to security risks, with half of shared executables containing malicious code
Even running as non-Administrator won't protect users from this malware exposure
money_quotes: Seven of the ten top downloads are pure p2p file sharing clients. The other three directly relate to media ripping.
Somehow I doubt freedom of speech is high on most users' priority lists.
Users will jump through any hoops to get things for free as in beer.
posts.key_insight: When it comes to software, most users care about 'free as in beer' (zero cost) far more than 'free as in speech' (open source freedom), and will accept enormous security risks to get content for nothing.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines a back-and-forth between Microsoft and IBM over a comparative study of J2EE and .NET performance. IBM's rebuttal blamed inexperienced developers for poor J2EE results, while Microsoft countered that their implementation choices, including stored procedures, weren't the unfair advantage IBM claimed. Atwood uses this exchange to reinforce his skepticism of both the 'developers aren't smart enough' excuse for J2EE complexity and the conventional wisdom that stored procedures provide significant performance benefits. The post highlights how vendor disputes often reveal more about platform weaknesses than objective truths.
key_points: IBM's defense of poor J2EE benchmark results relied on blaming developer inexperience and methodology rather than addressing platform complexity
Microsoft demonstrated that stored procedures provided no significant performance advantage in their tests, challenging long-held assumptions
Modern query processors in Oracle and SQL Server can optimize parameterized SQL to match stored procedure performance
Conventional wisdom about performance, especially from the early 1990s, should not be accepted without verification
money_quotes: If only developers were smart enough to use J2EE correctly!
Never take blanket performance claims at face value.
Particularly from 'accepted wisdom' dating into the early 1990s.
Further tests proved that the use of stored procedures in the application architecture had no significant performance impact one way or the other.
The query processors in Oracle and SQL Server both can optimize a parameterized query in SQL to deliver equivalent performance to a stored procedure.
posts.key_insight: When a platform's defenders blame developer inexperience for poor results, it's often an admission that the platform itself is too complex to use effectively.

posts.summary: This early technical post explains how to implement HTTP compression for ASP.NET websites hosted on third-party servers where you don't control IIS configuration. Atwood recommends the blowery.org HttpCompressionModule as a working solution that can be configured through Web.config. He documents a bug he encountered where the compression module interfered with his exception handling module's Response.Write output, and shares a code fix that checks for unhandled exceptions before attempting compression.
key_points: HTTP compression can be enabled at the application level via HttpModule when you lack server-level IIS access
The blowery.org HttpCompressionModule is a mature, well-tested solution for ASP.NET compression
Multiple HttpModules running simultaneously can create unexpected interaction bugs
Adding an early return when exceptions exist prevents compression from interfering with error output
money_quotes: How can we enable compression in that scenario?
We can implement HTTP compression on a per-website basis in ASP.NET through a compression HttpModule.
Clearly, some kind of problem related to having two HttpModules running at the same time.
Basically, in the case of any Exception – if it bubbles up this far, it's definitely unhandled – we want to return immediately and do nothing in the compression handler.
I am not sure if this is the optimal fix, but without it, all I get is the default .NET exception page. And who wants that?
posts.key_insight: When you can't control server configuration, HttpModules provide application-level hooks to implement features like compression, but you must be careful about interactions between multiple modules.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that fear of breaking code is a warning sign in developers and teams. He challenges the assumption that existing code is too precious to touch, asserting that all code is inherently broken and software development is a process of continual refinement. The best way to learn a codebase is to deliberately break it in controlled ways, which builds confidence and understanding. Developers who remain afraid of breaking things will never mature into professionals.
key_points: Fear of breaking code indicates an unhealthy relationship with a codebase and stunts developer growth
All code is broken - yours, mine, everyone's - so treating it as precious is misguided
Deliberately breaking and reassembling code is the most effective way to truly understand a system
Controlled destruction transforms intimidating codebases into opportunities for improvement and even fun
Fearlessness with code changes is essential for professional development
money_quotes: The absolute worst systems I've worked on are the ones where the developers practically tiptoe around the source code.
The main problem with fear of breaking the code is the implicit assumption that any code is really that good to begin with.
All the code we write is broken. Your code. My code. Everyone's code.
Software development isn't a science; it's a process of continual refinement.
I firmly believe that the absolute best way to learn a system is to break it.
If you can't break that codebase, and then piece it back together again – in every way you can think of – then you're going to be absolutely screwed when another developer breaks something.
When it comes to software, controlled destruction breeds confidence.
Developers who are afraid of broken code are developers who will never mature into professionals.
posts.key_insight: Controlled destruction of code builds the confidence and understanding necessary to become a professional developer, while fear of breaking things guarantees stagnation.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents his upgrade to a plasma television for use with a home theater PC, discovering that HDTV/EDTV displays work remarkably well as computer monitors compared to analog sets. After connecting his PC via VGA and achieving pixel-sharp output at 852x480, he notices an unexpected consequence: standard DVDs now look noticeably blurry compared to high-definition Windows Media content. This leads him to question whether DVD quality, encoded in aging MPEG2, is becoming obsolete in the face of higher resolution displays.
key_points: HDTV and EDTV displays make far superior computer monitors than analog televisions, supporting true resolutions from 850x480 up to 1024x768 or higher
Plasma TVs with VGA ports can connect directly to PCs, with nVidia drivers natively supporting HDTV resolutions and custom timing
High-definition Windows Media content reveals just how limited DVD quality actually is
DVD's MPEG2 encoding is showing its age, with even modest EDTV resolution exceeding what DVDs can deliver
money_quotes: HDTV or EDTV sets fare much better as computer displays.
My basic EDTV set produces an amazing pixel-sharp HTPC image at its maximum resolution of 852x480.
DVDs are quite blurry.
MPEG2 is starting to show its age.
Even 852x480 is more resolution than you need for DVD playback.
posts.key_insight: The transition to higher-resolution displays exposes the limitations of existing media formats, suggesting DVD quality may soon feel as outdated as VHS.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines how software complexity creates enormous hidden costs for organizations, citing research showing that 66% of IT projects fail or overrun due to complexity, with 98% of large projects falling short. He argues that while everyone claims to value usability, it remains underfunded because its benefits are difficult to quantify. The 'efficient' approach of building quick, hard-to-use solutions merely shifts costs from development budgets to countless hours of user frustration. Atwood calls for the industry to break this vicious cycle that makes users dread technology.
key_points: 66% of IT projects fail or overrun due to complexity, rising to 98% for projects over $10 million
Hidden complexity costs include 175 hours of unplanned network downtime per year and employees wasting a week annually fighting their PCs
Usability is expensive in development time and budget, and its ROI is hard to quantify, so it gets deprioritized
Building 'quick and dirty' solutions just hides the true cost behind user pain rather than eliminating it
money_quotes: I think everyone agrees that usability is a desirable goal, but what they don't acknowledge is that usability is expensive.
It's technically more 'efficient' – in terms of budget dollars – to build a quick and dirty solution that's hard to use.
All you're really doing, though, is hiding the true cost of your software behind hours of user pain.
We have to do better.
posts.key_insight: The perceived efficiency of building complex, hard-to-use software is an illusion—you're not saving money, you're just hiding the cost in user suffering.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces the publication of his ASP.NET CAPTCHA server control as a CodeProject article. The control has undergone several improvements including optimization through HttpModule and Cache objects, removal of insecure ViewState storage for CAPTCHA text, and addition of customizable character sets. He considers this version final after extensive testing and refinement, directing future updates and comments to the CodeProject article.
key_points: The CAPTCHA control was optimized using HttpModule and Cache objects for better performance
A security vulnerability was fixed by removing ViewState storage for CAPTCHA text
New customization option added via .CaptchaChars property for specifying valid characters
The control has been thoroughly tested and is considered production-ready
money_quotes: Removed ViewState for Captcha text (this isn't secure, doh)
This has been through quite a bit of testing and refinement, and should be considered final – for now anyway
posts.key_insight: Security improvements often come from recognizing that seemingly convenient features like ViewState can introduce vulnerabilities when used inappropriately.

posts.summary: Atwood uses a psychology experiment about rats crossing electrified grids to reach food as a metaphor for software usability. The 'Columbia Obstruction Device' showed that rats would endure more shocks for better rewards (cheese). He argues that most business software will never be compelling enough ('have enough cheese') to overcome poor usability. Since developers can't make document management systems as desirable as Napster or free music, they must focus on minimizing friction instead. The post concludes that for typical applications, reducing usability barriers is the only viable path to adoption.
key_points: Applications succeed either by being extremely compelling (cheese) or extremely easy to use (low shock) - most can only control the latter
Napster and ICQ proved that truly compelling products can succeed despite terrible interfaces
Most business software will never be inherently desirable enough to overcome usability friction
Usability is often perceived as optional, and for genuinely compelling products it sometimes is
The barrier to entry must be absurdly low for users to even consider trying typical software
money_quotes: Unless you are providing users free mp3s, or access to pornography, it's highly unlikely you will ever have enough cheese to overcome even the mildest of electric shocks.
The barrier to entry has to be absurdly low to even get people to look at your software – much less use it.
An application that does something really great that people really want to do can be pathetically unusable, and it will still be a hit.
Delicious cheese is a rare luxury that most developers working on typical business applications will never have.
What kind of crazy user looks forward to using a document management system?
Forget about the cheese: just make sure you aren't shocking your users.
posts.key_insight: Most software isn't compelling enough to overcome usability friction, so developers should abandon dreams of making their apps desirable and focus entirely on removing barriers to use.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits the VB.NET vs C# debate, reviewing Dan Appleman's updated ebook on the topic. He notes that despite both languages compiling to the same .NET runtime, C# developers inexplicably earn more than VB.NET developers for equivalent work. While acknowledging that language choice is largely superficial given the shared framework, Atwood admits he still converts C# code to VB.NET for better comprehension, suggesting the mental overhead of switching languages is real. He concludes that IDE quality matters far more than language choice, as the 'significant issues' in language selection are actually IDE features.
key_points: C# developers are paid more than VB.NET developers despite writing functionally identical .NET code
95-99% of the knowledge needed for VB.NET and C# development is identical due to the shared framework
Converting code to your preferred language isn't about inability to read the other—it's about reducing mental overhead
The 'significant issues' in language choice (background compilation, edit and continue, refactoring) are actually IDE features, not language features
IDE investment yields far greater productivity gains than language selection
money_quotes: C# developers are paid more than VB.Net developers to write the very same .NET framework code.

The existence of separate VB .NET and C# content is largely an indication that publishers, bookstores and conference organizers believe their customers are too stupid to realize the fundamental truth: that it's all about the framework.
I don't convert code because I feel morally obligated to; I do it because I feel like I don't really understand the code unless I've touched every line of it.
The mental overhead of putting on different language hats sure as hell isn't helping us get work done.
The effectiveness of your IDE is more important than any language choice you can make.
The real money play is to invest in the IDE, not the languages.
A kick-ass IDE will have a huge impact on your productivity compared to the utterly meaningless choice between editing VB.NET or C# in Notepad.
posts.key_insight: Language wars distract from what actually matters: your IDE's quality and your comfort with your tools determine productivity far more than syntax preferences.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the dark side of game development, contrasting the appealing nature of creating entertainment software with the brutal working conditions endemic to the industry. He opens by acknowledging that game development is technically the most challenging form of software development, requiring low-level coding, unusual hardware expertise, and optimization for both performance and fun. However, citing a viral blog post from an EA employee's spouse, he reveals that the reality involves crushing crunch periods of 85-hour weeks that destroy developers' health and wellbeing. Despite projects staying on schedule, management continuously extends mandatory overtime, ignoring warnings that exhausted workers introduce more bugs than they fix. Atwood concludes that this shortsighted practice damages projects, companies, and employees' lives.
key_points: Game development is uniquely difficult, combining low-level coding, exotic hardware, performance optimization, and the nebulous goal of making something 'fun'
The gaming industry normalizes extreme crunch culture, with mandatory 85-hour weeks becoming standard practice even when projects are on schedule
Extended crunch is counterproductive: fatigued developers introduce as many flaws as they remove, yet management ignores this reality
The human cost is severe, with workers experiencing physical illness, mental health deterioration, and burnout from sustained overwork
Industry veterans corroborate that these conditions are widespread, not isolated incidents
money_quotes: Game development is the most difficult kind of software development.
How do you simulate human opponents effectively? In comparison, my little business apps are a walk in the park.
After a certain number of hours spent working the eyes start to lose focus; after a certain number of weeks with only one day off fatigue starts to accrue and accumulate exponentially.
There is a reason why there are two days in a weekend – bad things happen to one's physical, emotional, and mental health if these days are cut short.
The team is rapidly beginning to introduce as many flaws as they are removing.
Every book I've read says this kind of shortsighted practice will ruin your project, and eventually your company – along with the lives of your employees.
posts.key_insight: The game industry's glamorous facade of creating entertainment masks a toxic crunch culture that destroys both the people making games and the quality of the games themselves.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques overly verbose XML documentation comments, using an absurdly complex template as an example of what not to do. He argues that comments should make code easier to understand, not harder, and establishes four principles for effective commenting. The post advocates for comments that stay close to code, avoid complex formatting, eliminate redundant information, and ultimately suggests that the best comments are ones you don't need at all. He concludes by promoting self-documenting code as the ideal approach.
key_points: XML comments provide useful IDE tooltip feedback but are verbose and prone to abuse as general documentation
Comment value decreases as distance from the referenced code increases, leading to outdated or misleading information
Complex formatting in comments discourages maintenance and signals potential inaccuracy
Redundant information like revision history belongs in source control, not comments
Self-documenting code is preferable to extensive commenting of 'tricky parts'
money_quotes: Comments are supposed to make your code easier to understand and maintain – not harder.
The value of a comment is directly proportional to the distance between the comment and the code.
Comments with complex formatting cannot be trusted.
The best kind of comments are the ones you don't need.
The only 'comments' guaranteed to be accurate 100% of the time – and even that is debatable – is the body of the code itself.
If you frequently write code full of 'tricky parts' and reams of comments, maybe it's time to refactor.
What next? Blood type? Mother's maiden name? Favorite color?
posts.key_insight: Comments should illuminate code, not document processes, and the need for extensive comments often signals code that should be refactored instead.

posts.summary: This brief technical post shares a tip from Scott Hanselman about deploying multiple /bin folders in ASP.NET applications. Jeff Atwood confirms he tested the technique and it works, demonstrating how to add a pre-compiled utility to an existing website by placing it in a subfolder with its own binaries. The key is modifying the parent app's Web.config to set up the probing path for child assemblies, plus a small modification to the child .aspx page header. This approach enables a pseudo plugin architecture for ASP.NET websites.
key_points: Multiple /bin folders can be deployed in ASP.NET by configuring assembly probing paths in Web.config
This technique enables a pseudo plugin architecture for existing ASP.NET websites
Pre-compiled utilities can be dropped into subfolders with their own binaries
The child .aspx page header requires an Assembly directive modification that can be made permanent in the source
money_quotes: What's really cool about this is that it lets you build a pseudo plugin architecture into your existing ASP.NET website.
Scott documents it perfectly; I'm here to tell you that I tried it, and it works.
If you don't do this, you'll get 'Assembly not found' exceptions.
posts.key_insight: ASP.NET's assembly probing path configuration allows you to create a simple plugin architecture by deploying pre-compiled components in subfolders with their own /bin directories.

posts.summary: This early technical post addresses a common ASP.NET deployment problem where ViewState validation fails in web farm environments. The issue stems from each server auto-generating unique machine keys, causing ViewState created on one server to be rejected by another. Atwood explains two solutions: synchronizing machine keys across all servers, or disabling ViewState MAC validation entirely. The post ends with a sardonic quip about security, hinting at the trade-off involved in the easier solution.
key_points: ASP.NET ViewState is cryptographically tied to its originating server by default, despite documentation suggesting otherwise
Web farm deployments require either synchronized machine keys or disabled ViewState MAC validation
The easier solution (disabling enableViewStateMac) comes with security implications that shouldn't be ignored
Service Pack 1 improved error messages to actually explain the problem, a small but meaningful debugging improvement
money_quotes: ASP.NET ViewState is tied to the particular server it came from by default – even though the documentation says it isn't.
This is done to prevent users from somehow tampering with the ViewState. Any change to the ViewState data on the client will be detected.
Either way, works great. Who needs all that stupid security anyway?
posts.key_insight: Web farm ViewState failures occur because ASP.NET's security-by-default approach conflicts with distributed deployment, forcing developers to choose between synchronized configuration or reduced security.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents his frustrating discovery that Process.Start in .NET 1.1 always runs under the ASP.NET worker process security context, ignoring any thread impersonation. He explores various workarounds including Win32 API calls and the MSKB impersonation methods, but finds none fully satisfactory.
