The post notes that .NET 2.0 will solve this with new properties on ProcessStartInfo. Atwood's attempt to use CreateProcessWithLogonW directly also failed, leaving him without a clean solution for his use case of capturing command output while running as a different user.
key_points: Process.Start in .NET 1.1 ignores thread impersonation and always inherits the parent ASP.NET process credentials
NET 2.0 introduces User, Domain, and Password properties on ProcessStartInfo to address this limitation
Impersonation is expensive and should be used sparingly
Win32 API workarounds like CreateProcessWithLogonW exist but are difficult to implement correctly in .NET
Combining credential switching with output redirection remains unsolved in .NET 1.1
money_quotes: Did you know that Process.Start always uses the security context of the parent ASP.NET process?
Way to rub salt in my wounds, Whidbey.
This is a very unfortunate limitation of .NET 1.1, as it severely limits what I can do with Process.Start in a web app.
Bear in mind that impersonation is a very expensive operation; it's not something you want to do often.
That requires a more masochistic workaround – calling the newer Win32 API method CreateProcessWithLogonW() directly.
Even if I could get that API call to work, I still wouldn't have the amenities of the Process class that I need.
posts.key_insight: Framework limitations often force developers into painful workarounds, and sometimes the only real solution is waiting for the next version.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares a reusable VB.NET Shell class he created for executing command line utilities from within ASP.NET applications and capturing their output. The class wraps System.Diagnostics.Process and handles multithreaded output/error capture, timeouts, and cleanup. He notes the current implementation is synchronous but could be extended to support asynchronous operation with events for long-running processes. The post is primarily a code share with minimal commentary, serving as a practical utility for developers facing similar challenges.
key_points: The Shell class encapsulates Process execution with output/error capture, timeout handling, and proper resource disposal
Separate threads are spawned to read stdout and stderr to prevent deadlocks during process execution
The class supports both synchronous blocking execution and optional threaded launch for async scenarios
Proper cleanup is implemented via IDisposable pattern and thread abortion on completion or timeout
The WorkingDirectory property must be set if executables aren't in the default system path
money_quotes: I wrote a little multithreaded .Process wrapper class to encapsulate this behavior. It's nothing magical, but it is handy for these scenarios.
Don't forget to set the .WorkingDirectory if your executables aren't in the default path.

If you have long running console processes, you might want to make this class asynchronous (e.g., non-blocking) and raise events for things like console lines being written, command terminating, etcetera.
posts.key_insight: Wrapping command line execution in a reusable class with proper threading, timeout handling, and resource cleanup makes integrating external tools into .NET applications significantly cleaner and more reliable.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that effective software development requires a balance between planning and coding, criticizing both extremes. He observes that real-world implementation constantly invalidates or transforms initial designs, making extensive upfront planning wasteful. The post specifically targets 'Software Architects' who create UML diagrams without writing code, calling this approach borderline laziness and a recipe for disaster. Atwood emphasizes that design is inherently messy and iterative, citing research showing even top designers use trial-and-error processes. His core message is that developers must prototype in code to make informed decisions rather than planning in abstract isolation.
key_points: Implementation and design have a tight feedback loop - plans made without code are often thrown away or altered beyond recognition
Over-planning is just as futile as no planning; software development is a 'wicked problem' requiring iterative approaches
The 'Software Architect' role that only produces UML diagrams without coding is criticized as lazy and dangerous
Design is inherently messy and trial-and-error even for top designers - initial solutions are quite likely to be wrong
Developers should make decisions based on experience and data gathered from actual code prototypes
money_quotes: Good programmers get off their butts.
Coding without planning is just as futile as coding with too much planning.
UML is great if you don't want to do any work; you just draw pictures of what it would look like if work was actually done.
You can't architect a real world application on a whiteboard.
When you're using Photoshop as a design tool, all things are possible. Visual Studio, unfortunately, isn't that forgiving.
Far from being a predictable, structurable, routinizable process, design is a messy, trial-and-error thing.
The initial design solution is quite likely to be wrong.
posts.key_insight: Real software architecture requires hands-on coding because design decisions made without implementation feedback are uninformed guesses that will inevitably be discarded or drastically altered.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces updates to his Shellicious code library, adding the ability to run shell commands both asynchronously and synchronously. The post demonstrates the new synchronous execution mode with a code example showing how to wait for command completion while doing other work. He also notes the addition of named threads for easier debugging, along with timeout handling via MaximumWaitSeconds and a new CancelExecution method for on-demand termination.
key_points: Added synchronous execution mode alongside the existing asynchronous option
Named threads improve debugging visibility by clearly identifying ShellErrorThread, ShellOutputThread, and ShellLaunchThread
The synchronous behavior respects the existing MaximumWaitSeconds timeout property
New CancelExecution method allows on-demand termination of running commands
money_quotes: And this time I remembered to give the threads names, which always helps in debugging.
So far so good. The synchronous behavior respects the same .MaximumWaitSeconds property as before, and there's a new .CancelExecution method if you want to bail out on demand.
posts.key_insight: Adding both synchronous and asynchronous execution options, along with proper thread naming, makes shell command libraries more flexible and debuggable.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines why Windows struggles with high-resolution LCD displays, arguing that the operating system's fundamental reliance on bitmap graphics prevents proper scaling. He recounts Robert Scoble's experience helping Dave Winer with his LCD settings, illustrating how Windows XP's tools are inadequate for modern displays. Atwood argues that only a complete UI redesign through Avalon (WPF) technology can solve this problem, and praises Microsoft's decision to backport it to XP. He notes that vector-based rendering has existed for years in Flash and NeXTstep/Mac OS X, questioning why Windows has been so slow to adopt it.
key_points: Windows XP is fundamentally a bitmapped UI that cannot properly scale to different resolutions
LCD screens have one native resolution, but Windows doesn't communicate this to users or provide adequate tools for adjustment
Large Fonts and DPI settings are 'hacky workarounds with major side effects' rather than real solutions
Avalon (WPF) represents the radical UI redesign needed to fix the problem, and backporting it to XP was a wise decision
Vector-based rendering existed in Flash and NeXTstep 10-15 years prior, making Windows's delay inexplicable
money_quotes: The sad truth is that Windows is hard coded to specific pixel sizes. It doesn't scale because bitmaps don't scale.
Oh sure, you can pretend that it scales by flipping on Large Fonts, or modifying the DPI setting in control panel, but these are just hacky workarounds with major side effects.
Windows is a bitmapped UI. If you run Windows, like it or not, you live in a bitmapped world.
Nothing short of a full-blown UI redesign – something far more radical than the change from Windows 3.1 to Windows 95 – will fix this problem.
Why is it taking Windows so long to catch up?
It sucks to be trapped in a bitmapped world when vectors are more powerful, more flexible, more elegant: just plain better.
posts.key_insight: Windows's reliance on bitmap graphics is a fundamental architectural limitation that requires a complete UI overhaul to support modern high-resolution displays properly.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood highlights Paul Graham's essay comparing American culture to software development, emphasizing that good programming is a messy, fast-moving process rather than a methodical one. The post argues that working slowly and meticulously on software is actually counterproductive—a form of premature optimization. Instead, programmers should build prototypes quickly to discover new ideas and avoid polishing mistaken initial concepts. Atwood distills this into his characteristic blunt advice: good programmers take action rather than overthinking.
key_points: Good software development is messy and iterative, not systematic and planned like building pyramids
Working slowly and meticulously is premature optimization—you end up with a polished version of a wrong idea
Fast prototyping leads to better insights than careful upfront planning
Good craftsmanship in software paradoxically means working fast, not slow
money_quotes: Code is not something they assemble painstakingly after careful planning, like the pyramids. It's something they plunge into, working fast and constantly changing their minds, like a charcoal sketch.
In software, paradoxical as it sounds, good craftsmanship means working fast.
If you work slowly and meticulously, you merely end up with a very fine implementation of your initial, mistaken idea.
Working slowly and meticulously is premature optimization.
Better to get a prototype done fast, and see what new ideas it gives you.
posts.key_insight: In software, speed enables learning—building fast prototypes reveals better ideas than carefully implementing your first flawed concept.

posts.summary: This brief post humorously acknowledges the unspoken reality of tech-savvy people returning home for Thanksgiving: the holiday visit is really about providing IT support for family members. Atwood quotes a Newsweek article that captures this universal experience of the technically competent being drafted into fixing parents' computers. The post is essentially a knowing nod to a shared experience among developers and tech workers.
key_points: Thanksgiving homecomings for tech workers inevitably involve computer repair duties
The gap between family expectations (warm holiday gathering) and reality (tech support) is a universal developer experience
Technical competence comes with unwritten family obligations
money_quotes: We are going home to fix our parents' computers.
It's funny because it's true.
posts.key_insight: Being the family's tech person means holidays come with an implicit IT support contract.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces a new CodeProject article introducing WebFileManager, a utility page for ASP.NET websites. The tool addresses a common developer pain point: managing files on servers you don't have direct access to. Rather than writing one-off pages for each file operation, WebFileManager provides a generic, reusable solution that can be dropped into any ASP.NET site. Atwood presents it modestly as practical rather than groundbreaking.
key_points: Developers often deploy to servers they don't control and lack filesystem access
A generic solution beats writing special-purpose pages for each file management need
Practical, reusable tools save time even if they're not technically innovative
Sharing tools with the community and soliciting feedback improves them
money_quotes: I often deploy ASP.NET websites to servers that I don't control.
Rather than writing a bunch of special purpose pages to deal with file management, I developed a generic WebFileManager page than can be dropped into any ASP.NET website.
Nothing earth shattering, but I find it very useful.
posts.key_insight: Building generic, reusable tools for common problems—even unglamorous ones like file management—is more valuable than writing one-off solutions.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood criticizes developers who publish sample code with empty, default AssemblyInfo metadata. He argues that if you're taking the time to make code publicly available, you should spend the extra 60 seconds to fill in basic assembly information like title, description, and company. The post also notes a gap in .NET's default assembly attributes: the absence of URL and email fields, which would require custom attributes to include.
key_points: Empty AssemblyInfo files in published code signal laziness and provide no useful metadata
Filling out assembly metadata takes minimal effort (60 seconds) relative to the value it provides
The .NET framework's default assembly attributes are incomplete, lacking URL and email fields
Proper versioning and metadata are basic professional courtesies when sharing code
money_quotes: So, basically, you're compiling into an assembly DLL or EXE with nothing but crappy, default metadata and versioning.
Please don't do this.
If you're going to the effort of making your code publicly available, take an additional 60 seconds to fill out the AssemblyInfo.
My only regret with assembly attributes is that they really should have included URL and email attributes by default.
posts.key_insight: If you care enough to share your code publicly, care enough to fill in the basic metadata that helps others understand what they're using.

posts.summary: This technical tutorial demonstrates how to extend .NET's AssemblyInfo file with custom attributes beyond the built-in ones. Atwood shows the complete implementation: defining custom attribute classes (AssemblyCompanyEmail and AssemblyCompanyUrl), applying them in AssemblyInfo, and retrieving all attributes via reflection into a NameValueCollection. The post follows up on his earlier argument that developers should properly populate their AssemblyInfo files, now showing how to go even further with customization.
key_points: Custom assembly attributes can be created by inheriting from System.Attribute and applying AttributeUsage targeting Assembly
The pattern for custom attributes is straightforward: a constructor that stores the value and a read-only property to expose it
Reflection's GetCustomAttributes method allows retrieval of both standard and custom attributes at runtime
A switch/case pattern can normalize attribute type names into friendly key-value pairs
money_quotes: To complement my previous post bemoaning the lack of respect for AssemblyInfo, I wanted to illustrate just how easy it is to add a few custom attributes to our AssemblyInfo file.
But I am sure there are other ways.
posts.key_insight: Extending .NET assemblies with custom metadata attributes is straightforward and enables richer self-describing components through simple class inheritance and reflection.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques the romanticized notion of marathon coding sessions as a badge of honor. He cites Steve McConnell's Code Complete, which dismantles Edward Yourdon's praise of 16-hour coding days and sleepless nights as 'programming machismo.' McConnell argues that these heroic efforts typically create more defects than they solve, requiring weeks of cleanup afterward. Atwood admits he sometimes falls into this trap himself but emphasizes that enthusiasm, while valuable, must not substitute for competent, sustainable work practices.
key_points: All-night coding sessions feel heroic but often introduce defects that take weeks to fix
Enthusiasm for programming is valuable but becomes counterproductive when it replaces competence
Time invested does not automatically equal value produced
The 'macho coder' mentality is seductive but ultimately harmful
Working with passionate people beats apathy, but passion needs boundaries
money_quotes: Those all-night programming stints make you feel like the greatest programmer in the world, but then you have to spend several weeks correcting the defects you installed during your blaze of glory.
This lusty tribute to programming machismo is pure B.S. and an almost certain recipe for failure.
Excitement is no substitute for competency. Remember which is more important.
I'd rather work with someone who is gonzo than someone who is apathetic – but there's definitely a point where it becomes a negative.
Don't invest tons of hours for the wrong reasons.
Time does not always equal value.
posts.key_insight: Heroic all-night coding sessions are counterproductive theater that creates more problems than they solve, making sustainable competence far more valuable than dramatic enthusiasm.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood criticizes Radio Shack's website for displaying raw ASP errors to users, using it as a jumping-off point to advocate for ASP.NET adoption. He takes issue with Joel Spolsky's argument that porting from classic ASP to .NET offers no ROI, calling it blind to the 'massive, obvious benefits' of the newer framework. While acknowledging he's not a fan of ASP.NET's 'winforms on the web' abstraction model, Atwood argues the framework represents an order of magnitude improvement over classic ASP, particularly for error handling.
key_points: Production websites from major corporations shouldn't expose raw error messages to users
ASP.NET provides dramatically better error handling capabilities than classic ASP
Joel Spolsky's 'no ROI' argument against porting to .NET ignores obvious technical benefits
Despite its flaws, ASP.NET is an order of magnitude improvement over classic ASP
Sometimes framework upgrades are worth the investment even when they don't directly add customer-facing features
money_quotes: If I was running a giant corporation, I think I'd hire coders who could develop a rational error handling strategy for our production website.
I'm not a big fan of the ultra-leaky 'winforms on the web' abstraction model, but there's no denying that ASP.NET – and the .NET framework – is an order of magnitude improvement over ASP.
It's difficult to comprehend how a smart guy like Joel could be so blind to the massive, obvious benefits of a straight port to ASP.NET.
Coherent global error handling is only the tip of the iceberg.
At this point, you could not pay me enough to write in ASP.
Ok, you could. But it would be really expensive.
posts.key_insight: Technical debt and outdated frameworks have real costs that go beyond feature development, and dismissing framework upgrades as 'no ROI' ignores the cumulative benefits of modern tooling.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood highlights frustrating UI design patterns using examples from Lotus Notes and nVidia drivers. He criticizes Lotus Notes' nonsensical dialogs as beyond redemption, then pivots to praise an nVidia feature that explains why controls are disabled. The core complaint is that disabled UI elements typically offer no feedback about why they're unavailable, forcing users to randomly experiment to understand the interface's state.
key_points: Lotus Notes serves as an example of enterprise software with incomprehensible UI dialogs
Disabled/greyed-out controls are a major usability problem because they don't explain themselves
Good UI design should tell users WHY something is disabled, not just that it is
Users shouldn't have to randomly click around to understand interface modality
Enterprise software quality often stagnates for years without improvement
money_quotes: I've given up criticizing Lotus Notes. There's no point. It's like making fun of the mentally challenged.
I hate the way disabled controls give you no feedback as to why they are disabled.
Why can't you click on that greyed out menu? Why is that button greyed out? Who knows. You just can't.
You have to suss out the meaning behind the modality by randomly clicking on stuff to see what enables and disables. Ugh.
In case you were wondering: no, things haven't gotten any better since 1999.
posts.key_insight: Disabled UI controls should always communicate why they're disabled rather than leaving users to guess through trial and error.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to developer outcry over Microsoft's decision not to include refactoring tools in VB.NET 2005. He argues that automatic refactoring tools are overvalued, that refactoring requires deep code understanding that can't be captured in menu items, and that third-party tools will likely outperform Microsoft's anyway. He criticizes developers who claim they can't be professional without IDE features, arguing that tools don't make better developers—experience does.
key_points: Automatic refactoring tools can't capture the deep understanding required for meaningful refactoring
Refactoring is a pure IDE task easily implemented as add-ons, making Microsoft's built-in version unnecessary
Third-party vendors focused solely on refactoring will likely produce superior tools than Microsoft's checkbox feature
Claiming you need specific IDE features to be a professional developer is misguided
Tools may increase productivity but won't help with the harder problems of software design and understanding
money_quotes: I'd question the competency of any developer who required a tool to perform these bread and butter coding tasks efficiently.
Refactoring is just a bullet point on a box for MS. Third parties bet their entire companies on refactoring add-ins. Who do you think is going to have the better product?
Implying that the lack of refactoring support in your IDE somehow keeps you from being a professional developer is just plain stupid.
You want to be a professional developer? Stop worrying about the tools and write some damn code.
They may help me be a more productive coder, but they won't help me write better software.
Ultimately, tools don't make you a better developer. Experience does.
posts.key_insight: Refactoring tools can make you more productive, but the hard work of understanding code, designing abstractions, and making good decisions happens in your head, not your IDE.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts his frustrations with the proliferation of remote controls in home entertainment setups and his search for a workable universal remote solution. After a failed attempt with a Marantz learning remote in 1998, he discovers the Harmony series which takes an activity-based rather than device-based approach to controlling electronics. He critiques the newer H688 model's redesigned button layout as a usability regression, noting that its mushy rubber buttons and contiguous design make it hard to operate by feel. He recommends the older H680/H676 models which retain the superior button layout of the original while offering identical functionality at lower prices.
key_points: Universal remotes often fail because a single remote nobody can figure out is no better than having multiple remotes
Activity-based design (press 'Watch a Movie' instead of configuring 4 devices) represents a major usability improvement over device-based universal remotes
The Harmony H688's redesigned buttons prioritized aesthetics over usability, making buttons hard to distinguish by feel
Simple usability testing or copying proven designs like the TiVo remote would have prevented obvious design failures
Internally identical products can have vastly different usability based solely on physical button design choices
money_quotes: I hate having a remote collection on the coffee table, but a single remote nobody can figure out is hardly any better.
Buttons may be prettier when they're grouped together in one smooth plastic swoosh, but it's nearly impossible to figure out what button you need to press.
A simple round of usability testing would have surely isolated this problem. Or they could have saved some money and just stuck with copying what works – the Tivo remote.
The mushy rubber buttons and contiguous key layout make it very hard to distinguish keys by feel, especially compared to the well-spaced, harder chiclet-style keys of the 659.
They're also cheaper! I browsed some of the threads on the Remote Central Harmony forums and everyone is having generally positive experiences with the harmony series.
posts.key_insight: Good product design requires prioritizing tactile usability over visual aesthetics, and the best solutions often come from copying what already works rather than redesigning for novelty.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood pushes back against the conventional wisdom that software projects require dedicated, pristine build machines with elaborate documentation and change control. He argues that while controlled build processes have merit, the 'magical build machine' concept creates harmful disconnects between developers and deployment realities. The post contends that if your build process requires special voodoo rituals to work, your project architecture is fundamentally flawed. Atwood emphasizes that every developer should understand how to produce reliable builds from their own machines and troubleshoot deployment issues. He notes that modern .NET deployment should be simple enough that elaborate build machine rituals are unnecessary.
key_points: Dedicated build machines can isolate developers from understanding deployment, creating an 'us vs them' mentality between development and production
If a project requires magical build machine rituals to compile, it signals architectural problems and 'voodoo programming'
Every developer should be capable of producing and troubleshooting a reliable build from their own workstation
Modern frameworks like .NET were designed for simple xcopy deployment, making elaborate build ceremonies unnecessary
Controlled build processes are valid, but teams should avoid injecting accidental 'magic' into development workflows
money_quotes: It runs on my box! Every developer on the team should understand how to produce a reliable build from their own machine.
If you use a magical build machine, you're implying that your project is so complicated to build that it takes special voodoo to get it to run.
Sacrifice a chicken, sprinkle salt over your shoulder, then re-image the build machine when the stars are perfectly aligned. That's the only way to get a 'clean' build!
A project that is this difficult to build does not inspire confidence. It also smacks of voodoo programming or programming by coincidence.
It is every developer's responsibility to write responsible code, code that doesn't cause a lot of deployment problems.
Use a build machine if it makes sense for your project, but be careful that you aren't injecting any accidental 'magic' into your development process along the way.
posts.key_insight: Build complexity that requires 'magical' dedicated machines often masks architectural problems; every developer should be able to build and deploy from their own workstation.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines McConnell's classification of 'gold-plating' as a classic development mistake, questioning whether it truly deserves that label. He argues that refactoring, which technically fits the definition of gold-plating, is essential for maintainable code. Atwood distinguishes between harmful over-engineering and the rare quality of developers who care enough to go the extra mile. He concludes that while gold-plated software exists, the far more common problem is software that's barely adequate—pure lead rather than gold.
key_points: McConnell identifies two types of gold-plating: requirements gold-plating (unnecessary features from the start) and developer gold-plating (implementing slick features not required by the product)
All refactoring technically qualifies as gold-plating since it consumes time without material user benefit, yet it's essential for maintainable code
The line between valuable refactoring and wasteful gold-plating is unclear and subjective
Most developers fall into the 'rosh katan' category—stopping at the first excuse rather than pushing through obstacles
Having a developer who gold-plates is actually a blessing compared to the alternative of minimal-effort work
money_quotes: Where does the refactoring stop, and the gold-plating begin?
I also have a hard time criticizing the few developers who care enough to gold-plate their code in the first place.
While there is certainly plenty of over-engineered, over-abstracted, gold-plated software out there, it's far more common to have... pure lead.
If you have a developer who is going the extra mile and gold-plating stuff, count yourself lucky.
With a bit of direction and focus you could have a developer producing real gold eventually. And that is rare indeed.
Gold-plating is a great metaphor for adding artificial value, like cheaply manufactured jewelry.
posts.key_insight: The real problem in software development isn't developers who care too much and over-engineer, but developers who don't care enough and stop at the first obstacle.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that programmers should apply the same 'steal, don't build' philosophy to design that they apply to code. He uses a humorous redesign of Jakob Nielsen's famously minimalist website as evidence that even basic HTML pages can be dramatically improved with simple design changes. Atwood admits to sympathizing with crappy layouts as a developer, having convinced himself that minimal design is virtuous because it's 'lightweight like Google.' The post showcases before-and-after examples that converted him into believing good design matters.
key_points: Great programmers research before building, but often fail to apply this same principle to graphic design
Developers rationalize poor design by claiming minimalism is a feature, not a bug
Simple design improvements can dramatically enhance even basic HTML layouts
The redesign of Nielsen's page proves that usability experts aren't immune to design criticism
Flash was already considered problematic in 2004, useful only for 'Mr. Wong episodes'
money_quotes: Good programmers write good code; great programmers steal great code.
Never design what you can steal.
I sympathize with crappy layouts. It's what I do!
I make really crappy, basic HTML layouts. Then I fool myself into believing crappy, basic HTML layout is a good thing because it's, you know, lightweight. Like Google!
As far as I'm concerned, the only proper use for Flash is to play Mr. Wong episodes.
posts.key_insight: Programmers who wisely reuse code should apply that same research-first, borrow-the-best approach to design instead of rationalizing their bad visual layouts as intentional minimalism.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software development should be treated as a practical, blue-collar craft rather than an academic exercise. He emphasizes that architects should remain developers first, focusing on whether their designs actually work for the team rather than adhering to fancy methodologies. Good architecture is validated by how well it fits developers' mental models and makes them productive, not by its theoretical elegance. While patterns serve as useful vocabulary for discussing design, they shouldn't become dogma that overrides practical concerns.
key_points: Software development is fundamentally about delivering working code to end users, not academic research or methodology compliance
Good architects never forget they're developers first—they gather feedback and watch for warning signs that their designs aren't working
Architecture quality is measured by whether it fits developers' mental models and increases productivity, not by adherence to textbook patterns
Design patterns are valuable as shared vocabulary but should not become constraints that override practical needs
Warning signs of bad architecture include difficulty explaining designs, making excuses, developers frequently 'getting it wrong,' and fielding many questions about supposedly simple things
money_quotes: Software development is not an academic exercise. It has more in common with the blue-collar 'build me a house' ethic than it does with ivory tower research.
It takes a good developer to make a good architect.
Forget the ten dollar acronyms and fancy diagrams.
A software architecture isn't automatically good just because it came from a $45 book on Amazon.
A software architecture is good when – with a little education – it fits the developer's mental model of the way things should work and it makes them more productive.
If your architecture is screwed up, believe me, you don't need a book to find that out. Just pay attention to the warning signs from your co-workers.
Don't be a slave to the patterns, either.
posts.key_insight: Software architecture should be validated by how well it works for the developers building with it, not by how closely it follows academic theory or industry patterns.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits his previous recommendation of Araxis Merge as the best visual diff tool after a co-worker suggested Beyond Compare. After testing it, he finds Beyond Compare not only matches Araxis Merge in features but actually surpasses it in several ways. The decisive factor is the dramatic price difference: Beyond Compare costs $30 versus Araxis Merge's $129. He also highlights that Beyond Compare doubles as a project deployment tool, adding extra value to an already compelling package.
key_points: Beyond Compare matches or exceeds Araxis Merge's visual differencing capabilities at roughly one-quarter the price
The volume discount makes Beyond Compare even more compelling for teams ($17 per license for 5+ versus $83 for Araxis)
Beyond Compare serves dual purpose as both a diff tool and project deployment tool
Quality software at fair prices inspires users to actually register and pay for it
money_quotes: Fatality.
It's better than Araxis Merge and it's one-third the price!
Beyond Compare is the kind of finely crafted software that inspires you to register it.
it also doubles as a kick ass project deployment tool
sometimes you get what you pay for
That's still $44 less than one copy of Araxis Merge!
posts.key_insight: The best developer tools combine superior functionality with accessible pricing, making the decision to pay for quality software an easy one.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines how poor office environments systematically destroy programmer productivity, drawing heavily from Peopleware and Joel on Software discussions. He argues that the prevalence of overtime work is actually a damning indictment of daytime office conditions, as developers seek quiet hours early, late, or at home just to concentrate. The post highlights the absurdity of companies investing in process improvements while ignoring the fundamental environmental problems that prevent focused work. Most frustratingly, management often treats workspace quality as an immutable force of nature rather than a solvable problem, even when employees explicitly identify it as their top concern.
key_points: Overtime often exists not to increase work quantity but to improve work quality by escaping daytime distractions
Companies invest heavily in software processes while ignoring the basic environmental conditions that prevent concentration
Management frequently treats workspace problems as unsolvable even when employees rank them as their primary concern
The irony of only managers having offices while expecting programmers to be productive in open spaces
Workspace quality significantly impacts both productivity and job satisfaction, yet remains largely unaddressed
money_quotes: A disturbing possibility is that overtime is not so much a means to increase the quantity of work time as to improve its average quality.
One wonders why anyone is concerned with implementing software development processes when most developers are having a hard time concentrating on any task for more than ten minutes.
Staying late or arriving early or staying home to work in peace is a damning indictment of the office environment.
The amazing thing is not that it's so often impossible to work in the workplace; the amazing thing is that everyone knows it and nobody ever does anything about it.
It was as though the programmers had complained that there was too much gravity, and management had decided after due reflection that they couldn't really do much about it.
Changing the environment is not beyond human capacity.
It may not be beyond human capacity, but it's hard to envision change when only managers have offices.
posts.key_insight: The widespread practice of working overtime or from home to escape office distractions is evidence that most workplaces are fundamentally broken for knowledge work, yet organizations treat this as an unsolvable problem while obsessing over process improvements.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his essential laptop travel accessories, focusing on practical solutions to common mobile computing frustrations. He recommends Microsoft's wireless mini-mouse for its ability to track on shiny surfaces and clever power-saving design. The post highlights the importance of external USB WiFi adapters with antennas for getting reliable signals in areas with weak coverage. He also advocates for retractable cables to minimize cable clutter while traveling. The piece reflects the practical hardware challenges of mobile computing in the mid-2000s era.
key_points: Built-in laptop pointing devices (touchpads, nubs) are inadequate for precise work - a quality wireless mini-mouse is essential
Free WiFi availability is overrated outside dense urban areas; laptop internal antennas often can't get usable signals
External USB WiFi adapters with directional antennas can be positioned optimally, independent of laptop orientation
Retractable cables solve the cable management problem for travelers
Small design details matter - like Microsoft's clever receiver-as-off-switch solution
money_quotes: Touchpads and nubs are fine when there's no other option, but for quicker, more precise work, you gotta have a mouse.
In my experience, the prevalance of free WiFi is wildly overrated.
You just can't expect to get a decent WiFi signal from your laptop's puny internal antenna except in the most optimal of conditions.
It is external and can be positioned for optimal signal, e.g., facing the correct direction, which may have no relationship to the way you want to sit and use your laptop.
Screw portability, I'm tempted to carry a cantenna with me for those truly difficult to reach signal locations, like my Mom's house.
I barely leave the house enough to qualify for the Road title, much less Warrior.
posts.key_insight: Mobile computing productivity depends heavily on having the right peripheral accessories to overcome the inherent limitations of laptop hardware.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the traditional database 'data layer' should be implemented as a web service rather than through stored procedures, binary remoting, or other tightly-coupled approaches. He builds on Rocky Lhotka's service-oriented architecture principles, emphasizing that services define trust boundaries and provide contractual interfaces. Drawing from painful personal experience with a .NET remoting implementation, Atwood demonstrates how choosing the wrong abstraction leads to developer friction, API bloat, and architectural debt. He contends that web services offer true platform independence, longevity, and force developers toward simpler, more maintainable APIs. The post also warns against the temptation to move business logic into the database, even with managed code support in SQL Server.
key_points: Web services provide genuine platform and technology independence that no other API abstraction offers - you could swap databases or operating systems without breaking clients
HTTP and SOAP are durable technologies that will outlive proprietary alternatives like COM+, making them safer long-term investments for formal APIs
Database performance is typically the bottleneck anyway, so the additional latency of a web service layer is negligible compared to query execution time
Binary remoting and tightly-coupled APIs create developer friction, reduce visibility, and lead to duplicate or inconsistent implementations
Moving business logic into the database (even with managed code) creates scalability challenges through increased resource consumption, locking issues, and GC pressure
money_quotes: A web service is the only level of abstraction that really buys you anything.
HTTP and SOAP are the cockroaches of platform technology. They'll probably outlive us both.
A web service forces you to keep your API methods simple and abstract. A good API is difficult to get right, and easy to overcomplicate.
Database performance is almost always the bottleneck anyway. Adding a web service to the mix doesn't cost you anything.
On a recent code review, I found three methods that all did the same thing. All of them had completely different names, of course.
Now we've committed the ultimate sin: we're repeating ourselves.
Give yourself options by choosing the right architecture early on.
posts.key_insight: Abstracting your data layer as a web service provides true platform independence and forces architectural discipline, while tightly-coupled alternatives inevitably accumulate painful technical debt.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores programming-specific fonts after reading Mike Gunderloy's suggestion to customize IDE fonts beyond the default Courier New. He compiles a visual comparison of popular monospace fonts including Andale Mono, Anonymous, Lucida Console, Monaco, Pragmata, ProFont, and Proggy Clean. Through testing, he concludes that monospace is essential, anti-aliasing is undesirable for code, and bitmap fonts offer precision but lack scalability. His personal recommendation is Proggy for most situations, with Lucida Typewriter or Pragmata as scalable alternatives for high-resolution displays.
key_points: Monospace fonts are essential for programming - proportional fonts don't work in an IDE
Anti-aliasing (including ClearType) is undesirable for programming fonts at small sizes
Bitmap fonts like Proggy offer superior precision but cannot scale, which matters on high-resolution displays
Font choice should adapt to your display resolution - what works at 1600x1200 may not work at 1920x1440
Some fonts auto-apply anti-aliasing even at 9 point, making them unsuitable for code
money_quotes: Lists of fonts are all well and good, but a picture is worth a thousand words.
I don't care for anti-aliasing of any kind on a programming font.
Bitmap fonts, such as Proggy, are very precise but don't scale. At all.
I don't recommend using Comic Sans as your programming font.
Note that some fonts decide to anti-alias themselves even at 9 point!
posts.key_insight: The right programming font is a surprisingly personal choice that depends on your display resolution, anti-aliasing preferences, and whether you value precision over scalability.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his frustration with forgetting XPath syntax between infrequent uses and his search for Aaron Skonnard's interactive XPath Expression Builder tool. After discovering the original tool had been moved and converted to inconvenient zip file downloads, he decided to host the interactive web pages on his own server. The post serves as both a personal note and a public service for developers who similarly struggle with XPath syntax. He provides links to both the classic 1.0 and improved 4.0 versions of the tool.
key_points: Infrequently used syntax (like XPath) is easily forgotten between uses
Interactive tools with real-time visual feedback are valuable for learning and remembering syntax
Distributing tools as zip files ruins the immediate accessibility that made them useful
Sometimes the best solution is to host useful resources yourself when original sources become inconvenient
money_quotes: I use Xpath queries about once a year, so of course I completely forget the syntax every time I come back to it.
And they're now distributed as zip files, which completely ruins the immediate feedback that made the interactive XPath builder pages so useful.
posts.key_insight: Interactive tools with immediate feedback are invaluable for working with syntax you use too infrequently to memorize.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the controversial use of DoEvents in .NET WinForms development for creating responsive user interfaces. He presents a pragmatic technique of moving heavy operations from Form Load to the first Paint event with DoEvents to improve perceived performance. While acknowledging that DoEvents is considered poor practice by many developers who advocate for proper threading, Atwood argues it represents a reasonable tradeoff for simple scenarios. He contrasts the ease of DoEvents against the genuine difficulty of implementing correct multithreaded solutions, ultimately suggesting that even Microsoft recommends DoEvents for simple problems while waiting for better threading support in .NET 2.0.
key_points: Moving heavy operations from Form Load to the first Paint event with DoEvents dramatically improves perceived performance by giving users immediate visual feedback
DoEvents is a legacy VB technique that Microsoft still considers legitimate for simple UI yielding scenarios, despite criticism from threading purists
The danger of DoEvents is that it can cause unexpected re-entrancy issues when users interact with the UI during processing
Proper threading with Control.Invoke is the 'correct' solution but introduces significant complexity around synchronization and shared state
For complex scenarios, modeling UI and background threads as separate processes communicating only through messages avoids shared memory pitfalls
money_quotes: Threading is a manly approach for tough guys, and it will put hair on your chest – but you may not have any left on your head when you're done.
Never underestimate the power of perceived performance.
Threading is hard. Dangerous, even. It's not a coding burden you take on lightly.
You've never truly debugged an app until you've struggled with an obscure threading issue.
When things start happening in asynchronous, indeterminate order instead of the deterministic 1-2-3 order you'd expect... things get absurdly difficult really quickly.
Yielding on the UI thread is a legitimate Windows programming practice. It always has been.
The button responds to the event and starts another processing thread but it isn't actually a thread here.
posts.key_insight: Perceived performance matters to users, and sometimes a 'dirty' hack like DoEvents is a reasonable tradeoff against the genuine complexity and danger of proper multithreaded programming.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares Craig Andera's elegant solution for handling .NET configuration files by using XML deserialization instead of writing custom parsing code. The technique allows developers to define a configuration class and automatically deserialize the .config file XML directly into an instance of that class. Atwood provides VB.NET code examples showing both the basic implementation and an improved version with better error handling. The post emphasizes the cleverness of using built-in XML serialization to eliminate tedious configuration parsing code, while acknowledging that debugging deserialization errors can be challenging.
key_points: Instead of writing custom code to read each config setting, you can deserialize the entire config section directly into a typed object
The config section must include a type attribute specifying which class to deserialize to
Your class must serialize to the exact same XML format as your config, or you'll get confusing deserialization errors
A helper method to serialize your class to console output helps verify the expected XML format
The improved version provides much better error messages by unwrapping inner exceptions and giving contextual feedback
money_quotes: Instead of writing a bunch of tedious code to read settings from a .config file, you deserialize an instance of the class using the .config file XML as the input!
Make sure your class serializes to the same XML format exactly – otherwise you'll get a bunch of non-intuitive deserialization error messages.
It really could be The Last Configuration Section You'll Ever Need.
Troubleshooting XML that won't deserialize can be... difficult.
posts.key_insight: Leveraging XML serialization to automatically map configuration files to typed objects eliminates boilerplate parsing code, though proper error handling is essential for debugging.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recommends Mark Russinovich's Process Explorer as the ultimate replacement for Windows Task Manager. He positions it as even more powerful than Task Manager Extension, which he had previously covered. The post highlights key features that make Process Explorer superior, including its ability to identify windows visually, show TCP/IP connections, and handle .NET processes natively. Atwood suggests this tool is ideal for power users who have completely outgrown the default Task Manager.
key_points: Process Explorer surpasses both standard Task Manager and Task Manager Extension in capabilities
Key differentiating features include drag-and-drop window identification and real-time color-coded highlighting
The tool provides deep process inspection including TCP/IP connections and string extraction
Native .NET process support makes it particularly useful for developers
Despite being potentially overwhelming, it's the definitive choice for power users
money_quotes: If Task Manager Extension is Task Manager on steroids, then Mark Russinovich's Process Explorer is Task Manager in a ripped anabolic fury, fueled by high octane rage.
Although it can be a little overwhelming – I think it just kicked sand in my face – it does have some features that I prefer over Task Manager Extension
If you've outgrown Task Manager completely, this is definitely the tool for you.
posts.key_insight: For developers and power users who need deep system visibility, specialized tools like Process Explorer offer capabilities far beyond what default system utilities provide.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood endorses Steve Krug's concept of 'happy talk' - the vacuous introductory text that welcomes users to websites without conveying useful information. He argues that this phenomenon extends beyond websites to all software interfaces, dialogs, documentation, and even code comments. The core message is that users don't have time for content-free pleasantries and won't read unnecessary text anyway. Atwood frames eliminating happy talk as a specific application of the broader principle to omit needless words.
key_points: Happy talk is recognizable by the 'blah blah blah' voice in your head while reading it - it conveys no useful information
This problem extends beyond websites to application interfaces, dialogs, documentation, and comments
Happy talk often appears on section front pages as filler when there's no real content
Users want to get to the substance immediately - they don't have time for textual small talk
Eliminating happy talk is a specific case of the broader principle to omit needless words
money_quotes: If you listen very closely while you're reading it, you can actually hear a tiny voice in the back of your head saying 'Blah blah blah blah blah…'
Happy talk is like small talk – content free, basically just a way to be sociable. But most web users don't have time for small talk; they want to get right to the beef.
You can – and should – eliminate as much happy talk as possible.
Don't put happy talk anywhere in your application – interface, dialogs, documentation, or even comments.
Happy Talk Must Die.
The less text you have in your app, the better, because users won't read it anyway.
posts.key_insight: Any text in your software that makes users hear 'blah blah blah' in their heads is wasting their time and should be ruthlessly eliminated.

posts.summary: Atwood reflects on the cultural significance of plug-and-play classic videogame emulators as holiday gifts, arguing they represent more than mere nostalgia. He highlights how many developers trace their careers back to childhood fascination with these primitive games, when the leap from player to creator seemed achievable. The centerpiece is the inspiring story of Jeri Ellsworth, a self-taught chip designer who single-handedly recreated the entire Commodore 64 on a single chip. Her success selling 70,000 units in one day on QVC demonstrates that the garage-tinkerer spirit of early Silicon Valley still thrives. Atwood uses her story to illustrate that the best developers are driven by passion and a do-it-yourself approach to problem-solving.
key_points: Classic videogame systems shaped many developers' career paths by making the jump from player to creator seem achievable
Jeri Ellsworth, a self-taught high school dropout, reverse-engineered the entire Commodore 64 onto a single chip
The do-it-yourself spirit of early Silicon Valley garage culture can still thrive even in an era of massive development teams
Great developers share a passion that makes problem-solving feel like fun rather than work
The ultimate compliment for a creator is having their work used and enjoyed by thousands of people
money_quotes: Many of us can trace our current careers all the way back to a childhood spent fascinated with these primitive home videogames.
It's an inspiring story. All the great developers I know are passionate about their work and have that same do-it-yourself approach to solving problems.
It's not even work; it's fun to solve problems and build things.
She's a pure example of following your interests and someone who won't accept that you can't do it.
It was a tribute back to the computer that started it all for me.
If you're lucky, the result of your passion will be used and enjoyed by thousands of people. That's the ultimate compliment.
posts.key_insight: The passion that drove early computing pioneers—turning childhood fascination into creation—remains the defining characteristic of great developers, and that do-it-yourself spirit can still flourish even in an era of massive development budgets.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood discusses the challenge of doing productive work on a laptop while actually using it on your lap, noting that real work typically requires a desk surface. He introduces a portable laptop stand (the PC Table Tote) that his mom found at a yard sale, which despite looking ridiculous, effectively solves the problem by allowing comfortable couch-based work with a proper mouse. The stand folds compactly and doubles its surface area for laptop and mouse placement.
key_points: Laptops are ironically difficult to use productively on actual laps, limiting their mobile utility
A portable laptop stand can bridge the gap between couch comfort and desk productivity
Sometimes the most effective tools look ridiculous but work well in practice
The ability to use a real mouse while on the couch significantly improves laptop productivity
money_quotes: For all their cool teeny-tiny productiveness, I've never been able to effectively use a laptop on... my lap.
To do real work, I have to find a desk or desk-like surface.
Initially I was quite skeptical – how can something this ridiculous looking actually work? But it does!
I can finally sit on the couch and work with my laptop at nearly full desktop speed, with a real mouse.
All I can tell you is, it looks ridiculous, and it's a bit expensive, but this PC Table Tote thing really works.
posts.key_insight: The gap between laptop portability and desktop productivity can be bridged with the right accessories, even if they look absurd.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood points out the irony of a web design article about reducing website clutter that is itself cluttered with ads and visual noise. The post quotes the article's list of common website annoyances like counters, flashy menus, and background music. Atwood then reveals the punchline: the article preaching against clutter is surrounded by excessive advertising. This brief post uses humor to make its point about the gap between advice and practice in web design.
key_points: Articles about best practices often fail to follow their own advice
Common website clutter includes counters, unnecessary links, flashy menus, and animations
The irony of cluttered content about decluttering speaks for itself
Sometimes the best criticism is simply showing the problem
money_quotes: From the Articles That Unintentionally Parody Themselves department
Indeed, how would one spot this kind of problem? Perhaps by reading the very article itself...
posts.key_insight: The most effective criticism of bad practices is often to let them speak for themselves.

posts.summary: Atwood reflects on his journey from self-taught BASIC programmer to the .NET ecosystem, where he encountered formal development patterns for the first time. While acknowledging the benefits of patterns and practices, he warns against 'architecture astronauts' - developers who become so obsessed with abstraction and methodology that they lose sight of building actual working software. He draws a sharp distinction between 'doers' who ship real products used by real people, and 'talkers' who get lost in UML diagrams and theoretical frameworks. The post argues that patterns should always serve the practical goal of solving user problems, not become ends in themselves.
key_points: The .NET platform brought VB developers into mainstream development practices, exposing them to patterns absorbed from the Java world
'Architecture astronauts' are developers who work at such high levels of abstraction that they lose touch with practical implementation
The distinction between productive developers and astronauts lies in proving ideas through working applications used by actual users
Companies focused on doing (like Adobe) succeed and generate revenue, while companies focused on talking (like ObjectSpace) go bankrupt
Patterns and practices are valuable only when framed in the context of solving real user problems
money_quotes: When you go too far up, abstraction-wise, you run out of oxygen.
It's very hard to get them to write code or design programs, because they won't stop thinking about Architecture.
Software isn't about methodologies, languages, or even operating systems. It is about working applications.
A UML diagram can't push 500 pages per minute through a RIP.
There are two types of people in this industry. Talkers and Doers.
Patterns and practices are certainly good things, but they should always be framed in the context of a problem you're solving for the users.
posts.key_insight: Software development patterns and architecture are only valuable when they serve the ultimate goal of building working applications that solve real problems for actual users.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood highlights a new blog called Spurious Pundit, sharing its insights on managing developers and the fundamental differences between technical and business mindsets. The post explores how managers often fail to communicate implicit expectations when delegating tasks, using a picture-hanging analogy. It then examines how technical work operates in a 'reality-based' universe where things either work or fail based on observable properties, while business success is often self-referentially tied to belief and confidence. Atwood endorses the blog's argument that humility and openness to criticism are essential virtues for technical work, contrasting sharply with the confidence-driven traits valued in sales and management.
key_points: Managers often fail to communicate implicit constraints and expectations when delegating tasks to less experienced developers
Pair programming is an effective way to onboard developers and transfer tacit knowledge
Technical work is 'reality-based' - code either works or fails based on objective properties, unlike business where belief can shape outcomes
The confidence and self-assurance that makes salespeople and managers effective can be detrimental to technical work
Humility, openness to criticism, and valuing people who poke holes in your designs leads to better technical solutions
money_quotes: Technical staff actually operate in a different universe from the business side of the house. They're 'reality-based' in the disparaging way that our current administration uses the term.

Put simply, shit will either work or fail based on the properties of the observable universe. No amount of personal motivation or focus-group work will let a spare, obsolete PC handle a million page hits a day.
On the business side of the house, to an alarming extent, believing things makes them true.
These traits which managers and sales people need to be effective are actually bad for technical folks. Arrogance means you overlook things.
Being able to sell them on it by force of personality doesn't make it better. Being aware of your own failings and limitations makes it better.
Learning to value the people who will poke holes in your designs - the ones who are 'not team players' - makes it better.
posts.key_insight: Technical excellence requires humility and openness to criticism, traits that are often at odds with the confidence-driven mindset rewarded in business and management roles.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that as CPU development shifts from faster clock speeds to multiple cores, software developers face a serious challenge: our programming languages and mental models are fundamentally ill-suited for concurrent programming. He draws on predictions from 2001 about multi-core processors and quotes several experts who warn that threading is extremely difficult to get right. Using Dan Appleman's vivid analogy comparing multithreading to a psychokinetic explosive, Atwood emphasizes that incorrect threading designs can be catastrophically expensive to fix. He concludes that without radical changes to programming paradigms, developers will struggle to adapt to a concurrent world.
key_points: CPU development is shifting from faster clock speeds to multiple cores, forcing developers to write concurrent code to gain performance
Current programming languages provide inadequate support for concurrent programming, making it unnecessarily difficult
Threading bugs are uniquely dangerous because design flaws cannot be patched later and have unlimited potential debugging costs
Most programmers lack experience with concurrent programming and underestimate its complexity
Linear thinking dominates programming, but concurrency requires reasoning about events that can interrupt each other at any time
money_quotes: We're pretty screwed if the only way to get more performance out of our apps is to make them heavily multithreaded.
We'll have to have much better language-level support for concurrent programming. What we have now sucks. Rocks. Hard.
Multithreading is the software equivalent of a nuclear device because if it is used incorrectly, it can blow up in your face.
Used incorrectly, it can destroy your reputation and your business because it has nearly infinite potential to increase your testing and debugging costs.
If your design is incorrect, it will be virtually impossible to patch up the problems later. And again, the potential cost to fix threading problems has no upper limit.
When you can't go forward, go sideways: more CPUs on the same die.
Writing software is hard enough as-is without adding a pinch of 'the most complex code in our app' throughout... your app.
posts.key_insight: The shift to multi-core processors represents a fundamental challenge for software development because our languages, tools, and ways of thinking are built for linear execution, not concurrency.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reviews Nero Recode 2 as the superior DVD ripping solution after experiencing unreliable results with alternatives like XMPEG and Dr. DivX. The software offers dramatically faster encoding speeds (90fps vs 50fps), better stability with no crashes, and superior video quality according to expert reviews. However, Nero's proprietary 'Nero Digital' format creates compatibility headaches, requiring either the bundled Nero Showtime player or a third-party decoder like 3ivx to play the resulting files. Despite this annoyance, Atwood strongly recommends the Nero Ultra suite as best-of-breed software for DVD encoding tasks.
key_points: Nero Recode 2 provides dramatically faster encoding (90fps vs 50fps) and superior stability compared to free alternatives like XMPEG or Dr. DivX
MPEG4 encoding can compress 9GB of DVD data to 700MB while maintaining near-DVD quality, thanks to being a more modern codec than MPEG2
Nero Digital format uses advanced MPEG4 with AAC audio but lacks broad decoder support, forcing users to install Nero Showtime or pay for third-party decoders
The K-Lite Codec Pack offers a free workaround for Nero Digital playback in Windows Media Player
Video encoding remains one of the few tasks where raw CPU power still matters significantly
money_quotes: I just ripped about 10 different DVDs with Nero Recode and it's been a painless point and click operation every single time – without a single crash!
I know it sounds implausible, but I can tell you from personal experience that it works, because MPEG4 is a more modern and efficient codec than MPEG2.
You're forced to install the annoying Nero Showtime application on any PC you want to watch your *.mp4 files from. Not acceptable.
Paying $7 for a decoder irks me.
Video encoding is one of the last great frontiers where PCs can never be fast enough.
We should be able to download a small set of decoders and watch our *.mp4 files in any application we want.
posts.key_insight: Superior software tools should prioritize reliability and ease of use, but proprietary formats that lock users into specific players undermine otherwise excellent products.

posts.summary: This brief post marks the 20th anniversary of Windows 1.0 in 2005, sharing screenshots of the original release. Atwood notes his personal history with Windows, having started with Windows 3.0 after using an Amiga. He admits being initially unimpressed with Windows, only becoming a believer with the release of Windows 3.1 and Windows For Workgroups 3.11. The post serves as a nostalgic reflection on how far Windows had come in two decades.
key_points: Windows 1.0 turned 20 years old in 2005
Atwood's first Windows experience was version 3.0, which failed to impress him compared to his Amiga background
Windows 3.1 and Windows For Workgroups 3.11 were the versions that finally won him over
money_quotes: Coming from an Amiga background, I was unimpressed.
It wasn't until Windows 3.1 and Windows For Workgroups 3.11 that I actually started to believe Microsoft had something worth using.
posts.key_insight: Windows took several iterations before it became a platform worth taking seriously, even for those who would later become its advocates.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood introduces a client-side JavaScript syntax highlighting solution he discovered on Alex Gorbatchev's blog. After expressing frustration with the primitive PRE tags on his own blog and reluctance to hack Perl-based solutions into Movable Type, he found Alex's dp.SyntaxHighlighter to be an elegant alternative. The tool supports multiple languages including C#, VB.NET, JavaScript, PHP, SQL, and XML. Jeff shares a VB.NET code sample demonstrating the highlighter in action and notes that browser compatibility testing is ongoing.
key_points: Client-side JavaScript offers a cleaner alternative to server-side syntax highlighting for blogs
The dp.SyntaxHighlighter supports multiple programming languages out of the box
Avoiding Perl hacks in Movable Type was a key motivation for seeking a JavaScript solution
Browser compatibility (Firefox and IE6 at the time) was an important consideration for adoption
money_quotes: Although I loves me some Regex, I don't know squat about PERL so I've been hesitant to hack anything fancier into my Movable Type install.
The vanilla PRE sections I've been using are serviceable, but primitive.
It's really quite slick, and it works with all kinds of code.
posts.key_insight: Client-side JavaScript syntax highlighting provides an accessible way for bloggers to improve code presentation without wrestling with server-side templating languages they don't know.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the value of learning from the mistakes of more talented people. He highlights two sources of wisdom: Rick Cattell's engineering career advice emphasizing that good technology alone isn't enough for success, and Hugh Macleod's creative advice about self-reliance and persistence. The post serves as a brief curation of hard-won lessons from experienced practitioners.
key_points: Even highly talented people make mistakes, which should be reassuring to all developers
Good technology accounts for only about 10% of real-world success
Building things that go unused is a common and frustrating career pitfall
Creative and business success requires self-reliance rather than waiting to be discovered
Putting in the hours and taking responsibility for your own experience are essential
money_quotes: It's always reassuring to be reminded that people much more talented than myself make mistakes, too.
Good technology is only 10% of 'real world' success
It's frustrating to spend years of your career building things that aren't used
The idea doesn't have to be big. It just has to change the world.
If your biz plan depends on you suddenly being 'discovered' by some big shot, your plan will probably fail.
You are responsible for your own experience.
posts.key_insight: Learning from the publicly shared mistakes of talented people is one of the most valuable shortcuts to avoiding wasted years building things that don't matter.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on rediscovering inline code ASPX pages after years of using ASP.NET's code-behind model. He walks through the simple conversion process and appreciates the benefits of single-file deployment and real-time edits. However, he acknowledges that inline code brings back the downsides of classic ASP: spaghetti code, limited intellisense, and poor debugging. He concludes that while code-behind remains his preference, inline has its place for utilities where deployment simplicity matters most, and notes that ASP.NET's model may be too restrictive with its monolithic web.dll recompilation requirement.
key_points: Inline ASPX pages offer deployment simplicity: single files that dynamically compile without rebuilding the entire solution
The conversion from code-behind to inline is straightforward, requiring only directive changes and code relocation
Inline code resurrects classic ASP problems: spaghetti code, limited intellisense, and inferior debugging experience
The code-behind model is overly restrictive, forcing full recompilation even for trivial changes to any page
There's room for a hybrid approach that balances deployment flexibility with code organization
money_quotes: It's sort of refreshing to rediscover inline code ASPX pages again

Deploying single web pages to a server without recompiling the entire solution? Making localized edits to single pages that take effect in real time? A single file that contains both code and markup in one convenient self-contained package? Revolutionary!
Inline ASPX pages also remind me of some things I didn't miss from the bad old days of ASP programming: spaghetti code, extremely limited intellisense, and crappy debugging
On the whole, I'll stick with code-behind, but I might vacation in Inlineville from time to time

The current ASP.NET code-behind model is a bit too restrictive; you end up with a humongous web.dll that has to be recompiled even if the tiniest bit of code in the most trivial web page in your solution changes
posts.key_insight: Both code organization patterns have legitimate trade-offs, and dogmatic adherence to either approach ignores the practical realities of different deployment and maintenance scenarios.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores a frustrating debugging quirk in .NET Windows Forms where exceptions thrown in the Form.Paint event don't break at the actual line of code, instead landing the debugger at the main application entry point. He demonstrates how the debugger misleads developers by showing the wrong breakpoint location, while the actual exception details are only visible in the debug output. The workaround involves enabling 'break on all CLR exceptions' in Visual Studio, but this causes breaks on every exception including handled ones. Atwood expresses frustration at the lack of satisfying explanations for this behavior, which stems from how PaintWithErrorHandling catches and re-throws exceptions.
key_points: Exceptions in Form.Paint don't break at the offending line of code - the debugger lands at the main entry point instead
The actual exception location IS present in the debug output, just not where the debugger breaks
Enabling 'break on all CLR exceptions' fixes the issue but causes breaks on every exception including handled ones
The behavior is caused by PaintWithErrorHandling catching and re-throwing exceptions back to the program entry point
This is a practical consequence of using the Form.Paint event for deferred work, a pattern inherited from VB6
money_quotes: The breakpoint the debugger puts us on is nowhere near the offending line of code.
As I'm sure you can imagine, this makes debugging... interesting.
I don't pretend to understand the arcane win32 api rules that cause exceptions to be somehow absorbed in Form.Paint() and passed back to the main application thread.
All I know is, it's incredibly annoying.
If any Windows Forms Coding Heroes want to step up and justify this in the comments, be my guest.
posts.key_insight: When debugging .NET Windows Forms applications, exceptions in Form.Paint events can mislead developers by breaking at the wrong location, requiring workarounds that come with their own annoyances.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on Steve McConnell's pyramid-building analogy for software projects, where daily progress must either move you closer to completion or reduce the remaining work required. He contrasts brute-force approaches that create an illusion of progress with smart planning that enables sustainable velocity. The post connects this to Joel Spolsky's 'Fire and Motion' principle—the idea that consistent forward movement, however imperfect, is what ultimately delivers results. Atwood concludes that good software comes not from genius or death marches, but from regular developers applying daily discipline and continuous improvement.
key_points: Progress that feels productive may still be insufficient—moving the block 10 meters when you need 100 means falling behind despite visible effort
Smart teams invest time upfront in planning and infrastructure (the 'rollers and roads') rather than immediately applying brute force
Continuous process improvement compounds over time, like adding extra rollers or creating harnesses to distribute work
Software projects are vulnerable to 'last-minute syndrome' because progress is intangible and urgency feels distant at the start
The key daily question for any project: Did we move one day closer, or reduce remaining work by one day?
money_quotes: 'Progress' doesn't necessarily mean sufficient progress.
Every day, a software project manager should ask, 'Did we move the block one day closer to our destination today? If not, did we reduce our remaining work by one day?'
Good software isn't magically conjured by superhuman geniuses, or cruelly constructed in endless, sadistic death marches by coding slaves.
Good software is built by regular developers using a daily regimen of hard work and careful planning.

You have to move forward a little bit, every day. It doesn't matter if your code is lame and buggy and nobody wants it. If you are moving forward, writing code and fixing bugs constantly, time is on your side.
Just spend a few hours every day moving the next stone block more efficiently than you did the last one, and it'll happen.
posts.key_insight: Sustainable software progress requires daily forward movement combined with continuous investment in working more efficiently—not heroic sprints or brute-force effort.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood demonstrates a simple technique for improving perceived application performance by moving initialization work from Form.Load to Form.Paint. By showing the form immediately and then doing work after the first paint, users perceive the application as faster even though the actual execution time is identical. He argues this three-line code change delivers better user experience than more complex threading solutions for minimal effort.
key_points: Moving work from Form.Load to Form.Paint makes applications feel faster without changing actual performance
The IsFirstPaint flag with DoEvents combo provides maximum benefit for minimum coding effort
Perceived performance matters more to users than actual performance
Proper threading would be technically superior but requires significantly more development effort
Showing UI immediately before doing work creates a better user experience
money_quotes: Perceived performance is more important than actual performance.
The amount of time is the same in both cases, but guess which one users will tell you is "faster?"
I guarantee that'll take a lot more work than three lines of code!
That's why I love the IsFirstPaint and DoEvents combo: maximum benefit for minimum effort.
posts.key_insight: Users judge application speed by when they see the UI, not by when work completes—so show the interface immediately and do heavy lifting afterward.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts his realization that he had been naively handling text encoding in his code, assuming all text could be treated as plain UTF-8. Drawing heavily from Joel Spolsky's famous Unicode article, he explains that every string must have a known encoding or it cannot be correctly interpreted. He shares his journey from ignorance to understanding, including the practical code he wrote to properly detect character encodings from HTTP headers and HTML meta tags. The post is a humble admission of a common programming blind spot among English-speaking developers.
key_points: There is no such thing as 'plain text' - every string has an encoding that must be known to interpret it correctly
Most encoding schemes share the same values for characters 32-127, which allows basic ASCII parsing before determining the actual encoding
Encoding information can come from HTTP Content-Type headers or HTML meta tags, and both should be checked
Defaulting to UTF-8 when no encoding is specified is considered a best practice in .NET
Many bugs only appear in edge cases - code can seem correct until it encounters non-English content
money_quotes: I had an ugly American view of strings.
There ain't no such thing as plain text.
If you have a string, in memory, in a file, or in an email message, you have to know what encoding it is in or you cannot interpret it or display it to users correctly.
I was doing a naïve, blanket UTF-8 conversion of this byte data.
It is right most of the time, which can lull you into a false sense of correctness.
