A lot of things are like that in software; you think you have it right, but you just haven't hit the edge conditions yet.
I apologize to all the non-English speaking users of my CodeProject articles – I'm fixing it!
posts.key_insight: English-speaking programmers often develop a dangerous blind spot about text encoding because their code appears to work until it encounters non-ASCII characters.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces his decision to purchase a 3-way MAME cocktail arcade cabinet kit after years of holding out. He explains the homebrew arcade market's evolution into an IKEA-like model where prefab cabinets ship for home assembly with standard PC components. The post details the trade-offs between upright and cocktail cabinet formats, with cocktail cabinets being more space-efficient but traditionally limited to vertical-orientation games. His chosen 3-way kit solves this by supporting both head-to-head and side-by-side play configurations.
key_points: The homebrew arcade market has matured into prefab cabinet businesses that ship flat-pack kits for home assembly with standard PC monitors and components
Cocktail cabinets offer space efficiency and portability advantages over traditional uprights, including the ability to roll on casters and be disguised with a tablecloth
Classic cocktail cabinets have a serious limitation: they only support vertical-orientation games with screen-flipping between player turns
The 3-way cocktail design solves orientation limitations by supporting both vertical (head-to-head) and horizontal (side-by-side) game configurations
Planned modifications include external speakers, a real arcade coin door, and LED lighting
money_quotes: After years of resistance, I finally buckled.
Over the last few years, the homebrew arcade market has spawned a number of businesses specializing in prefab cabinets – think IKEA meets MAME.
If I had the space, I'd choose a traditional upright arcade cabinet.
In the meantime, the cocktail is a better choice for me; it takes up less space, can roll on casters, and, if necessary, can be covered with a tablecloth.
You're never too old to finally realize that childhood dream: to be the kid who owns his own arcade game!
posts.key_insight: The convergence of prefab manufacturing and emulation technology has made owning a custom arcade cabinet an accessible DIY project rather than an impossible childhood fantasy.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces an update to his WebFileManager CodeProject article, adding zip file support and column sorting capabilities. He includes both code-behind and inline code versions and notes a new dependency on SharpZipLib. The post briefly documents two technical limitations he discovered: SharpZipLib's inability to modify existing zip archives, and Firefox's lack of support for column alignment in the COLGROUP tag.
key_points: WebFileManager now supports zipping files and column sorting
SharpZipLib cannot modify existing zip archives, only create new ones
Firefox doesn't support alignment in COLGROUP tags, making column styling cumbersome
Both code-behind and inline code versions are now included in the solution
money_quotes: SharpZipLib, like the Java class it apes, is completely incapable of modifying an existing Zip archive.
CSS and HTML both kinda suck when referencing table columns. Cells and rows, yes, columns, not so much.
posts.key_insight: A brief project update that documents practical limitations discovered in third-party libraries and browser implementations.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts a frustrating incident where a colleague refactored his working code in the name of performance optimization, introducing a bug in the process. The colleague replaced simple equality operators with .Equals method calls, citing marginal performance gains and dubious benefits like easier language conversion. Atwood systematically dismantles each justification, pointing out that the 'optimization' actually broke the code by failing to handle null values. He argues forcefully that readability and simplicity should always take precedence over micro-optimizations, especially when those optimizations are based on theoretical articles rather than actual application profiling.
key_points: Premature optimization that sacrifices readability often introduces bugs while providing negligible real-world performance benefits
The refactored code broke because calling .Equals on a null object throws an exception, demonstrating how 'improvements' can introduce regressions
Performance gains from using .Equals over the = operator are marginal and irrelevant in a database-driven web application where database queries dominate execution time
Code clarity enables analysis and maintenance, which is the true measure of performance - not nanosecond savings
Optimizations should be based on actual metrics from functional applications, not theoretical articles read on websites
money_quotes: Don't take working code and break it in the name of highly questionable 'performance' gains.
Can you imagine reading code littered with tons of .Equals calls instead of the simple, straightforward = operator?
Code that makes sense is code which can be analyzed and maintained, and that makes it performant.
It's worthless code. It's a distraction to maintenance programmers. That's the real performance cost.
Always, always write for simplicity and readability first.
If you must optimize, use real optimizations based on actual metrics in a functional application. Not hypothetical theory based on some article you read on some website.
Good luck calling the .Equals method on Nothing.
posts.key_insight: The real cost of misguided performance optimization isn't just wasted effort—it's broken code and reduced maintainability, making simplicity and readability the true performance wins.

posts.summary: This technical post documents the author's solution to handling HTTP deflate compression in .NET's WebClient class, which lacks built-in compression support. After a reader pointed out that his previous gzip-only solution failed for deflate-compressed responses, Atwood hunted down a fix using SharpZipLib. The post provides working VB.NET code that handles both gzip and deflate decompression, with the key insight being that the Inflater class needs a specific constructor parameter for raw deflate streams. Atwood notes that deflate compression is extremely rare on the web, making working code samples hard to find.
key_points: The .NET WebClient class lacks native HTTP compression support, requiring manual decompression
Deflate compression is extremely rare on the open internet compared to gzip
SharpZipLib's InflaterInputStream requires passing True to the Inflater constructor for HTTP deflate streams
A third HTTP compression type called 'compress' exists but is essentially unused
Community forums proved essential for solving the edge case when documentation was lacking
money_quotes: This is case of be careful what you ask for.
For whatever reason, deflate compression is extremely rare on the open internet.
In desperation – my OCD prohibits me from letting that last 1% case go – I turned to the only relevant Google result I could find.
There is also a mysterious, third kind of HTTP compression, compress. Ok, it's not all that mysterious, but nobody seems to use it. What's up with that?
posts.key_insight: When dealing with edge cases in HTTP compression, community knowledge often fills the gaps that official documentation leaves empty.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software project failure is the norm rather than the exception, and that experienced programmers understand success requires avoiding almost everything going wrong rather than just doing a few things right. Drawing on Steve McConnell's research, he notes that roughly 25% of projects are canceled outright and another 50% go over budget or schedule. He uses the FBI's $170 million Virtual Case File disaster as a contemporary example of how even well-funded projects fail spectacularly. The post concludes that developers who have lived through failures become more cautious and effective precisely because they understand the pain they can inflict on users.
key_points: The difference between beginners and experienced programmers is that beginners think hard work leads to success, while veterans know hard work might only prevent failure
Software project success depends less on avoiding a few mistakes and more on doing almost everything right
Industry data suggests 25% of projects are canceled, 50% go over budget/schedule, and only 25% finish on time—often with reduced scope
The FBI's $170 million Virtual Case File project serves as a stark example of how even massive investments can result in complete failure
Developers who have experienced failure cycles work harder to understand pitfalls and should be trusted more than those who deny ever being part of disasters
money_quotes: New programmers think if they work hard, they might succeed. Experienced programmers know that if they work really hard, they might not fail.
Actually succeeding in a software project depends a whole lot less on not doing a few things wrong but on doing almost everything right.
A quarter of the projects are canceled before they're completed and another 50 percent are completed over budget or behind schedule.
The depressing reality of software development is that your project has to be almost supernaturally well run to avoid failure, much less succeed.
Only developers who have been through a number of failure cycles can appreciate the pain they're about to inflict on users.
I'd seriously question the credentials – or at least the intellectual honesty – of any developer who denied being a part of any software disasters.
posts.key_insight: Software development success is not about doing things right—it's about the near-impossible task of avoiding the countless ways projects fail.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood highlights Bruce Tognazzini's list of the 10 most wanted UI design bugs, which catalogs persistent usability problems in graphical interfaces. Tognazzini, considered the father of the Macintosh UI, identifies issues ranging from power failure data loss to focus-stealing dialogs. While Atwood acknowledges that a couple items read as personal complaints about Apple design decisions, he agrees the majority represent genuinely frustrating UI problems that plague every GUI. The post also points readers to Marissa Mayer's perspective on Google's contrasting approach to user experience design.
key_points: Tognazzini's list identifies universal GUI problems that persist across platforms, not just Apple-specific issues
Focus-stealing dialogs, mysteriously dimmed menu items, and ASCII sort bugs remain unsolved decades into GUI computing
Some criticism in the list reflects personal design disagreements rather than objective usability flaws
The post contrasts traditional UI expertise (Tognazzini) with Google's emerging approach to user experience
money_quotes: If the computer loses power for more than a few thousandths of a second, it throws everything away.
Designers offer no way for users to discover why a given menu or option has been dimmed (grayed out), nor how to turn it back on.
15 Dec 2008 sorts as being before 2 Jan 1900.

You're working on a multitasking system, typing away merrily in window A. Meanwhile, some background task decides it needs your attention, pops up a dialog, and moves the keyboard input focus from the window you were working on to its dialog box.
Two of these come across as cranky 'I coulda done it better' complaints about specific Apple design decisions he doesn't agree with. But the rest are truly aggravating UI problems in every GUI.
posts.key_insight: Many fundamental UI design bugs identified decades ago remain unsolved in modern software, representing persistent failures in how we build graphical interfaces.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to criticism about inefficient string comparison in his code by benchmarking four different methods for checking empty strings in .NET. While s.Length = 0 is indeed 5-10x faster than s = "" or s = String.Empty, Atwood demonstrates that even the 'slowest' method takes only 30 milliseconds for a million comparisons. He argues that such micro-optimizations are meaningless in practice and that developers should focus on writing readable, maintainable code instead. The post concludes by invoking Eric Lippert's wisdom that understandable code is inherently more performant because it can be properly analyzed and maintained.
key_points: String.Length = 0 is 5-10x faster than string equality comparisons, but neither handles null strings
The actual performance difference is ~30ms over one million iterations on slow hardware
Micro-optimization debates distract from more important concerns like readability and maintainability
Code that makes sense to humans can be better analyzed and optimized than clever but obscure code
Developers often obsess over trivial performance differences that have no real-world impact
money_quotes: If I had a nickel every time someone told me that, I could have retired years ago.
We're still talking about a difference of 30 milliseconds, on the slowest computer I own, over a MILLION string comparisons!
It just doesn't matter! It just doesn't matter!
Arguments about which method results in code that is easier to read and easier to maintain will be gladly entertained. Arguments about speed will not.
Stop micro-optimizing and start macro-optimizing: per Lippert, code that makes sense is code which can be analyzed and maintained, and that makes it performant.
posts.key_insight: Obsessing over micro-optimizations that save nanoseconds per operation is a waste of developer time; readable, maintainable code is ultimately more performant because it can be properly understood and improved.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores why Mac users and PC users fundamentally talk past each other when debating computer purchases. He cites Ed Stroglio's analysis that PC users view computers as tools (screwdrivers) while Mac users view them as extensions of personal identity (fashion). This explains why spec-based arguments about price and performance fail to persuade Mac users—they're operating from entirely different value systems. The post captures an early example of the utilitarian vs. experiential divide in technology consumption.
key_points: PC users treat computers as tools judged purely on functionality; Mac users treat them as personal identity statements
Case modders and overclockers differ from Mac users because they take pride in what they've done to equipment, not mere ownership
Telling a Mac user that a Dell is cheaper is like telling a fashionista that Old Navy overalls last longer than designer jeans
The disconnect between the two camps is fundamentally about different value systems, not ignorance of specs
Understanding this divide explains why 'standard arguments for buying a PC fall on deaf ears'
money_quotes: For PCers, a computer is a tool, an animated screwdriver. You don't have an 'experience' with a screwdriver.
For Macsters, the object is an extension of themselves just as much as their clothing or interior decoration.
What such people are proud of is not mere ownership of the equipment, but what they've done to it to make it what it is.
Telling them that a Dell is cheaper and better is like telling them that Old Navy overalls are cheaper and last longer than Dolce & Gabbana jeans.
When you do that, what they hear is, 'Be a common pig like the rest of us' when the whole point of the purchase is to prove the opposite.
You're thinking screwdriver; they're thinking fashion outfit.
posts.key_insight: The Mac vs PC debate is irresolvable because each side operates from incompatible value systems—utility versus identity expression—making their arguments mutually incomprehensible.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses Jack Black's self-deprecating humor about compensating for lack of musical skill with intensity to illustrate a broader point about success in programming. He references a Sun instructor who won top evaluations despite admitting to being an average teacher, by focusing entirely on student outcomes rather than demonstrating personal expertise. The instructor abandoned traditional presentation methods and instead designed situations that made students actively learn. Atwood draws a parallel to software development, arguing that intensity and focus on results can overcome gaps in raw talent. The post challenges the intimidation programmers feel when comparing themselves to more talented peers.
key_points: Intensity and dedication can compensate for lack of natural talent in both performance and technical fields
The Sun instructor achieved exceptional results by shifting focus from 'proving I'm smart' to 'making them smarter'
Traditional presentation skills and credentials matter less than creating conditions for actual learning
What happens 'between their ears' matters more than what comes 'out of your mouth'
Programmers shouldn't be intimidated by more talented developers if they bring genuine intensity to their work
money_quotes: I'm not very good at acoustic guitar, but I make up for it with intensity.
I proved that a very average instructor could get exceptional results by putting the focus ENTIRELY on the students.
Assuming you meet some very minimal threshold for teaching, all that matters is that you help the students become smarter.
That usually has nothing to do with what comes out of your mouth, and has everything to do with what happens between their ears.
You, as the instructor, have to design and enable situations that cause things to happen.
Don't be cowed by the existence of thousands of developers far more talented than you are.
Who needs talent when you have intensity?
posts.key_insight: In programming as in teaching and performance, intense dedication to outcomes can overcome limitations in raw talent, so long as you meet a minimal competency threshold.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the common practice of adding namespaces to community-contributed code, arguing that most namespaces serve no functional purpose. He observes that while .NET's system namespaces are well-organized, community code namespaces are chaotic and often just combinations of random words. Atwood proposes that developers should skip namespaces entirely for most shared code, since they rarely solve actual collision problems. He concludes that namespaces on small projects are essentially vanity license plates that add complexity without benefit.
key_points: Community-contributed code namespaces are anarchistic and inconsistent, often using random word combinations like 'monkeyCode' or 'fuzzyAlienWare'
Most namespaces don't solve any actual collision or duplication problems—they're purely cosmetic
Namespaces only make sense for signed binaries with no source code; otherwise they're unnecessary overhead
Developers should focus on giving classes good descriptive names rather than adding namespace prefixes
The practice of adding namespaces 'just because' represents blind adherence to convention without considering actual needs
money_quotes: I have a modest proposal for you: how about no namespace at all?
Almost every time I see namespaces used, they're not functional. They don't solve any collision or duplication problems for me.
They're little more than vanity license plates for the author's code.
Community / open source code namespaces are an anarchistic babel.
Stop with the veiled ego tripping, give your class a good descriptive name, and have the cojones to leave it at that.
How many MhtBuilder classes are out there that I need to distinguish between?
posts.key_insight: Namespaces in community code are often just developer vanity rather than solving real organizational problems—if there's no actual collision to prevent, skip the namespace entirely.

posts.summary: Atwood argues that developers should prioritize books focused on ideas and theory over thick technical manuals, as conceptual knowledge has a much longer shelf life than tool-specific documentation. He points to The Mythical Man Month as an example of a 20-year-old book that remains more valuable than current technical tomes. The post also explores the meta-skill of learning to learn, emphasizing the importance of asking 'why' repeatedly until reaching foundational knowledge. Atwood concludes with a critique of overly complex systems like COM, suggesting that truly well-designed systems should be easy to understand without extensive training.
key_points: Books heavy on ideas and light on code have longer shelf lives and greater impact than tool-specific manuals
Theory and knowledge change slowly and incrementally, making them timeless investments
Effective learning requires connecting new information to existing knowledge through persistent questioning
Well-designed systems should be easy to understand - complexity requiring week-long training courses indicates poor design
The ability to learn how to learn may be more valuable than any specific technical knowledge
money_quotes: Books that are heavy on ideas and light on code end up having a longer shelf-life and make more of an impact.
Knowledge and theory never goes out of style, unless you stop improving on it.
The most important thing that I've ever learned is how to learn.
All learning is only as effective as well as it can be tied to what you already know.
The more you know, the easier it is to learn more.
Well designed systems are easy to understand.
Anyone can develop a complex system that takes a week long training course to comprehend. Developing a complex system that a developer can master in a day, well, that's a lot more impressive.
posts.key_insight: Investing in timeless conceptual knowledge and the meta-skill of learning yields far greater returns than chasing the latest technical documentation.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits Charles Moore's guiding principles from the creation of the FORTH programming language, arguing that these decades-old insights remain highly relevant today. The post highlights three core principles: keep it simple, don't speculate about future needs, and learn by doing it yourself. Atwood connects the first two to modern concepts like KISS and YAGNI, while noting the third is about the irreplaceable value of learning through personal experience. He emphasizes that simplicity isn't a passive state but an active force that must be deliberately applied against constant pressure to add features.
key_points: Complexity increases exponentially as capabilities are added, making the pressure to 'just add one more feature' dangerous
Speculative code for future extensions has near-zero probability of being used correctly and should be avoided
Writing your own implementations, even if they exist elsewhere, teaches you lessons that reading about best practices cannot
Simplicity requires active effort and hard choices - it's a force to be applied, not a passive goal
Ancient programming wisdom from languages like FORTH remains surprisingly applicable to modern development
money_quotes: As the number of capabilities you add to a program increases, the complexity of the program increases exponentially.
The things you might want to do are infinite; that means that each has 0 probability of realization.
If you need an extension later, you can code it later – and probably do a better job than if you did it now.
You have to make your own mistakes to truly learn.

There's a world of difference between someone explaining 'you should always index your tables because it's a best practice,' and having your app get progressively slower as records are added to the table.
Simplicity as a force that must be applied instead of a passive goal.
All too often, I see developers failing to make the hard choices necessary to keep their applications simple.
posts.key_insight: Simplicity in software is not a passive default state but an active force that must be deliberately applied against the constant pressures to add complexity.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood briefly discusses the slowing growth rate of hard disk capacity while noting that large storage arrays are still achievable. The post focuses on practical tools for visualizing disk space usage, comparing three utilities: overdisk, Treesize, and spacemonger. He appreciates overdisk's Tufte-inspired visual design but questions whether the fancy visualization actually provides more useful information than simpler approaches. The post is a quick, practical recommendation for developers dealing with mysteriously disappearing disk space.
key_points: Hard disk capacity growth was slowing compared to earlier rates, though 400GB drives and terabyte arrays were achievable in 2005
Visual disk space analyzers help solve the common problem of unexplained storage consumption
Aesthetic data visualization (like overdisk's Tufte-style approach) doesn't necessarily mean more useful information
Treesize works well for server environments where practical utility matters more than visual appeal
money_quotes: Hard disk space, like CPU speed, isn't increasing as fast as it used to.
Which begs the question – where does all that hard drive space go?
I like the Tufte-esque visual style of overdisk, but I'm not convinced the information is any more useful when presented in that format.
posts.key_insight: Pretty data visualization doesn't automatically mean more useful information—sometimes simpler tools that just show you what you need are more practical.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reviews his Logitech Harmony H680 universal remote after a month of use, concluding that despite several frustrations, it genuinely solves the multi-remote problem. He praises its effectiveness at controlling all his home theater devices but criticizes three main issues: the clunky web-based configuration interface that should be a desktop application, sluggish button response delays, and IR command repetition that causes double inputs. While recommending the product overall, he uses it as an example of how even well-designed hardware can be hampered by poor software decisions.
key_points: The Harmony remote successfully replaces multiple device remotes and lives up to its promise of simplified home theater control
Web-based configuration interfaces are poorly suited for complex device programming tasks that would benefit from native desktop applications
Default timing settings (200ms delays, triple IR command repeats) create frustrating lag and double-input problems that require buried advanced settings to fix
Good hardware can be undermined by suboptimal software design choices
The best settings to fix responsiveness issues are hidden deep in 'troubleshooting' sections with poor navigation
money_quotes: The short answer is, yes, it really works.
Harmony Central is a classic argument for smart client applications.
It works, but it's all very hacky and much slower than it needs to be. Square peg, meet round hole.
Plain HTML interfaces are a giant pain in the ass for complex applications like this.
None of these things are deal breakers, but they're annoying.
Good luck finding these advanced settings on the website, by the way.
posts.key_insight: Even excellent hardware products can be significantly hampered by poor software interface decisions, particularly when web applications are forced into roles better suited for native desktop software.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reviews the new version of RegexBuddy, highlighting its new GREP tool, visual debugging, and Unicode support. He praises Jan Goyvaerts' work, including his free regular-expressions.info tutorial site. The post takes a critical turn toward Microsoft's Visual Studio .NET find dialog, which inexplicably uses non-standard regex syntax instead of the .NET framework's own regex implementation. Atwood expresses frustration that this quirk persists into VS.NET 2005 and points to a third-party add-in as a workaround.
key_points: RegexBuddy's new version adds a built-in GREP tool, visual regex debugging, and full Unicode support
GREP represents the natural progression of regex mastery—applying patterns to files and folders instead of just strings
Visual Studio .NET's find dialog supports regex but uses bizarre non-standard syntax that differs from .NET's own regex classes
Microsoft's decision to use proprietary regex syntax in the IDE while shipping standard regex in the framework is puzzling and frustrating
Third-party add-ins exist to provide standard regex search in VS.NET, though they need polish
money_quotes: It's easily the best Regex tool available.
GREP is a natural (and very UNIX-y) progression once you've mastered regular expressions; consider the utility of Regexes on files and folders instead of strings and you've got the idea.
Microsoft decided to use bizarro world regular expression syntax.
Why couldn't Microsoft just use standard Regex syntax, like the built in .NET Regex classes?
If it's a compatibility issue, why not let us select between bizarro and standard Regex syntax?
posts.key_insight: Developer tools should use standard, well-documented syntax rather than proprietary variations—especially when the standard implementation already exists in your own framework.

posts.summary: Atwood argues that the IDE matters far more to developer productivity than the programming language itself. He observes that while programming languages have benefited from 30 years of computer science advances, visual IDEs remain surprisingly primitive. He proposes a radical approach: future language development should start with building a great IDE first, then design the language to be deeply integrated with it. The post concludes that the future will see IDE and language become synonymous, rather than the current approach of generic IDEs supporting multiple languages.
key_points: Choice of IDE has a bigger impact on productivity than choice of programming language
Visual IDEs are still in their infancy despite 30 years of language evolution
Future language development should prioritize IDE development before language design
Deep integration between a specific IDE and language will outperform one-size-fits-all approaches
Language syntax improvements ('syntactic sugar') should be the lowest priority after IDE excellence
money_quotes: The future of programming languages isn't a language – it's the IDE, dummy!
Which would you rather use: QuickBasic with the world's best IDE, or (insert your favorite language here) in Notepad?
We now have languages that are the product of thirty years of computer science, but visual IDEs are still in their infancy.
It's kind of frustrating to see computers rendering 3D worlds with 100s of frames/sec, but source code editors advancing only in very small steps.
I don't think a one-size-fits-all-languages IDE strategy is sustainable.
In the future, IDE will be synonymous with language.
posts.key_insight: Developer productivity depends more on tooling quality than language choice, and the most productive future lies in deeply integrated IDE-language combinations rather than generic editors supporting many languages.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that video codec fragmentation on Windows has become as problematic as the infamous DLL Hell, with multiple competing codecs causing widespread compatibility issues. He cites examples of audio sync problems, format incompatibilities, and the absurdity of Windows Media Center requiring an MPEG-2 codec it doesn't ship. Microsoft's response blaming licensing costs is dismissed as a copout when weighed against the massive support burden. Atwood calls on Microsoft to take responsibility as the only entity with authority to solve this industry-wide coordination problem by shipping default codecs and prioritizing customers over OEM relationships.
key_points: Multiple codec vendors all trying to decode the same formats creates a chaotic 'merit arms race' where quality and compatibility suffer
Windows Media Center's dependency on an MPEG-2 codec it doesn't include is an embarrassing product design failure
Microsoft's licensing cost excuse ignores the hidden costs of thousands of support issues and frustrated customers
The DirectShow merit system was designed for cooperation but fails when vendors game it competitively
Microsoft is uniquely positioned to solve this coordination problem but is abdicating that responsibility to appease OEMs
money_quotes: Codecs are the next DLL hell.
Are you counting how many things on the system can decode the same content? It's too many!
I would say a good 60-70% of the problems people have with Windows Media Player are due to codecs.
It literally cannot function at all without one – and yet does not ship with one in the box.
It wasn't really designed for a competing merit nuclear arms race.
At some point Microsoft has to take a clear stand for the customer.
OEMs will do what is best for them, not for us. Microsoft is the only consumer advocate that can help.
posts.key_insight: When an ecosystem of uncoordinated vendors creates compatibility chaos, the platform owner must step up as customer advocate rather than hiding behind cost excuses and OEM politics.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces the completion of his MAME cocktail arcade cabinet project after two weeks of intensive work. He has created a dedicated project page with comprehensive documentation including photos, product links, and explanations for his design decisions. The post serves as a brief update pointing readers to the more detailed documentation, noting that the information applies broadly to MAME cabinet builds of all types.
key_points: Completed a MAME cocktail arcade cabinet after two weeks of continuous refinement
Created comprehensive documentation with photos, product links, and design rationales
The documentation is applicable to MAME cabinets of all types, not just cocktail style
References workplace interest in classic arcade gaming (Golden Tee)
money_quotes: After two weeks of non-stop tweaking, I think my MAME Cocktail arcade is finally complete.
Most of the comments there apply to MAME cabinets of all types, so if you have any interest in classic arcade gaming at all, it's worth reading.
posts.key_insight: Thorough documentation of personal projects, including rationales behind decisions, creates valuable resources that can help others undertaking similar endeavors.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the debate around object-oriented programming and design patterns, prompted by Richard Mansfield's critique that OOP introduces unnecessary complexity for most programmers. While skeptical of Mansfield's extreme position, Atwood acknowledges that dogmatic adherence to patterns is equally problematic. He reviews the enduring influence of the Gang of Four's Design Patterns book while noting that even simplified pattern examples often feel overcomplicated. Atwood concludes that patterns serve better as shared vocabulary than as actual implementation blueprints, preferring simpler procedural solutions when they suffice.
key_points: OOP and design patterns can introduce unnecessary complexity for straightforward problems
Experienced developers like Mansfield who criticize OOP shouldn't be dismissed outright, even if their views seem extreme
The Gang of Four's Design Patterns remains influential after ten years, with Singleton, Factory, Observer, and Command being most commonly cited
Design patterns are more valuable as a common vocabulary for discussing design than as literal implementation templates
Simple solutions like database tables and procedural code often beat elaborate object hierarchies for real-world problems
money_quotes: OOP introduces an unnecessary layer of complexity to procedure-oriented design.
Too many people accept patterns as gospel, so I think a little counter-programming is healthy.
I still feel patterns are more useful as a common design vocabulary than as actual implementation models.
Even the radically simplified pattern examples in the book are far more complicated than they need to be.
Would I really design a point of sale system that used the Decorator pattern to represent coffee pricing? I think I'd use a simple relational database table and some procedural code.
If I needed to add a topping, I'd simply add a record to the table – no complex objects or inheritance models required.
posts.key_insight: Design patterns serve programmers better as a shared vocabulary for communicating ideas than as prescriptive templates to be literally implemented.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents a puzzling behavior change in .NET console applications where the AppDomain.CurrentDomain.UnhandledException handler no longer properly captures exceptions on the main thread. Despite registering an unhandled exception handler, applications display the standard .NET crash dialog first, only invoking the custom handler after the dialog is dismissed. He suspects this behavior changed with .NET 1.1 SP1 or XP SP2 but cannot confirm it. The post includes sample code and references to reproduce the issue, and notes that the only workaround disables crash dialogs for all .NET applications.
key_points: Unhandled exception handlers in .NET console apps show the crash dialog before invoking custom handlers, contrary to expected behavior
The behavior appears consistent across all machines tested, ruling out development environment quirks
MSDN documentation and sample code imply the expected behavior should work, but it doesn't
The only known workaround is a global setting that affects all .NET applications
The author suspects a regression introduced in .NET 1.1 SP1 or Windows XP SP2
money_quotes: you'll get the standard .NET crash dialog in a console app, even if you've registered an unhandled exception handler for your AppDomain
The compiled .exe behaves in the same way on every machine I tried it on
That's... not exactly the order I had in mind
it's also treating the symptoms rather than the disease: why can't we catch unhandled exceptions in console apps any more?
the MSDN sample code certainly implies that it's possible – but good luck getting that sample to print the expected output
posts.key_insight: Framework updates can silently break expected behavior in ways that contradict official documentation, leaving developers to discover regressions through painful trial and error.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reports on JetBrains' plans to bring ReSharper support to VB.NET in Visual Studio 2005. He expresses enthusiasm about this news while also pointing out the irony that ReSharper's flagship feature—real-time error detection without compilation—is something VB.NET has had natively since 1997. The post is a brief observation on how C# developers are catching up to productivity features VB developers have long enjoyed.
key_points: ReSharper VB.NET support was announced for Visual Studio 2005
ReSharper's highlighted feature of detecting errors without compilation already existed in VB since 1997
Background compilation significantly increases developer productivity
money_quotes: Smells like teen spirit background compilation, something VB has had since 1997.
Does it increase productivity? Absolutely.
posts.key_insight: Features celebrated as innovations in one language ecosystem may have existed for years in another, highlighting how siloed developer communities can be.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the Dreyfus Model of Skill Acquisition and its implications for software development, building on Joel Spolsky's critique of rigid methodologies. The model describes five levels of expertise, from beginners who need rules to experts who work primarily from intuition. Atwood argues that formal methodologies provide diminishing returns as developers advance in skill, and can actually degrade the performance of experts when enforced. He draws parallels between software development's current challenges and the nursing profession's struggles in the 1970s. The post warns that rule-based work is easily outsourced, encouraging developers to pursue challenging work that requires genuine expertise rather than following prescribed processes.
key_points: The Dreyfus Model describes five levels of skill acquisition, from rigid rule-following beginners to intuition-driven experts
Big-M methodologies are designed to bring everyone to a 'dismal but passable' level while frustrating talented developers
Expert performance actually degrades when forced to follow rigid rules designed for beginners
Work that can be precisely specified with rules is vulnerable to outsourcing, making low Dreyfus levels risky career positions
The nursing profession faced similar problems in the 1970s and the Dreyfus model helped address them
money_quotes: Methodologies are a great way to bring everyone up to a dismal, but passable, level of performance, but at the same time, they are aggravating to more talented people who chafe at the restrictions that are placed on them.
The value of big-Em methodology decreases very sharply as you climb the skill ladder.
What can companies effectively outsource? Stuff that can be specified precisely. Stuff that has rules.
When forced to follow set rules, performance is degraded.
We need to move away from the low Dreyfus levels and start occupying the higher Dreyfus levels if we are to make ourselves less vulnerable to job evaporation.
'No rules' isn't shorthand for an air of smug superiority, although there's certainly no shortage of that in our profession.
The kind of work that cannot be done by beginners slavishly following a big-Em methodology.
posts.key_insight: Developers should actively pursue challenging work with unknowns that requires genuine expertise, because rule-based work can be done by beginners or outsourced, while expert-level work demands the intuition and judgment that no methodology can replicate.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recommends the Wintellect ASP.NET FAQ as a valuable resource for ASP.NET developers. Despite being somewhat dated, he finds the FAQ contains genuinely insightful material covering both basic and advanced topics. The post highlights the value of well-constructed FAQs built from real-world questions.
key_points: Well-constructed FAQs based on real-world questions are invaluable resources
The Wintellect FAQ covers both basic and advanced ASP.NET topics
Good documentation ranges from mundane syntax questions to complex threading concerns
money_quotes: Nothing beats a well constructed FAQ based on lots of real-world questions.
It's a mixture of the mundane and the sublime.
Guaranteed to contain a few things you didn't know you needed to know about ASP.NET.
posts.key_insight: The best technical FAQs are those built from accumulated real-world questions, covering everything from basic syntax to complex architectural concerns.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the debate between static and dynamic typing by quoting Bruce Eckel's analysis of their tradeoffs. Eckel argues that while static typing catches certain errors, it creates an illusion of correctness and may sap energy needed for proper unit testing. Dynamic languages offer flexibility for rapid experimentation but require disciplined testing. Atwood proposes a third way: tightly coupling the IDE with the language so that the IDE can warn about type errors while still allowing dynamic compilation, giving developers the best of both worlds.
key_points: Static typing gives an 'illusion of program correctness' - it only checks syntax, not actual correctness
Dynamic languages excel at rapid experimentation and modeling, with low barriers to redesign
The extra effort of satisfying static type checkers may reduce energy available for unit testing
The future lies in tight IDE-compiler coupling, where the IDE handles type warnings while allowing dynamic flexibility
Next-generation IDEs could eliminate syntax errors entirely by directly manipulating parse trees
money_quotes: If we tightly couple the IDE and the language, the IDE could warn us about static typing errors, but give us the flexibility to treat the types dynamically when we compile.
I think statically typed languages give the illusion of program correctness. In fact, they can only go so far in determining the correctness of a program, by checking the syntax.
I suspect the extra effort required to jump through the static type-checking hoops may be less fruitful than we might believe.
It's very helpful to create models in a dynamic language, because there is a very low barrier to redesigning as you learn.
Give the IDE some functionality traditionally assigned to the compiler: that's the true leap of faith, and the only way to deliver the best of both worlds.
It's difficult to imagine reading a more unenthusiastic endorsement of static typing. Although it's true that static typing hasn't killed any puppies. Yet.
posts.key_insight: Rather than choosing between static and dynamic typing, the future of programming lies in IDE-compiler integration that provides type warnings without sacrificing dynamic flexibility.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues against isolating developers from end users, a practice he calls 'ivory tower development.' He contends that when developers work in isolation, shielded by business analysts, they inevitably create worse products because they assume users think like developers. The post advocates for exposing developers directly to users throughout the project lifecycle, noting that developers must witness firsthand how typical users struggle with software. Atwood extends this critique to internal enterprise software, suggesting that captive audiences lead to poor quality, and proposes that internal departments should operate like micro-ISVs selling to their users.
key_points: Isolated developers assume all users are developers, leading to products that fail real users
Business analysts often worsen the problem by shielding developers from direct user contact
Internal enterprise software tends to be poor quality because users have no choice but to use it
Developers must directly observe users struggling with software to overcome their 'Homo Logicus' blinders
Internal departments should operate like micro-ISVs, charging for their software to create natural market pressure for quality
money_quotes: In the absence of any other compelling evidence, developers assume everyone else is a developer.
The more isolated the developers, the worse the resulting end product.
Nothing removes a developer's Homo Logicus blinders faster than seeing a typical user struggle with basic computer applications.
Developers simply cannot comprehend that the average user doesn't even know what ALT+TAB does, much less how to use it. They have to see it to believe it.
I define internal projects as projects where users are forced to use your application whether they want to or not.
Sadly, lots of internal software sucks pretty badly. It's true. And it is sad.
I'd much rather work on projects with paying customers, or at least treat internal projects as if users were paying real money for your product.
posts.key_insight: Software quality degrades in direct proportion to how isolated developers are from their users, and the only cure is forcing developers to witness real users struggling with their creations.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares practical tips for working with Microsoft's Virtual PC 2004, focusing on performance optimization after the release of Service Pack 1. He explains that while CPU and network performance are acceptable, disk and video emulation create significant bottlenecks. The post recommends specific strategies like using disk images instead of physical media, ensuring VMs have adequate memory to avoid paging, and potentially using fixed-size disk images. He also documents the specific hardware that VPC emulates (S3 Trio, Intel 21140, SoundBlaster 16) and explains these choices were made for maximum compatibility.
key_points: Virtual PC disk performance is only 40-70% of host, making it the biggest bottleneck alongside video emulation
OS installations in VPC can take two or more hours even on fast hardware - plan accordingly
Never starve VMs for memory since paging to the already-slow emulated disk creates severe performance problems
Always use ISO and floppy disk images instead of physical media to avoid additional performance penalties
VPC emulates specific legacy hardware (S3 Trio, Intel 21140, SoundBlaster 16) chosen for broad OS compatibility
money_quotes: Even if you have fire-breathing PC hardware – and any self-respecting developer should, because time is money, and PCs are cheap these days – you'll be disappointed with Virtual PC performance.
Emulated disk performance is terrible.
If these guys start paging to disk, you'll be in a world of hurt.
The biggest performance problems in Virtual PC are caused by the slow emulated disk and video subsystems.
Always use disk image files!
This isn't that kind of emulation.
posts.key_insight: Virtual PC's performance bottleneck lies primarily in disk and video emulation, so optimizing memory allocation and using disk images instead of physical media are essential for a tolerable experience.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares an ASP.NET web version of his Windows Forms About Box utility, responding to reader requests. The page displays assembly information with expandable details showing all loaded assemblies and their attributes. The implementation uses an HTML template that can be customized without recompilation, pulling data automatically from assembly attributes. Atwood emphasizes the importance of properly populating AssemblyInfo attributes since that's where the About page derives its information.
key_points: The ASP.NET About Page mirrors the functionality of the Windows Forms version, showing loaded assemblies and their attributes
The HTML template approach allows visual customization without recompilation
Assembly attributes can be displayed using named properties or arbitrary attribute keys
Properly populating AssemblyInfo is essential since it's the data source for the About page
money_quotes: Oh, and populate your damn assembly attributes, because that's where all this information is automatically derived from
The main page is a HTML template; you can make it look however you want by modifying the markup – no recompilation required
Clicking details will provide a dump of all loaded assemblies in summary form, with links to the full list of the attributes for each assembly
posts.key_insight: Reusable About pages should pull information automatically from assembly metadata, making proper AssemblyInfo population a prerequisite for useful application diagnostics.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that serious software developers should prioritize keyboard usage over mouse input for maximum efficiency. He laments the lack of suitable keyboard options for developers, particularly ergonomic models that maintain standard key layouts for the navigation cluster (PgUp, PgDn, Home, End, arrows). After reviewing several keyboards and finding them all inadequate due to either non-ergonomic designs or 'mangled' key layouts, he settles on his discontinued Microsoft Natural Keyboard Pro. The post invites readers to share their own keyboard preferences and priorities.
key_points: Speed and efficiency in coding come from keyboard mastery, not mouse usage
Modern keyboards frequently ruin the navigation key cluster (PgUp, PgDn, Home, End, arrows) that developers rely on heavily
The combination of ergonomic split design AND standard key layout is nearly impossible to find
Keyboard manufacturers prioritize multimedia functions and 'cool' designs over developer usability
USB hub integration on keyboards is an undervalued feature that has largely disappeared
money_quotes: Speed = keyboard. A mouse? C'mon. That's so teenage girls can pick emoticons in AOL Instant Messenger.
If it doesn't have a keyboard shortcut, it's not worth doing.
There's a disappointing lack of keyboard choice for software developers.
What marketing weasel decided it was a good idea to default map the F1-F12 keys to hip new internet functions?
On some keyboards, the mangling is so profound that the arrow keys are no longer arranged in an inverted T. Sacrilege!
I was surprised how little difference there is between keyboard and mouse usage in Windows XP.
What is aggravating is running into non-Microsoft designed dialogs that have screwed up tab order, or worse yet, areas that are physically impossible to navigate to without the mouse.
posts.key_insight: Keyboard manufacturers optimize for consumer aesthetics and multimedia features at the expense of the standard key layouts that developers actually need for productivity.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood describes using GREP tools to filter through verbose, error-filled log files from a problematic third-party application. He demonstrates both GUI-based PowerGREP and command-line grep, providing practical syntax examples for common use cases. The post details his automated solution using batch files to grep logs, filter results, and email daily error reports. Along the way, he takes sharp jabs at the third-party vendor's code quality, noting they couldn't even implement a 1982 email standard correctly.
key_points: GUI GREP tools like PowerGREP provide accessible ways to apply regular expressions to filesystem searches
Command-line grep requires careful attention to escaping rules on top of regex escaping - using files for complex patterns is easier
A simple three-line batch file (GREP, GREP, BLAT) can create an automated daily error monitoring system
Third-party software quality is often disappointingly poor despite the assumption that vendors should write better code than internal teams
When debugging, sometimes you need to go back to basics like RFC standards from 1982
money_quotes: It's enough to make you almost appreciate UNIX. Almost.
If I wanted slow, buggy, and crash-prone, I would have written it myself.
It's sort of assumed that third party vendors are writing code of higher quality than we do internally. That is, after all, why they're in the software business, and we're not.
If using third party software instills a strong desire to rewrite the application, that's a damning indictment.
When you start typing complicated regular expressions at the command line, beware truly mind-melting command line escaping rules! And that's on top of the Regex escaping rules.
Way to adhere to the very first command in a standard written in 1982, people!
posts.key_insight: Simple Unix tools like grep, combined with automation, can transform an unmanageable debugging situation into a sustainable daily workflow - though dealing with poor third-party code quality remains an eternal frustration.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood surveys the tech landscape of 2005, examining browser performance benchmarks, new networking hardware, and the emerging importance of quiet computing. Browser tests show Opera outperforming Firefox and Internet Explorer across platforms, though compatibility issues persist. The post shifts to discussing gigabit routers becoming available in consumer-friendly all-in-one packages. The main focus becomes CPU efficiency and quiet computing, arguing that most users have more performance than they need and should prioritize low power consumption for silent PCs.
key_points: Opera consistently benchmarks as the fastest browser across Windows, Linux, and Mac, contrary to popular assumptions about Firefox
Firefox is not faster than Internet Explorer except for scripting, though it remains preferable for standards support and security
Gigabit switches integrated into wireless routers finally eliminate the need for separate networking boxes
CPU power consumption directly equates to heat production, making energy-efficient processors essential for quiet computing
The Athlon 64 Winchester and desktop Pentium-M offer the best balance of performance and energy consumption for silent builds
money_quotes: Firefox is not faster than Internet Explorer, except for scripting, but for standards support, security and features, it is a better choice.
Maybe the chief technical officer of Opera should spend less time dissing Microsoft, and more time wondering why Google can't write cross-browser compatible code.
When it comes to CPUs, unless you're doing a lot of video encoding, you probably already have more performance than you need.
The CPU is the #1 power consumer in your PC, by far – on the order of 50 to 100+ watts all by itself – and power consumption directly equates to heat production.
If you want a quiet computer, it's best to start with a CPU that won't undermine your efforts.
The most interesting processors are the ones with the best balance of performance and energy consumption.
posts.key_insight: Raw performance matters less than the balance between speed, power consumption, and noise—a quiet computer requires starting with an efficient CPU rather than trying to cool a power-hungry one.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood discusses a Microsoft MVP who created .NET code capable of defeating MSDN's visual CAPTCHA system. The author demonstrated his crack by comment spamming 94 fellow MVPs' blogs, then issued a half-apologetic, half-boastful explanation. Atwood notes the technique exploits the continuous lines of contrast between CAPTCHA characters and backgrounds. While finding the code technically interesting, Atwood clearly disapproves of the demonstration method, calling the author 'kind of a jerk' for spam-testing on real people's blogs.
key_points: CAPTCHA systems can be defeated programmatically by analyzing visual contrast patterns between characters and backgrounds
The MVP chose to demonstrate his exploit by actually spamming real blogs rather than using a controlled test environment
Security measures like CAPTCHA can provide a 'false sense of security' when they're breakable
There's an ethical dimension to security research - proving a vulnerability exists doesn't require exploiting real users
money_quotes: So, yeah, the author is kind of a jerk.
And he has some problems with capitalization and punctuation, too.
your blog uses CAPTCHA to provide a false sense of security
this is just my way of saying that we've got more work to do
posts.key_insight: Interesting security research can be undermined by poor judgment in how vulnerabilities are demonstrated, and proving you can break something doesn't justify actually breaking it on unsuspecting users.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that regular expressions are essential tools that every developer should learn, while acknowledging they can be taken to absurd extremes. He positions regex as the 'Swiss army knife' of web development, capable of replacing verbose procedural code with concise patterns. However, he draws a clear line between practical uses like phone number validation and the 6.2kb monstrosity required for RFC822 email validation. The post advocates for responsible regex use—keeping patterns simple and maintainable rather than attempting to solve every edge case with increasingly complex expressions.
key_points: Regular expressions replace multiple lines of procedural If..Then blocks with concise, configurable patterns
To the uninitiated, regex syntax appears completely indecipherable—'like Q*Bert on crack'
Most practical regex use cases are prosaic: validating phone numbers, extracting paths, parsing simple patterns
The 6,343-character RFC822 email validation regex exemplifies where regex crosses from use into abuse
All developers should learn basic regex because it produces better, more flexible, more maintainable code
money_quotes: I'm a huge fan of regular expressions; they're the Swiss army knife of web-era development tools.
Why use 6 lines of procedural If..Then blocks to process a string when you can do the same thing in a concise, 20 character Regex pattern?
Regular expressions are complicated. It's a balancing act.
You could say that all regular expressions are WTFs, and I wouldn't necessarily disagree with you.
All developers should learn to use basic regular expressions, because they'll produce better, more flexible, more maintainable code with them.
Used responsibly, regular expressions are a huge net positive.
This is a 6,343 character pattern. How many more characters does it take to reach the limit of sensibility?
I pity the poor developer who has to troubleshoot this behemoth.
posts.key_insight: Regular expressions are powerful tools that every developer should learn, but their power demands restraint—the line between clever and unmaintainable is measured in characters.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents his frustrating experience trying to flash a BIOS, which still requires bootable DOS disks via floppy drive in 2005. He argues that the PC industry's stubborn attachment to the 1.44MB floppy drive is absurd, especially given that Apple abandoned it seven years earlier with the original iMac. While alternatives like bootable CDs and USB flash drives exist, each comes with its own complications and inconsistent BIOS support. The post serves as both a practical guide to USB boot drives and a broader critique of the PC industry's excessive backwards compatibility.
key_points: The floppy drive was never reliable or useful, yet the PC industry continues to include it on every motherboard in 2005
Apple proved floppy drives were dispensable with the 1998 iMac, yet the PC world couldn't follow suit seven years later
Bootable CDs ironically still require floppy disk images, perpetuating the legacy dependency
USB flash drives are the true successor to floppies but suffer from inconsistent BIOS support
Backwards compatibility in the PC industry comes at the cost of user sanity and progress
money_quotes: The floppy drive must die.
If Apple can drop the floppy from the 1998 iMac, why can't the PC industry kill this pernicious thing off seven years later?
The 'depends on the phase of the moon, brand of media, and which computer it was formatted on' unreliability
I guess the price we pay for all this glorious backwards compatibility is sanity.
Elegant, it ain't.
Don't even get me started on PS/2 keyboard and mouse ports.
posts.key_insight: The PC industry's obsession with backwards compatibility perpetuates obsolete technology long past its usefulness, creating unnecessary friction for users who must work around legacy constraints.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents his investigation into why .NET's UnhandledException event doesn't work as documented, and shares Microsoft's response explaining the design rationale. The post reveals that in .NET 1.0/1.1, unhandled exceptions on non-main threads were silently swallowed, causing difficult-to-debug failures. Microsoft's Jonathan Keljo explains that .NET 2.0 will crash the application on any unhandled exception, making debugging significantly easier. Atwood reflects on his own painful debugging experiences caused by the old silent-failure behavior, particularly with drag-and-drop functionality on COM threads.
key_points: The UnhandledException event is a notification mechanism, not an exception handler - registering for it doesn't prevent the exception from being unhandled
In .NET 1.0/1.1, unhandled exceptions on non-main threads were silently swallowed, allowing apps to continue in a broken state
.NET 2.0 changes this behavior so unhandled exceptions on any thread will crash the application
The order of events changed in 2.0: UnhandledException event now fires before debugger attachment
Silent exception swallowing made debugging extremely difficult, as ThreadPool threads would die off without indication
money_quotes: The UnhandledException event is not an unhandled exception 'handler.' Registering for the event, contrary to what the documentation says, does not cause unhandled exceptions to be handled.

In v2.0, an unhandled exception on any thread will take down the application. We've found that it's tremendously easier to debug crashes than it is to debug hangs or the silent-stoppage-of-work problem.
This was generally evil, because what would often happen was, for example, that ThreadPool threads would silently die off, one by one, until your application wasn't actually doing any work.
Figuring out the cause of this kind of failure was nearly impossible.
I remember spending an entire day working on winforms drag and drop problems that were a side effect of this unwanted exception behavior.
posts.key_insight: Crashes are easier to debug than silent failures, making fail-fast behavior a significant improvement in error handling design.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood advocates for three LCD panels as the optimal developer desktop configuration, arguing that falling prices have eliminated cost as a barrier. He demonstrates that three 19-inch panels offer better value than a single large display, providing more screen real estate for less money. The post provides practical guidance on video cards, DVI connections, and software utilities for multi-monitor setups. Atwood predicts that dual monitors will become mainstream for average users within years, drawing a parallel to the inevitable shift toward multi-core CPUs.
key_points: Three 19-inch LCD panels at ~$1000 total provide 3840x1024 resolution, outperforming a single $1200 large display in both price and usable space
The price/performance sweet spot for LCDs is at 19 inches, with exponential price increases for larger panels making them poor value
DVI connections are essential for optimal LCD image quality, providing per-pixel digital crispness that analog VGA cannot match
Windows' poor font scaling makes extremely high-resolution single displays impractical until vector-based rendering arrives
Multi-monitor adoption will follow the same trajectory as multi-core CPUs - horizontal scaling when vertical improvements hit limits
money_quotes: In my opinion, price is no longer a valid reason to choose a less-productive single monitor configuration.
Is that really any match for the effective 3840x1024 you'd get with three 19″ Rosewill panels for $200 less?
Buying large LCDs rarely makes sense because of the exponential increase in price as the size goes up.
Windows is trapped in a bitmapped world. The pitiful, wonky 'Large Fonts' mode just isn't cutting it.
Always choose DVI if you want the best LCD experience.
Sometimes there's nowhere to go but sideways.
Two LCD panels will be a standard configuration for not just developers and power users, but a sizable percentage of mainstream Dell systems.
posts.key_insight: When vertical improvements in display technology hit diminishing returns, the logical path forward is horizontal expansion through multiple monitors - a principle that applies equally to CPUs and developer productivity.

posts.summary: Atwood argues that the PC industry has failed to match Apple's design sensibility, leaving consumers with a wasteland of either hideous or boring case options. He expresses hope that the Mac Mini's success will pressure Taiwanese manufacturers to create more visually interesting small form factor PCs. The ASUS Vento models represent a rare exception worth noting, though they come at a premium price. The post is a brief critique of the PC hardware industry's design apathy.
key_points: The beige box PC industry has ignored Apple's proven success with design-focused hardware
Most Taiwanese PC cases fall into two categories: hideous or boring
The Mac Mini could potentially spur better design from PC manufacturers
Premium pricing ($149) is required to escape the design wasteland
Dell's product design is essentially outsourced to Taiwanese manufacturers
money_quotes: The 'beige box' PC industry has been slow to capitalize on Apple's proven track record of design.
Yes, you can get whatever PC case you want, as long as you want either hideous or boring.
Don't make me bring up the Doggie case again.
'Taiwanese manufacturers' is a synonym for 'Dell's crack team of product designers.'
posts.key_insight: The PC industry's reliance on commodity Taiwanese manufacturing has created a design vacuum where consumers must choose between ugly, boring, or expensive.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that great software requires developers to have genuine ownership over what they build, drawing from his frustrating experiences with offshore projects where he became a middleman rather than a creator. He challenges the popular notion of 'egoless programming' as a fallacy, contending that ego-driven ownership is actually essential for quality work. Using Microsoft as an exemplar, he shows how smart organizations hire talented people and then get out of their way, making each developer 'god' of their domain. The post advocates for 'Open Kimono' management where trust replaces defensive oversight, ultimately concluding that developers should either seize ownership or find a new job.
key_points: Offshoring creates middlemen who have no emotional stake in the project, leading to mediocre software
The concept of 'egoless programming' is a dangerous fallacy - you can no more divorce ego from programmers than from managers
Great organizations hire smart people and get out of their way, giving developers autonomy over their domains
Trust-based 'Open Kimono' management brings out the best in teams by giving them something meaningful to form around
Renters don't take pride in their homes; only homeowners do - the same applies to software projects
money_quotes: I'm a middleman. I have no ownership stake in the project.
Renters don't take pride in their homes. Only homeowners do.
If you do want great software, you have to let the developers own what they're building.

At Microsoft, if you're the Program Manager working on the Excel macro strategy, even if you've been at the company for less than six months, it doesn't matter – you are the GOD of the Excel macro strategy, and nobody, not even employee number 6, is allowed to get in your way. Period.
A person you can't trust with any autonomy is of no use to you.
They're not just getting a job done. They're making sure that the trust that's been placed in them is rewarded.
If you're not working in an environment where ownership is encouraged, either wrest control from the powers that be, or start looking for another job.
posts.key_insight: Great software emerges when developers have genuine ownership and emotional investment in their work, not when they're reduced to middlemen executing someone else's vision.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines how outsourcing and globalization are reshaping software development careers, drawing on Daniel Pink's argument about right-brain versus left-brain work. He argues that routine, rules-based coding work will inevitably move overseas, but creative, communicative aspects of software development will remain valuable. Atwood reflects on how his own work has shifted from solitary programming to constant communication and collaboration. He embraces Uncle Bob's vision of software as craft requiring creativity and elegance rather than raw labor hours. The post concludes that understanding 'why' is more valuable than knowing 'how' - a skill that transcends technological change.
key_points: Routine left-brain work like basic coding is being outsourced, but right-brain creative work remains valuable domestically
Software development has already evolved from solitary programming to constant communication and collaboration
Good software comes from creativity, inventiveness, and drive for elegance - not man-hours and documented processes
Memorizing technical answers won't protect careers; asking good questions is the more durable skill
Understanding 'why' lasts forever while knowing 'how' becomes obsolete in about five years
money_quotes: Now that foreigners can do left-brain work cheaper, we in the US must do right-brain work better.
I can't even remember the last time I spent an entire day doing uninterrupted, solitary programming as I did in the early to mid 90s.
Developing good software is not about man-hours and raw labor; but about creativity, inventiveness, and a drive for elegance and beauty.
Memorizing the answers to difficult technical questions won't save our jobs.
We should be learning how to ask questions instead of answering them.
Asking 'why' is a more valuable – and more right-brained – skillset than merely knowing 'how.'
How lasts about five years, but why is forever.
posts.key_insight: The enduring value in software development lies not in technical knowledge that becomes obsolete, but in the creative, communicative ability to understand and ask 'why' - a fundamentally human skill that cannot be commoditized or outsourced.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents his build of a home theater PC using the Pentium-M processor, which was originally designed for laptops. The key advantage is dramatically lower power consumption compared to desktop Pentium 4 chips while maintaining comparable performance. His system achieves around 50-57 watts of actual power consumption, enabling a nearly silent build with only two fans. He provides a complete parts list and offers practical advice on component selection, particularly emphasizing the importance of quiet hard drives and low-power video cards for HTPC builds.
key_points: The Pentium-M consumes 97% less power under load than a comparable Pentium 4, enabling passive cooling that never exceeds 50°C even under stress testing
Lower power consumption directly correlates with reduced heat and noise, making the P-M ideal for living room use
Video cards are the second largest power consumers after CPUs, making efficient GPU selection critical for quiet builds
Hard drive noise is often overlooked but can dominate system acoustics; soft mounting with sorbothane helps but cannot fix a fundamentally noisy drive
The complete system draws only 72-81 watts from the wall while performing like a Pentium 4 2.6GHz
money_quotes: There's a direct correlation between power consumption and heat/noise. The cooler a system runs, the quieter it will be.
Try that with a Pentium 4 and you'll create your own personal China Syndrome.
The large, fanless Alpha heatsink never exceeded 50c under Prime95 torture test load, even when left running overnight.
Pretty amazing for a fully loaded 1.7ghz PC that performs like a Pentium 4 2.6ghz.
Can you get a faster card? Sure, at double or triple the power budget.
Buy a quiet hard drive or you'll regret it.
posts.key_insight: Repurposing mobile processors for desktop HTPC use delivers the best balance of performance, power efficiency, and noise reduction because laptop chips are engineered to do more with less.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood addresses a common workplace annoyance: coworkers who remote into Windows 2000 servers but never log out, blocking others due to Microsoft's two-user Terminal Services limit. He discovers the Windows Server 2003 Administration Tools Pack, a free download that provides numerous server management tools for XP users. The key tool he needed was Terminal Services Manager, which allows him to remotely terminate idle sessions. The post is a quick tip-sharing piece typical of early tech blogs, documenting a practical solution to a daily friction point.
key_points: Microsoft's Terminal Services had a two-user administrative limit that created bottlenecks when colleagues didn't log out
The Windows Server 2003 Administration Tools Pack provides remote management capabilities for Windows 2000 and 2003 servers from XP
Terminal Services Manager specifically allows terminating idle remote desktop sessions
The tools are free and install under Start > Programs > Administrative Tools
money_quotes: Some of my coworkers have an annoying habit of remoting into our Win2k servers and never logging out.
They also like to do this in pairs, which means nobody else can remote into the machines.
Sometimes it's just faster to manipulate the GUI via Remote Desktop.
All this for the low, low price of nothing.
The only part I care about is the Terminal Services Manager, which lets me terminate idle remote desktop sessions from target servers. Take that coworkers!
posts.key_insight: When dealing with workplace friction from shared resources, dig into the available administrative tools—Microsoft often provides free solutions buried in downloadable tool packs.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood celebrates an ingenious iPod hack where a reverse engineer solved a chicken-and-egg problem by outputting the device's firmware as sound through its piezoelectric element, essentially creating an acoustic modem. The hacker couldn't access the iPod's I/O hardware without studying the firmware, but couldn't extract the firmware without understanding the I/O. By encoding data as audio and recording it with a microphone in a padded box for eight hours, he successfully extracted the code needed to advance the iPodLinux project. Atwood draws a parallel to DirecTV's clever 2001 counter-hack that destroyed 100,000 pirate smart cards.
key_points: A reverse engineer solved an impossible-seeming bootstrap problem by converting firmware data into sound waves captured by microphone
The hack required creating a custom modulation scheme and letting the iPod transmit for eight hours in a sound-isolated box
The iPodLinux project needed the firmware to understand how to write software that properly interfaces with iPod hardware
Atwood admires hacks that demonstrate creative problem-solving when conventional approaches are blocked
The DirecTV counter-hack comparison shows appreciation for technical cleverness regardless of which 'side' deploys it
money_quotes: His ingenious solution was to use someone else's technique for making the iPod squawk and squeak, in order to write a program that output the firmware as a series of sounds
In effect, he turned the iPod and microphone system into an acoustic modem, and wrote his own modulation scheme for representing data as sound
To protect against background noise, he had to put the iPod and the microphone together inside a padded box, and let them sit for eight hours
Seems quite impossible without any knowledge about the IO-Hardware but I found a solution
To add a little pizzazz to the operation, DirecTV personally 'signed' the anti-hacker attack. The first 8 computer bytes of all hacked cards were rewritten to read 'GAME OVER.'
posts.key_insight: The most elegant hacks find unexpected pathways around seemingly impossible constraints by creatively repurposing available resources in ways their designers never intended.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores why large software projects fail at alarming rates, citing Watts Humphrey's analysis showing that while half of small projects succeed, none of the largest ones do. He argues that the solution lies in trusting and empowering technical professionals to manage themselves, while maintaining genuine oversight rather than autocratic control. However, he emphasizes that trust isn't a substitute for real management—it requires investment, personal motivation, and thoughtful team formation. The post critiques superficial management shortcuts like Microsoft's Shipit award, which actually undermined employee motivation by replacing genuine recognition with hollow gestures. Drawing on DeMarco and Lister's concept of 'measuring with your eyes closed,' Atwood argues that performance data should be used for individual self-assessment rather than punitive action.
key_points: Large software projects have dramatically higher failure rates than small ones, with Standish data showing zero success for the largest projects
Trusting and empowering technical professionals leads to extraordinary work, but it cannot be blind trust—genuine oversight is still required
