Performance measurement data should benefit individuals through self-assessment, not be used for punishment or promotion by management
Simplistic extrinsic motivators like awards are managerial copouts that excuse managers from harder work like investment, direct personal motivation, and thoughtful team formation
Rewarding team members differentially fosters competition that can harm team cohesion
money_quotes: If you trust and empower your software and other high-technology professionals to manage themselves, they will do extraordinary work.
Half of the smallest projects succeeded, while none of the largest projects did.

There's no question that performance reviews can have terrible effects, but what's the alternative? Give everyone a pat on the head, say 'nice work' and send them off to a nap with some warm milk and cookies?
Managing people is unbelievably difficult. Getting code to compile and pass all your unit tests? Piece of cake. Getting your team to work together? That's another matter entirely.
Management didn't even know that employees were already motivated for shipping software.
Facile shortcuts like the Shipit award undermine this relationship.
Any action that rewards team members differentially is likely to foster competition.
posts.key_insight: Effective software project management requires genuine trust and empowerment of technical professionals, but trust is an investment that demands real engagement—not a shortcut that excuses managers from the hard work of motivation and team building.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that development techniques appropriate for low-level framework and OS developers are often inappropriate for the vast majority of application developers. He introduces the concept of a 'Development Pyramid' where dozens of kernel developers support hundreds of OS developers, who support thousands of framework developers, who in turn support millions of application developers. The key risk he identifies is 'delusions of grandeur' - developers thinking they operate at a higher level of the pyramid than they actually do. For most developers building business applications, optimizing for development time (using a simple regex) beats optimizing for processor time (writing custom character-matching code).
key_points: The development techniques that make sense for framework developers (optimizing for performance) rarely make sense for application developers (optimizing for development time)
The 'Development Pyramid' has exponentially more developers at each level: dozens at kernel, hundreds at OS, thousands at framework, millions at application level
The biggest risk is 'delusions of grandeur' - choosing techniques that don't match your actual position in the pyramid
Learning low-level C programming may be less relevant than learning to work effectively with modern frameworks
For business application developers, readable one-line solutions beat optimized multi-line implementations
money_quotes: Since when are we writing an operating system?
Everyone likes to think that they're working on something fantastically complicated that will be used by millions of developers, but reality is... somewhat less exciting.
The real risk here is delusions of grandeur: thinking that you're working at a higher level of the pyramid than you actually are, and choosing techniques that don't make sense for your project.
Users who are far more concerned with leaving at 5pm every day than they are with the incomparable thrill of using yet another version of GenericBusinessApp.

A Regex that uses one line of code to validate 'letter,number,letter,number,letter,number' may not be faster in processor time, but it's certainly faster in development time – and for us, that wins every time.

Software development has made a steady progression from code to libraries to components to frameworks. Thankfully, you don't need to retrace this path just as you don't need to experience the dark ages to live in the post-renaissance world.
posts.key_insight: Most developers should optimize for development time and code clarity rather than raw performance, because they're building business applications, not operating systems or frameworks.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines various approaches to interviewing software developers, drawing from industry experts and his own experience. He critiques Joel Spolsky's interview methodology, particularly disagreeing with low-level C coding tests and abstract puzzle questions as outdated or irrelevant. Atwood highlights the importance of hiring interns as a way to capture talented developers before they leave the job market. He proposes his own interview approach: having candidates give a 20-minute presentation on their area of expertise, which he argues reveals passion, communication skills, and cultural fit more effectively than traditional interviews.
key_points: Traditional puzzle questions and low-level C coding tests are outdated interview techniques that don't reflect modern development work
The 'top 0.5%' hiring claim is misleading because great developers rarely appear on the open job market
Intern programs are a strategic way to identify and recruit talented developers early in their careers
A candidate presentation reveals passion, communication ability, expertise depth, and team compatibility better than traditional interviews
The people you work with matter more than the jobs themselves
money_quotes: I just can't muster any enthusiasm for completely random arbitrary problems in the face of so many actual problems.
It's pretty clear to me that just because you're hiring the top 0.5% of all applicants for a job, doesn't mean you're hiring the top 0.5% of all software developers.
In fact we hunt down the smart CS students and individually beg them to apply for an internship with us, because if you wait around to see who sends you a resume, you're already missing out.
I've worked with a few interns who were amazing developers. It's a bit like playing the slots, but when you hit the jackpot, you win big.
Who are they trying to hire, Will Shortz?
Jobs may come and go, but it's the people I've worked with that I always remember.
posts.key_insight: The best way to evaluate a developer is to have them present on their expertise, revealing passion and communication skills that traditional technical interviews miss.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood delivers a scathing critique of enterprise software based on his four years at a Fortune 50 company, arguing that 'enterprise' has become synonymous with 'crappy.' He observes that enterprise software vendors profit from a perverse business model where bugs in expensive products create demand for costly professional services to fix them. The post highlights how organizations become trapped by customizations, making switching costs prohibitive and forcing continued investment in mediocre products. Atwood concludes that 'enterprise' should be considered an insult rather than a badge of quality.
key_points: Enterprise software exhibits a linear relationship between price and bug count—more expensive means more buggy
The enterprise business model profits twice: first from selling buggy software, then from charging to fix it via 'professional services'
One-size-fits-all enterprise solutions are an illusion that drives sales but inevitably requires extensive customization
Heavy customization creates lock-in, making the cost of switching to competitors prohibitively expensive
Organizations get trapped in cycles of upgrading the 'devil you know' hoping the next version might be better
money_quotes: The term 'Enterprise,' as applied to software, is synonymous with 'crappy.'

The rampant bugs in their Enterprise Software – which we already paid millions for – force us to contract with the 'professional services' arm of said company, who then get to work busily fixing the bugs in their own product for $100/hour.
It's as if the salespeople watched one too many Warner Brothers cartoons, and they're expecting to get another delivery from ACME Corporation any minute now.
The idea of a one-size-fits-all software package – particularly one of this magnitude – is laughable.
It's easy to get sweet-talked into yet another version of the devil you know, on the off chance that hey – maybe this year it might not suck. As much.
The word 'Enterprise' is now so tainted that it's best used as an epithet.
Dude, your software sucks so much, it's enterprise software.
posts.key_insight: Enterprise software vendors have built a business model where product defects are a feature, not a bug—they profit first from selling expensive, buggy software, then from charging to fix it.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the RegexOptions.Compiled flag in .NET, which he had been using mindlessly assuming it would improve performance. He discovers that compilation trades an order of magnitude slower startup time for only 30% better runtime performance, while also consuming significant memory that can't be reclaimed. The alternative Regex.CompileToAssembly avoids dynamic compilation pitfalls but locks expressions in stone. Atwood concludes that unless you're in tight loops or processing huge strings, the tradeoffs rarely justify using compiled regex.
key_points: RegexOptions.Compiled takes 10x longer to start up but only yields 30% better runtime performance
The regular expression engine already caches all expressions in memory, so non-compiled regex avoids reparsing overhead automatically
Compiled regex uses Reflection.Emit which loads lots of code and memory that can't be freed (fixed in Whidbey)
Regex.CompileToAssembly avoids dynamic compilation pitfalls but makes expressions unchangeable at runtime
Performance optimizations that sound good on paper often have hidden costs that outweigh the benefits
money_quotes: It says 'compiled' so it must be faster, right? Well, like so many other things, that depends.
Compilation takes about an order of magnitude longer to startup, but yields 30% better runtime performance.
You should only use this mode for a finite set of expressions which you know will be used repeatedly.
The second time you build your non-compiled Regex, no additional interpreting overhead is incurred. And you get that for free.
Even though it sounds faster and all, you probably don't want to use RegexOptions.Compiled.
Only 30% faster? I'd want some actual benchmark numbers from my application before I could justify the loss of flexibility and the additional file dependency.
posts.key_insight: Performance flags that sound impressive often have hidden costs that outweigh their benefits—always measure before optimizing and understand the tradeoffs you're making.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts his journey from recommending Notepad2 as a Windows Notepad replacement to discovering its limitations with large files. After six months of use, performance issues with files over a few megabytes forced him to evaluate commercial alternatives. He settles on EditPad Pro for its speed with large files, excellent regex support, and lightweight approach that doesn't try to be an IDE. The post serves as both a practical tool recommendation and a reflection on how real-world usage reveals software limitations that initial enthusiasm misses.
key_points: Notepad2's performance degrades severely on files larger than a few megabytes due to Scintilla engine limitations
A basic text editor should handle files in the 5MB-100MB range without issues
The author's key criteria: large file handling, lightweight operation, extensive regex support, and not being a full IDE
EditPad Pro wins for its regex-centric design from the author of RegexBuddy and PowerGREP
Six months of daily use is needed to form an informed opinion about a tool
money_quotes: What good is Notepad doing anyone these days?
I don't think it's unreasonable to ask a basic text editing app to have decent performance on largish text files in the 5mb - 100mb range.
I felt guilty emailing Florian with questions since he was already providing such an excellent bit of software completely gratis.
I won't even pretend that I lived with these applications long enough to have an informed opinion about which one is 'best.' I didn't.
I don't need another full-blown IDE. I have Visual Studio for that.
I loves me some Regex.
Regex-y minds think alike.
posts.key_insight: The true test of any tool is sustained daily use, and enthusiasm for free software shouldn't prevent you from paying for something that actually meets your needs.

posts.summary: Atwood argues that software development fails not from lack of planning but from too much of it, especially when tackling 'wicked problems' that can't be fully understood without building something. He uses a classic joke about developers and managers to illustrate the communication gap between technical accuracy and practical usefulness. The post challenges the common management response to failed projects—adding more documentation and analysis—arguing this approach backfires for inherently uncertain problems. Instead, Atwood advocates for iterative development processes like Scrum that embrace change and put solutions in front of customers quickly.
key_points: Software engineering often addresses 'wicked problems' that can't be fully understood without actually building the software
Too much upfront planning kills projects more often than writing code does
Management's instinct to add more requirements documentation and analysis after failures makes wicked problems worse, not better
The communication gap between developers (technically correct but unhelpful) and managers (lost but expecting solutions) is a fundamental tension
Iterative development with customer feedback is the only practical approach when requirements can't be fully articulated upfront
money_quotes: Writing code doesn't kill projects: too much planning does!
The correct answer, of course, is that there is no correct answer.
You don't know where you are or where you are going, but you expect me to be able to help. You're in the same position you were before we met but now it's my fault.
Attempting to baseline requirements and then use an analytical approach to reach a solution is a recipe for disaster with wicked problems.
The customer really needed a tire swing but couldn't articulate that.
Since we're software developers, not mind readers, the only answer is to quickly put a solution in front of the customer and keep evolving that solution based on real usage.
Classic project management practices simply do not work for wicked projects.
posts.key_insight: Software projects fail not from insufficient planning but from applying rigid, analytical approaches to inherently uncertain problems that can only be understood through iterative building and customer feedback.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood presents a custom TraceListener implementation that provides IIS-style cyclic logging for .NET console applications. The solution leverages the built-in Trace infrastructure to automatically log application output without requiring console redirection. By using Trace.WriteLine instead of Console.WriteLine, applications can log their own output transparently. The implementation supports configurable file size thresholds, file count limits, timestamps, and process/thread IDs, all controllable via code or config files.
key_points: Custom TraceListener enables applications to be responsible for logging their own output rather than relying on external console redirection
The cyclic logging approach automatically manages log file rotation based on size and count thresholds
Configuration can be done either in code or declaratively through the app.config file's System.Diagnostics section
Log entries include timestamps, process/thread IDs, and calling method information for debugging context
Using Trace.WriteLine instead of Console.WriteLine provides logging behavior 'for free' with post-deployment configurability
money_quotes: I wanted the app to be responsible for logging its own output.
The neat thing is that we get this behavior for free – as long as I use Trace.WriteLine instead of Console.WriteLine, my console app logs its own output.
I can easily modify the logging behavior post-deployment by editing the .config file.
posts.key_insight: Building on .NET's TraceListener infrastructure allows applications to transparently manage their own logging with zero runtime overhead when disabled and full configurability when needed.

posts.summary: This brief post shares two Microsoft reference resources: a comparative list of programming language equivalents across VB, J#, C++, C#, JScript, and Visual FoxPro, and a Win32 to .NET API mapping guide. Atwood positions these as practical tools for developers working across Microsoft's language ecosystem. The post includes a tongue-in-cheek reference to the debate about whether .NET is truly a 'thin wrapper' over Win32.
key_points: Microsoft provided official documentation comparing syntax across their supported languages
The Win32 to .NET API map was a useful resource for developers transitioning from native to managed code
The 'thin wrapper' characterization of .NET over Win32 was a common but debated simplification
money_quotes: Since .NET is just a thin wrapper over Win32 (or so I've been told)
posts.key_insight: Cross-language reference materials were essential tools for developers navigating Microsoft's diverse language ecosystem in the early .NET era.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his conversion to managed code advocacy after the NIMDA worm devastated his organization in 2001, demonstrating how buffer overrun vulnerabilities in native code can cause catastrophic real-world damage. He examines the Quake II .NET port as a benchmark for managed code performance, finding that managed code runs at about 85% the speed of optimized native C++ code. The post argues that this performance tradeoff is entirely worthwhile given the security benefits of eliminating entire classes of vulnerabilities. Atwood closes with an appreciation for Mike Abrash, the legendary assembly programmer behind Quake's software rasterizer, praising his technical writing abilities.
key_points: The NIMDA worm exploiting IIS buffer overruns was a watershed moment that demonstrated the real cost of memory-unsafe languages
Managed Quake II benchmarks show .NET code performing at 85% of native C++ speed, and about 70% of hand-optimized assembly
In applications using 3D hardware acceleration, the performance gap between managed and native code becomes even smaller since the GPU is the bottleneck
Managed code benchmarks don't capture real-world issues like frame drops from garbage collection pauses
A 15% performance penalty is a reasonable trade for eliminating buffer overrun vulnerabilities entirely
money_quotes: It felt like a natural disaster without the 'natural' part – the first notable port 80 IIS buffer overrun exploit.
After surveying the carnage first hand, I immediately saw the benefit of languages where buffer overruns weren't even possible.
I'll gladly trade 15 percent of performance to live in a world where NIMDA can't exist. That's a no-brainer.
The techniques may be obsolete, but the problem solving he describes so compellingly is truly timeless.
In apps/demos/games that use 3D acceleration, expect the difference between managed and unmanaged code to be even smaller, as the bottleneck of rendering is the 3D card, not the CPU.
Our codegen is generating code with about 70% of the performance of the original hand optimized assembly. I personally think this is great.
posts.key_insight: Security vulnerabilities caused by memory-unsafe code exact such enormous real-world costs that trading 15% of raw performance for memory safety is an obviously worthwhile bargain.

posts.summary: This brief post serves as a curated index of Dr. Dotnetsky's Cool .NET Tips and Tricks series, a collection Jeff Atwood found valuable through frequent Google searches. Atwood highlights Peter Bromberg as a recurring name in .NET-related search results and recommends his alter ego's technical articles on Egghead Cafe. The post is essentially a resource compilation with light humor about missing article numbers. It was later updated when a reader found the missing issues.
key_points: Recurring expert names in search results signal reliable technical resources
Curated link collections were valuable for knowledge sharing in early blogging
Community corrections improve technical documentation over time
money_quotes: You always notice the names that appear frequently in your code related Google searches.
There are some great articles there, but the pick of the litter are the ones by Peter's alter ego, Dr. Dotnetsky.
OK, I know you're wondering: what the hell happened to issues 3, 6, 10, 13, and 15? I wish I knew. Maybe it has something to do with those drink recipes.
posts.key_insight: Developers build trust in technical resources by noticing which expert names consistently appear in their search results.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that VB.NET is experiencing a severe decline in adoption, with Visual Basic developers either refusing to upgrade or switching to C# instead. He points to survey data showing abysmal adoption rates and notes the irony that even VB.NET defenders admit to preferring C#. Atwood contends that Visual Basic's strength was always its massive developer community and ecosystem, not the language itself. With that herd advantage now belonging to C#, VB.NET has lost its core value proposition. The pragmatic developers who chose VB for its ecosystem benefits are now pragmatically choosing C# for the same reasons.
key_points: VB.NET defenders ironically undermine their case by admitting they prefer and use C# themselves
Survey data shows 43% of VB developers planned to reduce usage, with more migrating to C# than to VB.NET
Visual Basic's value was never about being the best language but about having the largest ecosystem and community
The network effects that made VB productive (third-party tools, code samples, libraries) now favor C#
Choosing VB.NET has become an unusual choice, which fundamentally contradicts the pragmatic ethos of VB developers
money_quotes: VB.NET is so awesome of an upgrade, in fact, that these noble defenders of the faith chose not to use it.
Either VB developers are choosing not to adopt VB.NET at all, or they're making an opportunistic switch to C#. In droves.
We'd have to be the crappiest mature independent thinkers ever if the best 'radical' language choice we could come up with was VB.NET.
We use VB because we want to be part of the herd.
VB developers are smart: they realize that moving with the herd confers certain advantages.
Choosing VB.NET, in the current state of the .NET world, is unusual. And that's fundamentally at odds with the design ethic and history of the language.
Code samples. Enterprise library. Open-source projects. You name it, it's easier, faster, and better in the language that the rest of the herd is using.
posts.key_insight: A programming language's value lies not in its intrinsic qualities but in its ecosystem and community size, and when those network effects shift to a competitor, pragmatic developers will follow the herd.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces improvements to his Logging TraceListener utility, adding date-based file aging alongside the existing size-based aging. The update introduces a templated filename system using String.Format codes, separate properties for specifying units of scale, and sensible defaults. He demonstrates the configuration both via .config files and programmatic creation, showing how he uses it to separate verbose HTML tracing from regular console output.
key_points: Files can now age by date in addition to size, with configurable units (kb, mb, gb, weeks, months)
Filename templating now uses a single FileNameTemplate property with String.Format codes for flexibility
The utility allows combining age, number, and size limits to control log file proliferation
Separating noisy trace output (like HTML) into dedicated log files improves signal-to-noise ratio
money_quotes: Including them with the regular console output is way too noisy.
With the configuration specified above, no more than 3 logs will ever exist, and no more than 3 per month, and never more than 512kb or 1mb in size.
posts.key_insight: Good logging infrastructure should provide flexible controls over file rotation by size, age, and count to manage disk space while maintaining useful diagnostic information.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that 'inspired laziness' is a valuable trait for productive software developers. He distinguishes between productive laziness—which involves strategic elimination of unnecessary work, managing interruptions, and automating repetitive tasks—and simple sloth. The post advocates for turning off email and IM notifications to protect focus time, quickly discarding inferior solutions rather than exhaustively researching all options, and letting machines handle repetitive work. Atwood concludes that if he ran a software company, he would seek out the laziest developers he could afford, because this type of laziness ultimately makes everyone's work easier.
key_points: Constant notifications from email and instant messaging are productivity killers that prevent developers from entering flow states
Choosing what NOT to work on is as important as choosing what to work on—quickly discarding inferior approaches saves enormous time
Interruption-driven work may suit management but is poison for software development
Truly lazy developers automate problems away permanently rather than solving them repeatedly
There's a crucial distinction between 'inspired laziness' that improves everyone's lives and simply not doing work
money_quotes: If you like getting work done, you learn to appreciate inspired laziness as the positive character trait it really is.
Choosing what not to work on is just as important – and arguably more important – than choosing what to work on.
It's a lot faster to download code than it is to write it.
For some fields, like management, interruption is how things get done. But it's poison for software development.
Truly lazy developers let their machines do the work for them.
Smart developers know that people don't scale – machines do.
If you want it done the same way every time, and with any semblance of reliability, you want the human factor removed as much as is reasonably possible.
If I was running a software company, I'd endeavor to hire the laziest people I could afford.
posts.key_insight: The best developers are productively lazy—they ruthlessly eliminate unnecessary work, protect their focus from interruptions, and automate problems away permanently rather than solving them repeatedly.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores creative physical feedback devices for continuous integration monitoring, building on the Java Lava Lamp concept and Ambient Orb approaches. He argues that making build status visible through ambient devices increases team awareness and accountability. The post introduces his own project using a BetaBrite LED sign, which offers more flexibility than novelty indicators with full text, colors, and animations. Atwood suggests that highly visible, fun status indicators transform build failures from ignorable notifications into team-wide events that demand immediate attention.
key_points: Physical ambient feedback devices make build status impossible to ignore compared to emails or web dashboards
The social pressure of visible build failures ('who broke it?') drives faster fixes and better accountability
LED signs offer advantages over lava lamps and orbs by displaying actual text information, not just colors
Continuous integration monitoring is just one application; real-time website stats, errors, and checkins could all benefit from ambient displays
Making metrics fun and visible changes team culture around code quality
money_quotes: A successful build (all tests pass) turns on the green lava lamp, and a failed build (or failed tests) turns on the red one.
Something that I started as something of a joke (it was April 1st after all) actually turned out to be a very useful tool in more ways than one.
Now, when the build breaks and the Orb pulses red, it's like a fire alarm around here. The first question out of everyone's mouth is 'who broke it?'

Because we continuously integrate our code and the automated build potentially runs every 15 minutes, determining what caused the failure is as simple as looking at what has been checked-in since the last successful build.
Since we consider the failure of a single unit test to be a failure of the entire build, if one test fails, the Orb pulses red.
This thing, for my money (and it was my money, for the record) beats the heck out of retro-kitsch novelty status indicators.
I have yet to see someone walk by my desk who isn't mesmerized by its hypnotic animation and colors.
This could just be that extra bit of cowbell your project needs to succeed.
posts.key_insight: Making build status physically visible and impossible to ignore transforms continuous integration from a background process into a team accountability mechanism.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that API and developer tool usability should be approached with the same rigor as end-user usability testing. Drawing on Microsoft researcher Steven Clarke's usability studies, he demonstrates that developers face the same cognitive challenges as regular users when learning new systems. The post highlights how observation-based usability testing revealed problems with .NET APIs that weren't obvious to their creators. Atwood emphasizes that while good code samples are crucial for developer experience, they shouldn't be used to paper over fundamentally flawed API designs. The core message is that when developers struggle with your API, the problem likely lies in your design, not in their competence.
key_points: Developer usability testing uses the same methods as end-user testing: personas, use cases, tasks, and observation
Code samples are often the first thing developers look for and set the tone for their entire experience with a new technology
Good documentation cannot compensate for APIs that expose abstractions at the wrong level
When developers can't find what they're looking for in docs, it may be because the expected abstraction simply doesn't exist in the API
If you're constantly answering questions about code you've written, the problem is likely your design, not your colleagues
money_quotes: When it comes to usability, there's no substitute for observation. It's absolute bedrock.
The reason the developers weren't successful when they were searching through the help was because what they were looking for simply didn't exist.
If you're answering a lot of questions from other developers about something you've written, it's probably not because your co-workers suck.
The API they were working with exposed a set of abstractions that were at the wrong level for these particular developers.
You have to be careful that you're not papering over bad API design decisions with good code samples.
posts.key_insight: APIs deserve the same usability testing rigor as consumer products, and frequent developer questions about your code signal design problems, not user incompetence.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that after their brains, a developer's most important asset is their rear end—specifically, the quality of the chair they sit in all day. He traces the cultural history of the Herman Miller Aeron chair from dot-com status symbol to bankruptcy auction fixture, while maintaining that it remains a genuinely good product. Drawing from personal experience with both the Aeron and Steelcase Leap, he concludes that developers shouldn't expect decent seating for less than $500. The post advocates treating a quality chair as a professional investment rather than a luxury expense.
key_points: Quality seating directly impacts developer productivity over an 8-hour workday
The Aeron chair became both a symbol of dot-com success and a casualty of its implosion, flooding the market through bankruptcy auctions
The realistic price floor for a decent ergonomic chair is around $500
A good chair should be viewed as a career investment, worth buying yourself if your employer won't provide one
Personal experience matters—Atwood used the same Aeron for years despite its cultural baggage
money_quotes: But what about our second most important asset – our rear ends?
You can't expect to get a decent chair for less than $500.
If you are spending less than that on seating – unless you are getting the deal of the century on dot-bomb bankruptcy auctions – you're probably making a mistake.
A good chair is absolutely one of the best investments you can make as a professional software developer.
As a developer, demand good seating, because it's necessary for productive work.
When Quokka shut its doors, it left behind hundreds of hastily abandoned Aeron chairs, clustered in the corners of the lifeless offices like refugees.
posts.key_insight: Investing $500+ in quality seating is one of the highest-ROI career decisions a developer can make, yet most undervalue this basic productivity multiplier.

posts.summary: Atwood reviews resources critiquing CAPTCHA implementations, noting a French developer's systematic analysis of visual CAPTCHA weaknesses and a W3C presentation arguing against CAPTCHAs. He finds irony in that the most secure CAPTCHAs are sometimes unsolvable by humans. While acknowledging valid accessibility concerns, he defends CAPTCHAs as pragmatically effective based on their widespread adoption.
key_points: The most secure CAPTCHAs can become so distorted they're not always human-solvable, defeating their purpose
CAPTCHA prevalence across the web is practical evidence of their effectiveness against spam
Accessibility remains a legitimate criticism of visual CAPTCHA systems
Security and usability exist in tension - making CAPTCHAs harder for bots also makes them harder for humans
money_quotes: Some of the "best" CAPTCHAs have this comment attached to them: not always human-solvable.
Their increasing prevalence is ample proof that they actually work.
If nothing else, they raise the bar much, much higher for spambots.
posts.key_insight: The widespread adoption of CAPTCHAs demonstrates their practical value despite valid criticisms about accessibility and the ironic problem of becoming too difficult for humans to solve.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on how Internet Explorer 6, once the dominant browser, has suddenly become the new pariah of web development—much like Netscape 4.7x before it. The IE7 announcement, rather than offering hope, has paradoxically highlighted how stagnant IE6 has become. Atwood draws a parallel to Visual SourceSafe's similar abandonment and decline. He criticizes Microsoft's decision to halt IE development for over three years, calling it a fundamental disregard for users. The post serves as both a critique of corporate complacency and a cautionary tale about abandoning core products.
key_points: IE6 has undergone a sudden perception shift from dominant browser to the one everyone wishes would disappear
Microsoft's three-year development freeze on a product used daily by millions represents a fundamental business failure
The IE7 announcement paradoxically made IE6's situation feel worse rather than better
Visual SourceSafe suffered the same fate of abandonment and became synonymous with what not to do
Even monopolies cannot escape the consequences of neglecting their core products
money_quotes: It's like we woke up one day, and IE6 had transformed overnight into the browser that we all wish would go away.
IE6 is the new Netscape 4.7x.
Somehow the IE7 announcement, which should be a glimmer of hope, is, paradoxically, making the situation worse.
That's possibly the ultimate WTF.
I don't care how big a monopoly you are. Abandoning development on core products is bad business and demonstrates near-total disregard for your customers.
SourceSafe is now basically synonymous with what not to do in source control.
posts.key_insight: Abandoning development on widely-used products, regardless of market dominance, inevitably transforms them from industry standards into cautionary tales.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that XCopy deployment—installing software by simply copying files to a folder—represents the gold standard for .NET application architecture. He contrasts this with the dependency hell of VB6, where OCX controls and registry entries made deployment nightmarish. While acknowledging that users still expect traditional installers for conveniences like Start menu icons and Add/Remove Programs integration, he maintains that developers should always minimize dependencies and work toward the simplest possible deployment model.
key_points: XCopy deployment was impossible in VB6 due to complex OCX and runtime dependencies, but .NET finally enables it
Projects that can't support XCopy deployment should be examined for architectural problems
Users aren't ready for XCopy deployment—they expect installers to handle icons, shortcuts, and program registration
Keeping project dependencies minimal should be a constant goal, even if full XCopy deployment isn't practical
The ability to run from a USB drive is a good litmus test for dependency management
money_quotes: XCopy is the gold standard for correctly architected .NET software projects.
If the design of your .NET project precludes XCopy deployment, take a long, hard look at what you're doing.
Don't just treat the symptoms of the disease.
I'm not sure users are ready for XCopy deployment. Do we really expect users to be able to unzip an archive to a folder?
Honestly, do you really think your program would end up in the Program Files folder if you left it up to the user? I'll tell you where it would go – on the desktop.
The installer is a distraction, but a necessary one for users' sanity.
Can your app run from a USB keychain?
posts.key_insight: The complexity of your deployment process is a direct reflection of your architectural decisions—strive for XCopy simplicity even when you need an installer for user convenience.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces the completion of his .NET API for controlling BetaBrite LED signs, which he previously mentioned developing for automated continuous integration feedback. The API wraps the Alpha Sign Communications Protocol and communicates via RS-232 serial connection. He has published the code as an article on CodeProject and provides a brief VB.NET code sample demonstrating how to allocate memory, set text with transitions, and control the sign's display sequence.
key_points: The API provides a simple .NET wrapper around the Alpha Sign Communications Protocol for BetaBrite LED signs
Physical hardware requirements include a serial port or USB-to-Serial adapter with a 25-50 foot RS-232 to RJ-12 cable
The code demonstrates memory allocation, text setting with various transitions (Rotate, WipeLeft, RollDown), and sequencing capabilities
money_quotes: Now go get you some of that sweet, hot LED sign action!
posts.key_insight: Physical computing projects like LED sign integration can be made accessible through clean API abstractions that hide the complexity of serial communication protocols.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his enthusiasm for regular expressions through a practical example of converting relative URLs to absolute URLs in HTML. He presents a VB.NET function that uses multiple regex patterns to handle various URL formats including href attributes, src attributes, and CSS @import statements. The post advocates for pretty-printing regular expressions using whitespace to improve readability, similar to how developers format SQL. Atwood notes the lack of tools for formatting regex compared to SQL formatting tools, and closes with a trivia note about ASP.NET's parser using regular expressions internally.
key_points: Regular expressions are invaluable for practical daily coding tasks like URL manipulation in HTML
Pretty-printing regex with whitespace (RegexOptions.IgnorePatternWhitespace) improves readability significantly
The tooling ecosystem for formatting regex lags behind SQL formatting tools
Complex URL conversion requires handling multiple cases: root-relative paths, folder-relative paths, and CSS imports
Even core frameworks like ASP.NET rely heavily on regular expressions internally
money_quotes: I'm continually amazed how useful regular expressions are in my daily coding.
We do this all the time with SQL. I can think of a half-dozen tools that will block of SQL and pretty format it – but I am not aware of any Regex tools that offer this functionality.
Did you know that the ASP.NET page parser uses regular expressions?
posts.key_insight: Regular expressions deserve the same readability practices and tooling support that we give to SQL, including pretty-printing and formatting tools.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that TreeView controls are fundamentally flawed UI components that developers overuse despite being unsuitable for most users. He outlines four key problems: rigid hierarchy requirements, difficult navigation, the expertise needed for proper categorization, and implied ownership relationships. Atwood advocates for flatter, more flexible data representations like Gmail's label system, which allows items to belong to multiple categories simultaneously. The post reflects his broader philosophy that programmers' preference for strict hierarchies doesn't align with how regular users think about organizing information.
key_points: TreeViews force rigid hierarchies that don't reflect how people naturally organize things, illustrated by the Gomer Pyle 'animal, vegetable, mineral' example
Tree navigation is frustrating due to constant expanding/collapsing, loss of context, and awkward scrolling in both directions
Proper categorization requires expertise in library science and information mapping that average users don't possess
Gmail's label system demonstrates a superior alternative by allowing items to have multiple classifications rather than forcing single-folder decisions
Developers are biased toward rigid structures but should design for users who prefer simple, flat lists
money_quotes: Trees aren't usable. They're a pain in the ass.
They may be necessary for developers who are forced to work in the strict, rigid world of software development, but they are unnatural, restrictive, and just plain unnecessary for average users.
Rigid categorization may seem like a good idea, but it doesn't work very well in practice.
Good luck finding anything in a tree; it's a navigational nightmare.
Categorization is extraordinarily difficult to do correctly unless you're an expert in the field. And even then, there is disagreement.
A flatter, less rigid representation of the data is almost always possible – and much easier for users to understand and manipulate.
Programmers may love rigidity – to each item its appropriate folder and meticulously named class hierarchy – but users prefer simple, flat lists.
posts.key_insight: Developers should resist defaulting to tree structures because users prefer flexible, flat organizational systems over the rigid hierarchies that programmers find natural.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the dangers of religious dogmatism in software development, citing Steve McConnell's warning against blind adherence to any single methodology. He argues that software development is inherently heuristic rather than deterministic, requiring developers to be eclectic and willing to try multiple approaches. The post humorously juxtaposes technical religious wars with actual religious perspectives on technology, featuring a church's critique of Microsoft's reliability issues. Atwood suggests that passion for our craft is healthy, but only when tempered with humor and flexibility.
key_points: Dogmatic adherence to any single design method, coding style, or practice is always inappropriate in software development
Software development is heuristic, not deterministic, which means rigid processes are doomed to fail
Developers must be willing to try multiple approaches—top-down, object-oriented, bottom-up, data-structure—without knowing which will work until after trying them
Religious wars in tech are an occupational hazard that developers eventually outgrow, but the scars remain
Passion about our craft is valuable as long as everyone retains their sense of humor
money_quotes: Any developer worth his or her salt bears the scars of a thousand tiny religious wars.

Religion appears in software development in numerous incarnations– as dogmatic adherence to a single design method, as unswerving belief in a specific formatting or commenting style, or as a zealous avoidance of global data. Whatever the case, it's always inappropriate.
Blind faith in one method precludes the selectivity you need if you're to find the most effective solutions to programming problems.
Software development isn't a deterministic process; it's heuristic, which means that rigid processes are inappropriate and have little hope of success.
You have to be willing to try several approaches, knowing that some will fail and some will succeed but not knowing which ones will work until after you try them.
I think it's great that we are passionate enough about what we do to have these kinds of discussions. As long as everyone retains their sense of humor.
posts.key_insight: Software development demands intellectual flexibility and eclecticism because rigid adherence to any methodology—treated as religious doctrine—undermines the heuristic nature of problem-solving that effective programming requires.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood draws a parallel between a scene from Three Kings about necessity driving human behavior and Steve Krug's concept of 'satisficing' in user experience design. The post argues that users don't carefully weigh all options—they choose the first reasonable one that meets their immediate needs. Atwood uses research on firefighters and other high-stakes decision makers to show this isn't laziness but a fundamental human behavior pattern. The core message is that designers must account for this reality rather than assuming users will invest time exploring all features. Designs built for idealized user behavior are destined to fail.
key_points: People do what is most necessary to them at any given moment, not what is optimal
Users satisfice—they choose the first reasonable option rather than the best one
Even high-stakes professionals like firefighters don't compare options; they take the first workable plan
Designs that assume significant user investment are doomed because they ignore actual behavior
Users have specific goals and will do only the absolute minimum necessary to achieve them
money_quotes: As in people do what is most necessary to them at any given moment.
Most of the time we don't choose the best option – we choose the first reasonable option, a strategy known as satisficing.
Make sure you're designing for what is necessary, rather than what is possible.
Designs that assume more investment on the part of the user are doomed, because they are designing for idealized behavior rather than actual behavior.
Users really don't care about your application – they have specific goals and will do only the absolute minimum necessary to achieve those goals.
The fire commanders didn't compare any options. They took the first reasonable plan that came to mind and did a quick mental test for problems.
posts.key_insight: Design for how users actually behave—doing the minimum necessary to achieve their goals—not for how you wish they would engage with your carefully crafted features.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces a major update to his CodeProject article about converting URLs to MHTML archive files using native .NET code. The MHTML format, based on RFC 2557, allows packaging entire web pages as single files that can be emailed or stored. The rewrite adds significant functionality including content encoding autodetection, HTTP compression support, frame handling, and authentication features. He positions this as useful for offline functionality in ASP.NET websites.
key_points: MHTML format packages entire web pages as single functional files based on RFC 2557
The rewrite adds international encoding detection, HTTP compression handling, and frame support
The solution enables quick offline functionality for ASP.NET sites via loopback HTTP requests
New features include HTTP authentication, proxy support, and configurable browser ID strings
money_quotes: It's basically a way to package an entire web page as a (mostly) functional single file that can be emailed, stored in a database, or what have you.
You may also know it as that crazy File, Save As, 'Web Archive, Single File' menu option in Internet Explorer.
This was a truly painful total rewrite, but it offers tons of new functionality.
posts.key_insight: MHTML archives provide a practical solution for capturing and distributing complete web pages as self-contained files, with applications ranging from offline browsing to database storage.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares a brief but pointed observation from John Carmack about his experiments with Java game development on mobile phones. The post highlights Carmack's frustration with performance issues, specifically the absurdity of text editing being slow on powerful hardware. Atwood endorses this sentiment as exceptionally quotable, suggesting it captures a fundamental truth about software bloat and performance expectations.
key_points: John Carmack experimented with cellphone game development using Java
Even basic operations like text editing can be inexplicably slow on powerful modern hardware
Software performance expectations often don't match hardware capabilities
money_quotes: there is something deeply wrong when text editing on a 3.6 ghz processor is anything but instantaneous
posts.key_insight: The gap between raw hardware power and actual software performance reveals deep inefficiencies in how we build software.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares the contents of his keychain as a 'geek rite of passage,' featuring a USB thumb drive, Leatherman Squirt multitool, and LED flashlight. He discusses how losing his previous multitool at airport security led him to discover the superior Squirt model. The post explores the emerging utility of USB thumb drives as floppy drive replacements, linking to various resources about portable software. Atwood concludes with a personal note about his failed experiments with PDAs and his avoidance of cellphones.
key_points: The Leatherman Squirt is superior to the Micra because ancillary tools can be used without opening the scissors/pliers
USB thumb drives have potential to become 'the new floppy drive' despite speed limitations
The value of a USB drive is in what software you put on it, not the hardware itself
Portable server stacks like Uniform Server enable running full WAMP environments from a keychain
Sometimes losing gear to inconvenient circumstances forces you to find better alternatives
money_quotes: It's a geek rite of passage: what's on your keychain?
USB thumb drives are way more boring than LED flashlights or multitools – it's what you put on them that makes them interesting.
With any luck, the USB thumb drive will become the new floppy drive.
Very frustrating, but it forced me to look for alternatives, and the Squirt is superior.
I've concluded that PDAs just don't work for me. I guess I'll be needing that mythical perfect smartphone, but there's one small problem: I don't typically carry a cellphone with me, either.
posts.key_insight: The tools developers carry reflect both practical utility and personal identity, with the software loaded on portable storage mattering more than the hardware itself.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood describes building his own multi-city Craigslist search tool after being frustrated with the site's city-by-city limitation and an existing solution's poor performance. He explains how he achieved dramatically faster perceived performance through HTTP compression and progressive rendering using Response.Write and Response.Flush. The post critiques ASP.NET's architecture for not supporting progressive page rendering, forcing developers to work around the framework. He also shares a technical workaround for disabling IIS6 dynamic compression on a per-folder basis to make Response.Flush work correctly.
key_points: Craigslist's per-city search limitation creates friction for users wanting national searches
Progressive rendering dramatically improves perceived performance even when actual load time is identical
ASP.NET's architecture fundamentally prevents progressive page rendering, requiring workarounds
IIS6 dynamic compression must be disabled for Response.Flush to work as expected
HTTP compression combined with progressive output provides the best user experience for long-running queries
money_quotes: There's no way to do any kind of progressive page rendering using the ASP.NET architecture.
There's a huge psychological difference between seeing immediate, if partial, results, and waiting the same amount of time looking at a blank screen.
I always try my best to design for progressive rendering, even in Windows Forms.
posts.key_insight: Perceived performance matters as much as actual performance—users prefer seeing partial results immediately over waiting for complete results, even when the total time is identical.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the limitations of regular expressions when parsing complex code structures. While acknowledging his love for regex and their effectiveness for common parsing tasks, he demonstrates how edge cases like nested tags and escaped characters can overwhelm even clever regex solutions. The post highlights Drazen Dotlic's work using the CoCo-R parser to colorize C# code, illustrating how full-blown parsers understand code in ways regex cannot. Atwood concludes that developers must sometimes abandon regex complexity and embrace proper parsing tools for challenging problems.
key_points: Regular expressions excel at solving day-to-day parsing problems cost-effectively, but full programming languages cannot be parsed with regex alone
Edge cases like nested HTML tags, escaped quotes, and improperly escaped characters expose fundamental regex limitations
Increasing regex complexity to handle edge cases eventually paints you into a corner
Parsers truly understand code structure while regex only matches patterns
CoCo-R and similar compiler-compiler tools provide a path forward when regex isn't enough
money_quotes: While you can hack around these problems with more and more regular expression cleverness, you eventually paint yourself into a corner with complexity.
Regular expressions don't truly understand the code that they are colorizing – but parsers do.
Depending on the problem you're attacking, at some point you have to bite the bullet and utilize a full-blown parser.
Because parsing is hard.
A full-blown programming language cannot be parsed with regular expressions.
There is an immensely bigger amount of common problems and small parsing needs that are very cost-effectively solved with regular expressions.
posts.key_insight: Know when to abandon increasingly clever regex solutions and embrace proper parsing tools – regex matches patterns, but parsers understand structure.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the key to becoming a better technical presenter is relentless practice combined with feedback from experienced observers. He shares practical advice from Scott Hanselman about font choices, screen readability, and machine setup for code demonstrations. The post emphasizes that while there are excellent resources available for improving presentation skills, actually practicing in front of audiences is what matters most. Atwood warns against the trap of merely reading about presentations without putting the advice into action.
key_points: Practice with feedback from experienced presenters is the most important factor in improving presentation skills
Technical setup matters: Lucida Console 14-18pt bold is the most readable monospaced font for code demonstrations
Creating a dedicated 'BigFonty' user account with pre-configured large fonts and accessibility settings saves time during demos
Reading about presenting without actually practicing won't make you a better presenter
money_quotes: Like most developers, I don't get a lot of experience giving presentations.
The golden rule is: practice, practice, and more practice.
Nothing disrespects an audience like making them wait while you ask 'Can you see this 8 point font? No? Oh, let me change it while you wait.'
Courier of any flavor or Arial (or any other proportionally spaced font) is NOT appropriate for code demonstrations, period, full stop.
The art of giving an effective presentation is, of course, a bajillion dollar business outside the insular world of software development.
Reading about being a better presenter will not make you a better presenter!
posts.key_insight: Consuming advice about presentations is no substitute for actually practicing them in front of real audiences with feedback.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood draws a humorous parallel between Rube Goldberg machines and software development, noting that the 'Rube Goldberg software design' concept had become a popular meme. He references a parody video by Rory Blyth and Scott Hanselman, Microsoft advertisements, and Honda's famous 'Cog' commercial as examples of this cultural moment. The post is a brief, playful observation that software development often resembles unnecessarily complex chain-reaction devices, delivered with tongue-in-cheek professionalism.
key_points: Rube Goldberg software design became a meme in 2005, parodied by developers and appearing in Microsoft ads
Honda's 2003 'Cog' advertisement represents the pinnacle of real-world Rube Goldberg devices
The Incredible Machine game from 1993 exemplifies the appeal of complex chain-reaction systems
The comparison between software development and Rube Goldberg machines is presented as satirically 'coincidental'
money_quotes: Any resemblance between any of the above and actual software development is, of course, completely coincidental.
We're professionals, dammit!
posts.key_insight: Software development often devolves into unnecessarily complex Rube Goldberg-like contraptions, despite developers' professional pretensions.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood discusses the common pitfalls of software prototypes, drawing from Tim Weaver's five laws that describe how prototypes inevitably become production code despite being intended as throwaway experiments. He contrasts prototyping with 'tracer code' from The Pragmatic Programmer—prototypes explore specific aspects and should be discarded, while tracer code builds a lean but complete architectural skeleton that grows into the final system. Atwood suggests that if developers lack the discipline to throw away prototypes, they should either use tracer code instead or develop prototypes in a completely different language to reduce the temptation to ship them.
key_points: Prototypes have a tendency to become permanent production code, inheriting all their shortcuts and poor practices
The distinction between prototypes (disposable exploration) and tracer code (lean architectural skeleton) is crucial for project success
Tracer code provides a working framework that developers can incrementally build upon while maintaining system integrity
Using a different language or environment for prototypes can help enforce the discipline of throwing them away
Poor prototype performance and inappropriate component choices will persist and worsen in production
money_quotes: Prototypes are designed to be thrown away.
Once in production a prototype will never die
Whatever poor coding practices you use to build your prototype will be replicated in the final production version
Prototyping generates disposable code. Tracer code is lean but complete, and forms part of the skeleton of the final system.
Think of prototyping as the reconnaissance and intelligence gathering that takes place before a single tracer bullet is fired.
If you don't have the discipline to throw away the prototype, you're defeating the very purpose of prototyping.
posts.key_insight: If you can't commit to throwing away your prototype, you should be writing tracer code instead—a lean but complete architectural skeleton that's designed to evolve into the final system.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains the evolution from simple CRC checksums to cryptographic hash algorithms, drawing from his early experience with 300 baud file transfers. He distinguishes between checksums (fast but insecure) and security-oriented hashes (slower but cryptographically sound), noting that CRCs can be easily manipulated while maintaining the same checksum value. The post surveys the hash algorithms available in .NET's System.Security.Cryptography namespace and explains when to use each type. He concludes that for simple comparison tasks, basic hash methods suffice, while security applications demand proper cryptographic hashes.
key_points: CRC checksums are designed for speed, not security, and can be intentionally manipulated without changing the checksum value
The pigeonhole principle explains why smaller hash sizes lead to collisions - an 8-bit CRC only has 256 possible values
For cryptographic hashes, bigger is better: each extra bit doubles possible keys and halves collision probability
Simple comparison tasks don't require security-grade hashes - basic methods like GetHashCode are sufficient
.NET provides several security-oriented hash algorithms including MD5, SHA variants, and MACTripleDes
money_quotes: I also learned a little bit about the pigeonhole principle when I downloaded a file with a matching CRC that was corrupt!
CRCs cannot be safely relied upon to verify data integrity (that no changes whatsoever have occurred), since it's extremely easy to intentionally change data without modifying its CRC.
Checksums are somewhat analogous to filesystem 'fingerprints'– no two should ever be alike, and any modification to the file should change the checksum.
That's probably because CRC is a simple algorithm designed for speed – not security.
If all you need to do is tell two things apart, you don't need fancy security hashes.
posts.key_insight: Choose your hash algorithm based on your actual needs: simple comparison tasks only need basic hashes, while security applications require cryptographic algorithms where every additional bit of hash size meaningfully improves protection.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents a frustrating bug in the .NET framework where customizing the wsdlHelpGenerator to sort web service methods alphabetically works fine in subfolders but fails with a configuration error when deployed to a root URL. The post walks through the simple four-step process of replacing the default help generator, then reveals the deployment gotcha. Despite searching extensively, Atwood cannot find any workaround and expresses frustration that this bug persisted into .NET 1.1 SP1.
key_points: The default .NET web service help page displays methods in random hash order rather than alphabetically
Customizing the wsdlHelpGenerator requires only a simple one-line code change from Hashtable to SortedList
The customization works in subfolders but breaks completely when deployed to a root URL
The bug persisted across .NET versions including 1.1 SP1 with no documented workaround
money_quotes: Why are the smallest bugs in the .NET framework always the most disproportionately frustrating?
you want to replace the default crappy, random hash sorted list of Web Service methods with one that's (shock!) in alphabetical order
I know, it's crazy talk, but bear with me.
This makes deploying to production a lot more, uh, thrilling than it would otherwise be.
This egregious bug in the .NET framework really pisses me off, particularly since it has persisted into 1.1 SP1.
I can find lots of people complaining about this in Google Groups, but I can't find one single workaround.
posts.key_insight: Small, obscure framework bugs that block straightforward customizations are disproportionately frustrating because they waste developer time on problems that shouldn't exist.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post where Jeff Atwood notifies readers about his upcoming speaking engagement at the 2005 Raleigh Code Camp at NC State. He will be presenting on User Friendly Exception Handling Strategies. The post expresses his enthusiasm about attending a technical conference after a long hiatus.
key_points: Jeff Atwood is speaking at the 2005 Raleigh Code Camp at NC State
His presentation topic is User Friendly Exception Handling Strategies at 10:15am
He notes it has been years since he attended a technical conference
money_quotes: I haven't had an opportunity to attend a technical conference in years, so I'm really looking forward to this!
posts.key_insight: A simple event announcement reflecting the value developers place on in-person technical community gatherings.

posts.summary: This is a brief announcement post sharing materials from Jeff Atwood's presentation at the Raleigh MSDN Code Camp. The post provides a download link for his 'User Friendly Exception Handling Strategies' presentation, which includes PowerPoint slides and Visual Studio .NET 2003 demo solutions. Atwood expresses enthusiasm about connecting with fellow passionate developers at the event.
key_points: Atwood presented on user-friendly exception handling at the Raleigh Code Camp
Presentation materials were made freely available including slides and working code demos
The post reflects the early blogging culture of sharing conference materials with the broader community
money_quotes: There's nothing better than geeking out with a bunch of guys and gals who are as passionate about this stuff as you are!
posts.key_insight: Developer community events create valuable opportunities for knowledge sharing that extend beyond attendees through freely shared presentation materials.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares Maciej Ceglowski's satirical critique of Paul Graham's 'Hackers and Painters' essay, which argues that programmers try to borrow unearned cachet from the art world. The core argument is that the painter-programmer analogy is arbitrary self-flattery, since painting offers social rewards (particularly romantic attention) that programming never will. Atwood extends this to critique the self-celebratory tendencies of prominent tech figures like Eric Raymond and Dave Winer, whose essays often become exercises in 'participatory narcissism.' However, he acknowledges the tension that personal, opinionated voices make for more compelling reading than egoless alternatives.
key_points: The hacker-painter analogy is arbitrary self-flattery rather than meaningful comparison
Programming lacks the social and romantic rewards that creative arts provide
Prominent tech essayists often engage in elaborate self-reflection disguised as cultural commentary
The 'what is a hacker' genre tends to describe someone who resembles the author
Strong personal voices in writing are more engaging despite the narcissism risk
money_quotes: Great paintings, for example, get you laid in a way that great computer programs never do.
Your job as a painter consists of staring at naked women, for as long as you wish, and this day in and day out through the course of a many-decades-long career.

The reason Graham's essay isn't entitled 'Hackers and Pastry Chefs' is not because there is something that unites painters and programmers into a secret brotherhood, but because Paul Graham likes to cultivate the arty aura that comes from working in the visual arts.
Raymond is the original perpetrator of the 'what is a hacker?' essay, in which you quickly begin to understand that a hacker is someone who resembles Eric Raymond.
These essays and this writing style are tempting to people outside the subculture at hand because of their engaging personal tone and idiosyncratic, insider's view.
After a while, you begin to notice that all the essays are an elaborate set of mirrors set up to reflect different facets of the author, in a big distributed act of participatory narcissism.
posts.key_insight: Tech luminaries' essays about hacker culture often reveal more about the author's self-image than about programming itself.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to criticism of tabbed browsing interfaces by pointing out the far worse inconsistency in Microsoft Office's own window management. He demonstrates how Word, Excel, Access, and PowerPoint each handle multiple documents and window closing differently, with Excel closing all documents when you close one, Access showing MDI windows in the taskbar, and PowerPoint refusing to actually close when you click close. The post argues that before criticizing tabbed UIs for violating OS window management conventions, we should acknowledge that Microsoft's flagship productivity suite already makes those conventions meaningless.
key_points: Word and Excel appear to be SDI but behave differently - Excel closes all documents when you close one, Word only closes the current document
Microsoft Access uses a hybrid MDI mode where each internal window appears in the Windows taskbar
PowerPoint only allows one instance and redefines 'close application' to mean 'close current document' when multiple presentations are open
The criticism of tabbed browsing for breaking window management conventions rings hollow when Microsoft's own apps are wildly inconsistent
money_quotes: When does 'close application' not mean 'close application'? Evidently whenever you have more than one document open in PowerPoint.
How could tabbed interfaces be any worse than the complete and utter lack of window management consistency in Microsoft's own office suite?
My needs are much simpler: how about some window management consistency in Microsoft's own office suite?
There can be only one PowerPoint, ever.
posts.key_insight: Criticizing new UI paradigms for breaking conventions is hypocritical when the established software from major vendors already implements those conventions inconsistently.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines NTLM authentication in ASP.NET, initially presenting it as a convenient way to avoid login dialogs on intranet sites. However, he methodically dismantles this convenience by revealing the hidden costs: a 50x performance penalty (1000ms vs 20ms per request), complex browser zone requirements, proxy incompatibilities, multi-domain headaches, and the burden of troubleshooting Windows account issues. After two years of experience, he concludes that the traditional login dialog might actually be the better choice despite NTLM's apparent elegance.
key_points: NTLM authentication requires three browser round trips for the challenge-response handshake, creating a ~1000ms overhead compared to 20ms for anonymous requests
IE only sends NTLM credentials automatically to 'Intranet Zone' sites, requiring fallback to insecure Basic authentication for other scenarios
NTLM ties website access to Windows account status, making developers responsible for troubleshooting password expirations, lockouts, and domain issues
IIS Directory Security settings override ASP.NET Web.config authorization rules, creating confusing configuration precedence
The apparent simplicity of 'free' authentication hides significant operational complexity and support burden
money_quotes: Why trouble the user with Yet Another Login Dialog when you can leverage the built in NTLM functionality of IIS and Internet Explorer?
And it's free – as long as you define 'free' as three browser round trips.
The last time I benchmarked it, almost 1000ms per request, compared to under 20ms for anonymous requests.
And Basic authentication is, uh, unsecure. Like 'barely better than plain text' unsecure.
This inevitably results in a lot of user complaints that 'I can't get to your intranet site, but all the others work – what's wrong with your site?'
Those other sites don't use NTLM. And I am put in the uncomfortable position of troubleshooting people's Windows account so they can get to our website.

I used to be a big believer in NTLM authentication in ASP.NET. However, after living with it for the last two years, I'm starting to wonder if we wouldn't all be better off with Yet Another Login Dialog.
posts.key_insight: Convenience features that eliminate user friction often just transfer that friction to developers and operations, sometimes at a much higher total cost.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores three methods for determining when a .NET assembly was built. The filesystem date method is unreliable, while the version-based approach works only if developers stick to default versioning conventions—and has a quirk where Visual Studio only updates build numbers when solutions are reopened. The most reliable method involves reading the embedded linker timestamp from the Portable Executable header, though Atwood discovered timezone issues when testing code from a developer who lives in GMT.
key_points: Filesystem dates are unreliable for determining actual build times
The default .NET version string encodes build date as days since January 1, 2000, and revision as seconds since midnight divided by 2
Visual Studio has a surprising behavior where it only increments build/revision numbers when a solution is closed and reopened, not on every build
The PE header's embedded linker timestamp provides the most reliable build date
Timezone handling is a common source of bugs, especially when sharing code across different regions
money_quotes: Visual Studio only increments the build and revision number when the solution is closed and reopened.
If you build fifty times throughout the day in the same solution, every single one of your builds will have the same version.
Dustin lives in GMT — that's the ultimate 'it runs on my machine!'
Even I can figure out GMT/UTC issues when they practically slap me in the face.
posts.key_insight: The most reliable way to determine a .NET assembly's build date is to read the linker timestamp from the PE header, not from the filesystem or version string.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his first experience adopting formal unit testing with NUnit, finding the implementation straightforward but the harder challenge being what and how to test. He argues that even basic unit tests are valuable because they force developers to systematically think about testing rather than relying on ad-hoc clicking around. However, he raises a practical tension: aggressive refactoring in early development stages creates a pile of failing tests, potentially discouraging either good testing practices or necessary design changes. He questions whether the test-first approach actually helps or hinders this balance.
key_points: Unit testing implementation is simple; the real challenge is determining what makes a good test
Most developers don't test at all—they just click around and ship if there are no exceptions
The true value of unit testing is forcing developers to systematically consider testing questions about their code
Early-stage refactoring creates tension with unit testing since design changes break existing tests
Test-first development may conflict with the reality of how projects evolve through major design iterations
money_quotes: The problem with unit testing, then, is not the implementation. It's determining what to test. And how to test it.
Most developers don't test at all! They key in a few values at random and click a few buttons. If they don't get any unhandled exceptions, that code is ready for QA!
The real value of unit testing is that it forces you to stop and think about testing.
Unit tests don't guarantee correct functioning of a program. I think it's unreasonable to expect them to.
Writing unit tests does guarantee that the developer has considered, however briefly, these truly difficult testing questions.

My main problem at the moment with unit tests is when I change a design I get a stack of failing tests. This means I'm either going to write less tests or make fewer big design changes. Both of which are bad things.
posts.key_insight: Unit testing's greatest value isn't catching bugs—it's forcing developers to systematically think through testing questions they would otherwise ignore.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces an update to his craigslist.org all-city search tool, adding for sale searching capabilities alongside the existing jobs search. The post lists several incremental improvements including subcategory selection, relative date display, visual highlighting for post age, and Google Maps integration. Atwood humorously notes he built this feature based on a single user's request, framing it as 'popular demand.' He closes with a joke about explicitly refusing to add personals search functionality.
key_points: Added for sale item searching to complement existing jobs search across all craigslist cities
Implemented UX improvements like relative dates, age-based highlighting, and Google Maps location integration
Built features based on individual user feedback, showing responsiveness to community requests
Drew a clear line at personals search, injecting humor into feature scope decisions
money_quotes: Due to popular demand one person's request, I added for sale searching
I'm open to any additional feature requests.
Except for an all-cities personals search, because that would be downright freaky. I just don't even want to know.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes one person's feature request is worth implementing, but knowing where to draw the line on scope is equally important.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces a new CodeProject article about .NET encryption, sharing his experience diving deep into System.Security.Cryptography classes over six months. He candidly admits that cryptography is difficult to understand and implement correctly. The post introduces his documented wrapper class designed to simplify encryption for other developers. It reflects his approach of learning by doing and then sharing that knowledge with the community.
key_points: Cryptography is inherently difficult, even for experienced developers
Six months of deep exploration were needed to feel confident with .NET's cryptography classes
Wrapper classes and documentation can help make complex security concepts more accessible
Understanding symmetric encryption, asymmetric encryption, and hashing theory is essential groundwork
money_quotes: Cryptography is hard.
posts.key_insight: Even experienced developers should approach cryptography with humility, investing significant time to understand it properly before attempting to simplify it for others.

posts.summary: This very brief post shares a humorous quote from a Wired article about someone whose life was profoundly shaped by the Street Fighter character Blanka. The quoted person admits to trying Blanka's fighting moves in real fights (unsuccessfully) and credits the character with helping him meet his wife. Atwood adds a dry observation about the impracticality of Blanka's signature electric attack.
key_points: Video game characters can have unexpectedly profound impacts on players' real lives and identities
The gap between game fantasy and real-world application is often comically wide
Gaming culture shapes personal identity in ways that extend far beyond the screen
