money_quotes: When I got into real fights, I even tried using some of his moves. They never worked.
I often ask myself, what would Blanka do? I even met my wife because of this game. So yes, I owe my whole life to Blanka.
I'm thinking that electricity move might be particularly difficult to pull off.
posts.key_insight: The devotion gamers feel toward fictional characters can shape their real-world identities and life paths in surprisingly meaningful ways.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts his serendipitous discovery of Edward Tufte's seminal work on data visualization while working IT at a market research company in 1995. The encounter revealed to him that his lifelong fascination with computers was partly about the data within them and how it could be beautifully displayed. He uses Tufte's example of transforming a mundane government manual illustration into a powerful visual explanation to demonstrate how simple changes can dramatically improve information design. Atwood recommends Tufte's three books as essential reading for anyone interested in visual design, while noting they raise an implicit question about where utility ends and art begins.
key_points: Tufte's books demonstrate that even mundane illustrations can be transformed into powerful visual explanations with simple, thoughtful changes
The attraction to computers isn't just about the machine itself, but about the data inside and how it's displayed
Effective information design exists in a 'strange territory somewhere between utility and beauty'
Tufte's practical approach implies but doesn't fully address the question of when information display becomes art
money_quotes: I had no idea that information could be so beautiful.
This example is powerful precisely because it is so very mundane – the illustration barely registers.
With a few simple changes, a generic illustration is transformed into a strikingly powerful visual explanation.
When does the display of information stop being utilitarian and start being art?
He expressed some surprise that he had no luck getting his market analysts to look at the book, but his crazy IT guy just happened to see it in passing and treated it like some new kind of religion.
posts.key_insight: The way we display information matters as much as the information itself, and thoughtful design can transform mundane data into something genuinely beautiful and powerful.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to Mark Russinovich's concerns about the memory footprint of .NET managed applications compared to native code. While acknowledging that managed apps do consume more resources, Atwood argues this is a reasonable tradeoff because developer productivity matters more than raw performance. He points out that the set of tasks requiring low-level languages shrinks as hardware improves, and that a developer can create multiple managed applications in the time it takes to write one native app. Atwood conducts his own measurements of .NET's baseline footprint and concludes the overhead is acceptable given the productivity gains.
key_points: The debate over managed code overhead has existed for decades and represents a fundamental tradeoff between performance and developer productivity
Hardware continues to improve while human cognitive capacity remains constant, making developer time increasingly valuable relative to machine resources
A talented developer can write several managed .NET apps in the time required for one unmanaged C++ application
The baseline memory footprint of minimal .NET applications (4-12 MB) is reasonable given the productivity benefits
Russinovich's comparison of managed vs native Notepad, while technically accurate, misses the larger economic picture of software development
money_quotes: Computers get faster every day, but our brains don't.
The goal of the .NET runtime is not to squeeze every drop of performance out of the platform – it's to make software development easier.
Would you rather have a single fast native app, or a dozen slower managed apps to choose from?
The subset of tasks that must be done in (insert favorite low-level language here) for acceptable performance gets smaller and smaller every day as hardware improves over time.
It's amazing to me that no matter how much memory we add, how much faster we make our CPUs, and how much faster we make our disks spin and seek, computing doesn't seem to get faster.
These baseline footprints seem totally reasonable to me, particularly considering the incredible productivity I get in exchange.
posts.key_insight: Developer productivity should be valued over raw application performance because hardware improves continuously while human cognitive capacity does not.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to an article arguing that Visual Basic's culture is detrimental to professional development, examining the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis applied to programming languages. While acknowledging that language culture can influence developers and observing the migration of talented VB developers to C#, Atwood firmly rejects the notion that language choice predetermines code quality. He argues that individual developer skill matters far more than language selection, noting that good coding principles transcend any specific language. The post concludes with a warning that C#'s current reputation for quality code is temporary, as Java's trajectory demonstrates.
key_points: Language culture and community can influence developers, but cannot determine their ultimate skill level or code quality
The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis applied to programming suggests languages shape thought, but Atwood finds this deterministic view troubling
Early adopters of new languages tend to be better developers, creating a temporary illusion of language superiority
Good coding practices transcend programming languages - you can write excellent or terrible code in any language
C#'s current reputation for quality will fade as it matures, just as Java became associated with mediocre 'Pattern Factory' code
money_quotes: I cannot agree that code quality is predestined by choice of language, environment, or IDE – it's almost entirely determined by the skill of the developer.
You can write FORTRAN in any language.
Just because a programming language allows you to write bad code doesn't mean that you have to do it.

You can drown in a bathtub with an inch of water in it, and you can easily write a completely unreadable and unmaintainable program in a language with no go-tos or line numbers, with exception handling and generic types and garbage collection.
The shiny patina of newness surrounding C# is attracting a disproportionate number of talented developers.
Today, any Java-related google query will return reams of truly mediocre 'explosion at the Pattern Factory' Java code. All I can say is, enjoy it while it lasts.
posts.key_insight: Developer skill overwhelmingly determines code quality; language choice is a red herring that temporarily correlates with quality only because talented early adopters cluster around new technologies.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the Windows Start Menu is a usability disaster that makes launching applications far harder than necessary. He catalogs its many design failures: non-alphabetical ordering, inconsistent folder naming by company rather than application, and cascading menus that obscure information. Drawing on usability research showing horizontal menus perform worst of all layouts, he concludes that the Start Menu's fundamental problem is its abuse of menu-based navigation. The post anticipates improvements in Windows Vista while questioning why users have suffered with this design since Windows 95.
key_points: The Start Menu defaults to install order rather than alphabetical, forcing users to manually sort or hunt for applications
Software vendors organize by company name rather than application name, requiring users to remember corporate structures they don't care about
Cascading menus don't scale—once you have multiple rows, folders obscure other content and drag-and-drop becomes nearly impossible
Usability research demonstrates that horizontal menus perform worst of all navigation layouts, yet the Start Menu doubles down on this pattern
The core problem is treating application launching as a menu hierarchy rather than a simple, visible list
money_quotes: The Windows start menu makes launching applications far more difficult than it should be.
So if I want to use CrazyApp, I have to remember to look for the MonkeyCorp menu. Why should we expect the user to know or care what the company name is?
Menus have poor usability.
It's clear that traditional menus have no place on web pages, and should be used sparingly in GUIs.
For launching applications, it's a usability train wreck.
A giant horizontal menu may have seemed like a good idea back when Windows 95 was launched – but clearly, it isn't.
posts.key_insight: The Windows Start Menu's fundamental failure is treating application launching as a cascading menu hierarchy when usability research clearly shows menus are the worst possible interface for this task.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses the ASP.NET canonicalization vulnerability as a springboard to critique the software industry's tendency to invent unnecessary jargon. He explains that canonicalization refers to converting data to its simplest, standard form—a concept important for security since file paths and URLs can be represented in multiple ways that bypass security checks. However, his main point is linguistic: 'canonicalize' is an invented word when 'canonize' already exists, exemplifying the tech industry's habit of creating needless complexity. He extends this criticism to other made-up terms like 'performant' and corporate buzzword-speak.
key_points: Canonicalization vulnerabilities occur when the same resource (file, URL) can be represented multiple ways, allowing attackers to bypass security checks
The word 'canonicalize' is artificially fabricated when 'canonize' already serves the same purpose
Making up new technical words is as problematic as using unnecessarily fancy words when simple ones exist
The tech industry perpetuates jargon for jargon's sake, creating Orwellian obfuscation
Corporate buzzword lists ('reinvent value-added markets') represent the logical endpoint of this linguistic decay
money_quotes: And popes don't canonicalize: they canonize.
The words 'canonicalize' and 'canonicalization' are artificially fabricated technical mumbo-jumbo.
Making up new words is just as bad as using fancy words in place of short ones.
The word 'performant,' if it existed, would be a noun – not an adjective. But it doesn't exist, so if you do see it in print, remember that it's not really there.
It's one thing to use technical jargon excessively, but the perpetuation of new jargon for jargon's sake is particularly Orwellian.
Why say 'This project's goals are orthogonal to the company's needs?' Admit it – if you were at home, you'd just say 'different from' or 'at odds with.'
posts.key_insight: The tech industry's compulsion to invent unnecessary jargon like 'canonicalize' when simpler words exist reflects a broader problem of preferring complexity over clarity.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood makes an emphatic case for always using parameterized SQL instead of concatenated SQL strings. He presents two compelling arguments: concatenated SQL creates massive SQL injection vulnerabilities that string escaping cannot fully protect against, and parameterized SQL offers significant performance benefits including pre-compiled execution plans and reduced network overhead. The post references Steve Friedl's detailed SQL injection attack examples as evidence of the security risks. Atwood concludes by comparing non-parameterized SQL to the infamous GoTo statement—a practice so harmful it should be universally avoided.
key_points: Concatenated SQL strings create gaping SQL injection vulnerabilities that escaping alone cannot fix
Parameterized SQL enables database query plan caching and pre-compilation for better performance
Parameterized queries reduce string concatenation overhead and network traffic
The security and performance benefits make parameterized SQL a non-negotiable best practice
Developers should actively ensure their coworkers also follow this practice
money_quotes: Give me parameterized SQL, or give me death.
You should never, ever use concatenated SQL strings in your applications.
Code like this opens your app to SQL injection attacks, and it's a huge, gaping vulnerability.
I know what you're thinking. No, escaping the strings doesn't protect you.
A more generic query form is presented to db, so it's likely already hashed and stored as a pre-compiled execution plan.
Non-parameterized SQL is the GoTo statement of database programming.
Don't do it, and make sure your coworkers don't either.
posts.key_insight: Parameterized SQL is not merely a best practice but an absolute requirement—it simultaneously eliminates SQL injection vulnerabilities and improves database performance.

posts.summary: Atwood argues that modern software development success often depends more on finding and integrating existing solutions than writing original code. He illustrates this through a colleague's experience adopting a pre-built progress indicator component instead of building one from scratch. The post emphasizes that reinventing solutions that already exist in mature form represents a failure of judgment. True development skill lies in recognizing what problems have already been solved and knowing how to combine existing building blocks effectively.
key_points: Writing code when mature solutions already exist represents a failure of judgment, not skill
Modern programming is about finding and connecting existing frameworks, not writing everything from scratch
The real challenge is distinguishing between what needs to be built (glue) and what can be reused (Legos)
Progress indicators provide significant psychological benefits to users during long operations, making waits feel shorter
Software development today is roughly half finding building blocks and half writing the glue between them
money_quotes: Sometimes, writing code means you've failed.
So much of what we do already exists, and in more mature, complete form.
The real challenge in modern programming isn't sitting down and writing a ton of code; it's figuring out what existing code or frameworks you should be hooking together.
The real development skill is correctly identifying which half is Legos and which half is glue.
In software development today, 1/2 the work is finding the building blocks, and the other 1/2 is writing the glue.
posts.key_insight: The mark of a skilled developer isn't the ability to write code, but the wisdom to recognize when existing solutions make writing code unnecessary.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood pushes back against the notion that .NET developers need deep C++ knowledge to be competent, arguing this fundamentally misunderstands the purpose of abstraction. He contends that managed code deliberately abstracts away memory management details, making it an optimization concern rather than a requirement. While acknowledging Joel Spolsky's point about leaky abstractions, Atwood sides with Bram's counterargument that abstractions are what make large-scale software development possible at all. He concludes that developers should respect abstractions and focus on fixing leaks rather than bypassing the abstraction layer entirely.
key_points: In managed languages like .NET, memory management becomes an optimization rather than a requirement, which is a crucial distinction from C++
Demanding detailed knowledge of underlying layers to be competent at a higher abstraction level is counterproductive and defeats the purpose of abstraction
Modern software relies on dozens of abstraction layers, and without them large-scale maintainable software would be impossible
While abstractions do leak occasionally, the proper response is to fix the leaks, not to abandon or bypass the abstraction
Knowledge of lower layers is valuable for troubleshooting but shouldn't be a prerequisite for competence at higher levels
money_quotes: In the world of managed code, memory management is an optimization, not a requirement.

It is a testament to the power of abstraction and how much we take it for granted that such a statement can be made at all, as if we always expected to be able to write large pieces of software in a maintainable manner.
Without these abstractions, we'd all be toast.
Arguing that you must have detailed knowledge of the layer under the abstraction to be competent is counterproductive.
We should respect the abstractions and spend most of our efforts fixing the leaks instead of bypassing them.
You should only care when it makes sense to do so, whereas in C++ you are forced to worry about the minutia of detailed memory management even for the most trivial of applications.
posts.key_insight: The whole point of abstraction is to free developers from lower-level concerns, and demanding mastery of those concerns undermines the productivity gains that abstraction provides.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores barcode technology after purchasing a USB CueCat scanner to experiment with UPC barcodes. He introduces QR codes as a more advanced alternative, noting their impressive data density compared to traditional barcodes. The post demonstrates QR code generation through a Japanese Windows application and laments that American cell phones and advertisers haven't adopted the technology. Written in 2005, Atwood correctly predicts that QR codes may become important as camera phones become standard.
key_points: The CueCat USB barcode scanner offers an affordable way to experiment with UPC scanning at home, despite its limitations compared to industrial readers
QR codes can store dramatically more data than traditional barcodes: up to 7,089 numeric characters or 2,953 bytes
QR codes were designed to be scanned via cell phone cameras, making them more versatile than laser-dependent barcodes
American adoption of QR codes lagged behind other markets in 2005, representing a missed opportunity
The technology was poised to grow as cameras became standard features on cell phones
money_quotes: It's fun to run around the house scanning in UPCs from household items, although the low-powered LED reader in the CueCat definitely pales in comparison to the industrial laser readers you'll find at your local supermarket.
Like so many things Apple, it's self-consciously cute where it should be practical, but the concept is sound.
It's a shame American cell phones and American advertisers haven't adopted QRCode.
However, it may be a preview of things to come as cameras become a standard feature of cell phones.
posts.key_insight: QR codes represent a significant evolution in barcode technology that enables camera-based scanning, a capability that would become ubiquitous as smartphones proliferated.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques UML (Universal Modeling Language) as a fundamentally flawed documentation standard despite its widespread adoption. Drawing from his experience working with offshore vendors, he argues that UML's imprecise semantics make it subjective—a fatal flaw for something meant to describe the precision of code. He highlights three core failures: lack of bidirectional synchronization with code, inability to capture important details like properties or static members, and no real advantage over plain text documentation. Atwood sides with The Pragmatic Programmer's advice to use whatever communication method works best rather than slavishly following UML notation.
key_points: UML's imprecise semantics cause subjective interpretation, which contradicts programming's inherent precision
UML violates DRY principles because diagrams and code don't stay synchronized automatically
UML cannot represent important programming concepts like Properties or Static members, forcing developers to read code anyway
Even Martin Fowler, a UML proponent, positions it mainly as informal whiteboard sketching rather than formal documentation
Plain text requirements documents are often more readable and useful than UML's lines, arrows, and stick figures
money_quotes: The last time I checked, programming was about the least subjective human activity on earth. So there's a huge disconnect.
If the UML changes, the code doesn't magically write itself. And if the code changes, the UML documents don't magically update themselves either.
I find it much easier to read a typical all-text requirements document than a mish-mash of lines, arrows, and primitive looking stick figures.
It seems incredible to us that anyone would seriously consider documenting information this dense using only simplistic stick people.
Don't be a slave to any notation; use whatever method best communicates the requirements with your audience.
UML can't capture many of the high-level details necessary to build software.
posts.key_insight: Documentation tools should serve clear communication, not become rigid standards that add complexity without solving the fundamental problem of keeping specifications synchronized with code.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces his new position at Vertigo Software, a well-known Microsoft-focused development shop. He highlights the company's impressive portfolio including Quake II .NET, IBuySpy, and the .NET Pet Shop J2EE performance comparison. The post marks a significant career move requiring relocation from North Carolina to California. Atwood expresses genuine excitement about joining what he considers a high-profile team of developers.
key_points: Vertigo Software was known for high-profile Microsoft technology demonstrations and sample applications
The company's portfolio included gaming (Quake II .NET), e-commerce (IBuySpy, Pet Shop), and smart client applications
This job required a cross-country relocation from NC to CA
Atwood had previously referenced Vertigo's work in blog posts about managed code performance and J2EE comparisons
money_quotes: I'm tremendously excited to join such a high-profile group of software developers, and I can't wait to start.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes career advancement means making big moves—both professionally and geographically—to work with teams whose work you admire.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recommends Robert Read's 'How to be a Programmer' as an essential guide for developers. The essay emphasizes that the hardest challenges in programming aren't technical but social—dealing with coworkers, customers, and the human dynamics of building software. Atwood highlights that while writing code requires intelligence and skill, it's 'child's play' compared to the interpersonal challenges of successful software development. He particularly recommends the team and personal skill sections of Read's work, noting their shared belief that social problems dominate technical ones in software projects.
key_points: The hardest part of software development is dealing with people, not writing code
Technical programming skill, while important, is secondary to social and interpersonal abilities
Robert Read's essay fills a gap by focusing on social problems that other classic programming books don't address
The concept of 'tribe' captures the loyalty and community aspects of development teams
Classic books like The Pragmatic Programmer and Code Complete should be supplemented with guidance on human factors
money_quotes: To be a good programmer is difficult and noble.
The hardest part of making real a collective vision of a software project is dealing with one's coworkers and customers.

Writing computer programs is important and takes great intelligence and skill. But it is really child's play compared to everything else that a good programmer must do to make a software system that succeeds.
This essay differs from those excellent works by emphasizing social problems.
Welcome to the tribe.
Robert and I have the same set of favorite books, but we also agree that most of the problems in software development aren't technical – they're social.
posts.key_insight: Programming skill is necessary but insufficient—the true challenge of software development lies in navigating the social dynamics of teams, customers, and organizational culture.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the lack of diversity in tech, particularly the underrepresentation of women as visible figures like authors and conference speakers. He references Julia Lerman's observation that there are as many speakers named Brian at TechEd as there are women speakers total. While acknowledging the 'People Like Us' tendency in hiring, Atwood argues the real solution isn't demographic quotas but hiring for cognitive diversity—bringing in people who are smart at different things rather than matching an ultra-specific skills checklist.
key_points: Women make up roughly 10% of IT workers, but their representation among authors and conference speakers is even lower
The 'PLU problem' (People Like Us) creates homogeneous teams, though Atwood argues it's about similar interests rather than conscious demographic bias
Hiring for identical skill sets misses the value that different cognitive approaches bring to a team
Visible role models are crucial for encouraging underrepresented groups to enter and stay in technology
The solution isn't lowering the bar but broadening what 'smart' means—hiring people smart at different things
money_quotes: Take a look at the speaker list for TechEd 2004. There are as many speakers named Brian (including one Bryan) as there are women speakers.
I don't agree with Kathy's implicit suggestion that you should intentionally hire people who aren't smart. You should intentionally hire people who are smart at different things.
Forget race, forget sex, forget ethnicity – hire for cognitive diversity.
Most employers hire using an ultra-specific laundry list of skills, and that's the deeper mistake.
What would an expert Smalltalk developer have to teach you as they learned C#?
posts.key_insight: True diversity in tech teams comes not from demographic checkboxes but from assembling people with genuinely different ways of thinking and complementary cognitive strengths.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood discusses how software projects fail when developers ignore fundamental practices, using Steve McConnell's paint can analogy from Rapid Development. The post opens with an example of a chaotic inherited codebase built by developers who each worked in isolation with wildly different approaches. McConnell's analogy compares software projects to painting jobs of varying scales: a doghouse can tolerate shortcuts, but a Boeing 747 demands strict adherence to instructions. Most software projects are 'house-sized' or larger, meaning cutting corners leads to significant technical debt. The core message is that mastering and following development fundamentals is what separates professional programmers from hobbyists.
key_points: Software projects commonly fail because developers don't follow established fundamentals, not because the problems are novel or difficult
The paint can analogy scales the consequences of ignoring instructions: doghouse (low stakes), house (medium), Boeing 747 (critical)
Knowing the fundamentals is the critical differentiator between hobby and professional programmers
Developers often skip best practices not due to conflicting visions but simply because 'they felt like doing something different'
Without mastering fundamentals, you lack project control and won't know if you're succeeding or failing until it's too late
money_quotes: They just felt like doing something different.
If you don't master the fundamentals first, you'll lack the project control needed to develop rapidly.
You won't even know whether you're succeeding or failing until late in the project.
Ignore the instructions if you wish, but do so at your peril.
Simply knowing the fundamentals is a critical differentiator between hobby programmers and so-called professional programmers.
You'll find the rank amateur; the pattern-a-holic; the global variable guy.
That's why The Daily WTF is more depressing than funny to me – we see the same classic mistakes day after day.
posts.key_insight: Most software failures aren't caused by hard problems but by developers who skip the fundamentals—not because they have better ideas, but simply because they felt like doing something different.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that optimism, while seemingly positive, is actually an occupational hazard in software development that leads to project failures. Drawing on Kent Beck and Steve McConnell, he distinguishes between healthy optimism grounded in data and destructive wishful thinking that ignores reality. The post identifies two major optimism-related pitfalls: wishful thinking that undermines planning, and heroic can-do attitudes that prevent honest progress reporting. Atwood advocates for pragmatism over blind optimism, arguing that under-promising and over-delivering is the wiser approach. He concludes that acknowledging the unknown takes more courage than making unrealistic promises.
key_points: Optimism becomes dangerous when it transforms into wishful thinking—hoping something works without reasonable basis for believing it will
Developer heroics and can-do attitudes often prevent honest status reporting, turning minor setbacks into disasters
The real art of managing programmers lies in knowing when to demotivate them and rein in excessive enthusiasm
Wishful thinking at the beginning of a project leads to big blowups at the end and may cause more software problems than all other causes combined
Pragmatism and under-promising while over-delivering is braver and more effective than blind optimism
money_quotes: Optimism is an occupational hazard of programming.
The real art of managing programmers lies in knowing when to demotivate them.
Wishful thinking isn't just optimism. It's closing your eyes and hoping something works when you have no reasonable basis for thinking it will.
Wishful thinking undermines meaningful planning and may be at the root of more software problems than all other causes combined.
Can-do attitudes escalate minor setbacks into true disasters.
Management placed a higher premium on can-do attitudes than on steady and consistent progress and meaningful progress reporting.
It takes a lot more bravery to acknowledge the unknown than it does to blindly promise the world.
posts.key_insight: In software development, the courage to acknowledge uncertainty and deliver honest assessments is far more valuable than the false bravery of optimistic promises.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the emerging dual-core CPU market in 2005, comparing AMD's Athlon 64 X2 against Intel's Pentium 4 D. While celebrating the hardware advances, he warns that multi-core architectures shift the performance burden from hardware to software developers. Single-threaded applications won't automatically benefit from additional cores, meaning programmers must learn to parallelize their code. He predicts four-core CPUs will follow quickly and that eventually throwing money at hardware won't solve performance problems—only rewriting algorithms for parallelism will.
key_points: AMD's Athlon 64 X2 benefits more from dual-core than Pentium 4 due to its on-die memory controller architecture
Single-threaded applications designed for single processors will not benefit from multiple cores without rewriting
Intel's Hyperthreading prepared the market for true dual-core designs
AMD's CEO suggests heterogeneous cores (different core types) as one possible future direction
The challenge is shifting from manufacturing transistors to figuring out what to do with them
money_quotes: Dual core designs are fantastic from a technology standpoint, but as a software developer, it's a scary trend.
If the only way we can increase speed is through extra parallelism (aka threading), our coding and debugging burden just went through the roof.

A single threaded (client) app that has been designed with a single processor and a single thread of execution in mind, will not benefit and therefore users will not benefit from multiple processors or multiple cores.
Making transistors is pretty trivial. We can make hundreds of millions of transistors. Figuring out what the hell to do with those transistors is the challenge.
One day, you won't be able to throw money at your hardware to make your app run faster.
You'll have no choice but to pour that money into parallelizing the algorithms inside your app, which is a far more difficult proposition.
posts.key_insight: The multi-core revolution transfers the burden of performance improvement from hardware engineers to software developers, who must now master the difficult art of parallel programming.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores his extensive history with computer mice, tracing the evolution from early mechanical designs to modern optical wireless models. After experimenting with various high-end mice from Microsoft and Logitech, he concludes that wireless mice aren't worth the added battery weight for his use case. He values lightweight design, a vertical scroll wheel, and thumb buttons above all other features. His recommendation is the Logitech MX518 for its adjustable DPI sensitivity buttons that work without drivers.
key_points: Mouse technology has evolved significantly: better ergonomics, more buttons, scroll wheels, mechanical to optical, and wired to wireless
Early optical mice were inferior to mechanical ones due to sensor limitations, but modern optical mice now deliver 1600 DPI resolution
Wireless mice add prohibitive battery weight - the author strongly prefers lightweight wired mice
The essential mouse features are: lightweight design, vertical scroll wheel, and left/right/back thumb buttons
Hardware-level DPI adjustment (400/800/1600) without drivers is a surprisingly useful feature
money_quotes: I've had something of a mouse fetish over the years, and I've probably owned every significant design revision from Microsoft and Logitech.
I've basically given up on wireless mice.
The mouse cord is a total non-issue for me; I can't recall a time I've ever fought with the cord on any mouse.
But the added battery weight of wireless is prohibitive.
Horizontal scroll wheels? Additional buttons (middle, forward)? Never use 'em.
Please note that any resemblance between me and a freakish obsessive is purely coincidental.
posts.key_insight: When it comes to input devices, simplicity and fundamental ergonomics (like weight) often matter more than feature-rich bells and whistles like wireless connectivity or extra buttons.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the common practice of 'dogfooding' (using your own software product) and argues that while it's clearly beneficial, it's harder to implement effectively than often acknowledged. He identifies three key obstacles: developers aren't representative of typical users, specialized business applications require domain knowledge developers lack, and working with low-quality software you can't control is demoralizing. Despite these challenges, he still advocates for aggressive dogfooding whenever possible, and suggests working the help desk as an alternative way to understand user pain when direct dogfooding isn't feasible.
key_points: Dogfooding works well when developers are the target audience (like Visual Studio or operating systems), but developers are highly unrepresentative of typical users and may add overly complex features
Specialized business applications require domain expertise that developers often lack, making meaningful dogfooding nearly impossible without significant exposure to the target audience
The quality and direction of team projects is often outside individual developers' control, making it psychologically difficult to use software you're not proud of
When direct dogfooding isn't possible, creating dedicated dogfooding test plans with real customers or working the help desk can provide similar user perspective benefits
money_quotes: Trying to use the product, as a customer would, found these showstoppers in a minute.
If the application you're writing isn't intended for expert users, having the developers dogfood it won't necessarily buy you much, because developers are highly unrepresentative of typical users.
Beyond fixing critical bugs, it could even hurt the application: developers tend to add advanced, complicated features that are useful to them.
The only dogfood guaranteed to taste good is your own.

If you are developing a product that you can't integrate into your day to day job then you should be creating a dogfooding test plan where you and interested customers spend dedicated time using the product the way it is meant to be used.
There's nothing quite as effective as sharing the customer's pain.
posts.key_insight: Dogfooding is a valuable practice, but its effectiveness depends heavily on whether developers actually resemble the target users—and when they don't, teams need alternative strategies like dedicated test plans or help desk rotations to gain genuine user perspective.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood highlights Johanna Rothman's catalog of 'Schedule Games' - common dysfunctional patterns in software project scheduling. He connects these to Steve McConnell's classic mistakes, noting the overlap with issues like optimism bias and denial. The post emphasizes that project failure comes from many small traps rather than single catastrophic errors, and that success requires getting almost everything right rather than just avoiding a few mistakes.
key_points: There are at least 11 distinct 'schedule games' or dysfunctional patterns teams play with project timelines
These patterns overlap significantly with McConnell's classic software project mistakes
Optimism, denial, and hope-as-strategy are recurring failure modes in scheduling
Project success depends on doing almost everything right, not just avoiding a few key mistakes
Regular project evaluation is essential to avoid slipping into these scheduling traps
money_quotes: There are a million ways to fail.
It's critical to periodically evaluate your project to make sure you haven't slipped into one of the many traps.
Actually succeeding in a software project depends a whole lot less on not doing a few things wrong but on doing almost everything right.
posts.key_insight: Software project success is not about avoiding a few big mistakes but about consistently executing well across many dimensions, requiring constant vigilance against the numerous scheduling traps teams naturally fall into.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood vents frustration about Windows XP's automatic update feature, which nags users every ten minutes to restart after installing updates. The dialog box cannot be dismissed and offers only 'Restart Now' or 'Restart Later' options, with the latter simply delaying the inevitable by ten minutes. Even killing the responsible process (wuauclt.exe) doesn't work as it respawns. Atwood crowdsources solutions from commenters and presents two fixes: stopping the Automatic Updates service or modifying Group Policy settings to disable the auto-restart nagging.
key_points: Windows XP's update restart dialog is undismissable and reappears every ten minutes if postponed
The wuauclt.exe process responsible for the nagging respawns when killed, like a 'bad zombie movie'
Users can stop the 'Automatic Updates' service temporarily via command line or Services panel
Group Policy settings offer a permanent fix by disabling auto-restart or extending the prompt interval
money_quotes: This is perhaps the Naggiest. Dialog. Box. Ever.
If you click Restart Later, it pops up again ten minutes later, like clockwork.
I tried killing wuauclt.exe, and like a bad zombie movie, it keeps coming back.
I want automatic updates, but I also want to restart my computer when I feel like it.
posts.key_insight: Good security features become user-hostile when they prioritize the system's needs over the user's workflow and autonomy.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores how visual conventions in web design communicate meaning even across language barriers, prompted by a usability experiment that stripped pages to their essential elements. He draws on Steve Krug's 'Don't Make Me Think' to argue that designers often feel pressure to innovate when conventions would serve users better. The post warns that reinventing the wheel rarely produces revolutionary results and usually just wastes time. Atwood concludes that innovation should be reserved for genuinely better ideas, while conventions should be leveraged whenever possible.
key_points: Page layout and formatting conventions are so powerful they communicate meaning even when you can't read the language
Designers are tempted to reinvent conventions because they're hired to be different and because awards rarely recognize 'best use of conventions'
If replacing a convention, the alternative must either have zero learning curve or add enough value to justify one
Most wheel-reinventing just amounts to time spent reinventing the wheel, not revolutionary breakthroughs
The 'never design what you can steal' ethos applies: use existing solutions when they work
money_quotes: I was reminded how powerful conventions can be; the page layout and formatting are strong cues for the content, even if I can't read a word of it.
Faced with the prospect of using a convention, there's a great temptation for designers to reinvent the wheel instead.
Praise from peers, awards, and high profile job offers are rarely based on criteria like 'best use of conventions.'
Sometimes time spent reinventing the wheel results in a revolutionary new rolling device. But sometimes it just amounts to time spent reinventing the wheel.
If you're going to innovate, you have to understand just how much value conventions add.
Innovate when you know you have a better idea (and everyone you show it to says 'Wow!'), but take advantage of conventions when you don't.
posts.key_insight: Conventions are so valuable that you should only break them when your alternative is either immediately obvious or dramatically better—and most innovations are neither.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the tension between code transparency and intellectual property protection in .NET applications. He acknowledges that .NET's JIT compilation makes reverse engineering trivially easy compared to traditional compiled languages. While Microsoft bundled Dotfuscator with Visual Studio to address this concern, Atwood argues that transparency has actually contributed to .NET's success by enabling developers to diagnose problems through decompilation. He concludes that developers should embrace transparency while implementing basic protections, focusing energy on improving their products rather than building elaborate copy protection schemes.
key_points: JIT-compiled languages like .NET carry metadata that makes reverse engineering significantly easier than traditional compiled code
.NET's openness and ease of decompilation has paradoxically contributed to its success and adoption
Obfuscation is only a 'stopgap' that raises the difficulty bar but cannot truly protect client-side code
Commercial component vendors should use basic obfuscation for trial versions but not invest heavily in protection
Frequent meaningful updates beat customers over the head with onerous security measures as an anti-piracy strategy
money_quotes: I believe you can attribute much of .NET's success to its transparency; it's free, easy to obtain, easy to write, and easy to reverse engineer.
Why pay for a component when you can download it, easily decompile it, and comment out the trial restrictions?
.NET's openness is also an indirect compliment to the open source movement, where 'security through obscurity' is a derogatory slur.
You're a lot more likely to beat would-be pirates through frequent, meaningful updates than you are by bothering your customers with increasingly onerous security measures.
I believe it's best to err on the side of transparency. That buys you a lot more in the long run.
I lock the front door of my house, too. Right after I activate my nuclear-powered laser attack robots.
posts.key_insight: Code transparency provides more long-term value than protection; invest in improving your product rather than elaborate copy protection that ultimately fails anyway.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the conventional wisdom that design should precede coding, arguing that for open source projects especially, starting with working code is essential. He quotes Charles Miller's observation that projects beginning with committees and design documents tend to fail, while those starting with functional code attract contributors who actually want to build. The post connects this to the nature of software development as a 'wicked problem' where full understanding only comes through doing. Atwood concludes that while some upfront thinking is valuable, there are rapidly diminishing returns for planning versus execution.
key_points: Open source projects should start with working code, not design committees
Working code attracts developers who want to code; design documents attract people who want to talk about coding
There's a rapid point of diminishing returns for thinking versus doing in software development
Software development is a 'wicked problem' - you can't fully understand it until you've solved it
Establishing credibility in open source requires demonstrable work, not just ideas
money_quotes: Working code attracts people who want to code. Design documents attract people who want to talk about coding.
Starting an open source project with no code and a committee trying to decide what to do next spells inevitable doom.
Good programmers get off their butts.
There's a rapid point of diminishing returns for thinking versus doing.
Software development is a wicked problem: you never fully understand the problem you're trying to solve until you reach the end.
The sooner you start, the better.
posts.key_insight: In software development, working code is more valuable than perfect plans because you can only truly understand a problem by actively solving it.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood weighs in on the stored procedures vs. ad-hoc SQL debate, ultimately siding with simplicity and parameterized SQL as the default approach. While acknowledging that 'it depends,' he argues that starting with stored procedures is premature optimization. He challenges two common pro-stored-procedure arguments: that they provide meaningful abstraction (they don't—they're tightly coupled to the database), and that embedding SQL in code is bad (it's actually good to use domain-specific languages where appropriate). He advocates for web services over stored procedures when true abstraction is needed.
key_points: Starting with stored procedures is premature optimization—parameterized SQL is simpler, safe, and fast
Stored procedures provide only the illusion of abstraction since they're tightly coupled to database schema changes
Web services are far superior to stored procedures for creating abstracted APIs with access control
Embedding domain-specific languages like SQL and regex directly in code should be embraced, not avoided
Every project is different, and actual performance should be measured before drawing conclusions
money_quotes: Writing a bunch of mindless stored procedures to perform every database operation you think you may need is definitely not what I'd call simple.
Stored procedures only provide the illusion of abstraction.
Make a few changes to the tables and your procs are toast – just like parameterized SQL.
Some programmers sneer at the idea of 'naked SQL statements clumsily embedded in other languages.' This is insane.
Use SQL to manipulate set-based data, Regular Expressions to manipulate strings, VB.NET to do COM interop, and C# for bitwise operations.
Why in the world would you write a 3-level deep For..Next loop to manipulate a string when you can express that same logic in 12 characters of regex?
If anything, we should be railing against the stupidity of being limited to a single, general-purpose language!
posts.key_insight: Default to simplicity with parameterized SQL rather than stored procedures, and embrace domain-specific languages like SQL directly in your code instead of hiding behind false abstractions.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the recurring pattern of online communities self-destructing due to their inability to moderate themselves, drawing on Clay Shirky's influential essay and the cautionary tale of Kuro5hin's decline. The post traces this phenomenon back to 1978's Communitree BBS, which was overrun by high school students posting inappropriate content because its founders couldn't reconcile moderation with their open-access ideals. Atwood argues that technical and social problems are inseparable in community management, and that as communities grow, hybrid rules become essential for survival. The piece concludes with practical advice: developers building social software must plan for bad behavior from the start or face inevitable regret.
key_points: Online communities consistently follow a pattern where openness and democratic ideals eventually enable malicious users to drive away the original community
The distinction between 'technical' and 'social' problems in community software is false—they are deeply intertwined and inseparable
Any unmoderated group will eventually debate whether to add moderation, with the probability approaching certainty over time
Communities founded on principles of free speech and open access often lack the philosophical framework to defend themselves from abuse
Social software may not scale as traditionally conceived—growth creates structural demands that challenge founding ideals
money_quotes: Technical and social issues are deeply intertwined. There's no way to completely separate them.
The likelihood that any unmoderated group will eventually get into a flame-war about whether or not to have a moderator approaches one as time increases.
The place that was founded on open access had too much open access, too much openness. They couldn't defend themselves against their own users.
The place that was founded on free speech had too much freedom. They had no way of saying 'No, that's not the kind of free speech we meant.'
People are bastard coated bastards with bastard filling.
If you're building software with social components, plan for the worst kinds of behavior from your users from the start.
posts.key_insight: Every online community will eventually need to develop hybrid technical-social controls to protect itself from its own users, and founders who fail to plan for this inevitability will watch their communities collapse.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores what makes blogs worth reading, building on Rory's framework that bloggers must want to write, believe they have something to say, and say it interestingly. He argues that blogs derive their value from being honest windows into people's passions and interests, offering practical knowledge alongside personal insight. Atwood adds two essential requirements: competent writing skills (developed through practice and reading) and enabling comments. He firmly distinguishes blogging from traditional publishing by emphasizing the two-way communication that defines the medium.
key_points: The three core requirements for blogging: wanting to write, believing you have something to say, and having an interesting way of saying it
Blogs succeed because they provide authentic glimpses into smart people's interests and passions
Writing skill, while difficult to develop, is essential and improves through practice and reading good writers
Comments are non-negotiable—without two-way communication, you're just publishing, not blogging
If blogging feels like work or you're worried about pleasing others, you'll struggle to maintain it
money_quotes: Blogs are interesting because they are honest windows into other people's interests and passions.
If writing a blog entry feels like work to you, or if you're worried about satisfying anyone other than yourself, then you'll have a difficult time maintaining a blog.
A blog without comments is not a blog. Period.
Without the social dialog of feedback, you're merely publishing – and publishing is something newspapers have been doing for hundreds of years.
The next best thing to actually writing is reading a lot of good writers.
If readers of your blog can't politely point out that you're full of crap – then whatever you're writing may be great, but it isn't a blog.
As it turns out, the world is full of fascinating, extremely smart people.
posts.key_insight: What distinguishes a blog from mere publishing is the combination of authentic personal voice and two-way dialogue with readers through comments.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that comparing software development to bridge building is fundamentally flawed because traditional engineering operates within the fixed rules of physics, while software is built on arbitrary human decisions that constantly change. He examines the alternative hypothesis that software is more like mathematics, but rejects this too, noting that math has objectively correct answers while software quality is subjective and requires both technical and aesthetic judgment. Atwood concludes that software development should focus on learning from its own best practices rather than trying to adapt processes from unrelated fields. The post challenges the common industry refrain that software needs to become more like 'real' engineering to mature.
key_points: Bridge building operates under unchanging physical laws while software is built on arbitrary conventions invented by humans in recent decades
More than half of a software developer's knowledge becomes obsolete in ten years, unlike civil engineering fundamentals
Software cannot be objectively verified as correct or good - it requires subjective judgment balancing efficiency and maintainability
The math comparison also fails because math has correct answers while software has many valid approaches to the same outcome
The software industry should focus on disseminating its own best practices rather than borrowing from unrelated disciplines
money_quotes: Software development is only like bridge building if you're building a bridge on the planet Jupiter, out of newly invented materials, using construction equipment that didn't exist five years ago.
God didn't invent x86.
More than half of everything I know will be obsolete in ten years; can any civil engineer say that?
Unlike math, software can't be objectively, formally verified to be correct. Or even good, for that matter.
With coding, the best you can hope for is to do something well.
Disseminating best practices to other developers is our biggest challenge, not adapting processes from unrelated industries.

If you ignore your right brain, and I'm talking generally about abstraction and aesthetics, then you can slap some code together that might work, but it also might be one hell of a maintenance nightmare.
posts.key_insight: Software engineering cannot mature by emulating traditional engineering or mathematics because it operates in a domain of human invention without fixed universal laws, making its own accumulated best practices the most valuable source of improvement.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood analyzes a performance comparison between C# managed code and C++ for a dictionary reader application. While the optimized C++ version eventually runs 2x faster, achieving this required extensive custom implementations of file I/O, string handling, memory allocation, and internationalization. Atwood argues that the managed code achieved excellent results with minimal effort, while the C++ optimizations required a top Microsoft developer to essentially rewrite core functionality. He concludes that when you factor in development time, the C++ 'victory' is pyrrhic, and the cases where such optimization tradeoffs make sense are shrinking.
key_points: Managed C# code performed competitively against C++ with far less development effort
The C++ version required custom implementations of file I/O, strings, allocators, and internationalization to achieve its 2x speedup
Diminishing returns set in well before the C++ version achieved maximum performance advantage
Development time should be factored into performance comparisons, not just execution time
The applications requiring native code optimization represent an increasingly small portion of software development
money_quotes: The managed code achieved a very good result for hardly any effort.
It's a pyrrhic victory once you divide the execution time by the development time of a top Microsoft C++ coder.
Now, for certain applications at the very tip of the development pyramid, this tradeoff may still make sense. But that list of apps gets shorter and shorter with every passing day.
Raymond Chen, who has 'fixed more Windows bugs than you've had hot dinners.'
posts.key_insight: Raw performance wins are meaningless if you ignore the development cost required to achieve them—managed code's 'slower' execution is often the smarter engineering choice.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the counterintuitive value of failure in professional development, drawing on Will Wright's hiring philosophy of preferring candidates with more failures on their resume. He connects this to Edison's famous reframing of failed experiments as discoveries, and a sociologist's research showing that the best surgeons are those who readily acknowledge and learn from their mistakes. The post argues that in software development, where project failure rates are high, the ability to honestly assess and learn from failures is more valuable than a track record of unexamined successes.
key_points: Will Wright actively seeks to hire people who have experienced more failures, viewing it as the best learning system
The difference between successful and unsuccessful surgeons lies not in technical skill but in their attitude toward failure and willingness to acknowledge mistakes
Asking candidates about their worst mistakes is a powerful interview technique - those who claim no mistakes are the worst candidates
Software development's high failure rate means professionals should expect to fail regularly and treat each failure as a learning opportunity
Refusing to acknowledge failures means cheating yourself out of the best professional development opportunities
money_quotes: I'm actually more likely to hire someone based on how many failures they've experienced. I think it's the best learning system.
I have discovered a thousand things that don't work.

Far more than technical skills or intelligence, what was necessary for success was the sort of attitude that Quest has – a practical-minded obsession with the possibility and the consequences of failure.
The people who said, 'Gee, I haven't really had one,' or, 'I've had a couple of bad outcomes but they were due to things outside my control' – invariably those were the worst candidates.
They had the ability to rethink everything that they'd done and imagine how they might have done it differently.
You should always be failing some of the time, and learning from those failures in an honest way.
posts.key_insight: The willingness to honestly acknowledge and learn from failures is a better predictor of professional success than a spotless track record, because those who claim no mistakes lack the self-awareness necessary for growth.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his experience hosting his website on a Microsoft Virtual Server during his move to California. Despite having a Xeon 2.8GHz server, he finds VM performance disappointing, with the system performing like hardware half as powerful. He notes that even without CPU emulation, the overhead of emulating motherboard hardware—especially disk and video—is substantial. The post concludes with practical advice: invest in fast dedicated disks and sufficient memory, though there's no silver bullet for VM performance issues.
key_points: VM performance overhead is much higher than expected, effectively halving the apparent CPU speed
Hardware emulation costs are substantial even when not emulating the x86 processor itself
Disk and video emulation are particularly expensive operations
Standard optimization advice applies: fast dedicated disks and adequate memory
The convenience of VMs comes with a significant performance tradeoff
money_quotes: I am disappointed with VM performance
Under actual use, it performs more like a 1.4ghz Pentium 4 – the older version with only 512kb L2 cache!
VMs are great for convenience, but the performance cost is quite a bit higher than I expected it to be – on both client and server.
Even if you aren't emulating the x86 processor, the cost of emulating the motherboard hardware is clearly substantial.
No silver bullet there; get the fastest disks you can afford, dedicate them to VMs, and make sure you have enough memory.
posts.key_insight: Virtual machine convenience comes at a steep performance cost, with hardware emulation overhead cutting effective performance roughly in half even on capable server hardware.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the ironic fall of John Dvorak, a pioneering tech columnist who now finds himself overshadowed by the very bloggers he dismisses. Dvorak, who was essentially blogging before blogs existed, has become a cranky critic of the medium while losing relevance to it. Atwood quotes Dvorak's dismissive characterization of influential bloggers as an insular 'groupthink' community creating 'A-bombs' of self-referential content. The post highlights how the web has inverted traditional power structures, leaving even prolific early contributors like Dvorak buried in search results behind newer voices.
key_points: Dvorak was essentially a blogger before blogging existed, having contributed content to the web since its earliest days
Despite being frequently wrong in his predictions (like dismissing the Mac mouse), Dvorak's candor made him compelling to read
Dvorak now dismisses influential bloggers as an insular community of 500 people engaged in 'groupthink'
Google search results show Dvorak has been surpassed in relevance by the bloggers he criticizes
The web enables rapid inversions of power where pioneers can be eclipsed by those who follow
money_quotes: John Dvorak was the archetypal cranky blogger, way before blogging was even a glint in Dave Winer's eye.
One thing is for sure: all those damn blogging kids need to get the hell off Dvorak's lawn.
It's fascinating how the web can cause these amazing inversions of power.

A guy like Dvorak who 'has been pounding the keyboard since the day the World Wide Web came online, and was one of the first and most prolific contributors of ongoing content to the Web' won't even rate the first page of your search results.
I have always enjoyed his candor and attitude despite the fact that he has been so wrong, so many times.
posts.key_insight: The web's democratization of publishing can rapidly invert influence hierarchies, leaving even pioneering voices eclipsed by the communities they dismiss.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines a Cornell psychology paper on the Dunning-Kruger effect, which shows that incompetent people lack the metacognitive skills to recognize their own incompetence. The research found that people scoring in the bottom quartile vastly overestimated their abilities, placing themselves in the 62nd percentile when they were actually in the 12th. Atwood connects this to software development, noting parallels with wicked problems, iterative development, and extreme skill disparities in programming. He argues that mocking bad code on sites like The Daily WTF is amusing but ultimately unhelpful, since incompetent developers remain unaware of their deficiencies. The real challenge is bootstrapping developers' skills to a level where they can recognize and correct their own mistakes.
key_points: The same skills needed to produce competent work are the same skills needed to recognize competent work—lacking one means lacking both
Research shows people in the bottom quartile dramatically overestimate their abilities, thinking they're above average
This paradox manifests in software development through wicked problems, iterative development needs, and extreme skill disparities
Improving someone's skills also improves their ability to recognize their previous limitations
Mocking incompetent code is entertaining but doesn't solve the underlying problem of skill deficiency
money_quotes: The skills that enable one to construct a grammatical sentence are the same skills necessary to recognize a grammatical sentence, and thus are the same skills necessary to determine if a grammatical mistake has been made.
To lack the former is to be deficient in the latter.
Participants scoring in the bottom quartile on tests of humor, grammar, and logic grossly overestimated their test performance and ability.
Although their test scores put them in the 12th percentile, they estimated themselves to be in the 62nd.
Paradoxically, improving the skills of participants, and thus increasing their metacognitive competence, helped them recognize the limitations of their abilities.
The worst software developers are profoundly bad; the best software developers are absurdly good.
Making fun of incompetence is amusing, but until these developers' skills are bootstrapped to a moderate level, they're going to keep pounding out more and more WTFs.
posts.key_insight: Incompetent developers can't recognize their own incompetence because the skills required to write good code are the same skills required to evaluate code quality—making education, not ridicule, the only path forward.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood highlights Rico Mariani's 'Peanut Butter Sandwich Test' for evaluating whether technical advice is actually useful or just stating the obvious. The test suggests that if you can substitute 'peanut butter sandwich' for the technical subject and the advice still makes sense, the advice is too generic to be valuable. Atwood then points readers to Rico's more prescriptive, actionable guidance on Windows performance monitor counters for .NET applications, along with additional resources on .NET memory performance counters.
key_points: The 'Peanut Butter Sandwich Test' exposes advice that is too generic to be useful - if the advice works equally well for peanut butter sandwiches, it's not specific enough
Good technical documentation should be prescriptive rather than generic
Effective performance troubleshooting guidance should include specific counter values and what healthy values look like
Links to concrete resources on Windows performance counters and .NET memory performance counters as examples of actionable advice
money_quotes: Use a cache where it provides benefits, and compare with, Use a peanut butter sandwich where it provides benefits. Both seem to work... that's a bad sign.
Yes what you are saying is true of [the system] but it's also true of peanut butter sandwiches.
Sometimes they say things that are so obvious as to be uninteresting.
posts.key_insight: Technical advice that could apply equally well to peanut butter sandwiches is too generic to be useful - good guidance must be specific and prescriptive.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares an archived page showing Google's original server infrastructure from 1999, marveling at how modest it was. The entire setup consisted of a handful of dual-processor Pentium II and UltraSparc servers with less than 2GB of total RAM and 366GB of storage. Some components were held together with Duplo blocks. This humble collection of hardware would grow to dominate the internet within just five years.
key_points: Google's entire 1999 infrastructure had only 1792MB of RAM, 366GB of storage, and 10 CPUs totaling under 3GHz
The hardware included consumer-grade components like Duplo blocks holding together a SCSI drive array
This modest setup grew to 'own the internet' in approximately five years
The mixed hardware included Pentium II, PowerPC, and UltraSparc processors
money_quotes: I don't know what's more impressive, the two dual Pentium II 300 servers, or the 90gb SCSI drive array made from Duplo blocks.
This motley bunch of hardware grew to own the internet in a little more than five years. That's incredible.
Humble beginnings, indeed.
posts.key_insight: World-changing technology companies can emerge from remarkably humble hardware beginnings, proving that vision and execution matter more than initial resources.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the legacy of Dani Bunten Berry, a pioneering game designer who championed multiplayer gaming long before the internet age. Berry, creator of the influential 1983 game M.U.L.E. and arguably the first commercial network multiplayer game Modem Wars, believed computers should foster social connection rather than isolation. Atwood notes the irony that Berry's vision of computers as social tools has been fully realized through the modern internet, with its constant connectivity through messaging, blogs, and other communication services. Tragically, Berry died in 1998, just before the internet explosion validated her design philosophy.
key_points: Dani Bunten Berry was a visionary game designer who prioritized multiplayer and social gaming in an era when single-player experiences dominated
M.U.L.E. (1983) was groundbreaking for its 4-player multiplayer design, requiring strategy over reflexes at a time when arcade games favored simple joystick skills
Modem Wars was arguably the first commercially released network multiplayer game, though it failed due to low modem adoption in 1988
The modern internet has transformed computers from solitary devices into inherently social tools, validating Berry's vision
Berry's famous aphorism about not wishing for more time alone with computers reflected her belief in technology as a medium for human connection
money_quotes: No one ever said on their deathbed, 'Gee, I wish I had spent more time alone with my computer.'
Programming has been described as 'a private love letter between the developer and the hardware.'
The explosive growth of the internet makes me wonder if any developer can truly be alone while using a computer today.

It's hard to imagine using a computer – any computer – without being immersed in instant messaging, blogs, message boards, email, audio/video meetups, and a dozen other 'always on' communication services.
It's unfortunate that she didn't live to see the internet fully expose the computer as the social tool she so clearly wanted it to be.
posts.key_insight: The pioneers who designed for human connection over technological isolation were vindicated by the internet's transformation of computers into fundamentally social tools.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares a recursive implementation of Page.FindControl for ASP.NET 2.0, addressing a gap in Microsoft's framework. Despite improvements in VS.NET 2005 over its predecessor, he notes that Microsoft still hasn't provided a built-in recursive option for finding nested controls. The post provides a simple recursive function by Oddur Magnusson that traverses the control hierarchy. Atwood also reveals an undocumented feature allowing colon-separated paths to locate controls directly.
key_points: Microsoft's Page.FindControl lacks a recursive overload, making it difficult to find nested controls
A simple recursive function can traverse the control hierarchy to find controls within containers like DataView or DataList
An undocumented feature allows using colon-separated paths (e.g., 'DataList1:_ctl0:TextBox3') to locate controls directly
VS.NET 2005 represents a significant improvement over VS.NET 2003, but still has gaps
money_quotes: I was mildly surprised to find that Microsoft still hasn't added a recursive overload for Page.FindControl.
This makes life much easier when you're trying to get to controls that are themselves contained within other containers.
Would it have killed Microsoft to add an overloaded, recursive version of this?
One interesting semi-undocumented feature of Page.FindControl: you can specify a full 'path' to a control using colons as separators.
posts.key_insight: Simple utility functions that framework vendors neglect to include often become necessary workarounds that developers must implement and share themselves.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores Paul Graham's provocative claim that design patterns indicate language deficiencies, using Lisp as the prime example. The post examines Peter Norvig's finding that most Gang of Four patterns become invisible in Lisp due to its malleability. Atwood engages with a wiki discussion proposing that design patterns are actually how programming languages evolve, similar to how linguistic abbreviations become standard words. While gently mocking Graham's sometimes condescending tone about Lisp superiority, Atwood ultimately agrees that excessive pattern reliance signals language limitations and that languages evolve slowly by absorbing common patterns into native features.
key_points: Peter Norvig found that 16 of 23 Gang of Four design patterns are 'invisible or simpler' in Lisp, suggesting patterns compensate for language limitations
Design patterns may represent the evolutionary mechanism for programming languages, similar to how contractions became standard English words
Even constructs we take for granted today (if-then-else, do-while) were once design patterns that became language features
Lisp's continued relevance despite being one of the oldest languages demonstrates how slow language evolution actually is
The .NET framework's evolution with constructs like 'Using', Nullable, and Generics shows patterns being absorbed into language syntax
money_quotes: When I see patterns in my programs, I consider it a sign of trouble.
The shape of a program should reflect only the problem it needs to solve.
Any other regularity in the code is a sign, to me at least, that I'm using abstractions that aren't powerful enough.
Has anyone ever considered that design patterns are the way that programming languages evolve?
One wonders, then, why Lisp hasn't become sentient and taken over the world by now.
Excessive reliance on design patterns is indicative of failings in the language.
Lisp is so malleable that design patterns immediately become part of the base language; they are indistinguishable from the original core language constructs.
posts.key_insight: Design patterns are not timeless best practices but rather symptoms of language limitations that eventually get absorbed into more expressive languages as native features.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores alternative keyboard input devices, prompted by a Tom's Hardware review of the Ergodex DX1. He reflects on the limitations of the standard QWERTY keyboard, which has remained essentially unchanged for 138 years despite technological advances. While showcasing various alternative input peripherals he's tried, Atwood argues that these devices work best as complements to traditional keyboards rather than replacements. He attributes this to the nearly insurmountable challenge of overcoming ingrained muscle memory, the same problem that has plagued the Dvorak keyboard layout.
key_points: The standard QWERTY keyboard has remained the dominant input device for 138 years despite its limitations
Alternative input devices like gaming keypads and customizable keyboards have consistently failed to replace traditional keyboards
Muscle memory from years of keyboard use creates an almost insurmountable barrier to adopting radically different input methods
The most practical approach for alternative input devices is to complement existing keyboards rather than replace them
Flexibility and customization, as offered by the Ergodex DX1, may be the key to making alternative input devices actually useful
money_quotes: The struggle to defeat a decade of existing keyboard muscle memory is a losing battle
So far, technology hasn't provided us with the ability to customize a hardware interface that meets our individual needs
Thus, we are usually forced to retreat to the 138-year-old quintessence of ubiquity: Christopher Sholes' 'QWERTY' keyboard
These keyboard alternatives don't replace but complement your existing keyboard by presenting an optimized subset of what you already know
It's really the same problem the Dvorak keyboard layout has always had
posts.key_insight: Alternative input devices succeed not by trying to replace the keyboard entirely, but by complementing it with optimized, specialized controls that work alongside ingrained muscle memory.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares a visual family tree tracing the evolution of video game controllers from 1980 to 2005. He reflects on how input devices have transformed from simple single-stick, single-button designs to complex dual-analog configurations. The post highlights the mid-1990s transition from 2D to 3D gaming as the catalyst for the most significant controller evolution. Atwood notes curiosities like keypad controllers and the Jaguar's adoption of this design, while pointing out the tree's incompleteness.
key_points: Game controllers evolved from single stick and button to dual analog axes over 25 years
The shift from 2D to 3D games around 1997 drove the most significant controller design changes
Keypad-equipped controllers were a quirky design convention that persisted through the Atari Jaguar
The visual family tree documents the lineage and influence between different console controllers
money_quotes: Remember when anything you could possibly imagine could be controlled with a single stick and a single button?
The most profound change – single digital axis to dual analog axes – was prompted by the broad switch from 2D to 3D games around 1997.
I had half forgotten how weird the controllers with keypads were, and that the Atari Jaguar was the last console to adopt this oddball convention.
posts.key_insight: The evolution of game controllers directly mirrors the evolution of games themselves, with the jump to 3D gameplay forcing the most dramatic rethinking of how humans interact with virtual worlds.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood comments on Apple's announced switch to Intel chips, noting the irony given the dominance of x86 architecture after Windows NT shed support for other architectures. He highlights that beyond the CPU switch, macOS has serious performance problems of its own, citing AnandTech benchmarks showing Apache and MySQL running 5-10 times slower on macOS than Linux on identical hardware. The post suggests these issues stem from macOS's hybrid kernel architecture that prevents applications from accessing kernel-level threads effectively.
key_points: Apple's switch to Intel marks another victory for x86 architecture dominance, following NT's abandonment of MIPS, Alpha, and PPC
macOS server performance was shown to be 5-10x slower than Linux on identical hardware for Apache and MySQL workloads
The performance problems stem from macOS's awkward hybrid kernel design combining Mach microkernel with FreeBSD in a way that limits threading access
The x86 architecture may become so dominant that future architectures will need to emulate it
money_quotes: After seeing NT slowly shed its MIPS, Alpha, and PPC versions, you have to wonder: will our children be using architectures that emulate some form of x86?
Apple's OSX has some performance issues of its own to resolve
