The whole 'multi-threaded Mach microkernel trapped inside a monolithic FreeBSD cocoon with several threading wrappers and coarse-grained threading access to the kernel,' with a 'backwards compatibility' millstone around its neck sounds like a bad fusion recipe for performance.
posts.key_insight: Apple's switch to Intel highlights x86's growing dominance, but the CPU change won't fix macOS's deeper architectural performance problems.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the diminishing returns of file compression algorithms, sparked by his experience compressing Windows XP virtual machine images. He found that 7zip produced files 17% smaller than WinZip but took 7.3 times longer to complete. Using benchmark data, he demonstrates that beyond an optimal compression level, performance degrades dramatically while gains become marginal. The post concludes that the right compression choice depends on how often data is compressed, how many times it's transferred, and network speed—there's no universal best algorithm.
key_points: Beyond a certain compression level, performance falls off a cliff with rapidly diminishing returns
Compression is fundamentally a zero-sum game: you can have efficiency or speed, but not both
The decision of which compression level to use depends on compression frequency, transfer count, and network speed
File-type specific compression and solid archiving can dramatically outperform generic compression
Most compression benchmarks focus on ratios while ignoring the extreme time costs of maximum compression
money_quotes: Beyond a certain optimal compression level, performance falls off a cliff.
I do end up with a file that is 17% smaller, but it takes 7.3 times longer. That sure doesn't seem like a very good deal to me.
When it takes 5 times longer for barely 8% more compression, you've fallen off a cliff.
Compression is the zero-sum game it really is: you can either have efficiency, or you can have speed.
If you're holding out for Hyper Turbo Extreme Compression X algorithm, you may be waiting a while.
Some of the more extreme space-optimized compression algorithms can take several hours to compress the same file!
posts.key_insight: Compression algorithms exhibit severe diminishing returns past an optimal point, and choosing the right compression level requires weighing time costs against actual transfer and storage needs rather than chasing maximum compression ratios.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the phenomenon of Flickr's popularity, attributing its success to its open API that enables a thriving ecosystem of creative third-party tools and hacks. He showcases various community-built applications that leverage Flickr's tagging system and social features. While impressed by the quality of amateur photography on the platform, he criticizes Flickr's lack of a rating system that would help surface the best content. The post serves as an early case study in how open APIs can drive platform adoption and innovation.
key_points: Flickr's success stems from its open API enabling community-driven innovation
The tagging system is central to Flickr's functionality and powers most third-party tools
Amateur photographers produce surprisingly high-quality work that rivals professionals
The platform lacks a rating system to surface the best content, forcing users to browse by tags or users they already know
Social features like favorites create mappable relationship networks between users
money_quotes: It's truly astonishing; a case study in what having an open API and community-driven content can do for your business.
Flickr is all about tags.
Amateur photographers take far better pictures, on the whole, than I could have ever possibly imagined.
After seeing this, who needs professional photographers?
My biggest frustration with Flickr is that there's no rating system for the pictures.
I'd rather just subscribe to some feed of highly rated pictures.
posts.key_insight: Open APIs transform products into platforms by enabling community innovation that no single company could create alone.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his most frequently used Visual Studio .NET keyboard shortcuts and encourages readers to improve their own keyboard proficiency. He lists essential shortcuts for debugging, navigation, and searching, then points to resources for discovering more shortcuts. He also created a macro that generates a comprehensive keyboard shortcut reference grouped by scope and sorted by key, and provides a diff showing what changed between VS.NET 2003 and 2005.
key_points: Mastering core keyboard shortcuts for debugging (F5, F10, F11) and navigation (F12, Ctrl+minus) significantly improves development workflow
The incremental find shortcut (Ctrl+I) is highlighted as particularly valuable
A custom macro can generate a personalized keyboard shortcut reference that's more useful than published documentation
Comparing keyboard shortcuts between IDE versions helps developers stay current with their tools
money_quotes: I've been trying to improve my use of keyboard shortcuts in Visual Studio .NET.
Incremental find (it's pure sex!)
But enough about me. Which keyboard shortcuts do you use most often?
You can generate a better keyboard reference yourself using my improved keyboard shortcut enumerator macro.
Unless you are a total Visual Studio Ninja I guarantee you'll find at least a few keyboard shortcuts in there that you didn't know about.
posts.key_insight: Investing time in learning and cataloging your IDE's keyboard shortcuts pays dividends in daily productivity, and building your own reference tools can be more valuable than relying on standard documentation.

posts.summary: Atwood expresses frustration at mysterious zero-byte IO.SYS and MSDOS.SYS files that keep reappearing in his Windows root directory despite repeated deletion. These legacy DOS files serve as unwanted reminders of older operating systems he no longer runs. The brief post captures the annoyance of dealing with vestigial system artifacts that refuse to stay deleted.
key_points: Zero-byte IO.SYS and MSDOS.SYS files mysteriously reappear in the root directory after deletion
These files are legacy artifacts from DOS/Win9x era that shouldn't exist on modern Windows
The persistence of these phantom files triggers mild OCD-like frustration
money_quotes: No matter how many times I delete these spurious DOS-related files, the next time I look – they're back
It's a gentle reminder of the Bad Old Days
What's next? AUTOEXEC.BAT and CONFIG.SYS? HIMEM.SYS and optimizing my high memory area? Argh.
posts.key_insight: Legacy system artifacts have a way of haunting modern operating systems, serving as persistent reminders of computing's messy evolutionary history.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood tackles the frustrating problem of copying code from Visual Studio .NET, which uses Rich Text Format instead of HTML. He explores existing workarounds using Word automation to convert clipboard content, but finds the resulting HTML unacceptably bloated and broken. Rather than accept this heavyweight solution, he builds his own RTF-to-HTML converter that directly parses the clipboard content. Along the way, he discovers that VS.NET's RTF clipboard format inexplicably omits background color information entirely, explaining a long-standing limitation.
key_points: Visual Studio .NET copies code to clipboard in RTF format rather than HTML, causing formatting issues when pasting elsewhere
Word automation can convert RTF to HTML but produces bloated, non-standard markup with artifacts like empty <o:p> tags
Building a custom RTF-to-HTML converter using regex parsing proved more effective than relying on Word's 'filtered' HTML output
VS.NET's RTF clipboard format contains background color formatting codes but no actual color table entries for them, a puzzling oversight
The solution involves parsing RTF color tables and converting RTF formatting tags to equivalent HTML span styles
money_quotes: If you were expecting something like standard HTML, think again, bucko!
Why use a hammer when you can use a frickin' ten ton wrecking ball?!
If this is Word's idea of 'filtered' HTML, I'd hate to see the unfiltered version.
I'm all for using ten ton wrecking balls, but not when they er... wreck stuff!
Why not convert the native RTF on the clipboard to HTML myself and cut out the middleman?
The actual background formatting codes are there, but there are absolutely no entries in the RTF color table for them. Weird.
posts.key_insight: When standard tools produce unacceptable output, sometimes the best solution is to understand the underlying format and build something simpler yourself.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines a 2004 survey of self-installable blogging software that revealed a striking imbalance: while numerous PHP/MySQL solutions existed, .NET had only one or two options. He quotes Owen Winkler's criticism that .NET developers were so enamored with the language itself that they neglected user experience, installation simplicity, and practical functionality. Atwood acknowledges the criticism has merit, even after installing Community Server (the successor to .Text) himself. The post reflects early tensions between developer-focused elegance and user-focused pragmatism in the .NET ecosystem.
key_points: .NET had remarkably few self-installable blogging solutions compared to the PHP ecosystem in 2004
Critics argued .NET developers prioritized 'pretty code' over usability, installation simplicity, and whether software actually worked
Even .Text, the primary .NET option, was criticized as too complicated for average users to install and use
Atwood admits the criticism is valid based on his own experience with Community Server
The post highlights a recurring tension between developer sophistication and end-user accessibility
money_quotes: The only thing that .net developers seem to care about is pretty code/pretty language. They don't care if the software works. They don't care if the software is easily installed.
Who cares about the applications you can create with it as long as you have this wonderful language to create them with.
This leads me to my final conclusion that .net developers (especially those coding blog software) just don't 'get it.'
I'm sure in a few years after everyone is done cooing over .net itself, .net developers will start to focus back on the user instead of how nice their new toy is.
The code in WordPress is admittedly a bit messy. But any coder worth his salt can easily figure it out.
.Text is the only blogware that anyone suggested for the Windows platform.
posts.key_insight: Developer infatuation with elegant languages and frameworks can come at the expense of building software that regular users can actually install and use.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses the Pontiac Aztek as a cautionary tale about design by committee, illustrating how a promising concept can be destroyed by too many compromises. The Aztek was actually ahead of its time as a crossover SUV, but committee decisions, cost-cutting, and the requirement to use an existing minivan platform produced a notoriously ugly vehicle. Atwood connects this to software development through Don Norman's observation that good design requires a dictator, not a committee. The post notes that Steve McConnell references the Aztek five times in Code Complete 2 as shorthand for compromised design. The core message is that quality must be planned from the beginning—you can't test your way from an Aztek to a Rolls-Royce.
key_points: Design by committee produces least-common-denominator results because everyone's input leads to endless compromises
The Pontiac Aztek was actually a good concept (crossover SUV) that arrived before the trend, but poor execution killed it
Cost-cutting decisions like using an existing minivan platform destroyed the original design proportions
Good software design requires a coherent vision from a single authority, not democratic consensus
Quality must be designed in from the start—testing cannot transform a fundamentally compromised design into excellence
money_quotes: You don't do good software design by committee. You do it best by having a dictator.
By the time it was done, it came out as this horrible, least-common-denominator vehicle where everyone said, 'How could you put that on the road?'
If you start the process with designs for a Pontiac Aztek, you can test it all you want to, and it will never turn into a Rolls-Royce.
Design would be by committee, the focus groups extensive. And production would have to stick to a tight budget, with all that sex appeal packed onto an existing minivan platform.
It looks the way Montezuma's revenge feels.
The Aztek is uniquely American. And too often, so is the least common denominator result of design by committee.
posts.key_insight: Coherent design requires unified vision and authority; committee-driven compromise transforms promising concepts into lowest-common-denominator failures that no amount of testing can redeem.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares Brad Wardell's story as evidence that success doesn't require talent or privilege. Wardell taught himself C programming from a single book and created Galactic Civilizations despite having no formal programming education. Despite being rejected by major companies due to attending a 'minor college,' working three jobs during school, and being cheated out of royalties by his publisher, Wardell built Stardock into a successful company. By age 24, he was a millionaire. The post uses this rags-to-riches narrative to argue that determination and hard work matter more than intelligence, education, or natural ability.
key_points: Wardell learned to program entirely from two books ('Teach Yourself C in 21 Days' and 'OS/2 PM Programming') and shipped a successful game using only techniques from those books
Major companies wouldn't consider his resume because he attended Western Michigan University instead of a prestigious school like University of Michigan
Despite being cheated out of royalties on his first successful game, he leveraged the publicity to build his company
Working three jobs during college hurt his GPA but gave him time to develop products that launched his career
The narrative frames success as primarily a matter of effort and persistence rather than talent, intelligence, or circumstances
money_quotes: The only problem was that I didn't know how to program. So I bought a book called 'Teach yourself C in 21 days' and a book called 'OS/2 Presentation Manager Programming.' With those two books I programmed Galactic Civilizations.

What that meant is that all the 'graphics' were merely iconic windows, not real images because OS/2 PM programming didn't include chapters on how to do graphics programming and I couldn't afford any more books.
I later learned from a friend who worked at Ratheon at the time that these big companies won't look at resumes from minor colleges like WMU.
If you bought a copy of Galactic Civilizations, that's cool but I never got a penny of it.
I'm not particularly intelligent. I don't enjoy working any more than anyone else. But I did what I had to do.
So what's the excuse of able bodied people who are poor? If I can make it, why can't others?
posts.tone: inspirational, bootstrap-mentality, dismissive of structural barriers
posts.key_insight: The post presents a survivor's narrative that attributes success entirely to personal effort while acknowledging but ultimately dismissing systemic obstacles like educational elitism and exploitative business practices.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines Cory Doctorow's decision to release his novel under a Creative Commons Developing Nations License, which makes the work free in low-income countries while charging in wealthier ones. This leads Atwood to explore the broader concept of zone pricing across industries—from Microsoft charging $299 for Office in the US versus $38 in Thailand, to pharmaceutical companies and oil industry practices. While acknowledging that giving developing countries a break is beneficial, Atwood questions whether such pricing strategies can survive in an era of global communication where consumers can easily find cheaper alternatives across zones.
key_points: Cory Doctorow's Developing Nations License creates a two-tier system: public domain for medium/low income countries, paid for everyone else
Zone pricing exists across many industries—software, pharmaceuticals, and gasoline—with companies charging different prices based on location and market factors
The US effectively subsidizes pharmaceutical R&D because drug prices are higher domestically than in other countries
Zone pricing systems that restrict goods movement, like DVD region coding, are increasingly ineffective
Global communication and internet commerce undermine traditional geographic price discrimination
money_quotes: Microsoft charges US customers $299 for a copy of Microsoft Office, and Thai customers $38.
On any given day, in any major U.S. city, a single brand of gasoline will sell for a wide range of prices even when the cost to make and deliver the fuel is the same.
Giving developing countries a break is clearly a good thing. But I wonder if these zone pricing strategies will continue to work in an era of increasing global communication.
Nobody's going to die if they can't fill up their Hummer H2 with premium. Or afford a copy of Microsoft Train Simulator 2006.
Any system that expressly prohibits goods from moving from zone to zone – like DVD region coding – doesn't work so well.
posts.key_insight: Geographic price discrimination may be well-intentioned for developing nations, but the internet's borderless nature makes such zone-based pricing strategies increasingly difficult to enforce.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains the technical significance of high dynamic range (HDR) lighting in video games, covering the nVidia 7800 launch event. He describes how traditional 32-bit color limits brightness to only 256 levels, vastly underrepresenting both real-world light (10^12 to 1 range) and human eye capability (1000 to 1). By moving to 64-bit values with 16 bits each for color and brightness, HDR enables dramatically more realistic rendering. Atwood quotes nVidia's chief scientist who calls HDR 'the single most significant change in visual quality' since shading, and compares the improvement to the jump from dial-up to broadband.
key_points: Traditional 32-bit color offers only 256 brightness levels, far below the human eye's 1000:1 dynamic range or reality's 10^12:1 range
HDR uses 64-bit values to provide 16 bits each for color precision and brightness intensity
LCD displays of the era had only 5-6 bits of brightness resolution, making HDR benefits invisible on them
The GeForce 7 was the first generation capable of delivering HDR without major performance penalties
Games without HDR look 'flat and featureless' with no highs and no shadow detail
money_quotes: Those 8 bits per color also represent intensity. That means the brightest white is 255, 255, 255 – only 256 times brighter than the blackest black.
I think that High Dynamic Range Lighting is going to be the single most significant change in the visual quality over the next couple of years. It's almost as big as shading.
Games without HDR look flat. They should, since they are only using a range of 256:1 in brightness – a small fraction of what our eyes can see.
It's like going from dial-up to broadband; you don't really know what you've been missing until you've seen it in action.
TANSTAAFL: increasing the data payload from 32-bit to 64-bit hurts performance.
posts.key_insight: HDR lighting represents a fundamental shift in visual quality because traditional color depth drastically underrepresents the brightness range that both reality and human eyes can handle.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores why programmers consistently produce poor user interfaces, using Battlefield 2's pregame UI as a launching point. He argues that while GUI builders make interface creation appear simple, truly great UIs cannot be automated or generated. The core problem is that programmers are trained to think code-first rather than user-first, starting with command-line programs in university that emphasize algorithms over usability. Atwood advocates for 'UI First Development' where the interface is designed before implementation begins, though he notes this approach remains uncommon in the industry.
key_points: GUI builders create a false impression that UI design is easy, when in fact great UIs require human creativity that cannot be automated
Server-side programming is actually easier to master because it's well-defined and repetitive, making it automatable
Computer science education trains programmers to think code-first through command-line programs, leaving them unprepared for UI design
The tools for creating UIs work well for simple cases but become inadequate for complex user scenarios
Designing the UI first before writing code is essential for end-user software but rarely practiced
money_quotes: They might be able to generate a rudimentary GUI, but a really GREAT GUI cannot be automated.

When you're working on end-user software, and it doesn't matter if you're working on a web app, adding a feature to an existing application, or working on a plug-in for some other application, you need to design the UI first.
That's the secret of server-side programming: it is very well-defined and repetitive. Thus, it can be automated.
We learned how to implement efficient algorithms for common computer science problems, but we never learned how to design a good UI.
Forms designers are great when you're working within the problem domain that forms are intended to solve, but once you step outside those problem domains, the work gets much harder.
posts.key_insight: Great user interfaces cannot be automated or code-generated—they require deliberate upfront design by people who think about users first and implementation second.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the broken window theory and its application to software development, drawing from the original 1982 Atlantic Monthly article and the Pragmatic Programmers' adaptation. He examines Philip Zimbardo's famous experiments showing how abandoned cars in both the Bronx and Palo Alto were eventually vandalized once initial signs of neglect appeared. The post argues that the mere perception of disorder triggers a negative feedback loop that can lead to complete system breakdown. Atwood concludes that programmers must sweat the small stuff because seemingly minor issues signal that no one cares, accelerating decay.
key_points: Neglect accelerates software rot faster than any other factor - leaving 'broken windows' unrepaired signals that no one cares about the codebase
The perception of disorder, not just actual disorder, triggers the negative feedback loop that leads to system breakdown
Even 'respectable' people will participate in destruction once communal barriers and the sense of mutual regard are lowered
The broken window effect applies universally - it happened in wealthy Palo Alto just as it did in the Bronx, once the initial damage occurred
Programming's extreme detail-orientation exists because losing control of small things creates the perception that everything is out of control
money_quotes: One unrepaired broken window is a signal that no one cares, and so breaking more windows costs nothing.
Neglect accelerates the rot faster than any other factor.
Untended property becomes fair game for people out for fun or plunder and even for people who ordinarily would not dream of doing such things.
The car in Palo Alto sat untouched for more than a week. Then Zimbardo smashed part of it with a sledgehammer. Soon, passersby were joining in.
If you're not on top of the details, the perception is that things are out of control, and it's only a matter of time before your project spins out of control.
Maybe we should be sweating the small stuff.
Don't leave broken windows unrepaired. Fix each one as soon as it is discovered.
posts.key_insight: Small signs of neglect in code signal that no one cares, triggering a psychological feedback loop where further degradation becomes acceptable and inevitable.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood humorously explores the widespread misuse of Comic Sans MS, a font he and his wife have turned into a running joke after spotting it in inappropriate contexts. He references the Ban Comic Sans movement, which argues that typefaces carry inherent meaning and using Comic Sans for serious purposes is like wearing a clown costume to a formal event. The post includes Vincent Connare's defense that he designed the font specifically for Microsoft Bob's cartoon dog character, never intending it for broader use. Atwood concludes by blaming Microsoft Bob for unleashing this typographic plague and jokes about the inevitable arrival of 'Comic Serif.'
key_points: Typefaces convey meaning independent of the words they display, making font choice a critical design decision
Comic Sans was designed for a narrow purpose (children's software with cartoon characters) but was inappropriately adopted for widespread use
The misuse of Comic Sans has become so prevalent that it spawned an organized movement to ban it
Microsoft Bob continues to be a source of regrettable software artifacts
money_quotes: Like the tone of a spoken voice, the characteristics of a typeface convey meaning. The design of the typeface is, in itself, its voice.
It is analogous to showing up for a black tie event in a clown costume.
There was no intention to include the font in other applications other than those designed for children when I designed Comic Sans.
The inspiration came at the shock of seeing Times New Roman used in an inappropriate way.
Proving, yet again, that anything which came from Microsoft Bob cannot be good.
At this point, we're bracing for the inevitable Comic Serif.
posts.key_insight: Fonts carry inherent communicative weight beyond their literal text, and using them inappropriately undermines your message—a lesson the world has aggressively failed to learn with Comic Sans.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains how to work around the artificial limitations Microsoft placed on IIS 5.1 in Windows XP Professional. The post celebrates the upcoming VS.NET 2005 integrated webserver that will eliminate IIS dependencies, while providing practical workarounds for developers stuck with VS.NET 2003. He details how to increase the 10 concurrent connection limit to 40 and how to run multiple root websites despite Microsoft's restrictions. The post reflects frustration with Microsoft's aggressive crippling of developer tools on consumer operating systems.
key_points: VS.NET 2005's integrated webserver eliminates painful IIS dependencies and installation issues
Windows XP's IIS 5.1 is artificially limited to one root website and 10 concurrent connections
The connection limit can be increased to 40 (hard maximum) using adsutil command-line scripts
Multiple root websites can be created via command-line hacks or GUI utilities, though only one can be active at a time
These artificial limits interfere with legitimate localhost development work
money_quotes: Windows XP's IIS 5.1 is thoroughly crippled out of the box.
Microsoft really, really doesn't want us hosting slashdot.org on our XP Pro box.
These limits are so aggressive that they can get in the way of legitimate localhost development.
Good riddance IIS, hello, Son of Cassini!
No more ditzing around with annoying IIS dependencies and install issues: aspnet_regiis, anyone?
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's artificial restrictions on developer tools in consumer Windows versions often hurt legitimate development work more than they prevent the misuse they're designed to stop.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques performance-based incentive systems using Microsoft's curve rating system as a case study. He argues that while performance metrics seem logical, making them visible to management transforms them into counterproductive tools. Drawing on research from social psychology, he presents evidence that rewards actually decrease performance, particularly for creative work. The post concludes that software development, as a creative activity, is especially harmed by reward-based motivation systems.
key_points: Performance metrics are only useful when kept private to the individual being measured; exposing them to management turns them into tools of control
Research from social psychology shows people expecting rewards perform worse than those expecting nothing, especially in creative tasks
Rewards create implicit punishments, discourage cooperation, encourage competition, deter risk-taking, and shift focus from the work itself to the reward
Microsoft's curve rating system exemplifies how well-intentioned performance systems can become demoralizing and self-defeating
Software development as a creative activity is particularly vulnerable to the negative effects of incentive-based management
money_quotes: Performance metrics are useful, but only privately to the person who is being measured.
The minute you make these performance metrics available to the manager as anything other than a sanitized average, you've turned them into a self-defeating cudgel to beat people into submission.
While rewards are effective at producing temporary compliance, they are strikingly ineffective at producing lasting changes in attitudes or behavior.
About two dozen studies from the field of social psychology conclusively show that people who expect to receive a reward do not perform as well as those who expect nothing.
For every reward, there is the lack of a reward, an implicit punishment.
Rewards undermine interest by focusing on the reward rather than the process itself.
posts.key_insight: Incentive-based performance systems undermine the intrinsic motivation that drives quality creative work like software development.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines a fundamental weakness in Bayesian spam filtering: spoofed emails that closely mimic legitimate correspondence from services like PayPal and eBay. Despite achieving 99.8% accuracy with his POPFile setup, these phishing emails slip through because they copy real email templates word-for-word. The core problem is mathematical: Bayesian filters learn from your inbox, so any attacker who knows what legitimate emails look like can craft messages guaranteed to pass. Atwood concludes that popular web services can no longer safely communicate with customers via email, pointing to eBay's shift toward on-site message centers as the inevitable response.
key_points: Bayesian filtering achieves high accuracy (99.8%) but has an exploitable weakness against spoofed emails that mimic legitimate correspondence
Phishing emails work by copying real email templates from services like PayPal, making them statistically indistinguishable from legitimate messages
The vulnerability is fundamental to how Bayesian filtering works: knowledge of valid email content allows attackers to craft messages that will pass
Major services like eBay are abandoning email communication entirely in favor of on-site message centers
The arms race between spam filters and spammers has reached a point where email itself is becoming unreliable for commercial communication
money_quotes: Bayesian filtering is useless against this type of spam; if the spammer knows what any email in your actual inbox looks like, he can construct one that will beat any Bayesian filter.
This is a strict requirement at the very heart of Bayesian filtering itself; any knowledge of valid contents (e.g., things that 'get through') has to be strictly eliminated.
Popular web-based services can no longer communicate via email with their customers.

That's like giving spoofers a free pass; once they have the 'template' email they can copy and paste it into a spoof email that is almost guaranteed to get past Bayesian filtering for users of that service.
I can't say I blame them; what other choice do they have?
posts.key_insight: Bayesian spam filtering contains an inherent mathematical vulnerability: any attacker who knows what legitimate emails look like can craft phishing messages that are statistically guaranteed to pass, forcing major services to abandon email communication entirely.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood discusses a frustrating limitation in Visual Studio .NET's code outlining feature: XML comments and function bodies are outlined as separate blocks rather than together. This forces developers to wrap functions with their comments in regions to achieve the expected collapsing behavior. Despite hoping for improvement, VS.NET 2005 exhibits the same problem. Atwood mentions working on a macro solution and recommends existing Documentator macros as a workaround.
key_points: Visual Studio .NET outlines XML comments and function code as separate collapsible blocks, which is counterintuitive
Using regions around functions with their comments provides the unified outlining behavior developers actually want
The problem persists in VS.NET 2005, indicating Microsoft didn't prioritize this usability issue
Macros offer a workaround for automatically adding regions to functions
money_quotes: The default Visual Studio .NET outlining support leaves a lot to be desired.
It's aggravating that there is no built in support for outlining the entire function and the comments.
I just checked, and VS.NET 2005 (aka Whidbey) has this same bad behavior. What a bummer.
posts.key_insight: When IDE features don't match developer expectations for code organization, workarounds like regions become necessary conventions despite adding ceremony to the codebase.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood expresses his long-standing dislike of XSLT, finding validation in Martin Fowler's similar conclusion. While Atwood has no issues with XML as a data format, he argues that XSLT's promise of transforming XML into 'anything' rarely delivers in practice. Fowler's experience moving from XSLT to Ruby for XML transformations reinforced Atwood's belief that procedural code is often simpler and easier than XSLT. The post questions whether XSLT's fundamental premise is realistic or just marketing hype.
key_points: XML is fine for hierarchical data storage, but XSLT as a companion technology is problematic
Martin Fowler, despite being proficient at XSLT, found Ruby significantly easier for XML transformations
Procedural code is often simpler and more practical than XSLT for transformation tasks
The promise of XSLT transforming XML into 'anything' may be fundamentally unrealistic
money_quotes: While useful in theory – 'using a simple XSLT transform, XML can be converted into anything!' – in practice, it takes painful contortions to do anything practical.
I concluded that it was easier and simpler to use procedural code to do the same thing.
I think this may raise some real questions about XSLT. There's still much I like about the power of XSLT, but I hate the syntax and the walls you keep running into.
Maybe the idea of XSLT transforming XML into 'anything' is fundamentally unrealistic – just more Write Once, Run Anywhere snake oil.
posts.key_insight: Technology that promises universal flexibility often fails in practice; sometimes straightforward procedural code beats specialized transformation languages.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares practical tips for improving the Windows XP command prompt experience. He walks through essential configuration changes like increasing buffer size, enabling QuickEdit mode, and expanding window dimensions. The post also covers lesser-known keyboard shortcuts including command history navigation, tab completion, and drag-and-drop file paths. For power users, he points to 4nt as a more capable shell replacement descended from 4dos.
key_points: The 'Open Command Window Here' right-click menu is one of the first quality-of-life improvements to add to any fresh Windows install
QuickEdit mode transforms copying and pasting in cmd.exe from painful to intuitive with mouse-based selection
Tab completion with SHIFT+TAB for reverse iteration works across all commands, not just specific ones
Drag and drop from Explorer windows directly inserts quoted paths into the command prompt
The F7 key provides a visual popup of command history rather than requiring arrow key navigation
money_quotes: Windows XP isn't known for its powerful command line interface.
And hoary old cmd.exe does have a few tricks up its sleeve that you may not know about.
Now we've got some room to actually see stuff!
QuickEdit mode enables copying from the command prompt by intuitively dragging and right clicking with the mouse.
But if you prefer Comic Sans here, be my guest!
If you're really a hard-core cmd.exe junkie (or maybe a UNIX user), you may want to look into the 4nt command shell replacement.
posts.key_insight: Even Windows' notoriously weak command prompt becomes significantly more usable with a few configuration tweaks and knowledge of its hidden keyboard shortcuts.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues against the trend of using RAID 0 (striping) on desktop computers, challenging the assumption that doubling hardware automatically doubles performance. He points out that RAID 0 literally doubles your chance of drive failure while providing negligible real-world performance benefits for typical desktop usage. Drawing on multiple technical sources, he demonstrates that the performance gains are only significant in server environments or specific applications, not for general desktop use. His conclusion is that simplicity and reliability should trump the allure of parallel hardware configurations.
key_points: Parallel hardware approaches (dual core, SLI, RAID 0) are not universal performance wins - they're only faster in specific circumstances
RAID 0 doubles your chance of complete data loss since either drive failing destroys the entire array
Hard drive failure is uniquely traumatic compared to other hardware failures because it means losing irreplaceable data
Real-world desktop benchmarks consistently show negligible performance benefits from RAID 0 striping
Marketing and power user enthusiasm have created an 'extraordinarily erroneous belief' about striping benefits
money_quotes: Of all the hardware failures you can have, drive failure is by far the most traumatic.
Losing a drive means you've probably lost your critical data, unless you have a good backup regimen in place. And 99% of us don't. Kudos to the 1% of you reading this who do.
RAID 0 literally doubles your chance of drive failure.
There is no place and no need for a RAID-0 array on a desktop computer.

The enthusiasm of the power user community combined with the marketing apparatus of firms catering to such crowds has led to an extraordinarily erroneous belief that striping data across two or more drives yields significant performance benefits.
There's a lot to be said for simplicity.
Just because current motherboard chipsets now make it easy and inexpensive to do this doesn't mean that you should.
posts.key_insight: Just because hardware parallelism is easy and marketed aggressively doesn't mean it's worthwhile - the real-world benefits rarely justify the added complexity and risk.

posts.summary: This is a brief link post where Atwood recommends a presentation by Wil Shipley about developing Mac software. Shipley, creator of Delicious Library, shares insights on indie software success, having earned $54,000 on launch day without advertising. Atwood describes the talk as both humorous and genuinely inspiring, giving it a strong endorsement to his readers.
key_points: Wil Shipley's Delicious Library earned $54,000 on day one with zero advertising spend
The talk covers why developing for Mac specifically can be advantageous
Indie software success is possible through quality product and platform choice
The presentation balances humor with practical business insights
money_quotes: One of the funniest and most oddly inspiring things I've read in a long time.
posts.key_insight: Indie developers can achieve remarkable commercial success by choosing the right platform and focusing on product quality over marketing spend.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood complains about the blindingly bright blue LEDs on his new router, exploring the science behind why blue LEDs are so problematic. Blue LEDs have 20 times the luminous intensity of red and green LEDs, and human eyes cannot focus sharply on blue light, causing a distracting halo effect. The post also touches on how blue light sensitivity increases in low ambient light and can disrupt sleep patterns. Despite this design flaw, Atwood recommends the router for its QoS features that allow gaming while downloading.
key_points: Blue LEDs have at least 20 times the luminous intensity of traditional red and green LEDs, making them painfully bright
The eye cannot focus blue light precisely on the retina, causing it to focus slightly in front and create a distracting halo effect
Human vision becomes far more sensitive to blue light when ambient light levels are low (Purkinje shift)
Blue light can suppress melatonin production, disrupt sleep patterns, and affect the immune system
The only practical solution for overly bright blue LEDs is to paint over them with black marker
money_quotes: Blue LEDs have at least 20 times the luminous intensity of old-fashioned red and green indicators.
While red or green light is focused precisely onto the retina, blue light is focused slightly in front of it, which causes a distracting halo around bright blue lights.
A blue light that is merely eye-catching on a brightly lit store shelf can become dazzling when the lights are low.
It's like getting tiny little blue LED punji sticks right in the eye.
Blue LEDs may look cool, but for actual functional use, give me green or red LEDs any day.
For ten years I had worked very hard to make these products. I worked twelve hours a day, seven days a week, except holidays.
posts.key_insight: The technology industry's embrace of blue LEDs prioritizes aesthetics over usability, ignoring the physiological reality that blue light is uniquely harsh on human eyes.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood compares Microsoft's Remote Desktop with VNC-based remote access solutions, explaining the trade-offs between each approach. Remote Desktop offers superior speed but has limitations including forced separate user sessions, no multi-monitor support, and artificial session limits. VNC, particularly TightVNC with its video hook driver, provides a viable alternative that shows the actual screen content rather than creating new sessions. The post serves as a practical guide for developers choosing between these remoting technologies circa 2005.
key_points: Remote Desktop is the fastest remoting protocol but is intentionally limited by Microsoft to one session on XP and two on server editions
VNC follows an older model of showing actual screen content rather than creating separate user sessions, which has both security implications and practical benefits
TightVNC's video hook driver dramatically improves performance over traditional polling-based VNC implementations
Neither solution handles multiple monitors well, leaving users with only the primary display
Hardware-level remote desktop solutions exist that can even display BIOS screens and Blue Screens of Death
money_quotes: Over a fast network, you can *almost* convince yourself that you're using the local machine.
In fine Microsoft tradition, it's intentionally crippled.
I suppose this is to keep us from setting up our own OS/360 timeshare boxes.
If you're both trying to use the computer at the same time, it's even more fun!
Why poll for changes when the video driver can tell you what the changes were?
If you ever used VNC in the past and were disappointed with how slow and CPU intensive it was, you should try again with TightVNC and the video hook driver.
You'll get the primary monitor, and you'll like it.
posts.key_insight: The best remote access solution depends on whether you need speed and isolation (Remote Desktop) or shared screen access to an existing session (VNC), with both having frustrating limitations that Microsoft and open source communities have only partially addressed.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses a workplace network performance issue to illustrate the value of back-of-the-envelope calculations in software engineering. Drawing from Programming Pearls, he shares Bob Martin's story of using simple arithmetic to expose a fatally flawed Olympic communication system design. Atwood then demonstrates practical application by establishing baseline measurements for network throughput, revealing that gigabit ethernet realistically delivers only 2-3x improvement over 100baseT due to various bottlenecks. His workplace investigation ultimately found the problem wasn't with the suspected desktop at all, but with server-side limitations affecting everyone.
key_points: Back-of-the-envelope calculations are standard engineering practice but too often neglected in computing
Loopback testing establishes absolute upper bounds before investigating network issues
Real-world gigabit ethernet delivers roughly 30 MB/sec, not the theoretical 125 MB/sec - about 2-3x improvement over 100baseT
Methodical baseline testing often reveals the problem isn't where you initially suspected
Simple arithmetic can expose fundamental design flaws before they become expensive failures
money_quotes: His calculations were just as simple as those about the Mississippi River and much more revealing. They showed that, under generous assumptions, the proposed system could work only if there were at least a hundred and twenty seconds in each minute.

That was Bob Martin's wonderful (if eccentric) way of introducing the engineering technique of 'back-of-the-envelope' calculations. The idea is standard fare in engineering schools and is bread and butter for most practicing engineers. Unfortunately, it is too often neglected in computing.
This is helpful because it establishes an absolute upper bound on network performance.
Bottlenecks elsewhere kept the overall throughput lower than I had hoped.
Even with those real world caveats, gigabit ethernet still offers 2 to 3 times the performance of 100baseT, which isn't exactly chopped liver, either.
posts.key_insight: Simple back-of-the-envelope calculations and systematic baseline measurements can quickly expose where performance problems actually lie, saving engineers from chasing the wrong solutions.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines whether the common preference for black text on white background in code editors is actually supported by research. He cites two studies that provide nuanced findings: while black on white is generally most readable, high contrast isn't always better than medium contrast, and the direction of contrast matters significantly. The studies found that darker text on lighter backgrounds consistently outperforms the inverse due to a phenomenon called halation, where light text on dark backgrounds can appear to glow and blur. Atwood concludes that while any syntax highlighting beats plain text, black on white schemes remain the best choice for readability.
key_points: Research shows contrast affects legibility, but high contrast isn't always superior to medium contrast
Halation (text appearing to glow/blur) makes light text on dark backgrounds harder to read
Darker text on lighter backgrounds consistently rated more readable than the inverse in every color combination tested
Any syntax highlighting scheme is preferable to non-colorized plain text
The most readable combination is black text on white background, with strong preference for combinations containing black
money_quotes: From these results, one can say that contrast affects legibility, but unfortunately, it does not seem to be as simple as high contrast being better than low contrast.
When light text is placed on a dark background the text may seem to glow and become blurred; this is referred to as halation, and it may make the text harder to read.
The most readable color combination is black text on white background; overall, there is a stronger preference for any combination containing black.
In every color combination surveyed, the darker text on a lighter background was rated more readable than its inverse.
I'll take any of these schemes over the non-colorized Notepad version, but I feel very strongly that black on white color schemes are the way to go for overall readability.
posts.key_insight: While developers often choose color schemes based on personal preference, research consistently shows that darker text on lighter backgrounds is more readable due to the halation effect that makes light-on-dark text appear blurred.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood pays tribute to the infamous 'Hot Dog Stand' color scheme from Windows 3.1, a garish red and yellow design that stood out among the otherwise sensible color options. He marvels at how Microsoft included this intentionally absurd scheme alongside perfectly reasonable alternatives, suggesting it was included as an internal joke. The post celebrates this bit of software history humor and references a joke about Microsoft employees decorating their houses in the scheme.
key_points: Windows 3.1 included mostly rational, reasonable color schemes - except for Hot Dog Stand
The scheme's deliberate absurdity among sensible options suggests it was an intentional joke by Microsoft
Hot Dog Stand has become a cultural touchstone for software industry humor
Even serious software sometimes contains Easter eggs and humor from its creators
money_quotes: The truly funny thing about this color scheme is that all the other Windows 3.1 color schemes are surprisingly rational, totally reasonable color schemes. And then you get to 'Hot Dog Stand.' Which is utterly insane.
And that makes it quite possibly the greatest color scheme ever devised.
… your house is decorated like the 'Hot Dog Stand' color scheme from Windows.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the most memorable moments in software history come from developers sneaking absurdist humor into otherwise serious products.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood advocates for using pass phrases instead of traditional passwords, drawing on advice from Microsoft security expert Robert Hensing. The post argues that memorable sentences like song lyrics or quotes are both easier to remember and more secure than complex character combinations like 'xYaQxrz!'. While acknowledging criticism that pass phrases can be attacked at the word level, Atwood counters that capitalization, punctuation, and numbers significantly strengthen them. The core insight is that security measures that force users to write down passwords on post-it notes actually decrease security, making pass phrases a clear win for both usability and protection.
key_points: Pass phrases meet password complexity requirements through capitalization and punctuation without requiring random characters
Traditional 'secure' passwords like 'xYaQxrz!' are both hard to remember AND vulnerable to brute-force attacks, offering the worst of both worlds
When passwords are too complex to remember, users write them down, completely defeating the security purpose
Word-level attacks on pass phrases are theoretically possible but capitalization, punctuation, and numbers add significant protection
Passwords only need to survive attack long enough for the next password change cycle
money_quotes: I don't want you to use passwords, I want you to use pass-PHRASES.
That password would not survive sustained attack with LC5 long enough to matter so in my mind it's pointless to use a password like that. You may as well just leave your password blank.
The average human worker just wants to do their job, and can't remember Syz8#K3! as a password.
If your password is so hard to remember that you have to write it down, then you have no security at all.
A password of 'Chris' because your son's name is Chris is a bad password, but a password of: 'My oldest son's name is Chris and he is 10 years old' is a good password.
Easier on the user, harder for hackers: that's a total no-brainer.
posts.key_insight: Security measures that ignore human behavior and force users into workarounds like writing down passwords actually decrease security, making memorable pass phrases superior to complex but forgettable passwords.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues against the common practice of explicitly initializing variables to their default values in .NET managed code. He observes that many developers habitually initialize variables to null or zero either at declaration or in constructors, a practice carried over from C++ where uninitialized memory was dangerous. Through benchmark tests across multiple processors and .NET versions, he demonstrates that explicit initialization introduces measurable performance penalties ranging from 8% to 36% slower execution. His conclusion is that developers should trust the runtime to handle default initialization rather than doing redundant work.
key_points: Explicit variable initialization is a C++ habit that doesn't translate well to managed code environments
Benchmark tests show initialization penalties of 8-36% depending on where and how variables are initialized
.NET 2.0's compiler optimizes away initialization on definition but still penalizes constructor initialization
Managed code runtimes guarantee default values, making manual initialization redundant
The #pragma warning feature can suppress uninitialized variable warnings if that's the motivation for initializing
money_quotes: Do we really want to play the I'm smarter than the runtime game again?
Ok, so maybe you're a masochist and you like extra typing.
Sure, maybe that's the convention in the wild and wooly world of buffer overruns C++, but this is managed code.
I recommend avoiding initialization as a general rule, unless you have a compelling reason to do so.
Clearly there's a substantial performance penalty for initializing variables in both .NET 1.1 and even .NET 2.0.
posts.key_insight: In managed code environments, trusting the runtime's default initialization is both cleaner and faster than explicitly initializing variables to default values.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the importance of pushing boundaries in professional settings, drawing from Scott Hanselman's blog and Chris Sells' advice about getting 'slapped' by your boss. The post endorses the idea that lack of criticism often indicates lack of meaningful contribution. Atwood argues that while there's a distinction between being pushy and pushing appropriately, the latter is essential for professional growth and impact.
key_points: If you're not being criticized occasionally, you're probably not taking enough risks or doing meaningful work
There's an important distinction between being pushy (annoying) and pushing (necessary advocacy)
Indifference and accepting the status quo are worse outcomes than occasionally getting pushback
Getting 'slapped' by management at least twice a year indicates healthy boundary-pushing
money_quotes: If you're not getting slapped by your boss at least twice a year, you're not pushing the envelope enough.
If you are not criticized, you may not be doing much.
There's a fine line between pushing and pushy, but you have to push.
You may get slapped, but so what? Anything is preferable to indifference or, worse, the same old clichés.
posts.key_insight: Occasional pushback from leadership is a sign you're contributing meaningfully, not that you're doing something wrong.

posts.summary: Atwood shares a classic joke about how software engineers, hardware engineers, and managers approach problem-solving differently. The punchline—that a software engineer's instinct is to reproduce the problem before fixing it—resonates because it reflects actual debugging practice. Atwood admits he does this 'at least once' every week, questioning whether this says something troubling about software engineering or simply reflects the enormous complexity developers face. The post uses humor to highlight a genuine tension in the field.
key_points: Software engineers instinctively try to reproduce problems before attempting fixes, unlike hardware engineers who dive into root cause analysis
The 'just try again' approach is so common it's become a running joke in the industry
This debugging instinct might reflect either a flaw in software engineering practices or the inherent complexity of software systems
The joke illustrates fundamental differences in how different technical roles approach problem-solving
money_quotes: Before we do anything, I think we should push the car back up the road and see if it happens again.
Geez, I dunno, just run it again and see if the problem recurs.
I don't know if it's a sad indictment of the state of software engineering or a not-so-subtle hint that software engineers deal with thousands of variables in even the simplest of programs.
posts.key_insight: The instinct to reproduce bugs before fixing them is either software engineering's embarrassing crutch or a rational response to managing overwhelming complexity—and we're not sure which.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reviews Joel Spolsky's 'The Best Software Writing I' anthology, using it as a launching point to discuss the power of storytelling in technical writing. He highlights Joel's central thesis that the 'show, don't tell' principle separates engaging writing from forgettable prose. Through Joel's extended anecdote about an Army Sergeant Major teaching by example, Atwood demonstrates how concrete stories are far more memorable and persuasive than abstract principles. While recommending the book as a valuable survey of excellent blog writing, Atwood also offers his characteristically blunt criticism of a few entries he found lacking.
key_points: The 'show, don't tell' principle is what separates compelling technical writing from boring, unconvincing prose
A concrete story is worth far more than an abstract principle—readers would rather hear ten 400-word stories than one lecture about leadership
Most people are unaware of the excellent software writing that exists in blog form
Even curated 'best of' collections contain entries that miss the mark—quality is subjective
Leading by example (as in the Sergeant Major story) demonstrates principles more effectively than stating them
money_quotes: The author had violated the number one rule of good writing, the 'Show, don't tell' rule. There was not a single story in the book.
I'll bet you'd rather sit through ten of those 400 word stories than have to listen to someone drone on about how 'a good team leader provides inspiration by setting a positive example.'
If he can clean a toilet, I can clean a toilet. There's nothing wrong with cleaning toilets.
The book works quite well as a survey overview of the outstanding blog content that most people still don't even know is out there.
It was chock full of sentences like 'A good team leader provides inspiration by setting a positive example.' What the eff?
posts.key_insight: Concrete stories and examples are infinitely more persuasive and memorable than abstract principles—this is the fundamental rule that separates great technical writing from forgettable advice.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood discusses the 'dancing bunnies problem' - the fundamental security challenge that users will bypass any technical barrier to get what they want. He references Larry Osterman's articulation of how users who want to see 'dancing bunnies' will circumvent any security measure to do so. Atwood argues that traditional mitigation techniques like privilege separation, download restrictions, and password requirements ultimately fail against determined user curiosity. He suggests two potential solutions: defaulting users to non-administrator accounts in Windows Vista, and high-speed virtualization that lets users play in disposable sandboxes.
key_points: Users will actively circumvent security measures to access content they want, regardless of technical barriers
Traditional security mitigations (privilege separation, download blocking, password requirements) all fail against user determination
The fundamental problem isn't technical - it's human nature and curiosity
Virtualization offers a pragmatic solution: let users do risky things in disposable sandbox environments
Operating systems should default to non-administrator accounts to limit potential damage
money_quotes: The user wants to see the dancing bunnies, so they click there. It doesn't matter how much you try to dissuade them, if they want to see the dancing bunnies, then by gum, they're going to see the dancing bunnies.
At the end of the day, the user still wants to see the dancing bunny, and they'll do whatever is necessary to bypass your carefully constructed barriers in order to see the bunny.
Protecting users from themselves is a lot harder than it used to be.
Users can't help themselves – they just have to poke the bunny.
If something bad happens, you just ball it up and throw it away.
posts.key_insight: Security systems ultimately fail not because of technical flaws, but because determined users will always find ways to bypass barriers that stand between them and what they want.

posts.summary: Atwood explores the fundamental philosophical difference between PC and Mac enthusiasts: PC users value the hands-on process of customization and personal investment, while Mac users appreciate polished, pre-designed products. He draws a parallel between hardware customization (case modding, overclocking) and software customization (open source, utilities, GUI tweaks). The post argues that the DIY ethic pervades the entire PC ecosystem, from hardware builds to Linux development to Windows power-user configurations. Atwood concludes that while he appreciates Apple's elegant integration of hardware and software, he personally derives more satisfaction from the tinkering process itself.
key_points: PC enthusiasts take pride not in ownership but in what they've personally done to make their equipment unique
The DIY ethic extends from hardware customization to open source software development
Open source is analogous to hot-rodding cars - you can tweak and customize, but with uncertain effects on overall quality
Even users of commercial software engage in DIY behavior through utilities, tweaks, and GUI modifications
Apple offers elegance out of the box; PC offers the satisfaction of building something yourself
money_quotes: What such people are proud of is not mere ownership of the equipment, but what they've done to it to make it what it is.
We make the beige box what we want it to be.

Apple's G5 systems are the product of a world-class design team and stamped out by the thousands; custom PC builds are the highly individual result of dozens or even hundreds of hours of personal investment.
Isn't building custom software a little bit like hot-rodding your automobile?
While I certainly appreciate Apple's ability to box up elegant hardware designs with an elegant UNIX GUI makeover, that just isn't for me. I have more fun when I do it myself.
posts.key_insight: The true appeal of the PC platform lies not in the end result but in the hands-on satisfaction of personal customization and investment.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the concept of testing performance limits by imagining components as 'infinitely fast' to reveal hidden bottlenecks. He examines three cases: null drivers showing Windows 2D graphics hit OS infrastructure limits by 1999, 3dfx's CTO explaining that infinitely fast GPU hardware would only yield 1.25x gains when games are CPU-bound, and early solid-state drives showing modest improvements except for pure I/O tasks. The post argues that removing one bottleneck often just exposes the next constraint in the system.
key_points: Making something 'infinitely fast' (a no-op or null driver) reveals the true performance ceiling imposed by surrounding infrastructure
By 1999, 2D graphics cards had essentially reached null driver performance, meaning Windows OS overhead was the actual bottleneck
3dfx's CTO calculated that infinitely fast 3D hardware would only improve game performance by 1.25x when the CPU is 80% utilized
Early affordable SSDs showed dramatic improvements only for pure storage tasks (3.8x file copy) but minimal gains for application loads
The 'infinitely fast' thought experiment is most valuable for identifying where the real performance problems lie
money_quotes: When it comes to performance, there's always a bottleneck. The question is, which bottleneck?
One way to make something infinitely fast is to make it a no-op. Instead of doing work, it does nothing.
Even if we replaced our hardware with INFINITELY fast hardware, you only get a small increase in performance (1.25x).
This probably says more about Windows overhead than it does about actual 2D performance.
It's hard to imagine the CTO of a hardware startup actually answering questions about the product he designed in the newsgroups. Only in 1998, I guess.
Having an infinitely fast solid state hard drive doesn't do a whole lot to improve raw performance. It's probably best used as a device to reveal where all the other performance bottlenecks are.
posts.key_insight: The thought experiment of making a component infinitely fast is most valuable not for achieving that speed, but for revealing all the other bottlenecks you couldn't see before.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood celebrates Steve Ballmer's infamous on-stage antics, presenting three classic videos as essential viewing for Windows developers. The post highlights Ballmer's willingness to embarrass himself publicly despite being a billionaire, from screaming until he needed vocal cord surgery to popping out of anniversary cakes. Atwood contrasts Ballmer's manic energy with the boring corporate style of competitors like Michael Dell. The post also briefly notes Ballmer's controversial positions on Linux, GPL, and DRM, but frames even these as part of his unique character. Ultimately, Atwood argues that Ballmer's sweaty enthusiasm is an asset for Microsoft's image compared to Linux's mascot.
key_points: Three Ballmer videos (Windows 1.0 pitch, Dance Monkeyboy, Developers chant) are presented as essential Windows developer viewing
Ballmer's willingness to make a fool of himself publicly is presented as admirable authenticity that most executives lack
His enthusiasm was so extreme he once required vocal cord surgery after screaming 'Windows' continuously
Ballmer held controversial views calling Linux 'communism' and the GPL 'a cancer' while advocating for DRM
The post playfully argues Ballmer is a better mascot for Microsoft than Tux is for Linux
money_quotes: I love the fact that Steve Ballmer is a billionaire 12 times over, and yet he isn't afraid to get up on stage and make a complete and utter ass out of himself.
You have to respect a man with that much unbridled enthusiasm.
I certainly can't see staid, boring old Michael Dell dancing around on stage, screaming about how much he loves his company at the top of his lungs.
His vocal cords required surgery after he screamed 'Windows, Windows, Windows' continuously at a Japan meeting in 1991.
A sweat-soaked Ballmer repeatedly chanted 'developers' at least 14 times in front of the bemused gathering.
Linux may have Tux, but we've got Ballmer. Pudgy cartoon penguin versus sweaty screaming billionaire? C'mon. That's not even close.
posts.key_insight: Authentic enthusiasm and willingness to look foolish can be more compelling than polished corporate presentations, even—or especially—at the highest levels of business.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood enthusiastically recommends the AMD Athlon 64 X2 dual-core processor for developers, noting its wide availability and superior compilation speeds. He highlights the newly released budget-friendly X2 3800+ at under $400 and suggests the retail package is worth the premium for its excellent stock heatsink. The post frames the purchase as practical preparation for Visual Studio 2005's increased system requirements.
key_points: The Athlon 64 X2 offers unmatched code compilation performance for developers
AMD's new X2 3800+ brings dual-core within reach at under $400
The retail package includes an unusually high-quality stock heatsink with copper inlay and heatpipes
VS.NET 2005's higher system requirements justify the hardware upgrade
money_quotes: As a developer, you will not find a CPU that compiles code faster.
In fact, it's not even close.
You'll certainly need something to counteract VS.NET 2005's higher system requirements.
And you get an AMD X2 sticker, too. A STICKER!
posts.key_insight: Dual-core processors have become practical and affordable enough that developers should upgrade now for dramatically faster compilation times.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that most software projects lack a clear, compelling vision statement that the team can rally around. Drawing from Scott Berkun's book on project management, he catalogs common failures in vision statements: being too broad, using jargon to hide weak ideas, being wishy-washy, or relying on authority rather than reasoning. He contrasts these failures with the original Palm Pilot's project goals as a canonical example of a great vision—specific, concrete, and actionable. The core message is that a vision statement isn't corporate fluff but the soul of a project.
key_points: Most developers can't articulate why they're working on what they're working on, indicating a lack of clear project vision
Good vision statements are short, succinct, and clear—like an internal elevator pitch that explains what you're doing and why it will succeed
Lame vision statements share common patterns: too broad, jargon-heavy, spineless, or relying on authority without reasoning
The Palm Pilot's 1996 project goals exemplify great vision: specific constraints around size, cost, simplicity, and PC sync
A vision statement provides emotional investment and alignment—without one, the project is set up for failure
money_quotes: If you asked each member of your team why they were working on what they are currently working on, what would they say? Could they even give you a coherent answer?
I'm talking about a simple vision statement everyone can rally around: here's what we're doing, and here's why it's going to rock.
Lame visions have no integrity; they don't offer a plan, and they don't express an opinion.
If the vision doesn't take a clear stance on what should happen, the team leaders will never fully invest emotionally in the effort, setting up the project for failure.
It uses complex language to hide the absence of strong ideas.
A vision statement isn't just marketing weasel-speak – it's the soul of your project.
And who wants to work on a project with no soul?
posts.key_insight: A project without a clear, specific vision statement that the entire team can articulate isn't just poorly managed—it lacks the soul that gives work meaning and direction.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood challenges a Slashdot poster's claim that desktop computers consume 450 watts of power, demonstrating through actual measurements that even a high-end PC uses only around 132 watts at peak. Using a $30 Kill-a-Watt meter, he measures his own beefy workstation under various conditions and breaks down power consumption by component. The post serves as a broader critique of how people spread misinformation based on guesses rather than data, advocating for empirical measurement over estimation whenever possible.
key_points: A Slashdot poster's claim of 450W power consumption for desktop PCs is wildly exaggerated - actual measured peak usage is around 132W for a high-end system
Simple, inexpensive tools like a $30 Kill-a-Watt meter can provide concrete data that debunks common misconceptions
Power supply efficiency matters - about 25% of wall power is lost in conversion, so wall measurements overstate actual PC consumption
Even rough estimates become far more valuable when backed by actual supporting data
The tech community often perpetuates misinformation about hardware requirements (like needing 500W+ power supplies) that simple measurement could disprove
money_quotes: Why spread guesstimated misinformation when you can simply measure the power usage with a widely available $30 device?
Estimating is only necessary when you can't easily measure.
When you meet people like this, gently urge them to stop estimating and start measuring.
It's amazing how much you can infer from such simple, basic data collection.
As estimates go, these are backed by supporting data. And that's a lot more useful than the unsubstantiated wild guessing of random Slashdot posters.
I don't think you can realistically build a desktop computer that uses 450 watts!
posts.key_insight: Measurement with simple tools beats speculation every time - stop guessing about things you can easily measure.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines Joel Spolsky's argument about whether hiring the best programmers matters, citing data from a Yale professor showing no correlation between time spent on programming assignments and grades achieved. While acknowledging the well-documented skill disparity in software development, Atwood pushes back on Joel's conclusion by noting it applies mainly to shrink-wrap software companies—a tiny slice of the overall IT market. For the vast majority of in-house and internal software development, hiring 'rock stars' may not be justified or necessary.
key_points: Extreme skill disparity is unique to software development, with roughly equal odds of working with a genius or a jackass
There is no correlation between experience and programming skill—talented interns can outperform 20-year veterans
Yale CS data shows no correlation between time spent on assignments and resulting grades
Joel's argument about needing the best programmers applies mainly to commercial shrink-wrap software, not the broader IT market
For internal/in-house software development, hiring 'rock stars' is rarely justified
money_quotes: Extreme skill disparity is unique to the profession of software development.
The odds of working with a genius or a jackass on any given job are about fifty-fifty.
There's no correlation between experience and skill, either. I've worked with amazingly talented interns and 20 year vets who produced terrible code.
There is absolutely no correlation whatsoever between time spent on the problem and the resulting score for these CS students.
Nobody hires Dolly Parton to sing at weddings.
Since Joel's commercial shrink-wrap software development is a tiny percentage of the overall IT market, does this mean that having the best programmers doesn't matter most of the time?
Sadly, I believe the answer is yes.
posts.key_insight: While top programming talent creates outsized value in product companies, the uncomfortable truth is that most software work doesn't require—or justify—hiring the best.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood celebrates Visual Studio .NET 2005's decision to enable Option Explicit by default, which prevents the compiler from silently creating new variables when programmers make typos. He argues that Option Explicit Off is 'pure, unmitigated evil' that has caused real problems in production projects. The post also discusses Option Strict, which enforces strong type checking and prevents automatic type coercion. While Atwood agrees Option Strict should probably be on by default, he acknowledges it has legitimate uses for late binding code, unlike Option Explicit Off which has no valid use case. He notes that partial classes in VB.NET 2.0 allow developers to selectively disable Option Strict for specific code sections. Ultimately, he's grateful for the Explicit default change even if Microsoft didn't go far enough with Strict.
key_points: Option Explicit Off allows the compiler to silently create new variables on typos, leading to bugs that are extremely difficult to track down
Option Strict Off enables automatic type coercion which can cause obscure bugs and generates slower IL code (hence 'Option Slow')
Default settings are incredibly powerful - developers often don't realize when dangerous options are enabled months into a project
VB.NET 2.0's partial classes allow granular control, enabling Option Strict Off only for sections that need late binding
Option Explicit Off has no legitimate use, while Option Strict Off has valid use cases for late binding code
money_quotes: It's about damn time.
There's nothing more vicious than making an innocent typo when referencing a variable and not knowing about it because the compiler silently declares a new variable for you.
Just talking about it makes my eye twitch uncontrollably.
Option Explicit Off is pure, unmitigated evil.
Laugh all you want, but this is the power of default values.
What I really needed was an option not to work with knuckleheads who turn it off.
I consider it a minor miracle that we even got Explicit. I'll take it.
posts.key_insight: Default settings in development tools are extraordinarily powerful because developers often don't examine them, making safe defaults a moral imperative for tool designers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his disappointment with Microsoft's new Consolas font after discovering it looks terrible without ClearType enabled. While acknowledging Consolas is one of the best ClearType-optimized fonts available, he finds it unusable for programming because it lacks proper hinting for display without font smoothing. He explains that ClearType works well on high-DPI displays but causes eye fatigue on typical monitors, creating a frustrating disconnect since Windows poorly supports high-DPI displays anyway.
key_points: Consolas was designed from scratch specifically for ClearType, making it nearly unusable without that font smoothing technology enabled
ClearType font smoothing works well on high-DPI displays (like 15" laptops at 1600x1200) but causes eye fatigue on typical monitors
The font lacks standard hinting for legible display at small point sizes (9-13pt) without ClearType
Windows has poor high-DPI display support, creating a disconnect with ClearType being a high-DPI technology
money_quotes: You know you've entered the highest pantheons of geekhood when you get excited about Microsoft's new fixed-width font, Consolas.
MY EYES! THE GOGGLES! THEY DO NOTHING!
Consolas is one of the best looking ClearType fonts I've ever seen.
Consolas looks like crap without ClearType!
Consolas isn't just optimized for ClearType, it can barely be used without it.
Consolas, you are dead to me.
It's a great technology, but it's also a high-DPI display technology, and Windows sucks for high DPI displays.
posts.key_insight: A font optimized exclusively for one rendering technology becomes useless when that technology doesn't suit your workflow or hardware.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines how hackers compromised the Xbox's security system by exploiting flaws in just 512 bytes of boot ROM code. Microsoft's approach was to store a small decryption routine in the Southbridge chip that would decrypt the larger Flash ROM contents, theoretically making it impossible to reverse-engineer or patch the system. However, the implementation contained fundamental security mistakes, including using a simple 32-bit value check instead of a proper hash function. Atwood uses this as an example of how even major companies can make basic security errors, while expressing hope that Microsoft learned from these mistakes for the Xbox 360.
key_points: Microsoft's Xbox security relied on 512 bytes of code hidden in the Southbridge chip to decrypt the main Flash ROM
