The compromise approach of small secure ROM plus encrypted external ROM was sound in theory but flawed in execution
A 32-bit value check was used instead of a proper cryptographic hash - a fundamental security mistake
Hardware-integrated code is harder to extract and replace than external chips, but more expensive to update
Even well-resourced companies like Microsoft can make 'newbie security mistakes' in critical code
money_quotes: I know virtually nothing about cryptography, and I could have told you that checking a single 32-bit value is a remarkably poor substitute for a real hash.
This small ROM can not be extracted easily, and it cannot be changed or replaced.
A good compromise is to store only a small amount of code in one of the other chips, and store the bulk of it in the external Flash chip.
If the ROM image could be fully accessed, it would be easy to reverse-engineer the code; encryption and obfuscation would only slow down the hacking process a bit.
posts.key_insight: Even a tiny amount of security-critical code can harbor multiple serious vulnerabilities when fundamental cryptographic principles are ignored, regardless of how sophisticated the overall security architecture appears.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the performance difference between Parse and TryParse methods in .NET, noting that TryParse avoids throwing exceptions which makes it faster. After updating a demo application from 2003 and fixing numerous bugs, he found the performance difference was less dramatic than originally claimed but still significant. He agrees with Alex Papadimoulis that while exceptions should generally be avoided in primary code paths, using them for business rule validation across application tiers is a reasonable trade-off when the context (like web requests across multiple tiers) makes the nanosecond cost negligible.
key_points: TryParse was extended to all basic datatypes in .NET 2.0, previously only available for Double in 1.1
The original demo app showing dramatic performance differences had significant bugs that overstated the gap
Exceptions are slower but not catastrophically so - the general rule of avoiding them in primary code paths still applies
Using exceptions for business rule validation across tiers is a reasonable architectural choice despite the performance cost
Context matters when evaluating performance trade-offs - nanoseconds don't matter in the context of a multi-tier web request
money_quotes: So, yeah, parsing with exceptions is quite a bit slower, but not 'did someone just downgrade my computer to a 486?' slower.
The general rule of avoiding exceptions in your primary code paths still applies.
Less code, less mess. Nanoseconds slower? Probably. A big deal in the context of a web request going through three physical tiers? Not at all.
As long as you're aware of the cost, this is a perfectly reasonable thing to do.
An iron-clad adherence to the 'avoid all exceptions' rule would be a net loss.
I think that there's a general consensus out there that Exceptions should be limited to exceptional circumstances. But 'exceptional' is a rather subjective adjective, so there's a bit of a gray area.
posts.key_insight: Performance rules like 'avoid exceptions' should be understood and applied thoughtfully based on context, not followed dogmatically - sometimes the cleaner code is worth the nanosecond tax.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood addresses the challenge of using Windows Performance Monitor (perfmon) for server diagnostics, noting that while Task Manager suffices for basic bottleneck identification, deeper analysis requires perfmon's overwhelming array of counters. He critiques Microsoft's performance monitor wizard for producing traces with far too much information, then provides a curated list of essential performance counters covering CPU, memory, disk, and network metrics. The post serves as a practical guide for developers who need to diagnose server issues without drowning in data, recommending CSV export and LogParser for more advanced analysis needs.
key_points: Task Manager is the first-line diagnostic tool for identifying whether bottlenecks are CPU, disk, network, or memory related
Microsoft's perfmon wizard produces overwhelming amounts of data that obscures rather than illuminates performance issues
A focused set of about 10 key counters across processor, memory, disk, and network is sufficient for general bottleneck diagnosis
For deeper analysis, exporting to CSV and using LogParser allows SQL-like querying and graphing of performance data
money_quotes: using perfmon is like trying to sip from a fire hose: there are a zillion performance counters that produce veritable mountains of data
I always resist doing this for as long as I can
It's perfmon gone wild!
This is way, way, WAY too much information
Those counters should be enough to give you a general sense of whether you're dealing with a disk, memory, CPU, or network bottleneck – without being too overwhelming
that's usually enough to get a rough idea of where we are in the bottleneck shell game
posts.key_insight: Effective performance monitoring isn't about collecting all available data, but about identifying the minimal set of metrics that actually answer your diagnostic questions.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that password security has shown no improvement over 25 years because the problem is fundamentally human, not technological. He cites research from 1979 and 2002 showing that roughly 30% of passwords can be cracked quickly in both eras. Rather than fighting human nature with complex random passwords like 'wX%95qd!', Atwood advocates for passphrases—longer strings of words that are both more secure and easier to remember. He calls on developers to support passwords of at least 128 unicode characters and to actively educate users that 'password' doesn't mean a single word.
key_points: Password security hasn't improved between 1979 and 2005 because it's a people problem, not a technology problem
Humans are inherently bad at generating randomness, making traditional complex passwords both hard to create and hard to remember
Even the worst passphrase ('this is my secret password') is far more secure than an average single-word password
Developers perpetuate the problem by enforcing short password length limits (8-16 characters)
User education must happen at the point of password creation, with examples of passphrases shown directly in the UI
money_quotes: When there's no measurable improvement in password security between 1979 and 2005, clearly we aren't dealing with a technology problem. We're dealing with a people problem.
Passwords are fundamentally broken because they aren't compatible with typical human behavior.
When humans make passwords, (they) are not very good at making up randomness.
The worst imaginable pass phrase (e.g., 'this is my secret password') is many times more secure than an average single word password (e.g., 'god123'). And it's easier to remember.
The greatest long term security threat isn't hackers. It's the perpetuation of the braindead 8-16 character password length limitation, and the idea that passwords are single words.
We have yet to see a company whose employees don't pick bad passwords.
posts.key_insight: Password security fails because we've been fighting human nature instead of working with it—passphrases align security with how people actually think and remember.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques Windows Media Player 10's user interface, arguing it represents a rare case of software that gets progressively worse with each version. He references other critics who share his frustrations with WMP's cluttered design and compares it unfavorably to iTunes' cleaner interface. Despite acknowledging that WMP 10 was actually an improvement over versions 8 and 9, Atwood maintains it still fails at basic usability. He points to the open-source Media Player Classic as evidence that users preferred Microsoft's simpler version 6.4, and notes that while the old player lacks modern features, its simple UI remains timeless.
key_points: Windows Media Player is a rare example of software whose UI degrades with each new version rather than improving
Feature bloat and mode-switching create unnecessary UI complexity, with 'bizarre little buttons' appearing contextually
iTunes offers a cleaner, more usable interface despite WMP technically doing more
The open-source Media Player Classic project exists specifically because users preferred the simplicity of WMP 6.4
Simple UI design is timeless, while technical features eventually become outdated
money_quotes: Windows Media Player may be the only windows application with a UI that gets progressively worse with each new version.
It is my media player of choice due only to overwhelming indifference on my part; I curse every time I use it.
These little niggling UI problems aren't enough to motivate me to switch to another media player, but they're painful and unnecessary.
What's really shocking is that WMP 10 was an improvement over WMP 8 and 9.
It was all downhill for Microsoft from version 6.4.
The old player is falling way behind on the technical playback details, but the simple UI is timeless.
posts.key_insight: Software that continuously adds features without UI discipline will eventually become worse than its simpler predecessors, proving that a timeless simple interface outlasts technical sophistication.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the famous 1978 Microsoft group photo, tracking down what happened to each of the 11 people pictured alongside Bill Gates and Paul Allen. The post reveals the diverse paths these early Microsoft employees took after leaving the company, from cattle ranching to developing Cabbage Patch dolls to psychedelic drug advocacy. Despite leaving a company that would become one of the most valuable in history, most accumulated modest fortunes in the millions while Gates and Allen became billionaires. The research draws from a 2000 Time article and other sources to piece together the fates of Microsoft's earliest team members.
key_points: The 11 people in Microsoft's 1978 photo took remarkably diverse career paths after leaving, including cattle ranching, environmental philanthropy, and golf course software
Most early Microsoft employees left within the first 5-7 years, with Marc McDonald notably leaving because the company was 'too big' in 1984
The wealth disparity between founders and early employees is stark: Gates at $50 billion versus most others at $1-20 million
One early employee (Marla Wood) sued Microsoft for sex discrimination after leaving
Microsoft's first employee, Marc McDonald, holds badge number 00001 and eventually rejoined through an acquisition
money_quotes: Marc McDonald: Programmer. Microsoft's first employee. Left Microsoft in 1984 because it was 'too big'
Bob O'Rear: Chief mathematician. Left Microsoft in 1993. Now a cattle rancher.
Bob Wallace: Production manager-designer. Left Microsoft in 1983. Was a psychedelic-drug advocate.
Marla Wood: Bookkeeper. Left Microsoft in 1980, then sued the company for sex discrimination. Now a self-described 'professional volunteer'.
posts.key_insight: The early employees of what became one of history's most valuable companies largely cashed out early and pursued unconventional second acts, suggesting that being present at a tech revolution's birth doesn't guarantee—or even predict—staying for the ride.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts the story of Intel's CPUID hack, where the Pentium 4 was assigned family code 15 instead of the logical 8 due to a bug in Windows NT 4.0. The NT code only examined three bits of the CPU family field, causing it to interpret family 8 as family 0, which crashed the system. Intel couldn't use family 7 either since that was already assigned to Itanium. The workaround of using 15 (binary 1111) ensured NT would read it as family 7, avoiding the crash while maintaining backward compatibility.
key_points: Before CPUID, detecting processor types required elaborate workarounds involving illegal opcodes and known processor bugs
Windows NT 4.0 only examined the first three bits of the CPU family code, a shortcut that worked fine until family codes exceeded 7
Intel chose family code 15 for Pentium 4 because its binary representation (1111) would appear as family 7 to the buggy NT code
Hardware decisions are sometimes dictated by the need to work around legacy software bugs rather than logical engineering choices
The hack demonstrates how technical debt in one system can propagate constraints across an entire industry
money_quotes: The worst hacks are always in software, but those software hacks have an insidious tendency to seep into the hardware, too.
These methods involved checking for illegal opcodes, using known bugs in prior processors, a voodoo doll of Charles Babbage, and a Ouija board.
The NT code only looks at the first three bits of the CPU family when configuring the system.
Windows NT goes wacko when it sees a CPU family zero. Serious wacko. Jack with an axe at the end of The Shining wacko.
This hack is nasty enough to make even Raymond Chen, the patron saint of nasty software hacks, blanch.
On the other hand, it is an instant upgrade from processor family 8 to processor family 15! Gee, thanks Windows NT 4.0!
posts.key_insight: Software bugs don't just affect software—they can permanently alter hardware design decisions, as Intel had to change the Pentium 4's family code to work around a three-bit limitation in Windows NT.

posts.summary: Atwood examines whether programming language choice significantly impacts productivity by comparing Code Complete's language productivity ratios with a practical test of reading and printing a file across multiple languages. While scripting languages like Python require fewer lines of code than Java or C#, he observes that most of the overhead in non-scripting languages comes from class and object-oriented boilerplate rather than the core logic. He cites research showing that programmer skill variance within a single language is as large or larger than variance between languages. Given the abandonment of C/C++ for mainstream work, the influence of individual skill, and the adoption of scripting conventions in Java and .NET, Atwood provocatively concludes that modern programming languages may be essentially equivalent.
key_points: High-level languages improve productivity by 5-15x over low-level languages like assembly and C, with each line of code accomplishing more
Scripting languages (Python, Perl, Ruby) require roughly half the lines of code as non-scripting languages (Java, C#) for equivalent tasks
Most overhead in non-scripting languages comes from class, function, and object-oriented cruft rather than the actual work being performed
Programmer skill variation within a single language is as large or larger than the variation between different programming languages
The distinction between scripting and non-scripting languages may be less meaningful as Java and .NET adopt dynamic language conventions
money_quotes: You save time when you don't need to have an awards ceremony every time a C statement does what it's supposed to do.
Most of the overhead in the non-scripting languages comes from the cruft associated with classes, functions, and object orientation.
The main work loop, if considered alone, is almost identical in every language!

For all program aspects investigated, the performance variability that derives from differences among programmers of the same language is on average as large or larger than the variability found among the different languages.
I can definitely respect the passion behind this statement, but the actual data doesn't support a magic bullet language effect.
Maybe all modern programming languages really are the same.
posts.key_insight: The productivity difference between programmers using the same language often exceeds the productivity difference between languages themselves, suggesting language choice matters less than we think.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood debunks the TV/movie trope of 'enhancing' low-resolution images to reveal hidden detail, explaining that bitmaps fundamentally don't scale well. He compares three interpolation methods—nearest neighbor, bilinear, and bicubic filtering—demonstrating that while each produces different visual results, none can actually create detail that wasn't in the original image. The post notes that even high-powered video cards don't bother with bicubic filtering, suggesting the improvement over bilinear is marginal. Atwood concludes that the real solution is abandoning bitmaps for vector graphics entirely.
key_points: The 'enhance' trope in movies where low-res images reveal infinite detail is pure fiction—bitmaps don't scale well
Bilinear filtering produces blurry but smoother results compared to chunky nearest-neighbor scaling
Bicubic filtering is sharper than bilinear but introduces artifacts like halation at color borders, and video cards don't implement it despite having transistors to spare
All interpolation methods are just guessing at missing pixels—the detail simply isn't there to extract
Vector graphics are the only true solution to the scaling problem
money_quotes: I don't know what kind of crazy infinite-detail fractal images these scriptwriters think we have.
Here in the real world, bitmaps don't scale worth a damn.
If bicubic is only offered by paint programs, you have to wonder, is it really worth it?
Either way you're interpolating, it's just a question of how sharp you like your simulated pixels to be.
The best solution of all is to move to a vector representation and give up on bitmaps – and interpolation – entirely.
posts.key_insight: No amount of clever interpolation can create image detail that never existed—the 'enhance' button is a Hollywood fantasy, and the only real solution is using vector graphics from the start.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to criticism of his earlier post about managed code's productivity benefits by clarifying his position on C/C++ versus higher-level languages. He distinguishes between 'mainstream programming' (the total volume of code written) and 'mainstream programs' (commercial shipping applications), arguing that performance-critical code represents a tiny fraction of all code written. He uses the cautionary tale of Corel Office for Java's 1997 failure—with its two-second delay between keystrokes—to illustrate why C/C++ still matter for performance-critical applications, while maintaining that their importance is diminishing.
key_points: The distinction between 'mainstream programming' and 'mainstream programs' matters—most code written isn't performance-critical commercial software
Performance-critical applications requiring C/C++ represent an incredibly small fraction of all code written annually
Corel Office for Java (1997) serves as a perfect example of why you can't ignore performance entirely—a two-second delay per keystroke is unusable regardless of the theoretical benefits
C, C++, and assembler remain valid tools but are of 'increasingly diminished importance' in most development contexts
The dream of platform-independent software through Java didn't pan out as promised in the 1990s
money_quotes: What I actually said was mainstream programming, not mainstream programs.
And in which of those applications will performance be the primary consideration? It's an incredibly tiny fraction!
Despite the incredible slowness and the sparseness of features, this is really, really cool
So, er, good luck with that.
Where is Corel Office for Java now? Seriously, where is it? I can't find any mention of it.
I argue that they are of increasingly diminished importance, but I would never propose that every application should be written in .NET.
At least not with a straight face.
posts.key_insight: While higher-level languages dominate the majority of programming work, the spectacular failure of performance-blind projects like Corel Office for Java proves that low-level languages remain essential for applications where speed actually matters.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood introduces Clean Sources Plus, an improvement on Omar Shahine's Clean Sources utility for .NET developers. The original tool removed build artifacts (bin, obj folders) before sharing projects, but Atwood identified a critical gap: it didn't remove source control bindings or user-specific settings files. His enhanced version adds these cleanup features plus a convenient zip functionality, making it easier to share pristine .NET solutions without the cruft of local development configuration.
key_points: The original Clean Sources tool only removed build folders, leaving source control bindings and user settings that shouldn't be shared
Clean Sources Plus removes bin/obj/Debug/Release folders, source control bindings from project files, and user setting files
Version 1.1 added a 'Clean and Zip' option and made deletion patterns configurable via config file
The tool was tested with C# and VB projects but may have edge cases with other project types
money_quotes: This application does one thing.
The source control bindings aren't removed!
The result is a very clean, minimal set of .NET solution files, suitable for upload or sharing.
The regex patterns used to determine what files and folders to delete are now set in the .config file.
If this app deletes all your source code, then it's Omar's fault.
posts.key_insight: When sharing code, removing build artifacts isn't enough—you also need to strip out source control bindings and local user settings to create a truly portable solution.

posts.summary: This post examines the reentrancy pitfalls of using Application.DoEvents() in Windows Forms applications. A colleague's code sample demonstrates how DoEvents processes all messages in the queue, not just paint messages, leading to unexpected method reentry. The post acknowledges these dangers but ultimately defends DoEvents as a pragmatic middle ground between frozen UIs and complex multithreaded code. Atwood suggests that despite its quirks, DoEvents remains valuable for specific use cases like improving perceived form load performance.
key_points: DoEvents processes ALL messages in the queue, not just paint messages, which causes unexpected reentrancy
Any method called directly or indirectly in response to message queue processing can be reentered during DoEvents
A Form.Busy property that only processes paint messages would be a useful addition to prevent input-related reentrancy
DoEvents represents a pragmatic compromise between unresponsive UIs and full multithreaded complexity
The technique can be valuable for specific scenarios like improving perceived form load performance
money_quotes: DoEvents processes all of the messages in the message queue, not just paint messages.
Always remember that DoEvents can cause methods to be reentered.
I think it's definitely the lesser of two evils: it's either this simplified cooperative yielding or full-bore multithreaded code.
DoEvents can be a big win with minimal effort in the right situations.
posts.key_insight: DoEvents is a dangerous but sometimes worthwhile shortcut—understanding its reentrancy implications lets you use it safely as an alternative to complex threading.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood introduces Microsoft LogParser, a tool that enables SQL-like queries across various log file types. He demonstrates its power through practical IIS log query examples covering traffic analysis, performance monitoring, and error tracking. While praising the tool as '96.44% awesome,' he notes limitations including missing standard deviation functions, Office dependencies for graphing, and unreliable CSV type detection. The post serves as both an introduction and practical tutorial for developers dealing with log analysis.
key_points: LogParser enables SQL-like querying of diverse log formats including IIS logs, event logs, CSV files, and even RSS feeds
The tool is powerful enough to warrant an entire book and an unofficial fansite
Practical examples cover common needs: top URLs, slowest responses, HTTP errors, bandwidth analysis
Key limitations include missing statistical functions, Office dependency for graphs, and CSV type detection issues
The concept extends beyond LogParser to WQL and various web query languages
money_quotes: Ask yourself this question: what if everything could be queried with SQL?
It's an incredibly powerful concept, and the LogParser implementation doesn't disappoint.
Although LogParser is 96.44% awesome, there are a few things that I didn't like about it.
I really, really need a standard deviation function. Min, Max, and Avg are nice but totally inadequate for determining how variable something is.
Microsoft's WQL (WMI Query Language) is similar but more annoying and less powerful.
posts.key_insight: Applying SQL's familiar query paradigm to log files transforms tedious log analysis into a tractable and even enjoyable task.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that no matter how sophisticated the backend architecture or code quality, users judge software entirely by its interface. He draws on examples from Yukihiro Matsumoto and Joel Spolsky to illustrate how a poor or unpolished UI can doom a project regardless of its technical merits. A demo with complete functionality but placeholder graphics led clients to fixate only on appearance. The post concludes that developers must prioritize UI work because to users, the interface literally is the application.
key_points: Users cannot see or appreciate backend complexity; they only experience the interface
A system with bugs can be fixed, but a system with a bad interface has nothing to build on
Clients and stakeholders will fixate on visual polish even when core functionality is complete
Technical architecture diagrams and internal quality are invisible to end users
UI deserves high priority from the start, not treatment as an afterthought
money_quotes: Is the fact that it's very hard to use not a bug in and of itself?
If your system has a bad interface, no one will use it.
The clients spent the entire meeting griping about the graphical appearance of the screen.
It was almost as if they thought they had hired painters.
I don't care how many kick-ass Visio architecture diagrams you have; as far as the user is concerned, the UI is the application.
You have to build an impressive UI if you want to be taken seriously.
Give your UI the high priority it deserves.
posts.key_insight: To users, the interface is the entire product—no amount of backend excellence matters if the UI fails to impress.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood enthusiastically recommends 'The Typing of the Dead,' a game that transforms typing practice into a zombie-shooting experience. He argues that software developers should be competent typists and presents this quirky game as both an effective typing tutor and entertaining multiplayer experience. The post celebrates the game's bizarre combination of educational software and B-movie horror aesthetics. Atwood also notes the interesting trivia that Mavis Beacon, the iconic typing tutor character, was never a real person.
key_points: Software developers should prioritize being decent typists as a fundamental skill
The Typing of the Dead successfully combines education with entertainment in an absurdly creative way
The game offers competitive 2-player network play with detailed statistics tracking
Mavis Beacon is a fictional marketing creation, similar to other tech mascots like Carmen Sandiego
money_quotes: Any self-respecting software developer should be a decent typist.
Typing of the Dead is like Mavis Beacon Teaches Typing... if Mavis Beacon was a flesh-eating zombie!

It's a challenging, remarkably well thought out typing tutor, a tongue-in-cheek b-grade zombie movie, and the most hilarious multiplayer game experience you'll probably ever have – all at the same time.
It's hard to explain just how wondrously bizarre Typing of the Dead is.
Mavis Beacon isn't a real person. She's an imaginary identity created entirely for the purpose of marketing.
posts.key_insight: The best way to improve a fundamental developer skill like typing is to make practice genuinely fun, even if that means fighting zombies.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the best programmers combine two counterintuitive traits: laziness and intellectual humility. Lazy programmers build tools to automate tedious work, while 'dumb' programmers maintain a beginner's mind that keeps them learning and asking basic questions. He observes that many developers avoid asking simple questions due to programming culture's macho attitude, yet the best developers he's worked with aren't afraid to look foolish. The post extends this philosophy beyond individual coding to business strategy: successful software acknowledges that users, like developers, take the path of least resistance.
key_points: Lazy programmers are valuable because they build automation tools to avoid repetitive work
Programmers who think they're smart stop learning and become uncritical of their own work
Asking 'dumb' questions is a hallmark of the best developers, not a weakness
The macho programming culture discourages asking basic questions, which hurts code quality
Understanding that people take the path of least resistance is key to building successful software
money_quotes: Nobody is really smart enough to program computers.
In the endless battle between programmer and compiler, give up early and admit that it's always you and never the compiler who is at fault.
Having the guts to ask basic questions is, ironically enough, a hallmark of the best developers I've ever worked with.
Programmers who know they are smart stop learning, stop being critical towards their own work.
The only way you'll ever succeed as a software developer is through humility and the Zen concept of beginner's mind.
Being lazy and dumb isn't just good career advice: it's the key to running a successful software business, too.
The only way to build great software is to make things as easy as possible for your users.
posts.key_insight: The paradox of great programming is that intellectual humility and strategic laziness outperform raw intelligence and hard work.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the emerging threat of poker bots to online gambling, profiling WinHoldEm creator Ray Bornert and his controversial software that automates poker play. The post explores how bots use screen-scraping to play autonomously, the countermeasures casinos deploy, and why Bornert frames selling bot software as 'civil disobedience.' Atwood argues that the rise of bots is inevitable given the financial incentives, drawing on his own experience with cheating in free online games. He concludes by analyzing technical countermeasures casinos could employ, including dynamic UI changes that would defeat screen-scraping approaches.
key_points: Poker bots like WinHoldEm use GUI screen-scraping to read cards and automate play, requiring specific display settings to function
The creator justifies selling bot software as 'democratizing' cheating tools, arguing bots became inevitable once poker moved online
Cheating is rampant even in free games with no money at stake, suggesting online poker with real money will be far worse
Online casinos fight back by scanning for bot software and engaging players in conversation to verify they're human
The most effective countermeasure would be randomizing the UI every hand, making screen-scraping impractical
money_quotes: If people are that willing to cheat for literally nothing, with zero money at stake, imagine what lengths they'll go to when real money is involved.
Nothing psyches you out more than the idea that another player can switch to perfect, bot-like skills at will.
Did they win because they were better than you, or because they cheated? There's just no way to know. It utterly destroys the game experience.
The reality is that the game changed the moment it moved to the Internet.
I'd rather be unethical than be a victim. This is intentional civil disobedience.
The rise of the bots is completely inevitable. There's simply too much money at stake.
A poker bot is your equipment.
posts.key_insight: When systems move online where automation is possible and money is at stake, cheating through bots becomes not just likely but inevitable, fundamentally changing the nature of competition.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the surprising origins of common programming terms and conventions that developers take for granted. He traces 'foo' and 'bar' back to pre-WWII comic strips rather than the commonly assumed FUBAR military acronym. The word 'widget' originated from a 1924 satirical play about corporate meaninglessness. He also explains that pangrams like 'Pack my box with five dozen liquor jugs' were used by the Beagle Brothers for font sampling, demonstrating how programming culture absorbed terminology from unexpected sources in entertainment and business.
key_points: The word 'foo' predates FUBAR, originating in the Smokey Stover comic strip from 1930-1952, where it appeared as nonsense phrases like 'He who foos last foos best'
'Widget' comes from the 1924 play 'Beggar on Horseback' where it represented meaningless corporate products in a satirical nightmare sequence
Pangrams used in font displays have varied histories, with Beagle Brothers choosing one involving alcohol to match their homebrew spirit
Many programming conventions have roots in pre-computer culture from comics, theater, and business school terminology
money_quotes: When 'foo' is used in connection with 'bar' it has generally traced to the WWII-era Army slang acronym FUBAR, later modified to foobar. Early versions of the Jargon File interpreted this change as a post-war bowdlerization, but it now seems more likely that FUBAR was itself a derivative of 'foo'
The word 'foo' itself had an immediate prewar history in comic strips and cartoons
He who foos last foos best
Part of the point, of course, is that no one ever tells him what 'widgets' are
It's a pangram: it uses all 26 letters of the alphabet in a single sentence
The pangram Beagle Bros chose is a great expression of their homebrew spirit: it's rather clever and it involves alcohol
posts.key_insight: Programming jargon that seems technical and modern often has whimsical origins in pre-computer entertainment and culture, from comic strips to satirical plays.

posts.summary: Atwood challenges Jakob Nielsen's 1996 assertion that web users don't scroll, presenting contradictory evidence from usability studies that found no negative correlation between below-the-fold clicks and user success. He traces how Nielsen eventually walked back this claim in 2003, acknowledging that users had learned to scroll. The post then pivots to a crucial distinction from Joel Spolsky: the difference between usability (efficiency for experienced users) and learnability (ease for first-time users). Atwood argues that any user who couldn't learn to scroll within hours wouldn't be using the internet long anyway, illustrating how overly cautious design advice can persist beyond its relevance.
key_points: Nielsen's 'users don't scroll' claim from 1996 was contradicted by actual usability studies that found clicks equally distributed above and below the fold
The distinction between usability and learnability is crucial—learnable interfaces may be cumbersome for experienced users, while usable interfaces may have steep learning curves
Design decisions should consider whether users are occasional or frequent—tourist kiosks need learnability, professional tools need usability
Users adapt and learn quickly; assuming permanent incompetence leads to unnecessarily restrictive design
Widely-held usability beliefs can persist long after evidence contradicts them
money_quotes: A user who can't learn to scroll within a few hours certainly won't be using the internet for very long.
When you sit somebody down in a typical usability test, you're really testing how learnable your interface is, not how usable it is.
Learnable user interfaces may be extremely cumbersome to experienced users.

If you make people walk through a fifteen-step wizard to print, people will be pleased the first time, less pleased the second time, and downright ornery by the fifth time they go through your rigmarole.
And that's why, when you press the brakes on your car, you don't get a little dialog popping up that says 'Stop now? (yes/no).'
Believe me, I've learned this the hard way; you're lucky if they read anything, much less the first paragraph.
posts.key_insight: Usability testing often measures learnability rather than true usability, and design advice based on novice behavior can unnecessarily handicap experienced users.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood enthusiastically recommends the second edition of Steve Krug's 'Don't Make Me Think,' a book on usability and UI design. He considers it the single most important book on usability that anyone should read, noting its accessibility even for non-technical readers like his wife. The post expresses mild frustration that details about what's new in the second edition aren't readily available. Atwood plans to buy the new edition and give away his old copy to spread usability awareness among colleagues.
key_points: Don't Make Me Think is the definitive single-book recommendation for learning usability and UI design
The book is accessible enough for non-developers to enjoy and learn from
Spreading usability knowledge to managers and fellow developers is valuable but getting them to actually read is the challenge
New editions of foundational books are worth keeping up with
money_quotes: If you only read one book on usability and UI, it should be Krug's Don't Make Me Think.
I even gave it to my wife to read, and she enjoyed it too. Is there any higher praise?
Now if only I could get them to read it...
posts.key_insight: Great usability resources are only valuable if you can actually get people to engage with them.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood celebrates the underutilized Windows key and its built-in keyboard shortcuts, then shows how to extend its power with third-party tools like WinKey and AutoHotKey. He argues that most daily computing tasks should be one key combination away. After trying various Run dialog replacements without success, he presents a clever hack: adding a personal shortcuts folder to the user's Path environment variable, enabling custom keywords in the standard Run dialog. This approach gives him the best of both worlds—default Windows key shortcuts plus unlimited smart keywords.
key_points: The Windows key has many built-in shortcuts beyond just opening the Start Menu that most users don't know about
Tools like WinKey let you map additional Windows key shortcuts for frequently used applications
Third-party Run dialog replacements (ActiveWords, AppRocket, SlickRun) are feature-rich but hard to adopt due to muscle memory
A clever Path environment variable hack makes the default Run dialog smarter without requiring new software
The goal is having all frequently used applications exactly one key combination away
money_quotes: All of my most frequently used applications are exactly one key combination away.
Wouldn't it be nice if the Start, Run dialog was just a little bit smarter?
I couldn't get myself out of the reflexive habit of Windows+R.
I've tried each and every one of these solutions, but nothing 'stuck.'
For me, it's the best of both worlds: I can leverage the default Windows key accelerators and also have an unlimited number of 'smart keywords' via my workhorse Run menu.
posts.key_insight: The best productivity enhancement often isn't a fancy new tool, but a clever hack that makes your existing habits smarter.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques 'Head First Design Patterns' for burying its most important advice—that simplicity should trump pattern usage—near the end of a 629-page book. He argues that the book irresponsibly encourages beginners to experiment with patterns when they should be learning to write simple code first. The post highlights how the book itself acknowledges patterns should only be used when practically needed, yet structures itself to promote pattern enthusiasm before delivering this crucial caveat. Atwood contends this approach is dangerous because junior developers already tend toward over-engineering, and encouraging pattern experimentation is like 'throwing gasoline on a fire.'
key_points: The book's most critical advice about simplicity appears on pages 594-595 of a 629-page book, which Atwood calls 'downright irresponsible'
Beginning developers naturally over-use patterns and objects; encouraging experimentation makes this worse
The best way to learn simple code is to write simple code, not to first master complex patterns
Patterns and complexity should be avoided until absolutely necessary—this should be lesson one, not the conclusion
The book contradicts itself by promoting pattern usage throughout while admitting simpler solutions are often better
money_quotes: Filling 593 pages with rah-rah pattern talk, and then tacking this critical guidance on at the end of the book is downright irresponsible.
This advice should be in 72 point blinking Comic Sans on the very first page.
Beginning developers never met a pattern or an object they didn't like.
Encouraging them to experiment with patterns is like throwing gasoline on a fire.
The best way to learn to write simple code is to write simple code!
Patterns, like all forms of complexity, should be avoided until they are absolutely necessary. That's the first thing beginners need to learn. Not the last thing.
Do you really want a junior developer using patterns everywhere? It's about as safe as encouraging them to 'experiment' with a gas-powered chainsaw.
posts.key_insight: Teaching developers to master design patterns before teaching them to value simplicity produces over-engineered code; simplicity should be the foundation of software education, not its capstone.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood introduces static code analysis tools as essential aids for understanding unfamiliar codebases, comparing them to Star Trek tricorders that provide diagnostic snapshots of code health. He argues these tools should be viewed as helpful recommendation engines rather than strict enforcement mechanisms. The post surveys the .NET static analysis landscape circa 2005, with FxCop as the dominant free option and its upcoming integration into Visual Studio 2005. Atwood provides a comprehensive list of alternative tools while noting the commercial options tend to be expensive and overlap with FxCop's capabilities.
key_points: Static analysis tools are most valuable for navigating unfamiliar codebases, providing a quick diagnostic snapshot of code quality
These tools should be framed as recommendation engines rather than correctness enforcers to avoid developer resistance
FxCop is the leading free option for .NET, with customizable rules and upcoming VS2005 integration
Commercial alternatives exist but offer questionable value given their overlap with free tools and high prices
The ability to selectively enable/disable rules and create custom profiles is essential for practical adoption
money_quotes: Navigating a new codebase can feel like like landing on an alien planet.
The last thing developers want is a Code Nazi peering over their shoulder.
I prefer to think of these tools as software tricorders, collecting a bunch of recommendations and metrics about our code. What we choose to do with that data is up to us.
You'd be surprised how helpful it is, particularly for identifying unused variables and functions.
You may also be surprised how annoying some of the rules are.
Don't bother if you're wearing a red shirt. In classic Trek fashion, you may not be around long enough to care.
posts.key_insight: Static code analysis tools are most effective when positioned as helpful diagnostic aids rather than rigid correctness enforcers, giving developers agency over how to act on the recommendations.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software configurability, while appealing to power users, often creates more problems than it solves. He observes that customized environments don't transfer across machines, making users helpless on stock systems and unable to collaborate effectively. Drawing on Joel Spolsky's insights, he notes that even power users eventually abandon customization because settings get lost during upgrades and don't propagate. Atwood concludes that excessive configurability represents a failure of design responsibility, and that the 'convention over configuration' philosophy—as exemplified by Ruby on Rails—forces better defaults and clearer decision-making.
key_points: Highly customized development environments cripple your ability to work on other machines or collaborate with colleagues
Even power users eventually stop customizing because settings don't persist across reinstalls, upgrades, and multiple machines
Every configuration option forces users to make decisions, adding cognitive load unrelated to their actual goals
Leaving design choices to users is 'the ultimate abdication of the designer's responsibility'
Convention over configuration forces developers to choose good defaults and commit to a strategy rather than hedging
money_quotes: I've crippled my ability to work on a stock Windows XP or Win2k3 box.
I've asked a lot of my 'power user' friends about this; hardly any of them do any customization other than the bare minimum necessary to make their system behave reasonably.
Every time you provide an option, you're asking the user to make a decision.
Don't make them think about anything outside the narrow focus of their immediate goal.
Leaving the design to the users is the ultimate abdication of the designer's responsibility to provide a quality product.
It is absolutely essential for the designer to give the users a carefully thought out set of defaults to start with.
You might come out ahead by intentionally choosing to make things not configurable.
posts.key_insight: Configurability is often a crutch that lets designers avoid the hard work of choosing good defaults, while simultaneously burdening users with decisions they shouldn't have to make.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares a small discovery he made while examining a Windows ME screenshot in a Joel Spolsky article. He noticed Qantas icons that were shortcuts to a Unix machine at Fog Creek Software. After investigating, he realized the machine name 'Qantas' was a clever reference to the movie Rain Man, where the character Raymond Babbitt obsessively insists that Qantas has never crashed. Atwood concludes with appreciation for Spolsky's sense of humor, noting he must be fun to work with.
key_points: A Unix machine at Fog Creek Software was named 'Qantas' based on evidence in old screenshots and forum posts
The name is a pun referencing Rain Man, where the autistic savant character famously declares 'QANTAS never crashed'
Small details in screenshots and documentation can reveal personality and culture of a workplace
Atwood expresses respect for Spolsky's humor even when disagreeing with his technical opinions
money_quotes: Even if I don't always agree with him, the guy has got to be fun to work with.
QANTAS. QANTAS never crashed.
posts.key_insight: The small, nerdy cultural references hidden in a company's technical infrastructure reveal something endearing about the people who work there.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares practical techniques for speeding up web browsing by disabling Flash and using a custom HOSTS file to block advertising domains. He walks through the steps to disable Flash in Internet Explorer, noting that Flash is mostly decorative 'chrome' that slows down browsing and causes memory leaks in Firefox. The post then explains how the HOSTS file can redirect ad domain requests to localhost, returning instant 404 errors instead of downloading bulky ad content. Atwood frames these optimizations as pro-speed rather than anti-advertising, emphasizing that the fastest downloads are ones you never make.
key_points: Disabling Flash removes visual distractions and reduces page load times, since Flash content is usually decorative rather than essential
Flash causes significant memory leaks in Firefox, making it worth disabling for browser stability
The HOSTS file can be used to redirect ad domains to 127.0.0.1, blocking ads at the network level before DNS lookup
This approach returns 404 errors in milliseconds instead of downloading 20-50kb ad files from remote servers
The techniques are framed as performance optimizations rather than ideological opposition to advertising
money_quotes: I've got nothing personal against Flash, mind you, but it's generally chrome.
I rarely need to see Flash movies as part of my normal web browsing.
I'm not anti advertising so much as I am pro speed.
And the fastest downloads of all are the ones you don't have to do!
When your browser tries to download an advertising image from, say, m.doubleclick.net, it returns in under a millisecond with a 404 'file not found' error.
posts.key_insight: The fastest way to speed up web browsing is to eliminate unnecessary downloads entirely, whether that's decorative Flash content or advertising files.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares a collection of Windows registry tweaks he's compiled and enhanced, building on Scott Hanselman's power user tips. The post provides practical modifications including adding command prompt access to folder right-click menus, fixing Windows XP's broken file search that ignores unknown extensions, improving the Disk Cleanup wizard by removing useless options and adding more aggressive cleanup rules, and adding useful shortcuts to the My Computer context menu. Atwood emphasizes these are quality-of-life improvements that should have been Windows defaults, while acknowledging the risks of registry editing and providing rollback files.
key_points: Windows XP's built-in search inexplicably ignores files without known extension associations by default, a design flaw that can waste hours of debugging time
The Disk Cleanup wizard ships with time-wasting features like 'compress old files' that can be fixed through registry modifications
Right-click context menus are highly customizable through the registry, allowing quick access to regedit, services, and Add/Remove Programs from My Computer
The built-in Send To folder provides a simpler alternative to registry hacks for adding items to right-click menus
Registry tweaks require caution but can dramatically improve Windows usability when the defaults are poorly designed
money_quotes: For some godforsaken reason, Start, Search only searches files with known extension associations by default.
I've run into this 'feature' before and I nearly pulled my hair out trying to figure out why it couldn't find the source file that I knew was there.
Nothing refreshes like giving the Windows undercarriage a little how's your father.
Once you try it, you'll wonder why these menu options aren't there to begin with.
This should probably go without saying, but we are deleting stuff here. Use this at your own risk.
posts.key_insight: Windows ships with baffling default behaviors that power users must fix themselves through registry hacks, revealing a gap between Microsoft's design decisions and what developers actually need.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his excitement about attending his first technical conference, the 2005 Professional Developers Conference. He notes the timing is significant with VS.NET 2005 and SQL Server 2005 releases imminent, and mentions his company Vertigo has work being featured in a keynote. However, he humorously admits his real motivation is meeting bloggers he follows, choosing sessions based on whose blogs he enjoys rather than technical topics. He closes with self-deprecating humor about blending into the crowd of developers.
key_points: This is Atwood's first technical conference despite having attended trade shows before
The timing coincides with Microsoft's 'magical third version' releases of VS.NET 2005 and SQL Server 2005
He's selecting sessions based entirely on bloggers he follows, not on technical content
He expresses surprise that not every presenter is a blogger, revealing his assumption about the blogging community's prominence
The post captures the early blogging era when developer blogs were creating new forms of professional community
money_quotes: This is the first technical conference I've ever attended.
And this is the magical third version of a Microsoft product – heady times indeed!
I'm only there for the chicks.
I'm selecting sessions solely based on blogs I enjoy with no regard whatsoever for topic.
I was surprised how few names in the session list I actually recognized; I guess I expected every presenter to be a blogger.
If anyone's looking for me at the PDC, just keep an eye out for a mildly overweight middle-aged white guy wearing glasses.
posts.key_insight: Technical conferences are as much about connecting with the people whose writing has influenced you as they are about the technical content itself.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood summarizes Marcus Ranum's influential essay on the fundamental flaws in computer security thinking. The post highlights six common but misguided approaches: default permit policies, blacklist-based security, reactive patching, glorifying hackers, relying on user education, and rushing to adopt new solutions. Atwood emphasizes that Microsoft's shift to 'off by default' with Windows Server 2003 demonstrates how addressing even one of these issues can dramatically improve security. The post concludes with a reflection on client-side security, suggesting that until PCs require hardware modifications to run custom software (like game consoles), client security will remain fundamentally compromised.
key_points: Default permit ('on by default') policies were the primary reason Windows security was considered an oxymoron for years
Blacklists are fundamentally flawed as a primary defense mechanism and should only serve helper roles
Security must be designed from the inside out - patching cannot fix fundamentally bad security architecture
Relying on user education as a security measure ignores the reality that users are fallible by default
Client PCs are inherently untrustworthy until hardware-level restrictions prevent arbitrary code execution
money_quotes: The good news is that Microsoft's new policy of 'off by default' that kicked off with Windows Server 2003 is really working.
This is why blacklists are, and always will be, a bad idea.
Security starts from the inside, not the outside. No amount of patching will fix a fundamentally bad security design.
A security system that fails to assume users are fallible and weak by default is destined to fail spectacularly.
You can always recognize the pioneers from all the arrows in their backs.
I think it's a myth that you can protect yourself from the client PC anyway – the client is always suspect.
posts.key_insight: Most security failures stem not from sophisticated attacks but from fundamentally flawed assumptions baked into system design from the start.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood pushes back against the claim that UI design is still in its infancy, pointing to documented UI pattern libraries and impressive demos from Windows Vista and Office 12 at PDC 2005. He acknowledges there's still progress to be made but argues the field has matured significantly. The post highlights Microsoft's strategy of incorporating web metaphors—like back/forward buttons, single-task pages, and ubiquitous search—into desktop applications. Atwood sees this convergence of web and desktop UI patterns as a positive evolution in interface design.
key_points: UI design has matured beyond the 'stone age' as evidenced by documented pattern libraries
Microsoft is aggressively incorporating web metaphors into Vista and Office
Web-native concepts like forward/back buttons, single-task pages, and search boxes are becoming desktop standards
The existence of formal UI pattern documentation suggests the field has reached a level of professional maturity
money_quotes: If UI design is in the stone age, why are there at least two sites which document known UI patterns?

I was struck by how aggressively Microsoft has folded web metaphors into Vista and Office – things like the forward and back buttons, focusing on a single task per page, and the ubiquitous search box.
posts.key_insight: The cross-pollination of web interface conventions into desktop software signals that UI design has evolved past its primitive era into a more mature discipline with documented patterns and shared metaphors.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that organizational cultures often punish negative thinking and risk identification, creating dangerous blind spots in software projects. Drawing on DeMarco and Lister's 'Waltzing with Bears' and the Challenger disaster, he shows how unwritten rules against being a 'spoiler' prevent teams from surfacing critical risks. Atwood reframes developers who identify problems as valuable team players demonstrating deep knowledge and concern for quality. He advocates for actively soliciting input on what could go wrong throughout a project's lifecycle, not just at the start.
key_points: Corporate cultures often have unwritten rules that discourage articulating risks, treating it as anti-team behavior
The Challenger disaster exemplifies how penalizing negative thinking can have catastrophic consequences
The biggest risk on any software project is the risks you haven't considered
Developers who proactively identify problems demonstrate deep application knowledge and concern for quality
Risk assessment should be continuous throughout a project's lifecycle, not just at kickoff
money_quotes: The culture of our organizations sometimes makes it impossible to talk about a really worrisome risk.
We are like a primitive tribe that tries to hold the devil at bay by refusing to say his name.
Articulating a risk shouldn't be seen as anti-team, but it often is.
Don't articulate a problem unless you want its immediate solution to become your responsibility.
The biggest risk on any software project is the risks you haven't considered.
You'll do a lot better at risk management if you encourage a culture of responsible risk assessment instead of mindless can-do heroics.
A less concerned developer would shrug this off as 'not their problem.' They get paid either way, right?
posts.key_insight: Healthy software teams must actively cultivate cultures where identifying risks is rewarded rather than punished, because the dangers you refuse to discuss are the ones most likely to sink your project.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his appreciation for the Moire screensaver from the DirectX 8.1 SDK, highlighting its multi-monitor support and hardware acceleration. He identifies a flaw: the screensaver always cycles through the same colors in the same order. After tracking down the original SDK source code, he collaborated with a C++ developer to modify the screensaver with a better random color algorithm. He provides both the original and modified source code, plus a compiled binary for those who just want to use it.
key_points: The Moire screensaver stands out for its multi-monitor support and efficient hardware acceleration
The original implementation had a predictable color cycling pattern that repeated identically each time
Atwood hunted down the DirectX 8.1 SDK to obtain the source code
He collaborated with a more skilled C++ developer to implement truly random colors
He shares both source code and binaries, lowering the barrier for others to use or modify it
money_quotes: I'm not a big screensaver enthusiast per se, but one of my all time favorite screensavers is definitely Moire from the DirectX 8.1 SDK.
It's simple yet visually striking, and it works seamlessly on multiple monitors.
One thing that always bugged me about Moire, though, was that it chose the same colors over and over.
Then I was able to hack in a more sophisticated random color algorithm with my completely negligible C++ coding skillz.
posts.key_insight: Even polished software has small annoyances worth fixing, and sharing your modifications openly benefits the broader community.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood addresses the problem of careless email communication, noting that the ease of sending emails often leads to thoughtless messages. He shares guidelines from Ole Eichhorn and 43 Folders about writing effective emails, including avoiding criticism via email, being judicious about recipients, and keeping messages concise. The post extends these principles to all communication methods, arguing that easier communication requires more deliberate thought. Atwood concludes that the real skill lies in knowing when to escalate between communication channels.
key_points: The ease of sending email inversely correlates with the effort people put into composing them
Email criticism and technical debates should be handled face-to-face or by phone instead
Effective emails require clear goals, strong subject lines, and brevity that fits on one screen
The same communication principles apply across all channels - IM, phone, and face-to-face
Choosing the right communication medium for the situation is itself a crucial skill
money_quotes: It's easy to fire off an email with barely any effort at all. And that's exactly how much effort goes into most emails: none.
Never criticize anyone in email.
Remember that email is a public and permanent record.
Assume no one will read more than the first two sentences of your email.
The key difference is the amount of effort required for each communication method: the easier it is, the more you need to consider before doing it.
The real art is knowing when to escalate from IM to email, from email to face-to-face, and when to drop the ultimate communication A-bomb: calling a meeting.
posts.key_insight: The easier a communication method is to use, the more deliberate thought you should put into whether and how to use it.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood provides a comprehensive guide to Windows Task Manager, an application he's used daily since its introduction with Windows NT 4.0 in 1996. He walks through each tab—Applications, Processes, Performance, and Networking—explaining hidden features and power-user tips that most people overlook. The post emphasizes that sorting process columns can diagnose almost any performance problem, and that commit charge is the single most important metric for understanding system health. Despite being nine years old at the time of writing, Atwood argues Task Manager's enduring utility is a testament to its original design quality, especially compared to other bundled Windows applets that have fallen out of use.
key_points: Task Manager's longevity and daily usefulness demonstrates excellent original design—unlike other Windows applets of similar age
Sorting columns in the Processes tab is the most powerful diagnostic tool for performance problems
Commit charge (not CPU or page file) is the single most important performance metric—if peak commit exceeds physical memory, your PC is thrashing
The default column set is too spartan; power users should enable additional columns via View menu
Double-clicking any tab's body area expands it to fill the entire window—a hidden but useful feature
money_quotes: Unlike virtually every other Windows OS applet of similar vintage I still use it every day. That's a testament to how well Task Manager was originally designed.
Sorting column headers is probably the single most powerful function in task manager. You can diagnose almost any performance problem by sorting the right process column!
Commit charge is the single most important section of the performance tab.
If the peak commit charge is greater than the physical memory in your PC, your PC is running out of memory and thrashing.
You want a reasonable amount of free memory, but free memory is also wasted memory – it should be utilized as disk cache whenever possible.
Charmap or WordPad, anyone? I didn't think so.
Here's to another 9 years of glorious Task Management!
posts.key_insight: A well-designed tool can remain useful for decades without significant changes, and Task Manager's enduring value lies in its ability to diagnose almost any performance problem through simple column sorting.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that verbose code is superior to concise code because code is written once but read many times. He uses examples from Monad (PowerShell), wget, and the VB.NET vs C# debate to illustrate how verbose, self-documenting syntax aids comprehension. Drawing heavily on Steve McConnell's Code Complete, he asserts that optimizing for write-time convenience over read-time convenience is a false economy. The post challenges the common criticism that VB.NET is 'too verbose' by reframing verbosity as a feature, not a bug.
key_points: Verbose command-line parameters (like --mirror vs -m) make code self-documenting and immediately understandable
The criticism that VB.NET is 'too verbose' treats verbosity as inherently negative when it's actually beneficial for readability
Saving keystrokes while writing code is a fool's economy since code is read far more often than it's written
Professional programmers write readable code regardless of whether they think they're the only ones who will read it
Studies show maintenance programmers spend 50-60% of their time just trying to understand existing code
money_quotes: People say VB.NET is too verbose like that's a bad thing.
Saving keystrokes while writing code is a fool's economy.
Is English too verbose? Would this post be easier to read in a court reporter's shorthand?
Making code readable is not an optional part of the development process, and favoring write-time convenience over read-time convenience is a false economy.
The idea of writing unreadable code because you're the only person working on a project sets a dangerous precedent.
A professional programmer writes readable code, period.
Habits affect all your work; you can't turn them on and off at will, so be sure that what you're doing is something that you want to become a habit.
posts.key_insight: Code readability through verbosity isn't a luxury or personal preference—it's a professional obligation that pays dividends across the entire maintenance lifecycle.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the unveiling of Office 12's ribbon interface at PDC 2005, arguing that it represents the death of the traditional main menu that has been a cornerstone of graphical user interfaces since the Apple Lisa. He explains that the ribbon consolidates all commands into a single, context-sensitive tabbed palette, eliminating the need to hunt through menus and toolbars. Atwood predicts this paradigm shift will spread across Microsoft's product line, third-party applications, and other operating systems, just as the toolbar did after Word introduced it in the early 90s. He also fact-checks Microsoft's press release, noting that toolbars appeared much earlier than 1997.
key_points: The Office 12 ribbon interface eliminates the traditional drop-down main menu entirely, replacing it with context-sensitive tabbed palettes
UI innovations in Office historically spread across the entire software industry, as the toolbar did after its introduction in Word
The main menu has been fundamental to the WIMP (Windows, Icons, Menus, Pointer) interface paradigm since the Apple Lisa era
Microsoft's own press materials incorrectly dated the toolbar's introduction to 1997, when it actually appeared in Word 1.1a and Excel 4.0 in the early 90s
money_quotes: We had just witnessed the death of the main menu.
There is no main menu.
That's huge. The main menu has been a cornerstone of the WIMP interface since way back in the days of the Apple Lisa.
There's no longer a stack of task panes and menus and toolbars to look through. There's just one place to look for commands.
The 'ribbon' is a similar paradigm shift. Eventually we'll all be using these tabbed palettes with nary a drop-down menu in sight.
I expect the traditional WIMP main menu to go the way of the dodo soon after the release of Vista and Office 12 in 2006.
I don't know what kind of crack those MS presspass guys are smoking...
posts.key_insight: Major UI innovations from Microsoft Office, like the toolbar before it and the ribbon now, tend to reshape the entire software industry's interface conventions.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood surveys the history of educational programming games, from the c-jump board game to classic Apple II titles like Robot Odyssey and Rocky's Boots, through to the 2001 game Mindrover. He traces a lineage of games that taught programming concepts through puzzle-solving and robot programming, highlighting how these games made learning boolean logic and circuit design engaging. The post serves as both nostalgia trip and gentle criticism of Microsoft's then-new Coding4Fun initiative, pointing out that the tradition of making programming fun extends back decades before corporate efforts to brand it.
key_points: The Oregon Trail, while not a programming game, established the template for educational computer games in 1971
Robot Odyssey and Rocky's Boots taught real programming concepts (boolean logic, inputs/outputs, circuit design) through robot puzzle games
Warren Robinett, creator of Rocky's Boots, also created the Atari 2600 BASIC programming cartridge and the influential game Adventure
Mindrover (2001) carried forward the tradition of programming games into 3D graphics
Microsoft's Coding4Fun site was arriving 20 years late to a party that Apple II games had started
money_quotes: Although the prospect of a computer programming board game sounds positively stultifying, there is a rich history of computer games based on programming.

Robot Odyssey was about programming robots to solve puzzles. In order to do that, of course, you'd have to wire up the guts of the robot, and that meant learning about connecting inputs to outputs, and boolean logic.
It had one of the best tutorial modes in any game I've ever played, which was critical for a game this complex.
Microsoft may have just launched their Coding4Fun site a few months ago, but the real programming fun started 20 years earlier.
posts.key_insight: Educational programming games that made learning genuinely fun existed decades before tech companies tried to brand 'coding as fun' as a novel concept.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood criticizes Microsoft's decision to rename exciting technologies like Avalon and Indigo to bland corporate names like 'Windows Presentation Foundation' and 'Windows Communication Foundation.' He draws on commentary from Jason Kemp and the Creating Passionate Users blog to argue that excessive professionalism strips products of their personality and appeal. The post suggests that companies lose their creative spark as they mature, trading passion for corporate safety. Atwood proposes that Microsoft could have retained both names, following Apple's approach with OS X codenames.
key_points: Microsoft replaced evocative codenames (Avalon, Indigo) with generic corporate names that lack personality
Companies often use 'professionalism' as justification for removing creativity and passion from their products
The obsession with conservative corporate naming may actually hurt rather than help market adoption
Apple successfully maintains dual naming (OS X Tiger, Panther) that preserves both personality and clarity
Growing organizations tend to shift from creative startup culture to stuffy corporate bureaucracy
money_quotes: Who wants to use those products? Why did some of the most exciting new software to come out of the Borg hive have to get boring-ass names?
I cannot count the times I heard the word 'professionalism' used as justification for why we couldn't do something.
Why do we go from the business equivalent of the unruly-but-creative teenager to a stuffy parent?
Can't we at least be the cool parent from down the street?
Slapping on the acronym-ready doublespeak titles 'Windows Communication Foundation' and 'Windows Presentation Foundation' is like tossing a giant soaking wet beige blanket over each one.
Hey Microsoft: who stole the soul?
posts.key_insight: Corporate obsession with 'professionalism' often kills the passion and personality that made products exciting in the first place, replacing evocative names with soulless acronym-friendly corporate speak.

posts.summary: Atwood recounts a workplace discussion about the best way to validate GUIDs in code, comparing try-catch exception handling versus regex approaches. What began as a simple question spiraled into debates about performance optimization, regex complexity, and benchmarking methodology. After extensive testing showed the performance differences were negligible when properly measured, Atwood concludes that the entire discussion was ultimately pointless. He argues that programmers should apply Occam's Razor to code: when performance isn't actually an issue, choose the simpler solution rather than optimizing prematurely.
key_points: Try-catch validation is the correct approach most of the time, but programmers love debating edge cases
Exception-based validation has performance penalties only when failures are frequent, looped, or high-volume
Initial benchmarks showed try-catch was 3-4x faster than regex, but proper benchmarking (compiled regex, outside loop, without debugger) made them nearly equivalent
The entire optimization discussion was pointless because performance wasn't actually a concern in this scenario
Occam's Razor for programming: given two similar code paths, choose the simpler one
money_quotes: This is my GUID. There are many like it but this one is mine. My GUID is my best friend. It is my life.
They never met an edge condition they didn't enjoy discussing ad nauseam.
Then someone suggested trying a regular expression. Oh great, now we have two problems.
It was definitely a fun discussion. I certainly learned a few things about GUIDs I didn't know. But it's also a pointless discussion.
Given two similar code paths, choose the simpler one.
Edge conditions and fancy techniques are interesting, but they're not necessarily a worthwhile use of time.
Sometimes the simple and stupid solution is all you need.
posts.key_insight: Programmers often waste time optimizing for edge cases and performance concerns that don't actually matter—the simple solution is usually good enough.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reviews the Microsoft Natural Ergonomic Keyboard 4000, declaring it the worthy successor to his beloved but discontinued Microsoft Natural Keyboard Pro from 1999. After years of struggling with inferior keyboards that lacked ergonomic split designs with standard key clusters, he's thrilled to find a keyboard that feels right again. He praises its solid build quality, padded palm rests, simplified multimedia buttons, and clever use of the center 'dead zone' for controls. Despite minor complaints about a smaller Enter key and missing USB ports, he concludes his keyboard quest is finally over.
key_points: The 2001 discontinuation of the Natural Keyboard Pro created a 'dark time' for programmer ergonomic keyboard enthusiasts who needed standard PgUp/PgDn clusters
Build quality matters enormously - most modern keyboards feel cheap and disposable compared to older premium models
The padded leatherette palm rests are surprisingly more comfortable than any plastic alternative
Strategic placement of controls in the keyboard's center dead zone means accidental button presses become impossible, which is an improvement even if you never use those buttons
Hardware that feels right to your hands is worth searching years to find
money_quotes: Now that I own one, I'm not excited any more. I'm ecstatic!
This was a dark time for ergo keyboard enthusiasts who happened to be programmers.
So many wired keyboards are stuck in that disposable, plastic-y $19.99 low-end ghetto nowadays.
My fingers feel at home again!
The odds of me accidentally hitting the dead zone buttons is nil. So this is a net gain even if I never click those buttons once.
My keyboard quest is over.
It's like, how much more black could this be? and the answer is none. None more black.
posts.key_insight: For programmers who spend their careers at the keyboard, finding hardware that truly fits your hands is worth years of searching and stockpiling when you find it.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores a nostalgic 'what-if' scenario where Windows 95 and NT were cancelled, leaving users stuck with Windows for Workgroups 3.11. He demonstrates the absurdity by showing WFW 3.11 struggling to render the modern Google homepage, complete with JavaScript errors. The post serves as both a trip down memory lane and a practical guide for tech enthusiasts who want to experience vintage Windows in a virtual machine. Atwood shares his own recollections of early web browsing and the rapid evolution of browsers during the mid-90s.
key_points: Windows for Workgroups 3.11 (codename 'Snowball') represents a fascinating alternate history where Windows evolution stopped
The JavaScript error on Google's homepage perfectly illustrates how far the web has evolved since 1998
Internet Explorer 5 actually had a Windows 3.x version, though most users had already moved to Windows 95 by then
Setting up vintage operating systems in virtual machines requires attention to details like DOS 6.22 and floppy disk image formats
money_quotes: I absolutely love the fact that we get a javascript error on the Google homepage.
Not unreasonable, considering WFW 3.11 was well on its way to total obsolescence before Google even made it out of a Stanford dorm room in 1998.
I distinctly remember using the first version of IE under Windows 95 – the one that didn't understand the <table> tag or animated gifs!
Most people are surprised to find that there was a Windows 3.x version of circa-1999 Internet Explorer 5.
posts.key_insight: The rapid obsolescence of once-cutting-edge technology becomes viscerally clear when you try to run modern websites on vintage operating systems.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood traces the history of artillery games from their origins on 1960s mainframes through DOS classics like Scorched Earth to modern incarnations like the Worms series. He highlights how these games, built on simple physics mechanics of wind, angle, and power, became programming samples and learning tools for generations of developers. The post features an interview with Scorched Earth creator Wendell Hicken, who describes reverse-engineering arcade games as a child and building his own versions at home. Atwood uses this history to illustrate the deep connection between games and programming, showing how simple gameplay mechanics can inspire decades of innovation and remain compelling entertainment.
key_points: Artillery games originated on 1960s mainframes, with programmers sharing them freely via punch cards and printer output
These games were commonly used as programming samples due to their simplicity and real physics calculations, including the famous gorilla.bas bundled with MS-DOS
Scorched Earth's creator learned programming by visiting arcades with a notepad to reverse-engineer games, then implementing them at home
Simple game mechanics can sustain decades of evolution and remain commercially viable, as demonstrated by the Worms franchise
The advice for aspiring game developers is to start simple and keep learning, building incrementally on working code
