money_quotes: Because they were simple, fun, and used some real physics calculations, artillery games were often used as programming samples.
There's nothing quite as satisfying as nailing your opponent with the laughably ineffective 'Funky Bomb.'
While other kids were dumping quarters into arcades, I would visit the arcades with a notepad, and try to figure out what the games were doing – then I'd go home and implement them myself.
If you have a really simple program that at least does something, then this can keep you motivated to keep making it better.
It's amazing how far a simple gameplay mechanic like wind, angle, and power can take you.
posts.key_insight: Simple, well-designed game mechanics can inspire generations of programmers and remain compelling across decades of technological change.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his experience customizing the Windows XP boot screen as part of a workplace MAME arcade cabinet project. He recommends StarDock's free BootSkin utility for easily swapping boot screens, noting the technical limitation of 16 colors at 640x480 resolution. Finding an existing Windows 1.01 bootskin poorly executed, he created and shares his own pixel-perfect version. The post exemplifies the tinkerer spirit of early 2000s PC customization culture.
key_points: BootSkin is a free utility that makes Windows XP boot screen customization accessible to non-experts
The XP boot process has a hard technical limitation of 16 colors at 640x480 resolution
Testing system-level changes in Virtual PC or VMware first is a sensible precaution
The author's attention to detail led him to fix a poorly-scaled community contribution rather than accept mediocrity
TweakUI PowerToy enables automatic login, useful for dedicated-purpose machines like arcade cabinets
money_quotes: Thankfully, that's 16 colors of your choice, not the classic default 16 'Windows' colors.
The author didn't do a good job of scaling the image, so it looked horrible. I reformatted it so it's pixel perfect.
If you're worried about the effects of this boot screen change, try it in a Virtual PC or VMWare image first.
posts.key_insight: Even small customization projects benefit from attention to detail and a willingness to fix what others have done poorly.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the tension between user customization and software usability, drawing on insights from a Microsoft Vista UI designer. The post uses the Honda Civic aftermarket modification analogy to explain why deep OS skinning isn't officially supported - it creates support nightmares and can accidentally harm usability. While acknowledging that power users should have customization options, Atwood argues that most of the 700 million Windows users are fine with defaults. The core tension is that user-created skins are often worse than professionally designed defaults, making broad customization a double-edged sword.
key_points: Deep UI customization is far more invasive than it appears on the surface, affecting supportability and usability
The Honda Civic analogy: manufacturers allow some customization, but extensive modifications void your warranty for good reason
Making customization broadly accessible to casual users risks making things worse when they accidentally install poor skins
Professional UI designers create defaults with usability expertise that random skin creators typically lack
The vast majority of users are well-served by thoughtful defaults rather than unlimited customization
money_quotes: But it is far more invasive than it may appear to be on the surface.
Once you make it a broad reaching thing that anyone can download and install – maybe even accidentally – you run the risk of really making things worse for people.
Depending on how much you do under the hood, you might void your warranty.
The default UI may not have flames/aliens/J-Lo on it, but it was probably built by designers versed in usability.
Customization is in the eyes of the beholder, of course, but the odds of a user picking a random skin that actually reduces usability is high.
posts.key_insight: Unlimited customization sounds like freedom but often degrades the user experience, because professional defaults embody expertise that casual customizers lack.

posts.summary: Atwood highlights an extensive 100-topic comparison between OS X and Windows XP, praising it for revealing subtle design differences between the two operating systems. He specifically calls out the OS X login dialog's wiggle animation as an example of elegant, unintimidating user feedback, while noting its potential shortcoming for novice users. The post also recommends the GUIdebook website as a resource for historical GUI screenshots, sharing his discovery of Excel splashscreen evolution as an example of unexpectedly interesting design history.
key_points: Detailed GUI comparisons reveal subtle but meaningful design philosophy differences between operating systems
OS X's wiggling login dialog exemplifies elegant feedback design that balances user-friendliness with potential novice confusion
Historical GUI documentation has surprising value for understanding design evolution
Visual design details like splashscreens can be unexpectedly fascinating when viewed in historical context
money_quotes: The author tries to be objective, which is admirable, but the extremely detailed comparison is worth reading mostly because it highlights a lot of subtle design differences.
This simple, elegant and unintimidating feedback lets users know that they need to try again.
Who knew splashscreens could be so... cool?
posts.key_insight: The most valuable insights about GUI design come not from declaring winners, but from examining the subtle differences that reveal each system's underlying design philosophy.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques the common practice of presenting users with blank screens when launching applications, arguing this creates 'Blank Page Syndrome' where infinite choice leads to paralysis. He contrasts Word 2003's empty document approach with Basecamp's strategy of showing sample populated data. The post advocates for pre-populated examples as a superior alternative to blank pages, help documentation, or wizards. However, Atwood cautions that example data must be clearly marked as fake, sharing his own experience of watching users try to interact with screenshots during usability testing.
key_points: Blank pages create choice paralysis similar to wandering a video store without a plan
Pre-populated example data demonstrates application functionality better than help pages or wizards
Sample data helps users visualize their goal state without constraining them to a rigid workflow
Example content must be clearly marked as non-functional to prevent user confusion
This approach embodies 'Show, Don't Tell' by demonstrating rather than explaining
money_quotes: When presented with infinite choice, it's sometimes hard to get started.
The use of real data in the example helps me visualize my goal. This is what I'll get if I use the application.
The example is a highly contextual alternative to 'help pages' or 'the manual.' As if I'm gonna read those.
It's not a wizard. I am not constrained to a list of tedious questions with Next and Forward buttons; I can do whatever I want from here.
There's nothing more cringeworthy than watching users try to click on a screenshot.
This is a fantastic alternative to the tabula rasa default.
posts.key_insight: Applications should show users what success looks like by presenting example data rather than intimidating them with a blank canvas.

posts.summary: Atwood explores the challenge of fitting a 128-bit GUID into a 20-character ASCII string for an e-commerce transaction ID. He explains why raw bytes won't work (control characters like carriage return and null are problematic), then systematically compares encoding schemes: hex (32 bytes), UUEncode (25 bytes), Base64 (22 bytes), and ASCII85 (20 bytes). ASCII85 wins by using the largest practical character set (85 printable characters), barely fitting the GUID into the constraint. The post demonstrates practical problem-solving while teaching encoding fundamentals.
key_points: Raw byte values can't be transmitted as text because ASCII includes non-printable control characters (null, carriage return, etc.)
The trade-off in ASCII armor is clear: fewer available characters means longer encoded strings
Hex encoding is simple but inefficient (32 chars), while ASCII85 is complex but optimal (20 chars)
Base64's trailing '==' is padding, not content, making its effective length 22 characters
Sometimes the 'latest and greatest' solution (ASCII85) is necessary when constraints are tight
money_quotes: So the question is, how do we represent a 128-bit integer in a plain text string that fits in 20 characters?
But wait a minute. ASCII 13 is carriage return! And good luck sending ASCII 0 (aka null) to anyone.
We're forced to use only printable ASCII characters. Which means we'll have to use more bytes to represent the same data; it's unavoidable.
So it is possible to fit a complete GUID in 20 printable ASCII characters using the latest and greatest ASCII Armor. But just barely!
posts.key_insight: When encoding binary data as text, your character set size directly determines your efficiency—more printable characters means shorter strings, with ASCII85 being the practical maximum at 85 characters.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his C# implementation of the ASCII85 encoding algorithm, ported from a C sample referenced on Wikipedia. The implementation is packaged as a VS.NET 2003 solution and includes both encoding and decoding functionality with data validation. He provides a simple API with Encode and Decode methods plus configuration properties for customization. The post includes a test harness demonstrating the algorithm against sample text, though Atwood acknowledges the demo of converting printable text to printable text isn't particularly impressive.
key_points: Provides a complete C# port of ASCII85 encoding/decoding algorithm from Wikipedia's C reference
Implementation includes data validation and configurable options like line length and prefix/suffix marks
Packaged as a VS.NET 2003 solution with test harness for string, binary, and error scenarios
ASCII85 is most useful for binary data encoding, not text-to-text conversion
money_quotes: Of course, converting printable text to, er... printable text... isn't exactly an earth-shattering demo.
Normally you'd be doing this on some kind of real binary data.
posts.key_insight: ASCII85 encoding is a practical algorithm for converting binary data to printable ASCII, and a clean C# implementation can be built by porting existing C reference code.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood passionately advocates for TaskSwitchXP, a free Windows utility that dramatically improves the default ALT+TAB task switching experience. He describes the default Windows behavior as physically painful to witness and positions TaskSwitchXP as his single most essential Windows enhancement. The tool adds mouse interaction, screenshot previews, and smooth navigation to task switching. Atwood notes that Windows Vista would later incorporate similar functionality, validating the need for such improvements.
key_points: The default Windows ALT+TAB behavior is so inferior that watching others use it causes genuine frustration
TaskSwitchXP adds mouse support, live screenshot previews, and keyboard/mouse wheel navigation to task switching
Despite being feature-rich with extensive configuration options, the tool works perfectly with default settings
Windows Vista would eventually adopt similar screenshot-based task switching, proving the concept's value
money_quotes: When I see people using the default, crappy Windows ALT+TAB task switching behavior, I experience physical pain.
Friends don't let friends suffer through the default Windows ALT+TAB.
If I was forced to run only one Windows enhancement on my machine, this would be my choice.
posts.key_insight: Small workflow optimizations like better task switching are so fundamental to developer productivity that tolerating inferior defaults is almost a personal failing.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits Jef Raskin's provocative question about why login dialogs require both username and password fields when password alone should suffice. He systematically dismantles common objections: usernames aren't secret (we broadcast them in emails), requiring unique passwords would actually improve security, and forcing users toward pass-phrases would create both uniqueness and memorability. The post argues that the username/password convention persists not because it's optimal, but because 'that's just how it's done.' Atwood calls on developers to take control and simplify authentication by removing the username field entirely.
key_points: Usernames provide no real security since they're publicly broadcast in emails and elsewhere
Requiring password uniqueness across users would actually be a security benefit, not a problem
Pass-phrases are more secure, more memorable, and naturally unique enough to serve as sole identifiers
The username/password convention persists due to inertia rather than sound design reasoning
Developers often reject improvements by claiming they have no control, but someone always does
money_quotes: Your password alone should be enough information for the computer to know who you are.
We broadcast our username in every email we send. There's no security in a username. It's public information.
Do we really want two users to have the same password? Doesn't that imply that the password is already fatally flawed?
The more I think about this, the more I think username/password is simply a bad convention that nobody has sufficiently questioned.
Either the programmers say that's just not the way it's done, or they say that they have no control over the sign-on procedure. But someone, of course, does have that control.
It's time to take control by evangelizing pass-phrases and pushing to remove the user field from login forms.
posts.key_insight: Convention often masquerades as necessity—the username field in login dialogs persists not because it's required for security, but because developers haven't questioned whether it should exist at all.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his discovery of the Atari Archives, which contains high-resolution scans of classic computing books including The Best of Creative Computing volumes from the 1970s. He reflects on Creative Computing magazine's role as the original DIY computer enthusiast publication, full of BASIC game listings and counterculture aesthetics. Atwood connects this history to his own programming origins, recalling countless hours typing game listings into early home computers. The post celebrates computing's countercultural roots while acknowledging that this formative experience permanently cured him of any desire to type code from printed pages.
key_points: Creative Computing was the original DIY computer enthusiast magazine, representing computing as an alternative culture in the 1970s
The Atari Archives preserves high-resolution scans of classic computing books and magazines that shaped early programming culture
Typing BASIC game listings from books into home computers was a formative experience that helped create a generation of programmers
David Ahl, Creative Computing's editor, was also the original author of Minesweeper in 1973
The early computing magazine culture presaged modern blog culture in its DIY, enthusiast-driven approach
money_quotes: In the 70s, computing was almost an alternative culture.
I spent god knows how many hours typing game listings from these books into the Apple //c, Coleco Adam, and TI 99/4a.
It's probably part of the reason why I'm a programmer today.
It's also the reason why I'll never type code in from a printed page ever, ever again. Ever!
His comments on what Creative Computing would look like today remind me a lot of the current blog culture!
posts.key_insight: The tedious act of typing in BASIC game listings from magazines was a formative programming experience for a generation, even as it permanently cured them of wanting to repeat the process.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood provides a practical roundup of techniques for managing ASP.NET viewstate, a notoriously problematic feature that bloats page sizes with Base64-encoded data. He covers four main strategies: disabling viewstate when possible, moving it to the bottom of pages for better SEO, compressing it for marginal size reduction, and storing it server-side. The post also highlights ASP.NET 2.0's improvements including ControlState separation and a new formatter that halves viewstate size. It's a concise, link-heavy resource post typical of mid-2000s technical blogging.
key_points: The first rule of viewstate is to turn it off whenever possible
Moving viewstate to page bottom improves Google indexing since Google only indexes the first N bytes
Compressing viewstate yields marginal benefits compared to plain text compression
Server-side viewstate storage (Session, Application, or Cache) is an alternative to client-side
ASP.NET 2.0 introduced ControlState and ObjectStateFormatter, significantly improving viewstate handling
money_quotes: The first rule of thumb, of course, is to turn it off whenever you can.
if you have 12 kb of Base64 encoded schmutz in your header, that isn't exactly helping people find your pages via Google searches
Here's to progress. Now pardon me while I crack my bullwhip.
posts.key_insight: ASP.NET viewstate is a necessary evil that requires active management through disabling, relocating, compressing, or moving server-side to avoid SEO penalties and performance issues.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares the discovery that hardware engineers, like their software counterparts, hide Easter eggs in their work. The post highlights a collection of hidden art etched into microchips, featuring an example of Mr. T on a T1 transceiver chip. Atwood points readers to the Silicon Zoo website, which catalogs these microscopic artistic touches found in integrated circuits. The brief post celebrates the playful creativity that exists even in the most technical hardware engineering.
key_points: Hardware engineers embed hidden art and jokes into microchip designs, similar to software Easter eggs
The Silicon Zoo website documents a collection of these microscopic artworks found on integrated circuits
The example shown features Mr. T etched onto a T1 transceiver chip, with his medallion appropriately reading 'T1'
money_quotes: Software engineers aren't the only ones planting secret Easter eggs in their products.
Who said hardware engineers didn't have a sense of humor?
posts.key_insight: The tradition of hiding Easter eggs and personal touches in technical work extends beyond software into the microscopic world of chip design, revealing a universal creative impulse among engineers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood discusses IDE customization, particularly color schemes and fonts, lamenting that there's no centralized gallery for sharing IDE layouts like CSS Zen Garden exists for web design. He showcases an appealing white-on-brown color scheme he found and shares his own Visual Studio setup with detailed rationale for each choice. The post invites readers to share their own IDE configurations, emphasizing that developers spend enormous amounts of time looking at code and should optimize their visual environment. Atwood's approach is methodical, citing his own research on code colorization and readability to justify his choices.
key_points: There's no CSS Zen Garden equivalent for IDE color schemes and layouts, which would be valuable for the developer community
100% contrast schemes (pure white backgrounds) are harder to read than slightly off-white backgrounds based on research
Syntax highlighting choices should be intentional - using background colors for strings helps identify spaces, and highlighting numbers can catch magic number issues
Font choice matters significantly for code readability, with Atwood recommending Pragmata, Proggy, or Lucida Typewriter
Lower-contrast dark themes (white on brown rather than white on black) can be easier on the eyes while still providing good readability
money_quotes: It's a shame that there's no CSS Zen Garden for IDE coloring and layout.
White on black color schemes can be hard on the eyes, but this one has less contrast.
Because my research on code colorization showed very definitively that 100% contrast schemes are harder to read, I changed my background from white to 248, 248, 248.
I like using a background color for strings so spaces and other 'non-characters' are easier to see.
Numbers tend to be significant in code (e.g., Magic Numbers). It's been helpful so far.
posts.key_insight: Developers should thoughtfully customize their IDE's visual environment based on readability research rather than accepting defaults, since they spend countless hours staring at code.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood enthusiastically reviews a new feature in Windows XP Media Center 2005's Update Rollup 2: non-linear stretch (or 'Intelligent Zoom') for displaying 4:3 content on 16:9 widescreen displays. He walks through the limitations of existing solutions—black bars waste screen space, zooming crops content, and uniform stretching distorts the image. The non-linear approach combines slight cropping with progressive edge stretching while keeping the center mostly undistorted. He also warns that persistent black bars can cause plasma TV burn-in, sharing his own experience of visible burn-in after 8 months.
key_points: Non-linear stretch progressively stretches more toward the edges while keeping the center relatively undistorted
Traditional solutions (zoom or uniform stretch) each have significant drawbacks—content loss or distortion
Black bars from 4:3 content can cause permanent burn-in on plasma displays after extended use
Microsoft's 'Intelligent Zoom' represents a practical compromise between competing display approaches
Atwood used white then grey bars to equalize wear on his plasma TV before this feature existed
money_quotes: The image is slightly cropped on the top and bottom, then progressively stretched more towards the edges; the center is barely stretched at all.
After 8 months, our plasma TV had noticeable burn-in on the edges where the "black bars" appear for the 4:3 content!
No content is lost, but we've distorted everything: it's wider and fatter.
Clearly zooming and stretching both have their drawbacks.
posts.key_insight: Non-linear stretch elegantly solves the 4:3-to-16:9 display problem by combining the best aspects of zooming and stretching while minimizing their drawbacks and protecting against screen burn-in.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood introduces the Code Snippets feature in Visual Studio .NET 2005, explaining the three types available: simple expansion, template expansion with variables, and surround snippets. He walks through the keyboard shortcuts needed to invoke each type and notes that VB.NET's implementation is more robust than C#'s, with many more built-in snippets and automatic Import statement handling. The post also covers snippet management options and third-party tools like Snippy for creating custom snippets without editing raw XML.
key_points: Code Snippets in VS.NET 2005 come in three flavors: simple expansion, template expansion with variables, and surround snippets
VB.NET ships with significantly more snippets than C# and supports automatic Import statement insertion
Snippets are stored as XML files with a .snippet extension in language-specific folders
While VS.NET provides snippet management UI, there's no built-in editor for creating new snippets
Third-party tools like Snippy provide a GUI alternative to editing raw XML snippet files
money_quotes: One of the most enjoyable new features in Visual Studio .NET 2005 is Code Snippets.
VB.NET's implementation of snippets is a bit more robust than the one in C#.
VB.NET has a zillion snippets shipping in the box where C# has maybe... two dozen?
Assuming you don't enjoy editing raw XML files, it might be easier to go with Microsoft's Snippy.
posts.key_insight: Code Snippets in VS.NET 2005 offer a significant productivity boost through keyboard-driven code expansion, though the implementation quality varies between languages.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores Wil Shipley's controversial stance against unit testing, arguing that beta testing with real users provides more value than automated test suites. Shipley, a veteran developer behind Delicious Library, contends that unit testing consumes enormous resources while finding few relevant bugs. Atwood synthesizes three key arguments: rare bugs don't matter, real testers actively try to break software in ways unit tests don't, and users behave irrationally in ways machines can't simulate. While acknowledging unit testing has a complementary role, Atwood concludes that shipping to beta testers represents the most valuable form of testing, and sacrificing beta testing time for unit test coverage is a serious mistake.
key_points: Unit testing often consumes significant engineering resources (setup time, ongoing maintenance) while finding bugs that may never affect real users
Real human testers actively attempt to break software through creative abuse—garbage input, edge cases, rapid clicking—while unit tests merely verify expected behavior
Users behave irrationally and have unpredictable environments that automated tests cannot simulate
Bug prioritization should be based on actual usage data rather than theoretical test failures
Unit testing should complement, not replace, real-world beta testing with actual users
money_quotes: Unit testing is a great way to pay a bunch of engineers to be bored out of their minds and find not much of anything.
If a bug exists but no user ever encounters it, do you care?
Real testers hate your code and will do whatever it takes to break it.
Users are crazy, period. Machines are far too rational to test like users.
The real and best testing occurs when you ship your software to beta testers.
If unit test coding is cutting into your beta testing schedule, you're making a very serious mistake.
YOU NEED TO TEST YOUR DAMN PROGRAM. Run it. Use it. Try odd things. Whack keys.
posts.key_insight: Automated unit tests verify that code works as intended, but only real users with their irrational behavior and chaotic environments can reveal the bugs that actually matter.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reviews Jakob Nielsen's list of top ten blog design mistakes and largely agrees with the advice, but argues that Nielsen missed the biggest mistake of all: disabling comments. He contends that true blogging requires two-way communication between author and reader, otherwise it's just traditional publishing. Atwood admits to violating several of the guidelines himself, including not having an author photo, which caused problems when trying to meet other bloggers at a conference. The post's title is a self-deprecating joke referencing Nielsen's observation that only people with too much time on their hands read blogs that cover many topics.
key_points: Disabling comments is the biggest blog design mistake that Nielsen's list overlooked
Two-way communication between author and reader is what distinguishes blogging from traditional publishing
Authors must actually read and respond to comments for it to count as real communication
Having an author photo matters for real-world recognition at conferences and events
Covering too many topics attracts only low-value readers who have excessive free time
money_quotes: You don't have a blog until you allow public, two way communication between the author and the reader.
And when I say communication, I mean it: the author has to actually read and even (gasp!) respond to the comments.
Otherwise you're just publishing content, like every other newspaper since the printing press was invented. Meh.
The only people who read everything are those with too much time on their hands (a low-value demographic).
I certainly regretted not having my picture on my blog when I went to PDC 2005 and belatedly realized it was virtually impossible for me to meet any other bloggers.
posts.key_insight: Blogging's defining characteristic is two-way communication with readers—without comments and author engagement, you're just another old-media publisher.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores whether disabling the Windows XP pagefile is viable for systems with 2GB or more of RAM, walking through the technical steps to do so. He acknowledges that while developers are successfully running without pagefiles, the theoretical performance benefits are questionable since systems with adequate RAM rarely use the pagefile anyway. The post weighs the risks, including potential out-of-memory errors and issues with memory-hungry applications like Photoshop. Ultimately, the expert consensus recommends keeping a small pagefile rather than disabling it entirely, as Windows only pages to disk when necessary.
key_points: With 2GB of RAM becoming common in 2005, disabling the pagefile became a more realistic option than in the 256MB-1GB era
If a system never runs out of physical memory, the pagefile theoretically never gets used anyway, negating performance benefits
Some applications pre-allocate large amounts of virtual memory on startup, which can cause problems without a pagefile
The recommended approach is a small initial pagefile (100MB) with a high maximum (1GB) rather than complete disabling
The 32-bit Windows memory architecture with its 2GB user-space limit actually helps constrain memory demands
money_quotes: If you have 2 gigabytes (or more) of memory in your PC, have you considered turning off your pagefile?
I've heard people talk about this before, but I had always disregarded it as crazy talk.
This idea was indeed crazy in a world where 256mb, 512mb and 1gb of memory were the norm. Now that 2 gb of memory is relatively common, disabling the pagefile isn't such a crazy idea any more.
If our systems were never running out of physical memory with 2gb, then theoretically the pagefile never gets used anyway.
When certain applications start, they allocate a huge amount of memory (hundreds of megabytes typically set aside in virtual memory) even though they might not use it.
This is one case where the 32-bit process memory limit of 4 gigabytes – which is really 2 gigabytes once you factor in the Windows kernel memory split – works in our favor.
posts.key_insight: Having abundant RAM doesn't automatically mean you should disable virtual memory, because the safety net costs nothing when unused but provides crucial fallback when applications misbehave.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the persistent problem of email spam, using the Nigerian 419 scam anthem as a jumping-off point to discuss why spam remains profitable despite being obvious. He explores various anti-spam techniques, from challenge-response systems (which have significant usability problems) to Bayesian filtering (which he uses via POPFile). The post highlights Markovian filtering as implemented in CRM114, which achieves near-perfect accuracy by analyzing phrases rather than individual words. Atwood notes that this technique can exceed human accuracy in spam detection, with the CRM114 author reporting zero classification errors over 10,000 emails.
key_points: Nigerian 419 scammers succeed because even low response rates (7 replies per 500 emails) convert to money 70% of the time
Challenge-response spam systems create awkward scenarios where two such systems can deadlock, each waiting for the other to respond
Markovian filtering analyzes weighted phrases rather than individual words, making it significantly more accurate than Bayesian approaches
CRM114's Markovian filtering achieves 99.5%+ accuracy, which actually exceeds measured human accuracy of 99.84%
The author's practical approach evolved from POPFile to K9, prioritizing simplicity alongside effectiveness
money_quotes: Like all spammers, they do it because it works.
When you get a reply, it's 70% sure that you'll get the money.
If you can't take two seconds to respond to the challenge, then just how important is that email you're sending me?
The 'I challenge your challenge!' scenario is particularly amusing.
Markovian filtering weights phrases and words, whereas Bayesian filtering only looks at individual words.
I received over 10,000 emails. About 60% were spam. I had ZERO classification errors. ZERO.
I, as an unassisted human, under nearly optimal conditions, am 99.84% accurate.
posts.key_insight: Spam filtering technology had already surpassed human accuracy by 2005, with Markovian techniques achieving better than 99.98% accuracy compared to humans' 99.84% under optimal conditions.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the wide skill gap between programmers by sharing an anecdote about a developer named 'Stinky' who didn't understand basic boolean negation. The post humorously suggests that IDE tools like Clippy could help struggling developers by detecting problematic code patterns in real-time. Atwood uses this as a jumping point to discuss VS.NET 2005's customizable exception intellisense, showing how developers can improve the default error messages. The underlying message balances humor with a genuine observation that development tools could do more to guide less experienced programmers.
key_points: Some programmers lack fundamental programming knowledge like boolean logic, yet still work as professional developers
IDEs could theoretically detect poor coding patterns and offer guidance in real-time, similar to Clippy
VS.NET 2005 allows customization of exception intellisense through XML editing to provide more helpful debugging messages
The industry joke about Clippy masks a real opportunity: proactive IDE assistance for common mistakes
There's a gap between formal CS education and practical programming skills that tools might help bridge
money_quotes: Even the best programmers make shitty software, with bugs. But some programmers are naturally proficient at creating this special kind of software.
To hell with education, your degree in computer science and the tons of books you read. You don't need any of that to be a champion developer like Stinky.
Just learn the copy-paste method, remember all the properties of Janus Gridex and ActiveBar control, and the world is yours.
After Bojan recovered from the shock of realizing that Stinky didn't know the basics of logical algebra...
It looks like you're writing a for loop!
posts.key_insight: Development tools could do more to actively guide programmers away from fundamental mistakes, turning the much-maligned Clippy concept into genuinely useful real-time code assistance.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood addresses a common regex puzzle: how to exclude specific words from matches rather than include them. He explains that while regular expressions excel at matching what you want, expressing what you don't want is trickier. The post presents two solutions using lookahead and lookbehind assertions, noting that negative lookahead has broader compatibility across regex implementations. He recommends specific tools for testing these expressions.
key_points: Regular expressions are designed for matching, not excluding, making exclusion patterns counterintuitive
Negative lookbehind provides one solution but has limited support and restrictions across regex flavors
Negative lookahead offers better compatibility and can achieve the same exclusion goal
The character class negation syntax [^'fox'] doesn't work for word exclusion because it operates on individual characters, not strings
money_quotes: Stating a regex in terms of what you don't want to match is a bit harder.
Regular expressions are great at matching. It's easy to formulate a regex using what you want to match.
posts.key_insight: Regular expressions are fundamentally built around inclusion rather than exclusion, so excluding matches requires creative use of lookahead or lookbehind assertions rather than intuitive negation syntax.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood calculates the real cost of running PCs 24/7, discovering his server costs $500/year and his media center PC $205/year in electricity. He walks through the math of converting watts to kilowatt-hours and applying local electricity rates to determine annual costs. The post then provides practical tips for reducing power consumption on always-on machines, from configuring sleep settings to choosing efficient power supplies and avoiding power-hungry video cards. Atwood emphasizes that understanding actual power draw through measurement is essential before making optimization decisions.
key_points: A 160-watt server running 24/7 costs roughly $500/year at California electricity rates (36 cents/kWh)
The Kill-a-Watt device ($30) enables accurate measurement of actual power consumption rather than guessing
Video cards are the second biggest power consumers after CPUs, with mid-range cards drawing 20+ watts even at idle
Power supply efficiency matters significantly—upgrading from 65% to 83% efficiency can provide meaningful savings
Small optimizations like disconnecting unused peripherals and configuring sleep modes add up over time
money_quotes: Have you ever wondered how much it's costing you to leave a computer on 24 hours a day, 7 days a week?
Once you get one, you'll inevitably go through a phase where you run around your home, measuring the power draw of everything you can plug into a wall socket.
So leaving my server on is costing me $500 / year, or $42 per month.
I'm not sure this is widely understood now, but after the CPU, the video card is by far the biggest power consumer in a typical PC.
The worrying number, though, is the idle one. Pay close attention to the video card you use in an 'always-on' machine.
At typical power loads (~90w), the average power supply efficiency is a disappointing 65%.
posts.key_insight: Understanding the true cost of always-on computing requires measuring actual power consumption, and the results—often hundreds of dollars per year per machine—justify investing time in power optimization.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines Charles Petzold's provocative essay questioning whether Visual Studio harms developers by imposing a rigid cognitive model on programming. While Petzold argues that IDE features like IntelliSense force bottom-up, linear coding and that code generation erects walls between programmers and their craft, Atwood offers a counterpoint. He notes that bad programmers write bad code regardless of tools, while RAD tools simply make good programmers more productive. Atwood ultimately disagrees with Petzold's nostalgic preference for Notepad coding, arguing that tighter IDE-language coupling represents the future of programming, not its corruption.
key_points: Petzold argues Visual Studio imposes a flawed cognitive model that degrades the programming experience, similar to how PowerPoint constrains presentations
IntelliSense encourages linear, bottom-up coding where variables must be defined before use, treating programmers like machines
Code generation in Visual Studio creates barriers between programmers and understanding, implying certain aspects of programming are only accessible through the IDE
RAD tools don't make good programmers write bad code - they simply let bad programmers write bad code faster while making good programmers more productive
The future likely lies in even tighter coupling between languages and IDEs, potentially making compilation outside the IDE impossible
money_quotes: Bad programmers write bad code. Good programmers write good code. RAD lets bad programmers write bad code faster.
It's not that IntelliSense is teaching us to program like a machine; it's just that IntelliSense would be much happier if we did.
As Visual Studio is generating code, it is also erecting walls between that code and the programmer.
It's just me and the code, and for awhile, I feel like a real programmer again.
Using Notepad to code may be an instructive exercise in minimalism for students, but no professional programmer can afford to build software this way.
A developer weaned on the Visual Studio .NET IDE is powerless outside that environment.
I think of it more as a scar I got after doing Windows programming for ten years.
posts.key_insight: While IDEs may impose cognitive constraints on how we program, the productivity gains they provide make tighter tool-language integration inevitable, regardless of purist objections about programmer autonomy.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares a Visual Studio .NET macro he created that allows developers to search Google for any highlighted text directly within the IDE. Unlike similar macros that only work in code windows, this version works with any highlighted text including output from the Output window. The post provides the complete VB.NET macro code along with step-by-step installation instructions for both VS.NET 2003 and 2005. Atwood recommends binding the macro to ALT+F1 as a natural extension of the F1 help key.
key_points: The macro improves on existing solutions by working with highlighted text from any IDE window, not just code editors
The implementation handles multiple window types including the Output window and HTML editor views
ALT+F1 is suggested as an intuitive keyboard shortcut since it extends the F1 help key pattern
The macro is cross-compatible between VS.NET 2003 and 2005 without modifications
money_quotes: I know what you're thinking: you've seen this macro before. Yeah, but this one goes to eleven.
It actually works with any highlighted text in the IDE – including highlighted text from the Output window
It's really quite handy; ALT+F1 is a totally natural chord and a logical superset of F1
posts.key_insight: Small developer tool improvements that work consistently across all contexts—like searching from any IDE window rather than just code—provide outsized productivity gains.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood addresses the frustrating limitation that Visual Studio copies code to the clipboard in RTF format rather than HTML, making it difficult to paste formatted code into web-based editors. He presents an improved macro solution that converts highlighted code to clean HTML while preserving syntax highlighting colors and indentation. The macro works across multiple Visual Studio versions (2002, 2003, 2005) and includes a feature to dynamically remove excessive indentation from copied snippets. He provides the downloadable macro along with step-by-step installation instructions.
key_points: Visual Studio copies code with RTF formatting instead of HTML, making web publishing difficult
The FormatToHtml macro converts RTF-formatted code to clean, span-colored HTML wrapped in a div
The macro dynamically removes excessive indentation from code snippets for cleaner output
The solution works across Visual Studio 2002, 2003, and 2005
Background colors are lost because VS doesn't include them in the RTF clipboard data
money_quotes: Copying code to your clipboard in Visual Studio is often an exercise in futility if you want anything more than plain vanilla text.
VS copies code to the clipboard with bizarro-world RTF formatting instead of the sane, simple HTML markup you might expect.
That's extra clean, well-formatted <span> colored HTML wrapped in a simple <div>.
This is how VS.NET 2005's CTRL+C copy functionality should behave.
The macro now dynamically removes excessive indenting from copied code.
posts.key_insight: Developer tools should respect common workflows like web publishing, and when they don't, it's worth building solutions to fill the gaps.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that boolean parameters in code and boolean UI elements like checkboxes create ambiguity and should be replaced with more explicit alternatives. He cites the .NET Framework Standard Library guidelines recommending enumerations over boolean parameters because they make code self-documenting. The same principle applies to user interfaces: radio buttons with adjective labels are clearer than checkboxes with verb labels. A checkbox labeled 'Lock' leaves users guessing about both the current state and what happens when unchecked, while radio buttons showing 'Locked/Unlocked' make both states immediately visible. The post connects API design principles to UI design, showing how the same clarity improvements apply to both domains.
key_points: Boolean parameters turn into magic numbers that obscure code meaning - Authorization('foo', true) tells you nothing about what 'true' means
Enumerations make code self-documenting by replacing opaque true/false with named states like AuthorizationCompletion.Pending
Checkboxes with verb labels create ambiguity about current state and alternatives - users can't tell if an action has happened or will happen
Radio buttons with adjective labels show both the current state and all alternatives clearly
The same boolean-to-enumeration principle that improves code readability also improves UI clarity
money_quotes: Boolean parameters make calls harder to read and harder to write.
If you're not careful, boolean parameters become magic numbers.
Check boxes can leave the user guessing what the alternative is.
Whether checkboxes or radio buttons are used, it is important to label with adjectives which describe the state of the affected object.
If verbs are used as labels, the user does not know whether the action has taken place or is yet to take place.
Whenever possible, use radio buttons instead of checkboxes.
This is conceptually identical to the code sample; we simply switched from a boolean to an enumeration.
It's amazing how obvious the benefits are in retrospect, but it sure wasn't obvious to me until today.
posts.key_insight: The same principle that makes code readable—replacing ambiguous booleans with explicit enumerations—also makes user interfaces clearer when applied to checkboxes versus radio buttons.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the Windows clipboard's single-item limitation is an outdated design that fails to match modern computing capabilities. Despite having powerful hardware in 2005, users still lose clipboard contents when copying new items. He advocates for a simple FIFO queue system that would preserve the clipboard's ease of use while adding power-user functionality. He recommends the lightweight utility clcl as a workaround and expresses disappointment that Vista apparently won't address this fundamental limitation.
key_points: The single-item clipboard is an archaic limitation that doesn't match modern hardware capabilities
A FIFO queue of clipboard items would add power without complicating basic usage
The proliferation of third-party clipboard utilities proves this functionality should be built into the OS
Simple improvements like clipboard history persistence across reboots dramatically improve workflow
The clipboard's simplicity is admirable but has crossed the line into being too simple
money_quotes: In this era of 3ghz processors, 1gb memory, and 500gb hard drives, why is the Windows clipboard only capable of holding a single item?
Adding a basic FIFO queue of clipboard items wouldn't affect typical usage – but it would provide much richer functionality for intermediate and advanced users.
The clipboard is a heck of a lot more useful to me when I don't have to constantly worry about losing the one item on it.
The clipboard is a model of simplicity. And that's admirable. But I think it's too simple.
To me, that's is a sign that better clipboard functionality really should be built into the operating system.
It'd be a darn shame if we're stuck with the archaic single item clipboard for another five years.
posts.key_insight: When dozens of third-party utilities exist to solve the same basic problem, that's a clear signal the functionality should be built into the operating system itself.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that incremental search is vastly superior to traditional find dialogs and should be standard in every application. He details the numerous usability problems with modal search dialogs—they obscure content, provide poor feedback, force users to commit to searches before seeing results, and require thinking about search direction. Drawing on Jef Raskin's 'The Human Interface,' Atwood demonstrates how incremental search solves all these problems by providing immediate feedback, allowing real-time adjustment, and staying out of the user's way. He concludes that once developers experience incremental search, traditional dialogs become unbearable.
key_points: Traditional find dialogs are fundamentally flawed: they're modal, provide no feedback until search completes, and obscure the content you're searching
Incremental search provides immediate feedback with each keystroke, allowing users to stop typing as soon as they find a match or correct mistakes instantly
The lack of dialogs in incremental search means minimal interruption to workflow—search, find, and resume working seamlessly
Users become so accustomed to bad interfaces that they 'no longer feel the pain' of poor design choices
Interface design principles strongly favor incremental search in almost all scenarios
money_quotes: Incremental search is so good that it makes traditional search dialogs completely obsolete.
This traditional method is rather punishing to the user, although most computer aficionados have become so accustomed to it that they no longer feel the pain.
If it isn't incremental, it's excremental.
You'll probably wonder why incremental search isn't included as a standard feature in every single Windows application.
The advantages of incremental searches are so numerous and the advantages of delimited search so few that I can see almost no occasions when a delimited search would be preferred.
Immediate search feedback allows you to adjust your search strategy in real time.
posts.key_insight: Good interface design provides immediate, continuous feedback rather than forcing users into commit-then-wait cycles that obscure the connection between action and result.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood delivers a scathing critique of Microsoft's GotDotNet developer community site, documenting its persistent reliability, performance, and usability failures. He argues that the site's poor quality reflects badly on the entire .NET ecosystem due to its official Microsoft association. After listing numerous failings and unfavorably comparing it to SourceForge, he calls for Microsoft to either fix it properly or shut it down entirely. The post ends with an unexpected twist: a revamped ASP.NET 2.0 version launched that same day, addressing his main complaints about speed and design.
key_points: GotDotNet suffered from chronic unreliability, poor performance, bad design, and limited functionality for over three years
The site's official Microsoft branding meant its failures damaged the reputation of the entire .NET platform
Even the mediocre SourceForge provided a better user experience than this Microsoft-sponsored alternative
Microsoft's failure to improve or kill underperforming products sends a negative signal to the developer community
The newly released ASP.NET 2.0 version finally addressed the core speed and design issues
money_quotes: GotDotNet is an embarrassing trainwreck of epic proportions.
Why is it that fully half of my interactions with GotDotNet are extremely unpleasant?
When you can't muster a user experience comparable to the thoroughly mediocre SourceForge, it's time to seriously reconsider your reason for existing at all.
GotDotNet drags down the entire .NET ecosystem. And that's a damn shame.
Either have the guts to kill GotDotNet outright, or put your alpha development triage team on it ASAP.
At least using it is no longer like randomly jabbing myself in the face with punji sticks.
posts.key_insight: Developer tools and platforms carrying a major company's branding must meet higher standards because their failures reflect on the entire ecosystem they represent.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains why he runs 32-bit Windows on his AMD Athlon 64 desktop despite being a fan of 64-bit hardware. He cites Tom's Hardware benchmarks showing negligible performance differences for desktop applications, making the driver compatibility headaches not worth it. However, he contrasts this with server environments, where Microsoft.com's own operations blog documents dramatic performance improvements from switching to 64-bit Windows Server 2003. The post advocates a pragmatic approach: avoid being a guinea pig on the desktop, but embrace 64-bit immediately on servers where the gains are substantial and measurable.
key_points: 64-bit Windows on desktop offers no noticeable performance improvement for typical applications, making early adoption pointless
Server workloads see dramatic performance gains from 64-bit, with response times dropping from 244ms to 53ms in some cases
The performance improvement on servers comes 'for free' as a drop-in replacement with unchanged 32-bit code
Being an early adopter means dealing with driver and OS compatibility issues without corresponding benefits
Microsoft's own operations blog is an underappreciated resource for practical server administration advice
money_quotes: I have no interest in a 64-bit OS on the desktop
In the absence of any measurable performance improvement for typical desktop apps, why in the world would you want to be a guinea pig for 64-bit driver and OS teething problems? Answer: you wouldn't.
On the server, however, a 64-bit OS is a completely different story.
It's literally free performance!
If you're running a web server of any significant load on a 32-bit server OS, I would run, not walk, to the nearest software store to grab a Windows Server 2003 x64 license.
I'm glad someone is laying the foundation for the next 10 years of computing.
posts.key_insight: New technology should be adopted based on measurable benefits in your specific use case, not enthusiasm for the technology itself—64-bit makes sense on servers where gains are dramatic, but not on desktops where you'd only inherit compatibility headaches.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood introduces and endorses the 'Flame Warriors' website, a satirical guide to the various personality archetypes found in online discussions. The site originated when its creator caricatured participants in a flame war, causing the combatants to retreat in embarrassment. Atwood presents the extensive taxonomy of online behavior types as a valuable resource for understanding forum dynamics. He concludes with self-deprecating humor, admitting he's been accused of embodying some of these less flattering archetypes himself.
key_points: Godwin's Law predicts that any sufficiently long online discussion will eventually invoke Nazi comparisons
The Flame Warriors guide emerged from an actual forum conflict where caricaturing the participants defused the situation
Online communities develop predictable behavioral patterns that can be taxonomized into recognizable archetypes
Self-awareness about one's own online behavior tendencies is valuable for forum participants
Even the guide's promoter admits to exhibiting problematic discussion behaviors
money_quotes: As an online discussion grows longer, the probability of a comparison involving Nazis or Hitler approaches 1.
While the forum burned I amused myself by caricaturing the chief antagonists.
Confounded at seeing themselves thus revealed, the combatants fled the field in disarray.
My own bad Internet behavior would certainly have provided sufficient material to populate an extensive rogue's gallery.
I've been accused of being a Tireless Rebutter and Ferrous Cranus at times, but that's all part of my boyish charm.
posts.key_insight: Online discussions produce predictable personality archetypes, and recognizing these patterns—including in yourself—is the first step toward more productive discourse.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood enthusiastically reviews Guitar Hero, declaring it potentially the best rhythm game ever made. He describes discovering the game through GameSpot's glowing review and immediately hunting down a copy. The post details the gameplay mechanics, the impressive guitar controller with its whammy bar and tilt detection, and the fantastic soundtrack featuring classic rock hits. Atwood shares the experience of setting up the game at work for an extended session that left him with blistered fingers. He also highlights the passion of the game's producers at Red Octane and Harmonix, who saw Guitar Hero as a way to get people excited about music through gaming.
key_points: Guitar Hero represents a significant evolution in rhythm games, combining smart difficulty scaling with an authentic guitar controller experience
The game's soundtrack features classic rock that spans generations, potentially introducing younger players to music from before they were born
The producers' vision was to make music accessible and fun through gaming, hoping to create the same cultural fever that games like Street Fighter II once generated
The game creates genuine physical engagement - Atwood literally got blisters from playing, echoing Ringo Starr's famous exclamation
Community engagement was central to the game's development, with producers actively participating in forums and events
money_quotes: Guitar Hero might just be the best rhythm game ever made.
But there's one big difference between other rhythm games and Guitar Hero: this one goes to eleven.
I got blisters on me fingers!
He said to me in the most emotionless tone 'man this is fun...'
I don't think clarinet hero would be the next big thing. If that's what it took, I'd be at the front of the line wanting to make the game.
Everyone fantasizes about being a full-on rock star. I think this game hopefully will get out there and touch people and get them to realize music is fun and accessible.
My hope for Guitar Hero is that it causes some a similar fever among youth where they'll just be talking about hammer on's, pull offs and classic rock from before they were born.
posts.key_insight: Great games tap into universal fantasies and make complex skills feel accessible, creating shared cultural experiences that bridge generations.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the connection between road signs and software iconography, sparked by Donald Knuth's hobby of photographing diamond-shaped highway signs. He argues that road signs represent a successful universal visual language that software designers should study and emulate. The post draws parallels between physical navigation and information navigation, suggesting both require clear, instantly understood visual cues. Atwood references the book '1000 Signs' to highlight how different cultures handle the same fundamental design challenge.
key_points: Donald Knuth, despite being a world-famous computer scientist, collects photographs of diamond road signs with GPS coordinates as a hobby
Road signs represent a proven universal visual language that transcends cultural and linguistic barriers
Software iconography and road signs share the same fundamental goal: guiding users safely to their destination
The book '1000 Signs' demonstrates both striking similarities and cultural differences in how signs are designed worldwide
A century of road sign design offers untapped inspiration for software graphic design
money_quotes: Road signs echo what we're trying to accomplish with icons and graphics in software: universal visual understanding.
When you're a world famous computer scientist, I guess you're entitled to a slightly different definition of fun than most people.
Road signs are a well understood lingua franca for travelers, whether they're in an airplane, automobile, or navigating the streets of New York City on foot.
Shouldn't software graphic design draw more inspiration from the last century's worth of cumulative design on simple, effective road signs?
The goal in both cases is the same: getting safely to your destination, whether you're navigating physical space or information space.
posts.key_insight: Software designers should look to the century-long evolution of road sign design as a model for creating universally understood visual interfaces.

posts.summary: Atwood observes the irony of people keeping physical calculators next to their computers and explores why this happens. He cites Jef Raskin's research showing that using a pocket calculator (7 seconds) is faster than launching the built-in calculator app (16 seconds). While Atwood demonstrates that keyboard shortcuts can beat the physical calculator, he acknowledges the process isn't simple. He argues this exposes a fundamental failure in GUI design—that computers still can't handle basic arithmetic as easily as a $5 calculator, and advocates for non-modal, OS-level calculation facilities.
key_points: Physical calculators persist alongside computers because launching calc.exe requires too many steps and context switches
Raskin's experiment showed pocket calculators were twice as fast as software calculators (7 vs 16 seconds)
Keyboard shortcuts can theoretically beat physical calculators, but the complexity defeats the purpose of simplicity
The real solution is OS-level, non-modal functionality that doesn't require spawning separate windows
Calculator mousepads are a symptom of GUI design failure, not user ignorance
money_quotes: Have you ever noticed how many people keep a physical calculator next to their computer? The irony is almost palpable.
It is much faster to grab a pocket calculator.
It took about 7 seconds... This took about 16 seconds.
I guarantee I could beat that 7 second time quoted for the physical calculator. But it's not exactly simple, is it?
Users with calculator mousepads are no laughing matter.
Whenever I see users like this, I'm reminded of how far we have to go in GUI design when the simple act of adding numbers together is so complicated.
posts.key_insight: When users resort to dedicated hardware to perform tasks their computers should handle effortlessly, it reveals fundamental failures in software design rather than user incompetence.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the poor quality of Windows default applets like Notepad, Calculator, and Paint reflects badly on Microsoft's overall commitment to quality. He proposes that Microsoft should license better third-party alternatives rather than ship decade-old, minimalist tools. Using HyperTerminal as a successful precedent, he outlines a win-win scenario where customers get better tools, third-party developers get sales opportunities, and Microsoft developers can focus on core OS work. The post criticizes the 'either-or' mindset that treats bundled utilities as unworthy of investment.
key_points: Default bundled applications matter because they're what 99.9% of users actually encounter on any given Windows machine
The quality of small utilities like Notepad and Calculator reflects the overall care put into the operating system
Microsoft successfully licensed HyperTerminal from Hilgraeve for Windows 95, proving the third-party licensing model works
Licensing better applets would benefit customers (better tools), developers (upsell opportunities), and Microsoft (focus on core OS)
Notepad shipping virtually unchanged from Windows 95 to Vista represents an unconscionable lack of progress
money_quotes: The applets that ship in the box with the operating system are, like it or not, the defaults.

But isn't the fit and finish of little applets like these – Notepad, Calculator, Character Map, Paint, Disk Cleanup, Compressed Folders, and dozens of others – indicative of the care and design that goes into the entire operating system?
If Microsoft can't be bothered to bundle a version of Notepad that has basic amenities like a toolbar, what hope does the rest of the operating system have?
The idea that we'll be viewing readme.txt files into 2010 with nothing but no-frills Notepad is unconscionable.
Why doesn't Microsoft simply license a better notepad and bundle it with Vista?
A third party whose entire business revolves around a calculator replacement will have a deeper, more vested interest in building a great calculator applet than any Microsoft employee ever will.
posts.key_insight: The quality of default bundled utilities reveals an organization's true commitment to user experience, and outsourcing to specialists often produces better results than half-hearted in-house efforts.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that developers should use Exception.ToString() instead of Exception.Message when handling errors, as the former provides crucial debugging information like stack traces. He extends this principle to a broader design guideline: every object should implement a meaningful ToString() method that provides useful information rather than just echoing the class name. Using DataSet as a prime example of poor ToString() implementation, he demonstrates how the default behavior returns useless output when it could display the actual data in a readable format.
key_points: Exception.Message loses valuable debugging context like stack traces; Exception.ToString() should be the default choice
Every custom object should implement a meaningful ToString() that represents what the object actually contains
Microsoft's own DataSet class violates this principle by returning just 'System.Data.DataSet' instead of its contents
Good ToString() implementations require extra work but provide debugging value that should have been built-in
money_quotes: Unless there's some compelling reason you need an ultra-terse version of the error, it's almost always better to use the provided Exception.ToString() method.
Any object you build should have a meaningful .ToString() method.
I expect a proper string representation of what you are, not a meaningless echo of your class name!
Seems perfectly logical to me, but you'll have to write your own custom ToString() implementation to get the behavior that should have been there in the first place.
posts.key_insight: A well-implemented ToString() method that reveals an object's actual state is essential for debugging and should be a standard practice, not an afterthought.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares the surprising origin of the Windows 95 startup sound, revealing it was composed by avant-garde musician Brian Eno. The post highlights Eno's creative process, where strict constraints (3.25 seconds, specific emotional qualities) led to unexpected artistic breakthroughs. Eno created 84 variations and found the exercise so valuable that it unlocked his own stalled creative work. Atwood connects this to the broader Windows 95 media phenomenon and notes the anticipation around the upcoming Windows Vista release.
key_points: The iconic Windows 95 startup sound was composed by Brian Eno, a famous avant-garde electronic musician
Eno was creatively stuck when Microsoft approached him, and he found the specific constraints liberating rather than limiting
He created 84 different pieces while working on the 3.25-second sound, becoming 'sensitive to microseconds'
The tight constraints ultimately broke a creative logjam in Eno's own longer-form musical work
Windows 95 had a massive marketing campaign including the Rolling Stones' 'Start Me Up'
money_quotes: I really appreciated someone coming along and saying, 'Here's a specific problem – solve it.'

The thing from the agency said, 'We want a piece of music that is inspiring, universal, blah-blah, da-da-da, optimistic, futuristic, sentimental, emotional,' this whole list of adjectives, and then at the bottom it said 'and it must be 3 1/4 seconds long.'
I thought this was so funny and an amazing thought to actually try to make a little piece of music. It's like making a tiny little jewel.
I was so sensitive to microseconds at the end of this that it really broke a logjam in my own work.
Then when I'd finished that and I went back to working with pieces that were like three minutes long, it seemed like oceans of time.
posts.key_insight: Creative constraints, even absurdly tight ones, can paradoxically unlock innovation and break through creative blocks.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood criticizes ASP.NET's page model for rendering entire web pages in memory before sending any content to the browser, eliminating the possibility of progressive HTML rendering. He notes that progressive rendering has been a core browser capability since Netscape 1.0 in 1994, providing significant psychological benefits to users who can see content loading rather than staring at blank pages. While workarounds exist using Response.Write and Response.Flush, they bypass the normal ASP.NET page lifecycle. Atwood argues this represents a regression in user experience caused by framework abstraction choices.
key_points: ASP.NET renders entire pages in memory before sending any bytes to the browser, preventing progressive rendering
Progressive HTML rendering has existed since Netscape 1.0 (1994) and provides documented psychological benefits to users
A blank page during 15-second database queries creates a poor user experience compared to showing partial content
Workarounds exist (Response.Write/Flush) but require abandoning the normal ASP.NET page lifecycle
Framework abstractions can inadvertently eliminate valuable browser capabilities that users depend on
money_quotes: One thing I dislike about ASP.NET is that it renders the entire web page in memory before sending one single byte of that page to the browser.
A blank page is a disappointing user experience.
What's even more galling is that HTML was originally designed to render progressively as content is received.
There's no opportunity for the browser to start displaying data to the user while the server is still chugging away on the data.
The user is stuck staring at a blank web page for the full 15 seconds until the operation completes on the server.
Regardless, it still sucks for users.
posts.key_insight: Framework abstractions that prioritize developer convenience can inadvertently destroy fundamental web capabilities that existed for good user experience reasons.

posts.summary: Atwood argues that while rigid hierarchical categorization systems like Yahoo's directory model are obsolete, hierarchies still have tremendous value as filters for disambiguating search results. Using the example of searching for 'Jaguar' (which could mean the car, OS X version, animal, or Atari console), he contrasts Google's flat search results with eBay's category-based filtering that lets users narrow results with a single click. He proposes that Google could automatically suggest semantic disambiguations similar to how they suggest spelling corrections, potentially using Markov chain analysis of existing web pages to identify common meanings.
key_points: Single-word searches carry semantic ambiguity that exists only in the user's head, not in the search box
While rigid hierarchical directories are obsolete ('evil'), hierarchies remain powerful as semantic-narrowing filters on search results
eBay's category filtering demonstrates how users can disambiguate with minimal effort compared to typing additional keywords
Google could extend its 'Did you mean...' feature beyond spelling corrections to semantic disambiguation
Such a disambiguation system could potentially be automated using Markov chain probabilities rather than manual categorization
money_quotes: The semantics of Jaguar only exist in my head, not in any search box.
My apologies to Yet Another Hierarchically Organized Oracle and the Open Directory Project, but rigid hierarchy is evil.
A rigid hierarchy is tremendously powerful as a semantic-narrowing filter on search results.
A single word should get me what I want.
If Google is going to suggest 'Did you mean...' corrections when I misspell a search term, why don't they do the same thing to disambiguate semantics?
In a brave new Google world of 'I'll just type in what I want and hit Enter' search, there may still be room for some quasi-evil hierarchy in there somewhere.
posts.key_insight: Hierarchies aren't inherently evil—they become powerful tools when used as optional filters to narrow search results rather than as rigid gatekeepers to content.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood advocates for adopting the apprenticeship model from traditional trades like electrical work to train software developers. He cites Rob Walling's argument that the 'listen, watch, do, review' cycle used in trade apprenticeships is superior to traditional classroom training. The combination of theoretical learning alongside practical, real-world coding experience produces better developers. Atwood notes that good interns who balance coursework with real code often become excellent developers. However, he acknowledges that effective mentorship requires matching skill levels appropriately—mentors and apprentices can't be too far apart in ability for the relationship to work.
key_points: Traditional software training fails because it only covers 'listen' and 'watch' while skipping the crucial 'do' and 'review' steps that drive real skill development
The apprenticeship model used by trades like electricians—combining night school theory with daytime hands-on work under a journeyman—could transform software training
Interns who work on real business code while simultaneously learning CS theory often develop into exceptional programmers
Effective mentorship requires appropriate skill-level matching; too large a gap between mentor and mentee makes productive collaboration impossible
Software development should cultivate formal apprentice-journeyman-master relationships rather than operating as a loose confederation of tribes
money_quotes: The 'do and review' is what inspires growth and advances skills.
The beauty of apprenticeship is that it tackles theory and experience in one fell swoop.
The mixture of theory by night and real world coding by day is particularly compelling.
Instead of a loose confederation of tribes, maybe we should be cultivating apprentice, journeyman, master relationships in software development.
I have difficulty mentoring developers who are too far apart from me in skill level. I'm too impatient.

If you're putting football players together on a field to scrimmage, don't mix professional players with high school players. The skill disparity is too great for them to actually play football together.
posts.key_insight: Software development training should adopt the trade apprenticeship model of 'listen, watch, do, review' because hands-on practice with mentor feedback—not passive classroom learning—is what actually builds developer skills.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood highlights Adam Bosworth's influential article about lessons learned from the web, emphasizing simplicity, loose coupling, and crowd wisdom as key architectural principles. Beyond the technical architecture, Atwood argues that the web browser's user interface innovations were equally critical to the web's success. He praises the single-click interface, the back button, and the forced simplicity of one-task-per-page design. The post also touches on Bosworth's argument for RSS as a replacement for XML's complexity, while noting the potential conflict of interest given Bosworth's employment at Google during the Google Base era.
key_points: The web succeeded because of 'unintuitive lessons': simple text formats beat complex binary ones, staleness is acceptable, and crowd wisdom works
Browser UI innovations (single-click, back button, single-task pages) were as important as architectural principles in popularizing the web
The back button is described as 'the most profound innovation in user interface in the last ten years'
RSS 2.0 is proposed as a simpler, more web-friendly replacement for XML's complexity
Webby UI principles became formalized as 'Inductive User Interface' and influenced client applications and operating systems
money_quotes: The browser popularized the web by making it dead simple to use
The back button. Easily the most profound innovation in user interface in the last ten years. Don't like where you are? Go back to where you were. It's totally natural.
Simple, relaxed, sloppily extensible text formats and protocols often work better than complex and efficient binary ones
Since websites are slow to load and can't display much at once, designers were forced to stick to one task per page
The wisdom of crowds works amazingly well
It is acceptable to be stale much of the time
posts.key_insight: The web's success came not just from architectural simplicity and loose coupling, but equally from browser UI innovations that made complexity invisible to users.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines a Wichita State usability study comparing reading speeds across twelve common web fonts. The study found a 40-second difference between the worst font (Corsiva) and best performers for two pages of text. Surprisingly, Courier—the only monospace font tested—was the second-worst performer, which Atwood notes has concerning implications for code readability. However, the researchers concluded that for typical short online reading, font differences may not matter significantly. Atwood's key takeaway is that avoiding large text blocks is more important than font choice.
key_points: A 2001 usability study found 13% reading time difference between worst and best fonts, which researchers considered 'relatively small'
Courier, the only monospace font tested, performed second-worst—barely better than the completely inappropriate Corsiva
The connection between reading paragraphs in browsers and reading code in IDEs is unclear, but results don't favor monospace fonts
For short online passages with conventional fonts, reading speed differences may not have real consequences
Good design that avoids large text blocks matters more than font selection
money_quotes: Courier – the only monospace font in the experiment – was the second worst font for reading time.
Courier barely performed any better than the abhorrent Corsiva!
Any connection between reading paragraphs in a browser and reading code in an IDE is unclear, but it certainly doesn't bode well for the use of monospace fonts in general.
Before worrying about fonts, be sure you aren't presenting giant blocks of text to the user in the first place.
Well-designed web pages avoid using large blocks of text, and so should your code.
It's an amazing source of usability experiments with actual data, hypotheses, citations, statistics, and all that other stuff that puts the science back into computer science.
posts.key_insight: While font choice matters less than we might think for short text, the poor performance of monospace fonts in reading studies should make developers question assumptions about code readability.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares insights from an Artima interview series with Erich Gamma, focusing on the concept of 'frameworkitis' - the problem that arises when frameworks try to do too much and become difficult to work against. Gamma advocates for small, focused frameworks over heavyweight ones, arguing that larger frameworks have steeper learning curves and are harder to maintain. The preferred approach is a toolkit of optional, smaller frameworks that developers can pick and choose from, paying the framework costs incrementally rather than upfront.
key_points: Frameworkitis occurs when a framework does too much or does things in ways you can't change, causing developers to fight against it
Toolkits avoid frameworkitis because they don't attempt to take control away from developers
