A Regex that works well when the input matches can still go nuts trying to match a non-matching string, consuming an exponential amount of time trying an exponential number of futile backtracking possibilities.
I'm definitely guilty of this. When I throw a regex together, I never worry about performance.
Looks like n-squared behavior to me.
When nesting repetition operators, make absolutely sure that there is only one way to match the same match.
posts.key_insight: Regular expressions can become performance time bombs not on expected input, but on malformed or non-matching strings, where backtracking engines try exponentially many futile combinations.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the concept of an optimal piracy rate while discussing his frustrations with StarForce copy protection, which has proven remarkably effective at preventing game piracy. He acknowledges StarForce's technical achievement in fending off crackers for nearly a year, something unprecedented in software history. However, he questions whether such aggressive protection is counterproductive, citing Chris Anderson's argument that some piracy actually benefits industries by seeding markets and allowing premium pricing. The post concludes that DVDs represent the ideal balance: basic protection combined with reasonable pricing that makes piracy not worth the effort for most consumers.
key_points: StarForce copy protection achieved what was thought impossible—preventing working no-cd patches for nearly a year after a game's release
Circumventing StarForce requires extreme measures like physically unplugging optical drives or using USB drives, which is deliberately cumbersome
There may be an optimal piracy rate that varies by industry, where too-aggressive protection chokes demand or pushes users toward piracy
Piracy serves functions like seeding technology markets (Windows/Office became de-facto standards partly through piracy) and allowing companies to price for the middle-to-top market
DVDs demonstrate 'good enough' protection: basic copy protection plus reasonable pricing means few bother to pirate them
money_quotes: For as long as there has been software, there have been crackers – and they've always won.
Any protection technology that is really difficult to crack is probably too cumbersome to be accepted by consumers.
The ubiquity of pirated Windows and Office have made them de-facto national standards in many countries.
No price can compete with free.
Maybe 15%-30% is simply the market saying that this is the optimal rate of piracy for those industries.
All in all, a giant pain in the ass. Which is entirely the point of copy protection.
They have a basic level of copy protection, but they're priced so reasonably very few people bother to pirate them.
posts.key_insight: Copy protection exists on a spectrum where being too effective can be as harmful as being too weak—the goal should be making legitimate use easier than piracy, not making piracy impossible.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the observed correlation between software development and traits associated with Asperger's Syndrome, noting that many successful programmers exhibit characteristics like intense focus, literal thinking, and preference for systems over social interaction. He references a Wired article documenting how tech culture has evolved to accommodate neurodiverse workers, and discusses the genetic component that may explain higher rates of autism spectrum disorders in tech hubs like Silicon Valley. The post examines 'assortative mating' among engineers as a potential factor in rising autism diagnoses in these communities. Atwood concludes by questioning who defines 'normal' and citing Hans Asperger's observation that some autism may be essential for success in science and art.
key_points: Software development naturally attracts people with Asperger's traits: obsessive focus, literal interpretation, preference for machines over people, and comfort with order and syntax
Tech culture has evolved to accommodate neurodiverse workers—merit-based respect, email over face-to-face communication, and tolerance for social eccentricity
Genetic factors and 'assortative mating' among engineers in tech hubs may explain higher rates of autism spectrum disorders in places like Silicon Valley
Major tech companies like Microsoft have responded by offering insurance benefits for behavioral training for employees' autistic children
The line between functional quirks and diagnosable conditions is ultimately arbitrary—traits that make someone 'odd' may also make them exceptional
money_quotes: I often joke that you have to be a little obsessive-compulsive to be a good developer.
You can be as off-the-wall as you want to be, but if your code is bulletproof, no one's going to point out that you've been wearing the same shirt for two weeks.
A WYSIWYG world, where respect and rewards are based strictly on merit, is an Asperger's dream.
High tech hot spots like the Valley, and Route 128 outside of Boston, are a curious oxymoron: They're fraternal associations of loners.
Replacing the hubbub of the traditional office with a screen and an email address inserts a controllable interface between a programmer and the chaos of everyday life.
It seems that for success in science and art, a dash of autism is essential.
posts.key_insight: The same cognitive traits that society sometimes pathologizes as Asperger's Syndrome—intense focus, literal thinking, systematic reasoning—are precisely what makes someone excel at software development, suggesting the tech industry is an inadvertent sanctuary for the neurodiverse.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that virtual machines will become the primary method of software distribution and execution. He uses VMs extensively himself to test new software in isolated environments before committing to installation. The post outlines five key advantages: security through disposable sandboxes, simplified installation via pre-configured images, OS-agnosticism, hardware acceleration from new CPU features, and the need to utilize increasingly powerful desktop hardware. Atwood predicts that VMs will eventually become first-class citizens in operating systems, creating a 'solipsist OS' where each application believes it's the only one running.
key_points: VMs provide ultimate security by allowing users to blow away potentially compromised sessions and start fresh
Software distribution as VM images eliminates complex installation processes entirely
OS choice becomes irrelevant when any application can run in its own pre-configured VM environment
Dedicated hardware virtualization from Intel and AMD will dramatically improve VM performance
Multi-core processors, terabyte drives, and expanded RAM finally provide enough power to make pervasive desktop virtualization practical
money_quotes: I don't want to junk up my primary install until I'm totally confident I know what that software does. It's guilty until proven innocent.
I think all software will eventually be distributed as virtual machine images.
No spyware or virus is virulent enough to escape a VM.
It's like formatting your hard drive every time you turn off your PC.
Who cares if your app requires Linux or OS X to run if I can boot it in a pre-configured VM within a few seconds?
Eventually, all applications will believe they're the only applications in the world. And they'll be right.
posts.key_insight: The convergence of cheap virtualization software, hardware acceleration, and abundant computing resources will transform software distribution from complex installations to simple, isolated, disposable VM images.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood traces the ironic history of how the DivX video codec completely eclipsed the identity of DIVX, an ill-fated pay-per-view DVD rental system from the late 1990s. The original DIVX required special players connected to phone lines and charged fees for viewing discs beyond 48 hours, generating intense online backlash. The format failed within a year, while the DivX codec—originally named as a parody with a winking emoticon—grew from a hacked MPEG-4 codec into a legitimate multi-million dollar business. The codec even absorbed the divx.com domain, completing the identity replacement.
key_points: The original DIVX was a consumer-hostile pay-per-view rental system requiring phone-connected players and viewing fees, which generated massive online opposition
The DivX codec was intentionally named to mock the failing DIVX format, using a winking emoticon in early versions
DivX has ethically questionable origins, starting as a hacked MPEG-4 codec and later forking commercially from an open source project
The open source XviD codec emerged as a response to DivX's commercial fork, spelling the name backwards as commentary
The domain divx.com changed hands completely, with the last DIVX snapshot in 1999 and DivX appearing in 2001
money_quotes: It's ironic that the popular DivX codec has all but obliterated the identity of the ill-fated DIVX pay-per-view rental system.
This particular bad idea was intensely unpopular online. In 1998 and early 1999, it felt like you couldn't visit a website without seeing an anti-DIVX banner plastered on it somewhere.
The format barely lasted a year.
It sure is funny when a little hacked codec name joke turns into a multi-million dollar business under the same exact name.
The name replacement is so complete that the divx domain has been subsumed as well.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes a satirical jab at a failing technology can outlive and completely subsume the identity of its target, transforming from joke into legitimate business.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood highlights an experiment where Windows XP was installed on hardware far below its minimum requirements—a 20 MHz Pentium 1 with 32MB RAM. The result was an extremely slow but functional system that revealed normally invisible boot processes. He contrasts this with even older hardware like the Osborne Executive to provide perspective on computing progress. The post also debunks the common myth about Osborne Computers' downfall being caused by pre-announcing future products.
key_points: Windows XP can technically run on hardware 10x slower than its minimum requirements, though barely
At 20 MHz, the system runs at 60% CPU capacity just sitting at the desktop
The extreme slowness makes normally invisible processes visible, like seeing boot screen color changes 'line by line'
The famous 'Osborne Effect' of killing sales by pre-announcing products is actually an urban legend
money_quotes: That puts the XP in back in Windows XP – Xtremely Pokey
The CPU is working at 60% of full capacity at the Desktop!
At 20 MHz you can see all 8 blues line by line!
But even a 20 MHz Pentium is still orders of magnitude more powerful than this Osborne Executive
posts.key_insight: Pushing software to run on absurdly underpowered hardware reveals the hidden complexity and processes that modern speed makes invisible.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the technical foundations of 3D positional audio through headphones, explaining how Head Related Transfer Functions (HRTFs) create realistic spatial sound by simulating how human ear shapes and heads filter audio. He traces the evolution from monaural to stereo to binaural to true 3D sound, lamenting the demise of Aureal's pioneering A3D technology. The post culminates in a review of Creative's X-Fi sound card, which Atwood praises for finally matching Aureal's quality with vastly improved HRTF algorithms and 128 simultaneous sound voices. He recommends the budget XtremeMusic model for headphone enthusiasts seeking quality 3D audio in games.
key_points: Headphones can actually deliver better surround sound than speakers for 3D positional audio because HRTFs are designed to inject processed sound directly into the ear canal
The shape of your outer ear (pinnae) dramatically affects how you perceive sound location, and HRTFs simulate these effects computationally
True 3D sound requires hardware acceleration because HRTF calculations are computationally expensive, making onboard motherboard sound inadequate for gaming
The X-Fi represented a massive leap in processing power, jumping from 4.1 million transistors in the Audigy 2 to 51 million, rivaling contemporary CPUs
Despite Creative's premium pricing, the entry-level XtremeMusic at $110 sacrifices almost nothing compared to the $300 models
money_quotes: The nice thing about headphones is that they don't bug your neighbors or your wife – and they're actually the best way to hear surround sound, too.
There's no way for speakers to do the job as well, because there's no way for them to stop each ear hearing the sound that's intended for other.
Onboard sound is simply not an option if you're a gamer of any kind.
Three hundred bucks for a sound card?
The X-Fi XtremeMusic sacrifices almost nothing compared to the fancier models and is priced within reason at around $110.
I always felt that Creative's 3D sound HRTF algorithms were never as good as Aureal's.
I noticed the improved 3D sound HRTFs immediately.
posts.key_insight: True 3D positional audio through headphones requires sophisticated HRTF algorithms with dedicated hardware acceleration, and after years of inferior alternatives, the X-Fi finally delivers on the promise that Aureal pioneered.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood makes a compelling case for code reviews as the most impactful practice for improving code quality. He cites Karl Wiegers' extensive experience with peer reviews and backs up the argument with statistical evidence from Steve McConnell's Code Complete. The data shows code reviews dramatically outperform testing alone in defect detection, with case studies demonstrating 80-90% error reductions and significant productivity gains. Atwood argues that code isn't truly complete until reviewed by another developer, and the only barriers are finding a respected peer and making time for the process.
key_points: Code reviews detect defects at 55-60% effectiveness compared to 25-45% for various testing methods
Organizations introducing code reviews saw error rates drop from 55% to 2% on one-line changes
The ROI is substantial: JPL estimates $25,000 saved per inspection by catching defects early
IBM's Orbit project using 11 levels of inspection delivered early with only 1% of expected errors
The main obstacles to code reviews are social (finding a respected reviewer) and logistical (making time), not technical
money_quotes: Peer code reviews are the single biggest thing you can do to improve your code.
If you're not doing code reviews right now with another developer, you're missing a lot of bugs in your code and cheating yourself out of some key professional development opportunities.
As far as I'm concerned, my code isn't done until I've gone over it with a fellow developer.
I would never work in a team that did not perform them.
Reviews cut the errors by over 80 percent.
The only hurdle to a code review is finding a developer you respect to do it, and making the time to perform the review.
Every minute you spend in a code review is paid back tenfold.
posts.key_insight: Code reviews aren't just a nice-to-have practice but are statistically proven to be more effective at finding defects than testing, making them the highest-leverage activity for improving code quality.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood criticizes Robert Scoble's demand that all websites work perfectly on mobile phones, arguing this represents unrealistic 'and a Pony!' thinking. Written in 2006, Atwood pushes back on the idea that websites should seamlessly scale from tiny cell phone screens to large desktop monitors. He points out that Scoble offers no practical guidance on how to achieve this impossible feat, instead just demanding it happen through what amounts to magic. The post captures the pre-responsive-design era's skepticism about cross-device compatibility.
key_points: Demanding websites work across all screen sizes (176x220 to 1920x1440) was seen as unrealistic in 2006
Critics who demand impossible features without offering implementation guidance are engaging in 'Pony thinking'
Simply calling out problems without understanding technical constraints is unhelpful criticism
The gap between what users want and what's technically feasible is often underestimated by non-developers
money_quotes: It'd also be nice if trees were made of cotton candy, and rain was delicious lemonade.
This is what I refer to as '... and a Pony!' thinking: the person asking the question doesn't know that what they're asking for is essentially impossible.
So you might as well throw a Pony in there while you're at it. Everyone loves Ponies.
It'd be helpful if he actually offered any thoughts on exactly how one would go about implementing a web site that scales up seamlessly seventy times in resolution.
But why explain yourself when you can just use magic?
posts.key_insight: When someone demands a feature without understanding the technical complexity involved, they might as well ask for a pony too—the request reveals ignorance of implementation realities.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues for minimizing third-party dependencies in software projects, drawing inspiration from the Excel team's famous motto 'Find the dependencies – and eliminate them.' He criticizes developers who compulsively adopt every new third-party control and framework, noting that deployment becomes a nightmare of license files and version management. While acknowledging the .NET Framework as a comprehensive base, he warns against stacking additional dependencies without clear justification. He balances this view by noting the Lava Flow anti-pattern but dismisses it as a non-issue for developers willing to aggressively delete unused code.
key_points: Third-party dependencies create deployment complexity through license management, version conflicts, and vendor inconsistencies
The Excel team's dependency-averse culture enabled them to ship on time with high-quality code and even build their own cross-platform compiler
Code outside the base framework should be 'guilty until proven innocent' – adopted only when offering significant, proven benefit
Aggressive code deletion prevents the Lava Flow anti-pattern where legacy code accumulates because no one understands it
The goal isn't zero dependencies but the smallest practical dependency stack
money_quotes: I think anything outside the base framework is guilty until proven innocent.
My code may suck, but it's a constant level of sucking that I can plan around.
Find the dependencies – and eliminate them.
When you're working on a really, really good team with great programmers, everybody else's code, frankly, is bug-infested garbage, and nobody else knows how to ship on time.
When you're a cordon bleu chef and you need fresh lavender, you grow it yourself instead of buying it in the farmers' market.
I am pathologically addicted to deleting code.
I don't care if it woulda, coulda, shoulda been used – is it being used right now? No? Then it's gone.
Strive to make your dependency stack as small as you possibly can.
posts.key_insight: Every dependency you adopt is a liability you must maintain, so treat external code as guilty until proven innocent and keep your dependency stack as minimal as possible.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recommends the O'Reilly book 'Designing Interfaces' which compiles UI patterns and techniques into a full-color reference. While acknowledging that the same information exists freely online across various UI pattern websites, he argues that the physical book format better serves visual design content. The post draws a parallel between code design patterns and UI design patterns, noting a crucial difference in their visibility to end users.
key_points: UI design patterns deserve the same systematic treatment as code design patterns
Physical books can be superior to digital for highly visual reference material
Several free online resources exist for GUI and UI pattern education
Unlike code patterns hidden in implementation, UI patterns are directly visible to users
money_quotes: Sometimes atoms are better than bytes.
Unlike any of the Design Patterns in that famous book, these patterns are visible to your users. Plan accordingly.
A Design Patterns for the eye instead of the mind.
posts.key_insight: UI design patterns differ fundamentally from code patterns because users directly experience them, making visual design literacy essential for developers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that traditional local help files are obsolete in the age of internet search. He builds on Microsoft's usability research showing that experts use help more than novices, but points out the research missed the bigger picture: users have abandoned built-in help for Google because it delivers better, more relevant results. Atwood contends that developers should stop building local help files entirely and instead invest in building online communities, wikis, and forums around their products. The post advocates for giving users control over these community resources, recognizing that while developers write the software, users must live with it.
key_points: Microsoft's usability research found that experts use help files more than novices, contrary to expectations—novices prefer to click around and experiment
The real reason help files fail isn't just searchability or context-switching; it's that internet search delivers vastly superior results
Local help files can't compete with the collective knowledge indexed by search engines and community forums
Developers should invest in wikis, forums, and online communities rather than traditional documentation
Successful products like RegexBuddy demonstrate that building a community knowledge base online serves users better than local help
money_quotes: It seems to me that the best 'help file' for your software is no help file at all. It's a Google MSN Search query.
If you are building local help files for your application, you're wasting your time. And more importantly, you are wasting your users' time.
Smart developers will stop wasting time on useless local help and build community around their product on the internet.
I just type what I'm looking for into MSN Search and bring the full weight of billions of dollars of search optimization to bear on the entire internet.
Local help seems rather quaint in comparison.
If you really want to help your users, set up a wiki for your product. Set up forums for them to participate in.
You may have written it, but they have to live with it.
posts.key_insight: The best documentation strategy is not to write help files but to build searchable online communities where users help each other, because collective internet knowledge will always outperform any locally-bundled documentation.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that bug triage is an essential but often frustrating part of software development because not every bug deserves to be fixed. He distinguishes between serious bugs that everyone agrees on (10-20% of reports) and a vast gray area of questionable issues. Using the example of airplane avionics versus in-flight entertainment systems, he illustrates how context determines acceptable bug tolerance. The key insight is that triage decisions should be based on actual user data rather than developer and tester speculation, since testers aren't real users and their bug reports should be weighted accordingly.
key_points: Bug triage evaluates four factors: severity, frequency, cost to fix, and risk of the fix introducing new problems
Only 10-20% of reported bugs are clear-cut serious issues; the rest fall into a gray area of pseudo-bugs
Bugs from actual customers should be weighted ten times higher than bugs from testers
Every code change introduces risk, so fixing bugs can actually make software worse
Triage decisions should be based on actual usage data, not developer guesswork
money_quotes: How do you distinguish between bugs that users are likely to encounter, and bugs that users will probably never see?
Testers aren't real users. I'd give a bug from a customer ten times the weight of a bug reported by a tester.
Nobody has the luxury of fixing all the bugs in their software.
Every code change is a risk. A development cycle that doesn't recognize this will churn indefinitely and never create a shippable product.
Sometimes a 'bug' should not be fixed.
We are shipping a product with a quality level that is well known. There will be no surprises and no mulligans.
Triage is about one thing: making life better for your users.
posts.key_insight: Shipping quality software requires accepting that some bugs won't be fixed, and the best triage decisions come from actual user data rather than speculation about what users might encounter.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood addresses a common frustration at tech conferences: presenters showing code in tiny, unreadable fonts. Writing from VSLive! 2006 San Francisco, he calls out speakers who force audiences to squint at 10-12 point IDE text. He recommends using magnification utilities, including Windows XP's built-in magnifier, and provides practical tips for using it effectively during presentations. The post is a brief but pointed reminder that presenter convenience shouldn't come at the expense of audience comprehension.
key_points: Presenters frequently make the mistake of showing code in fonts too small for audiences to read
Windows XP includes a built-in magnifier utility (magnify.exe) that's sufficient for most presentation needs
The magnifier can dock to screen edges, follow mouse/keyboard actions automatically, and scale from 1x to 9x
Pre-scaling fonts or using magnification tools should be standard practice for any code demonstration
money_quotes: I've spent a disproportionate amount of that time staring at tiny, unreadable 12 and 10 point IDE text.
Presenters, please don't do this to your audiences.
Everything I do is now perfectly visible – even from the last row of the room.
posts.key_insight: Technical presenters have a responsibility to ensure their code is readable by using magnification tools, because audience comprehension matters more than presenter convenience.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood catalogs the most common and infuriating mistakes he witnessed speakers making at a VSLive conference, presenting them as satirical 'tips' for how not to give a presentation. He criticizes self-promotion, excessive agendas, bullet-point overload, reading slides verbatim, and ignoring time limits. The post argues that professional speakers should know better than to commit these basic errors. His frustration stems from the gap between what speakers are paid to do and their actual performance.
key_points: Professional speakers frequently commit basic presentation mistakes that undermine their credibility
Presentations should show rather than tell—demonstrations beat bullet points
Slides packed with text and read verbatim insult the audience's intelligence
Self-promotion and detailed agendas waste precious presentation time
Respecting time limits and taking questions shows respect for the audience
money_quotes: They get paid to present to large groups because they're ostensibly good communicators.
Don't show the audience. Tell them.
Audiences can't read. It's your responsibility to do all the reading for them.
The audience can wait. While you're fixing things up, try that NASCAR joke again. It's hilarious.
The content and quality of your presentation speaks for itself.
This stuff would be funny if it wasn't happening every single day.
posts.key_insight: The most common presentation sins—self-promotion, text-heavy slides, and reading verbatim—all stem from making the presentation about the speaker rather than the audience.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that excessive use of unexplained acronyms in software development presentations and product descriptions alienates and confuses users. He illustrates this through a frustrating experience trying to determine a Dell laptop's screen resolution, which was obscured behind the meaningless acronym 'WUXGA'. The post traces how the PC industry's habit of naming resolutions after display adaptors devolved into 'ridiculous alphabet soup' with nested acronyms three levels deep. Atwood concludes that if acronyms must be used, they should always be explained upfront and in multiple discoverable places.
key_points: Software presentations are filled with meaningless acronyms that go unexplained, harming comprehension
The PC display industry's acronym problem stems from a once-sensible practice that devolved as Moore's Law drove resolution increases
Modern display acronyms like WUXGA are 'nested acronyms' three levels deep, making them nearly impossible to decode
Users shouldn't need external translation resources to understand basic product specifications
Acronyms in applications should be explained upfront and in multiple easily discoverable locations
money_quotes: They're chock full of meaningless acronyms.
You might expect the resolution of the display to be expressed in something typical and understandable, like pixels. But you'd be wrong.
What's a 'WUXGA?' You might make a stab at guessing it's a rare four-horned bovid found in Central Asia.

As the years passed and Moore's Law worked its magic on display resolution as well as compute speed, more and more mnemonics were coined as higher resolutions appeared and were adopted, resulting in today's ridiculous alphabet soup.
I needed an entire translation page just to determine that 'wide' XGA+ is probably 1366 x 768. Or thereabouts. I'm not entirely sure. Thanks a lot, Dell.
Don't do this to your users.
posts.key_insight: Acronyms that made sense in their original context become impenetrable jargon over time, and respecting your users means explaining technical shorthand rather than assuming shared knowledge.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood discusses the frustration of using Visual Studio 2005's Smart Tags feature for variable renaming, which he considers his most frequent refactoring activity. The default keyboard shortcut (Shift+Alt+F10) is nearly impossible to type comfortably, inherited from Microsoft Office compatibility. He discovers better alternatives like Ctrl+. and his preferred Alt+Down, which intuitively maps to the 'drop down' action. The post concludes with a nuanced take on customization: while learning defaults is generally safer, sometimes poor defaults force you to customize.
key_points: Variable renaming is the most common refactoring activity, making keyboard efficiency crucial
Microsoft's cross-product compatibility (Office and Visual Studio) resulted in an unusable keyboard shortcut
Physical keyboard shortcuts should map intuitively to their conceptual actions (Alt+Down for 'dropping down' a menu)
Excessive customization is self-defeating, but poor defaults sometimes leave no alternative
money_quotes: It's the most psychotic keyboard shortcut ever.
I constantly rename variables. It's probably the single most frequent refactoring activity I do.
Go ahead. Just try to type that. I double dog dare you.
But sometimes the defaults are poorly chosen and you have no alternative but to customize them.
Alt + Down... which is an excellent physical map to the conceptual activity of 'dropping down' a smart tag menu.
posts.key_insight: Good keyboard shortcuts should physically and intuitively map to their conceptual actions, and when defaults fail this test, customization becomes necessary despite its drawbacks.

posts.summary: Atwood explores the challenge of storing private encryption keys when both encryption and decryption happen on the same machine. While cross-machine scenarios are straightforward—keep the private key on a server, distribute public keys to clients—local-only encryption presents a fundamental security problem: users have physical control of their machines. He shares a reader's detailed response outlining storage options (machine store, user store, registry, smart cards) and warns against storing keys in regular strings due to .NET's garbage collection. His conclusion is sobering: true security requires keeping private keys on separate hardware, and local storage can only make key discovery 'inconvenient,' not impossible.
key_points: Cross-machine encryption is inherently more secure because the private key never leaves the server
When both keys must exist on the same machine, complete security is theoretically impossible since users have physical control
Storage options include machine key store, user key store, registry with ACLs, or smart cards—each with different security tradeoffs
Never store keys in regular .NET strings because garbage collection doesn't properly clear them from memory
Hardware-based solutions like smart cards or embedded keys (Xbox 360 style) provide the strongest local protection
money_quotes: I had the luxury of keeping my private key on a different physical server. I had never even considered the problem of private key storage on the same machine!
How in the world do you keep the private key away from the prying eyes of the local user?
It seems like an insoluble problem to me, since users have complete physical control over their own machines.
Remember, never store an instance of your keys or passwords in a string! The .NET GC does not clear these values very well, and they are visible in the string table memory for anyone to steal!
The only way to keep a private key truly private is to store it on a completely different machine.
If you must store the private key on the same machine, some vulnerability is inevitable. You can only make it inconvenient for a user to find the private key through software protection.
This is another reason why the user comments are the best part of a blog.
posts.key_insight: Local private key storage is fundamentally a losing battle against determined users with physical machine access—you can only raise the difficulty bar, never eliminate the vulnerability.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood defends Visual Studio 2005's Edit and Continue feature against critics who argue it promotes sloppy coding practices. He presents three critics' arguments that debugging should be a last resort and that Edit and Continue enables bad habits. Atwood draws an analogy to video games, comparing the debate to arguments about 'save anywhere' versus checkpoint-only saving. He argues that critics who don't want to use the feature simply shouldn't, rather than demanding it be removed for everyone. The core argument is that flexibility in tools doesn't harm disciplined users but helps those who benefit from it.
key_points: Critics argue Edit and Continue encourages sloppy debugging practices and introduces bugs by letting developers focus too narrowly on immediate fixes
The underlying argument from all critics is paternalistic: the feature should be removed because it's 'dangerous' and developers can't be trusted
The video game analogy exposes the flawed logic: hardcore gamers who oppose 'save anywhere' could simply choose not to use it themselves
Demanding removal of optional features is really demanding that everyone work the same way you do
There's a meaningful difference between encouraging best practices and forcing them by removing tool flexibility
money_quotes: Edit and Continue should be removed because it's dangerous. And we're clearly too stupid to be trusted with a dangerous tool like edit and continue!
If they're so hard-core, they should easily be able to resist the temptation to save their game.
What they're really saying is, 'everyone should play games the same way we do,' and veiling it in some macho rhetoric to intimidate people into agreeing with them.
There's a big difference between encouraging people to take challenges and forcing them to take challenges by removing flexibility from the product.
Not everyone plays games for the same reasons you do. And not everyone writes code for the same reasons you do, either.
Clearly, offering both options is more flexible and inhibits no one.
posts.key_insight: Arguing that optional developer tools should be removed because some might misuse them is paternalistic gatekeeping disguised as best practices advocacy.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces the release of .NET Pet Shop 4.0 on MSDN, developed by his company Vertigo Software. This version marks a significant departure from previous iterations by abandoning the Java benchmark comparison that defined earlier versions. Instead, Pet Shop 4.0 serves purely as a showcase for ASP.NET 2.0 features and migration best practices. The post traces the lineage of Pet Shop from its origins as a direct Java Pet Store port through its evolution driven by community feedback. Atwood emphasizes that community dialog shaped version 3.0's development and invites continued engagement.
key_points: Pet Shop 4.0 is no longer a Java benchmark comparison but a pure ASP.NET 2.0 showcase
The application underwent a complete rewrite and is no longer functionally equivalent to the Java reference application
Community feedback directly influenced Pet Shop 3.0's improved architecture
The project evolved through four major versions over the ASP.NET 1.0 to 2.0 era
money_quotes: Unlike previous versions of Pet Shop, this version is not a benchmark comparison with Java.
Dialog with the community is what drove Pet Shop 3.0 development.
posts.key_insight: Reference applications evolve from competitive benchmarks into practical showcases when the platform wars give way to genuine developer education needs.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines performance benchmarks across nine programming languages, noting that while C++ leads, most compiled languages cluster together with negligible practical differences. The real story is interpreted languages like Python and JavaScript, which historically suffered 100x performance penalties. However, hardware improvements over the past decade have made JavaScript fast enough for mainstream applications, enabling the Web 2.0 revolution. Atwood argues that we've reached a point where the performance cost of interpreted code simply doesn't matter for most use cases anymore.
key_points: Most compiled languages (C#, VB, Java, J#) perform within a similar range, making language choice less about raw speed
Interpreted languages historically suffered extreme performance penalties (100x+ slower than compiled code)
JavaScript performance improved roughly 100-fold between 1996 and 2006 due to hardware advances
The viability of JavaScript for mainstream applications only became possible around 2003 with faster processors
Performance improvements are plateauing, but computers are now fast enough that interpreted code penalties are acceptable
money_quotes: What I find really interesting, though, is how most of the languages clump together in the middle.
Clearly, the performance penalty for interpreted languages is extreme. How extreme? If you have to ask, you probably can't afford it.
JavaScript is the very backbone of Web 2.0.
In ten years, JavaScript performance has improved a hundredfold.
Our computers are now so fast that – with very few exceptions – we don't care how much interpreted code costs any more.
The viability of interpreted JavaScript for mainstream applications is a relatively recent development.
I think JavaScript is about as fast as it can get now without some kind of really advanced dynamic compilation scheme.
posts.key_insight: Hardware improvements have rendered the historical performance penalty of interpreted languages largely irrelevant, fundamentally changing which languages are viable for mainstream application development.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his appreciation for Ian Albert's meticulously hand-traced hazmat placard collection, noting the dedication required to create high-quality PDFs of each sign. The post includes a humorous warning about the practical dangers of using these placards decoratively. Atwood connects this to his broader interest in signage and iconography, suggesting that software designers should study pre-digital visual communication systems for inspiration.
key_points: Ian Albert created hand-traced PDF versions of hazmat placards, demonstrating impressive dedication to preserving industrial iconography
Hazmat placards serve critical safety functions - misusing them decoratively could cause dangerous confusion for emergency responders
Pre-digital iconography like safety signs offers valuable lessons for software interface design
Visual communication systems that predate computers were refined over decades to be instantly recognizable and unambiguous
money_quotes: I still think the software industry can learn a lot from studying pre-digital iconography.
Don't use these decoratively in a public place. When emergency workers see a hazmat placard they react accordingly.
You could get in some real trouble if your house is burning down and they have to call in the bomb squad because you've got an 'explosives' sign hanging somewhere.
posts.key_insight: Software designers should study established pre-digital visual communication systems like hazmat placards to understand how to create truly effective, universally understood iconography.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood traces the history of audio visualization software from its origins with Jeff Minter's 1984 Psychedelia through to the Xbox 360's Neon visualizer. He highlights how Minter's work on the Atari Jaguar CD-ROM's VLM-1 influenced an entire generation of PC visualization plugins, including WinAmp's popular Geiss and G-Force. The post also tells the story of G-Force creator Andy O'Meara, who was stuck on Navy submarine duty while his software became a phenomenon and got licensed by Apple for iTunes. Atwood concludes that despite the massive hardware improvements in modern PCs, visualization software like G-Force and Geiss hasn't evolved much beyond their 8-bit origins, while console-based Neon has leapfrogged them.
key_points: Jeff Minter pioneered audio visualization starting in 1984 and his techniques became the foundation for nearly all modern visualizers including WinAmp, Windows Media Player, and iTunes
The MP3 revolution and WinAmp made audio visualization mainstream on PCs in the late 1990s
G-Force creator Andy O'Meara had to serve on a nuclear submarine due to his Navy ROTC scholarship while his software became a phenomenon and landed an Apple licensing deal
Despite modern PC hardware capabilities, popular visualizers like G-Force and Geiss remain limited to 8-bit color with no hardware acceleration
Console-based visualization (Xbox 360's Neon) has far surpassed PC equivalents in visual quality
money_quotes: It took the MP3 revolution and WinAmp to make audio visualization mainstream.
One of the guys from Nullsoft came up to me and apologized for 'borrowing' the techniques I'd used on the Jaguar VLM for their own visualisations.

To this day much of the visualisation stuff that you see in the likes of Winamp, Windows Media Player and iTunes uses fundamentally the same technique – using feedback to amplify small source input dynamics – that I used in VLM-1.
For Andy O'Meara, creating a trend-setting computer graphics program has been a thoroughly depressing experience.
If I wasn't in the Navy, I'd be on tour with Seal working on visuals for him. Am I depressed about it? Yeah.
I suppose we'll have to wait another few years for the audio visualization developers to catch up to the hardware capabilities of newer PCs.
posts.key_insight: One pioneering developer's work from the 1980s established the fundamental techniques still used in virtually all audio visualization software today, yet PC visualizers have barely evolved while console versions have leapfrogged ahead.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that effective presentations embrace minimalism over information-dense slides. He contrasts Bill Gates' cluttered PowerPoint approach with Steve Jobs' clean visual style, strongly favoring the latter. The post highlights Lawrence Lessig's radical approach of using single words or images per slide, and points to Dick Hardt's Identity 2.0 talk as proof this style can work for other speakers. Atwood urges readers to watch these examples and consider adopting minimalism in their own presentations.
key_points: Simple, visually clean slides are more effective than bullet-point-heavy presentations
Lessig's method defenestrates the 'no more than five bullets per slide' rule by using single words or images
Presentations should motivate and arouse imagination rather than pound every detail into the audience
The minimalist style works beyond just Lessig—Dick Hardt's Identity 2.0 talk proves it's transferable
Many speakers fail at presentations due to lack of due diligence or simply not caring
money_quotes: When it comes to presentations, less is more.
Lessig doesn't just ignore the classic 'no more than five bullets per slide' rule in his presentation, he practically defenestrates it.
He uses a single phrase or image per slide. Sometimes there's only a single word on each slide!
You do not need to (nor can you) pound every detail into the head of each member of your audience either visually or verbally.
The combination of your words, along with the visual images you project, should motivate the viewer and arouse his imagination.
I still feel that some speakers haven't done the basic due diligence that's necessary to give a good presentation. It's either that or they just don't care.
posts.key_insight: The most captivating presentations abandon bullet-point conventions entirely in favor of radical visual minimalism that engages the audience's imagination.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his visceral hatred of Lotus Notes, describing it as the epitome of bad software despite its widespread corporate adoption. He uses the Guardian article 'Survival of the Unfittest' as a springboard to reflect on how terrible software can nonetheless thrive in enterprise environments. The post argues that Lotus Notes represents a perfect case study in what not to do when developing software, emphasizing that the user interface is the application itself.
key_points: Lotus Notes represents the nadir of software user experience, despite being used by millions
Bad enterprise software persists not because of quality but despite its absence
The cumulative effect of small annoyances can make software unbearable
Poor software can directly impact employee retention and job satisfaction
Studying terrible software like Lotus Notes can teach valuable lessons about what to avoid
money_quotes: Until you've used Lotus Notes, you haven't truly experienced bad software.
It is death by a thousand tiny annoyances – the digital equivalent of being kicked in the groin upon arrival at work every day.
I won't say that Lotus Notes was the reason I quit that job, but it was definitely a factor in my decision.
The UI is the application.
Lotus Notes is a train wreck of epic (enterprise?) proportions, but it might be worth studying to learn what not to do when developing software.
posts.key_insight: Enterprise software often survives not on merit but on inertia, and the accumulated friction of poor user experience has real consequences for both productivity and employee retention.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents the keyboard shortcuts that work consistently across all major browsers (Internet Explorer, Firefox, Chrome, and Safari). He tested every shortcut to compile a definitive list of cross-browser compatible shortcuts. The post also highlights a useful but lesser-known feature: holding left-click on forward/back buttons to see navigation history. Atwood expresses appreciation for browser developers converging on shared keyboard conventions.
key_points: Cross-browser keyboard shortcut compatibility requires testing across all major browsers
Some shortcuts work universally while others are browser-specific
Left-click-and-hold on navigation buttons reveals page history for quick jumping
Browser developers have made progress in standardizing common shortcuts
money_quotes: I tested every shortcut, and here's my list of keyboard shortcuts that work in all browsers.
Left-click-and-hold on the forward and back buttons will show a list of the last (n) pages to select from, if you want to go forward or back more than a single page at once.
This comes in handy at least once a week for me.
It's good to see browser developers standardizing on at least a few common keyboard shortcuts rather than making up their own.
posts.key_insight: Knowing which keyboard shortcuts work across all browsers saves time and reduces friction when switching between different browsing environments.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood addresses the common barrier that prevents people from blogging: fear of writing itself. He acknowledges that writing is genuinely difficult and requires real effort to master. However, he argues that this difficulty is precisely why hesitant writers should blog anyway—writing regularly is exercise that inevitably improves skill. He recommends treating writing as an extension of natural conversation and offers practical resources for non-writers looking to improve.
key_points: Writing is hard and can't be faked or bought—it requires genuine work to improve
Regular blogging serves as exercise that will inevitably make you a better writer
Avoiding writing due to intimidation creates a self-perpetuating cycle of never improving
Good writing should sound natural, like spoken conversation rather than 'writerly' prose
Fear of writing shouldn't prevent people from blogging if they have something to say
money_quotes: Writing is hard.
Write a small blog entry a few times every week and you're bound to become a better writer.
If you're not writing because you're intimidated by writing, well, you're likely to stay that way forever.
Consider your writing a natural extension of your spoken conversations.
If it sounds like writing, I rewrite it.
Don't let fear of writing keep you from doing it.
posts.key_insight: The intimidation that keeps people from writing is the very reason they should write regularly—avoidance guarantees stagnation while practice guarantees improvement.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the history and significance of the Hello World program, tracing its origins from the 1978 C Programming Language book back to a 1974 Bell Labs memorandum by Kernighan. He notes that while Hello World appears simple, it serves as a crucial sanity test for verifying that a complete programming toolchain is correctly installed. The post highlights the surprising complexity that can exist in GUI contexts and embedded systems, and points to the extensive catalog of Hello World implementations across hundreds of languages, including joke languages. Atwood closes by noting the existence of Hello World performance benchmarks, which he finds amusingly useless but fitting.
key_points: Hello World programs serve as essential sanity tests for validating that compilers, development environments, and runtimes are correctly configured
The tradition originated from Kernighan's 1974 Bell Labs tutorial and was popularized by the 1978 C Programming Language book
Despite their simplicity, Hello World programs can be surprisingly complex in GUI contexts or embedded systems
Setting up a complete programming toolchain from scratch to run even trivial programs can involve substantial work
The existence of Hello World performance benchmarks is acknowledged as completely useless but fitting for the spirit of the exercise
money_quotes: Configuring a complete programming toolchain from scratch to the point where even trivial programs can be compiled and run may involve substantial amounts of work.
Of course, it's a completely useless performance test. But that's very much in the spirit of Hello World.
The archetypal Hello World program has always had a calming effect on developers.
A 'hello world' program can be a useful sanity test to make sure that a language's compiler, development environment, and run-time environment are correctly installed.
Some, however, are surprisingly complex, especially in some GUI contexts; but most are very simple.
posts.key_insight: The humble Hello World program, despite its apparent triviality, serves as a meaningful validation that an entire programming toolchain is working correctly—making it far more valuable than its simplicity suggests.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that syntax highlighting has become essential for reading code and markup, not just a convenience. He contrasts viewing XML in plain Notepad versus a colorizing editor, demonstrating how dramatically readability improves with color. The post questions whether developers have become spoiled or whether colorization genuinely aids comprehension. Atwood concludes that while plain text editors work fine for prose, they're inadequate for code.
key_points: Syntax highlighting transforms incomprehensible 'angle bracket noise' into readable, structured content
Plain text editors like Notepad are adequate for prose but fail for code and markup
The visual distinction provided by colorization helps developers parse and understand code structure
The dependency on colorization raises questions about whether it's a genuine cognitive aid or learned preference
money_quotes: I can't bear to read them in black and white any more.
It's just a slew of angle bracket noise that makes my brain hurt.
We need colorization to truly understand what we're looking at.
Why is that? Are we just spoiled?
posts.key_insight: Syntax highlighting has evolved from a nice-to-have feature to a cognitive necessity for parsing the dense symbolic structures of code and markup.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the debate between C# and VB.NET is meaningless because both languages compile to the same .NET runtime and offer only superficial syntactic differences. He uses the Pepsi vs Coke analogy to illustrate that choosing between them isn't a real choice. The post expresses excitement about IronPython bringing a truly different language paradigm—dynamic typing and significant whitespace—to the .NET ecosystem. With the Visual Studio SDK finally providing IDE integration for IronPython, Atwood sees the original promise of .NET's language-agnostic runtime finally being realized.
key_points: The C# vs VB.NET debate is a red herring since both compile to the same runtime with only syntactic differences
Python represents a genuinely different paradigm with dynamic typing and indentation-based syntax
The .NET runtime's original promise was meaningful language choice, not just syntax variations
IronPython with Visual Studio IDE integration marks Python becoming a first-class .NET citizen
money_quotes: Choosing between C# and VB.NET isn't a meaningful choice. It's like choosing between .NET Pepsi and .NET Coke.
Either way, you're getting a cola flavored carbonated beverage.
If C# is Coke and VB.NET is Pepsi, then Python is Yoohoo.
Wasn't this the promise of the .NET runtime – that we'd have real, meaningful choice of language?
posts.key_insight: True language choice on a runtime platform means offering fundamentally different programming paradigms, not just syntactic variations of the same underlying approach.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines mouse gestures as an input mechanism, drawing on his experience with games like Myth, Black and White, and Darwinia where gestures felt awkward and unreliable. He surveys various gesture implementations across browsers and operating systems but remains skeptical of their mainstream potential. While acknowledging that gestures can save time on larger screens by eliminating the need to move to specific UI elements, he identifies critical usability barriers: lack of discoverability, difficulty of precise mouse control, limited gesture vocabulary, and the need for dedicated activation keys. His conclusion is that gestures remain a power-user feature that would need to be simple, discoverable, and system-wide to achieve mainstream adoption.
key_points: Mouse gestures in games like Black and White suffered from recognition problems and felt like extra work compared to clicking buttons or pressing keys
Gestures have a fundamental discoverability problem—there are no visual cues that they exist, unlike buttons which prompt curiosity
Fine motor control with a mouse is difficult for most users, and keyboard shortcuts can be equally effective without requiring precise drawing
The gesture vocabulary is inherently limited; beyond simple cardinal directions, complexity becomes overwhelming
Gestures are most useful when they have conceptual mapping to actions (diagonal slash to close) and save movement on large screens
money_quotes: It ultimately ended up feeling like extra work when I could have simply clicked an icon or pressed a key to cast the very same spell.
Gestures have the one-button mouse problem. There are zero visual cues that any of these fancy gestures are possible.
Have you ever tried 'writing' your name with the mouse? Very few users have that kind of fine motor control.
There are only so many gestures you can draw. Once you get beyond a dozen simple strokes in the cardinal directions, you're in trouble.
In short, gestures are for extremely advanced users only, perhaps even more so than traditional keyboard shortcuts.
For mouse gestures to become mainstream, they need to be simple, discoverable, and most definitely system-wide.
posts.key_insight: Mouse gestures fail as a mainstream input method because they lack discoverability and require precision that most users don't have, making them useful only for power users willing to memorize invisible commands.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood addresses Microsoft's release of C# code snippets for Visual Studio 2005, which finally brings C# to feature parity with VB.NET. However, he criticizes Microsoft's poor installation process that requires manually adding each snippet folder through the Snippet Manager. After investigating, he discovers that snippet paths are confusingly stored in both an XML file and the Windows registry, with only the registry actually being used. He solves the problem by packaging the snippets with a proper registry file for easy installation, and recommends 7zip for compression and WinRAR for decompression.
key_points: Microsoft released 295 C# code snippets but provided a frustrating installation process requiring manual folder additions
The snippet configuration is confusingly duplicated between an XML index file and the registry, with only the registry being functional
Atwood created a packaged solution combining the snippets with a registry file for one-step installation
7zip compression dramatically outperforms ZIP for small files (56KB vs 229KB)
WinRAR won the DonationCoder archive tool roundup and handles multiple formats including 7zip
money_quotes: I loves me some snippets, but this is unacceptable!
Why they chose to store this stuff in the registry *and* in an XML file is beyond me.
You can go from tired... to wired... in no time at all.
Now you can quickly get the snippets installed on your system without running a pointless installer that does virtually nothing for you.
posts.key_insight: When developer tools ship with poor installation experiences, the community will step up to create better solutions—even when the fix is as simple as a properly configured registry file.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the widespread assumption that icons are inherently superior to text in user interfaces. Through a series of toolbar examples, he demonstrates how icons often fail to communicate their purpose clearly, requiring explanatory tooltips that defeat their purpose. Drawing on Jef Raskin's 'The Humane Interface,' he argues that icons frequently obscure meaning rather than clarify it. His conclusion advocates for combining icons with text labels, praising the direction Microsoft took with the Office 12 ribbon interface.
key_points: Icons often require explanation through tooltips, which undermines their supposed advantage over text
The best toolbar designs combine icons with words rather than relying on graphics alone
Icons can have different cultural meanings, making them unreliable for universal communication
Dense icon-only toolbars create visual noise that makes individual functions harder to identify
Microsoft's Office 12 ribbon interface represents a positive shift toward text-enhanced UI elements
money_quotes: Sometimes, the best icon choice isn't an icon. It's a word.
Instead of icons explaining, we have found that icons often require explanation.
If you wanted to obscure or to encode an idea to keep it from prying eyes, substituting icons for the words might not be a bad start.
The obvious reaction, which I have observed repeatedly when users first see this facility, is – why not just use the words in the first place?
My eye is inexorably drawn to the toolbar buttons with icons and text. The rest are lost in a morass of 16x16 graphics noise.
Graphics alone isn't enough.
posts.key_insight: Icons often fail at their primary job of communication and work best when paired with text labels rather than standing alone.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood draws a parallel between MMORPGs like World of Warcraft and corporate programming culture, arguing that both wrongly reward time investment over actual skill. He uses the satirical game ProgressQuest to illustrate how absurd pure time-based progression is, then cites Weinberg's classic example of a programmer who spent 8 weeks creating a mess that another developer rewrote in one week with better results. The post warns against the 'EverQuest Syndrome' in software development, where long hours are mistaken for productivity and even rewarded by management. Atwood concludes that becoming a better programmer requires building genuine skills, not grinding through all-nighters.
key_points: Time invested in programming has little correlation with actual value produced - a skilled developer can create 10x more value than an average one working longer hours
The 'EverQuest Syndrome' describes the fallacy of equating long hours with good work, a trap both gamers and programmers fall into
Management often rewards visible effort (long hours) rather than actual results, leading to morale problems when the truth emerges
ProgressQuest satirizes pure time-based progression systems by removing all gameplay and focusing only on leveling up
Building a skill portfolio is the path to improvement, not grinding through marathon coding sessions
money_quotes: The very idea that time > skill is alien.
Just because you spend a lot of time doing something doesn't mean you're doing it well.
If you want to level up in your IDE, you can't do it by grinding through all-nighters. You have to build your skill portfolio.
Clearly, the first programmer had been rewarded for making a mountain out of a molehill.
The rewriting and debugging took exactly one week, working normal hours. Even considering that writing a program for the second time is far easier, the difference is significant.
The discovery of this misapplication of management largesse led to a severe drop in morale.
posts.key_insight: Programming skill development comes from deliberate improvement and building expertise, not from logging more hours at the keyboard.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that cryptic error codes are an inexcusable relic of 8-bit computing that have no place in modern software. Using examples from Windows Defender and Team Foundation Server, he demonstrates how error codes like '0x8024402c' or '26105' leave users completely helpless. Drawing on Alan Cooper's 'About Face', he outlines three principles for good error messages: be polite, be illuminating, and be helpful. He concludes that any dialog presenting only an error code represents a complete failure in software design.
key_points: Error codes are outdated artifacts from 8-bit computing that should have disappeared decades ago
From the user's perspective, cryptic error codes might as well say 'You have made a terrible mistake' - they provide no actionable information
Good error messages must be polite (never blame the user), illuminating (explain the problem scope and alternatives), and helpful (offer solutions directly)
Error code dialogs fail all three criteria - they're rude, unhelpful, and completely opaque
It's the program's job to solve problems, not dump them on users with meaningless numbers
money_quotes: Is it unreasonable to expect human-readable error messages instead of undecipherable error codes in the year 2006?
Just try to communicate like a human being instead of a computer.
Never forget that an error message box is the program reporting on its failure to do its job, and it is interrupting the user to do this.
The customer is always right.
It is wrong for the program to just dump the problem on the user's lap and wipe its hands of the matter.
Error code dialogs go 0 for 3: they're rude, unhelpful, and about as illuminating as a mineshaft.
A dialog presenting an error code is utter and complete failure. Don't ever use error codes.
posts.key_insight: Software should communicate errors like a polite, helpful human would - explaining what went wrong, what it means, and what can be done about it - not like a machine spitting out meaningless codes.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his approach to diagnosing internet connection problems using two lightweight tools after experiencing bandwidth issues from a traffic spike. He frames internet troubleshooting around two key questions: bandwidth (how wide is your connection) and latency (how fast is your connection). He recommends NetMeter for monitoring bandwidth usage over time and PingPlotter for tracking latency and packet loss. The post emphasizes that while these aren't enterprise-grade tools, their simplicity makes them practical for gathering evidence to present to ISPs.
key_points: Internet connection quality has two distinct dimensions: bandwidth (throughput capacity) and latency (response time)
Consumer internet connections are highly asymmetric, with download speeds often 10x faster than upload speeds
Simple, lightweight monitoring tools can be more practical than complex enterprise solutions for basic troubleshooting
Collecting data over days or weeks provides much stronger evidence than anecdotal complaints when dealing with ISP support
Visual graphs of connection performance are more persuasive to tech support than vague reports of slowness
money_quotes: The two most important questions to ask are: How wide is your internet connection? How fast is your internet connection?
Your typical cable modem is around 300 kilobytes per second when downloading (sometimes even faster), but only 30 kilobytes per second when uploading.
Unless your connection suffers from severe packet loss, this is a bit less of a priority than raw bandwidth.
Now, these are intentionally lightweight tools. They're no match for fancy, dedicated networking diagnostic apps. But they're simple to use and easy to gather data with.
When you're calling your ISP's tech support, having these kinds of graphs to email them carries a heck of a lot more weight than generic complaints about 'slowness.'
posts.key_insight: When troubleshooting internet issues, simple tools that collect data over time are more valuable than sophisticated one-time diagnostics because they provide concrete evidence for ISP support conversations.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his enthusiasm for CodeRush, a Visual Studio plugin that adds dynamic animations and visual feedback to code editing. He highlights how the tool makes refactoring operations visually engaging, showing changes like strikethroughs on removed parameters and synchronized typing across multiple locations. The post advocates for more interactive and game-like development environments, questioning why IDEs haven't kept pace with the graphical capabilities of modern computers. Atwood sees this kind of dynamic, non-modal tooling as the future of code editing.
key_points: CodeRush brings video game-like animations and visual feedback to code editing, making refactoring operations visually dynamic
The tool is non-modal and never interrupts workflow with dialog boxes, keeping developers in flow
Modern computers can render complex 3D worlds at high frame rates, yet source code editors have barely evolved
Dynamic visual feedback during refactoring (like seeing changes propagate in real-time) enhances the editing experience
Some may find the animations distracting, but once accustomed, plain text editors feel primitive
money_quotes: Making a video game out of your code.
When you change the name of a method in one place, the typing appears in several places at once, and is highlighted everywhere.
Some people may find this distracting but once you get used to this sort of thing it's hard to go back to a text editor that doesn't take advantage of the dynamic nature of Windows.
It's kind of frustrating to see computers rendering 3D worlds with 100s of frames/sec, but source code editors advancing only in very small steps.
Just try beating my high score in CodeRush. Just try!
posts.key_insight: Development tools should embrace the dynamic visual capabilities of modern computers rather than remaining static text editors, making coding more engaging and intuitive.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares a Visual Studio 2005 macro he created to enumerate all code snippets installed on a system, building on Francesco Balena's console application. The macro reads from the Windows registry to find both system-wide and per-user snippet paths, then generates an HTML file listing all snippets with their shortcut text. The post provides step-by-step instructions for installing and running the macro, noting it supports multiple languages including C#, VB, J#, and XML.
key_points: The snippet manager stores all its configuration in the Windows registry, which the macro reads to find snippet paths
The macro generates an HTML report of all snippets and opens it directly in the Visual Studio IDE
The tool supports enumerating snippets for multiple languages by changing a single variable
This continues Atwood's pattern of creating developer productivity tools as IDE macros
money_quotes: I found out the hard way that the snippet manager writes all of its changes to the registry.
Does anyone actually *use* J#? C'mon. Seriously.
posts.key_insight: Developer productivity can be improved by building small tools that surface hidden IDE capabilities, like enumerating all available code snippets from the registry.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood contrasts Google's enduring homepage simplicity with Yahoo's gradual feature bloat from 1996-2006, arguing that minimalism is a competitive advantage. He cites Google's obsessive word-counting discipline, 37signals' 'less is more' philosophy, and pushes back against Donald Norman's criticism that Google merely hides complexity. Atwood argues that users don't want to 'do anything else' beyond their core task, and that features ultimately don't matter to people who just want to complete a task and move on. The post champions relentless pursuit of simplicity as a design principle.
key_points: Google maintains homepage discipline through unusual feedback mechanisms like an anonymous user who emails word counts when they increase
37signals advocates 'one-downing' competitors rather than one-upping them with more features
Donald Norman's criticism that Google 'hides complexity' misses the point—users don't want the hidden features anyway
Feature bloat killed competitors like AltaVista while Google's restraint helped it dominate
Users don't want to learn, configure, or spend more than 10 seconds confused—they want task completion
money_quotes: It's like a scale that tells you that you've gained two pounds.
Instead of one-upping, try one-downing. Instead of outdoing, try underdoing.
Do less than your competitors to beat them.
Is Google simple? No. Google is deceptive. It hides all the complexity by simply showing one search box on the main page.
I don't want to do anything else. All I want is to find what I'm searching for.
They don't want to spend more than 10 consecutive seconds confused.
They just want simple, they want to get their task done and move on.
We should always be in pursuit of simplicity, in whatever form it takes.
posts.key_insight: True product simplicity isn't about hiding complexity—it's about recognizing that users genuinely don't want the additional features you're tempted to build.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on Steve Yegge's programming language comparison post, extracting a deeper lesson about developer growth. He highlights Yegge's observation that programming language features aren't inherently bad—developers who don't understand them are the problem. Atwood argues that recognizing your own inadequacy is essential for improvement, and that being dissatisfied with your old code is a healthy sign of growth. The post concludes that while all developers write bad software, the best ones are aware of it.
key_points: Developers often blame language features (like multiple inheritance) when the real issue is their own lack of understanding
'Sucking less every year' is the path to improvement for humble programmers
Being unhappy with code you wrote a year ago is a positive sign of growth
If you're satisfied with your old code, you've either stopped learning, can't improve it, or never revisit it—all bad signs
The best developers are the ones who recognize they write bad software
money_quotes: It's not MI that sucks, it's developers who suck. I sucked, and I still do, although hopefully less every year.
Sucking less every year is how humble programmers improve.
You should be unhappy with code you wrote a year ago.

If you aren't, that means either A) you haven't learned anything in a year, B) your code can't be improved, or C) you never revisit old code. All of these are the kiss of death for software developers.
We all write shitty software. But only the best developers realize they're doing it.
posts.key_insight: Continuous improvement as a developer requires the humility to recognize that you've always been inadequate and the determination to be less so each year.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his experience with the Coleco Adam computer, a deeply flawed machine he owned after the TI-99/4a. Despite its deafening daisywheel printer and unreliable cassette tape drives, the Adam inspired a devoted community that continued meeting at annual conventions a decade after the product's discontinuation. Atwood discovers a 1995 speech by Richard Drushel that reveals the emotional attachment people developed for this footnote in computing history. The speech argues that the technology itself becomes secondary to the human connections it enables. The post ultimately suggests that communities built around shared interests persist long after their original practical purpose fades.
key_points: The Coleco Adam had serious technical flaws including an extremely loud printer and unreliable tape storage that required frequent manual repair
A dedicated community continued holding annual conventions (ADAMCons) for over a decade after Coleco discontinued the computer in 1985
Richard Drushel's 1995 speech articulates that programming for a dead platform is about intrinsic interest rather than financial gain
The technology becomes merely 'a flimsy excuse' for gathering - the real value is the social connections and friendships formed
Self-sufficiency in programming skills matters most when external support systems disappear
money_quotes: I was completely unprepared for the depth of emotion and attachment people had developed for what was, at best, a footnote in computing history.
It's sad and irrational, yet oddly inspiring.

I'm an ADAM programmer because I'm intrinsically interested in the ADAM. I write software for me, and if other people find it useful, that's great, but I'll program whether anybody else cares about what I'm doing or not.
The ADAM brought you together, originally for some concrete and practical purpose, but now the ADAM connection is a historical artifact.
You must admit that the ADAM per se is only a flimsy excuse for your attendance this year. The real reason you're here is social.

If all the ADAM newsletters disappear, all the ADAM BBSes go off-line, no more ADAMcons are held, and you can't find anybody else who has an ADAM, then you, like Robinson Crusoe, can be self-sufficient on your own desert island.
posts.key_insight: Technology communities outlive their practical purpose because the human connections they foster become more valuable than the technology itself.

posts.summary: Atwood criticizes the common practice of splitting form inputs into multiple tiny textboxes, using phone numbers and addresses as examples. He argues that this design pattern prioritizes programmer convenience over user experience. The post advocates for accepting flexible input formats and designing forms that match how users naturally think about data, like mimicking a mailing label for address entry. The core message is that the one-time effort of writing more flexible parsing code is far outweighed by the cumulative time saved across hundreds or thousands of users.
key_points: Phone number fields split into three boxes force tedious tabbing when a single flexible field would work better
Address entry could be simplified by mimicking a mailing label format users already understand
The time investment of one programmer writing flexible parsing code pays dividends across all users
Intuitive interfaces should match real-world mental models, not database schemas
Developer convenience should never trump user experience in interface design
money_quotes: Why do HTML forms have to be death by a thousand tiny textboxes?
Forcing hundreds of users to do more work to make a single programmer's life slightly easier is a false economy.
We have to stop optimizing for programmers and start optimizing for users.
The developers will only code and debug this once. Users, however, will be forced to enter thousands of telephone numbers in that tedious, time-consuming tiny textbox way.
Everyone has filled out an address label before. It's completely and utterly intuitive, even if you live in another country.
posts.key_insight: The economics of software development mean that any extra effort spent making interfaces more flexible for users pays off exponentially, since code is written once but used thousands of times.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the tension between following conventions and innovating in software development. While he strongly endorses consistency in code ('do things the same way everyone else does' to aid maintainability), he argues that UI design deserves more flexibility. He contends that not all conventions are equally valuable—some are timeless while others exist simply because nobody questioned them. The key is to experiment methodically: understand the existing convention, have a reasoned argument for deviation, collect usage data, and make evidence-based decisions. Without such experimentation, we'd never have gotten innovations like TiVo's interface or Office's ribbon.
key_points: In code, always favor consistency over cleverness—the next person reading it (including future you) needs to understand it immediately
User interfaces should be internally consistent but not necessarily consistent with every other application in the world
Not all UI conventions are created equal: some are timeless, some exist by default because nobody questioned them, some outlive their usefulness
