Companies like Amazon, Yahoo, and Google succeed by serving experimental features to small user segments and collecting data
UI experimentation requires understanding the convention, having reasoned arguments for deviation, collecting usage data, and making data-driven decisions
money_quotes: We should, as much as possible, try to do things the same way everyone else does them.
If you did something clever, the next person to touch it will look at the code and not immediately understand.
User interfaces should be internally consistent, but not necessarily consistent with every other application in the rest of the world.
How do we discriminate between conventions that actually help us and those that are merely... expected?
UI experimentation is not only desirable, but necessary. If we don't experiment, we can't evolve UI forward.
If you're not collecting usage data, or your reason is 'it looks better this way,' then you're doing it wrong, and you should stick with the conventions.
posts.key_insight: Code should follow conventions religiously for maintainability, but UI conventions should be questioned and tested empirically—innovation requires experimentation, but experimentation requires data.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his excitement about attending SDWest 2006 and the opportunity to meet Steve McConnell, author of Code Complete. He reflects on discovering Code Complete in 1994 and how profoundly it influenced his career path. The book provided him with a mentor figure during the isolated pre-internet era of programming. After the conference, Atwood updates that McConnell graciously spent time with him and signed his copy of Code Complete 2 with a playful 'Coding Horror' reference.
key_points: Code Complete was a formative book that convinced Atwood to dedicate his life to software development
Programming in the pre-internet era was an especially solitary pursuit, making influential books even more valuable
Finding a 'coding hero' through their writing can provide mentorship even without direct contact
Meeting your heroes can be a positive experience when they live up to their reputation
money_quotes: McConnell was my first coding hero.
Programming is such a solitary pursuit, and particularly so in those pre-internet days.
Code Complete convinced me that writing software is what I want to do for the rest of my life.
In Code Complete, I found an amazingly passionate, incredibly literate, and consummately professional programmer to learn from.
posts.key_insight: The right technical book at the right time can provide crucial mentorship and career direction, especially when programming felt like a solitary pursuit before widespread internet connectivity.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the fundamental mistake creators make is assuming users care about them or their work. Drawing from Seth Godin and other sources, he emphasizes that users are inherently self-interested and only engage with content that directly benefits them. The post lists four criteria for earning user attention: solving problems, providing useful information, entertaining, or making users feel empowered. Atwood concludes with a practical test: always ask 'so what?' about anything you create.
key_points: Users are fundamentally self-interested - they care about themselves, not about you or your creation
The biggest web design mistake is believing people care about your website
Content must serve one of four purposes: solve problems, inform, entertain, or empower users
The 'so what?' test should be applied to everything you create - websites, apps, blog posts, presentations, emails
money_quotes: Users don't care about YOU.
Believing people care about you and your web site.
Users don't care about you as much as they care about themselves.
Users just aren't that into you. It's not personal. It's business.
Every time you create something – whether it's a website, a client app, a blog post, a PowerPoint presentation, or an email – ask yourself, 'so what?'
If you can't answer that question convincingly, reformulate and try again.
posts.key_insight: The 'so what?' test is the essential filter for everything you create - if you can't articulate clear user value, you need to start over.

posts.summary: This brief post traces the visual evolution of the OK button across twenty years of Windows operating systems, from Windows 3.0 through Windows Vista. While the button's appearance has changed significantly over time, the use of 'OK' has remained constant. Atwood explores the etymology of 'OK', citing research that traces it to 'oll korrect' - a product of a 1830s-1840s fad for comical abbreviations. The post connects everyday UI elements to deeper historical and linguistic roots.
key_points: The OK button has undergone significant visual changes across Windows versions while maintaining the same text
'OK' stands for 'oll korrect', originating from an 1830s-1840s fad for humorous misspelled abbreviations
Columbia professor Allen Walker Read definitively traced the etymology in academic articles from 1963-1964
The timing of OK's first written appearance in historical documents supports this origin theory
money_quotes: There is one constant, however: the use of OK.
The letters, not to keep you guessing, stand for 'oll korrect.'
They're the result of a fad for comical abbreviations that flourished in the late 1830s and 1840s.
posts.key_insight: Even the most ubiquitous and seemingly simple UI elements like the OK button carry unexpected historical depth, connecting modern software to 19th-century linguistic fads.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents his frustration with Firefox's memory management, showing it consuming over 900 megabytes after a few days of normal browsing. He identifies the core problem: Firefox never releases memory until all instances are closed. Despite implementing a commonly suggested workaround involving the session history setting, the problem persists. The post serves as an early critique of Firefox's memory issues that would plague the browser for years.
key_points: Firefox consumed over 900MB of memory in a single non-tabbed instance after a few days of use
The browser never releases memory until all instances are completely closed
The commonly suggested about:config workaround (browser.sessionhistory.max_total_viewers) proved ineffective
The author notes this wasn't a problem with IE6, despite preferring Firefox for its add-on ecosystem
money_quotes: That's over 900 megabytes of memory for a single, non-tabbed instance of Firefox.
Firefox never seems to release any of the memory it uses – until you close all instances of Firefox.
If you spend enough time browsing the web, and never close Firefox completely, you'll consume nearly a gigabyte of memory!
I'll look at Task Manager and Firefox will regularly be gobbling up over 400 megabytes. This isn't uncommon. It's normal.
I can close all instances of Firefox and reduce memory usage back to zero, but why should I have to?
posts.key_insight: Software that users love for its features can still fail them through poor resource management, and popular workarounds don't always solve fundamental architectural problems.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues against the 'good enough' mentality in design by using Target's redesigned pill bottle as an example. He pushes back on resistance to his earlier proposal for redesigning web form address inputs. The post challenges the notion that familiar designs shouldn't be improved simply because they work. Atwood questions why it took 50 years to improve something as common as a pill bottle and concludes that we should constantly strive to overcome shortcomings with better designs.
key_points: Users make design judgments almost instantly—within a twentieth of a second
'Good enough' is not a valid reason to avoid improving designs
The 50-year wait for a better pill bottle illustrates our collective failure to prioritize design improvements
Even products that don't 'need' redesigning can become meaningfully better through thoughtful iteration
money_quotes: Why did we have to wait 50 years for a better pill bottle?
We should constantly be striving to overcome existing shortcomings with better designs.
Current web forms are good enough. Yeah, they have some shortcomings. But why change what works? Why change what people are used to?
Did the kitchen whisk need to be redesigned? No. Is it better because it was redesigned? Absolutely.
posts.key_insight: The 'good enough' mentality is the enemy of progress—we should constantly challenge familiar designs rather than accepting their shortcomings simply because they're familiar.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that selling usability to business executives requires reframing it in terms they care about: revenue, customers, and costs. Citing Jared Spool, he notes that explicitly talking about usability or design fails to win executive buy-in. Instead, the focus should be on measurable business outcomes like conversion rates and customer retention. He provides compelling examples where simplified sign-up processes led to 300-500% increases in conversions, demonstrating that usability improvements sell themselves when framed as business results.
key_points: Business executives don't respond to usability terminology—they respond to revenue, expenses, customers, and shareholder value
The cost of switching to competitors is approaching zero in the internet era, making usability a competitive necessity
Reducing friction in sign-up processes can yield dramatic conversion improvements (300-500% increases)
The most successful usability consultants avoid using words like 'usability' or 'design' in their proposals
Usability improvements are easy to sell when translated into concrete business metrics
money_quotes: I learned quickly that business executives didn't care about usability testing or information design.
We found, early on, that the less we talked about usability or design, the bigger our projects got.
Today, I'm writing a proposal for a $470,000 project where the word 'usability' isn't mentioned once in the proposal.
The cost of switching to a competitor is approaching zero.
We offered more and asked for less.
You may have trouble selling usability improvements – but I don't think you'd have any trouble selling a three to five fold increase in sign-up rates.
posts.key_insight: Stop selling usability—sell the business outcomes that usability improvements deliver.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recommends two classic interview collections—'Programmers At Work' and 'Out of Their Minds'—as valuable resources for understanding the human side of programming. He argues that learning from other programmers comes not primarily from reading their code, but from understanding them as people. Despite being over 15 years old at the time of writing, these interviews remain packed with insights. The post is a brief recommendation that points readers toward humanizing resources about notable programmers and computer scientists.
key_points: The most valuable learning from other programmers comes from understanding them as human beings, not just reading their code
Classic programmer interviews retain their value even decades later
The 'human side' of software development is an underappreciated source of insight
money_quotes: It's remarkable how much you can learn from other programmers. Not by reading their code, mind you, but by realizing that programmers are human beings.
posts.key_insight: Understanding programmers as human beings, rather than just analyzing their code, is a remarkably effective way to learn and grow as a developer.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to Richard Grimes' analysis claiming Microsoft isn't committed to .NET, agreeing that native code makes sense for OS development but strongly disputing the claim that Microsoft hasn't used .NET at scale. He points to Windows XP Media Center Edition as a major counterexample—a product with its entire UI written in C# that has sold 6.5 million copies. Atwood notes the irony that MCE offers more functionality than XP Pro at a lower price point, suggesting it will continue to outsell other versions.
key_points: Native code is appropriate for OS development because managed code trades CPU cycles for safety, and virtualization can provide similar protections
Windows XP Media Center Edition's UI is almost entirely written in C# managed code, contradicting claims that Microsoft avoids .NET at scale
MCE has sold 6.5 million copies in three years, making it one of the fastest-selling Windows versions
MCE costs less than XP Pro despite being a superset with more functionality, positioning it for continued strong sales
money_quotes: If you're writing an OS, you need to get down to the metal.
Managed code trades off CPU cycles for protection from buffer overruns and other C++ nastiness, but I'd rather spend those CPU cycles on virtualization to protect myself.
From a technical standpoint, Media Center user interface functionality is almost entirely written in C# managed code, on top of native Win32 and DirectX Windows XP components.
So you're getting more functionality for less money.
I would say the 6.5 million sales of XP MCE to date clearly indicates Microsoft's intention to use .NET on a large scale on XP, wouldn't you?
posts.key_insight: The claim that Microsoft doesn't trust .NET is contradicted by the millions of sales of Media Center Edition, whose entire UI is written in C# managed code.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses Lewis Carroll's Red Queen parable to illustrate how rapidly software development knowledge becomes obsolete. He observes that constantly learning new technologies like AJAX only keeps developers in the same place—running to stay current. To actually advance, developers must study timeless topics like human factors and design that won't be obsolete in five years. The post challenges readers to examine whether their focus on the latest technologies is preventing them from learning more enduring knowledge.
key_points: Software development knowledge becomes obsolete faster than in most other fields
Learning new technologies is necessary just to maintain your current position, not to advance
Topics like human factors and design remain relevant while specific technologies fade
The Red Queen Effect applies perfectly to tech careers: running hard just to stay in place
Developers should prioritize foundational knowledge over chasing the latest frameworks
money_quotes: One of the peculiarities of software development is how rapidly knowledge becomes obsolete.
It takes all the running you can do, to keep in the same place.
Immersing yourself in new technologies is merely running as fast as you can to stay in the same place.
To get somewhere else, you must run twice as fast.
That means studying the topics that won't be obsolete in five years: human factors and design.
AJAX and Atlas are the hot topics du jour here at MIX06, but will we be using them in five years? Unlikely.
If you haven't read the top 5 books on that list, ask yourself – am I too busy running as fast as I can?
posts.key_insight: To truly advance as a developer, invest in timeless knowledge like human factors and design rather than constantly chasing technologies that will be obsolete in five years.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the growing problem of managing dozens of online logins with unique passwords, arguing that current solutions like fingerprint readers and password managers only add complexity rather than solving the underlying issue. He demonstrates that biometric authentication, particularly fingerprint readers, can be easily defeated with simple materials like gelatin molds or graphite powder. The post emphasizes that true security requires multiple factors—what you are, what you know, and what you have—and that relying on any single method is inherently insecure. Atwood expresses hope that Microsoft's InfoCard initiative might offer a more viable solution to the authentication problem.
key_points: Complex password management setups involving hardware, software, and synchronization services create bigger problems than they solve
Fingerprint readers are trivially defeated by gummi fingertips and other low-tech methods, accepting fake prints over 50% of the time
All biometric systems (fingerprint, iris, facial recognition) have significant vulnerabilities exploitable with common materials
Effective security requires combining multiple factors: something you are, something you know, and something you have
Switching from passwords to biometrics alone merely trades one set of insecurities for another
money_quotes: I have fifty online logins, and I can't remember any of them.

When you need two programs, a bit of hardware, your finger, and a file-sharing web service to solve a problem, you don't really have a solution for the login explosion. What you have is an even bigger problem.
The jelly thumb was ugly and bubble-y, but the scanner loved it. It thought the jelly finger was a real one more than 50% of the time.
Making a gummi fingertip is not difficult, and all current fingerprint readers are fooled by even marginal gummi fingertips a hundred percent of the time.
The real problem isn't the biometrics. The real problem is relying on a single method of security.
Switching from only using passwords to only using fingerprints is simply trading one set of insecurities for another.
Until InfoCard arrives, I guess I'll be clicking the 'Having trouble logging in' link. Again.
posts.key_insight: Adding more technology to solve authentication problems often creates greater complexity without improving security, because true security requires multiple independent factors rather than increasingly sophisticated single-factor solutions.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares a Gamasutra article about an experimental game development project where four grad students created rapid prototypes under strict constraints: each game made in under seven days, by one person, around a common theme. The project gained significant attention from the gaming industry and academia. Atwood notes that while few developers get to prototype games, the underlying principles of embracing failure and using short development cycles apply broadly to all software development.
key_points: Constraints breed creativity: limiting time to seven days and team size to one person forced focused, rapid iteration
The project's success attracted attention from industry professionals and academics wanting to replicate the approach
Rapid prototyping requires a specific mindset that can be learned and applied
The principles of game prototyping (embrace failure, short cycles) transfer to general software development
money_quotes: Each game must be made in less than seven days
Each game must be made by exactly one person
We will show you how to slip into a rapid prototyping state of mind
Few of us have the luxury of prototyping games, but the principles they outline (embrace failure, use short cycles, etc.) are broadly applicable to all software development
posts.key_insight: Artificial constraints like strict time limits and solo development can accelerate learning and innovation by forcing developers to embrace failure and iterate quickly.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines how adding 'just one more' UI element can dramatically increase user confusion, using Windows Live Local's two-textbox search as his primary example. He observes that virtually everyone he knows makes the same mistake of typing an address in the wrong box, despite the labels being visible. Through a series of stopwatch examples, he illustrates how complexity grows exponentially with each added control. The post concludes that while it's possible to add interface elements without adding confusion, it requires exceptional care to avoid breaking the user's mental model.
key_points: Users won't read labels or instructions on screen, even 'professional computer users'
Two of anything can be one too many when it introduces ambiguity about which to use
Interface complexity grows exponentially, not linearly, with each added element
Visual design choices like color coding can add elements without adding confusion
The simplest interface (one button) may be overloaded but at least prevents confusion
money_quotes: There's simply one textbox too many on that form.
It may seem irrational to declare that two of anything is one too many.
Users won't read anything you put on the screen, even so-called professional computer users like ourselves.
Everyone I know makes this mistake when using Windows Live Local search for the first time.
I don't even know where to begin. The complexity just went up exponentially.
If you have to add 'just one more...' of any UI element, be sure that you're not adding the one UI element that breaks the camel's back.
posts.key_insight: Every additional UI element risks being the one that tips an interface from usable to confusing, because complexity grows exponentially and users refuse to read instructions.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood defends the practice of repetition in communication and learning, arguing that most people don't fully absorb a message the first time they hear it. He distinguishes between mechanical repetition (simply restating the same sentence) and meaningful repetition that covers topics from different angles and perspectives. Drawing parallels to software development, he reframes the common criticism that developers repeatedly solve the same problems as valuable practice of fundamentals. He cites examples from Seth Godin on communication and expert performers who continuously revisit basics to maintain their edge.
key_points: At any given time, only a fraction of listeners truly hear your message, making strategic repetition necessary for effective communication
Mechanical repetition is useless; covering the same topic from different viewpoints and with varied resources enhances understanding
Expert performers distinguish themselves by continuously practicing fundamentals, even concepts they mastered long ago
The paradox of communication: long-form content gets skipped, but ideas need repetition to land, requiring multiple short exposures over time
Re-reading foundational texts like Code Complete annually is a form of productive fundamental practice
money_quotes: At any given time, only a fraction of the people listening to you will really hear what you're saying.
If you repeat your message a few times in different ways, you increase the odds of getting your message across.
Mechanical repetition isn't helpful. It doesn't enhance understanding.
The paradox is that the long stuff gets skipped. The long stuff gets ignored. Short books sell better, short commercials get more viewers. So repetition becomes essential.
They keep practicing the fundamentals.
I absolutely love practicing the fundamentals. I can never get enough of Hello World.
We're often accused, as software developers, of writing solutions to the same problem over and over. I don't see this as repetition, but practicing the fundamentals.
posts.key_insight: Effective communication and mastery both require strategic repetition—not mindless parroting, but revisiting core ideas from different angles until they truly sink in.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits the book 'About Face' and highlights its framework for building considerate software—applications that treat users with the same respect and helpfulness a thoughtful human assistant would provide. The post enumerates thirteen characteristics of considerate software, from remembering user preferences to knowing when to bend rigid rules. Atwood argues that most applications fail even basic usability criteria because programmers build for the computer's needs rather than human needs. The concept of 'fudgeability'—allowing intermediate states and out-of-sequence actions—is identified as a key casualty when manual systems become digital. The post serves as both a book recommendation and a manifesto for more humane software design.
key_points: Software should remember information and preferences rather than repeatedly asking users for the same data
Applications must be deferential—they can suggest and warn, but should ultimately do what users ask without passing judgment
Error handling should take responsibility rather than leaving users 'holding the bag' when things go wrong
The concept of 'fudgeability' from manual systems—allowing incomplete or out-of-sequence actions—is crucial but typically eliminated in digital systems
Considerate software uses idle time productively, anticipates user needs, and provides related information proactively
money_quotes: Not only is the program better suited to remembering than the human, the program is also inconsiderate when, acting as a supposedly helpful tool, it forgets.
Software is within its rights to express its opinion that we are making a mistake, but it is being presumptuous when it judges our actions.

The program not only isn't as capable as a human assistant who saw the name conflict and appropriately renamed the contracts, it isn't even as capable as a human assistant who put the two contracts in the same folder. It is stupider than a complete idiot.
Software whines at us with error messages, interrupts us with confirmation dialog boxes, and brags to us with unnecessary notifications.
Are you sure? Are you really sure? Are you really, really sure?

The characteristic of manual systems that let humans perform actions out of sequence or before prerequisites are satisfied is called fudgeability. It is one of the first casualties when systems are computerized.
There are strong human needs to be able to bend the system slightly.
posts.key_insight: Software should embody the helpfulness and flexibility of a considerate human assistant rather than rigidly enforcing the computer's implementation model at the expense of human needs.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his experience transitioning from a large AnthroCart computer desk to something smaller after moving to a small Bay Area home. He discusses how his professional-grade desk failed the 'Wife Acceptance Factor' due to its size, color, and office-like aesthetics. He settles on the IKEA Jerker as a budget-friendly alternative and notes the desk has developed a cult following online. The post is a casual invitation for readers to share their own desk recommendations.
key_points: Professional computer furniture often conflicts with home aesthetics and space constraints
The 'Wife Acceptance Factor' is a real consideration when choosing home office equipment
Bay Area housing costs force compromises on workspace size
The IKEA Jerker desk has inspired a devoted community of fans
Good enough solutions at $99 can replace premium options when circumstances change
money_quotes: And it's a fantastic computer desk. But its Wife Acceptance Factor (WAF) is abysmal for a few reasons.
Plus, we just bought a small home in the Bay Area, and we need to optimize for space.
There's something about the Jerker that inspires people: Behold! The shrine to the IKEA Jerker desk!
Although I really do like the Jerker – and the name alone is worth the 99 bucks – I'm still researching my options.
posts.key_insight: The best computer desk isn't necessarily the most feature-rich one, but the one that fits your actual living situation and keeps domestic peace.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques both the concept of 'Rock Star Programmers' and the common advice to improve by studying others' code. He agrees with Tom's criticism that the rock star metaphor is absurd since programmers don't get the glamorous perks that actual rock stars enjoy. However, Atwood pushes back on Tom's alternative advice that reading good code is the key to improvement. Instead, Atwood argues that active practice—writing your own code and making your own mistakes—is far more effective than passive study.
key_points: The 'Rock Star Programmer' label is meaningless because programmers don't receive any of the actual benefits rock stars get
Passively studying other people's code won't make you a better programmer, just as reading books alone won't make you a better writer
The best way to improve at programming is through active practice—writing your own code
Making your own mistakes on your own terms teaches faster than any other method
Studying code is helpful but should supplement, not replace, actually writing code
money_quotes: There is no such thing as a 'Rock Star Programmer,' so if you want to become one, you already have problems that reading a blog post can't fix.
Rock stars get sex, drugs, parties, limousines, fame, glory, dates with supermodels, and Rolling Stone covers. Good programmers get... uh... fewer compiler errors.
You won't become a better programmer by passively studying other people's code.
You don't magically become a better writer by reading a lot of books. You become a better writer by... wait for it... writing.
The absolute best way to improve is to write your own damn code!
Nothing teaches faster than making your own mistakes, on your own time, on your own terms.
posts.key_insight: You become a better programmer by writing code and making your own mistakes, not by passively studying others' work or chasing mythical 'rock star' status.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that 'SomethingManager' is one of the worst naming patterns in software development because it conveys nothing meaningful about what a class actually does. Drawing on Alan Green's critique, he explains that 'manager' has so many definitions that it essentially means 'looks after stuff vaguely.' Atwood then shares Steve McConnell's guidelines for better naming: describe everything the routine does, avoid vague verbs, make names as long as necessary, and establish consistent conventions. He concludes that naming is genuinely hard work, but that difficulty is appropriate because good names capture essential meaning. The quality of names can make the difference between code people dread and code they want to work on.
key_points: The word 'Manager' in class names is semantically empty—it could mean pooling, manipulating, auditing, or any vague 'looking after' of objects
If describing what a routine does makes the name ridiculously long, the problem isn't the name—it's that your routine does too much
The optimum variable name length is 9-15 characters; routines deserve longer names because they're more complex
Naming is never truly finished because code evolves, so names should change over time as understanding deepens
Inconsistent conventions for common operations (like getting an ID) create unnecessary cognitive load
money_quotes: There's nothing more ambiguous than a SomethingManager.
If that makes the name ridiculously long, the name isn't the problem. Your routine is.
Be specific. What does it do? If you can't answer that question succinctly, it may be time to refactor the code until you can.
Creating good names is hard, but it should be hard, because a great name captures essential meaning in just one or two words.
It's difficult to tell what something should be named until you're completely finished writing it.
Succinct, descriptive variable, object, and function names can make the difference between Daily WTF code and… well, code you'd actually want to work on.
posts.key_insight: Vague names like 'SomethingManager' are a code smell—if you can't name something precisely, you probably don't understand what it does well enough yet.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques the poor out-of-box experiences created when companies lose control of their user experience to third parties. He contrasts the iRiver H10's alarming orange 'STOP! Having Trouble?' warning flyer and Lenovo's bloatware-laden first boot with Apple's tightly controlled iPod experience. The post argues that Apple's success stems from owning the entire stack—device, software, and content licensing. Atwood concludes that third parties will inevitably degrade user experience if given the opportunity, praising Microsoft's Windows Media Center team for maintaining tight control over their core UI.
key_points: First impressions matter enormously—a warning flyer as the first thing users see sets a negative tone for the entire product relationship
Apple's competitive advantage comes from controlling the complete experience: hardware, software, and content licensing
Pre-installed bloatware on new PCs creates an 'abysmal software experience' that masks otherwise excellent hardware
Third parties will consistently prioritize their own interests over user experience when given access
Tightly controlling the core user experience while allowing external add-ins is a viable middle ground
money_quotes: Do you really want your user's out of box experience to be a bright orange warning sign that screams STOP! and Having Trouble?
That negative first impression sets the tone for the rest of the device's life. It's all downhill from there.
Nobody can screw that up behind Apple's back.
The operating system is loaded down with so many pre-installed craplets that the taskbar takes up half the screen.
The hardware is excellent, but you'd never know that based on the abysmal software experience you're about to have.
If I've learned anything about computers in the last 35 years, it's this: if you leave the user experience up to third parties, they will screw it up behind your back. Royally. Every single time.
posts.key_insight: Allowing third parties to influence your core user experience guarantees they will degrade it in service of their own interests—the only defense is maintaining complete control over the experience stack.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents a frustrating Windows XP hibernation bug that affects systems with 1GB+ of RAM, where the OS fails to hibernate due to insufficient contiguous memory. The post explains the technical cause (the Windows kernel power manager needs a proportionally larger block of contiguous memory as RAM increases) and provides a link to Microsoft's hotfix. Atwood criticizes Microsoft's policy of requiring users to call support to obtain necessary patches, noting that community members have thankfully mirrored the fix. He also reveals his own workaround limitations, stuck using hibernate instead of sleep due to motherboard quirks with his overclocked CPU.
key_points: Windows XP SP2 systems with 1GB+ RAM can randomly fail to hibernate due to memory fragmentation issues
The failure is particularly dangerous on laptops where users might bag a machine they believe is hibernating but is actually still running
Microsoft's hotfix exists but requires calling support to obtain it, which Atwood finds ridiculous
The bug's intermittent nature makes it harder to diagnose and more frustrating when it does occur
Even power users face cascading hardware/software compromises (Atwood can't use sleep due to overclocking issues)
money_quotes: You can imagine how catastrophic this could be on a laptop; you'd be putting a laptop in your bag that was still fully on!
The busywork exercise of calling Microsoft support to obtain a necessary hotfix. What a ridiculous policy.
I would use sleep, but the motherboard I use isn't smart enough to restore the correct overclocked CPU speed.
My system fails to hibernate after I've already initiated the hibernation process and walked away from it.
posts.key_insight: Essential bug fixes shouldn't be hidden behind support call barriers when the community is already sharing workarounds to bypass corporate gatekeeping.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the concept of balance as a unifying principle in programming, drawing from a 1989 interview with dBase creator Wayne Ratliff. Ratliff describes how balanced code should be visually uniform, with modules of similar size and weight, and compares programming to sculpting clay through iterative refinement. Atwood extends this to argue that balance encompasses the tradeoffs between complexity and simplicity that programmers constantly navigate. He connects code aesthetics to maintainability, suggesting that attention to internal code layout reflects broader attention to quality. The post concludes that well-balanced code leads to better applications because internal discipline manifests in external quality.
key_points: Balance in code encompasses visual layout, module size, logical structure, and the constant tradeoff between complexity and simplicity
Programming is like sculpting clay - iterative refinement through adding, removing, and restructuring until the code feels right
When code feels 'out of whack,' it often indicates an underlying design fault that needs rethinking
Code should be visually scannable - you should understand the general flow even without reading the actual characters
Internal code aesthetics, while invisible to users, correlate with external application quality
money_quotes: I like to make an analogy between writing code and sculpting a clay figure. You start with a lump of clay and then you scrape away, add more clay, then scrape away again.
When you have a good balance, it's as if you've discovered some basic physical underlying principle and implemented it.
You should be able to explain any module in one sentence, and things should be in alphabetical order, if possible.
When things get really out of balance, you know something is wrong. There's probably some inherent fault that makes it out of balance.
You should be able to understand the general flow of the code even if you can't read any of it.
If you're structuring your code to be accurate, consistent, readable, and maintainable, your application will work better – because it's balanced.
I think you could sum up reams of programming advice with that one concept: balance.
posts.key_insight: Balance is the unifying principle of good code - when modules are uniform in size and weight, when visual layout aids comprehension, and when complexity and simplicity are in equilibrium, you've likely discovered a sound underlying design.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that traditional menu and toolbar interfaces have reached their scaling limits, using an extreme Firefox extension screenshot and Office 2003's bloat as evidence. He references the observation that while these UI paradigms served applications well initially, they become unwieldy as features accumulate. The post suggests that web applications, which avoid these conventions entirely, point toward a better approach. Atwood concludes by questioning whether menus and toolbars are appropriate even for small applications anymore.
key_points: Traditional menus and toolbars worked well historically but fail to scale as applications grow in complexity
Office 2003 and heavily-extended Firefox demonstrate the breaking point of the menu/toolbar paradigm
Web applications succeed partly by avoiding drop-down menus and draggable toolbar icons entirely
The scaling problem isn't about bad design or unintelligent creators—it's an inherent limitation of the paradigm
Even small applications may no longer benefit from traditional menu and toolbar interfaces
money_quotes: Between this and the Office 2003 debacle, it's patently obvious that the whole menu-and-toolbar paradigm doesn't scale. At all.
There's a point beyond which menus and toolbars cease to scale well.
A flat menu with 8 well-organized commands on it works just great; a three-level hierarchical menu containing 35 loosely-related commands can be a bit of a disaster.
I'm now starting to question whether traditional menus and toolbars are even appropriate for small applications any more.
Web applications tend to be small by design – and you never see drop-down menus or tiny 16x16 draggable toolbar icons. And they're better without these things.
posts.key_insight: The menu and toolbar UI paradigm that defined desktop software has fundamental scaling limits that web applications avoid by design, suggesting these conventions may be obsolete even for simple applications.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the frustrating lack of standardization in consumer electronics power connectors, noting that while the 3-prong computer power connector has been a rare success, the proliferation of incompatible 2-pin connectors creates chaos for consumers. He criticizes Dell for introducing non-standard rounded power connectors despite building their business on PC standardization. The post discusses third-party universal power solutions as a workaround and highlights Jef Raskin's proposal for hermaphroditic connectors that could simplify the connector ecosystem. Atwood concludes pessimistically that true standardization is unlikely to happen.
key_points: The standard 3-pin computer power connector is one of few successful standardization examples in an otherwise chaotic connector landscape
Dell introducing non-standard power connectors contradicts their core business model built on PC standardization
The proliferation of different 2-pin power cords across devices creates significant consumer frustration and practical problems
Hermaphroditic connectors could dramatically simplify the connector ecosystem by reducing the number of required part types
Third-party universal power solutions exist but depend on having the correct interchangeable tips available
money_quotes: It's one of the few bright spots in an otherwise hopeless morass of incompatible consumer electronics connectors.
Given the three zillion different 2-pin power cords out there, it's a miracle that the other end of the cable is standardized.
How did we screw that up?
The best solution would be for manufacturers to standardize on a common set of power connectors. I won't be holding my breath until that happens.
I still have about a fifty percent chance of plugging a USB cable in right side up on any given day.
posts.key_insight: The chaos of incompatible power connectors represents a broader failure of the consumer electronics industry to prioritize user experience over proprietary designs.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques the 'Teach Yourself X in 24 Hours' book title convention, arguing that while it's marketing hype, it demeans the software development profession. He references Peter Norvig's famous essay on learning programming over ten years and uses the 'Seven Minute Abs' scene from There's Something About Mary to illustrate the absurdity of competitive time claims. Atwood acknowledges the author Scott Mitchell is talented but places blame on publishers for the misleading titles. His core argument is that such titles suggest programming is trivially easy to master, which misrepresents the craft to beginners and cheapens the profession.
key_points: The '24 hours' book title convention is marketing hype that implies software development is shallow enough to master quickly
Computer books disproportionately use time-based learning claims compared to other fields like music or physics
Such titles do a disservice to readers by setting unrealistic expectations about what learning to program actually requires
Software development is a craft that requires years of practice, not a mechanical skill learned in a time window
The publishers, not authors, are responsible for these misleading title conventions
money_quotes: If you can truly learn ASP.NET in 24 hours, what's to stop you from learning it in 23 hours? Or 20 hours? or 12 hours?
It implies that the field of software development is so shallow that a complete beginner can become competent in 24 hours.
Books with titles like Teach Yourself ASP.NET 2.0 in 24 Hours cheapen our craft.
There are no books on how to learn Beethoven, or Quantum Physics, or even Dog Grooming in a few days.

Any computer book with a length of time in the title is doing its readers a disservice by demoting software development from a craft you spend your life practicing to a mechanical activity that can be learned in a limited time window.
posts.key_insight: Software development is a lifelong craft, not a skill that can be meaningfully acquired in hours or days, and marketing that suggests otherwise cheapens the profession and misleads beginners.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains how to block bandwidth theft from hotlinking by using URL rewriting to check HTTP referer headers. After noticing his images being embedded on MySpace pages without permission, he implemented an ISAPI rewrite filter on IIS that serves a 'stop stealing bandwidth' placeholder image to unauthorized requests. The solution whitelists legitimate referrers while blocking others, all handled at the ISAPI level for universal URL coverage. He notes this functionality is standard in Apache but requires third-party tools for IIS 6, though IIS 7 promises native support.
key_points: Hotlinking steals bandwidth by serving images from your server to someone else's visitors
The HTTP referer header can be checked to verify if image requests come from authorized sources
URL rewriting rules can redirect unauthorized requests to a placeholder image or 404
Apache has native URL rewriting support while IIS 6 requires third-party ISAPI filters
Proper implementation should whitelist known domains and allow blank referers
money_quotes: I like to periodically watch the HTTP traffic on my server. I can see what I'm actually serving up over the wire, and how much bandwidth I'm using.
Every time I visit myspace, I feel a little bit stupider.
I have no idea why myspace is so popular. I guess the best I can hope for is that those damn kids stay off my lawn.
Take that, 26 zillion myspace users.
Unlike the cheesy ASP.NET 'URL rewriting' solutions, it also works on generic URLs, not just URLs that end in .aspx or some other extension.
posts.tone: sardonic, practical, tutorial-style with humorous tangents
posts.key_insight: Monitoring your server traffic reveals abuse you didn't know was happening, and URL rewriting provides an elegant solution to block bandwidth theft at the HTTP level.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that 'best practices' in software development are often driven by fear and ego rather than genuine expertise. Drawing on James Bach's critique, he suggests that developers inflate their recommendations into authoritative-sounding rules because they're afraid of being exposed as knowing less than they claim. The post warns against accepting prescriptive advice uncritically, noting that fast talkers often win arguments simply by sounding confident. Atwood concludes that honesty and humility about our limitations is more valuable than puffing up our approaches into intimidating doctrines.
key_points: The phrase 'best practice' should trigger immediate skepticism—it marks the transition from advice to evangelism
Fear of being exposed as impostors drives developers to inflate their approaches into authoritative-sounding rules
Fast talkers win meetings because people assume ideas that sound smart probably are smart
Developers attach their egos to their code frameworks, treating disagreement as personal attacks
Honesty about not having all the answers is difficult but necessary to avoid empty posturing
money_quotes: The very utterance of the words 'best practice' should set off warning klaxons, sirens, and flashing red lights.
At that moment, you've left the realm of opinion and advice. You've entered the realm of evangelism and true believers.
Too many of us assume that if it sounds smart, it probably is, especially when we aren't given the chance to think about it.
Like puffer fish, many of us feel that we need to huff ourselves up so that predators won't devour us. We fluff ourselves full of impressive words.
We're constantly living in fear that people will find out how little we know.
If someone doesn't agree with our approach, they're attacking us personally.
Being honest and humble is difficult – it's painful to admit that you don't have the answer.
Always avoid the empty trap of the puffer fish.
posts.key_insight: Best practices are often fear-driven ego protection masquerading as expertise, and intellectual honesty requires admitting the limits of our knowledge rather than inflating our approaches into intimidating doctrine.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood measures the actual power consumption of various laptop components using a Kill-a-Watt meter on his Dell Inspiron 300m ultraportable. Through systematic testing, he isolates the power draw of the LCD screen, hard drive, WiFi, CPU, and integrated graphics. His findings reveal that the CPU is the dominant power consumer, while hard drive sleep mode provides negligible savings. The post concludes with practical advice for maximizing battery life, particularly emphasizing screen brightness as an often-overlooked factor.
key_points: The CPU consumes far more power than any other laptop component, jumping from 15W idle to 26W under full load
Screen brightness accounts for roughly 25% of total idle power consumption (4W difference between min and max)
Hard drive sleep mode saves only 1 watt, making it essentially worthless as a power-saving measure
WiFi uses minimal power when idle but can add 3-4W during active data transfer
Integrated graphics add only about 3 watts even under 3D rendering load
money_quotes: The CPU is, by far, the biggest consumer of power in a laptop.
Putting your hard drive to sleep isn't worth it. Ever. You save a whopping... single watt. Why bother?
For maximum battery life, dim your screen.
I was surprised that the screen alone accounted for 25% of the total power draw of my laptop at idle.
You can moderate use of WiFi or your hard drive, but you can't exactly moderate the use of your screen. Plan accordingly.
If you want to drain your battery lickety-split, run a bunch of apps that peg your CPU at 100 percent.
It's extreme, all right. Extremely crappy.
posts.key_insight: Actual measurements trump assumptions—hard drive sleep modes are practically useless while screen brightness is a surprisingly significant and controllable factor in laptop battery life.

posts.summary: This brief post highlights lesser-known keyboard shortcuts for text editing that work universally across textboxes, including basic HTML forms. Jeff Atwood points out that while everyone knows arrow key navigation, many developers and users are unaware of the additional editing shortcuts available to them. The post uses visual aids (images) to demonstrate these shortcuts, emphasizing their universal applicability across web pages and operating system applications.
key_points: Standard keyboard shortcuts for text editing work universally across all textboxes, not just rich text editors
Many users only know basic arrow key navigation and miss out on more powerful editing shortcuts
These shortcuts work in vanilla HTML forms, including simple interfaces like Wikipedia's edit box
The shortcuts are consistent across web pages and native OS applications
money_quotes: Everyone knows how to use the arrow keys to navigate within textboxes. But not many people know there are a slew of handy keyboard shortcuts for editing text in textboxes.
And these keyboard shortcuts work everywhere, even in the most basic input areas – including vanilla HTML forms, such as the Wikipedia edit box.
All of these shortcuts work in any standard textbox, like the ones on every web page and in most apps for your operating system.
posts.key_insight: Universal text editing keyboard shortcuts are a hidden productivity multiplier that most users never discover despite being available in every standard textbox.

posts.summary: Atwood critiques the rhetorical fallacy of justifying tools or practices by saying they're 'better than nothing.' Using a quote about UML from James Bach, he argues that this comparison is inherently invalid because alternatives always exist. The real comparison should be between something and other viable options, not between something and a void. He applies this to modeling practices, arguing that code itself is a legitimate modeling tool, and praises Visual Studio's accessible class modeling features as a practical alternative to formal UML.
key_points: The phrase 'it's better than nothing' is a logical fallacy that shuts down consideration of actual alternatives
Alternatives to formal modeling tools like UML include mental models, pencil sketches, and code itself
Code is a perfectly reasonable place to model software
Accessible, informal tools are more likely to be adopted than heavyweight formal methods
The real competition for any tool is not 'nothing' but other approaches people might use instead
money_quotes: Our biggest competitor is silence.
A comparison is offered between something and nothing. Who could prefer nothing? Nothing is a void.
The comparison that matters is not between something and nothing, it's probably between something and some other thing.
Any comparison that ends in 'It's better than nothing' is an invalid one.
There are always alternatives, whether the person speaking chooses to acknowledge them or not.
Code is a perfectly reasonable place to model.
posts.key_insight: When someone defends a tool or practice by saying it's 'better than nothing,' they're avoiding the real question of whether better alternatives exist.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood addresses the minor annoyance of login prompts in virtual machine images, which typically don't need real security. He notes that sharing VM images requires communicating login credentials, adding unnecessary friction. While some developers use a workaround of setting the login background to display credentials, Atwood recommends Microsoft's Tweak UI PowerToy, which enables automatic login with no typing required.
key_points: Virtual machines rarely need the security that login prompts provide
Sharing VM images means also sharing login credentials, which is cumbersome
Some use background images containing credentials as a workaround
Microsoft's Tweak UI PowerToy offers a cleaner autologon feature
money_quotes: Virtual machine images typically don't need much security, so the login prompt is more of a formality than anything else.
I don't know why you would bother with the 'bitmap login background' method, as the autologin method is so much cleaner.
posts.key_insight: When security is unnecessary, remove the friction entirely rather than working around it.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the ongoing tension within Wikipedia between 'inclusionists' who want to preserve all human knowledge and 'deletionists' who want to curate only notable content. He references Jason Scott's talk at Notacon 3, which compared this debate to contentious issues like abortion and gun control. While Atwood personally leans toward the deletionist view, he acknowledges there's no objective way to determine notability. He concludes that inclusionists will inevitably win due to ever-decreasing costs of storage and bandwidth, and suggests this outcome may actually be fine since obscure content can simply be found via search rather than cluttering main navigation.
key_points: Wikipedia's community is deeply divided between inclusionists (keep everything) and deletionists (curate for notability)
The debate over what counts as 'notable' has no rational, objective solution
Technology trends favor inclusionists as storage, bandwidth, and computing costs continue to drop
The 'long tail' of micro-content doesn't need prominent placement—search makes it accessible without cluttering the main experience
Even seemingly trivial entries like obscure comic book characters represent the 'sum of human knowledge' to someone
money_quotes: The inclusionist versus deletionist debate [within Wikipedia] is as firm and strong as the abortion debate, gun control debate, or the death penalty debate.
Inclusionism says, Wikipedia, because it is a virtual encyclopedia, is capable of carrying the sum of human knowledge.
The deletionists take the attitude [that] Wikipedia is not a junkyard.
There's no rational way to decide what's 'notable.'
The inclusionists – and the ever-spiraling increase of storage, bandwidth, and CPU power – will win by default.
The long tail of micro-content doesn't need to appear in any massive table of contents; it's only a few search keywords away.
posts.key_insight: When there's no objective standard for notability and technology keeps getting cheaper, the path of least resistance is to keep everything and let search handle discoverability.

posts.summary: Atwood explores why UML never achieved the universal adoption that circuit diagrams enjoy in electrical engineering. He argues the fundamental difference is that software isn't constrained by physical laws—developers can change the rules at will, making formal diagramming less essential. Drawing on Fred Brooks' famous passage about the joys of programming, Atwood emphasizes that software's 'tractable medium' is both its greatest strength and the reason standardized visual languages struggle to take hold. He concludes that while UML has its uses, it will never be as indispensable to software engineers as circuit diagrams are to electrical engineers.
key_points: Circuit diagrams succeed as a universal language because electricity follows immutable physical laws, while software follows rules we invented and constantly change
Software development's flexibility—the ability to simply 'change the speed of light'—makes formal diagramming less necessary and less stable
Even with physical constraints, circuit diagrams are still idealized representations that require real-world compromises when implemented
Brooks' observation that programmers work in a 'tractable medium' of pure thought-stuff explains both the joy of programming and why visual standards struggle
money_quotes: Software, unlike electricity, isn't subject to God's laws.
If we decide the speed of light is not to our liking, we just change it.
The programmer, like the poet, works only slightly removed from pure thought-stuff. He builds his castles in the air, from air, creating by exertion of the imagination.
One types the correct incantation on a keyboard, and a display screen comes to life, showing things that never were nor could be.
Imagine the difficulty an electrical engineer would have working on your circuit diagram if, on that diagram, you had changed something fundamental, like the conductivity of copper.
Software development is far less amenable to formal diagrams because, well, it's something we just made up.
posts.key_insight: Software's freedom from physical constraints—the very quality that makes programming creative and joyful—is precisely why standardized visual languages like UML will never achieve the universality of circuit diagrams.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that technical skills alone are insufficient for career advancement in IT. Drawing from Rajesh Setty's advice, he explains how people naturally 'box' each other into simple categories when networking, but those who do something remarkable escape this categorization. Building a personal brand through public contributions like blogging, open-source projects, or speaking creates lasting career value that exceeds pure technical competence. Atwood acknowledges his own branding strategy with the Wumpus mascot, realizing it may be more strategic than he initially joked.
key_points: Technical competence is merely the minimum to stay employed—it won't distinguish you
People naturally categorize ('box') others into simple labels during networking; remarkable work breaks through this
Public contributions like blogs, open-source projects, books, and conference talks build personal brand
Personal branding advances careers more than technical skills alone
Any public activity that advances your skills contributes to your brand
money_quotes: Mere competence in a technical discipline is not enough. That's the minimum required to keep your head above water.
You need to be working on something that is remarkable or be remarkable yourself. In other words, you need to be working on your 'personal brand.'
As long as it's public, and it advances your skills, you're creating a personal brand. And that will help your career far more than technical chops ever will.
It's a 'boxing' contest.
Now I'm not so sure if it's a joke – or an actual branding strategy.
posts.key_insight: Public, remarkable work that builds your personal brand will advance your career far more than technical skills alone.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood draws a parallel between house painting and software development, arguing that most failures come not from lack of skill but from ignoring basic, well-documented practices. He observes that common painting mistakes are all about preparation and process, not technical proficiency. This insight extends to software: the industry now has established 'paint cans' like Joel Spolsky's 12-step test and various methodologies. The key to quality isn't becoming a virtuoso—it's simply following proven instructions consistently.
key_points: The most common mistakes in painting (and by extension, software) stem from ignoring basic instructions, not from lack of skill
Technical proficiency matters far less than following established processes and best practices
Software development has matured to the point where we have reliable 'paint cans' to follow—methodologies like XP, Scrum, and checklists like the Joel Test
Quality is achievable by anyone willing to follow documented practices, regardless of their skill level
The specific methodology you choose matters less than actually following one consistently
money_quotes: The single most common mistake in any project is failure to read and follow manufacturer's instructions for tools and materials being used.
None of the mistakes on this checklist have anything to do with my skill as a painter.

To guarantee a reasonable level of quality, you don't have to spend weeks practicing your painting skills. You don't even have to be a good painter. All you have to do is follow the instructions on the paint can!
For years, software development was an entire subdivision of badly painted houses.

The type of paint can you choose – and the instructions you follow – are highly debatable, of course. But make sure, at the very least, you're following the instructions on the paint can for your software development project.
posts.key_insight: Software quality comes less from individual brilliance than from consistently following established best practices—the real failure is ignoring the instructions we already have.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood discusses the transformative impact of Windows Remote Desktop on his workflow, praising its ubiquity and performance. He explains the special keyboard shortcuts needed when working in remote sessions, since standard Windows key combinations behave differently. The post provides practical tips for power users, including how to run remote desktop sessions on specific monitors in multi-monitor setups and how to optimize performance over slow connections by reducing color depth. Atwood positions Remote Desktop as so responsive that physically sitting at a computer seems almost obsolete.
key_points: Remote Desktop's inclusion as a default Windows component since XP/2000 Server made it truly ubiquitous and drove widespread adoption
Standard Windows keyboard shortcuts don't work as expected in Remote Desktop sessions, requiring knowledge of special Alt-based key combinations
Multi-monitor users can target a specific monitor for full-screen remote sessions through a non-obvious but persistent workaround
Color depth is the single biggest factor affecting Remote Desktop performance over slow connections, with 15-bit being a good compromise
Remote Desktop outperforms all other remote control tools the author has tested
money_quotes: It's so responsive that it almost makes the idea of physically sitting in front of a computer seem quaint.
The fact that it shipped in the Windows XP box – and as a default component of all the server operating systems since Windows 2000 – has done wonders for its adoption. It's truly ubiquitous.
Before you laugh, guess who else was on the three monitor tip back in the day? Google's Larry Page. And Bill Gates.
Color depth is the single biggest contributor to performance over a slow connection.
You may be tempted to go to 16-bit color or even 24-bit color to make things look prettier, but remember all those additional bits have to be transmitted across the wire.
posts.key_insight: Remote Desktop's bundling with Windows created ubiquitous remote access that fundamentally changed how developers work, but mastering it requires learning its quirks around keyboard shortcuts, multi-monitor setups, and bandwidth optimization.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques Windows Vista's User Account Control (UAC) implementation, arguing that Microsoft's approach to security through constant warning dialogs is fundamentally flawed. The post highlights how the excessive number of security prompts trains users to mindlessly click through them, defeating the entire purpose of the security measure. Atwood points out the philosophical contradiction of logging in as an Administrator but being treated as a regular user. He argues that this paternalistic approach makes the system less secure, not more, because users become habituated to dismissing warnings. The post concludes with practical suggestions for how Microsoft could implement a more sensible security model following established conventions from Linux and Mac OS X.
key_points: UAC's constant warning dialogs train users to click through without reading, creating a 'boy who cried wolf' security problem
There's a fundamental contradiction in letting users log in as Administrator while treating them as regular users
The security model could be simpler: make new users plain Users by default and only show warnings when they upgrade to Administrator
Microsoft ignored established security conventions from Linux and Mac OS X that handle this problem more elegantly
The feature was so poorly designed that disabling it entirely became a common workaround
money_quotes: The problem with the Security Through Endless Warning Dialogs school of thought is that it doesn't work.
All those earnest warning dialogs eventually blend together into a giant 'click here to get work done' button that nobody bothers to read any more.
The operating system cries wolf so much that when a real wolf – in the form of a virus or malware – rolls around, you'll mindlessly allow it access to whatever it wants, just out of habit.
Why doesn't Vista respect my choice to be an Administrator? Who is really in control here: me, or my operating system?
There's something awfully paternalistic about an operating system that lets me log in as an Administrator, but treats me like a regular User.
If you're going to treat me like a User, at least have the decency to create a regular User account for me.
It could be so much simpler if Microsoft just followed the established conventions.
posts.key_insight: Security measures that annoy users into habitual dismissal are worse than no security at all, because they create a false sense of protection while training users to ignore genuine threats.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood highlights Larry Gonick's mathematical cartoon series originally published in Discover magazine, covering topics from neural nets to cryptography to game theory. He catalogs Gonick's extensive bibliography of cartoon guides spanning history, science, and technical subjects. Atwood distinguishes between shallow quick-learning gimmicks (like 'Learn X in 24 Hours' books) and genuinely effective visual learning through well-crafted cartoons. He endorses cartoon-based learning as a legitimate and effective educational approach for complex topics.
key_points: Larry Gonick has created an extensive catalog of cartoon-based educational content covering mathematics, science, history, and technical topics
Visual and cartoon-based learning can make complex subjects like cryptography, neural nets, and game theory more accessible
There's a meaningful difference between gimmicky 'learn fast' approaches and genuinely engaging educational formats
Fun and engagement are important factors in effective learning
money_quotes: Although I made fun of the Learn ASP.NET in 24 Hours meme, I am a big fan of learning subjects in cartoon form.
You learn more when you're having fun doing it.
He's famous for his easy to grasp, beautifully illustrated treatment of complex topics.
posts.key_insight: Cartoon-based learning isn't a shortcut or gimmick—it's a legitimately effective way to understand complex topics because engagement and enjoyment enhance retention.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on a key lesson from a Human Factors usability course: features that users can't find or understand are effectively worthless. He illustrates this with a striking example from Microsoft Office 2007, where users celebrated 'new' drawing tools that had actually existed since Office 97. The post argues that discoverability is as important as functionality, and that developers should prioritize usability over features.
key_points: No matter how powerful a feature is, it provides zero value if users can't find or understand it
Microsoft Office users thought drawing tools were new in Office 2007, despite them existing for ten years since Office 97
Poor discoverability led users to waste time with elaborate workarounds (creating drawings in PowerPoint and copying them to other apps)
Developers should establish priorities: usability first, features second
money_quotes: No matter how cool the feature may be, if users can't find it – or understand it – you're wasting your time.
Make sure you have your priorities in order before you start: usability first, feature second.
When is a ten year old feature suddenly a 'new' feature? When users can actually find it!
posts.key_insight: A feature that users cannot discover might as well not exist—discoverability is not optional, it's essential to a feature's value.

posts.summary: Atwood compares server hardware from 2000 to 2006, demonstrating that Moore's Law has made servers dramatically more powerful and cheaper. He builds a spec comparison showing a 2006 homebrew server delivers roughly 10x the performance across most metrics for one-third the price of a 2000-era Dell PowerEdge. The key insight is that this 'extravagant excess' of cheap computing power enables the Web 2.0 era by making high-level, interpreted languages like Ruby and Python viable for production web applications. While hardware costs have plummeted, Atwood notes that relative to human labor costs, the equation hasn't fundamentally changed—but the abundance of cheap power now allows developers to trade CPU cycles for programmer productivity.
key_points: Hardware became roughly 100x cheaper between 1995-2005 due to Moore's Law and Linux enabling generic Intel boxes instead of proprietary Sun hardware
A 2006 homebrew server delivers 10x network bandwidth, 8x memory, 16x disk space, and 10x CPU power compared to a 2000 Dell server at one-third the cost
The abundance of cheap server power makes previously impractical high-level interpreted languages viable for production web applications
While hardware got cheaper relative to itself, the cost relative to human labor hasn't changed as dramatically
Web 2.0's technical foundation is built on these 'whatever box' commodity servers rather than expensive proprietary hardware
money_quotes: In the 10 years between Excite and JotSpot, hardware has literally become 100X cheaper.
Hardware has become 100X cheaper. It's two factors – Moore's law and the rise of Linux as an operating system designed to run on generic hardware.
This extravagant excess of server power makes it possible to use labor-saving software that wasn't viable in the year 2000.

You can build your site on extremely high-level software, such as interpreted languages like Ruby and Python, and still scale across thousands of simultaneous user requests... even on a single 'whatever box' server.
The relative costs of software and hardware – relative to the cost of human labor, I mean – haven't changed that much in the last six years.
posts.key_insight: The dramatic drop in hardware costs enabled Web 2.0 by making it economically viable to trade cheap CPU cycles for expensive developer time through high-level interpreted languages.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that blogs without comments enabled fail to qualify as true blogs, comparing them to church pulpits where authors preach without allowing dialogue. He contends that requiring readers to start their own blogs to respond publicly is elitist, as not everyone has the time or inclination to become a blogger. While acknowledging that comments don't scale well and can attract spam, he maintains that most bloggers should enable comments and experiment rather than presume low quality. The post was sparked by his inability to respond publicly to James Bach's critique because Bach's blog had comments disabled.
key_points: Disabling comments creates an elitist barrier that requires readers to have their own blogs to participate in public discourse
Comments are analogous to letters to the editor - a fundamental part of public dialogue that shouldn't be presumed worthless before trying
The spam problem is solvable with CAPTCHAs and daily moderation, making it a weak excuse for disabling comments
Focused, niche blogs tend to attract higher quality comments than general interest sites covering divisive topics
Comments only become unmanageable at scale levels that very few bloggers actually reach
money_quotes: A blog without comments enabled is not a blog. It's more like a church pulpit.
Are you really comfortable saying, in effect, unless you have a blog I am not interested in what you have to say? Because I'm not.
It's unreasonable to expect people that disagree with the tenets of your religion to build a church and start their own religion.
To deny public conversation by disabling comments right out of the gate – based on the presumption that the comments will be of low quality – is, again, awfully elitist.
I've found that the comments can be the best, most informative part of a blog.
Not everyone has the time or inclination to become a full-bore blogger.
Comments don't scale worth a damn.
posts.key_insight: Disabling blog comments is an act of elitism that transforms public discourse into one-way preaching and excludes the vast majority of readers who lack the resources to respond through their own platforms.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that developers should use dashes instead of underscores when substituting for spaces in filenames and URLs. Spaces cause practical problems: they must be quoted at the command line and get encoded as %20 in URLs. While underscores seem like a natural substitute, Google treats underscored phrases as single words, hurting searchability. The regex word character class (\w) also includes underscores but not dashes, reinforcing that underscores join words while dashes separate them. Atwood concludes that following established conventions is wiser than inventing personal standards.
key_points: Spaces in filenames require quoting at command line and become %20 encoded in URLs, making them inconvenient
Google treats underscored terms as single words, so 'kw1_kw2' won't match searches for 'kw1' or 'kw2' individually
The regex \w operator considers underscore a word character but not dash, meaning 'this_is' is one word while 'this-is' is multiple
Following established conventions beats inventing personal standards, even when the convention isn't perfect
money_quotes: I try to avoid using spaces in filenames and URLs. They're great for human readability, but they're remarkably inconvenient in computer resource locators.
The correct character to substitute for space is the dash.
If you use an underscore '_' character, then Google will combine the two words on either side into one word.
`This_is_a_single_word`, but `this-is-multiple-words`.
Like NutraSweet and Splenda, neither is really an acceptable substitute for a space, but we might as well follow the established convention instead of inventing our own.
That's how we ended up with the backslash as a path separator.
posts.key_insight: Use dashes rather than underscores to separate words in filenames and URLs because search engines and regex conventions treat underscores as word joiners, not word separators.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood addresses the overwhelming feeling developers experience trying to keep up with constantly expanding technologies, frameworks, and information sources. He shares reassuring perspectives from SecretGeek and Kathy Sierra that validate the impossibility of staying current with everything. Using Java's API growth from 200 to 3500 classes as an example, he demonstrates that even platform creators can't master everything anymore. Rather than fighting this reality, Atwood advocates for 'Just In Time' learning—knowing enough about new technologies to recognize when you'll need them, then learning deeply only when solving a specific problem.
key_points: The explosion of technology has made it literally impossible for anyone to keep up with everything, even experts who create the platforms
Feeling behind or inadequate about technical knowledge is universal and shouldn't be a source of guilt
Information anxiety can be managed by aggressive curation: finding good aggregators, cutting redundancy, and unsubscribing ruthlessly
Just In Time learning is a pragmatic strategy: learn enough to know what exists and when you might need it, then go deep only when solving an actual problem
Most new technologies you encounter, you won't actually need—aligning with KISS and YAGNI principles
money_quotes: Do you ever feel like you're buried under umpteen zillion backlogged emails, feeds, books, articles, journals, magazines, and printouts?
You are beautiful just the way you are.
You're not keeping up. I'm not keeping up. And neither is anyone else. At least not in everything.
By the year 2000, it had become impossible for even a Sun Java engineer – someone creating the API – to be familiar with everything in the standard library.
I don't worry about keeping up with the Joneses; I focus on the specific problem at hand.
I take a 'Just In Time' attitude to learning new technology.
I can't possibly learn everything. But I do try to learn enough to know what the new thing is, and when I might need it.
posts.key_insight: Instead of drowning in guilt about not keeping up with every new technology, adopt a 'Just In Time' learning approach—maintain surface awareness of what exists, but only dive deep when solving a specific problem that requires it.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses the screwdriver analogy to examine why software development has so many overlapping tools and languages. He acknowledges that having multiple tools is reasonable since each serves specific scenarios, just as different screwdrivers have unique uses. However, he criticizes the industry's tendency to adopt new tools based on hype rather than genuine need. He highlights two common mistakes: believing new tools will magically boost productivity (the silver bullet fallacy), and abandoning good tools simply because they've become mainstream. The post concludes that adding new tools is fine, but developers should ensure they're doing so for substantive reasons rather than following trends.
key_points: Multiple tools serving similar purposes can be justified when each excels in specific scenarios
The software industry too eagerly abandons existing tools for new ones based on myth and wishful thinking
No programming language is a silver bullet—the biggest productivity gains came from moving away from machine code, and subsequent improvements yield diminishing returns
