Elite developers drive a self-perpetuating cycle of language adoption, fleeing to new languages once their current ones become too popular
New tools should be adopted for substantive technical reasons, not because they're the hot new thing
money_quotes: The tools constantly change, but yet they do the same thing, create code to run on machines.
Software developers are awfully eager to throw out existing tools for new ones.
Unfortunately, these decisions are often based on myth and wishful thinking, and the decisions are typically made in favor of whatever the hot new thing of the moment is.

It is, after all, just another high-level language, and the biggest payoff from such languages came from the first transition – the transition up from the accidental complexities of the machine into the more abstract statement of step-by-step solutions.
Once those accidents have been removed, the remaining ones will be smaller, and the payoff from their removal will surely be less.
Elite developers notice too many riff-raff using their current programming language, and start looking for something that will distinguish them better from their mediocre colleagues.
It's OK to add a new screwdriver to your toolkit every few years. But make sure you're adding it for the right reasons.
posts.key_insight: The proliferation of programming tools isn't inherently bad, but the industry's habit of chasing new technologies for status or hype rather than genuine improvement leads to wasted effort and perpetual churn.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that professional credentials should be measured by projects completed—especially failed ones—rather than certifications. He criticizes environments where every project is declared a success regardless of actual outcomes, noting this hurts users. Drawing on research about surgeons, he shows that the best professionals are those who openly acknowledge and learn from their mistakes. The post concludes that failure is essential for growth, and developers should actively seek challenges that push them beyond their current abilities.
key_points: Certifications prove you can memorize technical information, but project experience—including failures—demonstrates actual competence
Organizational cultures that declare every project a success prevent honest assessment and harm end users
The best professionals are obsessed with failure and can readily discuss their mistakes in detail
Learning comes not from failure itself but from analyzing it, making changes, and trying again
Developers should deliberately overreach to find their limits and grow, failing in new ways each time
money_quotes: Your credentials should be the sum of the projects you've worked on.
If you really want to know if someone is competent at their profession, ask them about their failures.
Success is relative and ephemeral. But failure is a near-constant.
The best software developers embrace failure – in fact, they're obsessed with failure.
If you forget how easy it is to make critical mistakes, you're likely to fail.
Learning doesn't happen from failure itself but rather from analyzing the failure, making a change, and then trying again.
If you're not failing some of the time, you're not trying hard enough.
Do make sure you fail in spectacular new ways on each subsequent project.
posts.key_insight: The willingness to acknowledge and learn from failures is a far better indicator of professional competence than credentials or a track record of claimed successes.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood continues a dialogue with James Bach about the value of following instructions, responding to Bach's critique of his earlier 'paint can' post. Bach correctly identified Atwood's hidden assumption that painting skill only meant physical application technique, not the broader knowledge of when to prime or use certain materials. While Bach argues that most instructions are useless or harmful, Atwood counters that in practice, we rarely have access to experts and must rely on written guidance. He illustrates this with a real example where remembering a tip about Flotrol paint conditioner solved an actual problem. The post concludes that while questioning instructions is important, sometimes a bulleted list is genuinely helpful.
key_points: Bach's skill at identifying hidden assumptions is valuable - Atwood didn't realize his definition of 'painting skill' excluded following preparation instructions
Most instructions we encounter are ignored (click-wrap licenses, quickstart guides, shampoo bottles saying 'repeat') and many would be harmful if followed
A paint can is neither a supervisor, mentor, nor judge of quality - static instructions can't replace hands-on expert guidance
In practice, experts aren't available and bulleted lists are often all you have to work with
Atwood assumed readers would treat instructions as suggestions and guidelines, not recipes to follow slavishly
money_quotes: Being under Bach's intensive analytical microscope feels a lot like an interview with Hannibal Lecter. It's flattering, but it's also a little scary.
I have tremendous respect for Bach and the way he aggressively questions convention.
Mr. Bach is incredibly effective at sussing out all the assumptions you're making that you don't realize you're making.
I would have to say that most instructions I come across are unimportant and some are harmful.
Some things that look like instructions are not properly instructions at all, they are really just ritual phrases uttered to dispel the evil spirits of legal liability.
A paint can is neither a supervisor, nor a mentor, nor a judge of quality.
Most of the time, you're on your own, and the bulleted list is all you have.
But sometimes a bulleted list of instructions sure does come in handy.
posts.key_insight: While we should question all instructions critically, in the absence of expert guidance, a well-written bulleted list can be surprisingly valuable for solving real problems.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines guerilla redesigns of famously ugly but successful websites like Craigslist and useit.com, questioning whether such efforts are worthwhile. He argues that for content-based websites, usability improvements are essentially meaningless compared to the value of the content itself. Drawing on Joel Spolsky's observation that applications doing something people really want will succeed despite poor UI, Atwood extends this to content sites. He concludes that 'ghetto' design can be a valid choice when the content or community connection is strong enough, as evidenced by MySpace's success despite its terrible design.
key_points: Redesigning content-heavy sites like Craigslist is largely a waste of time because the content value dwarfs any UI improvements
Applications and websites that do something people really want will succeed despite terrible usability
Content-based websites are already 'so damn usable' that UI consulting adds no measurable value
'Ghetto' design is a valid choice when the platform successfully enables community and communication
The ultimate test of a product is whether it 'gets you laid or gets you paid' - design is secondary to this
money_quotes: An application that does something really great that people really want to do can be pathetically unusable, and it will still be a hit.
There is not a single content-based website online that would gain even one dollar in revenue by improving usability.
The content is such a strong attraction that you could probably change the stylesheet to use green text on a red background and usage would still continue to climb.
The redesign is clearly a small improvement, but it's just a statistical rounding error next to the value of the content.
Get a community together where people can communicate easily and you have yourself a winner.
He'll only fund for two reasons: if it gets you laid, or it gets you paid.
Design is important, but content is king.
posts.key_insight: For content-driven platforms, the value of what users can do or access completely overwhelms any considerations of visual design or usability polish.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains how hardware virtualization works and why the x86 architecture struggles with it. The core problem is that both the virtual machine manager (VMM) and guest operating systems need to run in Ring 0 privileged mode, forcing expensive software emulation. Intel and AMD's new hardware virtualization support creates a 'Ring -1' super-privileged mode exclusively for the VMM, eliminating the emulation penalty. Atwood predicts that virtualization will become ubiquitous, with everyone eventually running inside virtual machines by default.
key_points: The x86 architecture fails to meet the Popek and Goldberg virtualization requirements, making efficient virtualization difficult
Traditional virtualization requires the VMM to intercept and emulate problematic instructions because both VMM and guest OS compete for Ring 0 privileges
Hardware virtualization creates Ring -1, a super-privileged mode that gives the VMM unique control without software emulation
Future hardware may enable hot-swapping CPUs and memory without server shutdown
Atwood sees hardware virtualization support as a crucial step toward a future where we always run in virtual machines
money_quotes: In a virtual machine, you don't want that kernel and driver code, or the interrupt handlers, to really have full control over the hardware; you need the VMM to be able to transparently manage the system.
The VMM is constantly monitoring the execution of the virtual machines, interceding to redirect memory, storage and I/O requests to the specific allocated resources.
In effect, they create a super privileged mode (sometimes referred to as 'Ring -1'), which can only be used by the VMM.
The x86 architecture is not well suited to virtualization because it doesn't meet something called the Popek and Goldberg virtualization requirements.
I firmly believe that, in the not too distant future, we'll always be running in a virtual machine.
posts.key_insight: Hardware virtualization support creates a 'Ring -1' privilege level that finally gives hypervisors the architectural authority they need to manage virtual machines efficiently without costly software emulation.

posts.summary: Atwood shares a screenshot of a paradoxical Windows dialog box that creates an impossible situation for the user. The dialog apparently requires an action that cannot be completed due to the dialog itself being present. He references the famous quote from WarGames about the only winning move being not to play, suggesting that some software problems are best solved by abandoning the attempt entirely.
key_points: Catch-22 situations in software UI represent fundamental design failures
Sometimes the best solution to a software problem is to avoid engaging with it at all
Poor dialog design can create logically impossible user scenarios
money_quotes: A strange game. The only winning move is not to play. How about a nice game of chess?
posts.key_insight: Some software interfaces are so poorly designed that they create logically impossible situations, leaving users with no choice but to abandon the task entirely.

posts.summary: Atwood discovers that spammers are exploiting his Windows Server 2003 SMTP service through a 'Reverse NDR attack' - sending emails to nonexistent addresses on his domain so that bounce messages containing spam are forwarded to spoofed sender addresses. The default SMTP service accepts messages before validating recipients, making it too late to prevent the abuse. While he found a tarpit feature, it won't help because the core issue is the timing of recipient validation. Suppressing NDRs entirely would break RFC 2821 email protocol standards. He concludes he may need to purchase a commercial mail server unless he can find a way to reject invalid recipients before accepting messages.
key_points: Spammers exploit SMTP non-delivery reports by sending to fake addresses, causing the server to bounce spam-laden NDRs to spoofed sender victims
Windows Server 2003's default SMTP service validates recipients after accepting messages, which is architecturally too late to prevent this attack
The SMTP tarpit feature exists but doesn't solve this problem since it operates at a different stage
Suppressing NDRs entirely violates RFC 2821 and breaks legitimate use cases like typo notifications
The only real fix is recipient validation before message acceptance, which the built-in service doesn't support
money_quotes: Spammers are intentionally sending email messages to nonexistent email addresses on my domain.
The person in the 'from' address will get a non-delivery report from my server that includes the original spam message as an attachment.
The basic SMTP service is not smart enough to perform 'recipient filtering' – to reject email for users that don't exist at the time of submission.
The validation of the address occurs after the email delivery process begins, which is too late.
I hate to buy a commercial mail server to replace the simple SMTP and POP3 services provided with Windows Server 2003.
posts.key_insight: Email protocol design decisions about when to validate recipients can turn your own server into an unwitting spam relay through the abuse of non-delivery reports.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood expresses frustration with WYSIWYG editors that hide formatting tags from users, causing unexpected document corruption when users accidentally delete invisible markup. He argues that editors should at minimum provide an option to reveal formatting tags, comparing document editing in Word to 'walking through a field of formatting land mines.' He notes that browser-based editors like FreeTextBox at least allow switching to HTML view. Ultimately, he declares a preference for plain text editing with visible markup over WYSIWYG approaches.
key_points: Invisible formatting tags cause unpredictable document corruption when accidentally deleted
WYSIWYG editors should provide an option to reveal hidden formatting codes
Microsoft Word is particularly problematic, with innocuous edits destroying document formatting
Browser-based WYSIWYG editors are preferable because they allow switching to HTML view
Plain text editing with visible markup is more reliable than hidden formatting systems
money_quotes: The problem with invisible formatting tags is that they're, well, invisible.
Editing a Word document is a nerve wracking experience not unlike walking through a field of formatting land mines.
I like my markup clean, simple, and most of all – visible.
Thank goodness for my old friend, CTRL+Z.
Give us a way to optionally reveal the formatting tags in the document so I can tell what the heck I'm deleting.
posts.key_insight: Document editing systems that hide their underlying structure from users create fragile, unpredictable experiences where users cannot understand or control the effects of their edits.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood presents Jerry Weinberg's Ten Commandments of Egoless Programming from the 1971 book 'The Psychology of Computer Programming.' The commandments focus on separating personal identity from code, accepting criticism gracefully, respecting colleagues at all skill levels, and embracing collaboration over solo heroics. Atwood highlights how these principles remain relevant decades later, emphasizing that the human aspects of software development are timeless. The post serves as a reminder that technical excellence must be paired with interpersonal maturity.
key_points: Separate your identity from your code—criticism of code is not criticism of you as a person
Seek input from others proactively, especially when you think you don't need it
Treat less experienced people with respect to avoid reinforcing negative stereotypes about developers
Authority comes from knowledge, not position or title
Embrace change as a challenge rather than fighting it as an inconvenience
money_quotes: You are not your code.
No matter how much 'karate' you know, someone else will always know more.
The only true authority stems from knowledge, not from position.
Fight for what you believe, but gracefully accept defeat.
Don't be the guy coding in the dark office emerging only to buy cola.
Critique code instead of people – be kind to the coder, not to the code.
Nontechnical people who deal with developers on a regular basis almost universally hold the opinion that we are prima donnas at best and crybabies at worst.
The human principles of software are truly timeless.
posts.key_insight: The human and interpersonal aspects of programming—humility, collaboration, and emotional maturity—are timeless principles that matter as much as technical skill.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that good writing skills are essential for programmers, not for marketing purposes, but for effective communication and gaining influence. He critiques the concept of 'copywriting' as unnecessarily marketing-focused, preferring plain writing instead. Drawing on Joel Spolsky's advice, he emphasizes that the difference between good and great programmers lies in their ability to communicate ideas clearly. Atwood reframes 'power and influence' not as career ambition but as a practical currency that enables programmers to do their best work with autonomy.
key_points: Good writing is about communication, not marketing - drop the 'copy' from copywriting
The difference between tolerable and great programmers is communication ability, not technical knowledge
Clear writing lets other programmers use your code instead of rewriting it - without it, brilliant code languishes unused
Power and influence aren't goals but side effects that give programmers freedom to work as they see fit
Communication skills are a form of 'citizen leadership' rather than Machiavellian manipulation
money_quotes: The second you start thinking in terms of copywriting instead of writing, you've already lost.
Good writing is good writing. But good copywriting is marketing.

The difference between a tolerable programmer and a great programmer is not how many programming languages they know, and it's not whether they prefer Python or Java. It's whether they can communicate their ideas.

There's a lot of wonderful, useful code buried on sourceforge somewhere that nobody uses because it was created by programmers who don't write very well (or don't write at all), and so nobody knows what they've done and their brilliant code languishes.
Power and influence aren't what we're after. They're a side effect, a necessary evil, a form of currency that makes it easier for us to get things done.
Power and influence give you the freedom to architect and write code as you see fit.
Power and influence achieved solely on the basis of solid communication skills is a virtue, not a vice.
posts.key_insight: Writing well isn't about self-promotion—it's the currency that buys programmers the autonomy to do their best technical work.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques the trend of blogs cluttered with excessive widgets, icons, and navigation complexity. Using the 'flair' metaphor from Office Space, he points to examples of blogs with dozens of social bookmarking icons, multiple RSS subscription buttons, and confusing navigation that obscures content. He argues that blogs succeed because of their simplicity, and that adding visual noise makes them harder to read and navigate. The post concludes by suggesting RSS feeds as a solution that delivers content without the clutter.
key_points: Social bookmarking icons are useless visual noise - power users already know how to bookmark, and everyone else is confused by them
Blogs work because they're simple; adding flair undermines their core strength
Poor navigation choices (click-to-continue, buried browse links, limited archives) actively harm user experience
Statistics and widgets that interest the blogger are just noise to readers
RSS represents the ideal: content delivery without visual clutter
money_quotes: Why do people want their blogs to look like NASCAR vehicles?
Blogs work because they're simple. Adding a bunch of flair just makes them harder to navigate and more difficult to read.
The users of the above social bookmarking sites surely know how to bookmark a site without these 'helpful' icons. Everyone else is befuddled by 24 meaningless icons.
I'd love to browse the rest of Tom's entries, but he's made it awfully difficult for me to do so.
Perhaps this is really an argument in favor of RSS – all of the content with none of the excess flair.
I'm sure Scott finds them interesting, but they're just noise to me.
posts.key_insight: Every widget, icon, and feature you add to a blog should be evaluated from the reader's perspective, not the author's - most 'helpful' additions are just noise that obscures the content people came for.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the concept of egoless programming, drawing from Jerry Weinberg's classic 'The Psychology of Computer Programming.' The core idea is that developers should separate their self-worth from their code, enabling them to accept criticism and learn from mistakes during peer reviews. Atwood acknowledges a paradox: while many workers are apathetic about their jobs, passionate programmers often have the opposite problem—they care too much, treating their code like intimate love letters. This excessive attachment makes criticism feel personal and hinders collaboration. The solution is embracing reviews as learning opportunities rather than attacks on one's identity.
key_points: Egoless programming means tying your ego to your effort and learning, not to the perfection of your output
Peer reviews work best when everyone expects to both produce and find errors
Passionate programmers often struggle with ego because they care deeply about their craft
Early programming culture treated code as private property, making it unintelligible to outsiders
Disconnecting self-worth from job function makes it easier to embrace criticism
money_quotes: My ego is not tied to my 'perfect' or 'imperfect' work product. My ego is only tied to my attempts to do the best job I know how, and to learn from my mistakes, not the initial result of my work.
You are not your job. You are not how much money you have in the bank.
It's a lot easier to embrace criticism of your work when you don't let your work define who you are.
Those of us who love programming enough to become highly skilled at it tend to have the opposite problem – we care too much.
A program was regarded as the private property of the programmer. One would no more think of reading a colleague's program unbidden than of picking up a love letter and reading it.
This is essentially what a program was, a love letter from the programmer to the hardware, full of the intimate details known only to partners in an affair.
posts.key_insight: The best programmers must learn to separate their identity from their code, channeling their passion into continuous improvement rather than defensive attachment to their work.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the persistent and widespread phenomenon of software project failures, citing examples where hundreds of millions of dollars were lost on abandoned or broken systems. He draws from IEEE research and industry surveys showing that historically only a small fraction of IT projects truly succeed, with large projects facing near-zero odds of finishing on time. However, he notes significant improvement over a decade—success rates doubled from 1994 to 2004, largely due to smaller project scopes and iterative development replacing waterfall methods. The post argues that while failure itself is acceptable, the industry must learn from its mistakes through deliberate postmortem analysis to continue improving.
key_points: Software project failures are universal—they happen across all countries, company sizes, and sectors, costing billions annually
Large software projects have near-zero odds of finishing on time and roughly 50% odds of being canceled entirely
The industry has improved dramatically: project success rates doubled from 16% in 1994 to 34% in 2004, while failure rates halved
Smaller project scopes and iterative development (vs. waterfall) are cited as the primary drivers of improvement
Studying past failures through postmortem analysis is essential to avoiding common pitfalls
money_quotes: If Las Vegas sounds too tame for you, software might just be the right gamble.
The odds of a large project finishing on time are close to zero. The odds of a large project being canceled are an even-money bet.
Even Vegas prize fights don't get this bloody.

The history of software development is a tremendous success. Just look around you for evidence of that. But that success has a long, dark shadow that we don't talk about very much: it's littered with colossal failures.
What's particularly disturbing is that the colossal failures keep recurring year after year. The names and dollar amounts may change, but the story is otherwise the same.
Failing is OK. Failing can even be desirable. But you must learn from your failures, and that requires concerted postmortem introspection and analysis.
Once you know what the common pitfalls are, it's easier to avoid them.
posts.key_insight: Software project failure is endemic and costs billions annually, but the industry can and has improved dramatically by embracing smaller projects, iterative development, and systematic learning from past disasters.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood discusses the challenge of debugging localhost HTTP traffic on Windows, where ethernet sniffers cannot capture local packets since they bypass the regular network stack. He introduces Fiddler, a local HTTP proxy, as a workaround solution. While Atwood notes some quirks with IE7 compatibility, he highlights Fiddler's unique capabilities like setting breakpoints and modifying HTTP data mid-stream. Though he'd prefer using a standard sniffer, he acknowledges Fiddler as a reasonable alternative for localhost debugging scenarios.
key_points: Ethernet sniffers are valuable debugging tools that don't require system reboots to install
Windows sniffers cannot capture localhost traffic because those packets bypass the normal network stack
Fiddler serves as a local HTTP proxy workaround for this limitation
Fiddler offers capabilities beyond sniffing, including breakpoints and HTTP data tampering
The tool integrates well with IE and can work with Firefox through additional configuration
money_quotes: Ethernet sniffers should be a standard tool in your development troubleshooting toolkit, too.
Windows ethernet sniffers do have one significant limitation: they can't sniff localhost traffic.
Localhost packets don't pass through the regular network stack, so they're invisible to an ethernet sniffer.
With Fiddler you can set breakpoints and tamper with the HTTP data before it is sent or received.
On the whole, I'd prefer to stick with a sniffer for localhost debugging. But a HTTP proxy like Fiddler is a reasonable workaround.
posts.key_insight: When standard ethernet sniffers fail for localhost debugging on Windows, a local HTTP proxy like Fiddler provides both a workaround and additional debugging capabilities beyond simple packet capture.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques a common programming interview question—'how would you write a routine to copy a file?'—by showcasing David Pickett's response that exposes the question's absurdity through increasingly complex edge cases. The back-and-forth reveals how a seemingly simple task involves file attributes, compression, encryption, security implications, and backup software compatibility. Atwood argues that such questions are 'interview riddles' that don't deserve face-value responses. His ultimate point is that competent programmers shouldn't be writing file copy routines at all—they should use existing, tested solutions. The post satirizes the disconnect between artificial interview problems and real-world software engineering judgment.
key_points: Interview questions asking candidates to implement basic utilities like file copying ignore the massive complexity hidden in edge cases
A simple 'copy file' operation involves attributes, compression, encryption, security holes, and backup software compatibility
Good candidates should question the premise of implementing something that already exists
These 'interview riddles' don't test practical programming judgment
The best answer to 'how would you implement X' is often 'why would I?'
money_quotes: The only rational answer to 'how would you write a routine to copy a file?' is another question: why would any competent programmer ever write a file copy routine?
Look, just copy the damn file.
David's answers are even better – they implode the question under its own weight.
'How would you write a routine to copy a file?' is just another interview riddle; it doesn't deserve a face value response.
The last time I checked, moving mount Fuji wasn't a part of our business plan, either.
That could be a serious security hole. Especially if wherever this file copy function ends up supports arbitrary parameters.
posts.key_insight: The best programmers know when not to write code, and interview questions that reward reinventing solved problems test the wrong skills entirely.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while the book 'Refactoring' codifies useful guidelines, the ability to recognize 'code smells' should be an intuitive skill that develops early in a programmer's career. He presents a comprehensive catalog of code smells organized into two categories: those within classes (like long methods, duplicated code, and dead code) and those between classes (like feature envy, inappropriate intimacy, and solution sprawl). Atwood emphasizes that the crucial skill isn't knowing specific refactoring techniques, but developing the instinct to recognize problematic patterns in your own code. He takes a somewhat provocative stance that programmers who need a book to understand refactoring probably shouldn't be programmers at all.
key_points: Code smells are warning signs that indicate potential problems in code structure or design, and recognizing them should become intuitive early in a programmer's career
Smells within classes include long methods, duplicated code, speculative generality, and dead code - all symptoms of code that has grown unwieldy or unfocused
Smells between classes reveal relationship problems: inappropriate intimacy, feature envy, lazy classes, and solution sprawl indicate poor separation of concerns
The ability to 'smell' your own code is more important than knowing specific refactoring recipes - self-awareness trumps prescribed solutions
Aggressive refactoring should be a natural part of the programming craft, not something learned from a book
money_quotes: Any programmer worth his or her salt should already be refactoring aggressively.
Developing your 'code nose' is something that happens early in your programming career, if it's going to happen at all.
Duplicated code is the bane of software development. Stamp out duplication whenever possible.
Ruthlessly delete code that isn't being used. That's why we have source control systems!
Everyone loses in the 'what if...' school of design.
Classes should know as little as possible about each other.
You should have a compelling reason for every item you make public. If you don't, hide it.
The important thing, from my perspective, isn't the refactoring – it's learning to recognize the scent of your own code.
posts.key_insight: The most valuable skill in code quality isn't knowing refactoring techniques, but developing an intuitive sense for recognizing problematic patterns in your own code.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood draws a compelling parallel between professional cooking and software development, arguing that the culinary principle of 'working clean' translates directly to constant refactoring in code. He explores how restaurant kitchens enforce cleanliness as law rather than suggestion, with cooks who don't comply facing social pressure until they conform or quit. The post also examines 'mise en place' - the deliberate arrangement of workstations - as analogous to standardized development environments and shared tooling. Atwood concludes that good programmers should be obsessive about their setup while accepting that the craft evolves too rapidly for permanent satisfaction.
key_points: 'Working clean' in kitchens translates to constant refactoring in software - addressing code smells before they become catastrophic
Professional cooks enforce cleanliness through social pressure, with messy workers taunted until they improve or leave
Mise en place (station arrangement) parallels developers' standardized environments, common tools, and shared frameworks
The mess isn't from poor design but from not 'doing the dishes' after every meal - maintenance debt accumulates quickly
Good programmers should be borderline obsessive about their setup while accepting continuous improvement as necessary
money_quotes: The reason the kitchen is a mess is not because the kitchen is poorly designed, it's because we didn't do the dishes after every meal.
Working clean wasn't so much a suggestion, but rather a law.
Those cooks who didn't constantly clean would wind up in trouble. Their workspace would become so messy within a matter of an hour or two that the quality of food rapidly diminished.
For software developers, working clean means constant refactoring.
Working clean means following your nose and addressing those nagging issues before they become catastrophic.
Sharp knives have multiple uses, apparently.
Our craft changes too rapidly for us to ever be completely satisfied with the way we're working today.
posts.key_insight: Software development professionalism requires the same discipline as professional cooking: constant cleanup (refactoring) and obsessive attention to workspace organization, because small messes compound rapidly into catastrophic problems.

posts.summary: Atwood explores the tension between rigid methodologies and expert intuition in software development, using the chef/recipe metaphor. He pushes back against James Bach's dismissal of maturity models as worthless, arguing that recipes have value even for experts who occasionally need to reference them. The key insight is that both novice-friendly recipes and expert-level flexibility should coexist, avoiding religious adherence to any single methodology. He advocates for providing baseline practices while also offering alternatives for those who've outgrown them.
key_points: Instructions appropriate for novices may be inappropriate for experts, and vice versa
Dismissing all recipes/methodologies as worthless is as wrongheaded as demanding strict adherence to them
Even expert chefs occasionally benefit from referring to recipe cards
The solution is coexistence: start with common denominator recipes but provide alternatives for advanced practitioners
Avoid 'onerous, religious one-methodology-fits-all' thinking
money_quotes: Instructions that are appropriate for a novice may be totally inappropriate for an expert.
A maturity model is basically a gang of best practices hooked on crystal meth.
The idea that a recipe (e.g., a best practice, a methodology, or a maturity model) is completely worthless is just as wrongheaded as the idea that everything should be based on a strict recipe.
Even an expert chef may occasionally find it helpful to refer to a recipe card.
The point is to avoid an onerous, religious one-methodology-fits-all view of the world.
posts.key_insight: Best practices and methodologies have value at every skill level, but should be treated as helpful references rather than religious mandates—the goal is flexible coexistence, not dogmatic adherence.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that Apple's switch to Intel x86 CPUs has fundamentally changed the value proposition of Mac laptops. After years of Apple charging premium prices for underpowered PowerPC machines backed by dubious 'supercomputer on a chip' marketing, the new Intel-based MacBooks are now price-competitive or even cheaper than equivalent Windows laptops. He provides multiple price comparisons showing MacBooks undercutting Dell, Asus, and Fujitsu alternatives with similar specs. Atwood concludes that with Boot Camp enabling Windows compatibility, Apple laptops have become compelling options even for dedicated Windows users.
key_points: Apple's switch from PowerPC to Intel x86 CPUs made Macs genuinely competitive with PCs in performance because they literally became PCs
The new MacBook and MacBook Pro laptops are often $200-700 cheaper than Windows laptops with equivalent specifications
Apple's strategy has shifted from the old premium-pricing approach to the iPod-style market share strategy of competitive pricing
Boot Camp enables running Windows on Mac hardware, though some usability kinks like right-click support still need work
Apple's design quality gives them an edge even when prices are comparable to Windows alternatives
money_quotes: The new Apple models really are as fast as PCs – because they are PCs.
That's gotta sting a little.
Forget the reality distortion field advertising. Forget the fact that Apple just obsoleted their entire PC line overnight. Again.
iPods are not only competitively priced, they're often cheaper. Today's Apple is very different than the old Apple, but many people still haven't caught on.
The current crop of x86 Apple laptops are cheaper than nearly every other x86 laptop of equivalent spec.
Now you can get a MacBook cheaper than most of its PC rivals.
The rare few laptops that are comparable in price and specification can't compete with Apple's design chops.
My next wintel laptop might just be an Apple.
posts.key_insight: Apple's Intel transition transformed MacBooks from overpriced status symbols into genuinely cost-competitive machines that beat Windows laptops on both price and design.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines Microsoft's newly aggressive Windows Genuine Advantage (WGA) notification system, which creates mandatory delays and persistent nag screens for users with unvalidated Windows installations. He notes the misleading language around security updates, pointing out that Microsoft is socially obligated to provide critical patches to all machines regardless of license status to prevent compromised systems from being weaponized. The post details how WGA is deeply embedded and difficult to disable, requiring third-party hacks to circumvent. Atwood concludes by questioning whether Microsoft's enforcement approach has gone too far, referencing the concept of an 'optimal piracy rate' that suggests some piracy may actually benefit software companies.
key_points: WGA notifications include login delays, persistent balloon warnings, and increasingly dire messages over time
Microsoft must provide critical security updates to pirated machines to prevent them from becoming attack vectors
The WGA notification system is deeply integrated and cannot be simply disabled or killed as a process
Microsoft's remediation options mostly involve purchasing a new license with almost no path to a free replacement
Future Windows versions like Vista will implement even more aggressive discrimination between genuine and pirated copies
money_quotes: Microsoft is socially obligated to provide critical security updates to pirated machines.
Otherwise those vulnerable machines will eventually be compromised and potentially used in denial of service attacks and other nefarious schemes.
I suppose it's only malware if you're a pirate.
Notice the word 'Purchase' appears in three of those five options. There's almost no way to finagle a free cd-key out of this.
Get used to increased forms of online discrimination between genuine and pirated versions of software.
Microsoft is certainly entitled to protect their IP by annoying pirates into buying cd keys. But I wonder if they're veering too far into the enforcement side of the optimal piracy rate.
posts.key_insight: Anti-piracy measures that punish users too aggressively may backfire, and there's a practical limit to how much enforcement is optimal before it becomes counterproductive.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to Don Norman's criticism of Google's 'faux simplicity' by reframing the Google search box as the ultimate command prompt rather than a limited interface. He cites Bill de hÓra's observation that search may be replacing the desktop metaphor as the dominant computing paradigm after three decades. While acknowledging his unease about returning to command-line-style interfaces, Atwood concedes that typing to find what you want is often faster than visual navigation. The post questions whether usability experts need to reconsider their assumptions about interface design.
key_points: Don Norman's criticism that Google 'only does one thing' misses the point—the search box is a command prompt that can lead anywhere
The desktop metaphor, dominant for 30 years, may be giving way to a search-based paradigm
Google's oneboxes and integrated services demonstrate the search interface's hidden depth
Literal interpretations of the desktop metaphor (like Magic Cap) reveal its limitations
Typing and searching is often faster than point-and-click visual navigation, even within a single page
money_quotes: Once you realize that the Google search box is really a type of command prompt, the criticism that Google 'only does one thing' is... well, downright hilarious.
Search is the beginning of every command. You can go anywhere and do anything from there.

Perhaps the hunt and peck approach of searching (along with gaming) is becoming the dominant computing metaphor, replacing nearly 3 decades of user interfaces based on the metaphor of an office desktop.
There's a lot to be said for typing stuff to quickly get to what you want.

As much as I dislike the limitations of the desktop metaphor, I'm a little uneasy about the idea that the ultimate user interface we end up with in the next ten years will bear more resemblance to a command prompt.
I often bail out when attempting to visually point and click my way around crowded web pages; instead, I search for a relevant word on the page using incremental search.
posts.key_insight: The Google search box isn't a dumbed-down interface—it's a command prompt that can lead anywhere, suggesting that search may be the new dominant computing metaphor.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the disconnect between how digital cameras and computer monitors express resolution, questioning why cameras use megapixels while monitors use width x height dimensions. He points out that megapixel counts obscure important information like aspect ratio, making it impossible to know the actual shape of images without additional context. The post examines common display resolutions and their aspect ratios, noting that most PC monitors use 4:3 while cameras vary between 4:3 and 3:2 (inherited from 35mm film). Atwood advocates for clearer, more informative resolution specifications over marketing-friendly but less useful designations.
key_points: Megapixel counts hide aspect ratio information - a 1.9 megapixel image could theoretically be 192 x 10,000 pixels
Most common monitor resolutions (800x600, 1024x768) follow a 4:3 aspect ratio, while 1280x1024 is an oddball at 5:4
The 3:2 aspect ratio common in digital cameras derives from classic 35mm film dimensions
Cryptic acronyms like UXGA are less useful than explicit resolution numbers like 1600x1200
Megapixel marketing borrows the hard drive manufacturer trick of dividing by powers of ten to inflate numbers
money_quotes: Nobody buys a 21" LCD with 1.9 megapixels of resolution; they buy a 21" LCD that can display 1600 x 1200.
It looks like we're using the old hard drive manufacturer's trick of dividing by powers of ten.
Who knows, maybe that 1.9 megapixel camera is really taking 192 x 10,000 pictures. Pixels are pixels, right?
I'd much rather know that a display is capable of 1600 x 1200 instead of the cryptic designation UXGA.
I didn't realize how oddball the 1280x1024 ratio was.
posts.key_insight: Megapixel counts are a marketing convenience that obscures the aspect ratio information users actually need to understand what their images and displays will look like.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the rapid growth of broadband adoption in America, noting that by 2006 nearly half of Americans had high-speed internet at home, up from less than 5% during the dot-com bubble. He argues that the dot-com crash was partly inevitable because dial-up internet provided such a poor user experience that widespread adoption of web services was unrealistic. Despite the impressive growth, Atwood notes the US still lags behind other developed nations in broadband adoption. He poses the question of whether web developers can finally stop accommodating dial-up users.
key_points: Broadband adoption grew 40% year-over-year, reaching 42% of Americans by March 2006
The dot-com bubble's failure was predictable because 95% of users were stuck with painful dial-up experiences
Despite growth, the US lagged behind other developed nations in broadband adoption through 2004
The author personally hadn't used dial-up in at least five years and finally sold his vintage modems
money_quotes: Using the internet with a modem truly sucks.
When 95 percent of users are stuck with a crappy dial-up experience, it's unrealistic to expect widespread adoption of services offered via the internet.
At the height of the last dot-com bubble in mid-2000, less than five percent of Americans had access to a high speed internet connection at home.
One of the key ingredients for Web 2.0 success is pervasive high speed internet access.
Do web developers still need to accommodate dial-up users?
posts.key_insight: The dot-com bubble burst partly because investors ignored a fundamental constraint: you can't build successful internet services when 95% of your potential users have a miserable dial-up experience.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood compares touchpads and trackpoints as laptop pointing devices, firmly declaring himself a touchpad user. After switching to a Thinkpad T40 that has both input methods, he finds the touchpad superior due to its intuitive gestures like tap-to-click, edge scrolling, and pressure-sensitive dragging. He credits Synaptics drivers for making touchpads so capable. While acknowledging trackpoint improvements over the years, he notes that even IBM/Lenovo includes both options by default, suggesting the trackpoint alone isn't sufficient. He concludes that most laptop users probably just use an external USB mouse anyway.
key_points: Touchpads win on intuitive gesture support: tap clicking, edge scrolling, pressure-sensitive dragging, and speed-based cursor acceleration
Synaptics drivers deserve significant credit for making touchpads functional and usable over the years
The fact that IBM/Lenovo includes both input methods by default suggests neither alone satisfies all users
Trackpoints have improved (smoother motion, press-to-click) but still lack scrolling capability
The entire debate may be moot since most laptop users rely on external USB mice
money_quotes: I find myself forgetting to use the external USB mouse I regularly carry in my laptop bag. There is no stronger praise for any alternative input device.
If the trackpoint is such a great input device, why would anyone need a trackpad?
The Synaptics drivers are the only OEM input drivers I ever install.
I suspect the most common pointing device for most laptop users is an external USB mouse. Which renders the whole touchpad vs. trackpoint debate moot.
The fact that I can use any kind of alternative input device and not feel compelled to immediately plug in an external mouse is pretty amazing to me.
posts.key_insight: The best pointing device is whichever one you can use so effectively that you forget you have a better alternative in your bag.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood highlights the book 'Peopleware' after it was featured on Kevin Kelly's Cool Tools list. He endorses it as an essential resource for anyone managing projects or teams in software development. The post shares Kelly's review, which praises the book's practical wisdom on team productivity, the myth of overtime, and creating effective work environments. Atwood strongly recommends the book as a must-read for developers and project managers.
key_points: Peopleware has been a quiet but influential guide for Silicon Valley startups and successful projects for over 20 years
The book focuses on optimizing people rather than time management, addressing team dynamics, workspace needs, and the dangers of overtime
It targets project-level management rather than large organizational concerns, making it practical for hands-on work
The book's lessons on team cohesion, closure ceremonies, and physical workspace remain relevant decades after publication
money_quotes: Hard-won wisdom fills this small book: How to create a team, place, or company that is productive.
I learned about the myth of overtime, the need for closure and ceremonies, how teams jell, and why everyone should and can have a window.
I first read it decades ago and re-read it every time I embark on anything involving more than one person and several years of my life.
The message in the book touts productivity, without ever mentioning the dreary idea of time management.
It's more about optimizing people, and thus the title, Peopleware.
If you don't have a copy of Peopleware yet, what are you waiting for?
posts.key_insight: Sustainable software project success comes from optimizing people and team dynamics rather than managing time or enforcing overtime.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood observes that software development discussions tend toward extreme polarization, with people either loving or hating technologies rather than holding moderate views. He compares this to religious arguments and partisan political theater like Crossfire. While acknowledging that strongly polarized opinions can distort discourse (as seen in Amazon reviews), he argues that controversy has value: products that inspire strong reactions tend to be more interesting and successful than mediocre ones that elicit indifference. The key is maintaining perspective while creating things bold enough to inspire passion.
key_points: Software development debates mirror religious arguments, with people taking extreme partisan positions on everything from languages to tabs vs spaces
Online reviews suffer from selection bias - only those with extreme positive or negative experiences are motivated to write them
Controversy is actually desirable: products that inspire both love and hate tend to be more successful than mediocre ones
The worst reaction to your work is indifference ('Meh'), not hatred
While the love/hate dichotomy has value, it's important not to frame all discussions in purely partisan terms
money_quotes: Software development is basically a religion.
Heavily partisan discussion – lovers on one side, haters on another – isn't conducive to civilized discourse.
It's not debate at all; it's pure theater. Entertainment. Professional wrestling, if you will.
What about the vast, silent audience who liked it despite its flaws?
If nobody cares enough about what you're doing to tell you that they loved it or that they hated it, it's probably not interesting enough to be good, either.
The last reaction you want to anything you've done is a resounding 'Meh.'
Go out of your way to create something that will inspire people to love it. Unfortunately, that means a few people will probably hate it, too.
posts.key_insight: Creating something genuinely interesting means accepting that strong negative reactions are the inevitable flip side of passionate fans, and both are preferable to indifference.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood updates his recommended laptop travel accessories for road warriors, focusing on practical gear that solves real problems encountered while traveling. He switches from a Microsoft mouse to the Logitech V500 due to accidental power-on issues, highlights the value of carrying a mini power strip for crowded airports, and recommends compact wireless routers for networking on the road. The post is a straightforward gear roundup with specific product recommendations and the reasoning behind each choice.
key_points: The Logitech V500 mouse solves the accidental power-on problem with a clever pop-up panel that serves as switch, comfort enhancer, and USB receiver storage
Carrying a mini power strip is essential for crowded airports where outlets are scarce
Modern routers are cheap enough that carrying a mere switch or hub no longer makes sense
The trade-off between portability and functionality matters - the smallest travel router only has one ethernet port
money_quotes: If you've ever been trapped at a crowded airport with all the power outlets occupied by opportunistic travelers, you'll wish you had brought a mini power strip with you.
Given the prices of today's routers, there's absolutely no reason to carry something that's merely a switch or hub.
posts.key_insight: The best travel gear solves specific pain points you've actually experienced, like a mouse that won't accidentally drain its battery or a power strip that lets you share outlets with fellow travelers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques Eric S. Raymond's famous 'How To Ask Questions The Smart Way' FAQ, highlighting its ironic futility. The 32-page document demands that newcomers read it before asking questions, yet the very people who need it most would never find or read such a lengthy document. Atwood argues this represents a fundamental misunderstanding of user behavior that plagues the open source community. Rather than berating users into compliance, developers should create software that tolerates mistakes and guides users naturally. The post questions the true purpose of such gatekeeping documents when their intended audience will never see them.
key_points: Raymond's 'smart questions' FAQ is over 10,000 words long—an absurd length for something users must supposedly read before asking any technical question
The FAQ's hostile disclaimer calling confused newcomers 'idiots' and 'losers' reveals contempt rather than helpfulness
Users who ask clueless questions are precisely the ones who won't find or read a question FAQ, making the entire document self-defeating
The open source community's struggles with usability stem from trying to fix users through hostility rather than building tolerant, guiding software
The document's true audience is unclear—it certainly isn't the newcomers it claims to address
money_quotes: It's 32 pages, 1,066 paragraphs, 10,289 words, and 51,757 characters. That you must read before you ask a technical question of any kind.
Users who ask clueless questions haven't the foggiest idea where to find a question FAQ – and even if they did, they certainly wouldn't have the patience to read it.
You can't fix users by berating and browbeating them into better modes of behavior.
What you can do, however, is develop software that is tolerant of mistakes, software that gently guides users in the right direction.
This speaks volumes about the difficulty the open source community has had delivering usable software.
If we know the intended audience will probably never see it, who exactly was How To Ask Questions The Right Way written for?
posts.key_insight: Software communities can't improve user behavior through intimidating documentation; they must build systems that gracefully handle and guide confused users.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software developers must protect their hands and wrists from repetitive stress injuries, treating hand health as essential to their careers as pianists treat theirs. He advocates for a three-pronged approach: regular physical exercise for overall health and brain function, varying working positions and equipment to prevent repetitive strain, and using hand exercise tools like putty, powerballs, and grip strengtheners. The post emphasizes that RSI is a serious threat that can end programming careers, while offering practical, accessible prevention strategies. Atwood balances serious health advice with his characteristic playful tone, recommending desk toys that double as therapeutic tools.
key_points: Physical exercise benefits both body and brain, improving memory and executive function in ways directly relevant to programming work
Repetitive stress injuries are prevented through variation—different chairs, keyboards, mice, and working positions break up harmful patterns
Hand exercise tools like thinking putty, powerballs, and grip strengtheners serve dual purposes as fun desk toys and legitimate therapeutic devices
Hand and wrist pain should be taken very seriously by programmers since it can end careers
Even simple interventions like hourly stretching breaks and using different equipment at home versus work can help prevent injury
money_quotes: Software developers, like pianists, rely on their hands to practice their craft.
The brain-boosting (and prevention of brain decline) effects of physical exercise have been studied nearly to death. The confusing part is why so many humans do not exercise.
It's called repetitive stress injury for a reason.
Toys, particularly those with therapeutic value, are fun.
If you're experiencing pain in your hands or wrists while programming, you should take it very seriously – it's one of the few things that can put you out of commission as a programmer.
I am not a doctor. I'm just some random guy on the internet. So take this advice with a grain, nay, a warehouse full of salt.
posts.key_insight: Programmers depend entirely on their hands for their livelihood, making proactive hand and wrist care through exercise, ergonomic variation, and therapeutic tools an essential but often neglected aspect of a sustainable programming career.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the counterintuitive idea that Internet Explorer 6's near-total market dominance (95% by 2004) actually enabled the Web 2.0 revolution rather than stifling it. He traces the history of XMLHttpRequest from its introduction as a proprietary Microsoft feature in IE5 to its adoption by Mozilla and eventual W3C standardization. The post argues that when developers didn't need to worry about cross-browser compatibility, they were free to push browser capabilities to their limits. Atwood suggests that Microsoft's abandonment of IE development, whether intentional or not, paradoxically created the conditions for rich web application development that would eventually threaten Windows applications.
key_points: XMLHttpRequest, the foundation of AJAX and Web 2.0, was a proprietary Microsoft invention introduced in IE5 in 1999
IE6 achieved unprecedented 95% market saturation by mid-2004, creating a browser monoculture
Microsoft went nearly six years without releasing a new IE version, the longest gap in browser history
The monoculture freed developers from cross-browser compatibility concerns, enabling innovation
If Microsoft intended to stifle web development by abandoning IE, the strategy backfired spectacularly
money_quotes: XMLHttpRequest is a completely proprietary feature Microsoft introduced along with IE 5.0 in March 1999
IE6 market share peaked at around 95 percent sometime in mid-2004
If 95% of the world is browsing with IE 6, pursuing browser independence is a waste of time
If you don't have to worry about browser independence, you are suddenly free to exploit advanced browser techniques like XMLHttpRequest
The super-saturation and monoculture of IE6 from 2002 to 2004 created an incredibly rich, vibrant development platform where developers were free to push the capabilities of the browser to its limits
If you subscribe to the 'evil Microsoft' theory, Microsoft would have been far better off releasing new, mildly incompatible versions of Internet Explorer every year
Ironic, isn't it?
posts.tone: analytical, contrarian, historically grounded with a touch of irony
posts.key_insight: Browser monoculture, typically seen as harmful, paradoxically enabled Web 2.0 innovation by freeing developers from cross-browser compatibility burdens.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares an update on his everyday carry keychain items, highlighting three essential tools: a Leatherman Squirt pocket knife, a 1GB Iomega USB flash drive, and a Peak LED Matterhorn flashlight. He explains his preference for each item based on practical design considerations, particularly praising the flash drive's attached cap and the flashlight's superior brightness and runtime. The post reflects the author's appreciation for well-designed, functional tools that solve real problems like losing end caps or needing a detachable lanyard. He concludes by noting his ongoing search for a keychain-compatible pen and invites readers to share their own keychain contents.
key_points: The Leatherman Squirt multi-tool remains indispensable after a year, primarily for its scissors, screwdriver, and knife functionality
USB flash drives have become ubiquitous ('the new floppy disk'), but design details like physically attached end caps matter for daily use
The Peak LED Matterhorn flashlight represents a meaningful upgrade with three LEDs, detachable lanyard, and seven-hour runtime to half brightness
Good product design solves everyday annoyances like losing end caps or being unable to detach items from a keyring
money_quotes: USB flash drives are a dime a dozen these days. It's the new floppy disk.
So many USB flash drives have end caps that aren't physically attached to the drive. The first thing I do is lose them!
I just removed them in the photo to highlight the important stuff.
posts.key_insight: The best everyday carry tools are those with thoughtful design details that solve small but persistent annoyances in daily use.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the common perception that maintenance programming is low-status janitorial work, arguing instead that it's intellectually complex and technically demanding. He draws on Robert Glass's characterization of maintenance as requiring innovation under severe constraints, and cites Andy Hunt and Dave Thomas's view that essentially all programming is maintenance programming since code becomes 'maintained' within minutes of being written. While acknowledging Joel Spolsky's observation that developers prefer rewriting to maintaining because reading code is harder than writing it, Atwood argues for a balanced approach: assign your best developers to maintenance work, but also recognize when a complete rewrite is necessary to fix foundational problems.
key_points: Maintenance programming is intellectually complex and technically difficult, requiring developers to work simultaneously with concepts, design, and code under severe constraints
All programming is essentially maintenance programming - code becomes 'maintained' within the first 10 minutes of being written
The best developers should be assigned to maintenance work, not the least experienced or least talented by default
Developers have a knee-jerk tendency to rewrite rather than maintain because reading code is harder than writing it
Sometimes total rewrites are necessary - maintenance cannot fix an unsound foundation, as Mozilla and Windows NT demonstrate
money_quotes: Maintenance programming is widely viewed as janitorial work.
All programming is maintenance programming, because you are rarely writing original code.
It's only the first 10 minutes that the code's original, when you type it in the first time. That's it.
It's harder to read code than to write it.
We're programmers. Programmers are, in their hearts, architects, and the first thing they want to do when they get to a site is to bulldoze the place flat and build something grand.
We should probably have our best developers doing software maintenance, not whoever draws the shortest straw.
I've seen too many systems devolve into a patchwork of duct tape, spit, and a prayer.
Software maintenance, like Rodney Dangerfield, gets no respect.
posts.key_insight: Maintenance programming deserves respect and your best talent, but shouldn't become an excuse to avoid necessary foundational rewrites when the situation truly demands them.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores why software estimates are consistently wrong, drawing from Steve McConnell's book on software estimation. He demonstrates how developers' single-point estimates tend to cluster near best-case scenarios rather than realistic outcomes. The post introduces the concept of asking 'how long would it take if everything went wrong' to expose hidden work and true worst cases. Atwood identifies a systemic problem he calls 'Collusion of Optimists' where developers, managers, and executives all tacitly accept optimistic estimates because they align with desired outcomes. He advocates for starting with worst-case scenarios and working backward to arrive at more realistic estimates.
key_points: Single-point estimates naturally gravitate toward best-case scenarios, not realistic ones
Thinking through worst cases exposes additional work that exists even in the best case
People's 'worst case' estimates are often still optimistic rather than true worst cases
A 'Collusion of Optimists' occurs when developers, managers, and executives all benefit from accepting unrealistic estimates
Starting from worst-case and extrapolating backward produces better estimates than starting optimistic
money_quotes: In thinking through the worst case, I like to ask developers how long the task would take if everything went wrong. People's worst case estimates are often optimistic worst cases rather than true worst cases.
Considering that optimism is a near-universal fact of human nature, software estimates are often undermined by what I think of as a Collusion of Optimists.
Developers present estimates that are optimistic. Executives like the optimistic estimates because they imply that desirable business targets are achievable.
The software project is off and running with no one ever taking a critical look at whether the estimates were well founded in the first place.
While it's impossible to give a perfect estimate, it's a good idea to start with the worst case scenario and extrapolate backwards to the best case.
I'm ashamed to report that I've always used single-point estimates when estimating my work. This is the starting point for many project scheduling disasters.
posts.key_insight: Software estimates fail because everyone in the chain benefits from optimism, so deliberately starting from true worst-case scenarios is the only way to counteract this systemic bias.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques a colleague's icon-cluttered desktop, contrasting the 'portal philosophy' of filling the desktop with useful items against his own preference for never seeing the desktop at all. He argues that the desktop shouldn't be a destination but rather dead space you work over, not on. While acknowledging there's no objectively correct approach, he maintains that treating the desktop as a hub creates unnecessary context-switching that interrupts workflow. His ideal is popup searches and hotkeys that get you directly to what you need without revealing the desktop.
key_points: The 'portal philosophy' treats the desktop as valuable real estate to fill with frequently-needed items
Atwood's counter-philosophy: the desktop should never be visible because you should always be focused on your actual task
Revealing the desktop requires context-switching (Windows-D), which interrupts flow
Ad-hoc searches and hotkeys are superior to desktop icons for accessing resources
Desktop organization is ultimately a religious debate with no objectively correct answer
money_quotes: My goal is to never see the desktop.
I should always have the task I'm working on front and center, not the desktop.
The last thing I want is for my desktop to look like the Yahoo home page.
It's unhealthy to turn the desktop into an artificial destination.

It's like the Las Vegas strip; no matter how many zany attractions they add, eventually visitors have to come to terms with the fact that they've arbitrarily chosen to build those attractions in the middle of a vast, inhospitable desert.
Dead space is never useful. But it's not a destination, either.
posts.key_insight: The desktop should be invisible infrastructure you work over, not a destination you visit—treating it as a portal filled with shortcuts actually creates friction by forcing context switches away from your real work.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents his search for a new laptop that breaks from the corporate ThinkPad mold, establishing specific criteria including Core Duo CPU, sub-5-pound weight, dedicated graphics for Vista, and optional optical drive removal. After evaluating six options including ThinkPad, Samsung, MacBook Pro, Dell, and Sony, he selects the Asus W3J as the winner. The Asus hits all his requirements while offering a clean aluminum design and a swappable bay system. He notes that Asus manufactures Apple's laptops, which shows in the build quality, and shares news of silent spec upgrades for June shipments.
key_points: ThinkPads represent boring corporate conformity - carrying one signals you don't care about your tools
The ideal 2006 laptop needs Core Duo, dedicated graphics for Vista, under 5 pounds, and no mandatory optical drive
MacBook Pro hardware is excellent but wasted if you're only going to run Windows on it
Repeatedly choosing Dell represents 'a catastrophic failure of imagination' despite good value
Asus W3J wins by hitting all criteria plus offering a swappable bay for optical drive, battery, or hard drive
money_quotes: Thinkpads are uninspiring. They're the gray flannel suit of the IT industry.
Every time I pulled the T43 out of my bag, I felt like I was advertising the fact that I didn't give a damn.
Really, who uses optical drives any more? That's an extra half-pound I'd rather not carry around.
We live in a world of platform choices; repeatedly choosing a Dell is a catastrophic failure of imagination.
I don't trust Sony for drivers and support; their stuff is always pretty but vapid.
Asus is the OEM who manufactures Apple's laptops, and it shows.
posts.key_insight: The laptop you carry is a statement about whether you care about your craft, and settling for boring corporate defaults signals indifference.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the niche world of PC pinball simulators, sharing his enthusiasm for playing them on his custom MAME arcade cabinet. He laments that commercial pinball sims are a dying breed, with only a handful offering truly realistic physics. He recommends three standout titles—Pro Pinball series, Addiction Pinball, and Balls of Steel—while noting that community-driven projects like Future Pinball are filling the gap left by commercial developers. The post connects his current hobby back to childhood memories of building virtual pinball machines in Bill Budge's Pinball Construction Set.
key_points: PC pinball simulators are a dying commercial genre, with very few titles offering realistic physics simulation
The Pro Pinball series (especially Timeshock! onward) represents the gold standard for accurate pinball physics
Proper controllers with flipper buttons and nudge controls significantly enhance the pinball sim experience
Community projects like Future Pinball are picking up where commercial releases left off, offering full 3D table creation
The appeal lies in the fascinating challenge of digitally simulating analog, physics-based gameplay
money_quotes: There's something about the completely digital simulation of analog gameplay that fascinates me.

Of the few commercial pinball games that were ever released, only a handful can be considered true simulators, with realistic approximations of the complex newtonian physics at work on a pinball playfield.
As any Guitar Hero fan will tell you, sometimes the controller makes all the difference.
In Big Race USA and Fantastic Journey, you can even cause the ball to pop up off the table and hit the backglass!
The good news is that the community is picking up the slack in commercial pinball sim releases.
Future Pinball truly takes pinball sims to the next level – no more pre-rendered playfields!
posts.key_insight: When commercial software abandons a niche, passionate communities often step in to not only preserve it but push it forward with tools like Future Pinball that empower users to create their own content.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines Christopher Seiwald's 'Seven Pillars of Pretty Code' principles, which argue that visually appealing code tends to work better. While acknowledging the advice is reasonable, Atwood critiques Seiwald's own example code (make.c) as ugly, noting issues like embedded source control comments, fragile block alignments, and poor function naming. The post concludes by questioning whether focusing on cosmetic code formatting misses the bigger picture—that using higher-level languages might be a more meaningful pursuit of code beauty.
key_points: Code formatting preferences are subjective and language-dependent—what looks pretty in one language may look ugly to developers of another
Seiwald's seven principles include keeping columns narrow, breaking code into logical blocks, reducing distractions, and maintaining left-edge structure
Embedded source control information in comments is redundant when source control systems exist
Complex block alignments in code are fragile and difficult to maintain without IDE support
External cosmetic formatting may matter less than internal qualities like choosing appropriate abstraction levels
money_quotes: Code that looks good, works good.
Nothing obscures the essential visual cues more than a shift in style.
Even if your code is inscrutable and your comments jibberish, the reader can at least attempt to triangulate on the actual purpose.
The tighter the scope, the shorter the name. Long, descriptive names may help the first time reader but hinder him every time thereafter.
Clearly, pretty code is in the eye of the beholder.
I'm sure Ruby, Lisp, and Smalltalk aficionados feel the same way about C# code. I know most C# developers can barely force themselves to even look at VB.NET code.
Rather than focusing on the external, cosmetic attributes of the code, shouldn't we be searching for something prettier on the inside? Such as a higher level language?
posts.key_insight: Pretty code formatting is subjective and language-dependent, and obsessing over cosmetic appearance may distract from more fundamental improvements like choosing better abstractions or higher-level languages.

posts.summary: Atwood examines the comparison between Linux and Wikipedia adoption rates to argue that writing skills trump programming skills in importance. He highlights the stark difference in potential contributors: while only about 100,000 people can contribute to Linux, half a billion English speakers could edit Wikipedia. Drawing on Joel Spolsky's advice, he argues that a programmer's ability to communicate ideas—through documentation, specs, and persuasion—determines whether their code has any value. The post concludes that even for programmers, general writing and communication skills are far more important than technical abilities alone.
key_points: Wikipedia's barrier to entry (basic literacy) is dramatically lower than Linux's (programming skill), giving it access to thousands of times more potential contributors
The difference between tolerable and great programmers isn't technical knowledge—it's whether they can communicate their ideas effectively
Code that compilers understand is easy to write; code that humans understand is the real challenge
Brilliant code without clear documentation languishes unused, making communication skills essential for any programmer's work to have impact
Wikipedia achieved greater significance to average users faster than Linux precisely because of its lower contribution barriers
money_quotes: Wikipedia can draw on half a billion potential contributors; only about 100,000 people can code Linux.

The difference between a tolerable programmer and a great programmer is not how many programming languages they know, and it's not whether they prefer Python or Java. It's whether they can communicate their ideas.

There's a lot of wonderful, useful code buried on sourceforge somewhere that nobody uses because it was created by programmers who don't write very well (or don't write at all), and so nobody knows what they've done and their brilliant code languishes.
Programming, like all writing, is just another form of communication.
Writing code that the compiler understands is easy. Writing code that other people understand is far more difficult.
Technical programming skills are certainly important. But general writing and communication skills are far, far more important. Even if you're merely a humble programmer.
posts.key_insight: A programmer's ability to communicate their ideas through writing determines whether their code has any real-world impact, making communication skills more valuable than pure technical ability.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the dogmatic insistence on object-oriented approaches for simple tasks like XML generation. He contrasts 15+ lines of XmlWriter code against a simple 2-line string formatting approach that produces identical output. The post pushes back against developers who consider direct string output like Response.Write to be unprofessional or 'dreadful.' Atwood argues that the verbosity of objects like HtmlTextWriter and XmlTextWriter isn't always warranted, and that the 'write once, run anywhere' promise comes with real costs in complexity. He concludes that sometimes direct rendering is simply clearer and more practical than layering on OO abstractions you'll likely never need.
key_points: Object-oriented approaches can require many times more code than simple string formatting for basic XML/HTML generation
Some developers dogmatically insist everything must go through an object model, regardless of practical costs
The 'write once, run anywhere' promise of adapter patterns adds verbosity and complexity that's often unnecessary
YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It) applies to architectural decisions, not just features
Code readability and simplicity should sometimes trump architectural purity
money_quotes: That seems like a tremendous amount of code to do something relatively simple.
I've worked with developers who insisted that everything had to be generated through an object model, even if the object-oriented way required many times the amount of code.
The idea of 'write once, run anywhere' via a complex set of objects and adapters is a pleasant one, but it also adds a heavy burden of verbosity and complexity to your project.
And you probably aren't gonna need it anyway.
Sometimes it's simpler and clearer to render the HTML or XML directly to the page without all that OO cruft gunking up the works.
posts.key_insight: Architectural purity should never trump practical simplicity—when a 2-line solution is clearer than a 15-line OO approach, choose clarity.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the optimal text column width for readability, questioning whether the traditional 80-character line limit in code still makes sense. He references research from the Software Usability Research Laboratory showing that optimal reading depends on multiple factors: longer lines increase speed while shorter lines improve comprehension, with 45-65 characters being ideal.
