posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts troubleshooting a dual Opteron server experiencing spontaneous reboots after being repurposed for Virtual Server duties. After updating BIOS and drivers resolved one error but not the reboots, he turned to hardware diagnostics using Prime95 and Memtest86+. The testing revealed intermittent memory problems that only manifested with all 8GB installed. The solution was reducing memory speed from 200MHz to 166MHz in BIOS, allowing the full memory complement to operate stably. The post serves as both a troubleshooting guide and a reminder that software isn't always to blame.
key_points: Updating BIOS and drivers should be the first troubleshooting step for hardware issues - the equivalent of 'take two aspirin and call in the morning'
Prime95 is an excellent stability test that will error out almost immediately if CPUs or memory are unstable, making it efficient for troubleshooting
For multi-CPU systems, you must run multiple Prime95 instances to properly stress all processors
Memtest86+ and Prime95 complement each other - Memtest86+ found rare intermittent errors while Prime95 consistently failed immediately on blend tests
Sometimes reducing memory speed (200MHz to 166MHz) can resolve stability issues when removing memory isn't an option
money_quotes: If your PC can't pass an overnight Prime95 run, it absolutely, positively has a hardware problem.
This is always a good first troubleshooting step – it's the hardware equivalent of taking two Aspirins and calling in the morning.
Although software is notoriously unreliable, we can't always blame the software. Sometimes you really do have a hardware problem.
In my experience, Prime95 will error out almost immediately if your CPUs or memory are unstable. This is great for troubleshooting because you know quickly if there's a problem or not.
CPUs are almost never defective; it's usually a heat or power supply related failure.
You can troubleshoot hardware stability quite effectively with the right software: Memtest86+ and Prime95.
posts.key_insight: Hardware problems can masquerade as software issues, but with the right diagnostic tools like Prime95 and Memtest86+, you can definitively identify and resolve stability problems that no amount of software debugging would fix.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines George Miller's famous 1956 paper on the 'magical number seven' in short-term memory and questions its practical significance. He notes that even Miller himself was skeptical about the mystical nature of the number seven, and subsequent research suggests the real limit may be closer to four items. Atwood connects this to group dynamics research showing optimal team sizes of 8-12 people. He concludes that designers shouldn't rely on these magical numbers but should instead aim to eliminate the need for users to remember anything at all, using chunking and good design to reduce cognitive load to zero.
key_points: Miller's famous 'seven plus or minus two' limit on short-term memory may actually be closer to four items according to later research
Phone numbers demonstrate chunking in practice - we remember 555-1212 as groups of 3 and 4 digits, not as 7 individual digits
Optimal group size research suggests 8-12 people is ideal; beyond this, communication complexity explodes and individuals become categories
Well-designed systems should eliminate the need for users to rely on short-term memory rather than just staying under some threshold
Magical numbers are interesting coincidences but shouldn't be design targets - aim for zero memory burden instead
money_quotes: That's only 2.5 bits of information.
I think magical numbers are a red herring.
Tasks that can't be handled by a group of eight to 12 are probably too complex and should be broken down further.
Participation and commitment fall off in larger groups – mobility suffers; leadership doesn't develop naturally but is manipulative and political.
Ideally users shouldn't have to remember anything.
In a well-designed system, you should never need to fall back on your faulty, unreliable short-term memory.
The fewer bits of information users have to remember, the better.
Magical numbers are fun. But the ideal group size is always one, and the ideal number of things I should need to keep in my short-term memory is zero.
posts.key_insight: Don't design systems to accommodate human memory limits - design them so users don't need to remember anything at all.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces the arrival of Coding Horror vinyl stickers and offers them free to readers who email him within a 24-hour window. The stickers are professional die-cut vinyl based on vector art provided by Steve McConnell. The giveaway quickly exceeded capacity at 350 stickers, which Atwood humorously blames on publicity from Mike Gunderloy. The offer was limited to US and Canada due to international postage complexity.
key_points: Custom two-color die-cut vinyl stickers were created from high-resolution vector art provided by Steve McConnell
The giveaway reached its 350-sticker capacity within the promotional window
International shipping complexity led to US/Canada-only availability
money_quotes: As a thank you to my readers, anyone who emails me from now until midnight PDT Wednesday, August 16th gets a free Coding Horror sticker.
Dealing with postage rate scales makes my tiny American brain hurt.
posts.key_insight: Building reader community and engagement sometimes happens through simple gestures like giving away stickers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues passionately against using Visual SourceSafe, despite it still leading adoption surveys in 2006. He contends that SourceSafe gives developers a false sense of security while teaching bad habits like avoiding branching and relying on exclusive locks. The post acknowledges that SourceSafe was adequate in the late 90s but was never architecturally updated for modern practices. Atwood recommends switching to alternatives like Subversion or Team System and provides learning resources for developers wanting to understand modern source control. He frames source control knowledge as fundamental to calling oneself a software engineer.
key_points: SourceSafe remains the most used version control system despite being architecturally stuck in 1999
SourceSafe teaches developers harmful habits: avoiding branching, using exclusive locks, and making easy permanent deletions
CVS shares some of SourceSafe's problems, including lack of atomic check-ins
Source control is the absolute bedrock of software engineering and requires deep, sound knowledge
Free and inexpensive alternatives like Subversion already exist and are vastly superior
money_quotes: If you are serious about the practice of software development, you should not be using SourceSafe.
SourceSafe poisons the minds of software developers.
SourceSafe gives you the illusion of safety and control, while exposing your project to risk.
One of my biggest hurdles has been unlearning all the bad things SourceSafe taught me about source control.
Source control is the absolute bedrock of software engineering.
If my knowledge in this area isn't deep, wide, and fundamentally sound, can I really call myself a software engineer?
Even the latest version, SourceSafe 2005, absolutely reeks of 1999.
But whatever you do, please don't use Visual SourceSafe. Think of the children.
posts.key_insight: Using outdated source control systems doesn't just limit your tools—it fundamentally damages how you think about version control and software engineering practices.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that JavaScript's greatest innovation wasn't a technical feature but its inherent transparency through browser 'View Source' functionality. He contends that this forced sharing of source code created a Darwinian ecosystem where good techniques spread rapidly and bad ones die out. Atwood extends this argument to .NET development, pushing back against complaints about easy decompilation by suggesting code should be even easier to decompile. He concludes that developers should embrace source code transparency by shipping source alongside binaries, arguing that if your business model depends on code obfuscation, it's not much of a business model.
key_points: JavaScript's success stems largely from the 'View Source' menu forcing universal code transparency, not from technical features like closures or XmlHttpRequest
'Code Darwinism' describes how good techniques spread rapidly when source is visible, while bad techniques die out
Decompiling .NET code should be embraced rather than feared - visibility enables fixing bugs, understanding behavior, and learning
Business models dependent on code obfuscation are fundamentally weak
Source code availability provides peace of mind and customer confidence that escrow services cannot match
money_quotes: The view source menu is the ultimate form of open source.
If your business model is that dependent on clever, obfuscated source code tricks, it isn't much of a business model.
The real stroke of genius in JavaScript wasn't closures, or XmlHttpRequest; it was forcing people to share their source code with the community.
Good techniques are seen immediately and reproduce promiscuously. Bad techniques never reproduce and die out.
If you can see the source code, the binary is alive – it can evolve.
JavaScript is the world's most ubiquitous computing runtime.
Let's embrace the power of 'view source' by shipping source code along with our binaries.
posts.key_insight: Forced code transparency through 'View Source' created an evolutionary ecosystem that drove JavaScript's success, and the same principle should be embraced across all software development.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the growing power consumption gap between GPUs and CPUs, noting that video cards are overtaking processors as the largest power consumers in PCs. He analyzes power efficiency data from various graphics cards, highlighting the GeForce 7600 GT as an exceptional value for its performance-per-watt ratio. The post also addresses the practical challenges of passive cooling solutions and warns readers about potential overheating issues. Written during the Windows Vista era, it reflects on how mainstream users would soon need gaming-capable graphics cards for basic OS features.
key_points: Mainstream GPU power consumption is overtaking CPU power consumption, a trend previously only seen in high-end gaming systems
The GeForce 7600 GT offers exceptional power efficiency, delivering strong performance at only 36 watts under load
Passive cooling solutions require excellent case airflow to prevent overheating and system faults
Windows Vista's 3D effects would push mainstream users toward more powerful (and power-hungry) graphics cards
Newer manufacturing technology generally correlates with better power efficiency in graphics cards
money_quotes: Mainstream video card GPUs are about to overtake CPUs as the largest consumers of power inside your PC.
You'll need a moderately fast gaming video card to get the best-looking 3D effects in Windows Vista.
Perhaps the trick is to select a video card that offers the best bang for the watt.
Video cards tend to have small, whiny fans that can spin up to deafening levels under load.
Make sure you have good case airflow if you're going the passive route!
posts.key_insight: When selecting PC components, power efficiency and thermal management deserve as much consideration as raw performance benchmarks.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood criticizes Microsoft for releasing multiple distinct updates to DirectX 9.0c between 2004 and 2006 without changing the version number, making it impossible to determine which actual version is installed. He notes that while all versions maintain backwards compatibility, this practice confuses users and explains why games often demand to reinstall DirectX even when it appears to already be present. The post highlights Vista's eventual move to DirectX 9.0L as a return to sensible versioning.
key_points: Microsoft released numerous DirectX updates over two years while keeping the version number frozen at 9.0c
The lack of version differentiation makes it impossible to identify which actual release is installed
Games repeatedly demanding DirectX reinstallation is a symptom of this versioning confusion
Vista finally addressed the issue by incrementing to version 9.0L
money_quotes: So do you want version 9.0c, 9.0c, 9.0c, or perhaps... 9.0c?
Why is Microsoft abusing version numbers this way?
It's impossible to tell what version of DirectX 9.0 you're actually running.
I've installed several games over the past year which inexplicably demanded to re-install DirectX 9.0c; now I know why.
At least Vista stops the madness by finally changing the version number to DirectX 9.0L.
posts.key_insight: Version numbers exist to differentiate releases, and freezing them while continuing to ship updates creates confusion that undermines their entire purpose.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the meaningfulness of user count metrics for social networking platforms, using MySpace's claim of 100 million members as a starting point. He cites Robert Scoble's investigation of Windows Live Spaces, which found most member pages to be 'ghost towns,' and highlights Dare Obasanjo's perspective that actual usage matters more than signups. Using LiveJournal's transparent statistics as evidence, Atwood shows that only 1 in 5 accounts are 'active in some way' and only 1 in 10 updated recently. He concludes that reported user counts should be divided by five or ten to get realistic usage estimates, and calls for platforms to be more transparent about actual engagement.
key_points: Headline user counts for free services are essentially meaningless without usage data
LiveJournal's stats reveal only ~10-20% of accounts show any real activity
Multiple accounts per person further inflate already misleading numbers
Platforms should report actual usage metrics rather than total signups
A realistic estimate of active users requires dividing total accounts by 5-10x
money_quotes: Who cares how many users signed up for your free service? How many users actually use it?
I have a hard time believing that 1 in 3 Americans could conceivably be MySpace users.

We are more interested in the number of people who actually use our service and get value added to their lives by being able to share, discuss and communicate with their friends, families and total strangers.
Most of the member pages he visited were virtual ghost towns.

If the LiveJournal statistics are representative of other social networking websites, then we should immediately divide the total number of 'users,' 'spaces,' or 'accounts,' by five. Maybe even by ten!
posts.key_insight: Total registered users is a vanity metric; actual platform value should be measured by active, engaged usage, which is typically 5-10x smaller than reported user counts.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares lessons learned from building quiet PCs after his disastrous first home theater PC was so loud he had to turn the volume to 11 to drown it out. He argues that building a powerful yet silent PC is a genuine engineering challenge that requires starting with cool-running components rather than trying to fix noise problems after the fact. The post provides a systematic seven-point guide covering component selection, fan management, aftermarket cooling, hard drive dampening, and noise-reduction materials. Atwood cautions against the allure of fully passive cooling, noting that a few slow-moving fans can achieve 90% of the silence at a fraction of the effort and risk.
key_points: Heat and noise are fundamentally linked - CPU, video card, and power supply account for 90% of system heat and noise
Larger fans are quieter than small fans, and fan speed control is essential for noise reduction
Aftermarket coolers for CPUs and video cards are far more efficient than stock models
Hard drives must be dampened to prevent vibration transfer - never allow metal-to-metal contact with the case
Fully passive cooling is impractical for most builders; near-silent fans achieve 90% of the results with far less effort
money_quotes: Anyone can build a high-powered rig that sounds like a jet taking off. Building a high-powered rig that's so quiet your wife can't tell when it's turned on or off – now that's an accomplishment!
It's as fundamental as the first law of thermodynamics: heat has to be exhausted from the system; more heat equals more noise.
One of the most useful noise diagnostics is to stop every fan in the system, one by one, using your Mark I finger.
And remember, kids, always stop fans by touching them in the center, not in those whirling blades!
They treat the symptom and not the source; ideally you want to quiet the thing that is making noise – not hide it behind a layer of dampening material.
I'm ashamed to admit that I have something of an eggcrate foam fetish.
You can achieve 90 percent of the results you'd get with completely passive cooling using a few nearly-silent, slow-moving fans – at a fraction of the effort and risk!
posts.key_insight: The key to building a quiet PC is addressing noise at its source by selecting cool-running components from the start, rather than trying to mask the problem with dampening materials after the fact.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood provides a statistical breakdown of the free Coding Horror sticker distribution, showing geographic reach across the US, Canada, and internationally. The post documents 320 US requests across 38 states, 49 Canadian requests, and 21 international orders from 11 countries. California and Texas led US orders, while Ontario topped Canadian provinces. The post also showcases reader-submitted photos of stickers in action, including one humorously placed next to actual problematic code.
key_points: 320 sticker requests came from the US, with 38 of 50 states represented
California (32) and Texas (26) led state-by-state requests, with Washington and Florida tied for third
Canada contributed 49 requests, with Ontario (15) and Alberta (11) leading provinces
International reach extended to 11 countries including UK, Australia, Germany, and Latvia
The post transitions from statistics to community engagement by showcasing reader photos
money_quotes: Contrary to popular belief, I am fully aware that the United States isn't the only country in the world.
Will Sullivan's is appropriately positioned next to a real coding horror.
If you work with me, I'll give you a sticker. But only grudgingly.
posts.key_insight: Building a developer community often starts with small gestures like free stickers that create tangible connections between an online presence and its readers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that companies undermine their own investment by paying developers high salaries while providing inadequate tools and working conditions. He proposes a 'Programmer's Bill of Rights' that guarantees developers dual monitors, fast PCs, choice of input devices, comfortable chairs, fast internet, and quiet working conditions. The post frames these not as luxuries but as fundamental requirements for developer productivity. Atwood emphasizes that fixing these issues is neither expensive nor difficult compared to the salary investment companies already make, and encourages developers to demand these rights or find employers who respect them.
key_points: Companies waste money paying high salaries while crippling productivity with poor hardware and working conditions
Dual monitors, fast PCs with ample memory, and fast internet are essential productivity multipliers
Developers should choose their own keyboard and mouse, treating them as craftsman's tools they take ownership of
Quiet working conditions are critical because programming requires sustained mental concentration and flow state
These improvements are inexpensive relative to developer salaries and have outsized impact on productivity
money_quotes: It's unbelievable to me that a company would pay a developer $60-$100k in salary, yet cripple them with terrible working conditions and crusty hand-me-down hardware.
Time spent staring at a progress bar is wasted time.
Programmers should have the same relationship with their mouse and keyboard – they are the essential, workaday tools we use to practice our craft and should be treated as such.
Sure, you hire developers primarily for their giant brains, but don't forget your developers' other assets.
Good programmers never write what they can steal.
Programmers cannot work effectively in an interrupt-driven environment.
You can either change your company, or you can change your company.
posts.key_insight: Investing in developer tools and working conditions is a trivially cheap way to maximize the return on your much larger investment in developer salaries.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares a humorous description from Damien Katz about the frustrating reality of writing technical documentation. The process involves technical writers struggling to extract information from engineers who are initially unresponsive but become highly critical once draft documentation exists. The piece satirizes how engineers only reveal technical details when correcting what they perceive as errors. Atwood acknowledges having experienced both sides of this dysfunctional dynamic throughout his career.
key_points: Engineers tend to be unresponsive when asked directly how systems work, leaving technical writers to figure things out alone
The most reliable way to extract technical information from engineers is to write something wrong and let them correct it
Technical documentation emerges through an iterative process of writing, criticism, and revision rather than clear upfront communication
The dysfunction in documentation processes is universal and widely recognized in the industry
money_quotes: Ask engineer how the damn thing works. Deafening silence. Crickets. Tumbleweed.
Just start writing something. Anything.
Watch engineer become quite upset at how badly you've missed the point of everything.
As the engineer berates you, in between insults he will also throw off nuggets of technical information.
Collect these nuggets, as they are the only reliable technical information you will receive.
It's funny because it's true.
posts.key_insight: The most effective way to get technical information from engineers is not to ask them directly, but to write something incorrect and harvest the corrections from their indignant responses.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief, whimsical post about Jeff Atwood's fascination with the sporkfe - a combination spoon, fork, and knife utensil. He notes the Australian commercial version called 'Splayds' has existed for 50 years, but prefers the name 'sporkfe' since people don't typically eat with 'blades.' The post ends with self-deprecating humor about his impulse purchase.
key_points: The sporkfe combines spoon, fork, and knife into one utensil
The concept has been commercialized in Australia as 'Splayds' for 50 years
Atwood prefers 'sporkfe' over 'splade' as a naming choice
The purchase was an impulse buy he can't fully explain
money_quotes: It's a spoon. It's a fork. It's a knife.
Really, when was the last time you ate your food with a blade?
I have no idea why I bought these.
But at least I saved myself from the indignity of owning The Mother of All Swiss Army Knives.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes we're drawn to objects that cleverly combine multiple functions, even when we can't articulate why.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that programmers should avoid manipulating thread priorities, using a real-world example where setting a thread to BelowNormal priority caused a program to hang for over two minutes on single-processor machines. He references Joe Duffy's expertise on threading to explain how priority manipulation can cause priority inversion and thread starvation. The post notes that nearly all processes run at Normal priority for good reason, and that dual-core CPUs essentially serve as a hardware workaround for poorly written threaded code. Atwood concludes that developers should trust the operating system's scheduler rather than trying to outsmart it.
key_points: Setting thread priorities can cause unexpected starvation where higher-priority threads block lower-priority ones from ever completing their work
Nearly all processes on any system run at Normal priority, with only essential system processes elevated - this uniformity exists for good reason
Dual-core processors act as protection against poorly written threaded programs by providing a spare CPU when one gets pegged at 100%
The operating system scheduler is written by experts and attempting to outsmart it with priority tweaks almost always backfires
Even explicit Sleep calls don't help when priority starvation occurs - the lower priority thread simply never gets scheduled
money_quotes: Programmers love to futz around with thread priorities. As if programming with threads wasn't already dangerous enough, we've got to get in there and tweak thread priorities to make things run... er... 'better.'
This little threading demo highlights one of the reasons a dual-core computer is so desirable – it protects you from poorly written programs.
Hardware fixes to software problems are never pretty.
Messing with thread priorities is actually a very dangerous practice, and this is only one illustration of what can go wrong.
The consumer will just spin forever and unless a free CPU opens up, the producer will never produce. Oops!
Set up your threads at normal priority and let the operating system deal with scheduling them.
No matter how brilliant a programmer you may be, I can practically guarantee you won't be able to outsmart the programmers who wrote the scheduler in your operating system.
posts.key_insight: Thread priority manipulation is a dangerous optimization that typically backfires, and developers should trust the operating system's scheduler rather than attempting to outsmart it.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the growing challenge for hobbyist game programmers as budgets have exploded from $200,000 in 1992 to $10 million for next-generation titles. He argues that this risk-aversion has made publishers unwilling to fund innovative games, meaning groundbreaking titles like Myst, Civilization, or Sim City would never get funded today. The post highlights the indie games movement as a potential solution, along with new platforms like XNA Game Studio and mobile devices that could lower barriers to entry. Atwood reflects nostalgically on how many programmers, including himself, got their start by wanting to create games rather than just play them.
key_points: Game development budgets have increased 50x since 1992, making publishers extremely risk-averse and unwilling to fund innovative titles
The indie games movement offers hope by prioritizing gameplay over glitz and innovation over graphical trickery
In the early days, one person with one vision could create a complete game in six months using just BASIC
New platforms like XNA Game Studio and mobile devices may revive opportunities for hobbyist game programmers
The transition from game player to game programmer drove the industry in its early days but has become increasingly difficult
money_quotes: Today, Myst, Civilization, or Sim City would never get funded.
There's always been the calling to jump from just playing games to creating them.

It's crazy, of course, because the rush of playing a great game doesn't carry over to spending twenty straight hours in the basement trying to figure out why a level initialization routine fails ten percent of the time.
One person, one vision, and six months later a finished product that was snapped up by a publisher – pure creation. A new alternative for would-be novelists.
The dream is still alive in these days of 32-bit processors and 3D accelerators, but over the years the reality behind it has quietly slipped away and few have stopped to notice.
Creating a viable 'independent games' movement, where people can experiment, at lower budgets and with less risk, on quirky, offbeat, innovative games.
Is the dream of jumping from game player to game programmer still alive? It's certainly how I got my start in programming.
posts.key_insight: The explosion of game development costs has nearly killed the path from hobbyist programmer to game creator, but indie movements and new platforms may yet preserve this essential pipeline that built the industry.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while VB.NET's verbose syntax is more readable than C#'s terse inherited-from-C syntax, the differences are ultimately minor and shouldn't determine project success. The post's central thesis is that programming languages should not attempt to mimic natural human languages, using AppleScript as a cautionary example. AppleScript's English-like syntax creates a false sense of accessibility while actually obscuring how the language really works. Languages like Python and JavaScript succeed by being abstract but obvious, prioritizing clarity over English-likeness. The ultimate goal should be writing code that makes it clear what's actually happening, regardless of which language you choose.
key_points: VB.NET's explicit keywords (MustInherit, NotInheritable, Overridable) are more readable than C#'s cryptic equivalents (abstract, sealed, virtual), especially for maintenance programmers
Programming languages optimized for English-likeness actually become harder to use because the natural language metaphor obscures the underlying mechanics
AppleScript's attempt to look like English paragraphs backfired—you still need to understand the computer science concepts, but the syntax hides them
Python and JavaScript succeed by being abstract but obvious, rather than trying to appear English-like
The choice between similar languages like VB.NET and C# is ultimately meaningless; what matters is optimizing for clarity
money_quotes: Computer languages, however verbose you make them, shouldn't try to become proxy versions of spoken languages.
The language syntax is optimized for English-likeness, rather than being optimized for making it clear just what the f**k is actually going on.
AppleScript's natural language metaphor turns out to be more of a curse than a blessing.
Void is for sci-fi geeks; nothing is for human beings.
You can write FORTRAN in any language, so choose whatever language you're most comfortable with and optimize for making it clear what the f**k is going on.
Python and JavaScript's syntaxes are much more abstract than AppleScript's, but they are also more obvious.
If the success or failure of your project hinges on the minor syntax differences between two virtually identical .NET languages, you have much deeper problems than choice of language.
posts.key_insight: Programming languages should optimize for making code behavior obvious and clear, not for superficial resemblance to natural human language.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his approach to testing Windows Vista RC1 by booting from an external hard drive, and provides a practical guide to external drive connectivity options. He compares throughput numbers across USB 2.0, Firewire, and SATA interfaces, showing SATA's significant performance advantage. The post explains eSATA connectors as more robust versions of internal SATA and recommends enclosures that support both USB and SATA for flexibility. Atwood concludes by recommending Western Digital Raptor 10,000 RPM drives for maximum external drive performance.
key_points: SATA provides significantly better throughput (55 MB/sec) compared to USB 2.0 (35 MB/sec) and Firewire 400 (29 MB/sec)
eSATA connectors are more robust, better shielded versions of regular SATA connectors but have different shapes and aren't interchangeable
Dual-interface enclosures supporting both USB 2.0 and SATA offer the best flexibility for different use cases
Western Digital Raptor 10,000 RPM drives provide noticeably better performance and run quieter and cooler than expected
money_quotes: An even better option is to route your internal SATA connectors to the back of your PC and plug in the external hard drive using its native SATA interface for maximum performance.
The performance difference between USB, Firewire, and SATA can be dramatic.
I've switched to Raptors as my system drive on both my work and home PC, and the difference is quite noticeable.
They're also much quieter and cooler than I expected for such fast drives.
The Raptors aren't cheap, but you'll see the value of these drives the first time you boot up.
posts.key_insight: For maximum external drive performance, use native SATA/eSATA connections rather than USB or Firewire, and invest in high-RPM drives like the Western Digital Raptor series.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood investigates why burning a Vista RC1 DVD took nearly an hour and made his PC sluggish. He discovers that Windows had automatically downgraded his IDE transfer mode from Ultra DMA to PIO mode after detecting CRC errors. While this safety mechanism makes sense for hard drives with actual hardware problems, for optical drives it often triggers simply from reading scratched discs. After manually resetting the transfer mode, his burn time dropped from 56 minutes to just 4 minutes.
key_points: Windows automatically downgrades IDE/ATA transfer mode after detecting six CRC errors, displaying a yellow alert in Device Manager
CRC errors on hard drives indicate serious hardware problems, but on optical drives they often just mean a scratched disc was read
The transfer mode can be reset through Device Manager by selecting 'Let BIOS select transfer mode' or by uninstalling and redetecting the channel
PIO mode is dramatically slower than DMA mode - in this case, 56 minutes vs 4 minutes for the same DVD burn
money_quotes: I began to suspect something was awry with the IDE controller that the DVD-R drive is connected to.
This behavior is, of course, by design.
CRC errors are very dangerous for a hard drive – that means you've got some serious hardware problems. But for a DVD or CD drive, it probably just means you tried to read a scratched disc.
Don't worry, uninstalling won't cause any problems. Just reboot and the channel will be redetected with default settings.
Friends don't let friends use Programmed I/O Mode.
posts.key_insight: Windows' automatic IDE transfer mode downgrade is a well-intentioned safety feature that can severely cripple optical drive performance due to false positives from scratched discs.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques the overuse of modal alert dialogs in software, arguing they interrupt user flow without providing meaningful value. Using examples from Notepad2's search functionality and Windows Vista's delete confirmation, he demonstrates how these interruptions treat users as incapable of understanding context. He references Alan Cooper's concept of 'stopping the proceedings with idiocy' to frame unnecessary dialogs as a form of user-hostile design. The post concludes by noting that the web has thrived without dialog boxes, suggesting desktop software should follow suit.
key_points: Modal alert dialogs for routine operations like 'search wrapped' or 'not found' destroy user workflow by interrupting flow state
The delete confirmation dialog is especially absurd because the action is fully recoverable via the recycle bin
Users train themselves to mindlessly click through dialogs without reading them, making the dialogs both annoying and ineffective
Alan Cooper identifies interrupting user flow for no good reason as 'stopping the proceedings with idiocy'
The web has operated dialog-free for years and users don't miss them, proving they're unnecessary
money_quotes: Every time you send your users to an alert dialog, you have failed them.
In a perfect world, we should never see a single alert dialog. Ever.
It's the GUI equivalent of that annoying little kid from Jerry Maguire.
Interrupting a user's flow for no good reason is stopping the proceedings with idiocy and is one of the most disruptive forms of excise.
This alert dialog is utterly superfluous. It's just another button I have to click through to get my work done.
She did not read one single word of the dialog box text.
The web has been a dialog-free world for years, and nobody seems to miss them very much.
posts.key_insight: Alert dialogs are a symptom of lazy design that treats users as incompetent while simultaneously training them to ignore important information.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits Nathan Myhrvold's 1997 keynote about software's fundamental nature, finding his 'four laws of software' still relevant nearly a decade later. Myhrvold's central metaphor is that software behaves like a gas, expanding to fill any container, with growth limited only by Moore's Law and human ambition. Atwood notes one key shift: in the multi-core era, the burden of scaling has moved from hardware to software. The post concludes with a whimsical tangent about how human genetic uniqueness can fit on a floppy disk.
key_points: Software behaves like a gas, always expanding to fill available hardware capacity
Software growth and Moore's Law exist in a symbiotic relationship—each drives the other
Software development exists in a state of 'Perpetual Crisis' where rising complexity is constantly absorbed by rising customer expectations
The multi-core era has shifted the scaling burden from hardware limitations to software complexity
Human genetic uniqueness (~1.2MB) fits on a floppy disk, and all human genetic diversity fits in 3.7TB
money_quotes: Software always expands to fit whatever container it is stored in.
People buy new hardware because the software requires it.
Software development will never be easy; new software always has to push against the current complexity boundary if it wants to be commercially successful.
The burden of scaling now falls on the software.
The individually unique part of the human Genome can be stored in ~1.2 megabytes. Thus, you fit on a 3.5″ floppy disk.
posts.key_insight: Software development exists in perpetual crisis because success requires constantly pushing against complexity boundaries that expand with customer expectations.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the hardware requirements for Windows Vista RC1, noting it demands 10x the drive space, 8x the memory, and 4x the CPU power compared to Windows XP. He highlights Microsoft's new Windows Experience Index (WEI) as a surprisingly well-designed benchmarking tool that scores system performance. The WEI cleverly uses the lowest subscore as the overall rating, intuitively showing users their system's weakest component. While acknowledging the tool's practical value for identifying upgrade priorities, Atwood ends with characteristic humor about how the score will inevitably become another metric for bragging rights.
key_points: Vista's hardware requirements represent a massive jump: 10x storage, 8x RAM, and 4x CPU over XP, plus meaningful video card requirements for the first time
The Windows Experience Index uses the lowest subscore as the overall rating, making it immediately clear which component limits system performance
Despite increased requirements, Vista-capable PCs were available cheaply, with workarounds like USB drives for ReadyBoost cache
The WEI scoring mechanism is more meaningful than synthetic benchmarks because improvements directly correlate with user experience
Gaming represents a caveat in the scoring system since video card ratings matter differently for Aero/WPF apps versus actual games
money_quotes: Vista requires 10x the drive space, 8x the memory, and 4x the CPU power.
Your PC's performance is only as good as the slowest subsystem.
Unlike some of the new Microsoft Vista features, this one is remarkably well thought out.
When these scores improve, users win.
Just kidding. My WEI is bigger than yours.
posts.key_insight: A system benchmark that highlights your weakest component rather than averaging scores is more useful because it shows exactly where an upgrade will have the most impact.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques web developers who deliberately degrade their sites for Internet Explorer 6 users, coining this behavior 'technological racism.' He builds on Brian Kuhn's concept of technocentrism—the tendency for developers to identify so strongly with their preferred technologies that they become zealots. While acknowledging IE6's genuine problems, Atwood argues that punitive tactics like rendering sites in black-and-white for IE users are counterproductive and evoke uncomfortable parallels with historical discrimination. He advocates instead for positive evangelism that makes alternatives more attractive rather than attacking users of the status quo.
key_points: Technocentrism—identifying with particular technology 'cultures'—leads developers to become zealots who wage 'technology jihads' against competing platforms
Some web developers deliberately degraded their sites for IE6 users, showing content in black-and-white or ugly fonts while Firefox users got the full experience
While IE6 was genuinely problematic (the 'new Netscape 4.7x'), punitive tactics against users are counterproductive and ethically questionable
Using color-based discrimination between browsers evokes uncomfortable parallels with racism and segregation
Positive evangelism and making alternatives attractive is more effective than attacking the status quo
money_quotes: Participation and identification with these cultures brings with it a tendency to look at the world primarily from the perspective of one's own technological culture, or technocentrism.
We can all co-exist without all of us ending up resorting to some sort of technology jihad.

Creating a website that intentionally and maliciously makes content look worse in Internet Explorer 6, well, that's crossing the line from technocentrism into the disturbing realm of technological racism.
The zealotry and vitriol of technological racism is not a particularly effective way to realize change.
The best way to get rid of IE6 is through gentle evangelism.
Don't waste your time attacking the status quo. Instead, spend your time making the alternatives more attractive by supporting and encouraging them.
You'll always get more flies with honey than vinegar.
posts.key_insight: Technology advocacy succeeds through positive evangelism that highlights alternatives, not through punitive measures that alienate the very users you're trying to convert.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critically examines Joel Spolsky's recent controversial decisions, particularly the revelation that FogBugz is written in Wasabi, a proprietary in-house language based on VBScript. Atwood argues that creating your own programming language is a 'toxic decision' that contradicts Joel's previous sensible advice. He further challenges Joel's claim that Ruby would be too slow for FogBugz, pointing out logical inconsistencies in using a language that compiles to VBScript while citing performance concerns. The post questions whether Joel, once a revered voice in software development, has lost his way.
key_points: Writing your own proprietary programming language is a dangerous decision that contradicts sound software development principles
Joel's performance arguments against Ruby are inconsistent since Wasabi compiles to VBScript, which isn't known for speed
Performance-critical code sections can be written in compiled languages and called from other languages - you don't need one language for everything
Rejecting an entire language based on one small performance-critical section violates the 90/10 rule of optimization
Even respected industry figures can make decisions that seem to contradict their own established wisdom
money_quotes: Writing your own language is absolutely beyond the pale.
It's a toxic decision that is so completely at odds with Joel's previous excellent and sane advice on software development that people literally thought he was joking.
You couldn't possibly have heard it, but that was the sound of fifty thousand programmers' heads simultaneously exploding.
Never mind the fact that your company's flagship application is still written in a freaky custom language based on VBScript that only three people in the world know how to program.
I understand the philosophy that developer cycles are more important than cpu cycles, but frankly that's just a bumper-sticker slogan and not fair to the people who are complaining about performance.
Is there some law that states all code for a web application must be in the same exact language?
I reject this new, highly illogical Joel Spolsky. I demand the immediate return of the sage, sane, wise Joel Spolsky of years past.
posts.key_insight: Even the most respected voices in software development can make decisions that contradict their own advice, and the community should critically evaluate ideas based on merit rather than the reputation of who proposes them.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores Windows Vista's ReadyBoost feature, which uses USB flash drives to augment PC performance by caching the system pagefile. He examines why flash memory alone can't replace hard drives yet—citing speed limitations, cost, and durability concerns—but argues that hybrid drives combining both technologies represent the best near-term solution. Through benchmarking three different USB drives, he demonstrates the significant performance gap between cheap and quality flash memory, particularly in write speeds and small file transfers. He concludes that flash memory and traditional hard drives have complementary strengths, with flash excelling at random access while hard drives dominate sequential speeds.
key_points: ReadyBoost caches the compressed, encrypted pagefile on USB flash drives to bypass hard drive access for improved performance
Flash memory has three key advantages: decent read/write speeds, extremely fast random access times, and very low power consumption
Three factors prevent flash from replacing hard drives: speed (still slower than HDDs), cost per megabyte, and limited write durability (around 100,000 write cycles)
Hybrid drives with embedded flash cache (ReadyDrive) are even better than ReadyBoost because flash can be read before system boot
Cheap flash drives have dramatically worse performance than quality ones, with small file transfers being particularly painful on budget devices
money_quotes: Flash memory literally wears out after a fixed number of writes, usually 100,000 or so. Hard drives last many orders of magnitude longer.
Cheap flash drives are cheap for a reason – they skimp on performance.
There's a big disparity between read and write performance on flash drives. And small files are disproportionately painful to transfer through these devices.
Flash memory is exceptionally strong at random access; my fast WD Raptor drive can't touch these scores.
Traditional hard drives and flash memory are quite complimentary; they're strong in different areas.
Flash drives are the future. They will definitely replace hard drives in almost all low end and low power devices.
The price difference between the best performers and the worst performers isn't much, so you might as well buy the fast ones.
posts.key_insight: Flash memory and traditional hard drives have fundamentally different strengths—random access versus sequential speed—making hybrid solutions the optimal approach until flash technology matures enough to dominate entirely.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood discusses the value and dangers of software metrics, drawing from his experience surveying a large codebase. While static code analysis tools like nDepend and FxCop can identify potential trouble spots through metrics like cyclomatic complexity and method size, the real challenge lies in interpreting and acting on this data responsibly. Atwood warns against becoming a 'metrics maid' who uses measurements as a reward/punishment system, arguing that the Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle applies to development metrics only when they're weaponized. He advocates for a 'wall of metrics' approach where teams can collectively discuss and reflect on measurements rather than having them imposed punitively.
key_points: Static code analysis tools serve as 'software tricorders' that help developers navigate unfamiliar codebases by identifying potential trouble spots
Basic metrics like cyclomatic complexity, method size, and parameter counts can be valuable, but advanced metrics like code coverage offer even more insight
The temptation to use metrics as a reward/punishment system undermines their value and breeds resentment
Metrics can be beneficial even without explicit action - public visibility enables team discussion and reflection
The Heisenberg principle applies to software metrics only when they're used punitively, not when used collaboratively
money_quotes: Landing on a planet populated entirely by an alien ecosystem of source code can be overwhelming.
There's a huge temptation to become a meter maid – to use the metrics as a reward or punishment system.
Responsible use of the metrics is just as important as collecting them in the first place.
If Joe wrote a method with a cyclomatic complexity of 52, then he better get slapped with a complexity ticket, right? No excess complexity in the simplicity zone, you idiot!
I believe this is true for software development metrics only if you are using that metric to reward or punish.
Recording metrics on your project can be beneficial even if you don't explicitly act on them.
Having a public 'wall of metrics' might be a better idea. It can be a focal point for discussion about what the metrics mean to the team.
posts.key_insight: Software metrics are most valuable when used as conversation starters for team reflection rather than as weapons for individual judgment.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his current IDE font and color scheme preferences, including his adoption of ClearType and the zenburn color scheme as a dark alternative. He provides downloadable Visual Studio 2005 settings files and detailed instructions for importing and exporting font and color configurations. The post advocates for a community-driven 'Hot or Not' site for IDE color schemes where developers can share and rate their setups. Atwood also proposes a standardized code sample for comparing schemes that tests font legibility issues like distinguishing O from 0 and I from l.
key_points: Atwood has finally embraced ClearType after previously having a love-hate relationship with it
The zenburn vim color scheme offers a good dark background alternative to light themes
A standardized code sample is valuable for comparing IDE schemes because it highlights font legibility issues
Avoiding extreme contrast (pure white on pure black) improves code readability
The developer community would benefit from a centralized site for sharing and rating IDE color schemes
money_quotes: I've finally given in to the inevitability of ClearType.
I think it's a nice dark background yin to my light background yang.
What we really need is for some enterprising coder to create a 'Hot or Not' site for IDE color schemes.
The only specific advice I have for you is to avoid too much contrast – don't use pure white on pure black, or vice versa.
That's why my background is a light grey and not white.
posts.key_insight: IDE font and color scheme choices matter for code readability, and the developer community would benefit from standardized ways to share, compare, and evaluate these configurations.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques Windows Vista's excessive use of 'Windows' and 'Microsoft' prefixes throughout the operating system's UI, arguing that this redundant branding clutters the user experience. He praises Vista's new start menu search feature while pointing out that removing the repetitive namespace words would make navigation significantly easier. He extends this criticism to Visual Studio's add reference dialog, which compounds the problem by only supporting 'starts with' search rather than containing search. The post uses these examples to illustrate how corporate branding priorities can directly harm software usability.
key_points: Qualifying every Vista menu item with 'Windows' or 'Microsoft' creates unnecessary visual noise that hinders navigation
Vista's containing search in the start menu is a major usability improvement over XP's approach
Visual Studio's add reference dialog suffers from the same namespace pollution, made worse by limited search functionality
Marketing-driven naming conventions often conflict with user experience best practices
Repeated words, when overused, become meaningless noise rather than useful information
money_quotes: What's with all the Windows noise?
Wouldn't that list be so much easier to navigate if we deleted the words 'Microsoft' and 'Windows' from each entry?
I'm sure the very suggestion of dropping those key branding words will drive the marketing weasels apoplectic.
But who's more important? The users, or your marketing weasels?
Repeated words, if they're repeated often enough, are just babbling noise.
This is a concrete example of how unnecessary namespacing hurts usability.
Thank goodness the System namespace is actually named System and not 'Microsoft.Windows.dotNet.System.'
posts.key_insight: When branding requirements override user experience design, the result is cluttered interfaces where repeated marketing words become meaningless noise that actively hinders usability.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the common assumption that developers spend most of their time writing code, arguing instead that understanding existing code dominates their work. He cites research showing developers spend far more time modifying and comprehending code than creating new code from scratch. Atwood suggests that rewriting code is often the primary way developers achieve understanding, invoking Feynman's maxim 'What I cannot create, I do not understand.' He provocatively questions whether source code is even the best way to understand an application, suggesting that observing user behavior might be more illuminating than reading the code itself.
key_points: Developers spend approximately 5x more time modifying code than writing new code, and 3x more time understanding code than modifying it
Rewriting code may be a necessary cognitive process for most developers to truly understand how something works
Reading about how something works is a poor substitute for the experience of building it yourself
Source code may not be the best artifact for understanding an application—observing user behavior might be more revealing
Even developers who wrote the code themselves often struggle to explain exactly how their application works
money_quotes: What I cannot create, I do not understand.
Understanding code is by far the activity at which professional developers spend most of their time.
It's not that developers want to rewrite everything; it's that very few developers are smart enough to understand code without rewriting it.
New code becomes old code almost instantly. Write some new code. Go for coffee. All of sudden you've got old code.
Reading about how a wheel works is a poor substitute for the experience of driving around on a few wheels of your own creation.
Is source code truly the best way to understand an application?
Maybe the best way to understand an application is, paradoxically, to ignore the source code altogether.
posts.key_insight: True understanding of code often requires rewriting it, because comprehension and creation are fundamentally linked—and sometimes observing behavior reveals more than reading source code ever could.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood questions the logic behind massive 'desktop replacement' laptops, using Laptop magazine's humorous review of the 20-pound Dell XPS M2010 as a springboard. He argues that portability is the fundamental value proposition of laptop computers, making giant heavy machines somewhat pointless. Drawing on his own experience with various laptops ranging from 3 to 5.5 pounds, he concludes that ultraportables offer the best tradeoff between capability and the actual benefit of being portable.
key_points: The Dell XPS M2010 at 20 pounds with a 20-inch screen represents an absurd extreme in 'portable' computing
Desktop replacement laptops that weigh 10+ pounds defeat the core purpose of notebook computers
Some laptops sold without batteries ('Mobile PCs') that must stay plugged in reveal the contradiction in the category
Personal experience suggests 5.5 pounds is the absolute maximum acceptable weight for a truly portable laptop
Ultraportables in the 2-3 pound range represent the ideal balance of portability and function
money_quotes: Isn't *portability* the whole point of a notebook computer?
I never quite understood the fascination of those giant 10+ pound desktop replacement laptops.
You can even buy battery-less laptops that must be plugged in – so-called 'Mobile PCs.'
That's absolutely as large as I'm willing to go for a laptop.
Travelling with it was effortless.
At nearly double the weight it's also a more noticeable burden.
posts.key_insight: A laptop that sacrifices portability for desktop-level specs defeats the entire purpose of being a laptop in the first place.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the surprisingly young age of software development as a profession, noting that while programming languages date back to the 1950s, modern development practices only emerged in the late 1990s. He uses the Redwine-Riddle maturation model to explain why technologies like source control took 15-20 years to become mainstream. The post argues that design patterns should be viewed as signposts to language deficiencies rather than skills to master, and that the real bottleneck in software progress isn't hardware but human adoption speed.
key_points: Modern software development practices (Agile, refactoring, design patterns) are remarkably recent, mostly emerging between 1994-2001
Source control didn't achieve mainstream adoption until around 1999, despite CVS existing since 1986, following the Redwine-Riddle prediction of 15-20 years for technology maturation
Design patterns should be seen as symptoms of language deficiencies rather than solutions to celebrate and memorize
The pace of technological change has accelerated, but human adoption remains the bottleneck
Even 'new' languages like Ruby and JavaScript are barely teenagers, while foundational languages like FORTRAN and C are decades old
money_quotes: Even though we collectively have over fifty years of experience under our belt, the profession of software development is still very much in its infancy.
I believe that source control was not widely prevalent until 1999.
They found that it typically takes 15-20 years for a technology to evolve from concept formulation to the point where it's ready for popularization.

The best response to identification of these patterns is to ask what defects in those languages cause the patterns to be necessary, and how the languages might provide better support for solving these kinds of problems.
Multiple implementations of the same idea are almost always a mistake in programming.
What we haven't been able to cope with so well is how long it takes for the human beings to catch up with the hardware.
These patterns should be seen as defects or missing features in Java and C++.
posts.key_insight: Software development as a mature profession is far younger than we assume, and the real constraint on progress isn't technological capability but the 15-20 years it takes for humans to adopt and mainstream new ideas.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood enthusiastically recommends Company of Heroes as one of the best real-time strategy games ever made, noting its 95% review average puts it on track to be a top 10 PC game of all time. He was so engaged that he completed every optional objective in the campaign. The game features incredible graphics, destructible environments, and superior AI tactics that elevate the RTS genre. He also notes it's one of the first games with Microsoft's 'Games for Windows' certification and integrates well with Vista.
key_points: Company of Heroes has a 95% review average, putting it on track to be one of the top 10 best reviewed PC games of all time
The game elevates the RTS genre through graphical detail, destructible levels, and unparalleled unit intelligence
A substantial 1.8GB demo is available offering unlimited skirmish mode and two single player maps
It's one of the first games with Microsoft's 'Games for Windows' certification, integrating with Vista's Games menu
money_quotes: It's easily one of the best games of the year for the PC. And it's quite possibly one of the best real-time strategy games ever made.

Sure, the World War II genre has been done to death. But this is a real-time strategy game with incredible graphical detail, dynamic, destructible levels, and unparalleled unit intelligence and tactics.
It elevates the entire real-time strategy genre to the next level.
If you've ever played and enjoyed an RTS game of any type, buy this game immediately.
posts.key_insight: Company of Heroes represents a generational leap for real-time strategy games through its combination of visual fidelity, destructible environments, and intelligent unit behavior.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains why Windows Vista appears to use all available RAM, revealing it's actually a feature called SuperFetch that treats system memory as a cache. He argues that empty memory is wasted memory, since RAM is dramatically faster than hard drives. Vista aggressively pre-populates unused memory with data it predicts you'll need, improving responsiveness compared to Windows XP which left gigabytes unused. While Atwood endorses this approach, he notes it can cause issues during gaming when background disk activity competes with performance-sensitive applications.
key_points: System memory should be thought of as a cache between CPU and hard drive, not as a finite resource to be conserved
Empty cache memory is fundamentally wasted - it's expensive hardware doing nothing useful
RAM is roughly 37x faster than hard drives, making aggressive caching extremely valuable
Vista's SuperFetch learns usage patterns and preloads likely-needed applications into unused memory
The approach can backfire during memory-intensive gaming when low-priority disk threads compete for I/O
money_quotes: The most important rule of cache design is that empty cache memory is wasted cache memory.
Empty cache isn't doing you any good. It's expensive, high-speed memory sucking down power for zero benefit.
You have to stop thinking of system memory as a resource and start thinking of it as a cache.
If you have to ask how much going to the hard drive will cost you in performance, you can't afford it.
The question shouldn't be 'Why does Vista use all my memory?' but 'Why the heck did previous versions of Windows use my memory so ineffectively?'
The less free memory I have, the better; every byte of memory should be actively working on my behalf at all times.
Vista is trying its darndest to pre-emptively populate every byte of system memory with what it thinks I might need next.
posts.key_insight: Unused RAM is wasted RAM - modern operating systems should aggressively cache data in memory rather than leaving it empty, fundamentally changing how users should interpret 'free memory' metrics.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood highlights James Shore's nineteen-week change diary, which documents Shore's attempt to introduce agile practices while working as a regular developer with no formal authority. Shore, an experienced XP coach forced to take contract work during the dot-com bust, found himself on a decidedly non-agile team. The diary chronicles his efforts to effect organizational change from the bottom up. Atwood frames it as a compelling case study in grassroots change, noting the bittersweet mix of successes and failures while affirming that individual developers can make a difference.
key_points: Organizational change can be attempted without formal power or authority
Even experienced coaches and consultants sometimes end up as regular team members
Bottom-up change efforts yield mixed results—both successes and failures
Individual developers can make a meaningful difference in their organizations
money_quotes: As a peon, could I make the kinds of changes I made as a (damned good!) XP coach?
He was attempting to effect organizational change with no formal power.
It's a bittersweet story of success and failure, but it does show that one developer can make a difference.
posts.key_insight: Individual developers without formal authority can still drive meaningful organizational change, even if the results are mixed.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that professional testers are essential to software quality and developer growth, despite the pain they cause. He uses Michael Hunter's extensive testing checklist as evidence of how thorough professional testing must be. The post emphasizes that developers make terrible testers of their own code, and that even mediocre professional testers improve both software and the developers who write it. Atwood frames the relationship between testers and developers as beneficial adversity—painful but necessary for producing quality software.
key_points: Developers are the worst possible testers of their own code—barely better than having no testing at all
Professional testers force developers to improve by holding them to higher standards
The scope of thorough testing is overwhelming—even simple features like a Find dialog have billions of possible test cases
Testing checklists should be customized to context and used as thinking tools rather than rigid requirements
Surviving rigorous testing is what separates seasoned developers from amateurs
money_quotes: The absolute worst testers you can possibly have are developers. They're better than nothing. But barely.
Professional testers force you to become a better developer.
Sometimes it's painful. But in a good way, like a heavy workout.
For even the tiniest bit of something – the Find dialog box in your web browser, say, there are billions of possible test cases.
Verifying you have executed each of these test cases is not the point of the list. The point is to get you thinking about all of the testing you have and have not done.
Don't quail at the thought of all this testing you haven't done yet.
If you can pass that testing gauntlet, you've definitely earned your stripes as a seasoned software developer.
posts.key_insight: Professional testers aren't obstacles to developers but rather essential partners who force them to confront the true scope of quality software development.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that multitasking severely degrades productivity for software developers, drawing on Gerald Weinberg's research showing that adding just one extra project costs 20% of productive time. He cites studies demonstrating that constant interruptions from email and phone calls reduce cognitive performance more than smoking marijuana. The post emphasizes that programmers are particularly vulnerable because coding requires holding many details in memory simultaneously. Atwood concludes that developers should resist pressure to work on multiple projects and be honest about reduced output when multitasking is unavoidable.
key_points: Adding a single additional project to your workload wastes 20% of your time according to Weinberg's rule of thumb
Technology interruptions (email, phone, IM) cause a 10-point IQ drop - worse than the cognitive impact of marijuana
Most people have a 'big-ass blind spot' about how poorly they actually multitask
Programming is especially vulnerable to task switching because it requires keeping many details in short-term memory
Developers' natural optimism causes them to overestimate what they can accomplish while multitasking
money_quotes: Even adding a single project to your workload is profoundly debilitating by Weinberg's calculation. You lose 20% of your time.
Those distracted by incoming email and phone calls saw a 10-point fall in their IQ - more than twice that found in studies of the impact of smoking marijuana
Perhaps the biggest problem of all, though, is that the majority of people doing the most media multitasking have a big-ass blind spot on just how much they suck at it.
We believe we can e-mail and talk on the phone at the same time, with little or no degradation of either communication... But we can't!
The trick here is that when you manage programmers, specifically, task switches take a really, really, really long time.
The human brain seems to move it out of short-term RAM and swaps it out onto a backup tape where it takes forever to retrieve.
Be brutally honest with yourself – and your stakeholders – about how much you can actually get done under multi-tasking conditions. It's probably less than you think.
posts.key_insight: Multitasking doesn't make developers more productive—it makes them dramatically less effective, and honest acknowledgment of this limitation is essential for realistic project planning.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the impending arrival of the first terabyte hard drive, expected in 2007, after upgrading to a 750GB Seagate perpendicular drive. He traces the historical milestones of hard drive capacity—1GB, 10GB, and 100GB—noting that while the pace of growth slowed after 2001, perpendicular recording technology has restored momentum. The post nostalgically recalls the excitement of each capacity breakthrough while acknowledging that modern storage challenges have shifted from space constraints to organization problems.
key_points: The first terabyte desktop hard drive was anticipated to arrive in 2007, with 750GB drives representing the cutting edge in 2006
Hard drive capacity growth follows a roughly logarithmic pattern, with major thresholds (1GB, 10GB, 100GB) serving as memorable milestones
Perpendicular recording technology reinvigorated capacity growth after a slowdown period beginning around 2001
Storage concerns have evolved from worrying about space for applications and games to managing media files and virtual machines
As storage capacity grows, the challenge shifts from having enough space to finding things on massive drives
money_quotes: 750 gigabytes is a tremendous amount of storage space in a single drive – but it doesn't quite get us across the magical terabyte threshold.
The pace of capacity increase has dampened a bit since 2001, but perpendicular technology has gotten us (mostly) back on track.
I remember how excited I was to get my first gigabyte, 10 gigabyte, and 100 gigabyte hard drives back in the day.
I've long since stopped worrying about room for applications and even games. It's all media and virtual PC storage these days.
Of course, finding things on such a large drive is another matter.
posts.key_insight: Each order-of-magnitude increase in storage capacity feels like a milestone, but as drives grow larger, the problem shifts from having enough space to organizing and finding what you've stored.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his discovery of an 'I Rock at BASIC' t-shirt featuring the classic infinite loop program that prints a message forever. The post is a brief, nostalgic nod to the shared experience of learning to program, suggesting that writing this simple endless loop was a rite of passage for programmers of a certain generation. It celebrates the humble beginnings that many developers share.
key_points: The infinite GOTO loop printing a message is a universal first program for BASIC programmers
There's a shared cultural touchstone among developers who started with BASIC
Programming nostalgia has commercial appeal (the t-shirt)
money_quotes: We've all written this program at some point in our careers.
But only those of us who truly rock at BASIC.
posts.key_insight: The simplest programs from our coding origins—like an infinite BASIC loop—become shared badges of honor in developer culture.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to Steve Yegge's criticism of Agile methodologies by arguing that attacking Agile misses the point entirely. While Yegge celebrates Google's development culture as superior to Agile, Atwood contends that the real enemy is Waterfall and Big Design Up Front, which still plague most development teams. He worries that well-crafted anti-Agile arguments could be misused to justify keeping projects stuck in outdated methodologies. Atwood also draws an uncomfortable parallel between Google's talent-focused culture and Enron's, suggesting that Google's celebrated approach may be its own form of religion rather than objective best practice.
key_points: Criticizing Agile is counterproductive when most developers haven't escaped Waterfall and BDUF yet
Even 'bad' Agile represents a significant quality-of-life improvement over traditional methodologies
Google's celebrated development culture shares troubling similarities with Enron's 'talent myth' approach
Anti-Agile arguments, however well-reasoned, risk being weaponized to justify keeping teams in outdated practices
Google's 'good' agile may be just as much a religion as the 'bad' Agile it criticizes
money_quotes: Attacking Agile is a waste of time because most developers haven't even gotten there yet!
The real enemy isn't Agile, it's Waterfall and Big Design Up Front.
Even 'bad' Agile is a huge quality of life improvement for developers stuck in the dark ages of BDUF.
Rather than wasting time and effort on discriminating between 'good' and 'bad' Agile, we should be banding together in the name of Anything But Waterfall.
It's not very reassuring to think that the only difference between Enron and Google is their 'Don't be evil' motto.
One wonders if the 'good' agile at Google isn't just as much of a religion as the 'bad' Agile.
posts.key_insight: The software industry should unite against Waterfall methodology rather than fighting over which flavor of Agile is correct, because imperfect Agile still beats the alternative most developers are stuck with.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines whether software development truly parallels manufacturing, a comparison frequently made in Agile and Scrum literature. While his company adopted Scrum, he finds the manufacturing analogies in books like 'Lean Software Development' unconvincing. He distinguishes between development (creating a recipe through trial and error) and production (following the recipe repeatedly). Atwood argues that software development fundamentally differs from manufacturing because variability is embraced rather than eliminated, and requirements are fluid rather than fixed. He concludes that Agile methodology books would be stronger if they used software examples instead of manufacturing ones.
key_points: Scrum originated in manufacturing (auto and consumer products) before being adapted for software development in the 1990s
Development is like creating a recipe through experimentation, while manufacturing is like following a recipe repeatedly with minimal variation
In manufacturing, variability is the enemy; in software development, variability is the reason developers get up in the morning
Software projects are essentially custom one-off jobs for unique problems, unlike manufacturing's reproducible processes
Even when software requirements exist, success depends on solving the customer's shifting understanding of their problem
money_quotes: Variability is the enemy in manufacturing; in software, it's the reason we get up in the morning.
Every worthwhile software development project is a custom one-off job for a unique problem.
In software, we rarely have meaningful requirements. Even if we do, the only measure of success that matters is whether our solution solves the customer's shifting idea of what their problem is.
Think of development as creating a recipe and production as following the recipe. These are very different activities, and they should be carried out with different approaches.
The most dangerous phase of a violent sport is not exactly the sort of thing you want to add to your project.
When you tell customers your software developers use the Scrum process, they have absolutely no idea what you're talking about.
posts.key_insight: Software development fundamentally differs from manufacturing because it embraces variability and serves shifting requirements, making it more akin to recipe creation than recipe following.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood highlights the common developer trap of 'frameworkitis' - the tendency to procrastinate on actual projects by building elaborate frameworks instead. He shares Alex Gorbatchev's experience of failing to complete a blog engine five times because he kept getting sidetracked building libraries and thinking about future uses. The post suggests that simply writing code that works for the immediate need is more productive than over-engineering. Atwood also wryly notes that writing your own blog software might itself be a form of procrastination.
key_points: Frameworkitis is the habit of procrastinating by developing frameworks instead of shipping working software
Building for hypothetical future uses is a major obstacle to completing projects
Writing code that only does what you need right now helps projects move forward
Even the decision to write your own blog engine might be procrastination disguised as productivity
money_quotes: Procrastinating via developing frameworks.
Every time I have started working on my own blog, I delved deep into creating frameworks and needless to say, none of my previous 5 attempts went even as far as being able to post a new entry.
It really is very hard trying to stay away from creating libraries, thinking about future uses and what extra things I can put in that might used in the future.
Simply creating code that works and only does what I need it to really helps to move things ahead.
I wonder if writing your own blog software isn't a form of procrastination in and of itself.
posts.key_insight: The urge to build reusable frameworks instead of shipping working software is one of the most common ways developers sabotage their own projects.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents his Core 2 Duo system build, explaining his component choices and overclocking strategy. He details why he selected the ASUS P5B Deluxe motherboard, midrange DDR2-667 memory, the E6600 CPU with 4MB L2 cache, and an oversized Scythe Infinity heatsink. His core philosophy is buying upper-midrange components and overclocking into high-end territory for better value. He successfully pushed his $319 2.4GHz E6600 past the $999 X6800 extreme chip's performance, achieving 3.15GHz through careful FSB and voltage adjustments, resulting in a 50% performance improvement over his previous system.
key_points: Buy upper midrange hardware and overclock to high-end performance for maximum value
Extremely fast memory is overrated since the cache-to-main-memory penalty already dominates performance
L2 cache size matters more than clock speed in CPU selection
A quality CPU heatsink is essential for quiet computing and enables aggressive overclocking
Responsible overclocking requires incremental changes and extensive Prime95 stability testing
money_quotes: My general strategy with computer upgrades is to buy upper midrange and overclock myself into high-end territory for extra value.
Computer hardware is one of my few indulgences, but I do a freakishly obsessive amount of research before buying anything.
