I'm a big believer in cache, so I'm not willing to drop down to the E6300 or E6400 which only have 2 megabytes of L2. This might be a little irrational if you actually compare the performance, but so be it. I loves me some L2 cache.
If you want a quiet PC, buy the best CPU heatsink you can afford.
It's such beautiful, magnificent, glorious overkill. It barely fit in my case. That just made me love it all the more.
Sounds traumatic, but if you want to make an omelette, you have to break some eggs. Don't be afraid to break stuff.
posts.key_insight: Smart hardware purchasing means buying quality midrange components and leveraging overclocking knowledge to achieve high-end performance at a fraction of the cost.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reviews DEFCON, an indie PC game from Introversion Software that simulates global thermonuclear war in a minimalist, atmospheric style. The game draws heavily from WarGames and Dr. Strangelove, rewarding players for successfully nuking enemy civilian populations while creating a detached, bunker-like experience. Atwood highlights the game's unique 'Office Mode' that allows coworkers to play a real-time 8-hour game over LAN while working, with discreet notifications when nukes are incoming. Unlike Introversion's previous game Darwinia, DEFCON is primarily a multiplayer experience with limited single-player appeal.
key_points: DEFCON creates an atmosphere of cold war paranoia through minimal sound design and ambient music, making players feel detached from the millions of deaths they cause
The game's mechanics make winning difficult—launching attacks reveals your positions, often resulting in mutual destruction
Office Mode enables real-time LAN play over a full workday with a panic key to hide the game and system tray notifications for nuclear launches
The slow pace with 'think time' distinguishes it from rapid-clicking RTS games—battleships and bombers don't turn on a dime
The game is primarily multiplayer-focused, with the single-player AI offering limited narrative experience
money_quotes: It's like you're ten miles underground in a bunker, bringing the world to an end one city at a time, completely detached from the millions of deaths you are causing.
Games often end with both sides obliterated.
You can hit the Panic key (press escape twice) which immediately removes the game from the screen and places a discreet icon in your system tray.
Because everything is taking place in real-time you've got at least 30 minutes before those nukes land, so you've got plenty of time to respond without interfering with your real work too much.
The best way to conduct Global Thermonuclear War is with a couple of your closest friends.
posts.key_insight: DEFCON succeeds by embracing the grim absurdity of nuclear war simulation, creating an eerily detached experience that works surprisingly well as office background entertainment.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to Steve Yegge's 11,000-word critique of Agile by arguing that all software development methodologies are essentially religions—belief systems with little empirical proof. Rather than seeing this as problematic, Atwood contends that religious fervor around methodologies is actually beneficial: when developers truly believe in their approach, they become more effective and energized. He critiques Yegge for dismissing Agile as superstition while simultaneously evangelizing Google's methodology with equal fervor. Atwood concludes that the real danger isn't methodology-as-religion, but rather practitioners who don't recognize their own religious devotion to their beliefs.
key_points: Software development has always been a religion—developers band together around shared beliefs (Java vs .NET, static vs dynamic) with no scientific proof
Methodology religions aren't dangerous; they make developers more effective by giving them something to believe in
Steve Yegge criticizes Agile as superstition while being equally religious about Google's methodology
'Staying lightweight' isn't some natural state—most developers are trapped in organizations that don't give them that choice
The only truly dangerous people are the religious nuts who don't realize they are religious nuts
money_quotes: Software development is, and has always been, a religion. We band together into groups of people who believe the same things, with very little basis for proving any of those beliefs.
We may kid ourselves into believing we're 'computer scientists,' but when was the last time you used a hypothesis and a control to prove anything?
There's nothing wrong with a religion that preaches solid engineering.
In order for programmers to be effective, they have to believe in what they're doing. Whether that belief is scientifically and empirically provable is completely irrelevant.
The only truly dangerous people are the religious nuts who don't realize they are religious nuts.
Telling developers to stay lightweight is like telling depressed people to cheer up.
Most developers don't use agile software development. But it's not by choice. They weren't given a choice.
posts.key_insight: Treating software methodologies as religions isn't the problem—the problem is not recognizing that we're all true believers in something.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on whether to accept advertising on his blog after receiving multiple inquiries. He acknowledges that while he has no hosting costs and already uses subtle Amazon affiliate links, he's not opposed to making money. However, he outlines the challenges of advertising responsibly: standing behind products, accepting that ads transform a hobby into commercial work, and avoiding the tacky appearance of over-monetization. Ultimately, he concludes that for his blog, the negatives outweigh the benefits because he values writing purely for his own reasons rather than to drive ad revenue.
key_points: Responsible advertising requires standing behind the products you're indirectly selling and hand-picking what you promote
The first ad you accept fundamentally transforms your blog from a hobby into a commercial venture with new conflicts of interest
Over-monetizing with too many ad types makes a website look desperate and tacky
The author values writing for intrinsic reasons over the financial benefits of advertising
Affiliate links can be done tastefully when they're indistinguishable from normal useful links
money_quotes: I won't pretend that I don't like money, particularly here in the United States where money is synonymous with freedom.
The first ads you take convert your blog from a non-profit to a commercial venture. It's no longer a hobby; you're being paid to blog. It's work.
Using the Ronco spray-on monetization plan and filling your page with as many types of advertising and affiliate programs as possible smacks of desperation.
I like the idea that every time I write an entry, I did so purely for my own reasons, whatever they are, and not because I needed to drive ad revenue.
But advertising responsibly is difficult.
posts.key_insight: Accepting advertising fundamentally changes the nature of creative work from personal expression to commercial obligation, and that transformation may cost more than the revenue is worth.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the history and current state of computer chess, examining how brute-force approaches dominated early computer chess but strategic AI programs are now emerging as viable competitors. He compares his personal PC's chess-playing capability (4.45 million positions/second) to Deep Blue's 200 million, noting that desktop hardware lags 15 years behind specialized chess computers. However, the more significant development is that Type B programs like Deep Fritz can match Deep Blue's performance through smarter move filtering rather than raw computation. Atwood disputes claims that computers have definitively surpassed humans, citing evidence that top grandmasters have maintained drawn matches against the best programs, suggesting we've reached an equilibrium point.
key_points: Brute force (Type A) dominated computer chess history because computers struggle with the concept of 'good' positions, while strategic AI (Type B) programs are finally becoming competitive
Desktop PCs in 2006 were over 15 years behind Deep Blue's specialized hardware in raw chess calculation speed
Deep Fritz demonstrates that intelligent move filtering can match brute force computation - it achieves similar depth despite vastly inferior positions-per-second
Despite claims of computer supremacy, all post-1997 matches between world champions and top chess programs resulted in ties
The top 200 human players have held their ground against improving computers, suggesting a dynamic equilibrium rather than inevitable computer dominance
money_quotes: Chess remains the most visible and public benchmark of the relentless increase in computer speed over the last 50 years.
As it turns out, computers have a hard time with the concept of 'good.'
The fastest desktop PCs are more than 15 years behind Deep Blue in computer chess.
Despite its vastly inferior brute force, the Deep Blitz machine could already be a match for Deep Blue because of improvements in chess software.

Obviously, Kasparov did not evaluate 200 million chess positions per second when he defeated Deep Blue in game 1 of the 1997 match, thus the 200 million positions per second number is not a requirement to play chess at the world championship level.
The top computers have caught up with the top grandmasters, and right now I'm not convinced that the computers will zoom past the grandmasters.
The problem space of chess is so astonishingly large that incremental increases in hardware speed and algorithms are unlikely to result in meaningful gains from here on out.
Computer chess programs may have long ago crushed the rest of us in their inevitable Moore's Law march, but the best 200 chess players in the world are still holding their ground.
posts.key_insight: Smart algorithms that filter for quality moves may be more valuable than raw computational brute force, and human expertise at the highest levels may prove surprisingly resilient against machine optimization.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues against the IT industry's obsession with audience metrics like pagerank, traffic numbers, and presentation ratings. He shares his own approach of deliberately ignoring these numbers, citing Conan O'Brien's 'Field of Dreams' philosophy of focusing on creating content you believe in rather than optimizing for audience data. The post suggests that overthinking metrics leads to wasted energy and added stress. Instead, creators should trust their instincts and focus on making something they themselves would want to consume.
key_points: The IT industry has an unhealthy tendency to fetishize audience metrics like pagerank, backlinks, and attendance numbers
Conan O'Brien's success came from ignoring research and viewer data, focusing solely on making content he found funny
Obsessing over metrics adds unnecessary stress and distracts from the actual creative work
The best strategy is to create content you'd want to consume yourself rather than optimizing for external validation
Metrics have value but are easily overanalyzed at the expense of trusting your own judgment
money_quotes: We have a tendency to fetishize audience metrics in the IT industry.
I couldn't even tell you how many readers I have, or what my presentation ratings were.
Audience metrics aren't the reason I write, and they aren't the reason I present. They're incidental.
I don't look at research. I don't look at who's watching, or when they're watching. I've never been interested in any of that. I'm interested in doing what I think is funny.
If I'm looking at spreadsheets and time-lapse studies of viewing patterns, I think I'm wasting my time.
The day I care about keeping my blog readers happy is the day I'll stop blogging. Who needs the added stress?
Instead of obsessing over who does and doesn't link to you, concentrate on writing a blog entry you'd like to read.
You should trust your gut more than any metrics.
posts.key_insight: Trust your creative instincts over audience metrics—focus on making something you'd want to consume, and the audience will follow.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces his decision to quit LinkedIn after two years of membership, arguing the service provides no tangible benefits to users. He criticizes the platform's difficult account deletion process and compares it to a chain letter or pyramid scheme. Despite maintaining his profile and selectively approving connection requests, he found zero value beyond unwanted headhunter calls. He concludes that LinkedIn primarily benefits LinkedIn itself, not its users, and urges others to close their accounts.
key_points: LinkedIn makes account deletion unnecessarily difficult, requiring a support ticket rather than offering a simple opt-out
Despite two years of active membership and selective networking, Atwood received no meaningful professional benefits
The service functions like a digital chain letter, building marketing value for LinkedIn while providing little to users
If you can only reach someone through LinkedIn, that itself signals the connection isn't worth pursuing
Users should critically evaluate who truly benefits from their participation in social networking services
money_quotes: Linked In is the digital equivalent of a chain letter.
If the only way you can reach someone is through this nutty online social pyramid scheme, you don't deserve to be taken seriously.
Consider carefully: who really benefits from your participation in Linked In? I'll tell you who benefits: Linked In.
Why build Linked In's marketing database with your valuable time and information?
I've found that there really is no there there.
It's high time we put an end to this glorified chain letter of a service.
posts.key_insight: Social networking services that make it hard to leave while providing no clear user benefit are exploiting your data and time for their own gain.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques Scrum's 'chickens and pigs' terminology for daily standup meetings, arguing that the joke-based naming convention is confusing, unnecessarily derogatory, and undermines the methodology's credibility. The terminology originates from a joke about commitment levels—pigs sacrifice their lives (are fully committed) while chickens only contribute eggs (are merely involved). Atwood acknowledges the valid underlying principle that accountability matters in meetings, but argues the macho, insider-joke language makes it harder for outsiders to take Scrum seriously. He provides alternative terminology suggestions like 'Contributors/Observers' or 'Committed/Interested' that better communicate the concept without the baggage.
key_points: The chickens/pigs terminology requires explaining a joke before people understand the methodology, creating an unnecessary barrier to adoption
While the underlying principle of distinguishing committed team members from observers is valid, the derogatory framing toward 'chickens' is counterproductive
Scrum co-creator Jeff Sutherland explicitly intended the chicken term to be negative, calling non-committed attendees 'parasites' and 'suckers'
Better alternatives exist that communicate the same concept more clearly: Contributors/Observers, Committed/Interested, Players/Spectators
Good project managers shield their teams from organizational overhead, but insider terminology undermines that goal by creating communication barriers
money_quotes: I agree that everyone participating in the project should have 'skin in the game.' But not literally.
Pride in your project is one thing, but implying that you'd give your very life to see the project succeed is just a little too macho for my tastes.
The use of derogatory in-joke terminology harms the cause by making it harder for outsiders to take Scrum seriously.
And I wonder: how do you diplomatically break the news to a chicken that thinks it's a pig?
Although I don't agree with all of it, there are some solid software development principles in Scrum. It's a shame that stupid stuff like chickens and pigs get in the way.
posts.key_insight: Good methodologies can be undermined by insider jargon and unnecessarily alienating terminology that prioritizes cleverness over clear communication.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the 'Last Responsible Moment' principle from Lean Software Development, arguing that delaying decisions until you have maximum information leads to better outcomes. He distinguishes this strategic delay from mere procrastination, calling it 'inspired laziness' that serves as a risk avoidance strategy. The post connects this concept to YAGNI (You Ain't Gonna Need It) and argues against our natural tendency to over-prepare. Using his own workshop full of unused tools as an example, Atwood illustrates how premature commitments waste resources and time. He concludes that in the rapidly changing world of software, deciding too early can be more dangerous than deciding too late.
key_points: Decisions made too early in a project carry enormous risk and often result in throwaway work or crippling long-term consequences
The Last Responsible Moment is the point at which failing to decide eliminates an important alternative - not procrastination but strategic delay
Having the courage to say 'I don't know, but we're working on it' is often the best decision early in a project
Humans have a natural tendency to prepare for unlikely future needs, wasting resources on things they'll never use
In software's rapidly changing environment, premature decisions are arguably more dangerous than late ones
money_quotes: Making decisions at the Last Responsible Moment isn't procrastination; it's inspired laziness.
Decisions made too early in a project are hugely risky.
Often, having the guts to say 'I don't know' is your best decision.
The key is to make decisions as late as you can responsibly wait because that is the point at which you have the most information on which to base the decision.
Early decisions often result in work that has to be thrown away.
Be prepared. But for tomorrow, not next year.
Deciding too late is dangerous, but deciding too early in the rapidly changing world of software development is arguably even more dangerous.
posts.key_insight: Strategic delay of decisions until the last responsible moment isn't procrastination—it's a risk management technique that ensures you're working with maximum information rather than costly assumptions.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that when choosing between IE7 and Firefox, the community surrounding a product matters more than the product itself. While both browsers are comparable as modern web browsers, Firefox's vibrant ecosystem of extensions and add-ons gives it a decisive advantage. Microsoft's failure to cultivate community—requiring C programming for add-ons, outsourcing their extension site, and lacking cultural enthusiasm—explains IE's weakness. However, Atwood acknowledges that popularity brings risks, as the same extensibility that enables Firefox's thriving community also enabled IE's spyware problem.
key_points: Firefox and IE7 are roughly equivalent products technically, but Firefox wins because of its stronger community and extension ecosystem
Microsoft's technical decisions (requiring C for add-ons) and cultural indifference have prevented an IE community from forming
The same extensibility features that enable helpful add-ons can also enable malware—the difference is who your community attracts
Community doesn't happen accidentally; it must be intentionally cultivated through accessible tooling and active engagement
Open source culture creates fervor and devotion that proprietary products struggle to replicate
money_quotes: I visit websites, which I consider more important than what I use to view said websites.
It's not that Firefox is inherently a better product than IE7. It isn't. But Firefox has a stronger, more vibrant user community, and that ultimately makes it a better product than IE7.
Is the community around a product more important than the product itself?
Community doesn't happen accidentally.
There are far more people in the world capable of writing scripts than are capable of writing C code.
Community support can make or break a product.

The big difference is that Firefox has a community of web developers and hobbyists who build cool applications for it while most of the folks extending Internet Explorer in the Windows world are writing spyware and other kinds of malware.
posts.key_insight: A product's community ecosystem often matters more than the product's inherent quality, making community cultivation a critical but often neglected competitive advantage.

posts.summary: Atwood humorously confesses that building quiet PCs has led him to develop an obsession with oversized heatsinks. He showcases his collection of massive cooling solutions for various components including his video card, northbridge, and CPU. Despite his motherboard already having premium cooling, he had to replace the northbridge cooler due to persistent overheating issues. He contextualizes modern chipset heat output by noting that Intel northbridges now dissipate as much power as entire CPUs did in 1998.
key_points: Building quiet PCs naturally leads to prioritizing passive cooling with large heatsinks
Even premium motherboard cooling solutions can be inadequate for modern chipset thermal demands
Modern Intel northbridges dissipate nearly 20 watts, comparable to late-90s CPUs
Integrated graphics can push northbridge power dissipation above 30 watts
money_quotes: One side effect of building quiet PCs is that you tend to develop a giant heatsink fetish.
Intel CPUs didn't dissipate more than 20 watts under load until the introduction of the Pentium II 450 in 1998.
More like 30+ watts if you're using the craptacular integrated graphics on some motherboards.
posts.key_insight: The pursuit of silent computing inevitably leads to an appreciation for massive passive cooling solutions, as modern components generate surprising amounts of heat that stock coolers often can't quietly handle.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the classic 'iron triangle' of project management (time, resources, scope) and reveals its hidden fourth dimension: quality. He explains that when all three visible constraints are locked, quality becomes the silent variable that absorbs the pressure. Through his colleague Susan's stool metaphor and insights from agile practitioners, he suggests that software development's inherent variability means these constraints may be more flexible than traditionally assumed—improving quality can actually reduce time rather than trading off against it.
key_points: The iron triangle of project management (time, resources, scope) has a hidden fourth variable: quality, which becomes the release valve when other constraints are fixed
The traditional iron triangle assumes smooth processes, uniform productivity, and no spare capacity—conditions rarely true in complex software projects
Agile methods have demonstrated that improving quality can actually shorten delivery time rather than extending it
The metaphor evolves from triangle to tetrahedron to stool as the fourth dimension is acknowledged
Software development's variability means constraints may be more malleable than the 'iron' metaphor suggests
money_quotes: Something still has to give. There's an unstated fourth ingredient in the iron triangle: quality.
Just as Newtonian physics is now known to be a special case, the iron triangle is a special case that assumes the process is flowing smoothly to begin with.
If you improve qualities of service, such as reliability, you can shorten time.
The highly variable nature of software development means that, if you're careful, you may be able to build your stool out of a more malleable material than iron.
If you pull on one 'edge' of the triangle, the other sides have to give.
posts.key_insight: Quality is the hidden fourth constraint in project management, and unlike traditional thinking suggests, improving it can actually unlock flexibility in the other three constraints rather than competing with them.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood expresses frustration with a common annoyance caused by Microsoft's Windows Live Writer blogging tool. The software creates 'hidden' temporary posts for CSS style detection that frequently fail to delete themselves, polluting RSS feeds and search results. He notes over 19,500 Google results for these junk posts, calling out the poor implementation. The post is a brief, humorous complaint about software that inadvertently makes the internet worse rather than better.
key_points: Windows Live Writer creates temporary skeleton posts for style detection that are supposed to be hidden but frequently leak into public feeds
These junk posts pollute both RSS aggregators and Google search results, with over 19,500 indexed results
The ironic title suggests software meant to improve blogging is actually degrading internet quality
The issue stems from posts that should auto-delete but don't, requiring manual cleanup by users
money_quotes: Seriously. Enough with this already. I'm gonna hurt somebody.
Gee, thanks, Windows Live Writer. That's exactly 19,499 too many.
On the plus side, I did get the chance to bring two new GUIDs into the world.
posts.key_insight: Software features that fail silently can pollute shared spaces at scale, turning a minor implementation oversight into thousands of pieces of internet garbage.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the programmer's instinct to focus primarily on writing code, arguing that code alone has no value if it never ships or reaches users. Drawing on Ian Landsman's entrepreneurial advice and Steve Yegge's observations about famous programmers, he notes that most respected developers made their impact through writing prose, not code. Atwood argues that developers should invest in complementary skills like writing, speaking, and community participation rather than just deepening their coding expertise. The post concludes that while code matters, the activities surrounding it—communication, business, community—ultimately determine whether that code has any real-world impact.
key_points: Code has zero value if it never ships, nobody knows about it, or nobody can understand it
Developers spend more time understanding code than writing it, yet overvalue the writing part
Most famous programmers became influential through prose writing, not their actual code
Complementary skills like writing, speaking, and community participation distinguish developers more than coding ability alone
The ideal is to write code AND write about it in ways that inspire others, but time is finite so prioritize accordingly
money_quotes: The best code in the world is meaningless if nobody knows about your product.
The importance of the code we do write is absolutely dwarfed by everything else that goes on around it.
If nobody knows about your code, if nobody can understand your code, if for whatever reason your code doesn't even ship… then have you really accomplished anything by writing that code?
Most of the famous-ish programmers I respect have actually made their impact on me through writing, and it's usually just prose, with maybe a little code interspersed.
Try to spend some time talking to people instead of the compiler.
That's how you distinguish yourself from your peers. And that's ultimately how you become a better software developer, too.
If you have to choose between writing code and writing about code, remember which side of the equation is more important – and balance accordingly.
posts.key_insight: Writing code is a necessary but vastly overvalued activity for developers; the real differentiator is the ability to communicate about code and participate in the broader community around it.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that developers should avoid both over-specialization and becoming unfocused generalists, instead striving to become 'generalizing specialists' who master a few areas while maintaining broad competence. He responds to criticism of his earlier post about cultivating non-technical skills, clarifying that the goal is becoming a better software developer, not abandoning technical expertise. The post highlights how teams with rigid specialists create bottlenecks where people wait on 'the database guy' or 'the testing guy' instead of delivering complete features. Atwood emphasizes that in agile environments, the measure of success is working features, not completed individual tasks, making cross-functional skills essential.
key_points: A generalizing specialist is 'jack-of-all-trades and master of a few' - distinctly different from a generalist who masters none
Over-specialization creates process smells in teams, with incomplete work piling up as people wait for specialists
Agile teams measure delivery by working features, not individual tasks, which specialists alone cannot deliver
Cultivating non-technical skills serves the goal of becoming a better software developer, not abandoning technical work
Growth requires regularly exceeding your comfort zone and learning new skills as team needs change
money_quotes: Cultivating non-technical skills is, first and foremost, about becoming a better software developer.
If you wanted to be rich, famous, and get girls, then you picked the wrong profession. I'm sorry I had to be the one to break this to you.
A generalist is a jack-of-all-trades but a master of none, whereas a generalizing specialist is a jack-of-all-trades and master of a few. Big difference.
A common process smell in new agile teams is more and more stories/backlog items incomplete at the end of the iteration.
Specialists typically don't deliver working features, thus the problem.
I see too many software developers burrowing themselves deeper and deeper into the same skillsets and specialties.
To be a better software developer, grow from a specialist to a generalizing specialist.
posts.key_insight: The path to becoming a better software developer lies not in deeper specialization or broad superficiality, but in mastering a few areas while continuously stretching to learn complementary skills that help deliver complete, working features.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that CAPTCHAs remain highly effective against spam despite claims they've been 'broken.' He demonstrates through his own blog that even a 'naïve CAPTCHA' with a static answer blocks 99.9% of spam. Through OCR testing, he shows that minimal perturbation or noise defeats standard character recognition. He examines alternative attack vectors like 'Turing Farms' and concludes their economics make them impractical. The core insight is that CAPTCHAs work by attacking spam's economic model rather than being cryptographically unbreakable.
key_points: Claims that CAPTCHA is 'broken' are largely theoretical - there's no practical evidence of widespread automated defeats in the real world
Even the most basic CAPTCHA implementation (same word every time) blocks 99.9% of spam by raising the difficulty bar just slightly
Adding minimal noise or perturbation to CAPTCHA text completely defeats standard OCR engines
Human-powered attacks like 'Turing Farms' are economically unfeasible at $14,000 per day for a typical spam run
CAPTCHA's effectiveness comes from attacking spam's economic model, not from being technically unbreakable
money_quotes: If CAPTCHA is so thoroughly defeated, why is it still in use on virtually every major website on the internet?
I use 'naïve CAPTCHA' with 99.9% effectiveness.
Adding a tiny bit of noise or perturbation to the CAPTCHA text was all it took to break the OCR.
Researchers are really good, and the attackers really are not.
The real secret to CAPTCHA is that it hits spammers where they are most vulnerable: in the pocketbook.
The minute you put up a computational barrier, the entire economic model of spam comes crashing down.
If you're not using CAPTCHAs because you think they're compromised, then you're too gullible for your own good.
posts.key_insight: CAPTCHA's power lies not in being unbreakable, but in making spam economically unviable by imposing even minimal computational barriers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that build servers are critical infrastructure for software projects, comparing them to heart monitors that indicate project health. Despite his past skepticism, he now advocates for build servers as essential tools that provide machine-independent builds, scripted processes, automated testing, and continuous integration. The post outlines a progression from basic builds to advanced capabilities like multi-environment testing and static analysis. Atwood emphasizes that build servers create positive feedback loops where accumulated tests make builds increasingly valuable over time.
key_points: Build servers eliminate 'it works on my machine' problems by creating neutral, dependency-free integration points
The build process should be scripted, under source control, and essentially self-documenting
Teams should progress from daily/weekly builds toward continuous integration as the 'holy grail'
Compilation alone is insufficient - automated tests that verify the software actually works are essential
Build servers can integrate static analysis, code metrics, and multi-environment testing for advanced projects
money_quotes: The build server is critical – it's the heart monitor of your software project.
You can hate your co-workers, but it's irrational to hate the build server.
Sure, maybe all the code compiles. But does the software actually work?
Mere compilation is not enough.
A solid heartbeat from the build server leads to a confident development team.
The more tests you accrete into the build over time, the better the feedback is from the build, and the more valuable it will be to your project.
If you don't have a build server on your project, what are you waiting for?
posts.key_insight: A build server transforms from a simple compilation checker into a project's vital signs monitor, with each additional test and metric making it exponentially more valuable to the development team.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the most critical factor for virtual machine performance is running VMs from a separate physical hard drive rather than the same drive as your operating system. The disk I/O bottleneck is by far the biggest performance penalty in virtualization, as VM disk images routinely grow to multiple gigabytes. He recommends desktop users adopt a two-drive approach: a fast 10,000 rpm drive for the OS and a separate data drive for VMs. For laptop users, external USB 2.0 or eSATA enclosures provide a practical alternative that delivers noticeable real-world performance improvements.
key_points: Disk I/O is the primary performance bottleneck in virtual machines by a wide margin, not CPU or memory
Running VMs on the same drive as your OS forces them to compete for disk access, significantly degrading performance
A two-drive desktop setup with a fast OS drive and separate data drive is ideal for VM performance
External USB 2.0 enclosures with 7,200 rpm drives can outperform internal 5,400 rpm laptop drives for VM workloads
eSATA connections offer native SATA speeds and are becoming more common on motherboards
money_quotes: The primary performance bottleneck in virtual machines, by a very wide margin, is the hard drive.
Making the VM fight with your OS and swap disk makes this issue much, much worse.
It shouldn't be too surprising that the disk subsystem has a disproportionately large impact on overall virtual machine performance.
The real-world performance improvement gained from running a VM on an external USB 2.0 drive is quite noticeable.
It's also a good way to segregate your operating system and data in case you need to do a complete wipe of your operating system.
posts.key_insight: Virtual machine performance is dominated by disk I/O, so physically separating your VM storage from your OS drive eliminates the contention that causes the biggest slowdowns.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues against relying solely on Akismet for blog spam protection, despite its effectiveness as a community-powered blacklist service. He explains the fundamental concepts of whitelists, blacklists, and greylists, noting that each approach has inherent weaknesses including false positives and whitelist attacks. The post emphasizes that depending on any single external service creates dangerous dependencies—if Akismet goes down, blogs are either flooded with spam or unable to accept comments. Atwood advocates for defense in depth, combining CAPTCHA to verify human commenters with Akismet as a secondary filter for human-entered spam.
key_points: Blacklists, whitelists, and greylists each have distinct weaknesses—blacklists generate false positives, whitelists can be exploited by spammers using legitimate URLs, and greylists still require human moderation time
Relying on a single external service creates unacceptable dependencies: downtime means either spam floods or disabled comments
Akismet struggles with edge cases like compliment spam, gibberish from spammers testing algorithms, and human-crafted contextual spam messages
Defense in depth is essential—CAPTCHA validates human presence while Akismet catches human-entered spam, addressing different attack vectors
The scale problem is real: with 75 spam trackbacks per hour on one blog, expecting any single service to handle the entire internet's spam is unreasonable
money_quotes: The core problem is relying on a single method of defense against spam.
I hate dependencies, and I always strive to keep the number of dependencies I accept to an absolute minimum.
If you consider Akismet a success because you ignore the moderation queue entirely, have you really succeeded?
Abandoning CAPTCHA in favor of Akismet is unforgivable.
Anti-spam security starts at home.
For best results, use defense in depth and combine local anti-spam measures, such as CAPTCHA, with Akismet as a backup.
posts.key_insight: Never outsource your entire defense against any threat to a single external service—true security requires layered, independent protection mechanisms you control.

posts.summary: Atwood suggests that reviewing code you wrote five years ago provides a humbling perspective on your growth as a developer. He reflects on his own VBScript and Classic ASP code from 2001, noting how difficult it is to distinguish personal mistakes from limitations imposed by inadequate tools. He observes that improvements in frameworks and development environments have outpaced his personal skill growth. The post concludes with a forward-looking question about what blind spots we might have today that will be obvious in five years.
key_points: Reviewing old code is a valuable practice for measuring professional growth
It's often hard to separate personal coding mistakes from limitations of the tools and environments available at the time
Platform and tooling improvements can dwarf individual developer skill improvements over the same period
Developers should actively seek out alternative development environments to avoid wearing blinders
The code we write today will likely look just as dated in five years
money_quotes: Go read some source code you wrote five years ago for a real scare.
My goal is to suck slightly less every year.
It's difficult to tell my personal WTFs apart from the WTFs inherited due to limitations in the WSH languages and Classic ASP coding environments.
It's like trying to choose between using an old shoe or a glass bottle to hammer nails.
I also knew I was wearing blinders – and I also knew .NET was right around the corner. But what's around the next corner?
How will I look back on today's code five years from now?
posts.key_insight: The embarrassment we feel reading old code comes partly from our growth as developers, but equally from the primitive tools we were forced to use—and we should assume we're wearing similar blinders today.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood draws a parallel between classic arcade high score tables and modern web metrics like Technorati rankings, Alexa traffic stats, and Google PageRank. He begins with the observation that Asteroids introduced the first high score table, then shares an amusing anecdote about the Japanese game Gaplus including blood type in its rankings—which he initially thought was satirical commentary but turned out to be a genuine Japanese cultural practice. The post suggests that while we've moved beyond arcade games, our fundamental desire to rank and compare ourselves persists in new forms. The rhetorical question about blood type playfully underscores how arbitrary some of these ranking metrics can be.
key_points: Asteroids was the first video game to introduce a high score table, spawning a fixture of 80s-90s arcade culture
The inclusion of blood type in Gaplus's high scores reflects Japanese culture rather than developer satire—blood type is considered a meaningful personality indicator in Japan
Modern equivalents of high score tables include Technorati blog rankings, Alexa traffic metrics, and Google PageRank
Our competitive instinct to rank and measure ourselves has simply migrated from arcades to the web
money_quotes: I thought the inclusion of blood type in the high score table for Gaplus was a clever commentary on the meaninglessness of high score tables by the game's developers.
We may not play arcade games any more, but we still have our high score tables.
What's your high score? And more importantly, what's your blood type?
posts.key_insight: The human drive to compete for rankings and measure status against others hasn't disappeared—it's simply evolved from arcade cabinets to web metrics like PageRank and traffic stats.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the parallel evolution of personal computers and his own life, sparked by revisiting Robert X. Cringely's 'Accidental Empires' book and its PBS documentary adaptation 'Triumph of the Nerds.' He traces the rapid transformation from the primitive Altair 8800 in 1975 to the archetypal Apple II just two years later, noting how Apple established the template for all modern personal computers. Drawing from his own experience growing up with Apple computers and learning AppleBASIC, Atwood concludes that despite 30+ years of progress, both computers and he himself still have significant maturing to do.
key_points: The Apple II (1977) established the template for all modern personal computers: plastic case, expansion slots, CRT display, integrated keyboard, and floppy disk drive
The transformation from Altair 8800 to Apple II in just two years represents a remarkable leap in usability and design
VisiCalc was the first killer app that drove personal computer adoption before the IBM PC era
The author's formative programming experiences in AppleBASIC shaped his career despite never transitioning to the more expensive Macintosh
money_quotes: I've always felt like the home computer and I grew up together.
The Altair is barely recognizable as a personal computer, even though it is technically the first one.
Every modern home computer released after 1977 followed the template that Apple established for the industry.
It's difficult to get excited about a machine with no display and a row of dip-switches for input.
Computers still have a long way to go before they're fully grown up. And so do I.
posts.key_insight: The rapid evolution from primitive hardware to usable personal computers in the late 1970s established patterns we still follow today, yet both technology and those who grew up with it remain works in progress.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software customization, while often marketed as a benefit, can become a dangerous trap that locks organizations into their software choices. He uses the Tar-Baby parable to illustrate how each customization adds another point of entanglement. The more you customize software, the harder it becomes to upgrade to newer versions or switch to competitors. What vendors promote as 'extensibility' can quickly become a prison of your own making. Atwood warns that organizations should carefully consider the long-term costs of customization before committing to extensive modifications.
key_points: Vendors market customization as a feature, but it's as much a danger as a benefit
Extensive customizations can prevent upgrades to newer software versions
Customization makes switching to competitor products nearly impossible
The line between customization, extensibility, and full programming environment is blurry and dangerous
Each customization you add increases your entanglement with that software choice
money_quotes: In my experience, software customization is as much a danger as a benefit.
But like the tar-baby, extensive software customizations can trap you.
Extracting yourself from a particular software choice is difficult even in the best of circumstances.
But once you've performed extensive software customizations, extracting yourself from that software becomes nearly impossible.
Where do you draw the line between customization, extensibility, and full-bore programming environment?
The next time a vendor sells you on customizations, you should consider the parable of the Tar-Baby before you're stuck in it.
posts.key_insight: Software customization is a trap disguised as a feature—each modification you make increases your dependency on that software and makes future changes exponentially harder.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood highlights KeyTraino, a concept originated by Leon Bambrick that teaches developers keyboard shortcuts by showing them whenever they use mouse-based navigation. SlickEdit released a free Visual Studio 2005 add-in called Command Spy that implements this idea, tracking command execution and revealing associated key bindings. The add-in also includes whimsical features like the ability to add dancing banana graphics to the editor. The post is a brief endorsement of tools that help developers become more efficient through keyboard mastery.
key_points: KeyTraino shows keyboard shortcuts when users click menus or toolbars, teaching them faster alternatives
SlickEdit's Command Spy add-in tracks all executed commands and their associated key bindings
The tool helps developers learn shortcuts organically through their normal workflow
The add-in includes humorous extras like embedding custom graphics in the IDE
money_quotes: When you use the toolbar, the menus or the context-menus of an application, KeyTraino shows the alternative keystroke you could've used.
The main purpose of this tool is to allow you to learn what commands are bound to which keystrokes, so that you can work faster within the IDE.
Now that's productivity.
posts.key_insight: The best way to learn keyboard shortcuts is through passive reinforcement during normal work, not through memorization.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores screencasting tools available for Windows, arguing that screencasts can be as valuable as written content for demonstrations. He provides a comprehensive list of both free and paid options ranging from $28 to $500, shares his personal toolkit (GifgIfgiF for quick animated GIFs, Camtasia for advanced work), and highlights the often-overlooked challenge of codec selection. The post advocates starting with free tools to build competency before investing in premium options.
key_points: Animated GIF screencasts are particularly effective for small, embedded demonstrations
Codec selection is a major challenge since video codecs are optimized for movies, not GUI content
Windows Media Player has a dedicated screen codec optimized specifically for GUI capture
Start with free tools to build familiarity, then invest in paid options if screencasting becomes frequent
The right tool depends on whether you need quick captures or advanced editing and multiple output formats
money_quotes: If a picture is worth a thousand words, is a single screencast equal to a thousand word blog post?
One of the biggest challenges in screencasting is choosing an appropriate codec. Video codecs are optimized for movie content, definitely not for GUIs.
Get comfortable with how it works so you can whip out a screen capture session any time the situation calls for it.
posts.key_insight: Screencasting is a valuable communication tool for developers, but success depends on choosing the right tool for your needs and understanding that standard video codecs perform poorly for GUI content.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that web application speed remains critically important despite the trend toward rich AJAX interfaces. He cites research showing that users abandon slow sites—Google found a half-second delay caused a 20% traffic drop, and Amazon discovered even 100ms delays hurt revenue. Drawing on classic human-computer interaction research from 1968, he outlines the psychological thresholds for user attention (0.1s, 1s, 10s) and argues the 10-second limit is now outdated. He concludes that developers should prioritize fast, functional layouts over flashy slow ones, and use techniques like progressive rendering when true speed isn't achievable.
key_points: AJAX-heavy web applications are reversing the speed gains of the early web, with loading screens becoming common again
Google's experiment showed users requested more search results but engagement dropped 20% when the extra half-second load time was added
Amazon found that even 100-millisecond delays caused measurable revenue losses
The classic 1968 response time thresholds (0.1s instantaneous, 1s uninterrupted flow, 10s attention limit) still apply, though the 10-second threshold may now be closer to 5 seconds
When actual speed isn't possible, perceived speed through progressive rendering and clever feedback can substitute
money_quotes: Half a second delay caused a 20% drop in traffic. Half a second delay killed user satisfaction.
Unless you make it load fast, nobody will stick around long enough to find out what you have to offer.
We tried delaying the page in increments of 100 milliseconds and found that even very small delays would result in substantial and costly drops in revenue.
What's more important? Getting flash after 5 seconds, or functional no-frills layouts in less than a second? Let's get our priorities straight.
Speed still matters. And remember, the perception of speed is just as important as actual speed. If you can't be fast, be clever.
My mind now boggles at the fact that I now see loading pages that last several seconds when visiting Web sites more and more these days.
1.0 second is about the limit for the user's flow of thought to stay uninterrupted, even though the user will notice the delay.
posts.key_insight: User tolerance for delays is measured in fractions of a second, not seconds—even tiny speed improvements have outsized impact on engagement and revenue.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that Office 2007's Ribbon interface represents a revolutionary departure from the traditional WIMP (Windows, Icons, Menus, Pointing device) GUI paradigm that has dominated desktop software. He contends that by eliminating the main menu and de-emphasizing windows, Microsoft has essentially reduced the interface to just Icons and Pointing devices—the same model that powers web browsers. Atwood sees this as significant because Office traditionally sets UI trends that the rest of the industry follows, evidenced by IE7 and Vista already hiding their menus by default. He praises Microsoft for taking such a bold risk with their flagship product and declares it the first version of Office worth upgrading to.
key_points: Office 2007's Ribbon interface signals the death of the main menu as a fundamental GUI metaphor
The new interface reduces WIMP to just 'IP'—Icons and Pointing devices—mirroring web browser design
Web browsers have proven that users adapt more readily to simpler interfaces without traditional menus and windows
Microsoft's willingness to overhaul their flagship product's UI demonstrates unusual courage, especially compared to Vista's conservative approach
Where Office goes with UI conventions, the rest of the software industry tends to follow
money_quotes: The new Office 2007 user interface is one of the most innovative things to come out of Redmond in years.
It's nothing less than the death of the main menu as a keystone GUI metaphor.
If Office 2007 drops the W and M from WIMP, what are we left with? IP. Icons and Pointing Devices.
The world's most popular GUI environment, the web browser, has no Menus or Windows.
People seem to adapt to the web much more readily than traditional WIMP apps.
It's impressive that Microsoft was willing to make such a large change to their flagship application.
It's the first version of Office worth upgrading to.
posts.key_insight: Office 2007's abandonment of traditional menus aligns desktop software with the simpler, more intuitive interface model that web browsers have proven users prefer.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the failed effort to enforce well-formed XHTML on the web, citing Ian Hickson's study showing over 93% of tested HTML files contained syntax errors. Even Tim Berners-Lee acknowledged that the push for strict XML compliance didn't succeed because browsers never enforced it. Atwood argues that HTML's tolerance of malformed markup is a feature, not a bug—browsers aren't compilers and shouldn't fail on invalid code. The post concludes with a pragmatic, if provocative, question about whether developers should care about HTML validity when neither browsers nor users do.
key_points: A study of 667,416 HTML files found over 93% had syntax errors, suggesting malformed HTML is the norm rather than the exception
The XHTML transition failed because browsers didn't enforce well-formedness, so the HTML-generating public never moved
Even Google's homepage contained 63 HTML validation errors at the time of writing
HTML's tolerance of errors is intentional by design—browsers parse tag soup rather than failing like compilers
Showing constant 'this page is broken' warnings would be bad UX since almost every page would trigger them
money_quotes: Over 93%. That's only syntax errors in the HTML, not checking the CSS, the content types, the semantic errors.
The large HTML-generating public did not move, largely because the browsers didn't complain.
Like it or not, we live in a world of malformed HTML.
Browsers aren't compilers. They don't fail spectacularly when they encounter invalid markup. And nor should they.
HTML is, and always has been, tolerant by design.
We'll always be awash in a sea of tag soup.
Your browser doesn't care if your HTML is well-formed. Your users don't care if your HTML is well-formed. So why should you?
posts.key_insight: The web's tolerance of malformed HTML is a pragmatic reality that won, and standards purists lost, because browsers chose user experience over strict enforcement.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques Microsoft Project for embodying and reinforcing the waterfall methodology, arguing that the tool's visual design literally manifests as a waterfall flowing across the screen. He quotes another blogger's observation that project management in IT is a 'consensual hallucination' built on the false belief that projects can be predicted down to the day and dollar. Atwood suggests that while some level of planning is necessary, the rigid determinism that tools like Microsoft Project encourage is 'clearly insanity.' He recommends alternatives to waterfall methodology and Steve McConnell's book on software estimation, noting pointedly that McConnell covers estimation without mentioning Microsoft Project at all.
key_points: Microsoft Project's visual design literally embodies the waterfall methodology, with every project file appearing as a giant waterfall flowing left to right
The belief that project management can predict timelines and costs with precision is a 'consensual hallucination' common in IT management
While ballpark estimates based on experience are possible, expecting project plans to be flawless and unchangeable before any work begins is unrealistic
There's a troubling gap between reasonable goal-setting and the rigid '100% accurate, carved in stone' mentality that pervades management
McConnell's estimation book notably covers the topic without mentioning Microsoft Project, suggesting better approaches exist
money_quotes: Every Microsoft Project file I open is a giant waterfall, inexorably flowing across the page from left to right like Twain's mighty Mississippi river.
When you work in IT, you deal with the consensual hallucination of Project Management.
There is an almost universal belief that it is possible to predict ahead of time how long a project will take, how much it will cost and what will happen along the way.
Despite the fact this is clearly insanity, it is a terrifyingly common mindset in management ranks.
How did we move from 'let's have a clearly defined set of project goals and a strategy for how we'll get there,' to 'this is 100% accurate, carved in stone and will never change?'
posts.key_insight: The tools we use for project management often encode flawed methodologies into their very design, making it harder to escape practices like waterfall that don't match how software development actually works.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on John Maeda's 'The Laws of Simplicity' and its relevance to software development. He argues that programmers become so accustomed to complexity that they unconsciously build it into their work. New programmers default to creating impossibly complex applications, while only experienced developers can produce truly simple, understandable software. The core message is that simplicity requires active, deliberate effort rather than passive intention.
key_points: Programmers become desensitized to complexity because they're constantly immersed in it
Complex applications are the default output for inexperienced programmers
Only seasoned developers have the skill to produce genuinely simple software
Simplicity must be actively pursued as a force, not passively hoped for
Maintaining simplicity feels like 'back-breaking work' against entropy
money_quotes: Programmers swim in a sea of unending complexity.
We get so used to complexity as an ambient norm that we begin, consciously or unconsciously, projecting it into our work.
Impossibly complex applications are the default deliverable for new programmers.
Only seasoned software development veterans are capable of producing applications that are easy to understand and troubleshoot.
Simplicity isn't achievable as a passive goal; it's a force that must be actively applied.
Keep it Simple, Stupid. If only it was that easy.
It feels more like back-breaking work to keep things from inevitably devolving into complexity.
posts.key_insight: Simplicity in software isn't a natural state or passive goal—it's a force that must be actively and continuously applied against the gravitational pull of complexity.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software projects cannot be estimated or managed like traditional engineering projects because software development is fundamentally different in nature. Unlike construction or manufacturing where you can study hundreds of similar examples, software that has been built before can simply be purchased rather than rebuilt. This economic reality means the only software projects that get funded are novel, unproven ones that haven't been done before. Atwood concludes that software development is more akin to invention at Edison's laboratory than assembly-line manufacturing, requiring different management approaches.
key_points: Software engineering differs from other engineering because if something has been built before, you buy it rather than rebuild it
The economic reality that easy, low-risk projects don't get funded is what makes software development inherently hard and risky
Software development is invention and evolution, not assembly-line manufacturing
Traditional project management tools like MS Project are misapplied to software because managers see the word 'project' and assume similarity to construction
In software, developers define and control an entire universe rather than working within fixed physical constraints like gravity
money_quotes: The only kind of software we ever build is unproven, experimental software.
The only new software development projects undertaken are those that haven't been done before or those whose predecessors are not publicly available.
No sane business is going to spend money on building software that it can buy more economically.
In software development, if you're repeating the same project over and over, you won't have a job for very long.
We're paid to build high-risk, experimental airplanes.
Software invention has more in common with Mr. Edison's Menlo Park laboratory than the local hi-rise construction site.
In software engineering projects, you aren't subject to God's rules. You get to play God: define, build, and control an entire universe in a box.
posts.key_insight: Software projects are inherently harder to estimate than construction projects because the economics of the industry ensure that only novel, never-before-built systems get funded—everything else gets purchased off the shelf.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the fundamental limitation that computers, being deterministic machines, cannot produce truly random numbers. He compares .NET's two random number generators—System.Random (algorithmic) and System.Security.Cryptography.RandomNumberGenerator (which incorporates environmental factors)—and visualizes their output. The post explains that true randomness requires sampling the analog world through sources like radioactive decay, atmospheric noise, or even lava lamps. Atwood traces the history of random number generation from coin tosses to Rand Corporation's famous book of random digits, emphasizing that understanding this digital-analog divide is crucial to avoiding security vulnerabilities.
key_points: Computers are physically incapable of true randomness because they operate on deterministic algorithms
Cryptographic random number generators incorporate environmental factors (process IDs, timestamps, CPU counters) but are still ultimately pseudo-random
True randomness requires external entropy sources like radioactive decay, atmospheric noise, or lava lamps
Even seemingly random user input like mouse movements can be predictable due to OS buffering
Environmental entropy sources have practical limits—they must be harvested over time and may not keep up with high server loads
money_quotes: These cannot be random numbers because they're produced by a computer algorithm; computers are physically incapable of randomness.
A perfect set of encrypted data is indistinguishable from random data.
To have any hope of producing truly random data, you must reach outside the computer and sample the analog world.
Computers are great number crunchers, but they're lousy random number generators.
The world is random. Computers aren't.
Randomness is really, really hard for computers.
posts.key_insight: True randomness is impossible for deterministic computers to generate internally—achieving it requires bridging the gap between the digital and analog worlds by sampling real-world entropy sources.

posts.summary: Atwood investigates the Windows MAX_PATH limitation of 260 characters after encountering path length issues during a source code migration. He writes a C# utility to find the longest paths on his system and discovers that while NTFS technically supports 32,000 character paths, Windows APIs enforce the 260 character limit. Through exploring the technical constraints and historical context, he concludes that the real problem isn't the path limit itself but rather the human tendency to create unnecessarily deep hierarchical structures. He draws on Edward Tufte's critique of Sun Microsystems' documentation system to argue that excessive hierarchy is a human organizational failure, not a technical one.
key_points: Windows enforces a MAX_PATH limit of 260 characters despite NTFS supporting 32,000 character paths
There's a Unicode workaround using the '\\?\' prefix to access extended-length paths, but it has significant limitations
Microsoft's own Windows 8.1 installation creates paths up to 273 characters, exceeding what Explorer can handle
Edward Tufte criticized Sun's 8+ level documentation hierarchy when Dr. Spock's Baby Care manual only needed two levels
Deep path hierarchies are a symptom of poor human organization rather than a legitimate technical need
money_quotes: I think 260 characters of path is more than enough rope to hang ourselves with.

If you're running into path length limitations, the real problem isn't the operating system, or even the computers. The problem is the deep, dark pit of hierarchies the human beings have dug themselves into.
Dr Spock's Baby Care is a best-selling owner's manual for the most complicated 'product' imaginable – and it only has two levels of headings.
The NTFS filesystem supports paths of 32,000 characters, but it's largely irrelevant because the majority of Windows APIs you'd use to get to those paths only accept paths of MAX_PATH or smaller.
It is possible to create a path with the API that the shell UI cannot handle.
posts.key_insight: Path length limitations are rarely a technical problem—they're a symptom of humans creating unnecessarily deep hierarchies that shouldn't exist in the first place.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains why he refuses to buy an iPod despite respecting Apple's role in legitimizing paid digital music. His five core objections include the iPod's mainstream ubiquity making it uncool, lack of subscription service support, refusal to support WMA and other audio formats, missing features like FM radio, and the requirement to use iTunes rather than simple drag-and-drop file transfers. After evaluating alternatives, he recommends the Creative Zen Vision:M and iRiver Clix as superior choices that support subscription services and work as standard USB devices without proprietary software.
key_points: The iPod's mainstream adoption has stripped it of its cool factor as a technical accessory
Apple's purchase-only model ignores the value proposition of subscription services like Yahoo Music Unlimited at $6/month for unlimited access
The lack of WMA, OGG, and FLAC support is a deliberate political choice rather than a technical limitation
The requirement to use iTunes rather than simple drag-and-drop makes the iPod unnecessarily restrictive
Media Transfer Protocol (MTP) devices offer a better user experience by working without installing any software
money_quotes: When every soccer Mom carries an iPod, it's no longer a cool technical accessory. It's completely mainstream.
For that same six bucks a month, I could buy a whopping six tracks from the iTunes store.
The conspicuous lack of WMA support is a not-so-subtle f*ck you to the Windows community.
Clearly, the hardware is capable, but the political forces inside Apple won't allow it.
You can't even use it as an external hard drive without setting up a separate, special partition on the device first.
How did Apple get this so very, very wrong?
I respect the way the pioneering iPod has collectively led the industry out of the dark Napster ages.
posts.key_insight: Despite Apple's success in legitimizing digital music, the iPod's closed ecosystem and lack of format flexibility make it an inferior choice for power users who value openness and subscription services.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores Windows Vista's new search functionality, which replaces XP's familiar search syntax with Windows Desktop Search query syntax. He walks through the key differences, including the requirement for all-caps boolean operators and the distinction between indexed and non-indexed locations. The post highlights useful features like the Search Pane for learning syntax, the ability to search from the Start menu, and important caveats about file extension indexing. Atwood is generally positive about the upgrade, calling it a 'far richer search paradigm' than XP offered.
key_points: Vista's search syntax is completely different from XP, requiring users to relearn their search habits (e.g., boolean operators must be ALL CAPS)
The Search Pane serves as 'training wheels' that shows equivalent query terms as you use the GUI, helping users learn the new syntax
Search only extends to file contents in indexed locations, with a limited default set of indexed folders
The full-text indexer only indexes file extensions it recognizes, which users can modify in Advanced Options
Start menu search is highlighted as the 'coolest new feature' - just hit Windows key and start typing
money_quotes: Note that the boolean operator must be in all-caps to work. That was painful to figure out.
It's a far richer search paradigm than we ever had in XP.
Once you've learned the new Vista search syntax, you won't need the Search Pane training wheels any more; you can press CTRL+E and type in what you want. It's Google-icious.
I'm also very glad to see search now ignores compressed files by default. This was a real pain in XP, which insisted on digging through 600 megabyte ZIP files as a part of any search.
There's nothing to install, nothing to configure, searching just works in Vista. It's about time.
posts.key_insight: Vista's search represents a significant upgrade over XP, but requires users to completely relearn their search habits and understand the distinction between indexed and non-indexed locations to use it effectively.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the growing transistor counts in GPUs compared to CPUs, noting that NVIDIA's 8800 GTX surpasses Intel's quad-core processor in complexity. He explores how GPUs have evolved into stream processors capable of handling floating-point intensive tasks beyond graphics, citing Stanford's Folding@Home project as a key example. The post discusses emerging technologies like Brook Language and CUDA that enable C-like programming on GPUs. Atwood concludes that while CPUs and GPUs serve different purposes, the massive performance gains possible with GPU computing will drive many computational problems to migrate from CPU to GPU.
key_points: GPUs have surpassed CPUs in transistor count, with NVIDIA's 8800 GTX containing 680 million transistors versus Intel's quad-core at 582 million
Dedicated task-specific hardware dramatically outperforms general-purpose CPUs - Deep Blue's 1997 hardware still evaluates chess moves 25x faster than 2006's best CPUs
GPUs have evolved into stream processors that can handle non-graphics floating-point intensive workloads at scale
New technologies like ATI's Stream API and NVIDIA's CUDA are making GPU programming accessible through C-like languages
The symbiotic relationship between CPU and GPU will see many computing problems migrate to GPU for order-of-magnitude performance gains
money_quotes: GPUs now exceed the complexity of modern CPUs in terms of absolute transistor count.
Dedicated, task-specific hardware is orders of magnitude faster than what you can achieve with a general purpose CPU.
Ten year old custom hardware is still 25 times faster than the best general purpose CPUs. Amazing.
Modern GPUs are just as fast as custom hardware, but due to economies of scale are many, many times cheaper than custom hardware.
I expect the relationship between CPU and GPU to largely be a symbiotic one: they're good at different things.
I also expect quite a few computing problems to make the jump from CPU to GPU in the next 5 years.
posts.key_insight: The explosive growth in GPU transistor counts and the emergence of general-purpose GPU programming frameworks signals a coming shift where many computational problems will migrate from CPU to GPU for dramatic performance gains.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the debate between threaded and flat discussion formats in online forums, ultimately arguing strongly for flat discussions. He references Joel Spolsky's influential article on building communities with software, which advocates for seemingly counterintuitive forum policies like no registration and no user moderation. Atwood agrees with Joel's critique that threaded discussions, while appealing to programmer logic, don't match how real-world conversations flow. He notes that major forum platforms like phpBB and vBulletin default to flat views or refuse to implement threading entirely. The post concludes that until a better model emerges, simple flat discussions remain the superior choice despite their own minor limitations.
key_points: Threaded discussions appeal to programmer logic but don't reflect natural conversation patterns
Major forum platforms (phpBB, vBulletin) deliberately avoid or disable threading by default
Joel Spolsky's seemingly anarchic forum policies (no registration, no moderation, arbitrary deletion) actually work well in practice
Threaded views create painful UX with excessive clicking and 'pogo-ing' through hierarchies
Flat discussions have limitations but are far less problematic than threaded alternatives
money_quotes: Branching is very logical to a programmer's mind but it doesn't correspond to the way conversations take place in the real world.
Personally, I have yet find any threaded discussion format I like.
Flat discussion views have their limitations, too. But they're minor compared to the train wreck that is threaded discussions.
Reads like a recipe for disaster, doesn't it?
When it comes to writing social software, Joel's curmudgeonly advice may very well be the right approach.
posts.key_insight: What seems logical to programmers (branching threads) often fails in practice because software design must account for how humans actually behave, not how they theoretically should.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that developers should not design user interfaces, using 'The Dialog' as a cautionary tale of how developer-created UIs spiral out of control. He describes the common pattern where a simple dialog grows increasingly complex as developers add features without design oversight, eventually becoming unusable but shipping anyway because it's too late to fix. The post warns that developers are blind to the usability problems they create because they become accustomed to their own interfaces. Atwood concludes that good developers recognize their limitations and either copy well-designed interfaces or delegate UI work to design professionals.
key_points: Developer-created UIs often start simple but grow into unusable monstrosities through incremental feature additions
Developers become blind to UI problems because they grow accustomed to their own interfaces over time
The 'Dialog' anti-pattern describes how temporary developer tools accidentally ship in final products
Self-awareness about one's limitations is a mark of a good software developer
When lacking design skills, developers should copy existing good designs rather than creating original bad ones
money_quotes: Deep down inside every software developer, there's a budding graphic designer waiting to get out. And if you let that happen, you're in trouble.
Pretty soon the technical team are so used to seeing 'the dialog' the way it is that they become blind to its strange appearance.
Friends don't let friends produce Developer UI.
Part of being a good software developer is knowing your limits.
Either copy something that's already well designed, or have the good sense to stick to coding and leave the graphic design to the experts.
posts.key_insight: Good software developers recognize that UI design is a distinct skill they likely don't possess, and they either borrow proven designs or collaborate with design experts rather than inflicting their amateur interfaces on users.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that project postmortems are an essential but often neglected practice in software development. He contends that the specific format of a postmortem matters less than simply doing one, noting that most teams rush to the next project without reflecting on what went wrong. Drawing heavily from game development—one of the most demanding software disciplines—he highlights resources like Gamasutra postmortems and behind-the-scenes investigations of notorious projects. The post uses Duke Nukem Forever's legendary vaporware status as a cautionary example of what happens when development goes off the rails.
key_points: The difference between average and excellent developers is learning from mistakes rather than knowing the latest technologies
The specific postmortem methodology matters far less than actually conducting one
Game development serves as a rich source of postmortem knowledge due to its extreme difficulty and pressure
Most teams skip postmortems because they rush to the next project, then wonder why they repeat the same problems
Postmortems should capture both successes and failures to draw meaningful conclusions
money_quotes: The difference between average programmers and excellent developers is not a matter of knowing the latest language or buzzword-laden technique. Rather, it can boil down to something as simple as not making the same mistakes over and over again.
I don't think it matters how you conduct the postmortem, as long as you do it.
Most shops are far too busy rushing ahead to the next project to spend any time thinking about how they could improve and refine their software development process.
And then they wonder why their new project suffers from all the same problems as their previous project.
Game development is some of the most difficult software development on the planet.
If you don't conduct project postmortems, then how can you possibly know what you're doing right – and more importantly, how to avoid making the same exact mistakes on your next project?
posts.key_insight: Excellence in software development comes not from mastering new technologies but from systematically learning from past mistakes through deliberate reflection.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to Wil Shipley's criticism that Windows users don't support independent software developers by declaring 'Support Your Favorite Small Software Vendor Day.' He acknowledges that 90% of Windows software is crap but argues that Sturgeon's Law applies to all platforms. Atwood confesses to using Media Monkey extensively without paying and rectifies this by registering it. He challenges Windows users to find software they rely on daily and actually pay for it, arguing that professional developers should be willing to spend $20 a month supporting independent developers.
key_points: Wil Shipley claims Windows users don't believe in the 'little guy' and prefer safe, market-leading choices over quality indie software
Atwood concedes that 90% of Windows software is crap but notes this applies to all media per Sturgeon's Law
He declares December 1st, 2006 as 'Support Your Favorite Small Software Vendor Day' to counter negative stereotypes about Windows users
Professional developers should be able to spend $20/month supporting independent software creators whose tools they use daily
Supporting indie developers is both an obligation to them and to ourselves as users who benefit from their work
money_quotes: 90% of Windows software is absolute unfettered crap which should never be installed on any computer running any operating system. Ever.
But our 90% is larger than your 90%.
