Let's support the little guy who still gives a damn about creating small, beautiful, useful apps on an operating system that gets no respect.
Stop reading this post right now and buy that software.

If I can't come up with the scratch to spend a measly $20 a month supporting the very best work of my fellow independent software developers, can I really call myself a professional software developer?
I believe in the little guy writing cool Windows software. And by 'believe in,' I mean 'pay.'
posts.key_insight: Believing in independent software developers means actually paying for their software, not just using and appreciating it.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his curated list of geek gift guides for the 2006 holiday season, along with a personal recommendation for the iRiver Clix music player. He positions the post as a resource for finding quirky, offbeat tech gifts while acknowledging his own impulse-buying tendencies. The iRiver Clix is praised as a superior alternative to the iPod due to its broader format support, FM radio, and drag-and-drop simplicity. The post is essentially a link roundup with personal commentary, inviting readers to share additional gift guide recommendations.
key_points: Curates multiple 2006 holiday gift guides from sources like Cool Tools, ThinkGeek, Engadget, and CNET
Personally recommends the 4GB iRiver Clix as an iPod alternative with more features
Highlights the Clix's advantages: subscription services, WMA/OGG support, FM radio, no software required
Emphasizes the value of drag-and-drop simplicity over iTunes-style software dependencies
money_quotes: The only reason to grow up is so you can afford to buy yourself all the crap your parents wouldn't buy you when you were a kid.
It does everything the iPod refuses to do: subscription services, wma/ogg support, FM Radio (w/recording!), Flash Lite games, video playback, and voice recording.
Just plug it in, drag and drop your music on it, then play.
posts.key_insight: The best tech gifts are the ones that prioritize user freedom and simplicity over locked-down ecosystems.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his job title of 'evangelist' and argues that the religious connotations are actually fitting for software development, which he sees as inherently religious in its tribal debates and passionate advocacy. He contends that effective evangelism requires both education and entertainment, and that the core skill underlying it—clear communication—is what separates great programmers from merely tolerable ones. Atwood suggests that many developers are already evangelists without realizing it, as anyone who shares their technical excitement and convinces others of their ideas is practicing evangelism. The post frames evangelism not as proselytization but as the essential skill of communicating ideas effectively.
key_points: The religious connotations of 'evangelist' are appropriate because software development itself functions like a religion with its holy wars and tribal loyalties
Good evangelism requires both education and entertainment—without humor, you're just a sanctimonious bore
Communication skills are what separate great programmers from tolerable ones, more than technical knowledge
Many developers are already evangelists without the title, simply by effectively sharing their enthusiasm and convincing others
Writing code alone isn't enough—you have to make people believe in your code's value
money_quotes: Software development is a religion, and any programmer worth his or her salt is the scarred veteran of a thousand religious wars.
Entertainment is a crucial part of being an evangelist. Without it, you're a sanctimonious bore.

The difference between a tolerable programmer and a great programmer is not how many programming languages they know, and it's not whether they prefer Python or Java. It's whether they can communicate their ideas.
It's questionable how effective you can be by writing code alone. You have to convince us how awesome your code is. You have to make us believe in the glory of that code.
Evangelism is simply sharing your excitement and enthusiasm with other people in an effective way.
We're all disciples of the church of Von Neumann. Praise the lord and pass the ones and zeroes!
Laughing is the only sane, rational response to any kind of evangelism – it means you have a sense of humor.
posts.key_insight: Being a great programmer isn't just about writing code—it's about convincing others of your ideas, which makes communication and evangelism inseparable from technical excellence.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts his experience attending Edward Tufte's one-day course on presenting data and information in San Francisco. He explains that the course fee includes all four of Tufte's books because Tufte uses them as high-resolution handouts rather than relying on projectors. Despite having read the books before, Atwood found value in Tufte's guided commentary that revealed nuances he had missed. The post culminates in sharing Tufte's extensive reading list and resource recommendations for analytical design education, plus a curated list of 20th-century classics in information architecture.
key_points: Tufte uses his own books as high-resolution handouts during the course, avoiding PowerPoint and projectors for most content
The course includes all four Tufte books, making the net cost only about $130 after group discounts when factoring in their Amazon price
Despite his dry writing, Tufte is a funny and animated speaker who brings out nuances in the text that readers miss on their own
Tufte provides an extensive curated reading list spanning typography, design, cartography, data visualization, and statistics
The post catalogs Tufte's picks for 20th-century information architecture classics, from the Hertzsprung-Russell Diagram to the Internet itself
money_quotes: He uses the books as high-resolution handouts.
Edward Tufte is a funny, animated speaker.
There's nothing in the course you couldn't get directly from the books yourself, but the material is always more effective when it's presented by a good teacher.
Strunk and White, The Elements of Style, the last chapter on style (read once a year).

The quality of interface design may have peaked around 1990; too many computer screens today are overrun with operating system imperialism, featuritis, marketeering banners, and overproduced gimmicks.
The greatest dispersion and intensification of information since the printing of books with moveable type in Korea and China around 650.
posts.key_insight: The best teachers don't just present information—they guide you through material in ways that reveal nuances you'd miss reading alone, which is why high-resolution physical artifacts often beat digital presentations.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that three monitors represents the sweet spot for developer productivity, offering 50% more display area than dual monitors and a natural center point for focus. He compares himself to Bill Gates and Larry Page as fellow three-monitor users, emphasizing that this setup is fundamental to serious programming work. The post explores two main approaches: adding a second video card (preferred) or using the Matrox TripleHead2Go external device. While the TripleHead2Go has limitations like treating all monitors as one giant desktop, it excels for laptops and gaming where hardware-level abstraction enables impressive immersive experiences with peripheral vision.
key_points: Three monitors is the productivity sweet spot because it provides a natural center focus point and 50% more screen real estate than dual monitors
The traditional method of adding a second video card is preferred because the OS can recognize each monitor independently
The Matrox TripleHead2Go offers an alternative that works with laptops and games but creates a single giant desktop with significant usability downsides
Gaming benefits unexpectedly from the TripleHead2Go approach since it sidesteps multi-GPU acceleration issues and game compatibility problems
The 'giant desktop' approach has serious UX problems: maximized windows span all screens, dialogs split awkwardly, and the start menu lands on the wrong monitor
money_quotes: Once you have that large display area, you'll never go back, because it has a direct impact on productivity.
If you're only using one monitor, you are cheating yourself out of potential productivity.
I don't care how large a single display can be; you can never have enough desktop space.
Maximizing a window becomes an exercise in futility.
The combination of a three-monitor setup with head tracking is incredibly immersive, and has to be seen to be believed.
The traditional 'add another video card' method is still the preferred way to gain entry into the prestigious three monitor club.
posts.key_insight: Three monitors hits the sweet spot for developer productivity by providing both a natural center focus point and enough peripheral screen space, but achieving this properly requires the OS to recognize each display independently rather than faking one giant desktop.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines Moore's Law by comparing its theoretical predictions against real-world CPU performance benchmarks. He notes that transistor counts have actually been doubling every 24 months rather than the commonly cited 18 months. Using SysMark 2004 benchmarks, he demonstrates that it took 26 months for practical PC performance to double, closely tracking the transistor doubling rate. This validates the looser interpretation of Moore's Law as a reliable predictor of computing power increases.
key_points: The popular 18-month formulation of Moore's Law is inaccurate; transistor counts have actually doubled every 24 months
SysMark 2004 benchmarks provide a practical way to measure real-world performance gains calibrated against a reference system
The Pentium 4 EE 3.2 GHz doubled the performance of the P4 2.0 GHz in 26 months, closely matching transistor doubling rates
CPU transistor count serves as a fairly reliable indicator of overall system performance despite other components improving at different rates
money_quotes: The number of transistors on a CPU hasn't actually been doubling every 18 months; it's been doubling every 24 months.
That's why I prefer the second, looser definition of Moore's law: dramatic increases in computing power per unit cost.
It took 26 months to double real world performance in SysMark 2004.
CPU transistor count is fairly reliable indicator of overall performance.
posts.key_insight: Real-world CPU performance doubles approximately every two years, closely tracking transistor count increases and validating Moore's Law as a practical predictor of computing power growth.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores Edward Tufte's observations about the vast resolution gap between printed pages and computer displays. He demonstrates this by scanning sparklines from Tufte's 'Beautiful Evidence' at various resolutions, showing how printed material at 600-2400 DPI dwarfs the typical 72-100 DPI of computer monitors. The post serves as a practical illustration of why print media can achieve far greater information density than screens. Even cheap printers dramatically outperform the most expensive displays of the era.
key_points: Commercial printing operates at around 2400 DPI, while typical computer displays only achieve 72-100 DPI
Sparklines are a Tufte technique specifically designed to exploit print's superior resolution
Even a 7-year-old 600 DPI printer produces vastly sharper output than contemporary high-end monitors
The resolution gap explains why printed pages can achieve greater information density than screens
money_quotes: A recurring theme in Edward Tufte's books is the massive difference in resolution between the printed page and computer displays.
Printed pages lend themselves to vastly greater information density.
The difference in resolution between the most expensive computer display you can buy and a cheap off-brand printer really is astronomical.
We have a long, long way to go before computer displays can get anywhere near printer resolutions.
posts.key_insight: The resolution gap between print and screen isn't just noticeable—it's so vast that even obsolete printers dramatically outperform cutting-edge displays, fundamentally limiting what we can accomplish with on-screen information design.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that databases must be kept under version control alongside application code, noting that most development teams fail to do this despite it being critical for deployment consistency. He points out that deploying mismatched application and database versions inevitably breaks software. The post reviews Microsoft's Team Edition for Database Professionals as a comprehensive solution that goes beyond simple file-based version control to include test data generation, schema comparison, and database unit testing. Atwood emphasizes that the specific tooling matters less than actually implementing database version control, which he considers an obvious practice that remains surprisingly uncommon.
key_points: Databases should be treated as first-class citizens in version control, deployed in lockstep with application code
Many teams roll their own tools to reverse-engineer databases into files for source control, but commercial tools offer more robust solutions
Test data generation solves the common problem of developers working with inadequate data volumes and discovering performance issues too late
Schema comparison tools enable quick debugging of environment differences and automatic update script generation
Database unit tests are essential for detecting when schema changes break dependent applications
money_quotes: When I ask development teams whether their database is under version control, I usually get blank stares.
If you deploy version 2.0 of your application against version 1.0 of your database, what do you get? A broken application.
Your database should always be under source control right next to your application code.
Like peanut butter and chocolate, they are two great tastes that taste great together.

I wish I had a dollar every time an application I've worked on began to have performance problems because none of the developers had the foresight to test the app with more than a few rows of crappy, manually entered test data.

If you make a change to the database schema, how do you know if you've broken any applications that rely on that database? You know because you've written unit tests that validate the database. Right? Right?
Get your database under version control by any means necessary.
Making your database a first-class citizen in source control seems totally obvious in retrospect. Now if only we could convince more developers to actually do it.
posts.key_insight: Your database is not separate infrastructure but an integral part of your application that must be versioned, tested, and deployed together with your code.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents his upgrade to three matching Samsung 204B LCD monitors and reflects on how far LCD technology has advanced since 2003. He demonstrates through specs comparison that response times have improved dramatically from 25ms to 5ms, addressing the key criticism that made LCDs inferior to CRTs for gaming and video. The post provides practical advice for optimal LCD setup: always use DVI, set refresh rate to 60Hz, and use native resolution. While celebrating LCD victory over CRTs, Atwood acknowledges lingering issues with viewing angles, color reproduction, and backlight uniformity that still affect professional graphics work.
key_points: LCD response times improved from 25ms to 5ms between 2003-2006, eliminating the ghosting and streaking that previously made CRTs superior for video and gaming
Digital DVI connections eliminate the analog conversion issues that plague VGA connections, providing perfect image quality with no calibration needed
Unlike CRTs that required extensive tweaking of refresh rate, sizing, pincushion, and other analog settings, LCDs work optimally with minimal configuration
LCDs still have inherent limitations in viewing angles, true black reproduction, and backlight uniformity that matter for professional graphics work
The economics shifted dramatically: similar resolution monitors dropped from $660-840 to $320-350 in just a few years
money_quotes: Getting great image quality with an LCD is dead simple.

Using a VGA port with an LCD is downright pathological – it means you're converting the digital bits inside your video card to an analog video signal, then converting the analog video signal back to digital bits inside the LCD.
It's fair to say that LCDs have won the hearts, minds, and wallets of the public in 2006.
LCDs work best at their native resolution – when there is one pixel on the LCD for every pixel on the screen.
The earlier good news – that LCD displays generally don't need to be adjusted to produce good image quality – is also the bad news here. There's not much you can adjust on a digitally connected LCD.
Blacks are never quite as deep as they would be on a CRT. And the backlighting is never perfectly uniform.
posts.key_insight: By 2006, LCD technology had matured enough that its simplicity and digital precision outweighed its remaining technical limitations, marking the definitive end of the CRT era for most users.

posts.summary: This ultra-short post shares a humorous pie chart joke where the chart itself visually resembles Ms. Pac-Man. Atwood uses this as a springboard to make a quick observation about early video game design conventions, specifically how female characters were distinguished from male ones simply by adding a bow. The post is essentially a visual gag with a brief cultural commentary on 8-bit era gender representation in games.
key_points: Pie charts can accidentally (or intentionally) resemble classic video game characters
Early video game design used minimal visual cues like bows to indicate female characters
The 'Ms.' prefix plus a bow was the standard formula for creating female versions of male game characters
money_quotes: In the innocent era of 8-bit arcades, you made characters female by adding a cute little pixelated red bow.
posts.key_insight: Early video game design relied on the simplest possible visual shorthand—a red bow—to communicate gender, reflecting both the technical constraints and cultural assumptions of the 8-bit era.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the state of high-definition video playback on PCs in late 2006, testing various systems' ability to handle 720p and 1080p content. He discovers that mainstream PCs are only just becoming powerful enough to decode HD video, with dual-core processors being essential for smooth 1080p playback. The post examines the three competing video codecs (H.264, VC-1, MPEG-2) used by HD-DVD and Blu-Ray, noting that next-gen encoders look better but require significantly more CPU power. Atwood also discusses the potential for GPU-accelerated video decoding, though he notes the configuration process is frustratingly complex on Windows XP.
key_points: True 1080p (1920x1080) playback requires substantial CPU power that only dual-core processors could reliably provide in 2006
Microsoft's 'HD showcase' clips used 1440x1080 rather than true 1080p, likely due to performance limitations at the time
H.264 and VC-1 codecs offer superior quality and compression over MPEG-2, but demand significantly more processing power
GPU video acceleration can reduce CPU load by 20-50%, but proper configuration on Windows XP is daunting
Interlaced video modes are inferior to progressive and should be avoided whenever possible
money_quotes: It clearly requires a lot of CPU horse power to render high definition video.
The mainstream PC is only now catching up to the performance required to watch high definition video.
Woe to the poor consumer who buys a HD-DVD or Blu-Ray disc encoded with the ancient MPEG-2 encoder. It'll look awful and take up a lot more room than it should.
At least we have a few legitimate uses left for the ridiculous amounts of CPU power we've inherited over the years.
But good luck getting everything set up in Windows XP.
posts.key_insight: High-definition video represents one of the few genuinely demanding use cases that justifies the rapid increase in consumer CPU power, though the complexity of properly configuring hardware acceleration remains a significant barrier to mainstream adoption.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood questions whether the jump from DVD to HD-DVD/Blu-Ray resolution justifies the cost of new players, TVs, and media. He presents visual comparisons and cites David Pogue's experiment showing most people couldn't distinguish between 5 and 13 megapixel prints. While acknowledging that higher resolution sources do scale better even to lower-resolution displays, he argues the practical difference is highly subjective and represents diminishing returns compared to the earlier leap from standard TV to DVD. The format war, copy protection issues, and exploitation-focused business models further undermine the value proposition.
key_points: The resolution jump from 720x480 to 1920x1080 represents nearly 5x more pixels, but practical perceptibility is questionable at typical viewing distances
David Pogue's experiment showed 95% of people couldn't distinguish between 5MP and 13MP prints even up close, suggesting resolution differences are overrated
YouTube's success with 'appalling' video quality proves different rules apply to motion video than static images
Higher resolution sources provide benefits through supersampling even when displayed on lower-resolution screens
The format war, DRM pitfalls, and exploitative business practices make early adoption risky
money_quotes: How, exactly, do we explain the runaway success of YouTube, where video quality is never less than appalling? Clearly, the rules are different for images in motion.
I'm telling you, there was NO DIFFERENCE.
The practical difference between DVD resolution and next-gen DVD resolution is highly subjective.
Is anyone actually arguing that DVDs don't look good enough?
Both formats reek of exploitation.
Although I'll always be a fan of increased resolution, I'm not sure these additional pixels have earned my money yet.
I also think people tend to overestimate how much detail is needed for an image of a given size.
posts.key_insight: Resolution improvements face diminishing perceptual returns, and the technology industry often overestimates how much consumers can actually see—or care about—increased pixel density.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that hard drive temperature is critically undermonitored compared to CPU and GPU temperatures, despite hard drives being the most temperature-sensitive components in a computer. After experiencing a fan failure, he observed his drives climbing to 46C at idle with no airflow, and shares that he's already had one drive failure he attributes to heat. He cites research suggesting that each degree of temperature reduction extends drive life by 10%, and emphasizes that unlike other component failures, hard drive failures result in catastrophic data loss. His solution of adding an 80mm fan dropped temperatures by 13 degrees.
key_points: Hard drives have a much narrower safe operating temperature range (5-55C) compared to CPUs (up to 70C) and GPUs (up to 90C)
Each one-degree drop in hard drive temperature is equivalent to a 10% increase in service life
Rubber grommet mounting for noise reduction eliminates the auxiliary cooling benefit of metal-to-metal contact
Hard drives generate disproportionate heat relative to their power consumption due to mechanical friction
Unlike CPU or GPU failures, hard drive failures result in permanent data loss without proper backups
money_quotes: The hard drive is the most temperature sensitive device inside your computer.
Each one-degree drop of HDD temperature is equivalent to a 10% increase of HDD service life.

When your CPU, video card, or motherboard fails, you buy a new one and replace it. Big deal. Life goes on. But when your hard drive fails, unless you have a rigorous backup regime, you just lost all your data.
Failure of a hard drive tends to have catastrophic consequences for your data.
Hard drive temperature is arguably the most important temperature to monitor in your computer.
Increasing HDD temperature by 5C has the same effect on reliability as switching from 10% to 100% HDD workload.
posts.key_insight: While PC enthusiasts obsess over CPU and GPU temperatures, hard drive temperatures matter far more because drives have narrower thermal tolerances and their failure means losing your data, not just replacing a component.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while developers should first strive to write self-explanatory code, comments remain essential for explaining the 'why' behind implementation decisions. He emphasizes that code should be written primarily for human readers, not machines, citing classics like SICP and Knuth's literate programming. Through a concrete example, he demonstrates how poor code cluttered with inline comments can be refactored into cleaner, self-documenting code using meaningful names. However, he ultimately concludes that comments cannot be fully replaced because code alone cannot explain rationale, rejected alternatives, or contextual decisions.
key_points: Write code for human readers first, compilers second - this naturally reduces the need for excessive comments
Bad comments often serve as a crutch for poorly written code; refactoring with meaningful names is preferable
Comments are irreplaceable for explaining why decisions were made, not just how code works
More comments doesn't mean more readable code - sometimes fewer comments with better naming improves clarity
What seems obvious to one developer may be completely opaque to another without context
money_quotes: Programs must be written for people to read, and only incidentally for machines to execute.
The best kind of comments are the ones you don't need.
Code can't explain why the program is being written, and the rationale for choosing this or that method.
The comments in the snippet above – if you even noticed them – only clutter the code even more.
Code can only tell you how the program works; comments can tell you why it works.
Let us concentrate rather on explaining to human beings what we want a computer to do.
What is perfectly, transparently obvious to one developer may be utterly opaque to another developer who has no context.
posts.key_insight: Self-documenting code and good comments serve complementary purposes: code explains the how, while comments explain the why, and neither can fully substitute for the other.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood writes an appreciation post for Eric Lippert, a Microsoft blogger he considers one of his favorites. After listing numerous must-read posts from Lippert's blog covering topics from error messages to Hungarian notation, Atwood pivots to a humorous complaint: Lippert's blog design uses an eye-straining purple color scheme with Lucida Sans Unicode font. Atwood stages a mock 'intervention,' begging Lippert to abandon his purple aesthetic while acknowledging that great content deserves better presentation.
key_points: Eric Lippert exemplifies the thoughtful, near-genius programmers still working at Microsoft despite the company's problems
Great communicators possess the singular gift of making any topic interesting
Excellent content can be undermined by poor visual presentation and design choices
Even throwaway comments from skilled writers can be worthy of entire blog posts
Personal quirks and individuality have limits when they actively harm the reader experience
money_quotes: He's one of those people who reminds you that Microsoft, despite all its problems, still employs a lot of incredibly thoughtful, near-genius programmers.
Eric has the singular gift of all great communicators: he can make any topic interesting.
Even Eric's throwaway comments are worthy of entire blog posts.
Your content is incredible, Eric, but the presentation is killing your poor readers' eyesight.
It's time to let go of the purple crayon.
posts.key_insight: Even the most brilliant technical content suffers when wrapped in poor presentation, and readers shouldn't have to endure bad design to access great ideas.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces he's disabling trackbacks on his blog due to overwhelming spam, explaining that what was once a promising technology for linking conversations across blogs has become utterly broken. The trackback specification, published by Six Apart in 2002, lacked any authentication mechanism, making it trivially easy for spammers to abuse. Despite trying blacklists and other countermeasures for years, Atwood concedes defeat as spammers can register domains and mass-post faster than anyone can block them. He's replacing trackbacks with Technorati's link indexing service as a workaround, while emphasizing that social software designers must account for malicious users from the start.
key_points: Trackbacks were designed to create coherent conversations across different blogs by automatically linking related posts
The original Six Apart trackback specification had no authentication mechanism, making abuse trivially easy
CAPTCHA solved comment spam but is useless for trackbacks since they're machine-entered by design
Band-aid solutions like moderation queues and distributed blacklists aren't sustainable against determined spammers
Social software must be designed from day one with the assumption that some users will be malicious
money_quotes: I often am full of crap.
Trackbacks are a way of relating conversations across websites.
Unfortunately, trackbacks are so horribly and fundamentally broken that they're effectively useless.
CAPTCHA has completely solved my comment spam problem. But distinguishing between humans and machines is useless on trackbacks, which are all machine entered by definition.
These are only slightly larger band-aids over a massive, sucking chest wound.
Trackbacks, as we currently know them, are dead, kaput, expired.
That's what happens when you design social software without considering the impact of malicious users from the very beginning.
Design from day one with the assumption that a few of your users will be evil.
posts.key_insight: Social software that doesn't account for malicious users in its initial design will inevitably be exploited and rendered useless, no matter how good the original concept.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software developers' lack of assertiveness in negotiations with executives and managers leads to unrealistic project commitments that ultimately harm the business. Drawing on Steve McConnell's insights, he explains that technical staff often cave too easily when pushed on estimates because executives naturally probe for discomfort points. The post clarifies that assertiveness isn't about being aggressive, but about communicating openly as an equal participant. Atwood recommends Dale Carnegie's classic book and practical steps like public speaking or improv classes to build these skills.
key_points: Technical staff lose negotiations with executives not because their estimates are wrong, but because they aren't assertive enough to defend them
Executives assume technical people will push back when necessary, but they typically don't, sending implicit signals they can deliver more than they actually can
True assertiveness is about being straightforward and honest while seeking win-win outcomes, not about being aggressive or confrontational
The business ultimately loses when developers don't fight for realistic estimates, leading to months or years of pretending something impossible is achievable
Assertiveness is a learnable skill that can be developed through deliberate practice like public speaking or improv classes
money_quotes: As software developers, we're great at communicating with computers. But we're typically not so great at communicating with other people.
The business loses, because it sets up a situation in which it pretends for months or years that it can do something that it can't.
They assume, incorrectly, that technical staff will be assertive with them if they need to be, and that isn't the case.
Technical people who don't push back hard enough, soon enough, are implicitly sending a message that they can do more than they really can.
There's a reason that they're executives!
To be assertive is not, as some people imagine, to be overbearing and aggressive, but to be straightforward, open and honest.
Show a genuine interest in the people you meet, and you'll find it reciprocated tenfold.
Being technologically savvy isn't enough.
posts.key_insight: When developers fail to assertively defend their estimates, they inadvertently deceive the business into believing impossible commitments are achievable.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood observes that most users, including experienced software developers, inefficiently navigate login forms by constantly switching between mouse and keyboard. He documents the eight-step mouse-heavy process people commonly use versus the simple four-step keyboard-only alternative using Tab and Enter. The post argues that this basic keyboard efficiency should be common knowledge but surprisingly isn't. Atwood encourages readers to spread awareness of this simple technique to help colleagues save time during their daily login routines.
key_points: Even experienced developers often use inefficient mouse-heavy workflows for simple tasks like logging in
The keyboard-only login method (type, Tab, type, Enter) is dramatically more efficient than mouse navigation
Constant transitions between mouse and keyboard are a hidden productivity drain
Login forms are so ubiquitous that even small efficiency gains compound significantly
Developers have a responsibility to ensure standard keyboard navigation works in their login forms
money_quotes: Every time I watch someone do this, a little part of me dies inside.

I'm not just talking about casual users like our parents. I'm talking about our fellow software developers, and other users who work with the computer for most of the day. People who really should know better.
What kills me about this is all the needless, painful transitions between the mouse and the keyboard.
Your fingers are already on the keyboard while you're typing – just add a little Tab and Enter to the mix!
All I want is to save users a few precious seconds as they slog through the endless logins during their work day.
posts.key_insight: Small inefficiencies in ubiquitous interactions like login forms compound into significant wasted time, and even technically sophisticated users often fail to optimize their most frequent workflows.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines how YouTube solved the longstanding problem of video codec incompatibility by adopting Flash Video as a universal standard. He explains that playing video on computers has historically been frustrating due to the need for specific codecs, with dozens of variants across different formats. YouTube and Google Video chose Flash because it 'just works' across browsers and platforms with minimal friction. While acknowledging that Flash Video quality is significantly inferior to alternatives like QuickTime or Windows Media, Atwood argues that universal compatibility trumps quality for most web video use cases. He concludes that Flash Video has become the de facto internet video standard, exemplifying the 'worse is better' philosophy.
key_points: Video codec fragmentation created 'codec hell' where users needed specific codecs installed to play different video formats
YouTube chose Flash Video because it works universally across browsers and platforms with easy installation
Flash Video quality is dramatically worse than alternatives like QuickTime or Windows Media
For most web video content, universal accessibility matters more than video quality
The 'worse is better' principle explains why an inferior technology can win through simplicity and ubiquity
money_quotes: Playing video on a computer has always been a crapshoot.
As the old saying goes, we love standards: that's why we have so many of them.
That's why we call it codec hell.
YouTube elected to cut the Gordian knot of video codecs: they chose Flash Video, which 'just works' on most computers.
Flash Video quality is, in a word, hideous.
Quality is largely irrelevant for most video content on the web.
It'll never win any quality awards, but it's still recognizable as video. Therefore it wins by default.
Flash Video is the new internet video standard. Sometimes worse really is better.
posts.key_insight: Universal compatibility and ease of use can trump technical quality, as YouTube proved by choosing the inferior but ubiquitous Flash Video format over superior but fragmented alternatives.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores whether LCD HDTVs could serve as cost-effective alternatives to expensive large computer monitors. He notes that 30-inch monitors are disproportionately expensive compared to their smaller counterparts, costing twice as much for only 1.7x the pixels. Citing PC Gamer's editor, he argues that ultra-high resolutions like 2560x1600 can actually be counterproductive since text becomes harder to read. The post concludes that 1080p HDTVs offer a compelling value proposition: larger screens at significantly lower prices, thanks to economies of scale in the TV market.
key_points: Large computer monitors have diminishing returns on price-to-pixel ratio, with 30-inch displays costing 2x more for only 1.7x the pixels of 23-inch models
Higher resolution isn't always better - 2560x1600 on 30-inch monitors can make text harder to read than 1920x1200 on 24-inch displays
Operating systems still struggle with resolution scaling, leaving us trapped in a bitmapped world rather than vector-based rendering
LCD HDTVs benefit from economies of scale that make them substantially cheaper than equivalent-sized computer monitors
The 37-inch Westinghouse HDTV at $1,099 offers a larger display than Apple's $1,999 30-inch Cinema Display
money_quotes: 30" is nice, but you're paying 2x the price of the 23" for 1.7x the number of pixels.
Counter-intuitively, text and fonts are larger on 1920x1200 24-inchers than they are on 2560x1600 30-inchers.
You'll have to sit closer to a 30-inch monitor than you will a 24-inch monitor in order to comfortably read text.
We still live in a bitmapped world.
LCD HDTVs look like an outstanding deal for large monitor enthusiasts.
They're larger, and due to economies of scale, should always be substantially cheaper than computer monitors, too.
posts.key_insight: The pursuit of ever-higher monitor resolutions may be misguided when our software can't scale properly, making cheaper HDTVs with 'lower' resolutions potentially more usable than expensive high-DPI monitors.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the demanding hardware requirements for playing high-definition video on PCs in late 2006. He breaks down the CPU, GPU, and memory requirements, noting that even gaming-class systems struggle with 1080p playback. The post details the upgrade path needed for HD capability, including HDMI-capable graphics cards with HDCP support and expensive Blu-Ray or HD-DVD drives. Atwood also raises concerns about DRM restrictions that may eventually require digital-only output, effectively closing the 'analog hole' that currently allows playback on older equipment.
key_points: 1080p video playback requires extremely powerful CPUs by 2006 standards—faster than typical gaming requirements
GPU decode acceleration exists but is unreliable; fast dual-core CPUs are a simpler solution
HDCP copy protection is creating artificial requirements for certified video cards with HDMI output
The HD format war means consumers face expensive choices between Blu-Ray ($699) and HD-DVD options
Content protection schemes may eventually block analog output entirely, forcing hardware upgrades
money_quotes: The CPU requirement is off the charts, even by modern gaming standards.
Getting GPU decode acceleration working is a crapshoot at best.
Fast dual core CPUs are cheaper and certainly simpler than dealing with the hassle of offloading the decoding to the video card.
Gotta plug that pesky analog hole eventually, I suppose.
Some Blu-ray Discs or HD DVD titles may require a digital output instead of analog.
posts.key_insight: The transition to HD video on PCs reveals how copy protection schemes like HDCP are driving hardware requirements as much as the actual technical demands of high-resolution playback.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood catalogs common blog and internet clichés that have become overused, drawing from a Gawker post and reader comments. He acknowledges being guilty of some himself while arguing that catchphrases can be fun in moderation but become problematic when used unconsciously. The post advocates for self-awareness in writing and intentionally varying one's expression rather than relying on predefined patterns. Atwood compares catchphrases to exclamation points—rarely necessary but powerful when timed for maximum impact. He suggests using tools like an online cliché finder to audit your writing and encourages writers to stretch beyond comfortable linguistic habits.
key_points: Clichés and catchphrases become problematic when writers don't realize how often they're using them
Using a catchphrase is like ending a sentence with an exclamation point: rarely necessary, but time it for maximum impact
Good communication requires intentionally mixing things up and finding fresh ways to express ideas
Self-awareness is the key differentiator between effective catchphrase use and lazy writing
There are countless ways to communicate any idea, so limiting yourself to predefined patterns is a choice
money_quotes: Using a catchphrase is like ending a sentence with an exclamation point: rarely necessary, but when it is, time it for maximum impact.
Most people don't realize how often they're using a catchphrase, which is why they become overused and cliché in the first place.
I refrain from using the same words, the same phrases, the same stuff too often.
There are a million ways to communicate any idea. Why limit yourself to narrow, predefined patterns of thinking, writing, and speaking?
Stretch a little. Explain it a different way this time. Rephrase. Restate. Riff on the topic.
Using clichés is fine; just be sure you aren't using them as a substitute for real communication.
posts.key_insight: Clichés aren't inherently bad, but unconscious reliance on them signals lazy thinking—good writers stay aware of their linguistic habits and intentionally vary their expression.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores why PC clocks are notoriously unreliable timekeepers, discovering that PCs with wildly inaccurate clocks outnumber accurate ones 2:1. He traces the problem to cost-cutting by manufacturers who opt for 'good enough' real-time clock hardware, and shares his journey through Windows registry settings to increase NTP synchronization frequency. The post catalogs various software bugs, exploits, and failures that stem from clock inaccuracies, from Kerberos authentication failures to game cheating hacks. Atwood concludes that while we think of time as absolute, inside a PC it's surprisingly malleable—something developers often forget until it bites them.
key_points: PCs with clocks more than 8 minutes off outnumber those within 5 seconds of correct time by roughly 2:1
Manufacturers deliberately use cheap real-time clocks because accurate timekeeping isn't necessary for basic PC function—it's a cost/benefit tradeoff
Windows provides no visible UI to change time sync frequency; fixing it requires registry hacking
AC power problems (like daisy-chained UPSes) can cause dramatic clock drift of minutes per day
Clock inaccuracies create an entire class of bugs and exploits, from authentication failures to game speed hacks
money_quotes: PCs aren't very accurate timekeepers.
You certainly won't mistake PCs for atomic clocks any time soon.
However, servers tend to be synchronized much more aggressively with authoritative time sources.
This fix only addresses the symptom, not the problem itself. Why are PC clocks so inaccurate?
The real-time clock (RTC) built into most machines is far from reliable.
There is an entire class of software problems, bugs, and exploits involving the system clock.
We tend to think of time as an absolute, a universal interval that is the same everywhere. But inside the PC, time is a malleable material.
posts.key_insight: Time inside a computer is far less reliable and absolute than we assume, creating a hidden source of bugs and security vulnerabilities that developers routinely overlook.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on two very different books that share a common thread: unbridled love for computers. He discusses Russ Walter's 'Secret Guide to Computers,' a rambling, zine-like self-published guide that compares computers to drugs and romantic partners, and Charles Petzold's 'Code,' an elegantly written technical history. Despite their opposite styles—one chaotic and personal, the other austere and beautiful—both authors demonstrate deep dedication to computing. Atwood celebrates this passion, suggesting that genuine enthusiasm for computers, even when it borders on obsession, is something to embrace rather than apologize for.
key_points: Russ Walter's 'Secret Guide to Computers' is a self-published, unconventional guide that has evolved from a 17-page pamphlet in 1972 to a 607-page book, representing decades of genuine enthusiasm
Walter's D.I.Y. ethos and personal touch—including publishing his phone number for 24/7 support and using a 'copywrong' license—outshines polished commercial alternatives
Charles Petzold's 'Code' represents the opposite approach: elegant, austere, and technically beautiful, yet equally passionate about computing fundamentals
Both extremes of computer writing—the rambling zine and the refined technical history—stem from the same root: genuine love for the machine
Passion for computing, even when it seems excessive or obsessive, is something to celebrate rather than hide
money_quotes: Computers are like drugs: you begin by spending just a little on them but soon get so excited by the experience – and so hooked – that you wind up spending more and more to feed your habit.
His no-nonsense, no-budget D.I.Y. ethos outshines any number of polished, soulless commercial books.
Instead of a long, rambling love letter, Code is a collection of elegantly written sonnets.
Sometimes love makes you do crazy things.
I'm with Petzold and Walter. If loving computers is wrong, I don't want to be right.
Marrying a computer is much groovier than marrying a person: computers are good at 'getting it on' and they never argue.
posts.key_insight: The best technical writing comes not from polish or credentials, but from genuine, unashamed passion for the subject—whether expressed through rambling enthusiasm or elegant precision.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the Sugar UI developed for the One Laptop Per Child initiative, initially skeptical but ultimately impressed by its radical departure from conventional desktop interfaces. The UI places the child at the center of the experience, with programs arranged in a ring around their icon and nearby classmates visible for collaboration. Atwood appreciates that Sugar embodies several experimental GUI concepts he's previously advocated for, including edge-based menus, abandonment of file/folder metaphors, and seamless collaboration. While he admits the interface wasn't immediately intuitive to him as an adult, he acknowledges that children without preconceptions about computers might respond differently. The post concludes with Negroponte's provocative argument that teaching children Office applications is 'criminal' when they should be creating and exploring.
key_points: The Sugar UI represents the first radically different desktop interface in years, breaking from Windows and MacOS conventions
The interface centers the user (literally) with a stick-figure icon surrounded by running programs and nearby collaborators
Sugar incorporates proven UX principles: infinite-width screen edges, pie menus, and abandonment of file/folder organization
Developing innovative UI is inherently riskier than following established patterns, which explains why it rarely happens
Children without preconceptions about computers may find unconventional interfaces more natural than adults do
money_quotes: But when was the last time anyone tried a radically different UI on the desktop?
I don't know many kids that want to grow up to be 'Information Workers.'
Children should be making things, communicating, exploring, sharing, not running office automation tools.
Developing a radically different UI has to be especially difficult. Innovation and experimentation is much riskier than following the roadmaps from Redmond and Cupertino.
I have to admit that I didn't find the Sugar UI particularly intuitive or discoverable, even after using it for 10 minutes and learning the basics. But I'm not a child.
How interesting can sub-$100 hardware running Linux really be?
posts.key_insight: Radical UI innovation is rare because it's risky, but designing for users without preconceptions—like children—creates opportunities to break free from decades of accumulated interface conventions.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the history and cultural significance of the ping utility, created by Mike Muuss in 1983 at the U.S. Army Research Laboratory. He shares Muuss's account of coding ping in a single night to diagnose network issues, noting the creator's wistful reflection that had he known it would be his most famous accomplishment, he might have added more features. Atwood acknowledges that while ping is less useful on the modern internet due to filtering, it remains essential for local network diagnostics. The post culminates in sharing a legendary Amazon review that humorously interprets the children's book 'The Story About Ping' as a technical allegory for the ping utility.
key_points: Mike Muuss created ping in December 1983 in a single overnight coding session, including writing kernel support for raw ICMP sockets
Muuss expressed mild regret about not spending more time on what became his most famous creation
Ping's utility has diminished on the public internet due to widespread filtering, but remains valuable for local network troubleshooting
The famous satirical Amazon review brilliantly reinterprets a 1933 children's book as Unix networking documentation
Simple, focused tools that do one thing well can have lasting impact spanning decades
money_quotes: If I'd known then that it would be my most famous accomplishment in life, I might have worked on it another day or two and added some more options.
The code compiled just fine, but it didn't work – there was no kernel support for raw ICMP sockets! Incensed, I coded up the kernel support and had everything working well before sunrise.
Using deft allegory, the authors have provided an insightful and intuitive explanation of one of Unix's most venerable networking utilities.
The ping packet is described as a duck, who, with other packets (more ducks), spends a certain period of time on the host machine (the wise-eyed boat).
It's simple. It's utilitarian. And it does exactly what the sonar inspired name implies.

Who can read that passage on the Windows API ('Obscure, profound it was, and nebulous, So that by fixing on its depths my sight – Nothing whatever I discerned therein.'), without shaking their head with deep understanding.
posts.key_insight: The most enduring software tools are often simple utilities created quickly to solve immediate problems, yet their creators rarely anticipate the lasting impact of their work.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood summarizes Martin Fowler's article on test doubles, which clarifies the confusing vocabulary around pretend objects used in testing. The post adopts Gerard Meszaros's taxonomy that uses 'Test Double' as the umbrella term for any fake object used in place of a real one during testing. Atwood presents the four main categories: dummy objects (unused placeholders), fake objects (working but simplified implementations), stub objects (canned response providers), and mock objects (expectation-verifying replacements). He adds his own preferred term, 'Stunt Objects,' as a memorable alternative to the generic 'Test Double.'
key_points: Test Double is the generic term for any pretend object used in place of a real object for testing purposes
Dummy objects are passed around but never actually used, typically just filling parameter lists
Fake objects have working implementations but take shortcuts unsuitable for production
Stub objects provide canned answers only to calls programmed for the specific test
Mock objects are pre-programmed with expectations about which calls they should receive
money_quotes: Personally, I like to think of them as Stunt Objects.
The vocabulary for talking about pretend objects soon gets messy – all sorts of words are used: stub, mock, fake, dummy.
Dummy objects are passed around but never actually used. Usually they are just used to fill parameter lists.
Fake objects actually have working implementations, but usually take some shortcut which makes them not suitable for production.
Mock objects are pre-programmed with expectations which form a specification of the calls they are expected to receive.
posts.key_insight: Understanding the precise taxonomy of test doubles—dummy, fake, stub, and mock—eliminates confusion and enables more precise communication about testing strategies.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that Windows Vista's redesigned Start Menu, which allows users to type to search rather than navigate through menus, is the operating system's killer feature and a must-have upgrade. He demonstrates how pressing the Windows key and typing a few characters instantly finds programs, settings, files, and websites across multiple indexed locations. Atwood contrasts this with Windows XP's 'horrific mouse-centric cascading start menu' and argues this shift represents a fundamental change in computing interaction. He connects this to the broader 'Google era' where search-based navigation replaces the traditional desktop metaphor, concluding that typing is simply more efficient than pointing when dealing with the volume of content on modern computers.
key_points: Vista's Start Menu search is so transformative that it alone justifies upgrading from Windows XP on day one
Typing to navigate eliminates the need for manually configured hotkeys, shortcuts, and the Start > Run dialog
The feature requires zero configuration—it works out of the box across programs, files, settings, and web history
This UI shift acknowledges that Google's search-first paradigm was right: there's too much content for browse-and-click to work reliably
The change represents a victory of textual search over three decades of desktop GUI metaphors based on office desktop analogies
money_quotes: Vista's Start Menu lets you type what you want instead of pointing at it.
I've gone from tedious, manually defined hotkeys and shortcuts in Windows XP to simply typing what I want and letting the computer find it for me.
Best of all, I never have to take my hand off the keyboard.
The start menu has been a usability train wreck since its introduction in 1995.
There's nothing to install, nothing to configure, nothing to think about. It just works.
This new all-in-one keyboard style of navigation is unbelievably, amazingly efficient.
No more hunting for the right bunch of pixels to click; it's faster and easier to type to get to what you want instead.
Typing trumps pointing.
posts.key_insight: Search-based keyboard navigation is fundamentally superior to point-and-click interfaces because there's simply too much content in modern computing for visual browsing to remain efficient.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that default settings are among the most consequential decisions software developers make. Using Vista's built-in search as an example, he demonstrates how defaults that 'just work' without installation or configuration provide massive value to users. He contends that defaults drive software industry evolution by establishing baselines that competitors must match or exceed. The post criticizes the Unix/Linux model of infinite configurability as lacking meaningful standards. Atwood emphasizes that for most users, the default is the only value they'll ever use, making thoughtful default selection critical to both user experience and business success.
key_points: Third-party tools may offer similar functionality, but built-in defaults reach millions of users who would never install alternatives
Good defaults eliminate installation and configuration friction - they 'just work'
Defaults establish industry baselines that competitors must match, driving software evolution forward
Infinite configurability (like Unix/Linux) creates a problem by eliminating meaningful standards for comparison
Companies like Google, Microsoft, and Yahoo pay millions to influence defaults because they directly impact the bottom line
money_quotes: There's nothing to install. There's nothing to configure. It just works.
Defaults are arguably the most important design decisions you'll ever make as a software developer.
Defaults are how the software industry evolves.
For most users, the default value is the only value.

Choose good defaults, and users will sing the praises of your software and how easy it is to use. Choose poor defaults, and you'll face down user angst over configuration, and probably a host of tech support calls as well.
You should agonize over every default in your software. If you aren't, you're doing the user, and yourself, a disservice.
posts.key_insight: For most users, the default value is the only value they'll ever encounter, making thoughtful default selection one of the most impactful decisions a software developer can make.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines Google's overwhelming dominance as the gateway to the internet, using JavaLobby's Christmas delisting incident as a case study. When spam attacks caused Google to remove JavaLobby from its index, the site lost over 10,000 daily visitors overnight—illustrating how dependent websites have become on a single company. While acknowledging that Google's response was justified and their webmaster tools are helpful, Atwood raises concerns about the unprecedented concentration of power. He argues that Google's monopoly over internet discovery exceeds even Microsoft's desktop dominance, controlling access to 'the sum of all information on the internet.'
key_points: Most businesses receive 70% of their web traffic from Google, making them dependent partners rather than competitors
Google's spam detection actually found JavaLobby's hack faster than the site owners did, demonstrating both their reach and responsiveness
Google provides webmaster tools and even emails site owners about problems—more than any other search engine does
The power to delist a website effectively erases it from existence, giving Google unprecedented control over online visibility
This concentration of power over information discovery may be more significant than Microsoft's desktop monopoly ever was
money_quotes: The net isn't a directed graph. It's not a tree. It's a single point labeled G connected to 10 billion destination pages.
Most businesses on the net get 70% of their traffic from Google. These business are not competitors with Google, they are its partners, and have an interest in driving Google's success.
On any given day over 10,000 visitors arrive at Javalobby as a result of Google searches, and suddenly they stopped coming!
In essence, they have wiped out strategic positioning that we took years to build.
Google's lead over its competitors is so complete, so total, that if your website isn't in Google, it effectively doesn't exist.
I'm not sure the Microsoft monopoly has ever wielded that kind of power.
I'm not sure I'm comfortable with a single company having such near-absolute power over the sum of all information on the internet.
posts.key_insight: Google's dominance has transformed the decentralized internet into a centralized system where a single company controls whether websites effectively exist or not—a concentration of power that should concern everyone, regardless of how responsibly it's currently wielded.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines Bjarne Stroustrup's defense of C++ in a Technology Review interview and argues that the language's importance has diminished. While Stroustrup maintains that C++ is appropriately expert-friendly and that the problem is undereducated developers, Atwood counters that C++ made sense in an era of limited computing resources but is now overkill for most tasks. He argues that modern abundance of computing power means we should trade raw performance for developer safety and productivity. Atwood concludes that C++ remains valuable but is increasingly a niche language, and the most important quality of a modern language is how well it protects programmers from themselves.
key_points: C++ was designed for an era of scarce computing resources, but we now live in an era of abundance where naive solutions are often 'fast enough'
C++'s attempt to span all programming tasks from low-level to high-level makes it unnecessarily complex and dangerous for most modern development work
Stroustrup defends C++ by arguing developers are undereducated, but Atwood implies the real solution is using more appropriate, specialized languages
The most important quality of a programming language today isn't speed or generality, but how well it protects programmers from making mistakes
C++ has become a niche language suited only for specific tasks like operating systems and device drivers, not typical business applications
money_quotes: C++ is fast but unforgiving. It was an appropriate solution for an era of limited computing resources. But we've long since left that behind; we live in an era of abundance.
We have more computer power than we possibly know what to do with on the desktop. Computers get faster every day, but programmers' brains, sadly, do not.
It'd be a waste not to trade some of that abundant raw power to make things easier on us.
The most important question to ask about any language these days isn't how fast it is, or how general it is, but how well does it protect you from yourself?
C makes it easy to shoot yourself in the foot; C++ makes it harder, but when you do it blows your whole leg off.
There will always be a place in a programmer's toolbox for C++, but I'd argue that it's an increasingly a niche language for a very specific subset of programming tasks.
posts.key_insight: In an era of abundant computing power, the most important quality of a programming language is no longer raw speed but how well it protects developers from making dangerous mistakes.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to the 'Five Things You Didn't Know About Me' meme by offering a detailed tour of his office at Vertigo Software, revealing his personality through his workspace and personal anecdotes. He initially resists the meme, citing Kathy Sierra's advice that 'nobody cares' about speaker backgrounds, but comes to realize that blogs are as much about the writer as the content. The post showcases his carefully curated office filled with quirky items like a Pong clock, thinking putty, and vintage software boxes. He concludes with five personal revelations including being arrested for phreaking in 1987, being terrible at math despite loving computers, and meeting his wife while dancing incorrectly to The Safety Dance.
key_points: Blogs are as much about the writer's personality as their stated topic - avoiding personal content is just as much a mistake as over-sharing
A developer's workspace reflects their personality and values, from ergonomic equipment to quirky collectibles
Atwood's background includes being arrested for wardialing and phreaking as a teenager in 1987, highlighting the lengths early computer enthusiasts went to avoid long distance charges
Despite loving computers, Atwood struggles with math - scoring in the 99.9th percentile on verbal GRE but only 77th in math
Behind every fascinating blog is a fascinating person, and sharing personal details creates human connection with readers
money_quotes: Blogs, for better or worse, are as much about the writer as they are any other topic.
Personality is the essential ingredient that makes blogs so interesting, so compelling, so... human.
Behind every fascinating blog is a fascinating person.
I love computers, but I can't stand math. No matter how hard I apply myself, I'm terrible at it.
In the dark ages before the internet, all we had were BBS systems and modems. And long distance charges were incredibly expensive back then.
To get your BBS fix, you had to find some way around the long distance charges.
I turned around and there was this cute girl telling me 'you're doing it wrong.' As in, I was doing the Safety Dance wrong.
I do think I'd have sex with my computers if it was physically possible.
posts.key_insight: A blogger's personality and personal story are not distractions from their content but essential ingredients that make their writing compelling and human.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the PC industry fundamentally lacks design leadership, using Steve Jobs' 1995 criticism of Microsoft as a launching point. He contends that while Apple integrates taste and culture into products from inception, PC manufacturers treat design as an afterthought or ignore it entirely. Atwood illustrates this with examples of ugly PC cases, poorly designed software utilities, and Microsoft's own inconsistent design guidelines. He concludes that with Apple now competing in the x86 market, the PC industry must urgently develop design competence or face irrelevance.
key_points: Apple's competitive advantage stems from treating design as integral to product development, not as an afterthought
The PC hardware industry cannot even successfully copy Apple's designs, producing aesthetically inferior imitations
PC software vendors oscillate between garish custom interfaces and bland utilitarian designs, with no middle ground
Microsoft is the only company positioned to set PC design standards but fails to lead or even follow its own guidelines consistently
Jobs' vision that art and engineering are inseparable has remained consistent for decades and represents a genuine competitive threat to the PC industry
money_quotes: At Apple, taste and culture are designed into every product from day one.
In the PC world, taste and culture are rarely considered, and if they are, it's always as an afterthought.
There are no design leaders in the PC world.
If you want a PC that looks as good as it works, you have to make it yourself.
The design of a product, the art of it, is just as important as the engineering.
People put a lot of love into these products, and a lot of expression of their appreciation came to these things.
Why choose a beige box and a schizophrenic UI when you could have something that's beautiful and thoughtfully designed for about the same price?
posts.key_insight: Design leadership requires treating aesthetics and user experience as first-class engineering concerns from the start, not cosmetic additions bolted on after functionality is complete.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines whether software certifications are meaningful credentials for developers. He acknowledges a tension: while certifications might establish baseline competency for junior developers, he's seen certified senior developers who still lacked fundamental skills. Citing Tom DeMarco's 1998 critique that certification is really about power seizure by certifiers rather than societal benefit, Atwood ultimately sides with letting the market determine competence. His conclusion is that portfolios and demonstrated work matter far more than passing standardized tests, though certifications aren't inherently harmful if accompanied by real accomplishments.
key_points: Certifications are often sold by the same companies that make the products, creating an inherent conflict of interest
At junior levels, certifications might ensure baseline knowledge of fundamental concepts
Senior developers with multiple certifications can still be incompetent, proving certifications don't guarantee skill
Tom DeMarco argues certification is about power seizure by certifiers, not societal benefit
The market is a more competent and ethical selection mechanism than any appointed certification elite
money_quotes: That's nice, but show me what you've worked on.
Your credentials should be the sum of the projects you've worked on, and specifically how much you learned from your failures.
You should be spending your time building stuff, not studying for multiple choice tests.
Though the rationale for certification is always societal good, the real objective is different: seizure of power.
The real issue here is not certification; the real issue is de-certification.
Sorry about that Mr. Gates, in the brave new world, you wouldn't be allowed to write software.
I suggest that we have a perfectly fine selection mechanism at work today; it's called the market.
posts.key_insight: A developer's portfolio of completed projects and lessons learned from failures is far more valuable than any certification alphabet soup.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood analyzes the overwhelming presence of advertising on web pages, using a visual technique from 'Homepage Usability' to reveal that a full third of an SD Times article page is dedicated to ads—more than the content itself. He acknowledges that ad-supported content has become the only viable business model for the internet, despite his former hopes for micropayments. Drawing a parallel to Mike Judge's film 'Idiocracy,' which satirizes a future drowning in advertisements, Atwood suggests the dystopian vision is uncomfortably close to the present reality of web browsing.
key_points: A third of web page real estate is often dedicated to advertising, frequently exceeding the space given to actual content
Micropayments failed as a business model; ad-supported content is the only sustainable approach on the internet
The internet's strength at gathering communities and distributing content freely makes ad-supported models inevitable
The movie Idiocracy's satirical vision of advertisement-saturated surfaces uncomfortably resembles modern web design
AdWords built the foundation for today's ad-supported content ecosystem
money_quotes: A full third of the page is dedicated to advertising. That's more than the content itself!
I carried a torch for micropayments for years, but it's never going to happen.
Ad-supported content is the house that AdWords built.
It's difficult to criticize a system that works, a system that allows content creators to at least break even on their hosting costs.
It's supposed to be funny, but it's uncomfortably close to the experience of reading today's ad-supported internet content.
A tiny window of content, surrounded by dozens of flashing, animated advertisements.
Are we creating a world of endless advertising?
posts.key_insight: The internet's fundamental nature makes ad-supported content inevitable, but we should be uncomfortable with how closely our present reality resembles dystopian satire about advertisement overload.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood introduces Don Park's invention of Identicons—small visual glyphs that represent IP addresses or other data in an anonymized, visually distinctive way. He explains how these simple algorithmic images can make distinguishing between users much easier than reading text or numbers. Atwood and Jon Galloway were inspired enough to port the original Java implementation to .NET 2.0. The post positions Identicons as both practical (for privacy) and enjoyable (for user experience).
key_points: Identicons are visual glyphs that can represent any data reducible to bits, not just IP addresses
The algorithm produces beautiful, unique results that work well even at tiny sizes like 16x16 pixels
Identicons offer a privacy advantage over displaying raw IP addresses while remaining nearly as useful
The visual approach makes distinguishing between multiple pieces of information faster and more enjoyable than text
money_quotes: An Identicon is a small, anonymized visual glyph that represents your IP address.
Too much of the web what we read are textual or numeric information which are not easy to distinguish at a glance when they are jumbled up together.
Rather than printing everyone's IP address next to their comment, you can show their anonymized Identicon.
It's more private, it's almost as useful, and it's much more fun.
posts.key_insight: Visual identifiers like Identicons can make web experiences more enjoyable and private by replacing hard-to-parse textual data with distinctive, anonymized graphics.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the common wisdom that 'shipping is enough' by arguing that the true measure of a programmer's success is building software that people actually use. He quotes Chuck Jazdzewski's advice that programmers get paid to ship, but pushes further to question the value of shipping code that nobody will ever use or benefit from. Through Rich Skrenta's experience of realizing his side projects had more users than his day job at a major company, Atwood illustrates how tragically common it is for programmers to work on code that will never matter. He concludes that smart developers understand their job encompasses far more than coding—including documentation, interaction design, and cultivating user community—because without getting those right, the code itself becomes meaningless.
key_points: The ultimate metric of programmer success isn't shipping code, but how many users actually use your application
Many programmers at major companies spend their careers writing code that will never be used or noticed—which is as tragic as writing a blog nobody reads
Smart developers recognize that their job is to build software worth using, not just to write and ship code
Non-coding activities like documentation, interaction design, and user community cultivation are critical to software success
If you want to work on software people use, you must take responsibility for making that software worth using
money_quotes: You don't get paid to program, you get paid to ship.
How many users actually use your application? Now that's the ultimate metric of success.
So I made a simple resolution. I wanted to work on stuff that people would actually use.
What about the legion of programmers writing code paths that will never be traversed?
A smart software developer knows that there's no point in writing code if it's code that nobody will see, code that nobody will use, code that nobody will ultimately benefit from.
Why build a permanently vacant house?
Their job is to build software that people will actually want to use.
If you get that stuff wrong, it won't matter what kind of code you've written.
posts.key_insight: The true measure of a programmer's success isn't shipping code—it's building software that people actually want to use, which requires taking ownership of everything that makes software worth using.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that documentation is the critical factor determining whether software libraries succeed or fail, regardless of their technical merits. He synthesizes opinions from multiple sources who all emphasize that autogenerated API docs alone are insufficient—libraries need high-level overviews, practical examples, full API references, and code comments. The post criticizes the widespread tendency in open source to treat documentation as an afterthought, while praising the UrlRewriting.net project as a rare example of documentation done right. Atwood concludes by suggesting that agile documentation methods might help teams keep documentation current alongside rapidly evolving code.
key_points: Documentation requires four components: high-level overviews, practical examples, full API docs, and code comments—most libraries fail to achieve even one or two
Autogenerated documentation tools like JSDoc and Javadoc have caused harm by making developers assume API docs alone are sufficient
Good documentation makes users excited about the product itself, not the documentation
Open source projects particularly struggle with documentation quality
Agile documentation methods may offer a path to keeping docs current on fast-moving projects
money_quotes: If it isn't documented, it doesn't exist.
No matter how wonderful your library is and how intelligent its design, if you're the only one who understands it, it doesn't do any good.
The greatest library in the world would fail if the only way to learn it was reading the code (and, in fact, it already has to a large extent).
JSDoc is both good and bad for this: good because it makes API docs stupidly easy to generate, and bad because people assume that API docs are all you need.
Most treat documentation like an afterthought.
Not only does it have to be doc'd, but it has to be explained and taught and demonstrated.
The presence of good documentation makes the code a pleasure to use, too.
posts.key_insight: A library's technical excellence is irrelevant if developers can't learn to use it—documentation transforms code from a private achievement into a shared tool.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the subtle linguistic warfare between Microsoft and Netscape during the browser wars, highlighting Microsoft's style guide directive to use 'explore' instead of 'navigate' - a clear jab at Netscape Navigator. He points to Internet Explorer's 'Navigation Canceled' error message as evidence of deliberate corporate trolling. The post also recounts the famous incident where Microsoft placed a giant IE logo on Netscape's lawn after IE4's release, while noting the more recent friendly gesture of sending Firefox a congratulatory cake.
key_points: Microsoft's official style guide explicitly banned the word 'navigate' in favor of 'explore' - a pointed reference to their browser rivalry
The 'Navigation Canceled' error message in Internet Explorer appears to be deliberate wordplay mocking Netscape Navigator
Microsoft famously dropped a giant IE logo on Netscape's campus lawn after releasing IE4 in 1997
The browser wars rivalry evolved from hostile pranks to friendly gestures like congratulatory cakes for Firefox releases
Corporate style guides can serve as subtle weapons in competitive battles
money_quotes: My favorite entry, especially fun to find in light of Microsoft's legal problems arising in part from its relationship to Netscape Navigator, is this Orwellian directive
The wording of the 'Navigation Canceled' error message can't possibly have been a coincidence, given the intense rivalry with Netscape Navigator back in the heady days of Internet Explorer 3 and 4
The message now seems quaint in the wake of Netscape's near-irrelevance
I wonder which cheeky little monkey at Microsoft came up with this particular error message way back when
posts.key_insight: Even the most mundane technical decisions - like error message wording or style guide entries - can become weapons in corporate warfare, revealing the pettiness and playfulness that lurks beneath professional rivalries.
