key_points: Default 404 pages from IIS and Apache are unintelligible geek-speak that alienates average users
Error notification should be automatic - requiring users to report broken links is a complete failure of responsibility
404 pages should automatically search on the user's behalf using the erroneous URL, not just provide a search box
Popular or recent content links can help users escape their predicament with one click
Most websites fail spectacularly at 404 design, serving pages that are user hostile rather than helpful
money_quotes: Most 404 pages are unvarnished geek-speak.
Any 404 page that has the characters '404' on it, if not already an outright failure, is already well on its way to becoming one.
Repeat after me: it is not the user's job to inform you about problems with your website.
If you require the user to click a button to notify you about a 404, or if you require the user to fill out a broken link form, you have utterly failed your users.
Don't just put a search box on the 404 page and force the user to perform a search. That's a cop-out.
I sampled 404 pages from dozens of websites and most fail spectacularly, serving up 404 pages that are downright user hostile.
Never settle for the default 404 page. Replace it with a custom 404 page that is polite, illuminating, and most of all, helpful.
posts.key_insight: A 404 page is not a dead end but an opportunity to help confused users, and websites that treat it as anything less have failed their users.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the tradeoff between traditional integer primary keys and GUIDs in database design. He acknowledges the storage cost of 16-byte GUIDs versus 4-byte integers but argues the cost may be less significant than commonly assumed. He cites database expert Joe Celko's support for GUIDs and highlights scenarios where they shine, such as replication and generating keys outside the database. The post ultimately advocates for awareness of GUIDs as an option rather than universal adoption.
key_points: GUIDs use 16 bytes versus 4 bytes for integers, but the performance cost may be overstated
GUIDs feel more natural as identity values and are guaranteed truly unique
GUIDs are particularly useful for replication and generating keys outside the database
The choice between integers and GUIDs requires balancing specific tradeoffs for your use case
money_quotes: Each globally unique ID is like a beautiful snowflake: every one a unique item waiting to be born.
Using sixteen bytes instead of four bytes for a primary key? Have you lost your mind?
Those additional 12 bytes do come at a cost. But that cost may not be as great as you think.
Using a GUID as a row identity value feels more natural – and certainly more truly unique – than a 32-bit integer.
I'm not proposing that every database switch to GUID primary keys, but I do think it's important to know the option is out there.
posts.key_insight: GUIDs deserve consideration as primary keys despite their storage overhead, especially for distributed systems and scenarios requiring key generation outside the database.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the OLPC's BitFrost security model, which attempts to implement code access security—granting permissions to applications rather than just users. He draws a parallel to .NET's Code Access Security (CAS), which failed because it was too complex for developers to adopt. Despite the theoretical appeal of limiting what applications can do (like preventing Solitaire from accessing your documents), Microsoft eventually abandoned CAS in favor of ClickOnce's simpler full-trust model. Atwood expresses skepticism that BitFrost will succeed where CAS failed.
key_points: UNIX file permissions from 1971 remain virtually unchanged today, focusing on user access rather than controlling what programs can do with files
Code access security would allow applications to declare and shed unnecessary privileges at install time, limiting damage from compromised software
.NET's Code Access Security was a comprehensive but ultimately failed attempt at this same concept
Microsoft's CAS was so difficult to use that ClickOnce effectively abandoned it for full-trust applications
Security models fail when they're too cumbersome for developers to actually implement
money_quotes: A user can choose to protect her files from other people on the system, but has no control whatsoever over what her own programs are able to do with her files.
How, then, is it a surprise that we can't stop viruses and malware now, when our defenses have remained largely unchanged from thirty-five years ago?
The critical observation here is not that Solitaire should never have the ability to do any of the above (which it clearly shouldn't), but that its creators know it should never do any of the above.
Code Access Security is too hard.
You're now exactly one click away from running a full trust application that can do whatever it wants to on your machine, with no restrictions of any kind.
What good is a security model that's so cumbersome to use, nobody ever adopts it?
posts.key_insight: Even theoretically sound security models fail in practice when they impose too much complexity on developers, as demonstrated by .NET's abandoned Code Access Security.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares a web comic from Bug Bash that satirizes how software developers mistakenly believe they represent typical users. The comic illustrates the common disconnect between developers and the actual end users of their software. Atwood endorses the comic for hitting 'close to home' and links to related strips exploring how this misconception leads good software astray.
key_points: Software developers falsely assume they are representative of typical users
This self-perception blindspot contributes to software quality problems
The Bug Bash comic effectively captures this industry-wide issue
Developer assumptions about users can cause good software to go bad
money_quotes: Software developers truly believe, in their heart of hearts, that they are typical users.
posts.key_insight: Developers' false belief that they represent typical users is a fundamental cause of software usability failures.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood curates six influential top 10 lists from across the software development world, covering egoless programming, project failure signs, workplace tips, design school lessons, career wisdom, and essential books. The post demonstrates that programming wisdom transcends pure technical knowledge, drawing from design, communication, and interpersonal skills. By juxtaposing lists from different authors and disciplines, Atwood highlights recurring themes: humility, simplicity, communication, and the importance of shipping working software. He explicitly defends including a designer's perspective, arguing that software development blends creativity with engineering and benefits from cross-disciplinary advice.
key_points: Egoless programming principles emphasize humility, accepting mistakes, and separating your identity from your code
Multiple lists independently identify communication, simplicity, and avoiding over-engineering as critical success factors
Project doom signs include scope creep, second system syndrome, understaffing, and tackling unknown problems
Career wisdom from experienced developers centers on learning to say no, embracing failure, and recognizing that most creative work involves grunt work
Software development benefits from design thinking and creative disciplines, not just pure engineering mindsets
money_quotes: Understand and accept that you will make mistakes.
You are not your code.
The only true authority stems from knowledge, not from position.
Critique code instead of people – be kind to the coder, not to the code.
Process is no substitute for thinking.
95 percent of any creative profession is shit work.
If everything is equally important, then nothing is very important.
The difficult part of software development is communication.
posts.key_insight: The most enduring programming wisdom focuses not on technical skills but on humility, communication, and the discipline to keep things simple.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood analyzes Folding@Home statistics showing PlayStation 3 consoles dominating the distributed computing project, contributing 72% of total computing power despite representing a small fraction of clients. He compares performance across CPUs, the PS3's Cell processor, and GPUs, finding that specialized processors dramatically outperform general-purpose CPUs. GPU clients prove 18.6x faster than Intel Core Duo processors, while PS3s are 7.8x faster. Atwood concludes that for distributed computing workloads, general-purpose CPUs have become essentially obsolete compared to specialized hardware.
key_points: PlayStation 3 consoles contribute 72% of Folding@Home's computing power despite being only about 26,000 active clients versus 160,000+ Windows machines
GPU clients running on high-end ATI cards are 2.5x faster than PS3s and 18.6x faster than Intel Core Duo processors
The Cell processor's specialized architecture allows it to crunch work units far more efficiently than general-purpose CPUs
Power efficiency matters for distributed computing since both PS3s and gaming PCs consume about 200 watts under load
The performance gap between specialized and general-purpose processors is expected to widen over time
money_quotes: It's only a matter of time – a few weeks at most – before the PS3 constitutes more than 95 percent of the computing power in the entire Folding@Home network.
The PlayStation 3 can harness the considerable power of its specialized Cell CPU to crunch work units far more efficiently than any general purpose CPU ever could.
The GPU clients are almost 2.5 times faster than the PS3.
There's almost no point in adding general purpose CPUs to the folding network any more.
It's a waste of time, effort, and electricity.
For folding and other distributed computing efforts, it's the death of the general purpose CPU as we know it.
posts.key_insight: Specialized processors like GPUs and the Cell architecture have made general-purpose CPUs obsolete for massively parallel computing tasks, a trend that will only accelerate.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his guest article for The Daily WTF (now Worse Than Failure), which ironically criticized the very site that invited him. While acknowledging the entertainment value of laughing at bad code, he argues that mockery alone perpetuates the problem. The developers who most need to improve will never read WTF, so the burden falls on skilled developers to actively mentor and reprogram their struggling colleagues. He calls for the software community to take collective responsibility through mentoring, code reviews, and peer pressure rather than passive amusement.
key_points: Laughing at bad code is therapeutic and educational, but entertainment alone doesn't solve the underlying problem
The developers who could benefit most from WTF will never read it—they don't know they need help
Waiting for bad developers to improve on their own is a strategy that never works
The software community has a collective duty to actively mentor and develop struggling programmers
Every developer, even great ones, writes bad code sometimes—the schism between 'good' and 'bad' programmers is false
money_quotes: I can absolutely guarantee that the kinds of developers who could benefit most from reading WTF simply do not – and never will – read the WTF website.
Reprogramming WTF code isn't enough. We need to reprogram the developers producing the WTF code.

I would fear them because they're either bald-faced liars – believing that simply saying it repeatedly will somehow convince others into this fiction – or they're completely blind to their own weaknesses.
I believe it's our collective duty to leave the profession of software engineering better than we found it.
Waiting for the bad developers to come to their senses on their own. That strategy never works.
It's tempting to get your daily fix of drive-by amusement and move on.
posts.key_insight: Mocking bad code is cathartic but ultimately useless—the only way to reduce WTF-worthy code is for experienced developers to actively mentor those who write it.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that over-reliance on the mouse is holding back computer productivity, and advocates for deliberately learning keyboard shortcuts by forcing yourself to stop reaching for the mouse. He acknowledges mice have their place and remembers the pre-mouse era, but observes most users have swung too far toward mouse dependency. He recommends learning at least one new keyboard shortcut per day, noting the initial awkwardness pays dividends in long-term efficiency. While conceding that web navigation remains stubbornly mouse-centric, he maintains that mastering both input methods leads to optimal computing.
key_points: Keyboard shortcuts are almost always more efficient than mouse navigation, but people won't learn them while relying on the mouse as a crutch
The recommended approach is forcing yourself to learn at least one new keyboard shortcut per day
Applications should do more to make keyboard shortcuts discoverable, like Office 2007's approach of lighting up available shortcuts when accelerator keys are pressed
HTML and web navigation remain frustratingly mouse-centric, making keyboard-only browsing impractical for most users
The goal isn't eliminating the mouse entirely, but achieving balanced proficiency with both input methods
money_quotes: One of the quickest ways to increase your productivity on the computer is to go commando: stop using the mouse.
Stop for a moment, resist taking the easy way out with the mouse, and force yourself to learn at least one new keyboard shortcut per day.
Most people I've observed using the computer these days rely almost exclusively on the mouse, to the detriment of their overall computer experience.
It's a shame more applications don't go out of their way to make keyboard shortcuts more discoverable.
I guarantee whatever you're trying to do is possible – and ultimately quicker – if you persist with the keyboard.
posts.key_insight: Deliberate short-term discomfort in learning keyboard shortcuts yields lasting productivity gains, but you must actively resist the mouse to force the learning.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that practical experience is far more valuable than formal education for aspiring software developers. Drawing on Alistair Cockburn's battlefield metaphor, he contends that real software development happens in industry, not universities, because the field moves too fast and requires hands-on learning. While acknowledging that higher education teaches valuable communication and critical thinking skills, Atwood insists that when forced to choose, work experience should always win. He provides concrete advice: pursue internships aggressively, join user groups, contribute to open source, write articles, and start a blog.
key_points: Software development is learned through practice in industry, not through academic study alone—like forging samurai swords, you must test your work on the battlefield to know what works
Universities remain valuable for teaching communication, critical thinking, and how to interact with people, even if they can't keep pace with technical skills
When choosing between formal education and work experience in a fast-moving field like software, always choose work
Practical ways to gain battlefield experience include internships, user groups, open source contributions, technical writing, and blogging
The ability to communicate clearly about your code is as important as the code itself
money_quotes: You have to be on the battlefield.
I can't imagine learning the things I've learned while sitting peacefully in my office reflecting. Most of my original reflections and predictions were just wrong.
Software development only teaches you how to talk to your computer. Higher education is still worthwhile because it teaches you how to talk to people.
For a fast-moving field like computer science, the work you're doing is far more relevant than any classes you're taking.
If you must choose between formal schooling and work experience, always choose work.
Some of the best programmers I've ever met have been college interns.
The cleverest code in the world won't help you if you can't clearly communicate how that code works, or what it's for.
In software development, you learn by doing.
posts.key_insight: Software development skills are forged through real-world practice, not academic theory—seek out every opportunity to learn by doing actual work alongside experienced developers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores Alistair Cockburn's concept of software development as a cooperative game rather than a science or traditional engineering discipline. The post contrasts different 'games' being played: corporate projects where career advancement trumps delivery, and open-source development which operates as an infinite game without resource constraints. Atwood extends this to argue that fun and engagement are essential elements of software development, connecting Raph Koster's game design theories to application development. The core argument is that understanding software development through the lens of games provides better insights into team dynamics, resource allocation, and user engagement.
key_points: Software development is neither science nor engineering but a cooperative game of speed and competition against time and other teams
Military/corporate software projects often fail because participants are playing career-advancement games rather than delivery games
Open-source development succeeds differently because it's an infinite game without resource limits or fixed endpoints
Fun and engagement principles from game design apply to all software development, not just games
Social media and web applications like Amazon, eBay, and LinkedIn are essentially massively distributed games with ladders to climb
money_quotes: Software development is a 'game,' a game of speed and cooperation within your team, in competition against other teams.

The logic of the funny contractor behavior doesn't make sense until you realize they are playing a different game, in which different moves are called for. Then it suddenly all makes sense - even if you don't like it.
The game is interesting as long as it is interesting. Any number of players may show up, and they are not on a time-line.
We play games because we derive some kind of essential satisfaction from playing them.
Designing for fun isn't just important for game developers. It's important for all software developers.
Do users want to use your application, or are they forced to use it?
As soon as you give people a ladder to climb, they'll climb it.
Software development is a collaborative game that you play, willingly or not, with your team and your users.
posts.key_insight: Understanding that different stakeholders in software projects are often playing entirely different games—career advancement vs. delivery vs. intrinsic satisfaction—explains dysfunctional behavior and suggests that designing for fun and engagement is essential for both teams and users.

posts.summary: This April Fool's Day post pretends to abandon tech blogging in favor of cat content, introducing Jeff's two cats Floyd and Elsie. The joke included replacing the blog's front page with obnoxiously intrusive advertisements. Beyond the humor, the post genuinely discusses the joys of pet ownership and how cats interact with computer users. Jeff advocates for adopting rescue animals and notes that two cats are more entertaining than one. The meta-joke comments on both the banality of cat blogging and the horrors of early-2000s web advertising.
key_points: The post was an April Fool's joke combining cat blogging with intentionally terrible advertisements
Jeff adopted Floyd in 2001 as a shy rescue cat, then added Elsie a year later to keep Floyd company
Cats and computers create an inevitable conflict as cats love positioning themselves in front of keyboards
Two cats are better than one because they entertain each other and their owners
The joke works because cat blogging represents the antithesis of Coding Horror's usual technical content
money_quotes: If you're a cat owner and a computer user, you know how difficult it can be to get anything done with a cat around.
Cats love nothing more than curling up directly in front of your keyboard for attention.
We figured once you have the first cat, adding a second cat is hardly any additional work, and they can entertain each other when you're not around.
I'm not necessarily a 'cat person' so much as I am an animal lover.
I think the whole Dog vs. Cat war is ludicrous.
Cats are a far better match for lazy people like me.
posts.key_insight: Even a technical blogger can't resist the universal experience of cats interrupting computer work, making cat content the perfect April Fool's subversion of a programming blog.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood discusses his search for a new mouse to address hand discomfort, settling on the Microsoft Habu as the ideal combination of classic Intellimouse ergonomics and Razer gaming technology. He explains the key features he values in mice: wired connection, high resolution sensor, thumb buttons, and adjustable DPI. The post then dives into the technical details of USB polling rates, explaining how higher polling rates reduce cursor latency. Atwood recommends increasing polling rate from the default 125 Hz to at least 500 Hz for noticeably smoother cursor movement, while acknowledging this level of optimization is just scratching the surface of what serious gamers pursue.
key_points: The Microsoft Habu combines the ergonomic body of the classic Intellimouse Explorer with Razer's gaming mouse technology
The Habu uniquely stores all settings in onboard flash memory, making it the first mouse Atwood owned with firmware that persists across computers
Default USB polling rate of 125 Hz limits cursor updates to every 8 milliseconds, but this can be increased via software or hardware to 1000 Hz (1 ms)
Higher-end mice like the Habu and recent Logitech models automatically increase USB polling rate in hardware when plugged in
The difference between 125 Hz and 500 Hz polling rate is perceptible to users sensitive to cursor smoothness
money_quotes: The Habu seems to have inherited the best traits from its parents: the classic body of the Intellimouse Explorer, with the sophisticated brains of a Razer gaming mouse.
It's the first mouse I've ever owned with firmware.
Once you've configured the settings to taste, you can unplug the mouse, bring it to another computer, and those settings will be retained.
If you're sensitive to cursor smoothness at all, I can practically guarantee you will feel the difference between 125 Hz and 500 Hz.
If you think all this talk of high DPI mice and USB polling rates is obsessive, trust me, it's merely the tip of the iceberg.
posts.key_insight: Programmers who spend all day with a mouse in hand should pay attention to both ergonomics and technical specifications like DPI and USB polling rate, as these details meaningfully affect daily comfort and precision.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores Alistair Cockburn's metaphor comparing software development to rock climbing as a cooperative game. The analogy highlights shared characteristics: both are technical endeavors requiring skill, involve individual contribution within team contexts, depend on proper tools, require planning while embracing improvisation, and are fun yet challenging. Atwood notes one limitation—software builds upon itself across versions, unlike discrete climbs—and introduces Cockburn's 'Swamp Game' extension that addresses how teams must balance immediate goals with leaving better infrastructure for future teams. The post concludes that this metaphor is far superior to traditional engineering comparisons like bridge building.
key_points: Software development shares key traits with rock climbing: technical skill requirements, team dynamics, tool dependency, planning with improvisation, and intrinsic challenge
Unlike rock climbing, software has dual goals—reaching the summit AND making it easier for subsequent teams to reach it—which can conflict
The 'Swamp Game' metaphor explains why optimal strategies differ for one-off projects versus ongoing series of releases
There is no perfect formula for software success; teams must constantly adapt strategies to changing situations and prioritize competing outcomes
The rock climbing metaphor is more apt than the overused bridge-building comparison for understanding software projects
money_quotes: If you fall wrong on a rock climb, you can be killed or maimed. This is probably the one aspect of rock climbing that does not transfer to software development.
If programmers do not find their assignment challenging, they may quit, or start embellishing the system with design elements they find challenging.
The optimal strategies for a series of races are different than for a single race.
There is no closed-form formula for winning the game. There are only strategies that are more useful in particular situations.
Since a perfect outcome is not possible in an over constrained situation, they must choose which outcome to prioritize at the expense of which others.
The next time someone asks you why software projects are so challenging, invite them to go rock climbing with you.
posts.key_insight: Software development is a cooperative game where teams must balance achieving immediate goals with creating infrastructure that makes future iterations easier—a tension that has no perfect solution, only situational strategies.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while software licenses are tedious and lawyer-filled, they are a necessary evil for any publicly released code. He shares his regret at not licensing his CodeProject articles, which ironically prevented experienced developers from using code he intended to share. Without an explicit license, code is copyrighted by default, meaning others have no legal right to use it. The post provides a comparison table of common licenses from permissive (MIT, BSD) to copyleft (GPL) to help developers choose. His core message: stop releasing unlicensed code and pick any license to enable legitimate reuse.
key_points: Unlicensed code is copyrighted by default, giving others no legal right to use it even if publicly posted
Experienced developers actively avoid unlicensed code because of legal liability concerns
The proliferation of software licenses (like standards) makes choosing one confusing, but any choice is better than none
Licenses range from maximally permissive (public domain, MIT) to restrictive copyleft (GPL) with many variations in between
The author's failure to license his own shared code created the opposite of his intended outcome
money_quotes: When I read a software license, what I see is a bunch of officious, mind-numbing lawyerly doublespeak.
A proper software license is a necessary evil for any code you plan to release to the public.
Experienced developers won't touch unlicensed code because they have no legal right to use it.
We love software licenses like we love standards – that's why there are so many of them.
Without a license, the code is copyrighted by default. People can read the code, but they have no legal right to use it.
It's a minefield, people.
The next time you release code into the wild, do your fellow developers a favor and pick a license – any license.
posts.key_insight: Releasing code without a license doesn't make it free—it makes it legally untouchable, defeating the entire purpose of sharing it.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that Firefox, equipped with the Web Developer Extension and Firebug, serves as the essential IDE for web development. While he prefers IE7 for everyday browsing, Firefox's developer tools are indispensable for troubleshooting HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. The Web Developer Extension offers real-time editing capabilities, while Firebug provides deep JavaScript profiling, debugging, and network analysis. Atwood considers these tools so valuable that he donates to both projects, and suggests that developing web apps without them would be like writing .NET applications in Notepad.
key_points: Firefox with developer extensions is essential for web development despite IE7 being preferred for regular browsing
Web Developer Extension enables real-time editing of HTML and CSS with immediate visual feedback
Firebug offers advanced JavaScript profiling, debugging, and network performance metrics
These tools together transform Firefox into the Visual Studio equivalent for web development
Supporting small software vendors through donations is part of responsible developer practice
money_quotes: Firefox is my primary web development IDE
Everything is editable in real-time. Edit the CSS, edit the HTML, and watch your changes take effect as you type. It's like magic.
These tools transform Firefox into the Visual Studio of browsers
It'd be akin to writing a .NET application in Notepad
If you haven't used these tools yet, I envy the experience you're about to have
posts.key_insight: The right browser extensions can transform a web browser into a full-featured development environment, making real-time debugging and editing not just possible but essential to modern web development.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the SEO industry is fundamentally dishonest, comparing practitioners to pornographers who exploit prurient interests—except SEOs sell the promise of easy money rather than sex. He cites examples of deliberate system gaming on Digg and quotes Jason Calacanis's criticism that 90% of SEO is 'snake oil.' Atwood contends that legitimate 'white hat' SEO is simply good web design, while the rest is manipulation. Using an analogy of a musician who chased record labels instead of building a genuine fanbase, he concludes that focusing on SEO means prioritizing money over substance, which audiences will eventually recognize.
key_points: SEO practitioners are essentially selling get-rich-quick schemes through search traffic manipulation
The SEOmoz example of deliberately gaming Digg demonstrates the industry's manipulative core
Legitimate 'white hat' SEO is just basic web design principles, not a secret formula
The focus on SEO reveals a prioritization of money and connections over genuine content quality
Relying on SEO tactics is ultimately self-defeating because it neglects authentic audience building
money_quotes: SEOs are the new pornographers of the web.
Just as pornographers sell sex, SEOs sell money. They trade in get-rich-quick schemes via search traffic.
Although SEOs pay lip service to the quality of the content, it's clear that the focus is on one thing, and one thing only: naked, raw greed.
The only difference between SEO 'experts' and pornographers is that pornographers are more honest about what they're actually selling.
Hiring a SEO expert to increase the quality of your site's content is like renting a porn video for the plot and character development.
90% of the SEO market is made up of snake oil salesman.
Steven did not care about the music. Steven cared about the money.
When you focus on SEO, you're focusing on money and connections.
posts.key_insight: Focusing on SEO techniques over genuine content creation reveals a fundamental prioritization of money over substance, and audiences will eventually see through the manipulation.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on Electronic Arts' early days when the company positioned computer programmers as 'software artists' and creative visionaries, contrasting this with EA's current status as a corporate franchise machine. He recalls being inspired as a teenager by EA's 'We See Farther' ad campaign, which suggested programming could be cool and artistic. The post highlights EA's innovative origins, including creating the sports game franchise genre with Dr. J and Larry Bird Go One on One. Atwood quotes extensively from EA's original manifesto about nurturing independent software artists, then notes with some melancholy that this idealistic vision proved difficult to sustain.
key_points: EA's 'We See Farther' ad campaign in the 1980s portrayed programmers as artists and creative visionaries, not just technical workers
Early EA games were packaged to look like rock album covers, reinforcing the artistic identity
EA invented the sports game franchise genre with Dr. J and Larry Bird Go One on One
EA's original manifesto promised a supportive environment for 'talented, independent software artists' with room for big ideas to grow
The contrast between EA's artistic origins and its current corporate franchise model illustrates how difficult it is to maintain creative vision at scale
money_quotes: I wondered, could being a computer programmer be... cool?
According to Electronic Arts, computer programmers weren't just programmers; they could be software artists.
We learn, for instance, that we are more entertained by involvement of our imaginations than by passive viewing and listening.
We're providing a special environment for talented, independent software artists. It's a supportive environment, in which big ideas are given room to grow.

We think our current work reflects this very special commitment. And though we are few in number today and apart from the mainstream of the mass software marketplace, we are confident that both time and vision are on our side.
I sure wanted to believe in software artistry at the time. EA's history proves that this is an unusually difficult vision statement to realize.
posts.key_insight: The idealistic vision of programmers as creative artists, once championed by early EA, has proven nearly impossible to sustain as companies scale into corporate franchises.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that Amazon's Mechanical Turk service is failing because it relies on monetary rewards for tasks that would be better motivated intrinsically. He observes that almost two years after launch, the available tasks are largely spammy, poorly compensated, and require excessive oversight. Atwood contrasts this with Amazon's own successful unpaid user review system and other platforms like Folding@Home, Digg, and Reddit where people contribute valuable work without payment. Drawing on Mary Poppendieck's research on compensation, he suggests that extrinsic monetary rewards actually destroy intrinsic motivation over time, and that choosing payment as a reward model may be an irreversible decision that doomed Mechanical Turk from the start.
key_points: Mechanical Turk tasks are predominantly low-paying ($0.10), spammy, and require subjective human grading
Amazon ironically built its business on unpaid user reviews while struggling to make paid microtasks work
Intrinsic motivation drives people to contribute to Folding@Home, Digg, Reddit, and social networks without payment
Extrinsic rewards like money destroy intrinsic motivation over time according to compensation research
Once you introduce monetary rewards, you may never be able to return to intrinsic motivation models
money_quotes: What I find ironic about Amazon's Mechanical Turk service is that Amazon built an entire business around the value of user reviews.
That's user-submitted content that people invested countless thousands of man-hours on. And Amazon didn't pay anyone a dime to do it.
I think the secret to running a viable Mechanical Turk service is, paradoxically, to do away with payment.
the rates make working in a sweatshop seem lucrative
Many studies have shown that extrinsic rewards like grades and pay will, over time, destroy the intrinsic reward that comes from the work itself.
once you go down the path of monetary rewards, you may never be able to go back, even when they cease to be effective, as they inevitably will.
posts.key_insight: Paying people small amounts for microtasks may actually be worse than not paying them at all, because monetary rewards destroy the intrinsic motivation that drives successful crowdsourced platforms.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reviews Jakob Nielsen's 'Prioritizing Web Usability' and examines how web usability issues have evolved from 2000 to 2006. The analysis reveals that while 58% of usability problems remain issues, designer restraint has resolved more problems (21%) than either technology advances (10%) or user adaptation (11%). Atwood highlights research showing that 70% of usability guidelines from 1986 remain valid in 2005, spanning across completely different technology eras. He concludes that usability principles represent rare, enduring knowledge that developers should prioritize learning.
key_points: Designer restraint has resolved more usability issues than technology improvements or user adaptation combined
58% of web usability problems from 2000 were still issues in 2006, showing slow but steady progress
Nearly 70% of usability guidelines from 1986 (mainframe era) remain valid and relevant 20 years later
Relying on user education or technology fixes means waiting a long time for usability improvements
Usability knowledge is one of the few things in a developer's toolkit that remains useful decades later
money_quotes: Most of the immediate benefits are realized by designers who learn to follow usability guidelines.
Relying on user education or technology fixes to address usability issues means you'll be waiting a long time.
The core principles of usability are timeless.
But nearly 70 percent of the original guidelines continue to be both correct and relevant 20 years later.
It's one of the precious few bodies of knowledge in a developer's toolkit that will still be useful twenty years from now.
Designers are fallible, too, so there's no guarantee these problems won't crop up again later, or in slightly different forms.
posts.key_insight: Usability principles transcend technology changes and represent rare, timeless knowledge worth investing in because 70% of guidelines remain valid across decades of technological revolution.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the software patent debate by presenting contrasting viewpoints from Donald Knuth, Paul Graham, and Paul Heckel. Knuth's 1994 letter warns that software patents would freeze progress and drive talented developers away. Graham argues patents don't significantly affect software innovation because startups should simply ignore them. Heckel contends patents could encourage innovation by making industries more diverse and profitable. Atwood concludes that the real problem isn't patents themselves but the sloppy way they're granted and enforced by the U.S. Patent Office.
key_points: Donald Knuth argued in 1994 that none of the essential software of that era would exist if patents had been prevalent in the 1960s-70s
Paul Graham's contrarian view is that software patents don't significantly affect innovation, and startups should simply ignore other companies' patents
Paul Heckel argues software isn't inherently different from other technologies and patents could make the industry more diverse and innovative
The post presents multiple perspectives without dismissing any outright, showing intellectual honesty in a contentious debate
Atwood's conclusion shifts blame from patents themselves to the patent office's enforcement and granting practices
money_quotes: I cannot help but realize that none of them would exist today if software patents had been prevalent in the 1960s and 1970s.
If present trends continue, the only recourse available to the majority of America's brilliant software developers will be to give up software or to emigrate.
Most innovation in the software business happens in startups, and startups should simply ignore other companies' patents.
At least, that's what we advise, and we bet money on that advice.
Patents make industries more diverse in their offerings, more profitable, more innovative, and ultimately will make the U.S. more competitive.
The problem with software patents isn't the patents themselves. It's the sloppy, haphazard way the patents are granted and enforced.
If anything needs reforming, it's the U.S. Patent Office.
posts.key_insight: The software patent debate isn't black and white—the real problem may not be patents as a concept but rather the broken system that grants and enforces them.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines Twitter's severe scaling problems through the lens of their Ruby on Rails technology choice. A Twitter developer candidly admits that Rails' conveniences become performance liabilities at scale, and that Ruby itself is demonstrably slow. Atwood argues that interpreted languages like Ruby and Python carry inherent performance penalties exceeding 100x compared to compiled languages. While acknowledging that Twitter's issues are largely database-related, he questions whether platform choice should ever jeopardize service availability. His conclusion is pragmatic: users care about uptime, not how elegant your framework is.
key_points: Twitter's scaling crisis exposes the hidden costs of Rails' developer-friendly abstractions at massive scale
A Twitter developer frankly states 'Ruby is slow' and notes Python outperforms it for equivalent operations
Interpreted languages carry performance penalties exceeding 100:1 compared to compiled languages like C++
Rails' lack of multi-database support forces expensive workarounds like aggressive caching and read slaves
When platform choice threatens service reliability, the service must take priority over technology preferences
money_quotes: All the convenience methods and syntactical sugar that makes Rails such a pleasure for coders ends up being absolutely punishing, performance-wise.
Ruby is slow.
Interpreted languages are so slow that if you have to ask how much performance you're giving up, you can't afford it.
Your users don't care how cool the Rails platform is – but they sure do care about consistent availability of your service.
What's more important – the service, or the platform you deliver that service on?
It's not just cost, it's time, and time is that much more precious when people can't reach your site.
posts.key_insight: Developer experience and elegant abstractions are worthless if they prevent your service from actually serving users at scale.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that choosing a programming language is really choosing a platform, using Reddit's switch from Lisp to Python as a case study. The Reddit team found that Lisp's lack of widely-used, well-documented libraries made development harder despite the language's flexibility. When they moved to Python, they also had to evaluate web frameworks, ultimately choosing the lightweight web.py over Django due to Django's opinionated constraints. Atwood contends that what matters most isn't language performance or syntax, but the ecosystem of libraries, tools, and framework choices available. Users don't care what technology powers their service—only developers do—so pragmatic platform choices trump language loyalty.
key_points: When you choose a language, you're really choosing a platform with its ecosystem of libraries, tools, and frameworks
Reddit switched from Lisp to Python primarily because of Lisp's lack of widely-used, well-documented libraries
Language performance comparisons between dynamic languages are largely a red herring
Having a choice of web application frameworks within a platform is valuable—Reddit chose web.py over Django for more control
Users don't care what technology you use; only developers obsess over language choices
money_quotes: When you choose a language, like it or not, you've chosen a platform.
The platform around the language, more than the language itself, sets the tone for your development experience.
They didn't change languages; they changed platforms.
Your users don't give a damn what framework and language you're using. The only people who care about that stuff are other software developers.
God help you if your users are software developers; then you're really in trouble.
Lisp will never get in your way, although sometimes the environment will.
At the point which your choice of platform starts to jeopardize your service, you switch platforms, exactly as Reddit did.
posts.key_insight: Programming language choice is really platform choice—the ecosystem of libraries, frameworks, and tools matters far more than language syntax or theoretical performance.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that making information public rather than keeping it secret is the key to success in the modern web era. He builds on Marc Hedlund's observation that successful web businesses often reimagine old UNIX commands for the web, and Jason Kottke's insight that breakthrough services like Blogger, Flickr, YouTube, and Twitter succeed by making previously private activities public and permanent. Atwood extends this to argue that obscurity is not just unhelpful but irrational—if no one knows about your work, you don't get the full benefit from it. He concludes that in a world of infinitely searchable micro-content, every public contribution enriches everyone, and the default should always be openness.
key_points: Successful web businesses often translate old UNIX commands into web-accessible services (grep→Google, pine→Gmail, wall→Twitter)
The pattern behind Web 2.0 success is taking private activities and making them public and permanent
Companies that embrace transparency and publicize even their failings grow stronger than those clinging to secrecy
Obscurity is not protective—it's a source of powerlessness that prevents you from getting full value from your work
In a world of searchable micro-content, the inclusionists are winning and every contribution enriches the whole
money_quotes: Power, it seems, comes from public information. Secrets are only a source of powerlessness.
If no one knows you did X, did you really get all the benefits for doing X?
Until you've written a blog entry about X, posted Flickr photos of X, uploaded a video of X to YouTube, typed a Twitter message about X... did X really happen at all?
Everything's interesting to someone, even if that someone is only you and a few other people in the world.
We live in a world of infinitely searchable micro-content, and every contribution, however small, enriches all of us.
Obscurity sucks. But obscurity by choice is irrational.
When in doubt, make it public.
posts.key_insight: In the age of searchable micro-content, choosing obscurity over openness is irrational—power and value flow from making your work public, not from keeping it secret.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reviews '19 Deadly Sins of Software Security,' a book he unexpectedly found valuable despite receiving it as a conference freebie. He summarizes all 19 security vulnerabilities with CVE database counts, demonstrating that while C/C++ bear responsibility for buffer overflows and related issues, 16 of the 19 sins apply to all languages and platforms. Atwood emphasizes that security usability is the most interesting and challenging sin, as security inherently adds complexity that conflicts with user experience. He concludes that the book serves as an excellent cross-platform, language-agnostic primer on software security risks.
key_points: Only 3 of the 19 security sins are specific to C/C++; the other 16 affect developers on all platforms and languages
SQL injection (2,225), XSS (2,996), and buffer overflows (3,326) represent the most common vulnerability categories in the CVE database
Security usability is the most challenging problem because security inherently conflicts with ease of use
Many developers underestimate risks like network traffic exposure, weak password systems, and improper file access
The book provides practical, language-agnostic guidance rather than deep dives into specific security areas
money_quotes: Usability is tough under the best of conditions – and security is the worst of conditions, at least from the user's perspective.
Security only works if the secure way also happens to be the easy way.
It's true that C and C++ have a heavy cross to bear. But only 3 of the 19 sins can be completely lumped on the plate of K&R.
The other 16 apply almost everywhere, to any developer writing code on any platform. It's a sobering thought.
Most programmers underestimate the risk of transmitting data over the network, even if that data is not private.
Unfortunately, writing properly concurrent code is incredibly difficult.
posts.key_insight: Software security vulnerabilities are overwhelmingly language-agnostic problems, and the hardest challenge isn't technical—it's making security usable enough that users will actually follow secure practices.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts discovering a chain of bloggers spreading misinformation about Microsoft's Silverlight technology, claiming it couldn't download data via HTTP when the SDK clearly included a Downloader object for exactly that purpose. He traces how one blogger's erroneous claim was uncritically echoed by others without anyone taking five minutes to verify by downloading the SDK themselves. Atwood contrasts this behavior with professional journalism ethics, which require testing accuracy and seeking verification. While acknowledging bloggers aren't professionals, he argues they still bear responsibility for basic fact-checking before publishing or amplifying others' claims.
key_points: A blogger claimed Silverlight had no networking capabilities, which was demonstrably false—the SDK prominently featured a Downloader object
Multiple bloggers echoed this misinformation without doing basic verification that would have taken five minutes
The chain of uncritical repetition shows how false information propagates across the internet
While bloggers aren't held to journalist standards, they should still do homework before writing and verify claims before linking
Readers should not assume everything on the internet is true and should check facts themselves
money_quotes: At no point did anyone stop to actually verify the claims of blogger A, even in the most rudimentary, basic of ways.
All they had to do was download the SDK and look for themselves to confirm that his complaints were true. I'm talking five minutes, maximum.
Instead, they blindly parroted blogger A, assumed that all of his claims were valid, and perpetuated his mistake across the internet.
We may not be professional journalists – but we are still accountable for the words we write.
Check the facts yourself.
Putting in that extra bit of effort won't transform you into a journalist, but I can guarantee it'll make you a better blogger.
The strident tone of his post makes me think otherwise, but let's give him the benefit of the doubt.
posts.key_insight: The ease of publishing online creates a responsibility to verify information before spreading it—a five-minute fact-check can prevent the viral spread of misinformation.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that as of 2007, the tech industry has entered a second dot-com bubble, but one with more sustainable fundamentals than the first. He recounts the warning signs he ignored in 2000 and the painful aftermath for developers when that bubble burst. The key differences this time include widespread broadband adoption (50% vs 5%), viable advertising revenue models, and dramatically cheaper infrastructure through commodity hardware and open source software. While acknowledging the new bubble appears more focused on building real businesses than quick riches, he challenges readers to learn from the past and approach this era more wisely.
key_points: The 2000 dot-com bust led to three dark years for developers: offshoring, devalued IT careers, and diminished opportunities
Broadband penetration jumping from 5% to 50% fundamentally changed the viability of internet business models
Advertising networks like Google AdSense finally gave startups a realistic path to revenue
Open source and commodity hardware dropped startup infrastructure costs to near zero
The new bubble appears more focused on sustainable businesses than get-rich-quick schemes
money_quotes: You simply couldn't work in the field without having something miraculous or catastrophic happen to you. Or both at once.
Despite all the warning signs, it never occurred to me that I was working in a bubble. Until it popped.
The three years after the bubble burst were dark, dark times for software developers.
Seven years later, we're now clearly in the throes of another dot-com bubble.
The greasy odor of get-rich-quick isn't quite as overpowering as it was in 1999.
Bubbles are exciting times. Fortunes are made and lost; careers built and destroyed.
What will you do differently in this bubble?
posts.key_insight: Those who lived through the first dot-com crash have a responsibility to recognize bubble dynamics and make wiser choices this time around, even as the fundamentals appear stronger.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to the question of why there are no open source billionaires by arguing that this is a feature, not a bug. He contends that open source software is intentionally designed to devalue the obscene profit margins (often exceeding 80%) that commercial software companies enjoy. While open source practitioners can become millionaires, the cutthroat competition between free alternatives prevents monopolistic wealth accumulation. However, Atwood concludes with an important distinction: the real billionaires built services on top of open source software, like Google and YouTube, rather than selling the software itself.
key_points: Most competition for open source software comes from other open source projects, making it far more cutthroat than commercial software markets
The absence of open source billionaires is by design - the movement intentionally devalues the extreme profit margins in commercial software
Commercial software duplication yields profit margins regularly exceeding 80%, essentially 'legally printing money'
Open source can produce millionaires but not billionaires because you're distributing free software
The real wealth is built by companies using open source to create services, not by selling the software itself
money_quotes: Most competition for open source software comes from other open source software. It's far more cutthroat than the commercial software market could ever be.
To ask where the open source billionaires are is to demonstrate a profound misunderstanding of how open source software works.
The lack of open source software billionaires is by design.
Duplicating software is about as close to legally printing money as a company can get; profit margins regularly exceed 80 percent.
If you wanted to become obscenely rich by starting an open source software company, I'm sorry, but you picked the wrong industry.
There are real millionaires – even billionaires – who built companies on open source software. Just ask Larry Page and Sergey Brin.
The real money isn't in the software. It's in the service you build with that software.
posts.key_insight: Open source's lack of billionaires isn't a failure but an intentional feature that redistributes value from software vendors to the services built upon that software.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood compares two WPF technical books—one by renowned author Charles Petzold and another by lesser-known Adam Nathan—and concludes that Nathan's book is dramatically superior despite Petzold's reputation. The key differentiator is presentation: Nathan's full-color book uses diagrams, screenshots, sidebars, and scannable formatting that makes learning enjoyable. Petzold's greyscale tome is an endless sea of text and code that fails to engage readers despite his excellent writing. Atwood argues that in technical education, layout and visual presentation matter as much as content quality. He calls on publishers to produce more books like Nathan's engaging, blog-like format.
key_points: Reputation alone doesn't make a technical book worth reading—presentation quality matters enormously
Full color printing, diagrams, screenshots, and visual aids add crucial dimensions to technical learning
Scannability through bullets, tables, sidebars, and subheadings makes content more accessible than walls of text
Even excellent writing cannot compensate for poor layout and visual design in technical books
The best technical books read like well-designed blogs and compete with online content
money_quotes: The Nathan book is brilliant. It reads like a blog and competes toe-to-toe with anything you'd find on the web.
Petzold's book, in contrast, is a greyscale sea of endless text and interminable code.
There are so few diagrams in the book that you get a little thrill every time you encounter one.
One looks compelling, fun, and inviting; the other looks like a painful, textbook slog that's the equivalent of writing code in Notepad.
Petzold's an excellent writer, but writing alone can't make up for the massive layout deficiencies of his book.
I'd sure be buying a heck of a lot more technical books if more of them were like this one.
posts.key_insight: In technical books, visual presentation and scannability are not luxury extras but essential features that determine whether readers actually learn from the content.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces his appearance on the .NET Rocks podcast, a long-running developer-focused internet radio show dating back to 2002. He expresses gratitude for the opportunity and notes the show's history of interviewing notable developers like Steve McConnell. The 64-minute interview covers themes from his Coding Horror blog. Atwood humbly positions himself among the show's impressive roster of past guests, including his own company's CEO Scott Stanfield.
key_points: Atwood was interviewed on .NET Rocks episode 232, a podcast he had listened to for years
The show has a notable history going back to 2002 with interviews of influential developers like Steve McConnell
The interview runs 64 minutes and covers topics from the Coding Horror blog
Atwood demonstrates characteristic humility by noting others interviewed were 'far more interesting' than himself
money_quotes: .NET Rocks! is a long running internet radio talk show for software developers that goes all the way back to 2002.
They've interviewed some very notable software developers along the way including Steve McConnell, and many other people far more interesting than myself.
It was my great honor to participate in this week's episode of .NET Rocks!
posts.key_insight: Even established bloggers value participating in the broader developer community through podcasts and shared conversations.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores how JavaScript, HTML, and CSS are designed to be extremely forgiving of errors, hiding mistakes rather than halting execution. After accidentally leaving script debugging enabled, he discovered the web is riddled with JavaScript errors that browsers deliberately suppress. He contrasts this permissive approach with strict languages that punish programmers for tiny mistakes, and examines how XHTML's draconian error handling doomed it to failure. Atwood concludes that forgiveness by default, while alien to programmers conditioned to appreciate strict compilers, is essential for the web's massive global adoption.
key_points: The web is saturated with JavaScript errors that browsers deliberately hide from users to maintain usability
HTML and CSS exhibit bizarrely tolerant behavior, producing weird but functional results from invalid input rather than errors
XHTML's strict error handling - rendering nothing and showing error messages for any malformed markup - caused it to fail as a standard
Programmers trained on strict compilers have been conditioned to appreciate punishment for mistakes, but this approach doesn't scale for the web
Forgiveness by default enables the large-scale, worldwide adoption that made the web successful
money_quotes: The web is full of JavaScript errors. You can barely click through three links before encountering a JavaScript error of one kind or another.
Things that would cause show stopping compiler errors in any other language are at worst minor inconveniences in JavaScript.
When errors are ignored by default, what you end up with is an incredibly tolerant, extremely permissive programming ecosystem. If it works, it works, errors be damned.
I can't think of any other programming environment that goes to such lengths to avoid presenting error messages, that tries so hard to make broken code work, at least a little.
The permissive, flexible tolerance designed into HTML and JavaScript is alien to programmers who grew up being regularly flagellated by their compiler for the tiniest of mistakes.
Some of us were punished so much so that we actually started to like it.
They may not have realized it at the time, but the Draconians inadvertently destroyed the future of XHTML with this single, irrevocable decision.
Even though programmers have learned to like draconian strictness, forgiveness by default is what works.
posts.key_insight: The web's success depends on its forgiving nature - strict error handling that programmers are trained to appreciate would have prevented the internet's global adoption.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces his attendance at MIX07, Microsoft's conference that he favors for its inclusion of non-Microsoft perspectives. He mentions Vertigo's Family.Show WPF reference app presentation and invites readers to meet up. He also notes setting up a Twitter stream for conference updates, while acknowledging Twitter's reliability issues.
key_points: MIX stands out among Microsoft conferences for including diverse, non-Microsoft voices
Vertigo is presenting a new WPF reference application called Family.Show
Twitter's reliability issues are already apparent in 2007, with static asset servers going down
money_quotes: MIX is by far my favorite Microsoft conference, because it 'mixes' in a liberal dose of traditionally non-Microsoft folks for a broader range of perspectives.
posts.key_insight: The best vendor conferences are those that deliberately include outside perspectives rather than becoming echo chambers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood introduces readers to Bayesian reasoning through a classic medical probability puzzle that stumps 85% of doctors. He explains that while Bayes' theorem is counterintuitive and difficult for humans to grasp naturally, it has immense practical power—most notably as the foundation for modern spam filtering. Atwood goes further to introduce Markovian filtering as an even more effective technique that examines word phrases rather than individual words. The post ultimately argues that Bayesian reasoning extends far beyond spam detection and can be applied to discriminate between almost anything, explaining the fervent enthusiasm the theorem generates among its adherents.
key_points: Only 15% of doctors correctly solve a basic Bayesian probability problem, demonstrating how counterintuitive this reasoning is for humans
Bayes' theorem is the foundation for virtually all modern spam filters, first popularized by Paul Graham in 2002
Markovian filtering improves on Bayesian methods by examining relationships between phrases rather than individual words, using exponential weighting for longer spans
These statistical discrimination techniques can be applied far beyond spam to classify web pages, resumes, blog entries, log files, and more with up to 99.9% accuracy
Bayesian reasoning has generated a devoted following who view it as foundational to understanding probability and even the scientific method itself
money_quotes: Only 15% of doctors, when presented with this situation, come up with the correct answer.
Bayesian reasoning is apparently one of those things which, like quantum mechanics or the Wason Selection Test, is inherently difficult for humans to grasp with our built-in mental faculties.
In computer science, it's easy to demonstrate the immense power of Bayes' theorem: it's the basis for almost all spam filters in use today.
CRM114 learns, and it learns fast.
What is the so-called Bayesian Revolution now sweeping through the sciences, which claims to subsume even the experimental method itself as a special case?
What matters is that Bayes is cool, and if you don't know Bayes, you aren't cool.
posts.key_insight: Bayes' theorem is counterintuitive for humans yet extraordinarily powerful in practice, forming the foundation of spam filtering and countless other classification problems that computers can now solve with remarkable accuracy.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software developers should learn to use professional graphics editing tools as a fundamental skill. Drawing from his experience at MIX, he observes that development and design are increasingly interrelated disciplines. While developers shouldn't try to become designers overnight, understanding graphics fundamentals—like image formats and vector graphics—is essential. He recommends picking from tools like GIMP, Paint.NET, or Photoshop, and investing time to move past the learning curve of 'crappy' tools like MS Paint.
key_points: Software development and graphic design are increasingly interrelated, requiring cross-pollination of skills
Developers should understand graphics fundamentals: image formats (JPG vs PNG), vector graphics (Canvas, SVG, XAML)
GIMP is free and powerful but has an excruciatingly difficult interface that becomes a learning exercise in itself
Competence in professional graphics tools enables better collaboration with designers through shared toolsets
The goal isn't becoming a designer, but gaining enough proficiency to work effectively and even copy good designs when needed
money_quotes: I believe every software developer should have basic competence in a common graphics editor.

Although GIMP is both free and powerful, the interface is so excruciatingly difficult to use that by the time you become proficient, you'll be able to handle any graphics editor on the market with ease.
Try to wean yourself off crappy graphics tools like Paint.
Technically, this has nothing to do with writing code. But competence in a real graphics editor means you'll have a much easier time working with designers because you now share a common toolset.
Given enough practice with the tools, you might even be able to copy a good design yourself in a pinch.
posts.key_insight: Learning professional graphics tools isn't about becoming a designer—it's about sharing a common language with designers and expanding your effectiveness as a developer.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that developers who learn graphics editing tools also need to understand basic design principles to use them effectively. He recommends 'The Non-Designer's Design Book' as an accessible starting point for developers with zero design skills. The post uses the book's Joshua tree anecdote to illustrate how learning to identify design patterns changes how you perceive the world around you. Atwood emphasizes that design principles are universal and apply equally to websites, GUI applications, and traditional print media. The core message is that developers should invest in learning to see design, not just learning design tools.
key_points: Learning a graphics tool without understanding design principles is like learning an IDE without knowing how to code
The Non-Designer's Design Book provides practical, timeless guidance for technical people starting from zero design knowledge
The Joshua tree story illustrates a profound truth: once you can name something, you suddenly see it everywhere
Good design principles from 1935 remain relevant today because the fundamental need for clarity and visual hierarchy hasn't changed
The first step in design education is simply learning to see the design that already surrounds you
money_quotes: Turn the tables for a moment: would it be reasonable to expect designers to learn our favorite development IDE, purely as a tool, without any guidance on how to write code, too?
When you start at zero, there's nowhere to go but up.
Publishers, and even more so, readers want what is important to be clearly laid out. They will not read anything that is troublesome to read.
The technique of modern typography must also adapt itself to the speed of our times.
Once I was conscious of the tree – once I could name it – I saw it everywhere.
You begin at the beginning: by learning to see the design all around you.
All it takes to distinguish yourself is a little judicious application of the design guidelines in this book.
posts.key_insight: The first step toward better design isn't mastering tools—it's learning to see and name the design principles already at work all around you.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood endorses Jon Udell's argument that most keystrokes spent on private emails could have far greater impact as public blog posts. The post argues that blogging optimizes for maximum awareness and influence per keystroke, since the same information that helps three email recipients could benefit many more readers. Atwood describes his own habit of converting emails into blog posts when he realizes the information shouldn't be siloed. The core message is that making communication public by default creates powerful network effects with minimal extra effort.
key_points: Most keystrokes go into emails read by few people, when many more could benefit from that same information
Blogging optimizes the awareness and influence each keystroke can yield
Converting private emails to public blog posts costs almost no extra effort but radically expands reach
The notification of a message and its delivery don't have to coincide - you can notify privately while making the payload public
Default to public communication unless the topic is necessarily private and interpersonal
money_quotes: How many of those keystrokes flow into email messages? Most. How many people receive those email messages? Few. How many people could usefully benefit from those messages, now or later? More than a few, maybe a lot more.
Why not make each keystroke work as hard as it can?
That arrangement costs hardly any extra keystrokes, and hardly any extra time. But it's an optimization that can radically expand influence and awareness.
There's no reason to silo this information in a private email exchange.
The next time you find yourself typing more than a few sentences on your keyboard, stop and ask: are you maximizing the value of your keystrokes?
posts.key_insight: Every substantial piece of writing you create privately is a missed opportunity for broader impact - default to public sharing unless privacy is truly required.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines why phishing attacks remain devastatingly effective despite years of security awareness efforts. Drawing on a 1994 hacking book and a 2006 phishing study, he demonstrates that social engineering exploits fundamental human tendencies that don't change with technology. The study showed that even when users were warned to expect fake websites, 90% fell for well-crafted phishing pages, and no demographic factors correlated with resistance. Atwood concludes that since users will always be fooled by convincing spoofs, the only viable defense is preventing phishing pages from ever being displayed, through browser-based blacklists that block known malicious sites before users can interact with them.
key_points: Social engineering attacks exploit timeless human vulnerabilities—helpfulness, trust, and preference for ease of use—that persist regardless of technological advancement
A 2006 study found 90% of users fell for good phishing sites, with no correlation between vulnerability and education, age, sex, or computer experience
Users consistently ignore security indicators: 23% never checked address bars, and 15 of 22 dismissed fraudulent certificate warnings without hesitation
Traditional security measures like warnings, SSL indicators, and browser chrome are ineffective because users either ignore them or can't interpret them correctly
Browser-based blacklists that block phishing pages entirely represent the only effective mitigation, since any displayed phishing page will fool a significant percentage of users
money_quotes: Users who are in-the-know will always want to help the less fortunate ones who are not. Those who aren't will seek the advice of the gurus. And so social engineering and reverse social engineering live on.
I just wish (sort of) that they would realize what danger they put themselves in every time they act without security in mind.
Neither education, age, sex, previous experience, nor hours of computer use showed a statistically significant correlation with vulnerability to phishing. Everyone was vulnerable.
When presented with a spoofed web page, a large percentage of users will always fall for it. Forever.
Phishing is the forever hack.
It doesn't matter how many security warnings we present, or how much security browser chrome we wrap websites in.
The only security technique that can protect users from phishing scams, it seems, is the one that prevents them from ever seeing the phishing page in the first place.
posts.key_insight: Security measures that rely on user judgment will always fail against social engineering; the only effective protection is preventing the attack from ever reaching the user.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his favorite programming quote—Nathaniel Borenstein's satirical observation about software engineers preferring to write a parameterized DestroyCity procedure rather than a hardcoded DestroyBaghdad one. He uses this as a launching point to discuss how programming quotes can serve as entry points to understanding the influential figures in computer science. Atwood highlights Larry Wall's wisdom about avoiding premature features and finding the 'long-term lazy way.' The post encourages developers to explore quotes as a way to discover their intellectual influences, while noting that respecting a person's insights doesn't require dogmatic acceptance of their tools.
key_points: Programming quotes serve as windows into understanding the notable personalities who shaped computer science
The Borenstein quote perfectly captures programmers' instinct toward abstraction and generalization, even in absurd contexts
Larry Wall's advice emphasizes patience in design: don't implement features immediately, and seek the long-term lazy approach over short-term shortcuts
Respecting someone's wisdom doesn't require adopting their specific tools or languages
Browsing quotes helps developers identify who their influences are—or should be
money_quotes: No ethically-trained software engineer would ever consent to write a DestroyBaghdad procedure. Basic professional ethics would instead require him to write a DestroyCity procedure, to which Baghdad could be given as a parameter.
Never have programmers been more neatly summarized.
Don't throw in a feature when you first think of it. Think if there's a way to generalize it, think if it should be generalized.
There is a level of abstraction beyond which people don't want to go.
Try to come up with the long-term lazy way, not the short-term lazy way.
I don't have to dogmatically accept Perl to respect Larry Wall.
It's not about the language; it's about learning to understand programmers as human beings.
posts.key_insight: Programming quotes offer a gateway to understanding the giants of computer science as human beings, and you can learn from influential thinkers without dogmatically adopting their specific tools.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his identity as a Microsoft developer while examining why some programmers are abandoning the Microsoft ecosystem. He discusses Mike Gunderloy's decision to leave Microsoft development due to concerns about the company's anti-competitive behavior, while acknowledging the frustrating complexity of the .NET development stack. Atwood argues against the tribal mentality that forces developers to choose absolute loyalty to either Microsoft or open source, using Miguel de Icaza's reception at MIX as evidence of harmful insularity in both camps. He concludes that pragmatism and cross-pollination between communities serves developers better than partisan allegiance.
key_points: Microsoft's rapid technology churn creates a complex, burdensome development stack that rivals the difficulty of open source alternatives
The developer community suffers from a false dichotomy where programmers feel compelled to either fully embrace or completely reject Microsoft
Both Microsoft and open-source communities are too insular, with developers ignorant of important figures and innovations from the other side
Pledging allegiance to any platform over your own craft and growth is a disservice to yourself as a developer
Despite Microsoft's flaws, Atwood argues they remain preferable to hypothetical monopolies by Sun, IBM, Oracle, or Apple
money_quotes: As a software developer, you're doing yourself a disservice by pledging allegiance to anything other than yourself and your craft – whether it's Microsoft or the principle of free software.
We're allies, not enemies.
I'm not saying he should have given up on Microsoft. But I understand.
Choosing to be provincial and insular is a sure-fire path to ignorance.
Both the Microsoft community and the open-source communities are far too insular and provincial.
Sometimes, you wonder if choosing an environment where things change more slowly isn't a better long term evolutionary decision.
Yes, Microsoft is a near-monopoly, but as giant, evil monopolistic corporations go, you could do a lot worse.
posts.key_insight: Platform loyalty is a trap—software developers should transcend tribal affiliations and learn from all communities while remaining focused on their craft rather than corporate allegiance.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores zoomable user interfaces (ZUIs), a concept pioneered by Jef Raskin and being developed by companies like Humanized and Microsoft's Seadragon. He catalogs existing implementations across mapping sites, OS X Expose, mobile browsers, and real-time strategy games like Supreme Commander. Microsoft claims ZUIs offer navigation speed independent of object count, smooth transitions, and perfect scaling. Atwood argues that zooming is as natural and intuitive as scrolling, noting how effortlessly users adapt to it in applications like games and maps. He concludes that ZUIs are underutilized and should become integral to future desktop operating systems.
key_points: Zoomable UIs offer technical advantages: navigation speed independent of object count, performance tied only to bandwidth-to-pixel ratio, smooth transitions, and resolution-independent scaling
ZUIs already exist in mapping applications, mobile browsers, OS X Expose, and games like Supreme Commander, but remain rare in mainstream desktop software
The zooming metaphor in Supreme Commander demonstrates how naturally users adapt to ZUIs when well-implemented—constantly zooming between tactical and strategic views
The mouse scroll wheel's standardization has made zooming as natural an interaction as scrolling
ZUIs represent a continuation of Jef Raskin's human interface work, now being carried forward by his son Asa at Humanized
money_quotes: Zooming is a natural metaphor that people adapt to as easily as they do to scrolling.
What really struck me about zoomable UI is how intuitive and usable it is in the right situation.
You don't have to think; it just works the way you'd expect it to.
Speed of navigation is independent of the size or number of objects.
