Zoomable UI is woefully underused today, but I think it should be an integral part of our desktop operating systems in the future.
posts.key_insight: Zooming is as fundamental and intuitive an interaction paradigm as scrolling, yet remains drastically underutilized in modern software interfaces.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines Google's 2007 initiative to warn users about malware-infected websites directly in search results. He analyzes the technical mechanisms behind a specific malicious site and discusses the ethical responsibility that comes with Google's dominant position as the internet's de-facto start page. While applauding the effort, he questions why Google keeps malware sites indexed with warnings rather than delisting them entirely, as competitors do. He concludes that search engines have a unique responsibility to help combat web-based malware, which affects one in ten webpages.
key_points: Google implemented interstitial warnings for malware sites rather than delisting them, unlike competitors who removed such sites entirely
Users determined to see 'dancing bunnies' will bypass any number of warnings - security measures can only do so much against user intent
Legitimate websites can become malware vectors through compromised servers, unsanitized user HTML, or questionable advertising networks
Google's research found that one in ten webpages contains malicious code, making this a massive-scale problem
With great search dominance comes great responsibility - doing nothing would be irresponsible given Google's position as the internet's start page
money_quotes: To do nothing – to let these purely evil sites show up in Google results with no warning whatsoever – would be irresponsible.
Users will find a way to see the dancing bunnies if they really, really want to, no matter how many warnings and barriers you blast in front of them.
If you accept that Google wields the immense power of being the de-facto start page for the internet, then maybe this kind of policing effort comes with the territory.
Despite the individual ethics of the person using that one computer, a compromised computer will be used for attacks and spam against everyone.
I don't think it's fair to call this Google policing the web; it's just good, ethical business to filter out the evil.
It's scary how many ways this can happen.
posts.key_insight: Search engines bear ethical responsibility to protect users from malware, not as censorship but as a natural extension of their role as gatekeepers to the internet.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that despite .NET being marketed as a multi-language runtime, developers should just accept C# as the de facto standard due to the cognitive friction of switching between similar languages. However, he identifies two major frustrations with C#: case sensitivity (which he considers fundamentally wrong) and the 'compilation tax' - the need to constantly manually compile code to catch errors. He contrasts this with VB.NET's background compilation feature, which automatically flags errors as you type, dramatically reducing the feedback loop between making a mistake and discovering it. Atwood calls on Microsoft to add background compilation to C#, arguing it's an IDE feature that could be implemented without changing the language itself.
key_points: The choice between C# and VB.NET is essentially meaningless - like choosing between Coke and Pepsi - yet mixing them creates unnecessary cognitive friction
Most developers have settled on C# as the standard, making VB.NET developers face constant code translation penalties when integrating community libraries
The C# 'compilation tax' creates a compulsive loop of writing small amounts of code followed by manual compilation, wasting significant developer time
VB.NET's background compilation provides immediate feedback on errors without manual intervention, reducing turnaround time between mistake and fix to nearly zero
Background compilation is an IDE feature, not a language feature, meaning it could theoretically be added to C# without changing the language
money_quotes: There's simply too much cognitive friction when translating back and forth between these two very, very similar languages.
I've learned to suck it up and pretend C# is the only .NET language.
As an advocate for people over machines, I have to go on record stating that case sensitivity in a programming language is wrong, and will always be wrong.
When coding in C#, I feel like a monkey in a cage that dispenses food pellets when I press CTRL+SHIFT+B.
It's a complete waste of time, but you're compelled to do it through perverse incentives in the IDE.
With background compilation, I know immediately when I've made a mistake.
It reduces the turnaround time between mistake and fix to virtually nothing.
I may have accepted the C# penance, but I wish the saints at Microsoft would see fit to grant this one small wish to a converted VB.NET sinner.
posts.key_insight: Developer productivity is significantly impacted by tight feedback loops, and forcing programmers to manually compile to discover errors is an unnecessary tax that tools should eliminate through background compilation.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts the origin story of DONKEY.BAS, a simple BASIC game co-authored by Bill Gates and Neil Konzen during the early days of the IBM PC. Gates and Konzen wrote the program at 4 AM in an overheated closet that IBM required be locked for security. The Macintosh team famously mocked the crude game, amazed that Microsoft's co-founder would take credit for such poor work. Atwood notes that while DONKEY.BAS is an awkward part of Gates' programming legacy, Gates has shown good humor about it. Microsoft later created a tongue-in-cheek 3D remake in VB.NET to showcase how far BASIC had evolved over 20 years, though that version now suffers from compatibility issues with outdated DirectX dependencies.
key_points: Bill Gates was once an actual working programmer, with DONKEY.BAS serving as hard evidence of this era
The game was written at 4 AM in a locked, overheated closet because IBM required physical security for the prototype PC
The Macintosh team used DONKEY.BAS as an example of everything wrong with the IBM PC platform
Microsoft's VB.NET remake of Donkey demonstrated the evolution of BASIC over 20 years but now fails due to DirectX 8 dependencies
Various BASIC implementations have never been known for stellar compatibility across versions
money_quotes: It's hard to imagine now, but in the early days of Microsoft, Bill Gates was an actual programmer.
IBM insisted that we had to have a lock on the door and we only had this closet that had a lock on it, so we had to do all our development in there and it was always over 100 degrees.
We were amazed that such a thoroughly bad game could be co-authored by Microsoft's co-founder, and that he would actually want to take credit for it in the comments.
It's funny to think that DONKEY.BAS is part of Gates' legacy as a programmer.
It's a fairly short program, but it's also a painful reminder of how awkward programming was in 1981.
I suppose that's another enduring lesson of DONKEY.BAS; the various BASIC implementations have never been known for their stellar compatibility.
posts.key_insight: Even the most successful tech moguls have embarrassing early code in their past, and the primitive state of early personal computing produced software that looks absurd by modern standards.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood enthusiastically recommends the book 'Designing Interactions' by Bill Moggridge, co-founder of the influential design firm IDEO. The post celebrates the book's comprehensive coverage of digital technology design history, featuring interviews with key industry figures and stunning visuals. Atwood provides context on Moggridge's credentials, including designing the first laptop computer in 1979. He shares his collection of IDEO resources, including their Method Cards and other books, positioning IDEO as a giant in product design. The core message emphasizes that technology should be framed strictly in service to people.
key_points: 'Designing Interactions' is a comprehensive, visually stunning book covering the history of digital technology design with interviews from key figures
Bill Moggridge co-founded IDEO and designed the GRiD Compass, considered the world's first laptop computer, in 1979
IDEO's influence is pervasive - most people have used products they've designed
The IDEO Method Cards serve as prompts to consider different ways design should influence work
The fundamental principle worth remembering: technology should serve people, not the other way around
money_quotes: I think IDEO and Moggridge are on the right track: strictly framing technology in service to people.
I know it seems obvious, but it's one of those things that bears reminding every so often.
They're great little nudges to get you thinking about all the different ways design should influence what you're working on.
Nobody could afford it, of course, but it was an evolutionary milestone in computer history.
Perhaps that's why reading Designing Interactions is so inspiring.
posts.key_insight: Technology exists to serve people, and understanding the history and principles of human-centered design is essential for anyone building digital products.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood traces the history of the mouse wheel, crediting Microsoft's Eric Michelman as its inventor. The wheel emerged from Michelman's 1993 observation that Excel users struggled to navigate large spreadsheets, leading him to prototype a 'zoom lever' using a joystick. After feedback suggested adding scrolling functionality, and when Microsoft's hardware division was looking for a use case for a mouse-mounted wheel, the pieces came together. The feature shipped with the 1996 Intellimouse after Walt Mossberg convinced the team to make scrolling, not zooming, the default behavior.
key_points: The mouse wheel originated from watching Excel users struggle with navigation in large spreadsheets
Eric Michelman's original concept was a 'zoom lever' for the non-mouse hand, not a wheel on the mouse itself
The hardware and software teams at Microsoft had to align - hardware needed a use case, software needed hardware support
Office apps (especially Excel and Word) were the gatekeepers: if they supported a feature, the industry would follow
Walt Mossberg's feedback shifted the default from zooming to scrolling before the product shipped
money_quotes: The mouse wheel is the first true mouse innovation since the invention of the mouse itself.
At this point most people thought it was kind of wacky.
The hardware guys came back and said that they had considered adding a wheel to the mouse, but they didn't know what it would be used for.
If Excel and Word supported something, then the other Office apps would follow, and if Office as a whole supported something, then everyone else would follow too.
Document navigation answered that question, so they said that if I could get Office to support it, they would build it.
posts.key_insight: The mouse wheel's invention illustrates how great innovations emerge from observing user struggles, and how success depends on alignment between hardware capabilities, software support, and user feedback about default behaviors.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that user interface friction—the cumulative cognitive overhead of each step required to complete an action—is a critical factor in application usability. He presents compelling comparisons: IM requires 3 steps versus email's 10+, and Backpack's calendar entry takes 2 steps versus iCal's 11. The post highlights Reddit's frictionless sign-up overlay as an exemplary pattern and suggests that services like Twitter and Tumblr succeeded partly due to minimal interaction overhead. Atwood challenges developers to count the steps in their own applications and systematically reduce them, pointing to Google's single search box as an ideal to aspire toward.
key_points: Interface friction is the cumulative cognitive load from every click, keystroke, and decision a user must make
IM has 3x less interface overhead than email for sending a simple message
Website sign-up processes can involve a dozen or more hurdles before users can even begin using a service
Minimal friction can be a key competitive advantage, as demonstrated by Twitter, Tumblr, and Reddit
Developers should actively count and measure the steps required for common user scenarios in their applications
money_quotes: Whether you call it cognitive load, a sequence of hurdles, interface overhead, or just plain excise, it all adds up to the same thing: interface friction for the user.
Sand in the gears of their mind. One more unnecessary thing they have to think about before using your application.
How many steps does it take to do something in your application? Have you counted?
Reduced interface friction goes a long way toward explaining the popularity of services like twitter and tumblr.
What's the minimum amount of effort a user can expend to produce something? The answer could be a key competitive advantage.
That single input box on the Google homepage starts to look more and more like an optimal user experience.
You should continually strive to reduce the friction of your user interface.
posts.key_insight: The number of steps required to complete an action in your application directly impacts user adoption, and systematically counting and reducing those steps should be a continuous design priority.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that developers complaining about slow compilation on 'fast dual-core' computers may be using Pentium 4 CPUs, which are dramatically slower than newer architectures despite similar marketing claims. Using compilation and Mozilla benchmarks, he demonstrates that even the most expensive Pentium 4 Extreme Edition performs nearly 50% slower than midrange Core 2 Duo or Athlon 64 processors. The post makes an economic argument that given developer salaries, it's irrational for companies to hobble programmers with outdated hardware when a sub-$1000 upgrade could double their productivity.
key_points: Not all dual-core CPUs are equal - Pentium 4 dual-cores perform dramatically worse than Core 2 Duo or Athlon 64 alternatives
The fastest Pentium 4 (965 Extreme Edition at $1000) is nearly 50% slower at compilation than midrange competing CPUs
Real-world applications like compilers and browsers show even worse disparities, with Pentium D being almost twice as slow as Core 2 Duo
The economic argument: developer time is expensive, so cheap hardware upgrades have massive ROI
Tech Report called the Pentium 4 architecture 'lame-duck' - it's fundamentally flawed, not just slower
money_quotes: Not all dual core computers are created equal. Not by a long shot.
The fastest possible Pentium 4 is nearly 50 percent slower at compilation than a midrange Athlon 64 or Core 2 Duo CPU.
Given how much software developers are paid, it makes no economic sense to hobble them with old, slow PCs based on the underperforming Pentium 4 CPU.
I'm not talking about meaningless synthetic performance benchmark numbers; I'm talking about performance in real world apps that software developers use every day.
You could more than double your performance by upgrading to a middle-of-the-road Core 2 Duo CPU.
Isn't the value of your time worth at least that?
posts.key_insight: Developer hardware decisions should be driven by real-world productivity benchmarks and economic analysis of time saved, not by misleading marketing terms like 'dual-core' or high clock speeds.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that JavaScript, despite being initially dismissed by many programmers including himself, has become the indispensable programming language of the web. He traces JavaScript's journey from a 'toy language' to a mature platform, crediting the View Source culture and increasing browser capabilities for its success. While acknowledging JavaScript's frustrations—cross-browser issues, debugging pain, and imperfect design—he contends that its ubiquity and the viral nature of web development make it impossible to ignore. He pushes back against developers who avoid JavaScript by fleeing to alternatives like Silverlight, Flex, or Flash, arguing that understanding JavaScript's limitations is essential before seeking to transcend them.
key_points: JavaScript 'won' the web not because it was the best language, but because it was already everywhere when developers needed client-side interactivity
The View Source culture and copy-paste mashup capability gave the web its distributed evolution and made JavaScript's success possible
JavaScript is actually closer to functional languages like Lisp or Scheme than to C or Java, with first-class functions and closures
Despite alternatives from Microsoft, Adobe, and Sun, JavaScript's ubiquity guarantees its continued dominance
Developers should immerse themselves in JavaScript's limitations rather than blindly fighting them or fleeing to alternatives
money_quotes: What 'Ajax' means is 'Javascript now works.'
If you're the last man left on earth, it doesn't matter how ugly you are when the women come to re-populate the planet.
JavaScript is the world's most ubiquitous computing runtime.
The best way to transcend the limitations of JavaScript is to immerse yourself in those limitations.
JavaScript is the lingua franca of the web. Ignore it at your peril.
You get lambdas without having to balance all those parens.
If you give up the viral soft collaboration of View Source and copy-and-paste mashups... you lose what made the web great, and what made the web win.
posts.key_insight: JavaScript's dominance stems not from being the best language but from being universally available, and developers should embrace its limitations rather than flee to proprietary alternatives.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the paradox that as computer hardware costs approach zero, electricity becomes the dominant expense—especially for companies like Google running massive datacenters. He examines Google's innovative approaches to power efficiency, including custom 90% efficient power supplies and strategically located datacenters in low-cost power regions. Atwood then applies this analysis to consumer PCs, finding that while efficient power supplies exist, the savings for individual users are marginal—about $30 per year in his calculations. He concludes that what's a strategic business imperative for Google becomes a matter of collective public good when multiplied across millions of consumer PCs worldwide.
key_points: Bill Gates predicted hardware costs would approach zero, and history proved him right—but this shifts the cost burden to electricity
Google builds custom 90% efficient power supplies and locates datacenters in regions with cheap power as strategic business decisions
Standard PC power supplies peak at 85% efficiency around 250 watts, but most desktop PCs idle at 120-150 watts where efficiency drops
For individual users, upgrading to an efficient power supply saves roughly $30/year—barely justifying the hardware cost over three years
What doesn't matter for individuals becomes significant public good when multiplied across millions of computers worldwide
money_quotes: If server power consumption grows 20 percent per year, the four-year cost of a server's electricity bill will be larger than the $3,000 initial price of a typical low-end server.
It's not hard to do. That's why to me it's personally offensive that standard power supplies aren't as efficient.
Your PC is statistically idle 99% of the time it is turned on.
The real challenge is to deliver 90%+ efficiency at typical idle power consumption levels – 120-150 watts.
I'd save a whopping thirty bucks per year. That's not even enough to cover the cost of the energy-efficient power supply!
All this tells us is that Google's problems aren't necessarily our personal problems.
Considering the millions upon millions of computers in the world, more efficient PC power supplies are also part of the greater public good.
posts.key_insight: The economics of efficiency operate on completely different scales—what's a rounding error for individuals becomes a strategic imperative for corporations and an environmental necessity for society.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood celebrates Dell's decision to offer Ubuntu Linux on their desktop computers, marking the first time the company has ever sold PCs without Windows pre-installed. He frames this as ending the 'Microsoft Tax' - the practice of forcing customers to pay for Windows whether they wanted it or not. By comparing pricing between identical Dell systems with Vista ($679) and Ubuntu ($599), he shows customers can save $80 by choosing Linux. Atwood praises Dell for playing 'fair and square' rather than charging extra for the non-Windows option, and suggests this moment could be a turning point in desktop computing history.
key_points: Dell offering Ubuntu marks the first time they've ever sold desktops without a Microsoft operating system
The 'Microsoft Tax' refers to being forced to pay for Windows on hardware purchases regardless of whether you want it
The price difference ($80) reveals Dell's actual cost for a Windows Vista license, which is cheaper than buying OEM separately ($129)
Offering a free OS is effectively the same as selling hardware without any operating system
This represents a win for consumer choice regardless of one's operating system preferences
money_quotes: Until today, it was quite literally impossible to decline the Windows license when you bought a desktop from Dell.
If you bought a desktop PC from Dell, you got – and paid for – a copy of Windows, whether you wanted it or not.
Offering a free desktop operating system is effectively the same thing as selling hardware without any operating system.
Whether you're a fan of the latest open source operating systems, or just a fan of plain old-fashioned consumer choice, the end of the Microsoft tax is a win for customers.
But if you have no intention of running Windows, you just saved eighty bucks.
It's also quite possible today will be looked back on as an important turning point in the history of desktop computing.
posts.key_insight: Consumer choice in operating systems matters, and breaking Microsoft's bundling monopoly on major PC hardware represents a significant victory for both open source advocates and customers who simply want to pay only for what they use.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents his experiment upgrading to a high-efficiency power supply, measuring actual power savings with a Kill-A-Watt meter. He references Google's whitepaper showing that typical PCs waste 30-40% of power through inefficient power supplies, and their proposed solution of single-voltage 12V systems. His real-world testing showed approximately 10% power reduction across all load levels after the upgrade. He also highlights the often-overlooked issue of standby power consumption, noting his PC draws 5 watts even when powered off. The post concludes with practical advice for consumers looking to upgrade, recommending 80 PLUS certified power supplies despite their premium pricing.
key_points: Typical PC power supplies are only 60-75% efficient, wasting 30-40% of consumed electricity as heat
Google's proposed single-voltage 12V power supplies could achieve 85-90% efficiency at virtually no additional cost, but aren't yet available to consumers
Real-world testing showed a high-efficiency PSU delivers about 10% power savings across idle, load, and peak usage scenarios
Standby power consumption (5 watts when 'off') accounts for 6-16% of home energy use and 1% of global carbon emissions
The 80 PLUS certification is the reliable standard for efficiency claims, as many vendors misleadingly advertise 'up to 80%' efficiency only achievable under unrealistic loads
money_quotes: Most likely, the computer you're using wastes 30-40% of the electrical power it consumes because it is using an inefficient power supply.
You won't have to pay more for a higher-efficiency PC, because the power supply is actually getting simpler, not more complicated.
I was surprised to find that my PC uses 5 watts of power even when it's powered off.
Standby power use is now responsible for 1% of total carbon emissions on earth.

Many vendors cut corners by stating their power supplies offer 'up to' 80 percent efficiency, but what they don't tell you is that you'll only reach that level of efficiency under extreme power loads that are unrealistic for most desktops.
The only way to reduce your computer's power use to zero watts is to unplug it from the wall, or flip the power switch on the back of the power supply.
posts.key_insight: Upgrading to an 80 PLUS certified power supply delivers measurable 10% efficiency gains, and while the cost savings are modest for individuals, the environmental impact of widespread inefficiency—including often-ignored standby power—is substantial.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses the success of Desktop Tower Defense to illustrate how the internet has democratized economic opportunity for solo programmers. Paul Preece, a Visual Basic programmer with no professional game development experience, created a free Flash game that generates nearly $100,000 per year through advertising revenue alone. Atwood contrasts this with traditional publishing deals where middlemen take the lion's share—citing Roller Coaster Tycoon's creator earning $4 million while the publisher cleared $180 million. The post argues that while you might not become a millionaire, the barrier to entry for earning a solid income as an independent programmer has never been lower.
key_points: Tower Defense originated as a user-created mod for Warcraft III, demonstrating how communities can innovate beyond what game creators envision
Desktop Tower Defense generates roughly $100,000/year in ad revenue with only $130/month in hosting costs
The internet enables single programmers to bypass publishers and retain full control over their income
Traditional publishing deals are exploitative—Chris Sawyer earned $4 million from Roller Coaster Tycoon while his publisher made $180 million
Success like Paul Preece's is achievable for any motivated programmer with some luck, unlike the exceptional circumstances of traditional publishing deals
money_quotes: This is a game mode not created by Blizzard, but invented by multiplayer game modders using the tools provided with Warcraft III.
You'd be surprised how much money you can make by creating a flash game and giving it away for free on the internet.
The internet has created economic opportunity for a single programmer working alone.
No selling his soul to a publisher, no middlemen, just pure income, controlled directly by him.
That's the 'selling your soul' part.
Paul's success, on the other hand, is something that any programmer, with sufficient motivation and a little bit of luck, could potentially duplicate.
posts.key_insight: The internet has lowered the barrier for solo programmers to earn substantial income independently, making modest wealth achievable without sacrificing creative control to publishers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood highlights a 2003 column where tech journalist John Dvorak complained that Windows' System Idle Process was 'hogging' 95% of his CPU resources, demonstrating a fundamental misunderstanding of how computers work. Atwood explains that the idle process is simply a placeholder task that runs when the CPU has nothing else to do, and a high idle percentage actually means the system is working fine. The post uses this anecdote to gently mock a prominent tech writer for not understanding basic computer science concepts despite decades in the industry. Atwood provides the Wikipedia explanation of idle tasks to educate readers on why this 'problem' is actually normal system behavior.
key_points: John Dvorak, a veteran tech journalist, publicly complained about the System Idle Process consuming CPU cycles, not understanding it represents unused capacity
The idle task is a fundamental concept in operating systems that runs only when there's nothing else for the computer to do
On modern processors, the idle task executes HLT instructions that save power and reduce heat
A high System Idle Process percentage is normal and desirable—it means your CPU isn't overworked
Dvorak likely had an unrelated system problem that he incorrectly blamed on the idle process
money_quotes: From the 'you can't make this stuff up department'
When I hit Ctrl-Alt-Delete, I see that the System Idle Process is hogging all the resources and chewing up 95 percent of the processor's cycles. Doing what? Doing nothing?
Dvoraksayswhat?
His profound misunderstanding of how this fundamental bit of computer science works is a wee bit disturbing for a computer journalist of his tenure and stature
If the idle task is 'chewing up 95 percent of the processor's cycles,' that's normal: it simply means your CPU isn't working very hard on anything at the moment
posts.key_insight: Even veteran technology journalists can have embarrassing gaps in their understanding of fundamental computing concepts, which should remind us all to verify our assumptions before publishing opinions.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that programmers should have a deep appreciation for computer hardware, not just software. He recommends three books that celebrate computing history through photography: 'Core Memory' (a visual tour of the Computer History Museum), 'The Computer: An Illustrated History' (a broad pictorial history from abacus to iPod), and 'The Encyclopedia of Game Machines' (an exhaustive reference of gaming hardware). Atwood shares his experience visiting the Computer History Museum and playing Spacewar on the only functioning PDP-1 in existence. The post is a love letter to the physical machines that make programming possible.
key_points: Programmers who love their craft should also appreciate the hardware that enables it
The Computer History Museum in Mountain View is an essential destination for anyone interested in computing
Three recommended books provide different angles on hardware history: museum pieces, cultural context, and gaming systems
Physical computing artifacts, when preserved and documented through photography, become objects of beauty and historical significance
Hardware appreciation is a legitimate and rewarding aspect of programmer culture
money_quotes: I've never understood programmers who loved the craft of programming, but were disinterested in the underlying hardware – the very tool that allows them to practice their craft.
I have an unabashed love for computer hardware that borders on inappropriate. I'm not ashamed to admit it.
That's how I had the privilege of playing Spacewar on the original vector display of the only known functioning PDP-1 in the world.
Boing Boing called them magnificent portraits of the machines' pretty faces and equally beautiful guts, a stunning series of 'glamour shots' for nerds.
It's the only way to satisfy my insatiable hardware lust, short of the world's largest bank account.
posts.key_insight: The physical machines that enable programming are worthy of the same passion and appreciation that programmers give to their code.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the importance of grid systems in web design, highlighting a SXSW presentation by the New York Times design director. The post uses Yahoo's homepage as a case study, showing how a redesign can demonstrate grid principles without necessarily improving the original. Atwood emphasizes that learning to 'see' grids is the first step to mastering layout design. He advocates for grids as a starting point for UI design while cautioning against being enslaved by them. The key distinction between competent and great layouts lies in knowing when to follow grid constraints and when to break them.
key_points: Grids are fundamental to web design, constrained by factors like ad sizes, display dimensions, and browser chrome
Learning to visually identify grids in existing designs is the first step to mastering grid-based layout
Basic CSS grid layouts are well-documented and provide accessible starting points for beginners
The difference between competent and great design lies in using grids flexibly rather than rigidly
Constraints can be liberating when approached with the right mindset
money_quotes: Learning how to effectively utilize grids – without becoming a slave to them – can make the difference between a competent layout and a great layout.
Learning to see the grids is the first step.
When you're not sure where to begin with your UI design, start by building a grid.
Grids don't have to be a straitjacket. Approached with the proper mindset, they can be liberating.
Embracing the constraints of a grid in your design – and knowing when to break those constraints – is a crucial skill for designers, both neophyte and expert.
posts.key_insight: Mastering grid-based design requires both embracing constraints as a foundation and developing the judgment to know when breaking them serves the design better.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that code itself is a liability, not an asset, and that developers should strive to write as little of it as possible. Building on Rich Skrenta's assertion that 'code is our enemy,' Atwood reframes the problem: the real enemy isn't the code but the developers who write too much of it. He advocates for brevity as the starting point for all programming decisions, arguing that every line of code represents future debugging, maintenance, and cognitive load. The post uses a simple example (String.Empty vs "") to illustrate how developers often prefer verbosity for misguided reasons. Atwood concludes with a paradox: truly loving code means writing as little of it as possible.
key_points: Code is a liability that rots, requires maintenance, hides bugs, and increases organizational complexity
Developers are their own worst enemy because they love writing code too much
Programming involves constant trade-offs between brevity, features, speed, robustness, and time spent
The correct approach is to start with brevity and only increase other dimensions when testing reveals the need
Reducing code volume directly reduces the cognitive load required to understand a program
money_quotes: As a software developer, you are your own worst enemy. The sooner you realize that, the better off you'll be.
The best code is no code at all.
Every new line of code you willingly bring into the world is code that has to be debugged, code that has to be read and understood, code that has to be supported.
Every time you write new code, you should do so reluctantly, under duress, because you completely exhausted all your other options.
If you love writing code – really, truly love to write code – you'll love it enough to write as little of it as possible.
Code is only our enemy because there are so many of us programmers writing so damn much of it.
We never met a problem we couldn't solve with some duct tape, a jury-rigged coat hanger, and a pinch of code.
posts.key_insight: The measure of a great programmer is not how much code they can write, but how little code they need to solve a problem.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the historic 2007 joint interview between Bill Gates and Steve Jobs at the D5 conference, their first shared stage appearance since 1983. He highlights the mutual respect and even friendship that has developed between these longtime rivals after decades in the industry. The post features poignant quotes from both men about aging in tech and shared memories. Atwood connects their journey to his own experience growing up with computers, arguing that the modern microcomputer exists largely because of both men's influence.
key_points: Gates and Jobs hadn't appeared on stage together since 1983's 'Macintosh Dating Game' until this 2007 interview
Both tech pioneers expressed genuine mutual respect and reflected on being the 'oldest guys in the room' after starting as the youngest
Jobs quoted a Beatles lyric about having 'memories longer than the road that stretches out ahead' to capture their shared history
Atwood frames their history as inseparable from the personal computing revolution that shaped his own life and career
The interview revealed a surprisingly warm relationship between two figures often portrayed as bitter rivals
money_quotes: Finally seeing these two computer industry giants on stage interacting with each other was fascinating and at times even a little touching.
The last time these two guys were on stage together was in 1983 for the 'Macintosh Dating Game.'
It's clear that these two long-term rivals have a lot of respect for each other. They might even be friends.
Their history feels like our history, my history.
The microcomputer as we know it today is largely due to the influence of both Jobs and Gates.
These two guys have been my role models since the day I first booted a computer.
posts.key_insight: The shared history of Gates and Jobs isn't just corporate rivalry—it's the foundational story of personal computing that shaped an entire generation of developers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to critics who dismissed background compilation as a feature only for beginners and hacks. He argues that just as background spell checking has become universally beloved and enabled by default, background compilation offers the same unobtrusive benefits for catching common coding errors. Atwood rejects the notion that superior developers should eschew helpful tools, pointing out that for every Linus Torvalds there are ten thousand ordinary programmers who benefit from better tooling. He confesses his own 'neanderthal' blog writing setup as evidence that refusing better tools isn't noble—it's counterproductive. The post concludes that while these tools aren't substitutes for critical thinking, intentionally avoiding them is both patronizing and bad business.
key_points: Background compilation and background spell checking serve the same purpose: unobtrusive, automatic error detection that users can ignore or act upon
The 'infinite monkey software development model' is reality—most programmers aren't Linus Torvalds, and tools that help average developers represent real advancement
Deliberately avoiding helpful tools to prove expertise is counterproductive and often just bad business
Tools should never have the last say, but that's an argument for understanding limitations, not abandoning tools entirely
Background spell checking is beloved enough to be a default feature in major software—background compilation deserves the same acceptance
money_quotes: The infinite monkey software development model is what we're stuck with.
For every one Linus Torvalds, there are ten thousand programmers who aren't Linus Torvalds.
I see very little difference between background spell checking and background compilation.
Using the most primitive of tools to compose these blog posts isn't a glorious, sweeping validation of my expertise as a writer. If anything, it's an indication of my idiocy.
Any reasonably competent person knows that tools are never substitutes for critical thinking about what you're writing or coding.
Intentionally choosing not to use better tools because you're afraid they will become a crutch is, at best, cruel and patronizing.
posts.key_insight: Rejecting helpful development tools to prove your expertise isn't a sign of mastery—it's an unwillingness to let better tools help you be better at your craft.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that requiring users to log in before experiencing a website's value is a critical mistake that drives away the vast majority of potential users. He uses Facebook's API documentation as an example of this anti-pattern, noting that login barriers are not mere friction but 'brick walls' that stop users cold. The post highlights alternative approaches like Geni's streamlined onboarding and the anonymous cookie-based accounts used by Netvibes and Pageflakes. These services let users experience full functionality before asking them to commit to an account, allowing relationships to develop naturally over time.
key_points: Login barriers cause 99% of users to simply leave rather than sign up, making them far worse than ordinary UI friction
Users cannot evaluate whether a site is worth joining without first seeing what's behind the login wall
Anonymous cookie-based accounts allow users to experience full site functionality before committing to registration
Smart sites let the relationship develop naturally, waiting until users have genuine motivation to create an account
Using email as user ID eliminates the extra friction of choosing a username during signup
money_quotes: When presented with the login barrier, I did what 99% of all the people who encounter a login barrier do: I turned back.
Login barriers are far worse than friction – they're a brick wall.
Without sneaking behind the barrier, if only for a moment, how can the user possibly know if your site is worth the hassle of signing up?
You can do much, much better than the abysmal login barrier status quo.
The deep principle at work is that a site doesn't need to rush to secure a relationship with a visitor.
By removing the login barrier and blurring the line between anonymous users and named users, you're likely to gain a lot more of the latter.
posts.key_insight: The best way to convert anonymous visitors into registered users is to let them experience your product's full value first, allowing the relationship to develop naturally rather than demanding commitment upfront.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that asking users what they want is fundamentally flawed because people's stated preferences diverge dramatically from their actual behavior. He uses research showing consumers choose feature-rich products but become frustrated using them—the 'all-you-can-eat buffet problem.' The post advocates for observational testing over surveys, citing Amazon's A/B testing culture and Microsoft's telemetry program as models. Atwood concludes that whether through lo-fi usability tests or automated data collection, directly observing user behavior yields insights that questionnaires and expert intuition cannot match.
key_points: Users consistently overestimate how many features they want, then suffer 'feature fatigue' when actually using complex products
Asking users what they want is nearly useless for usability—you must observe actual behavior
A/B testing and telemetry allow 'silent observation' at scale, as demonstrated by Amazon's cart recommendations winning despite executive opposition
Cultural context matters: Korean consumers prefer complexity as a status symbol, showing that simplicity isn't universally valued
Even internal experts and intuition are unreliable—Amazon's SVP was wrong, and Microsoft's 'gurus' couldn't predict actual feature usage
money_quotes: What users say they will do, and what they actually do, are often two very different things.
You have to observe what users actually do.
It's impossible to see that you're creating a Frankenstein's monster of a product – until you attempt to use it.
Consumers' success or failure interacting with the prototypes tells us more than a thousand surveys, questionnaires, or focus groups ever could.
The culture of experimentation at Amazon, where data trumps intuition, and a system that made running experiments easy, allowed Amazon to innovate quickly and effectively.
I love users. Some of my best friends are users. But like all of us humans, they're unreliable at best.
In order to move beyond usability guesswork, there's no substitute for observing customers using your product.
Don't ask – observe.
posts.key_insight: User research based on what people say they want is fundamentally misleading; only by observing actual behavior—through usability testing or telemetry—can you understand how your product is really used.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the science behind how users navigate the web, drawing on research that compares information-seeking behavior to animal foraging. He traces the concept from Marcia Bates' 1989 'berrypicking model' through Pirolli and Card's 'information foraging' theory, which explains that users hunt for information the same way animals hunt for food. The core insight is that users follow 'information scent' and will instantly abandon pages that don't signal relevance. Atwood argues this behavior stems from basic human laziness—people want maximum benefit for minimum effort. The practical implication is stark: users form impressions in milliseconds and abandon sites within two minutes if they don't find value.
key_points: Users don't execute single queries but engage in iterative 'berrypicking'—moving fluidly between search and browse, following citations, subjects, and authors
Information foraging theory draws direct parallels between animal hunting strategies and human information-seeking: wolves hunt like search engines, spiders trap like filters
Users follow 'information scent'—they must sense they're 'getting warmer' or they'll abandon the hunt immediately
The underlying principle is human laziness: people seek maximum benefit for minimum effort, making them ruthless evaluators of content
Better search engines actually decrease time spent on individual sites because users can more easily find alternatives
money_quotes: Users will click the back button nearly instantly when they don't catch a whiff of the right information from the current page.
In any case, people like to get maximum benefit for minimum effort.
Informavores are unforgiving.
People left the sites they found useless within less than two minutes.
You better hope the information scent is strong on your page, because Informavores' fingers are always hovering over the back button.
So if you've ever wondered why users behave like animals online, now you know.
posts.key_insight: Users are informavores who hunt for content with the same ruthless efficiency as animals foraging for food—if your page doesn't immediately signal value, they're gone in seconds.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts discovering that his wife preferred Google Maps over the desktop application Microsoft Streets and Trips he had championed for years. Testing revealed that the web app loaded three times faster and required dramatically fewer steps for common tasks like getting directions. He observes that web applications have become faster, simpler, and more feature-rich than their desktop counterparts, which remain stuck in outdated UI conventions. The post concludes that desktop mapping applications are effectively dead except for niche offline use cases.
key_points: Google Maps loads in 3 seconds versus 9 seconds for Streets and Trips on a fast desktop PC
Getting directions requires 8 steps in Streets and Trips but only 2 steps in Google Maps
Web applications have turned browser limitations into strengths by offering online-enabled features like satellite view and real-time traffic
Desktop applications remain stuck in circa-1999 UI conventions of toolbars, menus, and right-clicking while web apps evolve rapidly
Web apps offer continuous updates and are free or ad-subsidized, eliminating yearly upgrade cycles
money_quotes: Because it's faster.
It's a mixed-up, topsy-turvy world we live in when web mapping applications are now faster and more convenient to use than their desktop equivalents.
All the innovation in user interface seems to be taking place on the web, and desktop applications just aren't keeping up.

Web applications are evolving online at a frenetic pace, while most desktop applications are mired in circa-1999 desktop user interface conventions, plopping out yearly releases with barely noticeable new features.
Google Maps turns all those browser-based application weaknesses into strengths.
It's hard not to look at the facts and conclude that desktop applications – at least desktop mapping applications – are dead.
posts.key_insight: The constraints of web applications have forced innovation in user experience that desktop applications, comfortable with their power advantage, have failed to pursue.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the visible differences between Apple's and Microsoft's font rendering after testing Safari 3 beta on Windows Vista. He provides side-by-side comparisons showing that Apple's approach produces noticeably blurrier text with lower contrast than Windows ClearType. While acknowledging there's no single 'best' way to render fonts, he expresses surprise that Apple, known for design excellence, ships what he considers inferior text rendering on LCD displays. The post sparked enough discussion that he later wrote a followup explaining the philosophical difference: whether to respect the pixel grid or prioritize font shape fidelity.
key_points: Safari 3 beta's font rendering on Windows appears blurrier with lower contrast compared to IE7's ClearType
Both Apple and Microsoft use RGB subpixel anti-aliasing, but with different contrast/sharpness strategies
Font rendering quality depends on display type, specific fonts, and personal preference
Apple's approach is surprising given their reputation for attention to graphic design details
The fundamental difference is philosophical: respecting pixel grid vs. preserving original font shapes
money_quotes: OS X antialiasing, especially, it seems, with the monospaced fonts, just isn't as good as Windows ClearType.
It looks like they've skewed the contrast of the fonts to an absurdly low level.
There really is no single best way to render fonts; results depend on your display, the particular font you're using, and many other factors.
This is exactly the kind of graphic designer-ish detail I'd expect Cupertino to get right, so it's all the more surprising to me that they apparently haven't.
It looks like neither Apple or Microsoft is wrong; it's a question of whether you respect the pixel grid.
posts.key_insight: The Apple vs. Microsoft font rendering debate isn't about one being wrong, but about a fundamental design philosophy choice: whether to preserve typographic accuracy or optimize for pixel-grid sharpness on LCD displays.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the philosophical difference between Apple and Microsoft's font rendering approaches: Apple prioritizes preserving typeface design even at the cost of blurriness, while Microsoft snaps fonts to the pixel grid for clarity. Citing designer Dave Shea, Atwood argues that Microsoft's ClearType approach better serves current low-DPI displays, as it automates the same per-pixel adjustments designers make manually. He acknowledges that high-resolution displays will eventually make this debate moot, but criticizes Apple for sacrificing present readability for a future that hasn't arrived. The post frames this as a pragmatic choice between competing values rather than a clear right or wrong answer.
key_points: Apple's font rendering preserves typeface design at the cost of blurriness; Microsoft's ClearType prioritizes pixel grid alignment for readability
Professional designers already manually snap type to the pixel grid in tools like Photoshop, which is exactly what ClearType automates
Research supports that snapping fonts to the pixel grid improves reading accuracy, not just aesthetics
High-resolution displays (200+ DPI) will eventually make this debate irrelevant, but the slow pace of display improvements means that day is far off
Typography is genuinely complex enough to justify being called 'computer science'
money_quotes: What do you respect more: the pixel grid, or the font designer?
I don't understand why Apple is asking us to sacrifice the present at the altar of the future.
Can't we have hinting at low resolutions, and accuracy at high resolutions, too?
Typography, if you haven't figured this out by now, is really complicated. It's one of the few areas of 'computer science' that actually justifies the title.

Snapping fonts to a pixel grid may very well be irrelevant when everyone is luxuriating in the glow of their 200 DPI monitors. Until that glorious day arrives, respecting the pixel grid certainly makes text a lot more readable for those of us stuck in the here and now.

Ten years ago I was using a single 17" 1024x768 display; now I'm using three 20" 1600x1200 displays. So you'll forgive me if I'm not overly optimistic about this theoretical jump from 100 DPI to 200 DPI.
posts.key_insight: When designing for current constraints, pragmatic solutions that work well today should take precedence over idealistic approaches optimized for a future that may be slow to arrive.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood investigates Dave Shea's prediction that 200 DPI displays will become standard 'in a few years,' examining the historical progression of display resolution from 1984 to 2007. His research reveals that display DPI has only improved by a factor of 1.6 over twenty years, from around 60-72 DPI to roughly 100 DPI. While high-resolution displays exist, they remain prohibitively expensive ($8,000-$20,000) and unavailable through mainstream consumer channels. The only place higher pixel densities appear is in laptops, where physical size constraints force manufacturers to pack more pixels into fixed screen sizes. Atwood concludes that reaching 200 DPI standard displays is unlikely even within ten years, contradicting the optimistic predictions of display technology advancement.
key_points: Display resolution has improved by less than a factor of two in twenty years, making it one of the slowest-advancing computer hardware categories
High-resolution displays (200 DPI) existed in 2007 but cost $8,000-$20,000, placing them far outside consumer reach
Consumer behavior favoring 'bigger is better' works against pixel density improvements, as manufacturers prioritize larger screens over higher DPI
Laptops are the only segment showing meaningful DPI increases because the fixed form factor prevents screens from getting physically larger
Even at 147 DPI (the highest laptop option available), displays were only halfway to the 200 DPI target
money_quotes: The resolution of computer displays has increased by less than a factor of two over the last twenty years.
I can't think of any other piece of computer hardware that has improved so little since 1984.
At consumer prices and through consumer outlets, high resolution displays simply don't exist.
The notebook form factor physically precludes the display from getting any larger.
If notebook vendors could somehow fit a folding 30" LCD panel into a laptop, they absolutely would.
Short of some kind of miraculous technological breakthrough, I can't see computer displays reaching 200 DPI in 'a few years.'
All the evidence of history – not to mention typical consumer 'bigger is better' behavior – is overwhelming.
posts.key_insight: Consumer market forces favoring larger screens over higher pixel density, combined with the prohibitive cost of high-DPI manufacturing, have caused display resolution to stagnate as one of the slowest-improving areas in computer hardware.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that as applications become more feature-rich, traditional navigation methods like menus and toolbars fail to scale, making incremental search essential for finding features. He uses Office 2007's ribbon as an example of UI evolution, noting that even this major improvement has limitations when users can't find commands not displayed in the ribbon. Atwood highlights Microsoft's experimental 'Scout' feature and similar implementations in tools like Expression Blend as evidence that incremental feature search is becoming necessary. He concludes that while simple applications can rely on traditional navigation, complex applications need search-based discovery to remain usable.
key_points: Menus and toolbars become unwieldy as applications grow - a flat menu with 8 commands works well, but hierarchical menus with 35+ commands become disasters
Office 2007's ribbon was a major UI innovation but still can't surface all features, leaving users unable to find commands they know exist
Incremental search for application features is becoming as important as incremental search within documents
Microsoft's experimental 'Scout' add-in for Office 2007 demonstrated the value of searchable feature discovery but was killed by internal politics
The pattern of searchable interfaces is spreading across Microsoft products including Vista's start menu and Expression design tools
money_quotes: As applications get larger and more complicated, incremental search is also useful for navigating the sea of features that modern applications offer.

There's a point beyond which menus and toolbars cease to scale well. A flat menu with 8 well-organized commands on it works just great; a three-level hierarchical menu containing 35 loosely-related commands can be a bit of a disaster.
How do you find what isn't in the ribbon?
Applications get more feature-rich over time, and navigation methods have to evolve to keep up.
It's faster to incrementally search for the feature we need.
posts.key_insight: As software complexity inevitably grows, incremental search transforms from a nice-to-have text navigation feature into an essential method for users to discover and access application functionality.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents his hands-on experience cleaning up a severe spyware infestation on his Windows XP machine, which he contracted by browsing with an outdated browser to download no-cd patches for games. He sets up a honeypot virtual machine to identify the culprit (GameCopyWorld), then walks through a detailed technical process using Process Explorer and AutoRuns to systematically eliminate the malware. The post serves as both a cautionary tale about browser security and a practical guide for removing stubborn spyware that hooks into system processes like Winlogon and Explorer.
key_points: Browsing with an outdated browser (even briefly) can result in immediate, devastating spyware infection just from visiting a website—no downloads required
A NAT router provides passive protection against infections, but active browsing with unpatched browsers remains extremely dangerous
Spyware removal requires stopping running processes, eliminating autorun entries, and sometimes killing individual threads attached to system processes like Winlogon
Sysinternals tools (Process Explorer and AutoRuns) are essential for identifying and removing malware that standard Windows tools cannot handle
Legitimate-looking websites recommended in community forums can still serve drive-by malware infections to vulnerable browsers
money_quotes: Intentionally choosing to browse the web with a three year old browser, as I did, is an incredibly dangerous thing to do.
Just from visiting the web pages, even if you don't download a single thing – the infestation is nearly immediate and completely devastating.
There should be a special circle of hell reserved for companies who make money doing this to people.
This is my machine, and I'll be damned if I will stand for any slimy, unwanted malware, adware, or spyware that takes up residence on it.
Unless it has a Company Name you recognize, it's highly likely to be a rogue application and should be terminated.
I have a new degree of sympathy for the poor users who become the unfortunate victims of spyware infestations.
We're smarter than the malware vendors.
Any reputable vendor will have no problem attaching their name to their work.
posts.key_insight: Spyware infestations are devastating but defeatable with the right tools and systematic approach—though prevention through keeping browsers updated is far easier than cure.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits Steve McConnell's list of classic software development mistakes, using the Gilligan's Island metaphor to illustrate how projects repeatedly fall into the same traps without escaping. He presents a case study showing how developers claim to be '99% done' while hiding significant remaining work, exemplifying the common self-deception in project estimates. The post catalogs 36 classic mistakes across people, process, product, and technology categories, along with newer additions like excessive multi-tasking and confusing estimates with targets. Atwood argues that experienced developers differ from inexperienced ones primarily in their ability to recognize when they're making mistakes, and that actively scanning for these classic errors is essential to project success.
key_points: The '99% done' phenomenon hides substantial remaining work, as the last 10% often takes as long as the first 90%
Classic mistakes are 'classic' because they're seductive - you must actively recognize when you're falling into them
Success in software projects depends less on avoiding a few wrong things than on doing almost everything right
The difference between experienced and inexperienced developers is recognizing mistakes as they happen
Survey data shows overly optimistic schedules (77%) and unrealistic expectations (73%) are the most common mistakes
money_quotes: The first ninety percent of the task takes ninety percent of the time, and the last ten percent takes the other ninety percent.
Every week there's some new, crazy scheme to escape the island, but at the end of the episode, the castaways always end up stuck on the island for yet another week.
Classic mistakes are classic because they're so seductive.
Actually succeeding in a software project depends a whole lot less on not doing a few things wrong but on doing almost everything right.
I've increasingly come to believe the only difference between experienced and inexperienced software developers is that the experienced ones realize when they're making mistakes.

If you're not actively scanning through the list of Classic Software Development Mistakes as you run your software project, you have no idea how likely it is you're making one of these mistakes right now.
Making mistakes is inevitable, but repeating the same ones over and over doesn't have to be. You should endeavor to make all-new, spectacular, never-seen-before mistakes.
posts.key_insight: The key to escaping perpetual project failure isn't avoiding all mistakes, but developing the self-awareness to recognize classic traps as you're falling into them.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues against solo software development, contending that while programmers may be drawn to solitary work with computers, working alone on software projects is ultimately counterproductive. He cites a cautionary tale about the dangers of being the sole developer: no code reviews, no process enforcement, no one to catch mistakes or share the workload. Despite the allure of complete control, solo development is 'debilitating' rather than empowering. Atwood reflects on his own introversion and early computing experiences, but concludes that modern software development is inherently collaborative, and programmers should actively seek out peers to learn from and work alongside.
key_points: Solo development eliminates accountability structures like code review, coding standards enforcement, and estimate verification
Working alone offers the illusion of control but actually leaves developers more vulnerable and less capable
The internet has transformed programming from a solitary activity into one that requires constant connection to collective knowledge
Programmers should deliberately seek out smart colleagues and aim to be 'the dumbest guy in the room' to accelerate learning
Software development is far more social than the stereotypical introvert programmer realizes
money_quotes: There is no process in a team of one.
In a vacuum, no one can hear you scream.

Working alone means complete control over a software project, wielding ultimate power over every decision. But working on a software project all by yourself, instead of being empowering, is paradoxically debilitating.
It's a shifting mirage that offers the tantalizing promise of relief, while somehow leaving you thirstier and weaker than you started.
No one ever said on their deathbed, 'Gee, I wish I had spent more time alone with my computer.'
What good are nifty coding tricks if you can't show them off to anyone?
If you're serious about programming, you should demand to work with your peers.
Endeavor to be the dumbest guy in the room, and you'll quickly discover that software development is a far more social activity than most people realize.
posts.key_insight: The freedom of solo development is a trap—real growth and sustainable software work require the accountability, mentorship, and collaboration that only come from working with other programmers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the Windows security epidemic stems from Microsoft's decision to make Administrator the default user account, dating back to Windows NT 3.0. He demonstrates how a completely unpatched Windows XP machine can be infected through drive-by browser exploits simply by visiting a webpage, but proves this attack is completely ineffective when logged in as a standard user. Atwood criticizes Microsoft for failing to make standard user accounts the default in Windows Vista, instead opting for the compromise of UAC prompts. He contends that setting up non-technical users as standard users is the single most important security measure for Windows machines.
key_points: Drive-by malware infections can occur simply by visiting a webpage with an unpatched browser, without clicking anything or downloading files
Running as a standard (non-administrator) user completely prevented the same infection that devastated an administrator account
Microsoft's original sin was making Administrator the default account type in Windows NT 3.0, a decision they perpetuated through Vista
The Windows software ecosystem has become so compromised that users cannot distinguish legitimate security software from the malware it claims to remove
Backwards compatibility concerns have led Microsoft to prioritize convenience over security, even when the security costs are enormous
money_quotes: When the only viable solution to sickness is to kill the patient, you have a problem of epidemic proportions.
It's nearly impossible to tell the good guys from the bad guys.
Choosing not to run as an Administrator is easily the single most important security tip for a Windows machine.
I think every technical computer user should have this experience, so they can see first hand, on their own machine, the profound evil that we're up against.
They'd probably have to buy a new computer.
I guess that's yet another thing we can sacrifice at the dark altar of backwards compatibility.
Microsoft didn't have the guts to make standard users the default – as they absolutely should have – in Windows Vista.
posts.key_insight: The Windows security crisis is largely self-inflicted by Microsoft's decades-long refusal to default users to non-administrator accounts, a simple change that would prevent most drive-by malware infections.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the absurdity of End User License Agreements (EULAs), arguing that no one actually reads them because they're impenetrable legalese that users must accept to use software. He highlights the EFF's findings about problematic EULA clauses that restrict free speech, enable monitoring, forbid reverse-engineering, and waive consumer protections. The post questions whether these click-wrap and shrink-wrap licenses are even legally enforceable, ultimately suggesting that ignoring EULAs entirely may be the most rational response to an irrational system.
key_points: EULAs are essentially unreadable contracts that users blindly accept, potentially agreeing to invasive terms like monitoring or liability waivers
Common EULA problems include bans on public criticism, forced automatic updates that compromise privacy, prohibitions on reverse-engineering, and disclaimers of liability for faulty software
The phrase 'READ CAREFULLY' in caps effectively means 'IGNORE THIS' because the terms are non-negotiable and often unreasonable
Both click-wrap (clicking accept) and shrink-wrap (opening packaging) licenses have questionable legal enforceability
The current EULA system creates an impossible situation where reading every agreement would be impractical, yet not reading them means accepting potentially harmful terms
money_quotes: For all I know, the EULA could specify that the software is going to install a keylogger, steal all my passwords and financial information, send incriminating emails threatening the president, format my hard drive, and then sleep with my wife.
I'm an end user, and I don't recall anything good ever coming from clicking that 'I accept' option.
The phrase means, 'IGNORE THIS.' That's because the small print is unchangeable and outrageous.
Why read the 'agreement' if you know that: No sane person would agree to its text, and Even if you disagree, no one will negotiate a better agreement with you?
Given the insanity of our current predicament, not reading the EULA could very well be the most rational course of action.
It's just another meaningless hoop I have to jump through before I can actually use the software.
posts.key_insight: The EULA system is fundamentally broken because it demands users accept non-negotiable, unreadable contracts, making willful ignorance paradoxically the most rational user behavior.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces his decision to add advertising to Coding Horror after years of resistance, but frames it as an ethical compromise rather than a sellout. He acknowledges the legitimate criticisms of blog advertising—loss of credibility, design degradation, and professionalism concerns—but accepts that advertising has become the inevitable online revenue model. The post outlines strategies for responsible advertising: being selective with ad placement, respecting readers through time-delayed ads and full RSS feeds, and preferring direct sponsor relationships over generic ad networks. His key justification is committing to donate $5,000 of ad revenue to .NET open source projects, with Microsoft matching the contribution.
key_points: Advertising has won so completely online that alternative revenue models are essentially non-viable for most websites
Direct sponsor relationships give bloggers maximum control over what appears on their site, unlike automated ad networks
Respecting readers means being selective: use heat maps for placement, delay ads on new content, keep RSS feeds ad-free
The hierarchy of blog monetization places Google AdSense at the bottom and direct fixed sponsors at the top
Giving back to the community through ad revenue donations transforms advertising from a necessary evil into a positive contribution
money_quotes: Kids are so used to being blasted with ads that they don't notice them.
Advertising sucks. But you know what else sucks? When people point out how stupid you are to throw away five figures worth of potential income.
Is it possible to advertise responsibly, with respect for your audience – and yourself?
Don't use the Ronco spray-on advertising approach – e.g., indiscriminately placing low-value Google AdSense units in every nook and cranny of your page.
It's an open secret amongst bloggers that the blog comments are often better than the original blog post, and it's because the community collectively knows far more than you or I will ever know.
Giving back to the community is what makes the pain of advertising worthwhile.
posts.key_insight: The ethical path to blog monetization isn't avoiding advertising entirely, but rather taking maximum control over ad placement, respecting your readers' experience, and channeling the revenue back into the community that made your platform possible.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces a $10,000 donation initiative to support open source .NET projects, with Microsoft matching his $5,000 contribution. He argues that open source projects have been treated as second-class citizens in the Microsoft ecosystem despite contributing enormously to the .NET community. Atwood cites several prominent developers who have made similar observations about Microsoft's failure to support the open source projects that help sell their platform. The post proposes splitting funds between established projects ($2,500 each) and up-and-coming ones ($500 each), determined by popular vote, with hopes of making this an annual event.
key_points: Open source projects are systematically undervalued in the Microsoft ecosystem despite being critical to .NET developers' productivity
Microsoft benefits financially from open source projects that drive adoption of their paid tools, yet doesn't complete the circle by supporting those projects
The .NET open source community is significantly smaller than Java's, partly due to Microsoft's failure to foster it
Supporting open source should be seen as strategic necessity for Microsoft's survival as a development tools vendor
Both established projects and emerging ones deserve funding, but through different tiers
money_quotes: Open source projects are treated as second-class citizens in the Microsoft ecosystem.
I think it must change if Microsoft wants to survive as a vendor of development tools.
Microsoft isn't doing anything to foster a healthy OSS community around .NET.

Those open source projects help support us. In turn, my company helps support Microsoft through the purchase of licenses and MSDN subscriptions. Yet Microsoft does not complete the circle by financially supporting any of those open source projects.
Open source software is at its best when you aren't obligated to do anything at all other than use it.
It's time for us to band together and do something about it.
posts.key_insight: When corporations profit from open source ecosystems without reinvesting in them, the community must organize to fill the gap and pressure those corporations to participate.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts a formative experience meeting an MIT-trained engineer who admitted he hadn't used anything from his coursework in his actual job. This initially shocking revelation led Atwood to understand that education's true value lies not in specific knowledge but in developing the ability to learn. He uses Wikipedia as an example of how modern tools can teach research and critical thinking skills far better than passive textbook reading. The post concludes that this meta-skill of learning how to learn is especially critical for software developers, whose field reinvents itself every decade.
key_points: The value of formal education isn't the specific material learned but developing the capacity to learn independently
Wikipedia represents an opportunity to teach students how knowledge is produced and verified rather than just consumed
Participatory, hands-on learning outperforms passive textbook reading
Software development requires continuous learning as the field reinvents itself roughly every decade
Understanding 'why' something works has lasting value while 'how' becomes obsolete within about five years
money_quotes: I can't think of a single thing from my MIT classes I've used on the job.
The value of education isn't in the specific material you learn – it's in learning how to learn.
Why read textbooks when you can help write one?
There's no substitute for learning on the battlefield.
Programming is, almost by definition, continuously learning: your entire career will be one long, unbroken string of learning one new bit of technology after another.
Every ten years the software development field reinvents itself, and it's our job to keep up.
If you don't like learning new things, you will despise software engineering. It's all we do.
In our field, how only lasts about five years, but why is forever.
posts.key_insight: Software engineering demands mastering the skill of learning itself because specific technical knowledge becomes obsolete every few years, but the ability to acquire new knowledge remains valuable forever.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood advises readers against buying the first-generation iPhone despite being a fan of Apple's entry into the mobile market. Drawing from his experience with a Samsung Blackjack smartphone, he argues that the iPhone's lack of 3G connectivity is a critical flaw that will make internet browsing painfully slow over EDGE connections. He dismisses Apple's suggestion to rely on WiFi as unrealistic, noting that free public WiFi is increasingly rare and expensive. While acknowledging the iPhone's revolutionary potential, Atwood predicts Apple will release a significantly improved second-generation model within 12 months and urges patience.
key_points: The mobile phone market is a 'sad, pathetic wasteland' that desperately needs Apple's competitive pressure to stop making user-hostile products
Having mobile internet access is transformative - like having the sum of the world's knowledge in your palm, anywhere
3G provides near cable-modem speeds while EDGE is painfully slow (around 10 kb/sec), making the iPhone's EDGE-only connectivity a serious limitation
Reliance on WiFi is not a viable alternative since free public WiFi hotspots are increasingly rare and locked down
Based on Apple's iPod iteration cycle, a significantly improved iPhone 2.0 with 3G is likely within 12 months
money_quotes: The mobile phone market is a sad, pathetic wasteland in desperate need of improvement.
It's like having the sum of the entire world's knowledge in the palm of your hand, wherever you happen to be.
3G is darn near a cable modem experience.
EDGE data connections are unbelievably painful.
If you think reliance on WiFi is an advantage of the iPhone, check your reality distortion field.
The 3G cellular data network frees me from dependency on expensive, unreliable, hard to find WiFi hotspots.
Never, never has it been more true for any technology product: wait for version 2.0 before buying.
Not all buying decisions have to be rational.
posts.key_insight: Revolutionary hardware with crippled connectivity is a poor value proposition when history suggests a dramatically improved version will arrive within a year.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood traces his history with Alan Cooper's 'About Face' book across its multiple editions from 1995 to 2007. He notes how each edition improved upon the last, with additional co-authors diluting Cooper's natural bombast while adding broader perspectives. The third edition features better organization, more detailed coverage of Goal-Directed Design, and improved visual design concepts. Atwood enthusiastically recommends the latest edition as the definitive version of this interaction design classic.
key_points: Each new edition of About Face obsoletes the previous one, with the third being definitively the best
Adding co-authors improved the book by balancing Cooper's bombastic tendencies with broader perspectives
The 2007 edition reorganizes content into three parts: process/users, interaction principles, and interface principles
Design books face the same obsolescence cycle as software itself
Goal-Directed Design process coverage was significantly expanded in the third edition
money_quotes: Seems quaint now, if not borderline obsolete, but this was 12 whole years ago. That's almost a lifetime in computer dog-years.
I love Cooper, but he can be awfully bombastic at times. Having a second author dilutes Cooper's natural bombast and adds another viewpoint for a broader perspective.
Cooper is obsoleting his own books at a frantic pace; it's almost as bad as software.
You're about to read one of the best books ever written on interaction design. Enjoy.
