I envy the experience you're about to have. For the rest of us, time to pony up the upgrade fee. Again.
posts.key_insight: Classic technical books require continuous revision to remain relevant, and each iteration can genuinely improve upon the last through additional perspectives and updated practices.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues against joining private social networking platforms like LinkedIn and Facebook, drawing a parallel between Facebook in 2007 and AOL in the 1990s. He contends that the open internet already provides superior social networking capabilities through public services like blogs, Twitter, and Flickr. The post references Jason Kottke's critique equating Facebook to AOL's walled garden approach, which ultimately failed against the open web. Atwood concludes that closed platforms cannot compete long-term with the public internet and urges readers to invest their time in open, searchable, public platforms instead.
key_points: The internet itself is the best social networking tool, and public services (blogs, Twitter, Flickr) should be where we invest our time
Facebook's walled garden approach mirrors AOL's proprietary platform strategy from the 1990s, which ultimately failed
In competitive markets, open and messy beats closed and controlled in the long run
Private platforms like Facebook prevent participation from non-users and aren't indexed by search engines, limiting their reach and value
Collaboration should result in public artifacts rather than content trapped in private gardens
money_quotes: We already have the world's best public social networking tool right in front of us: it's called the internet.
No matter how wonderful your walled garden is, it can't compete with the public, open internet.
In competitive markets, open and messy trumps closed and controlled in the long run.
Facebook is an intranet for you and your friends that just happens to be accessible without a VPN.
Maybe we shouldn't be so excited about the web's future moving onto an intranet.
Don't ask me to enter your private walled garden. There's no future in it.
posts.key_insight: Private walled gardens on the internet, no matter how popular, will always lose to the open web because openness enables universal participation and discovery.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while the Gang of Four's Design Patterns book is worth reading for shared vocabulary, developers should approach it critically. He raises two concerns: design patterns add complexity that simpler solutions might avoid, and frequent pattern usage may indicate language deficiencies rather than good engineering. Drawing on Mark Dominus's work, Atwood suggests the original inspiration—Christopher Alexander's 'A Pattern Language'—offers deeper insights about distributing design responsibility while maintaining coherence. The GoF book, he argues, reduced Alexander's thoughtful approach to mechanical code generation templates.
key_points: Design patterns are a form of complexity, and developers should exhaust simpler solutions before reaching for them
Repeatedly writing boilerplate pattern code suggests your programming language has fundamental limitations
The Gang of Four book obscures Christopher Alexander's original insight about distributing design responsibility through hierarchies while maintaining harmony
Alexander's architectural patterns addressed how to delegate design decisions without creating chaos—a problem equally relevant to software systems
Developers should read Alexander's 'A Pattern Language' to understand the ideas that patterns were meant to embody, not just memorize recipes
money_quotes: Design patterns are a form of complexity. As with all complexity, I'd rather see developers focus on simpler solutions before going straight to a complex recipe of design patterns.

If you find yourself frequently writing a bunch of boilerplate design pattern code to deal with a 'recurring design problem,' that's not good engineering – it's a sign that your language is fundamentally broken.
The 'Design Patterns' solution is to turn the programmer into a fancy macro processor.

How can you distribute responsibility for design through all levels of a large hierarchy, while still maintaining consistency and harmony of overall design? This is the architectural design problem Alexander is trying to solve, but it's also a fundamental problem of computer systems development.
Alexander's idea is a really good one, one that programmers might find useful. But very few programmers are going to find out about it, because they think they already know what it is.
Ideas are more important than code.
posts.key_insight: The original concept of design patterns was about distributing design responsibility while maintaining coherence—an idea more valuable than the mechanical code templates the GoF book reduced it to.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the fatigue that sets in after decades of chasing technology trends, questioning whether our constant digital immersion truly improves our lives. He draws on voices like Rick Strahl and Cliff Stoll to argue that technologists should periodically step back and critically evaluate technology's role rather than accepting it unquestioningly. While still believing computers change the world for the better, Atwood advocates for healthy skepticism and moderation, noting that investing in ourselves and maintaining analog experiences is essential. The post acknowledges the irony of a dedicated technologist preaching moderation while admitting he spends nearly all waking hours at a computer.
key_points: After twenty years of riding technology waves, developers often experience fatigue and a sense of repetition with diminishing returns
For every successful technology like TiVo or iPod, there are hundreds of failures, making it devilishly hard to predict what will matter in ten years
The 'always on, always connected' culture erodes boundaries between work and life, with people bragging about having no life outside work
Cliff Stoll's 1996 criticism that the internet lacks quality filters and editors because free publishing incentivizes low-value content remains partially prescient
Technologists have a responsibility to publicly debate where technology is heading and ask difficult questions rather than blindly following cyber-gurus
money_quotes: Riding the waves of technology in the computer industry is exhilarating when you're twenty, but there's a certain emptiness that begins to creep in around the edges by the time you're forty.

To us, technology is its own reward. But sometimes it's healthy, even for us technologists, to push back and ask hard questions about whether a particular technology is making our lives better – or worse.
People found entire careers on the shifting sands of technology, often on technologies that become utterly obsolete.
The dot-com bust was the best thing that ever happened to me up to that point. It caused me to recognize that people, not machines, are my passion.
It's our responsibility as citizens, as technologists, to debate where this stuff is likely to go and to ask difficult questions.
We forget to take time out to be analog for a little while.
Moderation in all things, including moderation.
posts.key_insight: Technologists should cultivate healthy skepticism about the technologies they embrace, periodically stepping off the digital treadmill to question whether their tools are genuinely improving their lives or just perpetuating an exhausting cycle.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood advocates for project postmortems as essential learning tools, using game development as a prime example. He highlights Game Developer Magazine's '5 wrongs, 5 rights' framework as an effective template for conducting retrospectives. The post emphasizes that postmortems should be given high priority—significant enough to be cover stories. Atwood argues that game development postmortems are particularly valuable because the industry's pressure-cooker environment surfaces every possible project pathology, making lessons transferable to all software projects.
key_points: The '5 wrongs, 5 rights' framework provides a balanced structure for honest project retrospectives
Postmortems should be treated as high-priority activities, not afterthoughts
Game development serves as an extreme case study because it combines unclear goals ('fun') with intense deadlines
Learning from others' failures is even more valuable than learning from your own
Honest assessment of failures is more valuable than sugar-coated retrospectives—publications reject postmortems that aren't candid
money_quotes: If you're not learning from your mistakes, or even better, learning from other people's mistakes, then your next project will have a rocky future at best.
Game development is such a pressure cooker.
It's incredibly challenging software engineering, with unclear goals (e.g., 'fun') under intense deadlines.
Every project pathology you're likely to see on your software project has probably already materialized on one or more of these games.
Postmortems that accentuate the 'what went right' and sugar coat the 'what went wrong' sections are dismissed by readers.
It's instructive to soak in the successes as well as gawk at the trainwrecks.
posts.key_insight: Game development postmortems are universally valuable because the industry's extreme pressure surfaces every project pathology that can afflict software development.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores image resizing algorithms beyond the standard bicubic and bilinear options, testing Genuine Fractals' fractal-based approach against traditional methods. He establishes practical guidelines: use bicubic for shrinking (sharpening effect) and bilinear for enlarging (smoothing effect). His 500% enlargement test shows fractal algorithms producing superior results with sharper edges and fewer artifacts, though with watercolor-like tradeoffs. The core message is pragmatic: reducing images is safe and straightforward, but enlarging is inherently risky since interpolated pixels can never match real ones. When forced to enlarge, understanding algorithm tradeoffs helps you choose the least bad option.
key_points: Bicubic works best for shrinking images (natural sharpening), bilinear for enlarging (natural smoothing)
Specialized algorithms like 2xSAI dramatically outperform standard methods for specific use cases like pixel art
Fractal-based resizing produces noticeably better results than bicubic at extreme enlargements, though with watercolor-like artifacts
Reducing images is safe (just discarding information), but enlarging is fundamentally risky since you're synthesizing fake pixels
Finding a higher-resolution source is almost always better than algorithmic upscaling
money_quotes: Enlarging images is risky. Beyond a certain point, enlarging images is a fool's errand; you can't magically synthesize an infinite number of new pixels out of thin air.
Interpolated pixels are never as good as real pixels.
Reducing images is a completely safe and rational operation. You're simply reducing precision and resolution by discarding information.
When you can't avoid enlarging an image, that's when it pays to know the tradeoffs between bicubic, bilinear, and more advanced resizing algorithms.
At least arm yourself with enough knowledge to pick the best of the bad options you have.
It'd be smarter to find a higher resolution scan or picture of whatever you need than it would be to upsize it in software.
posts.key_insight: Image enlargement is fundamentally a lossy operation where no algorithm can truly create detail that doesn't exist, so the best strategy is avoiding enlargement entirely or understanding algorithm tradeoffs to minimize the damage.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood outlines criteria for evaluating whether projects qualify as truly open source for his $10,000 donation initiative to the .NET ecosystem. He argues that many projects claim to be open source but fail to meet basic standards. Using NDoc as a cautionary example where source code was never publicly released, he establishes three concrete requirements: an OSI-approved license, public source control access, and evidence of accepting community contributions. These criteria align with the Free Software Foundation's four freedoms, emphasizing that open source is about active community development, not just releasing code.
key_points: Many projects claim to be open source but don't actually meet the criteria, often due to misunderstanding or lip service to the concept
True open source requires three elements: an approved license, public source control access, and demonstrated acceptance of outside contributions
A project with only one developer or a closed group ignoring outside input isn't truly open source in spirit
Microsoft's shared source licenses should be considered open source despite OSI's political refusal to recognize them
The essence of open source is active community development, not just code availability
money_quotes: How do you tell if something that calls itself 'open source' is really open source?
Many projects think they are – or at least some users may think they are – but they really aren't.
Is a project truly open source if it only has one developer?
Is a project truly open source if it has a cabal of three developers who summarily ignore all outside suggestions and contributions?
The spirit of open source is active community development.
Pick a license, any license! If your project does not have a license, or if you fail to make it stupid easy for us to determine what license your project uses... your project is ineligible.
If the only way to get to the source code is via HTTP download of a ZIP file... your project is ineligible.
posts.key_insight: Open source isn't just about making code available—it requires an explicit license, accessible source control, and genuine openness to community contributions.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents the first part of building a custom PC for Scott Hanselman, walking readers through the process of assembling a minimal bootable system. He argues that PC building is far less intimidating than most people believe, comparing it to assembling LEGO kits. The post covers the complete process from laying out parts through installing CPU, memory, cooler, power supply, motherboard, and video card. Atwood emphasizes that computer components are more durable than people think and that static discharge risks are overblown. The build took about two hours and successfully booted, demonstrating that with patience and common sense, anyone can build a PC.
key_points: Building a PC is like assembling LEGO - straightforward if you follow instructions and don't force anything
Computer components are surprisingly durable and static discharge risks are greatly exaggerated
The key to avoiding damage is patience - never force components that don't seem to fit
Aftermarket CPU coolers vastly outperform stock Intel coolers and are worth the investment
A minimal bootable system can be assembled in about two hours, even while taking photos and being extra cautious
money_quotes: If you can put together a LEGO kit, you can put together a PC from parts.
Computer parts are surprisingly durable. They aren't fragile. You don't have to baby them.
The risk of static discharge is overblown. I never wear anti-static wristbands, and I've yet to electrocute any components with static electricity.
I've learned through hard experience that 'maybe I need to use lots of additional force' is never the right answer when it comes to building PCs.
I've done some appalling things to computer hardware in my day, truly boneheaded stuff, and I think I've broken all of two or three items in the last 10 years.
If you have cats, like we do, you have curious cat helpers. Unfortunately, cat helpers aren't all that... helpful.
I know 'reboot and select proper boot device' doesn't look like much, but it means everything is working.
posts.key_insight: Building a PC is far less risky and difficult than commonly believed - patience and common sense matter more than specialized knowledge or anti-static precautions.

posts.summary: This post continues Jeff Atwood's detailed walkthrough of building a PC, covering the installation of storage drives, optical drive, operating system, and stability testing procedures. He methodically documents connecting hard drives using the Antec P182's vibration-isolating cage system, installing a DVD drive with rail mounting, and configuring BIOS settings. After installing Windows Vista, he emphasizes the importance of using updated drivers from manufacturer websites rather than bundled CDs. The bulk of the post focuses on burn-in testing using Prime95, 3DMark, and temperature monitoring to verify system stability before proceeding to overclocking.
key_points: A 10,000 RPM Raptor drive provides significant boot performance benefits and is highly recommended as a system drive
Motherboard driver CDs are outdated by the time they reach consumers - always download fresh drivers from manufacturer websites
If a system cannot complete a clean Windows install, the hardware is unstable - don't blame the software
Proper burn-in testing requires stressing both CPU (Prime95) and GPU (RTHDRIBL) simultaneously while monitoring temperatures
Physical temperature checking with a finger is a valid diagnostic technique - if it's too hot to touch for a few seconds, there's a problem
money_quotes: If your system can't finish a clean install of Windows, it's not stable. Period. It's tempting to blame Microsoft, but the only possible culprit if you have problems at this stage is the hardware.

Remember those driver CDs that came with the motherboard? Throw them right in the trash. They're way out of date by the time the motherboard gets from the factory, to the vendor, and then finally to you.

These tests aren't just for stability; they're also reality checks. Make sure these scores are in the ballpark for comparable systems. If not, you got something wrong in the build and somehow crippled your system's performance.
You'll know you're in the danger zone when something is too hot to leave your finger on for more than a few seconds.
I often use my built-in Mark I finger to touch various items in the computer and make sure they're within normal temperature ranges. You might call me 'The PC Whisperer.'
Looks like we have a stable, complete system. And when you have a stable, complete system, it's clearly time to overclock it until it breaks.
posts.key_insight: Building a reliable PC requires methodical verification at each step - from BIOS detection to OS installation to torture testing - because stability problems must be caught and resolved before moving forward.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood walks through the process of overclocking Scott Hanselman's newly built PC, demonstrating how to safely push a Core 2 Quad Q6600 from its stock 2.4 GHz to 3.0 GHz. He explains the basic math behind CPU clocking (bus speed times multiplier), the tools needed for monitoring and stability testing, and the methodical approach of making incremental changes. The post emphasizes that while overclocking sounds intimidating, it's actually straightforward with the right tools and patience. The payoff is significant: achieving performance equivalent to a $1,345 CPU while only paying $480.
key_points: CPU speed equals front side bus speed times multiplier, and since Intel locks multipliers, overclocking means increasing the FSB
Successful overclocking requires incremental changes, voltage adjustments, and extensive stability testing with tools like Prime95
CPU temperature is the enemy of stability - higher temps mean less likely stable overclocks
Any sign of instability means you've overclocked too far; start from a known stable baseline
The economic argument is compelling: getting $1,345 CPU performance for $480 by spending time instead of money
money_quotes: While overclocking, CPU-Z will become your new best friend.
Successful overclocking is a game of inches, not yards.
If your system shows any sign of instability, no matter how small, you've definitely overclocked too far.
CPU temperatures are the enemy of speed – all other things being equal, the higher the CPU temperature, the less likely it is your system will be stable.
Is the risk of instability and all that effort worth it for a tiny speed bump? Why bother?
I'd say saving nearly a thousand bucks is a pretty good argument in favor of overclocking.
posts.key_insight: Overclocking is accessible to anyone willing to be methodical and patient, and the financial savings can be substantial enough to justify the effort.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood profiles Steve Mann, an engineering professor who has lived as a cyborg with wearable computers since 1980. Mann wears a web of wires, computers, and sensors designed to augment memory, enhance vision, and monitor vital signs. Atwood notes that despite Mann's pioneering work spanning over two decades and reaching 'version 7' of his wearable rig, the field seems to have stagnated. The post observes that most wearable computing resources, including Mann's own website and MIT's wearable computing page, appear neglected or abandoned. Atwood finds this ironic given the futuristic nature of the technology.
key_points: Steve Mann has been a pioneer in wearable computing since 1980, wearing computer systems that augment his human capabilities
Mann's work gained mainstream attention around 2001 with a book and documentary, but appears to have plateaued since
The wearable computing field's web presence is surprisingly neglected, with most major resources not updated since the mid-2000s
Despite being on 'version 7' of his rig, Mann hasn't visibly updated since 1999
money_quotes: Steve Mann may be the world's original cyborg.

Steve Mann, an engineering professor at the University of Toronto, has lived as a cyborg for more than 20 years, wearing a web of wires, computers and electronic sensors that are designed to augment his memory, enhance his vision and keep tabs on his vital signs.
Somehow, I thought the cyborgs would have a more compelling web presence.
posts.key_insight: The pioneers of wearable computing, despite their futuristic vision, have ironically failed to maintain a compelling presence in the digital world they helped imagine.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to Jakob Nielsen's controversial article criticizing 'commodity bloggers' who produce shallow, derivative content. While acknowledging that Nielsen's statistical arguments are flawed and his advice is overly simplistic, Atwood agrees with the core message: bloggers should create in-depth, original content driven by expertise rather than just reacting to trending topics. He contrasts thoughtful responses to Nielsen's article with Robert Scoble's defensive, narcissistic reaction that ironically proved Nielsen's point. The post concludes that blogging rewards effort proportionally, and writers should inject their unique perspective rather than becoming 'link propagating automatons.'
key_points: Nielsen's core blogging advice boils down to 'don't suck' - not particularly actionable, but containing a kernel of truth about content quality
Scoble's defensive reaction to Nielsen's criticism ironically reinforced every negative blogger stereotype the article addressed
Nielsen's statistical modeling is flawed - experts having bad days don't suddenly become below average, just as Tiger Woods at his worst is still in the top 1%
The key distinction in valuable content is: in-depth vs. superficial, original vs. derivative, expertise-driven vs. reactively driven by outside events
What you get out of blogging is directly proportional to what you put into it - add the 'you' to stand out from commodity bloggers
money_quotes: Isn't it ironic how Robert's response reinforces all the negative stereotypes of bloggers that Nielsen addresses in that very article?
At its core, the blogging advice he offers is awfully simplistic – I'd summarize it as 'don't suck, and if you suck, stop sucking.'
When Tiger Woods has a bad day, he's not worse than average.
I'm reminded of the old statistical fallacy that fire stations must cause fires because there are more fire stations in those areas.
Let us know there's a unique human being in there, not just another mindless, link propagating automaton writing about the same damn current events everyone else is writing about.
The world already has enough commodity bloggers. When you post a blog entry, don't forget to add the you.
Yes, it takes more effort – but you're worth it.
posts.key_insight: Valuable blogging requires creating in-depth, original content driven by genuine expertise rather than reactively echoing whatever everyone else is discussing.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques Apple's maximize button behavior, which sizes windows to fit content rather than filling the screen. While acknowledging Apple's design philosophy has merit—since excessively wide windows create readability problems—he argues that forcing users to manage multiple overlapping windows is worse. He contends that window management is cognitive overhead that detracts from actual work, citing the death of MDI interfaces and the rise of single-window paradigms like web browsers as evidence. His solution preference is more monitor space rather than smarter window sizing.
key_points: Apple's maximize button sizes windows to content rather than screen size, which is intentional design, not a bug
Excessively wide text columns hurt readability, which is why newspapers use columns and good websites constrain text width
Managing multiple overlapping windows (X, Y, and Z-order) is cognitively expensive and represents 'excise' that prevents real work
Single-window interfaces like web browsers and TiVo dominate because users struggle with multi-window paradigms
More monitor space is a better solution than forcing users to deal with overlapping windows
money_quotes: Dealing with multiple windows is far too difficult, even for sophisticated computer users.
Adding Z-order in addition to the traditional X and Y positioning is one variable too many.
Manipulating windows is pure excise – extra work that stands between the user and completing their task.

The more windows you have to deal with, the less work you get done, and the more time you spend sizing them, moving them, bringing them to the top, and dragging them around so they aren't overlapping.
Apple's method of forcing users to deal with more windows by preventing maximization is not good user interface design. It is fundamentally and deeply flawed.
Users don't want to deal with the mental overhead of juggling multiple windows, and I can't blame them: neither do I.
Designers should be coming up with alternative user interfaces that minimize windowing, instead of forcing enforcing arbitrary window size limits on the user for their own good.
posts.key_insight: Window management is cognitive overhead that users shouldn't be forced to bear, and design philosophies that increase the number of windows users must juggle—however theoretically elegant—fail in practice.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores Tim Berners-Lee's Principle of Least Power, which argues that choosing less powerful languages makes data more accessible and analyzable. The post contrasts simple declarative formats (which anyone can parse and analyze) with powerful but opaque solutions like Java applets (which only humans can interpret). Atwood uses this principle to introduce his famous 'Atwood's Law': any application that can be written in JavaScript will eventually be written in JavaScript. The post recommends Berners-Lee's broader architectural writings as containing timeless web design wisdom.
key_points: Computer science has spent decades maximizing language power, but we now need to appreciate choosing the least powerful solution
Less powerful, declarative formats enable data to be retrieved, analyzed, averaged, plotted, and combined with other information
Powerful solutions like Java applets may offer cool interfaces but are completely opaque to search engines and automated analysis
Atwood's Law: any application that can be written in JavaScript will eventually be written in JavaScript
money_quotes: Computer Science spent the last forty years making languages which were as powerful as possible. Nowadays we have to appreciate the reasons for picking not the most powerful solution but the least powerful.
The less powerful the language, the more you can do with the data stored in that language.
The only way to find out what a Java applet means is to set it running in front of a person.
Any application that can be written in JavaScript, will eventually be written in JavaScript.
posts.key_insight: Choosing less powerful, more declarative solutions makes your data universally accessible and analyzable, while overly powerful solutions create opaque black boxes that only humans can interpret.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the surprisingly complex application installation processes on both Mac and Windows platforms. He quotes Ned Batchelder's frustration with Mac's multi-step DMG installation process, which requires manual folder management despite Apple's 'it just works' reputation. He notes that Windows isn't much better, with ZIP-distributed apps requiring manual setup and traditional installers overwhelming users with unnecessary choices. Atwood argues that most users simply want software to work without needing to understand filesystems or make dozens of configuration decisions. He champions 'No-Questions-Asked' installers as the ideal solution.
key_points: Mac's drag-to-Applications installation process contradicts its 'it just works' philosophy by requiring multiple manual steps
ZIP-distributed Windows apps create inconsistency as users install to different locations with varying levels of organization
Traditional Windows installers bombard users with unnecessary decisions about features, icons, file associations, and paths
Most users don't want fine-grained control over installation - they just want applications to work
The ideal installation experience is a single 'Install Me' button with no questions asked
money_quotes: Why does the Mac require the user to jump through a bunch of manual hoops to install an application?
That's a lot of tedious, error-prone steps I have to perform before I can run the application.
Even as a power user, I find this level of control not only unnecessary but onerous.
A typical user doesn't want this level of control, and they certainly don't want to learn about disks and folders.
Wouldn't a big giant button that says 'Install Me' be a better experience for the user?
That's a whole lot of thinking necessary to install a tiny little music player.
posts.key_insight: Software installation on any platform should prioritize user simplicity over power-user control, ideally reducing to a single 'Install Me' button rather than forcing users to make decisions they don't care about.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits a 1994 'Futurist Programming' manifesto that applied early 20th century Futurist art principles to software development, arguing against dogma and waste in programming. The manifesto rejected accepted practices like modularity, standards, and object-oriented methodologies in favor of speed and efficiency. Atwood notes that some predictions, like the rise of dynamic code generation, proved prescient. However, he warns that the core philosophy of rejecting dogma can itself become dogmatic, using the uncomfortable historical fact that the original Futurists were fascists as a cautionary note. The post advocates for critical examination of 'best practices' while acknowledging that some conventional wisdom remains useful.
key_points: The 1994 Futurist Programming manifesto rejected dogma in software development, calling for programs that prioritize user time and memory over programmer convenience
Hardware improved 200x over 20 years while programming practices stagnated since the invention of the subroutine
The manifesto's embrace of dynamic code generation anticipated the rise of dynamic programming languages a decade later
Critical examination of accepted wisdom is valuable, but skepticism of best practices can be taken too far
The original Futurist art movement's connection to fascism serves as a warning about ideological extremism
money_quotes: We react against the heavy religious atmosphere that surrounds every aspect of computer programming.

Computer hardware has realized performance increases of a factor of more than 200 in the last 20 years, while in program design very little progress has been made at all since the invention of the subroutine.
Fearing and attacking technology has become almost second nature to many people today.
Anything that is done for the convenience of the programmer at the expense of the user.
The core futurist philosophy that religion and dogma of all kinds should be examined critically is an important one.
You have to be careful that rejecting dogma doesn't itself become a kind of dogma.
Too bad they were all Fascists.
posts.key_insight: Questioning programming dogma and best practices is healthy, but anti-dogmatism taken to extremes becomes its own rigid ideology.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the key factors that determine whether software projects succeed or fail. He starts with Steve McConnell's hierarchy—project size, type of software, and personnel—then adds Bill de hÓra's 'three pillars' of version control, work item tracking, and build systems as equally critical infrastructure. The post also touches on framework choice, noting that frameworks designed for the web rather than around it dramatically impact productivity. Ultimately, Atwood concludes that project success isn't about doing one thing right, but about the daunting task of not doing anything wrong across multiple dimensions.
key_points: Project size is the single most significant determinant of software project success—large projects tend to fail
The 'three pillars' of version control, work item tracking, and build systems are first-order effects that impact everything else on a project
Framework and tool choices matter enormously—frameworks designed for the web outperform those that treat the web as something to work around
IDEs and even methodology choice may matter less than getting the fundamental infrastructure right
Software project success requires not doing anything wrong across multiple factors, not just excelling at one thing
money_quotes: Most software projects fail. But that doesn't mean yours has to.
Size is easily the most significant determinant of effort, cost, and schedule.
The version control system is a first order effect on software, along with two others – the build system and the bugtracker.
Things like IDEs, by comparison, don't matter at all. Even choice of methodology might matter less.
I suspect designing for the Web instead of around it is at least as important as language choice.
Maybe the real lesson here is that software project success isn't about doing any one particular thing right; it's the much more daunting task of not doing anything wrong.
posts.key_insight: Software project success requires systematically avoiding failure across multiple dimensions—size, team, infrastructure, and framework choice—rather than excelling at any single factor.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood concludes his series on building a custom PC for Scott Hanselman by detailing the final sound dampening steps and sharing the finished build specifications. He explains how foam damping creates an acoustic chamber around noisy components, noting it's a subtle but audible finishing touch for an already quiet system. The post includes updated component recommendations following Intel's price cuts and invites readers to participate in building their own PCs. Atwood frames PC building not as a necessary skill but as an expression of craftsmanship and passion for one's tools.
key_points: Sound dampening foam creates an absorptive chamber around components, providing subtle but noticeable noise reduction on already-quiet builds
The 64-bit version of Vista produced better Windows Experience scores than 32-bit on identical hardware
Intel's price cuts made quad-core processors more accessible for enthusiast builds
PC building is presented as an optional but rewarding pursuit that deepens understanding of hardware
The desire to understand your tools is framed as part of the passion that distinguishes true craftsmen
money_quotes: The goal is to build a sound dampening chamber around the motherboard, CPU, hard drives (particularly the hard drives), and video cards.
It won't magically convert a noisy PC into a quiet one, but it can make an already quiet PC that much quieter.
It's your PC; build it the way you want.
Building a PC at least once gives you a deeper understanding of your hardware.
The desire to try it at least once is part of the passion that a true craftsman should have for his tools.
As master craftsman they're passionate about their tools and ingredients. They want to know everything they can about them.
Building (and overclocking) PCs isn't for everyone. But I hope my series illustrated that it isn't particularly difficult – and it sure can be rewarding if you have the time and inclination.
posts.key_insight: Building your own PC isn't about resume skills or necessity—it's about cultivating the craftsman's passion for deeply understanding the tools of your trade.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines Garry's Mod as a case study in successful guerilla programming, highlighting how creator Garry Newman built an incredibly successful Half-Life 2 mod through iterative development and learning from existing code. The mod evolved from a simple experiment into a full-blown development environment inside a 3D physics-governed world. Newman learned the Source engine by studying Valve's existing implementations rather than from books, copying and adapting code he found. Atwood emphasizes Newman's advocacy for iterative development over perfectionism, noting that releasing early helps validate ideas and build community. The post positions Garry's Mod as transcending traditional sandbox games to become 'a sandbox for creating new sandboxes.'
key_points: Garry's Mod succeeded through iterative development rather than waiting for perfection - Newman advocates releasing early to validate ideas and build community
Newman learned the Source engine by reverse-engineering Valve's existing code, finding working examples more effective than learning from books
The mod evolved from a simple experiment into a Lua-scripted platform enabling users to create games, music videos, and other content
Releasing imperfect early versions is less risky than working in isolation for years only to discover your idea doesn't work
Garry's Mod transcends traditional sandbox games by being a platform for creating new sandboxes
money_quotes: I'm not the kind of person that can learn from books, I really need working examples.

The main argument against this is that they don't want to release a shit version of their idea and turn everyone off. Fair enough, but it's going to be so much worse if you work your balls off on it for 2 years then release, and your idea is still rubbish.
Iterating would have let you know that this idea isn't working out, so you could adjust it. In every update you're picking up more people playing your mod. You build a community.
I didn't know how to make rope so I thought back and realized that the Barnacle's tongue was rope. So I just pretty much copied that code into a simple weapon and it worked.
He's created a sandbox for creating new sandboxes.
posts.key_insight: Iterative development with early releases beats perfectionism because it validates ideas quickly, builds community incrementally, and prevents years of wasted effort on flawed concepts.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the problem of uncivil discourse in online comments, using a rude comment on Wil Shipley's blog as a springboard. He argues that while criticism is valuable, resorting to personal attacks and angry invective is self-defeating because it causes people to stop listening. Drawing on Miss Manners' wisdom about etiquette and Martin Luther King Jr.'s Letter From Birmingham Jail as a model of effective disagreement, Atwood concludes that the only way to persuade others is through civil, reasonable, and rational discourse. The post acknowledges the 'Greater Internet Fuckwad Theory' as a byproduct of online anonymity but insists we can do better.
key_points: Angry personal attacks negate any legitimate criticism you're trying to make - the minute you insult someone, they stop listening
Online anonymity enables bad behavior (the G.I.F.T.), but this isn't an excuse for incivility
Etiquette has social sanctions: people who behave badly get isolated and excluded from communities
Martin Luther King Jr.'s Letter From Birmingham Jail demonstrates how to express profound disagreement while maintaining respect
Effective persuasion requires respecting those you disagree with and earning their respect in turn
money_quotes: The minute you call someone an a**hole, they're no longer listening to you.
If you behave in a way that offends the people you're trying to deal with, they will stop dealing with you.
Insulting someone might make you feel temporarily vindicated, or give you a brief, cathartic moment of release, but you aren't convincing anyone of anything.
The fine art of disagreement: the ability to respect the people you disagree with, and to earn their respect in turn.
The only way to do that is to be civil, reasonable, and rational.
I don't object to criticism. Criticism is what comments are for. What I object to is criticism that resorts to least-common-denominator attacks.
The use of angry invective negates any criticism you were trying to make.
posts.key_insight: Effective disagreement requires civility - angry attacks are self-defeating because they cause your audience to dismiss both your argument and you.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that Google's 'I'm Feeling Lucky' button is a UI relic that should be removed. What started as a clever demonstration of Google's search effectiveness in the early days has become a vestigial feature used by less than one percent of users. Atwood contends that giving this rarely-used feature equal visual prominence with the Search button creates unnecessary cognitive load for millions of users daily. He draws a parallel to Strunk and White's writing advice to 'omit needless words,' arguing we should similarly 'omit needless buttons.' The post also touches on the gap between power users who use keyboard shortcuts and typical users who rely on visible buttons to interact with interfaces.
key_points: The 'I'm Feeling Lucky' button was a clever joke demonstrating Google's effectiveness, but has outlived its usefulness after a decade
Features used by less than one percent of users shouldn't have visual parity with primary actions
The cognitive load of mentally filtering out unnecessary UI elements multiplies across millions of users into significant collective cost
There's a fundamental divide between power users who use keyboard shortcuts and typical users who need visible buttons
UI minimalism should extend to removing vestigial features, not just avoiding new complexity
money_quotes: Does anyone actually use the 'I'm Feeling Lucky' button?
Google has won so decisively, so utterly, and so completely that the power they now wield over the internet actually scares me a little. Okay, it scares me a lot.
Giving a feature that's used less than one percent of the time parity with the 'Search' button is a needless distraction for users.

The thought necessary to mentally omit this needless button from the page may be miniscule – but multiply that by the millions upon millions of users who are affected, and all of a sudden it starts to add up to real time.
As shocking as this may be to us homo logicus, not everyone understands how that works.
They love their mice, and their big, fat, honking 'Search' buttons.
I urge us to Omit Needless Buttons.
posts.key_insight: Interface minimalism isn't just about avoiding new complexity—it requires the courage to remove beloved but obsolete features that no longer serve users.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software patents represent an existential threat to the software industry, creating a Mutually Assured Destruction dynamic where companies stockpile patents purely for defensive purposes. He cites Donald Knuth's position that algorithms are as fundamental to software as words are to writing, making them inappropriate for patent protection. Drawing parallels to the fashion and culinary industries—which thrive without patent protection—Atwood contends that software should follow a similar model. The post highlights the particularly dangerous nature of patents: they can block independent inventions created without any copying. With the exponential growth of software patents shown in accompanying data, Atwood warns of an impending 'apocalypse' if nothing changes.
key_points: Software companies are trapped in a patent arms race, building stockpiles purely for defensive counter-suits rather than to protect genuine innovations
Donald Knuth argues algorithms are as fundamental to software as words are to writing—patenting them would be like letting authors patent individual words
The fashion and restaurant industries prove that creative fields can thrive without patent protection, relying on execution and reputation instead
Unlike copyrights, patents can block independently developed inventions even when no copying occurred, creating a minefield for all programmers
Patent farming and submarine patents allow bad actors to embed patented principles into standards, then extract royalties from all implementers
money_quotes: Software companies are forced to build huge stockpiles of software patents solely to be used as deterrents.
Algorithms are exactly as basic to software as words are to writers, because they are the fundamental building blocks needed to make interesting products.
What would happen if individual lawyers could patent their methods of defense, or if Supreme Court justices could patent their precedents?
Patents give their owners the right to prevent others from using a claimed invention, even if it was independently developed and there was no copying involved.
Every time you write code – even a brand new algorithm in a clean room environment – you could be infringing a patent, somehow, somewhere.
It's probably not fair to say that software patents are 100% evil. But from what I've read, I'd say they're 99 and 44/100ths percent evil.
The fashion world understands that creativity is a collaborative and community affair. It's far too big, robust, and evolving for any one player to 'own' as a legal entitlement.
posts.key_insight: Software patents create a paradox where companies must amass patents they philosophically oppose, while every programmer unknowingly risks infringement simply by writing original code.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores 'Baby Duck Syndrome' - the tendency for computer users to imprint on the first system they learn and judge all others by comparison to it. He admits to being guilty of this himself, having avoided trying Visual SlickEdit because he's already comfortable with Visual Studio. The post examines how this imprinting creates a dilemma: we can't rationally compare alternatives without experiencing them, but our imprinting prevents us from gaining that experience. Atwood concludes that developers should push themselves to experiment with new tools even when satisfied with their current setup.
key_points: Baby Duck Syndrome causes users to prefer familiar systems and dismiss unfamiliar ones, regardless of objective merit
Software imprinting creates a catch-22: you can't rationally evaluate alternatives without experience, but imprinting prevents you from gaining that experience
Even experienced developers rarely have deep knowledge of multiple competing tools, making informed comparisons rare
Short-term productivity favors sticking with familiar tools, but long-term growth requires periodic experimentation
The familiar always seems more efficient than the unfamiliar, which may not reflect reality
money_quotes: Baby Duck Syndrome is the tendency for computer users to 'imprint' on the first system they learn, then judge other systems by their similarity to that first system.
You can't rationally compare alternatives with no experience in the alternatives.
Software imprinting robs you of that vital experience.
It can make it hard for you to make the most rational decisions about which software to use or when the learning curve of a given thing is worth the climb.
In general, it makes the familiar seem more efficient and the unfamiliar less so.
It's hard to find people who have used recent versions of two major editors, so it's hard to find anyone who can intelligently compare the features of various editors.
We should put allegiances and familiarity aside, and push ourselves harder to go beyond our software imprinting – otherwise, we literally won't know what we're missing.
posts.key_insight: Our instinctive loyalty to the first tools we learned prevents us from making rational choices about better alternatives, and overcoming this requires deliberate effort to experiment beyond our comfort zone.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that programmers must balance solitary coding with public engagement, but warns against becoming a pundit who only talks about programming without actually building things. He draws inspiration from the aggressive 'Do it f***ing now' mentality and adapts Glengarry Glen Ross's 'Always Be Closing' into 'Always Be Shipping' for developers. The post emphasizes that software developers will ultimately be judged by what they've built and shipped, not by the discussions surrounding their work. Atwood includes Marc Andreessen's hiring philosophy that values concrete achievements over passive participation, reinforcing the importance of tangible output over theoretical engagement.
key_points: Programmers naturally tend toward introversion and solitary work, but can also go too far in the other direction by talking more than coding
Becoming a pundit who only offers commentary without building anything concrete is a trap to avoid
The value of a feature is sometimes inversely proportional to how much it has been discussed
Hiring decisions should focus on what candidates have actually built and shipped, not what they were merely 'involved in'
Developers should spend at least as much time doing as discussing
money_quotes: Sometimes I worry that I spend more time talking about programming than actually programming.
The last thing the world needs is more pundits.
Pundits only add ephemeral commentary to the world instead of anything concrete and real.
Always Be Shipping.
It's helpful to discuss features, but sometimes the value of a feature is inversely proportional to how much it has been discussed.
Our job as software developers is to deliver features and solve business problems, not to generate never ending discussion.
Not been involved in, or been part of, or watched happen, or was hanging around when it happened.
Make sure you spend at least as much of your time doing as discussing.
posts.key_insight: Software developers are ultimately judged by what they ship, not by the discussions and commentary surrounding their work.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the disparity between Mac and Windows PC boot times, finding that while PCs often boot slower, much of this is due to third-party bloatware and anti-virus software rather than the operating system itself. A clean Windows install can boot just as fast as a Mac, but vendors compromise this by pre-installing craplets. He recommends using MSCONFIG to disable unnecessary startup programs and ultimately suggests that sleep and hibernation modes make boot time optimization largely irrelevant since users shouldn't be rebooting frequently anyway.
key_points: Clean Windows installs can match Mac boot speeds (22 seconds), but OEM bloatware typically doubles or triples boot times
Nearly half of PC boot time can be spent in the BIOS, which Microsoft has no control over
Anti-virus and security software are the primary culprits for degraded boot performance over time
MSCONFIG is the built-in solution for disabling unnecessary startup programs, and most startup items can safely be disabled
Sleep and hibernation modes are the real solution—the best boot time is no boot time at all
money_quotes: PC boot times really do suck, right? Well, maybe. It depends on the PC.
It's sobering to consider that almost half of the system's total boot time is spent in the third-party motherboard BIOS – something Microsoft has no control over.
You typically get Microsoft's operating system plus a bevy of performance-sapping craplets the third-party vendor was paid to install on your system.
In my experience, anything that wants to runs at boot almost never needs to.
It's generally safe to turn off almost everything in the MSCONFIG startup tab.
If you rely on commercial anti-virus, you are literally crippling your PC's performance.
Anti-virus software barely works these days anyway, so it's a raw deal no matter how you slice it.
Make use of those Sleep and Hibernation options in lieu of powering all the way down.
posts.key_insight: Boot time problems on Windows PCs are largely self-inflicted through bloatware and unnecessary startup programs, but the real solution is to stop rebooting entirely by using sleep and hibernation modes.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the hierarchy of software failures, arguing that data loss and corruption are far worse than crashes. He examines the tension between 'fail fast' development philosophy and user-friendly error recovery, noting that while failing fast helps developers find bugs, it frustrates users. However, attempting to silently recover from errors can lead to catastrophic data corruption. The solution is a balanced approach: fix problems when safely possible, but always prioritize protecting user data above all else.
key_points: Software failures exist on a spectrum from minor crashes to catastrophic data loss, with data corruption being the worst possible outcome
The 'fail fast' philosophy benefits developers but frustrates users who just want to get work done
Attempting to automatically recover from errors can mask problems and lead to worse failures later
Protecting user data is a 'sacred trust' that affects not just individual programs but the credibility of the entire software industry
Developers must balance being helpful (fixing problems automatically) with being safe (never risking data integrity)
money_quotes: A system that fails fast does exactly the opposite: when a problem occurs, it fails immediately and visibly. Failing fast is a nonintuitive technique: 'failing immediately and visibly' sounds like it would make your software more fragile, but it actually makes it more robust.
Computers are spectacularly unforgiving, so it's only natural for developers to reflect that masochism directly back on users.
Attempting to help the user by fixing the error could make things worse by leading to subtle and catastrophic failures down the road.
Protecting the user's data is a sacred trust.
If you harm that basic contract of trust between the user and your program, you're hurting not only your credibility – but the credibility of the entire software industry as a whole.
Once they've been burned by data loss or corruption, users don't soon forgive.
The guiding principle here, as always, should be to respect your users.
posts.key_insight: When software fails, protecting user data must always take precedence over avoiding error messages—crashes are recoverable, but lost data destroys trust forever.

posts.summary: Atwood documents the frustrating complexity of installing Visual Studio 2005, which requires seven separate installations just for a baseline setup. He argues that this undermines the traditional claim that commercial software stacks are easier to configure than open source alternatives. The post suggests that either open source tooling has matured significantly, or Microsoft's offerings have devolved into unnecessary complexity. Atwood concludes that paying for commercial software no longer guarantees time savings.
key_points: A complete Visual Studio 2005 installation requires at least seven separate components, not counting additional add-ons for newer technologies
The traditional argument that commercial stacks save configuration time compared to open source is increasingly hollow
The complexity exists partly because products were released out of order, but this doesn't excuse the end-user experience
Time investment in tooling setup is now comparable whether you choose commercial or open source options
money_quotes: I'm having a hard time seeing how Microsoft's commercial stack is any easier to configure than the alternative open source stacks these days
Either the open source stuff has gotten a lot more streamlined and mature, or the Microsoft stuff is somehow devolving into complexity
The argument that choosing a commercial development stack saves you time rings more and more hollow over time
Linux is only free if your time is worthless. But apparently your time can be worthless even if you've paid for the privilege
posts.key_insight: Paying for commercial software no longer buys you simplicity—the complexity tax now applies regardless of whether your stack is open source or proprietary.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares several curated resources for learning about data visualization techniques. He highlights the Periodic Table of Visualization Methods, a Smashing Magazine collection of modern visualization approaches, and the Favourite Web Awards site for web-oriented design inspiration. The post serves as a brief reference guide for developers looking to improve their visualization skills before building rich internet applications.
key_points: The Periodic Table of Visualization Methods offers a comprehensive taxonomy of visualization techniques
Smashing Magazine's collection showcases modern, practical data visualization approaches
The FWA site catalogs creative web designs that push boundaries on visualization
Reviewing existing visualization techniques before building applications leads to better outcomes
money_quotes: If you're a student of Tufte like I am, you might find it helpful to review a sample of what visualizations and techniques are possible (or even advisable) before plowing ahead on your next Rich Internet Application.
posts.key_insight: Before building data-rich applications, developers should study existing visualization catalogs to understand what techniques are possible and appropriate.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores an ironic consequence of increasingly large computer monitors: while more screen real estate seems inherently better, it actually creates new productivity challenges. Small monitors forced users into a simple, efficient workflow of one maximized window at a time, but large displays make this approach impractical due to excessive whitespace and unreadable long text lines. Atwood argues that users need some form of 'snap grid' to effectively manage window placement on large displays, whether through multiple physical monitors or software solutions like WinSplit Revolution or GridMove. He predicts that as large monitors become the default, operating systems will need to build in automatic window snapping functionality.
key_points: Small monitors paradoxically encouraged better workflow by forcing users to work with one maximized window at a time, avoiding window management overhead
Large displays (30 inches and beyond) make maximized windows impractical, creating excessive whitespace and unreadably long text lines
Multiple physical monitors provide a natural 'snap grid' that single large monitors lack, making window management more intuitive
Software solutions like WinSplit Revolution and GridMove can simulate the snap grid effect for users with single large displays
Operating systems should eventually build in automatic snap grid support as large monitors become ubiquitous
money_quotes: That's the large display paradox. Having all that space can make you less productive due to all the window manipulation excise you have to deal with to make effective use of it.
One of the advantages of small monitors, ironically, is that because they're small, they nudge users into a simpler, windowless method of working.
Users of 30-inch monitors face the terrible, terrible problem of how to effectively use all of that space.
I have a natural snapping grid because I use three physical monitors. It's a side-effect of the hardware, but a crucial one that I've absolutely come to rely on.
In the not-too-distant future, every user will have a monitor so large that maximizing a window no longer makes sense for most applications.
Instead of wasting time sizing, moving, and z-ordering windows, users only need to deal with one maximized window at a time.
posts.key_insight: More screen space doesn't automatically improve productivity—without proper window management tools or natural boundaries, large displays create overhead that can actually slow users down.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reviews his new Dell XPS M1330 laptop, focusing particularly on its groundbreaking 32GB solid-state hard drive. While the specs are similar to his previous laptop, the combination of LED display, SSD, discrete graphics, and sub-four-pound weight in an ultraportable form factor makes it exceptional. He details the frustrations of Dell's bloatware consuming half the limited storage and the need to immediately format new PCs. The review is ultimately a love letter to early SSD technology, predicting it will become standard for all laptops once prices drop and capacities increase.
key_points: The M1330 uniquely combines LED display, SSD option, discrete graphics, and ultralight weight - features rarely found together in 2007
Dell's bloatware problem is especially egregious on a 32GB SSD, with restore and media partitions consuming half the available space
SSDs deliver dramatically better performance, silence, and power efficiency compared to mechanical drives, but the 32GB limitation requires careful disk management
The PC ecosystem forces users to format and reinstall Windows immediately to get a clean system free of paid-endorsement craplets
Solid-state drives represent the clear future for laptops despite current size and price limitations
money_quotes: It's a sad fact of life in the PC ecosystem, but if you want a machine clean of bloatware and useless, paid-endorsement installed craplets, you have to raze it to the ground yourself immediately after unboxing it.
Nothing like booting up a system with an already-limited 32 GB storage device and finding you only have 16 GB of disk space available. Way to go, Dell.

After using a machine with a solid-state hard drive for a few days, it's clear to me that solid-state hard drives are absolutely the future for all laptops, and possibly even for desktops in some scenarios.
32 GB of space is enough to get by as a primary hard drive, but it definitely makes you realize how spoiled we've all become with our standard ginormous physical hard drives.
As William Gibson said, the future is already here – it's just unequally distributed.
Who still uses ye olde DVDs or CDs in this era of cheap 4 GB flash drives, broadband, and ubiquitous gigabit ethernet?
posts.key_insight: Early SSD technology, despite severe capacity limitations, delivers such transformative benefits in speed, silence, and power efficiency that it clearly represents the inevitable future of computing storage.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the Windows security crisis through the lens of his colleague's family members falling victim to eCard trojans, despite being tech-savvy. He argues that three fundamental changes are needed: running as non-administrator by default, abandoning ineffective blacklist-based antivirus software, and embracing virtualization as the future of security. The post criticizes both Microsoft for not enforcing limited user accounts in Vista and security software vendors for profiting from a culture of fear. Atwood envisions a future where Windows users, like Mac and Linux users, don't need antivirus software at all.
key_points: Running as Administrator is institutionalized in Windows and is the root cause of the security epidemic - switching to limited user accounts is the only proven long-term solution
Traditional antivirus software with blacklist approaches has become nearly useless, with average detection rates as low as 33% against active malware
Modern threats like rootkits that redirect DNS queries represent a worst-case scenario that traditional security tools cannot adequately address
Virtualization (both full-machine and software-based) represents the next frontier in security by protecting users from themselves
Security software vendors have a perverse economic incentive to maintain the status quo of weak Windows security because fear sells their products
money_quotes: These email-based attacks prey on those who might not be operating at 100% for whatever reason. That makes me white-hot mad.
Running as Administrator has long been institutionalized in Windows.
That shiny anti-virus product you just bought might be protecting you from just 2% of currently active and common malware.
Not only does anti-virus cripple your machine's performance, it doesn't even protect you adequately!
How do you protect users from themselves?
Windows security software vendors need the threats – and the more credible and fearsome the threats, the better – to make money.
The status quo of weak Windows security suits them just fine. It sells their products.
I dislike the culture of fear that permeates Windows security software marketing.
posts.key_insight: The Windows security problem cannot be solved by better antivirus software; it requires fundamental changes to user privilege models and the adoption of virtualization to protect users from their own mistakes.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the development of Clearview, a new typeface designed to replace Highway Gothic on U.S. road signs. The post explores how seemingly minor design decisions like font choice can have measurable, practical impacts on user experience. Clearview was created to solve problems of information clutter and halation (glare from reflective signs at night), particularly affecting elderly drivers. Testing showed a 16 percent improvement in sign recognition, giving drivers crucial extra seconds to make decisions. Atwood draws parallels between signage design and software GUI design, arguing that good design should be validated with experimental data rather than treated as mere aesthetics.
key_points: Road sign typography faces specific challenges including halation (glare from reflective materials) that particularly affects elderly drivers at night
The Clearview typeface achieved a 16% improvement in recognition over Highway Gothic in nighttime tests, translating to 1-2 extra seconds of decision time at highway speeds
Better design can solve problems more elegantly than brute-force solutions like simply making signs larger
Font legibility improvements are measurable through experiments, not just subjective aesthetic preferences
Signage design principles have direct parallels to software GUI design
money_quotes: You could improve readability by simply making the font bigger. But this would result in billions of dollars spent on larger signs that increase visual clutter on the roadways.
This isn't just aesthetics – it also results in a practical benefit for drivers. That's the best kind of design, and like all the best designs, they provide the data to prove it.

In nighttime tests, Clearview showed a 16 percent improvement in recognition over Highway Gothic, meaning drivers traveling at 60 miles per hour would have an extra one to two seconds to make a decision.
I've always considered road signs a rich field for study, as signage design has many parallels to modern GUI design in computer science.
So before you write off a design exercise as seemingly trivial as font choice, consider whether that tiny bit of design could improve the user experience, if only a little.
And more importantly – how would you measure the improvement?
posts.key_insight: Good design isn't just about aesthetics—it produces measurable improvements in user experience, and the best designers validate their decisions with experimental data.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that discipline is the most critical trait for developer success, more important than tools, languages, or methodologies. He observes that even great tools like source control become useless without the discipline to use them properly, often resulting in chaotic, unstructured codebases. Drawing on Steve McConnell's Code Complete, he notes that professional-scale projects require entirely new skills centered on conventions and engineering discipline. Atwood suggests teams need someone willing to enforce standards and calls on individual developers to take responsibility for maintaining discipline themselves.
key_points: Tools and languages are irrelevant without the discipline to use them properly
Source control systems often become messy dumping grounds due to lack of structured usage
Professional-scale development (5,000-500,000+ lines) requires fundamentally different skills than academic programming
Great creative work throughout history has been built on self-imposed structure and discipline
Teams benefit from having someone who enforces coding conventions before bad patterns become institutionalized
money_quotes: Without discipline, things like tools and languages are irrelevant.
The mere presence of a great source control system doesn't obligate anyone to use it in a structured, rational way. No. That takes discipline.
You can write FORTRAN in any language.
This type of effort requires not the same skills on a larger scale, but a new set of skills altogether.
Form is liberating.
Without this self-imposed structure and discipline, the 300 human figures would have been merely chaotic rather than the coherent elements of an artistic masterpiece.
All it takes to benefit from those conventions is a little old-fashioned discipline. And if it doesn't start with you, then who?
posts.key_insight: The difference between amateur and professional software development isn't better tools or languages—it's the discipline to use conventions consistently at scale.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques Yahoo's YSlow tool and its 13 rules for website optimization, arguing that advice designed for one of the world's largest web properties doesn't automatically apply to smaller sites. He analyzes the weighted scoring system and demonstrates how blindly following these rules can actually make sites slower. The post dissects specific high-weight recommendations like CDNs, ETags, and Expires headers, explaining why each can be counterproductive for sites that aren't serving millions of daily users. Atwood concludes that developers should understand optimization advice before implementing it, rather than chasing arbitrary scores.
key_points: Yahoo's optimization rules are designed for sites with millions of daily users, making them inappropriate or even harmful for smaller websites
YSlow's scoring system gives high weight to rules that can actively degrade performance for non-enterprise sites, like disabling ETags
CDNs can actually slow down sites below a certain traffic threshold, and their cost is prohibitive for most developers
Setting aggressive Expires headers risks serving stale content and forces filename changes on every update
Understanding why optimization advice works is more important than blindly implementing it to chase a score
money_quotes: Do you run a website like that? If so, what are you doing reading this instead of flying your private jet to a Bermuda vacation with your trophy wife?
Software developers love optimization. Sometimes too much.
If you fail to read the Yahoo advice critically, you might make your site slower.
There is no such thing as one-size-fits-all guidance.
If you have to ask how much a formal Content Delivery Network will cost, you can't afford it.
Strive to understand the advice first, then implement the advice that makes sense for your specific situation.
Either the web is full of terribly inefficient web pages, or there's something wrong with its scoring.
posts.key_insight: Optimization advice from tech giants is designed for their scale, and blindly applying it to smaller projects can make things worse rather than better.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood catalogs thirteen common blog design and content mistakes that undermine the medium's effectiveness, drawing from four years of intensive blogging experience. He argues that blogs work best when kept simple, focused on reader value rather than author vanity, and committed to original content over echo-chamber repetition. The piece critiques both design clichés (useless calendar widgets, excessive advertisements, giant blogrolls) and content clichés (diary-style posts, apology posts, meta-blogging about blogging). Atwood emphasizes that blogs should prioritize substance over flair, with every element earning its place by serving readers. He self-deprecatingly acknowledges breaking his own rules with the very post—using a numbered list format he criticizes—while urging bloggers to focus on creating original, valuable content rather than following trends.
key_points: Blogs work because they're simple; adding widgets, features, and visual clutter destroys their essential value
Images should contribute substantively to content—inserting random pictures to break up text is as harmful as inserting random words
The echo chamber effect is blogging's most pernicious problem: most posts merely regurgitate others' content with vapid commentary
Reader-focused content trumps self-expression: your audience visits to learn what your blog can do for them, not to read about you
Comments are essential to blogging—'a blog without comments is not a blog'—because community contributions often exceed the original post's value
money_quotes: But you should no more insert a random image into your writing than you would insert a thousand random words into your writing.
The calendar widget is the vestigial tail of blog engines, evidence of our primordial ancestors. But we've evolved; it's time to lose the tail.
Your readers aren't coming to your blog to read about you. They're coming to your blog to find out what it can do for them.
It's always been deeply disappointing to me that we have the whole of human history to talk about, and most people can't get past what happened today.
If everyone knows about it, what value does that information have?

Meta-blogging is like masturbating. Everyone does it, and there's nothing wrong with it. But writers who regularly get out a little to explore other topics will be healthier, happier, and ultimately more interesting to be around.
It's an open secret in the blogging community that the comments are often better than the original blog entry itself.

Before you add a new 'feature' to your blog, consider whether this feature will be useful enough to your readers to overcome the additional complexity it adds to the page. Hint: almost none of them are.
posts.key_insight: The best blogs relentlessly prioritize reader value over author vanity, keeping design simple and content original rather than echoing what everyone else is already saying.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that enforcing engineering discipline through authority or criticism is counterproductive, and that effective technical leadership requires leading by example and genuinely caring about teammates. He critiques the approach of developers who join teams and immediately start pushing best practices, forwarding articles, and pointing out flaws—behavior that inevitably breeds resentment. Drawing on Dennis Forbes' advice and Gerald Weinberg's 'Becoming a Technical Leader,' Atwood concludes that actions and real results earn credibility, while genuine human caring is the foundation of lasting influence. The post serves as a guide for developers who want to improve their teams without becoming the person everyone dreads working with.
key_points: Berating or lecturing coworkers into compliance doesn't work—you need to be a leader, not an enforcer
Credibility comes from hard work and real results, not from forwarding articles or suggesting silver bullets
Constructive criticism should be discreet; public criticism breeds defensiveness and damages relationships
If you want to propose something, put effort behind it—demonstrate a working prototype rather than just talking about ideas
Genuine caring about teammates is the foundation of effective leadership, and people can tell when you're faking it
money_quotes: If you want to pull your team up to a higher level of engineering, you need a leader, not an enforcer.
Even with the best of intentions, and armed with the top books on the reading list, you'll end up like Gunnery Sergeant Hartman ultimately did: gunned down by your own team.
The best way to earn credibility and respect is through hard work and real results.

Simply talking about implementing a team blog, or a wiki, or a new source control mechanism, or a new technology, is cheap. Everyone knows that you're just trying to claim ownership of the idea when someone eventually actually does the hard work of doing it.
The most effective kind of technical leadership is leading by example.
Don't fool yourself. If you don't really care about the people whom you lead, you'll never succeed as their leader.
So much of leadership is learning to give a damn about other people, something that us programmers are notoriously bad at.
posts.key_insight: Technical leadership isn't about enforcing standards or broadcasting best practices—it's about earning credibility through action and genuinely caring about the people you work with.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares a delightful moment of receiving a signed book from Charles Petzold, the legendary Windows programming author whom Atwood had previously criticized harshly in a blog post. Petzold's inscription playfully references Atwood's critique, hoping he 'didn't make too many coding or literary horrors.' Atwood acknowledges this gracious response demonstrates Petzold's good sense of humor about receiving public criticism. The post also notes the practical value of having contact information on your blog.
key_points: Charles Petzold responded to harsh public criticism by sending a signed copy of his new book with a self-deprecating inscription
The ability to accept criticism gracefully, even with humor, is a mark of professionalism
Having accessible contact information on your blog enables meaningful professional connections
Public criticism can sometimes lead to unexpected positive interactions between writers and their subjects
money_quotes: It's from one 'C. Petzold,' whoever the heck that is.
I rather harshly called Petzold's previous book 'a greyscale sea of endless text and interminable code.'
But if nothing else, it tells us that Charles Petzold has a good sense of humor.
And, indirectly, it demonstrates the value of an 'about me' page on your blog with your contact information.
