He contrasts their approach with his own preference for a single, focused online presence centered on his primary blog. Atwood argues that while there's no wrong answer, building a strong online identity requires careful focus rather than dilution across many platforms. The post reflects on the authenticity question of which online presence represents the 'real' person and concludes that strategic focus is important for distinguishing yourself online.
key_points: Veteran bloggers often maintain 20-30 different online identities across various websites, creating a fractured digital presence
It's impossible to truly keep all these websites updated, raising questions about which one represents the authentic self
Atwood prefers consolidating his identity into a single authoritative place rather than spreading across many sites
Building a strong online identity is an important strategy for professional distinction in an increasingly online world
The fragments of identity across platforms may cumulatively sum to a whole, but the core remains unclear
money_quotes: There's no way any one person could truly keep these 20 or 30 websites up to date.
So which one of these websites represents the real Laurel Krahn, the real Anil Dash?
I'm just amazed that these veteran bloggers feel they can actually maintain twenty or thirty different facets of their identity across all those disparate websites.
I struggle to write one lousy blog four to five times a week.
I'm more interested in shrinking my focus into an ever narrower and sharper point than I am in diluting my effort across dozens of different websites.
I do think building a strong online identity is an important strategy for distinguishing yourself in an increasingly online world.
posts.key_insight: A focused, consolidated online presence is more sustainable and authentic than fragmenting your identity across dozens of platforms.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains the origin and meaning behind his blog's name 'Coding Horror,' tracing it to an icon in Steve McConnell's influential book Code Complete. He describes how reading Code Complete in 1994 was a watershed moment in his career, transforming his understanding of professional software development. The key insight he gained was that becoming a professional developer means recognizing that your own code is often the problem—you are the coding horror. Atwood frames the blog not as entertainment about others' bad code, but as a humble exploration of programming topics that helps him learn while sharing that knowledge with others.
key_points: The 'Coding Horror' name comes from an icon in Steve McConnell's Code Complete, not from mocking bad code like The Daily WTF
Reading Code Complete was transformative—it showed that professional software development could be learned through research and humane guidance rather than ego
The moment you realize everything you write sucks is when you cross from amateur to professional developer
The blog exists as an exercise in learning through teaching, helping others while helping himself understand topics better
Writing and sharing knowledge transforms personal happiness into something real and meaningful
money_quotes: You're an amateur developer until you realize that everything you write sucks.
YOU are the Coding Horror.
We're all coding horrors.
I use it in times of trouble. I look into it and it shows me the source of my problems as well as the solution to my problems.
Here's this entire book about becoming a professional software developer, written in this surprisingly friendly, humane voice.
I blog to help others and also to learn. As it turns out both are aided by getting folks to actually read the stuff.
Happiness only real when shared.
posts.key_insight: True professional growth as a developer begins with the humbling realization that you yourself are the source of most coding problems, and wisdom comes from turning the critical mirror inward rather than outward.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the inherent tension between software piracy prevention and user experience, arguing that while registration keys are a necessary evil for commercial software, developers routinely make them unnecessarily painful. He traces the piracy debate back to Bill Gates' 1976 open letter to hobbyists, showing that human behavior around paying for software hasn't changed in decades. The post provides data suggesting piracy rates far exceed legitimate purchases, then pivots to practical criticism of how vendors implement registration keys poorly. Atwood concludes that since registration keys are often a user's first interaction with software, making them user-hostile creates lasting negative impressions.
key_points: Registration keys are an incomplete client-side solution to piracy, but they provide measurable data on piracy rates that would otherwise be unknowable
Without disincentives, people will naturally pay nothing for software due to its digital, impersonal nature
Human behavior around software piracy hasn't fundamentally changed since 1976 despite radical changes in computing
Common registration key mistakes include ambiguous characters, excessive length, poor formatting, and difficult entry processes
Registration keys are often the user's first experience with software, making poor implementation especially damaging to perception
money_quotes: Software is digital through and through, and yet there's one unavoidable aspect of software installation that remains thoroughly analog: entering the registration key.
I'll choose biased data over no data whatsoever, every time.
The digital nature of software makes it both easy and impersonal for people to avoid paying (note that I did not say 'steal'), which is an irresistible combination for many.
Unless you provide some disincentives, that's exactly what people will do – they'll pay nothing for your software.
Although computers have changed radically in the last thirty years, human behavior hasn't.
Quick! Is that an 'O' or an '0'? A '6' or a 'G'? An 'I' or an 'l'? A 'B' or an '8'?
The most rudimentary grasp of mathematics tells us that a conservative 10 character alphanumeric registration key is good for 197 trillion unique users.
Registration keys are often the user's first experience with our software – and first impressions matter.
posts.key_insight: Registration keys are a necessary evil that developers should implement thoughtfully, because a hostile first-run experience poisons users' perception of otherwise good software.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to concerns about Bugzilla's aging Perl codebase by arguing that code quality matters far less than working software. While acknowledging that Perl and Bugzilla haven't aged well, he points out that Bugzilla remains actively used by major open source projects like Linux, Mozilla, and Apache. He extends this argument to Joel Spolsky's controversial Wasabi language, noting that customers don't see or care about implementation details. The post concludes that programmers should focus more on user-facing concerns than internal code aesthetics.
key_points: Working software trumps beautiful code - users only care if applications function correctly, not how they're implemented
Bugzilla's success despite its aging Perl codebase proves that market adoption doesn't depend on code quality
Programmers have a natural bias toward rewriting in newer languages that may not serve customer interests
Language choice is invisible to customers - business success comes from delivering value, not technical elegance
The urge to geek out on the newest technologies should be balanced against what users actually experience
money_quotes: Users couldn't care less whether the underlying code is pretty.
All they care about is whether or not it works.
Nobody cares what your code looks like. Except for us programmers.
To the customer, the user interface is the application.
Language choice is completely invisible to potential customers.
As long as the customers are happy with the delivered application and sales are solid, who gives a damn what I – or any other programmers, for that matter – think?
We'd love any opportunity to geek out and rewrite everything in the newest, sexiest possible language.
posts.key_insight: Programmers' obsession with code aesthetics and modern languages often serves their own interests rather than their users', who only ever experience the working product.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the state of JavaScript performance in browsers circa 2007, noting that while JS performance improved hundredfold between 1996-2006, there's potential for further optimization through JIT compilation. He enthusiastically reviews Apple's new SunSpider benchmark as a well-designed tool for measuring real-world JavaScript performance. Running the benchmark across major browsers, he finds Safari performing exceptionally well, Opera strong but buggy, and Firefox surprisingly slower than IE in most areas. The post concludes that browser competition will drive JavaScript performance improvements in this increasingly important area.
key_points: JavaScript performance improved 100x between 1996-2006, enabling Web 2.0 applications
JIT compilation for JavaScript was largely unexplored frontier in 2007 browser technology
SunSpider benchmark was groundbreaking for testing real-world code rather than microbenchmarks
Safari showed surprisingly strong JavaScript performance even outside its native OS X environment
Browser market competition (Opera 9.5, Firefox 3, Safari 3, IE 8) was expected to drive performance improvements
money_quotes: Is it possible for browsers to run JavaScript significantly faster than they do today?
I've always thought that just-in-time optimizing (or even compiling) JavaScript was an unexplored frontier in browser technology.
The last time I looked for common JavaScript benchmarks, I came away deeply disappointed.
I had to use a beta version of Opera to get something other than invalid (NaN) results for this benchmark, which coincidentally summarizes my opinion of Opera. Great when it works!
If I've learned anything from the computer industry, it's that competition benefits everyone.
Here's hoping that a great JavaScript browser performance showdown spurs the browser teams on to better performance in this increasingly crucial area.
posts.key_insight: Browser competition and better benchmarking tools like SunSpider would drive the next wave of JavaScript performance improvements, moving beyond Moore's Law gains toward actual engine optimization.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the digital certificate system that powers internet security, questioning whether it actually provides meaningful protection. He points out that certificate authorities are essentially commercial operations that grant trust for a fee, with minimal verification. The certificate details presented to users are incomprehensible, even to programmers, making it impossible for average users to distinguish legitimate certificates from fraudulent ones. Using code signing as an example, he demonstrates how trivially easy it is to obtain a certificate with minimal identity verification. Atwood concludes that while certificates are better than nothing, they may ultimately provide a dangerous false sense of security.
key_points: Certificate authorities operate on a commercial model where trust is essentially purchased for $300-$2,500 with minimal identity verification
The certificate details shown to users are dense and incomprehensible, even to technical professionals
Most users don't notice security indicators like lock icons or address bar colors anyway
Obtaining a code signing certificate requires little more than emailing a driver's license copy
A false sense of security may be more dangerous than no security at all
money_quotes: The certificate authority business has always struck me as an odd relationship, because it's completely commercial and superficial.
Fork over your $300-$2,500, some nominal proof of your identity, and you're granted a certificate for a year. Does that imply trust?
I don't know about you, but none of that makes any sense to me. And I'm a programmer. Imagine the poor end user trying to make heads or tails of this.
If the only validation is an emailed copy of a drivers' license, that doesn't exactly give me the warm fuzzies.

I suppose digital certificates are better than nothing. But I also worry that they're incredibly confusing for the end user, easy to game, and ultimately provide a false sense of security – and that's the most dangerous risk of all.
posts.key_insight: Digital certificates conflate the appearance of security with actual security, creating a commercial trust system that's confusing to users and trivially easy for bad actors to exploit.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood synthesizes Steve Yegge's argument that code size is the primary enemy of software projects, using Yegge's decade-long experience maintaining 500,000 lines of Java code as a cautionary tale. The post argues that larger codebases require disproportionately more effort to maintain, and that Java's verbose, boilerplate-heavy nature exacerbates this problem. Atwood endorses the consideration of dynamic languages like Ruby, JavaScript, or Python as a way to write less code that accomplishes more. However, he warns against going too far in the other direction toward unreadable 'code golf' terseness. The core message is that developers should pursue every means possible to minimize code size while maintaining readability.
key_points: Code size is the most significant determinant of effort, cost, and schedule for software projects - a 10x larger system requires more than 10x the effort
Java's static typing and boilerplate requirements contribute to unavoidable code bloat that IDEs can manage but not eliminate
Dynamic languages like Ruby, JavaScript, and Python can potentially reduce codebase size by 50-75% compared to Java
There's a tradeoff between verbosity and understandability - minimizing code shouldn't devolve into unreadable 'Perl golf'
The best strategy is to write less code by any means necessary: smaller subprojects, iterative development, better programmers, or buying third-party code
money_quotes: Size is the enemy.
The best code is no code at all.
Java is like a variant of the game of Tetris in which none of the pieces can fill gaps created by the other pieces, so all you can do is pile them up endlessly.
If you personally write 500,000 lines of code in any language, you are so totally screwed.
Simple things should be simple, complex things should be possible.
One of the most fundamental and truly effective pieces of advice you can give a software development team – any software development team – is to write less code, by any means necessary.
Size really is the enemy. Right after ourselves, of course.
posts.key_insight: The most effective thing any development team can do is minimize the amount of code they write, because codebase size is the primary driver of complexity, cost, and eventual project failure.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his dismissive attitude toward Logo as a 'toy language' only useful for turtle graphics, then discovers it was actually a capable Lisp adaptation with robust facilities for lists, recursion, and I/O. He highlights Brian Harvey's three-volume computer science curriculum taught entirely in Logo as evidence of its depth. However, Atwood argues that while Logo deserves more respect than he gave it, the Processing environment represents its true spiritual successor for modern learners interested in graphics and visualization.
key_points: Logo was much more than turtle graphics—it was an accessible Lisp adaptation capable of teaching complete computer science curricula
Brian Harvey's three-volume book series proves Logo's capability by teaching the entirety of computer science using only Logo
Processing serves as Logo's spiritual successor, offering the same learn-by-doing philosophy with practical Java syntax
Processing deliberately prioritizes quick experimentation over software engineering orthodoxy, which is appropriate for education and prototyping
Domain-specific languages benefit from philosophies that minimize boilerplate and maximize immediate results
money_quotes: Logo is much more than the thin wrapper over turtle graphics I thought it was in 1986.
But turtle graphics still – how shall I put this? – suck.
Why force students or casual programmers to learn about graphics contexts, threading, and event handling methods before they can show something on the screen that interacts with the mouse?

The Processing project encourages a style of work that builds code quickly, understanding that either the code will be used as a quick sketch or that ideas are being tested before developing a final project.
This could be misconstrued as software engineering heresy. Perhaps we're not far from 'hacking,' but this is more appropriate for the roles in which Processing is used.
The ability to try things out quickly is a far higher priority than sophisticated code structure.
If you're truly looking for a modern Logo – leave the turtles behind and check out Processing instead.
posts.key_insight: Educational programming environments should prioritize immediate results and experimentation over architectural purity, removing barriers that prevent beginners from quickly seeing their code come to life.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that privacy is a fundamental human right, not merely something only criminals need to worry about. He reflects on his own shift from viewing privacy as overrated to recognizing it as essential to human dignity. The post examines how the proliferation of social networking and digital data collection has made 'privacy through obscurity' obsolete. Atwood cites Bruce Schneier's argument that the real debate isn't security versus privacy, but liberty versus control. He concludes that we must actively demand and protect privacy rights, even when we think we have nothing to hide, because neglecting this right effectively surrenders it entirely.
key_points: Privacy is an inherent human right essential to maintaining human dignity, not just a shield for wrongdoing
The old strategy of 'privacy through obscurity' no longer works in a world where digital artifacts can be woven into a complete tapestry of your life
The 'nothing to hide' argument is fundamentally flawed because it accepts the false premise that privacy is only about hiding wrongdoing
Widespread surveillance represents tyranny regardless of whether it comes from foreign threats or domestic authorities
Protecting privacy requires active effort; passivity amounts to surrendering the right altogether
money_quotes: Privacy is an inherent human right, and a requirement for maintaining the human condition with dignity and respect.
Only criminals have stuff to hide, right?
The real choice is liberty versus control. Tyranny, whether it arises under threat of foreign physical attack or under constant domestic authoritative scrutiny, is still tyranny.
And that's why we should champion privacy even when we have nothing to hide.
The default strategy of privacy through obscurity may have worked by default in the hodgepodge, sporadically digital worlds of the 80s and 90s. Not any more.
The rich and powerful gain some amount of privacy only because they can afford to grid their personal lives with a kind of digital body armor.
Demand privacy even if you don't think you need it.
Neglecting to protect our right to privacy is, in effect, giving up on privacy altogether.
posts.key_insight: Privacy is not about having something to hide—it's a fundamental human right that we must actively protect, because passively accepting its erosion means losing it entirely.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that PDFs are overused on the web despite their technical sophistication, creating unnecessary friction for users. While acknowledging PDF's superior layout capabilities compared to HTML, he contends that most PDF content could be presented just as effectively in HTML without the usability drawbacks. He criticizes the implicit elitism of PDF usage, where authors treat it as a status symbol signifying 'serious' content rather than choosing it for genuine technical requirements. The post exemplifies his 'worse is better' philosophy, arguing that HTML's accessibility and integration with the web ecosystem outweigh PDF's formatting advantages.
key_points: PDFs create usability problems: requiring viewer software, breaking browser navigation patterns, and needing special link annotations to warn users
Most PDF content doesn't actually utilize advanced formatting features that would justify the format choice over HTML
PDF usage often represents an implicit assertion of authority rather than a genuine technical decision
By 2008, HTML and CSS had evolved enough to handle most layout needs that previously required PDF
The web ecosystem benefits of HTML (searchability, hyperlinking, accessibility, advertising) outweigh PDF's formatting advantages
money_quotes: The advantages of PDF rarely outweigh the many disadvantages compared to plain old HTML.
A PDF is not merely a PDF; it's a statement. An implicit protest against the terrible limitations of the HTML used by the unwashed masses.
The massive inconvenience of PDF for the user rarely outweighs the minor HTML injustices righted through the power of PDF layout.
The layout better be mind-blowingly good to justify the use of the PDF format.
I'm surprised how many authors still reach for the safety blanket of PDF when they and their audience would be much better served with modern HTML.
I'll never understand how the founding editor of Wired could fall prey to such shallow PDF elitism – completely missing the obvious and inherent power of the world's HTML common denominator.
posts.key_insight: Technical sophistication matters less than user experience and ecosystem integration—choosing PDF over HTML is often an act of elitism rather than genuine technical necessity.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains the fundamental distinction between User and Kernel mode in operating systems, concepts enforced at the CPU hardware level. Kernel mode grants unrestricted hardware access but makes crashes catastrophic, while User mode isolates code to prevent system-wide failures. He explores the trade-offs in where device drivers should run, noting that video drivers historically needed kernel-mode performance but Vista began splitting them. The post examines how mode transitions are expensive, affecting everything from exception handling to webserver architecture, where both IIS and Linux servers moved code to kernel mode for benchmark performance. Atwood concludes with characteristic humor that User mode represents 'progress' by upgrading us from computers that crash catastrophically to ones that merely crash all the time.
key_points: User and Kernel modes are enforced by CPU hardware through protection rings, not just software conventions
The trade-off between performance and stability determines where code like device drivers should run
Mode transitions between User and Kernel are expensive, which is why exceptions slow down software
Webserver benchmark wars drove both Microsoft and Linux to move static content serving into kernel mode
Virtualization created Ring -1 below kernel mode, redefining the protection hierarchy
money_quotes: These two modes aren't mere labels; they're enforced by the CPU hardware.
Instead of your entire system crashing, only that particular application crashes. That's the value of User mode.
Do these drivers run in Kernel mode, for maximum performance, or do they run in User mode, for maximum stability?
Transitioning between User and Kernel mode is expensive. Really expensive.
User mode is clearly a net public good, but it comes at a cost.

The CPU's strict segregation of code between User and Kernel mode is completely transparent to most of us, but it is quite literally the difference between a computer that crashes all the time and a computer that crashes catastrophically all the time.
This is what we extra-crashy-code-writing programmers like to call 'progress.'
posts.key_insight: User mode's isolation from hardware is the invisible foundation that lets modern computers survive the buggy code we all write, trading some performance for the ability to crash gracefully rather than catastrophically.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares the detailed build of his home racing simulation rig, demonstrating how geeks can indulge expensive hobbies at a fraction of the cost. The setup combines a Playseat Evolution, Logitech G25 racing wheel with clutch and shifter, bass shakers for tactile feedback, and an 8-foot projection screen. He emphasizes practical choices like the G25's plug-and-play compatibility with the Playseat and the clever use of bass shakers to extend force feedback throughout the body. The post celebrates the intersection of programming passion and physics simulation, noting that accurate racing sims provide a fascinating playground for understanding real-world physics.
key_points: The complete rig costs under $2,000 total, compared to $100,000 for a real sports car
Bass shakers convert low-end audio to tactile rumbling, providing body-wide force feedback at minimal cost
The Playseat Evolution and Logitech G25 are designed to work together with matching mounting holes
For PC specs, only the video card and sound card matter significantly for racing simulations
Racing simulations appeal to programmers because they're physics playgrounds with mathematically precise behavior
money_quotes: One advantage of being a geek is that our habits – as such habits go – are not terribly expensive.
Built a nifty racing simulation rig that delivers many of the same thrills at a tiny fraction of the price.
Bass shakers are a clever, if decidedly low-tech, way to extend the sophisticated force feedback effects of the wheel to the rest of your body.
The bang for the buck is off the charts.
PCs are so cheap these days, it's hardly worth listing the hardware specs.
Simulation, by definition, needs to be accurate. Otherwise, well, it's not simulating reality, really, which is of course the idea of simulation.
There's something about the programmer in me that delights in the physics playground afforded by these simulations.
It certainly won't get you as much attention from the opposite sex as a real sports car would. But it's still a heck of a lot of fun, nonetheless.
posts.key_insight: Geeks can satisfy expensive hobbies affordably through clever DIY builds that leverage the physics simulation capabilities that make programming and gaming intellectually satisfying.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software developers, like magpies attracted to shiny objects, are too easily distracted by the constant stream of new technologies, tools, and frameworks. He documents his own fatigue with 'ultimate tool lists' and the relentless pace of change, citing other tech voices who have similarly abandoned the pursuit of the newest platforms. The post critiques how 'elite developers' drive adoption cycles that leave most programmers perpetually behind, creating ghost towns of abandoned technologies. Atwood concludes that users don't care about technology choices—they care about working software—and developers should resist letting the pursuit of novelty become their goal.
key_points: The relentless pace of new technologies creates fatigue rather than inspiration, making 'shiny' and 'new' feel mundane
Elite developers drive adoption cycles by fleeing to new languages when their current ones become popular, leaving regular developers constantly playing catch-up
Most programmers haven't experienced dynamic languages at all, yet thought leaders have already moved on from Ruby
Users care about working software, not whether it's built with cutting-edge technology or COBOL
There is no grand unified framework—the industry fails when it tries to create one, and pragmatic developers succeed by using what works
money_quotes: We are too easily distracted by shiny new toys and playthings.
Eventually, you grow weary of the endless procession of shiny new things.
I officially renounce the search for the newer and shinier.
The magpie developer sometimes loves change to the detriment of his own craft.
Users don't care whether you use J2EE, Cobol, or a pair of magic rocks.
We fail (as an industry) when we try to come up with the all-singing, all-dancing applications framework to end all applications frameworks.
Who cares what technology you use, as long as it works, and both you and your users are happy with it?
posts.key_insight: The pursuit of new technology should serve the goal of building working software, not become a goal in itself that distracts developers from their craft.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that application developers should never create files or folders in users' personal Documents folders without explicit permission. He compares the user space folder structures in Windows XP and Vista, noting how Vista's cleaner approach mirrors Unix conventions. Citing Apple's developer guidelines as a model, he emphasizes that user directories should only be modified by users themselves. Applications should store their files in designated AppData or Application Support directories instead. The post is a call to action for developers to respect the boundary between system/application space and personal user space.
key_points: User space (Documents folder) should be sacred territory that only the user modifies
Applications should store their files in AppData or Application Support directories, not in user documents
Vista's folder structure represents an improvement over XP, moving closer to Unix conventions
Apple's developer guidelines explicitly prohibit polluting user space and provide a good model to follow
Even sample files or clip art should go in Library/Application Support, not user directories
money_quotes: Keep your dirty, filthy paws out of my personal user space!
Applications should never create or modify anything in your documents folder without your permission.
Those who do not understand Unix are condemned to reinvent it, poorly.
We should be mad as hell, and we shouldn't take it any more.
These directories should only be modified by the user.
It is our obligation as programmers to let the user's folders belong to the user.
Do the responsible thing and store your application files in an appropriate location, not as virtual litter amongst the user's other files.
posts.key_insight: Applications that dump files into users' personal folders are violating a fundamental trust boundary, and developers have an ethical obligation to store application data in designated system locations instead.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software project success is determined far more by interpersonal dynamics than by technology choices. Drawing on Gerald Weinberg's famous observation that 'it's always a people problem,' he contends that the quality of relationships within a development team is the most accurate predictor of both job satisfaction and project success. He proposes that whether you genuinely like your coworkers is as critical a question as technical considerations when assessing project viability. The post concludes with a challenge: if you're not working with people who inspire you, what's stopping you from changing that?
key_points: Software development is still a young, primitive field prone to boom-bust cycles around supposed silver bullets
Agile methodologies succeed because they focus on people working together rather than on technology
Whether you like your coworkers is as valid a predictor of project success as codebase size or software type
Happy, healthy, socially functional development teams essentially never fail—but such teams are rare
Process and people issues, not technology, are what most often stop projects cold
money_quotes: No matter what the problem is, it's always a people problem.
The people you choose to work with are the most accurate predictor of job satisfaction I've ever found.
I have never seen a happy, healthy, gelled, socially functional software development team fail. It's a shame such teams are so rare.
The fastest way to get stuck is to think that it's all about the technology and to believe that you can ram your way through the other things.
Do you like the company of your teammates on a personal level? Do you respect your teammates professionally?
If you aren't working with people you like, people you respect, people that challenge and inspire you – then why not? What's stopping you?
posts.key_insight: Technical excellence matters far less than team chemistry—the people you work with determine whether your project succeeds or fails.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood celebrates Donald Knuth's 70th birthday by compiling tributes to the legendary computer scientist and reflecting on his enduring influence. The post highlights Knuth's major contributions: The Art of Computer Programming, TeX, and the foundational concept of algorithm analysis. Atwood connects Knuth's serious academic work with his playful side, revealing that a teenage Knuth published a satirical weights-and-measures system in Mad Magazine in 1957. The post argues that computer science, while mathematically rigorous, should also be fun—a balance Knuth exemplifies throughout his career.
key_points: Donald Knuth is arguably the most famous living computer scientist, whose Art of Computer Programming series and TeX typesetting system remain dominant decades later
Knuth pioneered the mathematical analysis of algorithms, one of the most important foundations of computer science theory
TeX's 'glue-and-boxes' model is praised as a masterful example of capturing complex problems with simple models
Knuth's dedication to perfection, regardless of time required, stands in contrast to modern conference-driven academic culture
As a high schooler, Knuth published the satirical 'Potrzebie System of Weights and Measures' in Mad Magazine, demonstrating his lifelong blend of rigor and humor
money_quotes: I don't know of any other software other than TeX implemented in the 1970s that remains absolutely and unquestionably dominant in its domain.

He was one of the first people to realize that an algorithm can be analyzed in a mathematical and intelligent way without running it. This is one of the most important starting points for computer science theory.
His perfect artisanship became the most refined of fine arts.
Don Knuth straddled both worlds effortlessly, gaining respect from 15 year old hackers and 50 year old researchers alike. And that's the most tremendous feat of all.
He has a habit of parrying all the really difficult questions with humor; indeed, he does this so often one comes to suspect humor is his answer.
Programming algorithms are hard science, backed by some serious math. Thanks for the reminder, Mr. Knuth, that computer science is indeed serious stuff, but it's also a lot of fun.
posts.key_insight: The greatest computer scientists combine mathematical rigor with playfulness and humor, reminding us that programming is both serious science and enduring art.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that computer science education fails students by neglecting practical software engineering skills like deployment and source control in favor of pure theory. He advocates for curricula that simulate real-world conditions, requiring students to use source control for every line of code and experience the pain of actual deployment. While acknowledging the importance of theoretical foundations like algorithms, he contends that the current imbalance leaves graduates unprepared for professional software development. Atwood endorses internships as a necessary supplement and supports Joel Spolsky's vision of a 'Julliard for programmers' focused on practical studio work.
key_points: Deployment and source control are fundamental skills that most CS programs completely ignore, leaving graduates unprepared
Students should develop software under real-world conditions including source control, actual deployment, and user bug reports
The pain and frustration of real software development is educational and should not be shielded from students
Theory remains important but should not come at the expense of practical engineering skills
Internships currently fill the gap but forcing students to rely on them represents a failure of formal education
money_quotes: If we aren't teaching fundamental software engineering skills like deployment and source control in college today, we're teaching computer science the wrong way.
Source control is the very bedrock of software engineering.

Will this be painful? Boy, oh boy, will it ever. It'll be excruciating. Students will hate it. They'll begin to question why anyone in their right mind would want to write software. Welcome to the real world.
Half of software engineering is pain mitigation.
If users can't get past the install step, none of the code you've written matters!

If you aren't cursing your web hosting provider every week, fighting with your source control system every day, deciphering angry bug reports from your users every hour – you aren't being taught computer science.
Today's computer science students should develop software under conditions as close as possible to the real world, or the best available approximation thereof.
posts.key_insight: True computer science education must include the unglamorous, painful realities of software development—deployment struggles, source control battles, and angry user bug reports—because half of software engineering is pain mitigation.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his 2008 everyday carry (EDC) keychain setup, continuing an annual tradition of documenting his pocket essentials. He carries a Leatherman Squirt S4 multitool, an 8GB Corsair Flash Voyager thumb drive, and a Fenix L0D-CE LED flashlight. The post reflects on how thumb drive capacities have grown exponentially while emphasizing that speed matters more than size at higher capacities. He expresses particular amazement at LED flashlight technology advances, noting that a single AAA-powered LED now outperforms traditional D-cell Maglites.
key_points: The Leatherman Squirt S4 has been a constant EDC item since 2005 due to its versatility in a compact form factor
USB thumb drive capacity doubled yearly (512MB to 8GB over four years), but transfer speed becomes the limiting factor at higher capacities
Modern LED flashlight technology produces more light from one AAA battery than old Maglites produced from three D-cells
There's often a tradeoff between size and performance - tiny thumb drives tend to be slower, forcing compromises
The internet has communities dedicated to optimizing everyday carry items, treating it as both art and science
money_quotes: In terms of strip-mining an obsession, the internet delivers.
If you're open to carrying a small multitool, I recommend the Squirt without reservation.
What good is a gigantic 16 GB thumb drive if it takes you an hour to transfer your data?
All the svelte, sexy, impossibly tiny USB thumb drives inevitably come with a hefty speed penalty.
Be sure to consider speed when buying your next high capacity USB drive, or like me, you may end up disappointed.
The L0D-CE produces more overall light than a 3-D cell Maglite. All this from one AAA cell.
I had no idea LED technology was advancing so rapidly.
posts.key_insight: As technology improves, the limiting factor often shifts from the obvious metric (storage capacity, brightness) to secondary concerns (transfer speed, battery life) that only become apparent through actual use.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares five essential browser keyboard shortcuts that he uses constantly despite not memorizing comprehensive shortcut lists. He argues that since the browser is the most-used application for most people, mastering a few key shortcuts—launching the browser, navigating to the address bar (Alt+D), accessing the search box (Ctrl+E), opening in new tabs (Alt+Enter), and using middle-click for tab management—can dramatically improve the browsing experience. He responds to a reader's suggestion about making links open in new tabs by pointing out this functionality already exists via middle-click. The post emphasizes that sharing 'obvious' knowledge is valuable because not everyone knows what you know.
key_points: A small set of frequently-used shortcuts provides more value than memorizing comprehensive lists
The browser deserves a dedicated keyboard shortcut for launching since it's the most-used application
Alt+D (address bar) and Ctrl+E (search box) work identically in Windows Vista File Explorer, showing how keyboard conventions can transfer across contexts
Middle-click is a powerful but underutilized convention for opening and closing tabs
Sharing basic knowledge isn't insulting—it's valuable because knowledge gaps exist everywhere
money_quotes: I spend more time in my browser than any other single application on my computer.
I only know a handful of web browser keyboard shortcuts, myself, and I probably use the same five shortcuts a hundred times a day.
Launching such a commonly used application should be completely frictionless.
I'm no keyboard purist. I promote fully two-handed computer usage, whether those two hands are tapping away on the keyboard or split between the keyboard and the mouse.
If the middle mouse button giveth, the middle mouse button can also taketh away.
I apologize if you feel I've insulted your intelligence with such basic shortcuts. But realize that not everyone knows what you know.
posts.key_insight: Mastering just five browser shortcuts you'll use constantly is far more valuable than memorizing dozens you'll forget, and sharing such 'basic' knowledge matters because not everyone knows what you know.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on typography as a bridge between engineering and design, sparked by visiting MoMA's Helvetica exhibit. He explores how Helvetica became a cultural touchstone while Arial emerged as Microsoft's cheaper knockoff. The post highlights Donald Knuth's decade-long quest to create TeX, showing how even legendary computer scientists become obsessed with typography. Atwood recommends 'Thinking with Type' as essential reading and traces his own font fascination back to pirate software crack screens on the Apple II.
key_points: Helvetica's influence on design culture is so significant it warranted both a MoMA exhibit and a documentary film
Arial exists because Microsoft chose the cheaper licensing option over Helvetica, spreading an inferior typeface worldwide
Donald Knuth's 'one-year hiatus' to improve typesetting became a ten-year project that produced TeX
Typography represents a rare intersection where engineers and designers share common ground and passion
Even primitive low-resolution fonts from the 1980s could convey style and feeling with minimal pixels
money_quotes: Helvetica – a humble font, nothing more than a collection of mathematical curves shaped into letterforms – had a huge impact on the world.
Arial is a tawdry, inferior knock-off of Helvetica, but which, to the detriment of the world, Microsoft chose to license for Windows simply because it was cheaper.
Typography and fonts are a rare and vital intersection point between software engineers and designers.
I wanted to try to capture the intelligence of the design, not just the outcome of the design.
This is not a book about fonts. It is a book about how to use them.
Even back in the mid-80s, these primitive fonts added a particular style, a feeling, an intonation to the text – and we only had a dismal little 280 x 192 screen to work with.
posts.key_insight: Typography serves as one of the few domains where the analytical mindset of engineers and the aesthetic sensibilities of designers naturally converge and collaborate.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces his upcoming keynote at CUSEC 2008, a Canadian university software engineering conference. He expresses genuine humility about being included alongside distinguished speakers like Tim Bray, Zed Shaw, and Dr. Jeffrey Ullman, noting that many of them have their own Wikipedia entries. The post highlights CUSEC's unique position as a student-organized, student-focused conference that has featured impressive speakers since its inception. Atwood also shares his first impressions of Canada, quoting William Gibson's observation about Canadian cities.
key_points: CUSEC is the only software-specific conference targeting students, organized by students from universities across Canada
The conference has attracted notable speakers including Dave Thomas, Kathy Sierra, Kent Beck, and David Heinemeier Hansson
Atwood expresses self-deprecating humor about being included among such distinguished company
This was Atwood's first visit to Canada, and he found it refreshingly different from American culture
His keynote was titled 'Is Writing More Important Than Programming?'
money_quotes: It's hard to shake the feeling that one of these things is not like the others, but I suppose the conference organizers had their reasons for inviting me, however crazy those reasons may seem to me.
How distinguished? Many of these folks have their own Wikipedia entries!
Canadian cities look the way American cities do on television.
It's too bad there isn't an equivalent student-run conference for American computer science students.
I'm enjoying Montreal and the Canadian perspective on life tremendously.
posts.key_insight: Student-organized technical conferences provide unique value by focusing on what matters to emerging developers rather than just professional concerns, while still attracting industry luminaries.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the primary goal of any presentation should be to entertain the audience, not just to inform them. He draws on Sesame Street's 38-year success as the archetypal example of 'edutainment' - combining entertainment with education. The show's creators discovered that mixing fantasy elements with reality and using quick, commercial-like segments kept viewers engaged. Atwood contends that this principle applies universally: if you don't entertain your audience first, they won't remember anything you said. Even if a presentation fails to fully educate, at least entertaining delivery ensures the audience's time wasn't completely wasted.
key_points: Every slide should provoke a reaction - whether controversial, unexpected, amusing, or thought-provoking
Entertainment is the prerequisite for learning; you 'trick' audiences into learning once they're engaged
Sesame Street's success (4,160 episodes over 38 seasons) validates the edutainment approach
Mixing fantasy elements with reality keeps audiences interested, even when delivering serious messages
A boring presentation wastes everyone's time, but an entertaining one that fails to educate still provides value
money_quotes: Every slide of your presentation should serve this fundamental vision statement. Is it entertaining?
Once your audience has engaged with your presentation, that's when you trick them into learning.
Deliver a series of short, sharp shocks that jolt your audience into a heightened state of engagement.
If the audience doesn't walk out of your presentation thinking 'gee, that was fun!' then I can practically guarantee they'll remember little about you or your talk.
There's nothing more stultifying than walking out of yet another interminable, droning presentation to realize that all you have to show for it is another hour of your life ticked away.
A simple dose of cartoon-like characters lets the humans deliver messages without causing viewers to lose interest.
posts.key_insight: Entertainment isn't optional decoration for presentations - it's the prerequisite that makes learning possible, because an unengaged audience learns nothing regardless of content quality.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the single-item clipboard in modern operating systems is an inexcusable limitation given today's hardware capabilities. He presents data showing that paste is the most-used command in Word 2003, accounting for over 11% of all commands, yet the OS clipboard remains stuck in a primitive state. Rather than accepting that individual applications must implement their own clipboard history solutions, Atwood advocates for OS-level improvements. He recommends ClipX as a solution and identifies three key features any modern clipboard should have: history, persistence across reboots, and a visual browser for selecting past items.
key_points: The single-item clipboard is an anachronism that hasn't evolved despite massive hardware improvements over the years
Paste is the most frequently used command in Word 2003, making clipboard functionality critically important to productivity
Applications like Visual Studio and Office implementing their own clipboard queues is a workaround for OS-level deficiencies
Even smartphones need clipboard functionality, as highlighted in early iPhone reviews
The three essential clipboard improvements are: history, persistence, and a visual browser
money_quotes: In this era of 3 GHz processors, 1 GB memory, and 500 GB hard drives, why is the Windows clipboard only capable of holding a single item?
For most users the default settings are the only settings.
I've learned that if the defaults aren't reasonably close to correct out of the box, then the software is probably doomed anyway.
We live in a copypasta culture.

It always seemed strange to me that applications had to implement their own oddball per-app clipboard queues to spackle over deficiencies in the operating system's braindead 'I can only remember one thing at a time' clipboard implementation.

Your clipboard history is dynamically saved to disk and will survive a reboot, so you can begin to rely on your clipboard as a sort of quick and dirty digital scrapbook. Isn't that how it should have been all along?
It's a mystery to me why none of the major operating systems have bothered improving the clipboard.
posts.key_insight: Operating systems have neglected fundamental productivity tools like the clipboard, forcing users and applications to work around limitations that should have been fixed at the platform level years ago.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood addresses the challenge of effectively screening software developer candidates via phone interviews, drawing heavily from Steve Yegge's influential guide. The post argues that a failed phone screen wastes enormous time and resources, making it crucial to filter out unqualified candidates before expensive in-person interviews. Atwood outlines five essential areas to probe: basic coding ability, object-oriented design, scripting and regular expressions, data structures, and bits/bytes fundamentals. The goal isn't to find perfect candidates but to identify total knowledge vacuums that disqualify someone from the role. He emphasizes that interviewers should drive the conversation and push candidates beyond their comfort zones rather than letting them recite past accomplishments.
key_points: Phone screen failures are extremely costly - each unqualified candidate reaching in-person interviews wastes at least 8 man-hours
Interviewers should control the conversation and push candidates into unfamiliar territory rather than letting them drone about past work
The goal is identifying complete knowledge gaps, not perfection - struggling and needing hints is acceptable
'One-trick ponies' who only know one language and claim ignorance of everything else are major red flags
A shocking percentage of candidates (25-35%) cannot solve basic scripting problems even with an hour and heavy hints
money_quotes: It is very expensive to get the phone screen wrong – a giant waste of time for everyone involved.
Don't let the candidate drive the interview.
Candidates who only know one particular language or programming environment, and protest complete ignorance of everything else, are a giant red warning flag.
What I'm looking for here is a total vacuum in one of these areas.
It's OK if they struggle a little and then figure it out. It's OK if they need some minor hints or prompting.
It's a bit hard to comprehend, like a potential truck driver somehow not being able to find the gas pedal or shift gears.
Computers don't have ten fingers, they have one. So people need to know this stuff.
Each time an unqualified candidate makes it to the hands-on test, you should be asking yourself – how could we have eliminated this candidate in the phone screen?
posts.key_insight: A rigorous phone screen that probes five fundamental areas of programming knowledge can save enormous time and resources by filtering out candidates who lack basic competencies before they reach expensive in-person interviews.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on 37signals launching Ta-da List with just 579 lines of Ruby code, using it as a jumping-off point for thinking about minimalist software development. He acknowledges the asterisk—Rails framework code, stylesheets, JavaScript, and HTML aren't counted—but still finds the achievement thought-provoking. The post challenges developers to consider how much they could accomplish if they committed to extreme constraints, treating 600 lines as a forcing function for simplicity.
key_points: 37signals launched a commercial product (Ta-da List) in 579 lines of Ruby code in 2005
The line count excludes the entire Rails framework, stylesheets, JavaScript, and HTML that made it possible
Embracing platform constraints can dramatically reduce the code needed for useful products
A 600-line limit serves as a thought experiment for prioritizing essential functionality
The question isn't just about lines of code but about how many more ideas could become reality with minimalist constraints
money_quotes: I've written stored procedures that are longer than that.
My current project has more lines of configuration than that.
What is the absolute minimum amount of code I could write that would make those ideas work?
How many more cool/useful things could you build if you promised yourself that each one would only be 600 lines of code?
Think of it as an exercise in minimalism.
Does your preferred language or environment allow you the freedom to create something interesting and useful with that constraint in place?
posts.key_insight: Imposing an artificial constraint like 600 lines of code forces you to ruthlessly prioritize what matters, potentially unlocking the ability to ship more ideas as working products.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that Apple's hardware-software model effectively turns Macs into expensive dongles that authenticate the right to run OS X, comparing this to the console gaming model. He explores why consumers don't seem to care about 'Freedom Zero' - the freedom to run software for any purpose on any hardware - despite advocates like Mark Pilgrim warning about proprietary lock-in. Atwood acknowledges the tension between the convenience of closed ecosystems ('it just works') and the philosophical importance of software freedom. He cites Jaron Lanier's argument that closed development has produced more innovative products like the iPhone, while open source has mainly produced 'polished copies.' The post ultimately leaves the question open: why do consumers consistently choose convenience over freedom?
key_points: When you buy a Mac, you're essentially buying a hardware dongle that authenticates your right to run Apple software - the most expensive copy protection scheme in computing
The console model succeeds because it eliminates complexity: no oddball hardware, incompatible drivers, or combinatorial configuration explosions
Freedom Zero - the right to run software for any purpose in perpetuity - matters philosophically but has gained no traction with mainstream consumers
Closed, 'tyrannically managed' development shops like Apple have produced more radical innovations (iPhone) than open source movements, which tend to create polished copies of existing designs
All non-Free software may be a 'dead end' in the long run, but those dead ends can still solve very real problems for users along the way
money_quotes: When you buy a new Mac, you're buying a giant hardware dongle that allows you to run OS X software.
There's nothing harder to copy than an entire MacBook.
People buy consoles like the Xbox 360 and Wii because they work with a minimum of fuss.
Outside of Planet Debian and my own personal echo chamber, nobody gives a sh*t about Freedom 0.
In the long run, the utility of all non-Free software approaches zero. All non-Free software is a dead end.
You hand the vendor a pile of cash and they allow you to do a handful of specific things with their device, for only so long as they're inclined to do so.
An honest empiricist must conclude that while the open approach has been able to create lovely, polished copies, it hasn't been so good at creating notable originals.
posts.key_insight: The market has decisively answered the freedom vs. convenience debate: consumers will consistently trade software freedom for products that 'just work,' regardless of the philosophical implications.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood presents Jamie Zawinski's blunt, no-nonsense backup strategy as the gold standard for protecting your data. The approach is elegantly simple: buy two external hard drives, use rsync for daily incremental backups, and keep one drive off-site for disaster recovery. Atwood endorses this KISS philosophy while offering Windows-specific alternatives like Acronis True Image for those who prefer GUI tools. The post emphasizes that backup complexity isn't the enemy—not having any backup strategy is. Whether you use command-line tools, commercial software, or cloud services, the critical point is to actually implement something before disaster strikes.
key_points: Multiple hard drives with incremental backups represent the most effective and accessible backup strategy for most users
A solid backup approach requires three elements: a simple repeatable process, daily routine execution, and off-site storage
The universe tends toward maximum irony—when your backup drive fails, replace it immediately before touching your computer again
Drive imaging software like Acronis provides Windows users with GUI-based rsync-equivalent functionality including bootable mirrors
Internet-based backup services offer viable alternatives, but having any backup strategy beats having none
money_quotes: When (WHEN) your internal drive scorches itself, you can just take your backup drive and put it in your computer and go.
The universe tends toward maximum irony. Don't push it.
OMG, three drives is so expensive! That sounds like a hassle! Shut up. I know things. You will listen to me. Do it anyway.
One thing's for sure: until you have a backup strategy of some kind, you're screwed, you just don't know it yet.
If backing up your data sounds like a hassle, that's because it is.
Don't wait until the weekend when you have time, do it now, before you so much as touch your computer again. Do it before goddamned breakfast.
If you have no possessions, you have nothing to lose.
posts.key_insight: The best backup strategy is the one you'll actually follow consistently—complexity is the enemy of execution, so keep it simple and do it anyway.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses Steve Martin's comedy philosophy to reframe the 'worse is better' debate in software development. Martin's insight that 'it was easy to be great' but 'hard to be good, consistently good, night after night' parallels the software engineering tension between ambitious perfection and reliable simplicity. Atwood traces Richard Gabriel's famous 'worse is better' essay, noting that even its author has vacillated on whether the principle holds true. The post connects Martin's lesson that greatness emerges from disciplined, consistent goodness to the software principle that minimal, incrementally improved systems often outperform grand architectural visions. Atwood concludes that rather than choosing sides in the worse-is-better debate, developers should focus on being consistently good and let greatness follow naturally.
key_points: Greatness is accidental and statistical, but consistent goodness requires hard work and discipline
The 'worse is better' philosophy suggests starting minimal and growing incrementally rather than building grand systems upfront
Even Richard Gabriel, who coined 'worse is better,' has written contradictory papers arguing both sides over the years
Steve Martin's comedy philosophy—that striving for reliable goodness beats chasing abstract greatness—applies directly to software development
Enough goodness slowly accreted over time usually trumps epic plans to create something great from scratch
money_quotes: It was easy to be great. Every entertainer has a night when everything is clicking.
What was hard was to be good, consistently good, night after night, no matter what the circumstances.
Being good – consistently good – is the real goal, and that takes hard work and discipline.
Like Steve Martin, you become great through applying yourself at being reliably good, night after night, venue after venue, time after time.
Great is the enemy of good.
Enough goodness slowly accreted over time usually trumps any epic (and usually ill-fated) plans to create any brand new great thing.
Strive to be consistently good, and the greatness takes care of itself.
posts.key_insight: Greatness in software (and comedy) isn't achieved by aiming for perfection, but emerges naturally from the disciplined practice of being consistently good over time.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that users consistently misrepresent their actual behavior, buying products for features they'll never use and claiming to love software they actually find frustrating. Drawing on Heidi Adkisson's research showing users aspire to use premium features but default to basics, he connects this to the 1987 'Paradox of the Active User' concept. Atwood invokes the TV character Dr. House's maxim 'Everybody lies' to explain that users lie primarily to themselves about their intentions and capabilities. The conclusion is that software designers must observe actual user behavior rather than trust self-reported preferences, since actions—especially those involving money—reveal truth more reliably than words.
key_points: Users buy products for aspirational features they never actually use, paying premiums for capabilities they'll never invest time to learn
The 'Paradox of the Active User' explains that users refuse to read manuals or learn systems properly, even when it would save them time long-term
People lie not from malice but from self-deception—their stated intentions diverge wildly from their actual behavior
Behavioral observation, not user surveys or feedback, should drive software design decisions
Economic thinking and studying actual transactions reveals truth because actions involving money are the most carefully considered
money_quotes: Ultimately, their aspirations may be different than the reality.
Observation is a powerful skill, and so is learning to disregard what people tell you in favor of judging them by their actions.
No actions are more carefully considered than those that result in money flowing out of your pocket.
People lie not because they're all evil liars, but because they're usually lying to themselves in some way.
Users never read manuals but start using the software immediately.
We must design for the way users actually behave.
Every user lies.
Observe whether or not they use the software, and how they use it. Rely on that behavioral data to design your software, and not the lies of your users, however well intentioned they may be.
posts.key_insight: Design software based on observed user behavior, not stated preferences, because users consistently deceive themselves about how they'll actually use products.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that databases deserve the same rigorous version control treatment as application code, yet many development teams inexplicably neglect this practice. He points out that deploying a new version of an application against an outdated database schema results in a broken application. The post references K. Scott Allen's five-part series on database version control philosophy and practice. Atwood emphasizes that the specific tools matter less than actually implementing version control for databases, citing the Agile Manifesto's principle that individuals and interactions trump processes and tools.
key_points: Databases are often treated as second or third-class citizens when it comes to version control, even by teams rigorous about code versioning
Application and database deployments must be synchronized - version 2.0 of an app against version 1.0 of a database equals a broken application
The specific tools used for database version control matter less than actually doing it
K. Scott Allen's series covers the full spectrum: baseline creation, change scripts, stored procedures, branching and merging
money_quotes: When I ask development teams whether their database is under version control, I usually get blank stares.
If you deploy version 2.0 of your application against version 1.0 of your database, what do you get? A broken application, that's what.
Your database should always be under source control, right next to your application code.
I don't know how you can call yourself a software engineer and maintain a straight face when your database isn't under exactly the same rigorous level of source control as the rest of your code.
It doesn't matter what tools you use – per the agile manifesto, individuals and interactions are more important than processes and tools.
posts.key_insight: A database without version control is just as inexcusable as application code without version control, and professional software engineers should treat both with equal rigor.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on a sealed Apple //c selling on eBay for $2,553, noting this price almost exactly matches the inflation-adjusted original 1984 retail price of $1,299. He uses this as a springboard to discuss how prohibitively expensive early Macintosh computers were, costing over $5,000 in today's dollars. Atwood shares his personal history of choosing an Amiga 1000 over a Mac due to cost constraints. He argues that modern pundits forget how exclusive Macs once were, accessible only to upper-middle-class families, and that pricing parity only came after Apple adopted commodity PC technology and Intel CPUs.
key_points: A sealed 1984 Apple //c sold for $2,553, nearly matching its inflation-adjusted original price of $2,670
Original Macintosh computers cost $5,100-$5,700 in 2007 dollars, making them inaccessible to most families
Apple's adoption of commodity PC technology and Intel CPUs eventually brought Macs to pricing parity with PCs
Personal computing history often involved choosing alternatives like the Amiga due to Mac's prohibitive cost
Modern Macs are competitively priced because they essentially are well-designed commodity PCs
money_quotes: The $1,299 that an Apple //c originally sold for in 1984 should cost about $2,670 in 2007 dollars, after factoring for inflation.
Expressed in 2007 dollars, that's $5,100 and $5,700 respectively.
Like all young geeks, I was instantly enamored with the advanced capabilities of the Mac line, but it was financially beyond the reach of my family.
I'm not sure I knew anyone that owned one of the original Macs; that's how far outside the realm of possibility they were, at least in the social circles we moved in.
For a very long time, Macs were the exclusive province of the upper middle class.
Today's Macs are quite competitively priced, largely because they are (well designed) commodity PCs.
posts.key_insight: Modern critics forget that early Macs were luxury items costing over $5,000 in today's dollars, and Apple only achieved competitive pricing by embracing commodity PC hardware.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts his costly mistake of not understanding Xbox 360's DRM model before purchasing downloadable content at work, only to discover he couldn't easily transfer it to his home console. He explains that Xbox 360 content is tied to both the hardware signature and the Xbox Live profile, creating a two-dongle system that severely limits portability. After finding Microsoft's memory card solution impractical, he was forced to repurchase $140.50 worth of songs he already owned. He argues this experience is a preview of computing's future, where locked-down devices like the Xbox 360 and iPhone represent the new normal for consumer technology.
key_points: Xbox 360 DRM ties purchased content to both the specific console hardware and the Xbox Live profile, requiring one or the other to access content
Hardware failure creates severe user penalties since replacement consoles have different hardware signatures, breaking offline access to purchased content
The future of computing likely involves fixed-function devices with tightly controlled hardware and commercially locked-down delivery networks
Understanding DRM mechanics before purchasing is essential because recovery options are limited and expensive
Console economics have always relied on overpriced accessories, but digital content licensing adds new complexity to the cost of ownership
money_quotes: For better or worse, products like the iPhone and Xbox 360 represent the future of computing.
Hawking overpriced accessories is just the way the console economy works.
There can only ever be one valid physical copy of an Xbox Live profile at any given time.
Microsoft has every right to protect their content, but to punish those who have had their consoles replaced due to failure is unacceptable.
I broke down and re-purchased 11,240 MS Points worth of Guitar Hero 2, Guitar Hero 3, and Rock Band songs through my personal Xbox Live profile on my home Xbox 360.
DRM sucks, but it's unavoidable and arguably the future, in the form of ubiquitous consumer devices like the Xbox 360 and iPhone.
I'm not asking you to like it. Nobody likes it. But at the very least understand how it works.
DRM ignorance is expensive.
posts.key_insight: As consumer technology moves toward locked-down devices with restrictive DRM, users must understand licensing mechanics before purchasing or face expensive consequences when their assumptions prove wrong.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents his quest to improve a marginal WiFi connection for his Xbox 360, which was barely reaching one bar of signal strength across 50 feet and multiple walls. He explores the world of aftermarket WiFi antennas, discovering that wireless networking is more art than science due to countless environmental variables. After researching options including DIY cantennas and commercial solutions, he upgrades his router antenna to a Hawking HAI7MD model. The result is a 5x improvement in connection strength, transforming a barely-functional connection into a rock solid one suitable for media streaming.
key_points: Wireless networking has so many variables (physical layout, environment, interference, firmware) that it's nearly impossible to predict if an antenna will work until you test it in your specific environment
DIY waveguide cantennas can outperform commercial designs by up to 9 dB (8x power increase) when built according to waveguide theory
Before upgrading antennas, you must verify your router has a removable antenna and identify the correct connector type (commonly RP-SMA or RP-TNC)
Customer reviews for WiFi antennas vary wildly, reflecting the complexity of wireless environments rather than product quality
A relatively modest antenna upgrade ($38) can transform a completely marginal connection into a reliable one
money_quotes: Wireless networking feels more like an art than a science.
It's almost impossible to tell if an antenna will work until you buy it and run network strength tests in your particular environment.
Every three dB is a doubling in power – that's three doublings (8x increase)!
If you can eat a big can of stew, you can make a high performance antenna.
Customer reviews are all over the map on these things, which says more about the immense number of variables in wireless networking than it does about the antennas themselves.
Upgrading my antenna increased my wireless networking connection strength by about 5x.
A better antenna can make the difference between a completely marginal connection and a rock solid connection.
posts.key_insight: When struggling with WiFi connectivity, upgrading your router's stock antenna to a better aftermarket model can yield dramatic improvements that no amount of repositioning can achieve.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that requiring specific years of experience in particular technologies is a counterproductive hiring practice that causes companies to miss out on brilliant engineers. He uses an anecdote about a company that initially rejected candidates lacking specific low-level coding experience, only to find their best hire was someone who lacked that exact experience but possessed exceptional learning ability. Atwood contends that after 6-12 months with any technology stack, developers either understand it or they don't, making additional years largely meaningless. He advocates for hiring passionate, self-educating developers who can learn whatever is needed rather than filtering for exact skill matches.
key_points: There is no correlation between years of experience and actual programming skill - after 6-12 months with a technology, you either get it or you don't
Companies obsessed with matching specific technology experience on laundry lists miss brilliant engineers who could quickly master those skills
The best software developers are passionate self-educators with proven coding ability, not people with exact technology matches
High year requirements in job postings signal a company that looks for the wrong things and likely has a team of 'stooges picked for the wrong reasons'
Experience matters, but employers should consider a developer's entire range of experience rather than narrow technological niches
money_quotes: You should be 95% certain that a candidate would be a great hire before they ever set foot in an interview room.
What software developers do best is learn.
There is no correlation between years of experience and skill in programming.
After about six to twelve months working in any particular technology stack, you either get it or you don't.
No matter how many years of 'experience' another programmer has under their belt, there's about even odds that they have no idea what they're doing.
Platform experience is merely a baseline, not a differentiator of real importance.
Time invested does not automatically equal skill.
posts.key_insight: The best predictor of a software engineer's future value is their ability to learn and adapt, not their years matching a specific technology checklist.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques web developers for neglecting basic keyboard navigation support, using eBay's login form as a case study. While the web is inherently mouse-centric, simple forms like login screens should work seamlessly with keyboard input. He identifies three common failures: not setting initial focus, illogical tab ordering, and broken Enter key submission. The post also exposes how major browsers (IE7, Firefox 2, Safari 3) fail to make focus indicators visible, with only Safari providing adequate visual feedback. Atwood argues that poor focus visibility forces users to rely on mouse clicking to orient themselves, undermining efficient keyboard navigation.
key_points: Web developers frequently fail to implement basic keyboard support: setting initial focus, logical tab order, and Enter key submission
Even simple four-field forms like eBay's login have broken tab ordering, which Atwood considers a sign of developer negligence
Most browsers in 2008 (IE7, Firefox 2) displayed embarrassingly subtle focus indicators, making keyboard navigation disorienting
Safari 3 was the only browser that got focus indication right with clear visual feedback
Users resort to mouse-clicking on form fields because they can't visually track where their keyboard focus is
money_quotes: When web developers screw up basic tab ordering on a form with four fields, that's veering dangerously close to 'I don't give a damn about my craft' territory.
No wonder users rely on the mouse so much. Most browsers do an embarrassingly bad job of making the focus obvious, so users feel compelled to click on fields to orient themselves.
Keyboard navigation relies heavily on the focus. In order to move from one area to the next, you have to be able to reliably know where you are.
I've seen many users log in using the excruciatingly slow mouse way, and it's not pretty.
What frustrates me is when web developers fail to pay attention to the most rudimentary of keyboard support in their designs.
I've seen far worse, web forms with tab orders that resembled a Rubik's cube.
posts.key_insight: Keyboard navigation fails not just because developers neglect it, but because browsers themselves make focus nearly invisible, creating a vicious cycle where users abandon the keyboard entirely.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the long-standing convention of obscuring passwords with dots or asterisks in GUI text fields, questioning whether this tradition serves users as well as we assume. After initially being appalled by Windows Vista's wireless network dialog that includes a 'display characters' checkbox, he came to appreciate its utility for catching typos. He contrasts this sensible feature with the absurdly over-engineered Lotus Notes login dialog that used random hieroglyphics and variable-length asterisks. Atwood concludes that password reveal options should be standard on all login dialogs, arguing that the security benefit of obfuscation is overstated compared to the usability cost.
key_points: Password obfuscation has been a GUI convention for decades, but its security benefits may be overestimated
The Lotus Notes 6.0 login dialog exemplifies how trying to 'improve' security can result in terrible user experience
Vista's wireless password dialog introduced a 'display characters' option that proved surprisingly useful for catching typos
Users can make informed contextual decisions about when to reveal passwords based on their environment
Real password security threats come from keyloggers and watching fingers, not shoulder-surfing the screen
money_quotes: Which would be sort of traumatic on several levels. Not to mention the security implications.
Who reinvents a perfectly standard dialog – and makes it so much worse?
On second thought, perhaps 'how can we make this worse?' was the design goal for Notes.
If you think you've made a mistake entering your password, tick the reveal box and find out.
I've come full circle. I now think the password reveal option should be available on all login dialogs.
Traditional GUI password obfuscation is a nice convention, but it's not the alpha and omega of password security.
If criminals really want to get your password, they'll be watching your fingers on the keyboard or using keylogger hardware.
posts.key_insight: Password obfuscation is a security theater convention that often hurts usability more than it helps security, and giving users the option to reveal their password respects their ability to make contextual security decisions.
