posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines why keyboard navigation fails on modern web pages, arguing that traditional tab-based navigation was designed for simple GUI dialogs with dozens of elements, not web pages with hundreds of selectable items. He highlights spatial navigation—using arrow keys to move to the nearest element in any direction—as a superior alternative already implemented in Opera and mobile browsers. The post advocates for this technique to be adopted more widely, while also lamenting Opera's tiny market share despite its innovative features.
key_points: Traditional keyboard navigation (tab/shift-tab) was designed for early GUIs with dozens of controls and completely breaks down on modern web pages with hundreds of selectable items
Spatial navigation uses arrow keys to move focus to the nearest element in any direction, respecting the visual layout rather than document structure
Opera already implements spatial navigation via shift+arrow keys, making it surprisingly effective for web browsing
Mobile browsers have solved keyboard navigation out of necessity, and desktop browsers should learn from them
Opera remains innovative but has vanishingly small market share, partly due to its history of being a paid product
money_quotes: Presented with a 20-fold increase in selectable items, it's not too difficult to see why traditional keyboard navigation techniques completely break down.
A user trying to use the keyboard to reach a link in the middle of the page might have to press the Tab key 125 times to reach it.
These heuristics respect the rendered visual representation of the page, not the structure of the document's object model or the original location of elements at design time.
Spatial navigation is worlds better than the insanity of pressing tab 125 times.
It's too bad Opera doesn't get more respect. I'm as guilty as anyone; when I'm testing something, I'll use IE, Firefox, and Safari in that order. Opera isn't even on my radar.
Now that the browser wars have heated up again, I hope there will be more cross-pollination of innovative features so everyone can benefit.
posts.key_insight: Mobile browsers solved keyboard navigation through spatial arrow-key navigation years ago, and desktop browsers should adopt this technique instead of clinging to tab-based navigation designed for a simpler era.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that programmers are too obsessed with technical metrics like code coverage while missing the bigger picture: whether users actually want to use their software. He references Alan Cooper's keynote about how interaction designers are marginalized by developers who control the code but often create poor user experiences. Atwood urges programmers to think beyond technical correctness and embrace interaction design principles. The ultimate measure of software quality isn't test coverage—it's user adoption and usability.
key_points: Code coverage and unit tests are valuable but meaningless if users won't or can't use the application
Alan Cooper describes programmers as 'Stay-Puft Marshmallow Man' barriers to good interaction design
Interaction designers are marginalized because they don't control the code, even though they understand user needs
Programmers need to learn interaction design principles and respond to user research rather than dismissing it
The real 'unit test' that matters is whether users choose to use your application
money_quotes: When users can't figure out how to use your app, when users pass over your app in favor of something easier or simpler to use, that's the ultimate unit test failure.
I want to run up to my fellow programmers and physically shake them: think bigger!
Cooper considers most programmers twenty-story marshmallow barriers to good interaction design.
We don't need to change interaction design; we need to re-orient organizations to build things right.
Keep your unit testing and code coverage in perspective – the ultimate unit test is whether or not users want to use your application.
All the other tests you write are totally irrelevant until you can get that one to pass.
posts.key_insight: Technical excellence in code is worthless if it doesn't serve users—the only test that truly matters is whether people want to use what you've built.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts his love for the original TiVo and explains how the Series 2 introduced hardware protections that prevented users from modifying the Linux-based software, despite the GPL's promise of software freedom. This practice, dubbed 'Tivoization,' became so controversial that Richard Stallman rewrote the GPL v3 specifically to prevent it. While Atwood acknowledges that TiVo technically complied with the GPL by providing source code, the hardware lockdown made that freedom meaningless in practice. He expresses ambivalence about the GPL v3's anti-Tivoization clause, understanding both the philosophical objections and TiVo's business realities.
key_points: TiVo Series 1 was hackable and ran on Linux, but Series 2 added hardware protections that validated software signatures, preventing modifications from running
The GPL guarantees freedom to modify and redistribute software, but TiVo's hardware restrictions made this freedom 'a sham, a facade' since modified code wouldn't execute
Richard Stallman was so incensed by Tivoization that he specifically rewrote GPL v3 to require companies provide signing keys needed to run modified software
GPL v3 includes a business loophole that doesn't require signing keys when distributing to businesses, though the FSF disapproves
Atwood empathizes with TiVo's business model dilemma: selling hardware at a loss requires preventing users from removing billing functionality
money_quotes: Since the Tivo's hardware validates the software, access to the Tivo software becomes effectively meaningless.
You can modify the software all you want, but you'll never be able to run it on your own Tivo!
You might, in fact, argue that Tivo subverted the very principles of the GPL they built their business on.
They do this in various ways. This is known as Tivoization because this is what the Tivo does.
Businesses can no longer adopt GPL software, then incorporate hardware protections to effectively prevent the software freedoms that the GPL specifically guarantees.
I also wonder how a company like Tivo could make money if users could simply recompile the Tivo software to stop phoning home and billing them.
I've already said that I think a compelling product can make me overlook the DRM, so maybe my opinion is already suspect.
posts.key_insight: Hardware-enforced software restrictions can technically comply with open source licenses while completely defeating their spirit, forcing a difficult choice between philosophical purity and practical business realities.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood celebrates Michael Abrash's 'Graphics Programming Black Book' and uses it to illustrate a fundamental truth about code optimization: there's no such thing as the fastest possible code. Through Abrash's anecdotes about word-counting program challenges, the post shows how even world-class optimizers can achieve unexpected speedups once they abandon the assumption that their code is already optimal. The post argues that the biggest barrier to performance isn't technical limitations but programmer mindset—believing you've already found the best solution prevents you from discovering faster approaches. Abrash's challenges resulted in order-of-magnitude improvements over already-optimized assembly code, proving that creative thinking from multiple perspectives consistently beats assumed perfection.
key_points: The belief that you have the fastest possible code is itself the biggest optimization barrier
A brilliant teacher makes any technical subject compelling, regardless of how dated or specialized it seems
Terje Mathisen expected 5% improvements but achieved near-doubling once he opened his mind to new approaches
Community challenges on Abrash's optimized assembly achieved 3x speedups, nearly an order of magnitude over the original C
Technical skill without humility about the limits of your own solutions caps your potential for improvement
money_quotes: There ain't no such thing as the fastest code.
The biggest optimization barrier that Terje faced was that he thought he had the fastest code possible.
The class topic doesn't matter when the instructor is a brilliant teacher.
Any code can be made faster than you'd expect, if you think about it long enough and from many different perspectives.
If you think there is, you're probably the barrier standing in the way of further performance, not the code itself.
The biggest problem of all – the one that sits behind the keyboard.
posts.key_insight: The assumption that your code is already optimal is the primary obstacle to achieving genuinely optimal performance.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reviews the book 'Beautiful Code' and finds it disappointing despite its impressive roster of authors. The core problem, he argues, is that the book focuses on literal code samples rather than the underlying ideas and algorithms that make solutions elegant. Most authors simply present production code and expect readers to see beauty, without explaining what makes it beautiful or providing sufficient context. Atwood concludes that true beauty in programming lies not in syntax or language, but in architecture, algorithms, and well-executed ideas that code merely represents.
key_points: The book fails because authors present code samples without explaining what makes them beautiful or providing necessary context
Showing code in specific languages (Ruby, Perl, Lisp) is like asking someone to appreciate poetry in a foreign language they don't speak
Beauty in programming exists in ideas, algorithms, and architecture - not in the code syntax itself
Programmers too often get distracted by surface-level language preferences (like VB syntax complaints) instead of focusing on underlying concepts
Only Yukihiro Matsumoto's chapter succeeded by actually explaining his philosophy of beautiful code rather than just showing examples
money_quotes: The beauty of code lies in the architecture, the ideas, the grander algorithms and strategies that code represents.
They literally show you a piece of poetry in a foreign language and ask you to appreciate it.
It's been a long time since I found *dst++ = *src++ beautiful.

Focusing on the code is exactly the wrong approach. It's like a detailed technical description of the paints, brushes, and techniques used to paint the Mona Lisa, without any of the historical or artistic context that makes it such an important painting.
Barroom doggerel expressed in French or Russian is never automatically elevated to the level of poetry.
Any possible appreciation of beauty requires deep understanding.
Apparently, for many of us, beauty really is skin deep.
posts.key_insight: True beauty in programming lies not in code syntax but in the underlying ideas, algorithms, and architecture that code represents - and explaining that 'why' matters more than showing the 'what'.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that choosing the right communication medium is crucial for productive conversations, especially when discussions become heated or complex. He shares personal experiences of getting into damaging fights over instant messaging that could have been easily resolved through phone calls or in-person meetings. The post emphasizes that different communication channels have different bandwidths for conveying nuance, emotion, and complex ideas. Citing David Allen, Atwood warns against the assumption that email or IM can handle all types of communication. The core message is to recognize when a conversation has outgrown its current medium and have the courage to escalate to higher-bandwidth channels like phone or face-to-face meetings.
key_points: Each communication medium has inherent limitations in conveying nuance, emotion, and complexity
Intense or sensitive conversations conducted over low-bandwidth channels like IM often cause unnecessary conflict and hurt feelings
Younger generations sometimes wrongly assume email is suitable for all types of communication
Escalating to higher-bandwidth communication (IM → email → phone → in-person) can defuse conflicts that text-based media inflame
Failing to escalate when needed ultimately hurts yourself and others unnecessarily
money_quotes: Don't underestimate the power of taking a previously siloed, private one-to-one communication medium and making it public.
I would get into knock down, drag out fights over IM where I became physically angry. And for what?
Because I abused instant messaging, because I wasn't brave enough to address the serious limitations of instant messaging as a communication medium, I ultimately hurt myself.
Understand the limitations of the communication medium you are using and know when to escalate to another, more appropriate one.
Know when to escalate from IM to email, from email to phone, and when to drop the ultimate communication A-bomb: a face-to-face meeting.
They make the assumption that email is a fine medium for communicating anything and everything.
Resist the urge to reply in kind, however tempting it may be.
posts.key_insight: Productive communication requires matching the complexity and sensitivity of your message to a medium with sufficient bandwidth to convey it properly.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while listening to users is essential, acting solely on user feedback without data is a mistake. He contrasts the advice to 'listen to users' with Jakob Nielsen's rule to 'not listen to users,' resolving the paradox by emphasizing observation over stated preferences. The key is to watch what users actually do through metrics and usage data, then correlate that with their feedback. Using examples from Valve's Steam hardware surveys and Team Fortress 2 gameplay statistics, he demonstrates how data-driven decisions reveal insights that feedback alone could never surface. The conclusion: user feedback is valuable input, but must always be validated against actual user behavior before taking action.
key_points: Users often don't know what they want, and their stated preferences frequently diverge from their actual behavior
The resolution to 'listen to users' vs 'don't listen to users' is to observe what they do rather than believe what they say
Detailed usage metrics and activity logs provide the objective data needed to validate or refute user feedback
Valve's TF2 class usage statistics revealed the Medic was underplayed - a problem user feedback alone couldn't have quantified
Acting on user feedback alone is guessing; acting on feedback correlated with usage data is the gold standard
money_quotes: Watch what people actually do. Do not believe what people say they do. Definitely don't believe what people predict they may do in the future.
Acting on user feedback alone is questionable. No matter how well intentioned, you're guessing. Why guess when you can take actions based on cold, hard data?
The last thing you want to do is fritter away valuable engineering time on features that almost nobody is using, and having usage data is how you tell the difference.
Most people will silently and forever walk away if your software or website doesn't meet their needs.
Ignoring your user feedback may be setting yourself up for eventual failure, but blindly acting on every user request is certain failure.
The users who care enough to give you feedback deserve your attention and respect. They're essentially taking it upon themselves to design your product.
posts.key_insight: Never act solely on what users tell you they want - always validate feedback against actual usage data to understand what they really need.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to David Heinemeier Hansson's inflammatory statements that Windows programmers are inferior and carry a 'stigma.' Atwood argues that while DHH's underlying point about open source platform compatibility has merit, his delivery is counterproductive douchebaggery that alienates rather than persuades. The post examines how tribal platform advocacy ultimately backfires as an evangelism strategy, since insulting people never converts them to your cause. Atwood concludes with an analogy from fighting games: skilled practitioners can win with any tool, and results matter more than platform choice.
key_points: DHH's claim that Windows programmers 'don't care about their tools' is a statement of taste disguised as objective truth
Abusing people into agreeing with you is a remarkably poor evangelism strategy that only produces hostility
There is no objective 'best' operating system - they all have tradeoffs and ultimately all suck in different ways
If inflammatory statements won't change minds and are counterproductive, their only purpose is the speaker's ego gratification
Skill means understanding your tools and maximizing their strengths rather than arguing about which tools are superior
money_quotes: I take issue with David's claim that, when it comes to computers and operating systems, there's any 'best' anything. In my considered opinion, they all suck.
He declared Coca-Cola the morally and aesthetically superior choice, instead of the simple preference for one type of sugar water over another that it really is.
When has abusing people into agreeing with you ever worked?

If you accept the premise that this kind of statement won't change anyone's mind, and is ultimately ineffective – even counterproductive – what are we left with? What purpose does the statement 'stigma of being a Windows developer' serve? I can only think of one: David gets off on putting other people down.
There's nothing more satisfying than kicking someone's ass with the worst character in the game.
Don't waste time arguing about the character select screen. Results speak loudest.
Playing well meant understanding your character and maximizing your strengths while exploiting your opponent's weaknesses.
posts.key_insight: Platform tribalism is ultimately just ego gratification disguised as technical evangelism - skilled developers prove their worth through results, not tool choices.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores Eric Sink's framework for categorizing software based on who uses it: MeWare (developer only), ThemWare (users only), and UsWare (both developer and users). He argues that ThemWare—where developers build for users they don't understand—produces predictably poor results because developers can't empathize with user needs. The best software emerges when developers are also users, allowing them to naturally understand what needs improvement. Atwood advocates for 'dogfooding' as a practice to bridge the gap between developers and users, even when it's challenging.
key_points: Software quality correlates strongly with whether developers actually use what they build
ThemWare developers see features in terms of implementation difficulty rather than user value
Becoming a user—even 'in spirit'—transforms mediocre ThemWare into superior UsWare
Dogfooding keeps developers honest and aligned with user needs
The goal should be working alongside users rather than against them
money_quotes: If I am building software that I don't use and don't know how to use for people I don't understand or even like, how good is my software going to be?
I probably see every feature in terms of how difficult it will be to implement, rather than how valuable it will be for my users.
Much of the best software is the best because the programmers are the users, too. It is UsWare.
To really care about the software you're writing, you have to become a user, at least in spirit.
Dogfooding keeps software developers honest.
Why work against your users by producing ThemWare when you could work alongside them to build UsWare?
posts.key_insight: The quality gap between mediocre and great software often comes down to whether developers experience their own product as users do.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines why Windows Vista's file copy feels slower than XP despite actually performing better in benchmarks. The post uses this as a case study for a broader principle: perceived performance matters more than actual performance. Vista's improved algorithm failed users because it violated key perceptual principles—waiting 12 seconds before showing estimates and being slowest at the end. Research shows progress bars that move smoothly and accelerate toward completion feel faster, even at identical durations. Atwood argues developers must think beyond benchmarks and consider how users experience processes.
key_points: Vista's file copy algorithm was technically superior but felt slower because it waited to show progress and was slowest at the end
Research demonstrates that progress bars moving smoothly and accelerating toward completion are perceived as faster, regardless of actual duration
The Vista team's focus on correct benchmarks while ignoring human perception led to user frustration
Performance optimization must consider psychological factors, not just technical metrics
Like magicians, developers can seemingly alter reality through understanding human perception
money_quotes: Perceived performance is more important than actual performance.
As with so many things in life, perception is reality: if users see file copying as slower, it is slower.
Fancy copy algorithms won't necessarily help you build a fast progress bar. But understanding how your users' brains work definitely will.
An understanding of which behaviors perceptually shorten or lengthen process duration can be used to engineer a progress bar that appears faster, even though the actual duration remains unchanged.
The idea that performance is determined largely by the user's perception rather than actual wall-clock time can be liberating.
Even if you get the technical parts right, with hard benchmark data to back you up, subtle human perceptual factors can still negate your work.
Don't make the same mistake the Vista development team did. Think more holistically than mere benchmarks alone.
Consider the user's perception of the process, too.
posts.key_insight: Technical correctness means nothing if it violates users' psychological expectations—perceived performance trumps actual performance every time.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the rapid defeat of what he considered 'unbreakable' CAPTCHA implementations from Google, Hotmail, and Yahoo in early 2008. Despite viewing CAPTCHAs as a necessary evil, he acknowledges that profit-motivated attackers have rendered them largely ineffective, with even a 20% success rate being devastating when applied at scale. He argues that diversification of CAPTCHA techniques may be more effective than strengthening individual implementations, since attackers tailor their tools to specific targets. The post explores alternative approaches like image recognition and trivia questions, while warning that CAPTCHAs risk becoming intelligence tests that alienate legitimate users.
key_points: All three 'unbreakable' CAPTCHAs (Google, Hotmail, Yahoo) were defeated within months, demonstrating how quickly security measures can fall
A 20% success rate is devastating because spammers use botnets to send thousands of simultaneous requests
CAPTCHAs still serve as 'speed bumps' against unsophisticated attacks, even if they can't stop determined commercial attackers
Diversification of CAPTCHA techniques may be more effective than strengthening any single implementation
There's an inherent tension between making CAPTCHAs strong enough to stop bots and keeping them accessible to legitimate users
money_quotes: I am no fan of CAPTCHA. I view them as a necessary and important evil, one of precious few things separating average internet users from a torrential deluge of email, comment, and forum spam.
A twenty percent success rate doesn't sound like much, but these spammers are harnessing networks of compromised PCs to send out thousands upon thousands of simultaneous sign-up requests.
With twenty percent success rate you might as well put a fork in that thing – it's done.
There's simply too much money to be made in email spam for the commercial CAPTCHA algorithms, regardless of how good they may be, to survive forever.
Perhaps proliferation and evolution of many different CAPTCHA techniques is the most effective prevention.
At some point, unfortunately, CAPTCHA devolves from a simple human reading test into an intelligence test or an acuity test.
I don't have the all answers, but one thing is for sure: I hate spammers.
posts.key_insight: The economics of spam mean that even the best CAPTCHAs will eventually be broken, making diversification and evolution more important than any single 'unbreakable' solution.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood promotes Microsoft's MIX08 conference, noting that while he won't attend personally, his company Vertigo is presenting something special in the keynote. He explains why MIX is his favorite Microsoft conference: its intimate size, blend of engineering and design, and inclusion of non-Microsoft perspectives. The post teases a mysterious rock-themed project and encourages developers in the Microsoft ecosystem to attend future MIX events.
key_points: MIX stands out from other Microsoft conferences by being small (2,000 attendees), merging engineering with design, and including diverse perspectives
Vertigo built something for the MIX keynote involving 'the power of rock' after two months of intense work
The conference appeals to Atwood despite his disinterest in gambling because of its professional and social value
Atwood is so confident in the conference quality that he suggests anyone who doesn't enjoy it has something wrong with them
money_quotes: MIX is by far my favorite Microsoft conference
What I love about Mix is that it seamlessly merges software engineering and design.
includes a lot of non-Microsoft folks, even those that are traditionally hostile to Microsoft, so there's plenty of perspective.
If you attend MIX and don't thoroughly enjoy the experience, then I dare say there's something wrong with you.
I've been specifically told 'it'll be epic; wear your pampers.'
posts.key_insight: The best tech conferences are intimate gatherings that bridge disciplines and welcome diverse perspectives, not massive corporate echo chambers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood mourns the loss of influential blogger Kathy Sierra, who stopped writing after receiving death threats, and Dare Obasanjo, who quit due to workplace and family pressures. He traces a recurring pattern where successful bloggers are driven to silence by the consequences of their popularity. While acknowledging that authors have every right to step away, Atwood argues that when unique voices voluntarily silence themselves forever, the bad guys win. He expresses both sadness and frustration that intimidation tactics succeed in removing valuable contributors from public discourse, ultimately concluding that when passionate bloggers stop writing, everyone loses.
key_points: Kathy Sierra's Creating Passionate Users blog reached the Technorati top 100 through sheer quality and passion for helping others succeed, making her silencing particularly tragic
A predictable pattern emerges: blog gains popularity, popularity creates problems, author stops writing, everyone loses
Intimidation and threats only work when people allow themselves to be intimidated—voluntary permanent silence means the bad guys have won
Even without death threats, subtler pressures like workplace complaints, family scrutiny, and unexpected audiences can drive bloggers to quit
Unique voices who share honest, insightful perspectives are irreplaceable contributors to public discourse
money_quotes: Kathy kicked ass because she wanted us to kick ass.
When a voice is voluntarily silenced forever, the bad guys have won. Fear wins.
Intimidation only works if you let yourself be intimidated; terrorism only works if you let yourself be terrorized.
I'm absolutely certain that when they stop writing, everyone loses.
Unique voices like Kathy's are what make the internet such a fascinating and wildly powerful Gutenberg press.
I've now gotten enough heat and taken enough inter-personal communication training classes to realize that some opinions are more trouble than they are worth.
Hearing them silenced makes me profoundly sad.
posts.key_insight: The internet loses something irreplaceable when intimidation—whether through explicit threats or subtle social pressure—succeeds in silencing the passionate voices who share their knowledge and perspectives freely.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood contrasts the ACM Code of Ethics with a disturbing real-world example of programmer malfeasance. A reader discovered that G-Archiver, a Gmail backup tool, was secretly emailing users' credentials to its creator's personal account. The reader found 1,777 stolen username/password combinations in the developer's inbox. Atwood uses this story to highlight how such violations damage the trust relationship between users and all programmers. He concludes by hoping ethical programmers outnumber malicious ones.
key_points: Professional codes of ethics exist for programmers but are easily violated in practice
A backup utility was secretly harvesting and storing user credentials via hardcoded email functionality
The discoverer took vigilante action by deleting the stolen credentials and locking the malicious developer out
Trust violations by one programmer damage the reputation of the entire profession
Even well-intentioned programmers can accidentally expose credentials, making ethical handling by others crucial
money_quotes: It's hard to square that with the following hair-raising tale
What I came across was quite shocking
Every time a user adds their account to the program to back up their data, it sends an email with their username and password to his personal email box
Upon getting to the inbox I was greeted with 1,777 emails with account information for everyone who had ever used the software
It's difficult to imagine any scenario where this isn't a completely malicious violation of people's trust
This is every user's greatest fear when giving out their login credentials
To see it realized hurts the trust relationship between users and every other professional programmer working today
I certainly hope there are more programmers out there like Israel Orange than John Terry
posts.key_insight: Unethical behavior by individual programmers damages the trust relationship between users and the entire software development profession.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood traces the history of ray tracing from the iconic 1986 Amiga Juggler demo to modern attempts at real-time implementation. Despite 22 years of exponential computing growth, ray tracing remains computationally expensive—Pixar's Cars took 15 hours per frame despite 300x more compute power than Toy Story. Real-time ray tracing efforts like the OpenRT project show promise but struggle to exceed 17 fps at low resolutions. The emergence of multi-core processors offers hope since ray tracing scales nearly perfectly with parallelization, but true real-time ray tracing at high resolutions remains a holy grail.
key_points: The 1986 Amiga Juggler demo brought ray tracing to personal computers for the first time, inspiring many careers in 3D graphics
Despite 300x more computing power over a decade, Pixar's render times increased from 2 hours to 15 hours per frame as artists demanded more detail
Modern GPUs achieve real-time 3D by 'cheating mightily' with approximations rather than true ray tracing
Ray tracing is ideal for parallelization—each pixel's ray can be calculated independently, scaling nearly perfectly across cores
Real-time ray tracing in 2008 barely achieves playable framerates at resolutions comparable to the original 1986 Juggler demo
money_quotes: The Amiga brought 3D raytracing graphics to the masses for the first time.
The average Cars frame took 15 hours, despite a 300x overall increase in compute power.
The artists have an essentially infinite appetite for detail and realism.
Modern GPUs are impressive pieces of silicon, but they cheat mightily when it comes to rendering a 3D scene.
It's possible we could render the ray traced Amiga juggler today in real time at close to 30 fps – but barely.
Real time ray tracing remains something of a holy grail in practice.
Who knows what we'll be able to render in the next 20 years?
posts.key_insight: Computing power grows exponentially, but our appetite for visual fidelity grows just as fast, keeping true real-time ray tracing perpetually just out of reach.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces his departure from his dream job at Vertigo Software to pursue opportunities created by his blog's success. Using the Choose Your Own Adventure books as an extended metaphor, he reflects on the risk of leaving a secure position for entrepreneurial unknowns. He commits to building things rather than becoming a 'full-time blogger,' citing respected industry figures who both write and create. The post hints at an upcoming startup venture with an unnamed collaborator, which would later become Stack Overflow.
key_points: Choose Your Own Adventure books were early programmer training, teaching branching logic and decision trees through interactive storytelling
Leaving a secure job for entrepreneurial risk is scary, but 'if it's not a little scary, then it's not the right choice'
The most respected people in tech aren't just writers—they build things too
Full-time blogging without building is a 'cop-out' that produces vapid commentary
Like the books' outcomes, most career paths have more failure states than success states, but you still have to choose
money_quotes: I feel like I'm still playing Choose Your Own Adventure.
Sometimes choosing your own adventure means closing one door to open another.
I'm not sure what exactly lies on page 10.
It's scary to trade the security of a safe, salaried job for the unknowns of your own small business.
If it's not a little scary, then it's not the right choice.
Failure is always an option.
I refuse to become a full-time blogger. I think that's a cop-out.
They're not just excellent writers and communicators. They build stuff, too.
The world has enough vapid commentary blogs. I want to build stuff—and talk about it.
posts.key_insight: The most meaningful career moves require accepting risk and choosing to build things, not just comment on them.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores why Turkey consistently appears in internationalization bug reports, using it as a case study for why software developers fail at i18n. The post examines specific issues like date formatting differences (3/14/2008 vs 14.3.2008), number grouping (commas vs periods), and most notably the 'Turkish I problem' where uppercase/lowercase conversions produce unexpected characters. Atwood argues the real question isn't what's wrong with Turkey, but what's wrong with developers who don't account for locale differences. He recommends testing code under the Turkish locale as a strong indicator of internationalization readiness.
key_points: Turkey frequently appears in i18n bug reports due to its unique locale rules that expose common developer assumptions
Date and number formatting differences between cultures cause parsing failures when code assumes American English format
The 'Turkish I problem' causes string comparisons to fail because Turkish has dotted and dotless I characters that don't map to ASCII expectations
Most modern languages have culture-aware functions built in, but developers must know when to use them
Testing under the Turkish locale serves as an effective bellwether for internationalization quality
money_quotes: As with so many other problems in software development, the question shouldn't be what's wrong with Turkey, but rather, what the hell is wrong with software developers?

The 'invariant' culture is every American programmer's secret dream realized: we merely close our eyes and wish away all those confusing languages and cultures and their crazy, bug-inducing date and number formatting schemes in favor of our own.
Instead of rudely asking your users to 'speak American' through the invariant culture, you could politely ask them to enter data in ISO international standard format instead.
Strings are where it really starts to get hairy.
If you guessed Turkey, you're wrong! Just kidding. Of course it fails in Turkey.
If your code can pass the Turkey test, you're doing quite well. Certainly better than most.
Force your code to run under the Turkish locale as soon as reasonably possible. It's a strong bellwether for your code running in most – but by no means all – cultures and locales.
posts.key_insight: Turkey's unique locale rules make it an ideal stress test for internationalization, exposing the Anglocentric assumptions that most developers unconsciously bake into their code.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues passionately for multiple monitor setups as a productivity booster for programmers, citing a University of Utah study showing 44-52% task completion improvements with larger or dual displays. He acknowledges the study's potential bias (funded by NEC) but notes it found diminishing returns with 26-inch screens, lending credibility. Atwood counters skeptics by emphasizing that even modest productivity gains easily justify monitor costs against programmer salaries. His core argument is that more screen real estate reduces window management overhead, freeing developers to focus on actual work rather than arranging windows.
key_points: University of Utah study found 24-inch monitors improved task speed by 52% and dual 20-inch monitors by 44% compared to single 18-inch displays
Productivity actually decreased with 26-inch screens, supporting the 'Large Display Paradox' that bigger isn't always better
Even skeptics who initially saw no difference between laptop and large displays were convinced by the research evidence
The cost of monitors is negligible compared to programmer salaries, making even 2-3% productivity gains worthwhile
Multiple monitors reduce 'window management excise' - the constant dragging, sizing, and arranging that distracts from real work
money_quotes: I'm always looking for ammunition for fellow developers to claim those second (and maybe even third) monitors that are rightfully theirs under the Programmer's Bill of Rights.
Let me be perfectly clear on this matter: more is more.
More usable desktop space reduces the amount of time you spend on window management excise.
Personally, I'd be ecstatic if I never had to size, position, or arrange another damn window for the rest of my life.
The price of a few monitors is negligible when measured against the labor cost of a programmer or information worker salary.
This isn't a zero-sum game. Where there is one large monitor, there could be two large monitors, or three.
Sometimes, more is more.
posts.key_insight: The minor cost of additional monitors is easily justified by even small productivity gains when measured against developer salaries, making multi-monitor setups an obvious investment for any programming team.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the paradox of Firefox's celebrated extension ecosystem, arguing that while extensions represent a triumph of community-driven development, they also reveal a failure in core product design. He contends that popular extensions are essentially users filling gaps that should have been addressed by default, since most users will never install add-ons due to the power of defaults. The post suggests that Firefox should systematically absorb the functionality of its most popular extensions into the core browser, using download metrics as product guidance. Atwood frames this as a Catch-22: without extensions, developers can't know what users want, but with them, there's less pressure to improve the base product.
key_points: Any installation at all is too much work for a significant percentage of users, who will use software exactly as supplied forever
Responding to feature requests with 'there's an extension for that' is irresponsible when the majority of users will never install extensions
Popular extensions serve as free product research, showing developers exactly which gaps needed filling
Firefox's extension ecosystem is both its greatest strength and an indictment of missing core functionality
If developers were doing their jobs properly, 99% of the features users want would already be baked into the shipping software
money_quotes: Any installation at all is too much work for a sizable percentage of the audience.
Why bother improving the core browser when you can force the user to install five extensions to duplicate that same functionality?
The majority of internet users, surprisingly, do not feel the need to install custom browser components.
If an extension is wildly popular and everyone urges you to install some 'crucial' or 'essential' extension – shouldn't that extension have been baked into the browser in the first place?
Half the value of a robust add-in ecosystem lies in product guidance – users are taking it upon themselves to fill the gaps and holes in your product.
If software developers were doing their jobs properly, an extension ecosystem wouldn't be necessary.
How many of those nifty extensions are specialized functionality – and how many are crude spackle over missing features that really should have shipped in the box?
posts.key_insight: Extension ecosystems are simultaneously invaluable user research and a crutch that lets developers avoid the hard work of building complete products.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores his decade-long journey with rechargeable batteries, from discovering NiMH technology through a 1998 John Dvorak column to understanding the pitfalls of modern marketing claims. He reveals that while battery capacity has nearly doubled in ten years, manufacturers hide the 'dirty little secret' of self-discharge rates that can drain batteries within a month. The post recommends hybrid or pre-charged varieties like Sanyo Eneloop over high-capacity marketing gimmicks, and advocates for the La Crosse BC-900 charger as the solution to poor charging practices that destroy good batteries. Atwood concludes that informed consumers armed with proper technology can overcome the gadget world's battery demands.
key_points: Battery capacity doubled from 1300mAh to 2500mAh between 1998-2008, but this metric alone is misleading
Self-discharge rates of 20-30% per month for NiMH batteries are the hidden factor manufacturers don't advertise
'Hybrid' or 'pre-charged' battery varieties offer much better real-world performance than high-capacity marketing claims suggest
Most bundled battery chargers are 'junk' that destroy good batteries through improper charging
Smart chargers with individual battery control and multiple modes can revitalize even marginal batteries
money_quotes: Do the math: It's like buying 1,000 alkaline batteries for less than 10 bucks. Imagine what this does to the lucrative disposable-battery business.
There is a dirty little secret that they don't want you to hear: self-discharge rate.
So putting a larger number on the box is ultimately a method of fooling consumers with marketing. Where have we seen that before? Oh right, everywhere.
The fast chargers, especially the cheap ones, excel at one thing: destroying perfectly good batteries.
I spent several hours after I got it plugging various batteries in it, trying different modes, and watching it work.
I'm not sure what the exact definition of geek is, but I think 'enjoys recharging batteries' has to be very high on that list.
posts.key_insight: Marketing metrics like battery capacity are meaningless without understanding the hidden technical realities like self-discharge rates, and informed consumers need quality tools to overcome industry deception.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that programmers should always assume bugs are their own fault rather than blaming compilers, operating systems, or third-party libraries. He illustrates this with a story from The Pragmatic Programmer about a senior engineer who wasted weeks convinced that Solaris's select system call was broken, only to fix the issue in minutes once he read the documentation. Citing studies showing 95% of errors are caused by programmers themselves, Atwood makes the case that taking ownership of problems builds both diagnostic skills and professional credibility. The post frames this mindset as essential to being a 'humble programmer' who earns respect through accountability rather than finger-pointing.
key_points: Blaming tools, compilers, or operating systems leads to 'voodoo computing' and wasted time on workarounds that don't address the real problem
Studies from 1973 and 1984 found roughly 95% of errors are caused by programmers, with only 5% attributable to compilers, OS, other software, or hardware
Taking ownership of bugs builds essential troubleshooting skills and creates an evidence trail if the problem truly lies elsewhere
Code ownership implies code responsibility - you should take full responsibility for failures even when technically not required
The 'select is broken' anti-pattern serves as a cautionary tale about the cost of refusing to examine your own assumptions
money_quotes: No matter how desperate you get, don't choose that path. Down that path lies voodoo computing and programming by coincidence. In short, madness.
An essential part of being a humble programmer is realizing that whenever there's a problem with the code you've written, it's always your fault.
It is much more likely that the bug exists in the application code under development.
We now use the phrase 'select is broken' as a gentle reminder whenever one of us starts blaming the system for a fault that is likely to be our own.
The flip side of code ownership is code responsibility.
You certainly don't earn respect or credibility by endlessly pawning off errors and problems on other people, other companies, other sources.

This is certainly a lot more work than shrugging your shoulders and pointing your finger at the OS, the tools, or the framework – but it also engenders a sense of trust and respect you're unlikely to achieve through finger pointing and evasion.
posts.key_insight: Assuming bugs are your own fault isn't just statistically accurate—it's a professional discipline that builds debugging skills, credibility, and the humility required for genuine expertise.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques Paul Graham's essay 'You Weren't Meant to Have a Boss' for its condescending portrayal of corporate employees as caged zoo animals compared to the 'wild lions' of startup founders. While acknowledging Graham's fair points about taking risks while young and the value of startups, Atwood argues that Graham has devolved into 'participatory narcissism' where his essays have become more about projecting his own reality than offering universal software engineering wisdom. Atwood, himself a recent convert to startup life, finds the implication offensive that traditional employment cannot be fulfilling. The post concludes with a note that this critique led to a productive in-person meeting with Graham.
key_points: Paul Graham's comparison of corporate employees to caged zoo animals is condescending and dismissive of fulfilling careers in traditional employment
Graham's essays have shifted from being about software engineering to being elaborate mirrors reflecting his own startup-centric worldview
Young programmers should take career risks, but founding a startup is not the only path to professional fulfillment
Graham's perspective is warped by his environment at Y Combinator where he only interacts with startup founders
Disagreement and critique can lead to deeper, more satisfying communication when both parties engage constructively
money_quotes: I'm not sure why Mr. Graham felt the need to draw this incredibly condescending parallel with company employees and caged animals in the zoo.
These essays are no longer about software engineering; they're about Paul Graham.
He projects his reality outward, reflecting it against the rest of us so brightly and so strongly that we're temporarily blinded.
I work with young startup founders in their twenties. They're geniuses, and play by their own rules. Oh... you haven't founded a company? You suck.
The problem with this particular essay is the way Mr. Graham implies the only path to true happiness as a young programmer lies in founding a startup.
On the whole, I think I preferred Paul Graham's essays when they were more about software engineering and less about Paul Graham.
We stop seeing our own reality and trade it for his, in a form of participatory narcissism.
That is, to me, the point of posts like this – some initial disagreement ultimately leading to deeper, more satisfying communication.
posts.key_insight: When thought leaders project their narrow experience as universal truth, their advice becomes less about helping others and more about validating their own choices.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood draws a striking parallel between The Sierra Network, the first online multiplayer gaming system from 1991, and the then-new Mytopia gaming service on social networks like Facebook and MySpace. He recounts his personal experience with TSN, noting its impressive graphics compared to text-only BBS games, though it wasn't engaging enough to keep him. The post observes with wry amusement that nearly two decades of internet evolution led to essentially recreating the same casual multiplayer gaming experience, just wrapped in Web 2.0 packaging.
key_points: The Sierra Network (1991) was the first online multiplayer gaming system, offering RPGs, simulations, card games, and social features
Early online gaming graphics were revolutionary compared to text-only BBS alternatives
Mytopia rebuilt the same casual multiplayer gaming concept for Facebook and MySpace in 2008
Technology often cycles back to reinvent previous ideas with new packaging
money_quotes: The ImagiNation Network (INN), aka The Sierra Network (TSN), was the first online multiplayer gaming system.
The graphics were incredible for that era, at least compared to the text-only BBS games that passed for online multiplayer gaming at the time.
I finally achieved online multiplayer satisfaction a few years later with Doom, Dwango, and Kali.
The resemblance, indeed, is astonishing.
It has to be some sign of the coming internet apocalypse when a startup has essentially rebuilt The Sierra Network in Web 2.0 fashion.
posts.key_insight: The tech industry has a habit of reinventing the wheel, rebuilding decades-old concepts with contemporary technology and calling it innovation.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the enormous process overhead required to make even trivial code changes at large companies like Microsoft, contrasting it with the streamlined approach of open source development. He references Eric Lippert's famous enumeration of all the roles involved in adding a single function—from developers to translators to senior managers. Atwood then poses a provocative question: does open source software actually need all that process to succeed, or is the lack of bureaucratic baggage actually an evolutionary advantage? He suggests that open source compensates through community involvement and ubiquity, allowing developers to focus on features that delight users rather than navigating mountains of process. The post concludes with a pointed critique that Microsoft's risk-averse culture, which rewards killing features over shipping them, results in software that feels like it was designed by the perpetually gloomy Eeyore.
key_points: A simple five-line code change at Microsoft can require five man-weeks of work due to process overhead including testing, documentation, localization, and management coordination
Open source software typically lacks formal processes like test plans, translated documentation, and accessibility compliance—and this may be a feature rather than a bug
Community-driven development allows specialized concerns (like localization) to be handled by those who care most about them, freeing core developers to build delightful features
Large companies create cultures that reward risk aversion, where employees are promoted for killing features by emphasizing testing costs and support burdens
The pessimistic, process-heavy approach results in software that rarely ships new features and lacks customer delight
money_quotes: What may seem like a no-brainer five line code change to us on the outside is perhaps five man-weeks of work once you factor in all the required process overhead.
Isn't the radical lack of process baggage in open source software development not a weakness, but in fact an evolutionary advantage?
What open source software lacks in formal process it makes up ten times over in ubiquity and community.
If the Elbonians feel so strongly about localization, they can take that effort on themselves.

If you openly reward and promote people for killing work by bemoaning the risk and the testing cost and localization impact of each feature, everyone is going to grab pitchforks and jump on that 'No can do! No can ship!' bandwagon.
It's damn near wake-worthy sometimes for a feature to actually get out into shipping bits.
All too often, it really does feel like Microsoft's software was designed by Eeyore.
posts.key_insight: Excessive process overhead at large software companies doesn't just slow development—it creates a culture that actively rewards pessimism and feature-killing, while open source's lack of bureaucracy may actually be its greatest competitive advantage.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits Robert Glass' 'Facts and Fallacies of Software Engineering' and discovers he has written about nearly every topic in the book over the past four years. He presents the book's 55 facts organized across categories like people, tools, estimation, reuse, requirements, design, testing, and maintenance, followed by 10 fallacies that sound true but prove problematic in practice. Atwood frames the simple one-sentence summaries as 'zen koans' worth pondering based on personal experience. He argues that regularly thinking about our craft advances the field by building shared memory and history in software engineering.
key_points: The book's 55 facts span critical areas: people factors, tools, estimation, reuse, requirements, design, testing, maintenance, quality, and efficiency
Ten fallacies are presented as things that sound true but fail in practice, including 'you can't manage what you can't measure' and 'given enough eyeballs, all bugs are shallow'
The book is a 'what-is' exploration meant to surface problems for discussion, not a 'how-to' guide with solutions
Glass emphasizes that maintenance consumes 40-80% of software costs and is probably the most important lifecycle phase
Regular reflection on these fundamentals helps the field collectively advance through shared understanding
money_quotes: The most important factor in software work is the quality of the programmers.
Adding people to a late project makes it later.
Hype (about tools and technology) is a plague on the house of software.
It is not surprising that software estimates are bad. But we live and die by them anyway!
Reusable components are three times as hard to build and should be tried out in three different settings.
Maintenance is a solution – not a problem.
Many researchers advocate rather than investigate.
We should be thinking about our craft every day, in some small way, on our own software projects.
posts.key_insight: Periodically revisiting fundamental truths about software engineering—even as simple one-sentence statements—serves as valuable professional meditation that advances both individual practice and the collective field.

posts.summary: This brief post uses a clever visual joke—bumper stickers and t-shirts showing 'I ♥ Unicode' rendered with HTML entities—to highlight the persistent challenges of character encoding in software development. Atwood poses the rhetorical question of why this is funny, implying that programmers who understand Unicode pain will immediately get the joke. He provides educational resources for those who want to understand the underlying issues. The post serves as both humor and a gentle nudge for developers to learn about Unicode properly.
key_points: The humor derives from the irony of Unicode-related merchandise failing to properly render Unicode characters
Understanding why this joke is funny requires knowledge of character encoding issues
Proper Unicode knowledge is essential for modern software developers
Atwood curates key resources including Joel Spolsky's famous Unicode article
money_quotes: Here's my rhetorical question to you: why is this funny?
Here's hoping the next programmer who sees this is laughing, too – and for the right reasons.
posts.key_insight: If you don't immediately understand why 'I ♥ Unicode' merchandise is hilarious, you need to learn more about character encoding.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the lack of standardization for the middle mouse button, contrasting it with the well-defined conventions for left and right clicks. He traces the history of multi-button mice, noting that even the mouse's inventor Doug Engelbart believed mice should have many buttons. Despite mouse wheels being common since 1996, no universal meaning has emerged for the middle click. Atwood observes that tabbed browsing has created an emerging convention (opening/closing tabs), but the behavior remains inconsistent across applications. He criticizes the modal autoscroll feature and calls on Microsoft and Apple to establish standard middle-button behaviors.
key_points: Apple's single-button mouse was a mistake according to Jef Raskin, who helped create the Mac - mice were meant to have multiple buttons
Left and right mouse buttons have well-defined, universal meanings (select/activate and contextual menu), but the middle button does not
Middle-click has become loosely associated with tabbed interfaces in browsers - opening links in new tabs and closing tabs
The autoscroll mode triggered by middle-click is an unwanted modal behavior that many users accidentally trigger
UNIX/X Windows has used middle-click for paste since the 1980s, but this never transferred to Windows or Mac
money_quotes: Mice were meant to have multiple buttons.
In all those years, all those millions of mice shipped, no standard convention has emerged for what it means to press the middle mouse button.
Middle click has become strongly associated with tabbed user interfaces, at least in popular web browsers.
I personally hate this behavior. I prefer to scroll explicitly with the wheel, and I often trigger this unwanted 'mode' when I've slightly missed middle-clicking on a link.

It's frustrating to me that millions and millions of mice have shipped with this button, and yet it's a total crapshoot what will happen when you press the middle mouse button in any given application under any operating system.
If the first and second mouse buttons have standard, well-defined meanings today – why can't the third button, too?
posts.key_insight: Despite decades of multi-button mice being standard, the tech industry has failed to establish consistent meaning for the middle mouse button, representing a missed opportunity for better user experience.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood draws a parallel between the failed automated baggage system at Denver International Airport in 1994 and the similar failures at Heathrow's Terminal 5 in 2008. Both projects promised revolutionary automated baggage handling but collapsed under real-world conditions. The Denver system was scrapped entirely after 11 years, while Heathrow's system failed spectacularly on opening day. Atwood suggests these failures stem from hubris and misplaced faith in technology rather than the technology itself. The post serves as a warning about the cyclical nature of overambitious software project failures.
key_points: Denver's automated baggage system, despite its impressive 26-mile track infrastructure, was completely abandoned by 2005 in favor of manual handling
Heathrow's Terminal 5 repeated the same mistakes 10 years later with an untested baggage system that failed on opening day
The root cause identified by MIT professor Richard de Neufville was 'hubris' - assuming the system would work perfectly at maximum capacity with no margin for error
Complex enterprise systems inevitably have errors and inefficiencies that ambitious automated designs fail to account for
The pattern of software project failure repeats because organizations don't learn from history
money_quotes: It wasn't the technology per se, it was a misplaced faith in it.
The main culprit was hubris.
That left no room for the errors and inefficiencies that are inevitable in a complex enterprise.
It had never been tested before in a live terminal.
Otherwise, all we'll end up with is another sad entry in the long, dismal history of software project failure.
posts.key_insight: Software project failures repeat across decades not because of technological limitations, but because of human hubris and the persistent belief that complex systems will work perfectly without accounting for real-world messiness.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits a frequently misquoted 1989 study by Bruce Tognazzini claiming that mousing is faster than keyboarding. He argues that this ancient quote is consistently taken out of context and used for the wrong reasons in keyboard shortcut debates. Atwood points out that Tog himself acknowledged keyboard shortcuts are productivity wins when memorized, particularly for two-handed operations like cut/copy/paste. The post advocates for 'two-fisted computing' that combines both input methods rather than treating them as competing approaches. Atwood emphasizes that the 1989 context of mouse-skeptical users is irrelevant to modern computing with its massive menu forests.
key_points: The famous Tog quote about mousing being faster than keyboarding is from 1989 and consistently misapplied in modern debates
Most people only read the first paragraph and miss Tog's own acknowledgment that memorized keyboard shortcuts provide productivity gains
Two-handed input (selecting with mouse, acting with keyboard) offers solid productivity benefits
The 1989 computing context of Windows 2.0 and mouse-skeptical users is completely different from today's complex menu systems
Keyboard and mouse should complement each other rather than compete
money_quotes: Test subjects consistently report that keyboarding is faster than mousing. The stopwatch consistently proves mousing is faster than keyboarding.
This ancient quote is invariably trotted out during any discussion of keyboard shortcuts, and always for the wrong reasons.
When the keyboard shortcut is already memorized and well understood, it's a clear productivity win.
I've long been an advocate of two-fisted computing – using both your keyboard and your mouse to the fullest.
Keyboard shortcuts don't replace my mousing; they complement it.

I doubt Tog envisioned today's world of main menus with literally thousands of commands, a menu tree – no, a menu forest – so dense that search or some other alternate UI metaphor is the only rational way to navigate it all.
posts.key_insight: The keyboard vs. mouse debate is a false dichotomy; mastering both input methods together is the real productivity strategy.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines Jamie Zawinski's documented struggles with Linux upgrades at his DNA Lounge nightclub, where routine system updates repeatedly broke critical functionality. Despite being a world-class software engineer who helped popularize the 'Worse is Better' philosophy, Zawinski ultimately gave up on upgrading his Linux systems entirely, preferring to run 2003-era software indefinitely rather than deal with the constant breakage. Atwood uses this as a cautionary tale about software complexity, arguing that if someone of Zawinski's caliber can't reliably upgrade Linux, ordinary developers have little hope. The post questions the conventional wisdom that systems must always be kept current with security patches.
key_points: Jamie Zawinski, despite being an exceptional programmer praised by Peter Norvig, found Linux upgrades so problematic that he abandoned them entirely
A routine Fedora 4 security patch broke audio functionality in ways that defied logical explanation, requiring an upgrade to Fedora 5 to fix
Zawinski's attempt to upgrade kiosks from 2003-era Red Hat to Ubuntu resulted in worse stability, forcing him to revert permanently
The post challenges the orthodoxy that systems must always run the latest security patches, suggesting the maintenance burden may exceed the risk of being hacked
Even clean installs on spare drives with controlled hardware couldn't prevent upgrade failures
money_quotes: F**k that noise. I'm done upgrading anything ever.
They'll be running a 2003 version of Linux forever, because I frankly have better things to do with my time.
Apparently, Linux is so complex that even a world class software engineer can't always get it to work.
If he throws in the towel on Linux upgrades, then what possible hope do us mere mortals have?
That's right: upgrading to the latest FC4: breaks the world. Giving up on FC4 and going to FC5: un-breaks it. Nicely done, guys.
If we get hacked, oh well. I have backups. It has got to be less work to recover from than constantly dealing with this kind of nonsense.
posts.key_insight: Software complexity has reached a point where even expert practitioners rationally choose to freeze their systems in time rather than endure the constant breakage that upgrades bring.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood introduces Core War, a programming game from 1984 where two assembly programs battle in a shared memory space, each trying to destroy the other. The game was inspired by early self-replicating programs like Creeper and Reaper. Programs are written in Redcode, a simplified assembly language, and executed alternately by a simulator called MARS. Three main strategies have emerged over 25 years: Paper (replication), Rock (bombing), and Scissors (scanning and targeted attacks). Despite being decades old, Core War remains an active community with ongoing competitions.
key_points: Core War pits two programs against each other in shared memory, taking turns executing instructions until one crashes
The game uses Redcode, a deliberately simple assembly language with 10-19 instructions depending on variant
Three balanced strategies exist: replicators that spread copies, bombers that write illegal instructions, and scanners that hunt and destroy
The game has roots in early computing history, inspired by self-replicating programs at research labs
After 25 years of evolution, modern Core War programs have become quite sophisticated
money_quotes: Two battle programs are entered into the memory array at randomly chosen positions; neither program knows where the other one is.
The aim, of course, is to destroy the other program by ruining its instructions.
The battle ends when MARS comes to an instruction in one of the programs that cannot be executed.
A second self-duplicating program called Reaper was written. Its purpose was to destroy copies of Creeper until it could find no more and then to destroy itself.
It's structured so that there is no 'killer app'; three broad strategies are possible, each with its own strengths and weaknesses.
As you might imagine after 25 years of battlefield evolution, some modern Core War programs are quite baroque by now.
posts.key_insight: Core War demonstrates that even with an extremely simple instruction set and constrained environment, strategic depth and lasting community engagement can emerge from well-designed competitive programming games.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that developers should design the user interface before writing any code, as the UI is what end users actually experience. He acknowledges this is difficult because most programmers are trained on command-line tools and algorithms, not interface design. The post advocates for paper prototyping and PowerPoint mockups as ways to visualize the product without falling into the trap of turning prototypes into production code. Atwood emphasizes staying away from development tools during the design phase to avoid 'pouring concrete too early' and prematurely committing to implementation details.
key_points: Design the UI first before writing code because the interface IS the application from the user's perspective
Paper prototyping is a timeless skill that will remain valuable regardless of technology changes, unlike specific programming knowledge
Avoid using development tools for prototyping to prevent the temptation of turning mockups into production code
PowerPoint or Keynote prototyping offers a logical step up from paper with mild interactivity while remaining easy to iterate
Visual design patterns and established UI palettes help enforce technology constraints while still allowing creative interface design
money_quotes: When you're working on end-user software... you need to design the UI first.
Remember, to the end user, the interface is the application.

It is extremely important at this point to stay out of technical development environments when mocking your user interface, or the temptation to turn the model into the product may be too strong for your team to resist.
Technology has the shelf life of a banana.
Once you've learned paper prototyping, you can use it in every project you do for the rest of your career.
I need to create an image in my mind of what I'm building, as it will be seen by the world, before I start pouring the concrete to make it real.
Use whatever tool you like, as long as it's not a development tool. You don't want something too powerful.
posts.key_insight: Designing the user interface before writing code forces developers to think about what they're actually building for users, and using low-fidelity tools like paper prevents premature commitment to implementation details.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood makes the case for why programmers should invest in quality mousepads, arguing that most desk surfaces fail to provide the consistency needed for optimal mousing. He establishes criteria for when a mousepad is necessary (uneven surfaces, sensor interference, desk damage) and provides a framework for choosing one based on factors like thickness, material, and size. His top recommendation is the Razer Vespula for its reversible dual-surface design with hard plastic core. The post reflects Atwood's characteristic attention to the seemingly mundane tools that developers use daily, treating the humble mousepad as worthy of serious consideration.
key_points: Most desk surfaces are inadequate for consistent mousing due to uneven texture, sensor interference, or susceptibility to damage from extended use
A quality mousepad serves one core purpose: staking out a consistent, reliable, and durable mousing surface on your desktop
Reversible double-sided mousepads with thin metal or hard plastic cores represent the optimal design approach
Material matters significantly - hard surfaces are preferred over cloth/fabric mousepads which tend to be 'floppy' and 'disintegrating'
Size considerations are important - quality gaming mousepads tend to be large (roughly iPad-sized or bigger)
money_quotes: I'd love to be a minimalist and go without any kind of mousepad, but I always end up needing one.

The average desktop often does not provide a consistent mousing surface; a well-designed mousepad does. That is its purpose – to stake out a consistent, reliable, and durable mousing surface on your desktop.
The general idea of a reversible, double sided mousepad with a thin metal or hard plastic core is close to the best of all worlds.
I could also talk about how I regularly lubricate my mice feet and mousepads, but then I'd worry that people might think I've gone too far with my mouse fetish.
I don't recommend the cloth / fabric branch of the XTrac family tree – or any mousing surface, for that matter.
It's my hope that after reading this, you'll be able to tell a well-designed, quality mousing surface from those cheap, floppy, disintegrating fabric things that are mousepads in name only.
posts.key_insight: The tools we interact with constantly deserve careful consideration, and even something as overlooked as a mousepad can meaningfully impact daily computing when chosen thoughtfully.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood provides a practical step-by-step guide for setting up Subversion on Windows, from server installation to client configuration with TortoiseSVN. He positions Subversion as the pragmatic choice for free source control due to its ubiquity and simplicity, while acknowledging the emerging distributed version control systems. The post emphasizes that any source control is better than none (especially better than Visual SourceSafe), and includes specific Windows configuration tips for ASP.NET developers. Atwood concludes with skepticism about whether distributed source control will gain mainstream adoption, given that most developers barely understand centralized version control.
key_points: Subversion offers the best combination of availability, simplicity, and community support among free source control systems
The entire server and client setup can be completed in under 30 minutes on Windows
TortoiseSVN's shell extension approach requires specific configuration to avoid system-wide performance issues
Distributed version control systems like Git are emerging but face adoption challenges due to their complexity
Most developers struggle to understand even basic centralized version control concepts like branching and merging
money_quotes: Source control is source control, as long as you're not using Visual SourceSafe.
Every good programmer always comments their source control actions, right?
Of course, none of this means your developers will actually understand branching and merging.
Some influential developers – most notably Linus Torvalds – hate Subversion and view it as an actual evil.
I've met precious few developers that really understood the versioning concepts in the simple centralized source control model.
I have only the vaguest of hopes that these developers will be able to wrap their brains around the vastly more complicated and powerful model of distributed source control.
It took fifteen years for centralized source control usage to become mainstream, so a little patience is always advisable.
posts.key_insight: The best source control system is one that developers will actually use and understand, which makes ubiquity and simplicity more valuable than theoretical superiority.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood invites readers to help name his upcoming developer community website, which would later become Stack Overflow. He reflects on the extreme difficulty of choosing a good domain name in an era of Web 2.0 startups and rampant domain squatting. The post presents a poll of potential names, with stackoverflow.com ultimately winning with 25% of the vote. Atwood emphasizes the importance of naming as a crucial early step in building a new product, while acknowledging that finding an available, memorable domain is remarkably challenging.
key_points: Naming a website is extraordinarily difficult, even when consulting experienced developers
The domain name landscape is a 'wasteland' of taken names and domain squatters
Community input can be valuable for major branding decisions
Having a name helps crystallize and communicate what you're building
Even serious companies sometimes end up with unintentionally problematic domain names (like experts-exchange.com)
money_quotes: Naming a website is hard. Really, really hard.
You begin to have a new respect for all the crazily-named Web 2.0 startups.
What were the people who named experts-exchange.com thinking? I'm not so sure they were.
It sure helps to put a name on it first.
I'm warning you: it's a wasteland out there.
You'll have to be pretty clever indeed to come up with an interesting, simple name that isn't taken – or, worse, domain-squatted.
posts.key_insight: Even for experienced developers building what would become one of the most influential programming websites, naming remains one of the hardest and most underappreciated challenges in launching a product.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood chronicles his lifelong obsession with arcade games, from childhood pilgrimages to discover new machines to adult pursuits of building authentic arcade hardware. He traces how this passion directly led to his programming career, as his father insisted he write games rather than just play them. The post serves as both memoir and practical guide, walking readers through escalating levels of arcade nostalgia: coffee table books, MAME emulation, custom cabinet builds, and commercial arcade controllers. Atwood positions arcade enthusiasm as a defining characteristic of true geek identity, suggesting that the compulsion to build MAME hardware is practically a credential requirement.
key_points: Arcade games were a gateway to programming for many developers, with parents using the promise of home gaming as motivation to learn BASIC
MAME emulation has become a rite of passage for geeks and the acid test for any new hardware platform
Building authentic arcade hardware is presented as a natural progression for anyone serious about retro gaming
The X-Arcade controller series represents the best affordable option for authentic arcade controls without custom builds
Adult geeks with disposable income can pursue arcade nostalgia to impressive extremes, including adding dedicated arcade wings to their homes
money_quotes: I can also attribute my career in software development to arcade games.
Like many software developers, my introduction to programming was my Dad telling me if I wanted to play video games at home, I had to write them first.
Tough love hurts.
It is the acid test for any new hardware platform, whether it's a phone, a PDA, or even a digital camera: can we make it run MAME?
Now that I'm thinking about it, I actually question the credentials of any geek who hasn't felt compelled to build hardware for MAME at some point.
I warned you. It's an addiction.
posts.key_insight: For many developers, the obsessive pursuit of arcade games wasn't just childhood entertainment—it was the crucible that forged their programming careers and continues to define geek identity into adulthood.

posts.summary: Atwood examines the growing reluctance among programmers to pay for commercial software tools, sparked by a comment stating 'we don't use software that costs money here.' He traces this shift not just to piracy or frugality, but to the rapid improvement of free alternatives like Eclipse, Subversion, MySQL, and Firefox. The post argues that commercial tool vendors can no longer rest on their laurels—they must continuously prove their value proposition against relentlessly improving free competition. Using examples from SmugMug and file comparison tools, Atwood concludes that survival in the face of 'free' requires delivering a genuinely premium experience that justifies the cost.
key_points: Free software alternatives have improved dramatically, with tools like MySQL and PHP evolving from 'toy' status to credible enterprise solutions
The competitive pressure of free products on commercial tools intensifies every year, forcing vendors to continuously innovate
