Commercial software can survive by being 'the first, the free, or the good'—and if you're neither first nor free, you must be demonstrably better
Free has its own weaknesses: it tends to be 'cheap, mass-produced, and the same for everyone'
The audience for development tools is uniquely challenging because the people who would buy them are exactly the same people who could build them
money_quotes: We don't use software that costs money here.
These people used to be called pirates. Now they're open source enthusiasts.
The competitive pressure of free products on commercial tools intensifies every year. It's relentless.
Resting on their laurels is a luxury they no longer have.
A free program need not be glamorous or even completely bug-free. It can garner a respectable following simply by not costing anything.
I've seen many times people struggle and struggle on with a clunky freeware app just because they're not willing to pay $20 for a significantly better alternative.
The type of people who would buy development tools are also exactly the same people who could potentially build them.
Free is indeed a competitive advantage. But free is also a weakness: it is cheap, mass-produced, and the same for everyone.
posts.key_insight: Commercial software can only survive against free alternatives by delivering a premium experience that justifies the cost—a challenge that grows harder every year as open source tools relentlessly improve.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces his $5,000 donation to the .NET open source community, fulfilling a promise made nearly a year earlier to give back advertising revenue. After months of deliberating over complex voting schemes, he simplifies the process and awards the entire grant to ScrewTurn Wiki based on community feedback. He emphasizes that open source projects are treated as second-class citizens in Microsoft's ecosystem and argues this must change. The post reflects on the personal financial risk of making this donation after leaving his job, while affirming his belief in the power of programmers building things together.
key_points: Open source projects are treated as second-class citizens in the Microsoft ecosystem, receiving virtually no support despite contributing significantly to the .NET community
Sometimes the best approach is to abandon elaborate plans and keep things simple - one winner gets everything rather than a complex voting bracket system
Giving back to the community is what makes the pain of advertising worthwhile and transforms a necessary evil into something meaningful
Real generosity involves some personal risk and discomfort - if it isn't a little scary, it's not worth doing
Microsoft matched the $5,000 contribution, bringing the total community investment to $10,000
money_quotes: Open source projects are treated as second-class citizens in the Microsoft ecosystem.
It's an open secret amongst bloggers that the blog comments are often better than the original blog post, and it's because the community collectively knows far more than you or I will ever know.
Giving back to the community is what makes the pain of advertising worthwhile.
But I figure if it isn't a little scary, it's not worth doing.
And I have found that you get back what you give, many times over.
There are no strings attached to this money of any kind. I trust the judgment of a fellow programmer to run their project as they see fit.
I wouldn't have kept banging out entries on this blog for the last four years if I didn't truly believe in the power of programmers collectively building useful stuff together.
posts.key_insight: Supporting open source isn't just altruism—it's an investment in the programming community that returns value many times over, and meaningful giving should involve enough personal risk to feel a little scary.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits his laptop power consumption analysis, this time measuring his newer Dell XPS M1330 ultraportable using a Kill-a-Watt electricity monitor. He systematically tests power draw from various components including the LCD, hard drive, WiFi, CPU, GPU, DVD drive, and fan. The data reveals that CPU and GPU are by far the largest power consumers, with the CPU jumping from 20W idle to 63W under full load. Based on these measurements, Atwood provides practical advice for maximizing battery life, recommending Vista's Power Saver mode which throttles CPU to 50% as the most effective strategy.
key_points: The CPU is the biggest battery drain, consuming 43W more under full load than at idle - far exceeding any other component
GPU-intensive tasks like gaming are the second largest power drain, adding 20-35W to baseline consumption
Screen brightness has minimal impact on power consumption, only varying by 3W across all settings
WiFi uses relatively little power when idle but can add 7W during active bandwidth transfers
Vista's Power Saver mode with 50% CPU throttle is the most practical way to extend battery life without constantly monitoring usage
money_quotes: It's awfully handy to know how much power the stuff around your house is using.
The rules of laptop power consumption haven't fundamentally changed.
Keep a serious eye on your CPU usage; you desperately want to avoid CPU intensive programs and websites while on battery.
They'll kill your battery life far faster than anything else you can do with your laptop.
On a dual-core CPU, giving up one core is usually no big deal for most tasks.
posts.key_insight: CPU usage is the dominant factor in laptop battery life, and enabling automatic CPU throttling through power management settings is more effective than manually monitoring and adjusting individual components.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques the frustrating practice of web session timeouts, explaining the technical reasons behind them (HTTP's stateless nature, performance concerns, and security considerations) while arguing that developers can and should do better. He acknowledges that servers need to manage memory and protect against session hijacking, but points out that solutions exist—like Gmail's approach of using JavaScript heartbeats to keep sessions alive. The post ultimately calls on developers to prioritize user experience over their own convenience, noting that users have lives beyond any single web application.
key_points: HTTP is stateless by design, requiring cookies to maintain session state between requests
Servers expire sessions primarily for performance (memory/database management) and security (limiting session hijacking windows)
Technical constraints don't excuse poor user experience—solutions like JavaScript heartbeats and HTTPS exist
Gmail demonstrates that indefinite sessions are technically achievable for mainstream web applications
Developers should test their applications for session timeout issues and design with realistic user behavior in mind
money_quotes: Every individual request your browser sends to a web server is a newborn babe, cruelly born into a world that is utterly and completely oblivious to its existence.
The browser definitely isn't the forgetful party here.
As a user, I can say pretty unequivocally that session expiration sucks.
Despite all rumors to the contrary, your users will not be dedicating their entire lives to using your web application in a punctual and timely manner.

Is it really fair to kick users all the way out of your web application, or worse, blindly reject data they've submitted – just because they were impudent enough to wait a few hours since their last supplication to the web server gods?
In most web apps, the penance is awfully severe for such a common sin.
posts.key_insight: Technical constraints like session management exist to serve users, not punish them—developers should invest in solutions that maintain security without sacrificing user experience.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces his new venture with Joel Spolsky: Stack Overflow, a Q&A site for programmers. He explains his motivation for leaving his job was to build something meaningful, not just blog full-time, citing industry role models who both write and build. Despite past public criticism of Spolsky, Atwood emphasizes that disagreement is healthy and expresses admiration for Joel's approach to building a sustainable programmer-friendly company. The goal is to unlock programming knowledge trapped in forums, help files, and unread books, making it easily searchable and accessible to all programmers regardless of their technology stack.
key_points: Atwood founded Stack Overflow with Joel Spolsky despite having publicly criticized him in the past, demonstrating that professional collaboration doesn't require complete agreement
The world's best tech voices (Paul Graham, Joel Spolsky, etc.) share one trait: they build things, not just write about them
Stack Overflow's mission is to 'collectively increase the sum total of good programming knowledge in the world'
The site was positioned as the anti-Experts-Exchange: accessible, clean, and without the sleazy search engine gaming
Good programming knowledge is trapped in forums, help files, and books nobody reads anymore, and Stack Overflow aims to liberate it
money_quotes: The world has enough vapid commentary blogs. I want to build stuff – and talk about it.
I distrust people who agree with me all the time.
If you agree with everything I write here, why would you bother reading? At that point, we're the same person.
There's far too much great programming information trapped in forums, buried in online help, or hidden away in books that nobody buys any more.
Stackoverflow is sort of like the anti-experts-exchange (minus the nausea-inducing sleaze and quasi-legal search engine gaming) meets wikipedia meets programming reddit.
I refuse to become a full-time blogger. I think that's a cop-out.
Better programming is our goal.
posts.key_insight: The most respected voices in tech don't just write—they build things, and the best programming knowledge needs to be liberated from obscure corners of the internet and made universally accessible.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents upgrading his custom-built PC by swapping in a new Intel E8500 CPU and overclocking it from 3.16 GHz to 4 GHz. He argues that the real value of building your own PC lies in the ability to incrementally upgrade components, something rarely possible with laptops or commercial systems. Through JavaScript benchmarks, he demonstrates that the 33% clock speed increase yields a meaningful 19% performance improvement. He maintains his controversial stance that quad-core CPUs are wasteful for desktop users since most applications can't utilize multiple cores effectively, making a fast dual-core with overclocking the smarter choice.
key_points: Building your own PC pays dividends through upgradability - you can swap components over time rather than replacing the entire system
Overclocking provides real, measurable performance gains: a 33% clock speed increase translated to 19% faster JavaScript benchmark times
Quad-core CPUs offer minimal practical benefit for typical desktop use since most applications are barely multithreaded
Sometimes you get lucky with upgrade paths - the E8500 was a drop-in replacement on a year-old motherboard without requiring new RAM or motherboard
BIOS flashing carries real risk - Atwood bricked his motherboard using Windows-based flash software and recommends boot CD or USB-based updates
money_quotes: In my opinion, quad-core CPUs are still a waste of electricity unless you're putting them in a server.
The economy of building your own PC also lies in upgrading it in pieces and parts to keep it up to date.
I overclocked my E6600 CPU from 2.4 Ghz to 3.2 Ghz, instantly increasing the value of the processor by about 800 bucks.
Compare the result of going from 3 GHz to 4 GHz with adding another two cores, which would produce exactly zero improvement in your JavaScript benchmarks.
Most apps are barely multithreaded, much less capable of taking advantage of all four cores.
Having four CPU cores won't help you much when they're all poking along at a leisurely 2 GHz.
Let this be a lesson to you, kids: don't trust Windows software developers!
posts.key_insight: The true value proposition of building your own PC isn't just the initial cost savings, but the ability to make targeted upgrades over time that maximize performance per dollar spent.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits his controversial stance that quad-core CPUs are wasteful for most users, acknowledging that software developers may be an exception. He argues that while benchmark data shows limited benefits from more than two cores for typical applications, developers have legitimate reasons to want many-core systems: testing parallelized code, compiling C++ projects faster, and simulating server workloads. However, he notes that managed code compilation in .NET doesn't show dramatic multi-core improvements, and points out that most modern developers write web applications in interpreted languages that achieve concurrency through different means entirely.
key_points: Dual-core CPUs provide meaningful benefits including protection from runaway processes, but quad-core advantages only appear in specific workloads like video encoding, 3D rendering, and scientific simulations
C++ developers see compelling compilation time improvements with quad-core CPUs, but managed code (.NET) compilation shows minimal benefit from additional cores
Software developers aren't typical users and may need multi-core systems to properly test and debug parallelized applications
Most mainstream developers write web applications in interpreted languages that achieve concurrency without threading, making desktop core count less relevant to their actual production code
The multitasking argument for quad-core is weak because scenarios that truly stress more than two cores border on the absurd for typical usage
money_quotes: Dual-core CPUs protect you from badly written software; if a crashed program consumes all possible CPU time, all it can get is 50% of your CPU.
The amount of multitasking required to seriously load more than two CPU cores borders on the absurd.
Software developers aren't typical users. Indeed, you can make a reasonable case that software developers are almost by definition edge conditions.
If you're a C++ developer, you need a quad-core CPU yesterday. Demand it.
I suggest starting with a goal of scaling well to two cores, as that appears to be the most challenging part of the journey.
Everything I've ever read on the topic of writing scalable, concurrent software goes out of its way to explain in excruciating detail how hellishly difficult this kind of code is to write.

Most developers aren't writing desktop applications today. They're writing web applications... this code somehow achieves massive levels of concurrency, scales to huge workloads, and drives some of the largest websites on the internet. All that, without thinking one iota about concurrency, threading, or reentrancy.
posts.key_insight: The value of many-core CPUs for developers depends entirely on what they're building—C++ developers need them desperately, while web developers using interpreted languages achieve concurrency through architecture rather than threads.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood pushes back against Dijkstra's famous claim that BASIC 'mentally mutilates' programmers, arguing instead that BASIC served as a crucial gateway to programming for an entire generation. He recounts his own nostalgic experience programming on the Atari 2600 BASIC cartridge, noting how every home computer of the era booted directly to a BASIC prompt. Rather than damaging minds, Atwood argues BASIC opened them—serving as an early test of programming aptitude and launching countless careers. The real problem, he suggests, isn't learning the 'wrong' language but programmers who refuse to evolve beyond their first paradigms.
key_points: Dijkstra's dismissal of BASIC as mentally damaging is elitist and ignores its role in democratizing programming
Every major home computer of the 8-bit era had BASIC burned into ROM, making it unavoidable for aspiring programmers
The real problem isn't learning a 'bad' first language—it's programmers who 'write FORTRAN in any language' regardless of what they learn
BASIC served as an effective early filter to identify those with programming aptitude and interest
The accessibility of BASIC (and later Turbo Pascal at $49.99) was crucial for democratizing programming education
money_quotes: It is practically impossible to teach good programming style to students that have had prior exposure to BASIC; as potential programmers they are mentally mutilated beyond hope of regeneration.
The largest hurdle I see isn't any particular choice of language, but the fact that programmers can write FORTRAN in any language.
To quote Pogo, we have met the enemy, and he is us.
I'm the only person crazy enough to actually try programming in this thing.
After writing my first 'program,' I got that same visceral little thrill of bending the machine to my will that I've always gotten.
For a long, long time, if you were interested in computers at all, you programmed in BASIC. It was as unavoidable and inevitable as the air you breathed.
BASIC didn't mutilate the mind, as Dijkstra claimed. If anything, BASIC opened the minds of millions of young programmers.
The spirit of BASIC lives on in all of us.
posts.key_insight: The accessibility of a programming language matters more than its theoretical purity—BASIC's ubiquity and low barrier to entry launched more programming careers than elegant languages ever could.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents his alarming discovery that a barely-trafficked WordPress blog was consuming excessive CPU resources, with the PHP FastCGI process running at unsustainable levels. He found that WordPress performs twenty database queries on every page load even when nothing has changed, which he considers fundamentally irresponsible design. The problem is so widespread that an entire ecosystem of caching plugins exists to fix it. Atwood argues that WP-Cache functionality should have been built into WordPress core years ago, comparing it unfavorably to ASP.NET's built-in caching solutions. He expresses relief that his own Coding Horror blog uses Movable Type's static rendering approach instead. The post concludes with an update noting that Matt Mullenweg provided MySQL optimization tips and that a Google Summer of Code project was finally addressing the issue.
key_points: A default WordPress install queries the database twenty times per page refresh, even when nothing has changed
The CPU usage problem is so common that a cottage industry of caching plugins (WP-Cache, WP-Super-Cache, Bad Behavior) has emerged to address it
Other frameworks like ASP.NET have had page output caching built in for years, making WordPress's omission inexcusable
Matt Mullenweg's dismissive response to the caching issue demonstrates poor prioritization by WordPress leadership
Static rendering approaches like Movable Type's, though derided as outdated, are far more resilient to traffic spikes
money_quotes: A default WordPress install will query the database twenty times every time you refresh the page, even if not one single element on that page has changed. Doesn't that strike you as a bad idea?
I think it's absolutely irresponsible that WP-Cache like functionality isn't already built into WordPress.
I cannot accept that a default, bare-bones WordPress install hasn't the first clue how to cache and avoid expensive, redundant trips to the database.

Their classic static rendering blog engine approach may be derided today, but I shudder to think of the number of times the Coding Horror webserver would have been completely incapacitated over the years by the naïve – no, that's too tame – brainlessly stupid dynamic rendering approach WordPress uses.
Yes, the easiest path, the path of least resistance, is to mindlessly query the database every time you're building a page.
For a bare-bones blog which is doing approximately nothing, this is a completely unacceptable result. It's appalling.
posts.key_insight: Popular software that prioritizes ease of development over sensible defaults creates systemic problems that shouldn't require third-party fixes to achieve basic acceptable performance.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while he believes in paying for software to support developers, the registration and licensing process often punishes paying customers with a worse experience than non-paying users get. Using SmartFTP as an example, he walks through the sixteen frustrating steps required to register software, including confusing tier selections, lengthy order forms, and complex license file management. He contrasts this with open source alternatives like FileZilla where donating is simple and requires no ongoing license management. The post concludes that registration friction may drive users toward simpler open source alternatives, even when commercial software is technically superior.
key_points: Paying customers often have a worse experience than non-paying users due to complex registration processes
Tiered pricing forces users to make uncomfortable decisions and feels like being 'gamed' through consumer surplus capture
License management creates ongoing burden: tracking files, computer-tied licenses, annual renewals, and credential management
The registration burden multiplies painfully across applications and team members in organizations
Open source donation models offer a friction-free alternative that respects the user's time
money_quotes: Our programmer salaries don't come from magical money trees. They come from customers laying down cold, hard cash for the software we've built.
Often, registering software leaves me with a worse experience than not registering.
I now feel like I'm being gamed.
Et voilà! In only sixteen fun and easy steps, I have registered this software and voted with my wallet!
I have apps that I simply don't use because I'm too lazy to re-register them on my new install.
Oh, and step three? There is no step three!
In the future, I think I'll be voting with my wallet for the registration process that makes my life easier, not harder.
posts.key_insight: Software vendors who make registration painful are inadvertently pushing customers toward simpler open source alternatives, undermining the very revenue they're trying to protect.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood makes a compelling case for building a home theater PC in 2008, detailing his upgrade to AMD's new 780G integrated graphics platform. He demonstrates that for under $250 (or $523 for a complete build), you can assemble a powerful, silent, and energy-efficient HTPC that outperforms dedicated media devices. The key breakthrough is AMD's integrated graphics chipset that finally delivers acceptable performance while consuming remarkably little power—just 46 watts at idle despite including a TV tuner and three hard drives. Atwood endorses Windows Media Center as excellent HTPC software and provides practical tips for display configuration using PowerStrip.
key_points: The AMD 780G platform offers DirectX 10 integrated graphics with HDMI/DVI output, making it ideal for HTPC builds at a fraction of previous costs
Power consumption has dramatically improved—a more powerful system now draws 46 watts versus 80 watts for the older, weaker build
Any modern dual-core processor is more than sufficient for 1080p and Blu-Ray playback, with smooth playback possible even at 1.5GHz
AMD's Cool n' Quiet technology provides superior power management, throttling down to 1GHz and barely over one volt at idle
Getting pixel-perfect display output to TVs requires utilities like PowerStrip since drivers often don't support non-standard TV resolutions
money_quotes: It's shocking how cheap and easy it is to build a home theater PC these days.
For the longest time, integrated graphics was synonymous with craptacular graphics.
FORTY. SIX. WATTS.
We're talking about a powerful modern PC here, with quite a bit of additional hardware you wouldn't find in most PCs, including a dual TV tuner PCI card and three hard drives.
Silence and efficiency were nowhere near this easy three or four years ago.
I'm also glad to have the opportunity to support AMD because I'm desperately afraid of a world where Intel is the only CPU vendor. And you should be too.
We use our home theater PC every day.
posts.key_insight: Building a capable home theater PC has become so affordable and energy-efficient that there's little reason not to replace dedicated media devices with a more versatile computer.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the paradox that while most programmers don't read books, the best programming books remain invaluable. He criticizes the technical book publishing industry for producing mostly low-quality, bloated, or overly basic content that has been rightfully supplanted by online resources. However, he argues that truly great programming books are timeless because they explain 'why' rather than 'how' and transcend specific languages or platforms. The post concludes with a passionate defense of classic programming literature and a call for developers to read the foundational texts that reward repeated reading throughout a career.
key_points: The average software developer doesn't own or read books about programming, a trend that predates the internet
Most programming books fail because they're too long, too basic, or focus on quick-fix solutions rather than deep understanding
The internet has made most programming books obsolete for practical, language-specific information
Timeless programming books focus on 'why' rather than 'how' and don't need to be replaced every five years
Stack Overflow was designed to complement classic programming books, not replace them
money_quotes: The average software developer, for example, doesn't own a single book on the subject of his or her work, and hasn't ever read one.
The internet has rendered programming books obsolete. It's faster, more efficient, and just plain smarter to get your programming information online.
There seems to be an inverse relationship between the size of a programming book and its quality.
The monomaniacal focus on right now and the fastest, easiest possible way to do things leads beginners down the wrong path – or as I like to call it, 'PHP.'
Programming book pornography. The idea that having a pile of thick, important-looking programming books sitting on your shelf, largely unread, will somehow make you a better programmer.
If you feel compelled to clean house on your bookshelf every five years, trust me on this, you're buying the wrong programming books.
The best programming books are timeless. They transcend choice of language, IDE, or platform. They do not explain how, but why.
posts.key_insight: While most programming books deserve their obsolescence, the timeless classics that explain 'why' rather than 'how' remain essential reading that distinguishes great developers from the rest.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood tackles the age-old debate of whether websites should use the 'www' prefix in their URLs. He argues that while this is often treated as a critical decision, it's ultimately a 'bikeshed discussion' that distracts from what really matters. He provides practical advice: pick one form and stick with it, ensure both versions work, and consider technical factors like subdomain usage and cookie scope. He notably admits regretting the choice of stackoverflow.com over www.stackoverflow.com due to cookie-related issues discovered years later.
key_points: Consistency matters more than which form you choose - splitting links between www and non-www hurts SEO more than picking the 'wrong' one
Both URL forms should work regardless of your preference - serving 404s for the 'wrong' form is bad internet citizenship
Cookie scope is a hidden gotcha: cookies on example.com are sent to ALL subdomains, which can cause problems at scale
The www prefix is effectively dead in spoken and printed communication, even if used technically
This debate is a classic example of bikeshedding - obsessing over trivial decisions while ignoring what matters
money_quotes: This is one of those intractable problems. Global wars have been fought over so much less.
If half your incoming links are split between the www and non-www forms of your URL, that will hurt you a heck of lot more than picking the 'wrong' prefix.
Serving up visitors who assumed you had a leading www prefix a 404 page is just plain bad internet citizenship, and borderline rude.
If you pick the domain example.com, be aware that all cookies you store on that domain will be sent to all subdomains… forever.
This is a major downside I didn't discover until years later, and it's big enough to make me regret choosing stackoverflow.com versus www.stackoverflow.com.
You should be far more concerned about the content behind that URL than the URL itself.
It's the kind of meaningless distraction that is parodied beautifully in the animated series Home Movies.
posts.key_insight: The www vs non-www debate is a classic bikeshed discussion - consistency and proper redirects matter far more than which form you choose, and your energy is better spent on the content behind the URL.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while multicore processors are overhyped for most daily computing tasks, DVD encoding represents an ideal use case where additional cores deliver dramatic performance improvements. He demonstrates how modern video codecs like H.264 can compress DVDs to a quarter of their original size with minimal quality loss, using the free Handbrake software. The post walks through his encoding workflow, including preserving original digital audio quality through AC3 passthrough. Atwood concludes that re-encoding DVDs transforms them from inflexible physical media into versatile digital files accessible across all devices, finally giving those idle CPU cores meaningful work to do.
key_points: Multicore CPUs are overrated for typical computing but perfect for video encoding, where doubling cores roughly halves encode time
Modern codecs like H.264 can reduce DVD size to 25% of original while maintaining near-identical quality
The DVD is becoming 'the new MP3' - compressed digital media we'll toss around casually
AC3 audio passthrough preserves original DVD digital audio quality without any degradation
Re-encoding transforms inflexible physical media into versatile files playable on any device
money_quotes: I say the DVD is the new MP3. We're going to start tossing these things around like candy.
We can turn our digital DVDs into better digital DVDs through superior computer science.
Like The Six Million Dollar Man, we can rebuild them better than they were before.
Seriously, I get chills. It's geek nirvana.
It's also a perfect use of those four CPU cores, which would otherwise lay idle 99% of the time.
A re-encoded DVD is so much more flexible than those physical hunks of round, reflective plastic.
Nearly the same digital video quality, exactly the same digital audio quality, all wrapped up in a single file less than a quarter of the original size of the DVD.
posts.key_insight: While multicore computing remains overhyped for general use, video encoding is the perfect niche application that transforms those idle CPU cores into a practical tool for liberating your media from physical constraints.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on finally purchasing a Garmin Nuvi GPS device after prices dropped dramatically from $500+ to under $200. He describes his previous workaround of using a laptop with external USB GPS, acknowledging it was unwieldy and sometimes dangerous. After two weeks of use, he enthusiastically endorses the Nuvi as offering an outstanding consumer experience comparable to the original TiVo. He argues that GPS devices have transitioned from high-end geeky toys to practical, affordable technology that everyone should own.
key_points: GPS devices have undergone a dramatic price crash, making them accessible to mainstream consumers
The Garmin Nuvi represents a breakthrough in user experience design for GPS products, prioritizing the complete consumer experience over just technical functionality
People will use suboptimal solutions (like laptops for navigation) to save money, even when those solutions are dangerous or inconvenient
Products that 'just work' can completely change user behavior and break old habits
The transition from geek toy to mainstream essential follows a similar pattern to TiVo and DVRs
money_quotes: After using it for about two weeks, I feel like an idiot for waiting so long.
I'd rate it up there with the original Tivo.
Everything about it just works, to the point that it completely breaks you of the old ways of navigation.
I used to think of GPS devices as high-end geeky toys.
People – myself included – will do crazy things to save a buck.
I'm what you might call an indoor enthusiast.
And I strongly suspect that once you have a Nuvi, like me, you'll wonder how anyone ever lived without it.
posts.key_insight: When technology becomes affordable and user-friendly enough, it transitions from niche geek toy to essential mainstream tool that makes people wonder how they ever lived without it.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains the Model-View-Controller (MVC) pattern by tracing its origins to Trygve Reenskaug's 1979 Smalltalk work and then making it concrete through familiar examples. He argues that the web itself embodies MVC perfectly: HTML as Model, CSS as View, and the browser as Controller. The post demonstrates how this separation of concerns has driven the success of both the web and popular frameworks like Django and Rails. Atwood concludes with a practical litmus test: if your application is skinnable, you've likely implemented MVC correctly.
key_points: MVC was invented by Trygve Reenskaug in 1979 for Smalltalk, making it one of software's oldest and most enduring architectural patterns
The web's fundamental structure (HTML/CSS/Browser) is itself an implementation of MVC, which helps explain the web's massive success
Proper MVC separation enables skinnability - the ability to completely change an application's appearance without touching its logic
The controller in web apps has two parts: the server that routes requests and the handlers that process them and interact with the database
Designers understand templates with holes (views) but struggle with logic - MVC's separation solves this practical collaboration problem
money_quotes: You're looking at MVC right now.
So if you believe the web has been at all successful – most signs I've seen point to yes – then you also have to acknowledge the incredible power of Model-View-Controller.
My experience is that designers don't understand loops or any kind of state. They do understand templates with holes in them. Everybody understands mail merge.
Skinnability cuts to the very heart of the MVC pattern.
If you have the view and the logic together, when you make a copy of the view you copy the logic as well. That breaks one of our primary rules as developers: have only one place to change anything.
If your app isn't skinnable, that means you've probably gotten your model's chocolate in your view's peanut butter, quite by accident.
I look at a website as nothing but a graph with edges with POSTs and GETs that routes pages.
posts.key_insight: The web's success validates MVC's power, and the ultimate test of proper MVC implementation is whether your application can be reskinned without touching its logic.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that consumers should buy music from Amazon's DRM-free MP3 store rather than Apple's iTunes, despite iTunes' market dominance. He explains that record labels are offering Amazon better terms and DRM-free music specifically to undermine Apple's growing power in digital music distribution. While acknowledging the irony that record labels created the DRM monster they now weaponize, Atwood dismisses arguments that this competition is 'unfair' to Apple. He contends that regardless of the labels' motives, the end result—DRM-free music becoming the norm—is a clear win for consumers that should be supported with purchasing dollars.
key_points: Amazon offers the same music as iTunes at equal or lower prices, with better quality (256-bit) and no DRM restrictions
Record labels are deliberately giving Amazon better terms to break Apple's dominance in digital music, despite having originally demanded DRM on iTunes
Once media is sold DRM-free, there's no historical precedent for reverting back to DRM—this represents irreversible progress
Being an advocate for users matters more than being an advocate for any particular company, even one as successful as Apple
Every purchase of DRM-encumbered music when DRM-free alternatives exist is an implicit vote for more consumer-hostile restrictions
money_quotes: Every purchase of DRM-ed music, in the face of Amazon's excellent alternative, is an implicit vote for more useless, aggravating DRM on your music.
I'd much rather be an advocate for my fellow users than an advocate of any one particular company.
I've been emotionally involved with companies before, and it rarely ends well. I find that corporations never reciprocate your love in quite the same way.
There's absolutely no historical evidence that a type of media, once it is officially sold DRM free, can somehow revert back to the DRM model.
The irony of the record labels attacking a Frankenstein monster of their very own creation is almost overwhelming.
I'll certainly agree that the stunning success of the iTunes Store is what led us to this competitive situation in the first place, and that's entirely to Apple's credit.
The RIAA and the major record labels are as close as you can get to pure evil while not actively killing small children, puppies, and kittens.
posts.key_insight: When corporations fight each other in ways that benefit consumers, users should embrace the outcome regardless of which company 'wins'—loyalty to users trumps loyalty to brands.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques the ubiquitous adoption of XML as the default format for configuration files, data storage, and structured text, arguing that its verbosity creates an 'angle bracket tax' that obscures actual information. He acknowledges XML's democratic appeal—it works and everyone uses it—but contends that because XML purports to represent everything, it ends up representing nothing particularly well. The post highlights alternatives like YAML and JSON that offer cleaner, more readable representations of the same data. Atwood doesn't declare XML fundamentally broken, but challenges developers to question whether it's truly the simplest solution for their specific use case rather than accepting it as the mindless default.
key_points: XML has become the default choice for nearly all text-based data storage, from config files to build scripts to comments, regardless of whether it's the best fit
The verbosity of XML buries actual information in 'an astounding amount of noise' with angle brackets and tags
XML was originally designed for document scenarios, not the data scenarios (SOAP, config files, databases) where it's now most commonly used
Alternatives like YAML and JSON offer cleaner, more human-readable representations with less syntactic overhead
The problem isn't XML itself, but its mindless, blanket application as a universal solution
money_quotes: If it's stored in a text file and needs to be retrieved and parsed, it's probably XML.
XML is like democracy. Sometimes it even works.
How much actual information is communicated here? Precious little, and it's buried in an astounding amount of noise.
Because XML purports to represent everything, it ends up representing nothing particularly well.
I spend a disproportionate amount of my time wading through an endless sea of angle brackets and verbose tags desperately searching for the vaguest hint of actual information.
It sure feels like, for any given representation of data in XML, there was a better, simpler choice out there somewhere.
Please think twice before subjecting yourself, your fellow programmers, and your users to the XML angle bracket tax.
posts.key_insight: XML's biggest flaw isn't that it's technically bad, but that its 'represent anything' universality has led developers to use it mindlessly as the default when simpler, more appropriate alternatives exist.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood humorously addresses the issue of keeping computer displays and keyboards clean, dividing programmers into those who touch screens and those who don't. He cites alarming research showing keyboards can harbor five times more bacteria than toilet seats, making the case for regular cleaning. For screens, he recommends simple soap and water with microfiber cloths. For keyboards, he explores options ranging from compressed air to the surprisingly effective method of running keyboards through the dishwasher. The post balances practical cleaning advice with Atwood's characteristic comedic outrage at screen-touchers.
key_points: Touching someone's display is a cardinal sin that divides programmers into two camps
Research shows keyboards can be up to 150 times over the recommended bacteria limit and dirtier than toilet seats
Microfiber cloths with mild soap are the best solution for cleaning displays
Dishwashing keyboards is a surprisingly effective cleaning method, though manufacturers advise against it
Compressed air is a good first line of defense but doesn't actually sanitize keyboards
money_quotes: When I said there were two types of programmers, here's what I really meant: Programmers who touch displays with their greasy, disgusting, bacteria-addled fingers. Programmers who don't.
If you touch my display, I'll kill you.
Displays are for viewing, not touching. Put down your damn sticky bun and go touch your own filthy display.
A keyboard was often 'a reflection of what is in your nose and in your gut.'
I am continually amazed by the horrifying state of many programmer's computer workstation keyboards and monitors.
Compressed air dusters aren't just for sneaking up behind your unsuspecting coworkers and friends and spraying them in the neck and ears.
Just because I can clean my display doesn't mean you should be touching it, Poky McSmudgypants.
posts.key_insight: The tools programmers touch every day harbor shocking amounts of bacteria, and while cleaning them is straightforward, preventing colleagues from smudging your pristine display remains an eternal struggle.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores what markup language Stack Overflow should use for formatting questions and answers, comparing HTML to lightweight alternatives like Markdown, Textile, BBCode, and Wikipedia markup. After researching humane markup languages designed to be easy to write and read, he unexpectedly concludes that HTML is often the better choice. He argues that HTML's ubiquity, security advantages through whitelisting, and the fact that programmers should already know it make it more practical than learning yet another markup syntax. However, he acknowledges that some clever features from Markdown and Textile could reduce mundane HTML busywork.
key_points: Lightweight markup languages like Markdown and Textile are readable but still require learning new syntax that maps to HTML you already know
Wikipedia's markup is effective but crosses into coding territory rather than simple presentational formatting
HTML offers advantages in simplicity (native web language), security (easier to whitelist safe tags), and universality (more people know it than any specific lightweight markup)
For programmer-focused sites, expecting users to know basic HTML is reasonable and avoids the 'magic' parsing behavior of text-based markup
The best approach may be HTML with some Textile/Markdown conveniences to reduce repetitive markup work
money_quotes: This is a site for programmers, so they should be comfortable with basic markup. None of that nancy-boy GUI toolbar handholding nonsense for us, thank you very much.
If you can sling code, a little bit of presentation markup is child's play.
I also found them disturbingly close to 'magic' for some of the formatting rules, to the point that I wished I could just write literal HTML.
If the source and destination are the web, why not use the native markup language of the web?
It's not at all clear that any existing lightweight markup language has critical mass, with the possible exception of Wikipedia's flavor.
If you're a programmer, you damn well better know HTML.
I'd much rather rely on a subset of trusty old HTML than expend brain cells trying to remember the fake-HTML way to make something bold.
posts.key_insight: For programmer-oriented tools, the familiar verbosity of HTML often beats the learning curve of 'simpler' markup languages that just add another layer of abstraction over what you already know.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the phenomenon of forking in open source software, using the Pidgin/Funpidgin split as a case study. The Pidgin developers made a controversial UI change to the text input field and refused to revert it despite user backlash, leading to the creation of Funpidgin. Atwood outlines the four possible outcomes of any fork: the fork dies, merges back, replaces the original, or both survive independently. He argues that while forking is a fundamental right in open source and embodies 'freedom zero,' it is extremely difficult to execute successfully due to the inertia around established projects.
key_points: Forking differs from simple branching by intent - a true fork aims to replace or compete with the original project
The Pidgin developers' refusal to accommodate user preferences on a UI change sparked a community revolt and subsequent fork
There are exactly four possible outcomes for any fork: death, merger, replacement of original, or coexistence
Established open source projects have tremendous inertia that makes successful forking very difficult
Forks face challenges including divided communities, needing to differentiate, and the original project's ability to absorb their improvements
money_quotes: Whether anyone else in the world will care about their crazy new version of the code is not at all clear, but that's not the point.
What is different about a fork is intent. In a fork, the person(s) creating the fork intend for the fork to replace or compete with the original project they are forking.
The community's response was swift: Oh yeah? Fork you!
Unlike the Pidgin developers, we believe the user should have the final say in what goes into the program.
Forking is incredibly difficult to pull off.
Just as in real evolution, I suspect that most forks die in vast, nameless numbers, before they become strong enough to engender any forked progeny of their own.
Forking is the absolute bedrock of open source software – but it is also not a path to be chosen lightly.
posts.key_insight: Forking is the ultimate expression of open source freedom, but the tremendous inertia around established projects means most forks fail before they ever gain traction.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that developers have a fundamental responsibility to handle application crashes gracefully and systematically. He emphasizes that relying on users to report errors is a failure of professional responsibility, and that automated error logging and reporting should be the first thing set up on any new project. The post distinguishes between mundane validation errors and catastrophic crashes that require different handling strategies. Atwood advocates for public error tracking that motivates teams through shared pain, and leveraging the Pareto principle to fix the most impactful bugs first. His core message is that crashes are inevitable, but ignorance of them is inexcusable.
key_points: Set up an error handling framework as the first thing on any new project - errors are inevitable but ignorance shouldn't be
Never rely on users to report crashes; implement automatic logging and diagnostic reporting to your servers
Replace default system crash screens with custom, helpful error pages that acknowledge fault and suggest workarounds
Maintain a public record of errors visible to the whole team to create shared motivation to fix issues
Apply the 80/20 rule: Microsoft found that fixing 20% of top bugs solved 80% of customer issues
money_quotes: If I've learned anything in the last thirty years, it's that I write shitty software – with bugs.
Errors are inevitable, but ignorance shouldn't be.
It is not the user's job to tell you about errors in your software!
If users have to tell you when your app crashes, and why, you have utterly failed your users.
It's bad enough that the user has to use our crashy software; are we really going to add insult to injury by pressing them into service as QA staff, too?
Most users won't bother telling you about problems. They'll just quietly stop using your application.
Crashes are incredibly annoying to your users. It's only fair that the team behind the software share a little of that pain for each crash.
posts.key_insight: Professional software development means accepting that your code will crash and building comprehensive, automatic error handling from day one rather than burdening users with the responsibility of reporting your failures.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques Twitter's error page for failing to provide meaningful information to users during outages. While acknowledging that the page is visually appealing and matches Twitter's brand, he argues it lacks crucial details like whether the outage is temporary or scheduled, system health status, and information about reliability improvements. He contrasts this with his earlier post about 'crashing responsibly' and suggests that error pages should establish honest communication between developers and users. The post uses Twitter's notorious reliability problems as a case study in how not to handle application failures.
key_points: Error pages should serve both developers (diagnostic info) and users (reassurance and helpful information)
Twitter's error page, while attractive, provides no actionable information about outage duration, system status, or maintenance schedules
Repeated exposure to the same uninformative error message erodes user trust - it becomes 'the static error page that cried wolf'
Transparency about reliability problems and improvement efforts is essential, especially for services users consider 'essential'
Crashing responsibly means establishing honest, open dialog between users and developers at the worst moment of that relationship
money_quotes: An 'essential service' is exactly the kind of thing you don't want to see error pages on.
Twitter's error page is conspicuously lacking any real information.
Is Twitter down for everyone, or just me?
This is the static error page that cried wolf.
Where's the improvement over time from the collection and analysis of these errors?
I sure wish they could be a lot more transparent about it.
Isn't that what crashing responsibly is all about – establishing an honest, open dialog between users and developers, even at the worst possible moment of that relationship?
posts.key_insight: Error pages represent a critical moment of truth in the user-developer relationship, and squandering them with uninformative, generic messages damages trust far more than the outage itself.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while PHP is objectively a poorly designed language—a 'random collection of arbitrary stuff' rather than a coherent language—its widespread success powering major websites like Wikipedia, Facebook, YouTube, and WordPress proves that language quality matters less than building compelling applications. He draws parallels between PHP and Visual Basic 6, noting both languages are derided by professionals yet achieve massive adoption. The core message is that sufficiently talented programmers can create great software even in terrible languages, making the choice of programming language secondary to execution and results.
key_points: PHP's design is chaotic and incoherent, resembling 'a virtual explosion at the keyword and function factory' rather than a properly designed language
The comparison between PHP and Visual Basic 6 is common, though Atwood argues it's unfair to VB
Major internet infrastructure (Wikipedia, Facebook, Digg, WordPress, YouTube) runs on PHP despite the language's flaws
Building a compelling application matters far more than the choice of programming language
Talented developers can write great applications in terrible languages, just as incompetent coders can write bad code in good languages
money_quotes: PHP isn't so much a language as a random collection of arbitrary stuff, a virtual explosion at the keyword and function factory.
It's a galactic supernova of incomprehensibly colossal, mind-bendingly awful suck.
I've written both VB and PHP code, and in my opinion the comparison is grossly unfair to Visual Basic.
Building a compelling application is far more important than choice of language.
Sufficiently talented coders can write great applications in terrible languages, too. It's a painful lesson, but an important one.
If PHP sucks so profoundly, why is it powering so much of the internet?
Just don't forget to call the maintain_my_will_to_live() PHP function every so often!
posts.key_insight: The success of an application depends far more on the talent and execution of its builders than on the theoretical elegance of the programming language used to create it.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores OpenID as a potential solution to the 'login explosion' problem where users must remember dozens of username/password combinations across websites. He walks through the OpenID authentication flow, acknowledging both its benefits (amortized account creation costs, one-click subsequent logins, no password storage for developers) and significant drawbacks (phishing risks, privacy concerns, usability issues, adoption inequality). Despite these criticisms, Atwood argues that OpenID represents a 'worse is better' solution worth pursuing for low-stakes credentials, even if it's unsuitable for banking-grade security. He calls on developers to adopt OpenID rather than creating yet another login system.
key_points: OpenID allows users to authenticate across multiple websites using a single set of credentials from a trusted provider, potentially solving the problem of managing 50+ different logins
The quality of OpenID experience depends heavily on provider choice - good providers use SSL, anti-phishing measures, and attribute exchange to share profile data automatically

Major criticisms include phishing vulnerability, privacy concerns (providers see all your logins), centralized risk if compromised, and the chicken-and-egg adoption problem between providers and consumers
Using URLs as identifiers instead of email addresses creates usability friction that makes adoption harder for average users
For developers, OpenID offers a huge advantage: not having to store passwords, which is 'the last business you want to be in'
money_quotes: I have fifty online logins, and I can't remember any of them!
The quality of your OpenID experience is heavily influenced by the provider you choose.
As an application developer, you no longer have to store passwords! That's a huge advantage, because storing passwords is the last business you want to be in.
Normal busy people will choose the familiar route time and time again.
It is a bit odd to ask users to associate themselves with an arbitrary URL instead of an email address.
I am willing to accept these tradeoffs for a partial worse is better solution.
There's absolutely no way I'd put my banking credentials behind an OpenID.
If we can't make OpenID work, at least for run of the mill, low-value credentials that litter the web in increasing numbers – what hope do we have of ever fixing the login explosion problem?
posts.key_insight: The login explosion problem is painful enough that imperfect solutions like OpenID deserve serious consideration and collective improvement, especially for the majority of low-stakes credentials that don't require banking-grade security.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood champions Clay Shirky as the preeminent thinker on internet community and social software, reviewing Shirky's book 'Here Comes Everybody.' The post argues that the internet represents the largest increase in human expressive capability in history, uniquely combining group formation with two-way communication. Atwood contends that understanding social dynamics is essential for all software developers since nearly all modern software is inherently social. He connects Shirky's thesis about collective action to his own work on Stack Overflow, suggesting that the internet enables people to accomplish meaningful things driven by passion rather than profit.
key_points: Clay Shirky is positioned as potentially one of the key historical figures of the internet era for his prescient insights into online community dynamics
The internet is revolutionary because it uniquely combines group formation with two-way communication—previous media could do one or the other, but not both
Almost all software is now social software, making understanding of community dynamics essential for developers
The shift from passive media consumption to active participation and creation represents a fundamental change in human behavior
The internet enables people to 'do big things for love' rather than requiring money for large-scale collective action
money_quotes: I don't think you'll find a smarter, more articulate writer on the topic of internet community than Clay Shirky.
It's better to do something than to do nothing. Even lolcats, even cute pictures of kittens made even cuter with the addition of cute captions, hold out an invitation to participation.

Media in the 20th century was run as a single race – consumption. But media is actually a triathlon, it's three different events. People like to consume, but they also like to produce, and they like to share.
If you're developing social software of any kind, this book should be required reading.
These days, almost all software is social software.
We're living through the largest increase in human expressive capability in history.
In this medium, freedom of speech, freedom of the press, and freedom of assembly are all now the same freedom.
Now, we can do big things for love.
posts.key_insight: The internet's unique ability to combine two-way communication with group formation represents a fundamental shift in human organizing capability, making community-building skills essential for all software developers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the ongoing war between Craigslist and spammers, using it as a cautionary tale for software designers. He documents how commercial tools have systematically defeated Craigslist's anti-spam measures, from CAPTCHAs to IP blocking to phone verification. Drawing on this as he builds Stack Overflow, Atwood argues that developers must assume some users will be malicious and design defenses accordingly. He emphasizes that community moderation combined with technical controls can help good users prevail, but only if designers proactively study and understand spammer techniques rather than remaining naively optimistic about human behavior.
key_points: Commercial spam tools have systematically defeated every Craigslist defense including CAPTCHAs, IP blocking, email verification, and even phone verification
Even partial spammer success undermines community trust and devalues legitimate users' participation
Wikipedia proves that goodness vastly outnumbers evil, but only with proper controls in place
Spammers are economically motivated professionals who get paid to find exploits, while defenders often remain ignorant of attack techniques
Failing to design for evil users is a fundamental failure of your community
money_quotes: When you design your software, work under the assumption that some of your users will be evil.
Wikipedia is a living testament to the fact that goodness vastly outnumbers evil.
The bad guys get paid to learn about their exploits. Are you willing to educate yourself about the complex evil that a tiny minority of powerful users are prepared to unleash upon your site?
Evil will always triumph, because good is dumb.
When you fail to design for evil, you have failed your community.
Allowing anonymous users to post creates a volatile situation where a dozen sufficiently motivated spammers can easily poison the well for thousands of typical users.
The most effective way to fight the evil spammers is to understand them.
posts.key_insight: Software designers must proactively assume malicious users exist and study their techniques, because naive optimism about human nature leads to broken systems that fail legitimate communities.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to criticism that he's positioning himself as an authority on software development by embracing his identity as an amateur rather than an expert. He argues that software is such a young field that almost nobody truly knows what they're doing, and real progress comes from amateurs who are too busy building to pontificate. While acknowledging his writing style can be intense and his takes sometimes oversimplified, he defends his approach as 'strong opinions, weakly held' - writing with conviction to spark discussion while remaining genuinely open to changing his mind. The post serves as both a defense of his blogging philosophy and an invitation for readers to challenge him.
key_points: Software development is so young that claiming expertise is either self-deception or dishonesty - amateurs make the real progress because they're busy living software rather than pontificating about it
Professional blogging doesn't make someone a journalist or authority; it's still amateur participation - a programmer writing about programming, not an observer studying programmers
Writing with a strong voice is more engaging and effective than hedging with disclaimers, but it's meant as an invitation to discussion, not a declaration of final truth
The 'strong opinions, weakly held' principle helps navigate uncertainty: conviction inspires rigorous argument development, while flexibility prevents confirmation bias
Being a better writer than programmer isn't a disqualification - intensity and passion can compensate for raw talent
money_quotes: I've always thought of myself as nothing more than a rank amateur seeking enlightenment.
Everything in software is so new and so frequently being reinvented that almost nobody really knows what they are doing. It is amateurs who make all the progress.
When it comes to software development, if you profess expertise, if you pitch yourself as an authority, you're either lying to us, or lying to yourself.
Experts are, if anything, more suspect than the amateurs, because they're less honest.
Everything I've ever written on this blog is a side-effect of my deep, lifelong love of my ongoing work as a programmer.
What I lack in talent, I make up in intensity.
Whenever I post in a strong voice, it is also an implied invitation to a discussion, a discussion where I often change my opinion and invariably learn a great deal about the topic at hand.
Weak opinions are problematic because people aren't inspired to develop the best arguments possible for them, or to put forth the energy required to test them.
posts.key_insight: In a field as young as software development, claiming expertise is a red flag - the most honest and productive stance is that of an engaged amateur who holds strong opinions loosely enough to learn from being wrong.

posts.summary: Atwood revisits the legendary 'Black Sunday' hack from 2001, where DirecTV executed a masterful counter-attack against smart card pirates who had been stealing satellite TV signals for years. The hackers had reverse-engineered DirecTV's 'H' cards and created an ecosystem of tools to bypass subscription controls. DirecTV's brilliant response involved sending seemingly useless code updates over two months that assembled into a devastating weapon, destroying an estimated 100,000 hacked cards one week before the Super Bowl. The attack was 'signed' by rewriting the first 8 bytes of hacked cards to read 'GAME OVER.' A 2008 Wired article finally revealed Christopher Tarnovsky as the mastermind behind this elaborate counter-hack.
key_points: DirecTV's H cards had design flaws that allowed hackers to reverse-engineer them and receive all channels for free, with no way for DirecTV to track abuse since users could unplug phone lines
The hacking community had defeated every DirecTV countermeasure for nearly two years, with hacked H cards selling for over $400 on eBay
DirecTV's winning strategy was sending attack code in ~60 parts over two months, disguised as useless updates, that assembled into a program capable of destroying pirate cards
The Black Sunday kill destroyed approximately 100,000 hacked cards in one evening, eliminating 98% of the hacking community's capability
Christopher Tarnovsky was later revealed as the architect of this sophisticated counter-attack
money_quotes: The first 8 computer bytes of all hacked cards were rewritten to read 'GAME OVER.'
Like a final piece of a puzzle allowing the entire picture, the final updates made all the useless bits of computer code join into a dynamic program, existing on the card itself.
The Black Sunday attack code was sent to pirate cards in about five dozen parts over the course of two months, like a tank transported piece by piece to a battlefield to be assembled in the field.
In a masterful, planned, and orchestrated manner, DirecTV had updated the old and ailing technology.
The IRC DirecTV channels overflowed with thousands of people who had lost the ability to watch their stolen TV.
For the first time, they could see a cunning mind at work on the other side.
It's a gripping tale of high stakes programming, a life of electronic warfare with millions of dollars at risk on both sides.
posts.key_insight: The most elegant hacks aren't brute force attacks but patient, strategic operations that hide in plain sight until the moment of execution.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood criticizes Windows Vista's abysmal fit and finish, arguing that its user interface is riddled with inconsistencies that undermine the entire operating system's credibility. He documents how Vista contains jarring UI discontinuities—mismatched fonts, inconsistent toolbars, and design elements from previous Windows versions—that are never more than two clicks away. Atwood argues that if Microsoft's own teams can't follow their own design guidelines, it signals a fundamental lack of craftsmanship that erodes user and developer confidence. He contrasts this with Apple's greater attention to consistency and urges developers to study and follow their platform's design guidelines, noting that consistency should be the minimum standard when beauty isn't achievable.
key_points: Vista's UI inconsistencies represent the worst fit and finish of any Microsoft OS, with different teams using wildly different design approaches
Small UI details matter enormously—mismatched fonts and inconsistent designs collectively signal 'no confidence' in the software's overall quality
When OS developers don't follow their own design guidelines, it undermines both user trust and third-party developer motivation to care about UX
Every major OS provides design guidelines that serve as a baseline for consistency, but these guides are only credible when the OS itself follows them
If you can't achieve beautiful design, consistency should be your minimum standard
money_quotes: Nothing is more disappointing than the overall fit and finish of Vista, which is truly abysmal.
You're never more than two clicks away from some discontinuity or visual gaffe that zaps you right back into the seven year old Windows XP 'experience.'
Aren't these trivial, nitpicky complaints? Yes. They are. And that's entirely the point. This little stuff matters.
In software, attention to detail is everything; all these glaring little oversights in Vista's user experience collectively add up to a huge vote of 'no confidence' in the whole shebang.
A mismatched font here, an ugly pixelated icon there, soon enough you feel that you're living in a neighborhood with an awful lot of broken windows.
These guides are only as good as the underlying operating systems they're based on, which are de facto reference implementations.
If you can't be beautiful, at least be consistent.
posts.key_insight: UI inconsistencies aren't trivial nitpicks—they're symptoms of deeper organizational dysfunction and erode confidence in the entire product's quality.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the state of large USB flash drives in 2008, noting the remarkable progress from 512MB to 16GB capacity in just three years. He compares USB flash drive performance to traditional hard drives, finding that even the best flash drives deliver only a fraction of hard drive throughput. Through benchmarking several 32GB drives, he determines the OCZ Rally 2 offers the best combination of performance and value. The post serves as both a practical buying guide and a reflection on how storage limitations affect the dream of carrying a complete portable computing environment.
key_points: USB flash drive performance (10-30 MB/sec write) is dramatically slower than typical hard drives (55 MB/sec), making portable OS booting impractical
The 32GB capacity offers the best price-per-gigabyte value, contrary to the common assumption that mid-range sizes are better deals
Random read/write speeds matter more than sequential throughput for running applications from USB drives
Manufacturing variance means identical drive models can perform differently depending on which flash chips were used
Storage capacity on keychains had grown from 512MB to 16GB in just three years, matching hard drive capacities from 1999
money_quotes: Barely 10 years later, we carry around that much on our keychains.
I'm starting to wonder why we don't just take our entire computing environment, operating system and all, along with us and boot it up on whatever computer we happen to encounter in the wild.
USB flash drive performance, even for the best models, is a small fraction of typical hard drive performance.
If you really were booting and running your operating system entirely from a USB flash drive, you'd feel like you had stepped back in time five full years.
Do you have that kind of time?
You should care deeply about read and write speeds.
You can never have enough storage on your keychain.
posts.key_insight: While USB flash drive capacities have grown impressively, their performance limitations mean they're still best suited for file storage rather than running complete computing environments.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood criticizes the common practice of websites asking users for their email credentials to import contacts, using Yelp as an example. He argues that email accounts serve as de-facto master passwords for online identity since password reset links route through email. Giving credentials to any third party creates multiple security vulnerabilities and contradicts the privacy policies these same companies publish. Atwood calls on developers to refuse implementing such features and users to never comply with these requests. The post concludes by highlighting emerging solutions like OAuth and contact APIs that provide limited, delegated access without exposing full credentials.
key_points: Email accounts function as master passwords for online identity because password recovery for virtually all services routes through email
Asking for email credentials contradicts the privacy policies that companies themselves publish, making their security commitments hypocritical
Even trustworthy companies create risk by potentially storing credentials insecurely, multiplying vulnerability points across the web
Developers have an ethical obligation to refuse building credential-harvesting features, regardless of business pressure
OAuth and contact APIs represent the right technological solution: granting limited access without full credential exposure
money_quotes: Your email account is a de-facto master password for your online identity.
They have effectively asked for the keys to my house in order to riffle through my address book.

Naïve internet users may not understand why it is such a profoundly bad idea to give out their email credentials to random websites. Worse, they might eventually get the idea that giving out their email credentials is typical or normal.
How did we end up in a world where it's even remotely acceptable to ask for someone's email credentials?
As a software developer, you should never ask a user for their email credentials. It's unethical. It's irresponsible. It is wrong.
If someone is asking you to code this, why? For what purpose?
Why can't I grant them temporary access to my public email address book without giving out the keys to my email kingdom?
posts.key_insight: Asking users for their email credentials is fundamentally unethical because email functions as a master key to online identity, and the technology industry should refuse to normalize this dangerous practice while building proper delegated-access alternatives.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the routine (function/subroutine) is the single greatest invention in computer science after the computer itself. He contrasts unstructured BASIC code with modern structured programming to illustrate the dramatic improvement routines brought to code readability and maintainability. While routines are now so fundamental as to be invisible, Atwood contends that mastering them remains the central challenge of programming. The post emphasizes that despite all our fancy new language features, the humble routine is what makes modern programming possible and deserves constant, deliberate practice.
key_points: The routine is arguably the single greatest invention in computer science, making programs smaller, faster, and more readable
Modern programming languages are surprisingly young - Ruby, JavaScript, and PHP are barely teenagers in 2008 terms
Routines are so fundamental they've become invisible, which is precisely the problem - they take a minute to learn but a lifetime to master
The core questions of programming now center on routine design: length, parameters, naming, relationships, error handling, and whether it should exist at all
Good programmers treat each routine like a highly polished diamond, regardless of what language they're working in
money_quotes: Aside from the invention of the computer, the routine is arguably the single greatest invention in computer science.
In large part, routines are what make modern programming possible.
That's the problem with routines: they only take a minute to learn, but a lifetime to master.
You can write FORTRAN in any language.
Wrestling with the ineffable essence of a routine is, almost to a first approximation, what programming now is.
The routines in your code should be treated like tiny, highly polished diamonds, each one more exquisitely polished and finely cut than the last.
Should this routine even exist at all?
posts.key_insight: While we obsess over new language features, the fundamental skill of crafting excellent routines remains the core discipline of programming and deserves lifelong practice.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood makes a passionate case for why blog comments are essential, arguing that a blog without comments fundamentally misses the point of blogging. He compares commentless blogs to Amazon without user reviews—technically functional but missing their competitive advantage. While acknowledging that comments require moderation work and attract spam and offensive content, he maintains the collective wisdom they provide is worth the effort. The post culminates with a hilariously nonsensical comment that, paradoxically, proves his point about the unexpected value comments can bring.
key_points: Blog comments often surpass the original post in value because communities collectively know more than any individual blogger
Comments provide immediacy that external discussion platforms like Reddit or Digg cannot match
Maintaining a healthy comment section requires consistent moderation work—a 'productivity tax' worth paying
The best part of a blog post often begins where the post itself ends
Unexpected, bizarre comments can restore faith in the entire commenting ecosystem
money_quotes: It's an open secret amongst bloggers that the blog comments are often better than the original blog post, and it's because the community collectively knows far more than you or I will ever know.
A blog without comments is like Amazon without user reviews. Is it really even worth using at that point?
The best part of a blog post often begins where the blog post ends. If you are offended by that, I humbly submit you don't understand why blogs work.
I like to refer to this as weeding my web garden. It's a productivity tax you pay if you want to grow a bumper crop of comments.
The myriad benefits of a robust comment ecosystem outweighs the minor maintenance effort.
I even welcome downright unconstructive criticism, if it's amusing or useful enough.
posts.key_insight: The value of a blog lies not in the author's monologue but in the many-way conversation it enables, making comments not optional but essential to the medium.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines Tim Bray's Wide Finder Project, a benchmarking exercise that challenges programmers to optimize a simple log-parsing Ruby script for multicore processors. The experiment reveals massive performance disparities within the same language depending on implementation, with the fastest versions often requiring significantly more complex code. Atwood argues that the real lesson isn't about choosing the right language, but about the need for programming languages to make parallelization nearly transparent to developers. He endorses Bray's vision that a one-character change should be sufficient to enable multicore optimizations, highlighting the tension between performance gains and code complexity.
key_points: The Wide Finder benchmarks show huge performance variations within languages (Ruby ranged from 1:43 to 50 seconds), proving language choice alone isn't a silver bullet for multicore performance
Tim Bray proposes that ideal multicore optimization should require minimal code changes—ideally just adding an asterisk to make loops parallel
Solutions that require more than twice the source code to leverage multicore are 'highly suspect' according to Bray's criteria
The naïve Ruby implementation of Wide Finder 2 takes 25 hours, while optimized versions beat it by nearly two orders of magnitude
Beauty in code can be deceptive—what matters more is the ongoing 'conversation between programmer and code' and domestic virtues like straightforwardness
money_quotes: Beauty is an idealistic fantasy: what really matters is the quality of the never ending conversation between programmer and code, as each learns from and adapts to the other.
The best code is no code at all.
When it comes to multicore performance, choice of language is no silver bullet.
In a perfect world, a one-character change to the original Ruby program would be all it takes to enable all the necessary multicore optimizations.
