The problem is that lots of simple basic data-processing operations, in my case a simple Ruby script, run like crap on modern many-core processors.
Anything that requires more than twice as much source code to take advantage of many-core is highly suspect.
Can you do better on the time without exploding either the code size, the code complexity, or the average programmer's head?
posts.key_insight: The challenge of multicore programming isn't finding the right language—it's making parallelization transparent enough that simple code can automatically leverage multiple cores without exploding in complexity.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains Markov chains through the lens of Garkov, a project that generates new Garfield comic dialogue using Markov text synthesis. He walks through how Markov chains work by showing progressively higher-order text generation examples from Programming Pearls, demonstrating how each letter or word is chosen based on statistical probability from a training corpus. The post then pivots to show the surprising practical applications of this simple technique: Google's PageRank algorithm is fundamentally a Markov chain modeling random web surfers, and hidden Markov models power advanced spam filtering. Atwood marvels at how such a simple statistical method—with no memory of previous states—can produce remarkably coherent output given sufficient training data.
key_points: Markov chains generate text by randomly selecting each word based on statistical probability of what historically follows the current word(s) in a training corpus
The technique is remarkably simple—no memory of previous states, just a lookup table and dice roll—yet produces surprisingly coherent output
Google's PageRank algorithm is fundamentally a Markov chain where states are pages and transitions are links between them
Hidden Markov models extend this concept to power spam filtering systems like CRM114 that outperform basic Bayesian filters
The quality of Markov output depends entirely on the input corpus, making the vast web and email archives ideal training data
money_quotes: What's amazing to me about Markov chains is how unbelievably simple they are.

A Markov chain has no memory of previous states: the next state is chosen based on a random dice roll and a lookup into a table of the states that tend to historically follow the current state in the input corpus.
Given an adequate input corpus, they work almost uncannily well, a testament to the broad power of rudimentary statistical inference.
PageRank can be understood as a Markov chain in which the states are pages, and the transitions are all equally probable and are the links between pages.
But Markov chains aren't just useful for automatically generating Paul Graham essay parodies. They're also quite practical.
If you play with the Markov text synthesizer, you'll quickly find that Markov methods are only as good as their input corpus.
But it's sure tough to imagine a more ideal input corpus for Markovian techniques than the unimaginable vastness of web and email, isn't it?
posts.key_insight: Markov chains demonstrate how remarkably powerful simple statistical methods can be—the same basic technique that generates absurdist Garfield parodies also underlies Google's PageRank and advanced spam filtering.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the surprisingly varied ways programmers pronounce ASCII characters when reading code aloud. Prompted by a comment using the term 'octothorpe' for the hash/pound symbol, he presents an extensive table from The New Hacker's Dictionary cataloging common and rare names for special characters. The post reveals that despite programmers' reputation for precision, there's remarkable inconsistency in how we verbalize symbols like !, #, *, and ~. Atwood highlights both practical pronunciations (bang, hash, splat) and whimsical alternatives (wax/wane for parentheses, Nathan Hale for asterisk), celebrating the quirky subculture of programmer jargon.
key_points: Programmers regularly need to read code aloud but rarely think about how inconsistent their character pronunciations are
The same symbol can have dozens of names - the # alone includes hash, pound, octothorpe, crunch, mesh, pig-pen, and tictactoe
Regional differences matter: Americans say 'pound' for # while most other cultures say 'hash'
The jargon includes both practical shorthand (bang, splat, pipe) and playful nonsense (wax/wane, embrace/bracelet, suck/blow)
Even the rigid, literal-minded programmer community embraces surprising linguistic creativity in this domain
money_quotes: How do you pronounce these unusual ASCII characters?
I'm often surprised to hear what other programmers name their ASCII characters.
There's far more variability than you'd expect considering the rigid, highly literal mindset of most programmers.
Underscores, pipes, octothorpes, curly braces – sheesh... I'll take a mild dose of verbosity if means I don't have to code something that looks like it's been zipped already!
So the next time a programmer walks up to you and says, 'oh, it's easy! Just type wax bang at hash buck grapes circumflex and splat wane,' you'll know what they mean. Maybe.
posts.key_insight: The varied and often whimsical ways programmers pronounce ASCII characters reveals an unexpected linguistic creativity within a community known for literal precision.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues against the common programmer tendency to work in isolation and only reveal code when it's 'finished.' Drawing from both open source development discussions and Jim McCarthy's 1995 book on software development, he explains how 'going dark' leads to problems: rejected code bombs, missed deadlines, and opaque, hard-to-maintain features. While agile practices in commercial settings naturally prevent this through short iterations, open source projects are especially vulnerable since there's no project manager enforcing visibility. The post concludes that sharing in-progress work, despite the discomfort, leads to better code and stronger project connections.
key_points: Dropping large 'code bombs' after months of isolated work forces teams to either reject the work outright or accept an opaque, hard-to-maintain blob
Programmer reluctance to share work-in-progress stems from fear of embarrassment rather than hubris—they want to appear infallible
Agile development practices effectively prevent going dark by requiring visible deliverables in short iterations
Open source projects face higher risk because there's no project manager enforcing incremental visibility
Code is the only currency that matters on open source projects—regular sharing builds credibility
money_quotes: Programmers don't want their peers to see mistakes or failures. They want to work privately, in a cave, then spring 'perfect' code on their community, as if no mistakes had ever been made.
Rather than think of programming as an inherently social activity, most coders seem to treat it as an arena for personal heroics, and will do anything to protect that myth.
In our world, three weeks is going dark.
The brilliant developer must be capable of performing on a team, making his work visible in modest increments and subjecting it to scrutiny as it matures.
It's effectively impossible to go dark if you're practicing any form of agile software development.
How do you expect your fellow coders to take you seriously if you aren't regularly showing them code? It's the only form of currency that matters on an open source project.
Hiding your code until it's 'done' may feel safer, but it isn't.
posts.key_insight: Programming is inherently social, and the fear of showing imperfect work leads to worse outcomes than the discomfort of sharing early and often.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the evolution of physics-based gameplay, from classic vector arcade games to modern simulations powered by increasingly powerful hardware. He highlights the introduction of NVIDIA's GTX 280 with its 1.4 billion transistors, noting that GPUs now outperform CPUs for parallelizable tasks like video encoding by a factor of ten. The post surveys available physics engines for developers and recommends several physics-based games including Bridge Construction Set and Armadillo Run. Atwood argues that complex physics engines represent an excellent way to utilize the dormant computational power in modern PCs.
key_points: Modern GPUs contain more raw computational power than CPUs for highly parallelizable tasks, with the GTX 280 featuring 1.4 billion transistors
GPU-based video encoding can be ten times faster than even the fastest quad-core CPUs
Technologies like CUDA, Microsoft's Accelerator, and Apple's OpenCL will make GPU computing more accessible to developers
Multiple physics engines are available for programmers including Havok, Newton, ODE, and NVIDIA PhysX
Physics-based games like Bridge Construction Set demonstrate how simulation can make subjects like civil engineering entertaining
money_quotes: Accurately simulating the physics of the real world has been the domain of supercomputers for decades.
Now that we almost have multicore supercomputers on every desktop, it's only natural that aspect of computing would trickle down to us.
Some of the highest performing hardware in your PC lies on your video card.
Compared to the newest, speediest quad core CPU, you can encode video ten times faster using a modern video card GPU.
Who knew civil engineering could be so much fun?
After playing this game, you will learn to love the power and beauty of the simple triangle.
Increasingly complex physics engines are an outstanding way to harness the massive computational horsepower that lies dormant in most modern PCs.
posts.key_insight: The massive parallel processing power of modern GPUs, previously reserved for graphics, is now accessible to developers through physics engines and general-purpose computing frameworks, opening new possibilities for simulation-based games and applications.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares a memorable maxim from the C2 Wiki about writing maintainable code: imagine the person who will maintain your code is a violent psychopath who knows where you live. While acknowledging this is dramatic, he argues that such shock tactics may be necessary to drive home the importance of code maintainability. He connects this to his broader belief that maintenance programming, though unglamorous, constitutes 99% of real-world coding work.
key_points: The 'violent psychopath' metaphor is a memorable way to emphasize writing maintainable code
Shock tactics can be effective for communicating important software development principles
Maintenance programming is not glamorous but represents the vast majority of coding work
Developers need to shift their mindset from writing clever code to writing readable code
money_quotes: Always code as if the person who ends up maintaining your code is a violent psychopath who knows where you live.
It may not be glamorous, but it's 99% of the coding work in this world.
posts.key_insight: Writing maintainable code isn't about elegance or ego—it's about empathy for the future developer who will inherit your work, even if that developer is you.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood celebrates C# 3.0's implicit type declarations (var keyword) as a welcome reduction in code redundancy. He argues that traditional C# and Java variable declarations force developers to repeat type information unnecessarily, hurting readability without providing real benefits. While acknowledging that explicit typing has its place, he advocates for aggressive use of implicit typing wherever it makes code more concise. He frames this feature as a 'gateway drug' to more dynamically typed languages, suggesting the industry is moving toward a hybrid approach of static and dynamic typing.
key_points: Implicit typing in C# is a compiler trick that maintains static typing while reducing syntactic redundancy
Traditional Java/C# declarations like 'BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader()' are unnecessarily verbose when the type is obvious from context
The var keyword should be used aggressively wherever it makes code more concise
Reducing redundancy in code should be pursued 'up to and including switching languages'
Implicit typing represents a step toward hybrid static/dynamic typing approaches
money_quotes: While writing C# code, I sometimes felt like I had entered the Department of Redundancy Department.
Is there really any doubt what type of the variable br is? Does it help anyone, ever, to require another BufferedReader on the front of that line?
This has bothered me for years, but it was an itch I just couldn't scratch. Until now.
Anything that removes redundancy from our code should be aggressively pursued – up to and including switching languages.
You might even say implicit variable typing is a gateway drug to more dynamically typed languages. And that's a good thing.
There's always a tradeoff between verbosity and conciseness, but I have an awfully hard time defending the unnecessarily verbose way objects were typically declared in C# and Java.
posts.key_insight: Type declarations that repeat information already obvious from context are pure redundancy, and languages should let developers eliminate that noise wherever possible.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that simply doing your programming job every day doesn't constitute real practice or professional growth. Drawing on research about expertise, he emphasizes that 'effortful study'—constantly tackling challenges just beyond your current competence—is what separates amateurs from professionals. He curates practice recommendations from Steve Yegge and Peter Norvig, noting that effective practice goes beyond code puzzles to include reading others' code, learning from interviews, and understanding hardware. Atwood concludes with his own minimalist kata: write a blog and contribute to open source projects, arguing that the ability to write both brilliant code and brilliant prose is the ultimate professional skill.
key_points: Doing your job daily doesn't qualify as real practice—you must set aside time for focused, deliberate study
What matters for expertise is not experience but 'effortful study': tackling challenges at the edge of your ability where failure is possible
Programming practice should be holistic, encompassing people skills, code review, and communication—not just solving puzzles
Reading and writing about code publicly (blogging) can be more professionally significant than years of private practice
Contributing to open source projects provides concrete, public evidence of your abilities and turns you from a talker into a doer
money_quotes: Contrary to what you might believe, merely doing your job every day doesn't qualify as real practice.
What matters is not experience per se but 'effortful study,' which entails continually tackling challenges that lie just beyond one's competence.
Unless you're failing some of the time, you're probably not growing professionally.
I may drive to work every day, but I'm far from a professional driver.
Programming is more about people than code, so there's a limit to how much you can grow from solving every obscure programming coding interview problem on the planet.
The people who can write and communicate effectively are, all too often, the only people who get heard. They get to set the terms of the debate.
Try to leave a trail of public, concrete, useful things in your wake that you can point to and say: I helped build that.
When you can write brilliant code and brilliant prose explaining that code to the world – well, I figure that's the ultimate code kata.
posts.key_insight: True professional growth in programming comes not from daily work experience but from deliberate practice at the edge of your abilities, combined with the capacity to communicate your ideas effectively to the world.

posts.summary: Atwood revisits his controversial critique of XML, clarifying that his goal was never to condemn XML outright but to encourage programmers to think critically about their tool choices. He argues that the mental overhead of parsing verbose XML syntax, while seemingly trivial, accumulates into significant cognitive costs when multiplied across all developers and projects. Drawing on research from 'Stumbling on Happiness,' he suggests we underestimate the impact of small daily annoyances while overestimating major events. His core message is that developers should evaluate tradeoffs rather than defaulting to XML because it's ubiquitous.
key_points: The controversy around his original XML post stemmed from readers treating it as an attack on XML itself rather than a call for critical thinking
Understanding alternatives to any technology helps you understand that technology better
The true art of programming is understanding what you could do and choosing what fits your situation best
Small cognitive costs like parsing XML verbosity compound significantly across the entire developer population
We underestimate the cumulative impact of minor daily annoyances while overestimating major life events
money_quotes: I try not to get emotionally involved with the tools and technologies that I use, if I can avoid it.
Actually understanding the alternatives helps you understand XML better, a little bit, too.
I think that is the absolute art of programming. It's understanding what you could do, and which one of those things fits your situation best.
I've learned to use a hammer, and I'm gonna hammer everything.
I blog to help others and also to learn. As it turns out both are aided by getting folks to actually read the stuff. Please pardon the necessary devices.
What's the mental cost of that insignificant effort times the number of developers in the world, times the number of projects in the world?
We also tend to radically underestimate the impact of the dozens of small events in our lives throughout the day.
This small stuff matters.
posts.key_insight: The cumulative cognitive cost of small annoyances like XML verbosity is vastly underestimated, and programmers should critically evaluate their default technology choices rather than accepting them blindly.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses the American Mensa website as an example of a critical web security mistake, challenging readers to identify the problem themselves. The post is deliberately brief and provocative, relying on a screenshot to make its point. Atwood's characteristic wit shines through as he contrasts Mensa's reputation for intelligence with what appears to be a basic security blunder on their website. The post serves as both a puzzle for readers and a warning about visiting sites that make this particular mistake.
key_points: The American Mensa website contains an obvious and dangerous web security mistake
Websites making this error should be avoided by users
Intelligence (as measured by Mensa membership) doesn't guarantee good technical decisions
The mistake is serious enough that users should 'desperately want to avoid' such sites
money_quotes: I may not be smart enough to join Mensa, but I *am* smart enough not to build websites like the American Mensa website.
Do you see the mistake? If so, can you explain *why* this is a mistake, and why you'd desperately want to avoid visiting websites that make this mistake?
posts.key_insight: Being smart in one domain (high IQ) doesn't translate to competence in another (web security), and even organizations that pride themselves on intelligence can make embarrassing technical mistakes.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that regular expressions are unfairly maligned due to a misunderstood Jamie Zawinski quote, which actually warns against overuse rather than use entirely. He contends that regular expressions are powerful tools that belong in every programmer's toolkit when used with restraint. The post demonstrates that regex readability is a skill issue, not an inherent flaw, showing how whitespace, comments, and proper tooling can make complex patterns comprehensible. Atwood provides practical tips including breaking complex patterns into smaller ones, using whitespace liberally, leveraging dedicated regex tools, and understanding that regex cannot replace proper parsers. He concludes that with responsible use and appropriate tooling, regular expressions offer significant wins in writing concise, powerful code.
key_points: The famous Zawinski quote is misunderstood - it warns against overuse of regex, not regex itself
Unreadable regular expressions are a skill and discipline problem, not an inherent limitation of the technology
Modern regex features like whitespace mode and comments can make patterns highly readable
Dedicated regex tools with syntax highlighting and real-time testing are essential for working effectively with patterns
Regular expressions are not parsers and should not be used for tasks requiring balanced tag matching
money_quotes: Regular expressions are like a particularly spicy hot sauce – to be used in moderation and with restraint only when appropriate.
If you read an incredibly complex, impossible to decipher regular expression in your codebase, they did it wrong.
Writing so that people can understand you is hard. I don't care if it's code, English, regular expressions, or Klingon.
You wouldn't read or write code in notepad, right? Then why in the world would you attempt to read or write regular expressions that way?
Do not try to do everything in one uber-regex. I know you can do it that way, but you're not going to. It's not worth it.
No matter how clever your regex, don't delude yourself: it is in no way, shape or form a substitute for a real live parser.
If you make regular expressions a part of your toolkit, you'll be able to write less code that does more.
posts.key_insight: Regular expressions aren't inherently unreadable or evil; they become problematic only through overuse, poor formatting, and lack of proper tooling - the same factors that make any code difficult to maintain.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines Bruce Schneier's controversial practice of running an open wireless network, using it as a launching point to discuss the broader tension between algorithmic security and real-world security needs. While Atwood understands Schneier's philosophical point about penetrating the 'veil of false algorithmic security,' he disagrees for practical reasons: bandwidth protection. The post then provides concrete guidance on wireless security, explaining why WEP is worthless, WPA requires extremely strong passwords, and WPA2 should be the standard. Atwood concludes that weak security measures create a dangerous illusion that may be worse than no security at all.
key_points: Bruce Schneier evolved from pure cryptography to recognizing security as fundamentally a people problem, not just an algorithm problem
Schneier's open wireless network is a philosophical stance meant to force deeper thinking about actual security rather than checkbox security
WEP encryption is so broken it provides no meaningful protection and should be treated as equivalent to an open network
WPA with weak passwords is vulnerable to offline brute force attacks, requiring 33+ character passphrases for real security
The illusion of security is more dangerous than acknowledged insecurity because it breeds complacency
money_quotes: This is the programming equivalent of realizing that Peopleware is ultimately a much more important book than The Art of Computer Programming.
By advocating the radical idea that your wireless network should be intentionally kept open, Bruce is attempting to penetrate the veil of false algorithmic security.
If you choose WEP, you have effectively chosen to run an open wireless network. There's no difference.
While brute force attacks are still for dummies, most people are, statistically speaking, dummies.
I desperately want to protect the thin sliver of upstream bandwidth my provider allows me.
If you've enabled WEP, or WPA with anything less than a truly random passphrase of 33 characters, you don't have security. You have the illusion of security.
And that is far more dangerous than no security at all.
posts.key_insight: False security that breeds complacency is more dangerous than openly acknowledged vulnerability, because it prevents people from thinking critically about their actual risk exposure.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reviews Charles Petzold's 'The Annotated Turing,' a book that guides readers through Alan Turing's seminal 1936 paper on computability. He emphasizes that Turing's thirty-six page paper established the mathematical foundation for all modern computers. Beyond computing, Atwood highlights Turing's contributions to breaking the Enigma code and founding artificial intelligence concepts. The post takes a somber turn addressing Turing's persecution for homosexuality and his tragic suicide at age 42. Atwood celebrates both Petzold's nine-year effort and Turing's enduring legacy through the Turing Award and memorial.
key_points: Every modern computer is based on the mathematical model from Turing's 1936 paper, just thirty-six pages long
Turing made three foundational contributions: computability theory, breaking Enigma in WWII, and proposing the Turing Test for AI
Turing was criminally prosecuted for homosexuality in 1952 and subjected to degrading treatment before his suicide at 42
The Turing Memorial statue depicts him holding an apple, symbolizing both his death by cyanide-laced apple and referencing Newton and forbidden love
Petzold's annotated guide took nine years to write and makes Turing's dense mathematical work accessible
money_quotes: The computer you're using to read this post is based on the mathematical model laid out in that thirty-six page 1936 paper. As are all other computers in the world.
Not only is Alan Turing the father of all modern computer science, he also was the single individual most responsible for breaking the Enigma code during World War II.
It pains me greatly to read about the degrading and inhumane treatment one of our greatest scientific minds was subjected to.
Reading the list of Turing Award recipients is humbling indeed, a reminder of not only how far we've come, but how far we have to go.
Mathematics, rightly viewed, possesses not only truth, but supreme beauty – a beauty cold and austere, like that of sculpture.
posts.key_insight: Alan Turing's thirty-six page 1936 paper created the mathematical foundation for all modern computing, yet the man who gave us so much was tragically destroyed by society's persecution of his identity.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines Microsoft's tendency to reinvent open source tools rather than integrate them, using their CodePlex source control client and MSTest as primary examples. While acknowledging the market argument that software diversity is normal (like soda flavors), he argues Microsoft's corporate directive against shipping open source code results in inferior clones that lack community support. He contends that this blindspot prevents obvious integration opportunities and forces developers back to superior open source alternatives. Atwood warns that Microsoft's inability to embrace open source could ultimately harm the company as open source software continues its exponential growth.
key_points: Microsoft's corporate culture compels them to rewrite open source tools to own the source code, often resulting in inferior clones with new bugs and no new features
MSTest exemplifies this problem - a NUnit clone that drove the Stack Overflow team back to the original open source version
The market argument for software diversity (like soda brands) partially justifies redundant tools, but misses the deeper issue of quality and community support
Microsoft's refusal to integrate open source prevents obvious improvements like first-class NUnit support in Visual Studio
The exponential growth of open source from 1999-2008 represents an existential threat that Microsoft's current strategy cannot address
money_quotes: Everybody I know – including our own Stack Overflow team – who has tried to use the MSTest flavor of unit tests has eventually thrown up their arms and gone back to NUnit.
There's simply no reason for MSTest to exist except to satisfy some bizarre corporate directive that Microsoft never ship open source code in their products.
It's a clone of NUnit with all new bugs and no new features, but it can be included in the box with Visual Studio and integrated into the product.
The more I think about this, the more I think Microsoft's utter inability to integrate open source software of any kind whatsoever into their products might just end up killing them.
The best way to 'beat' open source is to join 'em – to integrate with and ship open source components as a part of your product.
Source control is the bedrock of modern software engineering, and I've found precious few developers that truly understand how it works.
If I worked in the upper echelons of Microsoft, I'd be looking at the graph of open source software growth from the years of 1999 to 2008 and crapping my pants right about now.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's corporate refusal to ship open source code forces them to create inferior clones that waste resources and drive developers to the original tools anyway, a strategy that becomes increasingly untenable as open source continues to mature and grow.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that programmers should invest at least $500 in a quality ergonomic chair, treating it as a strategic career decision rather than a frivolous expense. Drawing from his decade-long experience with a Herman Miller Aeron, he emphasizes that chairs are one of the few pieces of equipment that last 10+ years while being used eight hours daily. The post surveys the top ergonomic chair options including models from Steelcase, HumanScale, and Herman Miller, providing resources for comparison shopping. Atwood ultimately upgraded to a Herman Miller Mirra after finding it more comfortable than his aging Aeron, calculating his chair cost just $7 per month over its lifetime.
key_points: Quality chairs require a minimum $500 investment - anything less is likely a mistake unless you find exceptional deals
Chairs are uniquely long-lasting equipment, with Atwood's Aeron lasting 10+ years compared to constantly changing tech
Cheap chairs create constant distraction while quality chairs 'melt into the background' enabling focus on work
The Aeron became associated with dot-com excess, symbolizing the anti-corporate startup zeitgeist of the 90s
Finding the right chair requires hands-on testing at specialty 'back stores' rather than big-box retailers
money_quotes: You can't expect to get a decent chair for less than $500.
A quality chair is so comfortable and accommodating it effortlessly melts into the background, so you can focus on your work.
A cheesy, cheap chair constantly reminds you how many hours of work you have left.
I can't think of any other piece of equipment I use in my job that has lasted me ten full years and beyond.
Choice of seating is as fundamental and constant as it gets in a programming career otherwise marked by relentless change.
Why not take the same care and consideration in selecting a chair as you would with the other strategic directions that you'll carry with you for the rest of your career?
By my calculations, the Aeron cost me about $7 per month over its ten year lifetime; I figure my continued health and comfort while programming are worth at least that much.
posts.key_insight: A programming chair is a decade-long investment in your health and productivity that costs mere dollars per month when amortized, making quality seating one of the smartest career decisions a developer can make.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues against the overuse of code folding directives like C#'s #region, which he considers harmful to code quality and maintainability. While acknowledging the importance of adapting to team coding standards, he makes an exception for this practice, which he views as enabling developers to hide poorly written code. He presents four main criticisms: regions are meaningless to compilers, they encourage hiding bad code, they mask excessive method length, and they compensate for editor deficiencies. His core message is that well-written code should be inherently readable without needing organizational crutches.
key_points: Code folding directives like #region are glorified comments that add lines to accommodate the editor rather than improve the actual code
Folding enables developers to sweep ugly or boilerplate code under the rug, where it rots unseen rather than getting properly refactored
The presence of folded code creates a false sense of organization while masking spaghetti code and excessive method length
Standard boilerplate regions represent editor deficiencies that should be handled automatically, not manually by developers
When joining a team, developers should generally adapt to existing practices, but some preferences warrant early discussion
money_quotes: I can't see anything! I have to manually expand those sections to browse any of the code in this class.
Code that hides from you is code that will rot in the most putrescent and painful way possible.
I daresay being able to see the damn code is more important than having it meticulously segmented into six pointless little named buckets.
Your code should be front and center at all times – exposed to as many programmers' eyes, and as much healing light, as possible.
If the code needs the crutch of folding to look organized, it's bad code.
Why, exactly, are we writing code to accommodate the editor?
I've always been wary of cowboy coders who rolled into an ongoing project on fresh horses and immediately started dictating terms.
posts.key_insight: Well-written code should be inherently readable and organized without requiring editor features to hide its complexity; if you need code folding to make your code presentable, that's a sign you need to refactor, not fold.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on how his coding style has evolved toward minimalism over his career, introducing the concept of 'Spartan programming' as a codified approach to writing austere code. The methodology focuses on simultaneous minimization across multiple dimensions: horizontal and vertical complexity, token counts, parameters, variables, and control structures. He outlines specific principles for frugal use of variables (minimizing their number, visibility, accessibility, variability, lifetime, and names) and control structures (favoring early returns). While not endorsing every rule, Atwood argues that minimalism is rarely the wrong choice for software development.
key_points: Spartan programming aims for simultaneous minimization across multiple code dimensions: nesting depth, line count, tokens, characters, parameters, variables, loops, and conditionals
Variable discipline includes minimizing their number, scope, accessibility, mutability, lifetime, and name length
Short-lived, tightly scoped variables can use concise names without sacrificing clarity
Early return statements and reduced control structure complexity are key techniques
Minimalism should be a default approach, though not an absolute rule
money_quotes: As I grow older and wiser even older as a programmer, I've found that my personal coding style has trended heavily toward minimalism.
I've tried to code smaller, even going so far as to write no code at all when I can get away with it.
Minimize variability of variables. Strive to make variables final in Java and const in C++.
Define variables at the smallest possible scope.
Minimize lifetime of variables. Prefer ephemeral variables to longer lived ones.
Minimalism isn't always the right choice, but it's rarely the wrong choice.
You could certainly do worse than to adopt the discipline of spartan programming on your next programming project.
posts.key_insight: Code quality improves when you systematically minimize complexity across all dimensions—variables, nesting, scope, and control flow—making minimalism a reliable default approach to programming.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood criticizes Apple's iTunes Store for requiring users to install custom software just to browse content, calling it fundamentally anti-web. He argues that clicking a link should resolve to a web page with information, not a demand to install an application. Atwood contrasts this with Amazon and other retailers that let users browse and purchase through standard web browsers. He compares Apple's walled garden approach to AOL, suggesting it represents a regression in how the internet should work. While acknowledging iTunes is necessary for syncing with Apple devices, he finds it user-hostile to require installation merely to browse a store.
key_points: Links to iTunes content require custom software installation instead of displaying web pages
Apple's approach creates an unnecessary barrier that locks out users on other platforms
Other major retailers like Amazon allow full browsing and purchasing through standard web browsers
The requirement to install software just to browse (not buy or sync) is user-hostile
Apple's walled garden approach mirrors AOL's failed strategy
money_quotes: Is there anything more anti-web than demanding users install custom software to display information that could have just as easily been delivered through the browser?
Or, you could bludgeon me with the digital equivalent of a giant stop sign.
Hey, 'it just works.' Except when it doesn't.
Let's imagine, for a moment, how utterly ridiculous it would be if I had to install the Amazon application to browse and purchase media from Amazon.
It's downright user hostile to demand installation of a special application merely to browse the store, and it is most certainly against everything the web stands for and was built on.
The last 'application' I can recall needing to install to get to things online was AOL.
posts.key_insight: Requiring custom software installation to browse content that could easily be displayed on a web page violates the fundamental principles of how the web should work and creates unnecessary barriers between users and information.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the progression from simple helper functions to C# extension methods to full dynamic language monkeypatching, using the example of adding a Left() method to the String class. While extension methods offer an elegant way to add functionality to existing classes, dynamic languages like Ruby, Python, and JavaScript allow developers to completely redefine core classes—a power that Atwood argues is frequently abused. He cites examples of subtle, hard-to-debug problems caused by monkeypatching in Ruby plugins and argues that most programmers lack the skill to responsibly redesign language fundamentals. The post concludes that monkeypatching should be treated as a last resort, not a first tool, and that such power should inspire humility rather than hubris.
key_points: C# extension methods provide a safe way to add functionality to classes without the ability to override or replace existing behavior
Dynamic languages like Ruby allow full monkeypatching, which can redefine fundamental class behaviors and lead to subtle, combinatoric bugs
Most code is statistically bad, so giving average programmers the power to redesign languages is a recipe for incomprehensible, difficult-to-debug systems
Language design is a specialized skill—there's a reason the most respected computer scientists become language designers
Monkeypatching should be a last resort, with developers preferring safer alternatives like mixins or locally extending individual objects
money_quotes: Try to imagine a world where every programmer you know is a wannabe language designer, bent on molding the language to their whims.
When I close my eyes and imagine it, I have a vision of the apocalypse, a perfect, pitch-black storm of utterly incomprehensible, pathologically difficult to debug code.
I know enough about programmers – myself absolutely included – to know the vast majority of us have absolutely no business whatsoever re-designing a programming language.
Is there anything more pure and godlike than programming your own programming language?
But if wielding that power doesn't scare and humble you a little, too, then maybe you should leave the monkeypatching to the really smart monkeys.

Patches interact in unpredictable, combinatoric ways. And by their nature, bugs caused by monkey patches are more difficult to track down than those introduced by more traditional classes and methods.
Monkeypatching should mean reaching for the meta-hammer as infrequently as humanly possible.
posts.key_insight: The power to dynamically modify programming languages should inspire humility and restraint, not enthusiasm—most programmers, including ourselves, lack the expertise to responsibly redesign language fundamentals.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the dogma of database normalization, arguing that the choice between normalized and denormalized schemas is a pragmatic engineering decision rather than a matter of principle. Using Stack Overflow's tagging system as context, he demonstrates how fully normalized designs require expensive multi-table joins while denormalized approaches sacrifice data integrity for speed. He emphasizes that for most applications, performance differences are negligible until reaching massive scale, and criticizes developers who cling to normalization as religious doctrine. The core argument is that database design should be driven by measurable performance needs and practical considerations, not academic ideals.
key_points: Database normalization eliminates duplication but requires expensive multi-table joins that hurt performance at scale
Denormalization simplifies queries dramatically but shifts data integrity responsibility to the application layer
Performance differences between normalized and denormalized schemas are negligible for most applications—'everything is fast for small n'
Engineers often zealously defend normalization on principle long after it stops making practical sense
The right approach is pragmatic: normalize when it provides measurable benefits, denormalize when performance demands it
money_quotes: Everything is fast for small n.
Which is better—a normalized database, or a denormalized database? Trick question! The answer is that it doesn't matter!
Scalability is not your problem, getting people to give a shit is.
Never, never should you normalize a database out of some vague sense of duty to the ghosts of Boyce-Codd.
Normalization is not magical fairy dust you sprinkle over your database to cure all ills; it often creates as many problems as it solves.
Fear not the specter of denormalization.
Normalize until it hurts, denormalize until it works.
posts.key_insight: Database design decisions should be driven by measured performance and practical team needs, not by adherence to academic principles of normalization.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the concept of 'gold plating' in software development—adding unnecessary features or polish—through the remarkable story of game developer Jeff Minter. Minter shipped Tempest 2000 for the Atari Jaguar with support for an analog rotary controller that didn't exist, building his own homebrew version to test it. This code sat dormant for fourteen years until enthusiasts created custom controllers to use it. While gold plating is typically considered wasteful, Atwood presents this as an exceptional case where a developer's vision and dedication created lasting value for a dedicated community.
key_points: Gold plating—adding unrequired features—is generally considered wasteful because it delays shipping and diverts resources from bugs and needed features
Jeff Minter added support for a non-existent analog controller to Tempest 2000, creating his own hardware prototype to test it
The dormant feature waited 14 years before enthusiasts built custom controllers to finally use it
Sometimes a developer's personal vision and craftsmanship can create unexpected long-term value
The story illustrates the tension between pragmatic shipping and artistic dedication in game development
money_quotes: Some developers love to gold plate their software.
It's usually considered wasteful to fritter away time gold plating old code in the face of new features that need to be implemented, or old bugs that could be squashed.
Jeff wrote in support for an analog rotary controller... that did not exist.
That's the very definition of 'not required.'
This code lied dormant in the game until a handful of enthusiasts, fourteen years later, cobbled together custom controllers to play the game as it was originally intended by the author.
If that isn't the ultimate case of gold plating your software, I don't know what is.
posts.key_insight: While gold plating is usually wasteful, a developer's stubborn vision can occasionally create features that outlast the platform itself, rewarding patient enthusiasts years later.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses a reader's story about a disruptive team member obsessed with JavaScript to explore the broader problem of 'bad apples' on software teams. He argues that problem personnel who campaign against project decisions and work against teammates become serious liabilities, regardless of their technical intentions. Drawing on McConnell's research, Atwood emphasizes that team leaders who fail to address poor performers create the most consistent complaint among team members. The post provides warning signs for identifying bad apples and makes a clear case that managers must act decisively to remove disruptive personalities before they poison the entire project.
key_points: Problem team members who actively campaign against project decisions become liabilities regardless of their technical skills or intentions
Research shows the most intense complaint from team members is leaders unwilling to confront poor performers - a significant management blind spot
Warning signs include covering up ignorance, excessive privacy demands, territoriality, revisiting settled decisions, and not pitching in during crunch time
You can develop skill but you can't develop a positive attitude - attitude problems must be addressed through removal
Delayed action on problem personnel causes compounding damage through code, relationships, and team morale
money_quotes: At the point where you're actively campaigning against the project, and working against your teammates – you're a liability to the project.
You don't have to be friends with everyone on your team, although it certainly helps, but a level of basic personal and professional respect is mandatory for any team to function normally.
More than any other single aspect of team leadership, members are disturbed by leaders who are unwilling to deal directly and effectively with self-serving or noncontributing team members.
You can develop skill, but you can't develop a positive attitude.
If your team leader or manager isn't dealing with the bad apples on your project, she isn't doing her job.
Removing someone from a team is painful; it's not fun for anyone. But realizing you should have removed someone six months ago is far more painful.
They'll slowly spread poison throughout your project, in the form of code, relationships, and contacts.
posts.key_insight: Team leaders who tolerate disruptive personalities aren't just failing that one relationship—they're signaling to every good developer that they'll be expected to succeed despite colleagues working against them.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood laments the persistent problem of 'tag soup' in web development—the messy intermingling of HTML, JavaScript, and server-side code in view templates. Drawing from his experience building Stack Overflow with ASP.NET MVC, he observes that this problem isn't framework-specific but endemic across all major web stacks including Rails, Django, and ASP.NET. He compares editing such code to playing the game Operation, where one wrong move sets off alarms. The post ends with an open question: is there a better architectural approach, or is increasingly sophisticated tag soup simply the best web development can offer?
key_points: Tag soup—mixing markup and server-side code—is a universal problem across all modern web frameworks, not specific to any one technology
Even 'good' tag soup is inherently difficult to read and maintain because it's hard to see where code ends and markup begins
The problem creates a fragile editing experience where changing HTML risks breaking code and vice versa
Despite framework evolution from classic ASP to Rails to Django to ASP.NET MVC, the fundamental tag soup problem persists
The author genuinely wonders if there's a fundamentally better approach waiting to be discovered
money_quotes: If you're not careful bordering on manically fastidious in constructing your Views, you'll end up with a giant mish-mash of HTML, Javascript, and server-side code.
Now you can double your pleasure: badly formed HTML, meet badly written code.
Do you find this readable? Can you see where the code begins and the markup ends?

Sometimes editing this stuff makes me feel like I'm playing Operation. I have to ever so carefully maneuver my metal tweezers into one tiny slice of code or HTML and make my changes without touching the edges and setting off that blasted electrical buzzer.
The tag soup problem seems to be endemic to all modern web development stacks.
Perhaps when it comes to mixing HTML and server-side code, some form of soup is unavoidable, a necessary evil.
Or is this year's newer, fancier, even-more-delicious iteration of tag soup as good as it ever gets for web development?
posts.key_insight: Despite decades of framework innovation, web development remains fundamentally challenged by the inherent messiness of mixing presentation markup with application logic—a problem that may be intrinsic to the medium itself.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the emerging world of ultra-low-power, tiny form factor PCs built around AMD's Geode platform. He walks through building a complete x86 PC using a Nano-ITX motherboard that consumes only 5 watts at idle—nearly ten times less than his already-efficient HTPC. The build costs around $200 and fits in your palm, using a CompactFlash card instead of a hard drive for zero moving parts. While performance is comparable to a Pentium II 300 MHz, Atwood sees this segment converging with mainstream computing through Intel's Atom processor and the netbook trend.
key_points: A complete x86 PC can be built in the Nano-ITX form factor for around $200, consuming only 5 watts at idle
Using CompactFlash instead of a hard drive eliminates moving parts and reduces power consumption further
Performance expectations should match Pentium II 300 MHz systems—capable but not suitable for modern web browsing
The ultra-low-power PC segment is converging with mainstream computing through netbooks and Intel Atom
Even smaller form factors like Pico-ITX (10cm x 7.2cm) exist for those who find Nano-ITX too large
money_quotes: This thing is, for all intents and purposes, a complete, standalone x86 PC that fits in the palm of your hand and sips five watts of power.
That's almost ten times less than my HTPC build I was so proud of!
You may recall those now-ancient boxes were still able to do some pretty amazing things in their day.

I would not build an ultra-lower power PC assuming it will be tolerable for day-to-day web browsing and email reading, unless you're comfortable using text mode or command-line interfaces exclusively.
With the emergence of Intel's Atom and 'netbooks' like the ASUS Eee, it's a segment that is dangerously close to becoming mainstream.
What could you do with a tiny, highly efficient x86 PC that boots up in under a minute?
posts.key_insight: Ultra-low-power computing has reached a point where a complete, palm-sized x86 PC consuming just 5 watts is both affordable and practical for specialized applications, signaling a broader shift toward energy-efficient computing.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that excessive comments are a code smell, not a virtue. He demonstrates through examples that most comments are unnecessary when code is properly written and refactored. The key insight is that developers should write code as if comments didn't exist, forcing themselves to create self-documenting code through good naming and structure. Comments should only be added as a last resort after all refactoring options are exhausted, and even then, writing good comments requires strong communication skills that many developers lack.
key_points: Comments are frequently used as a crutch for poorly written code rather than a genuine aid to understanding
Code tells you how it works; comments should tell you why - but most comments fail at this
The solution to unclear code is refactoring (like extracting well-named functions), not adding comments
Writing good comments is as much an art as writing code itself, requiring communication skills many developers lack
If code seems too complex to understand without comments, the code itself is probably bad and should be rewritten
money_quotes: You should always write your code as if comments didn't exist.
The code already tells us how it works; we need the comments to tell us why it works.
Junior developers rely on comments to tell the story when they should be relying on the code to tell the story.
To write good comments you have to be a good writer.
If your feel your code is too complex to understand without comments, your code is probably just bad.
Comments are narrative asides; important in their own way, but in no way meant to replace plot, characterization, and setting.

I think maybe some of us might be better off sticking to our strengths – that is, writing for the compiler, in as clear a way as we possibly can, and reaching for the comments only as a method of last resort.
posts.key_insight: Self-documenting code through refactoring and good naming is superior to comments, which should only be added as a last resort when all other clarity improvements have been exhausted.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood makes the case that programmers should understand hardware by building their own computers, walking through his recommended parts list for a developer PC. He argues that building a PC is straightforward and provides valuable insight into how software interacts with physical components. The post methodically covers each component choice—from motherboard to CPU cooler—explaining the tradeoffs between performance, noise, power consumption, and cost. He emphasizes practical wisdom like prioritizing clock speed over core count for most development tasks, investing in fast boot drives, and choosing efficient power supplies. The result is a $1100 developer workstation that balances performance with quiet operation.
key_points: Building your own PC is accessible ('If you can snap together a Lego kit, you can build a computer') and teaches programmers valuable lessons about hardware
Clock speed still matters more than core count for most development work—a 3.5 GHz dual-core outperformed a 1.8 GHz eight-core for SQL queries
Memory quantity matters far more than memory speed—cutting memory speed in half only reduced overall performance by 3%
Fast storage (10,000 RPM drives) provides significant developer productivity gains, especially for tasks like Visual Studio builds
Power supply efficiency and cooling design are underrated—an efficient PSU runs cooler and more reliably, while good coolers enable quiet operation
money_quotes: Maybe this is a minority opinion, but I find understanding the hardware to be instructive for programmers.
If you can snap together a Lego kit, you can build a computer.
Despite what Intel's marketing weasels might want you to believe, clock speed still matters very much.
Outside of a few niche activities, such as video encoding and professional 3D rendering, most computing tasks don't scale worth a damn beyond two cores.
By the time your system has to reach outside of the L1, L2, and possibly even L3 cache – it's already so slow from the system's perspective as to be academic.
It's all about putting the money where the most significant bottlenecks are.
An efficient power supply will run cooler and more reliably because it uses higher quality parts.
One of the hidden value propositions in building your own PC is the ability to easily upgrade it later.
posts.key_insight: Programmers benefit from hands-on hardware knowledge, and the key to building a good developer PC is understanding where the real performance bottlenecks are—fast storage and high clock speeds matter more than marketing-driven specs like core count or memory speed.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood donated $5,000 to an open source .NET project with no strings attached, only to discover four months later that the money sat completely untouched. The project coordinator explained that hosting costs were already covered by ads and donations, and legal complexities in Italy made running contests difficult. Atwood explores the paradox that open source projects, which are time-constrained rather than money-constrained, struggle to convert financial contributions into meaningful progress. He solicits ideas from readers for how the grant could be used effectively, expressing genuine bewilderment at this outcome.
key_points: Open source projects operate on donated time rather than money, making cash contributions difficult to utilize effectively
A no-strings-attached grant approach, while well-intentioned, may not account for the structural realities of how open source projects function
Converting money into open source progress requires intermediary mechanisms like bounties, internships, or hiring for documentation and marketing
Legal and administrative overhead can create unexpected barriers to using donated funds
The most painful outcome of philanthropy isn't rejection or misuse—it's complete inaction
money_quotes: Open source projects run on time, not money.
I was crushingly disappointed to find out the $5,000 in grant money has been sitting in the bank for the last four months, totally unused.
Open Source is to Traditional Software as Terror Cells are to Large Standing Armies – if you gave a terrorist group a fighter jet, they wouldn't know what to do with it.
Why did we bother doing this if nothing changes?
I'm absolutely dumbfounded to learn that contributing money isn't an effective way to advance an open source project.
That's painful to hear, possibly the most painful of all outcomes.
posts.key_insight: Open source projects are fundamentally time-constrained rather than money-constrained, which means well-intentioned financial donations often sit unused because the real bottleneck is volunteer hours, not funding.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood traces the origins of 'alpha' and 'beta' software terminology back to IBM's hardware testing conventions, then walks through the standard software release lifecycle phases from pre-alpha to gold. He observes that in the Web 2.0 era, these terms have lost their original meaning, with beta becoming increasingly elastic and companies eager to release alpha-quality code to external users. The 'perpetual beta' phenomenon, exemplified by Gmail staying in beta for over four years, has made the designation almost meaningless. Atwood suggests that perhaps software is never truly 'done' in the modern web age.
key_points: Alpha and beta terminology originated from IBM hardware testing conventions dating back to punched card machines
The traditional release lifecycle progresses through pre-alpha, alpha, beta, release candidate, and gold phases with increasingly external testing
Release candidate phase should only fix bugs that 'kill small children' - the most severe issues only
The definition of beta has become increasingly elastic, and companies are too eager to expose alpha-quality code to external users
Perpetual beta has become a running joke in Web 2.0, with services like Gmail staying in beta for years
money_quotes: The term beta test comes from an IBM hardware product test convention, dating back to punched card tabulating and sorting machines.
One of the most experienced software developers I ever worked with characterized the release candidate development phase thusly: 'does this bug kill small children?'
The software is finished – and by finished, we mean there are no show-stopping, little-children-killing bugs in it. That we know of.
The definition of beta grows more all-encompassing and elastic every year.
We are awfully eager to throw alpha quality code over the wall to external users and testers.
In the brave new world of web 2.0, the alpha and beta designations don't mean quite the same things they used to.
'loves you' is certainly more fun than 'gold,' but I'm not sure it's ever the same as done.
posts.key_insight: The Web 2.0 era has rendered traditional software release terminology nearly meaningless, as perpetual beta has become the norm and companies increasingly blur the line between internal and external testing.

posts.summary: Atwood uses the famous ceramics class parable from 'Art & Fear' to argue that producing large quantities of work leads to higher quality outcomes than obsessing over perfection. The ceramics students graded on quantity ended up creating better pots than those graded on quality, because they learned through iteration while the perfectionists merely theorized. He draws a direct parallel to software development: building many projects teaches you more than agonizing over building the perfect one. This applies equally to blogging, where committing to a regular schedule matters more than waiting for the perfect post.
key_points: The ceramics class experiment showed that students graded on quantity produced higher quality work than those graded on quality
Perfectionists spent time theorizing while practitioners learned through doing and failing
The principle applies across domains: software development, blogging, and any creative craft
Mental commitment to repeated practice is the prerequisite for improvement
Building the wrong thing still teaches you more than endlessly planning the right thing
money_quotes: The works of highest quality were all produced by the group being graded for quantity.

While the 'quantity' group was busily churning out piles of work – and learning from their mistakes – the 'quality' group had sat theorizing about perfection, and in the end had little more to show for their efforts than grandiose theories and a pile of dead clay.
Quantity always trumps quality.
Until you've mentally committed to doing it over and over, you will not improve. You can't.
If you aren't building, you aren't learning.
Rather than agonizing over whether you're building the right thing, just build it.
posts.key_insight: Iteration through quantity is the path to quality—you cannot improve without the mental commitment to build repeatedly and learn from your mistakes.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his own failures at project scheduling, using the Stack Overflow project's timeline slip as a case study. He diagnoses the core problem as developers (including himself) not maintaining detailed task lists, making any schedule estimates pure fantasy. The post argues that the infamous '90% done' syndrome stems from not knowing what remains to be done. Atwood recommends breaking work into day-sized tasks and maintaining visible progress tracking. He admits to being 'pathologically bad' about writing things down despite recognizing it as essential for realistic scheduling.
key_points: Schedule estimates without a detailed task list are 'utter fantasy' - you cannot know how long something will take if you don't know everything that needs doing
The '90% done' syndrome occurs when developers track progress in their heads rather than on paper, leading to endless undiscovered work
Tasks should be broken into 'inch-pebbles' of one day or less to enable accurate progress tracking
Visible status and daily tracking are essential for catching schedule slips early and identifying interruption problems
Even people who recognize these principles (like Atwood himself) struggle to follow them in practice
money_quotes: Most software project management books are crap. Just most of them.

This software developer does not have a detailed list of all the things he needs to do. Which means, despite adamantly claiming that he is 99 percent done – he has no idea how long development will take!
Until you've got at least the beginnings of a task list, any concept of scheduling is utter fantasy.
I'm almost pathologically bad about writing things down. Unless I'm writing a blog entry, I suppose.
Every time someone asks you what your schedule is, you should be able to point to a list of everything you need to do.
If you can't – the first item on your task list should be to create that list.
posts.key_insight: You cannot estimate when you'll be done until you've written down everything that needs doing, broken into tasks small enough to complete in a day.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares a humorous story about using deliberately terrible music as a form of workplace bonding and psychological warfare among programmers. What started as a joke—playing a compilation of notoriously cheesy 70s songs to torture coworkers—eventually led to genuine appreciation through sheer repetition. He draws a parallel to a similar story from Microsoft's Windows NT development, where Dave Cutler used Jim Nabors gospel albums and Alvin and the Chipmunks to torment his build team. The post is a lighthearted warning that asking programmers for coding music recommendations might backfire spectacularly.
key_points: Deliberately bad music became a daily ritual and 'team building' exercise that broke people's will like 'a Vietnamese prison camp in stereo'
Through forced repeated exposure, genuinely terrible music can wrap around and become enjoyable—Stockholm syndrome for your ears
This form of musical griefing has historical precedent in software development, notably during the creation of Windows NT
The post serves as a tongue-in-cheek warning about the dangers of asking programmers for music recommendations
Shared suffering through awful music can paradoxically become a form of team bonding
money_quotes: In short: we broke people. It was like a Vietnamese prison camp in stereo.
Maybe it's so bad, it has actually wrapped all the way around and it's... good again?
It was hard to tell which was more loathesome – Nabors gone gospel or Cutler gone musical.
Music licensing is the hardest problem in computer science!
So the next time you ask one of your fellow programmers to put on some background coding music for the team, think twice.
posts.key_insight: Programmer culture often bonds through shared ironic suffering, turning deliberate annoyances into beloved traditions through sheer repetition and collective endurance.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his evolving relationship with JavaScript, noting that by 2008 it had matured from a 'peculiar website oddity' to a viable programming platform. He acknowledges the pain of cross-browser compatibility issues but argues that JavaScript frameworks like jQuery, Prototype, and others have fundamentally changed the equation. These frameworks abstract away browser differences, making JavaScript development not just tolerable but genuinely enjoyable. He credits the 'JavaScript Ninjas' who built these frameworks for transforming web development.
key_points: JavaScript evolved from a quirky web feature to a legitimate programming platform capable of powering complex applications
Cross-browser compatibility remains painful, with 'hundreds of aggravating differences' between major browsers
JavaScript frameworks provide higher-level APIs that abstract away browser inconsistencies, eliminating the 'write-once-debug-everywhere' nightmare
It's now 'foolish' to roll your own JavaScript for even trivial browser tasks when mature frameworks exist
The author's experience building Stack Overflow with jQuery was the first time he genuinely enjoyed writing JavaScript
money_quotes: JavaScript now works. Just look around you on the web.
It is excruciatingly painful to write and test your complex JavaScript code across (n) browsers and (n) operating systems. It'll make you pine for the good old days of HTML 4.0 and CGI.
The JavaScript Ninjas have delivered their secret and ultimate weapon: common APIs.
They transform working with JavaScript from an unpleasant, write-once-debug-everywhere chore into something that's actually – dare I say it – fun.
Frankly, it is foolish to even consider rolling your own JavaScript code to do even the most trivial of things in a browser now.
Using JQuery while writing Stack Overflow is probably the only time in my entire career as a programmer that I have enjoyed writing JavaScript code.
It's sure pleasant to write code against solid, increasingly standardized JavaScript API libraries that spackle over all those infuriating browser differences.
posts.key_insight: JavaScript frameworks transformed web development from a cross-browser nightmare into an enjoyable experience by providing standardized APIs that abstract away implementation differences.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the dangers of Fake User Interfaces (FUI) used by malware distributors to trick users into installing malicious software. He walks through a real-world example where a legitimate lily society website was compromised to display convincing fake virus scans and security alerts to visitors arriving via search engines. The fake interface mimicked Windows security dialogs so effectively that even experienced users might hesitate. Atwood argues that while phishing filters help, they cannot keep pace with rapidly compromised websites, and user education remains an uphill battle. He ultimately questions whether there is any reliable defense against perfectly spoofed interfaces presented to non-technical users.
key_points: Malware distributors use FUI to create convincing fake security scans that mimic real Windows interfaces, complete with animated file lists and scrollbars
The hack was clever enough to only trigger for visitors arriving via search engines, keeping the site owners unaware of the compromise
Browser phishing filters provide some protection but cannot keep up with the rapid setup and teardown of compromised websites
The security software industry's culture of fear makes it harder to distinguish legitimate warnings from malicious ones
Even technically sophisticated users can be fooled by well-executed FUI, making protection of naive users nearly impossible
money_quotes: If it can happen to us, it can happen to anyone. Which means FUI can be quite dangerous in the wrong hands.
You and I may understand that distinction, but what about your parents? Your wife? Your children? Your less technically savvy friends?
It's increasingly difficult to tell the good guys from the bad guys. That's the downside of fear as a selling point: it cuts equally well in both directions.

Can you imagine your mother doing a web search on flowers – flowers, for God's sake – clicking on the search results to a totally legitimate website, and correctly navigating the resulting maze of fake UI, spurious javascript alerts, and download dialogs?
If the spoofing is good enough, the FUI extra-convincing, even a Linux or OS X user could be coerced into entering their admin password for a 'system security scan.'
Short of user education, which is a never ending, continuous uphill battle – how would you combat a perfectly spoofed FUI presented to a naïve user?
posts.key_insight: The fundamental security problem with fake user interfaces is not technical but human—when malicious software perfectly mimics legitimate security warnings, even savvy users struggle to distinguish friend from foe, and naive users have no chance.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that developers should commit code to source control frequently rather than waiting until features are complete. He contends that going days without commits creates integration problems and makes code effectively invisible to the team. The post distinguishes between broken code (unacceptable to commit) and incomplete code (perfectly fine to commit as stubs). Atwood proposes a 'software accretion' model where functionality is built up through many small, tested commits rather than large code drops. This approach enables continuous feedback, code review, and integration throughout development.
key_points: Working more than a day without checking in code creates serious integration problems
Code that isn't in source control effectively doesn't exist - it's invisible to the team
There's an important distinction between broken code and incomplete code; stubs and skeletons are valuable to commit
Developers who reject waterfall methodology often unknowingly practice waterfall-style source control
Software accretion - building through tiny, tested, frequent commits - keeps code always functional while growing incrementally
money_quotes: Check in early, check in often.
If the code isn't checked into source control, it doesn't exist.
I'd much rather have small fragments checked in periodically than to go long periods with no idea whatsoever what my coworkers are writing.
I'm not proposing developers check in broken code – but I also argue that there's a big difference between broken code and incomplete code.

Developers that wouldn't even consider adopting the old-school waterfall method of software development somehow have no problem adopting essentially the very same model when it comes to their source control habits.
Start with a tiny fragment of code that does almost nothing. Look on the bright side – code that does nothing can't have many bugs!
You always have functional software. It may not do much, but it runs.
posts.key_insight: Frequent small commits aren't just about backup—they make your work visible, enable collaboration, and paradoxically reduce risk by keeping code always functional.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts his frustrating experience debugging database deadlocks during the early development of Stack Overflow. Despite being a read-heavy web application with minimal writes, SQL Server 2005's pessimistic locking strategy caused frequent deadlocks between simple read queries and rare write operations. After investigating the NOLOCK hint and consulting experienced developers, he ultimately solved the problem by enabling 'read committed snapshot' mode, which uses copy-on-write snapshots instead of heavy locking. The post critiques SQL Server's enterprise-oriented defaults as poorly suited for typical web application workloads.
key_points: SQL Server's default pessimistic locking strategy assumes banking-level data integrity needs, which is overkill for most web applications
Simple read queries were being deadlocked by rare write operations, a counterintuitive behavior that violated reasonable expectations
The NOLOCK hint (dirty reads) works fine in practice for simple read-only queries despite theoretical concerns from database purists
Read committed snapshot mode provides a better balance for read-heavy web applications by using data snapshots instead of locks
MySQL's web-native heritage likely makes it less prone to these issues out of the box compared to SQL Server
money_quotes: It's as if one of the dining philosophers happened to glance over at another philosopher's plate, and the other philosopher, seeing this, screamed 'meal viewing deadlock!' and quickly covered his plate with his hands.
In theory there is no difference between theory and practice. In practice there is.
You'll find no shortage of database architecture astronauts who start dropping ACID science on you and all but pull the building fire alarm when you tell them you want to try nolock.
I'm deeply uncomfortable with errors I don't fully understand. This is the kind of stuff that quite literally keeps me up at night.
With nolock / read uncommitted / dirty reads, data may be out of date at the time you read it, but it's never wrong or garbled or corrupted in a way that will crash you.
I'm a little disappointed that SQL Server treats our silly little web app like it's a banking application.
It ain't rocket surgery.
posts.key_insight: Database systems designed for enterprise transaction processing often have pessimistic defaults that create unnecessary problems for read-heavy web applications, and practical experience should guide when to relax theoretical purity.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses a thinly-veiled personal anecdote about 'a friend' to explain how XSS vulnerabilities can lead to cookie theft and session hijacking. He demonstrates how malicious JavaScript injected through inadequate HTML sanitization can steal authentication cookies and allow attackers to impersonate users with administrative privileges. The post advocates for HttpOnly cookies as a simple but effective defense mechanism that prevents client-side JavaScript from accessing sensitive cookie data. While acknowledging that HttpOnly isn't a complete solution due to browser inconsistencies (particularly Firefox's XMLHttpObject bug), Atwood argues it significantly raises the security bar against common XSS attacks.
key_points: XSS has become the most common publicly reported security vulnerability, surpassing classic threats like buffer overflows and SQL injection
Cookies are the 'keys to the kingdom' for user identity - stealing them allows complete impersonation of that user
Writing custom HTML sanitizers is dangerous because clever attackers can construct malformed input that slips through
HttpOnly flag tells browsers to restrict cookie access to server-side only, blocking document.cookie access from JavaScript
While not perfect (Firefox had bugs allowing header access via XMLHttpObject), HttpOnly cookies are a 'set it and forget it' defense that makes cookie theft significantly harder
money_quotes: XSS is now the most common of all publicly reported security vulnerabilities – dwarfing old standards like buffer overruns and SQL injection.
Once someone has your browser cookies for a given website, they essentially have the keys to the kingdom for your identity there.
It's sort of ironic that the HttpOnly flag was pioneered by Microsoft in hoary old Internet Explorer 6 SP1, a browser which isn't exactly known for its iron-clad security record.
HttpOnly cookies don't make you immune from XSS cookie theft, but they raise the bar considerably.
It's practically free, a 'set it and forget it' setting that's bound to become increasingly secure over time.
If you develop web applications, or you know anyone who develops web applications, make sure they know about HttpOnly cookies.
posts.key_insight: HttpOnly cookies are a simple, nearly free security measure that dramatically reduces the impact of XSS vulnerabilities by preventing JavaScript from accessing authentication tokens.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces that he and his wife are expecting a baby, conceived through IVF after years of trying. He humorously frames the announcement in programming terms ('spawning a new process') and reflects on the irony of struggling to conceive while teenagers manage it accidentally. The post includes a sardonic nod to Richard Stallman's infamous dismissal of reproduction as less impressive than contributing to Emacs. Atwood closes by quoting Dave Eggers about the joy and responsibility of shaping a young mind, ultimately expressing his excitement to fill his child's head with his 'insane, crazy ideas.'
