key_points: The pregnancy was achieved through IVF after years of difficulty, highlighting the gap between expectation and reality in family planning
Atwood pokes fun at programmer culture by including Stallman's quote dismissing reproduction as trivial compared to software contribution
The extended Eggers quote captures the exhilarating sense of possibility and responsibility in raising a child
Atwood embraces the idea of being a formative (and potentially scarring) influence on his child as a 'proud family tradition'
money_quotes: I debated for days which geeky reference I would use as a synonym for 'we're having a baby.' The title is the best I could do. I'm truly sorry.
Despite the best of intentions, you really start to resent all those teenage couples who manage to get pregnant so awkwardly and accidentally.
It doesn't take special talents to reproduce – even plants can do it. On the other hand, contributing to a program like Emacs takes real skill.
I'm looking forward to stocking our unborn child's mind with all my insane, crazy ideas.
We are dangerous. We are daring and immortal.
I guess what I'm trying to say is that, with any luck, he or she will be scarred for life. That's a proud family tradition where I come from.
posts.key_insight: Even programmers who live in abstractions eventually confront the messy, unpredictable, deeply human experience of starting a family—and find it worth celebrating.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces the public launch of Stack Overflow after six months of development, framing it as a community-driven effort to aggregate and share programming knowledge. He defends the site against critics who claim it will 'dumben' developers, arguing instead that collective knowledge-sharing among 'amateurs' is more valuable than waiting for elusive experts. The post positions Stack Overflow as a hybrid of wiki, discussion forum, blog, and voting systems designed to surface good programming information with minimal friction. Atwood emphasizes that the site belongs to its community, not to him personally.
key_points: Stack Overflow combines elements of wikis, forums, blogs, and reddit-style voting to create a new type of programming Q&A site
The site aims to unlock programming knowledge trapped in forums, help files, and obscure books by making it easily discoverable
Critics worried the site would contribute to developer incompetence, but Atwood argues collective amateur knowledge beats inaccessible expert knowledge
True experts are often too busy to help, making community-driven knowledge sharing more practical than relying on authorities
The platform is designed for minimal friction so busy programmers can contribute and benefit in just a few minutes
money_quotes: Stack Overflow is sort of like the anti-experts-exchange (minus the nausea-inducing sleaze and quasi-legal search engine gaming) meets wikipedia meets programming reddit.
There's far too much great programming information trapped in forums, buried in online help, or hidden away in books that nobody buys any more.
We are all, as developers, hopelessly incompetent.

We all have these little tiny pieces of information and if we can just contribute a little bit, that information gets amplified, and maybe a thousand other dumb developers will benefit from my one little piece of good information.
The idea that you have all these experts waiting in the wings to do stuff is an illusion in my experience. There's really just a bunch of amateurs muddling along trying to do things together.
The people that are truly experts are too busy to even help, right? And if the experts are too busy to help, what difference does it really make if there are experts at all.
But who cares what I think; my opinion holds no particular weight. I'm just a member. This is our site.
posts.key_insight: Collective knowledge from many imperfect programmers is more valuable than theoretical expertise from authorities who are too busy to actually help.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses the ill-fated Windows Vista ads featuring Bill Gates and Jerry Seinfeld as a springboard to discuss tech advertising and his love of Code Complete. He critiques both Microsoft's opaque ad campaign and Apple's 'I'm a Mac' ads, noting the irony that the PC character played by John Hodgman is actually more likable than the Mac guy. The post pivots to highlight that Gates is shown reading from Code Complete in the ad, which Atwood considers his all-time favorite programming book and the namesake of his blog. He sees Gates' choice of that specific book as intentional and meaningful.
key_points: The Gates/Seinfeld Vista ads were poorly received and possibly cancelled, though they succeeded in generating conversation
Apple's Mac vs PC ads backfire somewhat because John Hodgman (the PC) is more likable and interesting than Justin Long (the Mac)
Advertising is fundamentally deceptive - Hodgman actually uses Macs in real life
Gates reading Code Complete in the ad was likely a deliberate choice reflecting his appreciation for the book
The quote Gates reads argues that inheritance and polymorphism don't automatically make a design good
money_quotes: The goal of advertising is to get people talking about it. Even if every one of those conversations starts with 'what the hell were they thinking,' hey – it's a conversation.
I'd much sooner associate myself with Hodgman than with Long.
The sleight of hand breaks down a bit when you realize that Hodgman actually uses Macs, but that's advertising for you: a giant pack of lies.
The fact that a design uses inheritance and polymorphism doesn't make it a good design.
On the whole, I'd call these ads opaque bordering on inane.
posts.key_insight: Even failed advertising campaigns can contain nuggets of genuine value, like Bill Gates quietly endorsing one of the best programming books ever written while the internet debates whether the ads make any sense.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains cross-site request forgery (CSRF/XSRF), a web vulnerability that exploits how browsers automatically send credentials with requests. Unlike XSS which requires developer mistakes, CSRF exploits the fundamental design of the web itself. The attack works by tricking authenticated users into unknowingly submitting requests to target sites, using techniques like hidden image tags or auto-submitting forms. Atwood walks through why simple fixes like requiring POST requests aren't sufficient, and presents three mitigation strategies: referrer checking (which he dismisses), secret hidden form tokens, and double-submitted cookies. He admits to previously underestimating CSRF and urges developers to verify their protections before attacks occur.
key_points: CSRF exploits how browsers automatically include cookies and credentials with every request, making it impossible for servers to distinguish legitimate requests from forged ones
Simple mitigations like requiring POST instead of GET are insufficient because attackers can create auto-submitting forms on malicious pages
The most reliable defenses involve server-generated tokens (hidden form values tied to sessions) or double-submitted cookies verified via JavaScript
Major sites including Gmail, Digg, and Wikipedia have been successfully attacked via CSRF, showing even sophisticated teams miss this vulnerability
Unlike XSS which stems from developer error, CSRF vulnerability exists 'simply because of how the whole Web is designed to work'
money_quotes: Since XSS gets all the limelight, few developers pay much attention to another form of attack that's equally destructive and potentially far easier to exploit.
Your application can be vulnerable to cross-site request forgery (CSRF) attacks not because you the developer did something wrong, but simply because of how the whole Web is designed to work. Scary!
From the webserver's perspective, there is no difference whatsoever between a real user initiated browser request and the above image URL retrieval.
With XSRF, malicious users can initiate any arbitrary action they like on a target website.
I thought we had our website vulnerabilities licked with XSS. I was wrong.
Don't make the same mistake I did! Understand how XSRF works and ensure you're protected before it becomes a problem.
posts.key_insight: CSRF is particularly insidious because it exploits the web's fundamental trust model rather than developer mistakes, making it easy to overlook even for security-conscious teams.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares Stack Overflow's remarkable growth trajectory, noting that Google went from 10% to 50% of their traffic within a month of launch. He discusses the uncomfortable reality of Google's monopoly over web search and explains how their initial indexing problems were solved by implementing a sitemap.xml file. Despite Stack Overflow being designed for easy crawling, the paginated question lists weren't being fully indexed without explicit sitemap assistance. The post captures both gratitude for Google's effectiveness and unease about depending so heavily on a single company for discoverability.
key_points: Stack Overflow grew from 10% to 50% Google traffic within one month of public launch, demonstrating the speed at which Google can become a site's dominant traffic source
Google's search monopoly is far more concerning than Microsoft's desktop dominance because it controls access to 'the whole of human knowledge'
Dynamic sites with paginated content require sitemaps because Google won't reliably crawl simple pagination URL schemes on its own
Outsourcing search to Google is a pragmatic choice because writing good search code is genuinely hard for small teams
The sitemap.xml format is essentially a sanctioned way to create a link-heavy page that tells search engines exactly what to index
money_quotes: When we say 'web search' we mean one thing, and one thing only: Google.
Google's current position as the start page for the internet kind of scares the crap out of me, in a way that Microsoft's dominance over the desktop PC never did.
Do we really trust one company to be a benevolent monopoly over... well, everything?

Writing search code is hard. It's far easier to outsource the burden of search to Google and their legions of server farms than it is for our tiny development team to do it on our one itty-bitty server.
Sometimes I'd even get copies of our content from evil RSS scraper sites that plug in their own ads of questionable provenance, which was downright depressing.
I'm a little aggravated that we have to set up this special file for the Googlebot to do its job properly.
But when 50% of your traffic comes from one source, perhaps it's best not to ask these kinds of questions.
I, for one, welcome our pixelated Google overlords!
posts.key_insight: The convenience of Google's search dominance creates an uncomfortable dependency where website owners must play by Google's rules because the alternative—building your own search—is impractically hard for most teams.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF/XSRF) attacks and how to prevent them, building on his previous post about the vulnerability. He clarifies that XSRF exploits the fact that websites verify requests came from an authorized user's browser, not that the user actually initiated the action. Citing recent discoveries of XSRF vulnerabilities in major sites like ING Direct, YouTube, MetaFilter, and The New York Times, he emphasizes that even well-resourced companies make these mistakes. He presents two prevention methods: the 'double-submitted cookie' approach requiring no server state, and a stronger server-side token generation method used by Stack Overflow.
key_points: Websites typically verify that requests come from an authorized user's browser, not that the user themselves initiated the action - this is the core XSRF vulnerability
Attackers exploit XSRF by gambling that users have validated login cookies stored, then tricking browsers into making requests on their behalf
Major websites including ING Direct, YouTube, MetaFilter, and The New York Times were found vulnerable to XSRF attacks, showing how pervasive the problem is
The 'double-submitted cookie' method prevents XSRF by requiring form submissions to include a pseudorandom value that matches a cookie value
A stronger approach generates and tracks unique random keys for every HTML form, which Stack Overflow implements
money_quotes: The key to understanding CSRF attacks is to recognize that websites typically don't verify that a request came from an authorized user. Instead they verify only that the request came from the browser of an authorized user.
Attackers are gambling that users have a validated login cookie for your website already stored in their browser.
If major public facing websites are falling prey to these serious XSRF exploits, how confident do you feel that you haven't made the same mistakes?
I'm saying this as a developer who has already made these same mistakes on his own website. I'm just as guilty as anyone.
It's our job to make sure future developers don't repeat the same stupid mistakes we made – at least not without a fight.
If you want to audit a website for XSRF vulnerabilities, start by asking this simple question about every single HTML form you can find: 'where's the XSRF value?'
posts.key_insight: XSRF vulnerabilities persist because developers mistakenly assume browser authentication equals user authorization, and the fix requires deliberately validating that each form submission was intentionally initiated by the user.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood defends his decision to write custom HTML sanitization code for Stack Overflow rather than using third-party libraries, responding to criticism that he should have used existing solutions. He argues that while code reuse is generally good, developers must identify their core business functions and build those themselves, even if it means making mistakes along the way. The post draws on Joel Spolsky's advice about outsourcing non-core functions while keeping critical competencies in-house. Atwood contends that by wrestling with HTML sanitization himself, he gained deep understanding of XSS vulnerabilities that he couldn't have acquired by simply importing a library. He concludes that the hard-won experience of building it yourself is invaluable when that code is central to your product.
key_points: Code reuse isn't always the right answer—core business functions should be built in-house regardless of available alternatives
Understanding comes from creation; using libraries without comprehension leaves dangerous knowledge gaps
HTML sanitization is deceptively difficult because browsers parse malformed HTML liberally, creating unexpected attack vectors
The 'not-invented-here syndrome' criticism doesn't apply when the code in question is foundational to your product
Experience earned through making your own mistakes provides deeper understanding than inherited solutions
money_quotes: Being a 'professional' developer, if there really is such a thing – I still have my doubts – doesn't mean choosing third-party libraries for every possible programming task you encounter.
If it's a core business function – do it yourself, no matter what.
What I cannot create, I do not understand.
I'm not so much talented as competent and loud.
In choosing to fight that HTML sanitizer battle, I've earned the scars of experience.
I don't have to take anybody's word for it – I don't have to trust 'libraries.'
Surely there's something, some core business competency, so important that you feel compelled to build it yourself, even if it means making your own mistakes.
posts.key_insight: For code that sits at the heart of your product, the understanding gained from building it yourself—mistakes and all—is more valuable than the safety of using someone else's library.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the recurring failure of automated obscenity filters, using the infamous 'clbuttic' example where naive text replacement turned 'classic' into 'clbuttic' by replacing 'ass' with 'butt'. He traces similar disasters including Yahoo's 2001 JavaScript security filter that transformed 'medieval' into 'medireview' and 'evaluation' into 'reviewuation'. The post argues that these failures stem from inexperienced developers implementing overly simplistic solutions without considering word boundaries or context. Atwood concludes that obscenity filtering is fundamentally unsolvable through code alone, comparing such filters to blacklists that admit failure before even starting.
key_points: Naive string replacement without word boundaries creates absurd artifacts like 'clbuttic' (classic), 'consbreastution' (constitution), and 'medireview' (medieval)
Even 'smarter' regex solutions with word boundaries fail because language is contextual - legitimate words like 'Great Tits' (the bird) get caught
Obscenity filters share the fundamental flaw of blacklists: they're reactive approaches that can never anticipate all edge cases

The pattern of inexperienced developers reinventing failed solutions repeats across generations - 'every day a brand new software developer is born and immediately begins repeating all the same mistakes'
Yahoo's JavaScript exploit filter demonstrates that even well-intentioned security measures can cause widespread collateral damage when implemented naively
money_quotes: Every day a brand new software developer is born and immediately begins repeating all the same mistakes we made years ago.
Obscenity filters are like blacklists; using one is tantamount to admitting failure before you've even started.
I'm doubtful it will ever be possible to solve this particular problem through code alone.
Oh, great, an inexperienced developer had a problem, and thought they would use regular expressions. Now they have two problems.
Replacing one thing with another, when it comes to human beings and written language, is an activity that is fraught with peril even in the best possible circumstances.
Bad ideas are just plain bad ideas, regardless of language or implementation choices.
posts.key_insight: Automated text filtering for obscenity or security is a fundamentally flawed approach because human language is too contextual and nuanced to be safely manipulated through simple pattern matching.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that marketing—not coding prowess—is the most important skill for software engineers to develop. Inspired by Steve Yegge's observations from Google and Amazon, Atwood contends that brilliant code is worthless until people understand, become interested in, and get excited about it. Using a D&D character stats metaphor, he illustrates how over-investing in technical skills while neglecting communication leads to code that never ships. The post advocates for a more balanced skill distribution where developers actively advocate for their work rather than expecting code quality alone to carry them.
key_points: Marketing at its core is about getting other people interested in your ideas, not being a 'marketing weasel'
Code is irrelevant until people understand it, become interested in it, and get excited about it
Programmers often blame external factors for their code not shipping when the real issue is lack of advocacy
A balanced developer with good communication skills will outperform a genius coder with poor interpersonal abilities
Marketing is fundamentally about human communication—historically a weak point for programmers
money_quotes: If there was one thing I could teach every engineer, it would be how to market.
All that brilliant code is totally irrelevant until people understand what you're doing, become interested in what you're doing, and get excited about what you're doing.
At its core, marketing is little more than an intermediate level course on fundamental human communication.
You probably spent too much time building your code and not enough time as an advocate of your code.
Sometimes, you become a better programmer by choosing not to program.
OK, you're a genius programmer who can code circles around everyone else. But you may never ship any of your code for reasons that you don't control. That's an illusion.
I had been reading dirty books. Marketing books. By choice, even.
posts.key_insight: Technical excellence without the ability to communicate and advocate for your work will leave even the best code unshipped and unappreciated.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to criticism calling his blog 'the most dangerous programming blog' by demonstrating the value of critical reading. He takes a potentially inaccurate blog post about Deflate vs GZip performance, tests it himself, and discovers the original claim was partially correct but hardware-dependent. Rather than being angry about misinformation, he argues that treating blog readers as capable of critical thinking makes them better programmers. The post is a defense of blogging culture and reader intelligence.
key_points: Blog posts shouldn't be taken as gospel truth but as 'germs of intriguing ideas' that prompt investigation
Testing claims yourself leads to deeper understanding, even when the original advice is inaccurate
Dismissing blog content as 'dangerous' implies readers are too dumb to think critically
The best content emerges from discussion in comments, not the posts themselves
Technical claims can be context-dependent - the GZip vs Deflate performance varied significantly across different hardware
money_quotes: When you say that the information presented on a blog is 'dangerous,' you're implying the audience is too dumb or inept to read critically.
I am a better programmer for having read that blog post. Even though, near as I can tell, it's offering inaccurate advice.
The best content always begins where the blog post ends.
Like any veteran internet user, I never take what I read on a blog – or any other site on the internet, for that matter – as fact. Rather, it's a germ of an intriguing idea, a call to action.
Maybe what I promote on this blog is dangerous: thinking for yourself.
My audience is far, far smarter than I will ever be.
I, for one, have too much respect for my audience to ever do that.
posts.key_insight: The 'danger' of programming blogs isn't misinformation—it's the assumption that readers can't evaluate claims critically and learn from the process of verification.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on purchasing a cheap Acer Aspire One netbook and discovering that for most computing tasks, a simple web browser is all most people need. He argues that netbooks represent a fundamental shift in computing—they're not small laptops but cheap, portable web browsers. As web browsers dominate application usage, the choice of operating system becomes increasingly irrelevant. He predicts netbooks will threaten both Apple (whose premium pricing becomes harder to justify) and Microsoft (creating an opening for Linux), while keeping Windows XP alive longer than expected due to Vista's heavy requirements.
key_points: Six of the top 10 most-used applications are web browsers or websites, making the browser the dominant computing interface
Netbooks aren't small laptops—they're an entirely new category: cheap, portable web browsers
Operating system choice is becoming irrelevant when users spend most time in browsers
Netbooks create a huge opening for Linux as prices drop toward $199 or $99, delivering browsers at near-zero marginal cost
The web browser as an operating system seemed stupid until practical experience with netbooks proved otherwise
money_quotes: At this point, who cares what operating system you run?
Choice of web browser will have a far more profound impact on most people's daily lives.
Netbooks are an entirely different breed of animal. They are cheap, portable web browsers.
The most popular application in the world is the web browser. By far. Number two isn't even close.
Pitching the web browser as a bona-fide operating system always seemed stupid to me. Or at least it did, until I sat down with my first netbook.
This is a huge opening for Linux; it's the ideal way to deliver a complete, modern web browser at nearly zero marginal cost to both the vendor and consumer.
If it runs Firefox 3, or Chrome, or Opera, that's about all I need.
posts.key_insight: The web browser has become so dominant that operating systems are becoming irrelevant commodities, and cheap netbooks are the harbinger of this transformation.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the deceptively complex problem of detecting and auto-linking URLs in text. While a naive regex approach seems simple, it fails when users enclose URLs in parentheses—a common practice—because parentheses are actually valid URL characters per the spec. Sites like Wikipedia and MSDN frequently generate URLs containing parentheses, making it impossible to simply ignore trailing parens. Atwood presents a more sophisticated regex solution with a whitelist of valid characters and post-processing logic, but ultimately concludes that website creators should avoid using unusual characters in URLs they generate.
key_points: Simple URL detection regex fails when users wrap URLs in parentheses, which is extremely common behavior
Parentheses are perfectly legal URL characters per RFC 1738, creating an ambiguity problem
Allowing arbitrary characters in URLs creates XSS vulnerability risks
A whitelist approach for URL characters combined with post-processing logic handles most cases
The real solution is for website creators to avoid generating URLs with unusual characters
money_quotes: URLs are simple things. Or so you'd think.
What's truly aggravating is that parens in URLs are perfectly legal.
You could force users to escape the parens, but that's sort of draconian, and it's a little unreasonable to expect your users to know how to escape characters in the URL.
Allowing arbitrary random characters in URLs is setting yourself up for XSS exploits, and I can tell you that from personal experience.
That's a whole lot of extra work, just because the URL spec allows parens.
Avoid using parens (or any unusual characters, for that matter) in URLs you create. They're annoying to use, and rarely handled correctly by auto-linking code.
posts.key_insight: URL parsing is a perfect example of how seemingly simple problems become complex when specs collide with real-world user behavior, and sometimes the best solution is to avoid creating the problem in the first place.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reviews 'HCI Remixed,' a book covering major milestones in human-computer interaction that he found valuable during travel. He highlights Douglas Engelbart's 1968 demonstration as a pivotal moment that debuted the mouse, hypertext, and collaborative computing decades before mainstream adoption. Atwood reflects on how visionaries like Engelbart are often dismissed as impractical in their time, only to be proven right later. The book's unique approach of having current HCI professionals reflect on influential papers rather than simply summarizing them adds valuable perspective. Atwood recommends it as an excellent primer on the field.
key_points: Douglas Engelbart demonstrated the mouse, hypertext, object addressing, dynamic file linking, and video collaboration in 1968 - innovations that took decades to become mainstream
Visionary HCI researchers are often ignored or dismissed as impractical in their own time
The book takes a meta approach by presenting current researchers' reflections on historic papers rather than direct summaries
The gap between HCI research breakthroughs and widespread adoption raises questions about which current researchers we're overlooking
money_quotes: All those trappings of modern computing that we take for granted today? Engelbart demonstrated them all two years before I was born.
Engelbart was so far ahead of his time in 1968 that his demonstration wasn't taken seriously – it seemed absurd and impractical.
It really makes you wonder which of today's HCI researchers we're ignoring but shouldn't be.
Reading this reminds me how far we've come, and yet how far we have to go.
posts.key_insight: The history of human-computer interaction reveals that visionary ideas are often dismissed as impractical, only to become the foundation of modern computing decades later.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines how modern netbooks fulfill Alan Kay's 1972 Dynabook vision of a portable, affordable personal computer for learning and creation. He compares the original specifications—under 4 pounds, $500, notebook-sized with quality display and storage—to 2008 netbooks and finds them remarkably aligned. However, Kay himself expresses disappointment in a Wired interview, arguing that browsers and limited computing represent a step backward from his original creative vision. Atwood agrees netbooks are revolutionary but critiques their mediocre battery life due to Intel's inefficient 945 chipset, despite the impressively efficient Atom CPU.
key_points: Kay's 1972 Dynabook concept predicted affordable, portable computing for creation and exploration—and it took 36 years to materialize as netbooks
Modern netbooks meet or exceed Kay's original specifications at lower inflation-adjusted prices, but Kay sees browser-based computing as disappointing passivity rather than creative empowerment
The Intel Atom CPU is remarkably power-efficient (1-2 watts under load), but Intel's legacy 945 chipset wastes power, limiting battery life to 2-2.5 hours
Atwood views netbooks as portable web browsers—inexpensive, ubiquitous portals to the internet—while acknowledging this may fall short of Kay's educational vision
money_quotes: It only took, what, thirty-six years?
I cringe every time I use a browser for many reasons.
The browser people had a chance to make a more integrated UI and functionality, but really did pretty much the opposite in almost all respects.
Intel did a fantastic job on the Atom CPU, but they completely phoned it in on the chipset.
I think netbooks will be as revolutionary as Kay originally predicted with his DynaBook concept.
Only 1 or 2 watts of the total ~25 watt idle power draw is attributable to the CPU, a tiny fraction of the overall power consumption.
posts.key_insight: The gap between technological capability and meaningful user empowerment shows that fulfilling a visionary's specifications doesn't necessarily fulfill their vision—Kay wanted tools for creation, not passive consumption.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that programming and writing are fundamentally the same craft, both requiring clarity and concision for human readers. He draws on Strunk and White's 'The Elements of Style' to show that the principles of good prose apply directly to code. While many programmers entered the field to work with logical machines, the cruel irony is that code must ultimately be readable by fallible humans. Atwood invokes Knuth's Literate Programming concept, which frames programmers as essayists concerned with exposition and style. The core message is that 'omit needless words' applies equally to sentences and to code.
key_points: Writing code that computers understand is hard, but writing code that humans understand is even harder
Programmers chose the field for its logic, yet must still master the human art of clear communication
Knuth's Literate Programming reframes programmers as essayists focused on exposition and style
Strunk and White's 'omit needless words' translates directly to eliminating unnecessary code
True programming success requires writing for both machines and fellow programmers
money_quotes: Writing programs that the computer can understand is challenging, to be sure. That's why so few people, in the big scheme of things, become competent programmers. But writing paragraphs and sentences that your fellow humans can understand – well, that's even more difficult.
You have to write programs that can be understood by both the computer and your fellow programmers.

Of all the cruel tricks in software engineering, this has to be the cruelest. Most of us entered this field because the machines are so much more logical than people. And yet, even when you're writing code explicitly intended for the machine, you're still writing. For other people.
The practitioner of literate programming can be regarded as an essayist, whose main concern is with exposition and excellence of style.

Vigorous writing is concise. A sentence should contain no unnecessary words, a paragraph no unnecessary sentences, for the same reason that a drawing should have no unnecessary lines and a machine no unnecessary parts.
Most of us spend our entire lives learning how to write effectively.
Coding, after all, is just writing. How hard can it be?
posts.key_insight: The cruelest trick of software engineering is that code written for logical machines must ultimately be readable by illogical humans, making programming an exercise in prose as much as logic.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood chronicles his long-standing addiction to graphics cards, tracing it back to the revolutionary 3D accelerators of 1996. He shares a remarkable usenet exchange with 3dfx co-founder Gary Tarolli, who explained the computational power of early GPUs. Atwood discusses his latest purchase, the NVIDIA GTX 280, and how it enables him to play Fallout 3 at maximum settings. The post includes practical hardware modding advice, like using a Dremel to improve GPU cooling efficiency. Throughout, Atwood celebrates the PC gaming culture of DIY hardware optimization while acknowledging his annual ritual of upgrading to the latest graphics technology.
key_points: The 1996-2001 era of 3D accelerators was a golden age where hardware creators directly engaged with enthusiasts on usenet newsgroups
Early GPUs like the 3dfx Voodoo had computational power equivalent to 40 Pentium-200 chips, and that relative gap between GPUs and CPUs persists today
Graphics card performance continues to roughly double yearly, making timing purchases around game release seasons strategic
Simple hardware mods like cutting the exhaust grille with a Dremel can reduce fan noise by 20%
The GTX 280 represents the sweet spot of performance and efficiency, being remarkably power-efficient at idle despite being a top-tier card
money_quotes: Hello, my name is Jeff Atwood, and I'm an addict. I'm addicted... to video cards.
So if you want to brag, you can say your graphics card has approximately the same compute power as 40 P5-200 chips.
There's nothing quite like having a little chat on usenet with the founder of the company who created the 3D accelerator you just bought. Like I said, it was a simpler time.
Every year since then I've made a regular pilgrimage to the temple of the GPU Gods, paying my tithe and bringing home whatever the latest, greatest, state-of-the art in 3D accelerators happens to be.
Pity the poor rubes who bought these cards at launch! Hey, wait a second – I've been one of those rubes for 10 years now.
That's what separates us from those knuckle-dragging Mac users: skill.
I do love this new video card, but I say that every year. I try not to grow too attached. I'm sure this video card will be replaced in a year with something even better.
posts.key_insight: The relentless advancement of GPU technology rewards patient buyers while punishing early adopters, yet the thrill of cutting-edge hardware makes the addiction worthwhile for PC gaming enthusiasts.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the concept of stop words in search engines—common words like 'the' and 'of' that are typically ignored during searches. He compares the surprisingly different stop word lists used by SQL Server, Oracle, and MySQL, then shares his 2004 experiment where he searched Google for 110,000 English words to measure their frequency. The post questions whether stop words remain relevant in modern computing, noting that Google has moved away from using them and now tries to determine when seemingly common words are actually meaningful to a query. Atwood suggests stop words may be a relic of early 90's computing limitations that powerful modern systems no longer need.
key_points: Stop words are common words that search engines traditionally ignore, but different databases have surprisingly inconsistent stop word lists with no clear rationale for their differences
In 2004, Atwood scraped Google for 110,000 English words to measure their frequency, finding 'the' had 522 million hits—now it returns 13.4 billion, showing 25x growth
Google appears to have abandoned traditional stop words, instead using algorithms to determine when common words are actually meaningful (e.g., 'the' in 'The Matrix')
Stop words can cause real problems when searching for things like the band 'The The' or phrases like 'to be or not to be'
Modern computing power may have made stop words obsolete—server farms can now handle the large result sets that stop words were designed to avoid
money_quotes: Stop words are words so common they are typically ignored for search purposes.
'The' is one of the most common words in the English language, so a naïve search for 'The The' rarely ends well.
You'd think a pure count of frequency, how often the word occurs, would be enough to make a common group of words 'stop words,' but apparently not everyone agrees.
I ended up with a MySQL database of 110,000 English words and their frequency in Google as of late summer 2004.
Indeed, I wonder if classic search stop words are relevant in modern computing; perhaps they're a relic of early 90's computing that we haven't quite left behind yet.
Sometimes words and phrases that might be considered stopwords or stop-phrases may actually be meaningful or important.
Apparently, at least to Google, stop word warnings are a thing of the past.
posts.key_insight: Stop words may be an outdated optimization from an era of limited computing power, and modern search engines are better served by contextually determining when common words are actually meaningful.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains NP-complete problems to working programmers, arguing that expert developers should recognize these intractable problems rather than waste time seeking perfect solutions. He uses the Travelling Salesman Problem as a canonical example, showing how brute-force approaches become computationally impossible even for moderate problem sizes. The post celebrates approximation algorithms and heuristics as practical solutions, noting that modern methods can solve TSP for millions of cities while staying within 2-3% of optimal. Atwood concludes that clever 'cheats' are often more valuable than searching for theoretically perfect but practically impossible solutions.
key_points: NP-complete problems are characterized by solution times that grow so quickly that even moderately large instances would take billions of years to solve optimally
Expert programmers should recognize NP-complete problems to avoid wasting time pursuing perfect solutions that have eluded generations of computer scientists
Modern approximation algorithms for problems like TSP can handle millions of inputs while staying within 2-3% of optimal solutions
Simple heuristics like First Fit Decreasing for bin packing guarantee solutions within 22% of optimal while being fast and easy to implement
The practical value of 'good enough' solutions often exceeds the theoretical value of optimal solutions that can't be computed
money_quotes: NP-complete problems are like hardcore pornography. Nobody can define what makes a problem NP-complete, exactly, but you'll know it when you see it.
What do expert programmers do when faced by an intractable problem? They cheat. And so should you!
Sometimes coming up with clever cheats can be more interesting than searching in vain for the perfect solution.
I can't find an efficient algorithm, but neither can all these famous people.
The time required to solve even moderately large versions of many of these problems easily reaches into the billions or trillions of years, using any amount of computing power available today.

An expert programmer should be able to recognize an NP-complete problem so that he or she does not unknowingly waste time trying to solve a problem which so far has eluded generations of computer scientists.
posts.key_insight: Recognizing when a problem is NP-complete and embracing clever approximations is more valuable than pursuing theoretical perfection that's computationally impossible.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that typing proficiency is the most fundamental skill for programmers, even more basic than coding knowledge itself. He cites Steve Yegge's observation that many professional programmers inexplicably never learn to type properly, calling it simply 'lazy.' Atwood contends that faster typing creates less friction between thinking and expressing ideas in code, reducing the chance of losing your train of thought. He shares his own typing test results (94 WPM) to establish credibility before making his case. The post emphasizes that this isn't about perfection but 'reasonable competency at a core programming discipline.'
key_points: Typing is the most fundamental skill for programmers - more basic than any computer science course
Many professional programmers still use hunt-and-peck typing, which is inexcusable laziness according to both Atwood and Yegge
Speed matters because it reduces the gap between having a thought and expressing it in code
A one-handed typist who does 70 WPM proves there are no valid excuses for poor typing skills
Touch typing isn't about perfection but reasonable competency at a core discipline
money_quotes: Like broken clocks, even pointy-haired managers are right once a day. Coding is just typing.
We are typists first, and programmers second.
I can't understand why professional programmers out there allow themselves to have a career without teaching themselves to type. It doesn't make any sense.
When you're a fast, efficient typist, you spend less time between thinking that thought and expressing it in code.
I have a friend, John, who can only use one of his hands. He types 70 wpm. He invented his own technique for it. He's not making excuses; he's typing circles around people who are making excuses.
Don't just type random gibberish as fast as you can on the screen, unless you're a Perl programmer.
There's precious little a programmer can do without touching the keyboard; it is the primary tool of our trade.
posts.key_insight: The gap between thinking and typing is where good ideas go to die, making typing proficiency the most underrated fundamental skill in programming.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the traditional distinction between bugs and feature requests is artificial and counterproductive. Using Visual Studio's four-year-old font handling issue as a case study, he demonstrates how this classification becomes a tool for developers to avoid work rather than serve users. From the user's perspective, anything preventing them from accomplishing their goal is equally problematic regardless of its technical classification. Atwood praises UserVoice for intentionally blurring this line and advocates for the industry to focus less on categorization battles and more on acting constructively on user feedback.
key_points: The bug vs. feature request distinction is arbitrary because from the user's perspective, both equally prevent them from accomplishing their goals
Developers weaponize the bug/feature request classification to avoid work by reclassifying inconvenient bugs as feature requests
Visual Studio has shipped with incorrect default font behavior since 2002, violating Microsoft's own top design guideline, yet remains unfixed after being reclassified as a feature request
Bug tracking systems fail us by forcing artificial categorization rather than focusing on user needs
Tools like UserVoice that blur the bug/feature distinction eliminate these organizational pathologies
money_quotes: There's no difference between a bug and a feature request from the user's perspective.
I think most bug tracking systems fail us because they make us ask the wrong questions.
Party like it's 1996, folks, because you'll get MS Sans Serif, and you'll like it.
Nudge things you don't want to do into that 'feature request' bucket, and proceed to ignore them forever.
Argue strongly and loudly enough that something reported as a 'bug' clearly isn't, and you may not have to do any work to fix it.
Stop dividing the world into Bugs and Feature Requests, and both of these project pathologies go away.

I wish we could, as an industry, spend less time fighting tooth and nail over definitions, painstakingly placing feedback in the 'bug' or 'feature request' buckets – and more time doing something constructive with our users' feedback.
posts.key_insight: The bug versus feature request classification often serves developers' avoidance of work rather than users' needs, and eliminating this distinction would force teams to actually address feedback rather than categorize it into oblivion.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines freelance coding marketplaces like Elance, oDesk, and RentACoder with skepticism about their practical value. He cites Dan Appleman's analysis showing these platforms are plagued by unrealistic projects, global wage competition, and high failure rates. While acknowledging his own bias as a professional programmer, Atwood finds the challenges of remote specification writing and project management make these sites impractical. He concludes that while these marketplaces shouldn't be dismissed entirely, professional developers should be able to demonstrate more value than anonymous low-bid contractors.
key_points: Freelance coding marketplaces create global wage competition where developers in low-cost regions can underbid significantly
Many posted projects are unrealistic (like eBay clones for $500), leading to frequent failures and rip-offs on both sides
Writing specifications is difficult enough with coworkers in the same room—doing it across thousands of miles with strangers compounds the challenge
These sites serve as a useful baseline for professional developers to demonstrate their superior value to employers
The author compares these platforms to dating sites—potentially useful, but a sign of trouble if someone can't find stable client relationships
money_quotes: As professional programmers, I think we're all naturally inclined to see these sort of low-bid contract sites as cannibalizing and cheapening our craft.

You'll be amazed at how little people in some parts of the world will bid. That's because a few dollars an hour can work well in a country where the average wage is a couple of hundred dollars a month.
What ends up happening in these cases is that usually somebody ends up getting ripped off (either the client or the consultant who underbid or fails to deliver).
Specification documents are hard enough to write when everyone involved is a coworker sitting in the same room.
They might be one way to find a client relationship, but I'd be highly suspicious of any professional developer who can't find a stable, long term relationship with a client eventually.
Surely you can demonstrate better value to your employer than the random, anonymous programmers on Elance, guru.com, RentACoder, or oDesk.
posts.key_insight: Low-bid freelance coding marketplaces are plagued by unrealistic expectations and communication challenges, but professional developers should view them as a baseline against which to demonstrate their superior value.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood confesses to being in perpetual email bankruptcy and argues that email is fundamentally broken as a communication medium. He explains that while each individual email seems trivial to send, the cumulative effect creates an insurmountable pile that overwhelms recipients. Drawing on Merlin Mann and Tantek Çelik's insights, he argues that individual humans simply don't scale to handle the volume of attention requests email enables. Atwood advocates for redirecting communication to public channels like blogs and forums, keeping necessary emails extremely short, and choosing the right communication tool for each situation.
key_points: Individual human beings don't scale - the requests for attention exceed anyone's capacity to respond by at least an order of magnitude
Email's low effort-to-send creates an asymmetric burden on recipients, like handing someone pebbles that accumulate into an immovable mountain
Email naturally encourages message bloat because people feel compelled to amortize the effort of writing by covering multiple topics
Public communication (blogs, forums, wikis) is superior because one response can help thousands instead of just one person
The right response to email overload is to avoid sending email whenever possible, out of respect for recipients
money_quotes: Email is such a funny thing. People hand you these single little messages that are no heavier than a river pebble. But it doesn't take long until you have acquired a pile of pebbles that's taller than you and heavier than you could ever hope to move.
The underlying problem is that individual human beings don't scale.
The net number of requests for my attention exceeds my ability to provide that attention by at least an order of magnitude.
Why answer one person's email directly when I could potentially answer a thousand different people's email with a single blog post?
We should avoid sending email out of a deep respect for our peers – so that they are free to communicate as effectively and as often as possible with us.
I've been desperately clawing my way out from under this mountain of pebbles.
posts.key_insight: Email fails because it allows low-cost sending but creates high-cost receiving, and individual humans simply cannot scale to meet the asymmetric demand on their attention.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores various metaphors for software development—from construction to farming to art—and argues that the gardening metaphor best captures how modern software, especially web applications, actually gets built. While Steve McConnell favors the 'construction' metaphor in Code Complete, Atwood contends that software is fundamentally 'soft' and alive, requiring continuous tending rather than one-time building. He emphasizes that the farming metaphor captures the commitment to craft, adaptation to changing conditions, and cyclical nature of software work. The rise of web development, with its frequent deployments and community content management, makes the gardening model especially apt.
key_points: Software development metaphors abound (science, art, process, driving, game, bazaar, construction, farming), but none perfectly captures the reality
The construction metaphor falls short because software is 'soft'—malleable and constantly evolving, unlike buildings
The gardening/farming metaphor captures essential truths: commitment to craft, adaptation to variables, seasonal rhythms, and sustainable growth
Web development particularly fits the gardening model with frequent deployments, organic feature growth, and the need to 'weed' user-generated content
The best software projects feel alive—constantly growing with regular releases and long-term commitment
money_quotes: I think we're leaving one metaphor on the table which more accurately reflects the way software is built in the real world: flail around randomly and pray you succeed by force of pure dumb luck.
Software is soft; buildings aren't.
All the best software projects I've worked were, for lack of a better word, alive.
The strength of the farming metaphor is the implied commitment to the craft.
Farmers do their best to control those variables, of course, but most of all they must adapt to whatever conditions they're dealt.
Sometimes software is working on its roots. Bolstering its infrastructure. It's growing underground where the public can't see it.
And when you don't weed your garden? The weeds threaten to choke out your crops. Eventually, your software garden looks neglected, and then abandoned.
posts.key_insight: The best metaphor for modern software development isn't construction but gardening—a craft requiring continuous tending, adaptation to changing conditions, and commitment to sustainable growth over time.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues against excessive logging practices in software development, sharing a cautionary tale from Stack Overflow where overzealous logging actually caused production deadlocks. The team spent days trying to diagnose the problem by adding more logging, which made things worse, until memory dump analysis revealed that logging itself was the culprit. Atwood outlines five major problems with logging: it adds code clutter, has performance costs, buries useful information that should be in the UI, creates noise that obscures real issues, and suffers from inconsistent severity definitions. Stack Overflow ultimately removed all general logging and relied exclusively on exception logging, a decision Atwood doesn't regret.
key_points: Excessive logging caused actual production deadlocks at Stack Overflow through a recursive locking bug between logging and database operations
Adding more logging to debug a logging problem is a trap that makes diagnosis harder, not easier
Valuable diagnostic information deserves to be surfaced in the application UI, not buried in log files
The more you log, the harder it becomes to find meaningful signals in the noise
Start with minimal logging (exception-only) and add more only when specific, verifiable needs arise
money_quotes: Why not log as much as you can whenever you can? Even if you're not planning to use it today, who knows, it might be useful for troubleshooting tomorrow. Heck, just log everything! What could it possibly hurt?
We spent days troubleshooting these deadlocks by... wait for it... adding more logging! Which naturally made the problem worse and even harder to figure out.
Visible logging code is clutter – like excessive comments, it actively obscures the code that's doing the real work in the application.
If it's worth saving to a logfile, it's worth showing in the user interface.
The more you log, the less you can find.
I am not anti-logging. I am anti-abusive-logging.
Resist the tendency to log everything. Start small and simple, logging only the most obvious and critical of errors.
We've since removed all logging from Stack Overflow, relying exclusively on exception logging. Honestly, I don't miss it at all.
posts.key_insight: Logging has a seductive appeal that leads developers to over-log, but excessive logging creates more problems than it solves—including obscuring real issues, adding code clutter, and in extreme cases, causing the very bugs you're trying to diagnose.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood chronicles his decision to finally adopt Blu-Ray technology for his home theater PC, noting that OEM drives have dropped to $99 and the hardware requirements for HD playback are now mainstream. He reflects on the frustrating lack of native OS support for HD playback in both OS X and Vista, while acknowledging Vista's controversial inclusion of copy protection mechanisms. The post draws parallels to his DVD adoption in 1998, observing the cyclical nature of technology adoption and how quickly impressive technology becomes pedestrian. He closes with a forward-looking question about how dated Blu-Ray might seem a decade later.
key_points: Blu-Ray drives have reached the $99 price point, making high-definition video playback viable for mainstream PCs
Neither OS X nor Vista natively support HD playback, requiring third-party software like PowerDVD or WinDVD
The full HD experience requires multiple components: HDMI output, H.264 acceleration, dual-core CPU, and a true 1080p display
Technology adoption follows predictable cycles - DVD seemed revolutionary in 1998 but is now pedestrian
Web-based video quality is approaching DVD levels through services like Vimeo
money_quotes: We're dangerously close to viable high definition video playback on typical, mainstream PCs.

It's a shame OS X and Vista don't natively support HD playback of any kind (although Vista does include some copy protection mechanisms specific to high-definition video playback, which was the source of great hue and outrage).
Funny how technology cycles repeat themselves.
I distinctly recall being an early adopter of DVDs back in 1998, almost exactly 10 years ago. The 720 x 480 resolution and Dolby Digital sound seemed so impressive back then.
DVD quality is pretty pedestrian by today's standards.
With that in mind, I wonder how quaint Blu-Ray will seem in 2018?
posts.key_insight: Technology adoption follows predictable cycles where today's impressive innovations become tomorrow's baseline expectations, making the timing of adoption a balance between capability and cost rather than absolute quality.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his fascination with the hacker zine 2600 and recommends 'The Best of 2600: A Hacker Odyssey' compilation as both historical archive and introduction to hacker culture. He argues that while much technical content is now obsolete, the social engineering techniques remain timeless and the best articles combine good storytelling with technical writing. Atwood also recommends the Wizzywig graphic novel series as a companion piece that captures the ambiance of early computing culture. The post is nostalgic yet forward-looking, suggesting the hacker odyssey continues today.
key_points: The best hacker writing combines storytelling skill with technical knowledge, making it timeless even when specific techniques become obsolete
Social engineering techniques from decades ago remain effective today because they exploit human nature rather than technical vulnerabilities
Early hacker culture was driven by curiosity, boredom, and daring rather than malice - contrary to stereotypes of antisocial teenagers
The sense of wonder from early computing - connecting to distant places via modem - still resonates today
Learning through alternative formats like graphic novels can effectively capture the spirit of an era
money_quotes: At its core, the best articles are just plain good storytelling combined with technical writing skills.
One of the true joys of the hacker world is the wealth of firsthand accounts that get shared throughout the community.
Whenever you got a bunch of bored, curious, and daring individuals together, it didn't really matter what the setting was.

It seemed incredible (and still does to me) that you could simply stick your telephone into an acoustic modem, type a few letters on a keyboard, and somehow be communicating with someone in an entirely different part of the country or even another part of the globe.
It's a little eerie how accurately he captured the ambiance of that era for me, all those fumbling, exploratory sessions with nascent online community.
It's fun to revisit the origins of my hacker odyssey, but I feel like we're nowhere near the end of it yet.
posts.key_insight: The enduring value of hacker culture lies not in specific technical exploits but in the combination of curiosity, storytelling, and community that drives people to explore and share their discoveries.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines PlentyOfFish.com as an inspiring example of extreme scalability, noting how Markus Frind built a top-30 US website serving 1.2 billion monthly page views on just five servers using ASP.NET. Despite the site's amateur appearance and minimal customer support, it became massively successful by being community-run and completely free. Atwood draws parallels to Stack Overflow, expressing hope that similar lean scaling is achievable. He acknowledges the challenge that free business models face: costs grow faster than revenue as databases expand. The post concludes with a romantic metaphor comparing programming to love letters, suggesting Stack Overflow is similarly built on passion.
key_points: PlentyOfFish serves 1.2 billion page views monthly with only 5 servers and one developer, proving massive scale doesn't require massive infrastructure
The site's terrible design didn't prevent success because it directly addressed a fundamental human need (dating/relationships)
Free business models have a hidden scaling problem: database maintenance costs grow 6x for every 2x growth in data
Community-run support can replace traditional customer service when the core value proposition is strong enough
The same Microsoft/ASP.NET stack can handle extreme traffic levels with proper architecture
money_quotes: Markus Frind built the Plenty of Fish Web site in 2003 as nothing more than an exercise to help teach himself a new programming language, ASP.NET.
The most sustainable and enduring business models either get you laid, or get you paid – and the more directly the better.
Your 'use case' should be, there's a 22 year old college student living in the dorms. How will this software get him laid?
The problem with free is that every time you double the size of your database the cost of maintaining the site grows 6 fold.
This is essentially what a program was, a love letter from the programmer to the hardware, full of the intimate details known only to partners in an affair.
He's accomplished all this on his own, using only five servers with the same Microsoft and ASP.NET stack we use.
posts.key_insight: Massive scale doesn't require massive resources—one developer with five servers can outperform teams with hundreds of machines if you're solving a real human need and letting the community do the heavy lifting.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood dissects Swoopo, a predatory auction site that combines elements of eBay, Woot, and slot machines to extract maximum money from users. The site charges 75 cents per bid while only raising prices by 15 cents, meaning users collectively pay far more than items are worth. Atwood explains how the site exploits the endowment effect—once users have invested in bidding, they irrationally continue rather than walk away. He argues Swoopo isn't really an auction site but a lottery or gambling operation, calling it 'pure, distilled evil in a business plan' that preys on human psychology while being almost certainly illegal in the US.
key_points: Swoopo's business model ensures the house always wins: a $229 iPod sold for $187.65 final price but generated $938.25 in bid fees, totaling $1,125.90
The 15-second auction extension on each bid prevents sniping but creates endless bidding wars that drain participants
The site exploits the endowment effect—a psychological phenomenon where people irrationally protect sunk costs rather than walking away
Swoopo offers increasingly predatory variants: penny auctions, cash auctions, and 'FreeBids' auctions that are essentially printing their own money
The company appears to drop-ship items after collecting payment, maintaining no inventory while reserving the right to substitute 'equivalent' items
money_quotes: So that $229 item ultimately sold for $1,125.90.
Even when you win, you can lose.
There's something else at work here, though, and it's almost an exploit of human nature itself.
So instead of doing the rational thing and walking away from a bad investment, you pour more money in, sending good money after bad.
It's pretty clear to me that Swoopo isn't an auction site.
The only way to win here is sheer dumb luck.
Or, of course, by not playing at all.
Swoopo is about as close to pure, distilled evil in a business plan as I've ever seen.
posts.key_insight: Swoopo represents a masterclass in exploiting human psychology for profit, weaponizing the endowment effect to turn a fake auction into a gambling operation where the house always wins.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his technical journey to achieve smooth high-definition Blu-Ray video playback on a home theater PC. After discovering that even modern dual-core CPUs struggle with some 1080p content, he finds that Media Player Classic Home Cinema's hardware-accelerated decoding dramatically reduces CPU usage from 75-80% down to 25-40%. The post provides detailed instructions for extracting video files from Blu-Ray discs and configuring Windows to use hardware acceleration through MPC-HC's codec filters. Atwood frames his DVD/Blu-Ray archiving as a convenience choice rather than piracy, preferring digital files over physical media management.
key_points: Hardware-accelerated video decoding on modern GPUs can reduce CPU usage from 75-80% to 25-40% for 1080p playback
Media Player Classic Home Cinema's filters can be registered system-wide to enable hardware acceleration in Windows Media Center
Even 'world's fastest' software decoders like CoreAVC can't compete with proper GPU hardware decoding
Digital file management is more convenient than handling physical media collections
Blu-Ray ripping is straightforward with the right tools - just find the largest .m2ts file in the BDMV/STREAM folder
money_quotes: It's easier to deal with a collection of digital files than it is to deal with a bunch of shiny, easily scratched plastic discs.
Using video hardware acceleration, we went from 75% CPU usage to 30% CPU usage.
As I get older, my desire to collect things is rapidly diminishing.
I already own more movies than I could possibly ever watch in one lifetime.
Once you register this magical codec, Windows Media Player (and thus, Windows Media Center) will use hardware accelerated high definition video playback.
That's a night and day difference between 'world's fastest' software and hardware accelerated H.264 decoding.
posts.key_insight: Hardware video decoding on modern GPUs isn't just marginally better than software decoding—it's transformatively more efficient, making the difference between stuttering playback and smooth performance on modest hardware.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood applies the 'uncanny valley' concept from robotics to user interface design, arguing that web applications which attempt to perfectly replicate desktop application conventions create user unease rather than familiarity. He explains how the uncanny valley phenomenon—where near-perfect simulations become disturbing rather than appealing—translates directly to software design. Using Zimbra's Outlook-like web interface as an example, he demonstrates how desktop conventions shoehorned into browsers violate user expectations. The post concludes that web applications should embrace their unique strengths rather than clone desktop paradigms.
key_points: The uncanny valley effect applies to UI design: applications that are almost-but-not-quite like their inspiration create unease
Web apps mimicking desktop conventions violate users' unwritten expectations of how web applications should behave
Simpler, more abstract representations allow users to project themselves into the experience (the 'Lake Empathy' corollary)
The more accurate a digital simulation becomes, the more visible its subtle imperfections become
Web applications should play to their unique strengths rather than attempting to clone desktop applications
money_quotes: When an android become too realistic and lifelike, suddenly people were repelled and disgusted.
If a character is very simple, more iconic than realistic, it's much easier for people to pour themselves into it – to view it not as a third party, but instead as a personal avatar.
The more accurate your digital representation of a person, the more visible the subtle imperfections become.
A web app that apes the conventions of a desktop application is attempting to cross the uncanny valley of user interface design.
When you build a 'desktop in the web browser'-style application, you're violating users' unwritten expectations of how a web application should look and behave.
One of the great strengths of web applications is that they aren't bound by the crusty old conventions of desktop applications.
If you end up anywhere near the uncanny valley of user interface, that sense of unease you feel is perfectly normal. You're clearly in the wrong place.
posts.key_insight: Web applications that try to perfectly imitate desktop applications fall into an uncanny valley where small imperfections become magnified, making them feel worse than either a native desktop app or a web-native design.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that given the high cost of programmer salaries versus the low cost of hardware, companies should default to solving performance problems by upgrading hardware rather than optimizing code. Using Stack Overflow as an example, he shows how $5,000 in servers delivered 50% better performance—less than one month of a programmer's salary. However, he acknowledges that hardware alone can't fix fundamentally bad algorithms, citing a classic example where a Trash-80 beat a DEC Alpha due to better code. He proposes a pragmatic approach: try hardware first, benchmark if needed, then optimize code only when necessary. The key insight is that premature optimization is dangerous and expensive, so companies should leverage the hardware-programmer cost imbalance strategically.
key_points: The cost of even modest programming teams vastly exceeds hardware costs, making hardware upgrades almost always the better first investment
Failing to provide programmers with good equipment is penny-wise and pound-foolish—a 5% productivity gain pays for itself within a year
No amount of fast hardware can compensate for poorly chosen algorithms or data structures
Code optimization is hard, dangerous, and tends to consume expensive developer time with diminishing returns
The recommended approach is iterative: throw hardware at the problem first, then benchmark and optimize only if still necessary
money_quotes: Hardware is cheap, and programmers are expensive. Whenever you're provided an opportunity to leverage that imbalance, it would be incredibly foolish not to.
The fastest hardware in the world can't save you from bad code.
Always try to spend your way out of a performance problem first by throwing faster hardware at it.
More computing sins are committed in the name of efficiency (without necessarily achieving it) than for any other single reason - including blind stupidity.
Optimizing your code is hard. And sometimes, dangerous. It is not something you undertake lightly.
If a one-time investment of $4,000 on each programmer makes them merely 5% more productive, you'll break even after the first year. Every year after that you've made a profit.
Everything is fast for small n. When n gets large, that's when things start to go sideways.
posts.key_insight: Before investing expensive programmer time in optimization, leverage the cost disparity between cheap hardware and expensive developers—but recognize that no hardware can fix fundamentally flawed algorithms.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood opts out of his usual elaborate geek gift guide due to the 2008 economic crisis, instead offering just two modest recommendations. He enthusiastically endorses the Planet Earth BBC series as the definitive high-definition viewing experience, praising its five-year production and stunning wildlife footage. His second recommendation is the Absolutely Mad DVD collection containing 50 years of Mad magazine issues, which he notes is refreshingly DRM-free. The post reflects a shift from consumerist enthusiasm to more restrained recommendations during uncertain economic times.
key_points: The 2008 economic crisis dampened enthusiasm for typical consumer gift guides
Planet Earth BBC series represents the pinnacle of high-definition video content, justifying the HD format
The Absolutely Mad DVD exemplifies good digital distribution: simple PDFs without DRM or custom software
Donald Knuth contributed to Mad magazine early in his career, writing about the Potrzebie measurement system
Quality content and DRM-free distribution matter more than quantity of gifts
money_quotes: I don't know if it's the global economic apocalypse, or what, but I'm having a hard time mustering the required level of enthusiasm for buying more stuff.
Filmed over five years, at a budget of 25 million dollars, and completely in high definition, the series is nothing less than revelatory.

I'm in awe of the footage they captured, imagining how many months they must have spent to filming get a single moment of an Amur Leopard stalking across the Siberian taiga, one of the last 30 in the world.
As far as I'm concerned, Planet Earth is the definitive high definition experience to date.
I should also mention that the Absolutely Mad DVD is delightfully free of DRM or crazy custom viewing software. Each issue is a simple, unencumbered PDF file in a folder on the disc.
Who knew it took this much work to be stupid?
And if not, well, at least you can use these two shiny discs to blind your enemies.
posts.key_insight: During times of economic uncertainty, the best gifts are those offering lasting value and respectful distribution rather than flashy consumption.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses the now-obsolete PC turbo button as a metaphor for the importance of speed in software development. He argues that just as you'd always want your hardware running at maximum speed, the same philosophy applies to software development workflows. Drawing on historical examples from the evolution of development environments—from batch processing mainframes to Turbo Pascal—he demonstrates how faster feedback loops dramatically increase programmer productivity. The post concludes by challenging readers to identify what's preventing them from working at maximum speed.
key_points: The turbo button metaphor illustrates that performance should always be maximized, not held in reserve
Fast iteration cycles are essential for developer productivity—from hardware to development tools to deployment practices
Historical progression from once-per-day batch compiles to instant feedback fundamentally changed what programmers could accomplish
Fred Brooks' research suggested interactive programming facilities at least double productivity in system programming
Speed improvements benefit both developers (faster build times, debugging) and users (responsive software)
money_quotes: The strange thing about a turbo button is that you'd pretty much always want it on; there's almost no situation where you'd want to keep some power in reserve for that extra 'oomph' required by, say, a particularly intensive Lotus 1-2-3 spreadsheet.
Then I got Turbo Pascal and life was forever changed. I could write, compile and debug applications virtually instantly and my need for speed has never looked back.
Still nothing was ever as instant as Turbo had been, despite the hardware being 100x faster.
As far as I'm concerned, everything on a PC should happen instantaneously. Or at least as close to instantaneous as the laws of physics will allow.
Simply doing everything faster, all other things being equal, will allow you to get more done.
Harr's data suggest that an interactive facility at least doubles productivity in system programming.
Never underestimate the power of pressing the software turbo button.
posts.key_insight: Developer productivity is fundamentally tied to the speed of feedback loops—faster tools and shorter iteration cycles compound into dramatically more effective software development.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts the emotional rollercoaster of receiving new Lenovo ThinkServers for Stack Overflow on Christmas Eve, only to discover they don't include drive mounting brackets. The servers require proprietary Lenovo drive trays that can only be purchased bundled with overpriced hard drives at $100 each, making his separately-purchased drives unusable. What began as an exciting geek Christmas quickly turned into frustration at vendor lock-in tactics. An update reveals the community helped him find compatible IBM drive rails on eBay for $25 each, partially salvaging the situation.
key_points: Lenovo ThinkServers ship without drive mounting brackets, forcing customers to buy overpriced bundled drives
The proprietary drive tray requirement represents a significant hidden cost and vendor lock-in tactic
Community knowledge (commenters finding compatible IBM rails) proved invaluable in working around the limitation
Even experienced engineers can be caught off guard by assumptions about what 'barebones' server hardware includes
The older IBM drive rails (model 42R4131) are cross-compatible with newer Lenovo servers, offering a cheaper alternative
money_quotes: Configuring servers on Christmas Eve = the best geek Christmas, ever!
It's amazing how quickly I went from best geek Christmas ever to worst geek Christmas ever.
All for want of a few lousy, stinkin' hard drive trays!
That's a heck of a premium to pay for a drive tray.
How sad to see perfectly good hardware go to waste.
Oh, and as an extra bonus 'up yours' to customers, they use Torx screws.
So the good news is I only have to buy $250 worth of drive rails, instead of $1000 worth. Or at least that's what I'm telling myself...
posts.key_insight: Vendor lock-in through proprietary accessories can turn an exciting hardware purchase into an expensive lesson, but community knowledge-sharing often provides workarounds.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the persistent 90% piracy rate that has plagued software from Bill Gates' 1976 Altair BASIC to the 2008 indie game World of Goo, arguing that piracy is an unavoidable reality regardless of price, quality, or DRM measures. He notes the particular tragedy of World of Goo's piracy rate given its low price, lack of copy protection, and creation by a small indie team. While acknowledging his own history as a young software pirate, Atwood argues that former pirates often become paying customers as they gain income. His conclusion is pragmatic: focus on making great products at fair prices rather than punitive DRM that only harms legitimate customers.
key_points: Software piracy rates have remained stuck at approximately 90% for over 30 years, from Altair BASIC in 1976 to World of Goo in 2008
DRM and copy protection measures harm paying customers more than they deter pirates, who will crack any protection system
Not every pirate represents a lost sale—many are people with no money who couldn't buy the software anyway, but may become paying customers later
The only effective anti-piracy strategies are making excellent products at fair prices, or adopting open source models where piracy becomes distribution
Even the most sympathetic circumstances (indie developers, low prices, no DRM, excellent quality) don't reduce piracy rates
money_quotes: 32 years later, and we've ended up back exactly where we started. That's not exactly a resounding affirmation of the human spirit, or anything.
Piracy is a natural state of affairs for users with lots of time and no money.
As programmers, we of all people should appreciate the message Bill Gates outlined in his original 1976 letter better than anyone else: buying software supports programmers.
The more DRM and anti-piracy devices you pile on, the more likely you are to harm and alienate your paying customers.
Every time DRM prevents legitimate playback, a pirate gets his wings.
Don't penalize the honest people to punish the incorrigible.
If you write software and charge money for it, your software will be pirated. Guaranteed.
posts.key_insight: Software piracy is an immutable fact of the industry that heavy-handed protection cannot solve—the only rational response is to focus on product quality and fair pricing while accepting that some percentage of users will never pay.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood endorses Joel Spolsky's response to a programmer considering leaving the industry, arguing that programming remains an excellent career with great pay and creative fulfillment.
