He observes that economic downturns serve a useful purpose by weeding out those who entered the field for the wrong reasons, leaving behind the passionate developers who truly love their work. Atwood contends that programmers who question whether they should stay in the field are probably answering their own question. He acknowledges this might sound harsh but argues it benefits everyone when uncommitted developers make room for those with genuine passion. He closes by admitting his own bias, noting he spent his Christmas vacation writing code for Stack Overflow.
key_points: Programming offers exceptional compensation and creative fulfillment compared to most careers, with six-figure salaries common for those with just bachelor's degrees
Economic downturns beneficially filter out 'careerist gold diggers' who entered programming for money rather than passion
Too many programmers 'had no business being paid to be a programmer' and actively harm the profession and their coworkers
If a programmer ever hints they might want to leave the field, they should be encouraged to do so
The best programmers have a lifelong passion that no economic conditions could shake
money_quotes: Programming: love it or leave it.
I mean this in the nicest possible way, but not everyone should be a programmer.
I've worked with far too many programmers who honestly had no business being paid to be a programmer.
One of the (very) few bright spots of the previous bubble was that it weeded out all the people who didn't truly love software development.
The only people left applying for programming jobs were the true freaks and geeks who, y'know, loved this stuff.
If a programmer ever hints, even in passing, that they might possibly want to exit the field – they probably should.
The best programmers I've known have all had a lifelong passion for what they do. There's no way a minor economic blip would ever convince them they should do anything else.
posts.key_insight: Programming should be reserved for those with genuine passion for the craft, and economic downturns usefully reveal who those people really are.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood poses a classic probability puzzle about determining the odds of a parent having both a boy and a girl, given that one child is known to be a girl. The post is intentionally brief, designed to provoke thought and discussion rather than provide an answer. Atwood warns readers not to cheat by searching or reading comments, hinting that the intuitive answer is likely wrong. The puzzle relates to Bayesian reasoning, as suggested by his link to a previous post about the 'Bayesian Conspiracy.'
key_points: The puzzle asks: if someone has two children and one is a girl, what are the odds they have a boy and a girl?
The correct answer is counterintuitive and likely to surprise most readers
The problem is related to Bayesian probability and conditional reasoning
Atwood deliberately withholds the solution to encourage independent thinking
money_quotes: What are the odds that person has a boy and a girl?
Consider your answer carefully, without doing a web search, or reading the comments to this post.
Don't cheat – but be prepared to explain your reasoning, because the solution might surprise you.
It's almost like some kind of conspiracy or something.
posts.key_insight: Intuitive probability reasoning often fails us, and problems that seem simple can reveal deep misunderstandings about conditional probability.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores a classic probability puzzle about determining the odds of a two-child family having a boy and a girl, given that one child is known to be a girl. He demonstrates why the intuitive answer of 50% is wrong and the correct answer is 66%, then connects this to the historical 'unfinished game' problem that Pascal and Fermat solved in their famous correspondence. Atwood shows how failing to enumerate all possibilities leads to errors, and validates the mathematical reasoning with a brute-force code simulation. The post illustrates how our intuitions about probability are often misleading and why rigorous enumeration of possibilities matters.
key_points: The correct probability is 2/3 (66%), not 50%, because we must use the additional information that one child is a girl to eliminate the BB possibility from four equally likely outcomes
Collapsing GB and BG into a single outcome is a fundamental error that even Blaise Pascal initially made
Early advancements in probability theory were driven by gambling problems, similar to how video technology was driven by pornography
Unfinished games create misleading probability calculations because the remaining possibilities are not equally likely
Brute-force simulation with code can validate counterintuitive mathematical results and help programmers recognize these patterns
money_quotes: While our intuitions regarding a priori possibilities are fairly good, we are easily misled when we try to use probability to quantify our knowledge.
If you made this mistake, you're in good company: so did Blaise Pascal.
It's amazing how many early advancements in math were based on gambling.
The unfinished games are not equally likely!
You don't need to be a mathematician to prove this. I'm just a crappy programmer, and even my crappy code can brute force the answer.
It was my hope in presenting this problem that you'll be able to recognize it too the next time you see it, even if the math behind it is not at all intuitive.
posts.key_insight: Our probability intuitions are fundamentally flawed, but programmers can use brute-force simulation to verify counterintuitive mathematical truths and train themselves to recognize these patterns in code.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that micromanagement creates 'zombies' - mindless employees who can only follow orders and lose the ability to make independent decisions. Using Kathy Sierra's diagnostic quiz and insights from Peopleware, he identifies micromanagement as the primary form of 'teamicide' that destroys team cohesion. The post emphasizes that the core issue is trust: managers who don't trust their teams signal this clearly through their actions, inhibiting team formation. Atwood concludes that if you can't demonstrate trust in your team members, you shouldn't be working with them at all.
key_points: Micromanagement transforms capable employees into dependent 'zombies' who can only function under constant supervision
Even small amounts of micromanagement indicate deeper organizational or leadership problems
True autonomy means the freedom to proceed differently from how your manager would - not just the freedom to be right
Defensive management practices like prescriptive methodologies signal distrust and destroy team cohesion
Trust must be actively demonstrated through actions, not just stated as a value
money_quotes: Micromanaging the people you work with is arguably the exact opposite of what a competent team leader or manager should be spending their time doing.
Who the heck wants to work with zombies anyway?
There is no message you can send that will better inhibit team formation.
The only freedom that has any meaning is the freedom to proceed differently from the way your manager would have proceeded.
The right to be right is irrelevant; it's only the right to be wrong that makes you free.
People who feel untrusted have little inclination to bond together into a cooperative team.
If you don't trust the people you work with – and most importantly, actively demonstrate that trust through your actions – should you really be working with them at all?
posts.key_insight: The freedom to make mistakes and proceed differently from your manager is the foundation of both individual autonomy and effective team formation.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the 2009 Twitter hack where an 18-year-old compromised multiple high-profile accounts, including Barack Obama's, by running an automated password guesser against Twitter's login system. The attack succeeded because Twitter allowed unlimited rapid-fire login attempts, and a support staff member had chosen 'happiness' as their password. Atwood argues that while brute force attacks remain impractical (taking centuries per user), dictionary attacks can try every word in the Oxford English Dictionary in under two days. He advocates for login throttling as a basic security measure—either through exponential delays or CAPTCHAs after failed attempts—and warns that fast modern hardware and broadband connections have made web applications newly vulnerable to attacks that were once mainly a desktop concern.
key_points: Twitter's failure to limit login attempts enabled an 18-year-old to compromise high-profile accounts through simple dictionary attacks
Brute force attacks remain impractical (8 billion attempts needed for a weak 8-character password), but dictionary attacks can exhaust 171,000 words in under two days
Login throttling through exponential delays or CAPTCHAs is basic security that prevents practical dictionary attacks
Modern fast hardware and broadband connections have created ideal conditions for web-based dictionary attacks that were once mainly a desktop threat
Even if you secure your own systems, you're only as safe as the weakest website where you've reused credentials
money_quotes: Cracking the site was easy, because Twitter allowed an unlimited number of rapid-fire log-in attempts.
Limiting the number of login attempts per user is security 101.
All it takes is one user with a weak password to provide attackers a toehold in your system.
At one attempt per second, we could try every word in the Oxford English Dictionary in slightly less than two days.
You better hope they're all smart enough to throttle failed logins – and that you're careful to use unique credentials on every single website you visit.
If you thought of dictionary attacks as mostly a desktop phenomenon, perhaps it's time to revisit that assumption.
posts.key_insight: Login throttling is such basic security that its absence at Twitter—combined with one employee's weak password—was enough to compromise the President-Elect's account, illustrating how fundamental security oversights create systemic vulnerabilities.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that meaningful success in technology and creative endeavors requires years of consistent effort, not overnight breakthroughs. Using examples from Gmail's seven-year journey to success, the Beatles' decade of practice before Sgt. Peppers, and his own blog's three years to gain readership, he demonstrates that the 'overnight success' narrative is both misleading and harmful. He advocates for finding intrinsic satisfaction in the process itself rather than fixating on outcomes, noting that Stack Overflow was built with the same long-term mindset. The key is deliberate practice combined with frugality to sustain the long journey.
key_points: Gmail took 7.5 years from inception to mainstream success, facing internal skepticism and predictions of doom even at launch
Peter Norvig's research shows expertise in any field requires about ten years of deliberate practice—there are no shortcuts, even for prodigies like Mozart
The 'overnight success' myth is harmful because it creates unrealistic expectations and causes people to give up prematurely
The process of building something meaningful should be inherently satisfying—success becomes almost irrelevant when you love the daily work
Atwood's own blog required three years of posting 3-5 times per week before achieving any real popularity in the software development community
money_quotes: This notion of overnight success is very misleading, and rather harmful. If you're starting something new, expect a long journey.
You must move very fast, otherwise you will never arrive, because it's a long journey!
The process is inherently satisfying. Waking up every day and doing something you love – even better, surrounded by a community who loves it too – is its own reward.
There appear to be no real shortcuts: even Mozart, who was a musical prodigy at age 4, took 13 more years before he began to produce world-class music.
I fully expect to be writing on this blog, in one form or another, for the rest of my life. It is a part of who I am.
Whether you ultimately achieve readers, or pageviews, or whatever high score table it is we're measuring this week, try to remember it's worth doing because, well – it's worth doing.
If you keep doing it long enough, who knows? You might very well wake up one day and find out you're an overnight success.
posts.key_insight: True success requires years of deliberate, consistent effort, but the journey becomes sustainable when you find the process itself inherently rewarding rather than fixating on outcomes.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses the Windows 7 Calculator redesign to illustrate a fundamental principle of software development: technical improvements that users can't see might as well not exist. He recounts how Microsoft completely rewrote Calculator's arithmetic engine in Vista with infinite precision math, yet received no credit because the UI remained unchanged. The post argues that visible polish in small utilities reflects the care put into an entire operating system. Atwood concludes that developers shouldn't bother improving products unless those improvements result in changes users can actually perceive and appreciate.
key_points: Microsoft rewrote Calculator's entire arithmetic engine for Vista with arbitrary-precision math, but nobody noticed because the UI stayed the same
The fit and finish of small bundled utilities like Notepad and Calculator reflects the perceived quality of the entire operating system
Raymond Chen's technically correct defense of invisible improvements ironically proves that being technically correct is irrelevant to user perception
Vista had substantial technical improvements over XP, but users shrugged and stuck with XP because improvements were invisible
Discoverability is paramount: if users can't see or find an improvement, it effectively doesn't exist
money_quotes: Don't bother improving your product unless it results in visible changes the user can see, find, and hopefully appreciate.
If the user can't find it, the function's not there.
Massive technical improvements. No visual improvement. And nobody noticed.
It's arguably the perfect Raymond Chen post – technically dead on, while simultaneously proving that being technically dead on is utterly irrelevant.
Isn't the fit and finish of little applets like these indicative of the care and design that goes into the entire operating system?
Vista had all the polish of a particularly dull rock.
posts.key_insight: Technical excellence that users cannot perceive is functionally equivalent to no improvement at all—visibility and discoverability are prerequisites for any change to matter.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood presents the CWE/SANS Top 25 Most Dangerous Programming Errors list, providing condensed explanations of each security vulnerability from buffer overflows to SQL injection. He argues that while none of these mistakes are new, their relative priorities have shifted as web applications dominate computing. The post serves as an awareness-raising exercise, comparing it to McConnell's list of classic development mistakes. Atwood admits that even knowing about these vulnerabilities doesn't guarantee avoiding them, confessing he's made some of these errors himself.
key_points: The Top 25 list covers vulnerabilities ranging from input validation failures to cryptographic weaknesses, with injection attacks and improper access controls featuring prominently
Web application security issues like XSS, CSRF, and SQL injection have risen in priority as web apps dominate mainstream computing
Awareness alone doesn't prevent mistakes - even security-conscious developers commit these errors
Many vulnerabilities stem from trusting user input or client-side security checks that attackers can bypass
Buffer overflows affect even 'safe' languages since interpreters, native code, and OS APIs are often written in C
money_quotes: Grow-your-own cryptography is a welcome sight to attackers. Cryptography is hard. If brilliant mathematicians and computer scientists worldwide can't get it right – and they're regularly obsoleting their own techniques – then neither can you.
Don't require your customers to secure your software for you! Try to be secure by default, out of the box.
Ignorance is the biggest software project killer of them all.
Hard-coding a secret account and password into your software is extremely convenient – for skilled reverse engineers.
Cross-site request forgery is like accepting a package from a stranger – except the attacker tricks a user into activating a HTTP request 'package' that goes to your site.
You may think that you're completely immune to buffer overflows because you write your code in higher-level languages instead of C. But what is your favorite 'safe' language's interpreter written in?
Obfuscating traffic using schemes like Base64 and URL encoding offers no protection.
posts.key_insight: Knowing about common security vulnerabilities is necessary but not sufficient - awareness must be paired with deliberate practice and humility about one's own fallibility.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the evolution from manual memory management in C to automatic garbage collection in modern languages, arguing that GC represents genuine progress. He addresses the anxiety some developers feel about resource disposal, noting the irony of code that obsessively closes, disposes, and nullifies objects. While acknowledging that explicit disposal can be an important optimization for scarce resources like database connections, he suggests most developers overthink it. The post uses humor to defuse the tension between trusting the garbage collector and wanting explicit control.
key_points: Garbage collection is genuine progress that saves programmer time and reduces hard-to-debug memory issues
Even with GC, some resources like database connections may need explicit immediate release rather than waiting for collection
The confusion between Close(), Dispose(), and setting to null reflects developer anxiety about resource management
Explicit disposal should be viewed as an optimization, not a default practice
Most developer anxiety about letting objects fall out of scope is unnecessary
money_quotes: I'd wager the majority of programmers alive today have never once worried about malloc(). I call this progress.
That is one hellaciously closed database connection. Why don't you take it out back and shoot it, while you're at it?
Most of the time, throwing memory and CPU at the problem is still cheaper than throwing programmer time at the problem.

Your average obsessive-compulsive developer sees that he's dealing with a semi-precious system resource, and immediately takes matters into his own hands, because he can do a better job than the garbage collector.
I view explicit disposal as more of an optimization than anything else.
The next time you feel anxious about letting objects fall out of scope, remember: you could always terminate them with extreme prejudice, if you feel it's necessary. But it probably isn't.
posts.key_insight: Trust your garbage collector—developer anxiety about explicit resource disposal is usually misplaced, and treating cleanup as an optimization rather than a requirement leads to simpler, more maintainable code.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that traditional browser font-only zoom is outdated and should be replaced by full page zoom as the default. He traces the history from Netscape Navigator 4.x when simple page layouts could handle arbitrary font scaling, to modern complex web applications where font-only zoom breaks designs beyond +1/-1 size changes. Using Digg as an example, he demonstrates how font scaling causes layout breakage while full page zoom preserves design integrity at any scale. He advocates for Firefox 3's approach of making full page zoom the default, noting it works on virtually every website without requiring designer changes.
key_points: Traditional font-only zoom worked in the early web era because layouts were simple, but breaks modern complex web designs
Web designers should test layouts at default, +1, and -1 font sizes as a minimum standard
Full page zoom (pioneered by Opera, popularized by Firefox 3) scales the entire page proportionally including images and layout
Full page zoom works universally without requiring any changes from web designers
Font-only zoom becomes a 'jumbled, unreadable mess' at larger scales while full page zoom remains usable
money_quotes: Pump it up until your eyes bleed; you're unlikely to break the design, because there's precious little design at all.
The traditional method of font-only browser sizing is a solution whose time has come and gone.
It is important to let the user control their browsing experience.
Full page zoom works on almost every web page in the world, with no changes whatsoever by the web designers.
Honestly, I can't see much use for traditional browser font sizing. It's increasingly fragile on today's web.
No real programmer would use that.
posts.key_insight: Full page zoom is superior to font-only zoom because it universally preserves web design integrity while still giving users control over their viewing experience.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts Alan Kay's surprising participation on Stack Overflow and shares Jeff Moser's field report from meeting Kay at a computing conference. The post explores Kay's ongoing work at Viewpoints Research Institute, his belief that the 'Computer Revolution Hasn't Happened Yet,' and his critique that we're using computers for distraction rather than becoming better thinkers. Atwood highlights Kay's observations about how programming languages spread through accident and convenience rather than merit, drawing a pointed parallel to PHP. The piece serves as both a tribute to Kay's contributions and a meditation on computing history's lessons for today's developers.
key_points: Alan Kay invented or co-invented object-oriented programming, GUIs, 3D graphics, and contributed to ARPANET's development
Kay believes computers should help us become better thinkers rather than serving as entertainment and distraction
Programming languages often spread through accident and convenience rather than intrinsic merit - Basic displaced the superior Joss simply because it was bundled with GE's timesharing system
Most groundbreaking computing innovations came from the ARPA/PARC era and progress has slowed since
Understanding computing history is essential for understanding the context of inventions and avoiding repeated mistakes
money_quotes: Computers can do so much more than we're using them for now (e.g. provide 'a teacher for every learner').
Our 'pop culture' is more concerned with 'air guitar' and 'Guitar Hero' rather than appreciating genuine beauty and expressiveness of real instruments.
It started spreading Basic around just because it was there, not because it had any intrinsic merits whatsoever.

If there's a big idea and you have deadlines and you have expedience and you have competitors, very likely what you'll do is take a low-pass filter on that idea and implement one part of it and miss what has to be done next.

The adoption of programming languages has very often been somewhat accidental, and the emphasis has very often been on how easy it is to implement the programming language rather than on its actual merits and features.
If you're using early-binding languages as most people do, rather than late-binding languages, then you really start getting locked in to stuff that you've already done.
posts.key_insight: The technologies that dominate our industry often win through historical accident and convenience rather than merit, and we repeatedly fail to realize the full potential of computing's big ideas.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the commonly observed overlap between programmers and musicians, drawing on insights from developers who are also musicians. He presents arguments about shared traits: both fields require zooming between technical details and big-picture thinking, both involve abstract interpretation, and both attract puzzle-solvers. However, Atwood remains skeptical, suggesting this comparison might be self-flattering mythology programmers tell themselves, similar to comparisons with painters or rock climbers. He concludes with a Frank Zappa lyric suggesting the real commonality is that end users don't actually care about technical skill—only results matter.
key_points: Musicians often become programmers (not vice versa) because programming pays the bills
Both disciplines require switching between intense focus on technical details and stepping back to see the whole picture
Music and code are both abstract—the written form requires interpretation and execution beyond what meets the eye
The musician-programmer comparison may be a self-flattering analogy programmers use to elevate their status
The real shared truth: end users don't care about technical virtuosity, only whether the product works for them
money_quotes: Musicians become programmers, generally not the other way around, simply because those gigs actually pay the bills.
Music is an abstract medium – the printed note requires interpretation and execution. Like the written line of code, there is often much more than meets the eye.
Instrumentalists have to zoom in to work with very repetitive technical details, and so become very focused... But, the best programmers are able to then zoom out and see the big picture.
I worry that this is just another convenient, self-fulfilling analogy we programmers use to puff ourselves up.
We're the programmers; programming is whatever we say it is.
They're pretty good musicians, but it don't make no difference if they're good musicians, because anybody who would buy this record doesn't give a f**k if there's good musicians.
Now that's the one thing programmers and musicians really have in common.
posts.key_insight: The romanticized parallels between programming and music may be self-flattering myths, but both fields share a humbling truth: technical excellence matters far less to users than whether the final product serves their needs.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood draws a parallel between grocery store self-checkout machines and open source software development, arguing that both succeed when they align self-interest with participation. He uses the self-checkout metaphor to explain why people willingly do unpaid work: they care most about their own outcomes. Atwood then critiques OpenOffice.org's declining developer community, attributing it to bureaucratic barriers that undermine the self-service nature of open source. He argues that open source projects fail when they create obstacles to contribution, destroying the intrinsic motivators of self-determination and autonomy. The post concludes that struggling open source projects should blame themselves, not the community, for failing to lower barriers to participation.
key_points: Self-checkout works because nobody cares about getting you checked out as much as you do - it's perfect alignment of self-interest
Open source software development is the ultimate self-service industry, where users can directly fix their own problems
OpenOffice.org's declining contributor base stems from 'demotivating and dysfunctional process steps' that prevent code from being included
Destroying self-determination and autonomy on an open source project makes it no better than closed source software
If programmers - who are inclined to understand and love this stuff - won't contribute, the project has fundamentally failed
money_quotes: Nobody else in that store cares about getting Jeff Atwood checked out nearly as much as Jeff Atwood does.
We programmers fundamentally grok the hoops that the self-service checkout machines make customers jump through.
Open source software development is the ultimate self-service industry.
You've created a self-service checkout machine so painful to use, so awkward to operate, that it gives the self-service concept a bad name.
Once you destroy the twin intrinsic motivators of self-determination and autonomy on an open source project, you're no better off than you were with traditional closed source software.
If you're having difficulty getting software developers to participate in your open source project, the community isn't failing your project. Your project is failing the community.
posts.key_insight: Open source projects thrive or die based on how well they enable self-service contribution - when bureaucracy makes participation painful, the project fails its community, not the other way around.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the distinction between programming and scripting languages, arguing that the immediate gratification of scripting is not just valid but perhaps superior for many use cases. He recounts his early computing experiences with BASIC, where a blinking cursor invited experimentation, contrasted with a miserable evening learning C that nearly drove him away from programming entirely. The post critiques Larry Wall's lengthy theorizing about scripting languages while Perl 6 remained vaporware for years—actions contrary to scripting's spirit of immediacy. Atwood embraces his identity as a 'scripter at heart,' noting that dynamic languages like Python and PHP are essential for the rapid iteration that modern web development demands.
key_points: The essence of scripting languages is immediate gratification—type something, see what happens, no compile cycle required
Languages differ not in what you can say but in what you must say, affecting conciseness in different dimensions
C's compile cycles and pointer management sucked the joy out of programming compared to BASIC's immediacy
Web applications require constant updates and iteration, making dynamic scripting languages more appropriate than traditional compiled languages
Pontificating about scripting language theory while delaying Perl 6 for years contradicts the very spirit of scripting
money_quotes: That's the ineffable, undeniable beauty of a scripting language. You don't need to read a giant Larry Wall article, or wait 8 years for Perl 6 to figure that out.
C'mon. Type something. See what happens.
How could C suck all the carefree joy out of my stupid little AmigaBASIC adventures?
I hadn't been programming at all. I had been scripting.
Unlike applications from the previous paradigm, web applications are not released in one to three year cycles. They are updated every day, sometimes every hour.
Rather than being finished paintings, they are sketches, continually being redrawn in response to new data.
I know, I know, I'll never be a real programmer. But I've come to terms with my limitations, because I'm a scripter at heart.
posts.key_insight: The distinction between 'real programming' and scripting is a false hierarchy—scripting's immediate feedback loop and rapid iteration are not just valid but essential for modern software development.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the concept of 'dogfooding' - using your own products - through the dramatic example of SawStop inventor Stephen Gass, who tested his table saw safety technology on his own finger. The post traces dogfooding's origins from Alpo commercials to Microsoft's internal adoption practices, then pivots to Gass's invention that can detect human tissue and stop a spinning blade in 1/100th of a second. Atwood argues that while dogfooding isn't always possible in software, developers should seek every opportunity to use their own products. The piece concludes with a pointed observation about Linux desktop developers who refuse to give up their terminals, suggesting this explains the state of Linux desktop software.
key_points: Dogfooding originated with Alpo dog food commercials and was popularized at Microsoft in 1988 when managers challenged teams to increase internal product usage
SawStop technology combines capacitance sensing to detect human tissue with a mechanism that stops blades in under 1/100th of a second
The inventor testing his own finger on a spinning blade represents the ultimate expression of product confidence
Even with safety technology, physics imposes limits - the saw still cuts about 1/16 inch per foot-per-second of movement
Linux desktop developers who won't give up terminals exemplify the failure to dogfood, which explains poor desktop Linux usability
money_quotes: Nothing exudes confidence like software developers willing to stick their own extremities into the spinning blades of software they've written.
This is a guy who really wanted to close the distance between him and his customers.
No matter how much I believed in my product, I think I would find it incredibly difficult to stick my finger in a spinning table saw blade.

It would be wonderful discipline for any software dev team serious about Linux 'on the desktop' to ban their own use of terminals. Of course, none of us have ever done this, and that explains a lot about the resulting products.
A table saw is an extremely dangerous tool... It should be obvious what would happen if a finger ever came in contact with the spinning blade.
This saw cuts you about 1/16 inch for every foot per second that you're moving.
posts.key_insight: The willingness to personally depend on your own product - to have real skin in the game - is the truest test of confidence in what you've built and the surest path to building something worth using.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the common programmer obsession with micro-optimizing string concatenation, using real benchmarks from Stack Overflow development to prove his point. While acknowledging that naive string concatenation in loops causes genuine performance problems (the 'Shlemiel the painter' problem), he demonstrates that outside of loops, the differences between various string-building techniques are negligible. His benchmarks show only 391 milliseconds difference across 100,000 iterations between the worst and best methods. The post argues that obsessing over such micro-optimizations distracts developers from what actually matters: writing maintainable, readable code.
key_points: String concatenation in loops is genuinely problematic due to quadratic performance, but outside loops the choice of technique barely matters
Benchmarks across five different string-building methods showed minimal real-world difference (587-979ms over 100,000 iterations)
Absolute rules like 'never use += concatenation' are red flags that indicate cargo-cult thinking rather than practical engineering
The general consensus is that concatenation only becomes a concern around 10+ strings, but even this varies by context
Micro-optimization obsession actively harms code quality by diverting attention from readability and maintainability
money_quotes: Every decent programmer knows that string concatenation, while fine in small doses, is deadly poison in loops.
It. Just. Doesn't. Matter!
The minute you begin worrying about tiny little optimizations, you've already gone down the wrong path.
Any time you see 'don't ever do X,' alarm bells should be going off.
You should be more worried about the maintainability and readability of your code than its performance.
That is perhaps the most tragic thing about letting yourself get sucked into micro-optimization theater – it distracts you from your real goal: writing better code.
posts.key_insight: Micro-optimization is seductive but ultimately theatrical—it makes programmers feel productive while distracting them from the harder, more valuable work of writing clear and maintainable code.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the death of his beloved Microsoft Natural Keyboard 4000, using it as a springboard to discuss what makes a good programming keyboard. He establishes three inviolable rules: never mangle the home key cluster, arrow keys, or function keys, as programmers depend heavily on these areas. Beyond these essentials, he acknowledges keyboard preferences are highly personal—ergonomic vs standard, clicky vs quiet, etc. He surveys popular options including the Microsoft Natural 4000, Das Keyboard, IBM Model M, and Apple's low-profile keyboard, ultimately arguing that a quality keyboard is one of the cheapest and best investments a programmer can make.
key_points: A quality keyboard is one of the best and cheapest career investments for programmers
Three keyboard areas are sacrosanct: the home key cluster, arrow key cluster, and function keys must never be non-standard
Programmers use function keys, home cluster, and arrow keys far more than average users, making standard layouts essential
Beyond the three rules, keyboard preference is highly personal (ergonomic, clicky, low-profile, etc.)
Keyboard switch technology was already mature in 1980—modern advances are mostly about wireless, aesthetics, and cost reduction
money_quotes: A quality keyboard is one of the best (and cheapest) investments you can make in your career.
These areas are sacrosanct for programmers.
Move those around and you might as well cut our fingers off while you're at it.
I don't know if this is God's way of punishing lapsed Visual Basic programmers, or what, but it's incredibly annoying.
Keyswitch technology was quite mature in 1980. Modern keyboard tech advances have mainly had to do with wireless interfaces, snazzy looks, and making cheap crud cheaper.
Give your keyboard the consideration it deserves; it is the one essential tool of our craft.
Pick whatever keyboard you like, as long as it's of reasonable quality, and you're comfortable typing on it for long periods.
posts.key_insight: Programmers should invest in quality keyboards that respect standard layouts for function keys, home cluster, and arrow keys—the areas we use most intensively.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the tension between wiki-style collaborative editing and traditional authorship on Stack Overflow. He examines competing research on Wikipedia contributions, noting that while insiders make most edits, outsiders contribute most actual content. Stack Overflow deliberately mixes both models: reputation and signatures provide authorship accountability, while wiki-style editing allows trusted users to improve content communally. Atwood describes implementing a diff-based algorithm to track authorship across revisions, ensuring that even heavily-edited posts properly attribute their original authors. The solution uses line-based comparison rather than character-level Levenshtein distance for performance reasons.
key_points: Jimmy Wales claimed 0.7% of Wikipedia users made 50% of edits, but Aaron Swartz's study measuring actual contributed letters showed the opposite: outsiders provide most content while insiders just make frequent small edits
Stack Overflow intentionally combines opposing paradigms: blog-style authorship with reputation systems alongside wiki-style communal editing
Knowing who wrote something fundamentally changes how we respond to it - authorship provides context about background, skills, and credibility
Implementing authorship tracking across revisions required abandoning character-level Levenshtein distance (too slow) for line-based diff algorithms
The system gives a 2x bonus multiplier to original authors and reports the highest percentage of authorship in the final revision
money_quotes: Insiders account for the vast majority of the edits. But it's the outsiders who provide nearly all of the content.
Authorship and wiki are fundamentally opposing goals.
Communal ownership is all well and good, but sometimes you still need to know Who Wrote This Crap.
How do you balance self-interest (vote for me) with selflessness (vote for this content)?
If you knew this question was from Turing Award winning computer scientist Alan Kay, would it change the way you reacted to it? Of course it would!
How can you possibly form a community when everyone is a random, anonymous contributor?
Wiki posts by Alan Kay look like they're from Alan Kay.
posts.key_insight: Successful community platforms must balance the wiki ideal of content over ego with the human need for authorship and accountability, because knowing who wrote something fundamentally shapes how we engage with it.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the debate over string concatenation performance in HTML generation misses the point entirely—the real problem is the fundamental approach. While templating engines are the conventional 'right' answer to replace string building, they quickly devolve into unreadable 'tag soup' when logic gets complex. Atwood proposes that instead of poking holes in HTML to insert code, we should treat HTML as code itself, allowing seamless mixing of markup and programming logic. He points to VB XML Literals as an existing example of this approach, suggesting the industry has been thinking about the code/markup divide incorrectly.
key_points: The performance debate about string concatenation is a red herring—the entire framing of the question is wrong
Traditional HTML templating starts clean but inevitably degrades into unreadable mixes of code and markup
The Pragmatic Programmer lesson applies: ask 'Does it have to be done this way?' before optimizing
The real solution is treating HTML as a first-class citizen in code, not as a separate thing requiring escape sequences
Languages like VB with XML Literals already demonstrate that code and markup can coexist naturally
money_quotes: The performance characteristics of building small string fragments isn't just a red herring – no, it's far, far worse. The entire question is wrong.
When faced with an impossible problem, identify the real constraints. Ask yourself: 'Does it have to be done this way? Does it have to be done at all?'
If your HTML templates can't be kept simple, they're not a heck of a lot better than the procedural string building code they're replacing.
Rather than poking holes in HTML to insert code, we should simply treat HTML as code.
Templating tends to break down because it forces you to treat code and markup as two different and fundamentally incompatible things.
We spend all our time awkwardly switching between markup-land and code-land using escape sequences. They're always fighting each other – and us.
We've all been doing it wrong.
posts.key_insight: The debate over how to build HTML strings efficiently is asking the wrong question—the real insight is that code and markup shouldn't be treated as fundamentally separate things requiring awkward bridging.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the conventional wisdom of 'don't reinvent the wheel' by arguing that reimplementing existing solutions can be a valuable learning exercise. He cites examples from James Hart, Charles Moore (creator of FORTH), and Bob Lee (Android core library lead) who all advocate for the benefits of rewriting code yourself. Moore's practice of rewriting subroutines across 18 different CPUs made him exceptionally skilled and produced smaller, faster code than standard solutions. Atwood concludes that the adage should encourage deep study of existing solutions rather than serve as a weapon against legitimate attempts to improve or learn.
key_points: Categorically refusing to solve problems with code once any library exists is too extreme an interpretation of 'don't reinvent the wheel'
Charles Moore's practice of writing his own subroutines across dozens of implementations made him highly skilled and produced superior results
The phrase 'don't reinvent the wheel' is often used to discourage competition or avoid learning new things rather than as genuine advice
Deep study of existing solutions should be the real takeaway from this principle, not prohibition against building alternatives
Most programmers who write standard subroutines have done so only once, limiting their expertise compared to those who practice repeatedly
money_quotes: Don't Reinvent The Wheel, Unless You Plan on Learning More About Wheels
Most of our current technology sucks, and even if it didn't, who am I to try and stop you?
After writing the same subroutine a dozen times on as many computers and languages, you'll be pretty good at it.
You can almost always replace 'Why reinvent the wheel?' with 'Please don't compete with me,' or 'Please don't make me learn something new.'
He never re-used his own code without re-examining it for possible improvements.
It's a bit dangerous if taken too literally – if you categorically deny all attempts to solve a problem with code once any existing library is in place.
More seeds, more blooms, I say. Don't build houses on kitchen sinks. Reinvent away.
posts.key_insight: The prohibition against reinventing the wheel should inspire deep study of existing solutions, not serve as a weapon against those who want to learn by building or legitimately improve on what exists.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the overwhelming dominance of Google in web search, using Stack Overflow's traffic data as evidence. Six months after launch, 83% of Stack Overflow's traffic came from search engines, with Google delivering 350 times more traffic than Yahoo, the second-place competitor. While acknowledging Google earned its position by 'nailing search,' Atwood expresses concern about the lack of competition and the implications of a single company controlling how people find information online. He draws parallels to the Microsoft antitrust concerns of the previous decade, questioning why Google escapes similar scrutiny despite its monopolistic position.
key_points: Google delivered 350x more traffic to Stack Overflow than Yahoo, making all other search engines effectively irrelevant
The search engine market has become even more lopsided over time - ratios that were 6-8x two years prior had grown to 350x
If every search engine except Google shut down tomorrow, most websites would see no meaningful change in traffic
The 'don't be evil' mantra and free products don't exempt Google from monopoly concerns that plagued Microsoft
Being removed from Google's index effectively means your website and business no longer exist
money_quotes: When we say 'web search' we mean one thing, and one thing only: Google.
Google delivers 350x the traffic to Stack Overflow that the next best so-called 'search engine' does. Three hundred and fifty times!
I find it profoundly disturbing that if every other search engine in the world shut down tomorrow, our website's traffic would be effectively unchanged. That's downright scary.
Where's the healthy competition? Where's the incentive for Google to improve? All I see is a large and growing monoculture that acts as the start page for the internet.
Calling the current state of search engine competition a horse race is an insult to horse races.
What we have here is a one horse race where all the other horses were shipped off to glue factories years ago.
Forget 'search conference,' you should be throwing a 'Google conference,' because there's no difference.
If Google, for whatever reason, decided to remove you from its search results, your website no longer exists.
posts.key_insight: Google's search dominance has reached a point where the entire concept of 'search engine competition' is a polite fiction, and every online business exists at Google's discretion.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to controversy over Joel Spolsky's criticism of Robert Martin's SOLID principles, arguing that while design principles have merit, there's a dangerous tendency to expand simple guidelines into elaborate rulesets. He uses the Star Trek Ferengi—whose 285 Rules of Acquisition govern every transaction—as a metaphor for programmers who believe sufficiently detailed rules can prescribe every situation. Atwood contends that skills, experience, and judgment matter more than any static ruleset, and that following rules blindly is for novices under supervision. The core message: study principles, but never let them substitute for critical thinking about your work.
key_points: Simple guidelines are useful, but expanding them into comprehensive rulesets creates dangerous complexity
Programmers are systematically disposed to believe they can 'program themselves' for success with sufficiently sophisticated rules
Following rules blindly is for novices; experienced engineers craft methods on a project-by-project basis
The Ferengi analogy illustrates how exhaustive rulesets inevitably get bent and reinterpreted to suit needs
Guidelines should be studied and respected, but never substitute for judgment and critical thinking
money_quotes: Expanding from a few guidelines on a paint can into a detailed painting manual is far riskier.
Pretty soon you'll find yourself believing that every possible situation in software development can be prescribed, if only you could come up with a sufficiently detailed set of rules!
At what point do you stop having a set of basic, reasonable programming guidelines – and start being a Ferengi programmer, an imperfect manifestation of the ruleset?
I value the skills, experience, and judgment of my team far more than any static set of rules.
Following is for novices who are under active supervision. Instead, I craft methods on a project by project basis.
I take responsibility for my choices. That's engineering for adults like us.
There's also a very real danger of hewing too slavishly to rulesets.
Rules, guidelines, and principles are gems of distilled experience that should be studied and respected. But they're never a substitute for thinking critically about your work.
posts.key_insight: Design principles are valuable distillations of experience, but believing you can prescribe every situation with sufficiently detailed rules is a dangerous illusion—critical thinking and judgment must always come first.

posts.summary: Atwood argues that programming rules, methodologies, and best practices face a fundamental paradox: the developers who most need them are the least likely to seek them out or follow them. He observes that the majority of programmers are 'unreachable' - they don't read blogs, books, or attend conferences, and can only be found through web searches for quick fixes. After surveying decades of software methodologies from structured programming to Agile, Atwood concludes that all of these elaborate systems can be distilled to four core principles: DRY, KISS, YAGNI, and writing actual code. He suggests that mentorship and invisible guidance from lead programmers may be more effective than formal methodologies.
key_points: The programmers who would benefit most from coding guidelines are the least likely to read or follow them - they are fundamentally 'unreachable'
Software methodologies proliferate partly because their proponents make a living consulting and teaching about them
Focusing on process over results is 'deadly' - inexperienced developers get bogged down trying to apply principles they don't understand
All programming methodologies essentially boil down to DRY, KISS, YAGNI, and actually writing code
Invisible guidance from experienced leads is more effective than explicitly mandating principles to junior developers
money_quotes: Throwing a book of rules at a terrible programmer just creates a terrible programmer with a bruise on their head where the book bounced off.
The defining characteristic of the vast majority of these so-called 'vocational' programmers is that they are unreachable.
So trying to follow them changes the focus from result to process. And that's deadly.
If it can't be explained on a single double-spaced sheet of paper, it's a waste of your time.
Remind me again – who, exactly, are we writing these principles, rules, guidelines, and methodologies for?
It's the job of the lead programmer or manager to see that good principles are followed, perhaps by guiding others invisibly, without explicitly mentioning those principles.
All those incredibly detailed rules, guidelines, methodologies, and principles? YAGNI.
posts.key_insight: The elaborate ecosystem of programming methodologies and best practices largely preaches to the converted, while the developers who need help most remain unreachable - suggesting we should focus on simple fundamentals and hands-on mentorship instead.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that true expertise is not about claiming authority or telling others what you know, but about asking the right questions and applying knowledge contextually. He confesses to having developed an 'anti-expert bias' after painful experiences with self-proclaimed experts, and points to Wikipedia's skepticism toward expert contributors as validation. Using Steve McQueen's firefighter character in The Towering Inferno as an exemplar, Atwood shows how real experts gather information through questions rather than dispensing proclamations. He endorses James Bach's framework for developing expertise through stages of overcoming obliviousness, intimidation, incoherence, and finally complacency. The conclusion: question everything, especially yourself, and build expertise through continuous learning rather than credential accumulation.
key_points: Self-proclaimed experts often produce worse results than humble amateurs because they stop learning and questioning
Wikipedia's anti-expert bias actually enables it to function by forcing all contributions to be evaluated on merit rather than credentials
True expertise manifests as asking the right questions and providing contextual direction, not declaring what you know
James Bach's expertise levels progress from overcoming obliviousness through intimidation and incoherence to finally overcoming mere competence
Building genuine expertise requires driving your own education, avoiding trivializing certifications, and relentlessly questioning everything including yourself
money_quotes: It troubles me greatly to hear that people see me as an expert or an authority, and not a fellow amateur.
Approaching software development as an expert, as someone who has already discovered everything there is to know about a given topic, is the one surest way to fail.
Experts are, if anything, more suspect than the amateurs, because they're less honest.
Being an expert isn't telling other people what you know. It's understanding what questions to ask, and flexibly applying your knowledge to the specific situation at hand.
Don't confuse experience with expertise.
Take nothing on faith. Own your methodology.
Drive your own education – no one else will.
So if you want to be an expert in practice rather than in name only, take a page from Steve McQueen's book. Don't be the guy telling everyone what to do. Be the guy asking all the questions.
posts.key_insight: True expertise is demonstrated not by proclaiming what you know, but by asking the right questions and maintaining the humility to keep learning.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood discusses Will Felps' research on how 'bad apples' in teams dramatically affect group performance. The study found that groups with a single negative member (a pessimist, jerk, or slacker) performed 30-40% worse than other groups, regardless of how talented other members were. Most troublingly, the bad apple's behavior was contagious—other team members began adopting similar negative traits. The only group that resisted this effect had a skilled leader who actively engaged everyone and diffused conflicts. Atwood concludes that while good leadership can mitigate the damage, the best solution is to remove bad apples entirely—even if you discover you're the problem.
key_points: A single bad apple causes teams to perform 30-40% worse, overriding the positive contributions of talented members
Bad apple behavior is contagious—team members start mimicking the negativity toward each other, not just in response to the bad apple
The worst team member is the best predictor of team performance, more than the best or average member
Skilled leadership can neutralize bad apple effects through active engagement, asking questions, and conflict diffusion
Self-awareness is critical: if you can't identify the bad apple, it might be you
money_quotes: Invariably, groups that had the bad apple would perform worse.
The worst team member is the best predictor of how any team performs.
It doesn't seem to matter how great the best member is, or what the average member of the group is like. It all comes down to what your worst team member is like.
Just how easy it is to poison any group.
If you can't tell who the bad apple is in your group, it might be you.
Other team members began to take on the bad apple's characteristics.
The obvious solution is to address the problem at its source: get rid of the bad apple. Even if it's you.
posts.key_insight: Team performance is determined not by your best people but by your worst—and negative behavior spreads like a virus unless actively counteracted by skilled leadership.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood draws a compelling parallel between physical storefront signs limiting students to prevent shoplifting and software rate limiting to prevent abuse. He explains that rate limits exist everywhere—from ATMs to email accounts to Xbox Live—as necessary defenses against bad actors. Using Stack Overflow as a case study, he describes how even well-designed systems can be defeated by determined humans willing to solve CAPTCHAs repeatedly, leading to additional user-based limits. The post categorizes three main approaches to rate limiting: per user/API key, per IP address, and per global action, each with distinct tradeoffs. Atwood argues that rate limiting is the first and most important defense against attacks, yet finds surprisingly little comprehensive documentation on the topic for developers.
key_points: Rate limiting exists everywhere in daily life, from ATMs ($300 daily max) to email accounts to convenience store student limits—addressing real problems that emerged historically
Determining 'how much is too much' is the art of rate limiting, requiring balance between preventing abuse and avoiding false positives
Even CAPTCHA-protected systems can be defeated by determined humans willing to solve them repeatedly, necessitating additional rate limits
Three classes of rate limiting exist: per user/API key, per IP address, and per global action—each with specific use cases and limitations
Without rate limiting, web applications face potential abuse at the speed of network bandwidth rather than physical human limitations
money_quotes: Determining how much is 'too much' – that's the art of rate limiting.

What if those 'students' were an infinite number of computer programs, capable of stealing items from your web store at a rate only limited by network bandwidth? Your store would be picked clean in a matter of minutes.
Not having any sort of rate limiting in your web application is an open invitation to abuse.
Even the most innocuous of user actions, if done rapidly enough and by enough users, could have potentially disastrous effects.
Rate limiting isn't always a precise science. But it's necessary, even with the false positives.
They are your first and most important line of defense against a broad spectrum of possible attacks.
Take a long, hard look your own website – how would it deal with a roving band of bored, morally ambiguous schoolkids?
posts.key_insight: Rate limiting is a fundamental and often overlooked defense mechanism that every web application needs, because online attackers can operate at speeds that make physical-world limitations irrelevant.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood advocates for a lightweight 'buddy system' approach to code review, arguing that informal peer review with a trusted colleague is highly effective yet far more practical than formal reviews or pair programming. He cites research showing code inspections catch 55-60% of defects compared to 25-45% for various forms of testing. The post challenges the institutional resistance to code reviews, noting that the best engineers actively seek out reviewers rather than viewing it as a sign of weakness. Atwood frames the coding buddy as a more appealing alternative to pair programming—you get the benefits of another set of eyes without being 'shackled to the same computer.'
key_points: A brief, informal explanation of your code to a fellow programmer is often all it takes to dramatically improve code quality
Code inspections have a 55-60% defect detection rate, significantly higher than unit testing (25%), function testing (35%), or integration testing (45%)
The best software engineers actively seek out reviewers—it's part of what makes them the best
The 'buddy system' offers the benefits of pair programming without the overhead or awkwardness of sharing a computer full-time
If you can't find a single programmer at your company worth adopting the buddy system with, you might need to change companies
money_quotes: I am continually amazed how much better my code becomes after I've had a peer look at it.
Just one brief attempt at explaining and showing my code to a fellow programmer – that's usually all it takes.
The best software engineers I have known actively sought out reviewers.
Anyone who needs his code reviewed shouldn't be getting paid as a software developer.
The average effectiveness of design and code inspections are 55 and 60 percent.
WTFs/minute is a perfectly acceptable unit of measurement to use with your coding buddy.
I think the buddy system is a far more practical way to achieve the same results.
One of the great joys of programming is not having to do it alone.
posts.key_insight: You don't need formal processes or pair programming to get the benefits of code review—just find one trusted colleague and make showing them your code a habit before you commit.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood discusses technical debt using Ward Cunningham's financial metaphor, drawing from his experience building Stack Overflow. After six months of development, the team had to pause new features to refactor their database, illustrating how even talented teams accumulate debt that must eventually be addressed. He argues that while technical debt is unavoidable, teams must budget time to pay it down periodically. Beyond the practical costs, Atwood emphasizes the psychological toll—accumulated small annoyances drain enjoyment from projects. He encourages developers to overcome their fear of breaking working code, framing refactoring as liberating rather than risky.
key_points: Technical debt is unavoidable on real software projects regardless of team talent—you cannot perfectly predict how early decisions will play out
Companies can reach a tipping point where they spend more maintaining legacy systems than adding new value
Technical debt payments don't produce visible customer features, making them politically difficult to justify
Accumulated small annoyances from technical debt become a major disincentive to work on a project, draining developer enjoyment
Refactoring should be viewed as liberating and energizing rather than scary—being willing to break things is essential
money_quotes: Like a financial debt, the technical debt incurs interest payments, which come in the form of the extra effort that we have to do in future development because of the quick and dirty design choice.
We have to stop working on new features for a while and pay down some of our technical debt.
If the debt grows large enough, eventually the company will spend more on servicing its debt than it invests in increasing the value of its other assets.
It's exactly these small annoyances, this sand grinding away in the gears of your workday, that eventually causes you to stop enjoying the project.
It can be scary to go in and rebuild a lot of working code that has become crufty over time.
It's liberating, even energizing to tear down code in order to build it up stronger and better than it was before.
He'd prefer we work on some crazy thing he calls revenue generation, whatever that is.
posts.key_insight: Technical debt isn't just a productivity drain—it's a morale killer that grinds away at developers' enjoyment until paying it down becomes essential for both the codebase and the team's wellbeing.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores file compression performance on multi-core servers, comparing 7zip's native algorithm against bzip2 for compressing large Stack Overflow backup files. He finds that while 7zip achieves better compression ratios, it only uses 2 CPU cores, whereas bzip2 can parallelize across all 8 cores for dramatically faster compression. However, bzip2's compression ratio barely improves beyond 'normal' settings, making the speed advantage less useful for maximum compression. The post highlights the gap between available hardware parallelism and software that can exploit it, leaving Atwood wishing 7zip would be rewritten to use more cores.
key_points: 7zip achieves superior compression ratios but only utilizes 2 CPU cores even on 8-core servers
Bzip2 can parallelize across multiple cores (up to 16), compressing 5GB in 2 minutes vs 7zip's 34 minutes at normal settings
Compression settings beyond 'normal' show diminishing returns - massive time increases for tiny size improvements
The optimal compression choice depends on a formula balancing compression time, file size, network speed, and how many times the file will be transferred
Multi-core hardware was outpacing software's ability to leverage it, even for embarrassingly parallelizable tasks like compression
money_quotes: I'm not a big fan of more than dual core on the desktop, but it's a no brainer for servers. The more CPU cores the merrier!
There's a reason most compression programs default to certain settings as 'normal.'

Above these settings, results tend to fall off a cliff; beyond that sweet spot, you tend to get absurdly tiny increases in compression ratio in exchange for huge order of magnitude increases in compression time.
Bzip2 uses more than 2 CPU cores to parallelize its work.
The ability to compress a 5 GB source file to a fifth of its size in two minutes flat is pretty darn impressive.
I can't help wondering how fast the 7zip algorithm would be if it was rewritten and parallelized to take advantage of more than 2 CPU cores, too.
posts.key_insight: Multi-core processors were becoming ubiquitous in 2009, but software like 7zip hadn't yet been rewritten to exploit that parallelism, leaving significant performance gains on the table for CPU-intensive tasks like compression.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores jumbo frames as a way to boost local network throughput for users transferring large media files over gigabit ethernet. He explains that the original 1,518-byte ethernet frame size was designed for an era of high error rates and low speeds, making it inefficient for modern gigabit networks. After enabling jumbo frames on his home network, he achieved a 20% throughput improvement in benchmarks. However, he cautions that jumbo frames require end-to-end support from all network devices, and incompatible equipment will simply drop oversized packets. The post serves as both a practical guide and a warning about the pitfalls of deviating from default network settings.
key_points: The original 1,518-byte ethernet MTU was designed for high-error, low-speed networks and is inefficient for modern gigabit transfers
Enabling jumbo frames reduced CPU utilization and increased throughput by approximately 20% in real-world testing
Jumbo frames require consistent support across all network devices - switches, routers, and NICs must all agree on frame size
Network equipment that doesn't support jumbo frames will silently drop packets, potentially causing mysterious failures
Large packets can negatively impact latency for real-time applications like gaming and VoIP
money_quotes: If the frame size is increased, the same amount of data can be transferred with less effort. This reduces CPU utilization (mostly due to interrupt reduction) and increases throughput by allowing the system to concentrate on the data in the frames, instead of the frames around the data.
There's a reason jumbo frames are never enabled by default: some networking equipment can't deal with the non-standard frame sizes.
Like all deviations from default settings, it is absolutely possible to make your networking worse by enabling jumbo frames, so proceed with caution.
Switches that don't support jumbo frames will drop jumbo frames.
If the size of the data payload can't be negotiated due to firewalls, the data will be dropped with no warning, or 'blackholed.'
Bigger isn't always better.
posts.key_insight: Jumbo frames can provide meaningful throughput gains for large file transfers, but the requirement for end-to-end device support and the silent failure modes make them a tweak that requires careful consideration.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the bikeshed effect—the tendency for discussions to expand on simple topics while complex technical issues receive less debate—and connects it to procrastination psychology. He cites research showing that abstract thinking leads to procrastination while concrete, action-oriented thinking promotes execution. Drawing from his experience with Stack Overflow, he observes that soft, broad questions attract disproportionate attention compared to narrow technical ones. Atwood argues this dynamic is a form of procrastination that even skilled developers fall prey to, and advocates for quickly moving past abstract discussions to actually building things.
key_points: The bikeshed effect states that discussion time is inversely proportional to topic complexity—anyone can debate paint colors, but few can argue about reactor design
Abstract thinking promotes procrastination while concrete thinking about nuts and bolts encourages immediate action
Open source projects are especially vulnerable to attracting people more interested in discussion than writing code
Stack Overflow experienced this phenomenon with soft questions getting more engagement than technical coding questions
The value of a software feature is inversely proportional to the amount of discussion it generates
money_quotes: Consensus is hardest to achieve in technical questions that are simple to understand and easy to have an opinion about.
Who are we kidding? It's fun to discuss what color the bikeshed should be painted.
Those who thought about the questions abstractly were much more likely to procrastinate – and in fact some never got around to the assignment at all.
I find that the amount of discussion on a software feature is inversely proportional to its value.
The sooner you can get away from airy abstractions, and down to the nuts and bolts of building the damn thing, the better off you – and your project – will be.
What's the hardest thing you have to do every day? Deciding what to ignore, so you can stop procrastinating and get stuff done.
The next time you feel yourself getting drawn into a protracted bikeshed discussion, consider: shouldn't you be building something instead?
posts.key_insight: Endless debates about simple topics aren't just inefficient communication—they're a disguised form of procrastination that keeps teams from building what actually matters.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines whether HTML validation is worth the effort, noting that nearly all major websites fail W3C validation yet function perfectly well. After validating Stack Overflow as HTML 4.01 strict, he found the process caught some genuine errors but also required pointless changes and arbitrary rule compliance. He dismisses XHTML validation as masochistic complexity without benefit. His conclusion is characteristically pragmatic: understand validation and what valid HTML means, but don't let perfect validation distract from building useful features and running your website.
key_points: Nearly every major website fails HTML validation, including Google, Microsoft, and Stack Overflow itself, yet they all work fine
XHTML offers no compelling advantage over HTML while adding complexity and uncertainty
Some validation rules are actively harmful, like forbidding the target attribute and requiring JavaScript workarounds
Validation has value as a strict lint-like tool that catches genuine errors and teaches you the official rules
Valid HTML matters far less than delivering features and running your website effectively
money_quotes: The web is, to put it charitably, a rather forgiving place.
The whole HTML validation exercise is questionable, but validating as XHTML is flat-out masochism. Only recommended for those that enjoy pain. Or programmers. I can't always tell the difference.
XHTML offers no compelling advantage – to me – over HTML, but even if it did it would also offer increased complexity and uncertainty that make it unappealing to me.

I couldn't help feeling that validating as HTML 4.01 strict, at least in our case, was a giant exercise in to-may-to versus to-mah-to, punctuated by aggravating changes that we were forced to make for no practical benefit.
Learning the rules of the validator, even if you don't agree with them, teaches you what the official definition of 'valid' is.
Nobody cares if your HTML is valid. Except you. If you want to.
Take my advice and do as you please.
posts.key_insight: Understanding validation rules has educational value, but pragmatic developers should prioritize shipping useful features over achieving perfect standards compliance that no user will ever notice.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood frames computer performance optimization as a shell game where bottlenecks constantly shift between four core resources: CPU, disk, network, and memory. He argues that effective performance monitoring requires visibility into all four areas simultaneously, not just CPU and memory. The post reviews Windows Vista's Reliability and Performance Monitor as a superior tool that finally provides this comprehensive view. Atwood explains how eliminating one bottleneck simply shifts the constraint elsewhere, making continuous monitoring essential. He details each section of the tool and how it helps identify which resource is currently impeding performance.
key_points: Computer performance bottlenecks exist across four resources: CPU, disk, network, and memory, and fixing one often shifts the bottleneck to another
Traditional tools like Task Manager only show CPU and memory, missing half the performance picture
The art of performance monitoring is getting your computer to tell you where the current constraint is
Moving averages for CPU usage are more useful than constantly shifting instantaneous readings
Real-time disk I/O visibility (process, filename, response time) is crucial for diagnosing performance issues
money_quotes: The computer performance shell game, also known as 'find the bottleneck,' is always played between these four resources: CPU, Disk, Network, Memory.
At any given moment, your computer is waiting for some operation to complete on one of these resources.
If you're interested in performance, the absolute first thing you have to do is determine which of these bottlenecks is currently impeding performance – and eliminate it.
At which point the bottleneck often shifts to some other part of the system, far too rapidly for your eye to see.
The art of performance monitoring is, first and foremost, getting your computer to tell you what's going on in each of these areas.
They only showed CPU load and memory usage. Those are frequently performance bottlenecks, to be sure, but they're only part of the story.
The computer performance shell game is nothing new; it is as old as computing itself.
posts.key_insight: Performance optimization is a continuous game of whack-a-mole where eliminating one bottleneck simply reveals the next, making comprehensive visibility across CPU, disk, network, and memory essential for effective diagnosis.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the concept of 'sharpening the saw' - the practice of improving as a programmer through activities beyond just writing code. He argues that while doing the work is essential, being too heads-down to reflect, discuss, or study actually impedes progress. The post recommends reading programming blogs with an open mind, looking for even one valuable insight rather than dismissing content that challenges your views. Atwood highlights Hacker News and programming reddit as valuable link aggregation sites for discovering quality content, while cautioning that excessive saw-sharpening can become procrastination.
key_points: The best way to improve is practice, but pure heads-down coding without reflection or study prevents real progress
Reading blogs effectively means looking for just one valuable insight rather than dismissing everything that challenges your worldview
Hacker News and programming reddit serve as excellent curated sources for programming content worth reading
A developer completely disinterested in self-improvement is a major red flag
Obsession with improvement can be healthy - it reveals natural motivation and drive
money_quotes: I am too busy sawing to take time to sharpen my saw.
You have to strike a mindful balance between practicing your craft and thinking about how you practice your craft.
We win if we can find just one thing in there that makes us better programmers.
It's a huge win if we read one hundred posts and learn one new valuable thing.
A developer who seems completely disinterested in it at all is a huge red flag.
People are often successful not despite their dysfunctions but because of them. Obsessions are one of the greatest telltale signs of success.
posts.key_insight: Balance doing the work with thinking about how you do the work - even finding one valuable insight from a hundred blog posts is a huge win for your growth as a programmer.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses Google Chrome's playful error messages as an example of exceptional software design that respects users. He argues that Chrome represents the first true advance in web browser technology since IE 4.0, with its attention to detail evident even in how it handles failures. The frozen tab with snowflakes and the 'Aw, Snap!' crash page demonstrate that developers took extra time to humanize frustrating moments. Atwood contends that error handling reveals how much developers respect their users and their own work, advocating that error messages should be both informative and enjoyable.
key_points: Chrome's error messages (frozen tabs with snowflakes, 'Aw, Snap!' crashes) demonstrate exceptional attention to user experience detail
Thoughtful error handling disarms user frustration rather than compounding it with cryptic codes
The quality of error messages reflects the overall respect developers have for users and their own project
Being fun and being informative are not mutually exclusive - both are achievable together
Stack Overflow applied this same philosophy to their CAPTCHA and error pages to ease user tension
money_quotes: Chrome is easily the best web browser I've ever used
Rather than being annoyed with Gmail, and blaming Chrome, I am completely disarmed by this error. It makes me laugh!
The way you handle errors speaks volumes about how much you respect your users, and ultimately, your own project
Remember, this stuff is supposed to be fun! Why not share some of that joy, that fun you had building your application, with your users?
Your error messages should always be informative and helpful. That's not optional. But as Google Chrome shows us, it is possible to do that while also being fun. And that's even better.
It's a major drag for your users to end up on a human verification page, or a big fat honking server error. So why not ease the tension a bit?
