posts.key_insight: How you handle errors reveals how much you respect your users—and great software finds ways to be both helpful and delightful even when things go wrong.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces the birth of his son Henry Burton Atwood, framing the experience through programming metaphors. He credits his partner Betsy for doing the 'real work' and provides a vivid, humorous description of the pain scale during childbirth. The post celebrates the arrival with geeky touches, including a C++ book photo op and creating a Twitter account for Henry with the message 'Hello World.' Atwood concludes by noting he'll approach parenting the same way he approaches programming—with no idea what he's doing.
key_points: The birth announcement is framed as 'spawning a new process' with the baby assigned a process id
Atwood provides a darkly humorous description of childbirth pain that goes well beyond a 10/10 scale
The post references the Mac vs PC ad featuring a C++ GUI Programming book as a joke gift
Henry's first tweet was 'Hello World'—the classic first program output
Atwood admits he approaches both parenting and programming with no idea what he's doing
money_quotes: All credit for creation of this new process of course goes to my partner Betsy who did the real work.
Now imagine taking that pain, packing it in to a rickety Oldsmobile, setting it on fire, then driving it off the edge of a cliff at maximum speed. Into a valley filled with white-hot molten lava.
It's a little bit worse than that.
It's all ponies and butterflies. Until the ponies and butterflies start spontaneously screaming.
Henry, who is genetically engineered to be awesome.
I will approach our new parenting adventure the same way I do programming – with absolutely no freaking idea what I'm doing.
posts.key_insight: Life's most profound experiences—like becoming a parent—can be approached with the same humility and humor as learning to code: acknowledging you have no idea what you're doing, but diving in anyway.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that online community participation is essentially playing the world's largest MMORPG, and that community websites should be designed with game mechanics to maximize engagement and fun. He uses the example of Mario Brothers to contrast how games can be simultaneously complex and easily learnable, unlike traditional applications designed merely for usability. Drawing on presentations about game mechanics in web design, he points to successful examples like YouTube, Yelp, and Flickr that incorporate points, leaderboards, and level-ups. The core message is that community sites are already de-facto games, and designers should explicitly embrace game-like elements rather than ignoring them.
key_points: Online community participation (forums, wikis, IRC, comments) constitutes the world's largest MMORPG whether we recognize it or not
Games can be simultaneously complex and easily learnable, something traditional applications fail to achieve because they design for usability but not fun
Successful community sites like YouTube and Flickr already use game mechanics: points, leaderboards, level-ups, and social exchanges
Not designing game-like aspects into community websites is the bigger long-term mistake
Community sites get better the more users actively participate, making them naturally suited to game mechanics
money_quotes: Like it or not, you're participating in the world's largest MMORPG.
Lurking is always free.
If you want to cultivate a community of participants instead of passive, zombie-like TV viewers who contribute nothing, you should be designing to maximize this fun.
Recasting the experience as a game means it can be simultaneously complex and easily learnable.
Traditional applications are designed to be usable but not necessarily fun. They've failed to design for fun.
You should shape and define your community by explicitly acknowledging and embracing the game-like aspects you want to encourage, rather than pretending they don't exist.
The first step in breaking our addiction to the world's largest MMORPG is to admit that we have a problem.
posts.key_insight: Community websites are already functioning as games, and failing to design for fun and engagement through explicit game mechanics is a critical oversight that leads to passive rather than participatory users.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues against the use of puzzle questions in programming interviews, using an absurdly convoluted meta-puzzle to satirize the practice. He proposes an alternative: having candidates give short technical presentations to the team, which better evaluates communication skills. Drawing from his experience with nurses during his child's birth, he illustrates how communication ability separates good professionals from great ones. The core argument is that once programmers pass basic competency, their ability to communicate effectively matters more than solving abstract puzzles.
key_points: Puzzle questions in interviews test memorization and abstract thinking but miss what actually matters for job success
The ability to communicate effectively is what separates good programmers from great ones
A 10-minute technical presentation reveals passion, communication skills, expertise, and cultural fit better than puzzles
Poor communication makes even competent professionals seem like rigid proceduralists who don't care
Hiring processes should optimize for real-world collaboration, not abstract problem-solving ability
money_quotes: I just can't muster any enthusiasm for completely random arbitrary puzzles in the face of so many actual, real world problems.
To go from good to great, you must be able to communicate effectively: with your teammates, your manager, the users, and ultimately the world.
The worst nurses were invariably the worst communicators!
In the absence of reasonable communication, it sure seemed that way.
For many programmers, effectively communicating those solutions to the rest of the team is the hardest part of the puzzle.
I'm pretty sure that you can't B.S. your way through a technical presentation to a handful of your peers if you truly have no idea what you're talking about.
Most programmers, once they pass the FizzBuzz level of competency, are decent enough. But coding chops aren't enough.
posts.key_insight: Programming skill is table stakes; the ability to communicate effectively with teammates and users is what truly determines a developer's impact and career success.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood celebrates 'five dollar words' in programming—uncommon terms with specialized meanings that provide moments of clarity when you discover them. Drawing from Eric Lippert's blog series, he explores three key concepts: idempotence (operations that produce the same result regardless of repetition), orthogonality (independent components that don't cause unexpected side effects), and immutability (values that cannot be changed after creation). Atwood argues that learning precise vocabulary for programming concepts represents a step toward mastery, transforming intuitive understanding into explicit knowledge. He invites readers to share their own favorite technical terms that marked milestones in their development as programmers.
key_points: Learning precise vocabulary for programming concepts is a step toward understanding and mastery
Idempotent functions return the same result whether called once or a thousand times, making them essential for caching
Orthogonality means components can be modified independently without unexpected side effects—like navigating north-south without affecting east-west position
Non-orthogonal systems create 'Whack-a-Mole' debugging where fixing one problem creates others elsewhere
Immutable objects like strings are never modified in place; operations create entirely new objects instead
money_quotes: Words you don't hear often, but also words that provide the thrill of discovery, that 'aha' moment as you realize a certain programming concept you knew only through experimentation and intuition has a name.
If modifying item #1 results in unexpected behavior in item #2, you have a major problem – that's a form of unwanted coupling.

We've all worked on systems where you make one small change over here, and another problem pops out over there. You constantly push them back – like that Whack-a-Mole game – and you just never finish.
There's nothing you can do to the number one that changes it. You cannot paint it purple, make it even or get it angry. It's the number one, it is eternal, implacable and unchanging.
Try to imagine your strings painstakingly carved out of enormous blocks of granite. Because they are – they're immutable!
Learning the right word to describe something you're doing is a small step on the road towards understanding and eventual mastery.
posts.key_insight: Discovering precise technical vocabulary for programming concepts you already understand intuitively marks a milestone in your evolution as a developer.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces his keynote at EclipseCon 2009 with Clay Shirky, discussing the theory and practice of online community design. He reveals that Shirky's book 'Here Comes Everybody' served as a blueprint for building Stack Overflow. Atwood expresses deep admiration for Shirky's work on internet culture and community. He also emphasizes his satisfaction in presenting to non-Windows developers, as Stack Overflow was always meant to serve the broader programming community regardless of platform or toolchain.
key_points: Clay Shirky's 'Here Comes Everybody' was used as a literal blueprint for building Stack Overflow
Stack Overflow was intentionally designed as a cross-platform community celebrating the craft of programming, not tied to any specific technology
The talk pairs theory (Shirky) with practice (Atwood) on designing for online communities
Developers who lock themselves into a single toolchain miss out on the broader programming world
money_quotes: I quite literally used Clay Shirky's book, Here Comes Everybody: The Power of Organizing Without Organizations, as a blueprint for building Stack Overflow.
It's Clay Shirky's Internet, We Just Live In It.
It pains me to see developers who let themselves get locked into some particular toolchain ghetto, without at least peripheral awareness of what else is going on in the programming world around them.
Good programmers are interested in everything.

We always intended Stack Overflow to be a tribute to the greater craft of programming, a place where you could rub shoulders with fellow programmers from all sorts of different backgrounds and professional interests.
posts.key_insight: Great developer communities are built on inclusive, cross-platform foundations where good programmers can learn from each other regardless of their specific technology choices.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains how having a clear competitor or 'enemy' can be a powerful marketing and positioning tool for your product. He draws from 37 Signals' advice about defining your product by what it isn't, then shares how Stack Overflow benefited enormously from positioning itself against the widely-disliked Experts-Exchange. The comparison instantly communicates Stack Overflow's value proposition and casts them as heroes. Atwood acknowledges this rivalry is mostly theatrical—a form of kayfabe—and harbors no actual ill will toward competitors.
key_points: Defining your product against a competitor helps users understand your value proposition instantly
Having a villain-like competitor makes your marketing message clear and generates passion
Stack Overflow's positioning as 'Experts-Exchange without the evil' became their most effective elevator pitch
The rivalry doesn't need to be genuine—it's a communication tool, not actual animosity
A disliked competitor is a gift because it automatically positions you as the hero
money_quotes: Sometimes the best way to know what your app should be is to know what it shouldn't be.
We're like experts-exchange, but without all the evil.
I never appreciated how easy Experts-Exchange makes it for us.
No matter how much we may suck, we can simply point to the experts-exchange website and we instantly become the hero.
This 'rivalry' is mostly useful as a way to explain what it is we do.
If you have an arch-enemy – the more horrible and evil and larger-than-life the better – consider yourself lucky. They're doing you a huge favor.
posts.key_insight: A well-chosen competitor serves as a shorthand that instantly communicates your product's value and positions you as the hero in your users' minds.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the common open source response of 'code it yourself' as unrealistic for most users and even many programmers. He argues that mastering an unfamiliar codebase just to implement a small feature requires time most people don't have. Instead, he proposes financial sponsorship as a middle ground between giving up and DIY coding. He shares his own experience sponsoring bugfixes for Stack Overflow's dependencies and wonders why aggregated sponsorship platforms haven't naturally emerged in the open source ecosystem.
key_points: The 'code it yourself' response to feature requests often functions as a polite dismissal rather than a genuine solution
Even skilled programmers rarely have time to master alien codebases for small improvements
Financial sponsorship offers a practical middle path for non-coders to influence project direction
Pooled funding could attract developer attention when individual requests wouldn't
The absence of natural evolution toward aggregated sponsorship systems raises questions about sustainability
money_quotes: Sometimes, when people say 'why don't you code it yourself?' they're really saying 'F**k you.'
Surely there's something between the extremes of 'give up' and 'code it yourself.'
Sponsorship seems like a constructive way for people who are unable or unwilling to write code to affect the direction of a project.

Even if you're the type of macho, gung-ho programmer who can master an alien code base just to get some small pet feature or bugfix in – do you honestly have the kind of time it would take to do that?
I am concerned that this sort of aggregated sponsorship system hasn't naturally evolved on its own by now.
I was happy to give back to the project, and it was certainly a more realistic option than us carving out a chunk of our own development time to contribute the bugfixes ourselves.
posts.key_insight: Financial sponsorship bridges the gap between passive users and active contributors in open source, offering a realistic alternative to the false choice between coding it yourself and accepting the status quo.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that English should be the de facto standard language for software development, not out of cultural imperialism but practical necessity. He notes that programming languages use English keywords, most technical resources are in English, and the hacker community has organically adopted English as its working language. He cites Eric Raymond's observation that English has a richer technical vocabulary and shares testimony from a Polish programmer who confirms that developers prefer English documentation over often-inaccurate translations. Atwood compares choosing a minority human language to choosing a minority programming language like VB.NET over C#—the friction of working against the mainstream creates unnecessary obstacles.
key_points: A disproportionate amount of programming knowledge, documentation, and code examples exist in English, making it the lingua franca of programming
The hacker community has its own culture that transcends national boundaries, with English serving as the practical common tongue for technical discussion
Non-native speakers often prefer English documentation because translations are frequently partial, inaccurate, or create confusing 'English-Polish soup'
Choosing a minority language (human or programming) creates friction: time spent converting, introducing bugs, and working with smaller knowledge repositories
This position is pragmatism rather than cultural imperialism—it's about maximizing the global repository of solutions and developer productivity
money_quotes: Shouldn't every software developer understand English?
We're not talking about normal everyday people. We're talking about programmers. Citizens of the internet. People who swear allegiance not to a country, but a compiler.
Hackers have their own culture, their own norms and standards for literacy.
It's a meritocracy of code, not language, and nobody (or at least nobody who is sane, anyway) localizes programming languages.
If everybody blogs and develops in English – our global repository of solutions and blog posts is much bigger and you have better chances of finding an answer to your problem.
Advocating the adoption of English as the de-facto standard language of software development is simple pragmatism, the most virtuous of all hacker traits.
posts.key_insight: English isn't the language of programming because of American dominance, but because a global developer community organically chose it as the most practical common language for sharing knowledge and getting work done.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood challenges Dijkstra's assertion that competent programmers need to be mathematically inclined, arguing from practical experience that most real-world code involves only basic arithmetic. He acknowledges that math skills can help in specific domains like 3D games, physics simulations, and compression algorithms, but contends that programming success depends more on right-brained sensibilities like abstraction and aesthetics. Atwood cites Steve Yegge's case for math but reframes it as a general recipe for intellectual curiosity rather than a math-specific requirement. He concludes optimistically that programmers are smart enough to learn whatever math they need just-in-time when tackling specific problems.
key_points: Most practical programming involves 'balancing your checkbook' math, not calculus-level complexity
Programming requires right-brained skills like abstraction and aesthetics that math education doesn't cultivate
Unlike math problems with correct answers, code quality is subjective and determined by maintainability and efficiency trade-offs
Math knowledge becomes essential only in specific domains: 3D graphics, physics, image processing, compression
Just-in-time learning allows programmers to acquire necessary math skills when specific problems demand them
money_quotes: I have not found in practice that programmers need to be mathematically inclined to become great software developers.
With math, there are correct answers. You're either correct or you're incorrect. With coding, the best you can hope for is to do something well.

If you ignore your right brain, and I'm talking generally about abstraction and aesthetics, then you can slap some code together that might work, but it also might be one hell of a maintenance nightmare.
While being a math wonk certainly won't hurt you as a programmer, it's very hard for me to draw a direct line from 'good at math' to 'good at programming.'
I think most programmers are smart enough to learn whatever math they need just in time to attack the problem at hand.

This reads like a broad recipe for becoming intellectually curious and building skill at solving abstract problems. Important programming skills, to be sure, but not necessarily exclusive to the study of math.
posts.key_insight: Programming skill depends more on right-brained abilities like abstraction and aesthetic judgment than on mathematical prowess, and most developers can learn whatever math they need just-in-time for specific problems.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood presents a tongue-in-cheek hierarchy of programmer career levels, from 'Dead Programmer' (the highest honor, where your work outlives you) down to 'Bad Programmer.' The post reframes the tired interview question 'where do you see yourself in five years' into a more meaningful reflection on long-term career aspirations. Atwood argues that 'Successful Programmer' - one who builds businesses around their code and gains freedom to choose their work - is the level most should aspire to, while acknowledging that fame without financial success is precarious. He emphasizes that talent in coding isn't everything; business skills and self-awareness about one's limitations are equally valuable. The piece ultimately challenges programmers to think beyond day-to-day coding and consider what lasting impact they want to make over a lifetime.
key_points: The highest achievement for a programmer is having your code and ideas transcend your death and become part of computing's permanent historical record
Success (building businesses, gaining freedom) is more valuable than fame alone - famous programmers still need day jobs
Self-awareness about being an 'average' programmer is rare and valuable; talent in coding isn't the only path to success
Business and people skills can matter more than raw programming ability for career advancement
The question of long-term career trajectory deserves serious personal reflection, not just pat interview answers
money_quotes: Your code has survived and transcended your death. You are a part of the permanent historical record of computing.
Famous is good, but successful is better.
At this level you are a good enough programmer to realize that you're not a great programmer. And you might never be.
Talent often has little to do with success. You can be very successful if you have business and people skills.
Don't knock the value of self-awareness. It's more rare than you realize.
Be bold. Figure out what you're good at, and pursue it. Aggressively.
These programmers have given themselves the real freedom zero: the freedom to decide for themselves what they want to work on.
Everything they touch turns into pain and suffering for their fellow programmers.
posts.key_insight: A programmer's ultimate career success isn't measured by technical skill alone, but by the lasting impact of their work and the freedom they've earned to pursue what matters to them.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores Michael Abrash's Pixomatic, a software-only 3D renderer that accomplishes the seemingly impossible task of making a CPU compete with dedicated GPU hardware. Testing it on his modern (for 2009) hardware, Atwood finds the renderer delivers playable framerates in Unreal Tournament 2004, achieving 30-60 fps at reasonable settings. However, he acknowledges this achievement is 'utterly pointless' from a practical standpoint, since even basic integrated GPUs vastly outperform it. Despite the lack of practical utility, Atwood celebrates the engineering achievement and expresses admiration for 'heroic coding' - pushing technology to its absolute limits even when the results have no commercial value.
key_points: Pixomatic achieved what many thought impossible: a pure software 3D renderer that could run modern games at playable framerates
On 2009 hardware, the software renderer delivered 30-60 fps in Unreal Tournament 2004 at 1024x768 with decent quality settings
The achievement is acknowledged as 'utterly pointless' since even cheap integrated GPUs vastly outperform software rendering
Intel's Larrabee represented an attempt to blur the line between CPU and GPU, potentially making such optimization work more relevant
There's inherent value in 'heroic coding' - pushing boundaries of what's possible even without practical application
money_quotes: Pixomatic was perhaps the greatest performance challenge I've ever encountered, certainly right up there with Quake.
Pretty amazing, right? It is! And utterly pointless.
My current video card renders Unreal Tournament 2004 at the highest possible resolution with every possible quality option set to maximum, at somewhere between 200 and 300 frames per second.
Despite the miraculously efficient assembly Abrash and Sartain created to make this possible at all, it's at best a carnival oddity.
I can't help admiring the engineering effort that went into building a viable 3D software renderer, something that seemed virtually impossible bordering on foolish.
I happen to like heroic coding.
posts.key_insight: There's profound value in pushing technology to its absolute limits through heroic coding efforts, even when the practical utility of the achievement is essentially zero.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the rise and fall of WordPerfect through the lens of the book 'Almost Perfect' by W. E. Pete Peterson. WordPerfect dominated word processing from 1985-1992 but failed to transition from DOS to Windows, institutionalizing a character-mode software culture that proved fatal. The company was notable for being run by programmers who made product decisions, cultivating excellence and community. Atwood draws parallels to other forgotten software pioneers like Electric Pencil, noting how quickly dominant products can become footnotes in computing history.
key_points: WordPerfect's failure stemmed from not adapting to Windows and institutionalizing DOS-era assumptions
The company was unusually programmer-led, with developers making product decisions rather than marketing
Fanatical dedication to cross-platform parity, even for platforms that made no business sense, contributed to their downfall
Software dominance can evaporate remarkably quickly—Electric Pencil went from 250,000 users to forgotten in just 3 years
Success came from dedication to excellent products and cultivating vibrant internal and external communities
money_quotes: You can dominate the world for years, only to be relegated to little more than a dimly remembered footnote in computing history a decade later.
They didn't just bet on the wrong horse, they institutionalized a software culture that lived and died on character mode assumptions.
It was somewhat unusual for a software company to let the programmers decide the future of its products.
They clawed their way to success over so many formidable opponents by simply dedicating themselves to creating an excellent product.
At times this put us in the position of developing solutions before we identified problems.
Like WordPerfect itself, he doesn't go out with a bang, but a whimper.
Some failure patterns are timeless.
posts.key_insight: Even total market dominance offers no protection when a company institutionalizes assumptions about technology that become obsolete—adaptability matters more than current success.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the uncomfortable question of whether programmers should take jobs in the adult industry, drawing from a 1996 account of social ostracism faced by a porn site developer and his own near-miss with such a job. He acknowledges that adult sites often present genuinely interesting technical challenges—payment processing, scaling, continuous deployment—that exceed typical business applications. However, he notes that working in this space requires constant white lies about your employment. Atwood extends this discomfort to companies like IMVU that aren't technically adult content but trade heavily on sexualized marketing. He concludes by admitting his own difficulty respecting technical excellence when it serves 'least common denominator interests,' while acknowledging this may be his own problem rather than an objective judgment.
key_points: Adult industry programming jobs often involve more interesting technical challenges than typical corporate CRUD applications, including payment processing, scaling, and continuous deployment
Working in the adult industry creates social complications—colleagues, friends, family, and future employers may judge you negatively regardless of code quality
Programmers in this space learn to tell 'white lies' about their work, using vague descriptions and invoking NDAs to avoid uncomfortable conversations
Companies that aren't technically adult content but use heavily sexualized marketing (like IMVU) occupy an uncomfortable gray zone that can still affect professional perception
The author admits struggling to respect technical excellence when it serves purposes he considers lowbrow, while acknowledging this is a subjective and potentially unfair position
money_quotes: Does working on the adult part of the net mean I'm a scumbag? Does it mean I'm sleazy? Does it mean I'm untrustworthy? Does it mean my code is bad?
I think the operative words were 'what will we tell our parents?' It's a fair question.
When I asked him what the environment was like, working daily with adult products, he had a one word response: weird.

I don't think I'd want to work for a company that couldn't see the benefit of having someone onboard that's worked with systems that must always work, have a known cost-per-minute of downtime, and are on a permanently continuous release cycle.
If you choose to work in the adult industry, you have to tell white lies about your work—small evasions about what your work is, and who it is for, depending on the audience.
I can't help questioning the ultimate value of that work.
It's difficult for me to respect software engineering in the service of such least common denominator interests.
posts.key_insight: The technical merit of your work exists independently from its social acceptability, but programmers must decide whether interesting engineering challenges are worth the personal and professional complications of working in morally ambiguous industries.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood presents a UI design principle that goes beyond simply making software easy to use. He argues that good interface design involves deliberately making desirable actions easy while making undesirable or risky actions more difficult. Drawing an analogy to animal training techniques, he frames UI design as a form of behavioral conditioning—rewarding good behavior with simplicity and discouraging unwanted behavior through intentional friction.
key_points: Good UI design makes the right actions easy and wrong actions awkward—not uniformly simple
Risky or dangerous operations should deliberately require more steps
UI design is essentially user training through behavioral rewards and friction
Making features hard to use is a legitimate design technique when used judiciously
The 'pit of success' concept means users should naturally fall into correct usage patterns
money_quotes: Make the right thing easy to do and the wrong thing awkward to do.
Sometimes there are things you don't want users to do. In those cases, you actually want your application, or at least certain areas of it, to be harder to use.
You don't get a sea lion to balance a ball on the end of its nose by nagging.
When you make features easy to use, you are rewarding user behavior you like.
When you intentionally choose not to make a feature easy to use, you are effectively ignoring user behavior you don't like.
If you aren't taking advantage of both techniques in your user interface—rewarding with simplicity, and judiciously ignoring with complexity where necessary—you aren't properly training your users.
posts.key_insight: Effective UI design is behavioral training: reward desired actions with simplicity and discourage risky ones with deliberate friction.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood dives into the eternal tabs vs. spaces debate, acknowledging its seemingly trivial nature while arguing it actually matters deeply. He cites research showing that consistent code formatting has a psychological basis—programmers build mental models that expect conventional patterns, and violations disrupt comprehension. The post warns against passive-aggressive 'formatting wars' where developers silently reformat each other's code, calling it one of the worst forms of teamicide. Ultimately, Atwood concludes that the specific choice doesn't matter as much as team consistency—then delivers a classic Atwood punchline declaring tab users morons.
key_points: The tabs vs. spaces debate has three camps: tabs only, spaces only, and a hybrid approach using tabs for indentation and spaces for alignment
Passive-aggressive code reformatting by team members (especially leads) is destructive teamicide that damages peer relationships and source control history
Research by Soloway, Ehrlich, and Shneiderman demonstrates that consistent code formatting has measurable psychological benefits for program comprehension
Experts remember well-structured code better than novices, but this advantage disappears when code is randomly formatted—proving conventions matter
The only project with no formatting disagreements is a solo project; the real challenge is building team consensus
money_quotes: The only programming project with no disagreement whatsoever on code formatting is the one you work on alone.
One of absolute worst, worst methods of teamicide for software developers is to engage in these kinds of passive-aggressive formatting wars.
It is not merely a matter of aesthetics that programs should be written in a particular style. Rather there is a psychological basis for writing programs in a conventional manner.
When statements were shuffled, the experts' superiority was reduced.
Choose tabs, choose spaces, choose whatever layout conventions make sense to you and your team. It doesn't actually matter which coding styles you pick.
What does matter is that you, and everyone else on your team, sticks with those conventions and uses them consistently.
That said, only a moron would use tabs to format their code.
posts.key_insight: Code formatting consistency matters more than which convention you choose because programmers build cognitive expectations around patterns, and violating those expectations measurably harms comprehension.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines whether open source experience is truly valued by employers, sparked by an anonymous developer's frustrating job search. Despite having architected multiple open source Java projects with publicly available code, this developer found that recruiters and interviewers consistently dismissed his work in favor of coding tests and 'commercial experience.' Atwood criticizes employers who won't even glance at a candidate's portfolio, calling such companies 'suspect.' However, he also suggests that not all open source work is equal—projects that solve obscure problems nobody cares about may not demonstrate real-world value. The piece concludes that open source experience is only truly valid if the project achieves some critical mass and actually serves users.
key_points: An experienced developer found that multiple employers completely ignored his open source portfolio, preferring coding tests or dismissing him for lacking 'commercial experience'
Companies that refuse to examine a programmer's prior body of work before interviewing are suspect employers you might not want to work for anyway
The choice of open source project matters as much as the programming itself—solving problems nobody cares about doesn't demonstrate real value
Open source experience is only meaningful if the project achieves critical mass and builds a real user base
The proprietary nature of commercial work often prevents candidates from demonstrating their actual contributions, yet it's still valued over transparent open source work
money_quotes: Absolutely nothing is more practical, more real, than working collaboratively with software developers all over the globe from all walks of life.
One of the reasons I worked so hard on open source projects was to make job interviews easier.
I'm not sure I'd want to work at a place where a programmers' prior body of work is treated as inconsequential.
A lot of open source projects are probably poorly written and made in response to a neat idea rather than to requirements from some user community.
Perhaps the choice of open source project matters almost as much as the programming itself.

Just as commercial software can't possibly exist without customers, perhaps open source experience is only valid if you work on a project that attains some moderate level of critical mass and user base.
Open source or not, if you aren't building software that someone finds useful... then what are you really doing?
posts.key_insight: Open source contributions only demonstrate real professional value when they solve problems people actually care about and attract a genuine user community—obscure passion projects, no matter how well-coded, may not translate to job market credibility.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that developers should prioritize building robust exception logging and monitoring systems rather than waiting for users to report bugs. He introduces 'Exception-Driven Development' as a methodology where teams ship software quickly, monitor error logs obsessively, and use that data to prioritize fixes. Drawing on Microsoft's Windows Error Reporting data showing that fixing just 1% of top bugs addresses 50% of customer issues, he makes the case that this approach is more practical than speculative TDD bug prevention. The core philosophy is that exception logs provide the most honest, actionable feedback about software quality.
key_points: Developers who learn about bugs from users rather than their own monitoring have failed at their job
Exception logs should serve as the team's de-facto to-do list, monitored daily or hourly
Microsoft data shows fixing 1% of top-reported bugs addresses 50% of customer issues, and 20% fixes 80%
TDD as a pure bug-fixing mechanism is like premature optimization—better to fix bugs users actually encounter
The speed at which you can fix bugs matters more than shipping bug-free code, which is impossible anyway
money_quotes: If you're waiting around for users to tell you about problems with your website or application, you're only seeing a tiny fraction of all the problems that are actually occurring.
When a user informs me about a bona fide error they've experienced with my software, I am deeply embarrassed. And more than a little ashamed.
Our exception logs are a de-facto to do list for our team.
If you fix a bug that no actual user will ever encounter, what have you actually fixed?
Exception logs are possibly the most powerful form of feedback your customers can give you.
The question isn't how many bugs you will ship with, but how fast can you fix those bugs?
Real software crashes. Real software loses data. Real software is hard to learn, and hard to use.
posts.key_insight: Exception logs are the most honest and actionable form of customer feedback—they tell you exactly what's broken without requiring users to articulate problems or developers to guess what might fail.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood dissects the gaming of Time Magazine's 2009 World's Most Influential People poll, revealing a cascade of basic security failures. The poll launched with no authentication, no input validation, and easily bypassed rate limiting. Time's attempts to fix the vulnerabilities were equally amateur, hiding a 'secret' salt in client-side Flash code. The result was hackers not only putting 4chan founder moot at the top but spelling out a message with the first letters of the top 21 names. Atwood argues this wasn't impressive hacking—it was shooting fish in a barrel against incompetent developers.
key_points: Time's poll launched without authentication, validation, or even range checking on vote values—basic web security 101 failures
Their attempted fix hid the authentication salt in client-side Flash, which hackers quickly extracted
The 13-second IP throttling could be bypassed by simply cycling through different candidates
The 'hackers' spelled out 'marblecake also the game' using the first letters of the top 21 names
Atwood dismisses this as not a real hack, contrasting it with genuinely impressive exploits like the Black Sunday hack
money_quotes: This isn't high security; this is web 101.
Any programmer with even a rudimentary understanding of how the web works would have thought of these exploits immediately.
When good is dumb, evil will always triumph.
Is it really a precision hack when your adversaries are incompetent?
Time eventually responded – with all the skill and expertise of... a team who put together the world's most insecure online poll.
So-called secret poorly hidden on the client: check!
I'm not sure they come any dumber than these clowns.
posts.key_insight: Exploiting a system with no security isn't hacking—it's walking through an open door, and the real failure belongs to developers who ignore basic web security principles.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the widespread practice of copying and pasting code from the internet, arguing that while copy-paste itself isn't inherently bad, the complete disconnect between pasted code and its source creates problems for maintenance and improvement. He acknowledges both the benefits (discoverability, transparency, community feedback) and drawbacks (no update propagation, blind copying, security risks) of internet-sourced code snippets. Rather than condemning the practice, he proposes a pragmatic solution: attaching GUID comments to code snippets so they can be tracked across the web. The proposal would allow developers to find all instances of a snippet, locate updates, and potentially share improvements back to the original source.
key_points: Copy and paste doesn't create bad code—bad programmers do; the tool itself is neutral
Internet code sharing follows 'Bambrick's 8th Rule': it's easier to find code from a stranger online than from a colleague nearby
The biggest problem with copypasta programming is the complete disconnect between pasted code and its original source
A simple GUID comment convention could create trackable lineage for code snippets across the web
Sometimes small code requires small solutions—over-engineering isn't always the answer
money_quotes: Copy and paste doesn't create bad code. Bad programmers create bad code.
David Parnas says that if you use copy and paste while you're coding, you're probably committing a design error.

It's far easier and much less trouble to find and use a bug-ridden, poorly implemented snippet of code written by a 13 year old blogger on the other side of the world than it is to find and use the equivalent piece of code written by your team leader on the other side of a cubicle partition.
Code is often 'demo code' and may purposely gloss over important concerns like error handling, sql injection, encoding, security, etc.
The most troubling limitation of copypasta programming is the complete disconnect between the code you've pasted and all the other viral copies of it on the web.
We're just following the fine software engineering tradition of doing the stupidest thing that could possibly work.
Sometimes, small code requires small solutions.
posts.key_insight: Instead of fighting the inevitable practice of copy-paste programming, we should embrace simple conventions like snippet GUIDs that make code lineage trackable across the web.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood celebrates Microsoft's announcement that Windows 7 will include a virtualized copy of Windows XP, seeing it as a significant step toward breaking free from backwards compatibility constraints. He argues that virtualization technology has matured enough to make running legacy applications seamless, with the virtual OS becoming invisible to users. While acknowledging Windows 7's implementation isn't as polished as competitors like Parallels, he views this as an important milestone that could free the industry from the burden of maintaining ancient APIs and allow genuine innovation in operating system design.
key_points: Windows 7 shipping with a licensed virtual XP copy represents a major shift in how Microsoft handles backwards compatibility
Windows XP's minimal system requirements (233 MHz, 64 MB RAM) make it trivially easy to virtualize on modern hardware
Modern virtualization solutions like Parallels' coherence mode can make virtualized applications appear native to users
Virtualization offers 'nearly perfect backwards compatibility' by running the actual legacy OS rather than emulating old APIs
The demand for perfect backwards compatibility has historically held back software innovation
money_quotes: The demand for perfect backwards compatibility has held the industry back for too long.
It frees software developers from the crushing weight of their own historical software mistakes.
The attraction of virtualizing older operating systems is that it throws off the eternal yoke of backwards compatibility.
While XP may have been the apple of 2001's eye, in computing dog years, it's basically... dead.
From the user's perspective, it's just another application in a window on their desktop. They don't need to know or care if the application is running in a virtual machine.

Instead of bending over backwards to make sure you never break any old APIs, you can build new systems free of the contortions and compromises inherent in guaranteeing that new versions of the operating system never break old applications.
posts.key_insight: Virtualization technology enables operating systems to finally break free from the crippling burden of backwards compatibility by running legacy systems alongside new ones, rather than forever supporting ancient APIs.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood applies the geek optimization mindset to an everyday carry item: the wallet. After his decade-old Tumi wallet wore out, he sought something thinner and discovered ultra-slim wallets made from sailcloth and Tyvek materials. He reviews the All-Ett wallet's remarkable thinness (thinner fully loaded than his old wallet empty) while noting minor drawbacks, then gets excited about Mighty Wallets with geeky designs like dot matrix printouts and digits of pi. The post also explores wallet pens as a way to always have writing tools available, demonstrating how the engineering mindset of continuous improvement can apply to mundane physical objects.
key_points: The fully populated All-Ett wallet is thinner than his old premium wallet was completely empty
Ultra-thin wallets eliminate the need to remove your wallet when sitting, reducing the chance of losing it
Geek culture extends optimization obsession from code and keychains to everyday carry items like wallets
The tradeoff between thinness and usability (fiddly pocket orientation) is worth it for items used only 3-4 times daily
Wallet pens offer a way to always carry writing tools without adding to keychain bulk
money_quotes: If we geeks obsessively optimize what's on our keychain, we'd be remiss if we didn't also obsessively optimize that other item most geeks carry around – our wallet.
The fully populated All-ett was thinner – much thinner! – than my old wallet was empty.
I no longer feel motivated to remove my wallet from my back pocket when I sit down. That's important.
I tend to lose my wallet when I leave it lying around various places. Not good.
Plus: digits of π! On a dot matrix print-out! Come on, man!
Given that I tend to open my wallet maybe 3-4 times per day, at most, that's a tradeoff I'm willing to make.
posts.key_insight: The engineering mindset of measurement, optimization, and thoughtful tradeoffs applies just as well to physical everyday objects as it does to code.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts receiving an anonymous email from someone who successfully logged into his Stack Overflow account by guessing his password. The ethical hacker discovered the password through some vulnerability Jeff had previously discussed on his blog, combined with identifying his OpenID provider from a recent post. Despite initially defending his password choice as not quite a 'dictionary word,' Jeff acknowledges he should have used better security for an account with moderator privileges. The post ends as a cliffhanger, challenging readers to guess how the password was discovered.
key_points: An anonymous ethical hacker successfully logged into Atwood's Stack Overflow account using a guessed password
The vulnerability combined a weak password with publicly revealed OpenID provider information
Atwood initially rationalized his weak password by claiming the account was for 'low-value logins' despite having moderator powers
The hacker responsibly disclosed the vulnerability rather than exploiting or selling the access
Atwood verified the breach was real by checking OpenID logs and finding an unrecognized IP address
money_quotes: These are the kinds of emails that make your blood run cold.
He wasn't bluffing. He really did log in as me.
The password was relatively simple, but I wouldn't go so far as to characterize it as a 'dictionary password'
Good thing I haven't made too many enemies. Today, I mean. So far. The day's not over, yet.
I'm curious enough to go digging around for holes, but too ethical to actually do anything with them.
As a Stack Overflow moderator I have unusual powers and probably should have used an alternate OpenID with more security.
posts.key_insight: Even security-aware developers rationalize poor password practices until they're personally compromised, revealing the gap between knowing better and doing better.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reveals how his password was compromised: not through guessing, brute force, or phishing, but because another site stored his password as an unsalted MD5 hash that was easily reversed. He admits to reusing a weak password across sites and using a low-value credential for his Stack Overflow moderator account. The post serves as both a cautionary tale about password storage practices and an argument for why developers should avoid storing credentials altogether, instead delegating to specialized identity providers like OpenID.
key_points: Unsalted MD5 password hashes can be trivially reversed using lookup tables, exposing users who reuse passwords across sites
The attacker was actually a well-meaning person who had complained to the vulnerable site about their poor password practices months earlier
Password reuse transforms a security failure on one site into a vulnerability across all sites where that password is used
Developers should 'get the hell out of the business of storing user credentials' and delegate authentication to specialized providers
Even security-conscious programmers like Atwood make basic mistakes like password reuse and using weak passwords for important accounts
money_quotes: We have met the enemy, and he is... programmers just like us.
The important thing to take away from this, if you're a programmer working on an application that stores user credentials, is to get the hell out of the business of storing user credentials!
Are you equipped and willing do everything necessary to protect idiots like me from myself?
I, for one, think that my OpenID provider is more secure than the average guy running a forum.
We outsourced our user credential system to people who are much better at it than us.
Failing to rate limit password attempts is strictly amateur hour stuff (and I'd argue borderline incompetence).
Programmers are the enemy. Hey... wait a second, I'm a programmer! GOTO 1
posts.key_insight: The weakest link in your security is often not your own application but some forgotten site with poor password practices combined with users who reuse credentials—making the case for delegating authentication to specialized identity providers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines Steve McConnell's Pseudocode Programming Process from Code Complete, a technique where developers write plain English descriptions before coding, then convert those descriptions into comments. Despite being a devoted fan of McConnell's work, Atwood admits he finds the technique impractical after multiple attempts. His objections center on preferring to think directly in code rather than English, and believing that starting with comments contradicts the goal of writing self-explanatory code. He concludes that while PPP has theoretical merits, he can only see pseudocode being useful in job interviews, and even then would prefer writing real code.
key_points: The Pseudocode Programming Process involves writing plain English descriptions of routines before coding, then converting those descriptions into inline comments
McConnell's rationale includes easier reviews, iterative refinement, cheaper changes at early stages, and automatic documentation
Atwood finds it easier to think about code directly in code rather than translating through precise English descriptions
Starting with comments contradicts the principle that code should be self-explanatory, with comments as a last resort
Despite respecting McConnell's methodology, Atwood acknowledges that refactoring, TDD, and even 'hacking' are valid alternative approaches
money_quotes: Pseudocode is sort of like the Tang of programming languages – you hydrate the code around it.
I find it easier to think about code in code.
There's something fundamentally... unrealistic... about attempting to using precise English to describe the nuts and bolts of code.
Comments should be a method of last resort, not something you start with.
One of the keys to the success of a project is to catch errors at the 'least-value stage,' the stage at which the least effort has been invested.
I have a hard time seeing pseudocode as useful in anything other than possibly job interviews.
posts.key_insight: Even well-reasoned programming methodologies from respected authorities may prove impractical in real-world application, and developers should trust their own experience over dogma.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the web browser address bar has evolved into a modern command line interface, powered by search engines rather than local operating systems. Chrome's approach of passing non-URI input to search engines enables powerful query capabilities including weather, calculations, conversions, and stock quotes. He notes that Google's search operators function similarly to UNIX command line parameters, while JavaScript bookmarklets provide scripting capabilities. Services like yubnub further extend this by enabling collaborative, crowdsourced command creation. Atwood concludes that despite historically preferring GUIs, the search-oriented, publicly accessible command prompt may represent the future of computing interfaces.
key_points: Browser address bars now function as command lines by passing non-URL input to search engines
Google's search features (weather, calculations, conversions, stock quotes) mirror traditional command line utility functions
Advanced search operators like filetype:, site:, and daterange: parallel UNIX command line parameters
JavaScript bookmarklets enable scripting capabilities within the browser address bar
Services like yubnub create a collaborative, crowdsourced command system that extends browser capabilities socially
money_quotes: I like to think of the web browser address bar as the new command line.
While web browsers should have some built-in smarts, they can never match the collective intelligence of a worldwide search engine.
Any command line worth its salt has some kind of scripting language built in, too, right?
Apparently we've spent the last 20 years reimplementing the UNIX command line in the browser.
Perhaps the command prompt – or more specifically, the search oriented, crowdsourced, world public command prompt – really is the future.
I was never a big command-line enthusiast; even way back when on my Amiga I'd choose the GUI over the CLI whenever I could. But maybe I bet on the wrong horse.
posts.key_insight: The browser address bar, powered by search engines and collaborative services, has become a globally accessible, crowdsourced command line that surpasses traditional CLIs by leveraging collective intelligence rather than local knowledge.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores why computers produce seemingly incorrect mathematical results, from Google's calculator errors to Excel's multiplication bugs. He explains that floating-point arithmetic requires approximating infinitely many real numbers with finite bits, leading to precision issues especially in subtraction operations. The post references the classic paper 'What Every Computer Scientist Should Know About Floating-Point Arithmetic' to explain how rounding errors are inherent to floating-point computation. Atwood concludes that these aren't really computer failures but rather programmer failures to properly handle the limitations of numerical representation.
key_points: Floating-point numbers have roughly 16 decimal places of precision, which sounds like a lot but can be lost entirely through subtraction of similar values
Squeezing infinitely many real numbers into finite bits requires approximation—rounding error is the characteristic feature of floating-point computation
While addition, multiplication, and division are very accurate, subtraction can lose precision catastrophically when two numbers agree to n figures
Real-world consequences can be severe: the Ariane 5 rocket exploded due to a floating-point to integer conversion overflow
These 'computer math errors' are really programmer errors—failures to understand and handle numerical representation limitations
money_quotes: Squeezing infinitely many real numbers into a finite number of bits requires an approximate representation.
Computers are awesome, yes, but they aren't infinite... yet.
The best we can do is work with approximations at varying levels of precision that are 'good enough' where 'good enough' depends on what you're doing, and how you're doing it.
Subtraction can be anywhere from exact to completely inaccurate. If two numbers agree to n figures, you can lose up to n figures of precision in their subtraction.
I'm starting to believe that it's not the computers that suck at math, but the people programming those computers.
posts.key_insight: Computers don't suck at math—programmers suck at understanding that finite representations of infinite numbers require careful handling of approximation and precision.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts a security vulnerability his team introduced by using ECB cipher mode in their .NET encryption code. While the code appeared to encrypt data properly, ECB mode encrypts each block independently without chaining, allowing attackers to substitute and rearrange encrypted blocks without detection. This flaw in their login state management could have enabled data tampering attacks. The post serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of using encryption libraries without fully understanding the underlying cryptographic concepts.
key_points: ECB (Electronic Codebook) mode is a dangerous cipher mode because it encrypts blocks independently, making identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext
Even experienced developers can miss critical security flaws when using encryption libraries they don't fully understand
The best approach to protecting secrets is to never send them to the client in the first place
Encryption code that appears to work correctly may still be fundamentally insecure
Block chaining modes are essential for secure symmetric encryption to prevent block substitution attacks
money_quotes: It's as true in life as it is in client-server programming: the only secret that can't be compromised is the one you never revealed.
Sure it's encrypted! It says Encrypt() right there in the method name, right? Wrong.
CipherMode.ECB? More like CipherMode.Fail!
It isn't encryption until you've taken the time to fully understand the concepts behind the encryption code.
If it doesn't have to be sent to the client, then don't!
I'm no Bruce Schneier, but I have a reasonable grasp of encryption concepts. And I still completely missed this problem.
posts.key_insight: Using encryption libraries without understanding the underlying cryptographic concepts can give you a false sense of security while leaving your application vulnerable to attack.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on how a flawed encryption function ended up in his codebase and traces its origins across the internet, finding 131 instances of the same broken code being copied and shared. He argues that the entire team bears responsibility for code quality, not just the individual contributor, invoking collective code ownership. The post introduces Scott Hanselman's metaphor of the internet as 'the bathroom wall of code' – a place where random code graffiti of varying quality gets scrawled and mindlessly copied. Atwood concludes by urging developers to apply rigorous peer review to any code snippets absorbed from the internet, as that perfect-looking solution could be dangerous graffiti.
key_points: Team collective ownership means everyone is responsible for every line of code, regardless of who wrote it
The same flawed encryption code was found in 131 different places across the internet, demonstrating how bad code propagates
Internet code snippets are like bathroom wall graffiti – some art, some vandalism, impossible to tell at a glance
Copy-paste code reuse without review spreads security vulnerabilities at scale
Extra-strength peer review is essential for any code absorbed from external sources
money_quotes: I invoke the First Rule of Programming: It's Always Your Fault.
Nobody owns the code, because everybody owns the code.
Yes, I failed. Because the team failed.
Heaven forbid someone copy and paste flawed code from the internet into their project!
This Encrypt() function is like the village bicycle: everybody's had a ride.
People, being people, have gone and scrawled a bunch of random code graffiti all over the damn internet.
That internet code snippet you're looking at, the one that appears to be just what you're looking for, could also be random graffiti scrawled on a bathroom wall.
posts.key_insight: Code snippets spread across the internet like graffiti, and without rigorous peer review, you're just copying someone else's mistakes.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the paradox of motivating programmers, arguing that traditional management techniques don't work for experienced developers. He uses a comic and Dave Thomas's expertise framework to illustrate that peer pressure and competition are far more effective than polite requests or coercion. The post distinguishes between approaches for novice developers (who benefit from rules and guidelines) and experienced ones (who respond to competitive challenges from peers). The core insight is that nothing motivates a programmer quite like another programmer criticizing their code.
key_points: Traditional motivational techniques like polite requests and coercion are ineffective or even counterproductive with skilled programmers
Peer pressure and competition are the most powerful motivators for experienced developers
Different experience levels require different management approaches: herd novices with rules, let experts race
The threat of code being rewritten by a peer is a surprisingly powerful motivator
Rules become less useful as developer expertise increases
money_quotes: Nothing motivates like having another programmer tell you they're rewriting your code because it sucks.
Peer pressure and competition are the key.
Don't try to race sheep, Don't try to herd race horses
If you have neophyte developers, herd them with maxims, guidelines and static rules.
If you have experienced developers, rules are less useful. Instead, encourage them to race: engage in a little friendly competition and show off how good they are to their peers.
posts.key_insight: Experienced programmers are best motivated not by rules or requests, but by peer competition and the prospect of having their code judged by fellow developers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that penny auction sites like Swoopo are fundamentally gambling operations disguised as legitimate e-commerce. The post recounts his exchange with a Swoopo investor who claimed there was no element of chance involved, a claim Atwood found laughable. He cites a programmer's detailed analysis showing that winning auctions is essentially random due to network latency and server-side processing advantages. Atwood calls for these sites to be regulated as gambling and urges readers to warn others and contact regulatory agencies.
key_points: Penny auctions profit primarily from selling bids, not from auction sales, creating an inherently exploitative business model
A programmer's technical analysis proved that manual bids cannot reliably win due to network delays, making outcomes essentially random
The 'bidbutler' server-side bidding service gives paying users an unfair advantage over manual bidders
Swoopo relies on regulatory agencies not understanding the technical mechanics that make it gambling
The only winning strategy is not to play—the house always wins while users lose millions
money_quotes: Swoopo is, at its heart, thinly veiled gambling.
We're programmers, and many of us develop websites for a living. It's a bit tougher to pull the wool over our eyes.
There is no way to reliably win an auction on swoopo.com without using their bidbutler service.
The only winning strategy at Swoopo, or any other penny auction site, is not to play.
On Swoopo, there are nothing but millions of losers – and by that I mean they are gambling and losing millions of real dollars to the house.
Swoopo is evil beyond the likes of Saddam Hussein, The Balrog, OSB, Darth Vader, and Barbra Streisand – combined.
posts.key_insight: Penny auction sites exploit technical complexity and regulatory ignorance to operate unregulated gambling operations that guarantee the house wins while users lose.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood provides a practical primer on RAID configurations for server environments, explaining the fundamental RAID levels (0, 1, 5, 6) and their trade-offs between performance, redundancy, and storage efficiency. Drawing from his hands-on experience building Stack Overflow's servers, he advocates for RAID 10 as the superior choice that combines mirroring's redundancy with striping's speed. He emphasizes that RAID is not a backup solution but rather a tool for improved uptime. The post culminates in exploring ZFS's RAID-Z technology, which offers self-healing data capabilities and eliminates the traditional RAID write hole without requiring special hardware.
key_points: RAID serves two primary goals: increased data reliability and increased I/O performance, with different levels offering different trade-offs
RAID 10 (nested striping + mirroring) combines the best of both worlds and may completely obviate the need for RAID 5
RAID is absolutely not a substitute for backups—it's about uptime and survivability, not data protection
ZFS's RAID-Z represents a next-generation approach with self-healing data, 256-bit checksums, and no need for special hardware
The economics of cheap commodity drives make it sensible to use many disks for both performance and reliability gains
money_quotes: I've always been leery of RAID on the desktop. But on the server, RAID is a definite must.
RAID is in no way a substitute for a sane backup regimen, but rather a way to offer improved uptime and survivability for your existing systems.
Some would say that RAID 10 is so good it completely obviates any need for RAID 5, and I for one agree with them.
Hard drives are cheap and getting cheaper every day – why not use a whole slew of the things to get better performance and better reliability for your servers?
Hard drives may be (mostly) dumb hunks of spinning rust, but it's downright amazing what you can do when you get a whole bunch of them working together.
With RAID-Z, ZFS makes good on the original RAID promise: it provides fast, reliable storage using cheap, commodity disks.
posts.key_insight: While RAID provides essential redundancy and performance benefits for servers, it's not a backup solution—and modern software-based approaches like ZFS's RAID-Z are pushing beyond traditional hardware RAID with self-healing capabilities and smarter data integrity checking.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces the public beta launch of Server Fault, a sister site to Stack Overflow designed specifically for system administrators and IT professionals. The post uses a humorous quiz format to identify sysadmins through cultural touchstones like the BOFH acronym and classic Unix texts. Atwood explains that while there's crossover between programming and IT communities, they have distinct skill sets that warrant separate platforms. Server Fault gets its own domain, user profiles, and reputation system to serve this different audience. The post reflects Atwood's personal interest in hardware and system administration despite primarily being a programmer.
key_points: Server Fault launched as a dedicated Q&A platform for system administrators and IT professionals, separate from Stack Overflow
The programming community and IT/sysadmin community are recognized as distinct groups with different expertise
Server Fault is explicitly not for personal computer troubleshooting - it's for professionals managing servers, networks, and enterprise desktops
Being a skilled programmer doesn't automatically translate to mastery of networking and server configuration, and vice versa
The site uses the same engine as Stack Overflow but maintains separate user profiles and reputation systems
money_quotes: If you answered 'yes' to any of the above, I am sorry to inform you that you may be a system administrator or IT professional.
Although there is certainly some crossover, we believe that the programming community and the IT/sysadmin community are different beasts.
Just because you're a hotshot programmer doesn't mean you have mastered networking and server configuration.
And I've met a few sysadmins who could script circles around my code.
As a programmer who loves hardware as much as software, I've wanted this site for months.
Who knows, maybe we lusers can learn something from each other.
posts.key_insight: Programmers and system administrators represent distinct professional communities with different expertise, and each deserves dedicated platforms that recognize their unique knowledge domains.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses an amusing dating disaster story to introduce the concept of NP-completeness to a general programming audience. The post centers on a woman who falsely claimed to have proved P=NP in college, which any computer science graduate would recognize as an immediate red flag. Atwood explains that NP-complete problems are those where solutions can be quickly verified but not quickly found, and that proving P=NP remains one of computer science's greatest unsolved problems. He concludes that understanding NP-completeness helps programmers recognize when problems are fundamentally intractable, allowing them to redirect efforts toward practical approximations rather than impossible optimal solutions.
key_points: NP-complete problems have solutions that can be quickly checked but require impossibly long computation times to solve optimally
No one has yet proved whether P=NP, and it remains one of computer science's most important unsolved problems
Recognizing NP-completeness is practically valuable because it lets you tell your boss the problem is genuinely hard, not that you're incompetent
When facing NP-complete problems, the pragmatic approach is to find efficient algorithms for special cases or approximate solutions
Basic computer science literacy serves as a useful filter for detecting imposters and frauds
money_quotes: Dating crazy people is one thing, but dating crazy people who claim to have proved P=NP is another matter entirely.
The defining characteristic of an NP-complete problem is that optimal solutions, using math and logic as we currently understand them, are effectively impossible.
I can't find an efficient algorithm, but neither can all these famous people.
At the very least, this would inform your boss that it would do no good to fire you and hire another expert on algorithms.
As with so many things in programming, the first step is learning enough to know when you're really screwed.
Unfortunately for poor Joey, this sad corollary to NP-completeness apparently applies to dating, too.
posts.key_insight: Understanding computational complexity helps programmers recognize genuinely intractable problems, freeing them to pursue practical approximations instead of impossible optimal solutions.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores how to handle criticism and hate as a blogger, drawing on Randy Pausch's insight that critics show they still care about you. While acknowledging that criticism represents engagement and conversation worth learning from, he argues that the most powerful tool for dealing with truly harmful content isn't arguing back—it's ignoring it entirely. Using the 'silent treatment' from The Great Brain books as an analogy, he explains that withholding attention is far more effective than any form of engagement. The post concludes with the paradoxical invitation to ignore him if readers feel strongly enough.
key_points: Critics engaging with your work, even harshly, means they still care enough to respond—silence is worse than criticism
Criticism is valuable because it represents a conversation where you can learn something from your audience's engagement
The most powerful way to make something go away isn't arguing or screaming—it's ignoring it completely
Attention is the currency of the web; withholding links and pageviews is more effective than any counterargument
The 'silent treatment' as punishment works because social exclusion is psychologically more painful than direct confrontation
money_quotes: When you see yourself doing something badly and nobody's bothering to tell you anymore, that's a very bad place to be. Your critics are your ones telling you they still love you and care.
Criticism, painful though it may be, is still a conversation.
There's something to learn from following that conversation.
I stopped reading the blog a while ago... The mystery of the non-reading Coding Horror reader. Another NP-complete problem, I guess.
If you think something sucks to the extent that it's actively harming the world and you want it to go away, leaving comments to that effect is not the way.
There is something much more powerful than criticism that you can bring to bear in these situations.
That's how you change the world. Not by arguing with people. Certainly not by screaming at them. You do it by ignoring them.
posts.key_insight: The most powerful response to content you want to eliminate isn't criticism or argument—it's complete withdrawal of attention.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents his journey to share Stack Overflow's cc-wiki licensed data via BitTorrent, discovering that while downloading torrents is trivially easy, sharing them is surprisingly complicated. He walks through the technical requirements including port forwarding, tracker setup, and using uTorrent's built-in tracker feature. The post serves as a practical tutorial while also advocating for LegalTorrents as a legitimate sharing platform. Atwood notes the irony that BitTorrent, designed to democratize file distribution, has a steep asymmetry between consuming and producing content.
key_points: BitTorrent sharing requires a tracker, external IP knowledge, port forwarding, and proper client configuration - far more complex than downloading
uTorrent can act as its own tracker by enabling bt.enable_tracker, eliminating the need for public trackers
LegalTorrents offers a legitimate platform for sharing Creative Commons content via BitTorrent with OpenID support
Most public trackers are 'disreputable' making legal content sharing awkward for creators
The protocol that democratizes bandwidth is itself asymmetric in accessibility - easy to consume, hard to share
money_quotes: BitTorrent radically shifts the economics of distribution. It's one of the most miraculous ideas ever conceived on the internet.
As far as I'm concerned, there should be a Nobel prize for computing, and the inventor of BitTorrent should be its first recipient.
Sharing files with BitTorrent is way more complicated than downloading them!
Get the furtive thrill of actually downloading legal content through BitTorrent for once!
Without a tracker, you're sort of hosed, as clients will never be able to find your file, much less each other.
Like so many things in Windows, it ain't pretty, but it gets the job done.

It's ironic that BitTorrent, which is justly famous for equalizing the highly asymmetric nature of most people's internet connections, is itself so asymmetric when it comes to sharing: trivially easy to consume, but awkward and confusing to share.
posts.key_insight: BitTorrent's elegant design for democratizing bandwidth distribution paradoxically fails to democratize the act of sharing itself, creating a consumer-producer asymmetry that undermines its egalitarian promise.

posts.summary: On Unix's 40th anniversary, Jeff Atwood reflects on the operating system's evolution from Ken Thompson's one-month coding sprint in 1969 to its modern descendants. He surveys the Unix family tree, noting the surprising survival of closed-source variants alongside Linux and BSD. Atwood, a longtime Windows developer, admits to developing a grudging respect for Unix philosophy—particularly its pragmatic 'worse is better' approach that prioritizes working solutions over elegant visions. He explores the cultural divide between Unix (programmer-focused) and Windows (end-user-focused) development, ultimately concluding that both platforms have valuable lessons for pragmatic developers.
key_points: Ken Thompson wrote the first Unix in just one month in August 1969, spending one week each on the OS, shell, editor, and assembler
The Unix family has evolved into open source (Linux, BSDs), mixed source (Mac OS X), and surprisingly persistent closed source variants (AIX, HP/UX)
Unix philosophy embodies 'worse is better'—a willingness to accept imperfection to avoid larger problems and find elegance in practical reality
The fundamental cultural divide: Unix values code useful to programmers while Windows values code useful to end users
Being a pragmatic developer means studying both platforms rather than engaging in tribal warfare
money_quotes: A willingness to live with a little less to avoid the bigger mess and a willingness to see elegance in the real rather than the vision.
Unix culture values code which is useful to other programmers, while Windows culture values code which is useful to non-programmers.
I'm a fan of whatever the heck works.

On one side, you have hundreds of command line applications, built in wildly different styles, with thousands of arcane command line parameters, all of which can be flexibly combined together to accomplish almost anything. And on the other side, you have the windows registry and MFC.
Sometimes, you just can't win.
I've developed a grudging respect for Unix.
posts.key_insight: The Unix philosophy of accepting imperfection to achieve practical elegance—'worse is better'—offers enduring wisdom for programmers regardless of their platform allegiance.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood enthusiastically recommends 'Regular Expressions Cookbook' by Jan Goyvaerts and Steven Levithan as the definitive regex reference for working programmers. He contrasts it with Friedl's 'Mastering Regular Expressions,' which he found too academic and lacking practical examples. The new book excels by providing real-world regex examples, covering common gotchas like flavor differences and catastrophic backtracking, and being updated for current standards. Even as a self-described advanced regex user, Atwood found new insights within the first 50 pages.
