key_points: Atwood openly identifies as a 'total regular expression fanboy' despite colleagues' eye-rolls at his regex-heavy code
He criticizes Friedl's classic regex book as too dry and academic for working programmers
The cookbook approach with practical, real-world examples is more valuable than abstract string-matching puzzles
Understanding regex gotchas like flavor differences between languages and catastrophic backtracking is essential
Even advanced users can learn new techniques from well-structured reference material
money_quotes: I am a total regular expression fanboy. It's almost embarrassing how much I love the damn things.
You might end up wondering if Q*Bert just vomited all over your source code. Or you might be programming in Perl. Is there any difference?
I only advocate responsible and judicious use of regular expressions when you happen to be dealing with strings. In the wrong hands, regular expressions can be dangerous.
I found it dry, a bit academic, and lacking in practical real world examples. It just didn't speak to me as a working programmer.
I consider myself a fairly advanced regex user and about 50 pages in I've already had three big 'oh, wow, I didn't realize that' moments.
Regular Expressions Cookbook manages to be simultaneously accessible and almost ridiculously comprehensive.
posts.key_insight: The best programming references prioritize practical, real-world examples and common pitfalls over academic completeness, because working programmers learn best from solutions they can immediately apply.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on a security incident where his team's encryption implementation failed, ultimately concluding that the root cause wasn't poor understanding, copy-pasting code, or inadequate code review—it was working at the wrong level of abstraction. Rather than using low-level cryptography primitives, they should have used a proven library like Bouncy Castle or Keyczar. He argues that abstractions are fundamental to programming progress and that higher-level libraries make it harder to misuse cryptography. The post advocates for deprecating insecure methods and making secure alternatives more attractive to developers.
key_points: The real problem with encryption failures often isn't understanding or code review—it's choosing to work with primitives instead of proven libraries
Programming history can be viewed as slowly clawing up the evolutionary tree of abstraction from assembly to modern frameworks
Rather than blocking programmers from low-level APIs, offer more attractive high-level alternatives
Libraries force developers to work at higher abstraction levels, making cryptographic misuse harder
Usability improvements are better handled by domain specialists who created the library than by framework generalists
money_quotes: The root problem was that we were working at the wrong layer of abstraction.
You can save a tremendous amount of time and effort by using the browser-independent framework that JQuery has spent untold man-hours testing, debugging, and proving in the field.
Don't reinvent the wheel, unless you plan on learning more about wheels.
You could view most of computer programming history as slowly, painfully clawing our way up the evolutionary tree of abstraction.
It's a testament to the power of abstraction that any of it works at all.
Putting roadblocks in front of programmers is tantamount to a challenge; why not offer them more attractive alternatives, instead?
So the next time you set out to write code – not just encryption code, any code – ask yourself: am I working at the right level of abstraction?
posts.key_insight: Security failures and many other programming mistakes often stem not from lack of skill or diligence, but from working at the wrong level of abstraction—proven libraries exist precisely to prevent us from reinventing wheels poorly.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that URL shortening services, popularized by Twitter's 140-character limit, pose a serious threat to the web's fundamental architecture. He explains how Twitter's refusal to implement basic hyperlink markup forced users to adopt third-party URL shorteners, which have evolved from simple utilities into vehicles for tracking, advertising, and content manipulation. Atwood contends that these services insert themselves as intermediaries in the web's hyperlink system, potentially undermining PageRank and the open web. He suggests that if URL shortening must exist, it should be handled by search engines who already index all URLs rather than opportunistic startups building businesses on web infrastructure fragmentation.
key_points: Twitter's technical decision to not support hyperlink markup forced mainstream adoption of URL shortening services
URL shorteners add a fragile layer of indirection to the web, creating new points of failure assembled from 'unvetted PHP and MySQL'
Modern URL shorteners have evolved from utilities into tools for tracking, advertising, framing content, and monetizing clicks
The hyperlink is the foundation of PageRank and the web itself, making URL shortener intermediaries fundamentally dangerous
Search engines would be the logical providers of URL shortening since they already cache every URL
money_quotes: The humble hyperlink, thanks to pervasive URL shortening, can now be wielded as a weapon.
Every tiny URL is another baby step towards destroying the web as we know it.
The current situation is completely accidental.
URL shorteners are no longer viewed as utility services to make URLs more convenient, but a way to subjugate, control, and perhaps worst of all, 'monetize' the web experience.
The internet is the house that PageRank built, and it's all predicated on hyperlinks.
As a software developer, you'll be fortunate to build one project that achieves critical mass in your entire life.
Twitter, I can assure you, is far more brain damaged than you can possibly imagine.
posts.key_insight: Twitter's arbitrary technical constraints accidentally created a thriving ecosystem of URL shortening parasites that threaten the web's fundamental architecture of transparent, direct hyperlinks.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the DiggBar framing controversy and concludes that while Digg's implementation is relatively ethical (opt-in only, uses rel=canonical, no ads), the broader security implications of framing are deeply concerning. He demonstrates that frame-busting JavaScript can be defeated by frame-busting-busting code, creating an unwinnable arms race. The post reveals that due to cross-domain JavaScript security restrictions, there's no reliable way to prevent a malicious site from framing your content. This makes clickjacking attacks—where users unknowingly perform actions on hidden pages—a serious and underappreciated threat that Atwood experienced firsthand on Twitter.
key_points: Digg's framing implementation is relatively ethical: opt-in for logged-in users, uses rel=canonical for SEO, serves no ads, and allows opt-out
Frame-busting JavaScript provides only a false sense of security because it can be defeated by counter-scripts using the onbeforeunload event and 204 HTTP responses
Cross-domain JavaScript security restrictions make it nearly impossible for framed sites to defend against determined attackers
Framing is the gateway to clickjacking attacks, where users perform unintended actions on hidden authentic pages
Websites should implement clickjacking countermeasures assuming they could be framed at any time, regardless of frame-busting code
money_quotes: If an evil website decides it's going to frame your website, you will be framed. Period.
Frame-busting is nothing more than a false sense of security; it doesn't work.
When life serves you lemons, make a lemon cannon.
Don't let frame-busting code lull you into a false sense of security, too.
If the framing site is evil, good luck. When faced with a determined, skilled adversary that wants to frame your content, all bets are off.
The users think that they are clicking the visible buttons, while they are actually performing actions on the hidden page.
I was a skeptic. I didn't want to believe it either. But once shown the exploits on our own site – fortunately, by a white hat security expert – I lived to regret that.
posts.key_insight: Frame-busting JavaScript creates a dangerous false sense of security because determined attackers can always defeat it, making clickjacking countermeasures essential for any website.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the Monty Hall problem not to explain its solution, but to marvel at how viscerally people reject the correct answer. He recounts how Marilyn vos Savant received thousands of angry letters—including from nearly 1,000 PhDs—when she correctly stated that contestants should switch doors. Even the legendary mathematician Paul Erdős refused to believe the solution until he watched a computer simulation. Atwood uses this as a meditation on how poorly human brains handle counterintuitive probability problems, suggesting we should be humble about our cognitive limitations.
key_points: 92% of Americans who responded to Marilyn vos Savant's column agreed she was wrong—when she was actually correct
Nearly 1,000 PhDs wrote angry letters insisting the correct answer was wrong, demonstrating that expertise doesn't prevent intuition from overriding logic
Paul Erdős, one of the 20th century's greatest mathematicians, refused to believe the solution even when shown a formal proof—only a computer simulation convinced him
The 'Monty Fall' and 'Monty Crawl' variants help illuminate why the host's intentional behavior matters to the probability calculation
Our brains are fundamentally not wired to handle counterintuitive probability problems well
money_quotes: Americans were united: Ninety-two percent agreed Marilyn was wrong.
If all those Ph.D.s are wrong the country would be in serious trouble.
The problem is so unintuitive one of the most notable mathematicians of the last century couldn't wrap his head around it.
Only after a colleague arranged for a computer simulation in which Erdos watched hundreds of trials that came out 2-to-1 in favor of switching did Erdos concede that he was wrong.
Apparently our brains are not wired to do these sorts of probability problems very well.
I've finally stopped getting dumber.
posts.key_insight: The Monty Hall problem reveals that even brilliant experts can be blindsided by their own intuition, suggesting we should all maintain humility about the limits of human reasoning.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the trade-offs between scaling up (buying one powerful server) and scaling out (buying many cheap servers) using a real-world example: plentyoffish.com's $100,000 monster HP server versus 83 commodity Lenovo servers at the same price point. While the raw hardware comparison favors scaling out (more CPUs, RAM, and disk space), he reveals hidden costs that dramatically change the calculus: Microsoft software licensing could add $83,000 to the scale-out approach, and power costs would be 14x higher. His conclusion is that scaling out only makes economic sense when using open source software, otherwise you're trapped choosing between expensive hardware or expensive licenses.
key_points: For the same $100,000, scaling out yields 10x the CPUs, 30% more RAM, and 10x the disk space compared to a single big-iron server
Microsoft licensing costs for 83 servers would exceed $83,000, nearly doubling the total investment
Power consumption for the scale-out approach would be nearly 14x higher, adding $20,000+ annually in electricity costs
Enterprise software licensing models create a hidden tax that fundamentally changes the economics of infrastructure decisions
The choice between scaling strategies depends heavily on your software stack and resource constraints, not just raw performance numbers
money_quotes: Scaling out is only frictionless when you use open source software.
Scaling up means paying less for licenses and a lot more for hardware, while scaling out means paying less for the hardware, and a whole lot more for licenses.
I have no idea if these are the right prices for Windows Server 2008 and SQL Server 2008, because reading about the licensing models makes my brain hurt.
Now which approach makes more sense?
I'm not picking favorites. This is presented as food for thought.
posts.key_insight: The true cost of scaling infrastructure isn't just hardware—software licensing and power consumption can completely flip the economics, making open source software the key enabler for horizontal scaling strategies.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the iPhone 3GS represents a revolutionary inflection point in software development, not just in hardware but in how software is distributed, purchased, and monetized. He predicts the iPhone will be judged more important than the original Macintosh, noting that Apple's historically controversial closed-ecosystem approach is actually perfectly suited to the cell phone market where proprietary systems are the norm. The App Store has democratized software development, allowing indie developers to reach millions of users and make real money selling apps for just 99 cents. Atwood sees this as the moment when software purchasing shifted from something only geeks do to something everyone does daily.
key_points: The iPhone 3GS is a 'no-compromises computer' that can replace GPS, netbook, Gameboy, camera, MP3 player, and many other devices
Apple's closed-ecosystem approach, problematic for computers, is perfectly matched to the inherently proprietary cell phone market
The App Store created an unprecedented opportunity for indie developers to reach worldwide audiences and build viable businesses
Revolutionary pricing economics mean users can fill their phones with apps for minimal cost while developers can still profit through volume
The iPhone marks a cultural shift where software becomes something everyone purchases daily, not just tech enthusiasts
money_quotes: I believe the iPhone will ultimately be judged a more important product than the original Apple Macintosh.
Apple was born to make a kick-ass phone.
A status quo of incompetent megacorporations who wouldn't know user friendliness or good design if it ran up behind them and bit them in the rear end of their expensive, tailored suits.
If you're a software developer, the iPhone can become a career changing device.
At these crazy low prices, why not fill your phone with cool and useful apps?
We will look back on this as the time when 'software' stopped being something that geeks buy (or worse, bootleg), and started being something that everyone buys, every day.
That's an honorable way to get rich programming, and a nice business alternative to the dog-eat-dog world of advertising subsidized apps.
posts.key_insight: The iPhone's true revolution isn't the hardware but the App Store ecosystem that democratized software distribution and made purchasing apps a mainstream consumer behavior.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the tension between the benefits of programming abstractions and their inevitable failures, using LINQ to SQL as a case study. He demonstrates how a seemingly simple database query can have unexpected performance characteristics due to underlying database complexities that the abstraction cannot hide. While acknowledging Joel Spolsky's observation that all non-trivial abstractions are leaky, Atwood argues this isn't a reason to abandon them. Instead, he advocates for a pragmatic approach: use abstractions when they make code easier to write and understand, but always be prepared to dig into the underlying implementation when problems arise.
key_points: Abstractions are powerful and necessary for maintainable software, but they inevitably leak implementation details
A LINQ to SQL query selecting all columns took 827ms while an equivalent approach using just IDs took 404ms - half the time
Database behavior is highly irregular due to indexes, query plans, memory constraints, disk I/O, and data types - things abstractions cannot fully hide
The pragmatic question isn't whether an abstraction leaks, but whether it makes code easier to write, understand, and troubleshoot
Good programmers embrace useful abstractions while understanding their limitations and being ready to work around them
money_quotes: It is a testament to the power of abstraction and how much we take it for granted that such a statement can be made at all, as if we always expected to be able to write large pieces of software in a maintainable manner.
The problem isn't Linq to SQL. The problem is that we're attempting to spackle a nice, clean abstraction over a database that is full of highly irregular and unusual real world behaviors.
We can't just pretend all our data is formatted into neat, orderly data structures sitting there in memory, lined up in convenient little queues for us to reach out and casually scoop them up.
I don't think I've ever used one that didn't leak like a sieve.
Does this abstraction make our code at least a little easier to write? To understand? To troubleshoot? Are we better off with this abstraction than we were without it?

Like desperate citizens manning a dike in a category 5 storm, we programmers keep piling up these leaky abstractions, shoring up as best we can, desperately attempting to stay ahead of the endlessly rising waters of complexity.
As much as I may curse Linq to SQL as yet another failed abstraction, I'll continue to use it. Yes, I may end up soggy and irritable at times. But it sure as heck beats drowning.
posts.key_insight: All useful programming abstractions leak, but the measure of their value isn't perfection—it's whether they make our code easier to write and understand than working without them.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts discovering that his server's 48 GB of RAM was limited to 32 GB usable because they were running Windows Server 2008 Standard edition. He uses this frustrating experience to critique the practice of market segmentation through artificial software feature restrictions. While acknowledging that tiered pricing can benefit poorer customers who might otherwise be priced out entirely, Atwood argues the real cost is cognitive overhead—forcing customers to navigate complex feature matrices and pricing tiers. He concludes that open source software's greatest advantage may be its simplicity: it only comes in one edition.
key_points: Artificial feature limitations in software editions exist purely for market segmentation and capturing consumer surplus, not technical necessity
Tiered pricing forces customers to spend mental energy comparing complex feature matrices instead of just using the product
Market segmentation can benefit poorer customers by offering lower-priced options, similar to how drug companies price medications differently for third-world countries
The practice is ubiquitous across the industry, from Microsoft to 37signals to Stack Exchange itself
Open source eliminates the cognitive burden of licensing decisions entirely—you never have to think about editions, feature matrices, or billing
money_quotes: A great disturbance in the Force, as if 17 billion bytes simultaneously cried out in terror and were suddenly silenced.
Open source software only comes in one edition: awesome.
The money is irrelevant; the expensive resource here is my brain.
What I object to isn't the money involved, but the mental overhead.

All that effort, all that poring over complex feature charts and stressing out about pricing plans, and for what? Just to get the one simple, stupid thing I care about – using all the memory in my server.
I'd almost be willing to pay for the privilege of not having to think about that stuff ever again.
posts.key_insight: The true cost of tiered software licensing isn't the money—it's the mental overhead of navigating artificial complexity to unlock capabilities your hardware already has.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to critics who claimed Stack Overflow could be cloned in a weekend by arguing that while the code itself is relatively trivial, the real work lies in the user experience and everything surrounding the code. He quotes Benjamin Pollack's insight that developers only see database schemas when looking at products, missing the tremendous polish that makes sites successful. Atwood admits Stack Overflow's source code is indeed 'kind of trivial' but emphasizes that his team's mission is creating the best experience, not the best code. He lists thirteen non-coding activities that actually drive Stack Overflow's success, from community moderation to podcasting to advertising. The post concludes that writing code is trivial and fun, but success requires stopping to do all the other 'trivial stuff' around the code.
key_points: Developers underestimate project complexity because they only see the database schema and basic HTML, not the polish that makes products successful
Stack Overflow's code is admittedly trivial, but the team's mission is to create the best experience, not the best code
Most of what makes Stack Overflow successful has nothing to do with writing code—it's community management, support, content, and business operations
Open source clones will eventually approximate commercial products, but motivated closed-source teams can outrun them for a while
Success requires stopping coding long enough to do all the other necessary work around the code
money_quotes: When you put a developer in front of StackOverflow, they don't really see StackOverflow.
A developer, asked how hard something will be to clone, simply does not think about the polish, because the polish is incidental to the implementation.
Despite Benjamin's well reasoned protests, the source code to Stack Overflow is, in fact, actually, kind of... well, trivial.
Not to write the best Stack Overflow code possible, but to create the best Stack Overflow experience possible.
We try very, very hard not to suck.
There's nothing particularly magical about the production of source code.

Writing code is trivial. And fun. And something I continue to love doing. But if you really want your code to be successful, you'll stop coding long enough to do all that other, even more trivial stuff around the code that's necessary to make it successful.
posts.key_insight: The code behind successful products is often trivial; what makes them succeed is the relentless focus on user experience and all the non-coding work that developers tend to dismiss as unimportant.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts his experience building a Markdown parser for Stack Overflow comments, using it as a parable about the dangers of insufficient testing. He initially crafted a regex that passed his simple test case, feeling confident it worked. However, when he tested against the actual database of 20,000+ real-world comments, he discovered numerous edge cases that broke his pattern. The post argues that developers must resist the seductive pull of shipping code after minimal testing and instead embrace 'brute force' testing against large, realistic datasets.
key_points: Initial confidence in code that passes a single test case is dangerously misleading
Real-world data contains edge cases that simple test strings never reveal
Having access to production data or large test corpora is invaluable for validation
Fuzz testing with random inputs can substitute when real data isn't available
Regex patterns for human input can never be perfect, but extensive testing builds reasonable confidence
money_quotes: I can feel in my bones that this thing freakin' works! It's almost like I'm being pulled toward shipping this code by some inexorable, dark, testing... force.

But wait. I have this whole database of real world comments that people have entered on Stack Overflow. Shouldn't I perhaps try my awesome regular expression on that corpus of data to see what happens?
Thank goodness I listened to my midi-chlorians and let the light side of the testing force prevail here!
So how do we fix this regex? We use the light side of the force – brute force, that is, against a ton of test cases!

This regex doesn't have to be (and probably cannot be, given the huge possible number of human inputs) perfect, but running it against a large set of input test data gives me reasonable confidence that I'm not totally screwing up.
If you think one or two simple test cases covers it, that's taking the easy (and most likely, buggy and incorrect) way out.
posts.key_insight: The temptation to ship code after it passes a single test case is a dark force that developers must resist by testing against large, realistic datasets.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents the progressive degradation of Evony's internet advertising campaign, which evolved from reasonable medieval-themed game promotions to increasingly sexualized images having nothing to do with the actual gameplay. The post uses this case study to critique the race-to-the-bottom mentality in internet advertising, comparing it to the dystopian predictions of the film Idiocracy. Atwood presents the ads in chronological order to show how the game's marketing abandoned any pretense of relevance in favor of clickbait tactics. The underlying business model—freemium games requiring massive player bases—creates pressure for attention-grabbing ads regardless of taste or accuracy.
key_points: Freemium games require enormous player bases to sustain their business model, creating pressure for aggressive advertising
Evony's ads progressively devolved from relevant medieval imagery to completely unrelated sexualized content
The advertising evolution mirrors Idiocracy's satirical prediction of marketing reduced to the lowest common denominator
Click-through optimization without ethical constraints leads to a race to the bottom in ad quality
The disconnect between advertisement content and actual product represents a fundamental dishonesty in marketing
money_quotes: This is about as far in the opposite direction as I could possibly imagine.
It's yet another way, sadly, the brilliant satire Idiocracy turned out to be right on the nose.

Evony, thanks for showing us what it means to take advertising on the internet to the absolute rock bottom... then dig a sub-basement under that, and keep on digging until you reach the white-hot molten core of the Earth.
I've always wondered what that would be like. I guess now I know.
The dystopian future of Idiocracy predicted the reduction of advertising to the inevitable lowest common denominator of all.
posts.key_insight: When advertising success is measured purely by clicks without ethical constraints, the inevitable result is a race to the bottom that abandons any connection to the actual product being sold.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that 'meta' work—discussing processes, governance, and navel-gazing about the work itself rather than doing the actual work—is a dangerous trap that can destroy communities and productivity. Using examples from blogging, podcasting forums, and MetaFilter, he demonstrates how meta-discussion crowds out substantive content and drives away newcomers who came for the actual topic. He acknowledges that some introspection is healthy 'sharpening the saw,' but warns that it easily becomes an addiction and replacement for real work. The post advocates for strict separation of meta-discussion from main content, as Stack Overflow eventually implemented, and urges readers to monitor how much time they spend on meta versus building and solving problems.
key_points: Meta-work is seductively easy compared to creating original content, making it an addictive substitute for productive work
Unchecked meta-discussion in communities acts like weeds, choking out the actual content that attracted members in the first place
Successful communities like MetaFilter survive by quarantining meta-discussion to separate spaces, creating a 'release valve' that protects core functionality
The meta problem isn't just a software issue—it's a universal human tendency applicable to all careers and walks of life
Progress comes from doing stuff, not from endlessly debating the process of how things are done
money_quotes: Meta-work becomes a reflex, a habit, an addiction, and ultimately a replacement for real productive work.
If enough productive work is replaced by navelgazing meta-work, then people will be killed. Or at least, the community will be.
So all they're talking about on this so-called 'podcasting gear' website is the podcasting gear website itself.
If you don't control it, meta-discussion, like weeds run amok in a garden, will choke out a substantial part of the normal, natural growth of a healthy community.

Meta-blogging is like masturbating. Everyone does it, and there's nothing wrong with it. But writers who regularly get out a little to explore other topics will be healthier, happier, and ultimately more interesting to be around.
It's far easier to introspect and write about the process of, say... blogging... than it is to think up, research, and write about an interesting new topic on your blog.
How is progress made in the world? By sitting around and debating the process of how things are done ad nauseam? Or by, y'know… doing stuff?
posts.key_insight: Communities and individuals must actively quarantine meta-discussion to prevent it from crowding out the substantive work and content that actually creates value.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on Tom DeMarco's surprising admission that his influential metrics-based approach to software engineering was wrong, and that software engineering as a discipline may be obsolete. DeMarco, author of seminal works like Peopleware, now argues that software development is fundamentally experimental and shouldn't be treated as traditional engineering. Atwood finds this both alarming and liberating, as it validates his own gradual realization that software development is craftsmanship rather than engineering. He argues that control is ultimately illusory in software projects, and the only path to success is cultivating professionalism and passion for the craft.
key_points: Tom DeMarco, a founding figure in software engineering methodology, publicly renounced his own metrics-based approach after decades of influence
Software development is inherently experimental in its conception, even if construction follows established patterns
The engineering metaphor for software has been misleading - craftsmanship is a more accurate model
Control and predictability in software projects are largely illusory regardless of process
Success comes from passionate craftspeople who care about their work, not from management methodologies
money_quotes: I'm gradually coming to the conclusion that software engineering is an idea whose time has come and gone.
What we do is craftsmanship, not engineering.
Control is ultimately illusory on software development projects.
Software development is and always will be somewhat experimental. The actual software construction isn't necessarily experimental, but its conception is.

The guys and gals who show up every day eager to hone their craft, who are passionate about building stuff that matters to them, and perhaps in some small way, to the rest of the world – those are the people and projects that will ultimately succeed.
Everything else is just noise.
posts.key_insight: Software development success depends not on engineering methodologies and metrics, but on cultivating craftsmanship and genuine passion among developers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that experienced software developers develop a deep hatred for software precisely because they understand how it's made. Using the example of frantically stopping his partner from installing bundled camera software, he illustrates how programmers know that most software is unnecessary, poorly written, and frustrating. He cites David Parnas on the quality problem in programming and argues that because most software is written by incompetent programmers (himself included), most software inherently sucks. The piece concludes that this hatred is actually a healthy sign of competence—a programmer who doesn't immediately identify their own code as the worst they've seen hasn't developed enough self-awareness yet.
key_points: Bundled software from hardware companies is typically unnecessary, poorly made, and written by programmers who don't prioritize quality
The software industry has a quality problem, not a quantity problem—one bad programmer creates two new jobs a year
Programmers hate software because they work at the 'sausage factory' and know how poorly it's made
The best software choice is often no software at all, or the minimum necessary from reputable sources
Self-critical awareness of one's own code quality is a marker of programming competence
money_quotes: Nobody hates software more than software developers.
One bad programmer can easily create two new jobs a year.
We work at the sausage factory, so we know how this stuff is made. And it is not pretty.
The best choice of software is often no software.
I don't look forward to installing new software. On the contrary, I dread it.
I know how hard it is to get it right.
You don't hate software enough yet.
posts.key_insight: A programmer's hatred of software—especially their own—is not cynicism but a hard-won understanding of how difficult quality software is to achieve, making self-criticism a reliable marker of competence.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reviews Windows 7 as it reaches its release-to-manufacturing milestone, characterizing it as what Vista should have been from the start. He argues that while Vista had a solid technical foundation, its poor polish and user experience drove users to stick with the aging Windows XP. Windows 7 addresses these surface-level deficiencies without fundamentally changing the core OS, making it essentially a polished Vista service pack. Atwood emphasizes that the real importance of Windows 7 is providing a compelling upgrade path to finally move the computing ecosystem off the nine-year-old Windows XP.
key_points: Windows 7 is fundamentally Vista with better polish, UI improvements, and performance optimizations rather than a revolutionary new OS
Vista's failure wasn't technical but experiential - it lacked the polish and thoughtful touches users expected
The computing ecosystem's health depends on users upgrading from outdated operating systems like XP
In-place upgrades from Vista to Windows 7 are relatively painless since the breaking changes already happened in Vista
The version number jump from 6.0 to 6.1 reflects the incremental nature of Windows 7's improvements
money_quotes: Vista was like a solid B student who shows up at your doorstep reeking of body odor and dressed in shabby clothing from the local thrift shop.
A world where people regularly use 9 year old operating systems is not a healthy computing ecosystem.
Within 5 minutes of installation it was immediately obvious to me – Windows 7 is the best Vista Service Pack ever!
Microsoft took that B student, gave him a bath and a makeover, and even improved his grades ever so slightly.
Where Vista was half-implemented and often clunky, Windows 7 is competent bordering on pleasant.
Windows 7 finally offers a compelling upgrade path from Windows XP. So from my perspective, mission accomplished. Three years late, but hey, who's counting.
I'm much more interested in the cool stuff you're creating than what OS you use to create it with.
posts.key_insight: A technically solid product can still fail if it neglects user experience and polish, and sometimes the best improvement is simply finishing what should have been done right the first time.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his five-year journey with Movable Type as his blogging platform, explaining why this Perl-based system was the right choice despite being a Windows developer. He praises Movable Type's static HTML publishing model for its performance and simplicity, which allowed his modest server to handle viral traffic spikes that would have crushed WordPress. The post also serves as a tribute to Anil Dash, who personally helped upgrade Coding Horror from version 2.66 to 4.2x. Atwood concludes that blogging changed his life and gives Movable Type his highest praise: 'It Just Works.'
key_points: Pragmatism over platform loyalty: choosing the best tool regardless of language or ecosystem is more important than sticking to familiar territory
Static HTML publishing provides massive performance advantages over dynamic systems, easily handling hundreds of thousands of pageviews on modest hardware
Simplicity and minimalism in software design (KISS principle) often outperform feature-rich alternatives
The people and community behind software matter as much as the technology itself
WordPress's CPU-intensive nature would have been unsuitable for handling viral traffic on a modest server
money_quotes: My attitude has always been to put religion aside and use what works, regardless of language or platform.
Why not do the simplest possible thing that could work?
It Just Works.
A Perl and MySQL based blogging platform may seem like an odd choice for a Windows-centric developer like me, but I felt it was the best of the available blog solutions at the time.
Today, we have embarrassing amounts of CPU power and memory in our servers, and a plethora of good virtualization solutions.
In all honesty, blogging changed my life.
I am a minimalist who likes his pages austere.
posts.key_insight: The best technology choices prioritize pragmatism over platform loyalty, and often the simplest solution that works reliably outperforms more sophisticated alternatives.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the surprising efficiency of paper as a data storage medium by comparing various visual encoding methods, from punch cards to QR codes to printed text. He introduces PaperBack, software that can store up to 500KB on a single printed page using bitmap encoding. The post argues that while digital storage formats constantly evolve and become obsolete, paper has proven archival longevity measured in centuries. Atwood makes the case that for critical data like cryptographic keys, combining digital encoding with physical paper offers unique advantages in durability and human-readability that modern storage media cannot match.
key_points: The alphabet itself is a form of visual encoding that we process automatically, but it's not the most efficient way to store data on paper
A single sheet of paper can store up to 500,000 bytes using bitmap encoding versus roughly 10,000 characters of printed text
Paper has superior archival longevity compared to digital media - lasting hundreds of years versus the uncertain lifespan of CDs, tapes, and hard drives
The storage industry is littered with obsolete formats, making long-term data retrieval uncertain even when physical media survives
For critical backups like cryptographic keys, paper serves as a reliable last-resort recovery method
money_quotes: The alphabet is no different than any other optical machine readable input, except the machines are us.
Paper the way we typically use it is criminally inefficient. It has a ton of wasted data storage space.
By looking on CD or magnetic tape, you are not able to tell whether your data is readable or not.

For paper, on the other hand, to claim it will last for 100 years is not even vaguely impressive. High-quality paper with good ink regularly lasts many hundreds of years even under less than optimal conditions.
The storage industry is littered with old now-dead ways of storing data.
Computer encoding formats and data storage schemes come and go.
A combination of simple digital encoding and the good old boring, reliable, really really old school technology of physical paper can still make sense.
posts.key_insight: Despite constant technological advancement in digital storage, paper remains a uniquely reliable archival medium because its longevity is measured in centuries and its readability doesn't depend on having compatible hardware.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software has traditionally been overpriced, using Valve's Steam sales data and iPhone App Store psychology to make his case. He shows that Valve's experiments with discounting revealed dramatic non-linear returns: a 75% discount generated a 1470% increase in revenue, not just units sold. Atwood contrasts Apple's $29 OS X upgrade with Microsoft's $119 Windows 7 upgrade, suggesting Microsoft would make more money at lower price points. The post advocates for pricing software at the 'why not?' threshold where impulse purchases become frictionless. While acknowledging this may not apply to all markets, he encourages software vendors to experiment with aggressive pricing strategies.
key_points: The iPhone App Store has fundamentally shifted consumer expectations about software pricing, making anything over $5 feel expensive
Valve's Steam data shows dramatically non-linear returns on discounts: a 50% price cut during Left 4 Dead's sale generated more revenue than the original launch
A 75% discount resulted in a 1470% increase in total dollar sales, not just units shipped
Microsoft's $119 Windows upgrade pricing likely leaves significant money on the table compared to Apple's $29 approach
For high-volume consumer software, pricing at the 'why not?' impulse-purchase level may maximize total revenue
money_quotes: But applications that cost $5 or more? Outrageous! Highway robbery!
Software should be priced low enough to pass the average user's 'why not' threshold.
It's sobering to think that cutting the price in half, months later, made more money for Valve in total than launching the game at its original $49.95 price point.
If this pattern Valve documented holds true, and if my experience on the iPhone App store is any indication, we've been doing software pricing completely wrong.
I'm sure the thought of that drives the Redmond consumer surplus capturing marketing weasels apoplectic.
I love buying software, and I know I buy a heck of a lot more of it when it's priced right. So why not?
posts.key_insight: For digitally distributed consumer software, pricing below the impulse-purchase threshold can generate dramatically more total revenue than traditional higher pricing, as demonstrated by Valve's Steam experiments showing a 75% discount yielding 1470% more dollars.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the paradox of COBOL, a language that allegedly runs 80% of the world's actively used code yet seems invisible to modern programmers. He notes that despite 220 billion lines of COBOL processing everything from ATMs to air traffic control, he's never met anyone who actively writes it. The post examines how COBOL sits atop 'dead language' lists while simultaneously being critical infrastructure, and highlights the challenge of maintaining 20-year-old systems with younger developers who see COBOL work as a punishment. Atwood closes with a comparison of C# and COBOL.NET code that speaks for itself about why the language feels both essential and extinct.
key_points: COBOL processes 200 times more daily transactions than Google searches, yet most programmers have never encountered it
The language simultaneously tops 'dead skills' lists while running 80% of the world's actively used code
Legacy COBOL systems face a maintenance crisis as younger developers resist working with the technology
Companies treat COBOL work as something to endure, promising developers a 'switch to other things at the earliest opportunity'
COBOL.NET exists as an attempt to modernize, but the verbose syntax compared to C# illustrates why the language struggles to attract new blood
money_quotes: There are over 220 billion lines of COBOL in existence, a figure which equates to around 80% of the world's actively used code.
If these 220 billion lines of COBOL code are truly running out there somewhere, where are all the COBOL programmers?

Did they write software systems so perfect, so bug-free, that all these billions of lines of code are somehow maintaining themselves without the need for legions and armies of COBOL programmers, decades later?
Y2k was like a second gold rush for Cobol programmers who were seeing dwindling need for their skills. But six-and-a-half years later, there's no savior in sight for this fading language.
Despite everyone pronouncing Cobol dead for a couple of decades, it's still around.
He simply tells them they must do some Cobol work, promising a switch to other things at the earliest opportunity.
COBOL: so vibrantly alive, yet... so very, very dead.
posts.key_insight: COBOL represents the ultimate technical debt paradox: infrastructure so critical it can never be replaced, yet so unloved that no one wants to maintain it.

posts.summary: Atwood examines how Web 2.0 platforms profit from user-generated content while users work for free, drawing parallels to sharecropping. He highlights how sites like MySpace and Facebook provide tools for self-expression while retaining ownership and economic rewards. The post questions whether this arrangement is exploitative, noting that individual contributions have trivial economic value but become lucrative when aggregated at massive scale. Users remain 'happy' because they seek social connection rather than profit.
key_points: Web 2.0 companies distribute the means of production but concentrate economic rewards among themselves
Users willingly contribute free labor because their motivation is self-expression and socializing, not money
Individual user contributions have trivial economic value; only massive aggregation creates lucrative businesses
The 'digital sharecropping' model parallels historical sharecropping where workers don't own what they produce
money_quotes: It's a labor of love.
MySpace, Facebook, and many other businesses have realized that they can give away the tools of production but maintain ownership over the resulting products.
One of the fundamental economic characteristics of Web 2.0 is the distribution of production into the hands of the many and the concentration of the economic rewards into the hands of the few.
It's a sharecropping system, but the sharecroppers are generally happy because their interest lies in self-expression or socializing, not in making money.
It's only by aggregating those contributions on a massive scale – on a web scale – that the business becomes lucrative.
posts.key_insight: Web 2.0 platforms have created a new form of digital sharecropping where users happily provide free labor for self-expression while companies capture all the economic value through massive aggregation.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to a programmer who dismisses web development as beneath 'real' programmers, arguing that the web has already won as the dominant software platform. He contends that web applications embody the principle of least power—doing the simplest thing that works—and that this is a feature, not a bug. The web's unmatched distribution capabilities mean any programmer who wants their software actually used should embrace it. While acknowledging web programming is 'downright kludgy,' Atwood argues the democratization it enables far outweighs its technical limitations.
key_points: The desktop application is already dead for most typical users—web apps have replaced them
The web embodies the 'principle of least power,' choosing simple solutions over powerful ones
Web distribution eliminates traditional barriers: purchasing, licensing, downloading, installing, maintaining
Even brilliant programmers benefit from web's reach to get code in front of users they'd never reach otherwise
Atwood's Law: any application that can be written in JavaScript will eventually be written in JavaScript
money_quotes: You hope everything doesn't 'move to the web'? Wake the hell up! It's already happened!
The web is the very embodiment of doing the stupidest simplest thing that could possibly work.
If you want your software to be experienced by as many users as possible, there is absolutely no better route than a web app.
Writing Photoshop, Word, or Excel in JavaScript makes zero engineering sense, but it's inevitable.
What's the point of all this craftsmanship if your software ends up locked away in a binary executable?
There's nothing sadder, for my money, than code that dies unknown and unloved.
Your crummy, half-assed web app will still be more successful than your competitor's most polished software application.
Pretty soon, all programming will be web programming.
posts.key_insight: Software that reaches users matters more than technical sophistication, and the web's unparalleled distribution makes it the inevitable platform for nearly all programming.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses Microsoft Bob and the Pinball 2000 project to illustrate that project failure is not only common in software development but often leads to future success. He notes that Bob's team went on to found Valve and create Half-Life, while the Pinball 2000 engineers continued thriving in their field despite their project being cancelled at the height of success. The post argues that failure is the norm in software development and that the real measure of a project isn't its market outcome but what you learned from it. Atwood concludes that the only true failure is a project from which you learned nothing.
key_points: Microsoft Bob, widely considered Microsoft's biggest flop, produced engineers who went on to found Valve and create industry-defining products like Half-Life
The Pinball 2000 project succeeded critically and commercially yet was still cancelled due to business forces beyond the engineers' control
Failure is the statistical norm in software development—most developers have worked on more failed projects than successful ones
The best surgeons (and by extension, engineers) are those who openly acknowledge and learn from their mistakes rather than deflecting blame
The journey and learning from a project matter more than the outcome—seeking failure isn't necessary because it will find you
money_quotes: The only truly failed project is the one where you didn't learn anything along the way.
Unless you're an exceptionally lucky software developer, you've probably worked on more projects that failed than projects that succeeded.
Odds are, you're working on a project that will fail right now.
Sometimes, even when your project succeeds, you've failed. Due to forces entirely beyond your control.
Failure is a wonderful teacher. But there's no need to seek out failure. It will find you.
The journey of the project should be its own reward, regardless of whatever happens to lie at the end of that journey.
Bob is less of a comic figure than a tragic one.
posts.key_insight: Project failure should be measured not by market outcomes but by whether you learned and grew from the experience, since failure is inevitable in software development and often precedes greater success.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the ego-driven motivations behind blogging, admitting that most bloggers write because they're 'raving egomaniacs' who love hearing about themselves. He shares a screenshot of someone who apparently had a frustrated reaction to his writing, celebrating it as validation. The post concludes with Atwood's belief that professional developers recognize their own code is flawed, and if his writing helps other programmers reach that same humbling epiphany, then his blog is serving its purpose.
key_points: Bloggers are primarily motivated by ego and the desire to hear others talk about them, despite rationalizations about public good
Having Google ego searches for your own name is standard practice in the blogging world
The difference between amateur and professional developers is that professionals realize everything they write sucks
Self-awareness about one's limitations as a developer is a critical professional milestone
Provoking strong reactions (even negative ones) in readers indicates the writing is having an impact
money_quotes: Most of us blog because we're raving egomaniacs.
We not only love to hear ourselves talk, we're incredibly eager to hear other people talk about us, and the more the better.
Nothing is sweeter to someone's ears than their own name.
As a software developer, you are your own worst enemy.
The sooner you realize that, the better off you'll be.
That's the tipping point between amateurs and professionals in our industry: the professionals realize everything they write sucks.
To the extent that I can become a conduit for other programmers to have that same epiphany in their own programming careers, that means it's working.
posts.key_insight: Professional maturity in software development comes from recognizing that your own code is deeply flawed, and helping others reach that humbling realization is valuable work.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses a game development war story about endless geometry patching to illustrate a common software development antipattern: continuously applying small fixes to fundamentally flawed code instead of addressing the root cause. The story describes a PS1 game port where floating-point to fixed-point conversion created countless geometry holes that testers found daily. Atwood confesses to falling into this same trap with Stack Overflow code, where fear of breaking working functionality led to months of incremental patches. He argues that the 'just this small change, just this once' rationalization is a dangerous lie developers tell themselves. The post advocates for overcoming fear and doing the fundamental rewrite when patching becomes a pattern.
key_points: The 'patch it again' cycle often stems from fear of rewriting working code, even when that code has become fundamentally flawed
There's never time to do it right, but there's always time to do it over - a costly paradox in software development
Working code that requires constant tweaking is a sign of underlying architectural problems that patches cannot solve
Self-awareness about the 'just this small change' rationalization is the first step toward breaking the patching cycle
Fear of breaking core functionality often leads to worse long-term outcomes than a proper rewrite would
money_quotes: There's never time to do it right, but there's always time to do it over.
It's the kind of code that, when submitted for code review, you're tempted to self-deprecatingly introduce as have you met my dog, patches?
The problem here was that the geometry was bad.
It was obvious this was working code which had become crufty over time, but it was working.
It's easy to mentally justify 'just this small change, just this once' rather than the fundamental rewrite you really need.
When you finally realize you've spent the last six months telling yourself the same 'just this small change, just this once' lie, then you've met your dog patches, too.
posts.key_insight: When you find yourself repeatedly patching the same code with 'just this small change,' you're not fixing the problem—you're postponing the inevitable rewrite while accumulating technical debt.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that corporate-speak and generic stock photos are communication barriers that harm rather than help businesses. He builds on the concept of 'happy talk'—self-congratulatory content that conveys no useful information—and extends it to visual elements like stock photos of diverse people smiling at computers. Drawing on examples from Jason Cohen and Kathy Sierra, he shows how misguided professionalism leads companies to adopt meaningless marketing language and sterile imagery. The post concludes with a simple directive borrowed from Elmore Leonard: if something looks corporate, change it.
key_points: Happy talk and corporate stock photos are visual and textual barriers that prevent genuine communication
Small companies mistakenly believe they need to look like big companies to appear legitimate
The word 'professionalism' is often used as justification to avoid authentic, passionate communication
Corporate doublespeak hides behind buzzwords and hyphens instead of explaining actual value
Emulating large company communication patterns means emulating what's wrong with them
money_quotes: Happy talk is the kudzu of the internet; the place is lousy with the stuff.
Given the beatific expressions, you'd think they were undergoing some kind of nerd rapture.
That's what you're supposed to do, right? That's what other companies do, so it must be right. Who am I to break with tradition?
I cannot count the times I heard the word 'professionalism' used as justification for why we couldn't do something.
It's a shame that this misguided sense of professionalism is sometimes used as an excuse to put up weird, Orwellian communication barriers between yourself and the world.
At best it is a facade to hide behind; at worst it encourages us to emulate so much of what is wrong with large companies.
If it looks corporate, change it.
posts.key_insight: Corporate professionalism in communication is often a facade that hinders genuine connection rather than building credibility.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reviews Dan Ariely's 'Predictably Irrational' and presents nine psychological manipulation tactics that marketers exploit to influence consumer behavior. The post examines cognitive biases including anchoring, the power of 'free,' loss aversion, and the endowment effect, providing concrete examples from behavioral economics research. For each tactic, Atwood offers practical defensive strategies to help readers recognize and resist manipulation. The core message is that understanding our irrational tendencies is essential self-defense against those who would exploit them.
key_points: Marketers deliberately exploit predictable human irrationality through tactics like false comparisons, anchoring, and leveraging 'free' offers
The endowment effect causes us to dramatically overvalue things we already own - Duke students valued basketball tickets they won at $2,400 while non-winners would only pay $170
Loss aversion is so powerful that MIT students sacrificed 15% of their earnings just to keep worthless options open, even when told the exact payoffs
Price creates expectations that affect perceived quality - subjects reported more pain relief from a fake painkiller priced at $2.50 than the identical pill priced at $0.10
Awareness of these cognitive biases is the first step to defending against manipulation - we don't need to be perfectly rational, just aware of when we're most vulnerable
money_quotes: If you don't understand how these irrational behaviors are triggered, the marketing weasels will use them against you.

You will tend to overestimate the value of items you get for free. Resist this by viewing free stuff skeptically rather than welcoming it with open arms. If it was really that great, why would it be free?
When you use a free service or product, you are implicitly endorsing and encouraging the provider, effectively beating a path to their door.
The value of what you've spent so far on a service, product, or relationship – in effort or money – is probably far less than you think. Be willing to walk away.
We feel compelled to preserve options, even at great expense, even when it doesn't make sense.
Price often has nothing to do with value. Expensive is not synonymous with quality.
Don't fall prey to the 'moneymoon'; just because you paid for something doesn't mean it's automatically worthwhile.

Everyone is irrational sometimes, and that's OK. We're not perfectly logical Vulcans, after all. The trick is training yourself to know when you're most likely to make irrational choices, and to resist those impulses.
posts.key_insight: Understanding the predictable patterns of human irrationality is essential self-defense against marketers who systematically exploit these cognitive biases for profit.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that email has become a productivity-destroying addiction mechanism, functioning exactly like a slot machine through variable reinforcement schedules. Drawing on Dan Ariely's explanation of Skinner's experiments, he shows how the unpredictable reward pattern of occasionally receiving useful emails keeps us compulsively checking, even though most messages are worthless. The post critiques how email has been overloaded to handle every type of communication, causing it to fail at all of them. Atwood proposes using the right communication tool for each situation—Twitter for social updates, phone calls for urgent matters, forums for discussions—rather than funneling everything through email.
key_points: Variable reinforcement schedules (unpredictable rewards) are more addictive than fixed schedules, which is why email checking becomes compulsive
Email has been overloaded to serve every communication need—urgent questions, long discussions, social sharing—causing it to fail at everything
The solution is matching communication medium to message type: phone for urgent, forums for discussions, social networks for casual updates
Checking email constantly undermines the flow state essential to productive work and perpetuates the multitasking myth
Practical countermeasures include disabling notifications, checking at scheduled intervals, and filtering to highlight emails from known valuable senders
money_quotes: We delude ourselves into thinking we're being extra-productive by obsessively checking and responding to our email, but in reality we're attending too frequently to our own desire for gratification and sabotaging our own productivity in the process.

Go ahead, pull the 'new email' lever. Take a chance. Most of the time you'll end up a loser, the proud recipient of yet another spam email, a press release you don't care about, or some irrelevant conversation someone has cc:ed you into.

We're so ecstatic to get that single useful email out of hundreds that we can't keep ourselves from compulsively pressing the new email lever over and over and over, hoping it will happen again soon, like the caged rats in Skinners' experiments.
When we treat email as the kitchen sink of communication, appropriate for everything, it simply ceases to work at all.
When the rewards ceased, the rats who were under the fixed schedule stopped working almost immediately, but those under the variable schedules kept working for a very long time.
Before you send that next email – or press the 'retrieve mail' button again – ask yourself: do I smell a rat?
posts.key_insight: Email's addictive power comes not from its usefulness but from its unpredictability—the same variable reinforcement mechanism that makes gambling irresistible keeps us checking our inbox compulsively, destroying the focused work we think we're protecting.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines Ted Nelson's ambitious Project Xanadu, a 1963 vision for hypertext that far exceeded what became the World Wide Web. He contrasts Xanadu's 17 comprehensive rules—covering identity, redundancy, bidirectional links, and micropayments—with the web's fundamental brokenness: link rot, fragmented identity systems, and no built-in redundancy. Despite the web implementing only 'simple, stupid, mindless hyperlinks,' it succeeded where Xanadu's perfectionism failed. Atwood argues this illustrates a crucial lesson: shipping an 80% solution that works beats dreaming of perfection that never materializes.
key_points: Ted Nelson envisioned hypertext in 1963 with features like bidirectional links, version management, and micropayments that the web still lacks
The modern web suffers from fundamental problems: link rot, fragmented identity, and no redundancy—all issues Xanadu aimed to solve
Google built a trillion-dollar business analyzing relationships between 'dumb hyperlinks' that barely work
The web succeeded precisely because it implemented the simplest possible version of hyperlinks rather than a comprehensive system
Shipping an imperfect solution that solves 80% of the problem beats never shipping a perfect one
money_quotes: Ted Nelson is the archetypal borderline autistic, non-conformist, free-thinking technologist.
The current state of hyperlinking is almost literally the stupidest thing we could build that works.
Frankly, the current system sucks beyond belief.
HTML is precisely what we were trying to prevent – ever-breaking links, links going outward only, quotes you can't follow to their origins, no version management, no rights management.
The odds of a hyperlink working are inversely proportional to the age of that hyperlink.
The bottom line is that a lot of the time it's OK to create a solution that solves 80% of the problem. Always remember that shipping is a feature.
It might not be the Xanadu you dreamed of – heck, it might even suck – but it'll at least have a fighting chance of existing in reality rather than fantasy.
posts.key_insight: The web's success over Xanadu proves that shipping a flawed but functional solution beats endlessly perfecting an ambitious vision that never exists.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that SSDs have finally matured into worthwhile investments after years of disappointing early adopters with poor performance and high prices. He recounts his own disillusionment with first-generation SSDs before being convinced by Linus Torvalds' endorsement of Intel's X-25 drives. The post documents the emergence of competitive alternatives like the Indilinx-controller Crucial drives, which match Intel's performance at lower prices. Atwood concludes that a $200-300 SSD represents the single most cost-effective performance upgrade available, far exceeding the perceptible benefits of CPU or memory upgrades.
key_points: Computer performance is limited by the slowest component, and users chronically underinvest in storage while overspending on CPUs
Early SSD drives were actually slower than magnetic drives despite vendor promises, causing widespread disappointment
Intel's X-25 series marked the turning point where SSDs finally justified their price premium
Competition from Indilinx-controller drives brought prices down while matching Intel's performance
SSDs eliminate the micro-pauses in daily computing in ways that CPU and RAM upgrades cannot match
money_quotes: Computer performance is typically limited by the slowest part in your system.
Running Windows Vista on an early 32 gigabyte SSD was an exercise in pain and frustration on so many levels.
At that moment, SSD drives came of age. And by that I mean, they began to justify their hefty price premium at last.
All those little pauses while your system pulls some chunk of data off the hard drive? They simply cease to exist.
$200 - $300 for a SSD is easily the most cost effective performance increase you can buy for a computer of anything remotely resembling recent vintage.
That's far more than I can say for most of today's CPU and memory upgrades.
The transition from magnetic storage to solid state storage is nothing less than a breakthrough.
posts.key_insight: The most impactful computer upgrade isn't the flashiest component but the one that eliminates your system's actual bottleneck—and for most users, that's storage.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reviews 'Coders at Work' by Peter Seibel, praising it as a rare example of genuinely skilled interviewing applied to influential programmers. He contrasts it with the typical low-quality internet interviews that serve as content filler. Atwood suggests the book could serve as a hiring filter—candidates who can't identify any of the 15 programmers interviewed should be an immediate no-hire. He also pays tribute to the book's inspiration, Susan Lammers' 1986 'Programmers at Work,' noting how both books demonstrate the timelessness of programming wisdom. The post concludes that understanding software requires studying not just code, but the people who create it.
key_points: Most internet interviews are lazy content filler, but 'Coders at Work' represents genuinely skilled journalism applied to programming luminaries
The book could serve as a job interview filter—inability to recognize any of the 15 featured programmers suggests a lack of engagement with the field
Great programming books can remain relevant for decades, as demonstrated by 'Programmers at Work' from 1986
The focus on people rather than just languages makes 'Coders at Work' more compelling than similar interview collections
Software development is fundamentally about people, not just code
money_quotes: If the internet has perfected anything, it's the art of the crappy, phoned-in, half-assed email 'interview.'
The purpose of most of these interviews is quick and dirty content filler with semi-famous folk spouting off whatever random thoughts they happen to have in their head at that exact moment.
Interviews take an enormous amount of time and an enormous amount of legitimate, skilled journalistic effort to get right. Almost nobody does.
If, after reading what these brilliant programmers have to say, you aren't motivated to research some programming topic mentioned inside, pack it in, because you aren't even trying any more.
It is a testament to the timelessness of not just code, but the art of coding.
Computers are mostly a reflection of the people using them.
In the art of software development, studying code isn't enough: you have to study the people behind the software, too.
posts.key_insight: Understanding software development requires studying the programmers themselves, not just their code—and recognizing the influential figures in your field is a basic marker of professional engagement.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on a fundamental lesson learned while building Stack Overflow: users simply will not read anything on the screen that isn't absolutely essential to their immediate task. Despite providing toolbars, help buttons, formatting guides, and real-time previews on the ask question page, users consistently submit poorly formatted questions. This 'user myopia' affects everyone from casual power users to professional programmers. The solution, Atwood concludes, is to place critical guidance directly in the user's unavoidable line of sight—such as pre-populating form fields with example formatting—rather than relying on peripheral help elements that users will ignore.
key_points: Users will only read the absolute minimum text necessary to complete their task, ignoring all peripheral help and guidance
Even sophisticated users like programmers on Stack Overflow fail to follow formatting instructions despite multiple forms of assistance
The gap between how UI designers see a page (full of helpful tools) versus how users see it (just the input field) is enormous
Help elements must be placed directly in the user's unavoidable path, not in toolbars or sidebars they can ignore
Pre-populating input fields with example content may be more effective than any amount of surrounding documentation
money_quotes: The plain fact is users will not read anything you put on the screen.
Users do read. But users will only read the absolute minimum amount of text on the screen necessary to complete their task.
I can't quite explain it, but this kind of user myopia is epidemic. It's the same problem, everywhere I turn.
We're not particularly good, but we do try very, very hard not to suck.
Think long and hard about placing things directly in front of them, where they are not just visible, but unavoidable. Otherwise they might not be seen at all.
Those who can't learn from history are doomed to repeat it, I guess.
posts.key_insight: Effective UI design must account for user myopia by placing essential guidance directly in the user's unavoidable line of sight, not in peripheral help elements they will instinctively ignore.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits the once-sacred web design rule about 'the fold' – the idea that critical content must appear before users need to scroll. While the 1996 belief that users don't scroll has been thoroughly debunked, Atwood argues there are still subtle dangers to watch for. He identifies three specific pitfalls: cramming too much above the fold, creating horizontal lines that make pages look complete, and using in-page scrollbars that confuse users. After analyzing his own site's analytics and design, he found they were wasting valuable space at the top with irrelevant UI elements, potentially creating barriers to scrolling for the 25% of users with smaller screens.
key_points: The 1996 rule that 'users don't scroll' was true at the time but became obsolete as users gained web experience – scrolling is now a fundamental skill
Some people still weaponize the outdated fold rule, demanding all content appear above an arbitrary line
Pages that 'look scrollable' encourage exploration – stark horizontal lines that align with the fold are the main factor that stops users from scrolling
Browser chrome (toolbars, tabs, status bars) means the fold is much closer than designers typically assume
The most important content should still go at the top, not because users can't scroll, but because it's basic good design
money_quotes: If you can't learn to scroll within an hour or so of using the web, you're going to have an awfully stunted experience – so much so that you're probably better off not using it at all.
Even today, people will cite the ancient, irrelevant rule of The Fold as if it's still law.
Users will explore and find your content – as long as the page 'looks' scrollable.
The fold is probably much closer than you think it is.
It's not only a basic rule of writing, it's also a basic rule of the web: put the most important content at as close to the top of the page as you can.
The fold is far less important than it used to be, but it isn't quite as mythical as Bigfoot and the Loch Ness Monster quite yet.
posts.key_insight: While the rigid 'above the fold' rule is outdated superstition, thoughtful designers should still prioritize top content and avoid visual elements that accidentally signal a page is complete.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces Stack Overflow Careers, a CV hosting and job matching service built on top of the Stack Overflow community. He reflects on Stack Overflow's first year, emphasizing that the platform's success comes entirely from the programmers who participate, not from the team behind it. The new careers product aims to fix what Atwood and Joel Spolsky see as broken hiring practices by connecting engaged programmers with companies that truly value engineering talent. The service offers free public CV hosting with optional paid features for job seekers and substantial fees for employers, inverting the typical job-seeking dynamic so companies pursue programmers rather than the reverse.
key_points: Stack Overflow's value comes from its community of participating programmers, not from its creators or code
The 'pay it forward' model of programmers helping peers creates a permanently archived knowledge base for future developers
Current programmer hiring practices (Monster, Dice, etc.) are broken and reduce candidates to acronym-matching
Stack Overflow Careers inverts the job-seeking dynamic: companies pursue programmers, not the other way around
Teaching new programmers is essential—'the future of our entire profession depends on it'
money_quotes: Stack Overflow is you.
The community is far, far smarter than I will ever be.
Bad code that isn't written, is bad code that another poor programmer won't have to debug.
The future of our entire profession depends on it.
Joel and I believe current hiring practices for programmers are incredibly broken.
Our goal isn't to put warm bodies in front of interviewers. Our goal is to create love connections.
In short, Stack Overflow Careers amplifies your awesome.
posts.key_insight: A thriving developer community creates value that can be channeled into better hiring practices, where great companies pursue great programmers rather than forcing developers to navigate broken job boards.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues for the importance of preserving early digital history, highlighting that while recent years are exhaustively documented online, the 1980s and early 1990s BBS era risks being lost forever. He profiles Jason Scott, creator of BBS: The Documentary and textfiles.com, as a crucial digital historian working to archive these early online communities. Atwood calls attention to Scott's Kickstarter campaign to fund full-time work on digital preservation projects, including the text adventure documentary Get Lamp. The post is ultimately a call to action for readers to financially support this preservation work, framing it as protecting shared geek heritage.
key_points: Early digital artifacts from the 1980s and early 1990s are at serious risk of being lost, unlike the exhaustively documented modern internet
Jason Scott's work through textfiles.com and BBS: The Documentary represents essential efforts to preserve pre-web online culture
Digital preservation work is valuable but typically unpaid, making crowdfunding a potential solution
The gap between third-party historical accounts and lived experience makes first-hand documentation crucial
This history belongs to a specific community of 'geeks, dorks, dweebs, nerds, and generally computer-obsessed misfits'
money_quotes: Today's internet is everything we couldn't have possibly dared to imagine twenty-five years ago, but there is a real risk of these early, tentative digital artifacts being lost forever in the relentless deluge of online progress.
Sure, every single thing that happened in 2004 is documented exhaustively online. But 1994? 1984? Not so much.
When the culture is your own, and when the experiences were your own, the gap between story and reality is that much wider.
Jason is nothing less than our generation's digital historian in residence.
While being a digital historian is great, it's not exactly something you get paid to do.
His history, is my history, is our history.
A history of geeks, dorks, dweebs, nerds, and generally computer-obsessed misfits, but nonetheless – it's something we all share.
posts.key_insight: The earliest era of online communities represents irreplaceable shared heritage that requires active preservation efforts before it disappears entirely.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood humorously explores the problem of invisible trailing whitespace in code, framing it as a source of programmer anxiety. He demonstrates how code can appear perfectly fine visually while harboring hidden whitespace issues that can cause problems in source control. The post acknowledges that programmers are detail-obsessed control freaks who agonize over seemingly minor issues because small mistakes can cascade into major failures. Atwood offers several practical solutions including post-build cleanup scripts, editor macros, and whitespace visibility toggles. While conceding the world won't literally end over trailing spaces, he playfully insists on vigilance against this invisible menace.
key_points: Invisible whitespace at the end of lines represents a category of code problems you can't see but that can still cause issues
Programmers are inherently detail-obsessed because even tiny mistakes like a misplaced semicolon can break everything
Practical solutions exist: post-build cleanup scripts, editor macros, whitespace highlighting rules, and always-visible whitespace mode
The obsession with invisible details is a defining trait of the programming profession, not a personal quirk
Source control makes whitespace issues more visible and problematic when checking in changes
money_quotes: Code that is visible is code that can be wrong. No surprise there. But did you know that even the code you *can't* see may be wrong, too?
