These are the questions that drive young programmers to madness.

I humbly submit that, well, you're not one of us. You don't appreciate what it's like to spend every freaking minute of every freaking day agonizing over the tiniest details of the programs you write.
Not because we *want* to, you understand, but because the world explodes when we don't.
If one semicolon is out of place, everything goes sideways. That's how programming works. It's fun! Sometimes! I swear!
We got into this industry because, quite frankly, we are control freaks. It's who we are. It's what we do.
Just because I can't see it doesn't mean it's not out to get me.
posts.key_insight: Programming demands obsessive attention to invisible details because even the code you can't see—like trailing whitespace—can cause real problems.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the infamous Stack Overflow answer about parsing HTML with regular expressions, which descended into Lovecraftian madness complete with corrupted unicode text invoking Cthulhu. While acknowledging this is a well-understood anti-pattern, he pushes back against absolute dogmatism, arguing that simple string processing tasks don't always require full parsing libraries. The real problem isn't regex itself but programmer ignorance about when each tool is appropriate. He concludes that understanding tools and their trade-offs matters more than blanket prohibitions.
key_points: The viral Stack Overflow answer about HTML parsing became legendary for its Cthulhu-themed descent into unicode madness
Parsing HTML is a solved problem - programmers should be 'lazy' and use existing libraries rather than hand-coding regex
The endless stream of novice developers attempting regex HTML parsing represents an ongoing education problem
Absolute prohibitions against regex for any HTML-related task are as wrongheaded as requiring full parsers for trivial string operations
Understanding the difference between true HTML parsing and simple string processing is the key skill to develop
money_quotes: Every time you attempt to parse HTML with regular expressions, the unholy child weeps the blood of virgins, and Russian hackers pwn your webapp.
Parsing HTML is a solved problem. You do not need to solve it. You just need to be lazy.
The real enemy isn't regular expressions (or, for that matter, goto) but ignorance.
It's more important to understand the tools, and their strengths and weaknesses, than it is to knuckle under to knee-jerk dogmatism.
Unless you have discipline and put very strict conditions on what you're doing, matching HTML with regular expressions rapidly devolves into madness, just how Cthulhu likes it.
We live in a world full of newbie PHP developers doing the first thing that pops into their collective heads, with more born every day.
Whatever method you choose – just don't leave the <cthulhu> tag open, for humanity's sake.
posts.key_insight: The danger of parsing HTML with regex isn't about regex being evil, but about programmers not understanding when simple string processing suffices versus when they actually need a real parser.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood satirically proposes 'Bad Code Offsets' as a tongue-in-cheek alternative to the hard work of actually becoming a better programmer. The concept parodies carbon offsets, allowing developers to assuage their guilt about producing bad code by donating money to open source projects. While presented humorously, the initiative has a real purpose: all proceeds go to organizations like jQuery, PostgreSQL, and the Apache Software Foundation. Atwood acknowledges that improving as a programmer through apprenticeship, practice, and community involvement is genuinely difficult work. The satire serves as both a fundraising mechanism for open source and a gentle critique of developers who don't invest in self-improvement.
key_points: Becoming a better programmer requires genuinely hard work including apprenticeship, practice, and community involvement
The 'Bad Code Offsets' concept cleverly parodies carbon offset programs while serving a real charitable purpose
All money from bad code offsets goes directly to open source projects like jQuery, PostgreSQL, and Apache
The goal is to subsidize quality open source code to counterbalance the bad code programmers inevitably produce
The satire gently mocks the desire for easy solutions to problems that require sustained effort
money_quotes: Let's face it: we all write bad code.
But that's a lot of work. Really freaking hard work!
It is, quite literally, the least you could do.
Bad Code Offsets provides the vehicle for balancing the scales of poor past practice while freeing us to pursue current excellence in code development.
For every ten thousand lines of mind-bendingly bad code produced, we hope to subsidize a thousand lines of quality open source code.
posts.key_insight: Self-improvement as a programmer is genuinely hard work, but supporting open source projects is a worthy way to contribute to code quality even if you can't eliminate your own bad code.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software developers should ship version 1 of their product even when it falls short of their vision, rather than delaying to achieve perfection. He acknowledges the many ways projects go wrong—schedule pressure, technical compromises, team friction, misunderstood customers—but contends that delaying release to fix these issues is a bigger mistake than shipping flawed software. The key insight is that real-world user feedback is more valuable than additional development time spent in isolation. By shipping early, teams can redirect their efforts based on actual usage data rather than assumptions. What matters most is not the initial quality but the team's velocity and responsiveness to user feedback after release.
key_points: Every software project ends up as a 'pale shadow' of the original vision due to schedule pressure, technical compromises, and unforeseen problems
Delaying release to fix known issues means missing out on discovering the 'unknown unknowns' that only real users can reveal
Three months of isolated polishing is less valuable than three months of iterating based on real user feedback
Team responsiveness and iteration velocity matter more than any single release's quality
Optimizing for near-perfect releases means optimizing for the wrong thing
money_quotes: At the end of the development cycle, you end up with software that is a pale shadow of the shining, glorious monument to software engineering that you envisioned when you started.
Yes, you did a ton of things wrong on this project. But you also did a ton of things wrong that you don't know about yet.

Instead of spending three months fixing up this version in a sterile, isolated lab, you could be spending that same three month period listening to feedback from real live, honest-to-god, annoying dedicated users of your software.
The velocity and responsiveness of your team to user feedback will set the tone for your software, far more than any single release ever could.
If you aren't embarrassed by v1.0 you didn't release it early enough.
To the extent that you're optimizing for near-perfect software releases, you're optimizing for the wrong thing.
Even if version 1 sucks, ship it anyway.
posts.key_insight: Real-world user feedback is more valuable than any amount of isolated development time, so ship early and iterate fast rather than pursuing perfection before release.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his experience implementing microformat support for Stack Overflow Careers CVs, finding the official documentation unhelpful and the implementation frustrating. While simple microformats like rel-tag and rel-me were easy to add, the hResume format proved ambiguous, fragile, and poorly designed. The core problem is that microformats overload CSS class attributes to carry data semantics, creating conflicts with existing styles and making the markup impossible to maintain without specialized knowledge. Despite his criticisms, Atwood concludes that microformats are still worth implementing because they're easy enough to add and Google's rich search results provide tangible user benefits while waiting for HTML5 data attributes.
key_points: Microformats overload CSS class attributes with dual meanings (styling and data), creating semantic ambiguity and potential naming conflicts with existing stylesheets
The hResume specification is surprisingly ambiguous despite claiming to be simple, requiring extensive interpretation and multiple implementation attempts to get right
Microformats handle unstructured, freeform data poorly, demanding rigidly structured fields that don't match how real-world web applications store information
There's no visual indication in code that a CSS class is a microformat field, making maintenance fragile and onboarding new developers difficult
Despite significant flaws, microformats provide enough practical value through Google's rich search results to justify implementation as a pragmatic hack
money_quotes: So you want me to take the ambiguous, crappy 'HTML' markup we already have and layer some ambiguous, crappy 'microformat' markup on top of it? And that's... a solution?
The minute you introduce a microformat into your HTML, the semantics of the class attribute have been permanently altered.
If you hire a new developer, how can they possibly be expected to know that 'postal-code' isn't just an arbitrarily chosen CSS class name, it's a gosh darned officially blessed microformat?
I'm theoretically competent, and if I can't implement hResume without wanting to cut myself and/or writing snarky blog posts like this, how can we expect everyone else to?
Maybe I have a particular aversion to getting my chocolate data structure mixed up with my peanut butter layout structure, but it totally skeeves me out.
While you could argue this is no different than the web and HTML in general – a giant wobbly teetering tower of nasty, patched-together hacks – something about it fundamentally bothers me.
I still think microformats are useful and worth implementing, if for no other reason than it's too easy not to.
posts.key_insight: Microformats are a fundamentally flawed hack that mixes data semantics with presentation markup, but their practical benefits and low implementation cost make them worth using despite their problems.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts how his Coding Horror blog suffered catastrophic data loss when a routine hard drive failure corrupted his virtual machine image, which had never been properly backed up. After initially blaming his hosting provider, he accepts full responsibility for not maintaining his own backups. He details the painful recovery process, discovering that text content was easily recoverable from web spider caches, but images were nearly impossible to find. The experience taught him that personal responsibility for backups is non-negotiable, and that recovery planning—not just backup planning—is what matters.
key_points: Taking personal responsibility for your data is essential—relying entirely on hosting providers is gambling with your digital life
Text content posted on the web is effectively immortal due to countless web spiders archiving it, but images are rarely preserved
Recovery planning is more important than backup planning: understanding how you'd actually restore data reveals the hard parts
The community response was overwhelming, with readers and fellow developers contributing cached content and proposing clever recovery schemes
Archive.org is a rare and valuable service attempting an impossible job of preserving web content including images
money_quotes: It is irresponsible of me to leave the fate of my data entirely in someone else's hands, regardless of how reliable they may or may not be.
Apparently, I was gambling that nothing bad would ever happen at the datacenter. Because that's what you're doing when you run without your own backups. Gambling.
I don't think you can actually lose text you post on the web. Not in any meaningful sense; I'm not sure it's possible.
If you can only back up one thing on your public website, it should be the images.
If you aren't personally responsible for your own backups, they are effectively not happening.
Some argue that we shouldn't be talking about 'backups,' but recovery.
Every day is International Backup Awareness Day.
posts.key_insight: True backup responsibility means personally owning your recovery process, not just trusting that someone else's systems will save you when disaster strikes.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents his process of rebuilding his 2.5-year-old PC with a new Intel Core i7 platform, arguing that building and upgrading your own PC maximizes value over time. He walks through the component selection process, explaining why the i7's triple-channel memory, true quad-core architecture, and maturing software ecosystem finally made upgrading worthwhile. The post serves as both a practical guide to PC rebuilding and a philosophical defense of DIY computing, emphasizing that hardware upgrades are cheap compared to programmer time. He details the $1000 rebuild process step-by-step, from assembling components outside the case to the first boot and BIOS update.
key_points: Building your own PC pays dividends through incremental upgrades over time, similar to buying versus renting a car
The Core i7 platform's six memory slots enable 12GB RAM without expensive DIMMs, making it a practical upgrade from Core 2
Hardware costs are trivial compared to programmer time - saving 15 minutes daily easily justifies $200-500 upgrades
PC building is fundamentally simple - 'if you can assemble a LEGO kit, you can build a PC'
A platform rebuild (motherboard, CPU, RAM, heatsink) transforms an old system into state-of-the-art while keeping case, PSU, and storage
money_quotes: A big part of the value proposition of building your own PC is upgrading it in pieces and parts over time.
Normal computers age in dog years – every year they get seven years older.
As far as I'm concerned, until everything on my computer happens instantaneously, my computer is not nearly fast enough.
Don't ask me why I need six cores and 24GB. To paraphrase a Zen master, if you have to ask, you do not know.
Hardware is Cheap, and Programmers are Expensive.
If you can assemble a LEGO kit, you can build a PC.
On the outside, it will appear to be the same car, but there's a lot more horses under the hood.
Forget the wattage rating and look at the quality.
posts.key_insight: Rebuilding your own PC is a cost-effective way to maintain top-tier performance, because hardware investments are trivial compared to the value of a programmer's time.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood critiques John Gruber's stewardship of Markdown, arguing that despite creating an excellent markup language, Gruber has abandoned his responsibilities as its maintainer. The post details specific Markdown bugs and design issues discovered through Stack Overflow's real-world usage, noting that fixes exist but were never officially released. Atwood argues that Gruber's refusal to collaborate with the community, host the project on a collaborative platform, or release updates has fragmented the ecosystem and stunted Markdown's growth. The core argument is that open source creators have ongoing responsibilities to their projects, and neglecting these duties harms both the project and the community that depends on it.
key_points: Markdown has fundamental design issues (auto-hyperlinking URLs, underscore handling, linebreak behavior) that Stack Overflow and GitHub both had to independently fix
Gruber has unreleased bugfixes (1.0.2 betas) and no official test suite, forcing implementers to hunt down unofficial resources
The project's static website hosting prevents community collaboration that platforms like SourceForge or GitHub would enable
Gruber's ego-driven approach to running the project mirrors Steve Jobs at Apple, blocking community contributions rather than embracing them
Open source projects need active parenting—without leadership, you get fragmented community efforts with no canonical direction
money_quotes: Markdown is the worst form of markup except for all the other forms of markup that I've tried.
Which leads me to the biggest problem with Markdown: John Gruber.
John is running this particular open source project the way Steve Jobs runs Apple – by sheer force of individual ego. And that sucks.
It's almost at the point where John Gruber, the very person who brought us Markdown, is the biggest obstacle preventing Markdown from moving forward and maturing.
Maybe it's true that you can't kill an open source project, but bad parenting is surely enough to cause it to grow up stunted and maybe even a little maladjusted.
Why work against the community when you can work with it?
Markdown is Gruber's baby, without a doubt, but it's also bigger than any one person. It's open source. It belongs to the community, too.
posts.key_insight: Creating successful open source software comes with ongoing responsibilities to the community, and neglecting those duties—even for your own creation—can make you the biggest obstacle to its success.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood passionately defends netbooks against critics who dismiss them as inferior laptops, arguing they represent a pivotal democratizing force in computing. He contends that netbooks fulfill the promise of 'a PC on every desk' by providing affordable, unrestricted access to the internet and computing power without the gatekeepers and fees that plague smartphones. Using Dave Winer's definition of netbooks—emphasizing user freedom, competition, and openness—Atwood positions them as instruments of user empowerment rather than compromised devices. He demonstrates this with a review of the Acer Aspire 1410, showing that modern netbooks deliver remarkable specifications at under $500, making powerful computing accessible to everyone.
key_points: Netbooks represent the culmination of four decades of computing democratization, providing unrestricted internet access without gatekeepers or monthly fees
Smartphones will never be 'free PCs' because they remain locked behind corporate walled gardens and toll roads, while netbooks offer true user freedom
The pattern of incumbent tech dismissing cheaper alternatives repeats throughout history (mainframes vs PCs, laptops vs netbooks)—and users always win
Modern CULV-based netbooks like the Acer Aspire 1410 offer remarkable performance (dual-core, 5-hour battery, 1366x768 screen) at just $439
Intel deliberately hobbles Atom CPUs to prevent netbook price erosion and protect market segmentation
money_quotes: Netbooks are the endpoint of four decades of computing – the final, ubiquitous manifestation of 'A PC on every desk and in every home.'
I'm talking about unlimited access to the complete sum of human knowledge, and free, unfettered communication with anyone on earth. For everyone.
I don't care how 'smart' your smartphone is, it will never escape those corporate shackles.
Smartphones are simply not free enough to deliver the type of democratic transformation that netbooks absolutely will.
That's why I love netbooks. In all their cheap, crappy glory. And you should too. Because they're instruments of user power.
The truly significant thing is this – the users took over.
Netbooks aren't an alternative to notebook computers. They are the new computers.

They may be pieces of junk to Mr. Jobs, but to me, these modest little boxes are marvels – inspiring evidence of the inexorable march of powerful, open computing technology to everyman and everywhere.
posts.key_insight: The true value of netbooks lies not in their specifications but in their role as instruments of democratic access to computing—cheap, open, and free from corporate gatekeepers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the surprisingly complex history behind newline characters in text files, explaining why different operating systems use different line endings (CR LF for Windows, CR for old Mac, LF for Unix). The terms Carriage Return and Line Feed derive from manual typewriters where they were physically separate operations. This historical baggage creates ongoing problems when text files are shared across platforms, leading to the 'onegiantunbrokenline' display bug or mysterious ^M characters. Atwood warns that mixing line endings in a single file through copy-paste is easy to do accidentally and hard to detect. He concludes that programmers need to understand this schism and use tools that can display invisible line ending characters.
key_points: Three main newline conventions exist: CR LF (Windows/DOS), CR (old Mac), and LF (Unix), plus two Unicode variants
The CR/LF distinction originated from physical typewriter mechanics where returning the carriage and advancing paper were separate operations
Early computer systems used CR+LF because teletype print heads physically needed time to return to the left margin
Mixing line endings in a single file is easy to do accidentally through copy-paste and creates hard-to-diagnose problems
Many editors, including Visual Studio, cannot display end-of-line markers, making detection difficult
money_quotes: Have you ever opened a simple little ASCII text file to see it inexplicably displayed as onegiantunbrokenline?
Did you ever wonder what was at the end of your lines? As a programmer, I knew there were end of line characters, but I honestly never thought much about them. They just... worked.

Confusing terms based on archaic hardware that is no longer in use, and is confounding to new users who have no point of reference for said terms; Completely arbitrary platform 'standards' for what is exactly the same function. Pretty much business as usual in computing.
The separation of the two functions concealed the fact that the print head could not return from the far right to the beginning of the next line in one-character time.
Even worse, it's possible to mix all three of these line endings in the same file.
Innocently copy and paste a comment or code snippet from a file with a different set of line endings, then save it. Bam, you've got a file with multiple line endings. That you can't see.
I find it fascinating that the mundane ASCII newline has so much ancient computing lore behind it, and that it still regularly bites us in unexpected places.
The Great Newline Schism is something you need to be aware of.
posts.key_insight: The seemingly simple newline character carries decades of computing history baggage that still causes real problems today, and programmers need tools that reveal these invisible characters.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that ideas are vastly overvalued compared to execution, using the Fundable vs Kickstarter story as a cautionary tale. He dismisses the notion that internet fame or connections guarantee success, emphasizing that even well-known founders must execute well or fail. Drawing on insights from Derek Sivers and Pixar's Ed Catmull, Atwood contends that great teams can transform mediocre ideas into successes while mediocre teams will ruin even brilliant concepts. He credits Stack Overflow's success not to the idea or his fame, but to assembling the right team of developers he had worked with before.
key_points: Ideas without execution are nearly worthless - execution is the true multiplier of value
The Fundable founder's failure to recognize his own execution problems illustrates a common blind spot among entrepreneurs
Internet fame and connections don't grant immunity from the need to execute well
Software execution means obsessing over every tiny detail that makes up your application
Building great teams is the foundation of successful execution - they can fix mediocre ideas or discard them for better ones
money_quotes: Ideas are worth nothing unless executed. They are just a multiplier. Execution is worth millions.
If you give a good idea to a mediocre group, they'll screw it up. If you give a mediocre idea to a good group, they'll fix it.
I don't care how internet famous you are; nobody gets a pass on execution.
This is all your app is: a collection of tiny details.
In software development, execution is staying on top of all the tiny details that make up your app.
The disconnect between idea and execution here is so vast it's hard to understand why the author himself can't see it.
If you're not constantly obsessing over every aspect of your application, relentlessly polishing and improving every little part of it – no matter how trivial – you're not executing.
posts.key_insight: Stop protecting your ideas and start building great teams, because a strong team can fix or replace a mediocre idea, while a weak team will destroy even a brilliant one.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on a two-month period where comments were disabled on Coding Horror due to technical issues, and what he learned from the experience. While initially relieved by the silence, he came to miss the community dialogue and realized that comments, despite their scaling problems, are essential to blogging. He acknowledges that at large scale, comments require active moderation or they devolve into chaos. The post announces the return of comments with new restrictions: no anonymous posting and stricter moderation to maintain quality discourse.
key_points: Comments don't scale - at high readership levels, unmoderated comments devolve into chaos and drive away thoughtful participants
The best insights on a blog often come from the comments section, as the community collectively knows more than any individual blogger
Disabling comments created an unexpected benefit: readers were forced to think for themselves rather than being influenced by others' reactions
The psychic burden of reading and moderating hundreds of comments weighs heavily on both writers and readers
Requiring authentication and stricter moderation are necessary trade-offs to maintain quality discourse at scale
money_quotes: Comments don't scale worth a damn.
At a large enough scale, comments require active moderation or they rapidly go sour.
The best part of a blog post often begins where the blog post ends. If you are offended by that, I humbly submit you don't understand why blogs work.
It's an open secret amongst bloggers that the blog comments are often better than the original blog post, and it's because the community collectively knows far more than you or I will ever know.
You mean I can say whatever I want, and nobody can contradict me underneath my very own post? Revolutionary!
In the reverberating echo chamber that is the internet, I think we would all do well to remind ourselves of that periodically.
If what you have to say isn't worth authenticating to post, it's probably best for both of us if you keep it to yourself anyway.
posts.key_insight: Online communities require active curation to thrive - the same openness that enables great discussions also invites dysfunction, making moderation an essential but exhausting responsibility.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits his 2007 article about programmers who can't program, sharing new evidence that the problem persists. He highlights an email from Mike Lin describing how he streamlined technical interviews to quickly identify candidates who cannot write even basic code. The post advocates for immediate technical screening, preferably via phone or web-based coding tools, to filter out non-programmers before wasting interview time. Atwood expresses frustration that people with no programming ability continue applying for programming jobs—and apparently sometimes getting hired. He concludes that inadequate industry-wide interviewing standards are degrading the profession.
key_points: The majority of programming job applicants cannot write even simple programs, a problem that persisted from 2007 to 2010
Leading with technical questions rather than rapport-building dramatically reduces time wasted on unqualified candidates
Phone and web-based coding interviews can identify non-programmers in 5-15 minutes
Inadequate interviewing standards across the industry allow non-programmers to get hired, which degrades the profession
Simple technical screening should be mandatory for every programming interview
money_quotes: Many candidates who show up for programming job interviews can't program. At all.
It's the equivalent of attempting to hire a truck driver and finding out that 90 percent of the job applicants can't find the gas pedal or the gear shift.
I schedule an actual interview with them if they can at least write a simple 10-line program. That doesn't happen often.
Why do people who can't write a simple program even entertain the idea they can get jobs as working programmers?
At least bad programmers can be educated; non-programming programmers are not only hopeless but also cheapen the careers of everyone around them.
Our industry-wide interviewing standards for programmers are woefully inadequate, and that's a disgrace.
posts.key_insight: Simple technical screening at the start of every programming interview is essential because an alarming number of applicants for programming jobs cannot program at all.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the common claim that uncompiled LINQ to SQL queries cause scalability nightmares, using Stack Overflow's success as counter-evidence. While acknowledging that LINQ queries do incur a per-execution parsing cost that compiled queries avoid, he demonstrates through benchmarking that this overhead amounts to less than one-third of a millisecond per query. The real issue isn't whether queries are compiled, but whether your application is making an unreasonable number of queries per request. Atwood concludes that blindly compiling all LINQ queries is a form of micro-optimization theater that trades developer productivity for negligible performance gains in most real-world scenarios.
key_points: Stack Overflow runs hundreds of uncompiled LINQ queries per page while serving 1.5 million pageviews daily without CPU scalability issues
The per-query overhead of uncompiled LINQ is less than 0.3 milliseconds—the real problem is applications making hundreds of thousands of queries
Rico Mariani's benchmarks show compiled queries can actually be slower in scenarios with many small batches versus few large batches
The compiled query syntax is ugly and contorted, creating maintenance burden that may not be worth the marginal performance gain
Database round-trip time typically dwarfs query parsing time, making LINQ compilation a classic micro-optimization
money_quotes: Querying the database is so slow (relative to typical code execution) that if you have to ask how long it will take, you can't afford it.
Stack Overflow has dozens to hundreds of plain vanilla uncompiled Linq to SQL queries on every page. What we don't have is 'scalability nightmares.'
Each uncompiled run of the query took less than one third of a millisecond longer.

At first I was worried that every Stack Overflow page was 1.5 times slower than it should be. But then I realized it's probably more realistic to make sure that any page we generate isn't doing 500 freakin' thousand queries!
Have we found ourselves in the sad tragedy of micro-optimization theater... again? I think we might have.
But compilation alone is not the entire performance story. Not even close.
posts.key_insight: Performance advice without real-world benchmarks and context is dangerous—what sounds like catastrophic overhead often amounts to micro-optimization theater when you do the actual math.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the often-overlooked corollary to Fitts' Law: while commonly-used UI elements should be large and easy to click, dangerous or irreversible actions should be intentionally difficult to trigger. He introduces Alan Cooper's 'ejector seat lever' metaphor to describe destructive actions that must never be accidentally activated. Using Gmail's closely-placed Archive and Report Spam buttons as a cautionary example, and WordPress's well-separated Update and Trash controls as a positive one, Atwood argues that thoughtful UI design requires considering both what users should easily click and what they should not.
key_points: Fitts' Law states larger, edge-positioned UI elements are easier to click, but designers rarely consider its inverse
Dangerous or irreversible actions ('ejector seat levers') should be small, distant from common actions, and hard to accidentally trigger
Gmail's interface places radically different actions (Archive vs Report Spam) dangerously close together with only 3 pixels separating them
WordPress demonstrates good practice by making the common Update button large and prominent while keeping Move to Trash small and distant
Undo functionality is valuable but preventing accidental clicks in the first place is superior design
money_quotes: If we should make UI elements we want users to click on large, and ideally place them at corners or edges for maximum clickability – what should we do with UI elements we don't want users to click on?
The one thing that must never happen is accidental deployment of the ejector seat.
I can think of a half-dozen applications I regularly use where the ejector seat button is inexplicably placed right next to the cabin lights button.
Every few days I accidentally click Report Spam when I really meant to click Archive.
Undo is powerful stuff, but wouldn't it be better still if I wasn't pulling the darn ejector seat lever all the time?
Uncommon or dangerous UI items should be difficult to click on!
posts.key_insight: Good UI design isn't just about making desired actions easy—it's equally about making dangerous actions hard to trigger accidentally.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that usability testing is essential for software projects but often neglected because teams don't know how to implement it. He recommends Steve Krug's books as accessible entry points for developers to learn usability fundamentals and DIY testing methods. The post also highlights eyetracking research as a valuable reference for teams who can't conduct their own studies. Atwood frames usability as an ongoing battle that requires commitment, but one that can be fought with proper training through these resources.
key_points: Projects without people who care about usability are doomed to fail
Steve Krug's books provide the most accessible introduction to usability for developers
Organizations should learn to do usability testing themselves since most can't afford dedicated professionals
Eyetracking research books can serve as proxy data for teams who can't conduct their own studies
Usability is an endless war, but accessible resources provide basic training for fighting it
money_quotes: Writing code? That's the easy part. Getting your application in the hands of users, and creating applications that people actually want to use – now that's the hard stuff.
If you don't have people that care about usability on your project, your project is doomed.
It's easy to parrot 'Usability is Important!' but you have to walk the walk, too.
Usability testing is one of the best things people can do to improve Web sites (or almost anything they're creating that people have to interact with).
Usability isn't really cheap or easy. It's an endless war, with innumerable battlegrounds, stretching all the way back to the dawn of computing.
These books, at least, are cheap and easy in the sense that they give you some basic training in fighting the good (usability) fight.
posts.key_insight: Usability expertise isn't optional for software teams—it's essential, and accessible books can democratize this knowledge for developers who otherwise wouldn't prioritize it.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood makes a passionate case for triple-monitor setups, celebrating that ATI's new video cards with three display outputs have finally made this configuration accessible to mainstream users. He walks through the technical hurdles of connecting three monitors, particularly the DisplayPort conversion challenge, while noting the benefits of simplified hardware configuration. The post serves as both a practical guide and advocacy piece, arguing that three monitors hit a 'sweet spot' with a natural center focus and 50% more display area than dual monitors. Atwood dismisses Nvidia's competing GTX 480 as wasteful and endorses ATI cards across all price points for this purpose.
key_points: Triple monitors now achievable with a single ATI video card upgrade, eliminating the need for exotic cards or dual video card setups
Three monitors provides a natural center focus that dual monitors lack, representing the productivity sweet spot before diminishing returns
DisplayPort-to-DVI conversion is the main technical hurdle, requiring either a $30 passive or $110 active adapter
Moving to a single video card simplifies configuration, reduces power consumption, and eliminates driver conflicts
For non-gamers, the cheapest Eyefinity-capable card ($100) is sufficient for all desktop GUI needs
money_quotes: As far as I'm concerned, you can never be too rich, too thin, or have too much screen space.
If you're only using one monitor, you are cheating yourself out of potential productivity.
With three monitors, there's a 'center' to focus on. And 50% more display area.
I don't care how large a single display can be; you can never have enough desktop space.
Basically, it makes triple monitors – dare I say it – almost a mainstream desktop configuration.
It turns out their new GTX 480 fails in other ways, in that it's basically the Pentium 4 of video cards – generating ridiculous amounts of heat for very little performance gain.
posts.key_insight: The technological barrier to triple-monitor productivity has finally fallen, making what was once an exotic setup accessible to anyone willing to navigate a minor DisplayPort conversion challenge.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains the surprisingly complex requirements for successfully sending email through code, arguing that simply calling an SMTP library is nowhere near sufficient. He walks through three critical infrastructure steps: configuring reverse PTR records with your ISP, implementing DomainKeys Identified Mail (DKIM) for cryptographic signing, and setting up SPF/SenderID DNS records. The post emphasizes that email's decades of abuse by spammers has created an ecosystem of anti-spam countermeasures that will silently drop messages that don't meet these requirements. Atwood provides practical verification methods using Gmail headers and Port25's email testing service to confirm proper configuration.
key_points: Sending email programmatically requires far more than just SMTP code - most emails will be silently dropped without proper infrastructure configuration
Reverse PTR records are the most critical requirement; without them, a huge percentage of emails never arrive
DKIM uses public-key cryptography to sign emails, proving they actually came from your domain
SPF/SenderID records document which IP addresses are authorized to send mail for your domain
Gmail's 'show original' feature and Port25's email verifier service are essential tools for debugging email delivery
money_quotes: Email is the cockroach of communication mediums: you just can't kill it.
Just because you send an email doesn't mean it will arrive.
Email is a well that has been truly and thoroughly poisoned – the digital equivalent of a superfund cleanup site.
The ecosystem around email is a dank miasma of half-implemented, incompletely supported anti-spam hacks and workarounds.
Don't even bother reading any further until you've verified that your ISP has correctly configured the reverse PTR record for the server that will be sending email.
By the time I've reached the point where I am forced to write code to send out email, I really, really want those damn emails to arrive.
And for those who are the unfortunate recipients of these emails: my condolences.
posts.key_insight: The gap between 'I wrote code that sends email' and 'my emails actually arrive' is vast, requiring DNS configuration, cryptographic signing, and ISP coordination that most developers never consider.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the practical pain points of CSS while managing multiple themed sites in the Stack Overflow family. Through his experience maintaining four (soon to be many more) CSS files for different site skins, he identifies CSS's fundamental violations of the DRY principle. He highlights specific frustrations: vertical alignment hacks, lack of variables forcing color repetition, and absence of nesting requiring redundant code blocks. The post introduces Less CSS and SASS as solutions that transform CSS into something more like a programming language with variables, mixins, and nesting. While uncertain when CSS3 will address these issues, Atwood argues these preprocessors solve real pain points worth understanding.
key_points: CSS fundamentally violates the DRY principle by forcing constant repetition of colors, values, and code blocks
Vertical alignment in CSS remains a hacky workaround compared to tables
CSS preprocessors like Less and SASS address real deficiencies by adding variables, nesting, and mixins
These tools transform CSS from static layout rules into something closer to a programming language
The need for these solutions becomes acute when maintaining multiple themed versions of the same site
money_quotes: CSS is kind of painful.
In short, CSS violates the living crap out of the DRY principle. You are constantly and unavoidably repeating yourself.
Vertical alignment is a giant, hacky PITA. (Tables work great for this though!)
In both cases we're transmogrifying CSS into a bit more of a programming language, rather than the static set of layout rules it currently exists as.
Even if you have extreme HTML hygiene and Austrian levels of discipline, CSS has some serious limitations in practice.
That number is only going to get larger. Much larger. That scares me a little.
posts.key_insight: CSS's lack of variables, nesting, and reusable components violates fundamental programming principles, making preprocessors like Less and SASS necessary tools for maintainable stylesheets at scale.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares lessons learned from building Stack Overflow with a distributed team, arguing that remote development can work but requires specific conditions and infrastructure. He discovered that coding alone was untenable and that having a coding buddy was essential for motivation and direction. The post outlines prerequisites for remote work: team members who genuinely love code, proven track records of excellence, and strong autonomous leadership with clear vision. Atwood then presents advice from Miguel de Icaza on scaling remote teams, emphasizing real-time chat, persistent mailing lists, and regular voice communication. He advocates for lightweight process including weekly status reports and meeting minutes. Despite the challenges, Atwood concludes that remote development represents the future of work and is worth the effort to figure out.
key_points: Working remotely alone as a programmer leads to paralysis and lack of motivation; the minimum viable team size is two
Remote work only succeeds with proven, passionate programmers who have track records of excellence - not newbies or those 'phoning it in'
Three essential tools for scaling remote teams: real-time chat, persistent mailing lists, and voice/video communication
Lightweight process matters: weekly status reports covering what was done, what's planned, and blockers, plus meeting minutes distributed to the team
Remote development requires more discipline than co-located work but represents the future of intellectual work
money_quotes: As programmers go, I'm fairly social. Which still means I'm a borderline sociopath by normal standards.
Don't even think about working remotely with anyone who doesn't freakin' bleed ones and zeros, and has a proven track record of getting things done.
Newbie programmers, or competent programmers who are phoning it in, are absolutely not going to have the moxie necessary to get things done remotely.
I know, I know, the whole reason we got into this programming thing was to avoid talking to other people, but bear with me here.
Noisy, argumentative, or useless things posted to the mailing list should be punishable by death. Or noogies.
Remote development is hard. It takes discipline to make it all work, certainly more discipline than piling a bunch of programmers into the same cubicle farm.
I believe remote development represents the future of work.
The shared source code was the mutual glue that stuck us together, motivated us, and kept us moving forward.
posts.key_insight: Remote development succeeds not through tools or process alone, but by assembling proven programmers who genuinely love code and giving them autonomy with clear leadership vision.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on why Stack Overflow succeeded while the original paid Stack Exchange model fell short, arguing that money is fundamentally poisonous to communities. He explains that Stack Overflow thrives because participants are motivated by intrinsic rewards—demonstrating mastery, teaching peers, and building something great—rather than financial incentives. Drawing on Dan Pink's research about motivation and the candle problem experiment, Atwood shows that monetary incentives actually impair creative problem-solving. He concludes by describing how he applies these principles to his own team management, focusing on inspiring people with purpose rather than controlling them with tasks and schedules.
key_points: Money is poisonous to communities—the commercial nature of paid Stack Exchange distorted everything from the start
Stack Overflow succeeds because people participate for intrinsic rewards: mastery, teaching, following interests, and building something collectively
Research shows monetary incentives make creative problem-solving worse, not better—they only work for repetitive mechanical tasks
Great management means inspiring people to yearn for the goal rather than micromanaging tasks and schedules
The satisfaction of demonstrating mastery within a community of peers cannot be bought at any price
money_quotes: Money is poisonous to communities.
I didn't co-found it because I wanted to make money. I co-founded it because I wanted to build something cool that made the internet better.
I don't care how much you pay me, you'll never be able to recreate the incredibly satisfying feeling I get when demonstrating mastery within my community of peers.
You've got a monetary incentive designed to sharpen thinking and accelerate creativity – and it does just the opposite. It dulls thinking and blocks creativity.
Traditional carrot-and-stick incentives are only useful for repetitive, mechanical tasks.
I've tried mightily to live up to the ideals that Stack Overflow was founded on when building out my team.
If you want to build a ship, don't drum up the men to gather wood, divide the work and give orders. Instead, teach them to yearn for the vast and endless sea.
posts.key_insight: Sustainable communities are built on intrinsic motivation—mastery, purpose, and autonomy—not financial incentives, which actually undermine creative collaboration.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that voice recognition technology has stagnated at around 80% accuracy since 2001, far below the 96-98% human transcription baseline, making it impractical for general use. He challenges the assumption that voice control would even be desirable, noting that spoken communication is inherently inefficient, places burden on the listener, and tends to be rambling and unclear. Through examples like YouTube's automatic captions and podcast transcription attempts, he demonstrates that machine-generated transcripts create more work to correct than starting fresh. He draws a parallel to handwriting recognition, which was once hyped but quietly disappeared from mainstream devices, suggesting voice recognition may follow the same trajectory.
key_points: Speech recognition accuracy has been stuck at 80% since 2001 despite massive increases in computing power, while human transcription achieves 96-98%
Voice communication is inherently inefficient: it's verbose, listeners only absorb half of what's said, and it burdens the receiver more than text
Correcting a machine transcript full of errors is often more frustrating than transcribing from scratch
A room full of people voice-controlling their computers would be both impractical and cacophonous
Handwriting recognition followed a hype-to-obscurity arc that voice recognition may also follow
money_quotes: It doesn't work.
With human discrimination as high as 98%, the unclosed gap left little basis for conversation.
Is voice control, even hypothetically perfect voice control, more effective than the lower tech alternatives?
Voice communication remains filled with pitfalls and misunderstandings. Even in the best of conditions.
What's worse: transcribing everything by hand, from scratch – or correcting every third or fourth word in an auto-generated machine transcript?
I'm also trying to imagine a room full of people controlling their computers or phones using their voices. Wouldn't we all end up hoarse and deaf?

More than ten years later, you'd expect to see massively improved handwriting recognition of some sort in today's iPads and iPhones, right? Well, maybe, if by 'massively improved' you mean 'nonexistent.'
posts.key_insight: The fundamental problem with voice control isn't the technology's accuracy—it's that spoken communication is an inherently inefficient medium that we shouldn't want to extend to our computers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses the film Groundhog Day as an extended metaphor for the limitations of A/B testing. He argues that Phil's decades-long repetition of the same day represents the purest form of A/B testing imaginable—optimizing every choice to achieve a desired outcome. Yet despite constructing the 'perfect' date through endless iteration, Phil is rejected because his choices weren't authentic. Atwood concludes that A/B testing is fundamentally empty: useful for smoothing details but incapable of creating anything meaningful or winning genuine connection.
key_points: Groundhog Day is actually a dark existential film about meaninglessness disguised as comedy, with Phil spending 30-40 years trapped in repetition
Phil's pursuit of Rita represents pure A/B testing—making thousands of attempts and optimizing each variable based on experimental results
Despite achieving a 'technically perfect' date through endless optimization, Rita rejects Phil because the choices weren't authentic
A/B testing can only achieve shallow local maximums, not genuine human connection or loyalty
The film's layered revelations through repeated viewings mirror its own themes of finding meaning through repetition
money_quotes: Repeating the same day for decades plays to our secret collective fear that our lives are irrelevant and ultimately pointless.
This is the purest form of A/B testing imaginable.
Phil wasn't making these choices because he honestly believed in them. He was making these choices because he wanted a specific outcome.
Although the date was technically perfect, it didn't ring true to Rita, and that made all the difference.
That's the problem with A/B testing. It's empty. It has no feeling, no empathy, and at worst, it's dishonest.
A/B testing is like sandpaper. You can use it to smooth out details, but you can't actually create anything with it.
You can achieve a shallow local maximum with A/B testing – but you'll never win hearts and minds.
posts.key_insight: A/B testing optimizes for measurable outcomes but cannot create authentic experiences—you can perfect the mechanics while losing the soul.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood presents his 2010 everyday carry (EDC) kit, comparing himself to Batman preparing his utility belt before leaving the house. He details four carefully selected pocket items: a fast 32GB USB drive, a Leatherman Squirt P4 multitool with pliers, a variable-brightness LED flashlight, and a small carabiner. However, the real focus is on how smartphones are rapidly consolidating all these gadgets into one device, replacing everything from cameras to GPS to gaming devices. Atwood marvels at the convergence trend and half-jokingly predicts Apple might add a flip-out blade to future iPhones to complete the takeover.
key_points: The transition from 512MB to 32GB USB drives in just five years represents remarkable technological progress
Pliers are far more useful than scissors on a multitool - a lesson learned from experience
LED flashlight brightness has progressed from bright to 'ridiculously blinding laser-like bright' in five years
Resist the urge to put everything on carabiners - only attach items you actually remove frequently
Smartphones are the ultimate convergence device, already replacing over a dozen separate gadgets
money_quotes: Like any self-respecting geek, I'm mostly an indoor enthusiast.
Now you can have a whole freakin' hard drive worth of files in your pocket. Just in case, you know, you have an emergency need to upload a virus to an alien mothership, or something.
After seeing how much more generally useful the pliers are, I am now a little ashamed to admit I ever carried the wussy scissors version.
Rest assured, everything here is carefully selected with the appropriate levels of monomaniacal attention to detail.
What self-respecting superhero would leave the house these days without their smartphone?
Smartphones really are the ultimate gadget.
At this rate, Apple could introduce a flip-out blade on the iPhone 7 and reduce my entire EDC kit to one item.
posts.key_insight: The smartphone represents the ultimate convergence device, steadily absorbing the functions of nearly every gadget a geek might carry, making dedicated tools increasingly optional.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to a Stack Overflow sysadmin's blog post questioning whether developers should have production access. Rather than giving a definitive answer, he uses an extended vampire/werewolf metaphor to illustrate how programmers and system administrators have different but complementary skills. He argues that the tension between these groups is universal across companies, but healthy teams find ways to leverage both skill sets. The key is giving teams sufficiently challenging work that requires collaboration, as infighting often stems from boredom rather than genuine disagreement.
key_points: The question of developer production access has no universal answer - it depends on the specific company and team dynamics
Programmers and sysadmins have fundamentally different skill sets that complement rather than compete with each other
Sharing processes and decisions publicly (as Stack Overflow does) benefits everyone by enabling learning from mistakes and successes
When programmers and sysadmins fight, it's often because they lack sufficiently challenging work that requires their combined skills
The ideal arrangement has each group focusing on their expertise while maintaining some shared access for emergencies
money_quotes: The saga of System Administrators versus Programmers is not a new one; I don't think I've ever worked at any company where these two factions weren't continually battling with each other in some form.
Let's all talk about this stuff in public, so that we can teach each other how to get better at whatever the heck it is we love to do.
If we don't walk the walk, why are we even doing this?
In my experience, when programmers and system administrators fight, it's because they're bored. You haven't given them a sufficiently daunting task.
Remember, it's not vampires versus werewolves. It's vampires and werewolves.
Just because you're a hotshot programmer doesn't mean you have mastered networking and server configuration.
posts.key_insight: Team conflicts between programmers and sysadmins usually indicate insufficient challenge rather than incompatibility - give them hard problems that require both skill sets and they'll unite instead of fight.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that speed of iteration is the most critical factor in winning competitive markets, drawing inspiration from a comedic movie quote and Boyd's Law of Iteration. He chronicles Stack Overflow's rapid development over five months while expressing concern they're still not moving fast enough. Using Google Chrome and Android as examples, he demonstrates how fast iteration can overcome initial quality disadvantages. The post makes the case that competitors who iterate faster will inevitably surpass those with better initial products but slower development cycles.
key_points: Speed of iteration beats quality of iteration - being faster at improving matters more than starting with a better product
Stack Overflow's strategy is to 'live in harmony with the web' by making the internet better rather than fighting competitors
Google Chrome went from nothing to best-of-breed browser in under two years through rapid iteration
Android doesn't need to be better than iPhone, just faster at improving to eventually win the mobile market
When obstacles arise, the strategy is to simply turn and keep moving forward rather than fight
money_quotes: Go that way, really fast. If something gets in your way… turn.
Speed of iteration beats quality of iteration.
attempting to get information out of those things is like panning for gold in a never ending river of sewage
We're not here to subvert or own anyone or anything. We just love community, and we love getting great answers to our questions.
Google went from nothing, no web browser at all, to best-of-breed in under two years.
Android doesn't have to be better than the iPhone. They just need to be faster at improving.

Apple has indisputably better taste – and an impeccably controlled experience. But at their current rate of progress, they'll be playing second or third fiddle to Google in the mobile space inside a few years.
posts.key_insight: In technology competition, the team that iterates fastest will eventually overtake competitors with better initial products but slower development cycles.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits the state of solid state drives a year after his previous coverage, arguing that SSDs remain the single best performance upgrade for any computer despite modest price declines. He notes that other PC components like CPUs and RAM have become commoditized and boring, while SSDs still offer a transformative experience. The post also highlights the emergence of hybrid drives like Seagate's Momentus XT, which combine flash memory with traditional platters to offer a compelling middle ground between pure SSDs and spinning disks. Atwood recommends hybrid drives for laptops and secondary machines where full SSD investment isn't justified.
key_points: SSDs remain the most impactful performance upgrade you can make, offering a 'life changing experience' that other components can't match
128GB has become the practical minimum SSD size, declining from $350 to $250, while 64GB requires too much disk space management
CPUs and RAM have become commoditized - they're all 'stupid fast' and cheap, making them boring upgrades
The Crucial RealSSD C300 is the current performance leader, though OS support for TRIM is critical to avoid degradation
Hybrid drives like Seagate's Momentus XT finally deliver on the 2006 promise of combining flash speed with platter capacity at reasonable prices
money_quotes: Solid state hard drives remain a life changing experience.
A solid state hard drive is easily the best and most obvious performance upgrade you can make on any computer for a given amount of money.
CPUs? All stupid fast at any price, with more cores and gigahertz than you'll ever need unless you're one of those freaks who does nothing but raytracing and video transcoding all day long.
I spend almost zero real world time waiting for the CPU to do anything on my computer.
Skip over the old and busted spinning rust hard drives, and choose a newfangled solid state hard drive.
Like all truly sexy things, what turns me on is the part I can't see – the sexy, sexy, sexy performance inside this baby.
A hundred bucks for near-SSD performance and decent capacity? Sign me up.
posts.key_insight: While most PC components have become commoditized to the point of irrelevance, storage remains the one area where spending more money delivers a genuinely transformative performance improvement.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts his surprise when YouTube's Content ID system automatically detected and blocked a 90-second movie clip he uploaded for fair use purposes in a blog post. Having previously dismissed such technology as impossible, he's now forced to acknowledge that Google has built an incredibly effective video fingerprinting system that compares every upload against millions of copyrighted works. While impressed by the engineering achievement, he's frustrated that his legitimate fair use claim was denied without explanation by the copyright holder. The post explores the tension between technological capability and the nuanced legal doctrine of fair use, concluding that the system may work 'too well' for those with legitimate transformative purposes.
key_points: YouTube's Content ID system can identify copyrighted content within hours of upload by comparing against millions of reference files - a feat Atwood previously thought impossible
The system processes over 100 years of video daily, checking every moment against every reference file in its database
Copyright holders can choose to Block, Track, or Monetize matched content, with no middle ground for fair use considerations
Atwood's fair use dispute was denied without explanation, highlighting how automated systems don't accommodate the nuanced four-factor fair use test
The only way to defeat the fingerprinting is to degrade audio and video so badly that you've effectively ruined your own content
money_quotes: Today's lesson, then, is be careful what you ask for.

It's so good that the only way to defeat it is by degrading your audio and video so much that you have effectively ruined it. And when it comes to copyright violations, if you can achieve mutually assured destruction, then you have won.
The idea that YouTube can really validate every minute of uploaded video against every minute of every major copyrighted work is unfathomable to me.

When YouTube promised to do this to placate copyright owners, I was sure they were delaying for time. But much to my fair-use-loving dismay, they've actually gone and built the damn thing – and it works.
Just, maybe, it works a little too well.
I was angry and I had a lot of free time. Not a good combination.
My intent is not to facilitate illegal use, but to 'quote' the movie scene as part of a larger blog entry.
posts.key_insight: Technological solutions to copyright enforcement can be so effective that they inadvertently crush legitimate fair use, creating a system where the nuance of law is replaced by the binary judgment of algorithms.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while routers have become commoditized household necessities, the best ones run open-source firmware like DD-WRT or Tomato. Frustrated by his aging router's stale proprietary firmware, he researched alternatives and discovered that open-source firmware transforms ordinary hardware into feature-rich, perpetually-updated networking powerhouses. He recommends the Buffalo Nfiniti router running DD-WRT as the ideal combination of affordable commodity hardware and powerful open-source software. The post concludes that this pairing of commodity hardware with open-source firmware represents the future not just of routers, but of all commodity hardware.
key_points: Routers have evolved from exotic network equipment to essential household items that everyone with an internet connection needs
Proprietary router firmware becomes abandoned as vendors move on to newer models, leaving users with outdated and potentially insecure devices
Open-source firmware projects like DD-WRT and Tomato provide perpetual community updates and dramatically expanded features
The combination of affordable commodity hardware with open-source firmware delivers better results than expensive proprietary solutions
Building a dedicated x86 PC for routing is unnecessarily complex when commodity routers have sufficient CPU power and memory
money_quotes: Anyone who pays for an internet connection needs a router.
The best router models all run open source firmware!
The truly great routers are available in 'awesome' edition.
I was growing increasingly uncomfortable with the status quo. Frankly, the prospect of three year old hardware with year old firmware gives me the heebie-jeebies.
DD-WRT scratches every geek itch I have.
Dedicating a whole x86 PC to routing is power inefficient, overly complex, and awkward.
The magic router formula these days is a combination of commodity hardware and open-source firmware.
Thanks to a wide variety of mature commodity hardware choices, plus infinitely and perpetually updated open source router firmware, I'm happy to report that now everyone can have a great router.
posts.key_insight: Open-source firmware transforms abandoned commodity hardware into perpetually-updated, feature-rich devices—a model that represents the future of all consumer hardware.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while touchscreens have their place, serious computer users—especially programmers—should appreciate and invest in quality mechanical keyboards. He contrasts the crippling text input experience on smartphones with the efficiency of proper keyboards, noting that most people unknowingly use inferior rubber dome keyboards. The post serves as both a love letter to mechanical keyboards and a practical guide for those wanting to explore this enthusiast space, covering switch types, keycap printing methods, and specific brand recommendations. Atwood acknowledges this borders on esoterica but maintains that anyone who values typing owes it to themselves to experience what a great keyboard feels like.
key_points: Touchscreen typing transforms users from content producers into passive consumers, which has concerning implications for a web built on user-generated content
Most keyboards today use cheap rubber dome or membrane switches that provide inconsistent, mushy feedback and degrade over time
Mechanical keyboards offer superior tactile feedback and durability but have become rare due to cost and public indifference
Choosing a mechanical keyboard involves considering noise level, tactile feedback, actuation force, key travel, and keycap printing method
While great keyboards aren't strictly necessary, those who love typing should experience what quality feels like
money_quotes: The minute I switch back to my regular keyboard, I go from being Usain Bolt to the Flash.
When was the last time you saw a hunt-and-peck pianist?
I can't take slow typists seriously as programmers.
The device that, when combined with the internet, turns every human being into a highly efficient global printing press.
What happens when people become trained to think of information and entertainment as something they receive and not something they create?
All this time, I've been typing on keyboards with least-common-denominator rubber dome innards.
The most important aspect of writing isn't the keyboard you use, but the simple act of getting out there and writing as much as you can.
posts.key_insight: For people whose work is fundamentally about producing text, the quality of their keyboard matters far more than most realize, and the shift toward touch interfaces risks turning a generation of creators into mere consumers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains how Firesheep, a Firefox add-in, exposes a fundamental vulnerability in how the web handles authentication through cookies. The tool simply listens to unencrypted WiFi traffic and captures session cookies that are broadcast in plain text, allowing anyone to impersonate logged-in users. While this vulnerability has existed since cookies were invented in 1994, the convergence of ubiquitous public WiFi, increased online identity investment, and better tools has made it a pressing concern. Rather than encrypting all web traffic, Atwood suggests we need a better identity protocol, while offering practical advice: use encrypted WiFi, always access email via HTTPS, and pressure websites to adopt HTTPS.
key_points: HTTP cookies broadcast your unique session identifier in plain text on every click, making them trivially interceptable on unencrypted networks
Firesheep isn't a sophisticated hack—it's more of an art project that packages well-known vulnerabilities into a point-and-click tool
The vulnerability has existed since 1994 but became critical due to ubiquitous public WiFi, increased online identity investment, and better interception tools
Encrypting all web traffic is a 'boil the sea' solution; a better identity protocol would be preferable
Email is the skeleton key to online identity—if your webmail doesn't support HTTPS, switch providers immediately
money_quotes: To a website, that's your fingerprint, DNA, and social security number all rolled into one.
This is the way the entire internet has worked since 1994.
It's more of an art project and PR stunt than an actual hack of any kind.
If your webmail provider does not support secure http, they are idiots.
Encrypting everything just to protect that one lousy cookie header seems like a whole lot of overkill to me.
Email is the de-facto skeleton key to your online identity. When your email is compromised, all is lost.
Firesheep may indeed have broken the web's cookie jar. But it was kind of an old, beat up, cracked cookie jar in the first place.
posts.key_insight: The web's fundamental authentication mechanism has been insecure since its invention, and Firesheep's real contribution was making this invisible risk visible to average users at exactly the moment when public WiFi and online identity made it matter.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the internet's model of requiring unique usernames and passwords for every website is fundamentally broken and unsustainable. Drawing an analogy to physical wallets, he notes that people carry only a few forms of identification in real life, not dozens. He advocates for 'Internet Driver's Licenses' - reusable third-party credentials like OpenID, Facebook, Google, or Twitter logins that can authenticate users across multiple sites. The post reflects on Stack Overflow's early adoption of OpenID in 2008 and calls for continued pursuit of simpler, federated identity solutions.
key_points: The one-login-per-website model forces most users to reuse the same weak password everywhere, creating massive security vulnerabilities
Physical wallets contain only 2-3 strong identity credentials plus a few weaker ones - the web should work the same way
Third-party authentication (Facebook, Google, Twitter, OpenID) has become widely accepted since 2008, proving the concept works
Different contexts require different credential strengths - blog comments need weak identity, banking needs strong identity
Stack Overflow deliberately chose OpenID to promote better internet identity practices rather than adding another username/password to the world
money_quotes: Every rinky-dink website on the entire internet demanded that I create a special username and password just for them.
This is the status quo of identity on the internet. It is deeply and fundamentally broken.
In the real world, we don't regularly hold two dozen forms of identity like we expect people to on the web.
Whenever a website needs to know who I am, they should ask to see my Internet Driver's License.
But when faced with a problem that appears intractable, is your solution to throw your hands up, mindlessly embrace the status quo, and wearily sigh 'whaddaya gonna do?'
Personally, I prefer to be the change I want to see.
Adding yet another meaningless username and password to the fabric of the web does not make it better.
posts.key_insight: The internet should adopt a tiered identity model mirroring physical wallets, where a few reusable third-party credentials replace the broken paradigm of unique passwords for every site.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his enthusiasm for Just Cause 2, an open-world sandbox game that he found unexpectedly addictive despite having limited gaming time while running Stack Overflow. He contrasts it favorably with Grand Theft Auto, which felt too much like work, praising Just Cause 2's pure chaos-driven gameplay where you literally advance the plot by blowing things up. The post also touches on the strong correlation between programmers and gamers, noting that gaming.stackexchange.com exceeded his expectations. Atwood recommends the game as an affordable holiday entertainment option.
key_points: There is a high correlation between programmers and gamers, evidenced by the success of gaming.stackexchange.com
Running your own business is 'crazy stupid' amounts of work, leaving little time for gaming or blogging
Just Cause 2 succeeds where GTA failed by being pure fun rather than feeling like work
The game's design philosophy of advancing plot through destruction creates uniquely entertaining emergent gameplay
The grappling hook and parachute mechanics transform simple traversal into creative experimentation
money_quotes: There is a high correlation between "programmer" and "gamer."
Running your own business is crazy stupid amounts of work when you turn it up to eleven.
Where GTA and its ilk often felt a tad too much like work for my tastes, Just Cause 2 is almost the opposite – it is non-stop, full blown open world pandemonium from start to finish.
One of the game's explicit goals is that you advance the plot by blowing stuff up.
Have you ever wondered what happens when you zipline a moving vehicle to a tree? Or a pedestrian? Or another vehicle?
Just Cause 2 is deliriously, stupidly, absurdly entertaining.
I spent my holidays on the beautiful island of Panau, and I don't regret a second of it.
posts.key_insight: The best games, like the best software, respect the user's time by making the core experience inherently fun rather than padded with obligations that feel like work.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood analyzes the 2010 Gawker network breach to extract lessons about password security for web developers. Gawker made critical mistakes: storing passwords rather than salted hashes, using archaic DES encryption that truncated passwords to 8 characters, and requiring users to create site-specific credentials. Users who authenticated via Facebook Connect or Twitter were unaffected. Atwood argues this breach exemplifies the fundamental problem with website passwords—the more sites you trust with credentials, the more likely one will be compromised. He advocates for federated authentication through trusted third parties like Google, Facebook, or OpenID.
key_points: Never store user passwords directly—always store salted hashes, yet this basic security practice is routinely ignored
The more websites you create unique logins for, the higher your statistical probability of being compromised by at least one incompetent site
Users who authenticated via third-party services (Facebook Connect, Twitter) were completely unaffected by the breach
Most users choose weak passwords and reuse them across sites, creating cascading vulnerabilities when any single site is breached
Federated authentication through trusted providers centralizes risk to companies with actual security competence
money_quotes: You should never, ever store user passwords. If you do, you're storing passwords incorrectly.
Here's the dirty truth about website passwords: the internet is full of websites exactly like the Gawker network.

The more web sites you visit, the more networks you touch and trust with a username and password combination – the greater the odds that at least one of those networks will be compromised exactly like Gawker was.
Does the need to post a comment on Gizmodo really justify polluting the world with yet another username and password?
I trust Google a heck of a lot more than I trust J. Random Website.
We're all better off without them.
When your corporate mission is to succeed by exploiting every quasi-legal trick in the book, surely you can't be surprised when you get the same treatment in return.
posts.key_insight: The fundamental problem with web passwords isn't just that sites implement them poorly—it's that the proliferation of site-specific credentials mathematically guarantees eventual compromise, making federated authentication through trusted providers the only sensible solution.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines a troubling trend where content scraper sites began outranking Stack Overflow in Google search results for Stack Overflow's own content in 2010. Despite Stack Overflow's Creative Commons licensing explicitly encouraging content sharing, the assumption was that Google would always rank the canonical source first. After investigating and working with Google's Matt Cutts to resolve specific issues, Atwood found his faith in Google's search quality shaken. He connects this experience to a broader pattern of declining Google search quality, with spammers and content farms increasingly dominating results, and wonders if algorithmic search itself may be fundamentally flawed.
key_points: 88% of Stack Overflow's traffic came from Google, making criticism of them feel risky but necessary
Content scrapers began regularly outranking Stack Overflow for its own Creative Commons-licensed content, which should be impossible given Google's stated policies against scraped content
The situation required users to create a browser plugin to redirect from scraper sites back to Stack Overflow - a depressing workaround
After investigation, issues were found on both sides and fixed, but Atwood's confidence in Google's algorithmic search was permanently shaken
Multiple respected voices were independently noting Google's declining search quality, suggesting a systemic problem rather than isolated incidents
money_quotes: When 88.2% of all traffic for your website comes from a single source, criticizing that single source feels… risky.
I've been told many times that Google isn't a monopoly, but they apparently play one on the internet.
Blaming Google would be like blaming gravity for my own clumsiness.
I started with the golden rule: it's always my fault.
Google, the once essential tool, is somehow losing its edge. The spammers, scrapers, and SEO'ed-to-the-hilt content farms are winning.
It's a scary thing to even entertain, but maybe gravity really is broken.
Is the next generation of search destined to be less algorithmic and more social?
posts.key_insight: When the canonical source of content is routinely outranked by scrapers copying that content, it signals a fundamental breakdown in algorithmic search that threatens the entire ecosystem of original content creation on the web.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood discusses upgrading to 24 GB of RAM in his desktop, made affordable by declining DDR3 prices and Intel's Core i7 triple-channel architecture. He debunks the famous misquote about Bill Gates saying '640K ought to be enough for anybody,' while noting Gates did acknowledge underestimating how quickly 640K would become limiting. Atwood argues that cheap, abundant RAM changes how developers think about memory—from a scarce resource requiring careful management to something plentiful enough to ignore.
