The Gossamer Condor story illustrates that solving for rapid iteration (rebuilding in hours not months) can crack problems that stumped others for 18 years
Interactive HTML5 visualizations like those in Victor's 'Ladder of Abstraction' show this future is technically achievable today
money_quotes: In some ways we've actually regressed from my ancient Visual Basic 6.0 days, when you'd get dynamically notified about errors as you typed, not just when you compiled or ran unit tests.
The idea that you should be able to type (or gesture, or speak) and immediately see the result of that change is simple, but extremely powerful.
It's speed of iteration in the small.
Why not show the effects of predicted changes, and change over time? Time is the missing element in a static display of code and rendered output.
The problem was the process itself, and along with it the blind pursuit of a goal without a deeper understanding how to tackle deeply difficult challenges.

Don't get me wrong, we're failing plenty fast with our existing tools. But I can't shake the feeling that we could fail even faster if we optimized our IDEs and code editors to better visualize the effects of our changes in real time as we make them.
posts.key_insight: The most important optimization in software development isn't getting code right the first time—it's minimizing the time between making a change and seeing its effects, because faster iteration cycles compound into dramatically faster learning and problem-solving.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood visits the Internet Archive headquarters in San Francisco and makes an impassioned case for its importance to digital civilization. He describes the organization's evolution from archiving web pages to attempting to archive everything—books, music, films, and physical media. The post emphasizes how linkrot destroys internet history within just five years, making the Archive's Wayback Machine essential. Atwood calls on readers to donate money or contribute obscure tech materials like shareware CDs and computer magazines. He frames the Internet Archive as a modern Library of Alexandria, driven by common good rather than commercial interests.
key_points: The Internet Archive has expanded far beyond web snapshots to include physical books, live music, films, and scanned texts—attempting to archive all human knowledge
Linkrot is pervasive and devastating; even five-year-old blog posts have numerous dead links that would be lost forever without the Wayback Machine
The Archive operates as a non-profit motivated by common good, unlike commercial entities with ulterior motives
They actively seek donations of obscure materials—shareware CDs, computer magazines, zines, pamphlets—that wouldn't normally end up in libraries
With 30 employees in San Francisco and 200 worldwide, plus massive storage infrastructure, the scale of the mission requires community support
money_quotes: Without the Internet Archive, the Internet would have no memory.
The Internet Archive isn't merely archiving the Internet any more, they are attempting to archive everything.
Civilization is developing severe amnesia as a result; indeed it may have become too amnesiac already to notice the problem properly.
The Internet Archive is the beginning of a cure – the beginning of complete, detailed, accessible, searchable memory for society.
There's seriously nothing we don't want. I don't question. I take it in, I put it in items. I am voracious. Omnivorous. I don't say no.

It is an unprecedented kind of open source archiving, not driven by Google or Microsoft or some other commercial entity with ulterior motives, but a non-profit organization motivated by nothing more than the obvious common good.
posts.key_insight: Digital civilization is developing collective amnesia as information disappears faster than it can be preserved, and the Internet Archive stands as our primary defense against losing our shared history.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explains the critical difference between checksums (fast, for data integrity) and cryptographic hashes (slow by design, for security), arguing that traditional hashing algorithms like MD5 and SHA-1 are dangerously obsolete for password storage. He demonstrates that modern GPUs can crack MD5 hashes at rates 150 times faster than CPUs, making brute-force attacks on short passwords trivially easy. The post provides concrete benchmarks showing that all 8-character alphanumeric passwords can be cracked in just 4 hours with consumer hardware. Atwood concludes that rainbow tables are now obsolete, salts alone are insufficient protection, and developers must use bcrypt or PBKDF2 exclusively for password hashing.
key_points: GPUs produce over 150 times the hash calculations per second compared to CPUs, equivalent to a 10-year leap in Moore's Law terms
MD5 is cryptographically broken and should be considered merely a checksum, while SHA-1 is also showing weaknesses
Rainbow tables are completely obsolete because GPU-based brute forcing is now cheaper and more effective
Salts are necessary but insufficient - they provide 'the illusion of security more than any actual security' against determined attackers
Secure hashes must be slow by design, which is why bcrypt and PBKDF2 are the only appropriate choices for password storage
money_quotes: Hashes, when used for security, need to be slow. The faster you can calculate the hash, the more viable it is to use brute force to mount attacks.

The GPU on a single modern video card produces over 150 times the number of hash calculations per second compared to a modern CPU. If Moore's Law anticipates a doubling of computing power every 18 months, that's like peeking 10 years into the future.
Rainbow tables, despite their recent popularity as a subject of blog posts, have not aged gracefully.
This will provide the illusion of security more than any actual security.
These days, salts alone can no longer save you from a person willing to spend a few thousand dollars on video card hardware, and if you think they can, you're in trouble.
We can't protect users dead-set on using 'monkey1' for their password from themselves.
If you've been compromised to the point that an attacker has your password database, it's reasonable to assume they either have or can get your secret, hidden salt.
posts.key_insight: The explosive growth of GPU computing power has rendered traditional password hashing algorithms and rainbow tables obsolete, making bcrypt or PBKDF2 mandatory for any security-conscious application.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while physical books have become obsolete containers for ideas, eBooks haven't yet fulfilled their promise as replacements. He celebrates the shift from atoms to bits for publishing, noting how the internet defeated Encyclopedia Britannica and how Google Books unlocks trapped knowledge. However, he documents serious problems with current eBooks: they're often more expensive than print versions despite having no physical costs, they come with restrictive DRM that gives publishers unprecedented control, and their formatting is frequently broken and inferior to print. Using 'Don't Make Me Think' as a case study, he shows how Amazon and Apple's eBook versions butcher the careful layout of the original. He concludes that publishers must make eBooks cheaper to reflect their reduced utility and ensure they faithfully reproduce print quality, or face extinction.
key_points: Physical books trap beautiful ideas in awkward, temporary physical relics - the internet is now the superior medium for getting written ideas to people
eBooks come with troubling tradeoffs: DRM gives publishers unprecedented control, they can't be loaned/resold/donated, and they're licensed rather than owned
Current eBook pricing is nonsensical - digital versions often cost more than print despite zero physical production costs
eBook formatting quality varies wildly and is often broken compared to print, with lost footnotes, typography, and layout
Publishers must price eBooks to reflect their reduced utility (no resale, no lending) and ensure they match print quality, or suffer Encyclopedia Britannica's fate
money_quotes: Books should not be celebrated. Words, ideas, and concepts should be celebrated.
In the never-ending human quest for communication, bits have won decisively over atoms.
With eBooks, book publishers now have an unprecedented level of control over when, where, and how you can read their books.
In the brave new world of eBooks, book publishers are waking up drunk with newfound power.

Once you've bought that physical book, you can do with it whatever you will: read it, burn it, photocopy it, share it, resell it, loan it, donate it, even throw it at passers-by as a makeshift weapon.
If I can get a new copy of a book for less than the eBook, no sale and screw you.
What's the point of a bookshelf full of books other than as an antiquated trophy case of written ideas trapped in awkward, temporary physical relics?
posts.key_insight: The transition from physical books to eBooks should liberate ideas and reduce costs, but publishers' DRM overreach and poor formatting are squandering the opportunity and creating products that are simultaneously more expensive and less valuable than print.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that documentation will always be inadequate because writing for humans is harder than writing for machines. The solution is to embrace reading source code directly, which represents the ultimate truth about how software actually works. He cites Brandon Bloom's philosophy that developers should clone and read the source of everything they depend on, treating all code in their stack as their own responsibility. The post emphasizes that true software craftsmanship requires understanding the code beneath you, not just consuming APIs and hoping the docs are correct.
key_points: Documentation will always be insufficient because writing clearly for humans is inherently harder than writing code for machines
Source code is the ultimate truth - more accurate and up-to-date than any documentation could ever be
Developers should git clone everything they depend on and be willing to dive into unfamiliar codebases
Reading source code is a skill that transforms developers - those who learn it wonder how they ever worked without it
If software runs on your machine, it's your software and your responsibility to understand it
money_quotes: No matter what the documentation says, the source code is the ultimate truth, the best and most definitive and up-to-date documentation you're likely to find.
When people report a problem with their stack, the first question I ask them is: 'Well, did you read the source code?'
The source code never lies.
Everyone's software becomes my software because all of their bugs are my bugs.
If it runs on my machine, it's my software. I'm responsible for it. I must understand it.
What inexperienced developers consider beautiful is superficial, and what they consider ugly, is battle-hardened production-ready code from master hackers.
Nobody reads other people's code for fun. Hell, I don't even like reading my own code.
posts.key_insight: The source code is always the definitive documentation, and developers who learn to read it directly rather than relying on documentation become fundamentally more capable at their craft.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that email security is critically important because email serves as the master key to your entire online identity. He uses a harrowing real-world hacking story to illustrate the dangers, then provides a step-by-step guide for enabling two-factor authentication on Gmail. The post emphasizes that the minor inconvenience of 2FA is vastly preferable to the catastrophic consequences of email compromise. Atwood acknowledges the friction involved but insists it's necessary, comparing it to bank vaults and door locks.
key_points: Email is the skeleton key to your online identity - losing control of it enables complete identity theft
Two-factor authentication using your cell phone makes email virtually hacker-proof
You should print backup verification codes and keep them with you at all times
App-specific passwords allow you to revoke individual application access without exposing your master password
The inconvenience of 2FA is trivial compared to the catastrophic cost of having your identity stolen
money_quotes: Your email is the skeleton key to your online identity.
When you lose control of your email to a hacker – not if, but when you lose control of your email to a hacker – the situation is dire.
You should start thinking of security for your email as roughly equivalent to the sort of security you'd want on your bank account.

To access your email, they'd need to somehow gain control of your cell phone, too. I can't see that happening unless you're in some sort of hostage situation, and at that point I think email security is the least of your problems.
But it is inconvenient in the same way that bank vaults and door locks are.
Shut up. I know things. You will listen to me. Do it anyway.
You're making life all too easy for the hackers who make a fabulous living from stealing and permanently defacing online identities just like yours.
posts.key_insight: Email security deserves the same level of protection as your bank account because email is the master key that unlocks your entire online identity.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the apps vs. websites debate through the lens of eBay's dramatically better mobile and tablet apps compared to its cluttered website. He argues that apps benefit from enforced constraints that lead to simpler, faster user experiences, while websites suffer from years of accumulated design debt and unlimited freedom to add complexity. However, he contends that websites remain essential for universality, discoverability, and consistent cross-device experiences. His ultimate conclusion is that apps won't kill websites—only websites that fail to maintain quality will be displaced by their app counterparts.
key_points: Constraints are a design strength: mobile apps' limited screen space and standardized UI controls force better, simpler design decisions
eBay's website illustrates how unlimited web freedom leads to accumulated complexity and poor user experience over time
Mobile-first design strategy helps create simple interfaces that scale up, rather than complicated ones that must scale down
Websites retain crucial advantages: universal browser access, no installation required, automatic updates, and consistent cross-platform experience
Apps and websites serve complementary roles—apps need websites as data sources and download hosts
money_quotes: It's not so much that the eBay apps are great, but that the eBay website is so very, very bad.
The nearly unlimited freedom that you get in a modern web browser to build whatever UI you can dream up, and assume as large or as small a page as you like, often ends up being harmful to users.
Design simple things that scale up; not complicated things you need to scale down.
Applications, unlike websites, can't be visited. They aren't indexed by Google.

Once you have an application installed on your device, how do you update it to add features or fix bugs? How do users even know if your app is out of date or needs updating? And why should they need to care in the first place?
Apps will always need websites, if for nothing else other than a source of data, as a mothership to phone home to.
Will mobile and tablet apps kill websites? A few, certainly. But only the websites stupid enough to let them.
posts.key_insight: The real competition isn't apps versus websites—it's disciplined, constraint-driven design versus the accumulated complexity that unlimited freedom enables.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts his experience helping his out-of-state mother fix her broken internet connection, which was preventing her from video chatting with her grandchildren. After considering buying her a new iPad or using Geek Squad's expensive services, he discovered Geekatoo, a platform connecting people with local tech-savvy helpers. For just $45, a local geek diagnosed the problem (a cable installer had connected her to a neighbor's WiFi) and fixed it within two days. The post serves as both a personal story and an endorsement of peer-to-peer tech support services over expensive corporate alternatives.
key_points: Grandparents' desire to stay connected with grandchildren via internet has become a critical dependency that creates urgent tech support situations
Traditional tech support options like Geek Squad are becoming so expensive ($299) that buying new devices ($399 iPad) is often more economical
Peer-to-peer tech support platforms like Geekatoo offer dramatically cheaper alternatives ($45) while supporting local geek communities
The actual technical problem was surprisingly mundane - a lazy cable installer had connected to a neighbor's WiFi instead of setting up proper infrastructure
The 'disposable computer future' is arriving where fixing old technology costs nearly as much as replacement
money_quotes: Grandchildren are like crack cocaine to grandparents.
I'm convinced that if our parents could somehow snort our children up their noses to get a bigger fix, they would.
When I can buy my mom a new iPad for $399, the idea of paying $299 just to have someone come out and fix her old stuff starts to feel like a really bad idea.
I suppose it's a preview of our disposable computer future, because it's increasingly cheaper to buy a new one than it is to bother fixing the old one.
I could send out the geek bat-signal!
Any website that can make me look like a hero to my mom, and keep my fellow Super User geeks gainfully employed doing superhero work on my behalf gets a huge thumbs up from me.
posts.key_insight: Peer-to-peer tech support platforms represent a better model than corporate alternatives, offering affordable help while keeping money in the local geek community.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the Radical Honesty movement, which advocates telling the truth 100% of the time without any filters. Through A.J. Jacobs' experimentation with the concept, Atwood explores the surprising benefits of honesty—efficiency, deeper relationships, and personal bravery—while acknowledging its potential for destruction. He ultimately rejects Radical Honesty as a lifestyle but extracts a valuable lesson: truth should be purposeful. The post concludes that honesty without consideration of purpose or consequences is harmful extremism, and sometimes silence or even lying is the wiser choice.
key_points: Radical Honesty proposes eliminating all filters between thoughts and speech, sharing everything including uncomfortable truths about relationships and work
There are genuine benefits to increased honesty: less mental energy spent parsing others' statements, more efficient communication, and the possibility of breakthrough conversations
Unfiltered truth can be destructive—the closer you are to someone, the more care you must take with honesty
The real lesson isn't to always tell the truth, but to ensure your honesty serves a purpose beyond simply 'because I could'
We teach children to always tell the truth while practicing far more nuanced honesty ourselves, revealing an inherent contradiction in how we approach truth-telling
money_quotes: Deceit makes our world go round. Without lies, marriages would crumble, workers would be fired, egos would be shattered, governments would collapse.
What we often don't acknowledge is that the truth is kind of scary. That's why we have a hard time being honest with ourselves, much less those around us.
Advocating telling the truth 100% of the time, no matter what, is harmful extremism.
The truth has a great capacity to offend and injure, and you will find that the closer you are to someone, the more care you must take to disguise or even conceal the truth.
Any child can blurt out the truth, without thought to the consequences. It takes great maturity to appreciate the value of silence.
Truth, real truth, is honesty with a purpose.
If your honesty has no purpose, if you can't imagine any positive outcome from being honest, I suggest you're better off keeping it to yourself. Or even lying.
posts.key_insight: Truth without purpose is just cruelty with good branding—real honesty requires asking what change you hope to create before speaking.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the scientific research on money and happiness, noting that emotional well-being plateaus at around $75,000 annual income. Beyond meeting basic needs, how you spend money matters more than how much you have. He synthesizes research from happiness scientists like Dan Gilbert into eight practical principles for spending money in ways that maximize happiness. The core insight is that most people squander their money's happiness potential because they don't understand what actually brings lasting satisfaction.
key_points: Emotional well-being increases with income only up to about $75,000 annually, after which additional money provides diminishing returns on happiness
Experiences create more lasting happiness than material possessions because they become unique memories that can be shared and appreciated over time
Frequent small pleasures are more effective for happiness than rare large purchases due to our rapid adaptation to change
Spending money on others and building social connections provides greater happiness returns than self-focused spending
We are poor predictors of what will make us happy, so following others' recommendations often leads to better outcomes than trusting our own judgment
money_quotes: Money is an opportunity for happiness, but it is an opportunity that people routinely squander because the things they think will make them happy often don't.
Things get old. Things become ordinary. Things stay the same. Things wear out. Things are difficult to share.
When it comes to happiness, frequency is more important than intensity.
Most people don't know the basic scientific facts about happiness – about what brings it and what sustains it – and so they don't know how to use their money to acquire it.
Happiness is a lot harder to come by than money.

Having the easy 'out' of insurance or a generous return policy can paradoxically lead to even more angst and unhappiness because people deprived themselves of the emotional benefit of full commitment.
posts.key_insight: Beyond a moderate income threshold, happiness depends not on how much money you have, but on understanding the science of what actually makes humans happy and spending accordingly.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses his experience upgrading automated cat feeders to illustrate a fundamental truth about software: products are nothing more than collections of tiny details. The old feeder worked but was annoying in countless small ways, while the new version addressed nearly every complaint. This parallels software development, where getting details right separates products customers love from ones they merely tolerate. At Stack Overflow, the team obsessively incorporated user feedback, completing over 1,500 feature requests since launch. The key is to ship something that works, then relentlessly refine based on real user feedback.
key_points: Products that technically work can still be frustrating if details are neglected - the old cat feeder functioned but had numerous small annoyances that accumulated over time
Great products come from obsessing over every tiny detail, not just the 'important' parts
It's acceptable to get details wrong initially, but you must listen to user feedback and continuously refine
Stack Overflow launched without comments and has since completed over 1,500 feature requests based on user feedback
If you ignore the small annoyances in your product, a competitor who cares about details will outperform you
money_quotes: This is all your app is: a collection of tiny details.
Getting the details right is the difference between something that delights, and something customers tolerate.
Your software, your product, is nothing more than a collection of tiny details.

If you don't obsess over all those details, if you think it's OK to concentrate on the 'important' parts and continue to ignore the other umpteen dozen tiny little ways your product annoys the people who use it on a daily basis – you're not creating great software. Someone else is.
I felt as if the Petmate Corporation™ was actually listening to all the feedback from the people who used their product, and actively refined the product to address our complaints and suggestions.
It's OK to screw up the details initially, provided you're listening to feedback from the people who use your product.
Imagine that. Someone who cares about the details just as much as you do.
posts.key_insight: The difference between software that delights users and software they merely tolerate lies entirely in the accumulation of tiny details that most developers dismiss as unimportant.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood pushes back against the 'everyone should learn to code' movement, arguing that programming is not a fundamental life skill like reading, writing, and math. Using NYC Mayor Bloomberg's pledge to learn coding as a jumping-off point, he contends that coding is a specialized skill that most people don't need for their jobs. He argues the movement wrongly assumes more code is inherently good, conflates coding with problem-solving, and oversimplifies the path to professional programming. Instead of learning to code, Atwood suggests people focus on research skills and effective communication, which have broader applicability.
key_points: Programming is a specialized skill like plumbing, not a fundamental life skill like literacy and numeracy
The goal should be solving problems, not writing code—ideally writing as little code as possible
Learning to code doesn't quickly translate to professional programming; it still requires thousands of hours of practice
Understanding how computers and the internet work is more valuable for most people than learning to code
Research skills and effective communication are more universally applicable than programming
money_quotes: Should you learn to write code? No, I can't get behind that. You should be learning to write as little code as possible. Ideally none.
Software developers tend to be software addicts who think their job is to write code. But it's not. Their job is to solve problems.
I would no more urge everyone to learn programming than I would urge everyone to learn plumbing.
Before you go rushing out to learn to code, figure out what your problem actually is.
One bad programmer can easily create two new jobs a year.
Please don't advocate learning to code just for the sake of learning how to code. Or worse, because of the fat paychecks.
posts.key_insight: Programming is a means to solve problems, not an end in itself, and advocating universal coding education confuses the tool with the goal while ignoring more fundamental skills like research and communication.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the history and purpose of Lorem Ipsum placeholder text, tracing it back to a Cicero text from 45 BC that was deliberately scrambled to be unintelligible. He explains that the point of 'greeking' text is to help viewers focus on design rather than content, though he acknowledges some designers argue against using boilerplate placeholders. The post catalogs an exhaustive collection of Lorem Ipsum alternatives and generators, from novelty options like bacon and hipster-themed text to literature-based and profession-specific variants. Atwood extends the concept to placeholder images, listing services for everything from kittens to gray boxes. He concludes that while the internet has vastly exceeded our placeholder text needs, the creative possibilities remain tempting.
key_points: Lorem Ipsum derives from a 45 BC Cicero text about pleasure and pain, deliberately rearranged to be incoherent
The purpose of 'greeking' is to obscure text meaning so viewers focus on design form rather than content
Using real content instead of placeholders can help avoid 'Blank Page Syndrome' in certain contexts
The internet has produced an overwhelming variety of Lorem Ipsum alternatives spanning themes from bacon to Samuel L. Jackson
Placeholder images have their own ecosystem, raising the question of what constitutes the 'Lorem Ipsum of images'
money_quotes: Greeking obscures portions of a work for the purpose of either emphasizing form over details or displaying placeholders for unavailable content.
The theory is that, since it's unintelligible, nobody will attempt to read it, but instead focus on other aspects of the design.

If you put readable text in the design, people might think the text is important to the design, that the text represents the sort of content you expect to see, or that the text somehow itself needs to be copyedited and updated and critiqued.
Of course the whole point of Lorem Ipsum is that the words aren't supposed to mean anything, so attempting to divine its meaning is somewhat… unsatisfying, perhaps by design.
But what is the Lorem Ipsum of images? Is there even one?
Like the market for ironic t-shirts, the Internet has ensured that our placeholder greeked text needs have not merely been met but vastly exceeded for the foreseeable future.
posts.key_insight: Placeholder text serves the crucial design function of preventing viewers from reading content so they can evaluate form, which explains both its deliberate incoherence and the internet's explosion of creative alternatives.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood responds to the controversy surrounding his earlier 'Please Don't Learn to Code' post by clarifying his actual position: he doesn't oppose learning to code, but opposes treating coding as a universal requirement or silver bullet. He argues that programming should emerge naturally from pursuing interesting problems rather than being learned for its own sake. Using the story of a 45-year-old attorney who discovered joy in building complex database solutions, Atwood illustrates that the path to programming should be through passion and genuine interest. He concludes that the only qualification needed to become a programmer is to 'fall in love with code.'
key_points: Coding skills should emerge from pursuing problems you find interesting, not from treating programming as inherently valuable
The history of computing has been about freeing people from needing to code, so universal coding mandates go backwards
Basic internet literacy doesn't require everyone to understand how computers work, just as driving doesn't require understanding auto mechanics
The specific technologies and languages of today may be unrecognizable in 20-30 years, making particular approaches less important than fundamentals
True programmers recognize fellow believers who are genuinely passionate about code, regardless of formal credentials
money_quotes: I'd argue that the people who need to learn to code will be spurred on most of all by honesty, not religious faith in the truthiness of code as a universal good.

I view the entire arc of software development as a field where we programmers spend our lives writing code so that our fellow human beings no longer need to write code (or even worse, become programmers) to get things done with computers.
I'd prefer that people spend their time discovering what problems they love and find interesting, first, and researching the hell out of those problems.
What I advocate is shamelessly following your joy.
I didn't become a programmer because someone told me learning to code was important, I became a programmer because I wanted to change the rules of the video games I was playing.
If you want to be a programmer, all you need to do is follow your joy and fall in love with code.
Any programmer worth their salt immediately recognizes a fellow true believer, a person as madly in love with code as they are, warts and all.
posts.key_insight: The path to becoming a programmer should be paved by genuine passion for solving interesting problems, not by treating coding as a mandatory skill everyone must acquire.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his success with Coding Horror and Stack Overflow, initially resisting the urge to dispense life advice since what worked for him might not work for others. He acknowledges he's 'crazy' and compares giving advice to Jared Fogle recommending Subway for weight loss. Despite his reservations, he distills his approach into three principles: embrace failure, work publicly, and focus on meaningful work. The core message is that fear—of failure, inadequacy, looking dumb—is the primary obstacle preventing people from achieving greatness.
key_points: Personal success strategies are highly individual and may not transfer to others
Three principles for success: Embrace the Suck, Do It in Public, Pick Stuff That Matters
Fear in its many forms (failure, inadequacy, exposure) is the main barrier to achievement
Working publicly creates accountability and accelerates improvement
Being serious about your goals is easier when you don't take yourself too seriously
money_quotes: The short answer is that I wouldn't.
In other words, what I did worked for me, but I'm crazy.
I find it's easier to be serious when I'm not being entirely serious.
You know what's better than being safe? Being AWESOME.
The only thing preventing us from being awesome is our own fear of sucking.
Mostly, I think it's the fear that gets us, in all its forms.
Who wants to live forever?
posts.key_insight: Fear of failure is the primary obstacle to achievement, so the path to success begins with accepting that you'll suck before you get good.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on how parenting has taught him fundamental lessons about human communication that extend far beyond raising children. He recommends the book 'How to Talk So Kids Will Listen & Listen So Kids Will Talk' as essential reading for anyone who interacts with humans of any age. The book's core techniques—listening, repeating what people say, and labeling their emotions rather than immediately jumping to solutions—have transformed his interactions with everyone, not just his kids. Atwood admits he was doing it all wrong by trying to solve problems for his children instead of guiding them to find solutions themselves. The post ultimately argues that the skills required for good parenting are the same skills required for effective human interaction at any age.
key_points: Parenting is far more difficult than any professional career, yet it offers unique insights into human nature and communication
The key to effective communication with children (and adults) is to listen, repeat what they say, and label their emotions rather than immediately providing solutions
Describing situations and explaining consequences is more effective than judging or giving commands
Children don't innately know how to handle emotions—they must be taught coping mechanisms by their parents
The communication techniques that work for children aged 2 work equally well for adults up to age 102
money_quotes: Nothing captures the continued absurdity of the human condition better than having a child does.
Parenting is by far the toughest job I've ever had. It makes my so-called career seem awfully quaint in comparison.
Language: Best. Invention. Ever.
The difference between a child who freaks out at the slightest breeze, and a child who can confidently navigate an unfamiliar world? The parents.
The techniques in this book are so brilliant, I use them in every human interaction I can, no matter the age of the conversant.
I've improved my interactions with all human beings from age 2 to 102.
Kids will teach you, if you let them. They'll teach you that getting born is the easy part. Anyone can do that in a day. But becoming a well-adjusted human being? That'll take the rest of your life.
posts.key_insight: The same communication techniques that help children feel heard and find their own solutions—listening, acknowledging emotions, and describing rather than judging—are equally powerful tools for improving every human interaction.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits his previous router recommendations, confirming that the combination of commodity hardware with open-source firmware remains the winning formula for home networking. After trying DD-WRT and OpenWRT, he settled on Tomato firmware for its simplicity and superior Quality of Service implementation. He recommends the Asus RT-N16 as an affordable workhorse option and the RT-N66U as the premium choice, both running Tomato to replace their mediocre stock firmware. The post emphasizes that QoS is essential for maintaining a responsive internet connection when multiple users share bandwidth.
key_points: The magic formula for routers is commodity hardware combined with open-source firmware like Tomato
Quality of Service (QoS) is crucial - it's the difference between responsive internet and brutally slow connections when bandwidth is shared
The Asus RT-N16 ($80) is an excellent budget choice while the RT-N66U ($190) is the state of the art for those wanting dual-band performance
Stock manufacturer firmware is consistently inferior to open-source alternatives like Tomato
Tomato firmware stands out for being 'gloriously simple' while having the most functional QoS implementation
money_quotes: The magic router formula these days is a combination of commodity hardware and open-source firmware.
QoS is the difference between a responsive Internet and one that's brutally dog slow.
Unknown to you, the guy in the next room or at the next table is hogging the internet bandwidth to download the Lord Of The Rings Special Extended Edition in 1080p HDTV format. You're screwed.
I'm so enamored of this one-two punch combo, in fact, I might even say it represents the future.
That's why we get our Tomato on, people!
I guess that's open source, too many choices with nobody to really tell you what's going to work reliably on your particular hardware.
posts.key_insight: Consumer router hardware is perfectly capable, but manufacturers consistently ship inferior firmware - pairing commodity hardware with open-source firmware like Tomato delivers both stability and essential features like proper QoS that stock firmware lacks.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues for abandoning physical media in favor of digital formats, specifically advocating for 192kbps variable bitrate MP3 encoding as the sweet spot between quality and efficiency. He challenges the audiophile community's claims about higher bitrates by citing research debunking 24-bit/192kHz audio and setting up a blind listening test. The post details his personal project to digitize his CD collection while disposing of the physical discs, and provides technical comparisons of file sizes across different encoding formats. Atwood's core argument is that efficiency should win when quality differences are imperceptible to human ears.
key_points: Physical media ownership is increasingly pointless when digital formats offer equivalent quality with greater convenience
Research shows 24-bit/192kHz audio is actually slightly inferior to CD quality while using 6x the storage space
The LAME encoder's 'standard' preset at ~192kbps average provides indistinguishable quality from raw CD audio for typical listeners
Efficiency gains from compression (7.1MB vs 51MB for a 5-minute track) outweigh theoretical quality benefits no one can perceive
Blind listening tests are the only valid way to settle debates about audio quality perception
money_quotes: I think I'm now at peace with the idea of not owning any physical media ever again, if I can help it.
spending 7× or 5× the bandwidth and storage for completely inaudible 'quality' improvements is a dagger directly in the heart of this efficiency-loving nerd
On the Internet, nobody knows you're a dog. Personally, I think you're a human being full of crap, but let's drop some science on this and see if you can prove it.
Its playback fidelity is slightly inferior to 16/44.1 or 16/48, and it takes up 6 times the space.
wouldn't you rather be able to fit 200 songs on your device instead of 100?
I'll just destroy them outright. Stupid atoms!
if the difference in encoding bitrate quality is as profound as certain vocal elements of the community would have you believe it is, that difference should be audible in any music track
posts.key_insight: Practical efficiency should trump theoretical perfection when the difference is imperceptible—a principle that applies far beyond audio encoding.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reveals the results of his crowdsourced blind listening test comparing MP3 files at various bitrates against raw CD audio. Over 3,500 participants rated five audio samples (all using the same Starship song), and the data shows that only the 128kbps CBR sample was distinguishably worse than the others. The 160kbps sample anomalously scored highest, likely due to position bias from appearing first. Statistical analysis confirms that beyond 128kbps, listeners cannot reliably distinguish between compressed MP3s and uncompressed CD audio, validating the LAME encoder's default 192kbps VBR setting as the optimal quality-to-size tradeoff.
key_points: Only 128kbps CBR was statistically distinguishable as lower quality compared to higher bitrates and raw CD audio
The 160kbps sample scoring highest despite lower bitrate suggests position bias contaminated results
192kbps VBR showed only a 'wafer thin' statistical difference from raw CD audio at the 95% confidence level
The experiment cost $103.72 for 800GB of data across 200k requests via Amazon CloudFront
FLAC compression could reveal bitrates by file size, providing an unintentional verification method
money_quotes: I've maintained for a long, long time that typical 128kbps MP3s are not acceptable quality. Even for the worst song ever.
Give us VBR at an average bitrate higher than 128kbps, or give us death!
More fuel for the argument that people can't hear a difference at bitrates above 128kbps, and even if they do, they're probably imagining it.
If we didn't throw out this result, we'd have to conclude that the 160kbps sample was somehow superior to the raw CD audio, which is… clearly insane.
I'm talking absolutely wafer thin here.
Even dogs won't be able to hear the difference between 192kbps VBR MP3 tracks and the original CD.
posts.key_insight: Beyond 128kbps, the perceived difference between MP3 compression and CD-quality audio is largely imaginary, making 192kbps VBR the practical sweet spot for audio encoding.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits the perennial criticism of PHP as a poorly designed programming language, using the metaphor of a double-clawed hammer to illustrate its fundamental brokenness. He acknowledges that despite years of criticism dating back to at least 2002, PHP remains dominant because it's cheap, popular, and runs everywhere with zero friction. Rather than adding to the pile of PHP critiques, Atwood argues that the programming community should focus on building compelling alternatives that match PHP's ease of deployment. He announces his intention to work on an open source project aimed at strengthening another language ecosystem to genuinely compete with PHP's ubiquity.
key_points: PHP's continued popularity proves that quality is irrelevant in technology adoption—cheap, popular, and ubiquitous always wins
The real problem isn't PHP's flaws (which are well-documented) but the lack of alternatives that match its ease of deployment
Critiquing PHP endlessly is less useful than building and promoting better tools
For open source web projects requiring zero-friction deployment, PHP remains nearly the only option, which should alarm developers
The goal should be making better alternatives so obviously superior that new programmers naturally choose them over PHP
money_quotes: PHP is the Nickelback of programming languages.
PHP isn't so much a language as a random collection of arbitrary stuff, a virtual explosion at the keyword and function factory.
The great crime of PHP is its utter banality. Its continued popularity is living proof that quality is irrelevant; cheap and popular and everywhere always wins.

If you want to produce free-as-in-whatever code that runs on virtually every server in the world with zero friction or configuration hassles, PHP is damn near your only option. If that doesn't scare you, then check your pulse, because you might be dead.
The best way to fix the PHP problem at this point is to make the alternatives so outstanding that the choice of the better hammer becomes obvious.
How do we encourage new programmers to avoid picking up the double clawed hammer in favor of, well, a regular hammer?
I've told myself a million times to stop exaggerating.
posts.key_insight: Complaining about bad tools is pointless without building alternatives so compelling and accessible that choosing them becomes the obvious choice.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that Windows 8 represents Microsoft's first truly revolutionary OS release since Windows 95, as the company attempts to compete with Apple's tablet dominance. He traces the stagnation of Windows innovation over 17 years while Apple redefined computing with the iPhone and iPad. The new Metro interface, combined with the traditional desktop, represents Microsoft's high-stakes gamble to remain relevant in the post-PC era. Despite the jarring Jekyll-and-Hyde nature of combining touch and keyboard paradigms, Atwood is cautiously optimistic about Microsoft's chances, especially given the reasonable $40 upgrade pricing.
key_points: Windows has been essentially unchanged since Windows 95, with 17 years of 'mostly minor and often inconsequential design changes'
Apple's iPad proved that tablets might be better than traditional PCs by sidestepping decades of accumulated cruft and hacks
Microsoft's earlier Surface Table failed not because of bad technology but because of inaccessible pricing and form factor ($10,000 vs iPad's $500)
Windows 8 combines two radically different paradigms - traditional desktop and touch-centric Metro - creating a learning curve but potentially a saner approach than Apple's hard iOS/OSX divide
The $40 upgrade pricing signals Microsoft finally understanding how to compete in the consumer market
money_quotes: The last truly revolutionary version of Windows was Windows 95.
Maybe a tablet is better than traditional PCs, because it sidesteps all the accumulated cruft and hacks the PC ecosystem has accreted over the last 30 years.
If you're Microsoft, this is the point at which you should be crapping your pants in abject fear.
It is nothing less than the first stages of the heat death of the PC ecosystem.
The best way to predict the future is to invent it.
In the post PC era, Microsoft is betting the company on Windows 8, desperately trying to serve two masters with one operating system.
Windows 8 is, in my humble opinion, the most innovative version of Windows Microsoft has released since Windows 95. Maybe ever.
posts.tone: analytical and cautiously optimistic, with characteristic irreverent humor
posts.key_insight: Windows 8 represents Microsoft's desperate but promising attempt to remain relevant by bridging the gap between traditional PC computing and the tablet-driven post-PC era that Apple created.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues strongly against writing traditional technical books, citing the poor economics for authors and the superiority of online content. He reveals that book writing is one of his top 10 life regrets, despite being a fan of reading books. The post announces his own blog-to-book compilations through Hyperink, demonstrating his preferred approach of creating online content first, then packaging it into book form. He criticizes the publishing industry's broken model where authors earn roughly $1-2 per book while publishers take the lion's share. Atwood also pushes back on those who view blogging as 'overhead,' arguing that if you can't explain your research in writing, you don't truly understand it. He concludes that aspiring authors should lower their expectations and start with regular blogging to see where it leads.
key_points: Traditional book publishing is economically broken for authors—John Resig earned just $1.87 per copy of a book selling for $29.54
Online content is superior: searchable, owned by you, globally accessible, interactive, and can generate perpetual ad revenue
The optimal approach is creating content online first, then optionally converting to print for 'the best of both worlds'
Writing up research as blog posts is essential—if you can't explain it clearly in writing, you don't understand it
Blogging generates ideas and insights that traditional research alone doesn't, as Steve Yegge noted
money_quotes: In short, do not write a book. You'll put in mountains of effort for precious little reward, tangible or intangible.
In the end, all you will have to show for it is an out-of-print dead tree tombstone. The only people who will be impressed by that are the clueless and the irrelevant.
When I buy books, I want most of that money to go to the author, not the publishing middlemen.
If you can't explain the results of your research to others, in writing, in a way they can understand, you don't understand it.

And if you aren't willing to publish your research in the form of a simple web page that anyone in the world can visit and potentially learn from, why did you bother doing that research in the first place?
I honestly think you have to be a little bit crazy to sit down and pound out an entire book these days.
Lower your expectations. The happiest authors are the ones that don't expect much.
posts.key_insight: Online content creation should precede book writing because it offers authors more ownership, reach, and longevity while allowing them to convert successful content into books afterward—never the reverse.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood addresses Stack Overflow's increasing strictness by using a deleted 'fun' question about programming jargon as a case study. He argues that while community-building content can be entertaining, Stack Overflow must prioritize learning over entertainment—functioning as 'a college, not a frat house.' Rather than let the content disappear entirely, Atwood preserves the top 30 user-submitted programming terms on his blog, since all Stack Exchange content is Creative Commons licensed. The curated list showcases programmer creativity and humor while illustrating the tension between community engagement and educational mission.
key_points: Stack Overflow naturally trends toward strictness as a necessary 'immune system' defense mechanism for maintaining quality
Questions that invite hundreds of opinion-based answers are polls, not questions, and don't serve the learning mission
The deleted jargon question represents the tension between 'fun' community content and educational value
Creative Commons licensing allows valuable deleted content to find permanent homes elsewhere
Programmer culture has a rich tradition of playful jargon, from 'Yoda Conditions' to 'Pokémon Exception Handling'
money_quotes: Stack Overflow – like most online communities I've studied – naturally trends toward increased strictness over time.
It isn't always a pleasant process, but it is, unfortunately, a necessary one if you want to survive.
A question that invites 386 different 'answers' isn't a question at all. It's an opinion survey, a poll, a List of X.
Stack Overflow is a college, not a frat house.

All the content on the site must exist to serve the mission of learning over entertainment – even if that means making difficult calls about removing some questions and answers that fail to meet those goals.
The process of taking a well-designed piece of code and, through a series of small, reversible changes, making it completely unmaintainable by anyone except yourself.
A bug in the application that has existed so long that it is now part of the expected functionality, and user support is required to actually fix it.
posts.key_insight: Online communities must balance entertainment and engagement against their core educational mission, even when that means removing popular content that doesn't serve learning goals.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that persuasion is the most critical skill for anyone wanting to effect change, whether in software engineering or life more broadly. Using a powerful scene from 'The Last King of Scotland' as a framing device, he explores how even the best ideas fail without the ability to convince others. He cites Steve Yegge's advice that marketing yourself matters more than writing amazing code, and Marc Hedlund's approach of being 'Lobbyist-in-Chief' rather than a traditional boss. Atwood emphasizes that persuasion works best through leading by example, building trust, and telling compelling stories—not just presenting data. He holds up Martin Luther King Jr.'s 'Letter from a Birmingham Jail' as the gold standard of persuasive writing.
key_points: The ability to persuade others is more important than being right—good ideas die without effective advocacy
Steve Yegge argues the most important skill for software engineers is marketing themselves and their projects, not writing code
Data alone isn't persuasive enough; you need to tell a bigger, more inspiring story
Persuasion works best through leading by example, building trust first, and being patient
Even without formal authority, anyone can influence their team through grassroots persuasion techniques
money_quotes: But you did not persuade me, Nicholas. You did not persuade me!
To have any hope of influencing others, you must be able to persuade them.
The one thing every software engineer should know is not how to write amazing code, but how to market themselves and their project.
I think of myself as Lobbyist-in-Chief.
The product is the result of the ideas that together we've agreed to pursue.
If I measure by click data alone, all Internet advertising should have breasts in it.
You need to tell a bigger, grander, more inspiring story than that to be truly persuasive.
Nobody ever changed anything by remaining quiet, idly standing by, or blending into the faceless, voiceless masses.
posts.key_insight: Being right is worthless if you can't convince others—persuasion is the essential skill that transforms ideas into action.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood chronicles his discovery of high-quality 27-inch IPS monitors available directly from Korean eBay vendors at a fraction of domestic prices. After the iPad 3's impressive IPS display made him dissatisfied with his TN monitors, he purchased three 2560x1440 IPS displays for under $1000 total. While acknowledging the grey market nature and no-frills experience (Korean labeling, basic DVI-only inputs, no OSD), he argues the stunning image quality makes the modest risk worthwhile. The post serves as both a buying guide and a call to escape what he calls the 'TN display ghetto.'
key_points: The iPad 3's high-quality IPS display raised expectations and made TN panels feel unacceptable
Korean eBay vendors sell premium 27-inch IPS monitors for around $350 shipped versus $700+ domestically
The monitors are no-frills (DVI-only, no OSD, glossy panels, basic build quality) but the panel quality is excellent
The price disparity represents a grey market opportunity that may not last
IPS superiority over TN is described as vast in every measurable dimension
money_quotes: The difference between TN and IPS is vast in every measurable dimension.
As my friend Scott Wasson of Tech Report fame says, 'every pixel on a TN panel is a bad pixel.'
It's worth it just to get out of the awful TN display ghetto most of us are stuck in.
In the past, I favored my wallet over my eyes, and chose TN. I now deeply regret that decision.
High quality IPS displays are no longer extortionately expensive, particularly if you buy them directly from Korea.
In the end, I decided my eyes deserve better than TN. Maybe yours do too.
I am now the proud owner of three awesome high resolution (2560x1440) 27" IPS LCDs, and I paid less than a thousand dollars for all three of them.
posts.key_insight: Programmers who spend all day staring at screens shouldn't settle for inferior display technology when better options have become surprisingly affordable through grey market channels.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the history and value of '20% time' policies, tracing the concept from 3M's 1948 '15 percent time' through HP's Friday afternoon experiments to Google's famous implementation. He argues that giving employees freedom to work on personal projects has produced some of the most successful products at these companies, including Post-It Notes, Gmail, and Google News. The post also examines Hack Days as a related but more structured alternative, citing Yahoo's 2005 implementation. Atwood concludes by outlining four prerequisites for successfully implementing such policies: schedule slack, acceptance of daydreaming, tolerance for failure, and respect for individual experimentation.
key_points: The 20% time concept originated at 3M in 1948, not Google, and HP formalized it with Friday afternoon 'lab stores' access
As many as half of Google's products reportedly originated from 20% time, including Gmail, Google News, and AdSense
Hack Days emerged as a more structured alternative, with Yahoo and Atlassian pioneering company-wide 24-hour building sessions
Atlassian's attempt to implement ongoing 20% time struggled because developers felt guilty taking time away from teammates
Four cultural prerequisites are essential: schedule slack, valuing daydreaming, accepting failure, and respecting bottom-up innovation
money_quotes: Many of their best and most popular products come from the thin sliver of time they granted employees to work on whatever they wanted to.
Every good work of software starts by scratching a developer's personal itch.
Daydreaming is not the antithesis of work; on the contrary, creative problem solving requires it.
Without failure, and lots of the stuff, there can be no innovation, or true experimentation.
You have to truly believe, as a company, and as peers, that crucial innovations and improvements can come from everyone at the company at any time, in bottom-up fashion.

Having some official acknowledgement that time spent working on whatever you think will make things better around these here parts is not just tolerated – but encouraged – might go a long way towards making work feel a lot less like work.
A portion of their paid time to chase rainbows and hatch their own ideas.
posts.key_insight: The most innovative products often emerge not from top-down planning but from giving employees structured freedom to pursue their own ideas—provided the company culture genuinely accepts failure and values experimentation over constant productivity.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts his teenage years as a computer enthusiast in the 1980s, when expensive long-distance calls and hourly fees made accessing BBSs and online services prohibitively costly. Driven by an 'all-consuming maniacal blood lust' for online communities, he wrote 'The Hacking Construction Set' - a polished AppleBasic toolkit for finding valid calling card PINs to obtain free long distance. This illegal activity eventually caught up with him when police arrived at his door in 1988. The case was dismissed due to his youth and the court's unfamiliarity with computer crimes, but he had to pay his own legal fees. Atwood frames this youthful transgression as part of the circuitous path that led him to create Stack Overflow, embracing his 'bad judgment' as leading to fascinating places.
key_points: The pre-Internet 1980s created genuine barriers to online participation - long distance calls at 20-30 cents per minute and slow modems meant hours of connectivity could cost $40+ in today's dollars
Atwood's obsession with early online communities was so intense he skipped his prom and describes it as 'all-consuming maniacal blood lust' rather than mere addiction
Rather than just solving his own problem, he over-engineered a complete toolkit with documentation, UI, and even intro music - then shared it freely on BBSs
The legal system in 1988 had no framework for understanding computer crimes, seeing only 'a smart kid with bad judgment who loved computers'
Atwood reframes his youthful lawbreaking as formative experience that eventually led to Stack Overflow and other achievements
money_quotes: I must confess I've grown to love my own bad judgment. It's led me to the most fascinating places.
But addicted, I think, is too weak a word for what I felt about being a part of these oddball, early online home computer communities. It was more like an all-consuming maniacal blood lust.
The spice must flow!

Because I was an overachieving self-taught programmer, I didn't just write a program. No, I went off and built a full-blown toolkit in AppleBasic, with complete documentation and the best possible text user interface I could muster.
I didn't just gold plate, I platinum plated this freaking thing, man.

All I did was upload a disk image to a few BBSs in 1986. After all that time, to discover that someone used and loved my little bit of software still gives me a little thrill. What higher praise is there for a software developer?
Using my own software got me in trouble with the law. And deservedly so; what I wrote the software to do was illegal.
posts.key_insight: The same obsessive drive and tendency toward over-engineering that gets programmers into trouble can also be the foundation for building things that matter.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood traces the evolution of computer crime from its origins in the 1980s to 2012, using classic hacker narratives from books like The Cuckoo's Egg and Ghost in the Wires. He argues that despite decades of improved technical security, the fundamental techniques of successful hacking have remained unchanged. The post details how modern hackers like those who compromised Mat Honan's accounts bypass hardened systems entirely by exploiting human vulnerabilities through social engineering. Atwood concludes that people, not computers, remain the weakest link in any security system.
key_points: Technical security has improved dramatically since the 1980s, making direct system attacks nearly futile for hackers
Social engineering - tricking people into revealing information - remains the most reliable and effective hacking technique across all eras
Modern hackers exploit customer service systems at companies like Amazon, Apple, and Netflix to chain together account takeovers
The hackers who compromised Mat Honan were teenagers doing it for amusement, not sophisticated criminals
Kevin Mitnick's success came more from his ability to manipulate people in conversations than from technical hacking skills
money_quotes: Social engineering is the most reliable and evergreen hacking technique ever devised. It will outlive us all.
The amount of military-grade security you have on your computer systems matters not at all when someone using those computers clicks on the dancing bunny.
Instead of looking for vulnerabilities in the company's hardened servers, he targeted some of the employees individually.
If you want to engage in computer crime, don't waste your time developing ninja level hacking skills, because computers are not the weak point.
People are.
The techniques are eerily similar. The only difference between Cosmo and Kevin Mitnick is that they were born in different decades.
Computer crime is a whole new world now, but the techniques used today are almost identical to those used in the 1980s.
posts.key_insight: Despite decades of technical security improvements, social engineering remains the dominant attack vector because human psychology is far harder to patch than software.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his decade-long quest for the perfect ultraportable PC laptop, culminating in the Asus Zenbook Prime UX31A—the best PC laptop he's ever owned. Despite finally finding hardware that matches his 2003 dream of reasonable power with extreme portability, he realizes the landscape has fundamentally shifted. Tablets and phones now offer superior battery life, instant-on convenience, and true portability that even the best ultrabook can't match. He concludes with a bittersweet observation that just as the PC industry finally learned to copy Apple's designs successfully, the relevance of laptops themselves may have passed.
key_points: The Asus Zenbook Prime UX31A represents the culmination of a decade of ultraportable laptop evolution, offering a high-quality IPS display, solid build quality, and decent battery life for around $1,050
PC laptops still suffer from the same problems as a decade ago: bloatware, ugly branding stickers, and trackpads that never quite work as well as they should
Tablets and phones have fundamentally changed the calculus of portable computing with 10-hour battery life, instant-on operation, cellular connectivity, and one-handed usability
Windows 8 represents the biggest step forward since Windows 95, but even its improved wake times can't compete with the instant-on nature of mobile devices
The laptop is increasingly relegated to situations requiring extensive typing or large screen real estate—a shrinking use case
money_quotes: The PC industry isn't exactly known for its design leadership, and it can be downright schizophrenic at times.
The Achilles heel of all PC laptops, the trackpad, is about as good as it ever gets on PCs. Which is to say still not great.
Every time I leave the house – heck, every time I leave the room – I have to decide what kind of computer I'm going to take with me, if any.
There is no form factor more compact than the touchscreen tablet.
Just try opening a laptop on a crowded subway train or bus. Or with, say, 3 toddlers running around your house. I dare you.
My laptop is increasingly a device I only take when I know I'll need to do a lot of typing, and/or I'll need a lot of screen space to work.

I've waited 12 years for the PC industry to get its collective act together and, if nothing else, successfully copy Apple's laptop hardware designs. Now that they (mostly) have, I wonder: is it too late?
I can't shake the feeling that this might end up being the last PC laptop I ever own.
posts.key_insight: By the time the PC industry finally produced the perfect ultraportable laptop, mobile devices had already redefined what portable computing means, rendering that achievement somewhat hollow.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts his emotionally devastating experience serving as an alternate juror on a five-week medical malpractice trial involving a man who died at 41, leaving behind a wife and three young children. The parallels to his own life—same age, same family structure, same career field—made the testimony unbearable, especially descriptions of the man's 4-year-old son witnessing his father's collapse. After breaking down while putting his own son to bed, Atwood grapples with the seeming pointlessness of the trial before arriving at a realization: sharing tragedy publicly is how humans cope and survive, and we are all collectively responsible for being part of each other's painful journeys.
key_points: The trial forced Atwood to confront mortality through disturbing parallels—the deceased shared his age, marriage year, family structure, and IT career
Parenthood opens emotional vulnerabilities that make witnessing others' tragedies almost unbearable
The search for someone to blame in tragedy is a universal human response to unfairness we cannot process
Public proceedings like trials serve a deeper purpose: communal witnessing and processing of grief helps society cope
The lows of life—whether our own or witnessed—are what give meaning to the highs
money_quotes: I did not expect to learn what death truly was in a courtroom in Martinez, California, at age 41.
Death is a room full of strangers listening to your loved ones describe, in clinical detail and with tears in their eyes, your last moments.
I feel like becoming a parent has opened emotional doors in me that I didn't know existed.
Somebody is to blame for this. Somebody must be to blame for this.
Nobody gets on a rollercoaster that goes in a straight line.
The pain of those valleys teaches us.
Sharing this tragic, horrible, private thing that happened to these poor people is how we cope.
I now know that I am to blame. We're all to blame. That's what makes us human.
posts.key_insight: Witnessing and sharing in others' tragedies is not pointless suffering but an essential part of what connects us as humans and helps us collectively survive.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines MG Siegler's claim that 'the PC is over' and finds himself largely agreeing, despite his identity as a PC enthusiast. He argues that desktop and laptop performance improvements have stagnated since around 2008, while smartphone performance has improved by factors of 4-20x in just four years. Atwood traces the historical pattern of computing miniaturization from mainframes to minicomputers to desktops, suggesting we're now in the transition to handheld devices. While desktops still serve power users who need extreme computing resources, he concludes that for most people, the traditional PC form factor is becoming obsolete as phones become capable enough to serve as primary computers.
key_points: Desktop and laptop performance improvements have essentially flatlined since 2008, with SSDs being the only meaningful upgrade
Smartphone performance has improved dramatically - 20x in browser benchmarks and 4x in CPU benchmarks in just four years
Computing history follows a clear miniaturization pattern: mainframes → minicomputers → desktops → handhelds
We passed the point of 'enough' computing power for typical users around the time dual-core CPUs became mainstream
The future of computing is making devices smaller and cheaper, not more powerful
money_quotes: What do you do when you have all the computing performance anyone could ever possibly need, except for the freakish one-percenters, the video editors and programmers?
Once you have 'enough' computing power, for whatever value of 'enough' we can agree to disagree on, the future of computing is, and always has been, to make the computers smaller and cheaper.

Imagine the despair of a hot-rodder who regularly sees the streets awash in boring Chrysler K-Cars and Plymouth minivans with more ponies under the hood than a sweet custom rig he built just two years ago.
I've started to lose interest in the upgrade treadmill of ever faster CPUs with more cores, more sophisticated GPUs, more bandwidth, more gigabytes of RAM.

Our phones are now so damn fast and capable as personal computers that I'm starting to wonder why I don't just use the thing I always have in my pocket as my 'laptop,' plugging it into a keyboard and display as necessary.
The PC is over… at least in the form that we knew it.
posts.key_insight: The PC isn't dying because something better replaced it, but because mainstream computing needs were satisfied years ago, making miniaturization the only meaningful frontier left.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood makes a detailed case for building and colocating your own servers rather than relying on cloud services like EC2. He walks through the economics, showing that a $2,750 custom-built server with top-tier specs costs less than two months of comparable EC2 hosting. While acknowledging that cloud services offer flexibility and reduced management overhead, he argues that for organizations willing to invest time upfront, the cost savings and performance benefits are substantial. Ultimately, he admits his primary motivation is simpler: building servers is fun.
key_points: A high-end custom server ($2,750) costs about two months of EC2 web application hosting ($17k/year), offering vastly better performance
Cloud hosting charges a massive premium for raw performance that you can get cheaply with dedicated hardware
Building your own servers makes sense when you want top performance, have capital to invest upfront, and are willing to spend time on setup and maintenance
SuperMicro motherboards with built-in KVM-over-IP make remote server management practical and accessible
The economics favor self-built servers over three years, but cloud services may still win if your organization values time over money
money_quotes: One brand new top-of-the-line server costs about as much as two months of EC2 web application hosting.
Building and colocating your own servers isn't (always) crazy, it isn't scary, heck, it isn't even particularly hard.
The one thing you consistently just do not get from cloud hosting solutions unless you are willing to pay a massive premium, per month, forever: raw, unbridled performance.
I'm happy to spend money on nice dedicated hardware because I know that hardware is cheap, and programmers are expensive.
But to be totally honest with you, mostly I build servers because it's fun.
Maybe the truly precious resource at your organization is people's time, not money, and that $51k is barely a rounding error in your budget.
$51,000 over three years is enough to pay for a lot of colocation and very high end hardware.
posts.key_insight: Cloud hosting extracts a massive ongoing premium for performance that you can own outright with modest upfront investment and effort—but the honest reason to build your own servers is because it's genuinely enjoyable.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares lessons learned from judging 340 Rails Rumble entries, realizing that his 30-second evaluation of each site mirrors how real users behave. He argues that this seemingly unfair time constraint actually reflects reality, since research shows users form judgments about websites in under a second. The post outlines five essential rules for creating effective web app homepages: load fast, explain your purpose clearly, show examples without requiring signup, provide obvious calls to action, and embrace your specific audience. Atwood concludes that the homepage should be the first thing developers work on, as it serves as both design document and vision statement.
key_points: Users judge websites in seconds—research shows first impressions form in as little as 50 milliseconds, making homepage quality critical
Your homepage must instantly explain what problem your app solves and why anyone should care, using an elevator pitch polished 'to the fine sheen of a diamond'
Show don't tell: provide screenshots or live examples without forcing users to sign up, watch videos, or click through slideshows
Embrace your target audience confidently rather than watering down your message to appeal to everyone
The homepage should be the first thing you build because it serves as the essential design document and vision statement for the entire project
money_quotes: Yes, deciding in 30 seconds is totally completely unfair, but that's also exactly how it works in the real world.
The opportunity cost to switch websites is one tiny little click of the mouse or tap of the finger.
The first challenge you have is not coding your app. It is explaining what problem your app solves, and why anyone in the world would possibly care about that.
Everyone knows that ideas are worthless, whereas execution is everything.
Only emperors and princes have that kind of time, man.
Half of all success is knowing your core audience and not trying to water it down so much that it appeals to 'everyone.'
A basic sketch of a homepage design is the first thing you should work on in any webapp, because it serves as the essential starting design document and vision statement.
posts.key_insight: Your website's homepage is not just marketing—it's the first and often only chance to prove your app is worth a user's time, so it should be the starting point of development, not an afterthought.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that Markdown, despite its widespread adoption across GitHub, Stack Overflow, and other major platforms, suffers from a critical lack of formal specification and leadership. He shares an email from David Greenspan of Meteor who proposes creating a proper spec and test suite for Markdown. Atwood calls for the major companies using Markdown—Stack Exchange, GitHub, Meteor, and Reddit—to band together and create an official standard. He directly appeals to John Gruber, Markdown's creator, for his blessing on this endeavor. The goal isn't to radically extend Markdown but to formalize what already works and eliminate ambiguities that cause fragmentation across implementations.
key_points: Markdown's popularity has led to fragmentation, with GitHub Flavor and Stack Overflow Flavor having subtle but frustrating differences
The original Markdown 'spec' is inadequate, leading to dozens of incompatible implementations
Major platforms using Markdown should collaborate to create a formal specification and test suite
The proposed standard would codify existing behavior rather than add new features, with only minor fixes for common 'gotchas'
Atwood seeks John Gruber's blessing to avoid forking the language and maintain community unity
money_quotes: I have grown to love Markdown over the last few years.
Markdown is a wonderful tool, but it does suffer a bit from lack of project leadership.
The so-called 'spec' is anything but, and there are dozens of different flavors of Markdown out there, all with differences in the way they behave.
I freaking love Markdown. I love it so much that I want to see it succeed and flourish over the next 20 years.

I propose that Stack Exchange, GitHub, Meteor, Reddit, and any other company with lots of traffic and a strategic investment in Markdown, all work together to come up with an official Markdown specification.
I don't want to extend Markdown by adding tons of crazy new functionality, or radically change the way it currently works.
I just want to solidify and standardize the simple, useful version of Markdown that is working so well for everyone right now.
posts.key_insight: Successful open source projects need active stewardship, and when a format becomes critical infrastructure across multiple platforms, its stakeholders must collaborate to prevent fragmentation through formal standardization.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that touch interfaces are the inevitable future of computing, but warns that touchscreen-only devices create barriers to writing that could harm communication. He reviews the Microsoft Surface RT as a breakthrough device that successfully combines touch with a keyboard, solving the text input problem that plagues tablets. Despite its first-generation flaws, Atwood sees Surface's form factor as the future of laptops, proving that touch and typing can work harmoniously together. The post reflects on how the natural human desire to touch screens has been validated by the tablet era, while expressing concern about a generation locked into minimal communication by on-screen keyboards.
key_points: Touch interfaces represent the natural evolution of computing, reducing devices to their absolute minimum—a flat slate that invites interaction
Touchscreen-only devices discourage writing paragraphs, creating a 'major regression' that could lock billions into minimal communication
The Surface RT proves that touch and keyboard can work harmoniously together, making it a 'laptop killer' rather than a tablet killer
First-generation devices like Surface RT offer glimpses of the future but will be obsolete within two years
