The 'gorilla arm' argument against touchscreens on laptops has been proven false—typing and touching are 'spectacularly compatible'
money_quotes: My strategy with new computing devices is simple: I buy 'em all, then try living with them.
Welcome to the future, here's your… rectangle.
If it doesn't have a keyboard, I feel that my thoughts are being forced out through a straw.
Anything that adds a significant barrier to the act of placing words on a page is kind of dangerous.

Surface is just like the first iPad in that it has all the flaws and rough edges you'd expect in a version one device. But it is also like the first iPad in that there is undeniably the core of something revelatory and transformative here.
Reviewers think Surface is intended to be a tablet killer, but it isn't. It's a laptop killer.
Surface proves that's not true; typing and touching are spectacularly compatible, at least for laptops.
Do not buy this device expecting longevity. Buy it because you want to see tomorrow today.
posts.key_insight: The future of computing lies not in choosing between touch and keyboard, but in combining them—and devices that force users to abandon writing represent a dangerous regression for human communication.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his discovery that USB flash drive technology has dramatically improved since he last checked in 2010. After upgrading from a 32 GB USB 2.0 drive to USB 3.0 devices, he's astonished by the performance leap—from 10-20 MB/s to 365 MB/s reads and 254 MB/s writes. The post, updated in 2015, tracks three years of progress showing doubled capacity and significantly improved speeds for the same price. He urges readers to upgrade their aging flash drives to experience near-SSD performance in a pocket-sized device.
key_points: USB 3.0 flash drives deliver dramatically better performance than previous generations, with speeds exceeding 350 MB/s reads
Technology progress in just 3 years (2012-2015) doubled capacity and improved speeds by 1.5-1.7x at the same $75 price point
Many people unknowingly carry outdated technology that's been far surpassed by current offerings
Modern USB 3.0 drives easily saturate USB 2.0 connections at around 27-30 MB/sec
The only notable downside to high-performance USB drives is slightly larger physical size
money_quotes: I woke up a few days ago and realized I was still carrying the same 32 GB USB flash drive on my keychain that I purchased in 2010. I thought to myself, this is an unacceptable state of affairs. Totally. Unacceptable.
365 MB/s reads and 254 MB/s writes? Yes please!
Now I feel like a total dork for continuing to carry around a 2010 era flash drive that I thought had decent performance at 20 MB/sec.
Now we can darn near carry pocket solid state hard drives on our keychains!
Nobody told me, man! So now I'm telling you.
posts.key_insight: Technology advances faster than our habits—periodically re-evaluating the tools we take for granted can reveal dramatic improvements we've been missing.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores his unexpected enthusiasm for the Microsoft Surface RT and the emerging category of touch laptops, arguing that touch interaction is becoming essential for all laptops. He compares the Surface RT with the Lenovo Yoga 13, finding that while the Yoga offers superior x86 performance, the Surface's design choices—fat screen with thin keyboard, small touchpad, and ARM efficiency—make it a better touch device. The post identifies key design challenges for touch laptops: screen bounce on thin hinged displays, oversized touchpads pushing screens too far away, widescreen awkwardness in portrait mode, practical size limits around 11 inches, and the critical importance of 8-10 hour battery life. Atwood concludes that ARM's power efficiency may eventually overcome the x86 compatibility advantage as chip performance improves dramatically.
key_points: Touch interaction complements keyboard use far more than expected, making touchscreens inevitable on all laptops
Intel x86 compatibility comes at a hefty cost: the Surface Pro is 44% larger and 33% heavier than the ARM-based Surface RT while offering shorter battery life
Traditional laptop design creates poor touch experiences—thin hinged screens bounce, large touchpads push screens away, and widescreen aspect ratios make portrait mode unusable
The 8-10 hour battery life threshold is psychologically significant: it eliminates charging anxiety and enables true all-day computing
ARM chip performance improvements (promising 75x gains by 2014) may eventually make x86 compatibility less critical as Intel struggles to match ARM's performance per watt
money_quotes: It is only a matter of time before all laptops must be touch laptops.
Intel inside comes at a hefty cost in weight, battery life, and size.
You need the inertia on the side you're touching.
A touchpad should be a method of last resort on a touch laptop anyway, because touch is more convenient.
There is truly something a little magical about that 10 hour battery life number.
I think we're beginning to uncover the edges of a world where lack of x86 compatibility is no longer the kiss of death it used to be.
In a land of ARM chips that pull an absolute maximum of 4 watts at peak, slapping Intel Inside will instantly double the size and weight of your device – or halve its battery life, your choice.
The Yoga 13 is just a nice laptop with a touchscreen slapped on it.
posts.key_insight: The best touch laptops require fundamental design rethinking—not just adding a touchscreen to existing laptop form factors—with ARM's power efficiency potentially trumping x86 compatibility as touch becomes ubiquitous.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on how a utility company's peer comparison energy report triggered his obsessive drive to reduce household energy consumption, despite having no practical reason to care. He recognizes this as the same gamification technique he built into Stack Overflow—showing users their metrics alongside peers to motivate behavior change. While celebrating the power of this approach, he warns that such systems are 'like firearms' and can lead to dark outcomes if designers don't carefully consider what behaviors they're incentivizing. He concludes by defining his life's work as designing 'massively multiplayer games for people who like to type paragraphs to each other.'
key_points: Peer comparison metrics are powerful motivators even when there's no tangible reward—people will obsess over meaningless points just to see numbers go up
The same gamification technique that drives Stack Overflow's success is being used by utility companies to reduce energy consumption
These systems are dangerous if designers don't think deeply about what behaviors they're actually incentivizing and their emergent effects
Atwood defines his work as channeling human obsession into positive outcomes that create reusable artifacts for the world
The effectiveness of peer metrics doesn't require material rewards—the comparison itself is the motivation
money_quotes: I have no particular reason to care about this stupid energy assessment report showing our household using 33% more energy than similar homes in our neighborhood. And yet… I must win this contest.

You've tricked me into becoming obsessed with understanding and reducing my household energy consumption. Something that not only benefits me, but also benefits the greater community and, more broadly, benefits the entire world.
These systems are like firearms: so powerful they're kind of dangerous if you don't know what you're doing.
Even though points on Consumating were redeemable for absolutely nothing, not even a gold star, our members had an unquenchable desire for them.
What I do, what I'm best at, what I love to do more than anything else in the world, is design massively multiplayer games for people who like to type paragraphs to each other.
I channel their obsessions – and mine – into something positive, something that they can learn from, something that creates wonderful reusable artifacts for the whole world.
posts.key_insight: Peer comparison metrics tap into deep human competitive instincts that can be channeled for good or ill—the designer's responsibility is ensuring they incentivize genuinely beneficial behaviors.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software developers should study historical projects—especially failures and noble experiments—before reinventing solutions. He uses Lucasfilm's 1985 Habitat project as a case study, highlighting how its creators discovered that users will behave unpredictably regardless of careful design. The key lesson: instead of trying to control user behavior, successful community builders should observe what users naturally do and build tools to help them do it better. Atwood connects this directly to Stack Overflow's evolution, noting that most successful features came from watching community behavior rather than top-down design.
key_points: Before reinventing anything, exhaustively research every existing solution—working or broken, old or new
Social engineering is inexact: you can influence and provide opportunities, but you cannot predict or dictate outcomes
The most effective approach is observing what users naturally do, then building tools to aid them rather than trying to herd them
Many Stack Overflow features (comments, meta discussions, duplicate handling) emerged from watching community conventions
People haven't changed in 27 years—computers have transformed radically, but human behavior remains constant
money_quotes: You shouldn't even think about reinventing a damn thing until you've exhaustively researched every single last wheel, old or new, working or broken, that you can lay your hands on.
In the most carefully constructed experiment under the most carefully controlled conditions, the organism will do whatever it damn well pleases.
Instead of trying to push the community in the direction we thought it should go, an exercise rather like herding mice, we tried to observe what people were doing and aid them in it.
We could influence things, we could set up interesting situations, we could provide opportunities for things to happen, but we could not predict nor dictate the outcome.
People are the source of, and solution to, all the problems you'll run into when building social software.
For every 3 features we introduced on Stack Overflow, at least two of them came more or less directly from observing the community.
posts.key_insight: The most effective way to build community software is not to dictate user behavior but to observe what users naturally do and build tools that help them do it with less friction.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that threaded discussions are a usability disaster for web communities, with most successful discussion platforms being flat rather than hierarchical. He identifies five core problems with threading: tree structures are cognitively difficult, replies get lost in the hierarchy, indentation pushes content off-screen, threading creates an illusion of private conversation in public space, and reading becomes work rather than flow. While acknowledging threading can work in limited doses, he advocates for strict caps on reply depth, pointing to Stack Exchange's single-level comments and Twitter's three-level context display as successful compromises.
key_points: Threaded discussions have largely failed on the web through 'software Darwinism' - most surviving web communities use flat discussion models
Tree structures impose cognitive overhead that doesn't match how humans naturally think about conversation
Threading creates the harmful illusion of private conversation when every public post is actually talking to everyone who will ever read it
Capping reply depth is the most effective way to get threading benefits without the usability disasters
Reddit and Hacker News demonstrate how threading forces readers to do 'menial labor' of collapsing and expanding tangents
money_quotes: Threaded discussion is ultimately too complex to survive on the public Internet.
Rigid hierarchy is generally not how the human mind works, and the strict parent-child relationship it enforces is particularly terrible for fluid human group discussion.
It absolutely kills me that there might be amazing, insightful responses buried somewhere in the middle of a reply chain that I will never be able to find.
Every public post you make on the Internet, reply or not, is actually talking to everyone who will ever read it.
As a lazy reader, I feel I've already done my part by deciding to enter the thread; after that all I should need to do is scroll or swipe down.
Full bore, no-holds-barred threading is an unmitigated usability disaster for discussion, everywhere I've encountered it.
If you build your Q&A system like a discussion system, it will devolve into Yahoo Answers, or even worse, Quora. Just kidding Quora. You're great.
posts.tone: opinionated, analytical, conversational with dry humor
posts.key_insight: Threading fails because it imposes a rigid hierarchical structure on inherently fluid human conversation, and the only way to make it work is to strictly limit reply depth.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the tragic death of Aaron Swartz, expressing disappointment in himself for not supporting Aaron's defense, in the government for its prosecutorial overreach, and in the perverse effectiveness of martyrdom as a tool for change. He draws parallels between ragequitting in online communities and the finality of suicide, revealing his own struggles with similar thoughts. The post argues that activists must persist through injustice rather than give up, and that inflicting generational trauma on loved ones is unacceptable regardless of circumstances. A 2025 update commits to continuing Aaron's path of activism and love.
key_points: The government's prosecution of Aaron Swartz was grossly disproportionate and contributed directly to his death, yet the hacker community failed to rally adequately to his defense
Martyrdom as a strategy for change is perversely effective but ultimately wrong - Aaron would have achieved far more by prevailing and continuing his work
Suicide is the ultimate ragequit, ending not just the current game but all future games, and inflicts unacceptable generational trauma on those left behind
The path of the activist requires fighting through injustice, not succumbing to it, even when the system is corrupt and the odds seem insurmountable
Atwood candidly admits to researching suicide himself, positioning the post as advice from someone who understands the darkness firsthand
money_quotes: Aaron was to us hackers: an exemplar of the noble, selfless behavior and positive action that all hackers aspire to – but very few actually achieve.
I never knew Aaron, but I knew Aaron.
The idea that Aaron killing himself was a viable strategy, more than going on to prevail in this matter and so many more in his lifetime, makes me incredibly angry.
It is another thing entirely to play the final move and take your own life. To declare the end of this game and all future games, the end of ragequitting itself.
Inflicting generational trauma is absolutely unacceptable under any circumstances, no matter how dire.
The greater the injustice, the greater wrong undone when you ultimately prevail.
But do not, under any circumstances, give anyone the satisfaction of seeing you ragequit. They don't deserve it.
Play other, better moves – and consider your long game.
posts.tone: raw, confessional, angry, grieving, ultimately defiant
posts.key_insight: Suicide is the ultimate ragequit that denies yourself future victories and inflicts unbearable trauma on others - the activist's path demands endurance through injustice, not surrender to it.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces the launch of Discourse, an open-source forum platform he co-founded after leaving Stack Exchange. He argues that while forum software has stagnated for over a decade, forums remain invaluable repositories of deep, specialized knowledge across countless niche communities. Atwood frames this as a philosophical pivot: after years of deliberately suppressing discussion at Stack Exchange to maintain signal-to-noise ratio, he's now championing open discussion. The new venture, backed by major VCs, aims to become 'the WordPress of forums' and improve civilized discourse across the internet.
key_points: Forum software hasn't meaningfully advanced in over a decade despite forums being crucial repositories of niche expertise
Stack Exchange's design philosophy deliberately suppresses discussion to maximize signal-to-noise ratio, but this approach isn't suitable for all community needs
Forums are 'the dark matter of the web' - unglamorous but containing incredible depth of passionate, specialized knowledge
Discourse is designed to be 100% open source with built-in moderation tools that let communities self-govern against bad actors
The company's mission is explicitly to raise the standard of civilized discourse on the internet through better software
money_quotes: All your software options for online community are, quite frankly, terrible.
Forums are the dark matter of the web, the B-movies of the Internet. But they matter.
If your goal is to have an excellent signal to noise ratio, you must suppress discussion.
I already built an X-Wing; could I build a better Tie Fighter?
These communities are incredibly passionate about incredibly obscure things. They aren't afraid to let their freak flag fly, and the world is a better place for it.
We're embarking on a five year mission to improve the fabric of the Internet.
Why don't you ask your community what they think of your thing? And if you don't have a community of users and customers around your thing, well, there's your problem right there.
posts.key_insight: The internet desperately needs better forum software because despite their terrible UX, forums remain irreplaceable repositories of deep specialized knowledge created by passionate niche communities.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood addresses career programmers who've realized programming isn't for them, acknowledging this is a legitimate concern that Stack Exchange sites ironically can't help with due to career questions being too narrow and opinion-based. He validates that programming requires genuine passion and suggests first examining whether the environment or peers might be the real problem before leaving the field entirely. Atwood then provides a practical list of related careers where programming skills remain valuable without requiring daily coding, including product management, QA, technical writing, and business analysis. The core message is that a technical background opens doors to adjacent professions where deep programming knowledge becomes a competitive advantage rather than a daily requirement.
key_points: Career questions are frequently closed on Stack Exchange because they tend to be narrow, opinionated, and only useful to the person asking
Programming requires genuine passion - if you haven't developed a taste for it after years, you probably won't suddenly discover one
Before leaving programming entirely, consider whether your environment and peers might be the actual problem rather than the profession itself
Many adjacent roles (PM, QA, technical writing, sysadmin) value deep technical backgrounds but don't require daily coding
Programmers who truly understand the nuts and bolts are rare in these adjacent roles, making technical knowledge a competitive advantage
money_quotes: Programming is indeed a field that does require some passion. If you've been programming for a few years and haven't developed a taste for it by now, it seems doubtful to me that anyone would suddenly develop one overnight.

I can understand deciding to outlaw the entire class of career questions because they're frequently soft, opinion-y, and highly specific to the person asking. It's easier to throw out the whole category rather than do the painful work of sifting through them all to reveal those few rare workable gems.

In many of these roles, people that truly know the nuts and bolts of programming are quite rare. That's unfortunate, because a deep technical background lets you actually understand and explain what is going on.
At the very least nobody can dazzle you with technical BS, because you're equipped to call their bluff.
There is a lot of stuff that goes on around programming that is not heads down code writing, where your programming skills are a competitive advantage.
Consider if the environment and peers may be the problem. Have you tried changing those up, first, before conclusively deciding you need to leave the field forever?
posts.key_insight: If you're a programmer who no longer wants to program, your technical background is still a valuable asset that can open doors to adjacent professions where deep technical knowledge provides competitive advantage without requiring daily coding.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that programmers universally suffer from 'the delusion of reuse' - believing their solutions are more generalizable than they actually are. He champions the 'Rule of Three': reusable components are three times harder to build than single-use ones, and should be tested across three different applications before being considered truly reusable. Atwood demonstrates his commitment to this principle through both Stack Overflow (which proved itself across programmers, sysadmins, and power users before expanding to 100+ sites) and Discourse (which deliberately limited itself to three partners before broader release). The post serves as both a warning against premature generalization and a practical framework for building genuinely reusable software.
key_points: Building truly reusable software is one of the hardest problems in programming, alongside naming and cache invalidation
The Rule of Three states reusable components take 3x the effort and should prove themselves across three different audiences before being trusted
Stack Overflow deliberately validated its engine across three distinct communities (programmers, sysadmins, power users) before expanding to Stack Exchange
Discourse intentionally turned away paying customers for months to focus on perfecting the product with just three partners
One successful use case might be luck, two gives confidence, but three proves genuine reusability
money_quotes: Every programmer ever born thinks whatever idea just popped out of their head into their editor is the most generalized, most flexible, most one-size-fits all solution that has ever been conceived.
We have the delusion of reuse. Don't feel bad. It's an endemic disease among software developers. An occupational hazard, really.
To build something truly reusable, you must convince three different audiences to use it thoroughly first.
One customer or user or audience might be a fluke. Two gives you confidence that maybe, just maybe, you aren't getting lucky this time. And three? Well, three is a magic number.
So yeah, I won't lie to you – I'm basically a total bummer. But I'm a total bummer with a plan.
The solution we constructed in Discourse was a decent start, but woefully incomplete – even wrong in some areas.
posts.key_insight: Don't assume you've built reusable software until you've proven it works for three distinctly different audiences - anything less is just the delusion of reuse.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces the CODE Keyboard, a mechanical keyboard he co-created with WASD Keyboards after years of dissatisfaction with existing options. He frames the keyboard not as a product pitch but as a celebration of written communication's power. Atwood argues that keyboards are among humanity's most important tools because they translate thoughts into words faster than anything else. He connects this to his broader work on Stack Exchange and Discourse, emphasizing that programming and community platforms are fundamentally about human communication, not computers. The post positions mechanical keyboards as essential tools for people who take writing seriously, while acknowledging most people get by with touchscreen keyboards.
key_points: The CODE Keyboard was born from Atwood's frustration that no existing mechanical keyboard combined backlighting, clean design, and proper functionality
Keyboards are positioned as revolutionary tools on par with the pen and printing press for enabling human expression
Programming success comes from communicating with humans through code, not just instructing computers
The web is fundamentally built on words, making quality keyboards essential for those who wield them professionally
Hardware development takes significantly longer than software, requiring years of partnership to produce
money_quotes: The projects I've worked on for the last eight years are first and foremost systems for efficiently communicating with other human beings, not computers.
Nothing lets you get your thoughts out of your brain and into words faster and more efficiently than a well made keyboard.
It's the most subversive thing we've invented since the pen and the printing press, and probably will remain so until we perfect direct brain interfaces.
The world is awash in terrible, crappy, no name how-cheap-can-we-make-it keyboards.
Strip away the images and gradients and vectors from even the fanciest web page, and you'll find that the web is mostly words.
I'm not here to sell you a keyboard. Buy, don't buy. It doesn't matter.
So… you're like… some kind of geek humanitarian?
posts.key_insight: Keyboards matter because the web is built on words, and for those who take writing seriously, the quality of the tool that translates thoughts into text is not a trivial concern.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents his 2013 HTPC upgrade, showing how the platform has evolved from a $1000, 80-watt machine in 2005 to a $300, 15-watt powerhouse in 2013. The upgrade focuses on swapping the motherboard, CPU, and RAM to Intel's Haswell platform, which delivers dramatically better GPU performance while consuming less power. He argues that HTPCs represent the future direction of PCs: increasingly capable while becoming more efficient and affordable. The build now doubles as a credible gaming machine thanks to Steam's Big Picture mode and the improved integrated GPU, pointing toward a console-like future for PC gaming.
key_points: Computing power has reached such overabundance that raw specs improvements matter less than efficiency gains
Haswell's integrated GPU doubled performance over Sandy Bridge while reducing power consumption by 32%
Memory speed matters when using integrated graphics because the GPU shares main RAM
The HTPC evolution mirrors broader PC trends: more capability at lower cost and power consumption
Steam Big Picture mode transforms the low-power HTPC into a viable living room gaming platform
money_quotes: We've reached such an absurd overabundance of computing power that slathering more on top no longer gets us much.
15 watts at idle! Incredible, isn't it?
This 15 watt idle HTPC we just built… is now a credible gaming machine!
When Gabe Newell talks about Linux as the future of gaming, this is the sort of machine he's referring to.
This little HTPC box just keeps getting more versatile over time, while costing me less (in power consumption, at least) every year.
I no longer own any laptops. Everything in our house is a tablet.
What can I say? I like me some media, man.
posts.key_insight: The HTPC's evolution from expensive power-hungry box to cheap efficient gaming machine exemplifies how PC development has shifted from raw performance gains to efficiency and versatility improvements.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares his 2013 update to his everyday carry kit, emphasizing his philosophy of minimalism that intensified after having children. He details how he reduced the size or eliminated items from his previous kit, swapping a larger Leatherman for the smaller Style model and dropping the USB flash drive entirely in favor of smartphone storage. He tests whether smartphones can replace flashlights (they can't) and explores the tradeoffs between different multi-tool options. The post concludes with an enthusiastic look at LED flashlight advances, highlighting an 850-lumen pocket flashlight that represents the fulfillment of Haitz's Law.
key_points: Having children prompted a deeper appreciation for minimalism and carrying less stuff
Smartphones have replaced USB flash drives for portable storage but remain inadequate as flashlight replacements
The best multi-tool is the one with only the functions you actually use - scissors, knife, and screwdriver cover most needs
LED flashlight technology continues advancing dramatically, with pocket-sized lights now reaching 850 lumens
Weight savings matter for everyday carry - the Style at 23g vs Squirt at 56g is significant
money_quotes: After having children I've come to appreciate the value of less stuff in my life.
I gave up the USB flash drive altogether in favor of storing files on whatever smartphone I'm always carrying around with me anyway.

In my testing smartphones make weak flashlights. It might replace one of those button battery style LEDs in an emergency, but it's not even close to what a decent AAA LED flashlight can do for actual nighttime navigation.
What is this obsession with nail files on multi-tools? Who are these people filing their nails all the damn time? I've never filed my nails once in my entire life!
The Style is leaner, smaller, lighter… meaner.
Less is more, folks.
If you've ever dreamed of carrying a lightsaber, and I know you have, wielding this baby will make you realize that dream is closer today than ever before.
posts.key_insight: The best tools are the ones stripped down to only the functions you actually use, because the weight and complexity you eliminate matters more than the features you might someday need.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses 'Masters of Doom' as a lens to examine how John Carmack and John Romero built id Software and revolutionized PC gaming through shareware distribution and technical innovation. The book chronicles how these programmers created an entire industry from scratch, pioneering the 'free demo, pay for more' model that presaged modern free-to-play games by decades. Atwood highlights Carmack's legendary work ethic and his principled stance against software patents, believing all progress builds on prior work. The core message is that the barriers to creating something great have never been lower—modern developers have access to tools, knowledge, and computing power that Carmack could only dream of, making his achievements more accessible to ordinary programmers today.
key_points: Id Software pioneered the shareware model for games (free first episode, pay for the rest), essentially inventing freemium gaming 27 years before it became the industry standard
John Carmack's anti-patent stance stems from the hacker ethic belief that all technology builds on prior work, and he regularly releases his engines under GPL for others to learn from

The barriers to creating great software have collapsed—where Carmack had to invent from first principles on barely capable hardware, modern developers have supercomputers and open source frameworks doing 90% of the work
'Masters of Doom' represents the gold standard for tech biography: meticulously researched over six years with hundreds of interviews, presenting multiple viewpoints that both subjects endorse
Success today requires dedication and execution, not brilliant technical innovation or millions in capital
money_quotes: In the information age, the barriers just aren't there. The barriers are self-imposed. If you want to set off and go develop some grand new thing, you don't need millions of dollars of capitalization. You need enough pizza and Diet Coke to stick in your refrigerator, a cheap PC to work on, and the dedication to go through with it.
All of science and technology and culture and learning and academics is built upon using the work that others have done before.
To take a patenting approach and say it's like, well, this idea is my idea, you cannot extend this idea in any way, because I own this idea—it just seems so fundamentally wrong.
If the world became a place in which he couldn't solve a problem without infringing on someone's patents, he would be very unhappy living there.

You know how game companies spent the last 5 years figuring out that free games with 100% in-app purchases are the optimum business model for games today? The guys at id had figured that all out twenty seven years ago.
Today you no longer need to be as brilliant as John Carmack to achieve success, and John Carmack himself will be the first to tell you that.
You have GitHub, Wikipedia, Stack Overflow, and the whole of the Internet. All you have to do is get off your butt and use them.
posts.key_insight: The golden age of accessible creation is now—where legendary programmers once needed to invent everything from scratch, today's developers inherit their work and have only self-imposed barriers to overcome.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the significant battery life gap between Windows and OS X, using the Surface Pro 2 and MacBook Air as case studies. Despite running nearly identical Intel Haswell hardware, the MacBook Air achieves nearly double the battery life of the Surface Pro 2 during WiFi web browsing. He traces this disparity back to 2009, noting that the gap has actually worsened over time—from 26% less battery life on Windows to 50% less in 2013. Atwood finds this particularly damning given Microsoft's push for Windows 8 as a tablet operating system, arguing that poor idle power management undermines that entire strategy. Neither he nor hardware reviewers can explain the technical basis for this difference, and no PC vendor has been able to match Apple's efficiency on the same hardware.
key_points: The Surface Pro 2 achieves only 6.7 hours of WiFi browsing versus the MacBook Air's 11+ hours on similar hardware
The Windows battery life disadvantage has worsened from 26% in 2009 to 50% in 2013
Installing Windows 8 on a MacBook Air cuts its battery life roughly in half compared to OS X
Microsoft cannot blame third-party drivers when their own hardware and software combination performs this poorly
Windows 8's tablet ambitions are undermined when its idle power management is worse than OS X's desktop performance
money_quotes: The Air is somehow producing nearly two times the battery efficiency of the best hardware and software combination Microsoft can muster, for what I consider to be the most common usage pattern on a computer today.

Microsoft positions Windows 8 as an operating system that's great for tablets, which are designed for casual web browsing and light app use – but how can that possibly be true when Windows idle power management is so much worse than the competition's desktop operating system in OS X?
Instead of the 26% less battery life in Windows that Anand measured in 2009, we're now seeing 50% less battery life.
Microsoft can no longer hand wave this vast difference away based on vague references to 'poorly optimized third party drivers.'
I just wish somebody could explain to me and Anand why Windows is so awful at managing idle power.
We're at a loss to understand why Windows' terrible – and worsening! – idle battery life performance isn't the source of far more industry outrage.
posts.key_insight: Microsoft's inability to match Apple's idle power management on identical hardware represents a fundamental software engineering failure that undermines Windows 8's entire tablet strategy.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the emerging potential of VR technology, sparked by John Carmack leaving id Software to become CTO of Oculus. He attributes the timing of consumer VR's viability to the 'peace dividend of the smartphone wars' – cheap, powerful components that were once military-grade technology. While enthusiastic about legendary programmers Carmack and Abrash working on VR, Atwood tempers expectations with his honest assessment of the current Oculus Rift developer kit's significant limitations: low resolution, cumbersome setup, lack of content, and anti-social nature. Despite these concerns, he remains cautiously optimistic that VR could see a breakthrough similar to Doom or Quake.
key_points: John Carmack leaving id Software for Oculus signals VR's serious potential – you don't abandon the company you founded without compelling reasons
Consumer VR became possible because smartphone component innovation made formerly 'unobtainium' military technology available cheaply
The current developer kit has major issues: extremely low effective resolution, complex setup, limited game support, and antisocial usage patterns
Having programming legends like Carmack and Abrash working on VR suggests we may be approaching a killer app breakthrough
True VR immersion requires many additional systems beyond the headset: positional audio, hand tracking, body tracking, potentially treadmills
money_quotes: An id Software without Carmack is like an Apple without Woz and Jobs.
Virtual reality is the stuff of programmer legend. Every software engineer that's ever read Snow Crash has dreamed of jacking into the metaverse.
They were essentially 'unobtainium' 10 years ago. This is stuff that used to be military industrial technology; you can buy it at RadioShack now.
I thought VR would be at best a novelty in my lifetime.
We could be on the verge of a Doom or Quake style killer app breakthrough in VR.
VR is a surprisingly anti-social hobby, even by gamer standards, which are, uh… low.
Nothing is quite as boring as watching another person sit down, strap on a headset, and have an extended VR 'experience.'
If you want some of the hardest practical problems in computer science to work on, bringing VR to the world is as ambitious (and fun!) a goal in software and hardware engineering I can think of.
posts.tone: cautiously optimistic, technically grounded, enthusiastic yet skeptical
posts.key_insight: The convergence of cheap smartphone components and legendary programming talent at Oculus creates unprecedented potential for consumer VR, though significant technical and social challenges remain before it becomes truly compelling.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood describes his approach to building software products like Stack Overflow and Discourse, emphasizing that the most critical phase comes after launch when you discover all your brilliant ideas were wrong. He advocates for 'complaint driven development' - systematically listening to user complaints and fixing the top issues repeatedly. Using Discourse's character limit requirements as a case study, he shows how multiple iterations of feedback-driven improvements were needed before finding a solution that actually worked. The core message is that building great software requires getting deep in the trenches with users, not waiting for expert validation.
key_points: Your pre-launch research and brilliant ideas will mostly be proven wrong once real users arrive - expect to spend a year fixing mistakes
Complaint driven development means identifying and fixing the top 3 things users repeatedly complain about, then repeating
You must live in your own software daily - if you aren't using it constantly, things will end in tears
90% of user feedback will be terrible, but the 10% that's transformative is worth the effort to find
Building community requires genuinely caring and listening, not waiting for heroic experts to provide answers
money_quotes: Hey, remember all those brilliant ideas you had based on all that painstaking, detailed research you did in step 1? Turns out once you put them in front of actual honest-to-god real world users they were all… completely… wrong.
It took us a full year of complaint driven development to get to software worth using.
You can find more problems in half a day than you can fix in a month.
If you aren't living in the software you're building, each day, every day, all day… things are inevitably going to end in tears for everyone involved.
The only thing I've ever seen work is getting down deep and dirty in the trenches with your users, communicating with them and cultivating relationships.
90% of the feedback you'll get will be terrible for a whole host of reasons. It's a lot easier to imagine some heroic expert swooping in and magically blessing you with the correct answer.
posts.key_insight: Great software isn't built from brilliant pre-launch ideas but from relentlessly fixing the complaints of real users after you ship.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that the mobile app ecosystem has devolved from an exciting innovation into a frustrating mess of nagging install prompts, fragmentation, and race-to-the-bottom economics. He critiques the absurdity of every company building apps when websites would suffice, highlights the burden placed on users to navigate millions of mediocre apps across incompatible platforms, and warns about the privacy dangers of 'free' apps that monetize user data. The post suggests the app ecosystem is recreating all the problems the web originally solved, while expressing hope that the web's cross-platform nature will ultimately prevail.
key_points: The explosion of apps has created a discovery and quality problem where 99% are junk, yet companies keep nagging users to install them instead of questioning whether they need apps at all
Platform fragmentation means developers must support at least four incompatible ecosystems (iOS/Android phones and tablets), recreating the siloed pre-web computing world
The race-to-the-bottom pricing has made 'free' the only viable model, which incentivizes apps to monetize user data or hide costs behind in-app purchases
Users bear the entire burden of discovering app quality, hidden costs, and whether apps will abuse their device access
The inconsistent user experience across platform-specific apps often makes websites preferable, yet companies invest millions in siloed apps instead of improving their cross-platform web presence
money_quotes: Why the hell are we building an app in the first place?
Your platform now has a million apps? Amazing! Wonderful! What they don't tell you is that 99% of them are awful junk that nobody would ever want.
When you buy an app, you don't really know what you're going to get.
Nothing terrifies me more than an app with no moral conscience in the desperate pursuit of revenue that has full access to everything on my phone.
All I can think is what shitty thing is this 'free' app going to do to me so they can satisfy their investors?
At the point where you find yourself praying for a duopoly as one of the better possible outcomes, that's… not a good sign.
The tablet and phone app ecosystem is slowly, painstakingly reinventing everything I hated about the computer software industry before the web blew it all up.
At some point, you just get fed up with all the inconsistencies and oddities and quirks and say to hell with these apps, can I please just use the website instead?
posts.key_insight: The mobile app gold rush has recreated all the fragmentation, inconsistency, and user-hostile economics that the open web originally solved, making the web's cross-platform nature more valuable than ever.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the 10-year anniversary of his blog Coding Horror, revealing he once turned down a $120,000 offer to sell it because the blog was integral to his identity. He credits this decision with enabling the creation of Stack Overflow and Discourse. The post explores his philosophy of choosing paths that scare you, acknowledges that blogging literally changed his life, and admits he'd been writing less due to pressure to produce only deeply meaningful content. He recommits to regular writing, arguing that he needs to write to process his experiences and that sharing creates happiness.
key_points: Atwood turned down $120k to buy his blog in 2007 because it was too integral to his identity, a decision that enabled Stack Overflow and Discourse
His philosophy is to always pick the choice that scares you a little, as challenging decisions push you to evolve and grow
As his audience grew, he felt pressure to only write deeply meaningful content, which paradoxically caused him to write less
Writing is how he processes experiences - his happiness only becomes real when shared with readers
Stack Overflow and Discourse are both rooted in the philosophy of large-scale communal shared happiness and love of learning
money_quotes: How can you sell yourself, even for $120k?
One of my philosophies is to always pick the choice that scares you a little.
The status quo, the path of least resistance, the everyday routine – that stuff is easy. Anyone can do that.
It is odd to meet developers that tell me they 'grew up' with Coding Horror.
Now, after 10 years, I'm finally an overnight success. And: old.
Blogging quite literally changed my life.
I can't fully process all the things that are happening to me until I write about them.
My happiness only becomes real when I share it with all of you.
posts.key_insight: The decisions that shape your life are the ones that scare you a little, and sharing your journey through writing creates both personal understanding and communal happiness.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores how self-doubt and the need to believe in oneself shape success, drawing on Dan Ariely's behavioral economics research. He argues that lying to ourselves is essential for functioning, yet chronic self-doubt can be paralyzing. The post examines the uncomfortable truth that self-promotion and confidence, traits more common in men, correlate with worldly success. Using a scene from the film First Knight, Atwood illustrates how detachment from outcomes—not caring whether you live or die—eliminates performance anxiety. His conclusion is that focusing on daily intrinsic satisfaction, rather than external validation or long-term anxieties, is the path forward.
key_points: Self-deception is a necessary part of human functioning—without it, we'd be crippled by existential fears about our worth and relevance
To succeed, you often have to believe your own hype, which creates an uncomfortable advantage for those comfortable with self-promotion
The 'trap you set for yourself' is caring too much about others' opinions, which clouds judgment and creates paralyzing self-doubt
Reducing stress to zero by not attaching to outcomes enables peak performance, as illustrated by Lancelot's fighting philosophy
The antidote is focusing on daily routines built around what you genuinely enjoy and believe in, rather than long-term anxieties
money_quotes: There is no trap so deadly as the trap you set for yourself.
We're born pretty darn great at lying to ourselves, and we get progressively better and better at it the older we become.
To a disturbing degree in this world, you have to believe your own hype to succeed.
Not caring what other people think of us and what we do is the path of the narcissist, the sociopath, and the insane.
Lancelot fights better than anyone else because he found a way to bring the stress of the situation to zero.
The opinions of other people matter, but they are the traps we set for ourselves.
This is what your life is: whatever it is you get up to do every single day.
posts.key_insight: Success requires believing in yourself enough to act, but the real freedom comes from focusing on daily intrinsic satisfaction rather than stressing about outcomes or others' opinions.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues against the 'Don't Read The Comments' movement, maintaining that comments are essential to blogs and provide crowdsourced knowledge despite their noise. He contends that toxic comment sections are the fault of website owners who fail to moderate, not an inevitable feature of online discourse. The solution isn't abandoning comments but building software that enables community self-governance—a lesson he learned from Stack Overflow. He announces that Coding Horror is switching to Discourse, the open-source discussion platform he created to help communities moderate themselves at scale.
key_points: Comments produce crowdsourced knowledge in aggregate and should be read alongside the main content, like following Wikipedia citations
If your website is full of toxic commenters, it's your fault as the site owner—moderation is a responsibility, not an option
Professional moderation doesn't scale: skilled moderators are rare, expensive, prone to burnout, and create governance challenges
The only sustainable moderation is community self-governance, which requires software designed to encourage positive behavior and mitigate negative behavior
Discourse was built to handle civilized discourse principles automatically, even if owners walk away
money_quotes: A blog without comments is not a blog.
If you want the complete picture, in the same way that you should follow all those little citation links in Wikipedia articles, you should read the comments.
If your website is full of assholes, it's your fault.
If you are unwilling to moderate your online community, you don't deserve to have an online community.
The only form of moderation that scales with the community is the community itself.
It's always a people problem. This is absolutely true. But it's also true that software can profoundly affect people's behavior.
Civilization begins with software that actively works to help you create safe environments for having reasonable conversations with other human beings.
posts.key_insight: Toxic online communities aren't inevitable—they're a design failure, and the solution is building software that empowers communities to govern themselves rather than relying on unsustainable professional moderation.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues against sophisticated to-do list systems in favor of a radically simple approach: mentally tracking just three things you need to accomplish each day. He contends that external tracking tools are crutches that hurt more than they help, and that the ability to know your three daily priorities without any tools is a fundamental skill everyone should develop. The post emphasizes awareness over self-criticism, encouraging readers to simply notice whether their current activity aligns with their three priorities.
key_points: External to-do list tools are crutches that do more harm than good
Everyone should be able to instantly name their three priorities for the day without any tools
This mental tracking skill should be practiced and developed like any other skill
The goal is awareness, not self-admonishment or forced routine changes
Simplicity and focus on what's truly important beats elaborate productivity systems
money_quotes: What three things do you need to do today?
You should be able to instantly answer this simple question, each day, every day, for the rest of your life. Without any tools other than the brain you were born with.
Whatever that thing is, it's a crutch. You don't need it. It's hurting you more than it's helping. Get rid of it.
I'm not asking you to admonish yourself or to make any changes to your routine. Just keep it simple, focus on the important things, and add a little layer of awareness.
If you don't have this skill, develop it. Practice, starting today. Right now.
posts.key_insight: True productivity comes not from better tools but from the mental discipline to know and focus on just three things that matter each day.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood addresses the gender imbalance in programming, where only about 20% of programmers are female, and asks what men can do to improve the situation. He references Sara Chipps's three reasons for wanting more women in tech: diversity leads to better products, the internet is being built disproportionately by men which affects its design, and technology jobs are increasingly essential for economic security. Rather than debating whether the imbalance is inherently problematic, Atwood focuses on practical actions men can take today to create a more welcoming environment. He proposes five concrete steps: following the Hacker School rules against condescending behavior, genuinely listening to others, calling out bad behavior, avoiding workplace romance, and eliminating alcohol from work events.
key_points: Programming is roughly 80% male, and the field shares traits with autism spectrum conditions which also skew 4:1 male
Diversity improves products, especially for social software designed for all humans - the Apple Store glass stairs anecdote illustrates how male-dominated design teams miss obvious issues
The Hacker School rules address common programmer behaviors that create unwelcoming environments: feigning surprise, well-actuallys, backseat driving, and subtle sexism
True listening means giving full attention rather than waiting for your turn to speak - a respect issue that transcends gender
Men have a responsibility to speak up when they witness problematic behavior toward women, rather than treating it as someone else's problem
money_quotes: You could say technology has a man problem.
There is no mythical men's club where it is OK to be a jerk to women.
It's not other people's job to make sure that everyone enjoys a safe, respectful, civil environment at work and online. It's my job. It's your job. It is our job.
People listened instead of just waiting for their turn to speak.
I get really, really tired of all the aggressive mansplaining in software development. Yes, even my own.
As a woman, the first time I saw them I thought 'thank god I'm not wearing a skirt today.' Such considerations were not taken in designing these stairs.
We have to start educating young girls about this now, or they may ultimately become the poorest demographic among us.
posts.key_insight: Rather than debating whether programming should be 50% female, men can take immediate practical steps to create more welcoming environments by curbing condescending behaviors, genuinely listening, and holding each other accountable.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the vast time differentials between human perception and computer operations, using the movie 'Her' as a launching point. He presents a striking table converting computer operation times to human-scale seconds, revealing that while a CPU cycle takes 1 second in human terms, a physical system reboot takes 32 millennia. Using Jim Gray's analogy of fetching data from Pluto, Atwood notes that even with SSDs being 10x faster than HDDs, disk access is still like traveling to Jupiter. The post concludes with the mind-bending observation that as computers get faster, the time disparity between human and computer perception only grows larger.
key_points: Computer performance bottlenecks come from four sources: disk, CPU, memory, and network - but the time scales between them vary enormously
When translated to human-perceivable seconds, a CPU cycle is 1 second while a physical system reboot is 32 millennia
Despite SSDs being 10x faster than spinning hard drives, disk access is still astronomically slow compared to CPU operations - equivalent to traveling to Jupiter instead of Pluto
Internet latency across continents translates to years in computer-perceived time
The faster computers become, the larger the time disparity grows between human and computer perception
money_quotes: That infinite space 'between' what we humans feel as time is where computers spend all their time. It's an entirely different timescale.
If the CPU registers are how long it takes you to fetch data from your brain, then going to disk is the equivalent of fetching data from Pluto.
So instead of travelling to Pluto to get our data from disk in 1999, today we only need to travel to… Jupiter.
To computers, we humans work on a completely different time scale, practically geologic time.
The faster computers get, the bigger this time disparity grows.
Computer performance is a bit of a shell game. You're always waiting for one of four things.
posts.key_insight: The time scales at which computers operate are so vastly different from human perception that even dramatic performance improvements barely dent the astronomical gaps between CPU speed and I/O operations.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that well-designed software can shape user behavior through timely, contextual reminders rather than upfront documentation or rules. Drawing on Dan Ariely's research showing that most people will cheat 'just a little' unless reminded of their values at the moment of temptation, Atwood explains that simple prompts delivered at precisely the right time are far more effective than FAQs, tutorials, or ethical guidelines. He demonstrates how this 'Just In Time' theory was applied in Discourse, where first-time posters see a civility reminder exactly when they begin typing. The approach works not because users are dumb, but because everyone occasionally needs a nudge to act on their better instincts.
key_points: The path of least resistance determines user behavior - software design either masters this or gets mastered by it
Research shows most people cheat 'just a little' to the extent they can still feel honest - laws and penalties have virtually no effect, but timely reminders do
Nobody reads manuals, FAQs, or tutorials - the people who most need help are exactly those who won't seek it out
The 'Just In Time' theory: deliver the minimum helpful reminder at exactly the right moment of potential misbehavior
Contextual nudges work because they tap into people's existing reserves of good intentions, not because users lack intelligence
money_quotes: The path of least resistance is everyone's best friend. Learn to master this path, or others will master it for you.
The purpose of locks, the locksmith said, is to protect you from the 98% of mostly honest people who might be tempted to try your door if it had no lock.
Most people will consistently and reliably cheat 'just a little,' to the extent that they can still consider themselves honest people.
A simple reminder at the time of the temptation is usually all it takes for people to suddenly 'remember' their honesty.
The bad news is Clippy was right.
In my experience, nobody reads manuals, nobody reads FAQs, and nobody reads tutorials.
We use these reminders not because we believe people are dumb; quite the contrary, we use them because we believe people are smart, civil, and interesting.
Turns out everyone just needs to be reminded of that once in a while for it to continue to be true.
posts.tone: persuasive, pragmatic, research-backed with characteristic Atwood wit
posts.key_insight: Effective software design doesn't lecture users upfront but delivers minimal, contextual reminders at the exact moment of temptation, trusting that people want to do the right thing and just need a timely nudge.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces the release of Standard Markdown (later renamed CommonMark), a long-awaited formal specification for the Markdown language. After years of fragmentation caused by the original Markdown's lack of a precise spec, a working group including representatives from GitHub, Reddit, Stack Exchange, and others collaborated to create an authoritative standard. The spec was primarily written by John MacFarlane, creator of Pandoc and Babelmark, and includes implementations in C and JavaScript. Atwood emphasizes that the chaos of 22+ parsers producing 15+ different outputs from the same input demonstrates why formal specifications matter, and calls on the community to adopt and improve the standard.
key_points: The original Markdown lacked a formal specification, leading to fragmented implementations where 22 parsers produce 15 different outputs from the same simple input
A working group of major stakeholders (GitHub, Reddit, Stack Exchange, open source community) spent months developing the specification
John MacFarlane, philosophy department chair at UC Berkeley and creator of Pandoc, wrote the entire spec and both reference implementations
The project aims to preserve Markdown's simple, ASCII email-inspired spirit while making it precisely parseable
Atwood calls on implementers and users to push for Standard Markdown compatibility across websites
money_quotes: Right now we have the worst of both worlds. Lack of leadership from the top, and a bunch of fragmented, poorly coordinated community efforts to advance Markdown, none of which are officially canon.
We spent months hashing out the details and agreeing on the necessary changes to turn Markdown into a language you can parse without feeling like you just walked through a sewer.

It's an indictment of the state of the Markdown ecosystem that any remotely popular implementation wasn't already testing itself against a formal spec and test suite. But who can blame them, because it didn't exist!
In Markdown, we literally built a Tower of Babel.
Have I mentioned that it's a good idea for a language to have a formal specification and test suites? Maybe now you can see why that is.
When you see the word 'Markdown' you have the right to expect consistent rendering across all the websites you visit.
posts.key_insight: Popular technologies without formal specifications inevitably fragment into incompatible dialects, and only collaborative standardization efforts can restore sanity to an ecosystem.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood publicly apologizes to John Gruber after a naming dispute over the 'Standard Markdown' project, which aimed to create an unambiguous specification for Markdown. The team had attempted to contact Gruber multiple times over two years without response, leading them to proceed with the name. When Gruber finally responded expressing that the name was 'infuriating,' Atwood immediately agreed to rename the project, shut down the domain, and apologize publicly. After multiple naming suggestions were rejected, the project ultimately became CommonMark, dropping 'Markdown' entirely from the name at Gruber's request.
key_points: The team worked on the Standard Markdown spec for two years and attempted multiple times to get John Gruber's feedback, receiving no response until after launch
The naming choice was meant to reflect technical goals of creating an unambiguous specification, not to claim ownership or authority over Markdown
Atwood takes responsibility for inconsistencies like not capitalizing 'Markdown' throughout the spec, acknowledging these oversights damaged the relationship
Despite the miscommunication being partly due to Gruber's non-responsiveness, Atwood accepts full blame and immediately complies with all three of Gruber's requests
The project evolved through multiple name changes: Standard Markdown → Common Markdown → CommonMark, with Gruber ultimately rejecting any use of 'Markdown' in the name
money_quotes: I assure you that we did not choose the name to make you, or anyone else, angry.
What can I say? We're programmers. We name things literally. And naming is hard.

Standard does have certain particular computer science meanings, as in IETF Standard, ECMA Standard. That was not our intent, it was more of an aspirational element of 'what if, together, we could eventually...'
We have only ever wanted to be Yet Another Flavor of Markdown.

Everything we've done, we did solely out of a shared love of Markdown (and its simple, unencumbered old-school ASCII origins), and the desire to ensure the success of Markdown as a stable format for future generations.
We then waited two weeks for a response. There was no response, so we assumed that John Gruber was either OK with the project (and its name), or didn't care.
posts.key_insight: Even well-intentioned technical standardization efforts can fail politically when they don't adequately navigate the social dynamics of open source ownership and naming.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software and websites should teach users through hands-on experience rather than documentation, drawing a parallel to how modern video games abandoned manuals in favor of tutorial levels. He reflects on how Stack Overflow emerged from viewing software development as a collaborative game, and notes that games succeed because they're egalitarian—anyone can just start playing. The post uses the example of MegaMan X to illustrate how 'level one' effectively serves as an interactive manual, teaching mechanics through actual gameplay. Atwood concludes that software designers should build 'intro stages' that let users learn by doing rather than reading.
key_points: Games are egalitarian—anyone can play without classes, certification, or prerequisites, which mirrors how many programmers learned to code
Nobody reads manuals, whether for games or software; documentation often collects dust even when painstakingly created
The first level of modern games is 'suspiciously easy' because it IS the manual—teaching through play rather than text
Learning by doing in a well-designed sandbox is more effective than tutorials, pop-ups, or video introductions
Software should follow gaming's lead: build great intro stages instead of documentation people won't read
money_quotes: You don't have to take classes or go to college or be certified: you just play.
Do you know anyone that bought the video game Halo, or Myst, then proceeded to open the box and read the manual before playing the game?
That's because level one, the intro stage, isn't really part of the game. It's the manual.
You learn by doing, provided you have a well designed sandbox that lets you safely experiment as you're starting out in the game.
Don't bother with all the manuals, video introductions, tutorials, and pop-up help dialogs. Nobody's going to read that stuff, at least, not the people who need it.
If you want to teach people about your software, consider how you can build a great intro stage and let them start playing with it immediately.
posts.key_insight: The best way to teach users your software isn't through documentation they'll never read, but through a carefully designed first experience that teaches by letting them actually use it.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that smaller online communities should reject the mute/block model used by Twitter and Facebook, instead actively enforcing community standards by removing bad actors. He contends that global platforms' commitment to hosting 'everyone' under the banner of free speech has created environments where harassment flourishes with no real consequences. The post advocates for community owners to treat their platforms as their own house, with rules that are actively enforced rather than leaving victims to manage abuse through muting. Atwood sees Discourse as embodying this philosophy—communities should show problematic users the door rather than asking other members to simply ignore bad behavior.
key_points: Mute and block features shift the burden to victims and allow harassment to continue unchecked by moderators
Global platforms like Twitter and Facebook are hamstrung by their aspiration to accommodate everyone, making meaningful moderation nearly impossible
Defending behavior by citing free speech is an admission that the behavior's only merit is not being illegal
Smaller communities have the power and responsibility to enforce standards that global platforms cannot
Community rules are meaningless without active enforcement—owners must be willing to remove people who violate them
money_quotes: Defending a position by citing free speech is sort of the ultimate concession; you're saying that the most compelling thing you can say for your position is that it's not literally illegal to express.
You can block, you can hide, you can mute. But what you can't do is show them the door, because it's not your house.

If someone can't behave themselves to the point that they are consistently rude and obnoxious and unkind to others, you don't ask the other people in the house to please ignore it – you ask them to leave your house.
Community rules are only meaningful if they are actively enforced.
Free speech doesn't mean we are obliged to host your content, or listen to you being a jerk to people.
The greatest power of online discussion communities, in my experience, is that they don't aspire to be global.
A well-oiled front door will save your community.
posts.key_insight: The strength of smaller online communities lies in their willingness to enforce standards and remove bad actors, rather than hiding behind the false neutrality of mute buttons and free speech absolutism.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that empathy is the fundamental basis of all stable long-term online communities, and that discussion software should be designed to teach and reinforce empathetic behavior. He identifies six 'dark patterns' of community behavior that erode empathy without being overtly hateful: endless contrarianism, axe-grinding, griefing, persistent negativity, ranting, and grudges. While hate and cruelty are easy to recognize and ban, these subtler behaviors require active recognition and intervention. The post emphasizes that communities should operate like groups of friends who care about each other, with disagreement being acceptable only when pursued with mutual respect.
key_points: Empathy is the fundamental basis of all stable long-term communities, and discussion software should teach civilized discourse rather than just mechanics
The difference between a healthy community and a toxic one is the degree to which disagreement is pursued, gated by mutual respect
Six subtle dark patterns erode community empathy: endless contrarianism, axe-grinding, griefing, persistent negativity, ranting, and grudges
Grudges are particularly dangerous because they can lead to all other dark patterns, and moderators holding grudges would be disastrous
Communities must actively recognize and intervene when these behaviors emerge, not just ban obvious hate and cruelty
money_quotes: The priority is on how you treat each other.
Empathy as the fundamental basis of all stable long term communities.

The goal of discussion software shouldn't be to teach you how to click the reply button, and how to make bold text, but how to engage in civilized online discussion with other human beings without that discussion inevitably breaking down into the collective howling of wolves.
Hate is easy to recognize. Cruelty is easy to recognize. You do not tolerate these in your community, full stop.
When the discussion becomes more about the person than the topic, you're in deep trouble.
Holding a grudge is like drinking poison and expecting the other person to die.

I expect you to act like a group of friends who care about each other, no matter how dumb some of us might be, no matter what political opinions some of us hold, no matter what things some of us like or dislike.
posts.key_insight: Building healthy online communities requires not just banning obvious toxicity, but actively recognizing and addressing subtle behaviors that gradually erode the empathy and mutual respect that make communities sustainable.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that 2014 marks a turning point where tablet performance has nearly caught up to desktop computers, validating his bet that the mobile web browser will become the dominant computing platform. He presents benchmark data showing the iPad Air 2 performing JavaScript within striking distance of his desktop machine. While praising Apple's shift to performance leadership and Android 5's aesthetic improvements, he notes Android's continued erratic JavaScript performance issues. The post serves as both a device review and a broader argument that the 'all computers will be tablets' future is arriving faster than expected.
key_points: Tablet JavaScript performance is now within striking distance of desktop performance, with iPad Air 2 scoring 225ms vs desktop's 180ms
The mobile web browser represents the path of least resistance that will capture computing's 'obscene amount of superpower'
Apple has shifted from aesthetic premium to raw performance leadership
Android 5.0 is aesthetically superior to iOS 8 but still suffers from erratic performance, especially in Chrome's V8 engine
The Surface Pro 3 serves as a preview of what iPad performance will look like in 2-3 years
money_quotes: One of my big bets with Discourse is that eventually, all computers will be tablets of varying size, with performance basically indistinguishable from a two year old desktop or laptop.
Apps are great and all, but there has to be some place for this year's bumper crop of obscene amount of computing superpower to go.
It's hard to believe we now live in a world where the Apple 'Premium' is no longer about aesthetics, but raw, unbridled, class-leading performance.

I patiently explain that it's a very nice third gen hardware design with a fully integrated keyboard cover, IE11 is a great touch browser, and that I'm mostly using the device as a tablet, as a sneak preview of what iPad 8 performance will look like.
Performance-wise, it is what I've come to expect from Android: erratic.
Remember how people in the year 2000 used to say how crazy and ridiculous it was, the idea that Anyone Would Ever Run Photoshop in a Web Browser? I mean come on.
posts.key_insight: The convergence of tablet and desktop performance validates betting on the mobile web browser as the universal computing platform of the future.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that online communities fundamentally misalign incentives by prominently displaying post counts, which encourages talking over listening. He presents evidence from the Ars Technica 'banana experiment' and Slate analytics showing that most commenters don't actually read the content they're commenting on. The post advocates for measuring and rewarding reading behavior instead of posting frequency, noting that lurkers who read everything are more valuable community members than prolific posters. Atwood describes how Discourse implements these principles through read time tracking, badges for reading, and trust levels based on reading rather than posting.
key_points: Displaying post counts next to usernames creates perverse incentives that encourage talking over listening
The Ars Technica experiment showed only comment #93 (page 3) was the first to mention the hidden 'banana' text, proving most commenters don't read articles before responding
Lurkers who read extensively are more valuable to community culture than frequent posters
Read time should replace post count as the key metric for measuring user engagement and trust
Design choices like removing pagination, real-time updates, and reading badges can shift behavior toward more thoughtful engagement
money_quotes: Be very, very careful when you put a number next to someone's name. Because people will do whatever it takes to make that number go up.
The best conversations are with people who spend most of their time listening.
The number of times you've posted in a given topic is not a leaderboard; it's a record of failing to communicate.
We don't need to incentivize talking. There's far too much talking already. We badly need to incentivize listening.

If you've posted five times in the last 10 years, but you've read every single thing your community has ever written, you are a far more important part of that community's culture than someone who posted 100 times in the last two weeks.
Forget clicks, forget page loads, measure read time!
If you want to become a true radical, if you want to have deeper insights and better conversations, spend less time talking and more reading.
posts.key_insight: Online communities should measure and reward reading behavior rather than posting frequency, because listening is the foundation of meaningful discussion.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the remarkable evolution of Amazon's Kindle eInk devices from 2007 to 2014, using detailed specifications to show how these single-purpose readers have become dramatically better, lighter, cheaper, and longer-lasting. He argues that eInk devices succeed precisely because they're bad at everything except reading, allowing users to escape the constant distraction of smartphones and tablets. The post reviews the Kindle Voyage as the first 300 PPI 'retina' eInk reader, praising its screen quality and new pressure-sensitive page turn buttons while criticizing Amazon's poor official case design and persistent typography issues. Atwood positions eInk readers as the 'scooter to the smartphone's motorcycle' - simpler, cheaper, and more focused on their core purpose.
key_points: The 2014 Kindle is half the size, 40% of the weight, twice the resolution, 10x the storage, and half the price of the 2007 original while lasting 6 weeks on a charge instead of 7 days
eInk devices succeed by being terrible at everything except reading, preventing the constant app-switching that plagues tablet and smartphone reading
The Kindle Voyage's 300 PPI display finally achieves 'retina' quality that makes text look like a high-quality printed page rather than pixels
There's a design lesson in stripping away features to focus on core functionality - the 'scooter vs motorcycle' principle
Despite hardware improvements, Amazon still hasn't fixed the typography and text justification issues that have plagued Kindles for years
money_quotes: eInk devices are also pleasant in a paradoxical way because they basically suck at everything that isn't reading.
That doesn't sound like something you'd want, except when you notice you spend every fifth page switching back to Twitter or Facebook or Tinder or Snapchat or whatever.
There's an object lesson here in stripping things away to get to the core.
When you experience 300 PPI in eInk, you really feel like you're looking at a high quality printed page rather than an array of RGB pixels.
They are the scooter to the motorcycle of the smartphone. Nowhere near as versatile, but as a form of basic transportation, radically simpler, radically cheaper, and more durable.
I finally have enough resolution to pick a thinner font than fat, sassy old Caecilia.
It is reasonable to expect more by now from a big company that ostensibly cares about reading. And has a giant budget with lots of smart people on its payroll.
posts.key_insight: Single-purpose devices that excel at one thing by deliberately excluding distractions can provide a better experience than versatile devices that invite constant interruption.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores what an ideal login experience should look like by applying the 'God algorithm' concept he learned from Randy Pausch: asking what the perfect, instantaneous solution would be, then working backward to get as close as possible. Since God wouldn't need a login dialog at all (users would already be authenticated), the goal becomes minimizing friction in the inevitable compromise. The post systematically examines every aspect of login UX: using email as identity, revealing whether emails exist in the system, seamlessly toggling between login and signup, supporting password managers, handling common mistakes like caps lock and email typos, enforcing minimum password quality, and rate limiting to prevent abuse. Atwood concludes that while a two-field form seems simple, it's deceptively complex, and the best approach is to avoid building login dialogs altogether when possible.
key_points: The 'God algorithm' concept asks what the perfect O(1) solution would be, then works backward to approximate it
Email should always be accepted as login identity since it's already required for password recovery
