Telling users when their email doesn't exist in the forgot password form is more user-friendly than the security-conscious alternative of being coy about it
Login and signup are so similar from users' perspectives that switching between them should be seamless
A login form must work with browser password managers, handle common user mistakes like caps lock and email typos, and rate limit attempts to prevent abuse
money_quotes: What's the God algorithm for this?
God wouldn't bother with a stupid Bubble Sort or Quick Sort or Shell Sort like us mere mortals, God would just immediately place the items in the correct order. Bam. One step.
How would God build this login dialog? And the answer is, of course, God wouldn't bother to build a login dialog at all.
This is obviously impossible for us, because God isn't one of our investors.
User identity is always email, plain and simple. What happens when you forget your password? You get an email, right? Thus, email is your identity.
Being user friendly is way more important than being secure.
Logging in involves a simple form with two fields, a link, and two buttons. And yet, after reading all this, I'm sure you'll agree that it's deceptively complex.
posts.key_insight: The best login experience comes from asking what the ideal frictionless solution would look like, then systematically removing every possible point of user confusion and frustration to get as close to that ideal as reality allows.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on how Beagle Bros software from his youth taught him that the best programs prioritize fun over features. He argues that interactive, animated learning experiences are far superior to passive reading, and that the vision of 'interactive textbooks' dreamed up in 1985 is now achievable through modern web technologies. The post catalogs numerous examples of interactive web-based educational content that embody this philosophy, from algorithm visualizations to playable programming tutorials. Atwood concludes that he wants future generations to learn from programs that invite experimentation rather than passive consumption.
key_points: The best way to create fun programs is to have fun writing them in the first place
Interactive, dynamic programs teach better than passive reading of textbooks
Modern web technologies (JavaScript, HTML5) can now deliver the interactive textbook experience that was only a dream in 1985
Learning should involve experimentation and play, not just reading
Platform-specific native apps are less desirable than web-based solutions for educational content
money_quotes: Instead of guaranteeing fancy features or compatibility or error free operation, Beagle Bros software promised something else altogether: fun.
Apparently, the best way to create fun programs for users is to make sure you had fun writing them in the first place.
They taught me how much more fun it was to learn by playing with an interactive, dynamic program instead of passively reading about concepts in a book.
One day, all books will be interactive and animated.
Imagine a world where textbooks didn't just present a wall of text to you, the learner, but actually engaged you, played with you, and invited experimentation. Right there on the page.
C'mon. Type something. See what happens.
I want them to learn, as I did, that our programs are fun to use.
posts.key_insight: The future of education lies in interactive programs that invite experimentation rather than passive reading, and modern web technology has finally made the 1985 dream of animated, playable textbooks a reality.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that despite Markdown's ubiquity across major platforms like GitHub, Stack Overflow, and Reddit, there's no definitive tutorial for learning it. He criticizes existing resources as merely 'OK' rather than great, and announces a contest to build an interactive, universal Markdown tutorial to be hosted on commonmark.org. The post also takes shots at WYSIWYG editors, arguing that visible formatting codes are superior for revision tracking and user control. The contest ultimately succeeded, producing the tutorial now at commonmark.org/help.
key_points: Markdown is everywhere (GitHub, Stack Overflow, Reddit) yet many people claim not to know it
WYSIWYG editors hide formatting in dangerous, invisible ways that break revision tracking and user control
All existing Markdown tutorials and references are adequate but none are truly great
Interactive, fun tutorials are more effective than static documentation
Open contests can harness community effort to build universal educational resources
money_quotes: It's always surprised me when people, especially technical people, say they don't know Markdown.
But how can we expect them to know that, if they grew up with wizzy-wig editors where the only way to make italic is to click a toolbar button, like an animal?
I personally don't like invisible formatting tags and I find that WYSIWYG is more like WYCSYCG in practice.
It's dangerous to be dependent on these invisible formatting codes you can't control.

What we really need is a great Markdown tutorial and reference page, one that we can refer anyone to, anywhere in the world, from someone who barely touches computers to the hardest of hard-core coders.
Judging will be completely at the whim of myself and John MacFarlane. Our decisions will be capricious, arbitrary, probably nonsensical, and above all, final.
Third prize is you're fired. Just kidding.
posts.key_insight: The developer community needs a single, universally excellent interactive Markdown tutorial rather than scattered adequate documentation.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood challenges Eric Raymond's 'Linus's Law' that open source code is inherently more secure due to many eyeballs reviewing it, using the Heartbleed vulnerability as a stark counterexample. Despite OpenSSL being critical infrastructure used by millions of servers, a catastrophic bug went unnoticed for two years. Atwood argues that most 'eyeballs' are users not developers, writing code is easier than reviewing it, and few programmers have security expertise. The industry response has been bug bounty programs, but Atwood expresses unease about this monetization of security research, noting it can create perverse incentives like hoarding exploits for bigger paydays and excluding projects without budgets from quality security review.
key_points: The Heartbleed bug disproved Linus's Law by demonstrating that even critical open source infrastructure can harbor severe vulnerabilities for years despite being theoretically open to review
There's a fundamental asymmetry between writing code and reviewing it - the volume of code produced far exceeds the capacity of qualified security reviewers
Bug bounty programs introduce problematic incentives: researchers may hoard bugs for bigger paydays, security becomes a 'me' goal instead of 'us' goal, and projects without money get ignored
The distinction between 'usage eyeballs' and 'development eyeballs' is crucial - most people using open source never look at the actual code
Low-quality bug reports from bounty seekers create additional work burden and adversarial dynamics between reporters and maintainers
money_quotes: Given enough eyeballs, all bugs are shallow.
OpenSSL, the library with this bug, is one of the most critical bits of Internet infrastructure the world has.
This should be some of the most well-reviewed code in the world. What happened to our eyeballs, man?
The act of writing (or cut-and-pasting) your own code is easier than understanding and peer reviewing someone else's code.
If we can't naturally get enough eyeballs on OpenSSL, how does any other code stand a chance?
Am I now obligated, on top of providing a completely free open source project to the world, to pay people for contributing information about security bugs that make this open source project better?
A ransomware culture of 'pay me or I won't tell you about your terrible security bug' does not feel very far off, either.
I am concerned that we may be slowly moving toward a world where given enough money, all bugs are shallow.
posts.key_insight: The economics of software security are broken: open source's theoretical advantage of many reviewers fails in practice, and while bug bounties fill the gap, they introduce perverse incentives that may ultimately harm the collaborative spirit that made open source valuable.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that most passwords are dangerously short, demonstrating through concrete math that even random 8-character passwords can be cracked in seconds by modern GPU arrays. He shows that password length matters more than complexity, with each additional character exponentially increasing crack time. The post advocates for a minimum of 12 characters for any password and encourages developers to either eliminate passwords entirely through OAuth, use browser-based password managers, or implement slow hashing algorithms like scrypt. Atwood grounds his argument in the reality that database breaches are inevitable, making offline hash cracking the primary threat model.
key_points: Users will always choose simple passwords and reuse them across services - this is an unchangeable human behavior developers must design around
A random 8-character password can be cracked in 2.2 seconds by a GPU array, while 12 characters pushes it to 1+ years
The best password is one you don't have to store - OAuth and browser password managers are preferable to custom password systems
Developers must assume passwords will be hashed with the weakest possible algorithm and plan accordingly
GPU-resistant hashing algorithms like scrypt with appropriate work factors are essential, not optional
money_quotes: The best password is one you don't have to store.
Unless your password is at least 12 characters, you are vulnerable.

Building an array of, say, 24 consumer grade GPUs that are optimized for speed hashing, is well within the reach of the average law enforcement agency and pretty much any small business that can afford a $40k equipment charge.
No matter what you tell them, users will always choose simple passwords.
This kind of leak happens all the time. And it will continue to happen forever.
You have to assume that every password you use will be hashed with the lamest, fastest hash out there.
Go lay down and put a warm towel over your face.
posts.key_insight: Password security isn't about complexity rules or user education - it's about length, and 12 characters is the new minimum for surviving modern GPU-powered hash cracking.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the true meaning of 'trolling,' arguing that the term originally referred to fishing for reactions rather than being a monster. He distinguishes trolls by their bad faith participation: they come to generate argument rather than learn, hold immutable positions regardless of evidence, and prioritize being right over genuine communication. The key test is whether someone is willing to entertain other perspectives and evolve their position. His advice for handling trolls remains the classic 'don't feed them' - walk away rather than give them the reaction they seek.
key_points: The original definition of 'troll' comes from fishing (trolling for reactions), not the fantasy monster
True trolls are identified by bad faith participation: they come to argue, not to learn or understand
The most telling sign of a troll is an absolutely immutable position defended against all evidence or reason
The fundamental question to ask anyone in a discussion is 'Why are you here?' - to learn or to fight?
The best response to trolls is still to walk away and not provide the reaction they're seeking
money_quotes: In any discussion that has a purpose other than gladiatorial opinion bloodsport, the most telling question you can ask of anyone is this: Why are you here?

The most striking characteristic of the worst trolls is that their position on a given topic is absolutely written in stone, immutable, and they will defend said position to the death in the face of any criticism, evidence, or reason.
Are you here in good faith?
People whose actions demonstrate that they are participating in bad faith – whether they are on the 'right' side of the debate or not – need to be shown the door.
I know nobody has ever convinced anybody to change their mind about anything through mere online discussion before. It's unpossible.
Responding to a troll just gives them evidence of their success for others to enjoy, and powerful incentive to try it again.
Even if sometimes, that troll is you.
posts.key_insight: A troll isn't defined by having controversial opinions, but by participating in bad faith - coming to argue rather than learn, and holding positions immune to evidence or reason.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood recounts how early in his career, a mentor named Bill O'Neil taught him the crucial lesson that writing code is only half the job—testing it ruthlessly is the other half. Through painful code reviews where his work was systematically demolished, Atwood learned that most bugs aren't in the code logic but in failing to anticipate the countless ways users will break your application. He argues that every programmer must embrace being their own worst enemy, actively trying to break their own code before others do. The post catalogs common 'falsehoods programmers believe' about names, time, geography, and addresses to illustrate just how many edge cases exist in real-world programming.
key_points: The truly difficult part of programming isn't writing code—it's handling the thousands of ways things can go wrong, most of them user-related
Professional programmers must learn to be their own worst enemy, deliberately breaking their UI and code before users do
Real-world data is messy: names, dates, times, addresses, and geography all have countless edge cases that violate programmer assumptions
Having a mentor or colleague who ruthlessly tests your code is invaluable for professional growth
If you don't break your own code, users will—and they'll either walk away or create support tickets
money_quotes: What I clearly had no idea about is testing my code.
I slowly, painfully learned that the truly difficult part of coding is dealing with the thousands of ways things can go wrong with your application at any given time – most of them user related.
If you are ever lucky enough to work with one, you should have a very, very healthy fear of professional testers. They are terrifying.
A key turning point in every professional programmer's working life is when you realize you are your own worst enemy, and the only way to mitigate that threat is to embrace it.
Act like your own worst enemy. Break your UI. Break your code. Do terrible things to your software.
You are tester zero. This is your responsibility.
A good programmer knows they have to do terrible things to their code.
If you don't, I guarantee you other people will, and when they do, they will either walk away or create a support ticket. I'm not sure which is worse.
posts.key_insight: Every programmer must embrace being their own worst enemy by ruthlessly testing their code, because real users will find every edge case you missed.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood pushes back against the overwhelming VR hype of 2015, arguing that after extensive hands-on experience with Oculus DK1, DK2, and a Survios rig, he found modern VR merely 'OK' rather than transformative. He systematically outlines six major technical hurdles VR must overcome: bulky headsets, wired connections, low effective resolution, extreme hardware requirements, lack of hand controls, and the need to compete with already-excellent 2D entertainment. While he respects the technical challenges and the talented engineers working on them, he predicts VR is far from the mainstream adoption that media coverage suggests is imminent. The one genuinely impressive experience was using his hands to interact with virtual objects, which he considers essential for VR's future.
key_points: Current VR headsets are too heavy, sweaty, and inconvenient - they need to be as easy to use as putting on sunglasses
The effective resolution of even 1080p VR feels like gaming at 640x480, requiring 4K screens to achieve acceptable visual quality
Hand tracking is the killer feature that transforms VR from passive observation to genuine immersion
3D experiences alone aren't compelling enough - 3D TV failed and VR needs more than just stereoscopic vision to succeed
VR isolates users from shared social experiences that make 2D entertainment so enjoyable
money_quotes: I tried VR and it was just OK.

To be honest, when I tried the DK1 and DK2, after a few hours of demos and exploration, I couldn't wait to get the headset off. Not because I was motion sick – I don't get motion sick, and never have – but because I was bored.
A virtual world experienced with just your head is quite disappointing and passive, like a movie or an on-rails ride.
Adding hands to VR was revelatory, the one bit of VR I've experienced to date that I can honestly say I was blown away by.
Experiencing VR with a mouse is like playing Guitar Hero with a controller.
Experiencing something in 3D, in and of itself, is just not that compelling.
Nobody 'enjoys' strapping two pounds of stuff on their face unless they are in a hazardous materials situation.
posts.key_insight: VR technology in 2015 fails to live up to media hype because the fundamental technical problems of resolution, convenience, and interaction design remain unsolved, and 3D immersion alone isn't compelling enough to overcome these barriers.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood warns that compromised routers represent a serious and underappreciated security threat, having personally witnessed two tech-savvy friends fall victim to router-based attacks. Unlike traditional malware that targets individual devices, a compromised router can intercept and manipulate all traffic from every connected device, making it the ultimate man-in-the-middle attack. Even HTTPS traffic isn't fully safe when attackers can manipulate DNS and forge certificates. Atwood provides practical defensive measures for both home networks (buying quality routers, updating firmware, disabling WPS/uPNP) and public WiFi (using only HTTPS sites and VPNs). He frames this as an ominous preview of IoT security challenges, arguing that every connected device is a powerful computer requiring serious security attention.
key_points: Router malware is more dangerous than computer malware because it can compromise every device on the network simultaneously
Attacks often come from 'inside the house' - malicious websites scripting your browser to access your router's admin interface with default credentials
Even HTTPS traffic can be subverted through DNS manipulation and forged certificates on compromised routers
Consumer router security is generally poor, with features like WPS and uPNP creating unnecessary vulnerabilities that should be disabled
The proliferation of IoT devices dramatically expands the attack surface since each is a small, powerful computer requiring security attention
money_quotes: Every time you use WiFi, ask yourself: could I be connecting to the Internet through a compromised router with malware?
Router malware is the ultimate man-in-the-middle attack.
If you can manage to systematically infect common home and business routers, you can potentially compromise every computer connected to them.
You're being attacked from the soft, creamy inside.
The calls are coming from inside your house!
Default SSIDs just scream hack me, for I have all defaults and a clueless owner.
The Internet of Things is a bunch of tiny, powerful computers, too – and they need the same strong attention to security that our smartphones, laptops, and servers already enjoy.
posts.key_insight: The router sitting in your home or office is a fully capable computer that can be weaponized against you, representing both the current reality and a warning sign for the broader Internet of Things.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood makes the case that 4K monitors have finally reached mainstream readiness in 2015, arguing that the combination of mature hardware, Windows 10's improved DPI scaling, and affordable high-quality panels like the Asus PB279Q make now the time to upgrade. He explains that 27-inch 4K displays achieve 'retina' quality at typical desk viewing distances, delivering the same visual leap that iPad users experienced going from non-retina to retina screens. While acknowledging remaining issues with app DPI scaling and the need for powerful graphics cards, he concludes these are minor trade-offs for what he calls the best computer upgrade in years. His enthusiasm extends to running a triple 4K monitor setup, fulfilling an 8-year quest for high-resolution desktop displays.
key_points: 4K monitors have matured from bleeding-edge early adopter territory to mainstream viability with proper OS and hardware support
At 27 inches, 4K resolution (163 PPI) achieves 'retina' quality at typical desk viewing distances of 21+ inches
Display scaling issues persist in some desktop apps, but browsers and operating systems now handle high DPI well
60Hz refresh rate is the absolute minimum for usable displays, and requires DisplayPort 1.2 to achieve at 4K
The jump from 1440p to 4K represents more than doubling pixel count, from 3.7 to 8.3 megapixels
money_quotes: Given my great experiences with modern high DPI smartphone and tablet displays, I want those same beautiful high resolution displays on my desktop, too.
1440p at 27″ is decent, but it's also… sort of an awkward no-man's land. Nowhere near high enough resolution to be retina, but it is high enough that you probably want to scale things a bit.
This 4K monitor is totally amazing and you should buy one.
It feels exactly like going from the non-retina iPad 2 to the retina iPad 3 did, except on the desktop. It makes all the text on your screen look beautiful.
For people who tell you they can't see the difference between 30fps and 60fps, just switch their monitors to 30hz and watch them squirm in pain.
The only good long term solution is to spread the gospel of 4K and shame them into submission with me.
In 2007, I asked Where Are The High Resolution Displays, and now, 8 years later, they've finally, finally arrived on my desktop.
posts.key_insight: After years of waiting, 4K displays have reached the sweet spot of price, quality, and software support that makes them the most transformative desktop upgrade available.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the state of PC hardware in 2015, noting that while desktop CPU performance has only improved 30-40% since 2011, mobile devices have become nearly 10 times faster in the same period. He argues that while the PC may be 'over' in terms of exciting CPU gains, storage technology—specifically PCIe NVMe SSDs—represents a genuine bright spot with 3x performance improvements. The post explains why disk speed matters so much: SSDs attack the PC's worst-case performance scenario by dramatically reducing latency variability. Atwood provides a practical parts list for a Skylake-based build centered around the new M.2 NVMe storage standard, positioning PCIe SSDs as an important evolutionary step for desktop computing.
key_points: Desktop CPU single-core performance improved only 30-40% from 2011-2015, while mobile CPUs became nearly 10x faster
PCIe NVMe SSDs offer 3x performance improvement over SATA SSDs and 10x reduction in drive latency
SSDs matter because they address the PC's worst-case scenario—the massive variability between CPU cache access (nanoseconds) and disk access (milliseconds)
Traditional HDDs have 11 million times more latency variability than L1 cache; SSDs reduce this to 166,000x
The Skylake platform's key innovation is connecting storage directly to PCI Express lanes via M.2
money_quotes: When your mobile devices get 10 times faster in the span of four years, it's hard to muster much enthusiasm for a modest 1.3× or 1.4× iterative improvement in your PC's performance over the same time.
If the CPU registers are how long it takes you to fetch data from your brain, then going to disk is the equivalent of fetching data from Pluto.
What I've always loved about SSDs is that they attack the PC's worst-case performance scenario, when information has to come off the slowest device inside your computer.
SSDs provide a reduction in overall performance variability of 66×!
The fun part of building a PC is that it's relatively easy to swap out the guts when something compelling comes along.
I believe mainstream PCI express SSDs are another important step in the overall evolution of desktop computing. Or its corpse, anyway.
posts.key_insight: While PC CPU improvements have stagnated, storage technology—particularly PCIe NVMe SSDs—offers genuinely compelling performance gains by attacking the system's worst bottleneck: disk latency variability.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines whether ECC (Error-Correcting Code) RAM is truly essential for servers, challenging the conventional wisdom that it's mandatory for reliable server infrastructure. He documents his 2016 Discourse server builds using consumer-grade i7 CPUs and non-ECC RAM, citing cost and performance advantages over Xeon processors. Through multiple academic studies, he demonstrates that soft memory errors are far rarer than commonly believed, with hard errors (actual hardware failures) being the dominant failure mode. He concludes that while ECC is cheap insurance, modern non-ECC RAM is already extremely reliable, and redundancy through multiple inexpensive servers may be more practical than making each individual server bulletproof.
key_points: Google's original 1999 server rack used commodity hardware without ECC RAM, suggesting that redundancy through multiple cheap boxes can substitute for individual server reliability
Ruby and similar languages demand exceptional single-threaded CPU performance, making consumer i7 CPUs at 4.0 GHz more attractive than slower multi-core Xeons
Modern commodity PC parts have become dramatically more reliable, with DDR4 showing zero failures in Puget Systems' 2014 data and SSDs dropping to 0.2% failure rates
Academic studies show soft memory errors (random bit flips) are two orders of magnitude rarer than previously predicted, with 70% of DRAM faults being hard/permanent errors
If ECC were truly critical to preventing catastrophic memory corruption, it would already be built into every computing device rather than remaining an enterprise-only feature
money_quotes: I see a prescient understanding of how inexpensive commodity hardware would shape today's internet.
When I get stressed out, when I feel the world weighing heavy on my shoulders and I don't know where to turn… I build servers. It's therapeutic.

Why is it that ECC is so essential anyway? If ECC was so important, so critical to the reliable function of computers, why isn't it built in to every desktop, laptop, and smartphone in the world by now?
I'm inclined to think that reliability measured by 'lots of redundant boxes' is more worthwhile and easier to achieve than the platonic ideal of making every individual server bulletproof.
Even if ECC only protects you from rare 1% hardware error cases that you may never hit until you literally build hundreds or thousands of servers, it's cheap insurance.
I deeply distrust the anecdotal voodoo behind 'but one of your computer's memory bits could flip, you'd never know, and corrupted data would be written!'

I find it very, very suspicious that ECC – if it is so critical to preventing these random, memory corrupting bit flips – has not already been built into every type of RAM that we ship in the ubiquitous computing devices all around the world.
posts.key_insight: The received wisdom that servers absolutely require ECC RAM deserves scrutiny—studies show soft memory errors are far rarer than believed, and building redundancy through multiple cheap commodity servers may be more practical than obsessing over individual server reliability.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood presents his 2016 HTPC build, documenting the evolution of his home theater PC from 2005 to present. The post details how Intel's Skylake architecture delivers modest CPU improvements but significant gains in idle power consumption (down to 10w) and integrated GPU performance (2x faster than Haswell). He argues that the HTPC now serves as a legitimate 720p gaming platform and, combined with Steam in-home streaming, can deliver experiences superior to dedicated consoles. The post concludes that consoles are essentially inferior PCs, and the 'golden age of x86 gaming' makes a well-built HTPC the smarter choice for home entertainment.
key_points: Skylake brings only 20% CPU improvement but reduces idle power by 33% to just 10w, thanks partly to Speed Shift technology handing clock control to the CPU itself
Intel's HD 530 integrated graphics is twice as fast as HD 4400, making 720p high-detail gaming viable on integrated graphics alone
Steam in-home streaming has matured to the point where high-end games can be played at 1080p from a gaming PC with no perceptible latency
The Xbox One and PS4 are 'effectively plain old PCs' with slow AMD CPUs and no SSDs, making them inferior to a well-configured HTPC for many use cases
The PicoPSU remains essential for efficient low-wattage operation and easier case maintenance
money_quotes: I've loved many computers in my life, but the HTPC has always had a special place in my heart.
Skylake is a legitimate gaming system on a chip, provided you are OK with 720p.
If those same games are available on PC, you'll have a better experience streaming them from a gaming PC to either a cheap Steam streaming box, or a generalist HTPC like this one.
The golden age of x86 gaming is well upon us.
Speed Shift hands over CPU clockspeed control to the CPU itself, so it can ramp its internal clock up and down dramatically faster than the OS could.
About all the Xbox One lets you do is sometimes play next gen games at 1080p without paying $200 or $300 for a fancy video card, and let's face it – the PS4 does that slightly better.
posts.key_insight: A thoughtfully built HTPC with modern integrated graphics and Steam streaming can outperform dedicated gaming consoles while serving as a versatile home entertainment hub.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood celebrates Apple open-sourcing Swift and uses the occasion to address why codes of conduct matter in open source projects. He counters Jared Spool's argument that codes of conduct are mere placebos by drawing on insights about empathy from parenting and Deborah Tannen's research on communication styles. Atwood argues that creating a feeling of safety is itself valuable—people want to know someone stands with them, not just that problems will be solved. He announces that Discourse is adopting a code of conduct and urges other open source maintainers to do the same, emphasizing that such public statements of intent matter even if they can't guarantee perfect outcomes.
key_points: Apple open-sourcing Swift is a positive step for the developer community, continuing the trend of open source winning over closed source
Codes of conduct aren't just about preventing bad behavior—they're public statements that make people feel seen and supported
Engineers, especially male engineers, often rush to fix problems rather than first listening and providing emotional validation
Creating a feeling of safety is itself valuable and shouldn't be dismissed as mere placebo
Public statements of values matter even when we can't fully live up to them or guarantee perfect outcomes
money_quotes: A feeling of safety is, in fact, what many people are looking for. It's not the whole story by any means, but it's a very important starting point. An anchor.
The quickest way to turn a child's frustration into a screaming, explosive tantrum is to try to fix their problem for them.
Nobody on planet Earth is better at arguing to the death over a set of completely arbitrary, made up rules than the average programmer.

We all want the same thing. Comfort from someone who can tell us that the world is safe, the world is not out to get you, that bad things can happen to you but you'll still be OK because we will help you.
This should not be controversial. It should be common. And saying so matters.
If we as programmers can't come together through our collective shared horror over PHP, the Nickelback of programming languages, then clearly I have failed.
posts.key_insight: Codes of conduct matter not because they can prevent all harm, but because publicly stating your values makes people feel seen and supported—and that feeling of safety is itself meaningful.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores Google's Zopfli compression algorithm, which produces 3-8% smaller files than even highly optimized PNG compression, while remaining fully backwards compatible with standard deflate decompression. He demonstrates impressive results on emoji sets (27% smaller than default) and other test cases, but reveals the significant tradeoff: Zopfli is approximately 80 times slower than standard gzip compression. Atwood argues this makes Zopfli ideal for pre-compiled resources that are compressed once and served millions of times, but unsuitable for user-uploaded content. He shares a practical example from Discourse's avatar system, where heavily optimized small images saw negligible gains, demonstrating that results vary by use case.
key_points: Zopfli achieves 3-8% better compression than PNGout while maintaining full backwards compatibility with standard decompression
The compression is approximately 80 times slower than gzip, making it impractical for on-the-fly compression
Zopfli works best for pre-compiled static assets that are compressed once and downloaded millions of times
Ultra compression levels in most tools offer diminishing returns with exponentially increasing CPU time
Already heavily optimized small images may see minimal gains from Zopfli
money_quotes: Take any random PNG of unknown provenance, apply PNGout, and you're likely to see around a 10% file size savings, possibly a lot more.
This is free bandwidth, people! It doesn't get much better than this!
But there is a catch, because there's always a catch – it's also 80 times slower. No, that's not a typo. Yes, you read that right.
You decide if that whopping 0.1% compression ratio difference between gzip -7 and gzip -9 is worth the doubling in CPU time.
You fall off an algorithmic cliff pretty fast, so stick with the middle or the optimal part of the curve, which tends to be the default compression level.
The first server to try Zopfli-ing a 10k × 10k PNG image is in for a hell of a surprise.
You probably only want to use Zopfli on pre-compiled resources, which are designed to be compressed once and downloaded millions of times.
The great thing about Zopfli is that, assuming you are OK with the extreme up front CPU demands, it is a 'set it and forget it' optimization step that can apply anywhere and will never hurt you.
posts.key_insight: Zopfli offers genuinely free bandwidth savings for static assets, but its 80x slower compression time means it should only be applied to resources that are compiled once and served millions of times, not user-uploaded content.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores replacing a dedicated router with a cheap, powerful mini-PC running standard Linux. After finding his aging Asus router's firmware increasingly outdated, he discovered that ~$350 Broadwell mini-PCs from AliExpress offer remarkable specs: i5 CPUs, 16GB RAM, SSDs, and dual gigabit ethernet. Benchmarks show these 'scooter computers' match or exceed typical cloud VMs and dramatically outperform the weak Atom CPUs found in expensive dedicated networking hardware. The passively-cooled, fanless design proved stable even under extreme CPU torture tests. Atwood concludes that commodity x86 hardware running standard Linux distributions is now a better choice than purpose-built networking appliances with custom firmware.
key_points: Cheap AliExpress mini-PCs (~$350) with real i5 CPUs outperform both cloud VMs and expensive dedicated networking hardware using weak Atom processors
Passive cooling of a 15W TDP x86 CPU is achievable but requires careful thermal management - the entire aluminum case acts as a heatsink
The 'scooter' metaphor captures the appeal: simple, minimal maintenance, just works - primitive machines whose simplicity is both strength and weakness
Purpose-built networking hardware like pfSense appliances ($1,400) use underpowered old Atom cores that can't compete with modern consumer mini-PCs
Standard Linux distributions on commodity x86 are easier to maintain and update than specialized router firmware like TomatoUSB
money_quotes: This is just a little box that runs a chopped up version of Linux, with a bit of specialized wireless hardware and multiple antennas tacked on… that we're not even using.
Why not just go with a small box that can run a real, full Linux distro? Wouldn't that be simpler and easier to keep up to date?
This isn't a Steve Jobs truck, a Mac Mini car, or even a motorcycle. This is a scooter.
Passively cooled computers are paragons of simplicity and reliable consumer electronics, but passively cooling a 'real' x86 PC is the holy grail.
If your CPU can survive an overnight run of mprime, I can assure you it's ready for just about anything the real world can throw at it, ever.
You're often better off building up a modern high power, low TDP x86 box and slapping a regular Linux distro on there.
Sure, 2.4 Ghz and 8 cores on that C2758 sounds reasonable – until you realize those are old Intel Bay Trail Atom cores.
posts.key_insight: Commodity x86 mini-PCs running standard Linux have become so cheap and powerful that they obsolete expensive purpose-built networking hardware with weak CPUs and hard-to-maintain custom firmware.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that while building computers has become trivially easy, the real challenge is proving that a newly built system is actually stable. He walks through a comprehensive testing regimen covering memory (Memtest86), OS installation, CPU stress testing (mprime/Prime95), disk health (badblocks, SMART, bonnie++), and network throughput (iperf). The post emphasizes that commodity parts may work individually but fail together under specific workloads, making systematic testing essential before deployment.
key_points: Building modern computers has become so simple that it's easier than assembling Lego kits, but booting successfully proves nothing about long-term stability
Memory testing with Memtest86 should be the first step since it works before any OS is installed and any errors whatsoever indicate instability
CPU torture testing with mprime/Prime95 running overnight in maximum heat mode is the gold standard for proving processor stability
Disk testing should include badblocks scans, SMART diagnostics, and benchmarks to verify both health and expected performance
The entire testing process is about catching faulty components in the lab before they become liabilities in production
money_quotes: Everyone breathes a sigh of relief when their newly built computer boots up for the first time, no matter how many times they've done it before. But booting is only the beginning of the story.
Yeah, it boots, great. Color me unimpressed. What we really need to know is whether that computer is stable.
There's no guarantee all those parts will work reliably together, in your particular environment, under your particular workload.
Because we're rational scientists, we test stuff in our native environment, and collect data to prove our computer is stable.
If you get any errors at all, anything whatsoever other than a clean 100% pass, your computer is not stable.

The bad news is that it's extremely rare to have any kind of practical, real world workload remotely resembling the stress that Mersenne lays on your CPU. The good news is that if your system can survive the onslaught of Mersenne overnight, it's definitely ready for anything.
I've identified faulty CPUs (once), faulty RAM, faulty disks, and insufficient case airflow early on so that I could deal with them in the lab, before they became liabilities in the field.
posts.tone: practical, methodical, authoritative with dry humor
posts.key_insight: A computer that boots is merely a computer that exists; only systematic stress testing of every component can prove it's actually ready for real work.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the ubiquitous startup advice to 'only hire the best,' arguing that this approach is both impractical and harmful. He points out that most startups fail regardless of hiring quality due to factors beyond their control, making the obsessive pursuit of top talent less critical than commonly believed. More importantly, he argues that ultra-selective hiring processes that reject candidates at the slightest doubt inadvertently optimize for implicit bias, favoring 'People Like Us' and undermining diversity. Atwood advocates for alternative approaches like audition projects (paid trial work periods), geographic diversity through remote work, and expanding hiring horizons beyond Silicon Valley's standard playbook.
key_points: The 'hire only the best' advice ignores that most startups fail due to product-market fit issues, not talent quality
Ultra-selective hiring that rejects candidates at any doubt accidentally optimizes for implicit bias and 'People Like Us' preferences
Research shows unconscious bias affects decisions significantly—from baseball card sales to orchestra auditions to police shootings
Audition projects (paid trial work) are more effective than traditional interviews because you actually work with candidates on real tasks
Geographic diversity through remote work can help achieve better overall diversity by not limiting hiring to a 30-mile radius of San Francisco
money_quotes: It doesn't matter how good the people are at your company when you happen to be working on the wrong problem, at the wrong time, using the wrong approach.

If your hiring attitude is that it's better to be possibly wrong a hundred times so you can be absolutely right one time, you're going to be primed to throw away a lot of candidates on pretty thin evidence.
When you do, you are accidentally optimizing for implicit bias.
I don't think I am one of the best. More like cranky, tenacious, and outspoken, to the point that I wake up most days not even wanting to work with myself.
If you want better diversity in the field, perhaps a good starting point is not demanding that all your employees live within a tiny 30 mile radius of San Francisco or Palo Alto.
Interviews, no matter how much effort you put into them, are so hit and miss that the only way to figure out if someone is really going to work in a given position is to actually work with them.
Every company says they want to hire the best. Anyone who tells you they know how to do that is either lying to you or to themselves.
posts.key_insight: The industry's obsession with hiring 'only the best' through highly selective interview processes inadvertently optimizes for implicit bias and homogeneity, and companies should instead embrace audition projects and geographic diversity to build better teams.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his mistaken 2006 prediction that computers had reached an inflection point in chess, then traces the remarkable progress from Deep Blue's 1997 victory to AlphaGo's 2016 defeat of Lee Sedol. He explains how the real breakthrough wasn't faster CPUs but the rise of GPUs, which are orders of magnitude faster at parallel computation and machine learning tasks. The post celebrates how consumer gaming hardware accidentally became the engine driving AI advancement, enabling sophisticated algorithmic approaches to problems far too vast for brute force. Despite the tongue-in-cheek lament about computers 'ruining' games, Atwood concludes that AlphaGo represents humanity's greatest triumph, not its defeat.
key_points: Deep Blue's specialized hardware evaluated 200 million positions per second in 1997, but modern chess engines on phones achieve grandmaster level while evaluating fewer than 20,000 positions per second - proving algorithmic efficiency matters more than raw speed
GPUs are roughly 150 times faster than CPUs at parallel computation tasks, making consumer video cards the unsung heroes of the machine learning revolution
In just 20 years, computers went from barely beating humans at Chess (problem space of 10^50) to definitively beating humans at Go (problem space of 10^170)
The cost of floating point operations dropped from $42,000 per gigaflop in 1997 to $0.08 in 2015, driven primarily by gaming video card development after 2007
AlphaGo represents a shift from brute force computation to sophisticated algorithmic approaches capable of tackling problems too vast to solve through raw processing power alone
money_quotes: In the space of just 20 years, computers went from barely beating the best humans at Chess, with a problem space of 10^50, to definitively beating the best humans at Go, with a problem space of 10^170.
Every time you see a new video card release, don't think 'slightly nicer looking games' think 'wow, hash cracking and AI just got 2× faster… again!'
If your password is only 8 characters, that's perilously close to no password at all.
Where Deep Blue was the more or less inevitable end result of brute force computation, AlphaGo is the beginning of a whole new era of sophisticated problem solving against far more enormous problems.
AlphaGo's victory is not a defeat of the human mind, but its greatest triumph.
Go has more possible moves than there are atoms in the universe.
We know that computers will continue to beat us at virtually every game we play, in the same way that dolphins will always swim faster than we do.
posts.key_insight: The AI revolution isn't powered by specialized supercomputers but by consumer gaming hardware, making every gamer an unwitting contributor to machine learning's exponential advancement.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the paradox of programming games that simulate tedious, low-level coding—like assembly language—and argues these games reveal something essential about great programmers. He traces the history from brutally difficult games like Robot Odyssey to modern examples like TIS-100 and Human Resource Machine, noting how they filter for people who genuinely enjoy the challenge of programming. Atwood connects this to hiring, suggesting that watching someone play through a programming game reveals more about their abilities than traditional whiteboard interviews. He highlights Starfighter's CTF-style hiring platform as a revolutionary approach and expresses regret that Stack Overflow never implemented yearly programming game events.
key_points: Assembly language programming is universally considered tedious, yet games like TIS-100 turn it into an engaging puzzle by adding mystery and context
The best programmers share a sense of play and experimentation—they bend code to their will out of sheer joy rather than obligation
Programming games like Human Resource Machine could be better interview tools than FizzBuzz because they reveal improvisation and genuine interest
Starfighter's CTF-style simulations represent a next-level hiring strategy that tests real troubleshooting and reverse engineering skills
The willingness to build unnecessary but creative solutions (like a wireless trading device for a game) signals the kind of programmer you want to hire
money_quotes: You save time when you don't need to have an awards ceremony every time a C statement does what it's supposed to.
This is the assembly language programming game you never asked for!
Programmers gotta… program.
Any kid who completes this game while still a kid is guaranteed a career as a software engineer.
Is this interview about demonstrating competency in a certain technical skill that's worth a certain amount of money, or showing me how you can improvise and have fun?
The best programmers I've known have all had a lifelong passion for what they do. There's no way a minor economic blip would ever convince them they should do anything else.
I've felt for a long time that Stack Overflow should be doing yearly programming game events exactly like this.
An arbitrary programming game, particularly one that goes to great lengths to simulate a fictional system, is a wonderful expression of the inherent joy in playing and experimenting with code.
posts.key_insight: The programmers worth hiring are those who play with code for the joy of it—and programming games that simulate tedious or complex systems are excellent at revealing who those people are.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores why people post cruel comments online, using examples of harassment directed at grieving parents. He argues that the abstraction of typing rather than face-to-face interaction enables cruelty, and that online abuse disproportionately targets women and minorities. Through examining cases of people who engaged with their harassers rather than simply blocking them, Atwood builds to his central thesis: people become cruel online because they're terrified. When confronted with evidence that tragedy can strike anyone randomly, the fear becomes unbearable, so they must make victims into monsters who somehow deserved their fate.
key_points: The abstraction of typing rather than speaking face-to-face enables people to say things online they would never say in person
Online abuse disproportionately targets women, people of color, and LGBTQ individuals, with straight white men largely immune from the worst harassment
Some remarkable individuals choose to engage harassers with empathy rather than simply blocking them, demonstrating the empathy their attackers lack
People post cruel things because facing the reality that tragedy can strike anyone randomly creates unbearable existential dread
By making victims into monsters who deserved their fate, people protect themselves from the terrifying truth that they have no control over random tragedy
money_quotes: As an exercise in empathy, try to imagine saying some of the terrible things people typed to each other online to a real person sitting directly in front of you.

It's easy to practice empathy when you limit it to people that are easy to empathize with – the downtrodden, the undeserving victims. But it is another matter entirely to empathize with those that hate, harangue, and intentionally make other people's lives miserable.

We want to believe that the world is understandable and controllable and unthreatening, that if we follow the rules, we'll be okay. So, when this kind of thing happens to other people, we need to put them in a different category from us.
They have to be monsters.

When faced with the abyss of pain and grief that parents feel over the loss of their children, due to utter random chance in a world they can't control, the overwhelming sense of existential dread is simply too much to bear.
The weight of seeing through the fear and beyond the monster to simply discover yourself is often too terrible for many people to bear. In a world of heavy things, it's the heaviest there is.
posts.key_insight: Online cruelty stems not from malice but from existential terror—when people see evidence that random tragedy can strike anyone, they must transform victims into monsters to preserve the illusion that following the rules keeps you safe.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood addresses the practical challenge of keeping kids entertained with media on long trips without buying expensive high-capacity iPads for everyone. He recommends the HooToo TripMate Titan, a $40 device that combines a 10400mAh battery with WiFi-based USB storage sharing. The post provides detailed setup instructions including firmware upgrades, storage recommendations, and how to access shared media from iOS and Windows/Mac devices using DLNA. The solution enables wireless media sharing across multiple devices for under $75 total, though it requires some initial configuration and user training.
key_points: The HooToo TripMate Titan at $40 combines external battery, WiFi USB 3.0 media sharing, wired-to-WiFi conversion, and WiFi bridging in one device
For compact high-capacity storage, budget M.2 SSDs in USB 3.0 enclosures dramatically outperform large USB flash drives
DLNA sharing requires no login and works seamlessly with apps like Infuse on iOS
The device has a quirk where the battery slowly drains even when powered off if a USB drive is attached
Simple solutions beat fancy ones - basic DLNA sharing is more practical for family use than complex Plex setups
money_quotes: this little device is amazing!
All this for only forty bucks!
They run absolute circles around large USB flash drives in performance!
The larger the drive, believe me, the more you need to care about this, like say you need to quickly copy a bunch of reasonably new media for the kids to enjoy before you go catch that plane.
With DLNA, no login is required!
But in my book, simple is good.
no matter where you go, there your… files… are.
posts.key_insight: A cheap WiFi storage device plus a flash drive provides a simple, effective solution for sharing media across multiple devices during family travel without expensive hardware upgrades.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that we've entered a golden age of x86 gaming, evidenced by Sony's unprecedented mid-generation PS4 upgrade (the Neo) that treats consoles more like PCs with swappable components. He reviews Intel's Skull Canyon NUC as the pinnacle of this trend - the first Intel on-die GPU that can match console gaming performance in a tiny form factor. While the NUC costs significantly more than a console at $970, it offers vastly superior CPU and SSD performance with the added benefit of external GPU upgradeability via Thunderbolt 3. Atwood predicts this configuration represents what console gaming will look like in a decade.
key_points: Sony's PS4 Neo represents a watershed moment where consoles adopt PC-style hardware upgrades mid-generation, signaling the convergence of console and PC gaming
Intel's Iris Pro 580 GPU is the first on-die Intel graphics that legitimately matches current console performance, achieving ~40fps in Battlefield 4 at 1080p medium settings
The Skull Canyon NUC's disk performance (measured in gigabytes per second) and CPU power (4x faster than console CPUs) vastly exceed current consoles despite similar GPU capability
Thunderbolt 3 external GPU support transforms the upgrade equation, making the GPU - the component that most needs upgrading - easily replaceable
Power efficiency favors the NUC dramatically: 15w idle versus 75w for consoles sitting at their dashboards
money_quotes: The golden age of x86 gaming is well upon us.
To release a new, faster model that is perfectly backwards compatible is almost unprecedented in the console world.
Zero games are meaningfully CPU limited today.
On-die GPU performance increases have massively outpaced CPU performance increases for the last 5 years.
If we are in a golden age of x86 gaming, this configuration is its logical endpoint.
I feel like the Xbox One is, overall, a worse gaming experience than the Xbox 360 was. And that's sad.
Did I mention it has skulls on it? Skulls!
posts.key_insight: The convergence of console and PC gaming on x86 architecture, combined with Intel's first console-competitive integrated GPU and external GPU upgradeability, signals a fundamental shift where the upgrade-friendly PC model will inevitably become the future of all gaming.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood revisits his passion for arcade emulation a decade after his original builds, discovering that the Raspberry Pi has fundamentally transformed the hobby. The Pi 3's $35 price point, combined with WiFi/Bluetooth, sufficient power to emulate N64/PSX/Dreamcast, and the mature RetroPie project, makes emulation accessible to anyone. Chinese all-in-one JAMMA boards and cheap IPS LCDs have further reduced costs and complexity. What once required $1,500+ can now be accomplished for $100-500 in a weekend, making it an ideal family maker project.
key_points: The Raspberry Pi 3 at $35 can emulate virtually every significant console and arcade game created before 1997, a capability that would have seemed magical to earlier generations
Three technological shifts enable cheap emulation: Raspberry Pi maturity, Chinese JAMMA boards (~$90), and affordable IPS LCD panels (~$100)
RetroPie has evolved from complex hobbyist software to near plug-and-play simplicity since 2013
The total investment has dropped from $1,500+ to $100-500, democratizing what was once an expensive niche hobby
A bartop arcade represents the sweet spot for most builders: reasonable size, portability, good screen size, and two-player support
money_quotes: I think I spent most of my childhood – and a large part of my life as a young adult – desperately wishing I was in a video game arcade.
The ascendance of Raspberry Pi has single-handedly revolutionized the emulation scene.
At this point you have a fully functioning emulation brain for well under $100 which is capable of playing literally every significant console and arcade game created prior to 1997.
Your 1985 self is probably drunk with power.
I remember spending well over $1,500 to build my old arcade cabinets. I'm excited that it's no longer necessary to invest that much time, effort or money to successfully revisit our arcade past.
Thanks largely to the Raspberry Pi 3 and the RetroPie project, this is now a simple Maker project you can (and should!) take on in a weekend with a friend or family.
posts.key_insight: The Raspberry Pi has democratized arcade emulation, transforming a $1,500+ expert hobby into a $100-500 weekend project accessible to anyone willing to learn.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines how Nextdoor, a neighborhood-focused social network, inadvertently became a hub for casual racism and racial profiling as it grew to include older, less progressive users. He argues that as social networks age beyond their young early-adopter base, they inevitably encounter more regressive social attitudes that correlate statistically with age. Rather than accepting this, Nextdoor redesigned their software with 'just in time nudges' that prompt users to focus on suspicious behavior rather than race when reporting incidents. This intervention reduced racial profiling posts by 75%, demonstrating that thoughtfully designed software can genuinely nudge people toward better behavior.
key_points: The smartphone revolution has brought the internet to populations who weren't traditional 'computer geeks,' fundamentally changing online communities
Social networks that start young inevitably age, and older users statistically hold more regressive social views—this is documented fact, not opinion
Nextdoor's reputation as a racial profiling hub emerged from the casual racism of well-connected neighbors
Software interventions at the right moment—'just in time nudges'—can measurably reduce problematic behavior (75% reduction in racial profiling posts)
The key design principle is focusing users on behavior rather than personal characteristics, applicable from Stack Overflow to parenting
money_quotes: The good news is that, at this moment, every human being is far more connected to their fellow humans than any human has ever been in the entirety of recorded history. Spoiler alert: that's also the bad news.
One unfortunate thing you'll find out when using Nextdoor is that your neighbors are probably a little bit racist.
The idea that regressive social opinions correlate with age isn't an opinion; it's a statistical fact.
I'm a huge fan of designing software to help nudge people, at exactly the right time, to be their better selves.
You never, ever judge someone by what they look like. Consider what they do instead.
For Nextdoor, quality is more important than quantity.
posts.key_insight: Thoughtfully designed software interventions, delivered at exactly the right moment, can measurably reduce harmful behavior by nudging users to focus on actions rather than personal characteristics.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood details his purchase and setup of a Virtuapin Mini, a $3,000 custom digital pinball cabinet that emulates any pinball machine using three screens. He provides a thorough technical walkthrough of the hardware, including the plunger kit, monitors, and internal components, while offering specific upgrade recommendations like switching to an IPS panel. The post reviews various pinball simulation software options, preferring Pinball Arcade for its legal licensing and 3D graphics over Visual Pinball's ripped designs. Atwood draws a philosophical parallel between pinball's struggle against video games and his project Discourse's battle against modern social media platforms, framing both as analog experiences trying to stay relevant in a digital world.
key_points: The Virtuapin Mini is a professionally-built $3k digital pinball cabinet using three screens (27" playfield, 23" backglass, 8" DMD) that can emulate any pinball machine ever made
An IPS panel upgrade for the playfield monitor is essential - the stock TN panel has poor viewing angles that significantly degrade the experience
Visual Pinball uses unlicensed rip-offs of real tables, while Pinball Arcade offers 60+ officially licensed, meticulously recreated tables in 3D
Cabinet mode support varies wildly across pinball sims due to separate licensing requirements for computer games vs. physical cabinets
Atwood sees pinball's struggle against video games as parallel to Discourse's mission to save forums from Facebook, Twitter, and modern social platforms
money_quotes: Plus you can emulate every pinball machine, ever!

The combination of janky hacked-together 2D bitmap technology used in the gameplay, and the fact that all those designs are rip-offs that pay nothing in licensing back to the original pinball manufacturers really bothers me.
90% of digital pinball simulation is a widescreen display in portrait mode.
Pinball is threatened by Video Games, in the same way that Forums are threatened by Facebook and Twitter and Tumblr and Snapchat. They're considered old and archaic technology.
The defining characteristic of each 'era' of pinball is that the new tables, once you played them, made all the previous pinball games seem immediately obsolete because of all the new technology.
This is exactly what we want to do with Discourse – build a forum experience so advanced that playing will make all previous forum software seem immediately obsolete.
posts.key_insight: Digital pinball cabinets represent not just nostalgic preservation but a model for how beloved 'analog' technologies can reinvent themselves to remain relevant against newer competitors.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that all web traffic should be encrypted by default, having evolved his position after the Snowden revelations and the 2016 US election. He presents three key reasons: privacy is an inalienable right that requires HTTPS, the performance penalty is now gone, and encryption prevents content tampering by ISPs and other intermediaries. The post highlights Let's Encrypt as a game-changing non-profit that provides free SSL certificates, breaking the cost barrier that previously prevented widespread adoption. Atwood concludes with a call to action for individuals and companies to donate to Let's Encrypt to support universal web encryption.
key_points: Without HTTPS, users have zero online privacy from WiFi snoopers, network providers, and governments
HTTPS now performs equal to or better than HTTP on modern devices, eliminating the historical performance excuse
ISPs like Comcast are actively injecting content into unencrypted web traffic, making tampering a real threat
Let's Encrypt is the first reliable free source of SSL certificates, removing the $30+/year cost barrier
Browser vendors and Google search rankings are actively pushing sites toward HTTPS adoption
money_quotes: Post Snowden, and particularly after the result of the last election here in the US, it's clear that everything on the web should be encrypted by default.
You have an inalienable right to privacy, both in the real world and online. And without HTTPS you have zero online privacy.
The performance penalty of HTTPS is gone, in fact, HTTPS arguably performs better than HTTP on modern devices.
Using HTTPS means nobody can tamper with the content in your web browser.
Imagine what it looks like when someone, or some large company, decides the rules don't apply to them?
Without that required cash each year, without the SSL certificate that you must re-purchase every year in perpetuity – you can't encrypt anything.
If you believe in an unalienable right to privacy on the Internet for every citizen in every nation, please support Let's Encrypt.
posts.key_insight: Universal web encryption has become both technically feasible and morally imperative, and Let's Encrypt has removed the last major barrier by making SSL certificates free.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the challenges of cooling high-performance CPUs in constrained server form factors, specifically 1U rack servers. After discovering that Intel's TDP ratings significantly understate actual power consumption (a 6-core Xeon drew 250W despite a 140W TDP rating), he systematically tests solutions: upgrading from aluminum to copper heatsinks, improving thermal interface material, and adding air ducting. The combination of all three changes allows the server to run stable under maximum load. He concludes by questioning whether his stress testing methodology (MPrime with AVX2 instructions) represents realistic workloads, acknowledging that real-world usage rarely hits these thermal extremes.
key_points: Intel's TDP ratings can be misleading - the 6-core E5-1650 drew 250W under load despite a stated 140W TDP, causing throttling in a 1U server
Copper heatsinks provide meaningful cooling improvement over aluminum in space-constrained environments (7°C difference in identical form factors)
Pre-applied thermal interface material on heatsinks is surprisingly poor quality; replacing it significantly delayed thermal throttling
Fan ducts/shrouds provide more cooling benefit than expected, enabling stable operation when combined with other improvements
MPrime's use of AVX2 instructions since 2011 creates artificially extreme heat loads that may not represent real-world server workloads
money_quotes: Modern CPU design is the delicate art of placing an inferno on the head of a pin.
When I get stressed out, when I feel the world weighing heavy on my shoulders and I don't know where to turn… I build servers. It's therapeutic.
I am here to tell you that Intel's TDP figure of 140 watts for the 6 core version of this CPU is a terrible, scurrilous lie!

If your PC's so marginal that a CPU running three or four degrees Celsius warmer will crash it, the solution is not to try to edge away from the precipice with better thermal compound. It's to make a big change to the cooling system.
Sometimes you get so involved with solving the problem at hand that you forget to consider whether you are, in fact, solving the right problem.
Tiny infernos in our pockets, each and every one.
posts.key_insight: Effective thermal management in constrained spaces requires addressing multiple factors systematically, but equally important is questioning whether your stress tests actually represent real-world conditions.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood breaks his 14-year silence on politics to address what he sees as a crisis in American democracy following the 2016 election. He rejects specific policies including the US-Mexico border wall and the Muslim travel ban, arguing they contradict America's founding values as a nation of immigrants. The post serves as both a personal reckoning—acknowledging his own passive relationship with democracy—and a call to action for civic engagement. Atwood outlines concrete steps he's taking, from calling representatives to donating to organizations like the ACLU and investigative journalism outlets. He distinguishes between nationalism, which he views as destructive, and the underlying American ideals of freedom, justice, and equality.
key_points: After 46 years of passively benefiting from democracy, Atwood recognizes the need to actively defend it through civic engagement
The post explicitly rejects both the border wall and Muslim ban policies while celebrating Mexican and Muslim immigrants as embodying American values
Atwood provides a detailed action plan: calling representatives, supporting journalism, donating to civil liberties organizations, and considering running for office
He draws a sharp distinction between harmful nationalism and the legitimate ideals (freedom, justice, equality) that America was founded on
The post frames this moment as potentially the most important time for activism since the civil rights movement
money_quotes: In my 14 years of blogging, I've never written a political blog post. I haven't needed to. Until now.

Imagine the bravery it takes to leave everything behind, your relatives, your home, your whole life as you know it, to take your entire family on a five thousand mile journey to another country on nothing more than the promise of a dream.
Muslim immigrants are more American than I will ever be, and I am incredibly proud to have them here, as fellow Americans.
We brought fact checkers to a culture war… and we lost.
I have benefited from democracy passively, without trying at all, for 46 years.
I pledge my allegiance to the American dream, and the American people—not to the nation, never to the nation.
I apologize that it's taken me 46 years to wake up and realize that some things, like the American dream, aren't guaranteed.
posts.key_insight: Democracy requires active participation, not passive consumption—the American dream must be continually defended through civic engagement rather than taken for granted.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that traditional password composition rules (requiring uppercase, lowercase, numbers, special characters) are counterproductive and should be abandoned in favor of a single, simple rule: length. He demonstrates that complex rules frustrate power users with password managers, encourage average users to create weaker passwords through 'creative' workarounds, and fail to account for Unicode and international users. Instead, he advocates for minimum length requirements, checking against common breached password lists, basic entropy validation, and blocking obvious cases like password matching username. The post references NIST's 2016 recommendation against composition rules and provides data showing that simply requiring 10+ characters eliminates 80% of the most common passwords.
key_points: Traditional password composition rules (uppercase, lowercase, numbers, symbols) don't actually improve security and actively harm usability for both power users and average users
Password length is the single most important factor - an 8-character password is 'perilously close to no password at all' with modern GPU cracking
Checking passwords against lists of commonly breached passwords (top 10k-100k) is far more effective than arbitrary composition rules
Unicode support matters - password rules and common password lists are often ASCII-centric and fail international users
Simple post-entry validation (blocking passwords equal to username/email, checking basic entropy) catches edge cases without burdening users with complex upfront rules
money_quotes: Password rules are bullshit.
These days, given the state of cloud computing and GPU password hash cracking, any password of 8 characters or less is perilously close to no password at all.
They heavily penalize your ideal audience, people that use real random password generators.
They frustrate average users, who then become uncooperative and use 'creative' workarounds that make their passwords less secure.
If you don't allow (almost) every single Unicode character in the password input field, you are probably doing it wrong.

There's no question that a hacker will submit these common passwords in a hack attempt – and it's shocking how far you can get, even with aggressive password attempt rate limiting, using just the 1,000 most common passwords.
These aren't hypothetical synthetic Jack the Ripper password rules some bored programmer dreamed up, these are real passwords used by real users.
In short, try to think outside the password input box, like a user would.
posts.key_insight: Replace complex password composition rules with a single requirement—sufficient length—supplemented by silent checks against known breached passwords, because length provides real security while composition rules just create frustration and false confidence.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the emerging technology of external GPU enclosures via Thunderbolt 3, using his own $500 Razer Core setup to power 4K gaming on a compact NUC system. He argues that while current costs are prohibitive, the technology represents a fundamental shift in how we think about computer upgradeability. The post details the technical specifications, practical limitations, and setup quirks of external GPUs while making the case that Thunderbolt 3's 40Gbps bandwidth is sufficient for high-end graphics cards with only modest performance penalties. Atwood envisions a future where any device with a Thunderbolt 3 port can transform into a gaming powerhouse, democratizing GPU upgrades for laptops and compact systems alike.
key_points: Thunderbolt 3 provides 40Gbps bandwidth (4.8 GB/s), enough for external GPUs with only 15-25% performance overhead
Modern CPUs are so fast that even budget processors won't bottleneck gaming - the GPU is almost always the limiting factor
External GPU enclosures offer a path to upgradeability for laptops and compact systems that traditionally couldn't be upgraded
Current costs ($500 for enclosure alone) need to drop dramatically for mainstream adoption
The technology works today but requires navigating quirks like cable length limits, multi-GPU management, and enclosure compatibility
money_quotes: I paid $500 for an external Thunderbolt 3 enclosure to fit a $600 video card, all to enable a plug-in upgrade of a GPU on a Skull Canyon NUC that itself cost around $1000 fully built. I know, it sounds crazy, and… OK fine, I won't argue with you. It's crazy.
Plain old regular HD, aka 1080p, aka 1920 × 1080, is one quarter the size of 4k, and ¼ the work. By today's GPU standards HD is pretty much easy mode these days. It's not even interesting.
It's so good that I'm now a little angry that every display that my eyes touch isn't OLED already.
The CPU and memory speeds available today are so comically fast that any device with a low-end i3-7100 for $120 will make zero difference in real world gaming at 1080p or higher.
Almost any small device you can buy with a Thunderbolt 3 port can now magically transform into a potent gaming rig with a single plug.
The future, as they say, is already here – it's just not evenly distributed.
I am painfully aware that costs need to come down. Way, way down.
posts.key_insight: Thunderbolt 3 external GPU enclosures represent a paradigm shift toward modular, upgradeable computing that could finally free laptops and compact systems from their traditional graphics limitations.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood shares Discourse's approach to security by actively testing their own password hashing defenses against modern GPU-powered cracking attacks. After implementing multiple layers of protection including admin password requirements, backup logging, and single-use download tokens, the team still assumed attackers would eventually get database access. They benchmarked their PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 hashes with 64k iterations against a GTX 1080 Ti and hired a penetration tester who cracked only 39 of nearly 12,000 hashes over three weeks. The exercise revealed their security was reasonable but identified room for improvement by switching to slower algorithms like bcrypt.
key_points: Defense in depth means assuming attackers will eventually get your database, so password hash resistance becomes your last line of defense
The two critical factors for password hash security are the algorithm (should be slow, especially on GPUs) and the work factor/iterations (as high as possible without enabling DoS attacks)
A professional penetration tester with sophisticated wordlists cracked only 39 out of 11,997 Discourse password hashes over three weeks using a GTX 1080 Ti
Nation-state attackers with thousands of GPUs could crack even 10-character alphanumeric passwords in hours, making algorithm choice critical for high-security scenarios
GPU cracking power increases dramatically between generations - the 1080 Ti achieved 40% higher hash rates than the 1080
money_quotes: That's why, if you know (or even suspect!) your database was exposed, the very first thing you should do is reset everyone's password.
The reality is that you probably won't know when your database has been exposed, at least not until it's too late to do anything about it.
You bet your sweet ASCII a full database download is what hackers start working toward the minute they gain any kind of foothold in your system. It's the ultimate prize.
In a single video card generation the attack hash rate has increased nearly 40 percent. Ponder that.
If we want Discourse to be nation state attack resistant, clearly we'll need to do better.
The only real protection you can offer your users is just how resistant to attack your stored password hashes are.
Don't wait around for hackers to attack you – hacker, hack thyself!
posts.key_insight: The best way to understand your security posture is to actively attack your own systems with the same tools and techniques real adversaries would use.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on his discovery of PlayerUnknown's Battlegrounds and argues that we are witnessing the birth of an entirely new gaming genre. He describes the unique psychological experience of the game—the loneliness, dread, and constant tension of being hunted by 99 other players on a vast, abandoned island. The game's mechanics of shrinking safe zones and scarce resources force increasingly desperate confrontations. Atwood draws a parallel between the game's brutal, luck-driven, winner-take-all nature and the anxious political climate of 2017 America, suggesting Battle Royale captures the zeitgeist of the moment.
key_points: PUBG represents the defining moment for Battle Royale as a genre, comparable to Counter-Strike's impact on competitive shooters
The game's horror comes not from monsters but from the realization that other humans are the real threat—it's 'the scariest zombie movie without a single zombie'
Approximately 85% of the game is chance: wrong place, wrong time means death, regardless of skill or caution
The psychological intensity comes from silence punctuated by violence, creating constant dread and visceral panic
The Battle Royale mode perfectly mirrors the anxiety of the current political moment: every person for themselves, winner takes all, impossible odds
money_quotes: We are witnessing the birth of an entirely new genre of game: the Battle Royale.
PUBG is, in its way, the scariest zombie movie I've ever seen, though it lacks a single zombie.
