It dispenses with the pretense of a story, so you can realize much sooner that the zombies, as terrible as they may be, are nowhere as dangerous to you as your fellow man.
Death comes quickly, without warning, and often at random.
The Battle Royale game mode perfectly captures the zeitgeist of the current moment.
It's an absolutely terrifying experience of every human for themselves, winner takes all, with impossible odds.
Battle Royale is not the game mode we wanted, it's not the game mode we needed, it's the game mode we all deserve.
And the best part is, when we're done playing, we can turn it off.
posts.key_insight: Battle Royale games succeed because they tap into a primal fear of other humans and mirror our anxious, winner-take-all cultural moment—offering catharsis precisely because we can turn them off.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on how the once-celebratory notion that 'software is eating the world' has taken on an ominous quality as technology's negative societal impacts become clearer. Writing at the end of 2017, he grapples with the uncomfortable realization that the software industry he helped build may not be the unambiguous force for good it was assumed to be. Through references to Black Mirror, Facebook scandals, and his own work building comment sections in the age of Trump, he confronts the moral complexity of being a programmer. He ultimately finds his answer in a children's book interview he'd forgotten giving: the purpose of software should be to serve people by helping them become the best versions of themselves, as Stack Overflow was designed to do.
key_points: Marc Andreessen's 2011 proclamation that 'software is eating the world' has transformed from an inspirational rallying cry into an ominous warning as tech's dark side emerges
Programmers, who typically see themselves as powerless nerds and social outcasts, must reckon with the fact that they now wield significant societal influence
The fundamental purpose of good software should be helping people become the best versions of themselves, not bullshit monetization
Stack Overflow's design philosophy embodied this principle: by helping others become better programmers, you inevitably improve yourself
The tech industry needs to embrace explicit codes of conduct and think more holistically about serving all people, not just personal or corporate interests
money_quotes: What do you do when you wake up one day and software has kind of eaten the world, and it is no longer clear if software is in fact an unambiguously good thing, like we thought, like everyone told us… like we wanted it to be?

That used to sound all hip and cool and inspirational, like 'Wow! We software developers really are making a difference in the world!' and now for the life of me I can't read it as anything other than an ominous warning that we just weren't smart enough to translate properly at the time.
Not on a platter, for bullshit monetization – but software that helps people be the best version of themselves.
By helping others become better programmers, you too would become a better programmer. It's unavoidable.
Software is easy to change, but people... aren't.
The key to becoming an experienced software developer is to understand that you are, at all times, your own worst enemy.
Donald Trump is basically a comment section running for president
posts.key_insight: In an era when software's dark side has become undeniable, programmers must refocus their work on the original promise: building tools that help people become the best versions of themselves.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood uses the infamous 2016 Podesta phishing attack as a case study to demonstrate that even security-conscious organizations with trained personnel can fall victim to simple phishing attacks. A single typo—writing 'legitimate' instead of 'illegitimate'—in an IT support email response led to a breach that rewrote history. Atwood argues that computers have become so pervasive that 'computer security' is now just 'security'—something everyone needs to understand, not just tech professionals. He distills security best practices into three essential rules: enable app-based two-factor authentication (never SMS), use passwords of 11+ characters, and use a password manager. For high-risk individuals, U2F hardware keys offer the ultimate protection against phishing.
key_points: The Podesta phishing attack succeeded not through sophistication but through a simple IT support typo, proving that human error defeats even security-trained organizations
Phishing attacks use harvested contacts to build ever-wider attack nets, using real email addresses, subject lines, and content to craft plausible messages
SMS-based two-factor authentication is fundamentally insecure due to interception, social engineering, and SIM-jacking vulnerabilities
Computers are now so ubiquitous that there's no distinction between 'computer security' and security in general—everyone needs basic security hygiene
U2F hardware keys are the only method that makes phishing literally impossible, as proven by Google's 100% success rate company-wide
money_quotes: We now live in a world where this kind of digital mischief is literally rewriting the world's history.
One word. He got one word wrong. But what a word to get wrong, and in the first sentence!
It's basically impossible, statistically speaking, to prevent your organization from being phished.
There is no longer any such thing as 'computer security.' There is only security.
If it's SMS, it's not secure, period.
Anything under 11 characters is basically the same as having no password at all these days.
You never outsource a core competency.
They're cheap, and the good news is that they literally make phishing impossible at last.
posts.key_insight: Computer security has become synonymous with personal security because ubiquitous computing means everyone—not just tech professionals—must adopt practices like app-based 2FA, password managers, and strong passwords to survive in a world where a single typo can change history.

posts.summary: On Stack Overflow's 10th anniversary, Jeff Atwood reflects on the platform's identity and future challenges. He argues that Stack Overflow is fundamentally a wiki for programmers rather than a discussion forum, meaning questions and answers are judged by their utility to future readers, not just the original asker. The duplicate question problem grows exponentially worse as the site scales, making it increasingly difficult for new questions to find a unique niche. The competitive peer review system, while motivating for professionals, creates anxiety that may alienate beginners and students. Atwood suggests the site may need beginner-friendly alternatives with more lenient rules, while acknowledging that Stack Overflow was deliberately designed for practicing programmers, not learners.
key_points: Stack Overflow is a wiki first - content is judged by utility to all future programmers, not just the person asking, which causes most user friction
The duplicate problem becomes exponentially harder at scale: threading a question through millions of existing questions without hitting a 'landmine' is nearly impossible
The competitive peer review system with reputation, downvotes, and closing is ideal for professionals but 'pretty much everything you would not want as a student or beginner'
There are three tiers of users: passive searchers (98%), stuck programmers asking questions, and professional contributors - with tier 2 experiencing the most friction
Stack Overflow may need to split into expert and beginner areas with different rulesets, similar to Math vs MathOverflow
money_quotes: Stack Overflow has probably added billions of dollars of value to the world in increased programmer productivity.
The world is filled with people that basically got lucky, and subsequently can't stop telling people how it was all of their hard work and moxie that made it happen.
I cringe so hard I practically turn myself inside out when people on Twitter mention that they have pointed their students at Stack Overflow.
The best way to motivate any programmer... is to subtly insinuate that another programmer could have maybe done it better.
You could learn how to program on Stack Overflow, in theory, if you are a prodigy who is comfortable with the light competitive aspects... But I suuuuuuper would not recommend it.
I find failure much more instructive, and when building a business and planning for the future, I take on the role of Abyss Domain Expert™ and begin a staring contest.

If Stack Overflow is working like it's supposed to, 98% of programmers should get all the answers they need from reading search result pages and wouldn't need to ask or answer a single question in their entire careers.
The strength of Stack Overflow begins, and ends, with the community of programmers that power the site.
posts.key_insight: Stack Overflow's greatest strength - being a wiki-style competitive peer review system for professionals - is also its greatest barrier to newcomers, and the platform must decide whether to build alternative spaces or accept its limitations.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood makes the case for colocating your own mini-PCs instead of renting cloud servers, demonstrating that you can get dramatically better performance at about one-third the cost over a three-year period. He details his 2019 'scooter computer' build featuring an i7-8750h with 32GB RAM and NVMe storage for $820, then benchmarks it against Digital Ocean droplets to show substantial performance advantages. The post walks through power consumption testing, colocation logistics with EndOffice at $29/month, and a detailed cost comparison showing $2,044 for colocation versus $5,760 for equivalent cloud resources. Atwood acknowledges cloud flexibility but argues that for dedicated long-term computing needs, owning your hardware is both economically smart and satisfying.
key_points: Cloud servers cost roughly 3x more than colocated mini-PCs for equivalent computing power over a multi-year period
The 2019 mini-PC build offers 3x the cores, 2x the memory, and 3x faster storage compared to the 2017 version for only $150 more
Benchmark tests show the mini-PC significantly outperforms Digital Ocean droplets in per-core, multi-core, and disk performance
Three years of colocated mini-PC ownership costs $2,044 versus $5,760 for comparable cloud resources
Mini-PC colocation isn't for everyone - cloud still wins for quick testing, flexibility, and when you don't need dedicated resources long-term
money_quotes: The cloud is just someone else's computer that you pay for the privilege of renting.
What if you could put your own computer 'in the cloud'?
Over the last three years of both experiments, I've concluded that these little boxes are plenty reliable.
This year's scooter computer offers 3× the cores, 2× the memory, and 3× faster drive. It is, as the kids say… an absolute unit.
You pay almost three times as much for a cloud server.

If you do have need for dedicated computing resources over a period of years, then building your own small personal cloud, with machines you actually own, is not only one third the cost but also… kinda cool?
Let's do our part to keep the internet fun and weird!
posts.key_insight: For long-term dedicated computing needs, colocating your own mini-PCs delivers dramatically better performance at one-third the cost of cloud servers, making hardware ownership a compelling alternative to perpetual rental.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that web obesity is primarily caused by advertising, not bloat in general, noting that ad blockers can reduce page sizes by 12x. He points out the irony of Google cracking down on ad blockers in Chrome despite their popularity, while ad blocking companies themselves profit from 'acceptable ads' programs funded by advertisers. As a solution for home networks, Atwood recommends Pi-Hole, a Raspberry Pi-based DNS server that blocks ads at the network level for all devices. The timing is particularly relevant given Chrome's restrictions on ad blocking extensions.
key_points: Web page bloat is primarily caused by advertising - a 12MB NPR page becomes 1MB with ad blocking enabled
Google is restricting ad blockers in Chrome now that they've achieved browser dominance, shifting the balance toward their advertising business
Ad blocking companies are complicit too, with Eye/o GmbH earning 50 million euros annually, including payments from Google's Acceptable Ads program
Pi-Hole provides network-wide DNS-based ad blocking for all devices, bypassing browser-level restrictions entirely
The solution requires minimal investment (~$68) and about an hour of setup time
money_quotes: The bad guy isn't web bloat, per se. The bad guy is advertising.
Through the simple act of running an ad blocker, you've reduced that website's payload by twelve times. That's like the most effective exercise program ever!
It's not hard! We knew how to make small websites in 2002. It's not like the secret has been lost to history, like Greek fire or Damascus steel.
I bet if you went to a client and presented a 200 kilobyte site template, you'd be fired.
Now that Google Chrome is the dominant browser, it is in a better position to shift the optimal point between the two goals which benefits Google's primary business.
It's kind of scary how powerful DNS can be, isn't it?
Unlimited, unfettered ad 'tech' has creeped into everything and subsumed the web.
posts.key_insight: Advertising is the true cause of web obesity, and as browser-based ad blocking becomes restricted by companies like Google, network-level solutions like Pi-Hole offer a way to reclaim a fast, lean web experience.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood, a self-described non-car person, chronicles his unexpected conversion to electric vehicle evangelism after purchasing a 2019 Kia Niro EV. He walks through the practical realities of family vehicle ownership, from station wagons to minivans, before making the leap to fully electric. The post details how the EV experience transformed his entire worldview on transportation, from the cleanliness and silence to the digital-first design philosophy. He traces the history of electric vehicles from the failed GM EV1 through the modern era enabled by lithium-ion batteries, arguing that EVs have crossed the threshold from experimental curiosity to genuinely superior technology. The conclusion is unequivocal: gas cars now feel obsolete, and the future is inevitably electric.
key_points: Electric cars represent a fundamental shift in the driving experience - they're cleaner, quieter, and feel more like spacecraft than traditional vehicles
The digital-first nature of EVs appeals to programmers and tech people in ways gas cars never could, feeling more like giant smartphones than mechanical machines
Home charging eliminates the need for gas stations entirely for most drivers, potentially making them as obsolete as phone booths
Mechanical simplicity (one moving part vs hundreds) means inherently better reliability and lower maintenance costs
The EV tipping point was lithium-ion batteries - price and capability are now competitive with gasoline vehicles
money_quotes: I've somehow gone from being a person who basically doesn't care about cars at all… to being one of those insufferable electric car people who won't shut up about them.

Electric cars feel like spacecraft because they move so effortlessly. There's virtually no delay from action to reaction, near immediate acceleration and deceleration… with almost no sound or vibration at all, like you're in freakin' space!
This electric car is squarely in the post-iPhone world of everyday digital gadgets. It feels more like a giant smartphone than a car.
I am a programmer, I'm a digital guy, I love digital stuff. And electric cars are part of my world, rather than the other way around.
Your house is now a 'gas station.'

Driving a car powered by barely controlled liquid fuel detonations used to be normal. But in a world of more and more viable electric vehicles this status quo increasingly starts to feel… deeply unnatural.
Gas cars seem, for lack of a better word, obsolete.
I have seen the future, and it is absolutely, inexorably, and unavoidably… electric.
posts.key_insight: Electric vehicles aren't just cleaner cars - they represent a paradigm shift that makes gasoline vehicles feel like obsolete relics of a pre-digital era.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood examines the dramatic price drop in electric scooters, using the Xiaomi M365 as a case study to demonstrate how declining lithium-ion battery costs are making electric vehicles increasingly accessible. He reviews his personal experience with the M365, finding it fun and practical at around $300, while discussing regulatory confusion, safety considerations, and the broader implications for urban transportation. The post contrasts the current electric scooter revolution with the failed promise of the Segway, arguing that affordable scooters are finally delivering the transformational technology that the $5,000 Segway was supposed to provide two decades ago.
key_points: Electric scooter prices have halved in three years (from ~$800 to ~$400 for the Xiaomi M365), directly reflecting declining lithium-ion battery costs
Lead-acid batteries killed early electric vehicles; lithium-ion maturation enabled the current electric everything revolution
46% of Uber rides are under 3 miles, suggesting massive potential for scooters to displace car trips
Regulatory frameworks are inconsistent and often illogical, with California requiring driver's licenses for scooters but not e-bikes
The 15 mph speed limit is appropriate - faster scooters become genuinely dangerous even for experienced riders in full safety gear
money_quotes: What killed the electric scooter back then is the same thing that killed the electric car of year 2000: terrible lead-acid battery technology.
The price of a popular, common electric scooter has halved in three years.
This electric scooter is incredibly fun, works great, and if you can get it for a price around $300, practically a no-brainer.
Pretty cool how electric vehicles are inherently digital, isn't it?
A Segway was $5,000 at launch in 2001, which is a whopping $7,248 in inflation adjusted dollars.
Here in 2019, cheap $200 to $300 electric scooters are basically the transformational technology the Segway was supposed to be, aren't they?
I do like where we're headed, even if it took us twenty years to get there.
posts.key_insight: Cheap electric scooters represent the democratization of personal electric transportation that the overhyped Segway promised but failed to deliver, made possible by two decades of lithium-ion battery improvements.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood documents his first PC upgrade in 5 years, shifting focus from raw performance gains to building powerful systems in smaller form factors. He compares several mini-ITX cases, ultimately choosing the Streacom DA2 for its balance of size and cooling capacity. The post details his high-end build specs and offers practical advice on case configuration, airflow optimization, and the tradeoffs of cramming powerful components into compact spaces. He concludes that miniaturization, not speed, represents the primary innovation in PC building for 2020 and beyond.
key_points: PC upgrades have become boring enough that 5 years passed between builds, reinforcing the idea that the PC era is waning
The future of PC building is shifting from more speed to smaller form factors that maintain full power
The DAN A4 SFX at 7.3 liters may be the smallest possible standard mini-ITX build, but the larger DA2 offers better cooling for high-end components
Removing the dust mesh panels for open-air cooling dramatically improved temperatures in the DA2
Modern PC building has become remarkably simple - installing M.2 drives, memory, CPU, and cooler on the motherboard gets you most of the way done
money_quotes: It took 5 years for me to muster up the initiative to get my system fully upgraded!
I guess that's yet another sign that the PC is over, because PC upgrades have gotten really boring.
The future of PCs may not necessarily be more speed, but in smaller builds.
The attraction here is maximum power in minimum size.
About 50% more volume for considerably more than 2× the power isn't a bad deal!
I had dramatically better temperatures after I did this, and it also made the build easier since the case can fully 'breathe' through the top and bottom.
If you haven't built a PC system recently, it's easier than it has ever been.
Perhaps that's the primary innovation in PC building for 2020 and beyond – packing all that power into less than 20 liters of space!
posts.key_insight: As raw PC performance gains have plateaued in relevance, the new frontier of enthusiast PC building has become achieving maximum power in minimum physical space.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces a collaborative open-source project to modernize the classic 1978 book 'BASIC Computer Games' by David H. Ahl, which he credits with shaping his entire programming career. The book was historically significant as the first computer book to sell a million copies, at one point outnumbering the personal computers that existed. Atwood criticizes a 2010 SmallBasic port for merely removing line numbers without applying modern programming practices like subroutines. The new project on GitHub aims to port all 101 games to 10 memory-safe modern languages including Python, Java, C#, JavaScript, and Rust. Atwood frames it as preserving precious computing artifacts while teaching modern programming practices, offering to donate $5 per contribution to Girls Who Code.
key_points: Early personal computers booted directly to command lines with BASIC interpreters, and users often had to type in entire programs by hand from books
BASIC Computer Games was the first computer book to sell a million copies, with more copies in print than personal computers existed at the time
A 2010 attempt to modernize these programs with SmallBasic failed to apply modern practices like subroutines
The new project ports games to memory-safe languages: Java/Kotlin, Python, C#, VB.NET, JavaScript, Ruby, Perl, Lua, and Rust
The project emphasizes collaborative development as a contrast to the isolated experience of typing programs from books in the 1980s
money_quotes: Those computers booted up to the command line.
Yep, believe it or not, circa 1983, this was our idea of a good time. No, we didn't get out much.
There were more copies of this book in print than there were personal computers, period!
Certainly we can do a bit better than merely removing the line numbers?
I think we owe it to the world to bring this book up to date using modern, memory safe languages that embody the original spirit of BASIC.
They are precious artifacts of early computing that deserve to be preserved for future generations.
Imagine we're all typing these programs in simultaneously together online, all over the world, instead of being isolated alone in our room in 1984, cursing at the inevitable typo we made somewhere.
posts.key_insight: Classic programming texts deserve active preservation through modernization that applies contemporary best practices, not just superficial syntax updates.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces a $10,000 charity bet with John Carmack about whether fully autonomous (SAE Level 5) self-driving cars will be commercially available in major US cities by January 1st, 2030. Atwood is betting against, arguing that people consistently underestimate the difficulty of achieving true full autonomy. He clarifies he's not opposed to autonomous vehicles—he'd love to have one—but sees the technical challenge as immense. The post also includes a tangential dismissal of VR as a world-changing technology, suggesting AR and projection have more near-term potential.
key_points: The bet requires SAE Level 5 autonomy: the vehicle handles ALL driving tasks under ALL conditions with zero human attention required
Atwood frames his skepticism as a challenge to the tech community, not opposition to the technology itself
The bet's scope is limited to top 10 US cities by population, with inflation adjustment provisions
Atwood is even more pessimistic about VR, claiming it won't be transformative 'in our lifetimes'
The wager is positioned as STEM publicity, with proceeds going to charity
money_quotes: I think everyone is underestimating how difficult fully autonomous driving really is.
I'd much rather spend my time in a vehicle reading, watching videos, or talking to my family and friends… anything, really, instead of driving.
VR just… isn't going to happen, in any 'changing the world' form, in our lifetimes.
Prove me wrong! Make it happen by 2030, and I'll be popping champagne along with you and everyone else!
A human passenger enters the vehicle and selects a destination. Zero human attention or interaction is required during the journey.
posts.key_insight: The gap between impressive demos of autonomous driving and true Level 5 autonomy—handling every edge case in every condition—represents a difficulty cliff that the industry chronically underestimates.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood argues that Elon Musk's destruction of Twitter, while tragic, ultimately revealed the fundamental danger of centralized platforms controlled by single individuals. He reflects on his long history with Twitter since 2007 and mourns its transformation under Musk's erratic management. Atwood contends that the debacle should push users toward decentralized, federated alternatives like Mastodon that empower users rather than exploit them. He frames this as a choice between digital sharecropping under billionaire whims versus the messier but more sustainable path of collective ownership. The post concludes with a charity auction benefiting LGBTQ youth, turning his Twitter critique into direct action.
key_points: Centralized platforms controlled by single individuals pose existential risks to communities and content, as demonstrated by Twitter's decline under Musk
The 'free' services model treats users as digital sharecroppers with no real ownership or control over their communities
Decentralized, federated alternatives like Mastodon represent the only sustainable long-term path forward for social media
Twitter's destruction, while sad, served as a valuable lesson about platform governance and the dangers of autocratic control
Building and maintaining healthy online communities requires work and isn't truly 'free' - the costs are just hidden in centralized platforms
money_quotes: It's been clear for quite some time that the early social media strategery of 'jam a million people in a colosseum and let them fight it out with free speech' isn't panning out.
Being beholden to the whims of a billionaire going through a midlife crisis isn't exactly healthy for society. Or you. Or me. Or anyone, really.
Elon unwittingly did us a favor by killing Twitter. He demonstrated the very real dangers of any platform run by a king, a dictator, a tyrant, a despot, an autocrat.
It's imperative that we also build decentralized services which empower users and give them control, rather than treating them like digital sharecroppers.
Those so-called 'free' services aren't really free.
Is it messy? Hell yes it's messy. But so is democracy; it's worth the work, because it's the only survivable long term path forward.
In the software industry, our goal should be to empower users, not exploit them.
posts.key_insight: The collapse of Twitter under autocratic leadership proves that decentralized, user-empowering platforms aren't just idealistic—they're the only sustainable architecture for online communities.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood explores the Fermi paradox—the contradiction between the high probability of extraterrestrial life and our complete lack of evidence for it. He draws a parallel to the absence of time travelers as similar evidence against the possibility of time travel. After surveying the Great Filter theory's explanations for why civilizations might fail to reach interstellar capability, Atwood proposes his own theory: advanced life doesn't build bigger, it builds smaller. Just as computers evolved from room-sized machines to pocket devices, truly advanced civilizations may have miniaturized themselves into the spaces between atoms and moments, becoming invisible to our crude detection methods.
key_points: The Fermi paradox presents a compelling logical puzzle: given the universe's age and size, Earth should have been visited by now
The absence of time travelers from the future may similarly suggest time travel is impossible
The Great Filter theory proposes that some stage of evolution is nearly impossible to pass, explaining the silence
Advanced technology trends toward miniaturization, not expansion—smaller is faster, more efficient, and requires less energy
Truly advanced civilizations may have no need to rearrange stars when they can engineer at the nanoscale
money_quotes: With a 13 billion year head start on evolution, why haven't any other forms of life in the universe contacted us by now?
It seems, to me at least, clear evidence that time travel is not possible, given the enormous amount of time behind us.
Like computers themselves, things don't get larger. They get smaller. And faster. And so does intelligent life.
Why build planet-size anything when the real action is in the small things?
Large is inefficient and unnecessary. Look at the history of computers: from giant to tiny and tinier.
It could be that it's the engineering of the small, rather than the large, that is inevitable.
If we can barely see far in the universe as is, there's no way we could possibly see into the infinite space and time between.
posts.key_insight: Advanced civilizations may become invisible not because they've died out, but because they've evolved beyond the large-scale engineering we're looking for—miniaturizing into the infinite spaces between atoms and moments where we cannot detect them.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces his family is donating $8 million immediately to eight nonprofits and pledging half their remaining wealth over five years to preserve the American Dream. He reflects on his hardscrabble origins, his parents' struggle with alcoholism, and how building Stack Overflow and Discourse created unexpected wealth. The post argues that rising costs of housing, healthcare, and education combined with extreme wealth concentration threaten the foundational promise of America. Atwood connects his work building digital democracies to the broader need for empathy and community, ultimately calling on others to support both immediate charitable needs and longer-term democratic reforms.
key_points: The costs of housing, healthcare, and education have risen dramatically beyond inflation—college tuition at state schools increased 8x in 30 years—making the American Dream increasingly unattainable
Extreme wealth concentration (top 1% controlling 32% of wealth while bottom 50% has 2.6%) is fundamentally incompatible with American ideals
42% of American adults did not vote in 2024, representing a crisis of democratic participation that needs solutions like making election day a national holiday and implementing ranked choice voting
Stack Overflow and Discourse represent digital democracy experiments that prove communities can govern themselves through voting, moderation, and shared empathy
Wealth creates distance from others' struggles—the more you have, the clearer inequality becomes, which compels action rather than hoarding
money_quotes: They taught me how to fully, deeply receive love, and the profound joy of reflecting that love upon everyone around you.
We built a digital democracy – of the programmers, by the programmers, for the programmers.
Digital democracy and empathy, for everyone.
In order for digital democracy to work, we need to see each other through our screens.
It was only after I attained the dream that I was able to fully see how many Americans have so very little.
The more wealth you attain, the more unmistakably clear it becomes how unequal life is for so many of us.
It is, for lack of a better word, unamerican.
Our family pledges half our remaining wealth toward an American Dream founded on love.
posts.key_insight: The American Dream is not complete when achieved individually—it only becomes meaningful when actively shared with others through both immediate charitable action and long-term systemic change.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces an upcoming talk with Alexander Vindman at Cooper Union's Great Hall, framing it as a continuation of his 'Stay Gold' essay about preserving American ideals. The post positions both speakers as defenders of democracy from different vantage points—Vindman through military service and whistleblowing, Atwood through building online communities. Atwood draws parallels between governing nations and moderating forums, arguing both require clear expectations, fair systems, and shared purpose. The core message is a call to move beyond individual charity toward systemic change that creates opportunity for everyone.
key_points: The American Dream in 2025 feels like a question rather than a promise, requiring active effort to maintain
Running countries and running online forums share the same fundamental requirements: clear expectations, fair systems, strong boundaries, and shared purpose
Building self-governing digital communities at scale turned out to be 'far more relevant to the future of democracy' than Atwood anticipated
The goal should be moving beyond individual generosity toward systems that create security, dignity, and possibility for all
Staying gold isn't passive—it requires work, action, and hard conversations
money_quotes: The American Dream is, at its core, the promise that hard work, fairness, and opportunity can lead to a better future. But in 2025, that promise feels like a question: How can we build on our dream so that it works for everyone?
Whether you're running a country or running a forum, the same rules seem to apply: people need clear expectations, fair systems, strong boundaries, and a shared sense of purpose.
That decision cost him his career but never his integrity.
Staying gold isn't passive. It takes work. It takes action. It takes hard conversations that ask us to confront where we've been, where we are, and who we want to be.
How to move beyond individual generosity and toward systems that create security, dignity, and possibility for all.
Building self-governing digital communities that scale turned out to be far more relevant to the future of democracy than I ever would have dreamed possible.
Imagine what we could do if we all worked together.
posts.key_insight: The lessons learned from building fair, self-governing online communities may be directly applicable to strengthening democratic institutions and creating systemic opportunity for all.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces a major philanthropic initiative focused on Guaranteed Minimum Income (GMI) as the key to restoring the American Dream. Drawing from his own journey from poverty to tech success, he argues that extreme wealth concentration has created a 'Second Gilded Age' where too many Americans lack the foundation to pursue opportunity. He outlines his family's pledge to donate half their wealth, with $50 million specifically funding GMI studies in rural communities including his parents' home counties. The initiative partners with GiveDirectly and OpenResearch to gather scientific data on how direct cash transfers can unlock human potential in America's poorest regions.
key_points: America has entered its greatest period of wealth concentration ever, surpassing even the original Gilded Age, with the top 1% controlling 32% of wealth while the bottom 50% has only 2.6%
GMI has historical precedent in successful American programs: Social Security cut senior poverty from 50% to 10%, and the Earned Income Tax Credit lifts millions out of poverty annually
The 2023 OpenResearch study showed that when poor people receive cash, they consistently share it with others in need, creating network effects that strengthen entire communities
Rural communities are the focus because they have higher poverty rates, fewer opportunities, and smaller populations ideal for controlled studies that can scale
Economic security is framed not as charity but as investment in untapped American potential and the foundation of democracy itself
money_quotes: We can no longer say 'Gilded Age.' We must now say 'The First Gilded Age.'
The American Dream isn't about us succeeding, alone, by ourselves, but about connecting with each other and succeeding together as Americans.
We cannot merely attain the Dream. The dream is incomplete until we share it with our fellow Americans.
Here in the land of opportunity, the pursuit of happiness has become an endless task for too many.

A little bit of money is incredibly transformational for people in poverty – the people who need it the most – the people who cannot live up to their potential because they're so busy simply trying to survive.
Economic security is not charity. It is an investment in vast untapped American potential in the poorest areas of this country.
A system where there are no guarantees creates conflict. It creates inequality.
Equality is connection, and connection is more valuable than any product any company will ever sell you.
posts.key_insight: The American Dream only reaches its full potential when shared, and Guaranteed Minimum Income is the most direct path to giving all Americans the foundation they need to pursue it.

posts.summary: Jeff Atwood announces the Rural Guaranteed Minimum Income Initiative (RGMII), his 'third and final startup,' funded by pledging $50M — half his remaining wealth — to address systemic poverty in America. Building on $21M already donated to various nonprofits, the initiative focuses on rural counties where dollars stretch further and poverty is more prevalent. The program funds direct cash transfers to families in generational poverty across three initial counties in West Virginia, North Carolina, and Mississippi, with ambitions to reach all 50 states. Atwood frames GMI as an evidence-based improvement over Universal Basic Income, citing study data showing cash transfers effectively meet basic needs and reduce hardship. He emphasizes open data, replicable science, and trust-based philanthropy modeled on venture capital funding of existing organizations like GiveDirectly and OpenResearch.
key_points: Atwood and his partner have donated $21M to nonprofits and pledged an additional $50M — half their remaining wealth — to fund guaranteed minimum income studies in rural American counties

GMI improves on UBI by directing cash to those who most need it rather than distributing universally, and existing study data consistently shows cash transfers work to reduce hardship and meet basic needs
The initiative targets rural counties specifically because dollars go further, poverty is more prevalent, populations enable tighter studies, and rural communities are chronically overlooked
All study data will be open and shared globally through UBIdata.io, following Atwood's pattern of open knowledge from Stack Overflow and Discourse
The program emphasizes 'trickle up' economics — research shows dollars targeted to lower-income families are spent quickly and reduce hardship, unlike trickle-down tax cuts which increase inequality
money_quotes: We have everything we need; how do we make sure everybody has what they need?
When, exactly, is enough?
You can't take a completely short term view and fight each individual fire reactively, as it comes. You'll never stop firefighting.
When you show someone what it feels like to just not be hungry for a little while, their view of the world changes. They feel trusted. They see possibility.
"Trickle up" economics works, whereas "trickle down" tax cuts for the rich increase income inequality and provide no significant effect on growth or jobs.
This is my third and final startup.
Simply giving money to those most in need is perhaps the most radical act of love we can take on... and all the data I can find shows us that it works.
Dreams, like happiness, are only real when shared.
posts.key_insight: Atwood argues that direct cash transfers to rural Americans in poverty are the most effective, evidence-based form of philanthropy, and commits half his wealth to proving it at scale through open, replicable science.

posts.summary: This appears to be a placeholder or stub entry pointing to the Stack Exchange website rather than an actual blog post with content. The entry contains only metadata (title, URL, slug) without any substantive text or arguments to analyze. Stack Exchange is the network of Q&A sites that grew out of Stack Overflow, which Jeff Atwood co-founded in 2008 with Joel Spolsky.
key_points: This is a URL reference to stackexchange.com rather than a blog post
No actual content is present for analysis
Stack Exchange represents Jeff Atwood's major contribution to developer communities
posts.key_insight: This entry appears to be a metadata stub or placeholder rather than an analyzable blog post.
